PfGDH showed sensitivity 69%, specificity 98%, and AUC 83%, compared to pfhrp2 (72%/100%/86%), pfhrp3 (71%/96%/84%), pfaldolase (29%/100%/64%), and pfldh (19%/100%/60%). Authors conclude pfgdh demonstrates notable diagnostic potential and could serve as a reliable alternative to overcome challenges posed by pfhrp2 gene deletions.

articles.headline: DHA-piperaquine addition to TMP-SMX improves safety in pregnant HIV+ women
articles.title_original: Maternal, Fetal, and Neonatal Outcomes of Additional Dihydroartemisinin-Piperaquine to Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole in Intermittent Preventive Treatment for Malaria in HIV-Positive Pregnancies: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Yunivita V, Maharani AJ, Bernadus CA, Sinaga EIJ, Simanjuntak ER
articles.details: Meta-analysis of three studies (1353 HIV-positive pregnant women) evaluating trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 800/160 mg with and without dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine 40/320 mg three times. DHA-PPQ addition showed non-significant trends toward lower maternal parasitemia (OR 0.83, p=0.44) and placental malaria (OR 0.66, p=0.10), but significantly reduced maternal adverse events (OR 0.78, p=0.003), including low hemoglobin and gastrointestinal events. No significant differences in fetal adverse events or birth outcomes. Authors conclude DHA-PPQ shows promising efficacy with improved maternal safety.

articles.headline: In vitro P. coatneyi culture reveals barriers to human zoonotic infection
articles.title_original: First continuous in vitro culture of Plasmodium coatneyi reveals insights into barriers to human infection.
articles.authors: Suwanarusk R, Chua ACY, Ezenwanne CS, Snounou G, Malleret B, Kosaisavee V, Lombardini ED, Imerbsin R, Sunyakumthorn P, Katakai Y, Sakaguchi M, Kyle DE, Kawai S, Annoura T, Rénia L, Bifani P, Kaneko O, Russell B
articles.details: Report of the first continuous in vitro culture of P. coatneyi, a simian parasite with P. falciparum-like sequestration and zoonotic potential (3.5% human infections identified in Malaysian samples). Culture was established in M. fascicularis erythrocytes for >40 days with 3% parasitemia post-cryopreservation. While P. coatneyi successfully invaded human erythrocytes, asexual development was arrested post-invasion with parasitemia undetectable by day 9, indicating failure to complete the 48-h developmental cycle. Authors identify a putative biological barrier to sustained zoonotic transmission and note the culture provides a platform for studying severe malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: EFFORT trial discusses tafenoquine vs primaquine for P. vivax radical cure
articles.title_original: Dissemination of EFFORT trial results in Indonesia: a stakeholder meeting report on tafenoquine and primaquine for radical cure of Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Pasaribu AP, Jimanto V, Ainur F, Saragih NR, Hadi H, Siagian DSP, Sinaga BJ, Satyagraha AW, Laihad FJ, Diptyanusa A, Ubra RR, Tekege O, Darmayanti L, Herdiana H, Adella J, Mannion K, Curry E, Poespoprodjo JR, Kenangalem E, Burdam FH, Sutanto I, Nelwan EJ, Ganadhi GJ, Shrestha B, Mnjala H, Hawley WA, Prameswari HD, Theodora M, Silalahi FSM, Susanto H, Immanuella F, Puspadewi RT, Fajariyani SB, Samad I, Supriyanto D, Muhafilah N, Isa R, Price RN, Thriemer K
articles.details: Dissemination report of the EFFORT trial, which evaluated safety and effectiveness of high-dose primaquine (7 mg/kg over 7 days) and single-dose tafenoquine (300 mg) versus standard 14-day low-dose primaquine for radical cure of P. vivax across four countries. In Indonesia, both interventions were co-administered with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine. A national stakeholder meeting held in May 2025 discussed evidence gaps regarding tafenoquine with ACTs, feasibility of implementing high-dose primaquine, and the need for strengthened G6PD testing.

articles.headline: Hospital malaria transmission risk from vectors and asymptomatic patients in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Nosocomial malaria transmission risk in hospital wards in Abeokuta Nigeria: entomological and parasitological evidence from secondary healthcare facilities.
articles.authors: Fawole JK, Lawal NF, Babalola AS, Ojo DA, Sam-Wobo SO, Idowu OA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in two secondary hospitals in Nigeria (134 participants: 80.6% inpatients, 19.4% caregivers) assessing nosocomial malaria transmission risk. Malaria prevalence was 74.6% (75.0% patients, 73.1% caregivers), primarily low-density infections, with only 3.7% using bed nets. Entomological surveys collected 714 mosquitoes including 80 Anopheles gambiae s.l. females, of which 81.3% were blood-fed (all human blood), 38.8% were parous, and 2.5% tested positive for P. falciparum sporozoites. Authors conclude the coexistence of asymptomatic parasitemia, infective vectors, and inadequate protection measures indicates substantial nosocomial transmission risk.

articles.headline: RTS,S and R21 vaccines show efficacy against childhood malaria in Africa
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of RTS, S and R21 malaria vaccines in children under five in Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Nurani KM, Kadernani N, Korir E, Estreed MA, Dhali A, Clinton R, Kadernani K, Kipkorir V, Omullo P, Masika M
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 randomized controlled trials (27,178 African children <5 years: 21,589 RTS,S, 5,589 R21) evaluating vaccine efficacy and safety. RTS,S demonstrated 32% efficacy against first clinical episodes (HR 0.68), 29% against multiple episodes (IRR 0.71), and 17-22% against severe malaria (RR 0.78-0.83). R21 showed higher efficacy: 68% against first episodes (HR 0.32) and 67% against multiple episodes (IRR 0.33). Severe adverse event incidence was 11.2% for RTS,S and 3% for R21, with no vaccine-attributable deaths. Authors conclude both vaccines demonstrate protective efficacy with acceptable safety.

articles.headline: Community and healthcare worker perspectives on malaria prevention in South Africa
articles.title_original: Community and healthcare worker contributions to malaria prevention: a culturally tailored behaviour change approach in Jozini, South Africa.
articles.authors: Baloyi RE, Ngcobo S, Kruger T, de Jager C
articles.details: Mixed-method study in Jozini, South Africa assessing malaria knowledge, attitudes, and practices among 384 community members (median age 36, 72% female) and 15 healthcare workers. Community awareness was high (96%), with 92% correctly identifying mosquito transmission; 97% had high knowledge, 79% positive attitudes, 64% good practices. Knowledge was significantly associated with age (χ2=9.61, p=0.01) but not with education or gender, and was not significantly associated with attitudes or practices (p>0.05). Healthcare workers possessed strong technical knowledge but faced operational challenges including irregular training and resource shortages.

articles.headline: ACT-451840 mechanism against drug-resistant P. falciparum MDR1 revealed by cryo-EM
articles.title_original: Structural and mechanistic insights into the inhibition of Plasmodium falciparum MDR1.
articles.authors: Zhao Z, Li J, Wang X, Liu X, Wang N, Xu H, Quan C, Gao Y, Zhang J, Wang X, Guo L, Kato N, Deng D, Jiang X
articles.details: Cryo-electron microscopy structural study of P. falciparum multidrug resistance protein 1 (PfMDR1) in complex with ACT-451840, a clinical phase I antimalarial candidate. Structure determined at 3.42 Å resolution shows ACT-451840 binds the central cavity and locks PfMDR1 in an inward-open conformation, inhibiting basal ATPase activity. Structural comparison with mefloquine-bound PfMDR1 reveals how ACT-451840 resistance mutations paradoxically sensitize parasites to mefloquine. Comparative analysis with human ABCB1 identifies selective inhibition of PfMDR1. Authors conclude the structural basis of ACT-451840's mechanism informs development of new antimalarials against resistant parasites.

articles.headline: Pyrimidine biosynthesis pathway reveals multiple drug targets in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Genetic analysis of pyrimidine biosynthetic enzymes in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rajaram K, Sievert ML, Elahi R, Blauwkamp J, Dillard LB, Absalon S, Prigge ST
articles.details: Genetic analysis of P. falciparum pyrimidine biosynthesis pathway enzymes (aspartate transcarbamoylase, carbamoyl phosphate synthetase II, dihydroorotase) given that P. falciparum depends entirely on de novo pyrimidine synthesis with PfDHODH inhibitors in clinical development. PfATC knockdown was lethal and not rescued by apicoplast metabolic bypass; N-terminal truncation impaired growth despite a predicted apicoplast trafficking peptide. PfCPSII, harboring Plasmodium-specific insertions, was essential for parasite proliferation. Engineering parasites to salvage uracil revealed PfDHO deletion causes uracil auxotrophy. Authors conclude multiple nodes within the pathway are vulnerable drug targets.

articles.headline: Knowledge-practice gap sustains malaria in Cameroonian village
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes and practices towards malaria in a peri-urban Cameroonian village: Persistent silent transmission despite satisfactory awareness.
articles.authors: Joko S, Poumachu Y, Ouko R, Kala-Chouakeu NA, Bamou R, Elanga-Ndillé E, Awono-Ambene P, Antonio-Nkondjio C, Tchuinkam T, Ndo C
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in Emana village, Cameroon with 249 participants (104 with complete sociodemographic data) screened for parasitemia by microscopy. Despite satisfactory knowledge and positive attitudes toward malaria prevention, practices were generally poor and significantly better among women than men (p = 0.014). Overall malaria prevalence was 24.9%, significantly higher in the 5–18 age group (13.25%, p = 0.027). A persistent knowledge-to-practice gap was identified across all subgroups, contributing to silent transmission.

articles.headline: PfEMP1 antibody gaps distinguish cerebral malaria from anemia
articles.title_original: Distinct Plasmodium falciparum Erythrocyte Membrane Protein 1 Domain Cassette-Associated Antibody Gaps Specific to Cerebral Malaria and Severe Malarial Anemia.
articles.authors: Travassos MA, Han P, Coulibaly D, Stucke EM, Zhou AE, Shrestha B, Nakajima R, Jain A, Taghavian O, Jasinskas A, Lawton JG, Laurens MB, Niangaly A, Pike A, Ouattara A, Berry AA, Adams M, Takala-Harrison S, Kouriba B, Kone AK, Rowe JA, Doumbo OK, Lyke KE, Felgner PL, Plowe CV, Thera MA
articles.details: Custom protein microarray with 79 PfEMP1 fragments from reference and sequenced field isolates measured serological responses in Malian children with cerebral malaria (CM), severe malarial anemia (SMA), concurrent CM/SMA, and age-matched uncomplicated malaria controls. Children with severe malaria showed distinct gaps in antibodies to specific PfEMP1 domain cassettes (DC 1, 8, and 13 for SMA; DC 13 for CM). Few PfEMP1 domain cassettes were differentially seroreactive overall, but seroreactivity to domains downstream from these cassettes differed between groups.

articles.headline: Essential RNA regulator PfSR-X2 controls parasite development
articles.title_original: The serine-arginine-rich protein PfSR-X2 modulates human malaria parasite gene expression during the intraerythrocytic developmental cycle.
articles.authors: Hu Y, Zhuang C, Li M, Wu J, Zhou L, Li M, Li X, Du X, Shang X, Bai Y, Zhao D, Chen S, Fan Y
articles.details: CRISPR-Cas9-mediated conditional knockdown of PfSR-X2, a serine/arginine-rich protein, in P. falciparum revealed it is essential for asexual blood-stage growth. Depletion resulted in reduced parasite proliferation and impaired merozoite production. Transcriptomic analysis showed marked stage-specific changes affecting genes involved in RNA metabolism, antigenic variation, host-parasite interactions, and regulatory pathways; the rif multigene family was strongly perturbed while overall var transcriptional pattern remained largely unchanged. PfSR-X2 acts primarily as a post-transcriptional regulator rather than a global splicing factor.

articles.headline: Mitochondrial cardiolipin synthase essential for parasite survival
articles.title_original: A prokaryotic-like Cardiolipin Synthase is essential to maintain mitochondrial function, lipid homeostasis and survival of blood stage malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Islam MM, Anwar MO, Yamaryo-Botté Y, Rath PP, Abbas M, Thakur V, Rathore S, Botté CY, Mohmmed A
articles.details: Inducible knockdown of PfCLS (prokaryotic-like cardiolipin synthase) in P. falciparum disrupted parasite development and was lethal. Loss of PfCLS and reduced cardiolipin content impaired assembly of mitochondrial respiratory super-complexes, and PfCLS-knockdown parasites exhibited hypersensitivity to atovaquone, which targets the mitochondrial electron transport chain. Lipidomic analyses revealed widespread perturbations in phospholipid homeostasis, and quantitative metabolomics showed disruption of the TCA cycle and amino acid levels.

articles.headline: Subclinical malaria common in Ugandan sickle cell patients
articles.title_original: Subclinical malaria in sickle cell disease in Northern Uganda: prevalence, predictors, and hematologic impact.
articles.authors: Matejak NJ, Ngolobe H, O'Connell CT, Otika D, McGowan K, Ogwang Q, Nakyeyune ML, Azrin S, Dieckhaus KD, Bongomin F
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 274 sickle cell disease patients at Gulu Regional Referral Hospital, Northern Uganda (median age 12 years, 54.4% male) conducted June–August 2025. Subclinical malaria prevalence was 23.4% by blood smear or rapid diagnostic test (11.7% smear-positive, 22.3% RDT-positive), with rural residence as the only independent predictor. Smear-confirmed parasitemia was associated with lower hemoglobin (6.87 vs 7.82 g/dL, p = 0.0027), but broader RDT-based definitions were not associated with hemoglobin differences. RDTs had limited clinical utility, possibly reflecting antigen persistence.

articles.headline: LEF1-LAG3 axis regulates CD4+ T cell malaria immunity
articles.title_original: The LEF1-LAG3 axis regulates CD4(+) T cell function during Plasmodium yoelii NSM infection.
articles.authors: Peng W, Liang G, Lu K, Mo F, Xie X, Chen M, Yuan H, Luo L, Wang X, Xu L, Wei H, Li L, Zhao S, Xie H, Pan X, Huang J
articles.details: Murine P. yoelii NSM infection model using single-cell RNA sequencing and flow cytometry showed infection induced splenomegaly and upregulated LAG3 on CD4+ T cells alongside activation markers (ICOS, CD69, Ki67+). These LAG3+ CD4+ T cells displayed an effector phenotype (CD44hiCD62Llo) and concurrent upregulation of co-inhibitory receptors (PD-1, TIM-3, TIGIT). LEF1 (lymphoid enhancer-binding factor 1) expression was significantly downregulated in CD4+ T cells post-infection, and dual-luciferase assays demonstrated LEF1 directly binds the Lag3 promoter as a transcriptional repressor.

articles.headline: Minimal DNA dose maintains sterile malaria liver-stage protection
articles.title_original: A prime-and-trap vaccination strategy to evaluate minimal requirements for malaria liver stage protection in mice.
articles.authors: Watson FN, Reynolds RA, Kalata AC, Shears MJ, Murphy SC
articles.details: Prime-and-trap vaccination strategy using hydrodynamic transfection of DNA-encoded Plasmodium yoelii circumsporozoite protein (CSP) defined minimal vaccine requirements for liver stage protection in mice. Progressive de-escalation of CSP-HDT trapping doses showed sterile protection against sporozoite challenge at doses as low as 5 ng DNA, but protection was lost at ultra-low 50 pg DNA dose, associated with similarly low levels of CSP-specific CD8+ T cells in the liver. Results suggest a threshold of hepatic CSP-specific CD8+ T cells is required for sterile protection.

articles.headline: Decentralized malaria parasite genomics enables global surveillance
articles.title_original: Malaria Parasite Genomics: Decentralization, Diversification, and Development Goals.
articles.authors: Amambua-Ngwa A, Neafsey DE, Volkman SK, Diallo MA
articles.details: This review examines how malaria parasite genomics has evolved from a descriptive, discovery-focused field into a global surveillance system informing public health decisions. The shift has been supported by wider access to sequencing and cost-effective targeted sequencing methods, alongside new applications enabled by decentralization and diversification of genomic data generation. The review outlines key development needs including standardizing data formats, analysis pipelines, and sharing practices, as well as approaches for translating genomic insights into public health action.

articles.headline: Climate warming expands P. vivax malaria risk across Brazil
articles.title_original: Climate change and Plasmodium vivax Malaria Risk in Brazil: Developing adaptive tool for Brazilian Municipalities.
articles.authors: C M Sousa T, Hacon SS, Pedra GU, Lemos CMG, Sallum MAM, Ladeia-Andrade S, Reis FB, Arcoverde G, Alves L, Ometto J
articles.details: Multilevel analysis using logistic Binary Model assessed malaria risk related to Plasmodium vivax under climate change scenarios for 2030 and 2050 under RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5 scenarios for all Brazilian municipalities. Key climate variables were maximum temperature, relative humidity, and Simple Daily Intensity Index (SDII). Maximum temperature (β = 0.35) was the most influential factor, followed by SDII (β = 0.19) and relative humidity (β = 0.12). Under both scenarios, malaria risk grows across Brazil, with greatest impact in the Amazon expanding to Southeast and Northeast by 2050.

articles.headline: Genetic diversity of RIPR invasion protein in P. vivax
articles.title_original: Unraveling the genetic diversity of RIPR, a key invasion protein in Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Cebrian-Carmona J, Gonzalez-Ceron L, Valero-Galvez V, Garrido-Cárdenas JA, Mesa-Valle CM
articles.details: Genetic diversity analysis of RIPR, a key invasion protein in P. vivax malaria.

articles.headline: Hydroxamic acid HDAC inhibitors show antimalarial drug potential
articles.title_original: Hydroxamic Acids as HDAC Inhibitor Drug Leads for Malaria.
articles.authors: Dawood WA, Macdonald JR, Anzenhofer C, Skinner-Adams TS, Reid RC, Kurz T, Fairlie DP, Andrews KT
articles.details: This review examines histone/lysine deacetylases (HDACs) as prospective drug targets for malaria. HDACs control posttranslational lysine acetylation modifications and regulate key Plasmodium processes including transcriptional and developmental control. Comparative analysis of more than 750 hydroxamic acids with published in vitro activity against P. falciparum provided insights into potency, selectivity, and physicochemical properties to guide future drug development. The review emphasizes HDAC inhibitors as a novel antimalarial class to address drug resistance.

articles.headline: Multi-species malaria vectors with widespread insecticide resistance
articles.title_original: Complex non-falciparum transmission and widespread insecticide resistance in Penka-Michel, a hyperendemic malaria focus of the Cameroon highlands.
articles.authors: Kiam BC, Ibrahima I, Mbida JAM, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Abate L, White SJ, V CTN, Tumamo BF, Morlais I, Ayong L, Lin JT, Ali IM, Juliano JJ, Dinglasan RR, Nsango SE
articles.details: 12-month longitudinal entomological study (September 2023–August 2024) in Penka-Michel, Cameroon characterized hyperendemic transmission (annual EIR 839.5 infective bites/human/night) driven by three Anopheles species: An. funestus (43.7%, IR 7.8%, EIR 0.9), An. gambiae (42.9%, IR 5.4%, EIR 0.6), and An. ziemanni (13.5%). P. falciparum predominated (70.2%), but significant non-falciparum burden occurred (P. malariae 27.5%, P. ovale 22.1%). Widespread insecticide resistance with resistant homozygotes in 70% of infected mosquitoes. L119F-GSTe2 mutations associated with P. ovale transmission (IRR = 1.15, p = 0.03) and L1014F-kdr with P. malariae (IRR = 2.2, p = 0.04).

articles.headline: P. falciparum ligand genes show regional expression differences
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum invasion ligand gene transcript profiles in different populations.
articles.authors: Lantero-Escolar E, Stewart LB, Singh B, Claessens A, Amambua-Ngwa A, Conway DJ
articles.details: Analyzed transcript levels of invasion ligands (eba and Rh genes) in P. falciparum from Malaysian (Sabah) and Gambian populations using reverse transcription quantitative PCR. Significant differences found in three ligand genes: eba175 higher in Malaysia, eba181 and Rh2b lower in Malaysia. eba181 transcript levels correlated with parasite multiplication rates in Malaysian isolates but not Gambian ones.

articles.headline: Multiple Plasmodium species imported to Trinidad despite eradication
articles.title_original: Imported malaria in Trinidad, West Indies: Detection of multiple non-endemic Plasmodium species.
articles.authors: Sant C, Mohammed N, Oura C, Sanford B, Kirk S, Ibeleme D, Nicholls S, Georges K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 153 suspected malaria cases in Trinidad (Dec 2018-Dec 2023) with 56 samples (36.60%) positive by both microscopy and PCR. Confirmed importation of P. vivax, P. falciparum, and P. ovale; P. malariae identified in 3 samples, 2 without travel history suggesting local transmission. Headache and nausea were statistically significant risk factors for PCR positivity.

articles.headline: Benin mosquito vectors show heterogeneous distribution and dual disease risk
articles.title_original: Species diversity and human biting rates of malaria and dengue vectors in central and northern Benin.
articles.authors: Hoyochi I, Zoungbédji DM, Konkon AK, Salako AS, Konkon AC, Adjovi RV, Adoha CJ, Fongnikin A, Sominahouin A, Baba-Moussa L, Akogbéto MC, Padonou GG
articles.details: Multimethod entomological survey in Benin collected 2,337 mosquitoes, identifying Aedes aegypti, Ae. albopictus, An. gambiae, and Culex species. 993 breeding sites inspected with 51.0% positive; household containers primary breeding sites for Aedes. Analysis revealed Aedes predominantly diurnal and Anopheles gambiae nocturnal biting; concurrent detection during both periods indicates combined malaria-dengue transmission potential.

articles.headline: HLA-A*33 protects against Toxoplasma but not Plasmodium falciparum
articles.title_original: HLA polymorphisms shape divergent outcomes of Toxoplasma and Plasmodium infection in Eastern Indian HbE/β-thalassemia cohort.
articles.authors: Bhattacharya S, Rahaman M, Suman S, Rout DK, Mondal S, Purwar S, Dhingra B, Shukla PC, Mukherjee G, Gupta M, Guha D, Bhattacharya MK, Dolai TK, Chakravorty N, Mukherjee B
articles.details: Study of 61 HbE/β-thalassemia patients and 50 controls examining HLA polymorphisms and parasite infection outcomes. HLA-A*33 carriers showed significant protection against Toxoplasma gondii with restricted intracellular replication and enhanced CD8+ IFN-γ+ responses, but not against P. falciparum. SPR binding showed A*33 higher affinity to Toxoplasma SAG2C peptide; HLA-C*07 showed potential negative association with P. falciparum and strong binding to P. falciparum MSP3 peptide.

articles.headline: Thrombocytopenia strongly associated with P. falciparum in Ghana
articles.title_original: Association between thrombocytopaenia and Plasmodium falciparum infection in Ghana.
articles.authors: Duneeh RV, Amoada ST, Yankey E, Asante JA, Allotey EA, Akorsu EE, Klugah MA, Ablordey K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 388 participants across 3 Ghana sites (June-Sept 2025) comparing haematological parameters in P. falciparum-positive and negative individuals. Thrombocytopenia showed strongest association with P. falciparum (adjusted OR=11.8, 95% CI: 6.18-24.7, p<0.001), with median platelet counts 152.0 vs 234.0 × 10⁹/L (p<0.001). Model incorporating haematological indices showed superior predictive performance for malaria diagnosis (AUC=77.6%) compared to sociodemographic factors alone (AUC=57.9%).

articles.headline: Ethiopia scoping review of malaria diagnostics methods and innovations
articles.title_original: Current practices and innovative diagnostics for Malaria in Ethiopia: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Bantie GM, Teshome DF, Asres GD, Moges NA, Tadege M, Awoke AK, Berneh AA, Ferede AG, Abebe DL, Tiruneh EK, Melese AA, Sewagegn N, Wubetu GY
articles.details: Scoping review of 13 studies on malaria diagnostics in Ethiopia (84.6% cross-sectional designs). Identified four diagnostic methods: light microscopy, rapid diagnostic tests (RDT), polymerase chain reaction (PCR), and loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP). Each diagnostic has unique advantages and limitations; authors conclude need for tailored, highly accurate, user-friendly diagnostics addressing limitations of each modality to support malaria elimination.

articles.headline: mRDT outperforms microscopy for malaria detection in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Evaluation of microscopy and first response™ malaria Ag PAN/Pf RDT compared to qPCR and hemoglobin levels in pregnant women at delivery in Busia County, Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Kipchirchir D, Wakoli DM, Imbusi G, Makandi R, Chimwani E, Chumbe SA, Ghebremichael M, Ong'echa JM, Bargul JL, Idris AH, Ondigo BN
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 236 pregnant women at delivery in Kenya (April 2023-March 2024) comparing diagnostic methods. Peripheral malaria prevalence: 7.2% (microscopy), 8.1% (RDT), 9.0% (qPCR); placental: 7.2% (microscopy), 15.7% (qPCR). Compared to peripheral qPCR, mRDT showed higher sensitivity (90%) but lower specificity (80%) than microscopy (80% sensitivity, 89% specificity). P. falciparum predominated (81.3% of peripheral cases); anaemia highly prevalent (69.9%) but not significantly associated with malaria in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Madagascar survey reveals malaria-anemia-micronutrient deficiency co-burden
articles.title_original: Burden and Co-Occurrence of Anemia, Micronutrient Deficiencies, Inflammation, and Malaria Among Women, Adolescents, and Children in Madagascar: A National Cross-Sectional Survey.
articles.authors: Rohner F, Rakotovao JM, El-Mallah C, Andrianarison R, Randrenarizo N, Wegmüller R, Rakotovao R, Ravaoarisoa L, Joyeux M, Wirth JP, Annan Z, Petry N, Wieringa FT, Bastaraud A, Randrianirina F, Labonté JM, Abdelnour A, Jones KS, Rezzi S, Rajoelina MT, Ralaimiadana AR, Vololontiana HM, Yasmine LL, Galetti V
articles.details: National cross-sectional survey (2024) across 23 Madagascar regions measuring malaria, anemia, inflammation, and micronutrient biomarkers. Malaria prevalence 4-14%; anemia 42% in preschool children and ~33% in older groups; iron deficiency 26% (preschool), 6-16% (others); zinc deficiency ~33% all groups; riboflavin deficiency 75% (preschool), ~90% (older); vitamin A deficiency 12% (preschool); folate deficiency 12% (adolescent girls), 21% (women). Multiple conditions frequently co-occurred but at levels expected by chance.

articles.headline: Community engagement shaped Nepal HIV, TB, malaria program priorities
articles.title_original: Community and stakeholder engagement in national priority setting and participatory research for HIV, Tuberculosis, and Malaria programs in Nepal.
articles.authors: Neupane S, Shiwakoti R, Aryal L, Nzima M, Lohar I, Sitaula A, Karmacharya U, Paudel K, Bhattachan A, Devkota S, Bhattarai RK, Adhikari B
articles.details: Qualitative study (Jan-June 2020) of community and stakeholder engagement in Nepal across 700 sessions with over 2,000 participants identifying national health priorities. Engagement process successfully incorporated community recommendations into national strategic documents, including HIV testing at border areas and multi-disease diagnostic initiatives. System gaps identified (drug stockouts, stigma, human rights barriers) were reflected in national planning referenced by Global Fund and USAID/PEPFAR partners; government pledged 20% domestic co-financing.

articles.headline: Education and medicine access affect malaria treatment delays in Uganda
articles.title_original: Determinants of time to malaria treatment-seeking behaviour among caregivers of children under five in Uganda: a case study of Apac district.
articles.authors: Odur B, Opio B, Nabulya O
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 240 households in Apac District, Uganda examining treatment-seeking behavior for malaria in children under five. Cox proportional hazards regression identified secondary education as increasing timely treatment likelihood (HR=1.67, p=0.044), while medicine unavailability (HR=0.61, p=0.001) and financial constraints (HR=0.49, p<0.001) significantly delayed treatment-seeking. All caregivers (70% female) eventually sought treatment.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine shows high efficacy for P. falciparum in Mozambique
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine for uncomplicated plasmodium falciparum malaria treatment in mozambique: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Nhama A, Aide P, Torres-Fernandez D, Varo R, Nhacolo A, Bassat Q
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of artemether-lumefantrine (AL) efficacy for uncomplicated P. falciparum in Mozambique including 6 studies with 1,862 participants across all three regions. Pooled uncorrected efficacy 91.0% (95% CI: 84.2-94.6), PCR-corrected 98.3% (95% CI: 96.9-99.1); early treatment failure 0.05%, late clinical failure 2.73%, late parasitological failure 5.3%; recrudescence 1.8%, new infections 8.6%. Authors conclude AL remains highly efficacious first-line treatment.

articles.headline: South Korea's malaria eradication service transformed public health engagement
articles.title_original: Mobilizing Public Health in South Korea: NMES (National Malaria Eradication Service) and its Legacy, 1945-1969.
articles.authors: DiMoia JP
articles.details: Historical analysis of South Korea's National Malaria Eradication Service (NMES), formed in early 1960s to address post-Korean War malaria rise. Examines strategies and pedagogy used to engage Korean public, particularly rural communities, transforming public health from an outside force into a familiar partner; discusses implications for citizen participation in public health mobilization.

articles.headline: Adjuvant supply chains for new TB and malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: Understanding adjuvant supply chains for new TB and malaria vaccines.
articles.authors: Gotham D, Frick M, Harrington M
articles.details: No abstract provided; title discusses supply chain considerations for TB and malaria vaccine development.

articles.headline: Malaria 25.2%, helminths 29.4% in pregnant women, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Malaria and Soil-Transmitted Helminths and Associated Factors Among Pregnant Women Visiting Mizan-Tepi University Teaching Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Duguma T, Fikadu S, Kochito S, Mohammed H, Demango F
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 330 pregnant women in Southwest Ethiopia (March-June 2024) found malaria prevalence of 25.2% and soil-transmitted helminth prevalence of 29.4%, with Ascaris lumbricoides predominant. Rural residence (AOR=2.011, p=0.024), farmer occupation (AOR=4.801, p=0.048), and stagnant water (AOR=2.150, p=0.008) increased malaria risk. Poor handwashing was strongly associated with helminth infection (AOR=6.468-8.826, p<0.01).

articles.headline: PvGAP panel enables P. vivax genomic surveillance
articles.title_original: PvGAP: Development of a globally-applicable, highly-multiplexed microhaplotype amplicon panel for Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Hubbard A, Solares E, Bradley L, Jeang B, Yewhalaw D, Janies D, Lo E, Yan G, Hemming-Schroeder E
articles.details: Developed globally-applicable microhaplotype amplicon panel (PvGAP) with 80 high-diversity targets and 8 epidemiological targets including drug-resistance markers for P. vivax genotyping of polyclonal infections. Field validation with Ethiopian samples and in silico analysis using MalariaGEN Pv4 genomes demonstrated robust amplification and competitive accuracy for relatedness inference across disparate geographic regions. Supports drug resistance surveillance, local vs imported case discrimination.

articles.headline: Plant extract achieves 81.92% mosquito vector repellency
articles.title_original: Phytochemical profiling, metabolomics, and molecular docking studies of Atriplex halimus aerial parts revealing potential insecticidal activity against the malaria vector Anopheles pharoensis.
articles.authors: Elhawary EA, Waheeb HO, Abdelhafiz AHA, El-Waseif AA, Shehata AZI
articles.details: Phytochemical profiling of Atriplex halimus identified 78 secondary metabolites, predominantly flavonoids and triterpenoids. The dichloromethane fraction at 6.67 mg/cm² exhibited 81.92% repellency against Anopheles pharoensis females in bioassays, with molecular docking revealing flavonoid-mediated mechanisms. Study supports natural product-based vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Open data critical for malaria control and equity
articles.title_original: Public Availability of Malaria Intervention Data Will Be Vital for the Acceleration of Its Control and Elimination.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Syed Shah AH, Yadav CP, Rahi M, Sharma A
articles.details: Policy advocacy noting 610,000 estimated malaria deaths in 2024, predominantly in sub-Saharan Africa, and arguing that restricted access to intervention data (diagnostics, antimalarial drugs, nets, vector control tools) hinders research and equitable outcomes. Using India as case study, authors document gaps in district-level data availability and propose standardized platforms to accelerate elimination.

articles.headline: Malaria cases rising in five high-burden African countries
articles.title_original: Turning the tide against malaria in high-burden African countries: Trends, threats, and solutions.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Sharma A
articles.details: Mann-Kendall trend analysis of WHO data (2015-2024) identified sustained case increases in Nigeria (p=0.002), Democratic Republic of Congo (p=0.002), Cameroon (p=0.005), Uganda (p=0.02), and Mali (p=0.04); mortality trends non-significant. Emerging threats include artemisinin and insecticide resistance, pfhrp2 gene deletions, and Anopheles stephensi invasion, compounded by climate change and conflict. Authors recommend strengthened real-time surveillance.

articles.headline: Geospatial modeling identifies 43.1% Malawi district as high-risk
articles.title_original: Mapping malaria risk using geospatial and AHP approaches in Nsanje District, Malawi.
articles.authors: Banda YM, Nyengere J, Saka K, Tholo H, Chisenga C, Njalammano J, Kamanga BN, Gondwe S, Mtewa AG, Abraham A, Kachedwa L, Chirombo J, Nguvulu A, Chinkaka E, Kadewa W, Mvula RLS
articles.details: Geospatial analysis in Nsanje District, Malawi integrated environmental variables (temperature, rainfall, elevation, slope, proximity to rivers) and socio-economic factors (health facility access, population density) using analytical hierarchy process. Temperature and rainfall were most influential hazard factors, with 20.4% and 35.1% classified as high/very high-hazard zones. Overall, 43.1% of district mapped as high-risk and 40.5% moderate-risk.

articles.headline: CDC guidance for investigating locally acquired malaria
articles.title_original: CDC Operational Guidance for Investigating Locally Acquired Mosquito-Transmitted Malaria - United States, 2026.
articles.authors: Ranadive N, Barratt JLN, Dotson EM, Gimnig JE, Lenhart AE, Levine RS, Mace KE, McElroy PD, Raphael BH, Sayre D, Steinhardt LC, Sutcliffe AC, Williams SG, Ridpath AD
articles.details: No abstract provided; 2026 CDC operational guidance document for investigating mosquito-transmitted malaria cases acquired within the United States.

articles.headline: Mathematical model: insecticide + treatment most effective
articles.title_original: Effects of insecticides and awareness on the dynamics of a delayed malaria model: A real-data calibration.
articles.authors: Khatun MS, Paul P, Ghosh D, Das P
articles.details: Time-delayed compartmental model incorporating incubation and awareness-implementation delays, calibrated with observed malaria case data from India, Democratic Republic of Congo, and Uganda. Bifurcation analysis revealed supercritical Hopf bifurcation and periodic oscillations; multiple delays destabilized the system earlier. Combination of insecticide measures and treatment was most effective for controlling infections.

articles.headline: Birthweight, prematurity reduce PCV-10 vaccine response Uganda
articles.title_original: Low birthweight and prematurity, but not malaria chemoprevention, are associated with reduced pneumococcal vaccine immunogenicity in Ugandan infants.
articles.authors: Musinguzi K, Sbarra AN, Bach F, Nankya F, Achom KB, Mwubaha C, Nayebare P, Nansubuga E, Kakuru A, Kizza J, Maato Z, Arinaitwe E, Press KD, Bagaya BS, Tukwasibwe S, Ssewanyana I, Nankabirwa JI, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Takahashi S, Jagannathan P
articles.details: Nested study within MIC-DroP trial of 195 Ugandan infants (high malaria transmission) completing PCV-10 vaccination series. Early-life malaria exposure and perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) were not associated with PCV-10 immunogenicity or seroconversion rates. Low birthweight (<2500g) associated with 82% lower antibody fold-change (p=0.003); preterm birth (<37 weeks) with 79% lower fold-change (p=0.018).

articles.headline: Cytokines IL-10, IFN-γ elevated in severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Interferon-γ and Interleukin-10 Profiles Correlate with Disease Severity in Sudanese Children with Plasmodium falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Hajali TA, Mekki MIE, Alaagib NA, Omer IIA, Yahia RM, Hamdan HZ
articles.details: Hospital-based case-control study of 105 Sudanese children (aged 3 months-16 years): 35 severe P. falciparum malaria, 35 uncomplicated, 35 controls. Plasma IFN-γ and IL-10 significantly elevated in severe vs uncomplicated malaria and controls (p<0.001), with strong positive correlation (rho=0.688, p<0.001). IL-10 (AUC=0.720) and IFN-γ (AUC=0.687) showed moderate discriminatory performance for severe malaria.

articles.headline: DHA-PPQ 100% effective; pfmdr1 CNV detected Indonesia
articles.title_original: The Impact of pfmdr1 Gene Copy Number on the Efficacy of Dihydroartemisinin-Piperaquine in Treating Malaria in West Sumba and Kupang Districts, East Nusa Tenggara, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Irdayanti I, Aswad H, Asih PBS, Rozi IE, Herdiana H, Diptyanusa A, Prameswari HD, Yusuf Y, Bahrani B, Hidayah N, Artika IM, Syafruddin D
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of 41 P. falciparum isolates from dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-PPQ) treated patients in East Nusa Tenggara, Indonesia using real-time PCR for pfmdr1 gene copy number variation (CNV). All 41 patients achieved adequate clinical and parasitological response on Day 42 (100% ACPR), but genetic analysis identified 87.81% single-copy and 12.19% multiple-copy pfmdr1, indicating piperaquine selective pressure.

articles.headline: P. falciparum resistance markers show heterogeneous distribution across Angola provinces
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial resistance in Angola: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Matias D, Chaves C, Cândido AM, Morais J, Antunes ML, Guler J, Nogueira F, Salamandane A
articles.details: This systematic review of 20 studies (2000–2024) examined molecular markers of antimalarial drug resistance in Angola. The pfcrt CVIET haplotype (chloroquine resistance) persisted in northern and coastal provinces; pfdhfr/pfdhps IRNGE (SP resistance) was detected in Cabinda and Zaire. No validated pfk13 mutations linked to artemisinin resistance were found. Pfmdr1 analysis showed declining 86Y and 1246Y frequencies with wild-type NYD and NFD predominating. Low pfmdr1 and pfpm2 CNV prevalence was reported, though recent data indicate emerging amplification in southern Angola.

articles.headline: Proactive vs. fixed-point malaria case management shows comparable benefits in Mali
articles.title_original: Impact of proactive community case management of malaria on malaria prevalence in Bankass, Mali: a cluster-randomised controlled trial.
articles.authors: Thwing J, Doumbia S, Traore A, Sylla M, Sogoba M, Kamara I, Samaké Y, Guindo L, Rapp S, Treleaven E, Chiu C, Whidden C, Kone A, Djimde AA, Johnson AD, Kayentao K
articles.details: In this 3-year cluster-randomized controlled trial covering over 100,000 people in Bankass, Mali, community health workers (CHWs) in the intervention arm visited households proactively twice monthly to offer malaria case management to all ages, while control CHWs offered services from fixed points. At endline, there was no difference between arms in parasite prevalence, fever prevalence, care seeking, or diagnostic test access among children under 5. Intervention CHWs achieved twice-monthly visits in less than half of households.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria in African schoolchildren: 34% pooled prevalence with high variation
articles.title_original: Prevalence and determinants of asymptomatic malaria infection among schoolchildren in Africa: insights from a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mohamed K, Mussema A, Simieneh A, Woldesenbet D
articles.details: This meta-analysis of 33 African countries found pooled asymptomatic malaria prevalence of 34% (95% CI: 28–40%) among schoolchildren, ranging from 5% in Ethiopia to 53% in Cameroon. P. falciparum was most common at 29.3% (95% CI: 21.88–36.73%), followed by mixed infections (2%), P. vivax (1%), P. malariae (1%), and P. ovale (0%). Male gender (OR=1.26), anemia (OR=5.51), not sleeping under bednet (OR=1.99), and previous malaria treatment (OR=3.82) were significantly associated with asymptomatic infection.

articles.headline: Community engagement barriers for indoor residual spraying require human-centered approaches
articles.title_original: Community-level barriers and facilitators of indoor residual spraying for malaria control: a systematic review of qualitative evidence from sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Pitua I, Okorobe PM, Apili L, Nandera KV, Acoo PG, Nantalaga KC, Lwembawo KD, Otto KA
articles.details: This qualitative systematic review of 10 studies from seven Sub-Saharan African countries (2010–May 2024) identified eight major themes affecting IRS adoption: operational/household labor burdens, perceived efficacy concerns, socio-political trust dynamics, programmatic communication, gender decision-making, structural incompatibility, implementation strategy inequity, and health infrastructure endorsement. The review found community engagement shaped by complex social, gendered, and political factors requiring reduced household labor, addressed nuisance insect concerns, respectful implementation, and strengthened community trust.

articles.headline: Apicoplast metal transporter protein identified; important for parasite transmission stages
articles.title_original: Identification of a Putative Metal Transporter in the Apicoplast of Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Nalder SA, Mayank AK, Liou W, Wohlschlegel JA, Sigala PA
articles.details: Using tetracycline-affinity proteomics, researchers identified an uncharacterized transmembrane protein (UCT) targeted to the P. falciparum apicoplast with predicted structure similar to CorA-family metal transporters. UCT is dispensable for blood-stage asexual parasites but knockdown impairs sexual parasite (gametocyte) development, which is critical for mosquito transmission. UCT expression is heightened in mosquito-stage parasites, suggesting broader apicoplast metal uptake mechanisms across parasite development stages.

articles.headline: D-dimer levels associate with severe falciparum malaria; moderate diagnostic accuracy
articles.title_original: D-dimer as a marker of severe disease in imported malaria: a retrospective observational study.
articles.authors: Poloni A, Rizzo A, Giacomelli A, Colaneri M, Arrigo C, Caronni S, Pieruzzi ME, Behring AH, Grosso S, Dolci A, Antinori S
articles.details: In this retrospective study of 85 P. falciparum malaria patients (63.5% male, median age 39 years), 21 (24.7%) were classified as severe malaria. Median D-dimer levels were significantly higher in severe versus non-severe cases (10,943 vs 2245 μg/L FEU; p=0.0005). In multivariable logistic regression, D-dimer was associated with higher odds of severe disease (aOR per 1000 μg/L FEU: 1.14, 95% CI: 1.06–1.23). Using cutoff 4864 μg/L FEU, sensitivity was 0.76, specificity 0.73, and overall accuracy 0.74.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 7.1% in Ethiopia; stagnant water and ITN non-use key risk factors
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated factors of microscopy-confirmed malaria among the general population in Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Faltamo TT, Assele DD, Meskele M, Tadesse T
articles.details: This meta-analysis of 14 studies involving 8,762 participants estimated pooled microscopy-confirmed malaria prevalence at 7.1% (95% CI: 4.9–10.2%) in Ethiopia, with highest rates in Amhara (10.7%) and southwest (7.3%). Significant determinants included stagnant water (OR=6.71), absent indoor residual spraying (OR=5.90), mosquito breeding sites (OR=6.13), travel to endemic areas (OR=5.86), houses with eaves (OR=4.5), malaria history (OR=3.54), and ITN non-use (OR=4.8). Trim-and-fill analysis suggested adjusted prevalence of 8.2%.

articles.headline: PBO-ceiling nets trial evaluates novel house screening for malaria prevention in Kenya
articles.title_original: Evaluating the impacts of piperonyl butoxide-treated ceiling nets on malaria risk among high-risk populations with limited access to conventional LLINs in Homa Bay Country, Kenya: study protocol for a household randomized control trial.
articles.authors: Ko YK, Kagaya W, Kanoi BN, Kongere J, Gitaka J, Minakawa N
articles.details: This trial protocol describes a household randomized controlled trial in Homa Bay County, Kenya, evaluating piperonyl butoxide-pyrethroid (PBO)-treated ceiling nets as a novel house-screening intervention. The trial includes 500 households randomized to three arms: PBO-ceiling nets alone, PBO-LLINs (one per two household members), or both. Primary outcome is Plasmodium infection prevalence by PCR at 12 months; secondary outcomes include entomological indicators (vector density by CDC light traps). The trial addresses evidence gaps for housing modifications as complementary malaria prevention.

articles.headline: Malaria-HIV coinfection burden 9% in Ethiopia; high rates of anemia and low CD4
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria-HIV coinfection and associated adverse outcomes in Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Yeshanew TM, Gidey B, Abebe B, Waktole G, Getie B, Getu T, Birhane N
articles.details: This meta-analysis of 10 studies estimated overall malaria-HIV coinfection prevalence in Ethiopia at 9% (95% CI: 3–15%), with higher burden in 2013–2025 studies (10%, 95% CI: –4–24) versus 2002–2012 (9%, 95% CI: 1–16). Associated clinical conditions showed prevalence rates of 31% (95% CI: 12–50) for severe anemia, 41% (95% CI: –8–90) for hyperparasitemia, and 35% (95% CI: 14–56) for low CD4 counts (≤200 cells/µL). Authors concluded malaria-HIV coinfection represents a significant health burden.

articles.headline: Landscape review identifies 67 malaria RDTs; most high-sensitivity and rapidly deployed
articles.title_original: Advances in Rapid Diagnostic Tests for Malaria: A Landscape Review and Analysis.
articles.authors: Zhang S, Lauff A, Morton JF, Kalanga N, Drain PK
articles.details: This landscape review identified 67 malaria rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) from 27 international manufacturers, with most RDTs simple to use on whole blood, providing results within 30 minutes, and reporting high (≥98%) diagnostic sensitivity and specificity. Although many malaria RDTs have WHO approval, clinical and implementation science studies are needed to determine optimal delivery and care strategies for RDTs in malaria-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Genomic surveillance in Eswatini detects artemisinin-resistant kelch13 cluster and SP resistance
articles.title_original: Genomic Surveillance Reveals Clusters of Plasmodium falciparum Antimalarial Resistance Markers in Eswatini, A Low-Transmission Setting.
articles.authors: Nhlengethwa N, Aranda-Díaz A, Vilakati S, Hubbard A, Routledge I, Lauterbach SB, Makhanthisa TI, De Amaral F, Chisenga M, Mangena B, Sikaala C, Chimumbwa J, Dlamini Q, Raman J, Smith JL, Dlamini SV
articles.details: This genomic surveillance study collected dried blood spots from 437 malaria-positive individuals across Eswatini (March–December 2023) and genotyped 12 resistance-associated genes. A validated artemisinin partial resistance marker, kelch13 P553L, was identified in 4 infections (0.9%, likely importation-linked). The dihydropteroate synthase/dihydrofolate reductase sextuple mutant (high-level SP resistance) was found in 7.8%; multidrug resistance 1 N86 was nearly fixed. Over 60% of infections were polyclonal; no hrp2/3 deletions detected.

articles.headline: Modeling shows larviciding at ≥60% coverage with 80% ITN coverage interrupts transmission
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of larviciding as a supplementary malaria vector control intervention in rural south-eastern Tanzania: A district-level simulation study.
articles.authors: Shirima G, Fairbanks EL, Gavana T, Kiwelu G, Nambunga I, Mlacha YP, Mirau S, Chaki P, Chitnis N, Kiware S
articles.details: This district-level simulation study adapted the Vector Control Optimization Model (VCOM) to evaluate larviciding combined with ITNs in rural southeastern Tanzania, using field data on mosquito mortality before (2016–2017) and after (2019–2021) implementation showing 61% immature mosquito mortality. At 80% ITN coverage, VCOM showed that larviciding coverage ≥60% substantially reduced mosquito densities and entomological inoculation rates (EIR) below 1 ib/p/year (transmission interruption threshold). Sensitivity analyses demonstrated high impact of targeting immature mosquitoes.

articles.headline: Crystalloid protein required for P. yoelii sporozoite infectivity
articles.title_original: A pleckstrin homology domain protein is involved in crystalloid formation in Plasmodium ookinetes and affects the maturation of infective sporozoites.
articles.authors: Tachibana M, Shinzawa N, Baba M, Torii M, Culleton R, Ishino T
articles.details: Study characterized pleckstrin homology domain protein (PyCryPH2) in Plasmodium ookinetes. Gene disruption resulted in irregular crystalloid microstructure and significantly reduced parasites' ability to invade mosquito salivary glands and infect mouse livers, despite normal sporozoite morphology. Findings indicate PyCryPH2 contributes to sporozoite maturation and infectivity.

articles.headline: Presumptive malaria diagnosis persists despite diagnostic capacity in Gabon
articles.title_original: Diagnostic and therapeutic practices for paediatric malaria: Gap between guidelines and clinical reality in south-east Gabon.
articles.authors: Biteghe-Bi-Essone JC, Imboumy-Limoukou RK, Noronha ID, Moukodoum DN, Ontoua SS, Atiga N, Kouna LC, Okouga AP, Lekana-Douki JB, Oyegue-Liabagui LS
articles.details: Prospective observational study of 3,195 febrile children (ages 0-16) from January 2021 to July 2023 in Gabon. Overall 41.6% tested positive by thick blood smear with 21.4% having severe malaria. Despite diagnostic capacity, 86.7% of smear-negative patients received presumptive diagnosis and 98.8% received antimalarials. Symptoms significantly associated with presumptive diagnosis: asthenia (aOR=1.41), headache (aOR=1.71), nausea-vomiting (aOR=1.95).

articles.headline: Meta-analysis finds no significant association between ITN use and falciparum infection
articles.title_original: Impact of Insecticide-Treated Nets on Plasmodium falciparum Infection Rates: A Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Karaca Biçakçi N, Çalmaz A, Ayyildiz Akin M, Tan CS, Barasarathi J, Oyinloye BE, Umidjon A, Egamberdiyev K, Avazmetova I
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 2021-2025 literature examining ITN/LLIN use and P. falciparum infection. Crude meta-analysis of six studies (n=7,466) showed pooled OR=0.67 (95% CI: 0.42–1.07; p=0.078); adjusted analysis of nine studies yielded aOR=0.88 (95% CI: 0.42–1.86; p=0.71). Substantial heterogeneity (I²=79.8%-88.7%) varied by population type and country, highlighting context-dependent associations and need for integrated vector-control strategies.

articles.headline: IPTp compliance low in Sierra Leone; antenatal contacts key to adherence
articles.title_original: Compliance with intermittent preventive malaria treatment in pregnancy at a tertiary hospital in Sierra Leone.
articles.authors: Lahai WK, Vandy AO, Prajitha KC, Fofanah BD, Kamau EM, Owusu H, Squire JS, Barrie HA, Kamara IF, Falama AM, Sesay N, Kamara ARY, Kanu F, Kanu MS, Kamara MM, Sesay MA, Koroma JA, Moses F, Lakoh S, Kenneh S, Grant LF
articles.details: Non-concurrent cohort study of 733 pregnant women using 2024 hospital data from Princess Christian Maternity Hospital, Sierra Leone. Only 8% (62/733) received the recommended minimum three IPTp doses; 55% received first dose. Women with fewer than four antenatal contacts had significantly higher risk of non-compliance (aRR=2.7; 95% CI: 1.6–4.5). Increased antenatal contacts may improve IPTp compliance.

articles.headline: Suboptimal artesunate dosing linked to unfavorable severe malaria outcomes
articles.title_original: Parenteral artesunate compliance and hospital outcomes in children under five with suspected severe malaria in Sierra Leone.
articles.authors: Kamara ARY, Kamara IF, Thekkur P, Falama AM, Sillah-Kanu M, Lahai WK, Fiona OO, George A, Fofanah BD, Sesay N, Tengbe SM, Farma-Grant L, Bah A, Thomas F, Tejan YS, Kanu JS, Lakoh S, Mustapha M, Zachariah R, Chandrasekharan Prathija K, Kenneh SM
articles.details: Cohort study of 735 children under five admitted with suspected severe malaria to a tertiary pediatric hospital in Sierra Leone during 2024. Only 57% (420/735) received correct weight-based parenteral artesunate dosing and scheduling. Hospital outcomes: 87% discharged, 10% unfavorable (59 deaths, 13 discharged against medical advice). Underweight status, multiple convulsions, and incorrect dosing were associated with unfavorable outcomes.

articles.headline: Spatial analysis reveals persistent malaria hotspots in Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Mapping malaria hotspots through spatial and spatio-temporal analysis in Sierra Leone, 2021-2024.
articles.authors: Falama AM, Omoniwa O, Kanu MS, Lahai WK, Kamara ARY, Ameh G, Kamara IF, Fofanah BD, Kenneh S, Kamara MN, Sesay MA, Sesay N, Kanu F, Alwani AA, Thekkur P, Zachariah R, Kanu MS, Lakoh S
articles.details: Geospatial cross-sectional study analyzing 7.4 million malaria cases in Sierra Leone 2021-2024 using DHIS2 surveillance data. Chiefdom-level incidence ranged from 21.2 to >750 per 1,000 population with significant spatial clustering (Moran's I > 0; P < 0.01). Space-time analysis identified high-risk districts (RR=1.2–1.9; P<0.01) and low-risk districts (RR=0.5–0.9; P<0.01). No significant rainfall association; persistent spatial heterogeneity indicates need for targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Correction: comprehensive genomic study of P. falciparum in Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Correction: Comprehensive genomic study of Plasmodium falciparum in Sierra Leone: genetic variation and resistance patterns.
articles.authors: Zhan XJ, Chen SB, Wang TY, Duan YW, Shen HM, Chen JH, Xie JJ
articles.details: Correction notice for comprehensive genomic study of Plasmodium falciparum examining genetic variation and drug-resistance patterns in Sierra Leone. Original work employed whole-genome sequencing to characterize parasite genetic diversity and resistance markers.

articles.headline: Vector ecology and serology reveal spatial malaria exposure variation
articles.title_original: Anopheles ecology and serological evidence of malaria exposure in clinically malaria-free areas of Menoreh Hills Central Java, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Muh F, Qanita NG, Hitipeuw D, Saputra EY, Nuranindita R, Wusono AD, Natanegara S, Daud MM, Rahayu AA, Pratama DA, Putri FA, Syahada JH, Fitriana F, Jun H, Jeon BY, Cahyani NKD, Pandupuspitasari NS, Lee MR, Ju JW, Han JH
articles.details: Cross-sectional study examining Anopheles ecology, larval habitat, host-feeding behavior, and serological evidence across four villages in Menoreh Hills, Central Java during near-elimination phase. Vectors associated with livestock environments with opportunistic host-feeding (human and livestock). Serological findings demonstrated marked variation in antibody responses between villages despite absence of clinical cases, indicating spatial heterogeneity in transmission intensity.

articles.headline: CRISPR platform enables screening of host factors in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Cas9-expressing HC-04 hepatocytes facilitate CRISPR-based analysis of Plasmodium falciparum sporozoite-host interactions.
articles.authors: Verzier LH, Hesping E, Doerflinger M, Herold MJ, Boddey JA
articles.details: Study engineered Cas9-expressing human HC-04 hepatocytes for CRISPR-based functional genomics of P. falciparum liver-stage infection. Validated system with successful guide-RNA-directed gene disruption and optimized traversal/invasion assays for cytometric readout. Disruption of 10 human genes previously implicated in bacterial and viral infections confirmed platform utility for genome-wide screening of host determinants of sporozoite infectivity.

articles.headline: Comment on rapid WGS of cryptic malaria cases in UK travellers
articles.title_original: Comment on Rapid Whole genome sequencing of Plasmodium DNA from cryptic malaria cases in UK travellers provides insights into infection origins, transmission, and antimalarial resistance.
articles.authors: Chen Q
articles.details: Comment on rapid whole-genome sequencing of Plasmodium DNA from cryptic malaria cases identified in UK travellers. Provides insights into infection origins, transmission patterns, and antimalarial resistance markers identified through genomic analysis.

articles.headline: Post-malaria fatigue syndrome resolves within three months
articles.title_original: Frequency and persistence of post-acute symptoms after chikungunya, dengue, Zika, and malaria in travellers: a prospective multi-centre study.
articles.authors: Hamer DH, Loreau JM, Huits R, Díaz-Menéndez M, Grobusch MP, Barkati S, Yansouni CP, Gobbi F, Bottieau E, Chen LH, Duvignaud A, Malvy D, Castelli F, Odolini S, De Pijper CA, Asgeirsson H, Moro L, Yamamoto K, Sánchez-Montalvá A, Zeana C, Kelly P, Piyaphanee W, Bourque DL, McCarthy A, Simon F, Gautret P, Mardon JB, Texier G, Javelle E
articles.details: Prospective multi-site observational study of 109 falciparum malaria patients enrolled from 2016-2021 at 15 GeoSentinel sites with follow-up at 1, 3, 6, 12, and 18 months. At month 3, only 11% (12/109) had persistent symptoms, predominantly fatigue which largely resolved by month 3. At 12 months, 1% (1/109) remained symptomatic. Impaired quality of life at month 3 in only 4% with malaria versus 18-66% for arboviruses.

articles.headline: Subclinical malaria driven by parasite adaptations and host immunity
articles.title_original: Mechanistic insights into subclinical Plasmodium infections: Unveiling the silent drivers of malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Ranjha R, Gupta A, Singh K, Sikka R, Anvikar AR, Gupta H, Bharti PK
articles.details: Review of mechanisms underlying subclinical and asymptomatic malaria that evade conventional diagnostics due to low parasite densities. Key drivers include parasite adaptations (density thresholds, antigenic variation, cytoadhesion, multiplicity of infection, drug resistance) and host factors (naturally acquired immunity, age, pregnancy, genetic polymorphisms, epigenetic influences). Subclinical infections associated with anemia, impaired cognitive development, and adverse pregnancy outcomes.

articles.headline: Micro-mosaic IRS deployment effectiveness for Zambia vector control
articles.title_original: Indoor residual spraying of distinct insecticide formulations in a household-scale micro-mosaic format: Feasibility and effectiveness for malaria vector control in rural Zambia.
articles.authors: Chinula D, Mumba K, Zulu R, Ndhlovu K, Kavishe DR, Hobbs NP, Kiware S, Reed T, Hamainza B, Killeen GF
articles.details: Pilot study of household-level micro-mosaic indoor residual spraying in southeastern Zambia, alternating DDT and clothianidin-deltamethrin formulations between households within macro-mosaic clusters. Over 1 year across 9 health facility catchment areas, CDC light-trap entomological surveillance in 15 houses per area captured over 1620 trap nights with very low Anopheles densities. No significant differences in primary or secondary vector densities across IRS regimes (p ≥ 0.1420). Micro-mosaic IRS offers comparable short-term effectiveness to conventional macro-mosaic deployments and may provide an additional option for managing insecticide resistance.

articles.headline: P. vivax malaria in NYC: rising imported cases among migrants
articles.title_original: An Increase in Imported Plasmodium vivax Malaria in New York City: Clinical and Demographic Trends Following Recent Migration.
articles.authors: Berto CG, Policarpio MA, Vargas-Pena C, Antwi M, Slavinski S, Abdool A, King RN, Kojaoghlanian T, Daily JP, Prasad N, Velasquez MR, Ridpath AD, Coyle CM
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of P. vivax notifications in NYC, 2018–2024, showed cases rising from 6/206 (2.9%) in 2018 to 63/346 (18.2%) in 2023 and 43/250 (17.2%) in 2024. Of 41 hospital admissions, 40 (97.6%) were recent migrants from South America and Asia. Severe malaria occurred in 10 patients (24.4%), with 5 requiring ICU care. Seven patients (17%) experienced relapses, mostly without liver-stage therapy. Timely diagnosis and liver-stage treatment are critical to reducing morbidity and relapses.

articles.headline: Severe falciparum malaria in pregnancy: case and management
articles.title_original: Severe malaria in pregnancy in the United States: A case report and management considerations.
articles.authors: Reyes Lizaola S, Kumari S, Di Francesco L, Chedid J, Cay Martinez C
articles.details: Case report of severe third-trimester P. falciparum malaria (5.4% parasitemia) in a nulliparous patient at 36 weeks gestation presenting with fever, headache, and abdominal pain after Ghana travel. Initial artemether-lumefantrine was escalated to IV artesunate; patient experienced preterm rupture of membranes, worsening thrombocytopenia, and preeclampsia with severe features before vaginal delivery and recovery. Newborn remained stable with no congenital malaria. Case highlights diagnostic delays in non-endemic settings and need for pregnancy-specific clinical management guidance.

articles.headline: Novel aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase inhibitors against falciparum
articles.title_original: Potent reaction hijacking inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum asparagine tRNA synthetase.
articles.authors: Ye X, Esherick LY, Ketprasit N, Narwal SK, Godoy LC, Boonyalai N, Dogovski C, Morton CJ, Rabie T, Famodimu MT, Tai CW, Yeo T, Le LHM, Leeming MG, Laureano De Souza M, Chenu E, Creek DJ, Delves MJ, Birkholtz LM, Duffy J, Lobb K, Durst G, Lee MCS, Fidock DA, Niles JC, Siegel MG, Tilley L, Xie SC
articles.details: Investigation of 4-amino-quinazoline sulfonamides targeting P. falciparum aminoacyl tRNA synthetases, demonstrating nanomolar potency against blood-stage parasites with up to 300-fold selectivity over mammalian cells. Targeted mass spectrometry identified Pf asparagine tRNA synthetase (Pf AsnRS) as primary target; selective knockdown and tolerance selection confirmed target engagement. Compounds show activity against transmissible stages and are refractory to resistance. AlphaFold modeling and recombinant enzyme studies confirm Pf AsnRS susceptibility to reaction hijacking with lower susceptibility in human AsnRS.

articles.headline: Anopheles diversity and zoonotic malaria in Thailand systematic review
articles.title_original: Systematic review of Anopheles abundance and meta-analysis of nonhuman primate malaria infection in mosquitoes in Thailand.
articles.authors: Weerakoon SN, Hii J, Nararak J, Sukkanon C, Ngoen-Klan R, Arunyawat U, Maneeon P, Thongsahuan S, Vythilingam I, Manguin S, Chareonviriyaphap T
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 92 studies (2013–2025) examining Anopheles species and nonhuman primate malaria in Thailand. Nine Plasmodium species documented including emerging zoonotic P. knowlesi and P. cynomolgi; P. vivax and P. falciparum predominated, with P. falciparum declining since 2012. Pooled nonhuman primate malaria infection prevalence was 0.01 (95% CI 0.00–0.03); An. sawadwongporni, An. minimus, and An. dirus showed 0.2% pooled prevalence by PCR. Thailand achieved parasite incidence reduction from 0.53 to 0.22 per thousand (2014–2024).

articles.headline: Deep learning for automated malaria diagnosis from blood smears
articles.title_original: A hybrid machine learning approach for automated malaria diagnosis from thin blood smear images.
articles.authors: Siddiqui GA, Bera A, Lhila A, Rajput AS, Nitika N, Bharti PK, Das A, Mahapatra T
articles.details: Hybrid Inception-v3 CNN trained on 15,938 thin blood smear images from India's National Institute of Malaria Research for automated malaria cell classification with Otsu thresholding for segmentation. Model achieved 98.9% training accuracy, 97.3% sensitivity, 99.9% specificity, and high F1 score and AUC values. Performance outperformed state-of-the-art machine learning classifiers and was comparable to conventional microscopy-based examinations. Results suggest potential to reduce reliance on expert microscopists in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: P. falciparum MSP-2 genetic diversity and multiplicity in Chad
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of Plasmodium falciparum MSP-2 (3D7/IC) and multiplicity of infection in N'Djamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Cedric Y, Diomède N, Hervé MM, Pacome TK, Abel D, Ngaryedji T, Saleh B, Hu H, Christelle Nadia NA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study (July–September 2024) of 217 patients in N'Djamena, Chad examining P. falciparum merozoite surface protein-2 (MSP-2) 3D7/IC allelic family polymorphism. P. falciparum prevalence 23.5%; MSP-2 3D7/IC molecular frequency 94.11%. Single-clone infections most frequent (64.65%), followed by double (33.33%) and triple (2.08%) infections. Phylogenetic analysis identified 8 clades with varying genotype distribution, demonstrating substantial genetic diversity reflecting ongoing transmission in the study area.

articles.headline: Novel Plasmodium species identified in stranded seabird
articles.title_original: A new Plasmodium Marchiafava & Celli, 1885 (Apicomplexa: Haemosporida) species in Cory's Shearwater (Calonectris borealis) [Cory]) (Aves: Procellariiformes) stranded in a coastal area in Brazil.
articles.authors: Dos Anjos CC, Damato BA, Ferioli RB, Sánchez-Sarmiento AM, da Mata AR, Barbosa CB, Neto HG, Chagas CRF, Valkiūnas G, Kirchgatter K
articles.details: First haemosporidian infection in Cory's Shearwater (Calonectris borealis), a migratory Atlantic seabird, found live-stranded on São Paulo coast, Brazil. Blood smears showed parasitemia 0.04%–2.09%; PCR cytochrome b gene 100% similar to Plasmodium lineage pLK06, phylogenetically within Novyella clade and morphologically related to P. vaughani. Necropsy showed hepatosplenomegaly with histopathological hepatitis and pulmonary hemorrhage. Represents first identification of pLK06 in Procellariiformes and description of Plasmodium borealis sp. nov.

articles.headline: Nonmalarial fevers predominate in southern Angola hospital
articles.title_original: Age-Related and Seasonal Variation in Malaria and Other Causes of Fever and Their Association With Clinical Outcomes in Southern Angola: A Hospital-Based Study.
articles.authors: Gonçalves HEM, Antunes ML, Seixas J, Ferreira MU
articles.details: Hospital-based cross-sectional study (September–October 2023, May–June 2024) of 849 febrile patients aged 2 months–94 years in two tertiary hospitals in Lubango, Angola. 67.6% tested negative for malaria; leading nonmalarial causes were upper respiratory (13.0%), lower respiratory (19.5%), and gastrointestinal (16.7%) infections. Malaria and lower respiratory infections independently associated with increased hospitalization (OR = 3.20 and 5.17; p < 0.0001); malaria and CNS infections linked to higher mortality (OR = 1.90 and 6.30; p < 0.0001).

articles.headline: Wolbachia wAnD reduces sporozoite loads in Anopheles demeilloni
articles.title_original: The presence of the wAnD strain of Wolbachia is correlated with lower levels of Plasmodium sporozoites and a less diverse microbiome in wild Anopheles demeilloni mosquito cephalothoraxes.
articles.authors: Sougoufara S, Bandibabone J, Chatterley L, Hughes I, Taberes CM, Ball J, Palliaser T, Dhokiya V, Heinz E, Hughes G, Walker T
articles.details: Quantitative PCR and 16S rRNA sequencing of wild-caught An. demeilloni from Democratic Republic of Congo examined wAnD Wolbachia strain, P. falciparum sporozoites, and microbiome composition. Overall sporozoite prevalence 1.3% (9/704); Wolbachia-positive abdomens showed significantly lower sporozoite rates than negative (p = 0.0329), with P. falciparum detected only in Wolbachia-negative cephalothoraxes. Cephalothorax microbiome composition differed significantly between Wolbachia-positive and negative (p < 0.05). Results suggest wAnD potential for malaria biocontrol.

articles.headline: Malaria and leishmaniasis epidemiology in Thailand systematic review
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of malaria and leishmaniasis in Thailand (2004-2025): A systematic review.
articles.authors: Apajamjarut W, Siriwongsanon N, Tonpon P, Boonlertworakul L, Joosiriwong R, Soe BK, Kaewmee S, Saingamsook J, Walton C, Lee SJ, Lin C, Dimopoulos G, Jariyapan N
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: PRISMA-compliant systematic review of 57 studies (2004–2025) on malaria and leishmaniasis in Thailand. Nine Plasmodium species documented including zoonotic P. knowlesi and P. cynomolgi; P. vivax and P. falciparum predominated, with P. falciparum declining since 2012. Six Leishmania species identified, L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis predominant in HIV-positive people. Seven provinces reported both diseases; shared risk factors include forest occupation, immunocompromised status, and population mobility. Integrated One Health surveillance recommended.

articles.headline: IL-6 plasma levels as discriminatory biomarker in malaria
articles.title_original: Plasma haem oxygenase-1 and interleukin-6 as adjunct host biomarkers associated with malaria.
articles.authors: Wakai T, Kintung I, Ogundimu T, Chinedu S, Afolabi I
articles.details: Cross-sectional study at Nigerian tertiary hospital comparing 304 participants (152 malaria-positive, 152 negative). Malaria-positive had significantly lower hemoglobin, hematocrit, RBC count, and platelets with higher temperature and WBC (all p < 0.001). In biomarker subset (n=40), IL-6 significantly elevated in malaria-positive (median 141.28 vs 106.58 pg/mL; p = 0.027); HO-1 non-significant (230.98 vs 193.73 ng/mL). IL-6 ROC AUC 0.709 (95% CI 0.531–0.874) with 72.7% sensitivity, 76.5% specificity, superior for discriminating malaria.

articles.headline: Testing natural and synthetic repellents against coastal malaria mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Do natural or synthetic excito-repellents work better? A study on coastal malaria vector Anopheles epiroticus in Ko Chang, Thailand.
articles.authors: Sukkanon C, Jabjai S, Ritthison W, Chareonviriyaphap T, Nararak J
articles.details: Study evaluated excito-repellency of four synthetic and two natural agents against laboratory and field strains of Anopheles epiroticus in Ko Chang, Thailand. For laboratory strain: deltamethrin (42%) and permethrin (35%) most effective as contact irritants; citronella oil (>50%) demonstrated strongest non-contact repellency. Field strain showed different pattern: DEET (64%) and deltamethrin (60%) most effective in contact; citronella and vetiver oils (~14%) had minimal non-contact effects. Results indicate importance of accounting for behavioral variation in vector control strategy design.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance may enhance or impair vector competence
articles.title_original: Insecticide resistance and malaria transmission: divergent effects on Anopheles vector competence.
articles.authors: Adeboye SO, Nwinyi OC, Ayanda OI
articles.details: Review of recent knowledge on effects of insecticide resistance on vector competence in Anopheles populations. Notes conflicting findings: some studies report parasite development impairment in mosquitoes exposed to insecticides, while others found vector competence enhanced by insecticide exposure. Relationship between insecticide exposure in mosquitoes and vector competence remains poorly understood. Authors argue integration of omics-based approaches embracing ecological reality is crucial for addressing contradictions and accurately predicting epidemiological implications of insecticide resistance.

articles.headline: Genetic resistance to pyrethroids increases in Kenyan malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Signature of resistance gene evolution and pyrethroid resistance escalation in the major malaria vector Anopheles funestus across Kenyan malaria-endemic regions separated by the Rift Valley.
articles.authors: Tchouassi DP, Mustapha AM, Rotich G, Milugo TK, Musimbi B, Oruni A, Tagne CSD, Kamau L, Gadji M, Tchouakui M, Torto B, Wondji CS
articles.details: Examined Plasmodium infection rates and resistance alleles in Anopheles funestus from western Kenya, coastal, and Kerio Valley regions. The 4.3kb-SV and G454A-Cyp9k1 alleles nearly fixed in western Kenya but declined toward Rift Valley and coast; L119F-GSTe2 increased across west-to-coast gradient. L119F-GSTe2-RR genotype associated with lower odds of Plasmodium infection (OR 0.2, P=0.046); mosquitoes with 4.3kb R allele had higher infection rates (OR 5.7, P=0.049). High pyrethroid resistance observed with intensity higher in Kerio Valley than western Kenya. Coastal populations showed extreme permethrin resistance (<10% mortality at 10× diagnostic dose).

articles.headline: P. falciparum myosin protein shows novel Rab-like domain structure
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum PfMyoF contains a Rab-like tail domain and associates with perinuclear membrane trafficking proteins.
articles.authors: Holmes AJ, Ilani P, Batters C, Kemp A, Aniweh Y, Amenga-Etego LN, Awandare GA, Rayner JC, Buss F
articles.details: Characterized PfMyoF, a class XXII myosin, using structural prediction, biochemistry, and imaging. PfMyoF is a plus-end-directed, processive motor with long neck region capable of binding up to six copies of calmodulin homologue PfCaM. The PfMyoF tail contains predicted WD40 and Rab-like domains not previously identified in any other eukaryotic myosin class. Pull-down experiments identified interactions with trafficking proteins including vesicle marker PfRab18. Localizes to perinuclear membrane compartment. Transient knockdown using glmS ribozyme system impairs growth, suggesting important function during asexual replication.

articles.headline: P. vivax crosses borders in South Asia despite declining overall prevalence
articles.title_original: Genetic evidence of cross-border Plasmodium vivax spread in a malaria pre-elimination region of South Asia.
articles.authors: Rai A, Sutanto E, Ghimire P, Wangchuk S, Drukpa T, Alam MS, Ghanchi N, Rahim AG, Rumaseb A, Trimarsanto H, Hoon KS, Adhikari N, Adhikari S, Banjara MR, Rijal KR, Nepal R, Regmi RS, Qurashi B, Zaidi SE, Beg MA, Ley B, Price RN, Thriemer K, Auburn S
articles.details: High-resolution genotyping using 93-microhaplotype vivaxGEN panel performed on P. vivax from patients in Bangladesh, Bhutan, and Nepal (2013–2023) and compared with Afghanistan, India, and Pakistan (2014–2024). Total samples: Nepal (n=19), Bhutan (n=27), Bangladesh (n=35), Afghanistan (n=159), India (n=24), Pakistan (n=213). High-complexity infections: 27.04% Afghanistan, 16.43% Pakistan, 28.57% Bangladesh; markedly lower in Bhutan (3.70%) and Nepal (5.26%). Country-wide identity-by-descent analyses revealed three genetic clusters partitioning Bangladesh and Bhutan from other countries, with two sub-populations in Bhutan separating local from cross-border imported cases.

articles.headline: Active EBV infection may predispose infants to severe P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Infants under 24 months with severe malaria have a serological profile of active infection with Epstein Barr Virus.
articles.authors: Cheng WT, Fogang B, Jain A, Davies DH, Felgner PL, Eboumbou C, Koki PN, Speck SH, Joyner CJ, Ayong LS, Lamb TJ
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 195 infants aged 6–24 months in Cameroon. Parasitemia determined by microscopy or RDT; disease severity classified by WHO criteria. EBV infection status determined by serology; anti-P. falciparum antibody magnitude, breadth, and in vitro invasion-blocking capacity quantified. 24% of children serologically positive for active EBV infection, with highest proportion of severe malaria cases in children with active EBV. Elevated magnitude and breadth of antibody response with increased invasion-blocking capacity observed in children with active EBV, though circulating parasitemia similar to uninfected children. Primary EBV infection may be risk factor for severe falciparum malaria in infants.

articles.headline: Fecal DNA sampling detects zoonotic P. knowlesi in wild macaques
articles.title_original: Tracking Plasmodium knowlesi Through Fecal DNA for Monitoring Zoonotic Transmission in Wild Macaques Across Southeast and South Asia.
articles.authors: Wannigama DL, Amarasiri M, Phattharapornjaroen P, Hurst C, Modchang C, Cynthia B, Miyanaga K, Cui L, Fernandez S, Melhem NM, Luk-In S, Singer AC, Ragupathi NKD, Htun TS, Sei K, Ngamwongsatit N, Hui NS, Shimotai Y, Ounjai P, Kanthawee P, Tacharoenmuang R, Kauba A, Eang C, Romone L, Dharne M, de Araujo JC, Ndatuwong LG, Werawatte WKCP, Chanthasiri T, Zhao J, Mori H, Besa JJV, Kurt Ö, Kanjanabuch T, Zahraei-Ramazani AR, Higgins PG, Aoyagi T, Kicic A, Trowsdale S, Hongsing P, Yang X, Wang Y, Khatib A, Sano D, Shibuya K, Abe S, Hamamoto H
articles.details: Noninvasive surveillance of Plasmodium knowlesi in wild macaques using 4752 fecal samples collected across 9 endemic countries. Parasite DNA detected in 390 samples (8.2%), with positivity rates ranging from 1.4% to 18.4%. Provides first field-based evidence that P. knowlesi DNA shed by macaques and present under natural field conditions can be detected. Validates fecal sampling as practical and scalable tool for tracking zoonotic malaria transmission.
