Results support integration of approach into forest-runoff and rural wastewater surveillance systems, offering new opportunities for early detection of pathogens and environmental monitoring at the human-wildlife interface.

articles.headline: Novel glutamine antagonist DON tolerated in malaria patients for cerebral malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Safety, Tolerability, and Pharmacokinetics of 6-Diazo-5-Oxo-L-Norleucine in Malawian Adults With and Without Malaria: A Phase 1 Dose-Escalation Clinical Trial.
articles.authors: Riggle BA, Chamzas A, Gopalakrishnan M, Nyirenda OM, Nampota-Nkomba N, Mallewa JE, Masonga R, Manyozo C, Zuze K, Liomba AM, Alt J, Rais R, Slusher B, Moodley N, Miller LH, Pierce SK, O'Brien NF, Laurens MB, Postels DG
articles.details: Open-label, prospective dose-escalation Phase 1 trial of single-dose intravenous DON in Blantyre, Malawi in healthy adults and those with uncomplicated malaria (dose-cohorts of 10 at 0.1, 1.0, 5.0, or 10 mg/kg). Enrolled 40 healthy adults and 38 with uncomplicated malaria. Transient asymptomatic creatinine elevation occurred 12 hours postinfusion in 18% of all participants. Nausea and vomiting uncommon at 0.1–1.0 mg/kg but more frequent at 5.0–10 mg/kg doses. All DON-related adverse events resolved rapidly without sequelae; no significant differences between healthy and malaria groups. Terminal half-life ranged 1.7–4.1 hours. DON well tolerated; data guides dosing strategies for adjunctive DON in children with cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Urbanization reduces avian malaria prevalence in house sparrows
articles.title_original: Interactions between urbanization, malaria infection and avian cloacal microbiome.
articles.authors: Garrigós M, Jiménez-Peñuela J, Saavedra I, Veiga J, García-López MJ, Garrido M, Ruiz-López MJ, Figuerola J, Moreno-Indias I, Martínez-de la Puente J
articles.details: Explored associations between habitat urbanization, avian malaria infection (Plasmodium and Haemoproteus), and cloacal microbiome composition in wild house sparrows. Urban birds showed lower parasite prevalence than natural and rural habitat birds. Association between avian malaria infection and cloacal bacterial microbiome composition depended on habitat type. In natural habitats, infected birds showed nearly significant increase in bacterial richness and significant differences in relative abundance of bacterial taxa compared to uninfected birds. Infection status not associated with microbiome parameters in rural and urban habitats. Habitat type is associated with avian malaria prevalence and may modulate parasite-microbiome relationships.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria carriers drive substantial transmission with cryptic P. ovale
articles.title_original: Uneven Plasmodium falciparum Transmission and Cryptic ovale Transmission From the Asymptomatic Reservoir in Bagamoyo, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Lin JT, Mathias DK, Yang G, Loya M, Muller MS, Basham C, Nyasembe V, Amagai K, Rutha I, Gaither C, Nyange M, Said H, Chhetri SB, Swinehart B, Lin FC, Dinglasan RR, Juliano JJ, Tarimo B, Ngasala B
articles.details: Direct skin feeding assays performed on persons >5 years infected with P. falciparum detected by RDT or PCR in Bagamoyo, Tanzania. Fifty colony-reared Anopheles gambiae fed on posterior calves; successful infection defined as ≥1 oocyst-positive mosquito midgut among 25 dissected 8 days post-feeding. Among 491 participants with median parasite density 5.1 parasites/μL, 22% were infectious to mosquitoes. RDT-positive individuals infected roughly twice as many mosquitoes as RDT-negative/PCR-positive persons. Overall 9.1% (342/3741) of mosquitoes fed on infectious carriers were parasite-positive. Half of infectious individuals infected a single mosquito; top 16 transmitters (3%) cumulatively infected 57% of infected mosquitoes. Unexpectedly, 44% of oocyst-positive feeds tested positive for P. ovale.

articles.headline: Pfs230 domain 7 is promising transmission-blocking vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Pfs230 Domains 7-10 Encoded by DNA Vaccines Induce Potent Complement-Independent Transmission-Blocking Antibody Against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Cao Y, Hayashi CTH, Tripathi AK, Takashima E, Tsuboi T, Harju TB, Kumar N
articles.details: DNA vaccines encoding Pfs230 domains D7–D10 developed and evaluated in BALB/c mice. Transmission-reducing activity (TRA) of antibodies from D7D10 DNA and protein vaccines evaluated using standard membrane feeding assays with in vitro-cultured P. falciparum gametocytes. Potent D7D10-specific antibody responses elicited in vaccinated mice. Anti-D7D10 IgGs purified from mice revealed strong dose-dependent TRA against P. falciparum in membrane feeding assays, independent of active complement presence. Western blot analysis suggested antibodies predominantly target domain D7. DNA vaccines encoding D7 and D7D8 similarly induced antibodies with strong TRA. Pfs230-D7D10, particularly domain D7, identified as novel and promising P. falciparum transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Fever enhances P. falciparum ring-stage sequestration in brain vessels
articles.title_original: Febrile Temperature Augments Ring-stage Plasmodium falciparum Adhesion to Brain Endothelial Cells.
articles.authors: Joof F, Hu R, Seydel KB, Cohee LM, Zheng Y, Smith JD
articles.details: In ring-stage parasites, febrile exposure increased transcript levels of exported parasite genes involved in trafficking of P. falciparum erythrocyte membrane protein 1 (PfEMP1) responsible for endothelial adherence. Febrile exposure accelerated PfEMP1 surface display in ring-stage infected erythrocytes, resulting in 2-fold increase in binding to brain endothelium in perfusable 3D human brain microvessel model. Parasite exposure enhanced binding of uninfected erythrocytes in 3D brain microvessels. Findings suggest complex interplay between fever and parasite biomass in pathogenesis of cerebral malaria, highlighting temperature-dependent mechanisms of ring-stage sequestration and PfEMP1 expression.

articles.headline: Bivalent VLPs with SPECT-1 and CSP enhance pre-erythrocytic vaccine immunity
articles.title_original: Bivalent virus-like particles expressing SPECT1 and CSP trigger pre-erythrocytic malaria immunity and protect against transgenic Plasmodium falciparum sporozoite challenge in mice.
articles.authors: Turan G, Mukhopadhyay E, Truby A, Fedorova K, Peralta Alvarez MP, Alharbi NK, Hill AVS, Salman AM
articles.details: Engineered hepatitis B surface antigen virus-like particles expressing SPECT-1 alone or combined with CSP epitopes, tested in prime-boost regimen with transgenic P. berghei expressing P. falciparum antigens. Bivalent VLP candidate N4 with Matrix-M adjuvant provided similar efficacy to R21 in BALB/c mice, though SPECT-1 addition reduced efficacy of candidate N2. NANP-specific IgG responses negatively correlated with blood-stage parasite load.

articles.headline: Apical polar ring proteins essential for Plasmodium invasion
articles.title_original: The apical polar ring is essential during the blood stage of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Gurung P, Back PS, Ali I, Hernandez GM, Dvorin JD
articles.details: Characterized PfAPR3 as an apical polar ring protein critical for asexual replication and host cell invasion. Conditional knockout of PfAPR3 blocked invasion despite parasite-RBC contact, due to failure to form tight junctions. Identified three additional APR proteins (PfAPR4, PfCHAKRA, PfAPR5); PfCHAKRA essential for cytoskeletal network organization, with PfAPR3 showing dual apical-basal localization during schizogony.

articles.headline: Geospatial analysis identifies environmental risk factors for malaria in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Environmental factors influencing mosquito breeding and malaria transmission using geospatial technologies in the Wabi Shebele sub-basin, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Merga BB, Tabor KW, Geletu MH, Zerihun E, Gemeda EA, Moisa MB, Gemeda DO
articles.details: Assessed seven environmental factors (LULC, LST, NDVI, NDWI, NDMI, elevation, rainfall) influencing mosquito breeding and transmission in Wabi Shebele sub-basin using geospatial techniques. High land surface temperatures (bare land 33.66°C, grassland 31.76°C, agricultural 31.42°C) and agricultural land (24.78%) provided favorable breeding conditions; forested areas (LST 26.98°C) and highland zones had lower malaria risk. Low elevation with high soil moisture and moderate-high rainfall were primary factors facilitating distribution.

articles.headline: Avian haemosporidian parasites infect cerulean warbler with low parasitemia
articles.title_original: EFFECTS OF AVIAN MALARIA IN A POPULATION OF CERULEAN WARBLER (SETOPHAGA CERULEA) WITH FIRST DOCUMENTATION OF HAEMOSPORIDIAN LINEAGES INFECTING THE SPECIES.
articles.authors: Grudens JB, Islam K
articles.details: Studied avian haemosporidian infections in 51 cerulean warblers over 2 years using microscopy and molecular methods. Parasitemia was low (<1% of cells infected) with no negative effects on male body condition. Documented 31.4% population prevalence and 11 unique lineages in Haemoproteus and Plasmodium genera, 2 novel. Findings advanced understanding of host-parasite interactions in this declining avian species.

articles.headline: Amplicon-based sequencing panels enable efficient P. vivax genomic surveillance
articles.title_original: PvGTSeq and PvCRiSP: Two amplicon-based targeted sequencing panels for Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Manrique-Valverde PC, Hasund CM, Kelley KA, Amaya-Romero JE, Arévalo-Herrera M, Brosula R, Calzada JE, Chenet SM, Corredor V, Early AM, Fontecha G, Forero-Peña DA, Herrera S, Lana JT, Laws M, Niles-Robin R, Obaldia N, Santamaria AM, Schwabl P, Auburn S, Neafsey DE
articles.details: Developed PvGTSeq (249 amplicons for resistance and population structure) and PvCRiSP (4 polymorphic amplicons for complexity of infection) targeting P. vivax using multiplexed PCR without proprietary reagents. Both achieved ≥75% recovery at parasitemias as low as 5 parasites/μL; PvGTSeq showed high accuracy (error rate 3.85e-4%–2.87e-3%). Validated across five Latin American countries with high power to discriminate samples and assign geographic origin to imported cases.

articles.headline: NANP2 and J1 epitope responses correlate with RTS,S vaccine protection
articles.title_original: Antibody fine specificity correlates with protection from malaria for the RTS,S vaccine in young African children: A post hoc analysis of a phase IIb randomised controlled trial.
articles.authors: Hysa A, Opi DH, Waterhouse J, Chishimba S, Horton JL, Kingston N, Netter HJ, Wetzel D, Piontek M, Feng G, Sacarlal J, Dobaño C, Kurtovic L, Beeson JG
articles.details: Post hoc analysis of phase IIb RTS,S trial (n=735 children 1–4 years old) examined antibody fine specificity to six CSP epitopes. High IgG to NANP2 epitope (aHR 0.838, 95% CI [0.716, 0.981], p=0.028) and J1 epitope (aHR 0.718, 95% CI [0.611, 0.844], p<0.001) significantly reduced clinical malaria risk; cross-reactive responses associated with higher Fc-mediated functional activities. Findings identify correlates of protection for improved CSP-based vaccine design.

articles.headline: Economic burden of malaria in Brazilian Amazon exceeds $181 million
articles.title_original: Economic burden of malaria in the Brazilian Amazon from a societal perspective.
articles.authors: Andrade MV, Noronha KVMS, Guedes GR, Carvalho LR, Bracarense H, Julião NA, Souza A, Diniz BC, Silva VA, Motta-Santos AS, Peterka C, Castro MC
articles.details: Assessed direct/indirect costs and quality-of-life losses from malaria in Brazilian Amazon using household survey (n=1,131 with episodes 2019–2022). Total economic burden in 2019 was approximately US$181.9 million PPP-USD, with Unified Health System bearing 72.4%; Amazonas, Pará, and Roraima states bore highest burden. Household burden predominantly driven by indirect costs, mortality, and health-related quality-of-life losses.

articles.headline: Activated platelets mediate cerebral malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: The role of activated platelets in cerebral malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Schlesinger M
articles.details: Investigates mechanisms by which activated platelets contribute to cerebral malaria pathogenesis. Study focuses on platelet biology in severe malaria and its neurological complications. Specific quantitative results not available from title alone.

articles.headline: Generative models infer demographic history of Anopheles gambiae populations
articles.title_original: On the use of generative models for demographic inference in malaria vectors from genomic data.
articles.authors: Eneli AA, Siu PC, Perez MF, Burt A, Fumagalli M, Mathieson S
articles.details: Applied generative deep learning to infer joint evolutionary history of A. gambiae populations from Guinea and Burkina Faso. Model selection demonstrated evolutionary model with migration fits better than no-migration model; method accurately captured population genetic differentiation. Findings support machine learning as valuable tool for understanding vector evolution and population dynamics relevant to malaria control.

articles.headline: Deformable Plasmodium gametocytes are immediately infectious upon release
articles.title_original: Defining the signature of deformable and infectious Plasmodium falciparum gametocytes.
articles.authors: Ravel D, Rijpma S, Beraldi D, Graumans W, Meerstein-Kessel L, Crouch K, Passecker A, van Gemert GJ, Blanken SL, Arinaitwe E, Rianne S, Ngotho P, Neafsey D, Voss T, Huijnen M, Bousema T, Marti M
articles.details: Used phenotypic assays and transcriptional analysis to track immature-to-infectious gametocyte transition. Found major transcriptional differences between input and deformable gametocytes, but high similarity between deformable and infectious stages. Results suggest deformable gametocytes released from bone marrow are immediately infectious without further activation; transcriptional markers identified for tracking infectivity.

articles.headline: Ruthenium-atovaquone complexes inhibit asexual and sexual Plasmodium stages
articles.title_original: Ruthenium Complexes of Atovaquone Acting on Multiple Stages of the Plasmodium Life Cycle.
articles.authors: Fabbri C, Marcon PHS, Santiago AS, Sousa CC, Quadros HC, de Sena Lamar Nunes L, de Menezes DDM, Santana RAG, Justiniano SCB, D'Alessandro S, Basilico N, Moreira DRM, de Araujo-Neto JH, Lopes SCP
articles.details: Synthesized and evaluated ruthenium complexes of broad-spectrum drug atovaquone for antiplasmodial activity. Complexes demonstrated broader activity against both asexual and sexual stages compared to atovaquone and exhibited faster action. Complexes blocked transmission at both gametocyte and oocyst stages in mosquito feeding assays, whereas atovaquone only blocked oocysts; first example of metal complexes with similar potency against both stages.

articles.headline: Platelet mTOR activation drives cerebral malaria pathogenesis via heme signaling
articles.title_original: Heme-induced ITAM signaling exacerbates malaria-associated neuropathogenesis through activation of platelet mTOR.
articles.authors: Portier I, Denorme F, Tolley ND, Kosaka Y, Andrianova I, Sigala PA, Lamb TJ, Zimmerman GA, Castro-Faria-Neto HC, Morrell CN, Rondina MT, Campbell RA
articles.details: Examined mTOR pathway in platelets during experimental cerebral malaria using P. berghei ANKA-infected mice. Platelet-specific mTOR deletion (mTORplt-/-) significantly increased survival independent of parasitemia and thrombocytopenia, with reduced brain platelet deposition and improved cerebral blood flow. Heme activated platelet mTOR downstream of CLEC-2-mediated ITAM signaling; blocking heme-induced platelet activation with cobalt protoporphyrin reduced experimental CM-associated mortality.

articles.headline: Repeated malaria infections don't shorten telomeres in children
articles.title_original: Cumulative Plasmodium falciparum infections do not drive long-term telomere shortening in Kenyan children.
articles.authors: Miglar A, Amadi D, Grannas D, Widman L, Mwacharo J, Musyoki J, Kai O, Bejon P, Maccharia A, Williams TN, Färnert A, Asghar M, Ndungu FM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 218 Kenyan children from moderate-transmission area and 90 controls measured telomere length at three timepoints (2007, 2010, 2013). Telomere length declined with age independently of cumulative malaria exposure. Asymptomatic P. falciparum parasitaemia was associated with positive change of 0.22 kb (95% CI 0.02-0.42, p=0.03). Repeated malaria infections were not associated with telomere shortening.

articles.headline: Merozoite physiology: motility, sensing, and red blood cell invasion
articles.title_original: Physiology of invasive Plasmodium merozoites.
articles.authors: Siddiqui MA
articles.details: Review article synthesizing current understanding of Plasmodium merozoite physiology during red blood cell invasion. Covers merozoite motility, signal sensing in response to ions and cell contact, secretion of specialized organelles, and gliding motility coordination. Discusses potential strategies for malaria control based on these invasion mechanisms.

articles.headline: Ex vivo culture preserves P. vivax transcriptome but reduces haplotype diversity
articles.title_original: Stage-aware transcriptomics reveals selective haplotype persistence in short-term ex vivo cultured Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Abagero B, Dumetz F, Ford CT, Tolosa T, Tesefay D, Lukas B, Shenkutie T, Popovici J, Yewhalaw D, Serre D, Lo E
articles.details: Study compared in vivo and short-term ex vivo transcriptomes from Ethiopian P. vivax clinical isolates using stage deconvolution and PvMSP1 haplotyping. Ex vivo culture modestly increased schizont representation while preserving overall transcriptome patterns (no significantly differentially expressed genes after stage adjustment), but reduced multiplicity of infection and non-randomly depleted specific haplotypes, indicating clonal selection and genotype-associated transcriptional heterogeneity.

articles.headline: FHR-1 deficiency linked to severe malarial anemia in children
articles.title_original: Factor H-related protein 1 deficiency and risk of severe falciparum malaria in Ghanaian children: a cohort analysis.
articles.authors: Byrd AU, Wahler L, Thye T, Reiss T, Bachmann A, Pradel G
articles.details: Cohort analysis of 500 Ghanaian children with different malaria manifestations examined plasma Factor H-related protein 1 (FHR-1) levels. One-third lacked detectable FHR-1. Logistic regression found no significant association between FHR-1 deficiency and severe malaria risk overall, but FHR-1 deficiency was overrepresented among children with severe anemia and multiple syndromes, with markedly lower hemoglobin levels.

articles.headline: Paper-based lysis device enables blood-based malaria diagnostics in low-resource settings
articles.title_original: Creating an improved workflow for paper-based malaria diagnostics by integrating total lysis of whole blood.
articles.authors: Prat-Trunas J, Code A, Avalos-Padilla Y, Fernández-Busquets X, Mace CR, Baldrich E
articles.details: Developed three-dimensional paper-based lysis device fabricated by laser cutting or wax-printing for malaria diagnostics in resource-limited settings. Device achieved 100% cellular lysis independently of sample composition and successfully detected malaria biomarkers from Plasmodium-infected samples with performance comparable to standard liquid lysis. Dried samples maintained antigen stability for over one week at room temperature, enabling delayed laboratory testing.

articles.headline: Artemisinin more potent than iron chelators via digestive vacuole accumulation
articles.title_original: Why are iron chelators not as effective as artemisinin in killing malaria parasites?
articles.authors: Sun J, Fang C, Si W, Qin X, Wang F, Li Y, Sun J
articles.details: In vitro study of P. falciparum 3D7 compared dihydroartemisinin (DHA), artemether (ATM), and desferrioxamine (DFO). DHA upregulated iron utilization genes at 3h post-treatment and induced ribosome biogenesis pathways, while DFO did not. DFO reduced P. falciparum in vitro but failed against P. yoelii in vivo. ATM+DFO combination less potent than ATM alone. Artemisinin's superior potency attributed to digestive vacuole accumulation enabling heme/iron homeostasis disruption.

articles.headline: Fever increases P. falciparum surface protein trafficking and cytoadhesion
articles.title_original: Physiological febrile heat stress increases cytoadhesion through increased protein trafficking of Plasmodium falciparum surface proteins into the red blood cell.
articles.authors: Jones D, Belda H, Broncel M, Fuchs G, Anaguano D, Nofal SD, Treeck M
articles.details: In vitro study of P. falciparum-infected RBCs exposed to physiologically relevant febrile heat stress (39°C). Heat enhanced cytoadhesion through increased trafficking of PfEMP1 and PSAC to infected RBC surface in four diverse clinical isolates, without changes in overall protein expression or developmental stage. Phosphoproteomics revealed enhanced phosphorylation and export of transmembrane-domain proteins at elevated temperature.

articles.headline: P. falciparum dual-targeting pyrophosphatases identified as potential drug targets
articles.title_original: Pyrophosphate homeostasis in multiple subcellular compartments is essential in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nwankwo I, Ke H
articles.details: Identified two isoforms of P. falciparum soluble pyrophosphatases (PfsPPase1, PfsPPase2) with distinct localizations via reverse genetics and biochemistry. PfsPPase1 localizes to cytoplasm only; PfsPPase2 to mitochondrion, apicoplast, and cytoplasm via 51-amino acid organellar targeting peptide. Both isoforms essential for asexual blood stage growth. This unique dual-targeting mechanism differs from model organisms and identifies them as promising antimalarial drug targets.

articles.headline: Hemozoin-associated proteins disrupt brain endothelial barriers in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum hemozoin-associated biomolecules induce brain endothelial cell barrier disruption in an in vitro model of cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Crotty KA, Clotea I, Ueberheide B, Cammer M, Sall J, Liang A, Rodriguez A
articles.details: In vitro study using human brain microvascular endothelial cells exposed to P. falciparum-infected RBCs identified barrier-disrupting activity in the hemozoin (crystalline heme) fraction. Activity attributed to biomolecules associated with hemozoin, not the crystal itself. Protease treatment inhibited barrier disruption (implicating proteins), while nuclease treatment had no effect. These hemozoin-associated proteins represent a key mechanism in cerebral malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Global malaria burden decreased while NTDs declining after 2016
articles.title_original: Global epidemiological trends, distribution of NTDs and malaria, and disease burden projections for the next 15 years.
articles.authors: Huang H, Gao Y, Zhu H, Xu Y, Li M, Meng F, Chen X, Du G, Ma W, Qian J, Li Y, Tan W
articles.details: Analysis of Global Burden of Disease 2021 data examining malaria and neglected tropical disease (NTD) trends from 1990-2021 at global and regional levels. Vector autoregression model incorporated sociodemographic index (SDI), environmental sanitation, and behavioral factors. Global malaria burden decreased from 1990-2021 (especially Sub-Saharan Africa), NTDs increased until 2016 then declined. SDI and sanitation significantly influenced burden in low-SDI regions.

articles.headline: RDT kits viable DNA source for malaria drug resistance monitoring
articles.title_original: Rapid diagnostic test kit as a source of DNA in comparison with filter paper for malaria molecular surveillance and drug resistance monitoring: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Woldegiorgis BT, Wondale B, Tamiru G, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 417 paired samples from malaria-positive cases in southern Ethiopia compared DNA recovery from used RDT kits vs filter paper dried blood spots (DBS). Nested PCR for 18S rRNA: 93.8% DBS vs 83.7% RDT samples positive. Positive percent agreement 89.2% for P. falciparum and 81.9% for P. vivax. Agreement higher in high parasite load cases (Kappa 0.75 vs 0.64 overall). pfmdr1 amplification success 96.7% RDT vs 100% DBS.

articles.headline: NEK4 kinase essential for P. berghei meiosis and zygote maturation
articles.title_original: A divergent Plasmodium NEK4 acts as a key regulator driving the early events of meiosis.
articles.authors: Yanase R, Hair M, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Brady D, Pasquarello C, Bottrill A, Bhanvadia S, Neal A, Tromer EC, Le Roch KG, Hainard A, Holder AA, Vaughan S, Guttery DS, Tewari R
articles.details: Study identified Plasmodium berghei NIMA-related kinase NEK4 as key regulator of meiotic initiation and zygote morphogenesis. NEK4 accumulates at microtubule-organizing center (MTOC) and apical polar complex (APC) post-fertilization. Pbnek4 deletion caused complete developmental arrest with blocked MTOC duplication, failed microtubule formation, uncondensed chromatin, and impaired nuclear migration and cell polarity. Transcriptomics and phosphoproteomics revealed NEK4 absence collapses meiotic and cytoskeletal regulatory networks.

articles.headline: Comment on cerebral malaria: mice versus humans
articles.title_original: Comment on: Cerebral malaria: of mice and men.
articles.authors: Carvalho LJM, Sanches GS
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Commentary discussing cerebral malaria research and translational implications between experimental models and human disease.

articles.headline: MSP2 genetic diversity in Cameroon indicates high transmission
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of the IC/3D7 allelic family of the merozoite surface protein 2 (MSP2) of Plasmodium falciparum and multiplicity of infection in four health facilities in the Mbouda Health District, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Cedric Y, Nadia NAC, Merveille TT, Pacome TK, Mathieu KT, Guy-Armand GN, Abel D, Edna GDM, Sandra WNI, Yval KT, Hu H
articles.details: Study of 481 samples from Cameroon health facilities found 28.5% P. falciparum prevalence with 15 distinct MSP2 alleles and 14.15% genetic diversity. Mean multiplicity of infection was 1.20, with 81.68% single-genotype infections, indicating high transmission intensity.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria and severe anemia cause long-term cognitive impairment
articles.title_original: Long-Term Cognitive Ability and Academic Achievement After Childhood Severe Malaria.
articles.authors: Bangirana P, Mellencamp KA, Ren J, Nakitende JA, Conroy AL, Datta D, Kautzman C, Goings MJ, Opoka RO, Namazzi R, Robberstad B, Idro R, John CC
articles.details: Follow-up of 889 Ugandan children 4–15 years post-episode showed cerebral malaria (n=184) and severe malarial anemia (n=249) associated with lower overall cognition (adjusted mean difference −0.41 and −0.31) and math scores (−0.46 and −0.32) versus unaffected controls. Acute kidney injury and elevated angiopoietin-2 at acute illness predicted worse cognitive outcomes.

articles.headline: Long-term neurocognitive outcomes after severe malaria
articles.title_original: Long-Term Neurocognitive Outcomes After Severe Malaria Infection: No Peace of MIND.
articles.authors: Sundararaman SA, Odom John AR
articles.details: Study examining long-term cognitive and academic achievement in children following severe malaria episodes.

articles.headline: PX1 PIN haplotype marks reduced artemisinin-lumefantrine susceptibility
articles.title_original: A Plasmodium falciparum PX1 haplotype is associated with reduced susceptibility to artemisinin and lumefantrine.
articles.authors: Bower-Lepts C, Jangra A, Kanatani S, Tripathi A, Mlambo G, McCotter-González A, Romano L, Orena S, Okitwi M, Niaré K, Rosenthal PJ, Conrad M, Sinnis P, Mok S
articles.details: Genetic cross and drug-selection experiments identified px1 polymorphisms as strong correlates of reduced susceptibility to dihydroartemisinin and lumefantrine. PX1 PIN haplotype (L1222P, M1701I, D1705N) enriched under drug pressure in Ugandan P. falciparum isolates and conferred growth advantage, explaining rapid allele frequency increase over two decades.

articles.headline: Outdoor residual spraying most cost-effective for P. knowlesi
articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness of outdoor residual spraying, insecticide-treated nets, and their combination for zoonotic malaria control in Sabah, Malaysia: a comparative analysis.
articles.authors: Firdaus MH, Wan Puteh SE, Md Idris Z, Zhueng TJ, Abdul Hamid MH, Suli Z, Abdul Manap M, Abdul Manaf MR
articles.details: Cost-effectiveness analysis in three Sabah districts compared vector control for zoonotic P. knowlesi malaria. Outdoor residual spraying averted 13 cases with lowest incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (MYR 2046 per case averted, ≈USD 470), versus higher costs for long-lasting insecticidal nets and combined strategies.

articles.headline: Global genomic surveillance reveals region-specific parasite adaptation
articles.title_original: Global-scale population genetic analysis of Plasmodium falciparum identifies region-specific patterns of malaria parasite adaptation.
articles.authors: Billows N, Dombrowski JG, Thorpe J, Vanheer L, Moss S, Gitaka J, Sutherland CJ, Marinho CRF, Thi Hong Ngoc N, Thi Huong Binh N, Thieu NQ, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: Analysis of 17,565 P. falciparum isolates from 39 countries identified region-specific adaptation. Vietnam isolates universally carried pfkelch13 C580Y artemisinin-resistance mutation; Brazil showed elevated identity-by-descent around pfcrt; pfKIC7 and pfKIC9 elevation in South America and Horn of Africa suggests alternative artemisinin resistance pathways.

articles.headline: Rapid nanopore sequencing enables continental-scale genomic surveillance
articles.title_original: Continental-scale genomic surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum malaria across sub-Saharan Africa with rapid nanopore sequencing.
articles.authors: Mwenda M, Mosler K, Bohmeier B, Chomba M, van Loon W, Mambwe B, Gaye A, Olorunfemi A, Suliman S, Yanogo NJ, Sow D, Ngom B, Zoure OAZ, Tibiri YNG, Ojeniyi F, Zongo A, Anoh EA, Achi V, Chiyesu C, Otieno S, Ogola B, Niangaly M, Carrasquilla M, Goboto DK, Feldt T, Tufa TB, Oliveira R, Schallenberg E, Sogoba Y, Ntalla C, Ouedraogo O, Otieno K, Opaleye O, Olowe A, Gibbons M, Drakeley C, Schubert G, Mockenhaupt FP, Portugal S, Deme AB, Soulama I, Ndiaye D, Ojurongbe O, Kariuki S, Shi YP, Schultz JS, Hawela M, Bridges DJ, Hendry JA
articles.details: Decentralized scale-up sequenced 1065 P. falciparum samples across six African countries in one year using novel nanopore protocol (5-hour turnaround, <$25 USD per sample). Protocol tracked antimalarial resistance genes, hrp2/3 deletions, vaccine target csp, and ama1 polymorphisms with offline bioinformatics dashboard.

articles.headline: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine superior to chloroquine for P. vivax
articles.title_original: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine versus chloroquine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria with concurrent or delayed high-dose primaquine in Brazil (Curavivax): an open-label, single-centre, randomised clinical trial.
articles.authors: Baía-da-Silva DC, Machado KVA, Rabelo ALR, da Silva Balieiro AA, Barbosa ASL, Costa MRF, Sampaio VS, Melo GC, Alexandre MAA, Alencar ACC, Nascimento CT, Jerônimo CMP, Marcovski MS, Barbosa LRA, Valle CS, Carrilho TAA, Vieira JLF, de Melo MM, Dinelly KMO, Omena AG, Brito-Sousa JD, Barreiros GA, Rocha YV, Filho ACA, Bassat Q, Alecrim MDGC, Siqueira AM, Lacerda MVG
articles.details: Open-label RCT of 418 patients with uncomplicated P. vivax assigned to chloroquine or DHA-PQ (40/320 mg or 20/160 mg, 3 days) with primaquine (15 mg daily × 14 days) day 0 or delayed. Day 42 recurrence: chloroquine+immediate primaquine 2.0% vs DHA-PQ+immediate 1.0% (HR 0.48, p=0.44); DHA-PQ+delayed 2.4% vs chloroquine+delayed 27.3% (HR 0.08, p<0.0001).

articles.headline: Plasmodium cardiolipin synthase essential for liver maturation
articles.title_original: A bacterial-type cardiolipin synthase in Plasmodium spp. supports mitochondrial respiration and is important for liver stage maturation.
articles.authors: Adhur KA, Nirdosh N, Chandramouli A, Basu S, Lahiri A, Kamat SS, Mishra S, Habib S
articles.details: Functional study of bacterial-type cardiolipin synthase in P. falciparum and P. berghei showed PfCls localization to mitochondria in blood stages. PbCls knockout parasites exhibited impaired asexual proliferation, reduced mitochondrial membrane potential and oxygen consumption, and severe defect in liver-stage maturation despite successful mosquito development.

articles.headline: Signal-dependent exocytosis triggers P. falciparum egress
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal dynamics of signal dependent exocytosis and parasitophorous vacuolar membrane rupture during Plasmodium falciparum egress.
articles.authors: Dedkhad W, Tran T, Fierro MA, Brooks C, Muralidharan V
articles.details: Live-cell imaging study showed signal-dependent exocytosis of egress-specific vesicles initiated ~3 hours before merozoite release. Parasitophorous vacuolar membrane ruptures at single site and rapidly expands; parasite ER calcium-binding protein (PfERC) required for egress vesicle exocytosis.

articles.headline: Systematic review: ACT cost-effectiveness for malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness of artemisinin-based combination therapies for first-line treatment of malaria among pediatric and adult populations: a systematic review protocol.
articles.authors: Ma B, Kr S, Jith A, Gr S, Janakiram C, John D
articles.details: Protocol for systematic review of cost-effectiveness of artemisinin-based combination therapies versus non-ACT or alternative ACT regimens as first-line malaria treatment in pediatric and adult populations. Will extract incremental cost-effectiveness ratios, costs per disability-adjusted life year averted, and quality-adjusted life years.

articles.headline: Plasmodium diversity and multiplicity in Southeast Asia scoping review
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and multiplicity of Plasmodium spp. infections in Southeast Asia: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Helen AP, Martini M, Jeon BY, Han JH, Muh F
articles.details: This scoping review analyzed 16 studies comprising 1,609 P. falciparum, 1,526 P. vivax, 37 P. malariae, and 188 P. knowlesi infections in Southeast Asia. P. falciparum showed polyclonal infection rates from 3.9% to 73.9% (mean MOI 1.05–4.9), while P. vivax displayed rates from 16.9% to 71.4% (mean MOI 1.1–1.91, heterozygosity 0.66–0.87). The authors conclude P. falciparum and P. vivax remain genetically diverse across Southeast Asia with heterogeneous transmission patterns.

articles.headline: Severe malaria clinical overview
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria.
articles.authors: Samandari T, Gillespie E
articles.details: No abstract available; article title suggests coverage of severe malaria clinical aspects and pathophysiology.

articles.headline: Cultural history of malaria in Hungary
articles.title_original: [Data of cultural history of malaria in Hungary].
articles.authors: Kiss L
articles.details: No abstract available; title indicates historical and cultural context of malaria in Hungary.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine reduces child mortality by 13% in three African countries
articles.title_original: Impact of introducing RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine on mortality in young children in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi: an observational evaluation of a cluster-randomised implementation programme.
articles.authors: Mwapasa V, Asante KP, Milligan P, Akech S, Oduro A, Mathanga DP, Fogelson A, Kwambai TK, Hamel MJ, Kapito-Tembo A, Gyan T, Westercamp N, Okine RNA, Moore KA, Pellaux-Furrer E, Stanley CC, Ansong D, Kariuki S, Njuguna P, Mvalo T, Welaga P, Otieno L, Snell P, Schellenberg D, Chimala A, Afari EA, Bejon P, Maleta K, Agbenyega T, Snow RW, Zulu M, Odei-Lartey E, Chinkhumba J, Samuels AM
articles.details: This cluster-randomized evaluation assessed RTS,S malaria vaccine impact in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi from 2019–2022, involving 1,289,504 children receiving dose 1 and 1,068,039 receiving dose 3 (71.1% coverage). Mortality surveillance tracked 5,576 deaths in implementation areas versus 6,152 in comparison areas among children eligible for three doses (mortality rate ratio 0.87, 95% CI 0.77–0.97, p=0.016). The authors conclude RTS,S introduction was associated with significant mortality reduction, averting approximately one in eight deaths.

articles.headline: Fatal P. relictum infection in critically endangered Fatu Hiva monarch
articles.title_original: First record of fatal Plasmodium relictum (GRW4) infection in a highly endangered Fatu Hiva monarch (Pomarea whitneyi) fledgling: a case report.
articles.authors: Igual I Beltrán M, Duc M, Blanvillain C, Bruslund S, Valkiūnas G, Pendl H
articles.details: This case report documents the first confirmed fatal P. relictum (GRW4) infection in a critically endangered Fatu Hiva monarch fledgling in French Polynesia. Necropsy revealed severe emaciation, hepatosplenomegaly, and high parasitemia. Molecular testing using nested cytochrome b PCR on dried blood confirmed single GRW4 infection. The authors conclude aggressive malaria prevention measures are justified to protect the critically depleted bird population.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum spreads across Uganda-DRC border
articles.title_original: Cross-border molecular epidemiology of Plasmodium falciparum drug susceptibility: insights from the Uganda-Democratic Republic of the Congo border.
articles.authors: Dilufwasayo PM, Balikagala B, Uchopa MA, Amaa JA, Ngoyi DM, Mita T
articles.details: This molecular epidemiology study sampled 536 individuals from 20 DRC villages near Uganda from May–June 2023, with 49.8% P. falciparum PCR-positivity. Drug-resistance genotyping revealed pfmdr1 N86 allele fixed (100%), with 50.3% carrying the NFD haplotype (reduced lumefantrine susceptibility), 4.3% carrying pfkelch13 A675V, and 1.3% C469Y (artemisinin partial resistance markers). Whole-genome sequencing showed DRC parasites clustered with Ugandan strains, indicating cross-border parasite migration. The authors conclude coordinated regional genomic surveillance and policy responses are essential.

articles.headline: PfPLP1 membrane pore formation mechanisms revealed
articles.title_original: Deciphering Membrane Pore Formation Mechanisms of Plasmodium falciparum Perforin-Like Protein 1 (PfPLP1).
articles.authors: Patil SB, Dasgupta S, Bhaumik P
articles.details: This computational study modeled P. falciparum perforin-like protein 1 (PfPLP1), a β-pore forming toxin, using all-atom and coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations. The protein comprises a canonical MACPF domain and an apicomplexan perforin β-domain (APCβ), with membrane binding initiated by cationic residues at the APCβ base. Simulations revealed water penetration between tetrameric oligomers and lipid bilayers, initiating pore opening through lipid reorganization. The authors propose PfPLP1 as a promising therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Malaria exposure accelerates tetanus antibody waning in children
articles.title_original: Assessing the effect of malaria exposure history on tetanus antibody waning rates among children in Jinja and Tororo Districts, Uganda.
articles.authors: Sbarra AN, Stone W, Tetteh K, Ssewanyana I, Musinguzi K, Rodríguez-Barraquer I, Jagannathan P, Bach FA, Drakeley C, Takahashi S
articles.details: This longitudinal study tracked tetanus antitoxin IgG in 597 children (3,064 plasma samples) from Uganda's Tororo (high malaria transmission) and Jinja (low transmission) districts from 2011–2017. Antibody half-lives were significantly longer in Jinja (15.8 years, 95% CI 8.8–28.5) versus Tororo (2.8 years, 95% CI 2.6–3.1), with hazard of sero-reversion 2.7-fold higher in Tororo (95% CI 1.7–4.1). The authors conclude malaria exposure history affects vaccine-induced antibody durability and suggest malaria endemicity should inform booster timing.

articles.headline: Drug resistance gene surveillance in Korea's P. vivax and P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Molecular Surveillance of Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Drug Resistance Genes in the Republic of Korea: 2022-2025.
articles.authors: Jung H, Shin HI, Ku B, Park E, Lee MR, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: This surveillance study monitored drug-resistance genes in blood samples from patients with P. vivax (soldiers, relapsed, imported) and P. falciparum (soldiers, civilians) in the Republic of Korea from 2022–2025. In P. vivax, high-prevalence Pvmdr-1 mutations included S515R, G698S, L845F, M908L, T958M, and F1076L in nearly all isolates, while moderate-prevalence mutations included T529 and E1233. In P. falciparum, Pfmdr-1 Y184F was highly prevalent and PfK13 A675V detected only in soldiers.

articles.headline: GMZ2.6c vaccine elicits T cell epitope responses in malaria-exposed Brazilians
articles.title_original: Cellular Immune Response and T Cell Epitope Mapping of Plasmodium falciparum Chimeric Vaccine Candidate GMZ2.6c and Its Components (MSP-3, GLURP and Pfs48/45) in Individuals Naturally Exposed to Malaria in Brazilian Amazon.
articles.authors: Baptista BO, Soares IF, de Souza HADS, Barros JP, Riccio EKP, Martorano RM, Rodrigues-da-Silva RN, Amoah LE, Singh SK, Theisen M, Lima-Junior JDC, Totino PRR, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Pratt-Riccio LR
articles.details: This immunological study evaluated cellular immune responses to the chimeric malaria vaccine candidate GMZ2.6c (containing MSP-3, GLURP, and Pfs48/45) in peripheral blood mononuclear cells from malaria-exposed individuals in the Brazilian Amazon, comparing those with active P. falciparum infection and non-infected individuals. P. falciparum-infected individuals showed enhanced CD8+ T cell activation to MSP-3 and Pfs48/45 and increased CD4+ TCM cells. T cell epitope prediction identified multiple sequences that elicited IFN-γ responses in both groups.

articles.headline: Influenza co-infection protects mice from malaria-associated lung injury
articles.title_original: Protective coinfection: influenza reprograms myeloid cells to limit CD8 T cell-mediated malaria pathology.
articles.authors: Reed JS, Nayan R, Deckers M, Cornwall DH, Ostrand-Rosenberg S, Evavold BD, Lamb TJ
articles.details: This experimental study demonstrated that co-infection with non-lethal influenza (X31) protects mice from malaria-associated acute lung injury (MA-ALI) by expanding Ly6C+ monocyte-derived alveolar macrophages that suppress pathogenic CD8 T cell responses in a contact-dependent manner. Protection occurred independently of parasite burden and type I interferon signaling. In vivo blockade of monocytic myeloid cells with gemcitabine eliminated the protective phenotype. The authors conclude therapeutics expanding suppressive myeloid cells warrant consideration for adjunctive MA-ALI treatment.

articles.headline: Genetic basis of avian malaria resistance in Hawaiian honeycreepers
articles.title_original: Evolution of Resistance to Avian Malaria Infection in an Endemic Hawaiian Honeycreeper.
articles.authors: Neddermeyer JH, Cassin-Sackett L, Paxton EH, Campana MG, Fleischer RC, Foster JT
articles.details: This genome-wide association study analyzed whole-genome sequences from 35 O'ahu 'amakihi (Hawaiian honeycreepers) to identify SNPs associated with malaria infection status determined by real-time PCR. The analysis identified 582 SNPs with annotations including genes regulating transcription, apoptosis, T-cell and complement activation, autophagosome assembly, and intracellular calcium flux. The authors conclude host resistance may involve restricting intracellular components essential for Plasmodium replication, implicating calcium signaling as a key mechanism.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine reduces child deaths by 13% in Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria vaccine reduces child deaths by 13% in Africa, WHO study reports.
articles.authors: Iacobucci G
articles.details: WHO study reporting a malaria vaccine reduced child deaths by 13% in Africa. Specific study design, population, sample size, and intervention details are not provided in the title.

articles.headline: RTS,S/AS01 vaccine projected to reduce clinical malaria cases 40%
articles.title_original: Estimating the impact of the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine in Benin: A mathematical modelling study.
articles.authors: Alohoutade SV, Hounsell R, Dandonougbo C, Aikpon R, Degila J, Silal S
articles.details: Mathematical compartmental model in Benin estimated RTS,S/AS01 vaccine impact on children under five over 10 years. At vaccine coverage matching DTP3 with current net use, the model projected 40% reduction in malaria clinical cases and deaths (2025–2034) versus nets alone; however, a 1.5-fold decrease in net use would offset these benefits. Authors conclude vaccination is an additional control tool but highlight risks of behavioral displacement from nets.

articles.headline: Maintaining malaria services during COVID-19: challenges and adaptations
articles.title_original: Delivering malaria services during the COVID-19 pandemic: challenges and lessons from an international non-governmental organisation.
articles.authors: Chestnutt EG, Marasciulo M, Magumba G, Maxwell K, Rassi C, Parkes A, Nelson C, Tibenderana JK
articles.details: Operational reflections from an international NGO on maintaining malaria services during COVID-19 pandemic. The team implemented a coordinated response including a dedicated taskforce, outbreak management plan with four risk phases, and adapted protocols for safer service delivery. The article provides practical insights for other organizations preparing for future operational challenges. No quantitative results provided.

articles.headline: Political economy analysis reveals barriers to malaria elimination Nigeria
articles.title_original: Accelerating Nigeria towards malaria elimination requires moving away from business as usual: insights from a political economy analysis.
articles.authors: Chestnutt EG, Meredith S, Ipaye B, Getachew D, Tibenderana JK, Anyachukwu E, Obot T, Maxwell K
articles.details: Political economy analysis of Nigeria's malaria program via desk review and stakeholder interviews identified factors hindering progress: limited government prioritization, weak citizen advocacy, poor partner coordination, and lack of communication channels between the program and decision-makers. Authors conclude that meaningful progress requires sustained political financing, citizen demand to drive political prioritization, improved partner coordination, and integration of malaria into national development plans.

articles.headline: High SP-resistance mutations in P. falciparum from pregnant Mozambique women
articles.title_original: Prevalence of pfdhfr-pfdhps Sextuple and Gametocyte-Associated Quintuple Sulfadoxine-Pyrimethamine Resistance Mutations in Plasmodium falciparum Isolates from Pregnant Women in Mozambique.
articles.authors: Drissi-El Boukili Y, Rovira-Vallbona E, Guetens P, Chiheb D, Kattenberg JH, Kestens L, Enosse SMM, Rosanas-Urgell A, Arnaldo P
articles.details: Molecular study of 100 P. falciparum isolates from pregnant women at delivery in Mozambique (2014–2015) examined sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) resistance in pfdhfr and pfdhps genes. Overall, 54% harbored quintuple mutant haplotypes and 7% sextuple haplotypes. Gametocytes detected in 34% of isolates and significantly associated with quintuple mutations (AOR = 7.5, p = 0.001), accounting for 80% of gametocyte-positive infections. Authors emphasize ongoing SP resistance surveillance to guide future intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy strategies.

articles.headline: Low routine immunization coverage (29%) in malaria-endemic communities
articles.title_original: Immunization Coverage and Associated Factors Among Children Aged 18 to 72 Months in Communities With High Malaria Transmission, Bioko Island.
articles.authors: Eburi Losoha E, Lopez Mikue MSA, García GA, Athuman T, Ross A, Daubenberger C, Merten S
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 297 children aged 18 months to 6 years in 13 malaria-endemic communities in Malabo District, Equatorial Guinea (September 2019–March 2020) assessed routine immunization coverage. Overall, 86/297 (29%) were fully immunized per national schedule; coverage similar whether verified by card (28%) or recall (30%). Younger children (18 months–2 years) had higher completion rates (45%) versus older children (2–6 years; 22%, aOR = 0.42, 95% CI 0.20–0.87). Coverage remained below national targets.

articles.headline: African k13 mutations show different artemisinin resistance phenotypes
articles.title_original: Artemisinin partial resistance at a crossroads: evidence for continental variation in Plasmodium falciparumclearance phenotypes.
articles.authors: Hosangadi V, Fidock DA
articles.details: Systematic review of 96 studies (35 reporting genotype-phenotype data) compared artemisinin resistance dynamics between Southeast Asia and Africa. In Southeast Asia, canonical kelch13 mutations consistently showed prolonged parasite clearance half-life with strong concordance to in vitro ring-stage survival (RSA). In contrast, emergent kelch13 mutations in East and Horn of Africa exhibited faster clearance kinetics despite elevated RSA survival. Authors conclude current surveillance thresholds may under-detect emerging artemisinin resistance in Africa and recommend region-specific surveillance criteria.

articles.headline: RCT comparing low-dose vs standard primaquine gametocytocidal efficacy
articles.title_original: Protocol for a multicentric open-label randomized controlled trial comparing low-dose (0.25 mg/kg) and standard-dose (0.75 mg/kg) primaquine for gametocytocidal efficacy and safety in Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Gulati P, Singhal R, Gupta A, Anjali, Tripathi PK, Kumar D, Sinha DP, Singh SP, Singh K, Baharia RK, Bhatt KN, Vasava AH, Gamit A, Patki MB, Malla WA, Kori L, Savargaonkar D, Chakma N, Parmar D, Alagesan L, Kashyap R, Singh K, Anvikar AR, Bharti PK, Nitika N
articles.details: Multicentric open-label randomized controlled trial protocol at four sites in India (target n=496) comparing low-dose primaquine (0.25 mg/kg day-1) versus standard-dose (0.75 mg/kg day-2) for gametocytocidal efficacy and safety in uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Efficacy assessed by gametocyte density and prevalence via microscopy and quantitative RT-qPCR for Pfs25 mRNA (days 1–14); safety assessed by hemoglobin changes (days 1–28). Enrollment began August 2025 and remains ongoing.

articles.headline: ITN ownership at 77% in Tanzania, disparities by sex and age
articles.title_original: Socio-demographic predictors of insecticide-treated bed net ownership and utilization for protection against malaria by rural community members across five regions of Mainland Tanzania.
articles.authors: Chacha GA, Seth MD, Mandai SS, Petro DA, Challe DP, Kisambale AJ, Budodo R, Madebe RA, Mbwambo RB, Bakari C, Pereus D, Aaron S, Mbwambo D, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Mandara CI, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 10,228 individuals across five regions of Tanzania (July–August 2023) assessed insecticide-treated bed net (ITN) ownership and use. Overall, 77.6% reported owning ITNs and 77.2% using them, but coverage varied significantly across districts (p<0.001). Females had higher odds of ownership and use than males (aOR = 1.27, 95% CI 1.12–1.45). Under-fives were more likely to own (aOR = 1.83, 95% CI 1.56–2.15) and use ITNs (aOR = 2.26, 95% CI 1.62–3.15) than adults. Coverage remained below 80% national target.

articles.headline: Actinomycin derivatives show improved selectivity against malaria
articles.title_original: Discovery of actinomycin derivatives with improved selectivity against malaria parasites from a Streptomyces culture library.
articles.authors: Teshima A, Teklemichael AA, Hirata A, Akimoto M, Taniguchi M, Muslimin R, Ogaki S, Ali A, Suzuki T, Mizukami S, Arakawa K
articles.details: Screening of Streptomyces culture library identified strain HUT6035 with antimalarial activity. Researchers isolated actinomycin X2 and X0ß (derivatives of actinomycin D) through fermentation and purification. Actinomycin X2 showed IC50 of 0.241 nM with selectivity index (SI) of 27.9, while actinomycin X0ß showed IC50 of 7.69 nM with SI of 106, both superior to actinomycin D (SI = 14.3). Authors conclude these actinomycin derivatives are promising antimalarial agents with improved selectivity.

articles.headline: PfCoronin mutations confer artemisinin resistance via hemoglobin uptake
articles.title_original: Cellular and molecular basis of PfCoronin function in artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Ullah I, Farringer MA, Khushu M, Burkhard AY, Hathaway E, Willett BC, Shin SH, Sharma AI, Mallick R, Istvan ES, Martin MC, Harrigan O, Shao KL, Dvorin JD, Hartl DL, Volkman SK, Bopp S, Absalon S, Wirth DF
articles.details: Mechanistic study examined how PfCoronin mutations mediate artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum. PfCoronin localizes to parasite plasma membrane, digestive vacuole, and vesicles; mutations disrupt localization and perturb PfActin homeostasis, altering ring-stage morphology and reducing hemoglobin uptake. Decreased hemoglobin uptake limits heme-dependent artemisinin activation, reducing drug efficacy. PfCoronin and PfKelch13 mutations act in distinct compartments without direct interaction, revealing independent resistance mechanisms. Authors conclude non-essential, stage-specific proteins can modulate artemisinin susceptibility.

articles.headline: STH exposure not associated with asymptomatic P. falciparum infection
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum Infection Was Not Associated with Exposure to Soil-Transmitted Helminths in Children in Esse, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Lajos L, Fogang B, Jensen A, Atchombat D, Cornwall DH, Donkeu C, Nana-Mbianda CM, Slam C, Djeunga HCN, Zhan B, Koki Ndombo PO, Ayong LS, Lamb TJ
articles.details: Longitudinal study of 134 school-age children in Esse, Cameroon (September 2019–March 2020) examined whether soil-transmitted helminth (STH) exposure associates with asymptomatic P. falciparum infection. At baseline, 94.8% were microscopy-positive and 85.8% had asymptomatic infections; 87.4% were seropositive for STH antigens. No significant difference in STH exposure between asymptomatic and symptomatic malaria children (85.7% vs 90.9%, p>0.05). No correlation found between anti-STH antibody level and P. falciparum load (p>0.05). Authors conclude STH exposure is not a major factor in asymptomatic P. falciparum carriage.

articles.headline: Correction to malaria elimination challenges in Thai-Myanmar border
articles.title_original: Correction: Challenges and responses of malaria elimination in a high-endemic area along the Thai-Myanmar border: A health systems perspective.
articles.authors: Saita S, Parker DM, Suk-Uam K, Phuanukoonnon S, Pooseesod K
articles.details: This is a correction notice for a previously published article on malaria elimination challenges in a high-endemic area along the Thai-Myanmar border. No new findings or data are provided.

articles.headline: dCas9-SSAP offers cleavage-free genome editing for Plasmodium
articles.title_original: The dCas9-SSAP as a promising genome editing tool in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Quansah E, Yang S, Jia Y, Yu L, Zhang C
articles.details: Review article discussing dCas9-SSAP (catalytically inactive Cas9 with microbial single-stranded annealing proteins) as a next-generation CRISPR-based genome editing tool for Plasmodium. Unlike classical Cas9 nucleases that create double-strand breaks and inheritable genetic scars, dCas9-SSAP is a cleavage-free editing approach. The review explores the mechanistic framework for dCas9-SSAP-mediated editing and its alignment with Plasmodium parasite biology, proposing this technology as a solution to genotoxicity pitfalls in existing CRISPR-Cas9 tools.

articles.headline: Novel surface antigens identified on P. falciparum-infected RBCs
articles.title_original: Identification of Novel Malaria Antigens Expressed on the Surface of RBCs Infected with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Shakri AR, Mohapatra AD, Badhai J, Anand A, Varghese A, Raj DK
articles.details: Researchers immunized mice with P. falciparum-infected RBCs and used phage display screening to identify potential vaccine candidates. Polyclonal sera demonstrated specific interactions with infected RBC surface antigens and showed significant parasite-killing activity in in vitro growth inhibition assays. Both known antigens and several previously uncharacterized antigens on the infected RBC membrane were identified, supporting further evaluation as vaccine candidates.

articles.headline: ACE polymorphisms associated with severe P. falciparum malaria case
articles.title_original: Potential Role of ACE Polymorphisms in Severe Malaria: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Pisapia R, Pomicino A, Minei G, Iadevito I, Perrella A
articles.details: Case report of a 40-year-old woman from Ivory Coast with severe P. falciparum malaria complicated by thrombosis. Laboratory findings included hyperparasitemia, acute kidney injury, and metabolic acidosis. Genetic testing identified ACE I/D polymorphism along with thrombophilic variants MTHFR C677T and A1298C, suggesting potential genetic factors in disease severity.

articles.headline: Long-acting ivermectin achieves sustained mosquito-killing efficacy
articles.title_original: Selection of the optimal long-acting injectable formulation of ivermectin for use in humans to target malaria vectors in Western Africa: evaluation of pharmacokinetics and mosquitocidal efficacy in cattle under laboratory conditions.
articles.authors: Zela L, Pooda SH, Porciani A, Beneteau S, Sagna AB, Le Lamer-Déchamps S, Moiroux N, Ouédraogo COW, Somé AF, Pennetier C, Roberge C, Belem AMG, Dabiré KR, Dayo GK, Mouline K
articles.details: Three long-acting injectable ivermectin formulations (LAIFs) based on BEPO depot technology were evaluated in cattle for pharmacokinetics and mosquitocidal efficacy. The mdc-STM-001 formulation showed the most favorable profile with Cmax of 34.5±12.7 ng/ml and 10-day hazard ratios >4 against both insecticide-susceptible Anopheles gambiae Kisumu and resistant VK5 strains for ≥60 days. All formulations met WHO Preferred Product Characteristics, with mdc-STM-001 maintaining cumulative mosquito mortalities >50% for ≥2 months.

articles.headline: Climatic factors and vector control influence malaria incidence
articles.title_original: The influence of climatic factors and vector control on malaria incidence in Southeast Iran: a 23-year longitudinal analysis.
articles.authors: Dehghan A, Rahimi Foroushani A, Rahimi M, Nejati J, Youssefi F, Nikpour F, Raeisi A, Hanafi-Bojd AA
articles.details: A 23-year longitudinal analysis examined the influence of climatic factors and vector control interventions on malaria incidence in Southeast Iran. Specific quantitative results are not provided in the title.

articles.headline: Substantial malaria-arbovirus co-exposure in remote Congo Basin
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Malaria and Arbovirus Co-Exposure in the Boende Health Zone, Northwestern Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Tshilumba SM, Larivière Y, Zola Matuvanga T, Mutwadi A, Engbu D, Kapour G, Lemey G, Salloum M, Champagne M, Mangungulu D, Van Damme P, Muhindo-Mavoko H, Tevuzula VM, Mariën J, Peeters M, Geertruyden JV, Mitashi-Mulopo P
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey in December 2023 of 379 adults in Inkanamongo village, Boende Health Zone, DRC. Malaria prevalence was 51.7% (95% CI: 46.7–56.7); overall arboviral seroprevalence was 78.4% (95% CI: 73.1–81.5), dominated by O'nyong-nyong (42.8%), Rift Valley fever (32.0%), and chikungunya (23.4%). Concurrent malaria and arboviral exposure occurred in 40.4% (95% CI: 35.6–45.4) of participants, with no significant sociodemographic predictors identified.

articles.headline: Worn LLINs ineffective against resistant vectors in refugee camp
articles.title_original: Physical integrity and bioefficacy of long-lasting insecticidal nets 18 months after use among Central African Republic refugees in Gado-Badzere, a high malaria burden camp in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nematchoua Weyou Z, Djemna Djieyep F, Lontsi-Demano M, Ngangue-Siewe IN, Tchigossou G, Ning Teh R, Adanzounon D, Kuokuo Kimbi H, Ule Ngole Sumbele I
articles.details: Cross-sectional evaluation of 440 long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs) 18 months post-distribution in Gado-Badzere refugee camp, Cameroon. Physical integrity: 60.7% in good condition, 14.5% acceptable, 24.8% deteriorated. All 30 LLINs tested were ineffective against field Anopheles gambiae s.l., with knock-down rate 1.5% and mortality rate 3.1%. An. gambiae (86.9%) and An. coluzzii (13.1%) were identified; net deterioration associated with frequent washing and harsh detergents.

articles.headline: Madagascar shows no artemisinin resistance markers in 2024 survey
articles.title_original: Updated mapping of Plasmodium falciparum kelch13 gene polymorphisms in Madagascar, 2024.
articles.authors: Roesch C, Ratovoson R, Delaunay C, Savary S, Duteurtre L, Saiman J, Villier V, Bourke C, Elison HE, Maminjanahary ET, Rabetokotany RL, Rakotondramanana HN, Mangahasimbola RT, Razafimaharo LY, Raobela O, Steinhardt L, Bowen A, Ariey F, Randremanana R, Coppée R, Witkowski B
articles.details: Nationally representative cross-sectional survey (January–May 2024) of 4,850 febrile patients at 65 facilities. Of 1,944 P. falciparum-positive samples, 963 (49.5%) underwent pfkelch13 genotyping via targeted amplicon deep sequencing: 885 (91.9%) wild-type 3D7, non-synonymous mutations in 1.8% (17/963), synonymous mutations in 6.2% (60/963), and one isolate (0.1%) with double mutations. No WHO-validated artemisinin resistance markers detected; most frequent polymorphisms were synonymous C469C (3.0%) and P417P (2.8%).

articles.headline: P. berghei infection protects against lethal Babesia via macrophages
articles.title_original: Immunity beyond borders: non-lethal Plasmodium confers cross-protection against lethal Babesia via macrophage activation.
articles.authors: Zafar I, Li Y, Kondoh D, El-Sayed SAE, Hasan T, Taniguchi T, Li H, Inoue N, Kato K, Xuan X
articles.details: Co-infection study in mice with P. berghei XAT and Babesia rodhaini. P. berghei infection significantly reduced Babesia parasitemia and splenic damage. Protection was mediated by macrophage-driven innate immunity with increased pro-inflammatory cytokines (IFN-γ, IL-12p70, TNF-α), balanced by increased IL-10, and elevated ROS/NO production. Macrophage depletion eliminated cross-protection, while NK depletion and SCID mice retained protection, confirming macrophage-dependent immunity.

articles.headline: Malaria-derived peptides show antibiofilm and anti-inflammatory activity
articles.title_original: Computer-Aided Design of Plasmodium chabaudi-Derived Peptides with Dual Antibiofilm and Anti-inflammatory Activities.
articles.authors: Quigua Orozco RM, Santos ADO, Rossetto JP, Madalozzo ES, Luchi LV, Rezende SB, Chan LY, Buccini DF, Macedo MLR, Mehta A, Craik DJ, Franco OL, Cardoso MH
articles.details: Three Plasmodium chabaudi-derived peptides (PcDBS1R1, PcDBS1R5, PcDBS1R9) generated using Joker computational design algorithm. PcDBS1R5 and PcDBS1R9 adopted α-helical structures with high amphipathicity and displayed antibacterial activity against Acinetobacter baumannii; PcDBS1R1 showed pronounced antibiofilm effects. All peptides significantly reduced nitric oxide production in LPS-stimulated macrophages with no cytotoxicity toward murine macrophages, suggesting immunomodulatory therapeutic potential.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine remodels gut microbiome, improving birthweight
articles.title_original: Beyond malaria prevention: sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine treatment in pregnancy selectively remodels the maternal gut microbiome to increase gestational weight gain and improve birthweight.
articles.authors: Waltmann A, Puerto-Meredith SM, Chinkhumba J, Mzembe E, Kayange M, Carroll I, Roach J, Mathanga DP, Gutman JR, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Longitudinal characterization of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) antifolate effects on pregnant gut microbiome versus dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) in intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy. Approximately 45% of SP's gestational weight gain advantage over DP was explained by microbiome changes. SP recipients achieved 126g higher birthweight (95% CI: 22.6–229.3g; p=0.019). Relative to DP, SP suppressed pathobionts and enriched anaerobic commensals, producing microbiome-mediated benefits distinct from conventional antibiotic dysbiosis.

articles.headline: Quantized Vision Transformers remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks
articles.title_original: Evaluating the Adversarial Robustness and Clinical Safety of Quantized Hierarchical Transformers for Edge-Based Malaria Microscopy.
articles.authors: Hasan U, Alghamdi TG, Nayeem MA
articles.details: Authors evaluated quantized Vision Transformer variants (Swin-Tiny, ViT-Tiny, MobileNetV3) on 27,558 malaria microscopy images. Post-training quantization achieved 3.9× model compression with only 0.11% accuracy loss and maintained out-of-distribution reliability. However, both models collapsed to 0% accuracy under iterative PGD adversarial attacks, while black-box transfer attacks maintained 81% accuracy. Quantized models meet mobile deployment constraints but lack inherent defense against targeted perturbations.

articles.headline: Erucamide from Streptomyces shows binding affinity for Plasmodium DXR
articles.title_original: Antimalarial Candidate Study from Three Different Fractions of Streptomyces hygroscopicus subsp. Hygroscopicus targeting Plasmodium DXR: An In Silico Approach.
articles.authors: Faratisha IFD, Fitri LE, Winaris N, Widodo N
articles.details: This in silico study screened 86 compounds from three Streptomyces fractions against Plasmodium DXR using molecular docking and 100 ns dynamics simulations. Compounds identified: 18 from fraction 13, 24 from fraction 50, 44 from fraction 51; 9 selected for antimalarial potential. Dibenzylamine and erucamide showed higher DXR binding than native ligand; erucamide complex demonstrated superior stability in dynamics. Further in vitro/in vivo validation is needed to confirm efficacy.

articles.headline: Multi-channel media exposure improves malaria prevention knowledge in Uganda
articles.title_original: Effect of cumulative exposure to media channels for malaria messages on knowledge of malaria prevention among women (15-49 years) in Uganda.
articles.authors: Natuhamya C, Obiora RU, Nwankwo GI, Agbi DM, Isiko I, Mwebesa E
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 7,124 women (15-49 years) from the 2018-2019 Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey assessed media exposure and prevention knowledge. Awareness varied: 76.5% knew bed nets vs. only 9.1% ITNs, 6.2% preventive medicine, 4.3% IRS. Multi-channel media exposure significantly improved knowledge of ITNs (p<0.001), preventive medicine (p=0.002), IRS (p<0.001). Secondary+ education increased multi-channel exposure 1.86-fold vs. uneducated women (95% CI 1.59-2.17).

articles.headline: Methylene blue maintains activity against kelch13-mutant artemisinin-resistant parasites
articles.title_original: Methylene blue retains in vitro activity against early ring-stage artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Verdier O, Christensen P, Kaewkanya C, Kobphan P, Beau C, Phyo AP, Imwong M, Chotivanich K, White NJ, Nosten F, Chaumeau V
articles.details: Ring survival assay tested methylene blue against 22 P. falciparum isolates with kelch13 wild-type (n=3), R561H (n=9), or P441L (n=10) mutations. Mean IC50 estimates showed no kelch13-mediated cross-resistance: wild-type 23 nM (95% CrI 15-37), R561H 27 nM (21-36), P441L 14 nM (10-20). Methylene blue suppressed ring-stage survival at low nanomolar concentrations across all genotypes, retaining activity against artemisinin-resistant parasites.

articles.headline: P. vivax infects Duffy-negative Sudanese populations through parasite adaptation
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax infection in Duffy-negative populations in Sudan: a systematic review and meta-analysis of host-parasite genetic adaptation.
articles.authors: Elfaki M, Ahmed S, Albsheer MMA, Hamid MMA
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 16 studies (5,753 Sudanese participants, 2005-2025) on P. vivax in Duffy-negative populations. Pooled prevalence of P. vivax among Duffy-negative individuals: 11.7% (95% CI 7.2-17.3%, I²=56%). Substantial genetic diversity in parasite Duffy Binding Protein (PvDBP) observed, including multiple haplotypes and both Malagasy-type and Cambodian-type gene duplications. Findings challenge the paradigm of complete Duffy-mediated protection and suggest parasite genetic adaptation enables Duffy-negative infection.

articles.headline: Environmental factors shape avian Plasmodium communities in Culex mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Environmental factors affect the communities of avian Plasmodium in two species of Culex mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Martín-López AI, Ruiz-López MJ, Martínez-de la Puente J, Magallanes S, Taheri S, Ruiz S, Figuerola J
articles.details: Mosquitoes collected from 16 southwest Spanish sites over two years were screened for Plasmodium by nested PCR and sequencing. In Culex pipiens, prevalence increased with NDVI while lineage richness declined with land surface temperature. In C. perexiguus, prevalence and richness increased with minimum temperature. Plasmodium communities differed between species, with similarity correlating with distance only in C. pipiens. Environmental factors and vector identity shape avian malaria transmission in changing environments.

articles.headline: Merozoite antibodies predict protection from experimental malaria infection
articles.title_original: Controlled human malaria infection in adults identify combinations of merozoite antigens associated with clinical immunity.
articles.authors: Ogwang R, Nkumama IN, Mwai K, Odera D, Nyamako L, Fürle K, Rosenkranz M, Kimathi R, Njuguna P, Sim BKL, Hamaluba M, Frank R, Kapulu MC, Bejon P, Tuju J, Osier FHA
articles.details: CHMI study of 142 Kenyan adults (86 clinically immune; ClinicalTrials.gov NCT02739763) measured IgG antibodies to 70 merozoite antigens using custom protein microarray (KILchip). Five statistical methods (Wilcoxon test, Cox model, Poisson regression, LASSO, random forest) identified MSP1, MSP11, RAMA, MSP7, PHISTB, and PTEX150 as consistently associated with clinical immunity. Antibodies against these antigens in combination predicted complete protection from experimental malaria infection.

articles.headline: Malaria co-infection occurs in nearly half of Yemeni IBS patients
articles.title_original: Systemic inflammation and malaria co-infection in irritable bowel syndrome: a cross-sectional study of 142 Yemeni patients.
articles.authors: Ali NT, Mehdi MAH, Abdullah RS, Gubran ANM, Al-Abd NM, Ali GS, Mohsen Ali H
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 142 Yemeni adults with IBS (Rome IV criteria, April-December 2024) measured malaria co-infection and inflammatory biomarkers. Malaria prevalence: 45.1% (64/142). Malaria-positive patients showed elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR, mean difference 0.56, p<0.001) and platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR, mean difference 22.6, p<0.001). In adjusted logistic regression, elevated NLR (aOR 3.21, 95% CI 1.89-5.45) and PLR (aOR 4.02 per 10-unit, 95% CI 2.24-7.18) independently predicted malaria positivity.

articles.headline: Regulatory T cells mediate clinical immunity but delay parasite clearance
articles.title_original: CXCR6+CD127- Tr1 cells balance immunity and persistence in Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Nideffer J, Bach F, Strubbe S, Lopez L, Zedi M, Nankya F, Briggs J, van der Ploeg K, Musinguzi K, Kim S, Garcia Romero A, Keya A, Camanag K, Lewis S, Abdelbasset M, Wang B, Boss A, Nansubuga E, Nankabirwa JI, Arinaitwe E, Takahashi S, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Rodriguez-Barraquer I, Kamya MR, Bacchetta R, Ssewanyana I, Haque A, Roncarolo MG, Jagannathan P
articles.details: scRNA-seq and validation identified CXCR6+ CD127– as markers for Plasmodium-specific Tr1 cells, which comprise ~90% of Pf-specific CD4+ T cells in children from malaria-endemic Uganda. Tr1 cells potently suppressed other CD4+ T cell proliferation via IL-10 secretion in vitro. Higher CXCR6+ CD127– Tr1 frequencies correlated with lower symptom probability given parasitemia but also with delayed parasite clearance in untreated asymptomatic children, suggesting these cells balance immunity and persistence.

articles.headline: P. malariae shows atypical genome organization and recovery from bottleneck
articles.title_original: Population genomics of Plasmodium malariae from four African countries.
articles.authors: Popkin-Hall ZR, Carey-Ewend K, Aghakhanian F, Oriero EC, Seth MD, Kashamuka MM, Ngasala B, Ali IM, Mukomena Sompwe E, Mandara CI, Kharabora O, Sendor R, Simkin A, Amambua-Ngwa A, Tshefu A, Fola AA, Ishengoma DS, Bailey JA, Parr JB, Lin JT, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of 77 P. malariae isolates from Cameroon (n=7), DRC (n=16), Nigeria (n=4), Tanzania (n=50; 2015-2021). No geographic population structure detected. Nucleotide diversity significantly lower than co-localized P. falciparum; linkage disequilibrium significantly higher. Targeted analyses identified selective sweeps in four erythrocyte invasion ligands and six antimalarial resistance orthologs. Demographic modeling suggests African P. malariae recovery from a bottleneck, with implications for genomic epidemiology of persistent infections.

articles.headline: Pirimiphos-methyl IRS substantially reduces malaria in pyrethroid-resistant settings
articles.title_original: Impact of indoor residual spraying and insecticide-treated nets on malaria burden in 8 districts in West Nile and Acholi regions, Uganda: a quasi-experimental study.
articles.authors: Namuganga JF, McDermott DP, Epstein A, Nankabirwa JI, Gonahasa S, Opigo J, Nabende I, Maiteki-Sebuguzi C, Kamya MR, Donnelly MJ, Dorsey G, Staedke SG
articles.details: Quasi-experimental study compared baseline (Dec 2020-Nov 2022), IRS with clothianidin-deltamethrin (Dec 2022-Dec 2023), and pirimiphos-methyl (Jan-Dec 2024) periods across West Nile (IRS + ITNs) and Acholi (ITNs only) Uganda. During IRS-1: incidence fell in both arms (intervention 720.9→547.9; control 523.4→455.2 per 1000 person-years), with non-significant 15% difference (aIRR 0.85, 95% CI 0.69-1.04). During IRS-2: intervention declined 79.3% (to 149.1), control 24.3% (to 396.2); 73% between-arm reduction (aIRR 0.27, 95% CI 0.22-0.34, p<0.001).

articles.headline: Pyrazole inhibitors show selective PfDHODH inhibition with antiparasitic activity
articles.title_original: Structure-based design of new pyrazole inhibitors targeting Plasmodium falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (PfDHODH).
articles.authors: Pippione AC, Villella N, Fornaseri F, Giorgis M, Sharma M, Rolando B, Sainas S, Salamanca E, Gimenez A, Cojean S, Vyas VK, Buccinnà B, Ramondetti C, Piccinini M, Poli G, Tuccinardi T, Alberti M, Miggiano R, Lolli ML, Boschi D
articles.details: Structure-based design optimized hydroxypyrazole compounds against P. falciparum dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (PfDHODH) through three iterative rounds with X-ray crystallography validation. Best compounds (derivatives 6, 7, 12, 13, 22) achieved PfDHODH IC50 1.3-5.9 μM, with selectivity over human enzyme (SI>30 in some cases). P. falciparum EC50: 15-22 μM. Mammalian cytotoxicity negligible (CC50 >150 μM except compound 22). Findings provide structure-activity relationships for next-generation PfDHODH inhibitors.

articles.headline: Mosquito-parasite protein complex identified as transmission-blocking target
articles.title_original: Disruption of the AgMOBP1-PfSyn5 interface abolishes malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Ramelow J, Niu G, Kostallas L, Lai L, Wang X, Li J
articles.details: Identified AgMOBP1 (Anopheles gambiae midgut protein) as host factor promoting P. falciparum transmission. Anti-PfSyn5 antibodies achieved complete transmission-blocking inhibition at 1 μg/mL with IC50 ~3 ng/mL (~20 pM), approximately one million-fold lower than normal human plasma IgG concentration.

articles.headline: Calcifer acts as mechanical stabilizer of parasite basal body
articles.title_original: Organelle mechanics: A brace for the Plasmodium basal body.
articles.authors: Engstler M
articles.details: Commentary identifying Calcifer as late-recruited stabilizer protecting Plasmodium basal body from mechanical stress during male gamete formation, providing evolutionary solution to biomechanical challenges during axoneme release and exflagellation.

articles.headline: P. vivax dominates persistent malaria threat in Ethiopia near Addis Ababa
articles.title_original: The persistent threat of malaria in Bishoftu Town: twelve-year trends and their implications for Addis Ababa.
articles.authors: Dufera BC, Mamo H
articles.details: Analysis of 252,610 blood films from Bishoftu Town (Jan 2011–Dec 2022) identified 5,696 cases (2.3%): P. vivax 63.8%, P. falciparum 31.0%. Peak incidence 804 cases in 2017; individuals >14 years represented 66.3% of cases. Persistent endemic transmission with fluctuating trends.

articles.headline: Invasive Anopheles stephensi vector first detected south of equator
articles.title_original: Serendipitous detection of invasive malaria vector Anopheles stephensi in Kisumu, Kenya in June 2022.
articles.authors: Ndenga BA, Owuor KO, Wambua S, Bartilol B, Maia M, Mwangangi J, Omukuti R, Chemutai S, Arabu D, Miringu I, Bosire C, Mwendwa K, Winter CA, Rono MK, Mutuku FM, Taylor R, Bisanzio D, LaBeaud AD, Gerken KN
articles.details: Molecular analysis of mosquitoes from urban slaughterhouse in Kisumu, Kenya (mid-2022) identified An. stephensi in 19.6% (11/56 ASVs) initially misclassified as An. gambiae s.l. Phylogenetic analysis of cox1 gene confirmed two clades with haplotypes clustering with Ethiopia and Sudan populations. First report south of equator.

articles.headline: Berzosertib repurposed for malaria with lower dose than cancer therapy
articles.title_original: Repurposing Drugs for Malaria through a Human Dose Prediction: A Case Study with Berzosertib.
articles.authors: Redhi D, Mulubwa M, Mmonwa MM, Njoroge M, Krugmann L, Chibale K
articles.details: Dose-fractionation study in P. falciparum-infected humanized mice evaluated berzosertib pharmacokinetics-pharmacodynamics. Predicted efficacious human dose: six daily IV doses of 75 mg (lower than cancer maximum tolerated dose of 440 mg IV twice weekly over 21-day cycles). Activity strongly dependent on time above minimum parasiticidal concentration.

articles.headline: Antibiotics repurposed as antimalarials targeting RAD5 and WD11 proteins
