articles.title_original: Repositioning Antibiotics Against Plasmodium falciparum RAD5 and WD11 as Novel Antimalarial Targets.
articles.authors: Albaqami AS, Oyewole OA, Adam KM
articles.details: Computational screening of 216 antibiotics and in vitro testing against P. falciparum 3D7. Puromycin showed 85% inhibition at 100 μg/mL; triple combination (puromycin + raltegravir + dicloxacillin) achieved complete inhibition. Puromycin + chloroquine showed synergistic effects in concentration-dependent manner.

articles.headline: Calcifer protein stabilizes malaria parasite basal body under mechanical stress
articles.title_original: Mechanoregulation of basal body integrity during Plasmodium exflagellation.
articles.authors: Guan J, Gong Y, Liang W, Cui H, Yuan J
articles.details: Identified Calcifer as essential for Plasmodium male gametogenesis and mosquito transmission. Parasites lacking Calcifer form intact axonemes but show rapid disintegration during exflagellation. Calcifer directly stabilizes basal body through wrapping structure at axoneme minus end, resisting mechanical stress from axoneme beating.

articles.headline: Malaria surveillance shows autumn peaks and spatial heterogeneity in Pakistan
articles.title_original: Spatial Distribution, seasonal dynamics and molecular confirmation of malaria in District Malakand, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Rabia R, Ullah W
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Analysis of clinical data from District Malakand (2011–2017): 41,863 tests yielded 6,246 positives (14.92% positivity rate). Males more affected (58.89%); highest cases in 1–20 year age group. Annual test positivity declined from 32.97% (2011) to 10.77% (2017). Autumn had highest cases (46.73%), winter lowest (7.09%).

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance confirmed in four African countries threatens treatment
articles.title_original: Africa cannot afford to wait for malaria treatment failure.
articles.authors: Pindolia D, Martinez-Vega R, Uzakbayeva G, Ishengoma D, Ngou O, Gosling R
articles.details: Artemisinin partial resistance (ART-R) now detected via molecular surveillance across eastern Africa, Horn of Africa, and southern Africa. Confirmed in Rwanda, Uganda, Eritrea, and Tanzania with signals in neighboring countries, threatening decades-old artemisinin-based combination therapies as malaria backbone treatment.

articles.headline: Fine-scale spatial analysis reveals stable malaria hotspots in Mali
articles.title_original: Analysis of clinical malaria hotspots according to transmission levels in two health areas of the Kolondieba health district, Mali.
articles.authors: Berthé I, Cissoko M, Diarra M, Mbaga DS, Koné M, Boly A, Togola OB, Haidara B, Théra I, Ongoiba A, Togola T, Mathias Dolo AD, Amaguiré Sy EHI, Konaté A, Coulibaly Y, Sissoko L, Bougoudogo I, Niangado B, Togola A, Traore D, Rabarijaona LP, Keita K, Traoré B, Tidiane Traoré CA, Sagara I
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 35,934 confirmed malaria cases (2019–2021) in Kadiana (high-transmission) and Kolondieba Central (moderate-transmission). Kadiana showed stable clusters with persistent hotspots (RR: 1.8–6.3). Kolondieba Central showed spatial expansion from localized (1–2 villages) to extensive clusters (RR: 1.7–2.2).

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasites detected in Midwestern US mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Heterogeneous distribution of avian malaria parasites across mosquito species: disproportionate involvement of Culex restuans in the Midwestern United States.
articles.authors: Ciloglu A, Mackay AJ, Cara C, Kim CH, Marguccio A, Yan J, Stone CM
articles.details: Screening of 6,170 unfed female mosquitoes from seven species (38 sites in Illinois) identified 44 positive pools (18.9%). Eleven avian haemosporidian lineages detected (9 Plasmodium, 2 Haemoproteus), including one novel lineage. Culex restuans harbored highest lineage diversity and infection rate.

articles.headline: Cis-regulatory elements identified in malaria vector germline for genetic control
articles.title_original: Decoding cis-regulatory elements in the germline of the human malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Chesters E, Ravenscroft L, Thompson TA, Sylla L, Tembely B, Kerr J, Hasenauer J, Page N, Winny T, Nolan T, Sharakhov I, Tonge D, Galizi R
articles.details: Systematic in silico approach identified hundreds of putative cis-regulatory elements (CREs) correlated with germline expression in Anopheles gambiae, linked to distinct developmental stages and chromosomal locations. Validation using dual-reporter assay showed conserved nucleotide mutations drastically altered male germline expression.

articles.headline: Sudan conflict disrupts malaria surveillance data completeness
articles.title_original: Impact of the 2023-armed conflict on routine malaria imapact indicators in Sudan (January 2020-March 2025): a mixed-methods analysis of surveillance data.
articles.authors: Alnaeem KF, Saha UR, Ahmed HF, Mergenthaler C
articles.details: Mixed-methods study of DHIS2 surveillance data from 17 Sudanese states (January 2020–March 2025) evaluated impact of April 2023 armed conflict on malaria reporting completeness and indicators. Interrupted time series analysis showed statistically significant decline in quarterly reported malaria case rates per 100,000 population at conflict onset (p=0.02), with substantial increases in missing reports. Key barriers included health facility destruction, workforce shortages, unpaid salaries, and communication breakdowns. Findings demonstrate conflict widened disparities in surveillance system capacity across states.

articles.headline: Synergistic antimalarial activity of chloroquine-nanoparticle combination
articles.title_original: Synergistic Antimalarial Activity of Alpha-Mangostin Chitosan Alginate Nanoparticles with Chloroquine in Mice Infected with Plasmodium berghei: The Potential of a New Antimalarial Drug Combination.
articles.authors: Tjahjani S, Hermanto F, Muchtaridi M, Aulifa DL, Haq FA
articles.details: In vivo efficacy study in P. berghei-infected Swiss Webster mice evaluated alpha-mangostin chitosan alginate nanoparticles (ACAN) combined with chloroquine at three weight ratios using Peter's 4-day suppressive test. ED50 values ranged from 7.626–9.196 mg/kg for most effective ratios (versus 82.13 mg/kg for least effective). Synergistic activity confirmed with ∑FED50 values far below 1 (0.069–0.414), indicating marked synergy. Combination showed particularly good antimalarial activity at 189:1 ACAN:chloroquine weight ratio.

articles.headline: Topography linked to malaria transmission intensity in Pakistan
articles.title_original: A Topographic Analysis of Malaria Transmission - Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan, 2019-2022.
articles.authors: Haq IU
articles.details: Study of malaria transmission patterns across topographic regions of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province, Pakistan (2019–2022) found high-altitude regions experienced low transmission while low-altitude regions showed high transmission rates. Authors conclude considering topography in malaria control strategies may help policymakers design more targeted and effective prevention measures to reduce malaria burden.

articles.headline: World Malaria Day 2026: Driven to End Malaria
articles.title_original: World Malaria Day 2026: Driven to End Malaria.
articles.authors: Zhou X, Chen J, Xia Z, Li S
articles.details: No abstract provided.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria pathophysiology and artesunate treatment
articles.title_original: [Cerebral Malaria].
articles.authors: Ohnishi K
articles.details: Clinical review of cerebral malaria, defined by impaired consciousness in malaria patients. Although parasites infect red blood cells rather than neurons directly, cerebral malaria results from parasitized RBC adherence to brain microvasculature endothelial cells causing vascular obstruction; however, detailed pathophysiology remains unclear. Management recommendation is immediate intravenous artesunate initiation upon clinical suspicion.

articles.headline: PfARMA N-terminal region targeted by naturally acquired malaria antibodies
articles.title_original: The N-terminal region of malaria vaccine candidate asparagine-rich merozoite antigen is immunodominant and targeted by polyreactive antibodies.
articles.authors: Garza R, Marchioni JM, Honeycutt JD, Hurlburt NK, Torres C, Garcia A, Neog S, Loranc E, Yemington E, Towers D, Ssewanyana I, Pancera M, Lavinder JJ, Jagannathan P, Greenhouse B, Bol S, Bunnik EM
articles.details: Study of naturally acquired B cell and antibody responses to asparagine-rich merozoite antigen (PfARMA) in P. falciparum-exposed individuals found responses dominated by IgM+ B cells and IgG targeting the N-terminal intrinsically disordered region (IDR1). Antibody levels increased with P. falciparum exposure but notably did not correlate with protection in age- and exposure-matched children. Approximately 30% of unexposed individuals showed polyreactive IgG cross-reactivity to IDR1, suggesting responses primarily reflect recent exposure rather than functional protective immunity.

articles.headline: Genetic diversity not associated with avian malaria infection
articles.title_original: Avian Malaria Infections Are Not Associated With Host Genetic Diversity: A Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Podmokła E, Pluciński B, Dubiec A
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 35 studies (27 bird species, 85 effect sizes) found no significant association between individual genetic diversity and avian malaria infection (Plasmodium, Haemoproteus, Leucocytozoon) prevalence or intensity, regardless of genetic marker type. Meta-regression analyses showed no significant influence from parasite genus, infection assessment method, migratory behavior, or latitude. Authors note that broad genetic diversity measures may obscure fine-scale genetic effects from specific alleles such as MHC variants.

articles.headline: Digital molecular diagnostic tools for malaria control
articles.title_original: How can the use of digital molecular diagnostic tools help to tackle malaria?
articles.authors: Chamai M, Jebreel WMA, Kabuya JB, Rakotomalala Robinson D, Cunnington AJ
articles.details: No abstract provided.

articles.headline: High Pfdhps mutation frequency indicates sulfadoxine resistance
articles.title_original: Polymorphisms of the plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase gene among patients attending LEPI and ADLUCEM hospitals in the west region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Edna GDM, Nadia NAC, Cedric Y, Raoul SNS, Christian NO, Pacome TK, Sidiki NNA, Sandra TNJ, Merveille TT, Line GAP, Yval KT, Hu H, Choukem SP
articles.details: Molecular epidemiology study of 286 samples (198 women, 88 men; mean age 29±20 years) from two health facilities in Mbouda, Cameroon found P. falciparum in 87 (30.41%). Parasite density averaged 2343±1240 parasites/μL in males and 2283±1483/μL in females. RFLP analysis of Pfdhps gene revealed high mutation frequencies: A581G 67.60%, A437G 45.07%, with wild-type frequencies of 22.53% and 48.87% respectively. High Pfdhps mutation frequency indicates substantial sulfadoxine resistance pressure in the region.

articles.headline: Human-wildlife malaria transmission via zoonotic Plasmodium in Amazon
articles.title_original: Zoonotic and Anthroponotic Plasmodium spp. Circulation between Wild Primates and Indigenous Community, Peruvian Amazon, 2007-2020.
articles.authors: Ulloa GM, Greenwood AD, Cornejo OE, Alonso H, Santolalla Robles ML, Montero S, Lescano AG, Mayor P
articles.details: Molecular survey of Plasmodium spp. in 141 Indigenous community members and 341 nonhuman primates (10 species) from Peruvian Amazon (2007–2020) using nested/quantitative PCR and sequencing detected parasites in 43.3% of humans and 51.9% of NHPs. P. vivax/simium predominated in humans (42.1%), while P. brasilianum/malariae predominated in NHPs (24.6%). Bayesian phylogenetic analysis revealed >99.9% sequence identity between human and NHP lineages, supporting shared parasite transmission. Children <8 years showed higher parasite loads; one-third of human infections were asymptomatic.

articles.headline: Meteorological drivers of malaria transmission in South Africa
articles.title_original: Understanding and modeling meteorological drivers of the incidence and spread of malaria in South Africa.
articles.authors: Wright CY, Kapwata T, Naidoo N, Maharaj R, Aleksandrov N, Tošić M, Blesić S
articles.details: Study of five district municipalities in Limpopo province linked weekly hospital admission data with regional temperature, rainfall, and evapotranspiration measurements. Wavelet transform spectral analysis identified characteristic time lags for malaria development. Authors developed a regression model to predict admission cases and identify critical meteorological thresholds initiating disease spread. Projections for 2021–2050 and 2051–2080 generated under Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP2.6 and RCP8.5) to assess climate-change impacts on future malaria burden.

articles.headline: Long-acting injectable ivermectin sustained impact on malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of ivermectin-based optimal strategies on malaria control: the role of formulation, coverage, and mosquitocidal efficacy timing.
articles.authors: Dongmo FJ, Porciani A, Moiroux N, Pooda HS, Dabiré RK, Beneteau S, Le Lamer-Déchamps S, Wondji CS, Mouline K, Djidjou-Demasse R
articles.details: Mathematical model compared three ivermectin regimens: monthly oral IVM (3×300 μg/kg for 4 months or 1×400 μg/kg for 3 months) and long-acting injectable ivermectin (BEPO® at 0.6–1.0 mg/kg). Under 50–90% population coverage, oral regimens reduced annual cases by 7–38% depending on regimen and data source. Single long-acting injectable campaign achieved 16–29% case reduction, increasing to 59–78% with four sequential campaigns. Study population excluded pregnant women and children <15 kg, demonstrating long-acting formulations provide sustained efficacy for residual transmission control.

articles.headline: Pharmacotherapy approaches for malaria in infants
articles.title_original: The impact of state-of-the-art pharmacotherapy for malaria in infants: a breakthrough?
articles.authors: Grobusch MP, Adegbite BR, Hanscheid T, Gupta N
articles.details: Title only; abstract not provided. Article examines state-of-the-art pharmacotherapy strategies for malaria treatment in infants.

articles.headline: Plasmodium and Haemoproteus in Malaysian poultry
articles.title_original: Molecular detection and genetic diversity of avian haemosporidia (Plasmodium and Haemoproteus) infecting red jungle fowls (Gallus gallus spadiceus) and domestic poultry in peninsular, Malaysia.
articles.authors: Fufa Ido G, Sharma RSK, Zakaria A, Lin LC, Ola-Fadunsin SD, Abdullah DA, Babjee SMA, Sani RA
articles.details: Molecular and microscopic survey of 713 poultry samples in peninsular Malaysia (241 red jungle fowl, 322 village chickens, 150 broilers). Plasmodium prevalence 4.6% by microscopy, 30.6% by PCR; Haemoproteus 1.1% microscopically, 10.7% by PCR. Molecular characterization confirmed P. gallinaceum, P. parasitense, and Haemoproteus sp. in avian hosts.

articles.headline: Plasmodium huffi in diverse avian host species
articles.title_original: No host too tough: New records of Plasmodium huffi in competent avian hosts, including Brazilian vultures and falcons.
articles.authors: Vieira LMC, Braga ÉM
articles.details: Molecular and morphological study of P. huffi in previously unreported avian hosts in Brazil: black vultures (Coragyps atratus, n=29), yellow-headed caracaras (Daptrius chimachima, n=6), and red-legged seriemas (n=14). First haemosporidian infection documented in New World vultures and first P. huffi report in Falconiformes. Parasite demonstrates high phenotypic plasticity across host species.

articles.headline: Agricultural insecticides for malaria vector control
articles.title_original: Malaria vector control: Can repurposed agricultural insecticides offer solutions to resistance issues?
articles.authors: Malone D, Zahouli JZB, Ekra AK, Lucas JR, Van Leeuwen T, Koudou BG
articles.details: Review evaluating agricultural insecticides (neonicotinoids, pyrroles, broflanilide, meta-diamides, growth regulators, ivermectin, methoxyfenozide, halofenozide) against Anopheles and other mosquitoes. Some are WHO-endorsed components of ITNs and indoor residual sprays; others under laboratory and field testing. Compounds demonstrated insecticidal activity against larval and adult stages and affected Plasmodium development, offering dual-action potential for transmission reduction.

articles.headline: New class of malaria vaccines in development
articles.title_original: Eizo Takashima-developing a new class of malaria vaccines.
articles.authors: Kirby T
articles.details: Title only; abstract not provided. Article focuses on malaria vaccine development work.

articles.headline: Uncomplicated malaria does not worsen COVID-19
articles.title_original: Uncomplicated malaria as a risk factor for COVID-19 duration and severity in western Kenya and Burkina Faso (MALCOV): a prospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Barsosio HC, Tangara B, Marlais T, Kaboré JMT, Tiono AB, Otieno K, Wanjiku M, Achieng M, Onyango ED, Ondieki ED, Aura H, Odawo T, Allen DJ, Hannan L, Tetteh KKA, Soulama I, Ouedraogo A, Serme SS, Soulama BI, Barry A, Badoum ES, Matthewman J, Brazal-Monzó H, Canizales J, Drabko A, Wu W, Kariuki S, Lesosky M, Sirima SB, Drakeley C, Ter Kuile FO
articles.details: Prospective cohort of COVID-19 patients in Kenya and Burkina Faso (n=742; 151 with P. falciparum malaria co-infection, 591 without). Time to symptom resolution similar (median 9 days with malaria vs 10 days without; aHR 1.14, 95% CI 0.91–1.42, p=0.26). Hospitalization (2% both groups) and mortality rates comparable; SARS-CoV-2 clearance slower at day 7 in malaria group (aHR 0.69, p=0.017) but similar by day 28. Previous malaria exposure associated with faster symptom clearance.

articles.headline: Malaria and COVID-19 relationship
articles.title_original: The fascinating malaria-COVID-19 relationship.
articles.authors: Awandare GA, Morang'a CM
articles.details: Title only; abstract not provided. Article explores interactions between malaria and COVID-19.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum under intensive control
articles.title_original: Impact of intensive control on malaria population genomics under elimination settings in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Li X, Arya GA, Thu AM, Landier J, Parker DM, Delmas G, Reyes A, Lwin KM, Sriprawat K, Nosten F, Anderson TJC
articles.details: Genome-sequenced 2,270 P. falciparum infections from 283 malaria posts over 58 months (2015–2020) in Kayin State, Myanmar. Intensive control (rapid detection/treatment, mass drug administration) reduced transmission by 97%. Parasite effective population size decreased; artemisinin resistance allele frequency stable until 2020, when single kelch13-R561H genotype predominated. Sustained localized transmission of unique genotypes documented with positive parasite relatedness ≤20 km; mass drug administration caused parasite founder effects.

articles.headline: Membrane protein complexes in gametocyte parasites
articles.title_original: Deciphering Membrane Protein Complexes in Plasmodium falciparum Gametocytes via Integrative Structural Systems Biology.
articles.authors: Mostafavi S, Maurer V, Ruwolt M, Schwartz J, Stein F, Niedermüller K, Gilberger TW, Liu F, Seuring C, Witt S, Kosinski J, Löw C, Filarsky M
articles.details: Cross-linking mass spectrometry and cofractionation analysis characterized membrane protein complexes in P. falciparum gametocytes, with representative complexes modeled using AlphaFold. Study identified known and previously undescribed host-pathogen and pathogen-pathogen membrane-associated complexes across parasite and erythrocyte cellular compartments. Results advance understanding of gametocyte biology relevant to transmission-blocking intervention development.

articles.headline: Proteomics for malaria pathophysiology and biomarkers
articles.title_original: Integrative proteomics to illuminate host-parasite interactions in malaria.
articles.authors: Attallah N, Srivastava H, Carlton JM, Idaghdour Y
articles.details: Perspective arguing proteomics should be core component of malaria systems biology, quantifying protein abundance, post-translational modifications, networks, localization, and turnover in host and parasite. Proteomics can identify mechanisms of cytoadhesion, endothelial dysfunction, immune modulation, tissue-specific pathology, and therapeutic response variability. Emerging high-throughput, single-cell, and spatial proteomics with AI-driven pipelines enable comprehensive system-wide profiling of host-parasite crosstalk.

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge and practices in mining workers
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes and practices of malaria control and prevention in a high-exposure occupational group: A cross-sectional survey of mining workers in Haut-Katanga Province in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
articles.authors: Liang C, Du Y, Frumence G, Ke X, Kasombo PM, Mukolo JM, Gu Y, Zhou H, Zhu G, Huang J, Cao J
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 485 mining workers in Haut-Katanga Province, DRC. 365 (75.3%) possessed adequate malaria knowledge; middle school education (aOR 0.37, 95% CI 0.22–0.64, p<0.01) and early morning/evening shifts (aOR 0.46, 95% CI 0.24–0.90, p=0.02) were barriers to knowledge. Most workers willing to use ITNs (99.3%) and indoor spraying (94.7%); self-reported use high (long sleeves 99.3%, ITNs 91.1%) but significant knowledge-practice gap exists.

articles.headline: Malaria care-seeking among Sudanese migrants in Egypt
articles.title_original: Sudanese migrants' pathways to malaria care in Egypt: An in-depth qualitative study.
articles.authors: Elafef E, Edris H, Abdelrahman S, Elnahry W, Hadjiabduli S, Abdelkhalek A, Adam A, El-Ghitany E, Hilali M, Hamed Arisha A, Bani I, Seedat F, Hargreaves S, Evangelidou S, Requena-Méndez A
articles.details: Qualitative study of 21 Sudanese migrants in Cairo (90.5% female); 24% developed malaria symptoms on arrival, others within six months. 19 participants (90.5%) initially used traditional remedies or self-medication without testing. Care-seeking barriers included lack of trust in healthcare, stigma, quarantine fears, drug side-effect concerns, and misconceptions. Improved access requires targeted health education addressing misconceptions and healthcare system strengthening.

articles.headline: Malaria burden from growth failure declining but reversing recently
articles.title_original: The unequal malaria burden attributable to child growth failure: a global analysis among children under five from 1990 to 2021.
articles.authors: Deng JJ, Cheng XE, Li S, Chang YW, Wang P, Tao SS, Pan HF
articles.details: Global Burden of Disease analysis (1990–2021) of malaria burden attributable to child growth failure in children under 5. Burden declined overall (AAPC −1.26, 95% CI −1.36 to −1.17) but reversed post-2019 (APC 3.66, 95% CI 2.38–4.80). Highest burden in males (ASMR 17.59/100,000), ages 6–11 months (31.63/100,000), and West/Central Africa (ASMR 88.09/100,000). Authors conclude males, infants 6–11 months, and low-SDI regions continue bearing disproportionately high burden.

articles.headline: Dual-antigen vaccine achieves 80% protection in mosquito challenge
articles.title_original: Robust Bioconjugated Antigens Induce Immune Responses Preventing Malaria Infection and its Transmission.
articles.authors: Sivakumaran N, Flores-Garcia Y, Naar CM, Chen S, Mehta M, van Leeuwen FWB, Tripathi A, Murugan R, Roestenberg M, Zavala F, Rehm BHA
articles.details: Dual-antigen vaccine (Pfs47-RTS,S-BP) combining pre-erythrocytic and sexual-stage Plasmodium antigens in biopolymer particles. Vaccine-induced antibody levels 5.3-fold higher than current RTS,S/AS01 protective thresholds. Against mosquito bite challenge, achieved 80.4% protection and 68.15% transmission-reducing activity. Vaccine demonstrated thermostability and induced durable antibody and T cell responses. Authors conclude vaccine shows strong potential to overcome current vaccine limitations.

articles.headline: Novel PfAPP inhibitors achieve nanomolar potency and selectivity
articles.title_original: Hydroxamate-based inhibitors reveal structural determinants of selectivity for Plasmodium falciparum aminopeptidase P.
articles.authors: Mills BJ, Gregory KS, Cozier GE, Taweechai S, Loughlan E, McConkey GA, Isaac RE, Foster R, Acharya KR
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship optimization of Plasmodium falciparum aminopeptidase P (PfAPP) inhibitors targeting proline-containing peptide cleavage. Hydroxamic tetrapeptide compound 6d achieved Ki 685 nM (vs apstatin Ki 16 μM). Further optimization to compound 6e achieved Ki 24 nM. Crystal structure analysis revealed selective binding mechanism via interaction with Tyr617 of the adjacent monomer. Authors propose these compounds provide foundation for potent, selective PfAPP antimalarial therapeutics.

articles.headline: Over half of febrile returned travelers remain undiagnosed
articles.title_original: High rate of undiagnosed cases of fever among returning travelers from tropical areas tested for malaria: a single-center retrospective study.
articles.authors: Caronni S, Pieruzzi ME, Salari F, Behring AH, Rizzo A, Poloni A, Grosso S, Mancon A, Galimberti L, Oreni L, Casalini G, Mileto D, Gori A, Dolci A, Giacomelli A, Antinori S
articles.details: Single-center retrospective study of 778 febrile travelers returning from tropical areas (2018–2024). 446/778 (57%) remained undiagnosed. Among diagnosed cases: dengue 12.2% (95 cases), malaria 9.3% (72 cases), pneumonia 5.4%, arboviral infection 13.6%. Diagnostic capacity improved over time, rising from 29.4% in 2018 to 57.8% in 2024. Authors conclude dengue has emerged as the predominant diagnosis in febrile returned travelers.

articles.headline: G6PD Biosensor outperforms rapid tests for intermediate deficiency
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Accuracy of Point-of-Care Devices for Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency Detection Among Malaria Patients: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Unnikrishnan G, Singh A
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 13 studies evaluating point-of-care G6PD screening in malaria patients. At 30% activity threshold, CareStart showed sensitivity 0.82 (95% CI 0.55–0.95) and specificity 0.96 (0.93–0.98); STANDARD G6PD Biosensor showed sensitivity 0.99 (0.96–1.00) and specificity 0.98 (0.97–0.99). At 70% threshold, CareStart sensitivity dropped to 0.54 (0.31–0.76) while Biosensor maintained sensitivity 0.96 (0.90–0.98). Authors recommend STANDARD G6PD Biosensor for guiding 8-aminoquinoline radical cure.

articles.headline: Natural compound brazilein kills P. falciparum via iron disruption
articles.title_original: Brazilin and its oxidized form "brazilein", the natural fluorescently traceable agents, toxify Plasmodium falciparum to pyknotic death via disrupting its iron homeostasis.
articles.authors: Kulaphisit M, Jaikamlue W, Jaitan N, Sirirak J, Tamdee P, Pomlok K, Chumchanchira C, Changkhong S, Wipasa J, Saenjum C, Lithanatudom P
articles.details: Study using Multispectral Imaging Flow Cytometry identified brazilin and its oxidized form brazilein from Caesalpinia sappan heartwood as anti-plasmodial compounds. Brazilein coordinately binds labile Fe2+ pools in Plasmodium falciparum and red blood cells, inhibits hemoglobin digestion, and binds AMA1 active site to block erythrocyte invasion. Iron homeostasis disruption combined with blocked invasion induces pyknotic death. Authors conclude brazilein is promising natural antimalarial with real-time tracking capability.

articles.headline: Dengue-malaria co-endemicity drives thrombocytopenia in Gabon
articles.title_original: Molecular evidence of co-circulation of different dengue virus serotypes associated with malaria co-infection among febrile children in Libreville, Gabon.
articles.authors: Moutombi Ditombi BC, Fritz M, Kombila Koumavor CA, Ndong Ngomo JM, Moussavou Mabika DA, Ndong Akomezoghe L, Agbanrin AA, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Leroy EM, Bouyou-Akotet MK
articles.details: Retrospective study of 151 febrile children in Libreville, Gabon (September–October 2022). DENV RNA detected in 18.5% (serotypes DENV-1 and DENV-3), malaria in 56.9%, and dengue-malaria co-infection in 9.3%. Children aged 5–10 years had higher odds of co-infection (OR 16.28, 95% CI 1.85–79.40, p=0.002). Severe anemia was most common in malaria (p=0.02), thrombocytopenia most marked in co-infected patients (p<0.01). Authors conclude findings highlight need for improved differential diagnostic approaches.

articles.headline: Tetrandrine shows strong computational affinity for P. falciparum HDP
articles.title_original: Atomistic simulations identify the Tetrandrine as potent anti-malarial drug candidate against Plasmodium falciparum targeting Heme Detoxification Protein (HDP).
articles.authors: Bux K, Abbasi U, Hasan KA, Hussain A, Malik F, Herwig R
articles.details: Computational drug discovery study using AlphaFold modeling and molecular docking to identify inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum heme detoxification protein (HDP). Tetrandrine (bis-benzyl-isoquinoline alkaloid) exhibited highest docking affinity (−13.0 kJ/mol). 100 ns molecular dynamics simulations confirmed complex stability, with MM-PBSA binding energy −30 kcal/mol. Authors propose tetrandrine as thermodynamically favorable HDP inhibitor for targeting artemisinin-resistant parasites.

articles.headline: Urine proteomics identifies P. vivax parasite biomarkers
articles.title_original: Discovery of parasite proteins in the urine of vivax malaria patients through mass spectrometry.
articles.authors: Tripathi H, Kar PP, Babu AP, Srivastava A, Pachori G, Bhatt TK
articles.details: Mass spectrometry study of urine from Plasmodium vivax infected patients identified diagnostic biomarkers for non-invasive malaria detection. Comparison with uninfected controls revealed 252 human proteins and 28 P. vivax proteins present only in infected samples, including rifin-like protein, single-stranded DNA-binding protein, and profilin. Gene ontology analysis identified proteins involved in key biological pathways relevant to both human host and parasite. Authors conclude urine biomarkers could enable non-invasive Plasmodium detection.

articles.headline: Reconsidering measurement and interpretation of malaria particles
articles.title_original: "What are we measuring?" reconsidering the biological identity and clinical interpretation of malaria-derived particles.
articles.authors: Lei Z
articles.details: Perspective on the biological identity and clinical interpretation of malaria-derived particles. Authors examine what these particles represent, how they should be measured, and their appropriate clinical interpretation.

articles.headline: Spatial repellent emanators reduce malaria 22.5% in camps
articles.title_original: The effectiveness of long-lasting spatial repellent emanators against malaria in humanitarian crisis settings in northern Nigeria: a two-arm pragmatic, open-label, controlled trial.
articles.authors: Allan RJ, Scherrer R, Estecha-Querol S, Weetman D, Paris L, Ba'abba Goni U, Idris Abdulhamid F, Nfornuh Alenwi B, Ahmad S, Cross CL, Ashir GM, Waziri M, Ntadom G, Messenger LA
articles.details: Six-month pragmatic trial in 24 displacement camps in Maiduguri, Nigeria with 1,655 children ages 6–10 years (842 intervention, 813 control). Spatial repellent emanators significantly reduced malaria incidence (incidence rate ratio 0.713, 95% CI 0.584–0.869, p=0.001) with protective efficacy 22.5% (HR 0.775, 95% CI 0.637–0.943, p=0.011). Also significantly reduced pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles gambiae density (IRR 0.0231, p<0.0001) and blood feeding (IRR 0.0195, p<0.0001). Authors conclude spatial repellents expand vector control options for humanitarian emergencies.

articles.headline: Cyclic peptides inhibit oocyst rupture protein dimerization
articles.title_original: Targeting the histone-fold dimerization interface of oocyst rupture proteins from Plasmodium berghei for antimalarial inhibitor discovery.
articles.authors: Ballabio F, Bertaso C, Villa M, Livero O, Del Cont Bernard A, Russo R, Gessmann R, Siden-Kiamos I, Preira CMF, Kumawat A, Gabrieli P, Camilloni C, Currà C, Masiero S, Nardini M, Gourlay LJ
articles.details: Structural study of Plasmodium berghei oocyst rupture proteins (ORP1, ORP2) targeting the mosquito phase of parasite transmission. Crystal structure of ORP heterodimer determined at 3.1 Å resolution. Yeast two-hybrid screening with cyclic peptide library identified six candidates inhibiting histone-fold dimerization in vitro. Authors propose targeting ORP dimerization as transmission-blocking strategy, noting in vivo efficacy for oocyst rupture inhibition requires further testing.

articles.headline: Flow cytometry quantifies malaria-derived particles in blood samples
articles.title_original: A novel flow cytometry approach to quantify malaria-derived particles.
articles.authors: Palasuwan A, Sophondilok K, Ampawong S, Srisutham S, Noulsri E, Palasuwan D
articles.details: This study optimized a flow cytometry approach using propidium iodide and RBC lysis to quantify malaria-derived particles (MDPs) in blood. MDP levels increased significantly in P. falciparum and P. vivax infections versus controls (<1 particle/µL), with strong correlations between parasite density and MDPs (R²=0.94 for P. falciparum, R²=0.91 for P. vivax). The authors conclude flow cytometry is reliable for routine diagnosis and treatment monitoring.

articles.headline: First efficacy evidence for multistage malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: First efficacy evidence for a multistage malaria vaccine targeting infection and transmission.
articles.authors: Moncunill G, Dobaño C
articles.details: This study presents the first efficacy evidence for a multistage malaria vaccine that targets both infection and transmission stages of the parasite.

articles.headline: Multistage malaria vaccine shows 76% efficacy in human challenge study
articles.title_original: Efficacy of ProC6C-AlOH/Matrix-M against Plasmodium falciparum infection and mosquito transmission: a phase 2, randomised, controlled human malaria infection study.
articles.authors: Kone M, Plieskatt J, Thienta M, Kamate B, Ofori EA, Gmeiner M, Haidara B, Kanoute MB, Traore B, Poudiouguo OA, Bayogo HS, Diarra I, Bengaly K, Zeguime A, Sylla D, Sacko A, Hoffman SL, McCall MBB, Theisen M, Sagara I
articles.details: In this phase 2 randomized controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) study, 34 malaria-experienced adults in Mali received three doses of ProC6C-AlOH/Matrix-M vaccine (100 μg) or control vaccine. After 94 days, 32 participants underwent CHMI with 22,500 P. falciparum sporozoites; by day 28 post-challenge, 6/16 vaccinated versus 13/16 control participants developed parasitaemia. Vaccine efficacy was 76% (95% CI 36-91, p=0.0022) by time-to-event analysis.

articles.headline: LSA3 protein required for completion of RBC invasion by malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Liver Stage Antigen 3 plays a role in completing the final stages of merozoite invasion of red blood cells.
articles.authors: Morita M, Jennison C, O'Neill MT, Kanoi BN, Ishino T, Tsuboi T, Boddey JA, Takashima E
articles.details: This study investigated Liver Stage Antigen 3 (LSA3), a merozoite dense granule protein, using in vitro cultured parasites and time-lapse imaging. LSA3-knockout parasites exhibited 65% reduction in normal RBC invasion completion and high frequency of aberrant accolé morphology. The authors conclude LSA3 plays an important role in the late-invasion phase of P. falciparum's erythrocytic cycle.

articles.headline: Computational modeling suggests antimalarials target food vacuole protein allosterically
articles.title_original: Computational characterization of a HotDog domain-containing hypothetical protein from the food vacuole of Plasmodium falciparum reveals potential allosteric regulation by antimalarials.
articles.authors: Gupta P, Kaushik NK, Sachdeva C, Mathur K, Pai SS, Saha B, Dhir S, Sharma A
articles.details: This computational study characterized PfHDDCP, a hypothetical food vacuole protein, using bioinformatics tools and molecular docking. The protein contains a HotDog fold characteristic of acyl-CoA thioesterases, with two putative ligand-binding sites: Binding-Site 1 predicted to bind canonical substrates (Acetyl-CoA), and Binding-Site 2 predicted to bind antimalarial compounds. Molecular dynamics simulations showed stable complexes and ligand-induced conformational changes.

articles.headline: High-altitude malaria transmission in Ethiopian highlands sustained locally, not imported
articles.title_original: High-Altitude Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Reservoirs in Ethiopia Are Not Linked to Recent Travel.
articles.authors: Ewnetu Y, Kayode TA, Oduma CO, Adeyemi-Kayode TM, Zemene A, Lemma W, Berhane N, Koepfli C
articles.details: This survey assessed subclinical malaria prevalence across 8 sites in Ethiopian highlands (altitude <1900-2900 m) in 1,241 individuals using qPCR and microscopy. P. falciparum prevalence by qPCR decreased from 67% at 1850 m to 16% at 2850 m; P. vivax ranged 10-34% with no clear altitude trend. Only 20.3% of subclinical individuals reported recent travel, which did not affect infection risk. The authors recommend sites up to 2500 m be considered endemic.

articles.headline: National genomic profiling of P. falciparum resistance in Zambian children
articles.title_original: National genomic profiling of Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial resistance in Zambian children participating in the 2018 malaria indicator survey.
articles.authors: Fola AA, Ciubotariu II, Dorman J, Mwenda MC, Mambwe B, Mulube C, Kasaro R, Hawela MB, Hamainza B, Miller JM, Bailey JA, Moss WJ, Bridges DJ, Carpi G
articles.details: This national study conducted genomic profiling of antimalarial resistance markers in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Zambian children participating in the 2018 malaria indicator survey.

articles.headline: Molecular characterization of malaria vectors in southwestern Nigeria
articles.title_original: Molecular Characterization and transmission indices of malaria vector in a rural community in Osun State south-west Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oyewusi RA, Surakat OA, Abdulsalam MM, Yusuff YO, Igenoza AJ, Iwalewa OZ, Ganiu OD, Busari LO, Adeleke MA
articles.details: This study conducted molecular characterization and assessed transmission indices of malaria vectors in a rural community in Osun State, southwestern Nigeria.

articles.headline: Ethiopia's test-and-treat malaria guideline shows moderate implementation fidelity
articles.title_original: Implementation fidelity of Ethiopia's Malaria test-and-treat guideline amid a resurgence in Amhara Region: A mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Gelaye KA, Asemahagn MA, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: This mixed-methods study assessed fidelity to Ethiopia's test-and-treat malaria guideline in 53 health facilities (38 public, 15 private) in Amhara Region. Overall mean implementation fidelity was 64.3% (SD 12.1), with 40% achieving high fidelity (≥75%); public facilities had higher fidelity than private (median 67% vs 63%, p=0.041). Higher fidelity was associated with greater participant responsiveness (β=3.4, p<0.001), stronger facilitation strategies (β=2.8, p<0.001), and lower perceived intervention complexity (β=2.1, p<0.001).

articles.headline: Dam construction alters vector density but not infection rates in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Characteristics and dynamics of malaria vectors around the Soum dam in Nanoro Health District, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ilboudo H, Sanon EDG, Hien DFS, Traoré F, Hansson H, Kaboré B, Tahita MC, Derra K, Rouamba E, Bouda I, Rouamba T, Alifrangis M, Sorgho H, Magnussen P, Tinto H
articles.details: This entomological study conducted monthly mosquito collections (March 2022-February 2023) in 5 villages around Soum dam in Burkina Faso. Among 11,378 Anopheles collected, An. gambiae s.l. was predominant (86.5%), with An. coluzzii comprising 91.5% of the complex. Vector density was highest in Soum (49.7%) but decreased with distance from the dam; overall sporozoite rate was 6.2%, though infection rates were higher in distant villages (9.5%-8.7%).

articles.headline: Plasmodium gains entry through alternative invasion pathway
articles.title_original: Plasmodium gets inside through the back door.
articles.authors: Jajosky RP, Stowell SR
articles.details: This article discusses an alternative mechanism by which Plasmodium parasites may invade host cells.

articles.headline: P. vivax malaria relapses in soldiers returning from Korean Peninsula
articles.title_original: To Break a Fever: Relapse of Long-Latency Plasmodium vivax Malaria After Service in the Korean Peninsula.
articles.authors: Robben PM, Woo NW, Merk AR, Jansen NK, Mody RM
articles.details: This case series describes 6 active duty military personnel with P. vivax malaria following exposure in Gyeonggi province, Korea (2021). All were males, mean age 23 years, with uncomplicated malaria and parasitemia <1% (0.1%-0.9%). Average time from symptom onset to diagnosis was 57 days (8-121 days); no chemoprophylaxis received. Patients were treated with artemether/lumefantrine and primaquine, with parasitemia clearance after mean 2.8 days.

articles.headline: Plasmodium knowlesi gains ability to invade Duffy-negative red blood cells
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi can adapt to infect Duffy-negative erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Zinga M, Ibrahim A, Mohring F, Chainarin S, Jonsdottir TK, Ngernna S, Amabilino-Perez B, Pholcharee T, Turkiewicz A, Campino S, Clark TG, Miao J, Cui L, Roobsoong W, Sattabongkot J, Moon RW, Nguitragool W
articles.details: In vitro adaptation study demonstrated P. knowlesi can bypass Duffy antigen receptor (DARC) and invade Duffy-negative erythrocytes. Genomic analysis revealed a recombination event between dbpα and dbpγ genes producing a chimeric dbpαγ gene. CRISPR-Cas9 reversion confirmed dbpαγ is essential for Fy- erythrocyte invasion. This expanded invasion plasticity has implications for parasite spread beyond Southeast Asia and understanding atypical invasion pathways.

articles.headline: Multiple heme sources in malaria-infected erythrocytes offer therapeutic targets
articles.title_original: Russian Dolls of Heme Metabolism in Malaria-Infected Red Blood Cells: Nested Vulnerabilities and Therapeutic Opportunities.
articles.authors: Adapa SR, Siddiqui FA, Jiang RHY
articles.details: Review of heme metabolism architecture in malaria-infected RBCs comprising three nested metabolic sources: host erythrocyte heme biosynthesis, parasite heme synthesis, and heme from hemoglobin digestion. Exogenous 5-ALA can perturb host heme biosynthesis, increasing protoporphyrin IX levels and sensitizing parasites to oxidative stress, though effects depend on parasite stage and exposure duration. These compartmentalized metabolic vulnerabilities may be exploitable for therapeutic intervention.

articles.headline: Genetic diversity metrics track P. falciparum transmission intensity across Tanzania
articles.title_original: Genetic metrics decode Plasmodium falciparum diversity: complexity of infection, parasite connectivity, and transmission intensity in Mainland Tanzania's diverse regions.
articles.authors: Pereus D, Fola AA, Bakari C, Seth MD, DeFeo R, Lyimo BM, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Mbata D, Popkin-Hall ZR, Moshi R, Mbwambo RB, Mbwambo D, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Giesbrecht DJ, Kulohoma B, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Juma G, Mobegi VA, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: Cross-sectional genomic survey of 7,199 P. falciparum samples from 100 health facilities across 10 Tanzanian regions (Feb-July 2021), using molecular inversion probes targeting 1,832 SNPs. Mean complexity of infection (COI) was 1.5 countrywide with 59.6% monoclonal infections; COI significantly higher in high/moderate transmission strata (p<0.001). Parasite genetic differentiation very low (F_ST 0-0.006) with most pairwise IBD <0.1 and only 1.8% of pairs sharing ≥50% IBD. These metrics can integrate into surveillance systems to monitor transmission patterns.

articles.headline: Automated analyzer misclassifies Plasmodium vivax as artefactual cells
articles.title_original: Classification of Plasmodium vivax as artefactual cells by the CellaVision DM9600: a system limitation in a non-endemic area.
articles.authors: Chien LC, Wu FT, Hsu SF, Su YD, Lai HY, Hsu HC, Hsiao CT, Cheng MY, Hsueh PR
articles.details: Case report of 46-year-old returning from Indonesia with fever and thrombocytopenia. CellaVision DM9600 automated cellular morphology analyzer flagged artefactual cells without diagnosis; PCR and blood smear confirmed P. vivax. Patient treated with artemether-lumefantrine and primaquine with rapid recovery and no relapse. Highlights system limitation in non-endemic areas.

articles.headline: Evidence-based malaria risk maps support harmonized travel medicine advice
articles.title_original: [Travel and malaria prevention : what lies behind the maps].
articles.authors: Veit O, Eperon G, Staehelin C, Chappuis F
articles.details: Swiss travel medicine methodology developed with other European countries to classify malaria risk areas using current epidemiological data. Harmonized preventive measures presented in detailed maps available on www.healthytravel.ch. Maps support travel medicine experts and primary care physicians in providing consistent, evidence-based advice to travelers visiting endemic areas.

articles.headline: Sociodemographic factors predict P. vivax exposure in Myanmar migrant populations
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic and behavioral determinants of Plasmodium vivax-specific antibody responses among short-term Myanmar migrants in Thailand.
articles.authors: Sripoorote P, Inthitanon N, Wattanagoon Y, Lim PS, Cui L, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM, Nguitragool W, Chotirat S, Longley RJ, Aung PL
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey (March-April 2025) of 300 Myanmar migrants in Thailand; 294 PCR-negative participants analyzed for P. vivax IgG to eight antigens using Luminex assay. 35.4% showed predicted recent exposure. Higher exposure odds with age 30-39 (aOR=3.11), secondary education+ (aOR=3.28), Karen ethnicity (aOR=3.68); protective factors included seasonal work (aOR=0.28), temporary work (aOR=0.26), income >5,000 THB (aOR=0.17). One-third of PCR-negative migrants showed recent exposure, supporting sero-surveillance for border elimination strategies.

articles.headline: ITN use and residential status predict malaria and anemia in Ghanaian students
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic malaria parasite carriage and anaemia among senior high school students in Tempane District of the Upper East Region of Ghana: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Awindago JA, Captain-Esoah M, Motey GA, Angyiereyiri ED, Azameti MK, Adjei MR, Donkor MN, Frempong KK, Dassah SD
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 290 secondary students (ages 15-22) in Upper East Region, Ghana. Malaria parasite prevalence 23.1%; 49.0% anaemic. Day students had significantly higher malaria (59.2%) than boarding students (15.8%, p<0.001). Not using ITN predicted malaria (aOR=15.97, 95% CI 6.33–40.32, p<0.001); being a day student also predicted infection (aOR=5.65, 95% CI 2.58–12.36, p<0.001). Malaria-infected students had lower hemoglobin (11.50±1.43 vs 12.56±1.35 g/dL, p<0.001).

articles.headline: Schistosoma mansoni infection associated with gut inflammation despite malaria co-endemicity
articles.title_original: Schistosoma mansoni Infections Are Associated With Fecal Calprotectin Markers of Gut Inflammation After Accounting for HIV, Hepatitis B, and Malaria.
articles.authors: Wilburn L, Bui HL, Akampurira P, Ddamba R, Oguttu DW, Nabatte B, Kabatereine NB, Chami GF
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in Uganda (2024) of 640 participants examined for S. mansoni (Kato-Katz) and fecal calprotectin (fCal) by ELISA. S. mansoni prevalence 49.1%; 74.4% had detectable fCal, 22.3% had fCal ≥100 µg/g. S. mansoni infection intensity positively associated with fCal outcomes (detectable: OR 1.20, ≥100 µg/g: OR 1.11, >250 µg/g: OR 1.26). HIV associated with fCal ≥100 µg/g (OR 2.52); malaria was uninformative. Persistent gut inflammation characterizes S. mansoni in chronically treated populations.

articles.headline: Outcrossing provides fitness advantage over selfing in P. falciparum parasites
articles.title_original: Genetic crosses using fluorescently labelled malaria parasites indicate advantages of outcrossing in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Li X, Jutzeler K, Abatiyow B, Rezakhani N, Haile M, Leeb A, Patel H, Kappe S, Nosten F, Cheeseman IH, Ferdig M, Anderson TJC, Vaughan A, Kumar S
articles.details: Genetic cross study using fluorescently labeled P. falciparum clones (NF54-mCherry, NHP4026-GFP) in 435 mosquitoes, genotyping 8,540 oocysts at 7–14 days post-infection. Decreasing proportions of outcrossed oocysts and increasing selfing over time, consistent with faster maturation of outcrossed oocysts. Substantial fitness advantage of outcrossing, likely from removal of deleterious mutations. Faster outcrossed maturation may maximize recombination in transmitted populations.

articles.headline: CD4 T-cell responses largely similar between P. falciparum and vivax infections
articles.title_original: Comparison of CD4 T-Cell Response in Plasmodium falciparum and vivax Malaria.
articles.authors: Nalubega M, Soon MSF, Andrew D, Ortega-Pajares A, Canning J, Dooley N, Loughland JR, Engwerda C, Kenangalem E, Price RN, Minigo G, Anstey NM, Oyong DA, Boyle MJ
articles.details: Flow cytometry study of CD4 T cells in adults with P. falciparum or P. vivax malaria. Phenotype, activation, and proliferation of CD4 T cell subsets largely comparable between species. Within peripheral T follicular helper (pTfh) cell compartment, species-dependent activation detected with relatively increased pTfh1 cells in P. falciparum. P. falciparum showed increased IL-10 production, including in IL-21-producing CD4 T cells.

articles.headline: FcγRIIa, not FcγRIIIb, mediates antibody-dependent neutrophil phagocytosis
articles.title_original: Antibody-Dependent Neutrophil Phagocytosis of Plasmodium falciparum-Infected Erythrocytes Is Mediated by FcγRIIa.
articles.authors: Saeed M, Aitken EH, Wines BD, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: Ex vivo study of purified neutrophils treated with TNF and phosphatidylinositol phospholipase C to selectively deplete FcγRIIIb and increase FcγRIIa. After treatment, FcγRIII decreased 79% while FcγRIIa increased 82%. When FcγRIIIb-depleted, antibody-dependent neutrophil phagocytosis (ADNP) of opsonized infected erythrocytes increased significantly (585%±108% vs 100%, p=0.042). FcγRIIa blocking reduced ADNP >5-fold (~17%±1.5%, p<0.05). FcγRIIa is the main phagocytic receptor; FcγRIIIb acts as decoy.

articles.headline: Travel importation accounts for 14–30% of malaria cases in low-transmission Uganda
articles.title_original: Quantifying the Role of Importation on Sustained Malaria Transmission in a Low-to-Moderate Burden Region of Southwest Uganda.
articles.authors: Epstein A, Aramanzan O, Nabende I, Kayondo TM, Obbo M, Tumwesigye R, Wiringilimaana I, Mbabazi M, Kagurusi BA, Tukwasibwe S, Ssewanyana I, Routledge I, Briggs J, Wesolowski A, Greenhouse B, Dorsey G, Arinaitwe E, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort study in Southwest Uganda (14 months) of 1,918 individuals from 400 households monitored for P. falciparum infections. 283 infections recorded (244 symptomatic, 39 asymptomatic). Travel-infection associations positive in lower-transmission villages (OR=4.38, 95% CI 1.80–10.64) and during low-transmission periods; strongest for short-distance trips to higher-incidence areas. Population attributable fraction: travel accounted for 14% of malaria cases overall in low-transmission villages, rising to 30% during low-transmission seasons.

articles.headline: Dobinin K disrupts redox system of chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Antimalarial Effects of Dobinin K against Chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium falciparum Through Disruption of the Parasite's Redox System.
articles.authors: He LF, Sun H, Cang LQ, Wang JD, Xiao CJ, Jiang B, Shen L
articles.details: In vitro study of Dobinin K against chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum Dd2 strain. Compound showed concentration- and time-dependent antiplasmodial activity affecting all parasite developmental stages. Dobinin K disrupted parasite redox homeostasis by increasing oxidative products, elevating GSSG/GSH ratio, inhibiting antioxidant enzymes (PfGR, PfTrxR, PfSOD), disrupting mitochondrial membrane potential, and triggering apoptosis. Authors conclude redox-system targeting represents an effective strategy against chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum, distinct from artemisinin mechanism.

articles.headline: LAMP assay shows comparable sensitivity to PCR for xenomonitoring Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Assessment of automated loop-mediated isothermal amplification-(LAMP-)based xenomonitoring for Plasmodium spp. in Anopheles mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Eisenbarth A, Adegnika AA, Boussougou-Sambe ST, Glyschewski L, Jacobs T, Kann S, Krüger A, Hagen RM, Ramharter M, Mehmel E, Ndalembouly AGD, Frickmann H
articles.details: Comparative evaluation of automated LAMP versus real-time PCR for detecting Plasmodium parasites in Anopheles mosquitoes. In artificially exposed mosquitoes, LAMP detected P. berghei in 34.9% (15/43) versus PCR in 51.2% (22/43). In field-collected African mosquitoes, LAMP detected parasites in 7.0% (12/172 pools) versus PCR in 7.6% (13/172 pools); overall field infection rate 1.2% (20/1711) with P. falciparum, P. malariae, and P. ovale. LAMP showed lower sensitivity for non-human Plasmodium but comparable reliability for human malaria parasites and feasibility for tropical field conditions.

articles.headline: Correction to study on naturally acquired immunity and P. falciparum virulence genes
articles.title_original: Correction: Controlled human malaria infection with Plasmodium falciparum demonstrates impact of naturally acquired immunity on virulence gene expression.
articles.authors: Bachmann A, Bruske E, Krumkamp R, Turner L, Wichers JS, Petter M, Held J, Duffy MF, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Kremsner PG, Lell B, Lavstsen T, Frank M, Mordmüller B, Tannich E
articles.details: Correction notice for a controlled human malaria infection study examining the impact of naturally acquired immunity on P. falciparum virulence gene expression. Original article DOI: 10.1371/journal.ppat.1007906.

articles.headline: Wastewater surveillance feasible for malaria detection in endemic countries
articles.title_original: Modeling and Laboratory Experiments to Assess Detection of Plasmodium falciparum in Wastewater: Insights for Wastewater-Based Surveillance of Malaria.
articles.authors: Chen W, Ochomo E, Muok E, Bibby K
articles.details: Mathematical modeling and laboratory study of P. falciparum detection in wastewater for surveillance applications. Modeling predicted wastewater-based surveillance would be challenging in nonendemic countries (requiring >1000-fold case increase for 50% detection at PLOD 3.0 log10 GC/L), but approximately 84% of endemic African countries could achieve this detection threshold. Laboratory experiments demonstrated first-order wastewater decay (0.035-0.11 h-1), >70% DNA loss from freeze-thawing, and HA filtration recovery <2%. Authors conclude dual modeling-experimental approach guides future malaria wastewater-based epidemiology applications.

articles.headline: Marker-free CRISPR/Cas9 enables iterative P. falciparum genetic modification
articles.title_original: A heterologous marker-free selection approach for CRISPR/Cas9-based gene editing in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Carrington E, Ballmer D, Niederwieser I, Thommen BT, Brancucci NMB, Voss TS
articles.details: Development of CRISPR/Cas9pyrR strategy for marker-free genetic engineering in P. falciparum, using pyrimethamine resistance mutations in pfdhfr-ts as selection mechanism. Generated transgenic lines with GFP-tagged nuclear proteins: PfGEX1 (localizes to nuclear envelope specifically in gametocytes) and PfNUP116 (localizes to perinuclear region in early gametocytes, undetectable in asexual parasites). Demonstrated sequential genetic engineering, additionally tagging PfAP2-G and PfNUP313. Authors conclude CRISPR/Cas9pyrR provides versatile method enhancing the genetic toolkit for malaria research.

articles.headline: Iron-fortified micronutrient powder reduces malaria-induced anemia in children
articles.title_original: Effect of Iron-Containing Micronutrient Powders on Growth and Malaria-Induced Anaemia Among Preschool Children in Ghana: A Cluster-Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial.
articles.authors: Tchum EKY, Koto JE, Kanyoke F, Opoku O, Ussher F, Dassah SD, Amoani B, Tchum SK, Nyarko E
articles.details: Cluster-randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 1,806 Ghanaian children aged 6-35 months. Children received daily micronutrient powder containing 12.5 mg elemental iron or placebo for 5 months. Iron-containing MNP did not improve height-for-age, weight-for-age, or weight-for-height z-scores (all p>0.4). However, intervention reduced risk of malaria-induced anemia by 5.0 percentage points (95% CI 0.4%-9.6%, p=0.032). Authors conclude iron-containing MNP is safe in malaria-endemic settings and reduces malaria-induced anemia, though integrated strategies needed for addressing growth faltering.

articles.headline: Novel P. falciparum invasion inhibitors identified; MMV006931 targets PfNCR1
articles.title_original: Unpacking boxes: identification of novel inhibitors of malaria parasite invasion.
articles.authors: Barnes CB, Schneider MP, Ventura OD, Razook Z, McCann K, Boulet C, Literski AH, Barry AE, Sleebs BE, Crabb BS, Gilson PR, Bullen HE
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Screening of 400 compounds from Medicines for Malaria Venture libraries identified 20 invasion inhibitors and 2 partial egress inhibitors. Six compounds showed invasion inhibition as primary mechanism with similar growth and invasion IC50 values. Two compounds (MMV006931 and MMV024850) directly blocked merozoite invasion. Whole-genome sequencing of MMV006931-resistant parasites identified mutations in P. falciparum sterol exporter PfNCR1. Mechanistic studies confirmed MMV006931 sensitizes parasites to sterol-dependent detergent lysis, establishing PfNCR1 as target and representing a novel antimalarial mechanism distinct from existing drugs.

articles.headline: Recent malaria strongly associated with anemia in HIV+ children on ART
articles.title_original: Anemia in HIV-Infected Children on ART in Uganda: High Risk Associations with Malaria, Opportunistic Infections, and Poor Adherence.
articles.authors: Anyelwisye CL, Ogwal JK, Kadogosa SE, Timuzigu R, Anyetaba C, Wagubi R, Olong C, John EA, Muwanguzi E, Okongo B
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 293 HIV-positive children on antiretroviral therapy at two clinics in southwestern Uganda (July-September 2025). Anemia prevalence was 6.14% (95% CI 3.4%-8.9%), with normocytic normochromic anemia predominant (72.2%). Independent risk factors in multivariate analysis: recent malaria episode (adjusted OR 9.61, 95% CI 3.26-28.34), opportunistic infection (adjusted OR 6.78, 95% CI 1.28-35.83), and poor ART adherence (adjusted OR 14.75, 95% CI 1.31-165.65). Authors conclude anemia remains significant despite lower prevalence than global averages, with critical need for integrated malaria control, opportunistic infection management, and adherence support.

articles.headline: Humidity is strongest climate driver of malaria in Rohingya refugee camps
articles.title_original: Seasonal Climate Drivers and Immunohematological Profiles of Malaria in Rohingya Refugee Camps, Bangladesh: A 4-Year Repeated Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Islam A, Akhi MM, Hossain A, Palit PK, Rahman N, Baidya S, Prome TM, Rahman M, Sultana A, Chowdhury MMH, Alam MS
articles.details: 4-year repeated cross-sectional study (2021-2024) in a Rohingya refugee camp in Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh with 582 participants (486 camp residents, 96 nonendemic controls). Among 345 confirmed malaria cases, peak transmission occurred during monsoon season (June-September), with P. falciparum showing earlier and sharper seasonal peaks than P. vivax. Relative humidity demonstrated strongest correlation with incidence (r=0.724-0.77), followed by rainfall and temperature. Infected individuals exhibited anemia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated ESR, more pronounced in P. falciparum infections. P. falciparum-positive individuals had significantly higher anti-Plasmodium antibody titers.

articles.headline: Spatial malaria clusters in pregnancy linked to antenatal care and climate in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Mapping inequity: spatial clustering of malaria in pregnancy in the Amhara region, Northwest Ethiopia (2018-2024).
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Asemahagn MA, Alemu K, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: Retrospective spatial analysis of routine malaria surveillance data (2018-2024) covering 5.8 million pregnant women in Ethiopia's Amhara region. Total of 53,478 confirmed malaria cases with annual incidence of 9.2 per 1,000 populations. Seven significant high-risk spatial clusters identified. Spatial regression showed malaria incidence negatively associated with ANC4 utilization (IRR 0.92, 95% CI 0.91-0.94), soil moisture (IRR 0.85, 95% CI 0.80-0.90), API (IRR 0.98, 95% CI 0.97-0.99), and temperature (IRR 0.65, 95% CI 0.58-0.73), but positively with ANC1 utilization (IRR 1.07, 95% CI 1.06-1.08). Authors conclude marked spatial heterogeneity driven by environmental conditions and antenatal care service disparities.

articles.headline: 10.4% of febrile children have malaria-bacteremia coinfection; seizures increase odds
articles.title_original: Malaria and bacteremia in febrile children presenting at a teaching hospital in Kumasi, Ghana.
articles.authors: Gyasi WA, Boampong K, Sylverken J, Badu K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 270 febrile children at a teaching hospital in Ghana (April 2022-July 2023). Malaria prevalence was 33% and bacteremia 31%, with 10.4% (95% CI ±3.6%) coinfection. Seizures were independently associated with increased odds of malaria-bacterial coinfection (adjusted OR 3.57, 95% CI 1.28-9.95, p=0.015). Staphylococcus aureus was most common gram-positive isolate (43.8%), Pseudomonas aeruginosa most common gram-negative (6.3%). Multi-drug resistant bacteria identified in 34.5% of bacterial isolates. Authors highlight need for effective antibiotic stewardship and resistance monitoring programs.

articles.headline: RTS,S and R21 vaccines provide moderate protection; monoclonal antibodies emerging
articles.title_original: Malaria Prevention: Progress to Date.
articles.authors: Grobusch MP, Schnyder JL, Schlagenhauf P, de Jong HK, Hanscheid T
articles.details: Review of malaria prevention strategies across endemic and nonendemic regions. Chemoprophylaxis in endemic areas involves intermittent preventive treatment and mass drug administration rather than daily prophylaxis. WHO-endorsed RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix) and R21/Matrix-M vaccines, rolled out in highly endemic sub-Saharan African countries, provide moderate overall protection in young children but significantly reduce severe disease and mortality. Monoclonal antibodies against malaria are in development as preventive treatment for immunocompromised or unvaccinated individuals and for disrupting seasonal transmission. Authors note dynamic evolution of malaria preventive tools with numerous developments on the horizon.

articles.headline: Trimeric NF-Y complex required for sporozoite release from oocysts
articles.title_original: Sporozoite egress from Plasmodium oocysts requires a trimeric NF-Y-like complex.
articles.authors: Preira CMF, Deligianni E, Andreadaki M, Tzagkaraki S, Kokkas E, Kołakowska A, Gessmann R, Bertaso C, Masiero S, Russo R, Nardini M, Siden-Kiamos I, Gourlay LJ, Currà C
articles.details: Plasmodium sporozoites escape from oocysts on mosquito midguts through a capsule rupture mechanism. This study identifies ORP3 as a third subunit of the Oocyst Rupture Protein complex, which forms a trimeric complex analogous to the NF-Y transcription factor. ORP3 deletion mutants failed oocyst excystation. The conserved NF-YA helix A1 domain was essential for trimerization and oocyst opening, confirmed by yeast-3-hybrid assays. Results suggest Plasmodium has re-purposed the NF-Y complex fold for sporozoite egress.

articles.headline: Fc region and epitope valency minimally required for CSP antibody protection
articles.title_original: Relevance of the antibody Fc fragment and epitope valency in protection against malaria sporozoites.
articles.authors: Flores-Garcia Y, Aguirre-Botero MC, Tripathi P, Locke E, Park M, Mathis-Torres S, Salgado-Jimenez B, Herrera-Arevalo S, Gladston A, Asady B, Coppens I, Amino R, Zavala F
articles.details: Antibody-mediated protection against Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites targeting circumsporozoite protein (CSP) was tested using Fab fragments and Fc-mutated antibodies (LALA, KA mutations). Fab fragments and Fc-mutated antibodies showed efficacy comparable to intact antibodies, indicating Fc functions are not essential for sporozoite neutralization. Binding to a single (NPNA)3 epitope strongly reduced liver burden; two separate epitopes achieved sterile protection, suggesting multiple antibodies and homotypic interactions are not required.

articles.headline: Anopheles maculatus emerges as malaria vector in Northeast India
articles.title_original: Anopheles maculatus s.l as potential malaria vectors with opportunistic feeding in Meghalaya, Northeast India.
articles.authors: Sihag AK, Rajkonwar J, Kharbisnop B, Sangma DM, Budnah C, Acharya P, Chellappan S, Hazarika G, Talukder P, Pebam R, Baruah K, Bhattacharyya DR, Hajong SR, Kaur H, Narain K, Bhowmick IP
articles.details: Molecular survey in Meghalaya (2019–2023) identified natural Plasmodium falciparum infections in An. maculatus sensu stricto and An. pseudowillmori. Among 215 specimens, 177 (82.32%) were An. maculatus s.s., with 49 (27.68%) infected; 33 (15.34%) were An. pseudowillmori, with 16 (48.48%) infected. Blood meal analysis showed opportunistic feeding: 15% on humans, 16% on cattle, 17% on both. FST analysis revealed significant genetic differentiation between populations. Results indicate An. maculatus members are emerging vectors with flexible feeding behavior.

articles.headline: Nanoparticle CIS43 junctional vaccine comparable to R21 at dose reduction
articles.title_original: Protective immunity against malaria by a nanoparticle CIS43-based junctional vaccine alone or in combination with R21.
articles.authors: Tripathi P, Koo JH, Chen X, Silva Pereira LD, Dillon M, Zhang B, Lofgren M, Nguyen KT, Teng IT, Bonilla B, Kerscher S, Kong WP, Henry AR, Stephens T, Tsybovsky Y, Weldon SR, Douek DC, Pierson TC, Batista FD, Idris AH, Seder RA, Kwong PD, Zhou T
articles.details: A self-assembling nanoparticle vaccine displaying tandem repeats of the PfCSP junctional epitope (CIS43-based) was compared to the WHO-recommended R21 vaccine. At 1 μg dose, the junctional vaccine and R21 showed comparable reduction in liver burden. At 0.25 μg, the junctional vaccine showed modest reduction versus R21. Combining junctional and R21 vaccines did not yield substantial improvement. The junctional vaccine elicited antibodies against both junctional and major repeat regions.

articles.headline: Chikungunya-malaria co-infection low but present in central Nigeria
articles.title_original: Seroprevalence of chikungunya virus infection and co-occurrence with malaria among patients with febrile illness in Oguma, the middle belt region of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Daudu MJ, Omatola CA
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 200 febrile patients in Oguma, Bassa LGA. Anti-chikungunya virus antibodies detected in 9 (4.50%) patients; IgM 4 (2.0%), IgG 5 (2.5%). Active chikungunya infection co-occurred with malaria in 3 (1.5%) patients; 1 (1.0%) had viral mono-infection. Malaria alone was present in 60 (30%) patients. Active chikungunya infection was observed only in males [4 (4.3%)], with rash and back pain frequently co-occurring (p<0.05).

articles.headline: Mass drug administration strategy with vaccines and digital monitoring for elimination
articles.title_original: Application Strategy and Research Progress of Large-Scale Population Drug Intervention in Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Cao Z, Gu Y, Li G, Deng C
articles.details: Review of mass drug administration (MDA) for malaria control, with estimated 282 million malaria cases globally in 2024. MDA plays an important role but faces limitations: long-term drug resistance risks, insufficient sustainability, and high regional adaptation failure rates. Authors propose an 'MDA+' strategy integrating vaccines, digital monitoring, and cross-border cooperation to optimize resource allocation and address multiple Plasmodium species including P. knowlesi.

articles.headline: DP+SP chemoprevention reduces birthweight; protective placental malaria effect partially offsets
articles.title_original: Effect mechanisms of different malaria chemoprevention regimens in pregnancy on infant growth outcomes: causal mediation analysis of a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Nguyen AT, Nankabirwa JI, Kakuru A, Roh ME, Aguti M, Adrama H, Kizza J, Olwoch P, Kamya MR, Dorsey G, Jagannathan P, Benjamin-Chung J
articles.details: Randomized trial (NCT04336189) comparing intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp): monthly DP, SP, or DP+SP in 761 infants followed post-birth. Compared to SP, DP+SP decreased mean weight-for-length z-score (WLZ) by 0.18 (95% CI -0.03, 0.39) at 1–3 months and 0.28 (95% CI 0.07, 0.49) at 4–6 months. DP+SP reduced active placental malaria (protective effect +0.06, 95% CI 0.02, 0.10 on WLZ), partially offsetting negative effects. Low birthweight had persistent mediating effect on linear growth.

articles.headline: PBO-LLINs show reduced mortality in resistant vector populations
articles.title_original: Reduced Efficacy of Piperonyl Butoxide Based Long-Lasting Insecticide-Treated Nets Against Malaria Vectors in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Kahindi S, Abel L, Kirwa E, Amunga M, Kimachas E, Omondi E, Cherono M, Mangeni J, Obala A, Markwalter C, O'Meara WP
articles.details: WHO cone bioassay tested 2,020 female Anopheles (1,480 wild-collected, 540 Kilifi-susceptible strain) against new and used piperonyl butoxide (PBO) long-lasting insecticide-treated nets (LLINs) in Bungoma County, Kenya (rolled out mid-2021). Mortality after new PBO-LLINs: 68% wild-collected, 34% after 24 months use. Anopheles arabiensis was primary survivor species. Permethrin susceptibility assay confirmed very low mortality with or without PBO, attributed to net aging and high local vector resistance.

articles.headline: L9LS monoclonal antibody achieves 43% protective efficacy in young children
articles.title_original: Safety and efficacy of the monoclonal antibody L9LS for malaria prevention in children exposed to perennial malaria transmission in Kenya: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 2 trial.
articles.authors: Steinhardt LC, Kwambai TK, Oneko M, Ouma E, Njoroge R, Callier V, Hu Z, Gutman JR, Yego R, Otieno K, Onoka K, Otieno L, Oduol K, Serebryannyy L, Lin BC, Adams W, Hickman S, Preston AC, Carlton K, Holdsworth M, Xiao Y, O Ter Kuile F, Odongo W, Murphy SC, Tran TM, Kariuki S, Crompton PD, Seder RA
articles.details: Phase 2 randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT05400655) testing L9LS monoclonal antibody in Siaya County, Kenya. In part 2, 324 children aged 5–59 months received two-dose L9LS (10–20 mg/kg, baseline and month 6), one-dose L9LS, or placebo. Over 12 months, 70 (66%) of 106 two-dose L9LS children had ≥1 P. falciparum infection versus 91 (83%) of 110 placebo (protective efficacy 42.7%, 95% CI 22.5–57.7; p=0.0003). No serious treatment-related adverse events occurred. Higher doses may be needed for greater efficacy in intense perennial transmission.

articles.headline: Antibody-based malaria prevention in intense perennial transmission
articles.title_original: Antibody-based malaria prevention in an intense perennial transmission setting.
articles.authors: Engwerda CR, Haque A
articles.details: Title only provided; no abstract data available for detailed summary.

articles.headline: RTS,S/AS01 vaccine safety monitoring methods compared
articles.title_original: Assessing safety in the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine implementation programme: an overview and comparison of methods.
articles.authors: Schellenberg D, Hamel M, Njuguna P, Furrer E, Praet N, Balakrishnan MR, Jain S, Maiga D, Schuerman L, Whitney CG
articles.details: Overview of safety monitoring approaches in the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine implementation programme. Methods included sentinel hospital and community-based mortality surveillance from phase 3 trial, strengthened pharmacovigilance for rare unanticipated signals, and cohort event monitoring for conditions of special interest. Summary describes strengths and weaknesses of complementary safety monitoring approaches.

articles.headline: Malaria research must balance immediate impact with next-generation preparedness
articles.title_original: Prioritising in a crisis: what next for malaria research?
articles.authors: Chestnutt EG, Afolabi MO, D'Alessandro U, Delves M, Gosling R, Jagoe G, Lasry E, Maiteki C, Moon RW, Oriero E, Pitt C, Tadesse F, Tibenderana JK, Rao VB
articles.details: Perspective article on malaria research priorities amid stalling control gains, funding shortfall, and rising parasite/vector resistance. Proposes dual mandate: protecting lives now while preparing for emerging biological and systemic threats. Three priorities identified: pragmatic, equity-focused implementation research for existing interventions; sustained innovation investment in chemoprevention, vaccines, monoclonal antibodies, vector control, and AI-enabled surveillance; and research on sustainable financing and data-informed decision-making.

articles.headline: Low vaccine awareness despite high caregiver acceptance in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Awareness, acceptability and determinants of the new malaria vaccine among caregivers of under-five children in Asaba, Nigeria: a multi-centre cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Ilikannu CO, Uwadia OM, Ilikannu SO, Agenu P, Mbachu CNP, Okoye NP, Uwaezuoke AC, Arinze B, Akinkunmi I, Jideije JA, Onwuasoeze C, Dan-Nwankwo C, Aladegbehingbe OE, Igboejesi PC, Onomuighokpo HO, Chukwu N, Igwe E
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 358 caregivers of under-five children in Asaba, Nigeria (January–June 2025) assessed awareness and acceptability of the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine. Only 36.3% were aware of the vaccine despite 85.8% willingness to vaccinate; main barriers included fear of adverse reactions (45.1%), lack of interest (19.6%), and spousal disagreement (13.7%). Female caregivers and civil servants were more accepting. The authors conclude that targeted awareness campaigns addressing safety concerns and spousal engagement are needed to improve vaccine uptake.

articles.headline: Global malaria experts discuss control strategies and regional challenges
articles.title_original: Unite against Malaria: challenges and achievements in malaria control around the globe.
articles.details: A World Malaria Day 2026 perspective featuring three regional experts—Dionicia Gamboa (Peru; molecular surveillance and vector genomics), Fitsum Girma Tadesse (Ethiopia and Horn of Africa; molecular epidemiology), and Leanne Robinson (Asia-Pacific/Papua New Guinea; epidemiology and implementation research)—sharing experiences in malaria control. The commentary contextualizes progress toward the WHO's Global Technical Strategy 2016–2030 goal to reduce malaria incidence and mortality by 90% between 2015 and 2030.

articles.headline: Multiple genetic pathways beyond Kelch13 drive artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Insights into the factors contributing to artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Sharma P, Verma K, Tyagi S, Singh K, Bharti PK
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: A narrative review examining molecular mechanisms of artemisinin resistance (ARTR) in Plasmodium falciparum. While Kelch13 mutations in the propeller domain are established resistance markers, additional genetic determinants also contribute, including reduced haemoglobin endocytosis, increased phosphatidylinositol-3-kinase levels, activation of the unfolded protein response, and polymorphisms in various other genes. Understanding these multiple pathways is essential for developing effective ARTR prevention strategies.

articles.headline: Structural biology accelerates malaria vaccine design via cryo-EM
articles.title_original: Atomic clarity: how structural biology is shaping blood-stage malaria vaccines.
articles.authors: Nema S, Rathore S, Banerjee M, Zhou ZH, Mohmmed A, Malhotra P
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: A review of how structural biology, particularly cryogenic electron microscopy (cryoEM), is advancing malaria vaccine development by revealing atomic-level details of the multiprotein surface complexes parasites use for host cell invasion during the blood stage. Recent advances in artificial intelligence and deep learning have improved cryoEM data analysis and structural modeling. Integration of structural, computational, and genomic approaches is accelerating progress toward broadly protective blood-stage malaria vaccines.

articles.headline: PBO nets outperform standard LLINs in adaptive vector control trial
articles.title_original: Adaptive intervention strategies for malaria vector control in western Kenya: a cluster, sequential, multiple-assignment, randomised, open-label trial.
articles.authors: Zhou G, Wang X, Lee MC, Machani MG, Kidwell KM, Atieli HE, Kazura JW, Githure JI, Zhong D, Yan G
articles.details: A two-stage cluster randomized SMART trial in Kisumu County, Kenya (March 2021–August 2024) with 47,614 participants across 84 clusters evaluated adaptive malaria vector control strategies. In stage 1, piperonyl butoxide-treated LLINs significantly reduced clinical malaria incidence versus standard pyrethroid LLINs at 1–6 months (aIRR 0.75, 95% CI 0.69–0.81; p<0.0001) and 7–12 months (aIRR 0.87, 95% CI 0.80–0.95; p=0.0012). In stage 2 non-responders, enhanced IRS outperformed biannual IRS (aIRR 0.67, p<0.0001). All adaptive strategies significantly reduced incidence compared to standard LLINs (all p<0.01).

articles.headline: Tinospora crispa synergizes with artesunate in rodent malaria model
