articles.details: Retrospective descriptive study of 1,262 pregnant women attending antenatal care in Amboasary-Sud, southern Madagascar in 2021, assessing intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP) coverage and malaria prevalence. Although 64.9% received ≥1 IPTp-SP dose, only 49.9% completed the recommended ≥3 doses. Malaria rapid diagnostic test positivity was 21.1% among symptomatic women tested (61/289), with 4.8% overall prevalence of confirmed symptomatic malaria (61/1262). Maternal and fetal complications occurred only in women without IPTp-SP (7 maternal, 3 fetal cases). Proximity to health facility (<5 km, aOR=1.54, 95% CI 1.14–2.08) and ≥4 ANC visits (aOR=2.38, 95% CI 1.76–3.21) independently associated with adequate IPTp-SP uptake.

articles.headline: Anopheles funestus shows seasonal habitat preferences; dry-season targeting more efficient
articles.title_original: Seasonal variation in aquatic habitat availability and use by the malaria vector, Anopheles funestus.
articles.authors: Kahamba NF, Kifungo K, Jumanne M, Trujillano F, Finda MF, Msugupakulya B, Baldini F, Nelli L, Okumu FO, Ferguson HM
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of aquatic habitats in 5 villages in south-eastern Tanzania during dry season (September–November 2021) and rainy season (February–May 2022) identified 2,824 habitats, of which 27% were colonized by Anopheles funestus larvae. Larval presence and abundance were significantly influenced by habitat type, season, and physicochemical factors (water depth, vegetation, algae, clarity, source). An. funestus was commonly found in river streams, ground pools, and ditches year-round but expanded habitat use during wet season to include spring-fed wells, rice fields, and dug pits. Findings suggest targeting persistent habitats during the dry season offers a more efficient window for larval source management.

articles.headline: Cabamiquine efficacy varies by Plasmodium species; P. malariae shows reduced susceptibility
articles.title_original: Antimalarial cabamiquine inhibits blood-stage of different field isolates of Plasmodium species.
articles.authors: Dembele L, Aniweh Y, Soulama A, Sogore F, Diakité O, Diallo F, Dao F, Amenga-Etego LN, Demarta-Gatsi C, Spangenberg T, Djimde AA
articles.details: Ex-vivo drug susceptibility study in Ghana and Mali comparing cabamiquine and dihydroartemisinin (DHA) efficacy against P. falciparum, P. ovale, and P. malariae field isolates. P. ovale showed decreased DHA susceptibility (median IC50=3.962 nM) compared to P. falciparum (p=0.0371) but similar cabamiquine susceptibility (median IC50=0.9441 nM). P. malariae isolates were less susceptible to cabamiquine (median IC50=3.915 nM, p=0.001) compared to P. falciparum but showed no difference in DHA susceptibility. Cabamiquine-resistant parasites showed limited cross-resistance with amodiaquine, lumefantrine, chloroquine, primaquine, and other drugs tested.

articles.headline: Malaria incidence and risk factors with hematologic and immunologic assessment
articles.title_original: Incidence and risk factors of malaria with hematological and immunological evaluation and integrable analysis of a fractional mathematical model.
articles.authors: Murad MA, Murad SA, Abdeljawad T, Alqudah MA
articles.details: Study assessing incidence and risk factors of malaria with hematological and immunological evaluation and mathematical modeling analysis.

articles.headline: Sex distorter male drive could suppress Anopheles gambiae populations
articles.title_original: Sex distorter male drive for resistance-resilient population control of the human malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Grilli S, Vertsimakha O, Marston L, Gonzalez IA, Burt A, Crisanti A, Bernardini F
articles.details: Development of Sex Distorter Male Drive (SDMD) technology in Anopheles gambiae using characterized germline-specific promoters (spo11 and vasa1). SDMD generates highly male-biased progeny and spreads through super-Mendelian inheritance, providing a simplified genetic construct design compared to previous approaches. This method aims to improve genetic stability and resilience against target-site resistance, a major challenge for genetic control strategies of malaria vectors.

articles.headline: Severe anemia and bleeding predict referral in severe malaria cases
articles.title_original: "Referral patterns and associated factors among adult patients admitted with Severe Malaria at Shedeho Meket Primary Hospital, Northeast Ethiopia".
articles.authors: Addis GT, Addis MT, Mihret Y, Addis ET, Tadesse TY
articles.details: Institution-based retrospective cross-sectional study of 165 adult patients admitted with severe malaria at Shedeho Meket Primary Hospital, Ethiopia, April 2023–April 2025. Of these, 94 (57%) were male with mean age 34.4 years; 151 (91.5%) recovered with no deaths reported; 23 (13.9%) were referred to other facilities. Hyperparasitemia (AOR=2.083, 95% CI 0.019–7.803), severe malarial anemia (AOR=3.052, 95% CI 2.066–13.232), and significant bleeding (AOR=8.012, 95% CI 2.066–21.232) were significantly associated with referral decisions, highlighting the importance of early detection of severe anemia and hemorrhage.

articles.headline: Editorial on malaria protective immunity from natural infection and vaccination
articles.title_original: Editorial: Dissecting malaria protective immunity: acquired by natural infection and/or vaccination.
articles.authors: Kanoi BN, Nguitragool W, Palacpac NMQ, Tsuboi T
articles.details: An editorial discussing malaria protective immunity and its acquisition through natural infection and/or vaccination.

articles.headline: Spleen retains Plasmodium sporozoites for hours after inoculation
articles.title_original: Retention and survival of Plasmodium sporozoites in the spleen.
articles.authors: Celli S, Thiberge JM, Formaglio P, Martins RM, Snounou G, Amino R
articles.details: Intravenously inoculated P. yoelii and P. berghei sporozoites were retained in the mouse spleen, with live GFP-expressing parasites visible in splenic parenchyma for at least 3 hours post-inoculation. Findings suggest the spleen plays a role beyond simply trapping and destroying sporozoites en route to the liver.

articles.headline: Malaria-induced lung injury via dendritic cell autophagy dysfunction
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection induces lung injury by modulating type 2 conventional dendritic cells autophagy via the STAT3-IRF4 signaling.
articles.authors: Hong C, Deng G, Jiang Z, Lu Y, Wu Q, Huang C, Tang J, Zhou H, Liu Q, Luo X, Zhao Y, Qi Y, Li Q, Ma X, Yang Q
articles.details: In P. yoelii-infected mice, abnormal accumulation and reduced autophagy of lung type 2 conventional dendritic cells (cDC2) drives Th1 differentiation and lung injury via the TLR3/7-STAT3-IRF4 signaling pathway. JAK inhibitor JSI-124 suppressing STAT3 phosphorylation nearly abolished the effect; IRF4 knockout in cDC2 restored autophagy and reduced lung inflammation.

articles.headline: Malaria parasites drive cytotoxic Vδ2+ T cell expansion in lymphoid tissue
articles.title_original: Cytotoxic Vδ2+ T cell subsets expand in response to malaria in human tonsil and spleen organoids.
articles.authors: Press KD, Bach F, Sola E, Camanag K, Dooley NL, Ivanawati A, Oyong D, Nalubega M, Kakuru A, Matsiko S, Nankya F, Musinguzi K, Nalwoga A, Nansubuga E, Ategeka J, Ebusu C, Odongo B, Kashiwagi C, Ji X, Miranda M, Tachiwa-Appiah J, Sandhu K, Kamalyan L, van der Ploeg K, Boyle M, Wagar LE, Davis MM, Jagannathan P
articles.details: Human tonsil and spleen organoids exposed to P. falciparum-infected RBCs showed significantly increased Vδ2+ γδ T cell frequencies. Single-cell RNA/TCR sequencing revealed clonal expansion with activated, cytotoxic phenotypes expressing granzymes and interferon-stimulated genes. Analysis of Ugandan children with asymptomatic parasitemia confirmed expanded Vδ2+ T cells with enhanced cytotoxic potential.

articles.headline: PRMT inhibitors show activity against P. falciparum in vitro
articles.title_original: Protein Arginine Methyltransferase Inhibitors Target Multiple Stages of Plasmodium falciparum Parasites In Vitro.
articles.authors: Opperman D, Rabie T, Maré M, Naude M, van der Watt M, Thibaud JL, Shannon M, Sanders N, Bolscher JM, van der Laak R, Willemsen R, Bronkhorst A, Boonyalai N, Lee MCS, Birkholtz LM
articles.details: Chemical repositioning identified protein arginine methyltransferase (PRMT) inhibitor TC-E 5003 as active against multiple developmental stages of P. falciparum parasites. PfPRMT1 identified as the primary target through structure-activity relationship studies. The compound's novel mechanism targeting P. falciparum PRMTs opens a new pathway for multistage antiplasmodial drug development.

articles.headline: P. malariae and P. ovale frequently misidentified as P. vivax in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: The hidden malaria: Misidentification of Plasmodium malariae and Plasmodium ovale in low-transmission setting targeted for elimination in Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Getachew H, Wang C, Habtamu K, Demissew A, Degefa T, Birhanie SK, Kazura JW, King CL, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in southwestern Ethiopia (2019–2022) re-evaluated 245 P. vivax-positive samples by qPCR. Of these, 87.7% (n=215) were confirmed P. vivax, but 3.7% (n=9) were P. falciparum, 3.3% (n=8) P. malariae, 2.4% (n=6) P. ovale, and 2.8% (n=7) mixed infections. Neither RDTs nor microscopy detected P. malariae or P. ovale in any samples.

articles.headline: AL and ASAQ remain efficacious for P. falciparum in Sierra Leone children
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria among children 6-59 months in three sentinel sites of Sierra Leone, 2021-2022.
articles.authors: Cavros I, Abanyie F, Kamara ARY, Chalobah F, Fofana N, Ahmed J, Larbi K, Kennedy C, Horton B, Bajic M, Richardson M, Matlock B, McCaffery J, Kanu S, Schnabel D, Labor TSA, Cash S, Plucinski M, Komeh JP, Sillah-Kanu M
articles.details: In vivo study of 496 children (6–59 months) in Sierra Leone evaluated artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) efficacy. AL uncorrected Kaplan-Meier efficacy was 89.5% (95% CI 84–95%) in Bo, 96.1% (92–100%) in Bombali, and 93.2% (89–98%) in Kenema; ASAQ was 92.0% (86–98%) in Bo and 94.5% (90–99%) in Bombali. PCR-corrected efficacy reached 99.1–100% at all sites. No validated Pfk13 mutations detected.

articles.headline: Malaria microscopy quality varies by facility; low parasite densities missed
articles.title_original: Malaria microscopy evaluation and quality assurance in rural clinics of Rarieda and Alego Usonga sub-counties of Siaya County, western Kenya.
articles.authors: Schultz JS, Towett O, Obiet K, Seda B, Otieno K, Omondi D, Odongo W, Seffren V, Wafula P, Oreri E, McDermott DP, Staedke SG, Kwambai TK, Kariuki S, Gutman JR
articles.details: Quality assurance evaluation of microscopy in 29 Kenyan health facilities examined 1,494 blood smears (501 positive). Concordance between routine and expert re-reading was 91%; agreement between facility and reference slides was 86%. Facility sensitivity was 76%, specificity 92%. Parasite density strongly predicted diagnostic accuracy: 1–100 p/μL had lower accuracy (OR 0.12; 95% CI 0.05–0.29), while >10,000 p/μL had higher accuracy (OR 6.08; 95% CI 2.22–25.1).

articles.headline: Socioeconomic factors determine malaria treatment delays in West African children
articles.title_original: Determinants of late malaria treatment among under-five children in West Africa.
articles.authors: Shittu SB, Shittu RO, Adedini SA
articles.details: Secondary analysis of Malaria Indicator Survey data from Mali, Niger, Nigeria, and Senegal examined treatment delay determinants in under-five children. Key factors by country: Senegal—child sex (AOR 0.41; 95% CI 0.25–0.66), maternal wealth (AOR 0.037; 0.03–0.96), residence (AOR 1.82; 1.04–3.16); Nigeria—maternal wealth (AOR 0.54; 0.30–0.96) and education (AOR 0.49; 0.29–0.83); Niger—residence; Mali—maternal age (AOR 0.60; 0.38–0.95).

articles.headline: PfVPS4 knockdown reduces asexual replication and gametocyte conversion
articles.title_original: PfVPS4, an ESCRT AAA-ATPase, is essential for asexual proliferation and gametocyte sexual conversion in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Yang W, Wu J, Zhou Y, Yu Z, Shang X, Huang J
articles.details: CRISPR-Cas9 conditional knockdown of PfVPS4 (ESCRT AAA-ATPase) in P. falciparum reduced asexual progeny by 84%, decreased merozoite numbers, and reduced gametocyte conversion rate by 46%. Biochemical analysis showed PfVPS4 ATPase activity optimal at pH 7.5 and 37°C with Vmax 2.23 ± 0.053 U/mg and Km 0.086 mM. Essential catalytic residues (D213, E214) and species-specific residues (T161, I288) were confirmed.

articles.headline: WWII-era visual media campaigns promoted malaria control compliance among soldiers
articles.title_original: Attention for Compliance: Visual Media in Second World War Anti-Malaria Efforts.
articles.authors: Marble WS, Shanks GD
articles.details: Historical account of public health media campaigns during World War II to increase soldier compliance with malaria control measures and chemoprophylaxis. Multiple approaches included humor, command authority, linking prevention to war effort, and incentivizing early return home. Media artists, including Dr. Seuss, contributed mosquito cartoons. Collectively, medicines, engineering, and media contributed to malaria control in the US military.

articles.headline: Border malaria elimination hindered by operational constraints, not awareness
articles.title_original: Challenges and responses of malaria elimination in a high-endemic area along the Thai-Myanmar border: A health systems perspective.
articles.authors: Saita S, Parker DM, Suk-Uam K, Phuanukoonnon S, Pooseesod K
articles.details: Mixed-methods study of malaria elimination response in Tha Song Yang District, Thailand examined 24 key informant interviews and 388 household surveys. Day 1 and Day 3 case investigation adherence improved to 96% and 100%, but Day 7 response adherence declined to 69% by 2022. Despite high community knowledge (75.77%) and positive attitudes (94.59%), key operational constraints included migrant populations, workforce shortages, reduced funding, and remote logistical barriers.

articles.headline: Mini chamber test validated as high-throughput bioassay for chlorfenapyr ITNs
articles.title_original: The mini chamber test: a novel bioassay for bioefficacy testing of chlorfenapyr treated nets used in malaria control.
articles.authors: Lewis A, Jones-Brown R, Harvey G, Praulins G, Reid E, Lugenge AG, Mechan F, Gleave K, Lees RS
articles.details: Study developed and optimized a mini chamber test as animal-free, higher-throughput alternative to tunnel tests for evaluating chlorfenapyr insecticide-treated nets (ITNs). Using pyrethroid-susceptible and -resistant mosquitoes on pyrethroid-chlorfenapyr ITNs, overnight exposure achieved 99% 72-hour mortality with the mini chamber versus 70% with tunnel test. The test showed a dose-response relationship with chemical content measured by HPLC in washed and field-used nets.

articles.headline: P. vivax underdiagnosis and pvmdr1 amplification in Brazilian Amazon
articles.title_original: Hidden Infections, Emerging Resistance: Underdiagnosed Plasmodium vivax and multidrug resistance-1 (pvmdr1) gene Amplification in the Guiana Shield.
articles.authors: Puça MCSB, Pereira Mascarenhas ME, Huguet QB, Salazar YEAR, Nogueira IR, Louzada J, de Pina-Costa A, de Siqueira AM, de Silva MO, Pereira DB, Ladeia-Andrade S, Ferreira MU, de Oliveira Ferreira J, Gil JP, de Sousa TN
articles.details: Study of 727 confirmed malaria cases from Brazilian states revealed that P. vivax was frequently misclassified in microscopy when co-circulating with P. falciparum. pvmdr1 copy number variation showed geographic heterogeneity: nearly all western Amazon isolates carried single copy, while 60% of Roraima isolates exhibited amplification, rising to over 70% after 2020. Authors conclude that inadequate drug exposure may drive selection of drug-resistant parasite strains.

articles.headline: Wealth, age, and urbanization shape P. falciparum infection risk in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Risk factors and geospatial associations with Plasmodium falciparum malaria infection and IgG seroprevalence: Nigeria, 2018.
articles.authors: Jaramillo-Underwood A, Ogunniyi A, Zhou H, Uhomoibhi P, Mba N, Ndodo N, Dawurung AB, Abubakar AG, Okoye MI, Iriemenam NC, Maire M, Steinhardt L, Rogier E, Ihekweazu C
articles.details: Analysis of 89,432 blood samples from a 2018 nationwide Nigerian survey showed P. falciparum current/recent prevalence of 38.3% (95% CI: 37.2-39.5%) by antigenemia and seroprevalence of 48-87%. Children aged 5-14 years had highest odds of active infection compared to children under 5. The wealthiest category showed 5-fold reduction in active infection odds and 10-fold reduction in exposure odds versus the poorest; urban living associated with 30% reduction in active infection and 60% reduction in exposure.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in non-endemic Europe: a German hospital experience
articles.title_original: Malaria: 11-Year Experience With Imported Cases at a German University Hospital and Epidemiological Trends Amid the COVID-19 Pandemic.
articles.authors: Mester P, Kunst C, Schmid S, Zimmermann K, Bülow S, Rennert J, Lampl BMJ, Schilling T, Müller M, Pavel V
articles.details: Retrospective study of 58 malaria patients treated 2012-2023 at a German university hospital, with median age 37.5 years (65% male). P. falciparum caused 96% of infections. 31.6% required intensive care and 98% survived. Only 17% had taken malaria prophylaxis, and thrombocytopenia was the primary laboratory abnormality. Authors note that despite rarity in Europe, severe cases occur with high morbidity, and delayed diagnosis contributes to preventable deaths.

articles.headline: Climatic extremes and migration drive malaria risk in South Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria at the margins: climatic extremes, migration, and urban informal settlement risk in South Africa.
articles.authors: Matamanda SH, Musakwa W, Dzomba A
articles.details: Study examined climate data (2000-2024) and laboratory-confirmed malaria cases across three South African provinces plus migration data (2017-2024). Humidity was positively associated with malaria incidence in KwaZulu-Natal and Mpumalanga, while lag-1 rainfall showed significant association with Limpopo malaria. Post-flood (April-May 2022) migration into Thembelihle increased markedly. Authors conclude that climatic extremes and post-shock mobility reorganize malaria risk, requiring tail-aware, lag-sensitive surveillance.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium biology: persistence, host specificity, and viral associations
articles.title_original: New experimental insights into the biology of avian malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Valkiūnas G, Iezhova TA, Chagas CRF, Tchoumbou M, Himmel T, Petrone ME, Duc M
articles.details: Experimental study transferred three avian Plasmodium species (P. circumflexum, P. vaughani, P. relictum) to Eurasian siskins, monitoring infections up to 185 days. No tissue stages were detected, suggesting persistence via erythrocytic merogony. P. relictum showed unusual gametocyte-dominated parasitaemia. The study detected Matryoshka RNA virus (MaRNAV-9) in association with P. circumflexum for the first time. Authors conclude this provides new insights into avian malaria diagnostics and persistence.

articles.headline: Scale-up of HIV, TB, malaria programs averts billions in PHC costs
articles.title_original: The benefits of investments to combat HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria for primary healthcare from 2000 to 2023: An economic modeling analysis.
articles.authors: Su JS, Stover J, Pretorius C, Winskill P, Sweeney S, Hallett TB, Menzies NA
articles.details: Economic modeling across 108 low- and middle-income countries (2000-2023) estimated that scale-up of HTM (HIV, tuberculosis, malaria) services averted 6.9 billion outpatient visits (95% UI [4.4, 10.5]) and 3.9 billion inpatient bed-days (95% UI [2.5, 5.9]), representing US$135 billion (95% UI [71, 250]) in averted costs. In 2023, these reductions represented 4.4% of hospital bed capacity and 1.6% of government health spending nationally; for low-income countries, 22.9% and 5.1%.

articles.headline: Deep learning model for mosquito classification with 99.42% accuracy
articles.title_original: MosQNet-SA: Explainable convolutional-attention network for mosquito classification with application as a RESTful API for dengue and malaria risk mapping.
articles.authors: Masud MA, Akter S, Sultana N, Islam MS, Abu Yousuf M, Noori FM, Uddin MZ
articles.details: Study presents MosQNet-SA, a convolutional-attention network achieving 99.42% accuracy on classification of 1,000 images across three mosquito species (Aedes, Anopheles, Culex). The model incorporates spatial attention mechanisms and depthwise separable convolutions for computational efficiency. Authors developed a RESTful API for real-time mosquito classification and disease risk mapping, with explainability enhanced through Saliency, GradCAM, LIME, and Kernel SHAP methods for public health practitioners.

articles.headline: K13 mutations confer high-level artemisinin resistance in Bangladeshi parasites
articles.title_original: Functional assessment of prevalent kelch13 mutations reveals high-level artemisinin resistance potential in Bangladeshi Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nima MK, Bhattacharyya N, Shoue D, Park J, Phru CS, Sazed SA, Padmanabhan PK, Kumar S, Alam MS, Ferdig M, Mukherjee A
articles.details: Functional study used CRISPR-Cas9 to introduce three K13 mutations (C580Y, F446I, R561H) into Bangladeshi P. falciparum isolates. Ring-stage survival assays showed C580Y and R561H conferred marked artemisinin resistance increases in both sensitive and resistant backgrounds, with R561H showing extreme resistance (RSA: 30.09% ± 1.9%) in the CHT-R strain alongside asexual fitness advantage (5.1% per generation). F446I had only modest effect. Authors conclude Bangladeshi parasites are genetically primed to sustain high-level artemisinin resistance.

articles.headline: IFN-γ signaling in T cells mediates malaria-associated lung pathology
articles.title_original: Comprehensive immune profiling reveals IFN-γ signaling in T cells mediates parasite phagocytosis in a rodent malaria model.
articles.authors: Chen S-s, Yang Q, Zhong Y, Liu D, Zhou L, Wei H-c, Li C-l, Song J, Wang Y-h, Hu X, Chen L, Lin J-w
articles.details: Study using rodent malaria model and comprehensive transcriptomics found IFN-γ signaling in T cells plays important role in malaria-associated lung pathology. Disruption of IFN-γ signaling reduced lung parasite load and attenuated pathology by enhancing T cell-monocyte interaction. Disruption led to increased CD8+ Ly6C+ proinflammatory monocytes with elevated phagocytic capacity. Authors highlight importance of IFN-γ signaling in T cell-monocyte interaction in malaria-associated lung pathology.

articles.headline: Novel K13 and K13-independent resistance variants in African P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Novel Pfk13 and Pfubp1 genotypes in African Plasmodium falciparum isolates exhibiting reduced susceptibility to the antimalarials artemisinin and lumefantrine.
articles.authors: Pratt S, van Schalkwyk DA, Stewart L, Hocke EF, Mannan S, Berry C, Muwanguzi-Karugaba J, Nolder D, Rogers C, Chiodini PL, Henrici RC, Moss S, Billows N, Phelan J, Clark TG, Campino S, Sutherland CJ
articles.details: Analysis of 50 P. falciparum isolates from UK travelers (2012 onwards) from Kenya, Uganda, Zambia, and Namibia identified reduced in vitro ring-stage susceptibility to artemisinin associated with novel and established variants in pfk13 propeller/non-propeller domains, plus variants in pfap2μ, pfcoronin, and pfubp1. Among 39 evaluable isolates from 15 countries, six of the ten least susceptible to lumefantrine were from Uganda travelers. Authors report pfk13-dependent and -independent partial artemisinin resistance arising in multiple African settings.

articles.headline: P. falciparum csp haplotype diversity in Cameroon pre-vaccine implementation
articles.title_original: Haplotype diversity of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein prior to malaria vaccine implementation in Kaelé Health District, Cameroon, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Ali IM, Vasquez Martinez B, Tchuenkam VPK, Sadler JM, Gorman CC, Nsango SE, Junior VV, Ateba JM, Wanda GY, Bailey JA, Dinglasan RR, Lin JT, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Study analyzed 100 P. falciparum-positive dried blood spots (2022-2023) from Cameroon using 4CAST amplicon sequencing of four genes. csp was genotyped in 35% of samples, identifying 22 haplotypes (He = 0.908; π = 0.021). The vaccine-matched haplotype was present in 20% of genotyped infections. T cell epitopes showed signs of balancing selection. ama1 and sera2 exhibited higher genotyping success and diversity, with mean within-participant haplotype counts of 2.8 and 2.1 respectively.

articles.headline: Antibody avidity and magnitude in malaria vaccine responses
articles.title_original: Relationship between antibody avidity, Fc-mediated functional activity and longevity of malaria vaccine responses in clinical trials.
articles.authors: Horton JL, Chan JA, Kurtovic L, Reiling L, Feng G, Persson KEM, Sacarlal J, Anders RF, McCarthy JS, Dobaño C, Boyle MJ, Beeson JG
articles.details: Analysis of two malaria vaccine trials—Phase 1 MSP-2 trial in malaria-naïve adults and Phase 2b RTS,S trial in African children—examined association between antibody avidity, Fc-mediated functional activities, and longevity. IgG magnitude correlated strongly with avidity, but avidity was not a major determinant of Fc-mediated function (complement fixation, Fcγ-receptor binding, opsonic phagocytosis). IgG magnitude was a stronger determinant of function than avidity. Avidity showed only weak and antigen-specific association with longevity.

articles.headline: PvDBP gene amplification enables P. vivax immune evasion in vivo
articles.title_original: PvDBP gene amplification is associated with functional immune evasion by Plasmodium vivax in vivo.
articles.authors: Baldor L, Seng D, Heng S, Khim N, Hor S, Thin S, Salinas N, Tolia N, Chitnis C, Mueller I, Foroughi S, King C, Lo E, Witkowski B, Flamand C, Feufack-Donfack L, Popovici J
articles.details: Longitudinal study of 657 individuals in Eastern Cambodia (2019-2020) examined whether multi-copy pvdbp parasites evade anti-PvDBP antibodies. Multi-copy parasites ranged from 35% in low-prevalence villages to 47% in high-prevalence areas (p=0.0246). Among individuals with high binding-inhibitory antibodies, 87% (40/46) were infected with multi-copy parasites versus 38% (193/514) without antibodies (p<0.0001). Multi-copy parasites were more frequent in asymptomatic carriers, indicating pvdbp duplication helps parasites avoid host immunity.

articles.headline: US severe malaria cases concentrate near trauma centers for artesunate access
articles.title_original: Mapping Hospitals That Treat Severe Malaria Cases in the United States: Evidence to Inform Health Systems Strategies for Intravenous Artesunate Supply.
articles.authors: Ravishankar K, Mace KE, Koiso S, Ridpath AD, Gulbas E, Thwing J, LaRocque RC, Williams S, Ryan ET, Brown TS, Hyle EP
articles.details: Spatial analysis of 1466 hospitalized severe malaria cases (2012-2018) reported to CDC identified geographic distribution and trauma center proximity. Cases were reported from 292 of 3142 US counties (9.3%) and concentrated in New York, Maryland, Texas, DC, and Pennsylvania. 95.8% of contiguous US cases were within 2 hours driving time of a Level 1 trauma center. Findings support targeted intravenous artesunate distribution strategies.

articles.headline: Artesunate treatment of severe malaria in nonendemic countries
articles.title_original: Artesunate Treatment of Severe Malaria in Nonendemic Countries: When, Where, How, and What Now?
articles.authors: White AC Jr
articles.details: Article examines artesunate treatment protocols for severe malaria management in countries without endemic transmission. Specific details about timing, location, treatment approaches, and outcomes are not available from the title alone.

articles.headline: Pharmacy malaria diagnosis and treatment in Kenya shows high adherence
articles.title_original: Guideline-concordant care and outcomes for pediatric malaria cases: descriptive evidence from pharmacy-based fever management in Kenya.
articles.authors: Dieci M, Nekesa C, Kayaro NI, Vernon J, Graetz I, Winskell K, Cummings JR
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort study of 239 febrile children under 5 at 39 private pharmacies in Kenya assessed guideline-concordant malaria care. 69% received malaria diagnostic testing; of those tested, 59% were malaria-positive and 78% received appropriate antimalarial treatment. 88% of malaria-positive children treated with antimalarials fully recovered at two-week follow-up. Results demonstrate high pharmacy adherence to malaria diagnostic and treatment guidelines.

articles.headline: Dak Lak malaria cases decline dramatically toward elimination by 2030
articles.title_original: Malaria situation in a highly endemic province in the Central Highlands of Vietnam: a retrospective analysis.
articles.authors: Nguyen-Tien T, Tran V, Nguyen TM, Hoang PH, Le DM, Maude RJ
articles.details: Retrospective surveillance study of 2147 malaria cases in Dak Lak province, Vietnam (2017-2022) progressing toward 2030 elimination goal. 58.2% were indigenous cases and 39.1% imported, with P. falciparum dominant. Malaria cases dramatically declined, approaching zero since 2021. Males, ethnic minorities, and people in high-transmission areas were at higher risk. Seasonal peaks occurred in October-November with temperature and rainfall associations.

articles.headline: Kidney biomarkers predict acute kidney injury and mortality in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Kidney and endothelial injury biomarkers as predictors of complications and mortality in patients with malaria: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Nasserala JCL, Leitão FNC, Dos Santos AVR, Martins AMC, Meneses GC, de Souza RN, da Silva Junior GB, De Francesco Daher E, de Deus Morais MJ
articles.details: Systematic review (PRISMA 2020, PROSPERO registered) evaluated renal and endothelial injury biomarkers as predictors of acute kidney injury complications and mortality in malaria patients. Combined evaluation of biomarkers with clinical and laboratory parameters improved AKI characterization and risk stratification. Elevated biomarker levels were consistently associated with increased risk of renal complications and adverse outcomes, representing promising tools for early detection and monitoring.

articles.headline: Mozambique mass drug administration achieves 90% operational coverage with DHP
articles.title_original: Operational performance of a programmatic mass drug administration campaign for malaria in southern Mozambique: a comprehensive mixed-methods evaluation of implementation outcomes.
articles.authors: Fuente-Soro L, Tusell M, Vecchio J, Nhangave A, Bapu K, Riley C, Materrula F, Dimene M, Sibindy S, Candrinho B, Aide P, Guinovart C
articles.details: Mixed-methods evaluation of programmatic mass drug administration (pMDA) with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine in Chidenguele, Mozambique (December 2022-February 2023) in ~59,271 target population. Operational coverage reached 90.2% (569/631) of those present, though programmatic coverage was 73.9% (569/770). Community acceptance was high at 96.2% (607/631), and 84.1% considered the intervention appropriate. Implementation fidelity varied, and challenges remain in reaching 80% coverage target.

articles.headline: Dengue co-infections masked in malaria-endemic Kenya; algorithm revision needed
articles.title_original: Unmasking non-malarial mosquito-borne infections among febrile children in malaria-endemic regions of western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ogony J, Karanja S, Ayodo G, Akinyi I, Onguru D, Angira C, Menya D, Mangeni J
articles.details: Screening study of 1004 febrile children under 5 in Busia and Kisumu Counties, Kenya tested for both malaria and dengue. Of 380 screened-positive, 215 (21.4%) had P. falciparum alone, 90 (8.9%) had dengue fever alone, and 75 (7.5%) had co-infections. Busia had highest P. falciparum-only infections (23.4%) while Kisumu had highest dengue-only infections (12.6%). Findings highlight need to revise febrile illness screening algorithms.

articles.headline: African-specific genetic variants protect against severe malaria and bacteremia
articles.title_original: African-specific genetic loci determine iron status and risk of severe malaria and bacteremia in African children.
articles.authors: Muriuki JM, Mentzer AJ, Band G, Chong AY, Macharia AW, Mogire RM, Abuga KM, Mitchell R, Gilchrist JJ, Webb EL, Ndungu FM, Raffield LM, Ekunwe L, Bentley AR, Sirima SB, Madhi SA, Hill AVS, Prentice AM, Bejon P, Hemani G, Smith GD, Sandhu MS, Elliott AM, Williams TN, Adeyemo A, Atkinson SH
articles.details: Genome-wide association study of 3928 African children and 2868 African American adults identified genetic loci regulating iron status and associations with severe malaria and bacteremia. Variants at GTF3C5 (rs2905094) and DUP4 haplotype (rs552439837, Dantu blood group) confer protection against severe malaria and bacteremia. CHCHD7/SDR16C5 variant (rs73596248) increases hepcidin levels and reduces risk of K. pneumoniae and S. aureus bacteremia. European polygenic risk scores showed limited applicability to African populations.

articles.headline: Public awareness and education key to malaria vaccine rollout success
articles.title_original: Public awareness and education: key to the success of the malaria vaccine rollout in Sudan.
articles.authors: Mohammed AAEA, Elagib HKE, Ibrahim MAA, Rafai MAA, Ali ODMH, Yaseen OYM
articles.details: Article discusses the importance of public health communication and education strategies for successful malaria vaccine implementation in Sudan. Specific details regarding intervention strategies and outcomes are not available from the title alone.

articles.headline: Biological control and genetic techniques offer sustainable malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Exploring the Scope and Status of Biological Interventions in Malaria Control.
articles.authors: Kumari S, Siddaramegowda R, Beeranthbail A, Shankar G, Kumar M, Sinha S, Anvikar A, Chalageri VH, Tripathi PK, Marinaik SB
articles.details: Narrative review of biological interventions for malaria vector control addressing limitations of conventional approaches (insecticide resistance, environmental concerns, climate change). Approaches reviewed include entomopathogenic fungi, natural predators, microbial agents, plant-derived compounds with larvicidal properties, sterile insect technique, and genetically modified mosquitoes. These biological methods represent viable alternatives for sustainable malaria control and potential eradication.

articles.headline: Malaria, diarrhea, and respiratory infections reduce child growth velocity
articles.title_original: Associations of Malaria, Diarrhea, and Respiratory Infections with Growth Velocity of Children in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Hiruy HN, Liu E, Worku A, Manji KP, Duggan CP, Berhane Y, Fawzi WW
articles.details: Longitudinal analysis of 2397 Tanzanian children followed for 18 months (30,079 monthly visits) examined associations of infections with growth velocity. Malaria reduced height velocity by 0.14 cm/month (95% CI: -0.23 to -0.05). Respiratory tract infections reduced height by 0.10 cm/month (95% CI: -0.15 to -0.04) and weight by 37 g/month. Diarrhea had largest impact: -0.26 cm/month height and -120 g/month weight. Integrated strategies addressing infections may improve child growth.

articles.headline: PfCRT chloroquine transporter regulated by redox-dependent mechanism
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Chloroquine Resistance Transporter (PfCRT) Is a Redox-Dependent Drug Transporter.
articles.authors: Chernitsky-Hamd D, Roy R, Schemm J, Schall A, Petros M, Willems A, Roepe PD
articles.details: Molecular study using site-specific mutagenesis and molecular dynamics to investigate how PfCRT (chloroquine resistance transporter) function is regulated by redox potential in the parasite's digestive vacuole. Disulfide bonds at C289/C312 and C301/C309 pairs are required for chloroquine transport activity; four additional cysteines (C101, C139, C171, C328) modulate transport. Large conformational changes in loop 7 occur with redox switching.

articles.headline: ChAdOx1-MVA liver-stage vaccine generates high CD8 response but fails protection
articles.title_original: Phase I/IIa study to assess the safety, immunogenicity and efficacy of ChAdOx1-MVA vectored vaccines expressing a novel liver-stage malaria dual antigen LS2 by sporozoite challenge in malaria-naïve adults.
articles.authors: Silman D, Flaxman A, Datoo M, Edwards NJ, Ramos-Lopez F, Mitton C, Mair C, Bellamy D, Bowyer G, Morter R, Halbroth B, Venkatraman N, Folegatti PM, Marshall J, Poulton I, Bajer A, Salman AM, Berrie E, Baum J, Blagborough AM, Crocker W, Roberts R, Lawrie AM, Spencer AJ, Gilbert SC, Ewer KJ, Hill AVS
articles.details: Phase I/IIa trial in 10 UK adults vaccinated with ChAdOx1 LS2 prime and MVA LS2 boost expressing LSA1 and LSAP2 antigens (6 unvaccinated controls). Vaccine was well-tolerated with high CD8+ T-cell immunogenicity (median 4473 SFC/10^6 PBMC). During controlled human malaria infection by mosquito, all vaccinated volunteers became infected with median time to diagnosis 13 days versus 13.25 days in controls, demonstrating no protective efficacy.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance widespread in Odisha malaria vectors, especially DDT
articles.title_original: Mapping the Insecticide Resistance Landscape of Malaria Vectors in Odisha, India (1993-2024): A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Barik TK, Velamuri PS, Bhuyan KB, Sabar S, Mohapatra P, Sahu B, Das MK, Pati S, Dash AP, Raghavendra K
articles.details: Scoping review of insecticide resistance in Anopheles mosquitoes in Odisha (1993-2024) compiled from 28 published sources. An. culicifacies, the major malaria vector prevalent in all 30 districts, showed confirmed resistance to DDT in 26 districts (≤90% mortality), malathion in 17 districts, and deltamethrin in 10 districts. Multiple insecticide resistance emergence signals urgent need for novel insecticide modes of action and integrated vector management.

articles.headline: Malaria-HIV co-infection in Sub-Saharan Africa: epidemiology and management
articles.title_original: Insights into Malaria and HIV Co-infection: Trends and Impact in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Ayoola AO, Okanlawon BM, Idowu EM, Adedokun TT, Ige AA, Olorunfemi AB, Ojurongbe OD
articles.details: Narrative review examining epidemiology and clinical management of concurrent malaria-HIV infection in sub-Saharan Africa. Evidence indicates integrated malaria and HIV services may improve patient outcomes and health system efficiency. Review identifies persistent gaps in surveillance systems, diagnostic capacity, treatment interactions, and health infrastructure. Continued progress in diagnostics, therapeutics, and health infrastructure alongside community engagement necessary to address dual disease burden.

articles.headline: 2La inversion in Nigerian An. coluzzii shows adaptive geographic structuring
articles.title_original: Spatial eco-climatic gradients drive adaptive 2La chromosomal inversion polymorphism in Anopheles coluzzii, a major malaria vector in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Adeogun AO, Adetunji O, Babalola AS, Izekor RT, Oyeniyi TA, Adesoye O, Wahedi J, Busari LO, Aniekeme G, Ajayi S, Chabi J, Awolola ST
articles.details: Study of 2La chromosomal inversion karyotypes in Anopheles coluzzii across 12 Nigerian states representing southern, central, and northern ecological zones. The 2La/2La homokaryotype predominated in hot, arid Sahelian and Sudan savanna zones, while standard 2La+/2La+ arrangement predominated in humid southern forest regions. Clear latitudinal cline observed with heterozygosity peaking in central Nigeria, suggesting aridity and humidity are major determinants of inversion distribution.

articles.headline: Housing modifications provide low-cost malaria and cholera prevention strategy
articles.title_original: Household and community-based approaches to malaria and cholera prevention: A narrative review with case studies from Uganda and Mozambique.
articles.authors: Guido G, Totaro V, Lochoro P, Ictho J, Occa E, Chhaganlal K, Segala FV, Dall'Oglio G, Mechendura BF, Gomonda E, Putoto G, Penco F, Asaduzzaman M, Nicastri E, Farkas FB, Iatta R, Lakatos B, Ali RW, Saracino A, Di Gennaro F
articles.details: Narrative review with case studies from Uganda and Mozambique examining housing-based interventions (screened windows and doors, sealed eaves, elevated flooring, insecticide-treated barriers, improved latrines, proper waste disposal, hand-washing facilities) for malaria and cholera prevention. Combination of structural modifications and behavioral changes at community level are low-cost, community-acceptable, and implementable strategies that can complement existing large-scale control programs in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Avian malaria detected in peri-urban but not urban mosquito populations
articles.title_original: Urbanization and Vector Species Shape Avian Malaria Prevalence in Mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Parra P, Veiga J, Garrigós M, Panisse G, Garrido M, Martínez-de la Puente J
articles.details: Assessment of avian malaria prevalence in two mosquito species across urban and peri-urban habitats. No infections were detected in either species in urban areas, while Culex pipiens mosquitoes from peri-urban sites showed 2.53% avian malaria prevalence. Findings highlight influence of urbanization on parasite transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Ivermectin-induced Loa loa encephalopathy in malaria co-infected patient
articles.title_original: Probable Loa loa-associated encephalopathy following ivermectin administration in a patient with malaria infection: A case report from the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Posite CM, Findama JK, Vwiravwahali MK, Molo IM, Bunduki GK
articles.details: Case report of a 37-year-old woman from Ituri Province, DRC presenting with meningeal signs and altered mental status after ivermectin administration for loiasis, complicated by concurrent malaria and high Loa loa microfilarial burden. Blood smear revealed Plasmodium trophozoites and Loa loa microfilariae; cerebrospinal fluid analysis demonstrated Loa loa microfilariae without bacterial or fungal infection. Following antimicrobial therapy, albendazole, and supportive care, patient recovered completely with microfilarial clearance after 19 days hospitalization.

articles.headline: Malaria in pregnancy in DRC conflict zones: 17-40% prevalence, fragmented response
articles.title_original: Time to Act; Let Us Unite to Eliminate Malaria Among Pregnant Women in War Zone From Eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo: A Review.
articles.authors: Akilimali A, Mehmood L, Njazi PI, Ali B, Maqbool S, Younas M, Sagide M, Rizvi SVZ, Munsab R, Rizvi SUA, Fatima S, Alhassan A, Wei CR, Langat AK
articles.details: Narrative review examining malaria in pregnancy (MiP) in eastern DRC conflict zones (2007-2025). Findings indicate MiP prevalence between 17% and 40%, with significant 26% asymptomatic pooled estimate creating silent transmission reservoirs. Early-stage parasitemia linked to reduced umbilical artery resistance and fetal growth restriction. Armed conflict decimated health infrastructure causing critical shortages of insecticide-treated nets and intermittent preventive treatment; innovations like R21/Matrix-M vaccine implementation hindered by fragmented coordination and funding gaps.

articles.headline: Agent-based modeling shows broad-coverage strategy beats concentrated protection
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal agent-based modelling of malaria.
articles.authors: Walker CR, Anwar MN, Bräuninger L, Richards J, Ataide R, Thang ND, Thang NX, Canavati S, Flegg JA
articles.details: Development of stochastic spatiotemporal agent-based model of Plasmodium falciparum transmission capturing interaction between human and mosquito agents using mosquito climatic suitability estimates and household location data. Model applied to Vietnam investigating intervention distribution strategies. Study found that providing some level of protection to a wide range of households reduces malaria prevalence more compared to providing strong protection to a limited number of households.

articles.headline: P. falciparum-Schistosoma coinfection: 10-13% prevalence in SSA children
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum coinfection with Schistosoma haematobium and Schistosoma mansoni among children in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Hassan U, Siddiqa A, Zhu Y, Zhao J
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 21 observational studies examining P. falciparum-Schistosoma coinfection in children aged 0-17 years in Sub-Saharan Africa. Pooled prevalence of P. falciparum-S. haematobium coinfection was 10.0% (95% CI 6.0-14.0%), highest in West Africa (13.0%, 95% CI 6.0-22.0%), followed by Central Africa (9.0%, 95% CI 7.0-10.0%), and East Africa (3.0%, 95% CI 0.0-11.0%). P. falciparum-S. mansoni coinfection prevalence was 13.0% (95% CI 6.0-24.0%), with East Africa highest (25.0%, 95% CI 13.0-39.0%), Central Africa (3.0%, 95% CI 2.0-5.0%), and West Africa (2.0%, 95% CI 1.0-3.0%).

articles.headline: Respiratory distress in cerebral malaria driven by malarial pneumonopathy
articles.title_original: Malarial pneumonopathy in Malawian children with cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Wynkoop HJ, Lintner-Rivera M, Taylor T, Seydel KB, Pensulo P, Ayamie M, Chetcuti K, Vidal LLM, O'Brien NF
articles.details: Prospective observational study of 97 Malawian children (6 months to 12 years) with WHO-defined cerebral malaria examining prevalence and pathophysiology of respiratory distress. Twenty-seven participants (28%) met respiratory distress criteria. Despite elevated lactate (median 7.0 mmol/L), metabolic acidosis was sole disturbance in only 12 (12%); 33% showed primary respiratory alkalosis. Lung ultrasound revealed subcentimeter consolidations in 88% and larger consolidations (≥1 cm) less commonly. Respiratory distress not associated with increased mortality; findings suggest pulmonary microvascular obstruction and interstitial inflammation drive respiratory dysfunction.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine safety monitoring in Ghana, Kenya, Malawi
articles.title_original: Safety monitoring of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine: experiences and lessons from routine pharmacovigilance in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi.
articles.authors: Ashie A, Mandale M, Ndalama A, Darko D, Seaneke S, Boateng EK, Sabblah G, Asamoa-Amoakohene A, Amponsa-Achiano K, Mohammed NT, Jalang'o R, Nkansah E, Adu-Boahen Y, Siyoi F, Toroitich A, Khaemba C, Nambwa P, Jain S, Okine RNA, Mssusa AK
articles.details: Pharmacovigilance study monitored safety of RTS,S/AS01E vaccine through routine surveillance systems in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi during pilot implementation. Causality assessment by national expert committees found no new safety concerns. Challenges included underreporting of adverse events, delays in data submission, and poor report quality despite targeted system strengthening.

articles.headline: Spatial modeling identifies malaria hotspots in southwestern Benin
articles.title_original: Identification of malaria hotspots in southwestern Benin through spatial joint modelling of malaria incidence and vector abundance.
articles.authors: Monteiro GM, Aïkpon RY, Dandonougbo C, Sedda L, Djogbenou LS
articles.details: Joint Bayesian spatial modeling of Anopheles gambiae and funestus counts with malaria incidence in eight communes identified hotspots primarily on western shores of Lake Ahémé and in Atchannou, Sè, Avloh, and Grand-Popo districts. An. funestus showed stronger spatial correlation with malaria incidence (119.95 km²) than An. gambiae (89.90 km²), with 67.29 km² showing synergistic effects.

articles.headline: Pediatric malaria severity independent of prior immune exposure
articles.title_original: Pediatric malaria in a non-endemic European setting: clinical patterns, laboratory markers, and the ambiguous role of immunity.
articles.authors: Caiazzo R, Panero B, Carello M, Coppola C, Di Tonno R, Markowich AH, Stracuzzi M, Valentino MS, Grosso S, Gigantiello A, Giacomet V
articles.details: Retrospective study of 74 pediatric malaria cases (median age 9 years) in Milan (2004–2024): 62.2% P. falciparum, 27% P. vivax; 23% developed severe malaria with no significant difference by presumed immunity status. Serum sodium inversely associated with hospitalization length (ρ=−0.284, p=0.020); CRP positively with fever duration (p=0.033) and negatively with platelet count (p<0.001).

articles.headline: Serum uric acid common but not predictive in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Serum Uric Acid as a Biomarker of Disease Severity in Children With Complicated Malaria: A Cross-Sectional Study From Eastern India.
articles.authors: Kumar M, Jha S, Kumar H, Sarangam B, Munda PK, Garg V, Mudgal SK
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 102 children (age 1–18) with complicated P. falciparum malaria in eastern India (September 2022–May 2024) found hyperuricemia in 80.4%, elevated liver enzymes (SGOT 72.5%, SGPT 83.3%), and elevated bilirubin (65.7%); adverse outcomes in 29.4%. Abnormal SGOT, SGPT, and bilirubin significantly associated with adverse outcomes (p≤0.011), but serum uric acid was not (p=0.115).

articles.headline: Global malaria epidemiology and control strategies review
articles.title_original: Malaria in the 21st Century: Global Disease Burden, Epidemiological Insights, and Strategic Control Approaches.
articles.authors: Alharbi BF, Ahmed MA
articles.details: Comprehensive review synthesizing WHO World Malaria Report 2024 and Global Burden of Disease estimates on global malaria epidemiology, regional trends, determinants, and control strategies. Addresses socio-economic factors, intervention methods, and emerging challenges including drug resistance, climate change, and limited funding. Concludes with policy priorities for achieving WHO Global Technical Strategy elimination goals by 2030.

articles.headline: Rwanda P. falciparum prevalence fell 53% despite artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of Falciparum malaria in Rwanda: Shifts in parasite prevalence and risk factors between the 2014-15 and 2019-20 Rwanda Demographics and Health Surveys.
articles.authors: Zuromski J, Young NW, Gashema P, Iradukunda V, Bosco NJ, Sadler JM, Gaither C, Munyaneza T, Connelly SV, Lee L, Goel V, Muvunyi CM, Dieu Butera J, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA
articles.details: Analysis of 7,127 dried blood spot samples from 2019–20 Rwanda Demographic and Health Survey using ultra-sensitive qRT-PCR found national P. falciparum infection prevalence of 7.7% (95% CI: 6.8–8.7%), predominantly low-density infections (median parasitemia 7.3 parasites/µL). P. falciparum prevalence decreased 53% relative to 2014–15 survey; male sex, lower wealth, and lower elevation were risk factors despite emerging artemisinin-resistance mutations.

articles.headline: K13 double mutations confer ozonide artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Double Mutations in Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 drive resistance to next-generation artemisinin derivatives in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Bower-Lepts C, Ward KE, Wittlin S, Stokes BH, Yeo T, Qahash T, Small-Saunders JL, Park H, Uhlemann AC, Llinás M, Fidock DA, Mok S
articles.details: Study of Cambodian K13 mutant parasites under increasing artefenomel (OZ439) pressure identified novel K13 A212T mutation conferring OZ439 resistance. Double mutations R539T+A212T and C580Y+A212T determined resistance, associated with accelerated parasite recrudescence following OZ439/OZ277 exposure. Metabolomic profiling showed increased glutathione, nucleotide, and aspartate-glutamate metabolism, suggesting resistance via enhanced redox regulation rather than altered drug activation.

articles.headline: Imported malaria comprises 89% of cases in Asia Pacific elimination
articles.title_original: Estimating the burden of imported malaria in the Asia Pacific Region: A Systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Penjor K, Thapa B, Tsheten T, Mazumdar S, Bagheri N, Kotepui M, Wangdi K
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 42 studies from 9 Asia Pacific countries (435,454 total cases) found imported malaria prevalence of 89.0% (95% CI: 75.9–95.5). Imported cases predominated among males (94%), P. falciparum (57%), and those from African origin (64.3%). Prevalence ranged from 95% in Sri Lanka and China to 3.2% in South Korea.

articles.headline: Sub-Saharan malaria burden in women reduced substantially since 1990
articles.title_original: Fatal and non-fatal health outcomes of malaria among reproductive age women in Eastern Sub-Saharan Africa, from 1990 to 2023.
articles.authors: Tadesse S, Korma W, Sherfa A, Bedewi J, Alemseged EA, Angaw Y, Assefa L, Fekadu Y, Ashenafi G, Tsegaye D, Gemechu T
articles.details: Global Burden of Disease 2023 study estimated fatal and non-fatal malaria outcomes among reproductive-age women in Eastern Sub-Saharan Africa (1990–2023). In 2023, prevalence ranged from 19,627 per 100,000 in Mozambique to 842 in Eritrea and 1,603 in Rwanda; substantial reductions occurred across the region from 1990 to 2023, except in Djibouti and Ethiopia.

articles.headline: High pyrethroid resistance and persistent transmission in Tanzania baseline
articles.title_original: Baseline malaria burden and pyrethroid resistance in Muheza, Tanzania informing a cluster randomized trial of the 3D window screens.
articles.authors: Kathet S, Bwana VM, Fereji MA, Mhando JB, Magogo FS, Mwingira V, Kisinza W, Meri S, Khattab A
articles.details: Baseline study of 20 hamlets in Muheza, Tanzania among 778 children (6 months–14 years) found malaria prevalence 40.2%, anemia 55.2%, with Anopheles gambiae and funestus present. High pyrethroid resistance: 56.5% mortality to permethrin, 52.8% to deltamethrin; kdr-East allele frequency 0.45. Annual combined EIR 27.7 infectious bites/person/night; age ≥5 years (aOR=3.03, 95% CI: 2.09–4.40) and lack of bed net use were risk factors.

articles.headline: Risk-based reactive case detection improves malaria yield in Indonesia
articles.title_original: Adapting reactive case detection strategies for high-risk populations to increase malaria infection detection in Aceh Province, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Bennett A, Cotter C, Zarlinda I, Silaen MG, Lina RN, Cueto C, Elyazar I, Jacobson JO, Ekawati L, Hsiang MS, Noviyanti R, Smith JL, Coutrier FN
articles.details: Study comparing standard household-based versus risk-based reactive case detection (targeting forest exposure) in Aceh, Indonesia among 47 index cases (28 P. knowlesi infections). Standard household RACD of 847 contacts yielded 0.1% infection (1/847 by LAMP/PCR), while risk-based RACD of 179 forest-exposed contacts yielded 4.5% LAMP-positive (8/179) and 1.7% PCR-confirmed (3/179).

articles.headline: Correction to timing study of Anopheles biting and interventions
articles.title_original: Correction: A matter of timing: Biting by malaria-infected Anopheles mosquitoes and the use of interventions during the night in rural south-eastern Tanzania.
articles.authors: Namango IH, Moore SJ, Marshall C, Saddler A, Kaftan D, Tenywa FC, Makungwa N, Limwagu AJ, Mapua S, Odufuwa OG, Ligema G, Ngonyani H, Matanila I, Bharmal J, Moore J, Finda M, Okumu F, Hetzel MW, Ross A
articles.details: Correction notice for article on malaria-infected Anopheles biting behavior and timing of intervention use in rural southeastern Tanzania (DOI: 10.1371/journal.pgph.0003864).

articles.headline: ITNs reduce malaria mortality more than climate factors
articles.title_original: Assessing the role of interventions and climate on malaria mortality among children under five years of age: insights from two decades of data from the Health Demographic Surveillance System of Nouna, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Traoré N, Zabré P, Millogo O, Sié A, Vounatsou P
articles.details: 20-year surveillance (2002-2021, analysis 2013-2021) of children <5 malaria mortality in Burkina Faso using wavelet and Bayesian temporal models. ITN coverage reduced mortality 41% (MRR=0.59, 95% BCI 0.42-0.79); rainfall increased mortality 59% (MRR=1.59, 95% BCI 1.18-1.95); LST decreased mortality 32% (MRR=0.68, 95% BCI 0.52-0.86); ACT showed no significant effect. Authors conclude sustained ITN focus needed alongside climate-informed planning.

articles.headline: Inflammation does not mediate BMI-vitamin A relationship
articles.title_original: Inflammation Does Not Mediate Relationships between BMI/BMI-for-Age Z-Score and Retinol or Retinol-Binding Protein in Adult Women or Young Children without Underweight, Wasting, or Malaria: An Analysis of 24 Surveys.
articles.authors: Davis JN, Williams A, Arnold CD, Addo Y, Oaks BM, Wirth JP, Suchdev PS, Young MF, Engle-Stone R
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 24 surveys (16,771 women, 24,707 children) from BRINDA project, excluding those with underweight, wasting, or malaria. Multivariate regression examined relationships between BMI/BMI-for-age z-score, inflammatory biomarkers (CRP, AGP), and retinol/RBP. Among women, BMI associated with retinol/RBP in 5/13 surveys; inflammation partially mediated in 3/13. Among children, BAZ associated with retinol/RBP in 3/22 surveys; inflammation partially mediated in 1/22. Inflammation from overweight/obesity does not substantively impact vitamin A assessment.

articles.headline: African countries inadequately integrate social protection policy
articles.title_original: Assessing the integration of social protection into infectious disease policies: a multi-country analysis of TB, HIV, and malaria strategies in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Munyangaju I, Osório D, Nacarapa E
articles.details: Policy document review of TB, HIV, and malaria strategies (2015-2025) from Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, Nigeria, and Zambia using five-domain health systems framework. 111 documents reviewed; no country institutionalized catastrophic-cost monitoring. Overall readiness ranged 2.6-2.9 (0-3 scale). Malawi (2.9) and Zambia (2.8) highest; Kenya (2.7), Mozambique (2.6), Nigeria (2.7) lower. Implementation/coordination strongest (3.0); financial protection weakest (2.1). Significant gaps in monitoring, budgeting, and accountability.

articles.headline: PfEMP1 IgG breadth associates with malaria immunity childhood
articles.title_original: IgG and IgM responses to PfEMP1 domains associated with semi-immunity to clinical malaria in Burkinabe children under five.
articles.authors: Dankyi BO, Yuguchi T, Rojrung R, Nagaoka H, Kanoi BN, Tiono AB, Nebie I, Ouedraogo A, Tanaka K, Miyake Y, Miura K, Sattabongkot J, Sirima SB, Tsuboi T, Takashima E
articles.details: Serological cross-sectional (n=80 Burkinabe children aged 0-60 months) measuring IgG/IgM responses to 271 P. falciparum PfEMP1 domains via AlphaScreen. Pre-season IgG responses to 132 domains (49%) remained protective against clinical malaria after FDR correction; IgG breadth increased with age. Only 5 IgM responses nominally protective, none survived FDR correction. Opsonic phagocytosis correlated with protection for one antigen only. Broad PfEMP1-specific antibodies mediate early-life protection via mechanisms beyond phagocytosis.

articles.headline: P. vivax relapses frequent, genetically diverse, transmissible
articles.title_original: Frequent and genetically diverse Plasmodium vivax relapses contribute to malaria transmission in Cambodia.
articles.authors: Seng D, Ko K, Orban A, Heng S, Ea M, Tat B, Feufack Donfack LB, Eng V, Tebben K, Khim N, Lefèvre T, Cohuet A, Sommen C, Flamand C, Serre D, Popovici J
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 59 Cambodian P. vivax patients treated with artesunate; 90-day follow-up via qPCR/microscopy every 2 days and genome sequencing. 81% experienced ≥1 relapse (up to 4 relapses; median 20 days to first). Polyclonal infections relapsed more than monoclonal. Relapse parasites ranged from genetically identical to unrelated to initial infection. Membrane-feeding showed 56% of relapse infections (many <100 parasites/μL) infectious to An. dirus. Hypnozoites sustain transmission despite clinical apparency and low density.

articles.headline: Hemoglobin AA associated with asymptomatic malaria
articles.title_original: The Role of Haemoglobin Variants and Red Blood Cell Polymorphisms in Asymptomatic Malaria: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Aninagyei E, Boatey CA, Deku JG, Boakye AA, Nyarkotey E, Larbi G, Acheampong DO
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of asymptomatic community members (4 endemic Ghanaian communities) screened for P. falciparum via PfHRP2, genotyped for ABO and hemoglobin variants via electrophoresis, multivariate logistic regression. HbAA significantly associated with asymptomatic malaria (AOR=2.1, p=0.02); red blood cell polymorphisms significant overall (p<0.001). Higher odds for O Rh D-positive (AOR=2.39), O/HbAA (AOR=1.15), B/HbAS (AOR=1.22) but not statistically significant. Complex host genetic control of asymptomatic carriage.

articles.headline: Hydroxamate inhibitors target P. falciparum metallo-aminopeptidase
articles.title_original: In silico targeting of plasmodium falciparum metallo-aminopeptidases using hydroxamate inhibitors.
articles.authors: Sodum N, Rao V, Chhabra V, Kumar N, Rao CM
articles.details: In silico molecular modeling evaluating cinnamoyl sulfonamide hydroxamate derivatives (NMJ 1-8) against P. falciparum PfA-M1 metallo-aminopeptidase using docking and molecular dynamics. Extra-precision docking scores ranged -1984 to -1989 kcal/mol. NMJ-2 and NMJ-3 showed strongest binding affinities; MD simulations confirmed stable complexes with persistent interactions at key zinc-coordinating residues. Both compounds satisfied ADME pharmacokinetic parameters and overlapped interaction patterns with reference inhibitor tosedostat. NMJ-2 and NMJ-3 proposed as promising scaffolds for preclinical antimalarial development.

articles.headline: Duffy negativity rare but permits P. vivax infection
articles.title_original: Genetic variation in the Duffy blood group among vivax malaria patients and its impact on disease susceptibility.
articles.authors: Tsigie M, Baldor L, Feufack-Donfack LB, Shenkutie TT, Khim N, Melese D, Abera A, Girmachew F, Feleke SM, Hailemariam A, Tasew G, Tollera G, Hailu M, Popovici J, Abebe A, Lo E
articles.details: Cross-sectional molecular epidemiology (Ethiopia, Jan-June 2024) of 485 microscopy-confirmed P. vivax samples genotyped for Duffy ACKR1 via TaqMan qPCR. 93.8% monoclonal P. vivax, 6.2% mixed P. vivax/P. falciparum; 75.1% Duffy heterozygous, 24.5% homozygous, 0.4% Duffy-negative (n=2, both mixed infections, low parasitemia). Duffy negativity not absolute barrier to P. vivax infection; findings suggest alternative, DARC-independent invasion pathway exists and warrants investigation.

articles.headline: Outdoor early-morning biting limits bed net effectiveness
articles.title_original: Mosquito vector composition and biting behavior of malaria vectors in forest versus humid savanna eco-epidemiological settings of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Kiam CB, Aabate L, Bayibeki AN, Ibrahima I, Nanssong-Vomo CT, Tumamo BF, Onguene C, Awono-Ambéné PH, Ayong LS, Moukoko CEE, Morlais I, Nsango SE
articles.details: Entomological survey (April-July 2019) in Mfou forest (n=1,817) and Tibati savanna (n=1,834) using human landing catches, molecular identification (qPCR), parity dissection of 9 Anopheles species. Mfou dominated by An. gambiae s.s. (68.96% collections); indoors EIR=0.84, outdoors 0.60 ib/p/n. Tibati by An. funestus s.s. (49.95% collections); indoors EIR=0.25, outdoors 0.23 ib/p/n. Peak biting 4-5am: 2.68-2.31 bites per person per hour outdoors. Substantial outdoor/early-morning activity indicates bed nets insufficient; complementary strategies needed.

articles.headline: Malaria burden high in first year of life Uganda
articles.title_original: Natural History and the Burden of Malaria During the First Year of Life in the High-Transmission Setting of Uganda.
articles.authors: Aguti M, Nankabirwa JI, Kizza J, Kakuru A, Ssemukuye T, Adrama H, Olwoch P, Opira B, Odongo B, Camanag K, Nakalembe M, Clark T, Rosenthal PJ, Dorsey G, Jaganathan P, Kamya MR
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 855 infants in Busia District, Uganda (2021-2025, before vaccine rollout); follow-up to 1 year via active/passive case detection, microscopy, qPCR every 4 weeks. Over 706.7 person-years: 662 malaria episodes; microscopic parasitemia prevalence 7.9%, combined microscopic+submicroscopic 21.8%. Sickle cell trait (HbAS) conferred 39% protection against symptomatic malaria; modern housing and higher maternal education independently protective. LLIN distribution October 2023 (alpha-cypermethrin + chlorfenapyr) followed by 80% incidence reduction; need for age-targeted interventions emphasized.

articles.headline: Malaria forecasting reveals state-specific seasonal patterns Nigeria
articles.title_original: Exploring the past and forecasting the future of malaria in selected Nigerian states: A time series modelling approach using wavelet and SARIMA.
articles.authors: Bakare EA, Mogbojuri OA, Bakare DA, Odewusi OJ
articles.details: Time series analysis (2015-2024) of confirmed malaria cases from Nasarawa, Kwara, Zamfara States using wavelet decomposition and SARIMA models. Nasarawa: strong annual cycle, 70% peaks September. Kwara: semi-annual/annual/multi-annual cycles, peaks mostly August. Zamfara: moderate annual cycle, consistent September peaks (shifted to August in 2024). Nasarawa-Zamfara synchronized; Kwara asynchronous. SARIMA forecasts 2025-2026: Nasarawa 51,482 cases/month (95% CI 23,899-116,073); Kwara 20,850 (CI 3,381-202,511); Zamfara 67,463 (CI 27,317-171,418).

articles.headline: Duffy null selection may explain low P. vivax Oman
articles.title_original: Adaptive admixture at ACKR1, the Duffy blood group locus, may have shaped Plasmodium vivax prevalence in Oman.
articles.authors: Haffener PE, Al-Riyami AZ, Al-Zadjali S, Busby GBJ, Al-Mahdhuri S, Al-Rawahi M, Al-Hosni S, Al-Marhoobi A, Al-Sheriyani A, Leffler EM
articles.details: Population genomics (n=100 Omanis, whole-genome sequencing, ancestry inference) showing 15.6% (95% CI 10.8-20.3%) African genetic ancestry overall; 76% African ancestry at Duffy locus with high FyES (Duffy null) frequency. Selection coefficient at FyES 0.038 (95% CI 0.027-0.051), likely introduced ≥59 generations ago. Additional rare Duffy null allele identified across Arabian Peninsula populations. P. falciparum-protective alleles higher frequency without enriched African ancestry. East African admixture history shaped Duffy null prevalence and influenced regional P. vivax epidemiology.

articles.headline: Framework prioritizes conserved T cell epitopes for vaccine
articles.title_original: TEpiNom: A computational framework integrating population data to prioritize Plasmodium falciparum T cell epitopes.
articles.authors: Laurenson AJ, Pierce BG, Takala-Harrison S, Laurens MB
articles.details: Computational decision-support framework (TEpiNom) integrated P. falciparum sequence diversity from 18 African countries, predicted T cell epitope-HLA binding, and HLA frequencies from 24 sub-Saharan populations. Analyzed 42 vaccine candidate proteins; identified 2265 MHC I and 1992 MHC II conserved epitopes spanning parasite life stages. Prioritized MHC I epitopes achieved 100% predicted inter-locus MHC I coverage; MHC II epitopes provided 100% coverage per parasite life stage.

articles.headline: Perceived control of malaria increases vaccine hesitancy
articles.title_original: Impact of caregivers' perceptions of malaria on their hesitancy toward childhood malaria vaccination.
articles.authors: Kretchy IA, Bonful HA, Opoku-Mireku M, Koduah A, Opare-Addo M, Marfo AFA, Atobrah D, Klobodu HK, Alhassan Y, Kretchy JP, Opuni KFM
articles.details: Cross-sectional household study in six Ghanaian regions surveyed 2,590 adults on malaria vaccine hesitancy and disease perceptions. Malaria vaccine hesitancy prevalence was 44.8%. Greater perceived control and understanding was associated with increased hesitancy (aPR = 1.15; 95% CI: 1.09-1.21; p < 0.001). Familiarity with malaria weakened preventive behavior, indicating need for locally resonant public health messaging.

articles.headline: Iron-oxide nanoparticles enhance anti-MSP-1 antibody responses
articles.title_original: Iron-oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) enhance malaria vaccine antibody response.
articles.authors: Al-Abboodi A, Falih IQ, Al-Asadi M, Hussein BA, Al-Saady MAAJ, Abdullah TA, Al-Saady M, Nhat T, Le ND, Le PC
articles.details: BALB/c mice (n=8/group) received iron-oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) conjugated to P. falciparum merozoite surface protein-1 (PfMSP-1 19-kDa C-terminal fragment, 25 μg) via four regimens: control, soluble PfMSP-1 alone, SPION-PfMSP-1 single dose day 0, or SPION-PfMSP-1 days 0/21/42. Three-dose SPION-PfMSP-1 produced highest and most durable anti-PfMSP-1 IgG titers throughout observation period compared to soluble protein alone, supporting SPIONs as vaccine-delivery platform for blood-stage antigens.

articles.headline: Plasmodium juxtanucleare hyper-endemic in Thai chickens
articles.title_original: Molecular prevalence and phylogenetic characterization of Plasmodium juxtanucleare in Thai native chickens and fighting cocks across Kalasin Province, Northeast Thailand.
articles.authors: Insee J, Thanasuwan S
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 181 blood samples from Thai native chickens (n=112) and fighting cocks (n=69) across 18 districts in Kalasin Province, January-April 2025, detected Plasmodium via mitochondrial COXIII gene PCR. Overall molecular prevalence 62.98% (114/181); higher in native chickens (67.85%) vs fighting cocks (55.07%), p > 0.05. Prevalence varied significantly among districts (20%-100%, p < 0.05). All isolates clustered as Plasmodium juxtanucleare with 99%-100% nucleotide identity.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance widespread across India
articles.title_original: Genetic profiling of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance markers in Indian Plasmodium falciparum field isolates.
articles.authors: Narang G, Jakhan J, Gupta D, Singh V
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 307 P. falciparum isolates from 14 Indian states (1993-2023) with 243 Pfdhfr and 241 Pfdhps successfully sequenced. Most frequent Pfdhfr mutations S108N (77.8%) and C59R (61.7%); Pfdhps showed A437G (31.9%) and K540E/N (4.98%). 83% of isolates carried resistant alleles; double and triple mutants predominated. Gradual rise of higher-order mutants from North-East regions threatens India's 2030 malaria elimination target.

articles.headline: Quality assurance strengthens malaria diagnostic test reliability
articles.title_original: Quality assurance in rapid diagnostic tests for accurate diagnosis of malaria.
articles.authors: Ahmed N, Yadav A, Ranjha R, Anvikar AR, Bharti PK
articles.details: Review of quality assurance measures essential for rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) for malaria point-of-care diagnosis. Inaccurate RDT performance leads to misdiagnosis, inappropriate treatment, increased mortality, and potential drug-resistant strain emergence, delaying malaria elimination. Enhanced quality assurance protocols crucial for maintaining diagnostic accuracy, enhancing disease surveillance, and bolstering global malaria elimination initiatives.

articles.headline: PyTRAg7 mediates parasite sequestration in splenic fibroblasts
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii tryptophan-rich antigen 7 mediates infected erythrocyte adhesion to splenic fibroblasts via binding to vimentin.
articles.authors: Zhu Y, Zhang H, Sun Y, Han S, Cheng Y
articles.details: Study of P. yoelii tryptophan-rich antigen 7 (PyTRAg7) in splenic parasite-host interactions using protein binding assays and gene-edited parasites. PyTRAg7 binds vimentin on mouse splenic fibroblasts, activating NF-κB p65 signaling and increasing ICAM-1 and integrin β1 expression. PyTRAg7 deletion reduced infected RBC adhesion to fibroblasts, lowered splenic parasite burden, delayed parasitemia onset, and prolonged host survival. TRAgs are conserved in human malaria parasites.

articles.headline: Comparative evaluation of P. falciparum synchronization methods
articles.title_original: Comparative evaluation of synchronization methods for Plasmodium falciparum: Efficiency, practicality and experimental applicability.
articles.authors: Deng Y, Yin Y, Li Y, Xu J, Han JH, Liu Y, Lu F
articles.details: Quantitative evaluation of four P. falciparum 3D7 synchronization methods using microscopy, flow cytometry, and novel polystyrene fluorescent microspheres (PFMs20) for objective late-stage assessment. Sorbitol achieved 88.37% ring-stage purity; Percoll-Sorbitol (60%) showed 87.47% late-stage binding efficiency; discontinuous Percoll-Sorbitol (70%-40%) achieved 88.12% schizont enrichment; Plasmion yielded 73.37%. Decision matrix provided: sorbitol for ring-stage studies, Percoll gradients for stage-specific investigations, Plasmion for resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Opsoclonus myoclonus syndrome follows severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Opsoclonus myoclonus syndrome in an HIV patient, following plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Dominique ETH, Caroline K, Daniel GM, Tchokote SE, Mélanie MA, Laura N, Gishlen I, Sandrine MD, Sandra DGN, Mahaman SAT, Raissa MTJ, Yacouba MN
articles.details: Case report: 47-year-old HIV-positive woman on HAART (undetectable viral load) developed post-infectious opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome two weeks after artemether/lumefantrine treatment for severe P. falciparum malaria. Presented with multidirectional ocular saccades, trunk myoclonus, aggressive behavior, sleep disturbances, and severe ataxia. Partial response to IV methylprednisolone; complete symptom resolution with rituximab addition and functional autonomy restoration. Suggests relationship between malaria and OMS in HIV infection.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine deployment and mortality trends examination
articles.title_original: Vaccines mean malaria deaths should be falling - not rising.
articles.details: Article examines relationship between malaria vaccine deployment and mortality trends; abstract not provided; unable to extract specific quantitative findings or study design from title alone.

articles.headline: P. vivax merozoites activate NK cells via ADCC
articles.title_original: Accessing invasion-competent Plasmodium vivax merozoites reveals antibody-dependent NK cell activation.
articles.authors: Salvador J, Guselli L, Yeat R, Lay S, Thol T, Ya N, Feufack-Donfack L, Heng S, Bohaud C, Orban A, Baldor L, Tat B, Chitnis C, Cantaert T, Popovici J
articles.details: Study established robust platform for synchronized release and isolation of invasion-competent P. vivax merozoites from ex vivo cultures using cGMP-dependent protein kinase inhibitor ML10 for reversible egress inhibition and liposome-based mechanical liberation. Yielded highly pure merozoites with recovery >75% preserving invasive capacity. Direct functional evidence: antibody-opsonized P. vivax merozoites activate human natural killer cells consistent with antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) mechanisms.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance markers absent despite delayed clearance
articles.title_original: Border Region Surveillance of Malaria Drug Resistance, Northern Burundi, 2023-2024.
articles.authors: Niyomwungere D, Sinarinzi P, Caspar E, Thiebaut L, Strubel PE, Tibiri YNG, Ma L, Rurihafi J, Ndizeyimana P, Hakizimana P, Niyizompa N, Witonze A, Ndayiragije V, Ndayisenga F, Ndikumukiza M, Niyonkuru O, Niyonsaba JC, Bangirinama C, Bigirimana JL, Ntahondi N, Mugisha L, Nibakire M, Nzoyikorera N, Sinzinkayo D, Menard D, Nyandwi J
articles.details: Study of 423 children <5 years with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in 8 northern Burundi health facilities (December 2023-June 2024) treated with artemether/lumefantrine; 4.5% remained parasitemic on day 3. No pfkelch13 mutations (ART-R markers) detected. Antifolate/4-aminoquinoline resistance widespread: dhfr triple mutant N51I/C59R/S108N nearly fixed (92%), dhps double/triple mutants common (41%/47%), pfcrt CVIET predominated (84%). Delayed clearance and multidrug-resistant haplotypes may precede artemisinin resistance establishment.

articles.headline: Spatial malaria patterns linked to bed net use across sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Spatial epidemiology of malaria by bed net utilization in 19 sub-Saharan African countries: a DHS-based study (2013-2023).
articles.authors: Yitageasu G, Demoze L
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 126,424 individuals across 11,594 clusters from DHS data in 19 sub-Saharan African countries. Pooled malaria prevalence was 24.0% (95% CI 18–30%), with regional variation: Central Africa 26%, West Africa 25%, East Africa 20%. Prevalence by bed net: no net 32.75%, untreated net 28.91%, insecticide-treated nets 13.23%. Significant spatial clustering detected (Global Moran's I = 0.159, p ≤ 0.001) with 34 malaria clusters overall, indicating geographic heterogeneity can guide targeted control strategies.

articles.headline: AMA1 haplotype variants not associated with malaria symptoms
articles.title_original: Distribution of Plasmodium falciparum apical membrane antigen-1 cluster one loop haplotypes and their association with malaria symptoms in Bandiagara, Mali.
articles.authors: Ouattara A, Adams M, Niangaly A, Coulibaly D, Kone AK, Traore K, Cissoko Y, Kouriba B, Diallo DA, Plowe CV, Doumbo OK, Djimdé A, Laurens MB, Takala-Harrison S, Thera MA
articles.details: Three-year incidence study of 100 children in Bandiagara, Mali examining apical membrane antigen-1 (AMA1) cluster one loop haplotypes. After adjusting for location, specific c1L haplotypes were not associated with malaria symptoms or disease phenotypes. None of the most frequent haplotypes linked to clinical malaria (temperature >37.5°C and parasitemia ≥2,500 parasites/μL). Findings suggest symptoms may be driven by infections with new alleles rather than previously encountered variants.

articles.headline: African malaria programs adapt strategies to climate change impacts
articles.title_original: Climate change and malaria control in Africa: country experiences and strategic responses.
