articles.authors: Yahouedo AG, Merle CS, Diallo I, Ndiop M, Muhire A, Mukashema C, Mutabazi A, Hakizimana E, Muteba C, Kaseba AN, Tanoh MA, Aimain AS, Coulibaly KA, Razanakotomalala V, Diar MSI, Djaskano MI, Kodindo I, Ayala D, Caminade C, Diouf I, de Chevigny T, Ngou O, Pothin E, Girond F, Tchouatieu AM, Fleutelot E, Noseda V, Deuve JL
articles.details: Synthesis of testimonies from six African countries (Chad, Côte d'Ivoire, DRC, Madagascar, Rwanda, Senegal) on climate variability influencing malaria epidemiology. National malaria control programs adopted adaptive strategies including climate-health data integration, surveillance strengthening, early warning systems, and community engagement. Systemic barriers including limited data integration capacity, infrastructure deficits, and financial constraints impede implementation, requiring sustained political commitment and long-term investment in climate-resilient health systems.

articles.headline: ZY19489 resistance blocks piperaquine efflux, restoring drug susceptibility
articles.title_original: Collateral hypersensitivity between ZY19489 and piperaquine neutralizes PfCRT-mediated drug efflux and Plasmodium falciparum resistance.
articles.authors: Okombo J, Ndiaye T, Qahash T, Moura IMR, Gil-Iturbe E, Hagenah LM, Bridgford JL, Bonatto V, Ward KE, Yeo T, Narwal SK, Orta LV, Anderson I, Dhingra SK, Pasaje CFA, Park H, Kim J, Guido RVC, Jiménez-Diaz MB, Angulo-Barturen I, Niles JC, Mancia F, Uhlemann AC, Mok S, Quick M, Winzeler EA, Leroy D, Llinás M, Thathy V, Fidock DA
articles.details: Characterization of ZY19489, a triaminopyrimidine in Phase Ib clinical trial. Asexual parasites pressured with ZY19489 acquire low-grade resistance via novel PfCRT mutation with substantial fitness cost. ZY19489-resistant parasites lose chloroquine resistance and become hypersusceptible to piperaquine (PPQ). ZY19489 blocks mutant PfCRT-mediated PPQ and chloroquine efflux, creating an evolutionary trap where resistance to ZY19489 restores PPQ activity, supporting rationale for pairing as resistance-refractory combination.

articles.headline: Scoping review of African stakeholder views on gene drive mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Knowledge, perspectives, and risk perceptions on gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control among African stakeholders: a scoping review protocol.
articles.authors: Sogodogo A, Diarra S, Keita S, Diallo I, Hartley S, Telly N, Dolo H, Sanogo D, Kané F, Tangara CO, Diakité M, Sangho O, Kayentao K, Fritz H, Winch PJ, Doumbia S
articles.details: Protocol for scoping review to understand knowledge and perspectives of African stakeholders regarding gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control. Systematic search across PubMed, Embase, Science Direct, Cochrane, and Google Scholar for scientific articles and gray literature exploring African stakeholder perceptions and risk assessments. Screening and thematic analysis planned to synthesize findings on stakeholder acceptance and governance implications of gene drive technologies.

articles.headline: High-throughput screening identifies nematocidal compounds from antimalarial library
articles.title_original: High-throughput phenotypic screening of Medicines for Malaria Venture's Hit Generation Library 1 identifies new nematocidal chemotypes.
articles.authors: Taki AC, Byrne JJ, Chang BCH, Laleu B, Wells TNC, Jabbar A, Gasser RB
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Phenotypic screening of 139,916 compounds (98.7% of Medicines for Malaria Venture Hit Generation Library 1) against Haemonchus contortus larvae using infrared-based motility assays (mean Z' = 0.799). Identified 272 primary hits (0.194%) and 110 confirmed active compounds. Results: 39 compounds with IC50 < 10 μM, 33 causing complete developmental arrest at ≤12.5 μM; 4 compounds non-toxic to HepG2 cells (CC50 ≥20 μM); 16 candidates met stringent criteria for medicinal chemistry progression.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax gametocytes found in bone marrow with pancytopenia
articles.title_original: A mysterious foe in a case of pancytopenia with splenomegaly: Plasmodium vivax gametocytes in the bone marrow.
articles.authors: Patel GR, Singh V
articles.details: Clinical case report of Plasmodium vivax infection presenting as a mysterious case of pancytopenia with splenomegaly, featuring an unusual finding of P. vivax gametocytes in bone marrow. The case highlights a remarkable and atypical presentation of malaria with severe hematologic involvement.

articles.headline: Gut microbiome composition predicts Plasmodium parasitemia levels
articles.title_original: Gut check: converging evidence links microbiome to malaria risk.
articles.authors: Berry AA
articles.details: Convergent evidence from studies in rhesus macaques and human volunteers demonstrates that pre-infection microbiome composition predicts subsequent Plasmodium parasitemia levels. Bifidobacterium identified as a potentially protective bacterial genus. Findings suggest the gut microbiome is an underappreciated determinant of malaria parasite burden independent of host genetics and acquired immunity.

articles.headline: Var gene transcriptional switching mechanisms enable immune evasion
articles.title_original: Mechanistic insights into coordinated var transcriptional switching in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Visone JE, Florini F, Hadjimichael E, Patel V, Deitsch KW
articles.details: Mechanistic study of Plasmodium falciparum var gene family and PfEMP1 antigenic variation. Transient var2csa locus activation mediates var switching; non-coding RNAs from var2csa repress its transcription and affect switching frequencies. An upstream open reading frame in var2csa 5'-UTR destabilizes mRNA via nonsense-mediated RNA decay, promoting alternative var gene activation. These coordinated mechanisms enable chronic parasitemia and antibody evasion.

articles.headline: Single antimalarial drugs with multiple targets combat resistance
articles.title_original: Polypharmacology in malaria treatment: single drugs, multiple mechanisms, greater impact.
articles.authors: Milić M, Obi P, Vanderwal CD, Ben Mamoun C, Le Roch KG
articles.details: Review of polypharmacology strategy in antimalarial drug design: single compounds with multiple mechanisms achieving combination-therapy-like efficacy. Natural products (quinine, artemisinin) exemplify multitarget approaches reducing resistance likelihood compared to single-target agents. Discussion covers opportunities and challenges in developing next-generation antimalarials with polypharmacology to address rising drug-resistant Plasmodium strains and prolong therapeutic durability.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance markers detected in Brazilian Amazon parasites
articles.title_original: Emergence of artemisinin-based combination therapy resistance markers in Plasmodium falciparum from the Brazilian tri-border region of the Guiana Shield.
articles.authors: Salazar YEAR, Rezende AM, Puça MCSB, Louzada J, Mascarenhas MEP, Lagström S, Fletcher D, Oliveira-Ferreira J, Gil JP, de Sousa TN
articles.details: Targeted deep sequencing of 99 P. falciparum isolates from Roraima State, Brazil (2016–2020) examined pfcrt, pfmdr1, pfk13, and pfpm2/3 variants. Results: pfmdr1-NFD haplotype 6%, pfcrt-C350R 7%, pfpm2 amplification 5%, pfpm3 amplification 6%, pfpm2/3 hybrid 4%; no validated artemisinin-resistance pfk13 mutations detected. Variants associated with reduced susceptibility to lumefantrine and piperaquine. First report of pfpm2/3 hybrid and pfmdr1-NFD in Brazil.

articles.headline: Anopheles microbiome manipulation as complementary malaria control
articles.title_original: Exploring the gut micriobiome in Anopheles mosquitoes: A promising new approach to malaria control.
articles.authors: Minwuyelet A, Atenafu G
articles.details: Critical review of Anopheles gut microbiome as malaria control strategy, examining bacterial, fungal, and viral components and mechanisms: immune modulation, nutrient competition, direct antiparasitic activity, and interference with mosquito physiology. Significant caveats limit deployment: laboratory bias, oversimplification of microbial consortia, context-dependent outcomes, and ecological uncertainties. Review provides balanced framework for responsible translation of microbiome-based interventions while addressing field deployment challenges.

articles.headline: Two divergent VAR2CSA lineages in P. falciparum Thailand
articles.title_original: Two divergent VAR2CSA lineages with distinct evolutionary signatures circulate in Plasmodium falciparum from Thailand.
articles.authors: Chaikitgosiyakul S, Kuamsab N, Tia T, Putaporntip C, Jongwutiwes S
articles.details: Sequenced complete 9.1–9.9 kb var2csa genes from 31 P. falciparum isolates across five Thai provinces, identifying 24 distinct haplotypes with high genetic diversity (h=0.97±0.02). Analysis revealed two major circulating lineages: dominant 3D7-type and distinct Dd2-type (7 isolates), with extreme divergence at the 3′ end showing nucleotide diversity π=0.4253 vs 0.1033 (p<0.0001) and pronounced evolutionary split (Dxy=0.506, Da=0.444). Authors conclude this bipartite VAR2CSA variation is essential for developing broad-protection vaccines.

articles.headline: Novel bisquinoline antimalarials combat drug-resistant malaria
articles.title_original: Designing novel bisquinoline antimalarials from historical 4-aminoquinolines to combat drug-resistant malaria.
articles.authors: Handford MJ, Li Y, Riscoe T, Zhang X, Kelly JX, Riscoe MK
articles.details: Structure–activity relationship study optimized bisquinoline analogs as 4-aminoquinoline alternatives against drug-resistant P. falciparum. Compound 25 demonstrated potent in vitro activity (IC50<20 nM) against drug-sensitive and multidrug-resistant strains with improved metabolic stability (t₁/₂=121 min) and potent in vivo efficacy (ED₅₀=0.32 mg/kg/day), achieving complete curative protection at reduced dose versus ADC-028, though with moderately reduced oral bioavailability (F=43%, T₁/₂=27.2 h).

articles.headline: 5-ALA does not potentiate artemisinin against malaria
articles.title_original: 5-ALA does not potentiate dihydroartemisinin against Plasmodium falciparum malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Behrens HM, Henshall IG, Spielmann T
articles.details: Correspondence reporting that 5-aminolaevulinic acid (5-ALA), which potentiates artemisinin in cancer, does not enhance artemisinin activity against P. falciparum parasites. Authors conclude co-administration of artemisinin and 5-ALA is not a promising malaria treatment strategy.

articles.headline: Digestion genes in Anopheles aquasalis malaria vector
articles.title_original: Genomic profiling of digestion related enzymes in Anopheles aquasalis a major coastal neotropical malaria vector.
articles.authors: Alencar RM, Sepulveda CCP, D'Elia GMA, de Souza IB, Duarte APM, Santana RA, Nacif-Pimenta R, Godoy RSM, de Melo GC, Monteiro WM, Secundino NFC, Koerich LB, Pimenta PFP
articles.details: Identified 241 digestion-related genes in neotropical vector An. aquasalis through reciprocal BLAST and phylogenetic analysis: 56 carbohydrate-digestion, 51 lipid-digestion, and 134 protein-digestion genes. Genes were conserved with other anophelines and include targets for vector control (larvicidal toxins, enzymes) and transmission-blocking vaccines (aminopeptidase N1, carboxypeptidase B). Authors highlight these findings support vector-control research.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/3 deletions detected in 3.5% of malaria travellers
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum histidine rich protein 2/3 gene deletions affecting rapid diagnostic test performance in travellers.
articles.authors: Detzlhofer O, Lötsch F, Kirchner J, Schneider R, Weiss-Tessbach M, Winkler S, Walochnik J, Schubert L
articles.details: In a traveller cohort, pfhrp2/3 deletions occurred in 4 of 115 patients (3.5%) with no double deletions observed. Notably, 51.3% of patients had visited countries anticipated to exceed the WHO 5% pfhrp2/3 threshold by 2028. Authors recommend continued monitoring of pfhrp2/3 deletions in non-endemic settings to guide rapid diagnostic test strategies.

articles.headline: Estimated 491 annual P. falciparum infections in SSA refugees Sweden
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum infections and number needed to screen among refugees arriving from sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Bergstrand T, Wyss K, Eliasson I, Kaldhusdal V, Requena-Méndez A, Färnert A
articles.details: Modeled prevalence using Malaria Atlas Project data applied to 56,248 SSA refugees arriving in Sweden (2015–2022) to estimate asymptomatic P. falciparum infections. Results: 3,930 (7%) total infections including 1,789 children and 2,141 adults (851 women of reproductive age). Estimated 491 annual asymptomatic infections (range 356–546), with 80% from high/moderate-transmission countries; number needed to screen was 2–14 in these populations. Authors conclude these estimates can guide screening strategies.

articles.headline: ISTNet transformer architecture for malaria cell classification
articles.title_original: ISTNet: a multi-scale transformer-based architecture for malaria cell classification.
articles.authors: Wu P, Gao X, Yu H
articles.details: Multi-scale transformer-based neural network (ISTNet) designed for automated malaria cell classification. Deep learning architecture applies vision transformers to malaria microscopy image analysis.

articles.headline: Correction to cerebral malaria adhesion receptors study
articles.title_original: Correction to: Mapping the expression of endothelial adhesion receptors for Plasmodium falciparum-infected erythrocytes in fatal cerebral malaria in Malawian children.
articles.details: Erratum to prior study mapping expression of endothelial adhesion receptors for P. falciparum-infected erythrocytes in fatal cerebral malaria in Malawian children.

articles.headline: pfcrt/pfplasmepsin2 piperaquine-resistance tracking Southeast Asia 25 years
articles.title_original: Changing genetic profiles of Plasmodium falciparum piperaquine resistance in Southeast Asia over 25 years.
articles.authors: Srisutham S, Suwannasin K, Saejeng A, Khantikul N, Sugaram R, Phyo AP, Proux S, Pongvongsa T, Nguon C, Tripura R, Chau NH, Lek D, Dang Trung NH, Peto TJ, Callery JJ, van der Pluijm RW, Amaratunga C, Dhorda M, von Seidlein L, Mayxay M, Thuy-Nhien NT, Newton PN, Day NPJ, Ashley EA, Nosten FH, Smithuis FM, White NJ, Dondorp AM, Imwong M
articles.details: Analysis of 898 P. falciparum isolates from five Southeast Asian countries (1995–2023) tracking pfcrt mutations and pfplasmepsin2 copy-number variations linked to piperaquine resistance. Results show geographic and temporal variation: Cambodia pfplasmepsin2 amplification peaked at 88% in 2015 post-DHA-piperaquine introduction, followed by H97Y and G353V increases; Eastern Thailand and Vietnam showed rapid increases peaking 2017–2018. Over 65% of pfcrt-mutant parasites carried pfplasmepsin2 amplification; microsatellite analysis indicated selective sweeps.

articles.headline: Placental malaria infection impairs infant immune neurocognitive development
articles.title_original: Malaria in pregnancy at the frontline: a delicate balance.
articles.authors: Kokuhennadige V, Aitken E, Rogerson S
articles.details: Review of malaria in pregnancy pathogenesis, immunity, and offspring outcomes. Placental P. infection causes maternal morbidity and adverse birth outcomes; new evidence indicates antenatal infection impairs infant immune and neurocognitive development. While sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine improves birth weight despite parasite resistance, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, though more effective against parasitemia, does not improve birth outcomes alone or combined with SP. Authors recommend interventions targeting first-trimester infections and vaccines priming primigravidae.

articles.headline: Blood group B and α-globin deletion increase vivax susceptibility
articles.title_original: Genetic Determinants for Vivax Malaria Susceptibility in Chiplun (Ratnagiri), Maharashtra, India: A Case-Control Study.
articles.authors: Roshan S, Kanjaksha G, Arti S, Kanchan M, Jyoti J, Shivam S, Ajit G
articles.details: Case–control study of 228 P. vivax cases and 2,149 controls in Maharashtra, India examining genetic susceptibility factors. Blood group B+ was associated with increased malaria risk (OR=1.36, 95% CI: 1.02–1.80, p<0.05) while A+ was protective (OR=0.72, 95% CI: 0.52–0.99, p<0.05). Most notably, homozygous-α3·7 deletion showed significantly higher susceptibility (OR=3.33, 95% CI: 2.00–5.53, p<0.001), contradicting protective effects in other populations. CR1 polymorphisms and Rh phenotypes showed no significant associations.

articles.headline: CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing for antimalarial drug resistance Africa
articles.title_original: Innovative approach in malaria research: Harnessing CRISPR-Cas9 for antimalarial drug-resistance studies in Africa.
articles.authors: Ajibaye O, Dada IS, Mbye H, Nwankwo IC, Oriero E, Amambua-Ngwa A
articles.details: Comprehensive review of CRISPR-Cas9 system applications in antimalarial drug-resistance research across Africa. The technology aids elucidating resistance pathways, validating molecular markers, and enhancing diagnostics. A scoping review revealed significantly low adoption of CRISPR in Africa despite existing technological and regulatory barriers. Authors note strong potential for accelerating antimalarial drug discovery and advancing precision medicine; strategic application in malaria-endemic regions could bolster control and elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Monocyte and neutrophil ratios identify malaria in pregnant women
articles.title_original: The clinical significance of monocyte-to-lymphocyte and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratios in malaria infection and parasitaemia among pregnant women in Nanoro, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Traore O, Bihoun B, Koné FT, Lengane AL, Tahita MC, Kere AR, Bonko MDA, Compaore ZMJ, Dabone KCS, Yaro APK, Zango SH, Kaboré B, Kiemde F, Bouda I, Rouamba T, Ilboudo H, Natama HM, Sorgho H, Valéa I, Traoré-Coulibaly M, Belem AMG, Tinto H
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: This cross-sectional study of 270 third-trimester pregnant women in Burkina Faso examined inflammatory blood markers for malaria detection. Monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio (MLR) was significantly higher in malaria-infected women (0.48 vs 0.40, p=0.021) and in those with high parasitaemia (OR=7.16), with ROC values of 0.60-0.61. The authors conclude that MLR and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio may serve as supportive diagnostic markers to aid early risk stratification in antenatal settings.

articles.headline: Ferroptosis inhibitor alleviates experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Ferrostatin-1 alleviates experimental cerebral malaria by regulating immune cell functions and brain endothelial ferroptosis.
articles.authors: Yao S, Zhou X, Liao T, Yao C, Sun M, Gou H, Hu X, Liu J, Zheng L, Zhao Y, Cao Y
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Using a murine Plasmodium berghei ANKA cerebral malaria model, the authors found that iron metabolic dysfunction and ferroptosis occur in spleen and brain. Treatment with ferrostatin-1 decreased parasitemia, extended survival time, alleviated neurological symptoms, and improved blood-brain barrier integrity. The mechanism involved dual-axis regulation: enhanced dendritic cell antigen presentation and M1 macrophage polarization with increased Th1 responses, plus inhibition of ferroptosis in brain endothelial cells and reduced cerebral CD8+ T cell infiltration.

articles.headline: Alkaloids inhibit enzyme required for parasite transmission
articles.title_original: Computational evaluation of indole alkaloids as inhibitors of 3-hydroxykynurenine transaminase in malaria vector control.
articles.authors: Makaye AI, Kwiyukwa LP, Paul L, Banyikwa A, Vuai SAH
articles.details: This computational study evaluated indole alkaloids as inhibitors of 3-hydroxykynurenine transaminase (3-HKT), an enzyme essential for P. falciparum gametogenesis in Anopheles mosquitoes. Virtual screening and molecular dynamics simulations identified spiroindolone (EP4) and asperpenazine (SP4) as the most promising inhibitors with strong binding affinities (-7.38 and -7.21 kcal/mol) and stable interactions. The authors propose EP4 and SP4 as candidate compounds for anti-mosquito insecticide design pending in vitro and in vivo validation.

articles.headline: Genetic engineering targets parasite recognition in salivary glands
articles.title_original: Genetic control of malaria transmission: a salivary gland-centric perspective.
articles.authors: Eze S, Nguyen VT, Morvay A, Lee Y, Burini BC
articles.details: This perspective article examines the molecular mechanisms of Plasmodium sporozoite recognition and invasion of Anopheles salivary glands as a target for genetic vector control. The authors review multiple stages of parasite development within mosquitoes but highlight gaps in understanding the precise mechanisms of this critical step. They argue that advancing knowledge of sporozoite-salivary gland interactions will enable design of more effective transgenes for mosquito population replacement strategies.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria more common in wet season and males
articles.title_original: Longitudinal Assessment of Asymptomatic Malaria Infections in a Suburban Community in Lagos, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Owoloye A, Ligali FC, Musa AZ, Aina O, Idowu ET, Oyebola KM
articles.details: This surveillance study screened 1,860 asymptomatic individuals in Lagos using RDT, microscopy, and PCR, detecting P. falciparum in 2.7%, 8.8%, and 11.7% respectively. Asymptomatic infection was more frequent in the wet season (14.9%) with peak in June (36%), compared to dry season (7.2%), and was more common in males (12.9%) and school-age children (14.1%). The authors conclude that increased surveillance for asymptomatic malaria is needed nationwide to understand transmission reservoirs, particularly targeting younger individuals during high-transmission seasons.

articles.headline: Parasite enzymes essential for host lipid metabolism
articles.title_original: Defining the minimal enzymatic requirements for fatty acid scavenging from lysophosphatidylcholine by erythrocytic Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Liu J, Fike KR, Dalal S, Dapper C, Klemba M
articles.details: This study identified three parasite-encoded enzymes (XL2, XLH4, and PARE) required for Plasmodium falciparum to utilize host lysophosphatidylcholine as a source of choline and fatty acids. While loss of PARE alone had no phenotypic effect, simultaneous knockout of all three enzymes prevented parasites from scavenging lipids and abrogated replication in defined media with lysophosphatidylcholine as the sole fatty acid source. The findings establish that blood-stage P. falciparum is dependent on parasite-encoded enzymes for lipid catabolism.

articles.headline: FDA drugs computationally identified for cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Computational Drug Repositioning for Targeting PfEMP1: Potential Therapeutics for Cerebral Malaria in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Verma K, Patel K, Yadav A, Gopikrishnan M, Sharma R, Mohan M, Mani A, Bharti PK
articles.details: This computational study used virtual screening, molecular dynamics simulations, and ADME analysis to identify FDA-approved drugs targeting P. falciparum erythrocyte membrane protein 1 (PfEMP1), a key mediator of cerebral malaria pathophysiology through infected cell sequestration. Top candidates (Lumacaftor, Vilazodone, Tucatinib, Lenvatinib, Hydrocortisone Cypionate) showed favorable docking energies (-9.1 to -8.3 kcal/mol), oral bioavailability, and stable interactions confirmed by molecular dynamics. The authors propose these repurposed drugs as potential new therapeutic agents for cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Women show high acceptance of maternal malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: Assessing Readiness for Future Maternal Malaria Vaccines: Knowledge, Practices, and Vaccine Attitudes Among Women of Reproductive Age in Malawi.
articles.authors: Kaur M, D'Alessio F, Kajombo MC, Nkolokosa M, Olesen OF
articles.details: This facility-based cross-sectional survey in Malawi assessed knowledge, practices, and attitudes toward future maternal malaria vaccines among women of reproductive age. Although general malaria knowledge was high and 53.4% reported fever during pregnancy, vaccine acceptance was very high (97% willing to accept a future vaccine). Interestingly, higher knowledge was associated with lower vaccine acceptance in some districts, suggesting that context-specific delivery strategies will be important for effective vaccine introduction.

articles.headline: Humidity reshapes malaria vector thermal suitability
articles.title_original: Humidity shapes the thermal niche of Anopheles stephensi, an invasive malaria vector.
articles.authors: Johnson B, Huxley PJ, Brown JJ, Hollingsworth BD, Bump ER, Laurent BS, Skrotzki J, Johnson LR, Pascual M, Wimberly MC, Mohanty A, Murdock CC
articles.details: This experimental study examined how humidity modulates the thermal performance of Anopheles stephensi, an invasive malaria vector, across five humidity levels (30-90%) and seven temperatures (16-38°C). When incorporated into demographic models, humidity significantly altered population fitness projections compared to temperature-only models, suppressing growth in hot, arid regions while enhancing fitness in humid or high-elevation climates of South Asia and Africa. The authors conclude that multiple environmental drivers must be integrated into climatic suitability models to accurately predict vector distributions.

articles.headline: Acyl carrier protein essential for parasite organelle biogenesis
articles.title_original: Acyl Carrier Protein is Essential for Apicoplast Biogenesis in Malaria Parasites Independent of Fatty Acid Synthesis.
articles.authors: Geher SWR, Falekun S, Pita-Aquino JN, Swift RP, Okada M, Jami-Alahmadi Y, Wohlschlegel JA, Prigge ST, Sigala PA
articles.details: This study found that acyl carrier protein (ACP) is essential for blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum growth through an unexpected mechanism independent of its canonical role in fatty acid synthesis. Loss of ACP blocks apicoplast biogenesis and kills parasites by destabilizing pyruvate kinase II (PKII), the sole source of nucleoside triphosphates in the apicoplast organelle. The authors establish ACP as a critical hub in apicoplast metabolism and identify it as a potential therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Adaptive surveillance improves malaria vector monitoring
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the Entomological Adaptive Surveillance Framework for malaria vector monitoring: a comparative field trial with routine surveillance in Ghana and Mozambique.
articles.authors: Sedda L, Jalilian A, Opiyo M, Gowelo S, Marrenjo DJ, Atta-Obeng C, Boampong E, Oppong SK, Owusu-Akrofi O, Thomsen E, Tatarsky A, Malm KL, Candrinho B, Lobo NF
articles.details: This field trial compared an Entomological Adaptive Surveillance Framework (EASF) with routine surveillance in Ghana and Mozambique for monitoring Anopheles mosquitoes. The EASF strategically selects sampling locations using Bayesian spatiotemporal models and environmental criteria, outperforming routine surveillance by yielding higher mosquito catch rates, improved model predictions with lower uncertainty, and more efficient identification of informative sites. The authors conclude that adaptive surveillance offers a more precise approach enabling programs to dynamically respond to transmission systems.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminths cause haematological abnormalities in children
articles.title_original: Haematological responses in children under 5 years with malaria and soil-transmitted helminth coinfections in Delta State, Nigeria: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Odibo EO, Nmorsi OPG, Egwunyenga OA, Mgbere O
articles.details: This hospital-based cross-sectional study of 269 children under 5 in Nigeria examined haematological changes from malaria and soil-transmitted helminth (STH) coinfection. Children with either infection, especially coinfection, showed macrocytic hypochromic patterns (increased mean corpuscular volume, reduced mean corpuscular haemoglobin) with only mild reductions in hemoglobin and red blood cell counts. Host factors and hemoglobin genotype, rather than parasite density, were the primary drivers of haematological abnormalities, with the most severe derangement in sickle cell disease patients with coinfection.

articles.headline: High missed IPTp-SP opportunities among pregnant women in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Prevalence and inequalities of missed opportunities for intermittent preventive therapy (IPTp) of malaria among pregnant women: an analysis of 2018 Nigeria DHS.
articles.authors: Ladan SM, Lima NP, Orimadegun AE, Wado YD, Akinyemi JO, Jatau AA, Blumenberg C
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 2018 Nigeria DHS data examining 12,498 women aged 15-49. Among women with ≥4 antenatal care visits, 75% received <3 intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP) doses (42.5% of total sample). Worst-case scenario (<4 ANC visits and <3 doses): highest in oldest age group (44.7%), rural residents (50.4%), no formal education (61.6%), poorest wealth quintile (64.0%). Probability of worst-case was 75% for poorest uneducated women versus 5% for wealthiest educated women. Authors conclude high prevalence and socioeconomic inequality in IPTp-SP delivery require urgent policy attention.

articles.headline: Helminth coinfection reduces malaria parasitemia and inflammation in children
articles.title_original: Helminth coinfections mitigate clinical, parasitological, and immune outcomes in Mozambican children with malaria.
articles.authors: Cuamba I, Santano R, Grau-Pujol B, Vidal M, Aguilar R, Cossa A, Jairoce C, Mejia R, Nhabomba A, Muñoz J, Moncunill G, Dobaño C
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 441 malaria-symptomatic children aged 2-10 in Mozambique; 74 with malaria, of which 22% (16) coinfected with soil-transmitted helminths (STH). STH coinfection associated with lower P. falciparum density (P=0.005), lower fever (P=0.047), and reduced pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-8, TNF, IFNα, IP-10, MCP1, MIG, MIP1β, GM-CSF) and anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-5, IL-10) (P<0.05). Enhanced antibody responses to P. falciparum antigens SSP2, PTRAMP, MSP3, RH5, α-gal and elevated total IgE. Authors conclude STH coinfection associated with decreased parasitemia and enhanced antibody responses, suggesting milder malaria episodes.

articles.headline: Community health volunteers expand malaria diagnosis and treatment coverage
articles.title_original: Community health volunteers improve access to malaria case management in Siaya County, Kenya.
articles.authors: Odongo W, Seffren V, Obiet K, Seda B, Towett O, Zhou H, McDermott DP, Kariuki S, Staedke SG, Ter Kuile F, Kwambai TK, Samuels AM, Gutman JR
articles.details: Analysis of 1.4 million encounters by 936 community health volunteers (CHVs) and 86 health facilities across 713 villages in Siaya County, Kenya (2021-2023). CHV coverage adequate (≥1 per ≤500 people) in 82.5% of villages versus 30% for health facilities. CHVs managed 28.1% of encounters (12.1% of suspected, 15.6% of confirmed malaria cases). CHVs recorded higher test positivity rates (82.0% vs. 64.3% for health facilities). Incorporating CHV data increased malaria incidence estimates by 18% (467 to 549 per 1,000 population). Authors conclude CHVs extend access to diagnosis and treatment, particularly in areas with limited facility coverage.

articles.headline: Correct ITN use reduces malaria risk in Zambian children
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with malaria amongst under-five children in Senga Hill District, Northern Province, Zambia, a community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Shaba-Santu A, Moonga L, Ogata S, Hayashida K, Simuunza MC
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study of 216 under-five children in Senga Hill District, Zambia (November 2022-August 2023). Overall malaria prevalence 31% (95% CI 26-38%) by rapid diagnostic test. Correct ITN hanging reduced odds of malaria by 88% (OR=0.012, 95% CI 0.005-0.304). Having two ITNs and recent indoor residual spraying (IRS) reduced odds by 86% (OR=0.14, 95% CI 0.040-0.510) and 58% (OR=0.42, 95% CI 0.21-0.85) respectively. Male children had 204% increased odds (OR=3.04, 95% CI 1.52-6.07). Authors conclude high prevalence indicates need to strengthen ITN and IRS use.

articles.headline: Ivermectin increases mosquito mortality but shows unclear malaria reduction
articles.title_original: Efficacy of ivermectin for malaria vector control: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials.
articles.authors: Ramadan S, Talkhan A, Mansour A, Kanaan D, Abbas AN, Awad A, Sahel OB, Hamzeh LH, Alfaki A, Hany M, Elnouty M, Amin MHJ
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 10 randomized controlled trials involving 63,192 participants evaluating ivermectin for malaria vector control. Community-level analyses showed no significant reduction in malaria prevalence with very wide confidence intervals. Entomological trials demonstrated increased mosquito mortality following ivermectin exposure (RR 1.89; 95% CI 1.26-2.83) with substantial heterogeneity (I²>97%). Ivermectin was generally well tolerated with no significant increase in adverse events. Authors conclude current evidence does not demonstrate consistent reductions in malaria incidence or prevalence, though laboratory studies suggest mosquitocidal effects.

articles.headline: Guinea healthcare providers report gaps in malaria control implementation
articles.title_original: Healthcare providers' knowledge, perceptions and practices of malaria control strategies in the Republic of Guinea: insights from a qualitative study.
articles.authors: Diallo EM, Langlet A, Onyango LA, Kourouma M, Kambou BM, Diallo FB, Bufferne A, Petrova A, Deyra M, Gay C, Camara A, Gerbaud L
articles.details: Qualitative study (December 2022-March 2023) with 63 individual interviews and 18 focus groups of healthcare providers across 9 health districts in Guinea. Analysis identified 264 modalities grouped into 45 subthemes and 9 themes. Providers reported high knowledge of malaria but identified gaps in insecticide-treated net distribution/use, delayed seasonal malaria chemoprevention implementation, and non-compliant case management practices including overcharging, provider complacency, and over-reliance on clinical diagnosis. Authors conclude persistent operational barriers in ITN distribution, SMC implementation, and case management require sustained improvements and strengthened community-based initiatives.

articles.headline: 2La inversion shows ecological gradient in Nigerian mosquito populations
articles.title_original: Spatial distribution of the 2La chromosomal inversion in Anopheles gambiae populations across Nigeria: ecological associations and implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Adeogun AO, Izekor RT, Babalola AS, Oyeniyi TA, Adetunji O, Adesoye OA, Wahedi J, Busari LO, George A, Ajayi S, Chabi J, Awolola ST
articles.details: Study of 1200 Anopheles mosquitoes across 12 Nigerian states; 399 An. gambiae s.s. karyotyped for 2La chromosomal inversion. Distinct south-north cline observed: 2La/2La homokaryotype predominated in arid northern zones (Sahelian/Sudan savannas), 2La+/2La+ dominant in humid southern regions, heterokaryotype at moderate frequencies in transitional zones. Linear regression confirmed significant correlation between inversion frequency and latitude (R²=0.68-0.73, P<0.001). Allele frequencies conformed to Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium except Bayelsa and Kebbi States. Authors conclude 2La inversion linked to ecological adaptation with direct implications for region-specific vector control targeting.

articles.headline: Microfluidic device demonstrates flow-dependent parasite invasion in vitro
articles.title_original: Microfluidic platform for automatic quantification of malaria parasite invasion under physiological flow conditions.
articles.authors: Kals E, Kals M, Introini V, Vodenicharski B, Kotar J, Rayner JC, Cicuta P
articles.details: Experimental study developing a microfluidic device with four channels mimicking post-capillary venules at different flow velocities to study P. falciparum invasion of erythrocytes. Synchronized parasites injected and invasion/egress quantified using automated video analysis. Wild-type and transgenic P. falciparum lines tested, including those with genes encoding invasion proteins deleted. Deletion of erythrocyte binding antigen 175 (PfEBA175) significantly impacted invasion under flow (P<0.05) but not in static culture. Authors establish for the first time that flow conditions critically affect parasite invasion in a genotype-dependent manner.

articles.headline: Poly(I:C) and R848 adjuvants boost liver-stage malaria antigen responses
articles.title_original: Poly(I:C) and R848 adjuvants elicit sizeable humoral immunity to liver stage malaria antigens.
articles.authors: Tandel N, Thakkar M, Ahuja M, Shah J, Trivedi DD, Pied S, Singh AP, Prajapati C, Tyagi RK, Dalai SK
articles.details: Immunogenicity study in BALB/c and C57BL/6 mice testing PfCSP (P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein) and PbSLTRiP (P. berghei liver-stage antigen) formulated alone or with dual toll-like receptor adjuvants (Poly(I:C) and R848). Measured antibody kinetics 30-80 days post-immunization. Coadministration of adjuvants evoked greater antibody responses than antigen alone. PfCSP-specific IgG remained detectable through day 80 with gradual decline. PbSLTRiP produced progressive IgG increases with detectable IgG2c/IgG2a responses and modest avidity changes. Authors conclude dual adjuvant combination induced detectable antibody responses warranting further investigation.

articles.headline: Malaria-associated acute kidney injury drives mortality in endemic areas
articles.title_original: Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury: A Key Driver of Mortality in Endemic Regions.
articles.authors: Olwit G, Gutanamuka TB, Chris-Uchendu HC, Mugisha AI
articles.details: Focused literature review (2015-2025) synthesizing evidence on malaria-associated acute kidney injury (MAKI) across age groups and Plasmodium species. MAKI results from multifactorial process involving intravascular hemolysis, microvascular obstruction, inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and volume depletion. P. falciparum most common cause, though P. vivax and P. knowlesi increasingly recognized. MAKI associated with prolonged hospitalization, increased renal replacement therapy, and higher mortality. Evidence indicates survivors at risk of incomplete renal recovery and chronic kidney disease progression. Authors conclude MAKI is critical driver of acute mortality and long-term kidney disease requiring early recognition and post-recovery renal monitoring.

articles.headline: School-aged children harbor high asymptomatic P. falciparum prevalence
articles.title_original: School-aged children as a silent reservoir of Plasmodium falciparum: findings from a cross-sectional survey conducted in Burkina Faso in 2022.
articles.authors: Tarama CW, Soré H, Yade MS, Niyomwungere D, Débé S, Kinda R, Ganou A, Ménard D, Gansané A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey in 3 Burkina Faso districts (July 2022) enrolling 1,127 children aged 6 months-10 years. Asymptomatic P. falciparum prevalence 28.0% by rapid diagnostic test and 31.9% by microscopy (qPCR reference). School-aged children (5-10 years): 66.5% prevalence by microscopy versus 21.6% in <5 years. Gametocyte carriage 3.9% overall, twice as high in school-aged children (67 vs. 32 gametocytes/μL). Microscopy superior: sensitivity 76.7% vs. 67.7%, specificity 100% vs. 96.2%, PPV 100% vs. 83.8% compared to RDT. Authors conclude school-aged children are major asymptomatic reservoir warranting inclusion in surveillance and control strategies.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi malaria affects forest workers in Indonesian Aceh
articles.title_original: Socio-demographic characteristics and parasite density patterns of Plasmodium knowlesi Malaria in Aceh, Indonesia: insights from hospital-based data.
articles.authors: Maulana T, Marthoenis M, Almira CS, Moe S, Abas AL, Htay MNN
articles.details: Retrospective hospital-based study of 40 P. knowlesi patients (January 2023-April 2024) in Aceh Besar, Indonesia, confirmed by microscopy and PCR. Median age 31 years (IQR 25-43); 67.5% farmers/plantation workers, 10% orangutan caretakers. All presented with fever and headache; treated with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine and primaquine with complete recovery. Parasite density analysis (35 of 40): 42.9% high, 45.7% moderate, 11.4% low. Median hospital stay 3 days, with longer stays observed in patients with lower parasite density. Authors conclude P. knowlesi predominantly affects adult males in forest-related occupations, requiring targeted surveillance and context-specific interventions.

articles.headline: PfVFT1 antibodies inhibit malaria parasites through multiple immune mechanisms
articles.title_original: Vaccine-induced mouse antibodies targeting Plasmodium falciparum PfVFT antigen inhibit blood stages through multiple mechanisms.
articles.authors: Goh YS, Mao H, Hor PX, Loh CY, Chang ZW, Hermsen CC, Nosten F, Sauerwein RW, Rénia L
articles.details: This study identified PfVFT1 as a protective antigen by screening sera from controlled human malaria infection (CHMI)-protected individuals. Vaccine-induced mouse anti-PfVFT antibodies inhibited parasite reinvasion into red blood cells, promoted complement-mediated parasite lysis, and supported phagocytosis and antibody-dependent cellular inhibition. These findings demonstrate that PfVFT1-specific antibodies engage multiple effector mechanisms relevant to antimalarial immunity.

articles.headline: Plasmodium kinases regulate gametogenesis machinery for transmission control
articles.title_original: Correction: Divergent Plasmodium kinases drive MTOC, kinetochore, and axoneme organisation in male gametogenesis.
articles.authors: Yanase R, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJ, Markus R, Brady D, Bottrill AR, Holder AA, Guttery DS, Tewari R
articles.details: This study elucidates how Plasmodium kinases regulate the organization of microtubule organizing centers (MTOC), kinetochore, and axoneme structures during male gametogenesis. These kinases represent potential targets for transmission control through disruption of parasite sexual-stage development.

articles.headline: Malaria causes acute kidney injury in over half of African children
articles.title_original: A Study Using Point-of-Care Creatinine Testing in a Nigerian Primary Health Care Centre: Malaria as the Leading Cause of Acute Kidney Injury Particularly in Children.
articles.authors: Ugwem-Ikuru Y, Laurent-Ordu V, Emem-Chioma P, Erekosima I, Lewis D, Poulikakos D
articles.details: This prospective study at a Nigerian primary health facility found malaria in 61.1% of 424 patients (293/424) and 69.9% of 123 children. Acute kidney injury (KDIGO criteria) was diagnosed in 70/123 (56.9%) of children (stage 1: 20.3%, stage 2: 21.2%, stage 3: 15.4%), with 70% (49/70) of AKI cases associated with malaria. AKI prevalence was highest in children under 5 years and declined with age.

articles.headline: STAT1 deficiency improves survival and reduces anemia in malaria models
articles.title_original: Role of STAT1 in modulating the host immune response to Plasmodium yoelii 17XL-infected murine blood-stage malaria.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Du C, Xu Q, Chen Z, Feng X, Han S, Yan Y, Wang B, Lv Z, Zhu G, Cao J, Cheng Y
articles.details: This study identified STAT1 as a key upregulated protein in Plasmodium yoelii 17XL-infected murine spleens. STAT1 knockout enhanced mouse survival, substantially reduced peripheral parasitemia and splenic parasite burden, and decreased IFN-γ-producing CD8+ T cells and IL-10-producing CD4+ T cells at day 7. Notably, STAT1 deficiency increased hemoglobin and reticulocyte proportions, alleviating malarial anemia in infected mice.

articles.headline: Blood donation malaria positivity rises in India; transfusions under-monitored
articles.title_original: Transfusion-transmitted malaria in India: A national survey of epidemiology and testing practices.
articles.authors: Jacobs JW, Choudhury N, Arora S, Jindal A, Bhatnagar N, Basavarajegowda A, Shastry S, Chandra T, Daniel D, Drews SJ, Bloch EM
articles.details: This national survey of 262 Indian blood facilities found 97.7% (256/262) conducted routine malaria screening, primarily with rapid diagnostic tests (89.1%). Among 9.3 million donations tested 2020–2024, 1,231 were malaria-positive (13.27 per 100,000 units) with rising annual rates from 3.4 to 22.0 per 100,000. Only 3.1% of facilities reported transfusion-transmitted malaria cases, and less than half participated in external quality assessment (45.3%) or performed lookback investigations (33.6%).

articles.headline: PfAP2-V master transcription factor controls parasite proliferation and cytoadhesion
articles.title_original: The DNA-binding protein PfAP2-V regulates erythrocyte invasion and pathogenesis of the human malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Zhang Y, Wang C, Li Q, Li A, Zhang Y, Su Z, Yu L, Zheng K, Liu T, Jiang N, Zhang Y, Sang X, Feng Y, Chen R, Wei G, Zhang Q, Chen Q
articles.details: This study identified PfAP2-V as a master transcriptional regulator of virulence-associated genes in Plasmodium falciparum, including var genes encoding PfEMP1 cytoadherence proteins. Conditional knockdown decreased intraerythrocytic proliferation, caused surface topology abnormalities in infected red blood cells, and markedly reduced cytoadhesion to human endothelial receptors (CD36, ICAM-1, PECAM-1/CD31). PfAP2-V directly associates with chromosomal regions to facilitate chromatin interactions and transcriptional activation of pathogenic factors.

articles.headline: Tanzanian surveillance reveals vector behaviors and bednet coverage gaps by age
articles.title_original: Nationally representative programmatic surveillance of mosquito and human behaviours that influence human exposure to malaria transmission and the impact of vector control across Tanzania.
articles.authors: Baravuga ME, Michael P, Kihwele F, Masanja B, Mmbaga S, Mlalwe AT, Namango IH, McCallum AJ, Mirzai N, Mwalimu CD, Lazaro S, Killeen GF, Kiware S, Ferguson HM, Govella NJ
articles.details: This nationally representative survey across 25 Tanzanian sentinel districts over three years measured entomological and anthropological indicators through mosquito electrocuting traps and questionnaires. Anopheles arabiensis was widespread (64.9% outdoor-seeking), while A. gambiae s.s. and A. funestus s.s. were localized with different feeding preferences. Most people were outdoors 18:00–21:00 hours, with adolescent males showing lowest bednet use, and higher dry-season temperatures correlated with reduced bednet use.

articles.headline: Healthcare workers show high RDT accuracy in Benin but misrecord negatives
articles.title_original: The accuracy of recording malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) results in public health facilities in Benin; results from the MaCRA project.
articles.authors: Ahogni I, Avanon H, Hueha C, Kpemasse A, Aissan J, Affoukou C, Accrombessi M, Aponte JJ, Hilton E, Cooper S, Griffith K, Humes M, Lindblade KA, Ngufor C
articles.details: This study assessed malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) recording in 16 Benin facilities across 35,720 tests using digital RDT reader verification against an independent reference panel. Overall agreement was 94.3% (Cohen's kappa = 0.88), but 5.0% of results were misrecorded as positive versus 0.7% as negative. Accuracy was highest in children under 5 (96.7%) and lowest in patients over 15 (91.6%), with better performance among healthcare workers with ≥10 years experience and access to electricity and internet.

articles.headline: Microscopy misses 60% of malaria cases; PCR reveals higher prevalence
articles.title_original: Microscopy underestimates submicroscopic malaria infections in Ethiopia's southern rift valley: a community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Jima B, Wondale B, Eligo N, Wudineh F, Wegayehu T, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: This community-based cross-sectional study of 4,745 participants in Ethiopia compared microscopy and nested PCR for malaria detection. Microscopy detected 2.7% prevalence, but PCR confirmed only 67% (65/97) of these cases, yielding 1.4% PCR-corrected prevalence and revealing 6.1% submicroscopic infections in microscopy-negative samples. Species misclassification was common (P. falciparum 19.5% misidentified as P. vivax; P. vivax 14.0% as P. falciparum), with cumulative prevalence by PCR ranging 4.1–9.6% across sites.

articles.headline: PfVPS13L1 lipid transfer protein essential for parasite schizogony
articles.title_original: A bridge-like lipid transfer protein is critical for generation of invasive stages in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Guillén-Samander A, Perepelkina N, Horáčková V, Behrens HM, Rodriguez Cruz HO, Mesén-Ramírez JP, Ribeiro-Holbein A, Haberkant P, Stein F, Spielmann T
articles.details: This study identified PfVPS13L1, a bridge-like lipid transfer protein at ER-inner membrane complex (IMC) contact sites, as critical for malaria parasite invasive-stage formation. PfVPS13L1 mediates bulk lipid transfer from the ER to the nascent IMC during schizogony. Loss-of-function of PfVPS13L1 reduced IMC growth and produced smaller anucleated progeny with impaired schizogony, demonstrating its essential role in generating viable invasive stages.

articles.headline: Fungicide exposure reactivates chronic avian malaria infections
articles.title_original: Chronic exposure to a common azole fungicide is associated with reactivation of chronic Plasmodium infections in farmland birds.
articles.authors: Pigeault R, Bichet C, Bellot P, Brischoux F, Fritsch C, Angelier F
articles.details: This study exposed house sparrows to tebuconazole, a triazole fungicide commonly used in agriculture, during an 11-week period starting in late autumn. Among birds with baseline 70% chronic malaria infection, tebuconazole-exposed birds showed a 20% increase in prevalence while control birds showed the expected winter decline. No difference in parasite load was observed, suggesting tebuconazole promoted parasite reactivation from exo-erythrocytic stages rather than enhanced replication.

articles.headline: P. vivax Pv_LISP-2 induces naturally acquired antibodies in endemic populations
articles.title_original: Naturally acquired antibodies to Plasmodium vivax Pv_LISP-2, a potential liver stage vaccine antigen.
articles.authors: Donassolo RA, Soares da Veiga GT, Nisimura LM, Bittencourt NC, Salazar YEAR, Barros DKADS, Lopes SCP, de Sousa TN, Costa FTM, Albrecht L
articles.details: This study evaluated naturally acquired antibodies against Pv_LISP-2, a Plasmodium vivax liver-stage protein, in plasma from Brazilian Amazon populations exposed to vivax malaria. Pv_LISP-2 fragments were recognized by IgM and IgG antibodies, with acute-phase responses dominated by IgM followed by IgG induction, particularly against Pv_L.seq1. Predominant subclasses were cytophilic IgG1 and IgG3; IgM correlated with parasitemia and symptom duration, supporting further investigation of Pv_LISP-2 as a potential liver-stage vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: LLIN ownership 71.5% but usage 59.9%; barriers to use in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Lasting Insecticidal Net (LLIN) Ownership, Utilization, and Associated Barriers in Malaria-Endemic Communities of Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Waldetensai A, Tasew G, Yewhalaw D, Takie H, Gidey B, Kinde S, Gemechu F, Yirga S, Kinfe E, Hailemariam A, Tadesse H, Solomon H, Assefa G, Dilu D, Bashaye S, Wuletaw Y, Abdulatif B, Kebede T, Tadiwos S, Gebrewold G, Hailu S, Tesfaye F, Tollera G, Hailu M, Guiyun Y, Eukubay A, Gebresillassie A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 9,222 households (34,427 individuals) across Ethiopia, October 2024–January 2025. LLIN ownership was 71.5% (sufficient LLINs 58.3%), with utilization 59.9% overall (rural 72.7%, urban lower). Pregnant women (78.5%) and children <5 (67.2%) had highest usage. Major barriers included 60.5% lack of confidence hanging nets and 64.1% misconception that risk is seasonal. Overall utilization below 80% threshold for community-wide protection.

articles.headline: P. falciparum pfdhps A581G mutation emerging in Senegal, reaches 14.9% by 2023
articles.title_original: Emergence of Plasmodium falciparum pfdhps A581G mutation in Southern Senegal under Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention pressure, 2020-2023.
articles.authors: Diallo MA, Tine A, Sow D, Sene A, Gaye A, Ndiaye YD, Gomis J, Dieye B, Deme AB, Yade MS, Ngom B, Garba MN, Ndiaye IM, Diongue K, Badiane AS, Seck MC, Ndiaye M, Ndiaye D
articles.details: Dried-blood-spot samples from therapeutic-efficacy studies in two Senegal districts (Kolda, Kédougou), 2020–2023, analyzed using targeted amplicon deep sequencing. pfdhfr triple mutant (N51I+C59R+S108N) near-fixed (>90%); pfdhps A437G common (47–100%). The A581G mutation emerged in 2022 at 8.1% in Kolda, rising to 14.9% Kolda and 6.5% Kédougou by 2023. Frequencies similar across age groups ≤5 and 6–10 years. Growing A581G prevalence warrants monitoring despite continued clinical efficacy.

articles.headline: Young male labor migrants from Nepal at high risk of imported malaria
articles.title_original: Understanding imported malaria in western Nepal: Implications for malaria control and elimination.
articles.authors: Kandel S, Koirala M, Dahal G, Sah RK, Upadhayaya P, Jongcherdchootrakul K, Thitichai P
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study of 113 imported malaria cases in Nepal, October 2023–September 2024. All cases were male, majority aged 20–29 years (46%); 99.11% had traveled to India for work (96.47% to Maharashtra, 95.6% to South Mumbai). 71.43% worked in catering services; 85.84% lacked fixed housing. Only 56.64% received pre-travel malaria information; 59.3% unwilling to use LLINs citing unsuitable living conditions. Risk concentrated in well-defined urban hotspots.

articles.headline: Vector species composition depends on ecosystem integrity and available host blood sources
articles.title_original: Blood host preferences and competitive inter-species dynamics within an African malaria vector species complex inferred from signs of animal activity around aquatic larval habitats.
articles.authors: Walsh KA, Kavishe DR, Duggan LM, Tarimo LJ, Msoffe RV, Elisa M, Govella NJ, Eichhorn MP, Kaindoa EW, Butler F, Killeen GF
articles.details: Larval surveys across ecosystem gradient in southern Tanzania: domesticated settlements, partially encroached Wildlife Management Area, and Nyerere National Park. An. arabiensis dominated domesticated areas (positively associated with human/cattle activity), while An. quadriannulatus dominated intact ecosystems (associated with impala, warthog, possibly bushpig as likely blood hosts). Self-sustaining An. arabiensis populations found >40 km inside protected areas, supported by unidentified wild hosts. Such refuge populations beyond reach of insecticidal interventions may confound elimination efforts but enable resistance management strategies.

articles.headline: CYP2D6 genetic variants predict primaquine failure risk in vivax malaria patients
articles.title_original: Reduced cytochrome P-450 (CYP) 2D6 activity and Plasmodium vivax malaria risk in Amazonians: A retrospective, population-based cohort study.
articles.authors: Corder RM, Puça MCSB, Prete CA, Ladeia WA, Rodrigues PT, Johansen IC, de Sousa TN, Ferreira MU
articles.details: Retrospective cohort study of 466 P. vivax–infected participants in Brazil; analyzed time-to-event data for recurrence within 6 months post-chloroquine/primaquine treatment (3.5 mg/kg total). Null/low CYP2D6 activity (activity score ≤0.25) had 2.22-fold increased recurrence risk vs activity score >1.0 (p=0.004). Intermediate activity (0.5–1.0) showed no increased risk. Community-wide incidence (997 Amazonians, 26.6 vivax cases/100 person-years, 5-year follow-up) did not differ significantly by CYP2D6 activity.

articles.headline: Federated Vision Transformers achieve 98.15% accuracy for privacy-preserving malaria detection
articles.title_original: FedMal-XAI: an explainable federated vision transformer leveraging knowledge distillation for privacy-preserving malaria detection.
articles.authors: Bhuiyan TA, Rahman A, Khan FI, Noori FM, Masum AKM
articles.details: Federated learning system using Vision Transformers (ViTs) for automated malaria detection from blood smear segmented red blood cell images, addressing diagnostic subjectivity, logistical complexity, and data privacy. ViT-B/16 achieved 98.15% accuracy; FedDistill-DeiT 97.79%; FedAvg-Swin-T 97.75%; FedDistill-Swin-T ROC-AUC 0.9977. Federated ViT models outperformed centralized CNN ensemble (ResNet50+VGG16), delivering reliable, scalable, interpretable malaria screening with privacy guarantees.

articles.headline: Heat shock protein Gp96 biomarker elevated in severe malaria; novel drug target
articles.title_original: Heat shock protein Gp96 (Grp94) in malaria: functional insights at the host-parasite interface and therapeutic perspectives.
articles.authors: Djoumoi D, Diouara AAM, Diop M, Nguer CM, Mbengue B, Thiam F
articles.details: Review of host heat shock protein Gp96 (endoplasmic reticulum-resident Hsp90 paralog) in malaria pathogenesis and immunity. Elevated circulating Gp96 associated with severe malaria phenotypes, functioning as danger-associated molecular pattern (DAMP), serving as potential biomarker of host stress and immune dysregulation. P. falciparum expresses parasite homolog PfGp96 with conserved Hsp90 domain architecture and parasite-specific adaptations. Biological functions, client repertoire, and drug-target validity of PfGp96 remain poorly defined.

articles.headline: PfNCR1 protein localized to membrane contact sites required for cholesterol homeostasis
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Niemann-Pick Type C1-Related protein relies on its physicochemical properties for membrane contact site localization required for cholesterol homeostasis.
articles.authors: Ray A, Istvan ES, Goldberg DE, Garten M
articles.details: Mechanistic study of P. falciparum PfNCR1 protein localizing to host-parasite membrane contact sites (~3–4 nm gap). A 141-amino acid helix-linker-helix (HLH) domain unique to Plasmodium spp. is necessary for efficient localization to narrow contact sites via physicochemical properties. GPI-anchoring isolated HLH domain or human ATG3 amphipathic helix sufficient for targeting fluorescent protein to endogenous PfNCR1 regions. Degree of PfNCR1 localization qualitatively correlates with ability to maintain cholesterol homeostasis.

articles.headline: High vivax genetic diversity and clonal hypnozoite activation drive relapses in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: High genetic diversity, clonal activation of hypnozoites and relapse of Plasmodium vivax isolates in low-transmission setting of Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Getachew H, Zhong D, Habtamu K, Abossie A, Demissew A, Tsegaye A, Degefa T, Wang C, Zhou G, Lee MC, Kazura JW, King CL, Yewhalaw D, Yan G
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 370 blood samples from 215 individuals in Ethiopian sugarcane plantations; pvmsp1 gene amplicon deep sequencing. High haplotype diversity (67 unique haplotypes, Hd=0.799, nucleotide diversity π=0.044), low multiplicity of infection (MOI=1.4), 34.6% polyclonal infections. Among 82 patients with 1–5 recurrent episodes, paired analysis showed 81.3% genetic homology with 55.6% identical alleles, indicating most relapses from clonal hypnozoite activation. Diverse genotypes introduced by migrant workers sustain transmission.

articles.headline: qPCR detects silent malaria infections missed by microscopy and RDTs in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Uncovering silent malaria reservoirs in Southern Ethiopia: community-based molecular detection of asymptomatic and sub-microscopic infections and their implications for diagnosis and control.
articles.authors: Bogale A, Tefera S, Taye Gebremeskele B, Kassie A, Dube SJ, Tekleselassie T, Gemechu T, Abebe AA
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study of 525 participants in Gedeo Zone, Ethiopia, September–December 2024. qPCR detected substantially higher asymptomatic infection prevalence (31.2%) versus microscopy (17.5%) and RDT (13.1%); sub-microscopic infections 16.6% (P. vivax 7.9%, P. falciparum 7.2%, mixed 1.6%). Sub-microscopic infections more common in ages 15–24 years and females. qPCR superior sensitivity reveals undetected transmission reservoir missed by symptom-based strategies and conventional diagnostics.

articles.headline: Pfmdr1 Y184F mutation prevalence rising sharply in Ethiopia: 91% by 2025
articles.title_original: Incidence of kelch13 and Pfmdr1 gene mutations associated with antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Yeshanew TM, Brhane BG, Begashew BA, Bayisa GW, Birhane N
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 15 studies (11,269 samples, 5,521 P. falciparum cases) in Ethiopia evaluating kelch13 and Pfmdr1 gene mutations. Pooled kelch13 prevalence: R622I 11%, A675V 1%, P441L 0%. Pooled Pfmdr1 prevalence: D1246Y 6%, Y184F 77%. Y184F mutation increased significantly over time: 66% (2007–2014) to 91% (2015–2025). Most resistance markers rare/absent; however, Y184F rise indicates growing antimalarial drug resistance concern.

articles.headline: Cameroon malaria vaccine rollout achieves 56% first-dose but 28% third-dose coverage
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the first malaria vaccine introduction: ongoing learning opportunities during the introduction into routine immunisation in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Josue Kedakse TSN, Kaba M, Baonga SF, Mboussou F, Njoh AA, Amani A, Efouba G, Kouontchou Mimbe JC, Njinkeu A, Yohou KS, Nembot Mekoueng R, Takamiya M, Wirngo M, Hedje J, Jalloh MF, Walldorf JA, Habimana P, Impouma B
articles.details: Mini-postintroduction evaluation of malaria vaccine rollout in Cameroon across 42 health districts in April 2024. First-dose coverage 56%; second-dose 31%; third-dose 28%; all regions experienced high drop-out rates. Among caregivers, 88% aware of vaccine introduction (60% informed by health workers), 81% cited vaccine protection as main reason to vaccinate. 60% knew importance of continuing LLIN use post-vaccination. Mini-PIE methodology recommended for other countries.

articles.headline: Household economic burden of malaria in Indonesia
articles.title_original: The household economic burden of human-only and zoonotic malaria, compared to other causes of acute febrile illness in Indonesia.
articles.authors: Abraham P, Lubis IND, Novivanti R, Trianty L, Nisa FA, Kariodimedjo P, Amalia R, Cahyadi RAD, Mossadeq Al Qorny T, Perlambang YA, Permatasari R, Surendra H, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ, Devine A
articles.details: Health facility-based cross-sectional survey of 2244 patients in Indonesia (153 malaria cases: 97 P. vivax, 35 P. knowlesi, 12 P. malariae, 3 P. falciparum, 1 P. ovale, 5 mixed) quantified household costs. Mean total cost: US$33 (SD=57) for malaria vs. US$17 (SD=38) for non-malarial febrile illness, with 16% of malaria patients experiencing catastrophic expenditure. Indirect costs (lost productivity) represented 70% of malaria costs, indicating need for economic considerations in malaria control policy.

articles.headline: Occupation and activity timing linked to malaria risk
articles.title_original: The Effect of Head of Household Occupation and Activity at Sunrise and Sunset on Malaria Prevalence in Sussundenga, Mozambique.
articles.authors: Jorgenson B, Earland DE, Bibe AF, Novela A, Muhiro V, Ferrão JL, Searle KM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 297 individuals in 84 households in Mozambique examined malaria infection associations with head of household occupation and sunrise/sunset activity. Outdoor vs. indoor occupation showed minimal difference (0.015, 95% CI −0.12–0.15). Interaction analysis revealed greater malaria prevalence in those with outdoor-occupation heads active at sunrise/sunset (0.33, 95% CI 0.037–0.62), suggesting combined exposures may modify risk.

articles.headline: Anopheles darlingi genomics reveal population structure and insecticide selection
articles.title_original: Population genomics of Anopheles darlingi, the principal South American malaria vector mosquito.
articles.authors: Tennessen JA, Brosula R, Chabanol E, Bickersmith S, Early AM, Laws M, Kelley KA, Grillet ME, Gamboa D, Lucas ER, Duchemin JB, Quiñones ML, Sallum MAM, Bergo ES, Moreno JE, Nagi S, Arisco NJ, Sooklall M, Niles-Robin R, Castro MC, Cox H, Gendrin M, Conn JE, Neafsey DE
articles.details: Whole-genome analysis of 1094 Anopheles darlingi from six South American countries demonstrated deep geographic population structure, high genetic diversity with 13 putative segregating inversions, and absence of cryptic sympatric species. Strong selection signals on cytochrome P450 genes, likely driven by insecticide exposure, indicate ongoing vector adaptation challenges for malaria elimination in Latin America.

articles.headline: Parasite TRAg7 drives inflammation via macrophage receptor CD71
articles.title_original: Interaction of Plasmodium yoelii tryptophan-rich antigen 7 with CD71 on macrophage membrane regulates host inflammatory response.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Chen Z, Du C, Lei Y, Li J, Zhang H, Huang X, Wang B, Zuo S, Lv Z, Cao J, Han S, Cheng Y
articles.details: Experimental study showed Plasmodium yoelii tryptophan-rich antigen 7 (TRAg7) binds macrophage CD71 receptor, activating NF-κB p65 signaling to promote proinflammatory cytokine production. Genetic deletion of TRAg7 in mice decreased parasitemia, reduced inflammation, and improved survival, revealing a parasite mechanism of immunopathology and identifying a potential therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Host SNARE proteins hijacked for Plasmodium liver-stage survival
articles.title_original: Host SNARE Proteins Mediate Lysosome and PVM Fusion to Support Plasmodium Liver Infection.
articles.authors: Atchou K, Kramer N, Bindschedler A, Schmuckli-Maurer J, Caldelari R, Heussler VT
articles.details: CRISPR/Cas9 knockout study in HeLa cells infected with Plasmodium berghei identified SNARE proteins VAMP7, VAMP8, Vti1B, and Stx7 recruited to the parasitophorous vacuole membrane at distinct timepoints. VAMP7-Vti1B and VAMP7-VAMP8 double knockouts significantly reduced parasite growth, with Vti1B dominant; Vti1B inhibition impairs lysosome-PVM fusion without host toxicity, suggesting potential as host-directed antimalarial therapy.

articles.headline: G6PD deficiency heterogeneous across Indonesia for primaquine treatment
articles.title_original: G6PD deficiency in Indonesia: a systematic review and update of prevalence and variant maps in the context of malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Sadhewa A, Panggalo LV, Nanine I, Fadilah I, Hutagalung J, Kenangalem E, Limardi PC, Malik SG, Noviyanti R, Pasaribu AP, Poespoprodjo JR, Prameswari HD, Price RN, Puspadewi RT, Setiadi W, Trianty L, Sudoyo H, Sutanto I, Syafruddin D, Kevin Baird J, Ley B, Elyazar IRF, Satyagraha AW
articles.details: Systematic review of 45 studies (1964–2024) found heterogeneous G6PD deficiency prevalences across Indonesia: 0.0–19.9% in males (<30% activity, n=23,166) and 0.8–44.6% in females with deficient/intermediate activity (n=6,729); 15 class B variants identified. Routine G6PD screening is recommended for high-daily-dose primaquine treatment for P. vivax elimination, though comprehensive data remain lacking in high-endemicity areas.

articles.headline: Anopheles abundance and species distribution in urban Accra
articles.title_original: Urban malaria vector dynamics in Accra, Ghana.
articles.authors: Mohammed Sabtiu AR, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Owusu-Asenso CM, Abdulai A, Sraku IK, Halou DK, Doe RT, Boadu EN, Mensah SKE, Azumah JD, Adu-Barima S, Awotwe LE, Walker A, Yanney SA, Ebuako AA, Eyeson NAS, Appiah AA, Obeng BC, Amoateng G, Danquah GA, Abusah NE, Agbotah EE, Kwarteng RO, Boateng J, Hinne IA, Appiah-Kwarteng C, Forson AO, Aboagye-Antwi F, Afrane YA
articles.details: Entomological survey in Accra over dry and rainy seasons (2022–2024) collected 20,945 host-seeking and 1,613 resting Anopheles mosquitoes across 15 sites. An. gambiae s.s. dominated host-seeking populations; An. coluzzii dominated resting populations. Abundance was highest in irrigation and peri-urban sites, lowest in low-socioeconomic areas, identifying irrigation and peri-urban areas as transmission hotspots requiring targeted control.

articles.headline: IL-10 suppresses parasite control independent of IFN-γ
articles.title_original: Early enhanced control of Plasmodium yoelii infection in IL-10-deficient mice is independent of IFN-γ, IL-12, and the humoral response.
articles.authors: Jones MD, O'Neal KA, Zeltner SL, Gouch AR, Stumhofer JS
articles.details: In P. yoelii-infected mice, IL-10 deficiency led to lower parasitemia compared to wild-type, despite increased TH1 polarization and IL-12/IFN-γ production. Loss of IL-10 reduced germinal center B cells and plasmablasts, but B cells were not critical for early parasite control. IL10−/− mice lacking IFN-γ and B cells still limited parasite expansion, indicating IL-10 suppresses host-protective pathways independent of these mechanisms.

articles.headline: R21/Matrix-M catch-up campaigns and boosters avert cases
articles.title_original: Public health impact of catch-up vaccination or additional booster doses with pre-erythrocytic malaria vaccine R21/Matrix-M: a modelling study.
articles.authors: McCain K, Topazian HM, Challenger JD, Okell L, Winskill P, Ghani AC
articles.details: Stochastic transmission modeling of R21/Matrix-M vaccine evaluated catch-up campaigns (ages 6 months–14 years) and booster strategies across transmission intensities. In moderate-high transmission (45% PfPR2-10), catch-up targeting 6-month–2-year children averted 358 uncomplicated cases per 1000 doses (95% CrI 113–570); 5-year booster averted 835 clinical cases per 1000 doses (95% CrI 605–1274). Benefits varied by transmission setting and seasonality.

articles.headline: Malaria outbreak frequency declining in India with hotspots
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of malaria outbreak years in India provides key insights into malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Yadav CP, Hussain SSA, Tarique ST, Maheshwari V, Rahi M, Sharma A
articles.details: Analysis of malaria outbreak years in India (2011–2020) using case counts exceeding 3-year moving average + 2.5×moving standard deviation identified 402 outbreak years across districts. Peak outbreaks in 2011, 2014, and 2015 were followed by sharp decline, reaching lowest numbers in 2020. Seven states (Mizoram, Tripura, Jharkhand, Odisha, Madhya Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Meghalaya) identified as outbreak-prone, with >70% of districts experiencing ≥1 outbreak year.

articles.headline: Host macrophages eliminate transmission-blocking bacteria for parasites
articles.title_original: Host macrophages/monocytes promote malaria transmission by modulating mosquito microbiota via SR-A-mediated phagocytosis.
articles.authors: He B, Li M, Guo S, Fang J, Fan Y, Tan N, Liu T, Li J, Zhu F, Zhang J, Xu W
articles.details: Study demonstrated macrophages and monocytes enhance malaria transmission by suppressing transmission-blocking bacteria (E. anophelis, S. sonnei, K. oxytoca) in mosquito midguts via scavenger receptor A (SR-A) phagocytosis. Depletion of macrophages/monocytes or anti-SR-A antibodies enhanced anti-Pfs25 monoclonal antibody efficacy, achieving complete transmission blockade in P. berghei-pfs25. These findings reveal parasite exploitation of host immunity for transmission and suggest anti-SR-A as adjunct to transmission-blocking strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria-dengue co-infections documented in Papua with genotyping
articles.title_original: Co-infections of malaria and dengue in Timika, a highly endemic malaria area in Central Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Rana B, Trianty L, Santoso MS, Puspitasari AM, Nisa FA, Amalia R, Carlier L, Tetteh K, Noviyanti R, Sasmono RT
articles.details: Screening of 69 malaria-positive patients in Timika, Papua identified 3 co-infections with dengue virus (4.3%): 1 with DENV-2 and 2 with DENV-4. Malaria parasites were P. vivax, P. falciparum, and P. malariae. Whole-genome sequencing of DENV-4 revealed Genotype II closely related to Makassar strains. Findings highlight malaria-dengue co-infection challenges and support importance of combined molecular surveillance in endemic areas.

articles.headline: MDA effectiveness generalizes to non-trial communes with environmental variation
articles.title_original: Mapping the local effectiveness of mass drug administration for malaria using transportability methods.
articles.authors: Roh ME, Tong Y, Heitmann GB, Jia J, Ba EKC, Ndiaye JL, Fogelson A, Milligan P, Seck A, Diallo A, Lo AC, Baiocchi M, Gosling R, Bennett A, Hsiang MS, Benjamin-Chung J
articles.details: Cluster RCT in Senegal showed MDA reduced malaria incidence by 55% in seasonal chemoprevention areas. Machine learning transportability models estimated 36-65% incidence reductions across 74 non-trial communes, varying by precipitation, vegetation, and temperature. MDA showed no post-intervention effectiveness.

articles.headline: Plant extract shows promise as environmentally friendly mosquito control
articles.title_original: Eco-friendly mosquito control: Assessing the larvicidal and repellent effects of Dorema aucheri extract on Anopheles stephensi, the malaria vector.
articles.authors: Hosseinpour A, Vahedi M, Hamedi A, Soltanian N, Kazemi F, Rezaee E, Soltani A
articles.details: Hydroalcoholic extract of Dorema aucheri demonstrated dose-dependent larvicidal activity against A. stephensi with 98% mortality at 400 mg/L (LC50: 98.54 mg/L green section, 104.72 mg/L white section). Repellency reached 90% at 0.5 mg/cm2 (green) with complete protection lasting 52-64 minutes.

articles.headline: Human behavior critically mediates malaria intervention effectiveness
articles.title_original: Investigating the impact of human behavioural factors on the effectiveness of malaria interventions in malaria-endemic regions: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Agbo LN, Orish VN, Husaini DC, Madaki PD, Orisakwe OE
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Systematic review of 59 studies (2000-2025, 85% low bias, 83% sub-Saharan Africa) on behavioral factors and malaria interventions identified key barriers: ITN ownership-use gaps from discomfort/misconceptions, healthcare-seeking delays from cost/distrust, reliance on informal providers. Concludes intervention success requires locally tailored approaches addressing behavioral and experiential barriers.

articles.headline: Anti-GPI antibodies prevent cerebral malaria lethality in mouse models
articles.title_original: Anti-GPI scFv as a Promising Tool for Intervention Against Cerebral Malaria in Mice.
articles.authors: Klein SG, Mota KCP, Borges BC, Vieira MS, Neves MM, Mendes LS, Ferreira FB, de Lima IL, Santos FAA, Bastos LM, Lima WR, Goulart Filho LR, Vieira da Silva M
articles.details: In C57BL/6J mice infected with P. berghei-ANKA, anti-GPI scFv administered days 0, 3, 6 post-infection prevented lethality in 71.4% of animals and promoted weight recovery. Treatment inhibited neurological and systemic signs, reduced parasitemia, and improved hematological and histopathological parameters in brain, lungs, and kidneys.

articles.headline: Educational frameworks support Plasmodium knowlesi prevention in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Educational frameworks for zoonotic malaria prevention: a narrative review integrating behavioural theory, participatory design, and instructional models for Plasmodium knowlesi in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Hijazi MHA, Jeffree MS, Woon FC, Jelip J, Avoi R, Ahmed K
articles.details: Narrative review synthesizing educational interventions for malaria prevention shows approaches consistently improve knowledge and preventive practices when grounded in behavioral theory. Proposes integrated framework combining health belief model, community-based participatory research, and instructional design for P. knowlesi prevention, addressing forest-edge work, land-use change, and local inequities.

articles.headline: Systematic review reveals gaps in SSA malaria epidemiological models
articles.title_original: A systematic review of epidemiological models for malaria transmission in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Adegoke TM, Nji AM, Silal SP, Ali IM, Wondeu ALD, Madeleine PF, Sylvanie MT, Tah CF, Antonio-Nkondjio C, Bigoga JD, Leke RFG, Stresman G, Cui L, Oladoja OM, Adegoke GK, Obisesan KO, Mbacham WF
articles.details: Systematic review (PRISMA guidelines) of 102 malaria modeling studies in sub-Saharan Africa found 52.9% transmission models, 21.6% intervention models, 9.8% optimal control models, 15.7% cost-effectiveness models. Key gaps identified: limited drug/insecticide resistance incorporation, migration dynamics, climate variability. Recommends improved model alignment with policy priorities.

articles.headline: Acute liver failure from Plasmodium falciparum in non-endemic region
articles.title_original: Acute Liver Failure Due to Plasmodium falciparum: A Rare Case from a Non-Endemic Country.
articles.authors: Batıbay E, Yüksekyayla O, Polat M
articles.details: Case report of acute liver failure caused by P. falciparum infection in a non-endemic country. Limited details available without full abstract; represents rare hepatic manifestation of malaria.

articles.headline: CLIP protein essential for Plasmodium merozoite and sporozoite invasion
articles.title_original: The CLAMP-Linked Invasion Protein (CLIP) plays an essential role in Plasmodium berghei zoites.
articles.authors: Unhale T, Das S, Marinach C, Briquet S, Franetich JF, Boeykens L, Sterckx YG, Silvie O
articles.details: CRISPR-based conditional genome editing in P. berghei shows CLIP (CLAMP-Linked Invasion Protein) deletion impairs blood-stage parasite growth and blocks merozoite erythrocyte invasion. In transmission stages, CLIP-deficient sporozoites show reduced invasion of mosquito salivary glands and hepatocytes with impaired cell traversal and gliding motility.

articles.headline: Plasmodium knowlesi genomics reveals severity mechanisms independent of burden
articles.title_original: Integrative genomics of Plasmodium knowlesi reveals parasite-intrinsic regulators of severe human malaria.
articles.authors: Westaway JAF, Benavente ED, Kucharski M, Nayak S, Huy DTQ, Auburn S, William T, Rajahram GS, Piera KA, Trimarsanto H, Hoon KS, Bourke C, Jantim A, Manah AM, Gotulis W, Moon RW, Amato R, Barber BE, Drakeley C, Anstey NM, Field MA, Bozdech Z, Grigg MJ
