articles.details: Integrative GWAS, transcriptomic, and eQTL analysis of clinical P. knowlesi isolates identified parasite transcriptional programs associated with WHO-defined severe disease including stress-response and host-interaction pathway activation. Genetic regulation of immune-evasion variant antigen families (SICAvar, kir) and chromatin regulators. Severe malaria driven by genetically regulated parasite programs not fully explained by parasite burden.

articles.headline: Malaria control sustainability and decolonization in historical Palestine
articles.title_original: The Zionist decolonisation of rural malaria control in Palestine from 1922 onwards - what it teaches.
articles.authors: Alexander A
articles.details: Historical account of malaria control in Palestine 1918 onwards. After British Army ceased control in September 1918, disease returned. Zionists developed first sustainable malaria control program for entire country, established as early decolonized approach implemented by and for all inhabitants, preceding modern community-based control models.

articles.headline: Odorant-binding protein mediates host-seeking behavior in malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Functional characterization of AsOBP21f, an odorant-binding protein involved in human odor detection in the malaria vector Anopheles sinensis.
articles.authors: Li F, Xu O, Zhou J, He S, Chen B, He Z
articles.details: AsOBP21f odorant-binding protein from A. sinensis preferentially expressed in olfactory tissues, shows selectivity for hydrophobic C10-C15 molecules with strong binding to methyl tridecanoate (attractive), dodecanal (repellent), decanal, pentadecanoic acid. RNAi silencing reduced antennal response to host odors and significantly decreased blood-feeding success.

articles.headline: China-Africa network advances collaborative malaria control and elimination
articles.title_original: The Institution-based Network on China-Africa Cooperation for Malaria Elimination: fostering a transcontinental hub for strengthening China-Africa cooperation on malaria control and elimination efforts.
articles.authors: Lu S, Ding W, Huang L, Liu L, Xiao N, Abdulla S, Okpeku M, Thiongane A, Gibba B, Kompaore SCB, Lv S, Li S, Weldedawit MA, Zhou XN, Wang D
articles.details: Institution-based Network on China-Africa Cooperation for Malaria Elimination (INCAM) established 2018, convened six collaborative fora 2019-2025 to promote knowledge exchange on malaria control. Leverages WHO-certified Chinese malaria-free status to support African control efforts through product innovation, research collaboration, and evidence-based policy development.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence rising in Central Ethiopia, 2019–2024
articles.title_original: A six years trend analysis of malaria prevalence in Central Ethiopia Region.
articles.authors: Teshome Z, Animut A, Negash Y, Giday M, Aklilu E
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 368,490 suspected cases from 12 districts and 2 towns in Central Ethiopia (2019–2024) found 86,604 Plasmodium positive cases (23.5%), with 61% P. falciparum and 39% P. vivax. Cases showed an increasing trend since 2019, peaking in May (15.9%), autumn (31.5%), and spring (29.5%); prevalence and species distribution differed significantly across districts, months, and seasons. Authors conclude sustained intervention scale-up is needed.

articles.headline: Angiotensin II for P. falciparum refractory septic shock
articles.title_original: Angiotensin II use in refractory septic shock due to Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Meconi T, Pazzi M, Papa R, Ruggiano P, Conti D
articles.details: No abstract available; title suggests clinical management of septic shock complications in severe P. falciparum malaria using angiotensin II.

articles.headline: Malaria Prophylaxis
articles.title_original: Malaria Prophylaxis.
articles.authors: Stachler L, DeVos E, Samandari T
articles.details: No abstract available; title indicates focus on malaria prevention strategies.

articles.headline: PfHP1 phase separation controls var gene silencing in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Phase separation of heterochromatin protein 1 regulates virulence gene expression in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Mamatharani DV, Purkayastha D, Niederwieser I, Rai SK, Gaur P, Ganji M, Mukhopadhyay S, Voss TS, Karmodiya K
articles.details: PfHP1 (heterochromatin protein 1) undergoes phase separation in vitro and compacts AT-rich DNA, tunable by RNA and PfH2A.Z. Phase-separation-defective mutants show dispersed nuclear localization, altered chromatin binding, and derepression of up to 54 of 60 var genes. Hi-C analysis reveals loss of heterochromatic interactions, establishing PfHP1 phase separation as a key mechanism for var gene silencing and antigenic variation.

articles.headline: Anopheles pseudopunctipennis peaks oviposition at dusk in Mexico
articles.title_original: Daily pattern of oviposition and fecundity in Anopheles pseudopunctipennis Theobald, the main vector of malaria in Mexico.
articles.authors: Villarreal-Treviño C, González-Cerón L, Ríos-Delgado JC, Penilla-Navarro RP
articles.details: Study in Tapachula, Chiapas characterized oviposition patterns in nulliparous and parous females under insectary and semi-field conditions. Both groups exhibited primary peak between 18:00–20:00 hours post-sunset; nulliparous females ceased oviposition after midnight, while parous females continued until 6:00 a.m. Parous females laid more eggs than nulliparous females. Authors recommend pyrethroid insecticide application from dusk until midnight for vector control.

articles.headline: Entomological outcomes in malaria vector control trials lack standardization
articles.title_original: A systematic review of entomological outcomes and sampling approaches used in the evaluation of cluster randomised controlled trials for malaria vector control products.
articles.authors: Githu V, Michael P, Biggs J, Cook J, Kiware S, McConnachie A, Johnson P, Ferguson HM
articles.details: Systematic review of 62 cluster randomized controlled trials of malaria vector control (1992–2021, 74% in Africa) identified 13 different entomological outcomes and 12 sampling methods with high variation. 70% of trials were rated high-risk due to lack of power analyses and inadequate randomization of sampling points. Only the number of entomological clusters significantly predicted outcome precision. Authors recommend standardized reporting guidelines for entomological trial components.

articles.headline: Data for cultural history of malaria in Hungary
articles.title_original: [Data for cultural history of malaria in Hungary].
articles.authors: Kiss L
articles.details: No abstract available; title indicates historical data on malaria in Hungary.

articles.headline: Indole peptidomimetics potent against drug-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Indole Peptidomimetics Show Potent and Selective Activity against Drug-Resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Januário MAP, Valdes TA, Maluf SEC, Mendes GR, Bonatto V, Moura IMR, Mourão PS, Corrêa AG, Guido RVC
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship analysis of indole-based peptidomimetics identified LSPN954 (IC50 1.7 μM vs 3D7, CC50 ≥100 μM, SI=59) and LSPN959 (IC50 1.0 μM vs 3D7, CC50 ≥100 μM, SI=95) as lead compounds. Both showed slow-acting profile, additive effects with artesunate, and retained efficacy against resistant strains (Dd2, K1, Dd2R_DSM265, 3D7R_MMV848) with no cross-resistance. Compounds show low cytotoxicity on human cells.

articles.headline: P. falciparum vaccine development: recent advances and challenges
articles.title_original: Recent Advances, Bottlenecks, and Future Directions in Plasmodium falciparum Vaccine Development.
articles.authors: Turan G, Boggio MJ, Othman AS, Nnaemeka V, Hill AVS, Salman AM
articles.details: Review article citing 282 million malaria cases and 610,000 deaths in 2024, disproportionately affecting children <5 years and pregnant women in sub-Saharan Africa. Discusses how complex Plasmodium lifecycle hampers vaccine development, emphasizing challenges in identifying protective antigens and optimal formulations. Highlights vaccines' potential for reducing morbidity, mortality, and transmission through durable protective immunity.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine with boosters averts 21.8% severe malaria in Benin
articles.title_original: Assessing the potential impact of the RTS,S/ASO1 vaccine on malaria burden in Benin: a mathematical modelling approach.
articles.authors: Gbaguidi GJ, Loum MA, Gning LD, Tahiho AK, Diallo EM, Koutou O, Dembélé A, Topanou N, Ketoh GK
articles.details: Mathematical modeling study (EMOD) parameterized with entomological, epidemiological, and climatic data from Benin (2010–2023) evaluated RTS,S/AS01 vaccine via EPI strategy. Scenario with 90% coverage and 2 boosters (Scenario 9) achieved highest impact: 9.8% PfPR averted, 11.06% cases averted, and 21.83% severe cases averted by 2030. Higher coverage and booster doses showed sustained decline in malaria incidence through 2030.

articles.headline: Placental P. falciparum malaria common in Eastern Uganda pregnant women
articles.title_original: Prevalence of placental Plasmodium falciparum malaria and associated factors among pregnant women in health facilities in Eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: Rosset AA, Gerald B, Hussein M, Doreen N, Wahabu WA, Rudolf B, Hope M, Bagenda CN, Edgar K, Moses O
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 180 pregnant women (mean age 23.2±5.8 years) in Mayuge district, Uganda. Placental malaria prevalence: 11.67% (95% CI 7.71–17.28%) by microscopy and 23.89% (95% CI 18.19–30.71%) by qPCR. Age 15–19 years (aOR 5.87, p=0.023) and no antenatal care attendance (aOR 4.27, p=0.011) significantly increased odds of placental malaria. Authors recommend improved ANC attendance awareness.

articles.headline: P. ovale asymptomatic carriage frequently recurs in coastal Tanzania
articles.title_original: Frequent Plasmodium ovale recurrence in coastal Tanzania.
articles.authors: Carey-Ewend K, Shepard H, Yang G, Odas M, Peter EE, Dominick MC, Rutha I, Amagai K, Vasquez-Martinez B, Mulei LA, Chhetri S, Kharabora O, Adams JH, Lin FC, Bailey J, Edwards JK, Gower EW, Juliano JJ, Ngasala BE, Lin JT
articles.details: Longitudinal study of 74 asymptomatic adults and children >5 years in Bagamoyo, Tanzania with baseline Po parasitemia by PCR, followed up to 6 months with biweekly testing. Only 26% (19/74) had persistent Po to week 4. 55% (41/74) developed recurrent Po at median 10 weeks [IQR 4–12 weeks]; 9 had second recurrence. Microsatellite genotyping showed most pairs were heterologous recurrence. Risk appeared to increase in dry season prior to rainy season.

articles.headline: P. vivax and P. simium host shifts reveal zoonotic risks
articles.title_original: Genomic Insights Into Host Shifts Between Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium simium in Latin America.
articles.authors: Lefebvre MJM, Degrugillier F, Arnathau C, González C, Rondón S, Link A, Chaves A, Benavides JA, Campos AAS, Dos Santos E, Huff R, da Silva CMD, Vanderhoeven E, De Thoisy B, Fontaine MC, Prugnolle F, Rougeron V
articles.details: Screened 719 monkey samples from five Latin American countries, identifying 23 Plasmodium-positives with four yielding reliable genomic data. Analysis of 19 P. simium and 405 P. vivax isolates revealed recent genetic exchanges between species; P. simium originated from host jump approximately 1-2 centuries ago. Genome-wide scans detected positive selection signals in genes involved in primate and mosquito interactions (PvRBP2a, PvRBP1b, PvCMRP1, PvPAT, Pvs47), indicating host adaptation and highlighting zoonotic risks requiring inclusion of monkeys in malaria prevention.

articles.headline: Fish oil preserves gut but not lungs in P. berghei infection
articles.title_original: Differential Effects of DHA-Rich Fish Oil Supplementation on Intestinal and Pulmonary Alterations in Experimental Malaria Caused by Plasmodium berghei NK65.
articles.authors: Pereira LPMC, Silva-Rodrigues JH, Pinheiro FM, Machado LF, da Silva ACF, Marques CB, Gomes MT, Gonçalves FMM, Bezerra-Bellei JC, Brum G, Dias VC, Hipólito JR, Bastos AN, Nascimento HLDS, E Silva FMC, D'Avila H, Neto AF, Reis Junior JL, Rocha VN, Ribeiro-Gomes FL, Castro JMA, Scopel KKG
articles.details: Examined DHA-rich fish oil supplementation effects on P. berghei NK65-infected C57BL/6 mice. Despite partial preservation of intestinal morphology and reduced intestinal shortening, supplementation did not translate into improved pulmonary outcomes. Results underscore strain- and pathogenesis-dependent effects of omega-3 supplementation, highlighting the importance of considering diversity among Plasmodium strains when investigating nutritional or immunomodulatory interventions.

articles.headline: 134 hospitalized malaria patients in Madagascar: high co-infection burden
articles.title_original: Clinical Characteristics and Outcomes of Hospitalized Malaria Patients in Rural Madagascar.
articles.authors: Kasprowicz D, Korzeniewski K, Wilczyńska W
articles.details: Three-year retrospective study (2023-2025) of 134 patients at rural district hospital; median age 15, 52.2% female. P. falciparum predominated (94.0%), with 20.1% severe malaria including 10.4% cerebral malaria. Co-infections were frequent (52.2%), most commonly Schistosoma haematobium (14.2%) and typhoid fever (12.7%). IV artesunate initiated in 97.8%; median hospital stay 2 days, 12.7% prolonged (≥5 days). Prolonged stay significantly associated with cerebral malaria, parasitemia ≥5%, blood transfusion, and age <15 years (all p≤0.034).

articles.headline: Floodable vegetation and microclimate drive malaria vector biting in Amazon
articles.title_original: Reframing vector control: land use and climate shape malaria risk in Amazonian communities.
articles.authors: Acosta AL, Bergo ES, Figueira EAG, Conn JE, Sallum MAM
articles.details: Measured Nyssorhynchus darlingi human biting rate (HBR) at 70 sites across four Amazonian ecoregions. Recorded 14,689 mosquito bites across 384 events. HBR was highest in areas with intermediate forest degradation, positively associated with temperature, and negatively with humidity. Floodable vegetation was the strongest land cover predictor at 500-m scale. Intrazonal variation exceeded interzonal differences, suggesting local environmental conditions strongly influence vector activity and supporting climate-sensitive, spatially targeted vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Multiple malaria control interventions reduce severe malaria and parasitemia
articles.title_original: Progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria among children in settings receiving different combinations of malaria control interventions in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Okek EJ, Jehopio J, Sande OJ, Musinguzi B, Awor S, Asio J, Le DK, Lutwama J, Alison AK, Bongomin F, Ocan M, Kayondo J
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 12 studies involving 9,283,160 individuals. No significant difference in progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria between settings receiving different intervention combinations (OR 0.56, 95% CI 0.31-1.02, p=0.06). However, simultaneous deployment of multiple control interventions significantly reduced severe malaria (OR 0.41, 95% CI 0.30-0.56, p<0.001), parasitemia (OR 0.52, 95% CI 0.38-0.70, p<0.001), and gametocytes (OR 0.41, 95% CI 0.28-0.60, p<0.001). Single interventions offered no additional protection against progression compared to no interventions.

articles.headline: Discarded malaria RDTs reveal DENV-3 circulation in Haiti, 2023
articles.title_original: Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.
articles.authors: Matulis GA, Smith HP, Hall G, Katich RS, Delp KL, Douglas CE, Williams J, Hirsch S, Kumar R, Pshea-Smith I, Lilak AA, Okech BA, Ricks K, Stefan CP, Existe A, Kugelman JR, Sutherland I, Dunford J, Boncy J, von Fricken ME, Koehler JW
articles.details: Leveraged nucleic acids in discarded malaria RDTs collected 2021-2023 from febrile patients in two Haitian departments for arbovirus surveillance. Of 73 flavivirus-positive/indeterminate samples, 68 tested positive for DENV-3 by quadraplex assay, all from 2023. NGS and phylogenetic analysis showed high sequence similarity to Caribbean sequences from 2022-2023. No samples tested positive for alphavirus (0/517) or Oropouche virus (0/293). Discarded RDTs represent a context-specific tool for remote arbovirus surveillance and characterization amid public health disruptions.

articles.headline: Malaria parasitemia associated with elevated liver enzymes and dyslipidemia
articles.title_original: Malaria parasitemia and its association with liver function parameters, and lipid profile among malaria-infected adult patients in western Ethiopia: A comparative cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Checkole N, Cheneke W, Ibrahim T, Bekele S, Teklay B, Tefera M, Gebretsadik A, Fikadu B, Kassahun GB
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 302 participants (151 malaria patients, 151 controls) in Ethiopia, December 2021-March 2022. Majority of malaria patients (70.86%) infected with P. falciparum with parasitemia distribution: 30.46% low, 46.36% moderate, 23.18% high. Mean aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, alkaline phosphatase, and serum bilirubin were significantly higher in malaria patients than controls (p<0.001). Total cholesterol, HDL, and LDL cholesterol were significantly lower in malaria patients (p<0.05), indicating malaria-associated liver dysfunction and dyslipidemia.

articles.headline: In silico bispecific antibody designed to block Plasmodium invasion
articles.title_original: Computational epitope discovery and in silico design of a bispecific antibody model (COMED hook) to block Plasmodium invasion into erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Köseoğlu AE, Gül N, Duran T, Öztürk C, Hekim N
articles.details: Computational study identifying conserved B-cell epitopes on P. falciparum and human erythrocytes to design bispecific antibody (COMED hook) blocking erythrocyte invasion. Analysis identified 123 antigenic P. falciparum epitopes (58 conserved), 92 erythrocyte epitopes (12 selected as host-targeting domains). Two highly conserved Plasmodium epitopes (KHVETWTQRVQNM, LHSNFYIKRF) showed favorable binding profiles. Designed antibodies exhibited computationally favorable sequence and docking profiles compatible with COMED hook construction, generating testable predictions for experimental validation.

articles.headline: Flow cytometry quantifies non-fluorescent Plasmodium liver-stage infection
articles.title_original: Flow cytometry-based evaluation of hepatic infection by non-fluorescent Plasmodium parasites.
articles.authors: Teixeira B, Nunes-Cabaço H, Mota MM, Fontinha D, Prudêncio M
articles.details: Developed novel flow cytometry method for quantitative assessment of hepatic cell infection by non-fluorescent Plasmodium parasites using parasite proteins HSP70 (cytoplasm marker) and UIS4 (parasitophorous vacuole marker). Method enables rapid, cost-effective quantification of hepatic infection and intracellular parasite development. Addresses regulatory and technical challenges for evaluating reporter-free whole-sporozoite vaccine candidates and serves as versatile tool for malaria research.

articles.headline: Thalassemia shows protective trend against malaria but lacks statistical significance
articles.title_original: Exploring the protective effects of thalassemia against malaria in Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Donkor AB, Nyarko SB, Asare KK, Asa-Atiemo CKM, Kusi KA, Afrane YA, Amoah LE
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 African studies (2013-2025) examining thalassemia protection against malaria. Eight studies provided data for meta-analysis. Thalassemic individuals showed lower odds (OR 0.856) of developing malaria than non-thalassemic individuals, but association was not statistically significant with substantial heterogeneity. Alpha thalassemia mechanistically reduces cytoadhesion and rosette formation and enhances immune clearance, with favorable Hp2-1 haptoglobin phenotype interactions. Further research needed to elucidate molecular pathways.

articles.headline: Fosmidomycin-clindamycin-artesunate: PK reveals clindamycin underdosing in children
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of fosmidomycin and clindamycin in combination with artesunate for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Gabonese children and adults.
articles.authors: Pfaffendorf C, Dejon-Agobé JC, Edoa JR, Maïga-Ascofaré O, Ahenkan E, Adegnika AA, Ramharter M, Wicha SG, Mischlinger J
articles.details: Open-label RCT in Gabon of 40 patients (ages 3-57) with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria receiving oral artesunate (2 mg/kg), fosmidomycin (30 mg/kg), and clindamycin (10 mg/kg) every 12 hours for three days. Population PK modeling revealed one-compartment models with first-order absorption for both drugs. Body weight significantly influenced clearance and volume of distribution; body temperature affected fosmidomycin clearance. Fosmidomycin weight-based dosing achieved consistent exposure across age groups, but clindamycin produced substantially lower exposure in children <12 years. Modeling suggests clindamycin 12 mg/kg for children <35 kg would equalize exposure across ages.

articles.headline: MERISCREEN Pf HRP-II RDT shows 94.8% accuracy in Ghana field evaluation
articles.title_original: Evaluation of malaria rapid diagnostic test kit performance in a high-transmission setting: a facility-based cross-sectional study in rural Ghana.
articles.authors: Boadu S, Axame WK, Abanga WA, Saeed FU, Adjei MR, Kubio C
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study of 404 children under 5 in six health facilities in Ghana (July 2024) comparing MERISCREEN Malaria Pf HRP-II RDT performance against microscopy. Sensitivity 98.1% (95% CI 92.6-99.7), specificity 92.6% (95% CI 88.9-95.2), positive predictive value 82.4% (95% CI 75.8-87.5), negative predictive value 99.1% (95% CI 96.6-99.8), overall accuracy 94.8%. Real-world performance marginally lower than manufacturer-reported values, but results deemed reliable for guiding treatment in areas lacking microscopy if storage guidelines and user training are maintained.

articles.headline: NxTek™ and Biocredit RDTs outperform standard malaria tests
articles.title_original: Evaluation of NxTek™ Eliminate Malaria-PF test and biocredit rapid diagnostic tests for detecting Plasmodium species in Southern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Bogale A, Tefera S, Taye Gebremeskele B, Mitiku A, Degefa T
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 384 febrile subjects in Southern Ethiopia (September–November 2024). Biocredit Pf/Pv RDT demonstrated 97.9% sensitivity and 97.4% specificity for P. falciparum and 94.5% sensitivity and 97.5% specificity for P. vivax (vs. microscopy). NxTek™ achieved 87.6% sensitivity and 91.3% specificity for P. falciparum; SD Bioline yielded only 51.3% sensitivity for P. falciparum. Biocredit RDTs met WHO diagnostic criteria and outperformed conventional tests.

articles.headline: Urban malaria in Gabon shows heterogeneous transmission with shifting hotspots
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal heterogeneity of urban malaria in Libreville, Gabon (2012-2023): neighborhood-level hotspot analysis for micro-stratified control.
articles.authors: Mohamed-Djawad MH, Moutombi Ditombi BC, Ndong Ngomo JM, Mbang Nguema OA, Agbanrin AA, Pongui Ngondza B, Kono HN, Nymane T, Ombago J, Migueba V, James LAJ, Moussavou Mabicka DA, Mihindou CJ, Ndong Akomezoghe L, Ndong Mouity JT, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Bouyou-Akotet MK, M'Bondoukwé NP
articles.details: Analysis of 9,507 children in Libreville, Gabon (January 2012–December 2023) showed Plasmodium falciparum prevalence varying by area: semi-rural 50.9% (95% CI 47.9–54.0), semi-urban 46.5% (95% CI 43.8–49.3), shanty-town 44.6% (95% CI 42.6–46.6), and urban 38.0% (95% CI 33.1–43.2) in 2023. WHO classification shifted from mostly mesoendemic in 2015 to 41.5% hyperendemic neighborhoods in 2023. Spatial hotspot analysis identified clusters that changed over time, supporting micro-stratified control strategies.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance, new antimalarial candidates, and diagnostic innovations
articles.title_original: Advancing global malaria control: therapeutic strategies, drug discovery, and formulation innovations.
articles.authors: Sarma K, Sarma H, Deka J
articles.details: This review discusses ongoing challenges in malaria control, including rising artemisinin partial resistance associated with Pfkelch13 mutations. It highlights new drug candidates in development (Z439, KAF156, MMV371, MMV055/167, INE963, GSK701) and optimized artemisinin-based combinations as emerging treatment strategies. The review emphasizes the importance of new drug regimens due to partial artemisinin resistance and improvements in diagnostic tools from microscopy to PCR and drug formulation innovations.

articles.headline: Microbiota-barrier-innate immunity framework for hierarchical malaria control
articles.title_original: Reinventing malaria control: hierarchical intervention strategies for African settings based on the microbiota-barrier-innate immunity axis.
articles.authors: Zhu Y, Wu S, Qi Y, Wang J
articles.details: This review proposes a unified 'microbiota-barrier-innate immunity' axis linking gut microbiota, host innate immunity, and barrier integrity in malaria pathogenesis. Plasmodium infection disrupts this axis, causing microbiota imbalance, metabolic dysfunction, and intestinal barrier leakage. The authors present a three-tier intervention pyramid (foundational nutritional regulation, targeted microbial intervention, precision bioengineering) tailored to African settings with limited resources, offering an implementable approach addressing drug resistance and vaccine limitations.

articles.headline: K13 P441L artemisinin resistance detected during Ethiopia 2024 malaria resurgence
articles.title_original: Recurrent parasitemias with artemisinin partial resistance mutations during the 2024 Ethiopia malaria resurgence: a case series.
articles.authors: Geleta D, Brhane BG, Abera A, Belachew M, Sime H, Gebreegziaxher A, Getu M, Ali A, Tesfaye N, Habtetsion M, Kokobe B, Kebede M, Worku Z, Tasew G, Tadesse G, Tollera G, Fola AA, Bailey JA, Juliano JJ, Parr JB, Abte M, Assefa A
articles.details: Case series of 15 patients with confirmed recurrent Plasmodium falciparum malaria in central Ethiopia. Genotyping for drug resistance markers using PfSMARRTer sequencing identified K13 P441L (WHO-candidate artemisinin-resistance marker) in 3/15 (20%) patients. Other antimalarial drug resistance markers (pfcrt, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, pfdhps) were largely fixed in the population, with evidence of clonal expansion. Findings underscore the need for strengthened molecular surveillance integrated with routine case monitoring.

articles.headline: High-density peptide array maps 283 immunogenic P. vivax antigens
articles.title_original: Comprehensive characterization of Plasmodium vivax antigens using a high-density peptide array.
articles.authors: Asawa RR, Hazzard B, Tebben K, Tan J, Cantaert T, Berry A, Tolia N, Popovici J, Serre D
articles.details: High-density peptide array with 4.2 million peptides covering all Plasmodium vivax genes was used to characterize antibody responses in infected and malaria-naïve individuals. Researchers identified 283 commonly immunogenic proteins in symptomatic individuals, including proteins involved in erythrocyte invasion, nucleoporins, and many uncharacterized proteins. Notably, asymptomatic individuals showed a unique antibody response pattern against PIR proteins potentially associated with protection against clinical vivax malaria, providing a comprehensive resource for vaccine and diagnostic development.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria caused by Plasmodium vivax; full recovery achieved
articles.title_original: Cerebral malaria caused by Plasmodium vivax in an afrodescendant adult in Colombia: case report.
articles.authors: Sastre-Martínez AD, Gutiérrez MAG, Vallejo-Serna RA, Muñoz-Lombo JP
articles.details: Case report of a 43-year-old woman from southwestern Colombia with cerebral malaria caused by Plasmodium vivax (confirmed by thick blood smear). Patient was treated with intravenous artesunate followed by chloroquine and primaquine, achieving full recovery. Although P. falciparum traditionally causes cerebral malaria, P. vivax can also cause it through mechanical and inflammatory mechanisms, underscoring the importance of early parenteral artesunate and intensive supportive care.

articles.headline: Abbott RDT sensitivity varies by transmission setting; misses PCR cases
articles.title_original: Field and laboratory evaluation of Abbott-Bioline™ Malaria Ag Pf/Pv RDT performance in a high-transmission setting: contrasting results with a low-endemic area.
articles.authors: Fenomanana J, Ravoavy A, Rakotoarisoa M, Platon L, Wade A, Chala B, Tibiri YNG, Muller A, Thiebaut L, Caspar E, Strubel PE, Ménard D, Ratsimbasoa A
articles.details: Laboratory evaluation of four Abbott-Bioline™ Malaria Ag Pf/Pv RDT lots showed inter-lot variability with detection failures between 97–373 parasites/µL. Field evaluation in Madagascar of 218 febrile patients (70.2% prevalence by PCR) found Abbott-Bioline™ achieved 99.1% sensitivity and 93.7% specificity against microscopy, but only 73.2% sensitivity against PCR. Parascreen® RDT showed similar results. Both tests missed approximately 25% of PCR-positive infections, demonstrating highly context-dependent performance.

articles.headline: How plants synthesize artemisinin, crucial antimalarial drug
articles.title_original: Botanical mystery solved: how plants make a crucial malaria drug.
articles.authors: Thompson B
articles.details: No abstract provided. Based on the title, this article explains how plants synthesize artemisinin, the crucial antimalarial drug that forms the basis for artemisinin-based combination therapies used worldwide as first-line treatment for malaria.

articles.headline: Malaria coinfection increases egg shedding in nematode-infected mice
articles.title_original: Coinfection with malaria alters the fecundity and within-host persistence of an intestinal nematode.
articles.authors: Bourbon L, Dusuel A, Groetz E, Rialland M, Roche B, Faivre B, Sorci G
articles.details: Experimental study in mice coinfected with Plasmodium yoelii and intestinal nematode Heligmosomoides polygyrus. Coinfected hosts excreted more worm eggs than single-infected controls, reflecting plastic adjustment of worm fecundity modulated by host Th2 immunity. Coinfection inhibited IL-4, IL-13, and GATA-3+ CD4+ T cell responses. Extended patent period with cumulative egg excretion through day 99 post-infection, suggesting coinfection enhances nematode transmission potential.

articles.headline: Targeting Plasmodium falciparum purine salvage pathway for antimalarial development
articles.title_original: Targeting Plasmodium falciparum with purine antimetabolites as a therapeutic strategy.
articles.authors: Tashie W, de Koning HP, Duah-Quashie NO, Quashie NB
articles.details: This review examines P. falciparum's exclusive dependence on the purine salvage pathway, lacking de novo purine biosynthesis. Plasmodium falciparum Equilibrium Nucleoside Transporters (Pf ENTs) are highlighted as potential conduits for purine-analog antimalarials. Purine analogs that efficiently enter parasites through Pf ENTs and undergo selective activation can disrupt purine metabolism and nucleic acid synthesis. The review discusses the framework for target-based design of purine-analog therapies to address rising antimalarial resistance.

articles.headline: K13 C580Y confers artemisinin tolerance in Ethiopian Plasmodium falciparum
articles.title_original: Functional validation of the Plasmodium falciparum K13 C580Y mutation in recently collected Ethiopian isolates.
articles.authors: Mukherjee A, Assefa AB, Turlo CV, Needham LC, Shoue D, Qahash T, Belachew M, Tadesse D, Kassie E, Berihun M, Brhane BG, Parr JB, Ferdig MT, Adane S, Tesfaye A, Zuromski J, Fola AA, Tasew G, Tollera G, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA
articles.details: Functional validation study introducing K13 C580Y mutation into recently collected Ethiopian Plasmodium falciparum isolates using CRISPR-Cas9. Ring-stage survival assays with dihydroartemisinin (DHA) demonstrated significantly elevated DHA survival in edited parasites relative to isogenic controls, establishing that C580Y confers artemisinin tolerance in contemporary Ethiopian genetic backgrounds. This provides causal evidence supporting genomic surveillance findings and validates C580Y as a functional artemisinin-resistance marker.

articles.headline: P. vivax serology screening for hypnozoite detection in falciparum patients
articles.title_original: Predicting risk of Plasmodium vivax microscopy-detected episodes using serological markers in patients with Plasmodium falciparum malaria: a multi-country diagnostic performance evaluation.
articles.authors: Hafidzah M, Degaga TS, Christian M, Alam MS, Hossain MS, Baird JK, Sutano I, Smith L, Argyropoulos D, Amelia AR, Noviyanti R, Robinson LJ, Price RN, Mueller I, Thriemer K, Longley RJ
articles.details: Multi-country study (Ethiopia, Indonesia, Bangladesh) evaluating serological markers to predict P. vivax infection risk in 244 P. falciparum patients over 63 days. IgG antibodies showed 82% sensitivity but only 27% specificity; algorithm re-training improved specificity to 68% at cost of reducing sensitivity to 68%. Results suggest limited utility for guiding radical cure based on microscopy-detected episodes, though markers may support implementation through community engagement strategies.

articles.headline: Wolbachia in Anopheles funestus Tanzania: detection and P. falciparum co-infection
articles.title_original: Detection of supergroup B Wolbachia strains and their co-infection with Plasmodium falciparum in wild Anopheles funestus in southeastern Tanzania: implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Mmweteni RE, Philbert AB, Mkandawile G, Abilahi F, Abbasi S, Msemo J, Milonge S, Tumbo FA, Mwalugelo Y, Muyaga LL, Msaky D, Kipekepeke AR, Okumu FO, Kaindoa EW, Baldini F
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 400 wild An. funestus in southeastern Tanzania (Mar–Jun 2024) detected Wolbachia in 46.5% by primary PCR and 70.8% by nested PCR; 0.8% (3 mosquitoes) carried P. falciparum sporozoites and only 0.3% showed Wolbachia-P. falciparum co-infection. Sequencing confirmed supergroup B Wolbachia specific to Dipterans. Study is the first report of Wolbachia-P. falciparum co-infection status in An. funestus in Tanzania.

articles.headline: Delayed malaria care-seeking in sub-Saharan African children under five
articles.title_original: Delay in malaria care-seeking for children under five in Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Amoadu M, Alhassan A, Odoom E
articles.details: Systematic review per JBI and PRISMA guidelines synthesizing prevalence and determinants of delayed care-seeking in children under 5 in sub-Saharan Africa. Prevalence ranges from 2% to 95.8%, with barriers including financial constraints, distance, traditional treatments, and health-system shortages. Delays associated with increased mortality, progression to severe malaria, and neurological/hematological complications.

articles.headline: Stochastic metapopulation model forecasts malaria in western Kenya
articles.title_original: Assessing the impact of climate and control interventions on spatio-temporal malaria dynamics using a stochastic metapopulation model.
articles.authors: Angelakis A, Beloconi A, Nyawanda BO, Khagayi S, Kariuki S, Munga S, Munywoki PK, Bigogo G, Vounatsou P
articles.details: Process-based stochastic model integrating climate, interventions, immunity, infectivity, and migration to forecast spatio-temporal malaria dynamics in western Kenya lowlands (2008–2019). Model accurately captured subpopulation-level heterogeneity and can support localized forecasting under future climate scenarios, generalizable to other endemic regions.

articles.headline: Antimicrobial peptide LL-37 targets infected erythrocytes in vitro
articles.title_original: LL-37 selectively targets Plasmodium-infected erythrocytes and exhibits antimalarial activity.
articles.authors: He X, Zhang Y, Lou J, Wu J, Xu S, Zhu G, Tang J, Fang Y, Cao J
articles.details: LL-37 human antimicrobial peptide showed antiplasmodial activity against drug-sensitive and drug-resistant parasites in vitro by targeting infected RBCs through phosphatidylserine externalization and cholesterol depletion. Elevated LL-37/CRAMP plasma levels observed in malaria patients and infected mice; exogenous administration reduced parasitemia and improved survival in mouse model. However, therapeutic efficacy was limited by rapid in vivo peptide degradation and high concentrations required.

articles.headline: Gross neurologic deficits after severe malaria in children: meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Prevalence and risk factors of gross neurologic deficits in children after severe malaria: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Okullo AE, Ouma S, John CC, van Hensbroek MB, Ojiambo KO, Otike C, Kinengyere AA, Conroy AL, Ocan M, Obuku EA, Idro R
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 41 studies (11,635 children from 16 sub-Saharan and Asian countries) on prevalence and risk factors of gross neurologic deficits post-severe malaria. Cerebral malaria (CM) showed 15.2% (95% CI: 11.5–18.8) deficit prevalence at discharge, decreasing to 5.7% (95% CI: 1.2–10.2) at 12 months; broader severe malaria only 2.4% (95% CI: 2.0–2.8). Sub-Saharan Africa: 14.2% vs. East Asia & Pacific: 2.3%. Risk factors: profound coma, coma ≥48 h, multiple convulsions, hypoglycemia, acute kidney injury.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic parasitemia associated with trauma-related splenic rupture
articles.title_original: Chronic Malaria Is Associated With Trauma-related Splenic Rupture Requiring Splenectomy.
articles.authors: Shanti PAI, Alexander K, Candrawati F, Andries B, Kambuaya NN, Rini H, Amelia AR, Puspitasari AM, Amalia R, Rahmayenti DA, Leonardo L, Prayoga P, Trianty L, Pava Z, Kenangalem E, Auburn S, Price RN, Laksanawati IS, Buffet PA, Noviyanti R, Anstey NM, Poespoprodjo JR, Kho S
articles.details: Cross-sectional comparison in Timika, Papua, Indonesia: 87.9% (29/33) of patients undergoing trauma-related splenectomy (2015–21) had PCR-detectable asymptomatic P. falciparum or P. vivax parasitemia, compared to 38.6% (697/1807) in 2013 household survey (P < 0.0001). Suggests chronic asymptomatic infection increases splenic fragility and rupture risk following trauma in endemic areas.

articles.headline: False-positive malaria RDTs common in Ugandan children under 5
articles.title_original: False-positive Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Tests are Prevalent Among Children Under 5 Years of Age in Uganda.
articles.authors: Cassidy CA, Shook-Sa BE, Boyce RM, Ciccone EJ, Gower EW, Young AM, Edwards JK
articles.details: Population-based analysis of 2018–2019 Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey (MIS): 10.7% (849/6786) of microscopy-negative children under 5 had false-positive mRDTs (mRDT(+)/microscopy(-)). Prevalence was higher with recent fever (prevalence difference 17.2%; 95% CI: 13.7–20.6%), recent antimalarial use (14.7%; 7.1–22.3%), and anemia (8.1%; 5.9–10.3%); lower with recent antibiotic use (−17.6%; −22.5 to −12.7%). Weighted lasso model with clinical/environmental variables achieved AUC 0.79.

articles.headline: Duffy polymorphism and age modulate vivax infection risk in Amazon
articles.title_original: How Duffy Blood Group (FY) Polymorphism and Age Modulate Vivax Malaria Risk at the Community Level: A Population-based Retrospective Cohort Study in the Amazon.
articles.authors: Prete CA Jr, de Sousa TN, Naziazeno IM, Puça MCSB, Ladeia WA, Rodrigues PT, Johansen IC, Paula GA, Ferreira MU, Corder RM
articles.details: Population-based retrospective cohort of 1,737 Amazonians with 7,878.9 person-years follow-up. P. vivax incidence averaged 25.8 cases/100 person-years, heterogeneously distributed (0–11 cases per person). Fy-negativity remained major resistance trait contributing to structural zeroes in incidence distribution. Fya/Fyb polymorphism differences in infection risk observed in youth were attenuated with age, suggesting differential clinical immunity development rates between susceptible Fy(b+) and less-susceptible Fy(a+) individuals.

articles.headline: Praziquantel shows moderate antimalarial activity in asymptomatic adults
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Praziquantel for Treatment of Plasmodium falciparum Infection in Asymptomatic Gabonese Adults: A Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Mischlinger J, Pechmann K, Werner J, Zinsou AH, Vogler M, Ndoumba WN, Okwu DG, von Wedel C, Kurth F, Kreidenweiss A, Bachmann A, Adegnika AA, Zoleko-Manego R, Kremsner PG, Sarpong F, Badjina L, Ramharter M, Mombo-Ngoma G
articles.details: Double-blind randomized placebo-controlled phase 2b trial in Lambaréné, Gabon, with 44 semi-immune asymptomatic P. falciparum-parasitemic adults randomized to placebo or 40 mg/kg praziquantel daily × 3 days, followed 7 days. Praziquantel showed statistically significant hourly parasitemia reduction (β = −0.006, P < 0.001) vs. placebo (β = −0.0013, P = 0.3); however, only 41% (9/22) achieved parasite clearance by day 7 vs. 27% (6/22) placebo.

articles.headline: P. vivax vaccine candidates show extreme genetic diversity
articles.title_original: Extreme Genetic Diversity and Signatures of Balancing Selection in Plasmodium vivax Blood-Stage Antigens.
articles.authors: Bareng APN, Naung MT, Martin E, Mehra S, Munro JE, Takala Harrison S, Barry AE
articles.details: Analysis of 289 P. vivax whole-genome sequences from Asia, Americas, Oceania for genetic diversity of 14 blood-stage vaccine candidates. AMA1, CyRPA, RBP1a, and P41 exhibited high genetic diversity and signatures of balancing selection on linear and 3D protein structures across all endemic regions, consistent with immune selection. Other antigens (CSP, TRAP, MSP4/9/10, GAMA, S12, ARP, S25, S28) showed low-to-intermediate diversity. Vaccine strain Sal-1 sequences had extremely low global frequencies.

articles.headline: Budget impact of tafenoquine and G6PD testing for vivax in Brazilian Amazon
articles.title_original: [Expansion of the use of technologies to reduce cases of malaria due to Plasmodium vivax in the Brazilian Amazon: a budget impact analysis].
articles.authors: Costa MGSD, Zimmermann I, Costa ACCD, Moraes AC, Pinto M
articles.details: Budget impact analysis for Brazilian SUS (5-year horizon, Amazon reference population) modeled incremental costs of incorporating tafenoquine and G6PD quantitative point-of-care test for patients under 16 and by weight, plus expanded primaquine testing. Incremental budget impact: BRL 12.5 million. Model predicted tafenoquine incorporation would reduce hospitalization costs ~9% and recurrence costs ~11%; test reagent cost and waste significantly impacted results.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance absent; lumefantrine and SP efficacy at risk in Angola
articles.title_original: Molecular markers of antimalarial drug resistance in pfk13, pfmdr1, pfdhfr, and pfdhps genes of Plasmodium falciparum in a rural municipality in Cubal, Benguela Province, Angola (2022-2023).
articles.authors: Mediavilla A, García X, Martínez-Vallejo P, Molina-de la Fuente I, Febrer-Sendra B, Silgado A, Francisco K, Maturana CR, Martins JF, Nindia A, Martínez-Campreciós J, Aznar ML, Oliveira-Souto I, Molina I, Sulleiro E, Berzosa P
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 139 PCR-confirmed P. falciparum infections in rural Angola (2022-2023). Sequenced drug-resistance polymorphisms: pfk13 (n=120) 10% with mutations (5 nonsynonymous, none validated for artemisinin); pfmdr1 (n=100) Y184F 43%; pfdhfr/pfdhps (n=101) triple mutant 77%, A437G 98%, K540E 24%; 58% IRNG, 18% IRNGE classification. Conclusion: No validated artemisinin resistance, but Y184F frequency and IRNGE haplotypes raise concerns for AL and SP long-term efficacy.

articles.headline: pfhrp2-negative parasites show distinct adhesion phenotype in vitro
articles.title_original: Phenotypic and genotypic characterization of Plasmodium falciparum field isolates lacking pfhrp2 gene.
articles.authors: Bendezu J, Larrauri-Aguilar P, Bautista R, Villasis E, Vinetz JM, Gamboa D
articles.details: In vitro study of 5 P. falciparum field isolates from Peruvian Amazon (3 pfhrp2+, 2 pfhrp2-). Measured cytoadherence (CSA, HUVEC), rosetting, and pfhrp2/pfhrp3 gene expression via qPCR. pfhrp2+ isolates: 2/3 showed rosetting or CSA cytoadherence. pfhrp2- isolates: adhesion to HUVEC. pfhrp2 expression higher in pfhrp2+ isolates; pfhrp3 similar or lower. Genomic stability maintained over year-long culture. Conclusion: pfhrp2 status associates with distinct adhesion phenotypes; findings preliminary, require validation in larger isolate panels.

articles.headline: Multi-pathogen co-infections common; alter immune response in endemic region
articles.title_original: Unravelling complex interactions during Toxoplasma, Plasmodium, and Leishmania co-infections in French Guiana.
articles.authors: Néron K, Fesel C, Demar M, Pied S
articles.details: Seroprevalence study in French Guiana (2012-2022); n=253 (patients with Toxoplasma, Plasmodium, Leishmania; healthy controls). Measured seroprevalence via chemiluminescence/ELISA and plasma cytokines/chemokines via multiplex assay. ~60% seropositive for ≥2 pathogens; 2.4% with concomitant infections. Significant differences in IL-10, IL-17, IL-33, IFN-α between single and co-infected patients. Conclusion: Complex co-infection dynamics; insights inform public health strategy and biomarker development in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Anopheles stephensi habitat suitability projected to decline under climate change
articles.title_original: Suitable ecological niches of invasive malaria vector under present and projected climatic conditions in South of Iran.
articles.authors: Bozorg-Omid F, Abbasi M, Yaghoobi-Ershadi MR, Hanafi-Bojd AA
articles.details: MaxEnt modeling of An. stephensi distribution in Hormozgan Province, Iran; 72 occurrence points, bioclimatic data from WorldClim v2.1. Assessed current and future habitat suitability using SSP climate scenarios; model performance AUC 0.765, TSS 0.519. Predicted significant decline in suitability under future scenarios, especially western/central Hormozgan. Altitude and precipitation most influential variables. Stable refuge in Bashagard County. Conclusion: Projected habitat loss signals need for adaptive vector control and climate-integrated intervention planning.

articles.headline: Bangladesh achieved >90% malaria reduction; 2030 elimination threatened by resistance and mobility
articles.title_original: Malaria in Bangladesh, 2000-2025: Navigating the Headwinds From Control to Elimination.
articles.authors: Mamun AA, Kayenat MSA, Islam KAMI
articles.details: Narrative review of Bangladeshi malaria literature (2000-2025). Cases reduced >90% from 2008-2020 via vector control, RDTs, ACTs; burden concentrated in Chittagong Hill Tracts. P. falciparum dominant; emerging non-kelch13 artemisinin-partial resistance noted. P. vivax radical cure challenges due to G6PD deficiency; outdoor-biting vectors; large asymptomatic reservoir and high cross-border mobility. Conclusion: 2030 elimination attainable but requires intelligence-driven approach with genomic surveillance, G6PD screening, and targeting of forest-going populations.

articles.headline: Healthcare workers' vaccine beliefs and barriers in Southwest Nigeria
articles.title_original: Healthcare workers' beliefs, readiness, and acceptance of malaria vaccine in Southwest Nigeria: addressing misconceptions and uptake barriers.
articles.authors: Esan DT, Adeleke TO, Rasaq NO, Ajisafe ZA, Ramos CG
articles.details: Study examined healthcare workers' beliefs, readiness, and acceptance of malaria vaccine in Southwest Nigeria, addressing misconceptions and barriers to vaccine uptake. Specific study design, population size, and findings not available from title alone.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine in Uganda costs 3.5% of health budget
articles.title_original: The Cost and Budget Impact of Malaria Vaccine Introduction in Uganda.
articles.authors: Ochanda PN, Ekirapa-Kiracho E, Mayora C, Ssempala R, Nakimuli B, Namirembe B, Ssennyonjo A, Ssengooba F
articles.details: Budget impact analysis based on prospective studies in Uganda and sub-Saharan Africa. Compared vaccination costs to savings from reduced malaria treatment costs. Treatment costs for complicated malaria in children <5: $1.1M before vaccination, $297,465 after. Annual vaccination cost $29M (24% of immunization budget, 3.5% of total health budget). Over 5 years: total impact $145.5M, net cost $141M (range $106-176M with coverage variation). Conclusion: Substantial budget commitment required; prioritize funding and cost-effective targeting strategies.

articles.headline: SP chemoprevention coverage, not frequency, drives dhps A581G resistance emergence
articles.title_original: Modeling the Impact of Malaria Chemoprevention with sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine on the spread of Plasmodium falciparum dhps A581G mutation.
articles.authors: Mziray N, Okell LC, Kiware S, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Ainea N, Ishengoma DS, Nyerere N
articles.details: Age-structured compartmental model simulating malaria transmission and SP-resistant parasite competition in settings with dhps K540E near fixation. Tested 3 vs 5 monthly chemoprevention cycles at 0-80% coverage. At 45% coverage: dhps A581G increased from 11% to 20.6% (3 cycles) or 27.8% (5 cycles). Coverage level explained 58% of resistance spread (ηp²=0.58); treatment cycles 9.3%; interaction 9.0%. Conclusion: High coverage accelerates A581G emergence; trade-off between immediate prevention and long-term resistance requires careful planning and surveillance.

articles.headline: R21 vaccine uptake low in Sudan; knowledge and supply gaps main barriers
articles.title_original: Suboptimal uptake of the malaria vaccine in Sudan: what's behind the blinds?
articles.authors: Malik EM, El-Hassan HM, Ali MAA, Alsoni KAA, Alnair NMA, Noureddin AA, Aladani ISA, Adam MAB, Saad FM, Elkhidir TA, Alshareif BA, Khalafalla M, Al-Haddad NFK, Mohammed NYE, Eltayeb DIH, Ahmed N
articles.details: Cross-sectional mixed-methods study in Gedarif and Blue Nile States, Sudan (2024); 416 mothers/caregivers of children <5 (99% response rate). Vaccine uptake: 36.7% (95% CI 31.1-42.8%, n=94/256 eligible). Low awareness: 57.7% aware of vaccine, 36.7% knew dose number, 25.7% knew starting age; 53.1% perceived malaria non-significant. Attitudes positive but insufficient knowledge; barriers included vaccine stockouts and geographic access. Conclusion: Suboptimal uptake due to knowledge and health system barriers, not vaccine hesitancy.

articles.headline: Hemozoin-associated proteins disrupt blood-brain barrier in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum hemozoin-associated biomolecules induce brain endothelial cell barrier disruption in an in vitro model of cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Crotty KA, Clotea I, Ueberheide B, Cammer M, Sall J, Liang A, Rodriguez A
articles.details: In vitro study using human brain microvascular endothelial cells exposed to P. falciparum-infected RBCs or purified hemozoin. Investigated mechanism of barrier disruption: hemozoin crystal alone did not induce disruption, but associated biomolecules did. Protease treatment inhibited barrier disruption; nuclease treatment did not, indicating key protein role. Conclusion: Identified mechanism of P. falciparum-mediated cerebral malaria pathogenesis (hemozoin-associated proteins → endothelial dysfunction); findings may enable novel therapeutic targets.

articles.headline: RTS,S fourth-dose timing flexible; 6-12 months post-third dose optimal
articles.title_original: Timing of the fourth dose of RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine in perennial settings: a modelling study.
articles.authors: Figueroa-Downing D, Kelly SL, Cavelan A, Penny MA, Malinga J
articles.details: OpenMalaria individual-based stochastic transmission model; compared fourth-dose timing (6-, 9-, 12-, 15-, 18-month intervals post-primary series) across transmission intensities. Primary series averted 73-93% of uncomplicated and 74-92% of severe cases; fourth dose added 8-38% uncomplicated and 9-36% severe cases averted. Flexible timing supported, particularly 6-12 months post-third dose; recommendation 15-21 months age. Conclusion: Fourth-dose timing can align with country health touchpoints; interval flexibility supports coverage optimization.

articles.headline: Extreme weather reduces pregnancy chemoprevention through disrupted antenatal care
articles.title_original: Extreme weather events and malaria prevention in pregnancy: a mediation analysis of antenatal care and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine use in two malaria-endemic counties of Kenya.
articles.authors: Matanda DJ, Mchenga M, Amadi JA, Nakuya Z, Okoth O, Otieno B, Obare F
articles.details: Cross-sectional household survey in Kisumu and Migori counties, Kenya; women with live births in prior 24 months. Examined mediation: whether extreme weather reduced IPTp₃+ (≥3 SP doses) through reduced ANC4+ attendance (≥4 visits). IPTp₃+ lower in shock-exposed (49.5%) vs unexposed (58.8%); weather reduced ANC4+ (β=-0.23, 95% CI -0.41 to -0.05). Total effect on IPTp₃+: -0.45 (95% CI -0.68 to -0.23), 76.6% mediated through ANC; attenuated to -0.27 after adjustment. Conclusion: Extreme weather disrupts pregnancy prevention primarily via ANC disruption.

articles.headline: P. vivax-dengue coinfection causes ischemic stroke
articles.title_original: Ischemic stroke during dengue-Plasmodium vivax coinfection in a young woman: a case report.
articles.authors: Nati-Castillo HA, Saavedra-Torres JS, Cardenas JM, Gaibor-Pazmiño A, Ortiz-Prado E, Izquierdo-Condoy JS
articles.details: A 23-year-old Colombian woman with P. vivax malaria (8,200 parasites/μL) and acute dengue (NS1+, IgM+) presented with left anterior cerebral artery ischemic stroke after 7 days of fever. Despite parasite and virus clearance, the case demonstrates synergistic endothelial injury from coinfection. Treatment with artesunate-primaquine, supportive dengue care, and aspirin resulted in neurological improvement with rehabilitation.

articles.headline: P. falciparum PPCS is novel drug target with prokaryote features
articles.title_original: The Plasmodium falciparum PPCS is a unique heteromeric complex with prokaryote-like activity and is a target of pantothenate analogs.
articles.authors: Liu X, Jin R, Domingo R, Kotzé TJ, Guan J, Hamann AR, Chu A, Hart-Smith G, Spry C, Sexton AE, Crooke Z, Venkadeswaran A, Yuan X, Creek DJ, Auclair K, Corry B, Strauss E, Saliba KJ
articles.details: Two P. falciparum phosphopantothenoylcysteine synthase (PPCS) enzymes form a unique heteromeric complex essential for coenzyme A biosynthesis. Unlike eukaryotic PPCS, the P. falciparum complex has prokaryote-like features and cannot use ATP. The complex is targeted by multiple pantothenate analog inhibitors, representing a novel antimalarial drug target distinct from human PPCS.

articles.headline: Stakeholder preferences for next-generation malaria chemoprevention in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Improving malaria chemoprevention coverage in pregnancy: Surveying stakeholder preferences for new product profiles and community-delivery approaches across five African countries.
articles.authors: Audibert C, Borghini-Fuhrer I, El Gaaloul M, Ramachandruni H, Tchouatieu AM, Majeres Lugand M
articles.details: Survey of 254 stakeholders (pregnant women, nurses/CHWs, clinicians, policymakers) across 5 African endemic countries assessed preferences for next-generation IPTp products versus sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine. Top priorities included safety in all trimesters, short duration, and few tablets daily. Discrete choice experiment favored products without food restrictions. Long-acting injectables with ≥3 months' coverage and CHW delivery were noted as potentially enhancing adherence.

articles.headline: Systematic review protocol: pediatric malaria burden in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Burden, risk factors, and clinical outcomes of pediatric malaria in Nigeria: A systematic review and meta-analysis protocol.
articles.authors: Ibrahim OR, Abdulkareem J, Issa A, Alao MA
articles.details: Protocol for systematic review and meta-analysis to estimate prevalence of laboratory-confirmed pediatric malaria in Nigeria using standardized WHO case definitions. Will search 6 databases through June 2025, pool data with random-effects models, and assess primary outcome of prevalence (overall and severe vs uncomplicated) and secondary outcomes including risk factors and outcome modifiers by age, sex, and geography.

articles.headline: Mitochondrial genes upregulated in cerebral malaria and hepatic dysfunction
articles.title_original: Differential expression of mitochondria-associated genes in clinical samples of Plasmodium falciparum showing severe manifestations.
articles.authors: Gujarati S, Singal BR, Gupta D, Kochar SK, Kochar DK, Das A
articles.details: Profiled mitochondrial sense and antisense transcripts in 22 P. falciparum clinical isolates grouped as uncomplicated malaria (n=6), cerebral malaria (n=4), and hepatic dysfunction (n=12) using microarray and RNA-seq. Cerebral malaria isolates showed upregulation of tricarboxylic acid cycle and electron transport genes, indicating disrupted mitochondrial bioenergetics. Identified natural antisense transcripts as potential regulatory molecules and non-conventional antimalarial targets.

articles.headline: HLA population coverage of P. falciparum CD8+ epitopes for vaccine
articles.title_original: Population HLA coverage of experimentally characterized P. falciparum CD8 (+) T cell epitopes as potential components of a multi-epitope malaria vaccine construct: an in silico insight.
articles.authors: Siame C, Abdul-Mumin R, Agyekum G, Akuffo L, Afari E, Yankey CAB, Kusi KA
articles.details: In silico analysis evaluated 16 experimentally validated CD8+ T cell epitopes from 3 P. falciparum antigens (CSP, AMA-1, TRAP) for global HLA coverage. CSPep5 (IQNSLSTEW) showed highest global coverage (>85%) and West African coverage (93.35%). Eight epitopes were 100% conserved across 16 P. falciparum strains. Combining highly conserved epitopes with broad HLA coverage could support development of universal/pan malaria vaccines.

articles.headline: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis: first case following P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis following malaria: expanding the spectrum of post-malarial neurological syndromes.
articles.authors: Deng S, Wang W, Lu W, Wang S
articles.details: Case report of 40-year-old man who developed anti-NMDAR encephalitis 11 days after P. falciparum malaria recovery. Presented with seizures, altered consciousness, and acute psychosis. Anti-NMDAR antibodies confirmed in cerebrospinal fluid via immunofluorescence and cell-based assay without concurrent infection or malignancy. Rapid improvement with first-line immunotherapy. Expands spectrum of post-malarial neurological syndromes.

articles.headline: Cytokine profiles reveal malaria infection in pregnant primigravid women
articles.title_original: Cytokine-Driven Immune Phenotypes at Delivery as Indicators of Malaria Infection Among Primigravidae in Burkina Faso: An Exploratory Analysis.
articles.authors: Traore O, Rouamba T, Zango SH, Sorgho H, Valea I, Traore-Coulibaly M, Schallig HDFH, Tinto H
articles.details: Exploratory study of 33 primigravid women (mean age 19 years) in Burkina Faso measured antibodies to PfCSP, PfAMA-1, EBA-175 and plasma cytokines (IL-4, IL-10, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ) at delivery. Multivariate analysis identified 3 immune clusters: low-inflammatory, TNF-α-dominant, and highly pro-inflammatory. All women in the most inflammatory cluster were P. falciparum-positive (Fisher's exact test, p=0.04), with distinct cytokine profiles reflecting current infection state.

articles.headline: Deep learning for automated malaria detection in blood smear images
articles.title_original: Automated Malaria Ring Form Classification in Blood Smear Images Using Ensemble Parallel Neural Networks.
articles.authors: Pongpanitanont P, Suttidate N, Nuinoon M, Khampeeramao N, Laymanivong S, Janwan P
articles.details: Developed parallel neural network framework with CNN and multi-head self-attention for binary classification of malaria ring-form parasites in thin blood smear images. Used 27,558 erythrocyte crops (128×128 pixels) with on-the-fly augmentation. Model#1 achieved ROC-AUC ~0.99, 95.67% mean cross-validation accuracy, and 97% accuracy on independent test set (macro F1-score: 0.97). Model#3 with increased complexity showed performance decline (95% accuracy) from higher false-positive rates. Requires external validation before clinical deployment.

articles.headline: Intravenous drug abuse and P. falciparum-HIV coinfection risk
articles.title_original: Intravenous drug abuse - a concern for falciparum malaria along with HIV.
articles.authors: Makkar A, Panda PK
articles.details: Title indicates article addresses intravenous drug abuse as a risk factor in the context of P. falciparum malaria and HIV coinfection, but specific study details are not available from the title alone.

articles.headline: Community co-created malaria innovations in conflict-affected Cameroon
articles.title_original: Co-creation of community-based innovations to improve access to malaria treatment in conflict-affected regions of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Omam LA, Tanue EA, Omam EN, Metuge A, Nganmou I, Epie C, Kebam G, Ako S, Bessem ME, Hawkings H, Maxwell K, Zoungrana Y, Berryman E, Jarman E, Noukeme BM
articles.details: Participatory co-creation workshop in October 2021 with government, community leaders, internally displaced persons, and community health workers in South West and Littoral regions of Cameroon developed 3 community-based interventions: (1) Community Health Participatory Approach replacing top-down dialogue; (2) Health Voucher System providing subsidized testing, treatment, and transport; (3) Supportive Supervision Model enhancing CHW capacity. Built on formative research in 80 conflict-affected communities.

articles.headline: Ethiopia's malaria program: systemic gaps threaten elimination efforts
articles.title_original: Resurgence of malaria in Ethiopia: a national landscape analysis of systemic and operational gaps.
articles.authors: Sasie SD, Aragaw FM, Assele DD, Argaw ZM, Afele MA
articles.details: National landscape analysis (2016-2025 literature and stakeholder consultations) assessed 6 operational domains of Ethiopia's malaria response. Found ITN ownership ~64% (lower in Oromia/urban areas), IRS coverage ~50% with pyrethroid resistance, digital surveillance operational in ~80% facilities but with delayed reporting, diagnostic challenges from RDT stock-outs and pfhrp2/3 gene deletions, and recovery capacity limited (only ~50% facilities achieved timely commodity replenishment). Cross-cutting constraints included delayed financing, fragmented digital systems, and limited entomological surveillance.

articles.headline: Malaria-helminth coinfection rare in Ugandan school children
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Coinfection with Malaria and Intestinal Helminths among School-Aged Children in Nwoya District, Northern Uganda: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: McGowan K, Bongomin F, Otika D, O'Connell C, Matejak N, Hamer DH
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 190 school-aged children (median age 13 years) in rural Uganda. 75.3% tested positive for P. falciparum by RDT but only 13.2% had detectable parasitemia on blood smear. Intestinal helminth infections were uncommon (3.2%), and all children with helminths were malaria RDT-positive. Results suggest effective mass drug administration for helminth control and possible persistent antigenemia in malaria-treated children.

articles.headline: High malaria parasitemia linked to anemia and hepatic dysfunction
articles.title_original: Plasmodium Parasitemia and Its Relationship With Hematological, Liver, and Kidney Function of Patients With Falciparum Malaria in the Eastern Region, Ghana.
articles.authors: Badu A, Darban I, Mensah K, Antwi MH, Appeaning M, Kwakye AS, Buckman TA, Owusu VK, Mintah DT, Agyei RA, Yeboah C, Acorlor D
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 362 participants (71.8% adults, 69.3% female) in Ghana. Increasing parasitemia levels were significantly associated with lower hemoglobin, RBC, MCV, MCH, and platelet counts, plus elevated liver enzymes (ALT, AST; p<0.05). No significant changes in kidney function markers (urea, creatinine) across parasitemia levels. Results highlight hematologic and hepatic alterations in severe parasitemia.

articles.headline: Mathematical model identifies optimal malaria control strategies with immigration
articles.title_original: Data-driven modeling of imported malaria in Morocco and the impact of population migration.
articles.authors: Toufga H, Benrhmach G, Lhous M, Tridane A
articles.details: Data-driven compartmental model of malaria transmission accounting for human immigration, validated using imported malaria cases in Morocco (1990–2023). Model used least-squares fitting, Extended Kalman Filter, and LSTM neural networks for parameter estimation. Optimal control analysis identified significant reduction in human and vector infections through combined interventions: awareness, prophylactic treatment, infected-population treatment, and vector control with insecticide.

articles.headline: Low utilization of malaria prevention in pregnancy services, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Factors influencing the utilisation of malaria preventive services during pregnancy in Ile Ife, Nigeria: a mixed method approach.
articles.authors: Inyangudo GN, Ojo TO, Oluwabunmi F, Stanley E, Olaniyi B
articles.details: Mixed-method study of 200 pregnant women across ten healthcare facilities in Nigeria. Long-lasting insecticidal net ownership was 62.5%, but utilization only 47.5%. Intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine uptake was 50.8% for ≥1 dose, but only 7% received ≥3 doses. Barriers included commodity stock-outs, transportation costs, distance to facilities, and preference for faith-based homes over antenatal care.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention with SPAQ shows excellent safety profile
articles.title_original: Incidence and trends of adverse drug reactions during seasonal malaria chemoprevention among children: evidence from Ghana's pharmacovigilance database, 2015-2023.
articles.authors: Otoo BAF, Opoku-Mireku M, Seaneke SK, Darko DM, Sabblah GT, Bonful HA
articles.details: Pharmacovigilance analysis of 2,097 adverse drug reactions reported during seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SPAQ: sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine) in Ghanaian children aged 3–59 months (2015–2023). Most common adverse reactions were fever (22.7%), vomiting (21.5%), and diarrhea (16.9%); serious reactions occurred in 8.6% of cases. Incidence declined dramatically from 220.63 per 100,000 doses in 2015 to 0.34 in 2023.

articles.headline: Cryptic Anopheles diversity revealed by COX1 phylogenetics in Indonesia
articles.title_original: Malaria Vector Surveillance in Indonesia: COX1 Phylogenetic Reveals Monophyletic Clades and Cryptic Diversity in Anopheles Mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Senjarini K, Hasanah LNU, Wathon S, Oktarianti R, Labes A
articles.details: Molecular species identification of Anopheles mosquitoes from two Indonesian sites using COX1 DNA barcoding compared with morphological identification. Phylogenetic analysis identified eight monophyletic clades with strong bootstrap support (≥99% for six). Specimens morphologically identified as An. aconitus and An. minimus were molecularly confirmed as An. flavirostris, revealing species misidentification. Dominant vectors: An. sundaicus (68%) in Bangsring, An. flavirostris (13%) in Hargowilis.

articles.headline: Pfgch1 gene amplification associated with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance mutations
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum GTP-cyclohydrolase 1 (Pfgch1) Gene Copy Number Variation and Its Implications in antimalarial resistance from Northeast India.
articles.authors: Sen A, Sharma P, Sharma S, Srivastava B, Singh K, Anvikar A, Bharti PK
articles.details: Quantitative PCR analysis of Pfgch1 gene copy number variation in 101 P. falciparum isolates from Northeast India. Higher Pfgch1 copy numbers were significantly associated with mutations in Pfdhfr and Pfdhps genes, which confer sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance. Findings suggest Pfgch1 amplification may compensate for antifolate-resistance mutations through the folate biosynthesis pathway, providing insight into polyresistant P. falciparum evolution.

articles.headline: PbSERA3 essential for hepatic merozoite egress in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei serine repeat antigen 3 (PbSERA3) is required for hepatic merozoite egress.
articles.authors: Singh D, Patri S, Veeda N, Verma CK, Kavati A, Segireddy RR, Kolli SK, Arun Kumar K
articles.details: Laboratory study using conditional Pbsera3-edited P. berghei sporozoites. Mutant parasites invaded hepatocytes and developed normally in late liver stages but failed to initiate blood-stage infection. PbSERA3, orthologous to PfSERA6 in P. falciparum, is processed by subtilisin-like protease SUB1 during liver development. Results identify PbSERA3/PfSERA6 maturation as critical for hepatic egress and a potential antimalarial therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Commentary on structural challenges in malaria vector control programs
articles.title_original: Musings from a Vectosaur: Malaria in 2026.
articles.authors: Lluberas MF
articles.details: Opinion essay examining global malaria control challenges documented in the 2025 Africa Malaria Progress Report. Argues that structural fragmentation and over-reliance on long-lasting insecticidal nets and indoor residual spraying limit effectiveness. Identifies key challenges: insecticide resistance, behavioral shifts in mosquitoes, Anopheles stephensi expansion into African urban areas, and climate change. Advocates for integrated vector management with coordinated national programmes.

articles.headline: Malaria and G6PD deficiency significantly increase childhood anemia risk
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the impact of malaria and erythrocyte G6PD deficiency on anaemia outcomes among children in Jos Northcentral Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okoli CA, Wamu DI, Deffi F, Leshak PD
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 100 children (1–5 years) in Nigeria. 55% had malaria parasitemia, with 24 (43.6%) G6PD deficient; overall 49% G6PD deficient, 28% anemic. Malaria and G6PD deficiency had significant combined impact on anemia outcomes (F=7.327; p=0.002), with 37.5% of G6PD-deficient malaria-positive children becoming anemic. Findings support mandatory G6PD screening before antimalarial administration in endemic areas.

articles.headline: Severe malaria case fatality rate 9.1% with convulsions, anemia predictors
articles.title_original: Treatment outcome and associated factors of severe malaria among admitted children in the Gambella Region, Southwest Ethiopia: a retrospective study design.
articles.authors: Animut A, Bekana T, Siraj SZ, Ponpetch K, Tasew G, Kinfe E, Mekonnen Z
articles.details: Retrospective study of 417 children (1 month–14 years) hospitalized with severe malaria in Ethiopia (May 2022–May 2024). Overall case fatality rate was 9.11%. Highest mortality occurred with severe anemia (31.8%), convulsions (27%), pulmonary edema (19.7%), and coma (18.5%). P. falciparum caused 91.7% of admissions and 89.5% of deaths. Convulsion, severe anemia, and comorbidities independently predicted mortality.

articles.headline: Pyrimethamine-resistant parasites show fixation versus drift patterns
articles.title_original: Contrasting evolutionary trajectories of pyrimethamine resistance in human and zoonotic malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Putaporntip C, Somkuna J, Rojrung R, Tia T, Kuamsab N, Jongwutiwes S
articles.details: Comparative genomic analysis of dhfr-ts genes in 241 P. falciparum and 115 P. vivax from Thailand versus zoonotic parasites (18 P. knowlesi, 64 P. inui). P. falciparum showed high prevalence of quadruple IRNL resistance mutant indicating strong directional selection; P. vivax showed wild-type alleles and incomplete fixation. Zoonotic parasites exhibited higher mutation counts, haplotype diversity, and rare variants, suggesting purifying selection and genetic drift.

articles.headline: Blood-stage malaria parasites employ unconventional iron-management strategies
articles.title_original: Fifty shades of iron: Unorthodox mechanisms of iron acquisition and utilization in blood-stage Plasmodium parasites.
articles.authors: Loveridge KM, Sigala PA
articles.details: A review synthesizing knowledge of how blood-stage P. falciparum manages iron during red blood cell infection. The parasite genome lacks many canonical transporters, storage proteins, and regulatory circuits essential for iron homeostasis in model organisms, yet the parasite employs unconventional strategies for iron uptake, distribution, and utilization. The review identifies major unanswered questions and therapeutic targeting opportunities in Plasmodium iron metabolism.

articles.headline: Protective antibody targets and functions identified in P. vivax-infected children
articles.title_original: A longitudinal study of children identifies antibody Fc-mediated functions and antigen targets of immunity to Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Opi DH, Longley RJ, Takashima E, Spelman T, Tayipto Y, Schoffer K, Brewster J, Reiling L, Wines BD, Kiniboro B, Siba P, Harbers M, Hogarth M, Tsuboi T, Robinson LJ, Mueller I, Beeson JG
articles.details: In a longitudinal cohort of Papua New Guinea children at risk of P. vivax malaria, researchers quantified antibody functions to multiple P. vivax antigens. They identified specific antigens targeted by antibodies with Fcγ receptor interactions and complement fixation capacity, and determined which antigen subsets were most protective. The findings provide leads for developing multi-antigen P. vivax vaccines eliciting multi-functional antibody responses.

articles.headline: 4-hydroxycoumarin efficiently attracts anthropophilic Anopheles in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Field evaluation of efficacy and selectivity of select benzopyranone attractants for anthropophilic malaria vectors in Madagascar.
articles.authors: Ralimanana SV, Ramarosandratana NH, Andrianjafy MT, Rambala Rakotomena NAH, Duclos MC, Ravaomanarivo LH, Mavingui P, Ramiharimanana FD, Mbaye A, Métay E, Vestalys Ramanandraibe V, Lemaire M
articles.details: In Madagascar highlands, CDC light traps baited with synthetic attractants were evaluated for selectively capturing anthropophilic Anopheles species. The attractant 4-hydroxycoumarin showed high efficiency (KI=80%) and selectivity (SI=5.1), while 7-decyloxychromone showed similar attraction but lower selectivity (KI=69%; SI=1.6). Combined attractants showed no synergistic effect, and consecutive trapping nights showed decreased or eliminated anthropophilic Anopheles numbers, suggesting mass trapping as a complementary vector control approach.

articles.headline: High ITN ownership but widespread misuse in northern Cameroon
articles.title_original: Awareness, attitudes, and control measures regarding malaria and mosquitoes in the North Region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Danga SPY, Ngadvou D, Yonki B, Ezeike AK, Djonga A, Nukenine EN, Esimone CO
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 500 households in 50 villages in North Region Cameroon found universal malaria awareness (100%) and nearly universal identification of mosquito transmission (99.6%), though 42.4% also attributed transmission to cold weather. ITN ownership was nearly universal (99.6%), but 90.4% reported misuse (35.8% for tying animals, 7.4% for fishing). Only 19.0% sought treatment within 24 hours, with self-medication (43.2%) driven by perceived high hospital costs (39.6%) and staff carelessness (14.3%).

articles.headline: HRP2-RDT effective despite 11.8% pfhrp2/3 deletions in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Diagnostic challenges of histidine-rich protein 2-based rapid diagnostic tests due to pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions in asymptomatic malaria in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Mazigo E, Jun H, Lee WJ, Louis JM, Fitriana F, Syahada JH, Muh F, Chun W, Park WS, Lee SJ, Na S, Han ET, Lu F, Kidima W, Han JH
articles.details: In a cross-sectional survey of 3,489 asymptomatic Tanzanian participants across high and low transmission villages, HRP2-based RDTs detected 77.6% of qPCR-positive cases (compared to 53.8% by light microscopy), with 90.0% overall accuracy and substantial agreement (κ=0.736). Multiplex qPCR found pfhrp2 or pfhrp3 deletions in 11.8% of 787 P. falciparum isolates (2.4% dual deletions), more frequent in high-transmission villages (9.7%) than low-transmission areas (2.2%), with all deletion-bearing isolates RDT-negative.

articles.headline: Preliminary discriminating concentration for broflanilide in An. gambiae
articles.title_original: Determination of the preliminary discriminating concentration of broflanilide against malaria vector mosquito Anopheles gambiae by multi-centre susceptibility testing.
articles.authors: Ando Y, Mori K, Bradley J, Snetselaar J, Small G
articles.details: WHO bottle bioassays at nine research facilities determined the discriminating concentration of broflanilide, a novel indoor residual spray active ingredient, against insecticide-susceptible Anopheles gambiae s.s. Kisumu strain. The preliminary discriminating concentration was set at 18 μg/bottle. These results provide a benchmark for susceptibility monitoring of wild mosquito populations in sub-Saharan Africa as VECTRON™ T500 is introduced for malaria vector control.

articles.headline: ITN use high but IRS uptake limited in northern Uganda
articles.title_original: Utilization of malaria control interventions and associated factors among women of reproductive age in Lira City, Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Apio E, Angwech H, Opio B, Okwir G, Opoke R, Malinga GM, Opiro R
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 629 women of reproductive age in Lira City found high ITN utilization (84.1%) and moderate IPT uptake (68%), but low IRS coverage (32.8%). While 96.8% were knowledgeable about ITNs and 91.9% perceived IRS as effective, 51.7% cited bad odor and discomfort as concerns about IRS, and 21.9% doubted its safety. Age, house type, net-related challenges, marital status, and health education exposure significantly predicted intervention utilization.

articles.headline: Climate thresholds for malaria transmission identified in Colombia
