articles.authors: Clucas D, Bennett C, Harding R, Pettikiriarachchi A, Baldi A, Randall LM, Steel R, Mellin R, Hobbs M, Caiazzo S, Mwangi MN, Fielding KL, Hickey PF, Baldwin TM, Amann-Zalcenstein D, Emery-Corbin SJ, Mzembe G, Moya E, Braat S, Jex A, Demir AY, Verhoef H, Phiri KS, Biggs BA, Tham WH, Boddey JA, Pasricha SR, Ataíde R
articles.details: Study of Malawian pregnant women (n=711) from REVAMP trial found iron deficiency associated with 50% reduced P. falciparum parasitemia (95% CI 30-64%, P<0.0001). Iron-deficient mice showed improved survival (15.5 vs 7.0 days) and protection from cerebral malaria (83% vs 17% survival). Findings support WHO recommendations for iron supplementation combined with malaria prevention.

articles.headline: ANG2/ACE2 dysregulation in malaria-COVID-19 coinfection in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Elevated ANG2/ACE2 and immune responses associated with Plasmodium falciparum and SARS-CoV-2 coinfection in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Tchoupe EB, Kameni MN, Eric MB, Taya JB, Kamdem SD, Numfor Nkah L, Ama Moor V, Tepa A, Neba FR, Afum-Adjei Awuah A, Amuasi JH, Netongo PM
articles.details: Study of 96 Cameroonian participants (COVID-19 n=28, malaria n=28, co-infection n=16, controls n=24) found co-infected patients had elevated liver enzymes, renal markers, and erythropoietin. Co-infection triggered elevated IFN-γ and IL-1β, while ANG2 was elevated in COVID-19 and co-infection groups. ACE2 showed strong discriminatory power (AUC 0.85 for COVID-19, 0.77 for malaria), suggesting diagnostic and prognostic value in coinfection.

articles.headline: hrp2/3-deleted P. falciparum spread despite reduced transmission in Peru
articles.title_original: Malaria control and the unexpected spread of diagnostic-resistant Plasmodium falciparum in Peru.
articles.authors: Gyuricza IG, Fola AA, Simkin A, Thwai KL, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Johri P, Henry CM, Cabrera-Sosa L, Porras-Laymito G, Cheng Q, Watson OJ, Gamboa D, Valdivia HO, Parr JB
articles.details: Analysis of 1,215 P. falciparum samples from Peru (2003-2018) showed rapid spread of hrp2/3-deleted (diagnostic-resistant) parasites following PAMAFRO control efforts. MIP sequencing across >2,000 loci revealed reduced genetic diversity, increased clonality, and fixation of hrp2-/3- lineages. Population modeling indicated selection coefficient s≥0.03 was required for fixation; genetic bottleneck from malaria control increased drift likelihood 4.5-17-fold. Findings suggest fixation driven by demographic changes plus selective advantage unrelated to RDT use.

articles.headline: Post-transfusion malaria remains rare but underrecognized risk
articles.title_original: Post-transfusion Malaria in Morocco and Non-endemic African Countries: A Systematic Review of Reported Cases.
articles.authors: Harandou M, Nejjari MA, Naciri M, Yahyaoui A, Hami A
articles.details: Systematic review identified 20 published post-transfusion malaria cases in non-endemic African countries, with only 6 described in sufficient detail. Most affected immunocompromised or medically complex recipients. P. falciparum predominated, with fever occurring days post-transfusion and diagnosis via blood smear. Investigation suggested donor asymptomatic carriage from endemic-area origin or travel. Findings highlight need for improved donor risk assessment and hemovigilance.

articles.headline: UAV aerial imagery identifies malaria vector breeding sites in Tanzania
articles.title_original: High-resolution UAV dataset for identification of breeding sites for malaria and dengue vectors in rural and peri-urban Tanzania: An experimental approach with Gemma 3N E4B architecture.
articles.authors: Nyambo DG, Lyimo TM, Sauli E
articles.details: Study used high-resolution UAV imagery from Tanzania (Mtwara and Tabora regions) to identify mosquito breeding sites. From 585 aerial images collected, 471 (80.5%) contained useful information identifying 19 breeding site classes. Gemma 3 N E4B multimodal model was trained on labeled images, achieving exceptional convergence with 96.7% loss reduction (from 9.620 to 0.319) within 117 steps. Approach demonstrates potential for automated surveillance of vector breeding habitats.

articles.headline: Machine learning and molecular docking identify antimalarial compounds
articles.title_original: Gene Expression, Docking and Machine Learning in Malaria Drug Discovery: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Dangana RS, Ebhohimen IE, Malgwi SA, Ugbaja SC, Okpeku M
articles.details: Systematic review of 2014-2024 literature (64 studies) examined computational approaches to malaria drug discovery. Molecular docking (32.4%) was dominant method; compounds like isorhamnetin and myricetin 3-O-glucoside showed high binding affinities (ΔG < -13 kcal/mol) to Plasmepsin II and PfLDH. Machine learning models (random forest, SVM) achieved AUC up to 0.87 for predicting bioactivity and resistance. Flavonoids and terpenoids identified as candidate compounds; targets included haemoglobin degradation and glycolysis pathways.

articles.headline: Communities in Tanzania perceive climate change driving malaria increase
articles.title_original: Community perceptions on climate change and its impacts on malaria transmission in South-eastern Tanzania.
articles.authors: Nshatsi NC, Mponzi WP, Mwalugelo YA, Msaky DS, Simbeye S, Rite E, Okumu FO, Philbert A, Angelo T, Kaindoa EW
articles.details: Mixed-methods study of 384 community members plus 83 participants in interviews/FGDs in SE Tanzania examined climate perceptions and malaria. 86% reported climate changes (altered seasons, flooding); 67.5% linked climate to malaria transmission. 70% perceived higher malaria in rainy seasons; 59.5% reported increased vector-borne disease risk. Only 26.6% regularly received climate-health information despite 96.6% wanting it. Findings highlight need for community-centered climate adaptation strategies.

articles.headline: Resource constraints limit severe malaria guideline adherence in Uganda
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers to adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines in children at Mbale Regional Referral Hospital, Uganda: a health workers' perspective.
articles.authors: Chelangat SC, Nakawuki A, Orech S, Oboth P, Nekaka R, Iramiot JS, Mbwali I, Chalo RN, Epuitai J, Adongo PR, Ssenyonga LVN, Olowo S
articles.details: Qualitative study of 21 healthcare providers at Mbale Regional Referral Hospital identified facilitators and barriers to severe malaria guideline adherence in children. Facilitators included resource availability, optimal working conditions, and manageable workloads. Key barriers were resource constraints, staffing/workload issues, delayed referrals/lab services, and limited working hours. Patient-related factors included financial limitations and cooperation. Study highlights need to address systemic resource shortages and staffing challenges.

articles.headline: Early hominin arrival shaped evolution of human malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Early hominin arrival in Southeast Asia triggered the evolution of major human malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Singh US, Harbach RE, Hii J, Chang MS, Somboon P, Prakash A, Sarma D, Broomfield BS, Morgan K, Albert S, Das A, Linton YM, Carlton JM, Walton C
articles.details: Phylogenomic analysis of 11 Leucosphyrus Group Anopheles species using 2,657 nuclear genes and 13 mitochondrial genes revealed that monkey-feeding behavior is ancestral (Pliocene origin in Sundaland). Anthropophily (human feeding) likely evolved once via adaptive introgression in early Pleistocene, generating multiple descendent anthropophilic species. Origin timing (~1.8 Mya) coincided with early hominin (Homo erectus) arrival in Southeast Asia and rainforest loss/fragmentation. Findings provide independent evidence supporting early human colonization of SE Asia.

articles.headline: ARK1 kinase regulates Plasmodium spindle formation and transmission
articles.title_original: Plasmodium ARK1 regulates spindle formation during atypical mitosis and forms a divergent chromosomal passenger complex.
articles.authors: Nagar A, Yanase R, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Abel S, Pashley SL, Mishra A, Eze A, Rea E, Brady D, Bottrill AR, Vaughan S, Le Roch KG, Guttery DS, Holder AA, Tromer EC, Sharma P, Tewari R
articles.details: Study identified Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) as key regulator of Plasmodium spindle formation and mitotic progression. Conditional ARK1 depletion disrupted spindle biogenesis, kinetochore segregation, and cytokinesis in both schizogony and male gametogony, affecting transmission. Interactome analysis revealed ARK1 forms non-canonical chromosomal passenger complex with divergent INCENPs (lacking canonical Survivin/Borealin). ARK1-INCENP interface identified as potential multistage antimalarial target.

articles.headline: Multi-stage antimalarials enhance seasonal chemoprevention in children
articles.title_original: Combining seasonal malaria chemoprevention with novel therapeutics for malaria prevention: a mathematical modelling study.
articles.authors: Braunack-Mayer L, Malinga J, Nekkab N, Kelly SL, Möhrle JJ, Penny MA
articles.details: Individual-based transmission model evaluated combining novel therapeutics (vaccines, monoclonal antibodies, long-acting injectables) with seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) in children under five. For high-transmission settings with 3 SMC cycles, pre-liver stage therapeutics required protection half-life >230 days for >23% reduction in severe malaria cases during deployment and >5% reduction 5 years post-stop. Multi-stage therapeutics provided greater public health impact. Findings support prioritizing multi-stage candidates for clinical development.

articles.headline: PfATP4 inhibitor compound shows transmission-blocking activity in mice
articles.title_original: Identification of Compound 15 (MMV1581361) as a PfATP4 Inhibitor with Transmission-Blocking Activity and In Vivo Efficacy in a SCID Mouse Model of Malaria.
articles.authors: Onyame HA, Brand S, Baud D, Basarab GS, Hawryluk N, Canan S, Togoh MD, Abdulai R, Eduku R, Awal M, Owoare RB, Chatterjee B, Singha Roy D, Mahapatra AKR, Bhowmik S, Putatunda P, Crespo B, Sanz L, Calvo D, Wittlin S, Tumwebaze PK, Borrows J, Amewu RK
articles.details: Structure-activity optimization of screening hit MMV020136 identified compound 15 (MMV1581361), a PfATP4 inhibitor with nanomolar activity against drug-sensitive and drug-resistant P. falciparum. Single 25 mg/kg dose showed efficacy in humanized mouse model with chloroquine-like kinetics. Notably, compound demonstrated potent transmission-blocking activity: 90% inhibition of male gamete exflagellation, 98% reduction in oocyst intensity, and 88% transmission block. Development was deprioritized due to PfATP4 resistance risk despite advanced in vivo profile.

articles.headline: Corrigendum: Raf kinase inhibitor and PfCDPK1 interactions
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Plasmodium falciparum Raf kinase inhibitor is a lipid binding protein that interacts with and regulates the activity of PfCDPK1, an essential plant-like kinase required for red blood cell invasion" [Biochem. Biophys. Res. Commun. 749 (2025) 151350].
articles.authors: Sharma M, Krishnan D, Singh A, Negi P, Rani K, Revikumar A, Munde M, Bansal A
articles.details: Corrigendum updating a prior publication about Raf kinase inhibitor as a lipid binding protein that interacts with PfCDPK1, an essential plant-like kinase required for red blood cell invasion by P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Nested PCR detects lower malaria prevalence than microscopy in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Unmasking Malaria: Microscopy and nPCR Reveal the Hidden Misdiagnosis of Plasmodium Infections Among Febrile Pediatric in Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Getie S, Mekonnen GG, Worku L, Birhanie M, Meda AL, Abere A, Noedl H
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 302 febrile children in Northwest Ethiopia (March-October 2015) comparing malaria diagnosis methods. Microscopy detected 22.2% Plasmodium prevalence versus 18.2% by nested PCR. Age ≥5 years showed highest prevalence (25.6%). Age, bed net non-use, and lack of symptom awareness were significant risk factors (p<0.05). Emphasizes need for improved microscopy quality control.

articles.headline: Anopheles cruzii comprises at least five cryptic species
articles.title_original: Anopheles (Kerteszia) cruzii, the main malaria vector in the Brazilian Atlantic Forest, is a complex of at least five cryptic species.
articles.authors: Voges K, Dias GR, Dupim EG, Pitaluga AN, Vanderlinde T, Carvalho Pinto CJ, Rezende HR, Uno F, Forrester SJ, Chong J, Carvalho AB, Rona LDP
articles.details: Genomic analysis of 55 Anopheles specimens identified at least 5 cryptic species within An. cruzii sensu lato and 3 distinct lineages in An. bellator, with high genetic differentiation (FST 0.4-0.7). Species showed sympatry in some locations, suggesting vector competence may vary among cryptic species, impacting Atlantic Forest malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Host kinases regulate Plasmodium vivax liver-stage development variability
articles.title_original: Host kinase regulation of Plasmodium vivax dormant and replicating liver stages.
articles.authors: Glennon EKK, Wei L, Roobsoong W, Primavera VI, van Zyl EM, Tongogara T, Yee CB, Sattabongkot J, Kaushansky A
articles.details: Kinase regression study evaluating host kinase activity effects on P. vivax liver-stage parasites from multiple patient isolates. Identified host kinases regulating schizont and hypnozoite numbers and schizont size. Found variability in host phosphosignaling dependencies between parasite forms and across isolates, suggesting one source of heterogeneity in field P. vivax relapse phenotypes.

articles.headline: Bradykinin contributes to vasogenic edema in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Bradykinin Contributes to Vasogenic Edema in Murine Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Pinheiro AS, Teixeira DE, Silva-Aguiar RP, Shim YJ, Merkulova AA, Silbak S, Skomorovska-Prokvolit Y, Midem D, Ogolla S, Burckhardt BB, Gangnus T, Scharfstein J, Caruso-Neves C, McCarty OJT, Gailani D, Bader M, Rosenthal PJ, Dent AE, Janse CJ, McCrae KR, Pinheiro AAS, Kazura JW, Schmaier AH
articles.details: Clinical and murine study showing bradykinin (BK) contributes to cerebral malaria pathogenesis. 40% of CNS malaria children versus 18% uncomplicated malaria had circulating markers of BK release (cleaved kininogen). Genetic deletion of kallikrein-kinin pathway components protected mice from neurologic deterioration and brain edema. Pharmacological PRCP inhibition combined with artesunate reversed neurologic deterioration in P. berghei-infected mice.

articles.headline: GFP-DD reporter assay quantifies live Plasmodium proteasome activity
articles.title_original: Profiling 26S Proteasome Activity of Plasmodium falciparum Monitored by a Live-Cell Assay.
articles.authors: Gonçalves AF, Lima-Pinheiro A, Sampaio-Marques B, Ferreira PE
articles.details: Study developed a live-cell GFP-DD reporter assay to monitor 26S proteasome activity in P. falciparum. GFP-DD fluorescence inversely correlates with proteasomal degradation and increases upon ligand stabilization (Shield-1) or MG132 proteasome inhibition. Dose-dependent response to MG132 confirmed. Establishes platform for phenotypic screening of proteasome inhibitors and artemisinin-resistance studies.

articles.headline: Scoping review of malaria trends in urban Bamako
articles.title_original: Trends in malaria indicators in urban and peri-urban areas of Bamako: a scoping review protocol.
articles.authors: Toure M, Keita S, Keita B, Arou AZ, Diallo I, Keita M, Kane F, Sanogo D, Diallo M, Tangara CO, Kone A, Magassa MH, Sogoba N, Diakite M, Doumbia S
articles.details: Scoping review protocol examining malaria epidemiology trends in urban and peri-urban Bamako from 2002-2025. Will synthesize data on prevalence, incidence, clinical manifestations, morbidity, mortality, and burden to identify transmission shifts and inform tailored urban control strategies addressing rapid urbanization and emerging vectors like Anopheles stephensi.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum malaria prevalent in West Cameroon alongside falciparum
articles.title_original: Distribution of falciparum and non-falciparum malaria among symptomatic malaria patients in Dschang, West Region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Tchuenkam PVK, White SJ, Potlapalli V, Keming EM, Muller MS, Kaunda DBN, Kharabora O, Dinglasan RR, Parr JB, Tume CB, Lin JT, Juliano JJ, Ali IM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 431 febrile patients in West Cameroon (2020) found overall malaria prevalence 54.8% [95% CI 50.1-59.8]. P. falciparum dominated at 53.4%, but P. ovale at 4% and P. malariae at 0.9% were common, with 8.9% mixed infections. Both P. ovale subspecies (6 curtisi, 3 wallikeri) detected among 12 genotyped P. ovale. Emphasizes need for continued non-falciparum species monitoring.

articles.headline: Eight antenatal visits increase malaria prevention in pregnancy uptake
articles.title_original: Impact of eight or more antenatal care visits on intermittent preventive treatment of malaria uptake during pregnancy and facility-based delivery in Ghana: a propensity score matched analysis.
articles.authors: Agbi DM, Mwebesa E, Jimmy AI, Valère SK, Kerich GK
articles.details: Propensity score matched analysis of 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey examined eight or more antenatal care (ANC8+) visits' impact on intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp-SP). Among women, 38.7% attended 8+ visits, 85.4% delivered in facility, 66.5% received ≥3 IPTp-SP doses. ANC8+ attendance increased facility delivery by 8.2 percentage points (ATT 8.2, 95% CI 4.6-11.9) and optimal IPTp-SP by 11.1 percentage points (ATT 11.1, 95% CI 6.8-15.4).

articles.headline: Malaria parasite RNA disrupts host immune cell splicing
articles.title_original: Nuclear import of malaria RNA rewires splicing in host immune cells.
articles.authors: Abou Karam P, Kiper E, Ziv T, Yadid S, Kozela E, Zharoni N, Nevo R, Alfandari D, Otesh H, Cruz Camacho A, Lubelsky Y, Rotkopf R, Weizman E, Cossin M, Rosenhek-Goldian I, Petrovich-Kopitman E, Porat Z, Shoshani O, Ulitsky I, Levy C, Melamed Z, Regev-Rudzki N
articles.details: Mechanistic study showing P. falciparum early transcribed membrane protein mRNAs are exported from infected RBCs, evade cytoplasmic degradation, and are imported into host immune cell nuclei where they bind splicing machinery components (ACIN1, PNN). This interaction disrupts host splicing regulation, causing widespread transcript misprocessing and altered expression of immune function proteins.

articles.headline: Diagnostic methods for protozoan diseases in Brazil
articles.title_original: Diagnostic methods for protozoan diseases: a review focused on leishmaniasis, Chagas disease and malaria.
articles.authors: Matos ADS, Rodrigues-da-Silva RN, Toledo TS, Ataides LS, da Motta ND, Rodolphi CM, Soares IF, Ribeiro FN, Alencar ALC, Maia FM, Lima-Junior JDC, Morgado FN
articles.details: Review examining diagnostic approaches for three protozoan diseases—leishmaniasis, Chagas disease, and malaria. Discusses immunological and molecular diagnostic tools and emphasizes integration of clinical and epidemiological information with sensitive and specific diagnostic techniques. Highlights diagnostic role as strategic tool for early detection, monitoring, and control of infections in endemic areas.

articles.headline: Malnutrition increases malaria risk in children receiving seasonal chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Malaria and malnutrition in children under the Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) coverage in the health district of Nanoro, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Compaore EW, Sondo P, Kabore B, Rouamba T, Kouevi AFC, Bayala IMC, Kambou SAE, Millogo KS, Bouda I, Ouedraogo DF, Hien SF, Kazienga A, Derra K, Rouamba E, Gneme A, Tinto H
articles.details: Longitudinal survey of 425 children (6-59 months) in Nanoro, Burkina Faso (2020-2022) during seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) delivery. Among 260 malnourished and 165 adequately nourished children, malaria incidence was 974.47 versus 214.47 per 1000 child-months. Malnourished children had 1.42-fold increased risk of uncomplicated malaria (95% CI 1.03-1.94, p=0.028). Recommends combining SMC with nutrient supplementation.

articles.headline: Malaria infection drivers in Chinese workforce in Uganda
articles.title_original: Epidemiological characteristics, prevention and control status, and factors associated with malaria infection among overseas Chinese workforce in Uganda.
articles.authors: Ji J, Gao X, Lei L, Yang T, Zhou A, Wang Y, Chen L, Cui Q, Zhu Y
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 798 overseas Chinese workers in Uganda (2022–2023) found 44.5% self-reported malaria history. Male sex (AOR 1.92), longer work duration (AOR 1.38), negative prevention attitudes (AOR 2.49), high mosquito biting frequency (AOR 1.31), and nearby malaria cases (AOR 1.95) increased infection risk; bachelor's degree or higher (AOR 0.65) and living with spouse (AOR 0.57) were protective.

articles.headline: Thrombotic disseminated intravascular coagulation in severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation With a Predominant Thrombotic Phenotype in Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Gisca E, Santos MG, Costa S, Santos CR, Araújo A
articles.details: Case report of 61-year-old with severe P. falciparum malaria (parasitemia >25%) from Angola complicated by disseminated intravascular coagulation with thrombotic phenotype, presenting with profound thrombocytopenia, elevated D-dimer, metabolic acidosis, and multiorgan dysfunction. Despite intravenous artesunate and anticoagulation, the patient developed progressive peripheral ischemia requiring amputation and died, illustrating an underrecognized thrombotic-predominant presentation.

articles.headline: Limited evidence on malaria programs during COVID-19 pandemic
articles.title_original: Implementation of malaria control programmes during the COVID-19 pandemic in the Southern African Development Community Elimination 8 countries: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Muzamhindo DN, Chironda G, Tsoka-Gwegweni JM
articles.journal: Afr J Prim Health Care Fam Med
articles.details: Scoping review of malaria control implementation during COVID-19 in SADC E8 countries (targeted for elimination by 2030). Of 658 articles screened, only 7 met inclusion criteria with most published in 2021; no publications in 2020. Review identified significant research gaps on malaria programs during pandemic disruptions, calling for policies to maintain program continuity during future health emergencies.

articles.headline: Microhaplotype diversity predicts malaria prevalence across Uganda
articles.title_original: Accuracy of Plasmodium falciparum genetic data for estimating parasite prevalence and malaria incidence in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kiyaga S, Mbabazi M, Katairo T, Kabbale KD, Asua V, Nsengimaana B, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Mwubaha C, Nakasaanya J, Watyekele E, Ayitewala A, Tukwasibwe S, Mulondo J, Nsobya SL, Agaba B, Maiteki-Sebuguzi C, Kamya MR, Kateete DP, Nabende JN, Jjingo D, Mboowa G, Batte C, Ssewanyana I, Aranda-Díaz A, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ, Conrad M, Greenhouse B, Briggs J
articles.details: Cross-sectional study using amplicon deep sequencing of 165 microhaplotypes in 3,563 symptomatic P. falciparum cases from 26 Ugandan health facilities. Parasite prevalence ranged 5.0–49.23% and incidence 91–1,667 cases per 1,000 person-years across sites. Complexity of infection (COI) and effective COI reliably predicted prevalence (r=0.79), while between-host relatedness provided best incidence prediction (r=0.37), supporting genomic metrics for transmission surveillance.

articles.headline: Cross-border movement and malaria importation in Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Role of cross-border movement on the importation of malaria cases in Mudzi district, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Fambirai T, Chimbari M
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 619 malaria-positive cases in border district Mudzi, Zimbabwe (females 53.47%). Only 16.32% (101/619) reported cross-border movement history, predominantly aged 10–49 years (96%); 7.92% were Mozambique residents. High proportion of cases without documented cross-border travel suggests local transmission remains the primary burden despite border proximity.

articles.headline: Correction: blood purification therapy in severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Correction: The blood purification therapy-based strategy effectively rescued the severe falciparum malaria patient experiencing cytokine storm-driven MODS.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Huang X, Hong B, Song J, Lu Y, Ma J, Wang X, Hu W, Zhang Y, Xuan H
articles.details: Correction notice to a prior article on blood purification therapy in severe P. falciparum malaria with cytokine storm and multiorgan dysfunction.

articles.headline: High frequencies of dhfr/dhps mutations in Kenyan falciparum
articles.title_original: Persistent and Circulating Plasmodium falciparum dhfr and dhps Mutations in Busia County, Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ndung'u L, Thiong'o K, Karani L, Gitahi S, Kimani F, Ngugi MP, Kiboi D
articles.details: Molecular survey of 66 P. falciparum isolates from western Kenya (Nov 2024–Jan 2025) using PCR and sequencing of drug-resistance markers. High prevalence of dhfr mutations (N51I 85.2%, C59R 75.4%, S108N 32.8%, S108T 19.7%) and dhps mutations (A437G 92.3%, K540E 40%) detected. Circulation of classical triple-mutant (N51I+C59R+S108N with dhps A437G) and rare quintuple haplotypes indicates ongoing selection pressure from sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine use.

articles.headline: Temperature-responsive chromatin remodeling in falciparum ring stages
articles.title_original: Integration of ATAC-seq and RNA-seq reveals temperature-responsive regulatory regions in Plasmodium falciparum asexual stages.
articles.authors: Zhang Y, Zhao Y, Zheng G, He S, Xiao J, Ma H, Huang J, Qi Y
articles.details: Integrated ATAC-seq and RNA-seq analysis of P. falciparum asexual stages (ring and trophozoite) at human body temperature (37°C) versus mosquito-mimicking temperature (26°C). Low temperature induced 1,083 differentially accessible chromatin regions (1,081 gains) predominantly in promoters in ring-stage parasites, enriched in genes for host-cell interactions and antigenic variation, with 41 genes showing concordant chromatin accessibility and expression changes; trophozoites showed no significant reconfiguration.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic P. vivax in Argentina despite malaria-free status
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic and submicroscopic Plasmodium vivax infection: a study previous to malaria-free certification in Argentina.
articles.authors: Dantur-Juri MJ, Coello-Peralta RD, Calle-Atariguana DE, Arevalo-Bozada MM, Duque PL, González-Piñeres N, Rougeron V, Degrugillier F, Dentice-Maidana S, Liria-Salazar J, Zaidenberg MO
articles.details: Retrospective molecular surveillance of 92 blood samples from Argentina collected during 2013–2018 malaria surveillance program, analyzed in 2022 using cytochrome b sequencing. One asymptomatic, submicroscopic P. vivax infection was detected in a 66-year-old from northwestern Argentina with no travel history to endemic regions, representing the first molecularly confirmed submicroscopic P. vivax infection in the Southern Cone region and raising concerns about silent circulation.

articles.headline: Knowledge-practice gap in malaria prevention among Fulani pregnant women
articles.title_original: Knowledge of malaria and preventive practices among Fulani pregnant women in the savannah Region of Northern Ghana.
articles.authors: Salifu Y, Nantomah B, Lasong J
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 159 nomadic Fulani pregnant women in Northern Ghana (April–June 2022). While 61% had good malaria knowledge and 54.7% owned insecticide-treated nets, only 28.9% used them the previous night; 71.1% demonstrated poor preventive practices overall. Antenatal care attendance was protective for knowledge (AOR 0.31), while more children predicted poor practice (AOR 0.29), indicating knowledge-practice gaps related to implementation barriers.

articles.headline: Climate-driven Anopheles forecasting model predicts malaria outbreak risk
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal modelling and prediction of Anopheles mosquito abundance in Tanga and Unguja, Tanzania: climatic drivers and insights for malaria early warning and vector control strategies.
articles.authors: Njotto LL, Kulaya NB, Derua YA, Malongo BB, Francis F, Schiøler KL, Hansson H, Wang CW, Saleh F, Baraka V, Alifrangis M, van der Deure T, Senyoni W, Cronie O, Stensgaard AS
articles.details: Spatio-temporal study of adult Anopheles abundance (4,312 Tanga, 1,450 Unguja) sampled monthly over 24 months using Generalized Additive Mixed Models with climate covariates. Region-specific drivers: in Tanga, higher maximum temperature and precipitation (concurrent and 2-month lag) increased abundance; in Unguja, non-linear temperature (peak <27.5°C) and 2-month precipitation lag were significant. Models predicted Anopheles peaks in Unguja in November 2023, preceding documented malaria surge in late 2023, demonstrating potential as early warning system.

articles.headline: Anopheles blood meal sources and Plasmodium detection in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Molecular xenomonitoring for identifying Plasmodium parasites and blood meal sources of mosquitoes in malaria endemic villages adjacent to the southern Rift Valley Lakes, South Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Eligo N, Woldeyes D, Tamiru G, Jima B, Wudneh F, Wegayehu T, Wondale B, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: Cross-sectional entomological survey of 446 Anopheles mosquitoes from 12 endemic villages in South Ethiopia using CDC light traps and molecular xenomonitoring. Plasmodium vivax detected in 2.0% (9/446) and P. falciparum in 0.2% (1/446) of specimens. Blood meal analysis (85% of mosquitoes) identified goats as primary source (67%), followed by humans (23%), cattle (19%), and dogs (13%). Anopheles arabiensis dominated (85% of 9 species).

articles.headline: Precipitation increases malaria risk in Mali children; temperature effect unclear
articles.title_original: Associations between precipitation, temperature, and malaria prevalence in children under 5 in Mali.
articles.authors: Hayden NJT, Syer J, Yanow SK, Yamamoto SS
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 2012–2021 Mali DHS/MIS data examined RDT-diagnosed malaria prevalence in children aged 6–59 months in relation to precipitation and temperature. With a 3-month lag, precipitation significantly increased malaria odds (OR 1.005, 95% CI 1.002–1.009), while minimum, maximum, and average temperatures decreased odds (OR 0.761, 0.871, 0.822 respectively). The authors conclude precipitation is a malaria risk factor, but temperature alone appears insufficient to explain changes in malaria prevalence.

articles.headline: Malaria Complex III has dual role in protein processing and electron transport
articles.title_original: Dual function of mitochondrial complex III in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rell RS, Shukla A, Morrisey JM, Okoye IC, Mather MW, Vaidya AB
articles.details: Mechanistic study in P. falciparum showed Complex III of the mitochondrial electron transport chain serves dual functions: ubiquinone regeneration and mitochondrial protein processing via MPPα. Conditional knockdown revealed MPPα is essential for parasite survival even when electron transport requirements are bypassed, and MPPα depletion increased parasite sensitivity to proguanil. Proteomics identified MPPα associations with multiple mitochondrial proteins, suggesting Complex III integrates both redox and protein-processing functions.

articles.headline: Genomics distinguishes imported from locally acquired malaria in Malaysia elimination efforts
articles.title_original: Whole genome sequencing to inform the epidemiology of Plasmodium falciparum malaria in the elimination setting of Malaysia.
articles.authors: Tan MKI, Billows N, Divis PCS, Daneshvar C, Edgeworth J, Singh JC, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing analysis of 21 P. falciparum isolates from Malaysia (2008–2010) using nanopore sequencing compared to 9,518 reference isolates revealed that 13 of 15 cases likely originated from endemic regions outside Malaysia, supported by travel histories and high multiplicity of infection. Identity-by-descent analysis showed clustering in only 2 cases, indicating isolated introductions rather than sustained local transmission. The authors conclude genomic approaches effectively distinguish imported from locally acquired cases in malaria-elimination settings.

articles.headline: Transmembrane proteins BTP2 and BLEB direct parasite cell division and separation
articles.title_original: Transmembrane proteins mediate basal complex assembly and individual daughter cell formation in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Back PS, Wagner E, Llopis PM, de Oliveira RG, Mottinelli M, Ferrins L, Dvorin JD
articles.details: Expansion microscopy and nanoscale-imaging studies of three transmembrane proteins in the P. falciparum basal complex (BTP1, BTP2, BLEB) revealed that BTP2 is essential for daughter-cell separation; parasites lacking BTP2 remain enveloped by membranes but fail to separate, while contractile function persists. BLEB marks a plasma-membrane region excluded from daughter cells. The authors propose a three-step biogenesis model integrating these findings and implicating transmembrane proteins in coordinating simultaneous daughter-cell formation.

articles.headline: Carbamate resistance in Anopheles gambiae vectors: acetylcholinesterase mutations and metabolic detoxification
articles.title_original: Mechanisms of carbamate resistance in the malaria vector: Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Cleanclay WD, Akanni MH, Bajepade TI, Ajeoge JK, Zakari S
articles.details: Review synthesizing evidence on carbamate resistance mechanisms in Anopheles gambiae, the primary malaria vector in Africa. Carbamates inhibit acetylcholinesterase to cause mosquito paralysis and death; resistance arises through target-site mutations in the acetylcholinesterase gene and enhanced detoxification via esterases and cytochrome P450s. The authors emphasize that understanding these mechanisms is critical for managing insecticide resistance and maintaining indoor residual spraying (IRS) effectiveness in areas of pyrethroid resistance.

articles.headline: HRP2-RDT and microscopy show lower malaria detection sensitivity than PCR
articles.title_original: Diagnostic challenges in malaria detection: A comparative diagnostic performance of HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests, microscopy, and PCR at Bichena primary hospital, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Minwuyelet A, Yewhalaw D, Atenafu G
articles.details: Cross-sectional diagnostic comparison in 274 Ethiopian participants (Dec 2024–Feb 2025) evaluated HRP2-RDT, microscopy, nested PCR, and hematology. PCR detected 23.4% infections, microscopy 20.1%, and HRP2-RDT 19%; HRP2-RDT showed lower sensitivity (79.1%), NPV (94.1%), and accuracy (94.9%) versus PCR. Microscopy had 100% specificity and PPV but only 85.9% sensitivity. The authors conclude both methods missed cases detected by PCR, potentially due to low parasitemia and pfhrp2 deletions.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Ethiopia linked to ecology, health systems, and behavior
articles.title_original: Understanding malaria resurgence in the Amhara Region, northwestern Ethiopia: a qualitative study of perceived drivers from stakeholder and community perspectives.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Gelaye KA, Asemahagn MA, Muchie KF, Yenesew MA
articles.details: Qualitative study in Ethiopia (Feb 2025) conducted 28 key-informant interviews, 2 focus groups (n=20), and 12 in-depth interviews to understand malaria resurgence in Amhara Region, where confirmed cases rose from 1.0 million in 2018 to >7.3 million by Oct 2024. Participants identified ecological drivers (irregular rainfall, irrigation, water storage), health-system challenges (stockouts of drugs/diagnostics, delayed vector control), and behavioral factors (low perceived risk, traditional healers, net misuse). Authors conclude locally tailored, equity-focused interventions integrating behavioral insights with strengthened supply chains and surveillance are needed.

articles.headline: Expired malaria RDTs remain valid for up to nine months post-expiration
articles.title_original: Test Performance of Expired Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Tests: A pilot diagnostic accuracy study conducted in Western Uganda.
articles.authors: Ndizeye R, Gunderson AK, Baguma E, Giandomenico DA, Ntaro M, Mulogo EM, Boyce RM
articles.details: Pilot diagnostic-accuracy study of 200 participants in western Uganda assessed whether expired mRDTs (SD Bioline Ag Pf/Pan, First Response Ag pLDH/HRP2) performed consistently with non-expired reference tests. All tests up to 9 months post-expiration were valid and consistent; all tests 10–12 months post-expiration were invalid. The authors conclude these preliminary findings warrant further research into the utility of expired mRDTs in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: ITN use reduces malaria odds by 13% in Ghanaian children under five
articles.title_original: Evaluating the effectiveness of insecticide-treated net use in preventing malaria among children under five: a quasi-experimental study.
articles.authors: Osei KM, Adiak AA, Han W
articles.details: Quasi-experimental analysis of nationally representative Ghana survey data examined ITN effectiveness in 11,481 children using multilevel regression and propensity-score weighting. ITN use was associated with 13% reduction in malaria odds (adjusted OR 0.87, 95% CI 0.81–0.93) and 2.2% absolute risk reduction. Spatial analyses revealed misalignment between intervention coverage and malaria burden. The authors conclude ITN provides modest but meaningful protection, with contextual and behavioral factors likely explaining the limited effect size.

articles.headline: YOLOv3 deep learning framework enables automated Plasmodium vivax blood-smear detection
articles.title_original: Enhanced YOLO-based framework and benchmarking for automated Plasmodium vivax detection.
articles.authors: Prathap VM, Yadav S
articles.details: Comparative evaluation of YOLO object-detection variants (v3, v4, v5, v8) for automated P. vivax parasite identification from blood smears. The authors propose a novel framework combining YOLOv3 with a modified MobileNetV2 backbone and Transformed Convolutional Layer for multi-scale texture analysis of dense thick-smear images. The approach achieves improved precision, recall, and mean Average Precision compared to baseline models and is proposed for deployment in clinical and field settings.

articles.headline: Moxifloxacin inhibits malaria plasmepsin II via catalytic dyad hydrogen bonding
articles.title_original: Physico-Chemical Insights into the Catalytic Inhibition of Plasmodium falciparum Plasmepsin II Activity by Repurposing Fluoroquinolone Drugs.
articles.authors: Syed SF, Thakur P, Patil M, Choudhary S
articles.details: Mechanistic study evaluated fluoroquinolone drugs (ofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin) as inhibitors of P. falciparum plasmepsin II (PlmII), an aspartic protease essential for hemoglobin degradation. Moxifloxacin showed the most potent inhibition (IC50 0.15±0.02 μM) through hydrogen bonding with the catalytic dyad (Asp34 and Asp214). Molecular docking and quantum mechanics calculations confirmed this binding mode, with donor-acceptor distances of 2.8–3.3 Å consistent with strong hydrogen bonding. The authors conclude precise catalytic interactions are essential for effective PlmII inhibition.

articles.headline: Primaquine plus chloroquine rapidly reduces vivax transmission in Ethiopia trial
articles.title_original: Efficacy of primaquine-chloroquine combination on Plasmodium vivax transmission and parasite clearance in Ethiopia: a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Melaku M, Wondale B, Tamiru G, Getu R, Kumsa T, Tesfaye Y, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: Open-label RCT in Ethiopia enrolled patients aged ≥15 years with P. vivax mono-infection (n=56 completed transmission studies, 49 completed 28-day follow-up). Primaquine 0.25 mg/kg daily×14 days plus chloroquine versus chloroquine alone. By day 1 post-treatment, primaquine-chloroquine showed significantly greater reductions in mosquito infectivity (P<0.0001), oocyst counts (P<0.001), and circumsporozoite protein presence (P=0.002). Gametocyte counts were lower at day 1 (P=0.02). No difference in asexual parasite clearance by day 3 (P=0.42). The authors conclude primaquine addition rapidly reduces transmission despite some patients remaining infectious at 24 hours.

articles.headline: An. gambiae dominates malaria vectors in Ethiopian highlands
articles.title_original: Species Occurrence and Seasonal Variation of Malaria Vectors in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Assemie A, Mulu D, Mekuriaw E, Muluken W
articles.details: Entomological survey of 721 larvae collected October 2023–June 2024 across four villages in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia, using morphological identification. An. gambiae s.l. was predominant (43.82%, n=316), with An. pharoensis least abundant (7.9%, n=57). Larval density varied by habitat: ditches (2.61/sample), swamps (1.5), riverbeds (0.8), water pans (0.14); mean 1.11 larvae/sample (p=0.012). Conclusion: An. gambiae is the primary local transmission vector.

articles.headline: Age and socioeconomic factors drive malaria disparities in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic and healthcare determinants of malaria infection among children under five in Burkina Faso: analysis of the 2021 Demographic and Health Survey data.
articles.authors: Kinda R, Gansane A, Traore TI, Kaboré NF, Debe S, Sore H, Nonkani WG, Guelbéogo MW, Tougri G, Tarama CW, Ilboudo SR, Sanou GS, Savadogo LGB
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 5674 children aged 6-59 months from 2021 Burkina Faso DHS using modified Poisson regression. Overall malaria prevalence 14.1% (95% CI 13-15), with substantial regional variation (5.1%-29.3%). Risk increased with age (aPR: 1.46-2.07 for 13-35 months) and was higher in rural areas (aPR 2.21), electricity-deprived households (aPR 2.01), and poor households (aPR 1.60). Conclusion: Multiple individual and contextual vulnerabilities drive malaria disparities.

articles.headline: Plasmodium retrocopies contribute to virulence-gene family expansion
articles.title_original: Comprehensive computational insights into Plasmodium retrocopies reveals potential roles in virulence-associated functions.
articles.authors: Das S, Ghosh S, Thakur S
articles.details: Computational analysis identified 724 high-confidence retrocopies across 23 Plasmodium species genomes. Retrocopies were frequently intact and under purifying selection, with detectable transcription during intraerythrocytic stages; some showed FPKM values comparable to or exceeding parental genes. Domain and pathway enrichment analyses linked retrocopies to cytoadhesion, chromatin remodeling, stress response, and cytoskeletal organization, with episodic expansions in primate-infecting lineages. Conclusion: Retroposition is an underappreciated mechanism contributing to Plasmodium genomic plasticity and virulence.

articles.headline: α-Thalassemia shows no significant protection against P. falciparum carriage
articles.title_original: α-Thalassemia has no association with asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum carriage in three ecological zones of Ghana.
articles.authors: Donkor AB, Bernasko FG, Abdulai A, Eyeson NAS, Abban RL, Boadu EA, Kusi KA, Afrane YA, Amoah LE
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 1401 asymptomatic individuals across Ghana's three ecological zones analyzed for α-thalassemia genotypes and P. falciparum carriage using multiplex PCR and RDT/microscopy/PCR. Malaria prevalence varied by zone (23.5%-36.5%, p<0.0001). Homozygous recessive individuals showed numerically higher odds of asexual parasites (OR 1.23) and gametocytes (OR 1.46) vs. wild type, but differences were not statistically significant (p=0.447). Conclusion: No significant association between α-thalassemia genotype and P. falciparum carriage detected.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi awareness suboptimal in high-risk Thai communities
articles.title_original: Community awareness and health providers' perspectives on zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi malaria in Thailand: A mixed-methods assessment.
articles.authors: Sripoorote P, Inthitanon N, Wattanagoon Y, Cui L, Nguitragool W, Fornace K, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM, Aung PL
articles.details: Mixed-methods study in southern Thailand assessing awareness of P. knowlesi malaria among 300 adults in three endemic districts via cross-sectional survey and 28 healthcare-provider interviews. Mean awareness score was 10.7/23 (SD ± 2.9; range 3.0-20.0). Female individuals, those living farther from health facilities, those receiving malaria education, and those with higher attitude scores showed significantly higher awareness (p<0.05). Conclusion: Awareness remains suboptimal; integrated, proactive, male-focused health education is needed.

articles.headline: pir gene upregulation associated with severe P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Comparative clinical transcriptome of pir genes in severe Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Boopathi PA, Singh S, Roy P, Tahbildar S, Kochar SK, Kochar DK, Das A
articles.details: Comparative transcriptomic study using custom microarrays on parasites from 23 Indian patients: uncomplicated (n=6), hepatic dysfunction (n=12), cerebral malaria (n=5). Parasites were analyzed via microarray hybridization identifying differentially expressed pir genes. Cerebral malaria: 24 pir genes upregulated, including vir subfamily E (n=8) and pvpir subfamily H (n=6). Hepatic dysfunction: 28 pir genes upregulated, including vir subfamilies E (n=9) and C (n=6). Conclusion: pir gene subfamily patterns are associated with severe P. vivax manifestations.

articles.headline: Folic acid supplementation shows minimal interaction with antifolate antimalarials
articles.title_original: Folic acid supplementation and malaria susceptibility and severity among people taking antifolate antimalarial drugs in endemic areas.
articles.authors: Qi YP, Crider KS, Gutman J, Centeno-Tablante E, Fothergill A, Daniels K, Yeung LF, Mai CT, Mehta S, Williams JL, Finkelstein JL
articles.details: Cochrane systematic review of 8 trials (3486 participants) evaluating folic acid supplementation effects on antifolate antimalarial efficacy, including prevention and treatment trials using sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, atovaquone-proguanil, and proguanil. In one prevention trial, little to no difference in parasitaemia was observed between pregnant women receiving folic acid (with iron) plus antifolate vs. iron plus antifolate (RR 1.21; 95% CI 0.56–2.62; p=0.63; n=643). Most treatment trials used doses exceeding the tolerable upper intake level (>1.0 mg/day). Conclusion: Limited evidence suggests folic acid supplementation does not substantially interfere with antimalarial drug efficacy.

articles.headline: Medieval malaria prevalence in Netherlands tracked via skeletal markers
articles.title_original: Mapping medieval malaria in the Netherlands: A bioarchaeological approach using cribra orbitalia as a proxy.
articles.authors: Schats R
articles.details: Spatial epidemiological analysis of cribra orbitalia (indicating chronic anemia from malaria) across 28 medieval archaeological sites in the Netherlands. Global Moran's I confirmed significant positive spatial autocorrelation; Local Indicator of Spatial Association identified High-High clusters in the Southwest and Low-Low clusters in the East. Linear regression failed to explain patterns with broad-scale environmental variables. Cribra orbitalia prevalence was significantly positively correlated with historical (1938) estimates of Anopheles atroparvus density. Conclusion: Malaria likely contributed importantly to disease burden in certain medieval Dutch regions.

articles.headline: Adaptive immunity imposes reproduction-survival tradeoff on malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Immunity can impose a reproduction-survival tradeoff on human malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Patterson DD, Childs LM, Stopard IJ, Chitnis N, Serrato-Arroyo S, Greischar MA
articles.details: Mathematical within-host model incorporating adaptive immunity to track malaria parasite dynamics across infection age. When transmission investment is constant, increased investment reduces infection duration and parasite fitness. Model shows optimal transmission investment ~5%, lower than predicted by immunity-independent models. When strategies vary with infection age, parasites benefit from delaying transmission to allow faster within-host multiplication. Conclusion: Adaptive immunity can impose a reproduction-survival tradeoff, affecting parasite infectiousness timing and evolutionary responses to control.

articles.headline: P. ovale species show seasonal antagonism with P. falciparum in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Seasonal Variation and Interspecies Dynamics Among Plasmodium falciparum and ovale Species in Bagamoyo, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Carey-Ewend K, Marten A, Muller J, Peter EE, Odas M, Dominick MC, Muller M, Chhetri S, Amagai K, Rutha I, Kisandu F, Beka L, Kharabora O, Popkin-Hall ZR, Bailey J, Edwards JK, Gower EW, Juliano JJ, Ngasala BE, Lin JT
articles.details: PCR-based screening of 7173 asymptomatic individuals (aged ≥5 years) in coastal Tanzania over 5 years (2018-2022) using real-time 18S rRNA PCR and species-specific assays for Poc/Pow detection. P. falciparum prevalence 27.4% (associated with younger age, male sex, higher rainfall); P. ovale prevalence 11.5% (no significant age/sex/rainfall associations). P. ovale peaked during months with lower P. falciparum prevalence, with fewer Pf-Po co-infections than expected by chance. Poc/Pow co-infections were common (23% of Po-positive isolates). Conclusion: P. ovale species show complex seasonal and co-transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Most submicroscopic P. vivax infections clear spontaneously in Thailand
articles.title_original: Spontaneous Clearance of Polymerase Chain Reaction-Positive Plasmodium vivax Parasitemia in Northwestern Thailand: An Epidemiological Perspective.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Petchvijit P, Suk-Aum K, Htwe KZZ, Lawpoolsri S, Nguitragool W, Cui L, Kaewkungwal J, Sattabongkot J
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort study in northwestern Thailand (2018-2023) with 14 rounds of mass blood surveys screening ~5000 individuals by microscopy and PCR for P. vivax. Of 210 total P. vivax infections, 154 were PCR-positive only (submicroscopic); 101 met inclusion criteria for follow-up analysis. Most infections (72/101, 69.9%) cleared spontaneously within 3 subsequent surveys. Turnbull survival analysis showed persistent infection probability 25.0% (95% CI 4.0-100.0) by day 47 and 7.0% (95% CI 1.0-38.0) by day 232. Conclusion: Naturally acquired immunity enables spontaneous clearance of submicroscopic P. vivax parasitemia.

articles.headline: P. vivax source-sink dynamics threaten Amazon malaria elimination
articles.title_original: The Source-Sink Dynamics of Plasmodium vivax May Undermine Malaria Elimination Efforts in the Amazon: An Epidemiological and Population Genomic Study.
articles.authors: de Albuquerque NRM, Corder RM, Johansen IC, Ladeia WA, Rodrigues PT, Ladeia-Andrade S, Silva JC, Ferreira MU
articles.details: Analysis of 5.5 million malaria case notifications (2003-2023) and whole-genome sequencing of 408 P. vivax isolates across South America to characterize parasite dispersal in the Brazilian Amazon. Nearly one-third of infections in Brazilian residents were acquired outside their home municipality; only 1.7% were imported from other South American countries. Large cities (Manaus, Porto Velho) function as parasite sinks surrounded by high-risk rural source localities. Parasites >1000 km apart remained genetically connected. Conclusion: Source-sink dynamics require targeted interventions at high-risk mobile populations and source localities to achieve elimination.

articles.headline: Peptide vaccine combining CSP and mosquito AgTRIO improves survival in mice
articles.title_original: A Multivalent Peptide Vaccine Against Malaria, Targeting Plasmodium Circumsporozoite Protein and Mosquito AgTRIO.
articles.authors: Chuang YM, Ledizet M, Mattessich M, Fikrig E
articles.details: Researchers developed a chimeric peptide vaccine (PfAg) containing regions from P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein and mosquito AgTRIO. Mice immunized with PfAg generated robust antibody responses to both antigens. After exposure to P. berghei-infected mosquitoes, PfAg-immunized mice showed significantly improved survival compared with controls.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic factors and water storage shape malaria prevention in urban India
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic and household water management determinants of malaria and other vector-borne disease prevention in Urban Gujarat, India.
articles.authors: Batheja D, Samria D, Wimberly MC, Pascual M, Baharia RK, Mohanty AK, Desai V, Vaishnav K, Sharma R, Kohli V, Sharma S, Anvikar A, Murdock C, Nandi A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 4,074 households in Ahmedabad and Surat during 2023 found 77 malaria/dengue/chikungunya cases (18.90 per 1,000 households). Socioeconomic status, caste, and family size were significantly associated with disease reporting and prevention behaviors. Households in the richest wealth quintile were more likely to adopt prevention measures, while impermeable water storage was associated with reduced active prevention use.

articles.headline: DSM265 shows favorable PK and safety profile in early clinical trials
articles.title_original: Evaluating the safety, pharmacokinetics, and antiparasitic activity of DSM265, a novel antimalarial plasmodium dihydroorotate dehydrogenase inhibitor: a systematic review and meta-analysis of early clinical trials.
articles.authors: Sula I, Jihwaprani MC, Ahmad I, Khan TA, Al Abdin MG, Ali MM, Ming LC
articles.details: Meta-analysis of seven clinical trials evaluated the safety and pharmacokinetics of DSM265, a novel plasmodium dihydroorotate dehydrogenase inhibitor. At 400 mg oral dose, DSM265 achieved Cmax of 9.3 μg/mL (T1/2 112.1 h, AUC 1,601.9 h·μg/mL) and was associated with increased adverse events (RR 1.46, 95% CI 1.15–1.84). The drug demonstrated selectivity for P. falciparum over P. vivax and a favorable safety profile, though preclinical teratogenicity signals have halted further development.

articles.headline: Bloodless diet sustains mosquito rearing while preserving malaria research traits
articles.title_original: Effects of a bloodless diet on fitness and malaria susceptibility in Anopheles mosquitoes from Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: de Sales Hien DF, Sare I, Sib AMAP, Poda BS, Velez R, Marques J, Silveira H, Diabate A, Bilgo EM
articles.details: Researchers compared Anopheles coluzzii and An. gambiae reared on blood versus bloodless diet across seven generations. By the 6th generation, blood-fed mosquitoes showed higher P. falciparum oocyst prevalence than bloodless-fed animals, though prevalence rates were similar in the 3rd generation. Bloodless-fed mosquitoes showed increased longevity by the 7th generation, enabling sustained vector rearing essential for malaria research and vector control programs.

articles.headline: High frequency of Pfdhps resistance mutations in N'Djamena, Chad
articles.title_original: Resistance profile of the Plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase gene in three hospitals in the city of Ndjamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Cedric Y, Djakbé DL, Ngaryedji T, Nadia NAC, Pacome TK, Anaud NKJ, Esther DD, Alain DC, Ismail IA, Saleh B, Abel D, Hu H
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 220 patients in N'Djamena identified overall P. falciparum prevalence of 39.54%, with highest rates in ages 0–5. The Pfdhps resistance gene was detected in 37.93% of positive cases; A437G mutation present in 9.09% of samples and A581G in 54.54%. A581G was more prevalent in men and Fansidar users, underscoring the need for strengthened antimalarial drug resistance monitoring.

articles.headline: Severe malaria predominates in PLHIV with low CD4 counts
articles.title_original: Clinical and biological features of complicated and asymptomatic malaria among PLHIV and HIV-negative adults: An observational study in Libreville, Gabon.
articles.authors: Moutombi Ditombi BC, Manomba Boulingui C, Mbang Nguema OA, Mihindou CJ, Pongui Ngondza B, Ndong Ngomo JM, Essomeyo Mebale M, Ntsame Owono MM, Mabicka Moussavou AD, Mayandza C, Mbondoukwé NP, Kono HN, Bouyou Akotet MK
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in Gabon identified 56 P. falciparum-infected PLHIV and 66 HIV-negative individuals. Severe malaria was more common in PLHIV (48.2% vs 12.1%), while asymptomatic malaria predominated in HIV-negative persons (60.6% vs 25.0%). Clinical features in PLHIV included impaired consciousness (26.2%), repeated seizures (14.3%), severe anemia (14.3%), and prostration (16.7%). Median CD4 count was significantly lower in PLHIV with severe malaria (150 cells/µL) versus asymptomatic malaria (304 cells/µL).

articles.headline: Correction to population genetics of P. vivax in Papua New Guinea
articles.title_original: Correction: Population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Fola AA, Mehra S, Razook Z, Lautu-Gumal D, Nate E, Lee S, Kattenberg JH, Koepfli C, Kazura J, Ome-Kaius M, Laman M, Robinson LJ, Mueller I, Barry AE
articles.details: Correction to previously published article on population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.

articles.headline: Achillea millefolium extract shows potent in vitro activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: In Vitro Antiparasitic Activity and Phytochemical Profiling of Rhaponticum repens and Achillea millefolium Methanolic Extracts Against Plasmodium Falciparum and Leishmania major.
articles.authors: Singh K, Siyadatpanah A, Garba A, Singh J, Norouzi R, Ben Said M, Belkahia H
articles.details: Methanolic extracts of two medicinal plants were tested in vitro against P. falciparum and L. major. Achillea millefolium was more effective against P. falciparum, achieving 94% inhibition at 2.0 mg/mL with IC₅₀ of 0.52 mg/mL and high selectivity index (26.3), while Rhaponticum repens showed greater activity against L. major. Both extracts demonstrated low cytotoxicity on mammalian cells, supporting further investigation as leads for drug discovery.

articles.headline: Machine learning predicts antimalarial efficacy of Malaria Box compounds
articles.title_original: Hybrid Computational Framework Integrating Ensemble Learning, Molecular Docking, and Dynamics for Predicting Antimalarial Efficacy of Malaria Box Compounds.
articles.authors: Moreno M, Cuesta SA, Mora JR, Márquez Brazon EA, Paz JL, Agüero-Chapin G, Pérez-Pérez N, García-Jacas CR
articles.details: Researchers developed an ensemble machine-learning framework with molecular docking and dynamics simulations to predict antimalarial activity of Malaria Box compounds. Classification achieved accuracy of 0.738 (10-fold) with good sensitivity and specificity. Regression modeling within active and very-active classes achieved Q² values of 0.793–0.810. Molecular docking against P. falciparum Cytochrome B revealed strong binding affinities with stable ligand-protein complexes confirmed by 200-ns molecular dynamics simulations.

articles.headline: Helminth co-infection modulates antimalarial IgG responses in endemic populations
articles.title_original: The association of helminth infection against antimalarial antibody responses in malaria infection and vaccination: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Pinandyo SH, Ali S, Aurelio B, Amoah AS, Kaisar MMM
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 29 studies examined helminth infection's association with malaria antibody responses. Meta-analyses from eight studies showed heterogeneous effects: four studies found higher antimalarial IgG in helminth-infected versus uninfected individuals (SMD −0.44, 95% CI −0.61 to −0.27), while another four found marginally lower responses in S. haematobium-infected persons. Three studies reported better IgG1 and IgG3 responses in S. haematobium-infected individuals; helminth modulation depends on parasite species and malaria antigen type.

articles.headline: Lactate dehydrogenase biomarker identifies severe malaria in imported cases
articles.title_original: pLDH to identify severity in imported malaria: Implementing smartphone video analysis for rapid clinical decision-making.
articles.authors: Pedreira-Rincón J, Balerdi-Sarasola L, Villanueva G, Fleitas PE, Jimenez A, Mayor A, Muñoz J, Subirà C, Alvarez-Martinez MJ, Petrone P, Camprubí-Ferrer D, Parolo C
articles.details: Retrospective cohort study of imported P. falciparum malaria evaluated diagnostic and severity assessment capabilities of PfHRP2 and pan-pLDH biomarkers using lateral flow assays and smartphone-based video analysis. While PfHRP2 achieved perfect diagnostic accuracy, pan-pLDH excelled at identifying severe malaria cases and outperformed PfHRP2 for severity assessment. Smartphone-based video analysis reduced testing time to under 6 minutes while maintaining pan-pLDH's performance.

articles.headline: High dengue and chikungunya seroprevalence in Brazil-French Guiana malaria region
articles.title_original: Co-circulation of Plasmodium vivax and Arboviruses in the Brazil-French Guiana Border.
articles.authors: Cerilo-Filho M, Medeiros EDS, Arouca ML, Sampaio MP, Menezes RAO, Gomes MDSM, Silva JRS, Baptista ARS, Storti-Melo LM, Machado RLD
articles.details: Analysis of 300 serum samples from Brazil-French Guiana border (102 malaria cases, 198 controls) collected 2014–2015 showed high IgG reactivity to dengue and chikungunya viruses with frequent co-reactivity, while Zika virus detection was minimal. The overlapping transmission of P. vivax and arboviruses in this region highlights the need for integrated surveillance strategies to address co-circulation and co-infection risks.

articles.headline: Single-dose primaquine with DP reduces malaria in SMC
articles.title_original: Agent-based simulation of seasonal malaria chemoprevention strategy in Southern Tanzania: comparing dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine with or without primaquine.
articles.authors: Mfala CT, Nyambo DG, Mwaiswelo RO, Tchuenche JM, Clemen T
articles.details: Agent-based simulation of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) using dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) in Tanzanian children (N=2,275, baseline prevalence 11.5-16.3%). Three monthly rounds of DP alone reduced prevalence to 7.1% at 180 days; adding single low-dose primaquine (SLDPQ) further reduced it to 3.9%, with effects sustained across temperature scenarios. The model supports scaling SLDPQ addition to DP-SMC for malaria control.

articles.headline: Education and media increase safer sex negotiation
articles.title_original: Safer Sex Negotiation Among Women in Sexual Unions in Tanzania: Analysis of the 2022 Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Kessy SA, Stephano EE, Tibenderana JR, Mtoro MJ, Kessy IP, Majengo VG, Oguma ED, Mwalingo TP, Bago MH, Nyundo AA
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey data examining safer sex negotiation among women in sexual unions (N not specified, overall prevalence 75.8%, 95% CI 74.0-77.5). Higher education (APR 1.16-1.21), media exposure (APR 1.14), employment (APR 1.06), and STI awareness (APR 1.17) were significantly associated with safer sex negotiation. Results highlight factors for context-specific interventions to promote sexual autonomy.

articles.headline: Climate change threatens malaria eradication progress
articles.title_original: Climate change could hinder malaria eradication efforts.
articles.authors: O'Leary K
articles.details: Perspective on how climate change could hinder malaria eradication efforts. Title only; no abstract provided with specific quantitative data.

articles.headline: SARIMAX forecasts seasonal malaria peaks in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Modelling the association of rainfall and temperature with malaria incidence in Adamawa State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Bakare EA, Dukundane D, Salako KV, Kakaï RG, Okoronkwo C, Nelson E
articles.details: Time series modeling (SARIMAX) of malaria incidence in Adamawa State, Nigeria using monthly data (January 2015-April 2024) with lagged temperature and rainfall as covariates. The model captured seasonal patterns and generated forecasts for May 2024-December 2025. Predictions include peaks of approximately 67,052 cases (August 2024) and 80,004 cases (October 2025). Results support climate-based early warning systems for targeted malaria control.

articles.headline: Correction: Ethiopian malaria trends and global warming
articles.title_original: Author Correction: Malaria trends in Ethiopian highlands track the 2000 'slowdown' in global warming.
articles.authors: Rodó X, Martinez PP, Siraj A, Pascual M
articles.details: Author correction to a prior study examining the relationship between malaria trends in Ethiopian highlands and changes in global temperature patterns since 2000.

articles.headline: Community engagement builds acceptance of livestock ivermectin
articles.title_original: Community perceptions and acceptance of ivermectin for malaria control on Sumba Island, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Timoria D, Dewi C, Bøgh C, Baskoro T, Nurcahyo W, Testamenti VA, von Seidlein L, Kobylinski K, Chambers M
articles.details: Social science study alongside the SLIM (Sumba Livestock Ivermectin for Malaria) trial in Indonesia (November 2022-September 2023) examining community perceptions of ivermectin for livestock treatment and potential human mass drug administration. Qualitative data from 75 participants (59 in focus groups, 16 interviews) and approximately 650 community event attendees. Initial skepticism was addressed through trust-building and engagement with local leaders; communities showed acceptance of livestock treatment though safety concerns about children in MDA were raised.

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection alters avian gut microbiota composition
articles.title_original: Microbial community composition variation in relation to malaria infections in Junco hyemalis.
articles.authors: Amaya-Mejia WS, Sehgal RNM, Yeh PJ
articles.details: Comparative analysis of gut microbiota in wild Oregon Juncos infected (N=8) versus non-infected (N=8) with Plasmodium relictum GRW04. Infected birds had significantly lower alpha diversity and depleted bacterial taxa (Muribaculaceae, Acidobacteriales, Pseudomonas, Escherichia-Shingella). Co-occurrence network analysis revealed fewer connections and missing hub taxa in infected birds. Results suggest parasite-mediated dysbiosis.

articles.headline: Six-pronged approach contained P. vivax outbreak in Suriname
articles.title_original: A six-pronged approach to manage a Plasmodium vivax outbreak in a low- and middle-income country on the road to malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Labadie-Bracho M, Baldewsingh GK, Hiwat van Laar H, Adhin MR
articles.details: Descriptive study of P. vivax outbreak response in Suriname (November 2019-August 2021) involving 179 infections among 153 patients from imported cases. Initial strategies ('1-3-7' surveillance, vector control, community engagement) were complemented by mass screen-and-treat, mass drug administration (MDA), parasite genotyping, and targeted 'Radical Cure' treatment. MDA was more effective than mass screen-and-treat; directly observed therapy achieved 95.1% efficacy by day 180. Implementation of 'Radical Cure' ended the 22-month outbreak.

articles.headline: Low RTS,S malaria vaccine uptake in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Uptake of malaria vaccine (RTS,S/AS01) among children aged 6-24 months: A cross-sectional survey conducted in the Western region of Cameroon, 1 year following the vaccine introduction.
articles.authors: Ateudjieu J, Nana Djapou MC, Bekoa Onana BK, Tatsabong Tiomeni AA, Okoumokath Mpande GL, Kapso Nanguep D, Buh Nkum C, Ateudjieu Kenfack DW, Bissek AC
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of RTS,S/AS01 vaccine coverage in Cameroon among children aged 6-24 months (N=399 from 55 clusters). First-dose coverage was 31.20%, declining at subsequent doses (22.61% dose 2, 17.70% dose 3); 56.49% of dose-1 recipients completed the full 3-dose schedule. Administration timeliness was 50-57%. Parental perception was strongly associated with vaccination uptake (adjusted OR 15.54, p<0.001 for dose 1). Performance indicators fell below expected levels.

articles.headline: Limited evidence for gender-focused malaria interventions
articles.title_original: Gender-responsive and -transformative interventions in HIV, TB and malaria programmes: a review of evidence.
articles.authors: Bhushan A, Mishra S, Orth Z, Riha J
articles.details: Rapid review of gender-responsive and -transformative interventions in HIV, TB, and malaria (2004-2024) in low- and middle-income countries. Systematic searches identified 42 primary research articles—all focused on HIV, with none on TB or malaria. Interventions categorized into five types (resilient health systems n=6, gender norms change n=9, empowerment n=26, law/policy n=1). Multi-component, multi-level approaches showed more sustained improvements than single-component efforts. Review highlights the significant evidence gap for gender-focused malaria interventions.

articles.headline: P. falciparum resistance to multiple drugs in Colombia
articles.title_original: Antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from the Pacific Coast of Colombia.
articles.authors: Michie K, Chiu B, Briggs C, Corredor V, Matta-Cortés L, Rayner JC, Echeverri DF
articles.details: Assessment of P. falciparum isolates from Colombian Pacific Coast across 23 years using quantitative PCR, sequencing (including PfKelch13), and in vitro sensitivity testing. Multiple resistance-associated genotypes and phenotypes were detected, particularly for chloroquine and mefloquine with high mutation prevalence. No artemisinin-resistance associated polymorphisms were found. Results indicate long-term maintenance of historical resistance and argue against using chloroquine or mefloquine as artemisinin partner therapies.

articles.headline: IPTp-SP fails due to high drug resistance in Uganda
articles.title_original: Recurrent Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia and drug resistance mutations during intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kizza J, Katairo T, Kakuru A, Nsengimaana B, Esilu T, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Gerlovina I, Hathaway N, Briggs J, Tukwasibwe S, Kiwuwa SM, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: Trial comparing intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) with monthly sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), or DP+SP in Busia, Uganda (N=1,377 samples from pregnant women). Baseline: nearly universal PfDHFR/PfDHPS mutations (26.5% with high-level I164L+A581G). At 28 days, cumulative parasitemia risk was SP 57.8% vs. DP 4.1%, DP+SP 3.9%; symptomatic malaria was SP 9.3% vs. DP 1.1%, DP+SP 0.3%. SP treatment selected for resistance (high-level mutations increased to 35.2%), questioning the intervention's value.

articles.headline: Optimized chemiluminescent PRR assay reduces antimalarial screening time
articles.title_original: Using Next Generation Chemiluminescent Probes to Improve the Plasmodium falciparum in vitro Parasite Reduction Ratio (PRR) Assay.
articles.authors: Hellingman A, Göttle NL, Walz A, Brancucci NMB, Wittlin S, Mäser P, Rottmann M
articles.details: Developed lacZ/β-galSENSOR-based PRR assay reducing incubation from 14-28 days to 7 days while maintaining pharmacodynamic parameters (lag phase, parasite clearance time, PRR, maximum killing effect). The [3H]-hypoxanthine incorporation method failed to reliably detect parasites. Novel assay enables efficient evaluation of antimalarial compounds without radioactive facilities, providing robust pharmacodynamic profiles with higher throughput and lower equipment requirements.

articles.headline: Nanotechnology offers promise for herbal malaria remedies
articles.title_original: Nano-Enabled Herbal Remedies for Malaria Treatment: A Review of Recent Advances.
articles.authors: Xu C, Fatima A, Fatima M, Aqib AI, Zaheer T, Rab SO, Saeed M, Arif Z, Li K
articles.details: Review of nanotechnology-based approaches to malaria treatment using herbal remedies. Nanoparticles offer advantages including high drug-loading capacity, targeted delivery, improved biocompatibility, and reduced toxicity compared to conventional treatments. Green nanotechnology-based antimalarial therapies show potential therapeutic benefits and cost-effectiveness. Review discusses applications in vector control, drug-resistant malaria prevention, and commercialization potential.

articles.headline: Mali P. falciparum isolates remain susceptible to frontline drugs
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum field isolates drug susceptibility in Mali.
articles.authors: Maiga FO, Dembele L, Dama S, Diakite O, Diallo F, Sogore F, Maiga M, Dao F, Dara N, Alhousseini ML, Traore OB, Djimde AA
articles.details: Ex vivo susceptibility testing of 26 P. falciparum isolates from three endemic Mali sites against 14 antimalarials. Current frontline drugs highly potent: dihydroartemisinin (median IC50 < 6 nM), lumefantrine (median IC50 < 50 nM), pyronaridine (median IC50 < 10 nM). Chloroquine showed marked variability (IC50 ∼50-1300 nM). Chemopreventive agents sulfadoxine and pyrimethamine had high IC50 values (>1000 and >2000 nM). Next-generation compounds cabamiquine and GNF179 demonstrated strong activity (IC50 < 10 nM).

articles.headline: Iran nears malaria elimination despite cross-border transmission challenges
articles.title_original: Iran's Journey Through Malaria: From Past Challenges to Future Elimination-A Narrative Review.
articles.authors: Hanifian H, Nateghpour M
articles.details: Narrative review synthesizing malaria control in Iran (2000-2025). Iran dramatically reduced indigenous cases from thousands in early 2000s to <300 annually by mid-2010s, then achieved multiple consecutive years of zero indigenous transmission. Key achievements: integrated vector management, community engagement, cross-border initiatives. 2022 resurgence with mostly imported infections highlighted vulnerabilities. Challenges include insecticide resistance and Anopheles stephensi spread.

articles.headline: Machine learning identifies kinase inhibitors with antimalarial potential
articles.title_original: Small Molecule Kinase Inhibitors with In Vitro β-Hematin Formation and Plasmodium falciparum Protein Kinase G Inhibitory Activity Identified Using Machine Learning.
articles.authors: Thibaud JL, Salomane N, Harries S, Wicht KJ, Coulson LB, Kuter D, Chibale K, de Villiers KA
articles.details: Combined molecular docking and machine learning screening of kinase inhibitors against two P. falciparum targets: β-hematin formation and cGMP-dependent protein kinase (PfPKG). Three compounds identified: Derazantinib (IC50 88 µM for β-hematin, 0.160 µM for PfPKG), Pazopanib (219 µM and 0.330 µM), Afatinib (234 and 2.61 µM). Derazantinib more potent than chloroquine (IC50 22 µM) for β-hematin inhibition.

articles.headline: Novel avian malaria lineage detected in Spanish vultures
articles.title_original: Unraveling Occurrence Patterns and Diversity of Avian Malaria Parasites in Iberian Obligate and Facultative Scavenger Birds.
articles.authors: Oliva-Vidal P, Muriel J, Marzal A, López Del Aguila GM, Martínez JM, Margalida A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 383 blood samples from European vultures and scavenger birds in Spain. Overall haemosporidian prevalence 3.4%: Leucocytozoon 2.3%, Plasmodium 1.6%. Black kites showed highest prevalence (8.3%), followed by cinereous vultures (5.3%). Identified 10 new host-parasite interactions and novel Leucocytozoon lineage (lGYPBAR01) infecting all European vultures except griffon vulture. Suggests global warming may facilitate spread of infected vectors.

articles.headline: SARIMAX forecasts increasing malaria cases in Ethiopia through 2030
articles.title_original: Forecasting malaria incidence in a resource-limited urban setting with climate variables as exogenous regressors: time series analysis using a SARIMAX model in Bahir Dar, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Gelaw TT, Abera MA
articles.details: SARIMAX(1,2,2)(1,2,2,12) model using monthly data (January 2018–June 2025) and weather variables forecasted malaria incidence in Bahir Dar, Ethiopia for 2026-2030. Model revealed consistent increasing trend in predictions. Weather variables showed no statistically significant predictive relationship with malaria incidence (p > 0.05). Mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) was 28.3%, indicating reasonable predictive accuracy. Results suggest potential breakdown in current malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: AI-assisted miLab platform outperforms microscopy for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Evaluating the diagnostic performance of miLab™ for detection of malaria parasites using nPCR as reference standard.
articles.authors: Addae EK, Awortwe-Quaicoe T, Sackey B, Ansah RO, Larbi R, Dazangapai KDE, Frimpong JO, Amoako TOO, Asamoah A, Ayisi-Boateng N, Nkrumah B, Bekoe FA, Owusu M
articles.details: Hospital-based cross-sectional study of 300 suspected malaria patients in Ghana comparing miLab™ (AI-assisted automated platform), microscopy, and nested PCR (reference standard). MiLab™ demonstrated sensitivity 94.23%, specificity 98.98%, accuracy 97.33%, versus adjudicated microscopy sensitivity 85.58%, specificity 97.96%, accuracy 93.67%. MiLab™ parasite density estimates ranged 0.95–5.34 log parasites/µL (median 3.52). MiLab™ offers reliable rapid diagnosis suitable for resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic and cultural barriers delay care for severe malaria
articles.title_original: Young children with severe malaria in the Kapolowe health zone: an analysis of their parents' health-seeking behaviors and the challenges they face.
articles.authors: Kanyamukenge DB, Maseko T, Khote FL, Makenga G, Mavoko HM, Tevuzula VM, Mulangu AM, Sompwe EM, Rietveld H, Manyando C, Van Geertruyden JP, Kakudji A, Bastiaens H
articles.details: Qualitative study using 30 focus group discussions in Kapolowe health zone, DRC, with adapted three-delays model. Mothers frequently delayed seeking care for severely ill children under five and resorted to self-medication with modern and traditional remedies.
