Barriers included geographical distance, impassable roads, lack of transportation and facilities, financial costs (direct and indirect), and cultural preferences for traditional practices and prayer. Findings highlight need to strengthen health systems accounting for community realities.

articles.headline: SD Bioline RDT shows high sensitivity but moderate specificity
articles.title_original: Field performance of the SD Bioline Malaria Ag P.f. rapid diagnostic test among children under five in Nigeria: insights from the 2021 Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Olubiyi B, Alabi A, Isiko I
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 2021 Nigerian Malaria Indicator Survey with 9,067 children aged 6-59 months. SD Bioline Malaria Ag P.f. RDT compared to microscopy (reference standard) showed sensitivity 88.6%, specificity 75.2%, positive predictive value 49.5%, negative predictive value 96.0%, accuracy 78.1%, Cohen's kappa 0.496 (moderate agreement), ROC AUC 0.816. High NPV indicates reliability for ruling out malaria; however, moderate specificity and low PPV risk overestimating prevalence.

articles.headline: Severe malaria with cerebral disease predominates in Pakistan
articles.title_original: Burden of Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: clinical insights from a prospective study in Jacobabad, Sindh, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Noor M, Junaid M, Furqan A
articles.details: Prospective observational study of 260 hospitalized malaria patients in Jacobabad, Pakistan (2024-2025): 217 uncomplicated (83.5%), 43 severe (16.5%). Severe malaria significantly associated with jaundice, bleeding, pallor, respiratory distress, seizures, cerebral malaria, anemia, and thrombocytopenia. Overall case fatality rate 16.2% in pediatric patients (0-15 years), with cerebral malaria identified as most fatal complication. Clinical and hematological parameters serve as prognostic indicators.

articles.headline: CYP variants alter drug metabolism but preserve P. vivax cure
articles.title_original: Pharmacogenetic Profiling of Cytochrome P450 Enzymes in Plasmodium vivax Patients Treated with Chloroquine and Primaquine: Implications for Personalized Malaria Therapy.
articles.authors: Phompradit P, Muhamad P, Cheoymang A, Na-Bangchang K
articles.details: Pharmacogenetic study of 99 Burmese P. vivax patients treated with standard chloroquine (25 mg/kg) and primaquine (15 mg/kg/day for 14 days). Most frequent alleles: CYP3A5*3 (71.7%), CYP2D6*10 (30.6%). CYP3A5*3 associated with altered desethylchloroquine levels; CYP2C19*3 associated with faster parasite clearance. All patients achieved complete parasite clearance and fever resolution without recurrence despite high prevalence of reduced-function variants. Findings underscore need for pharmacogenetic surveillance in elimination settings.

articles.headline: Engineered mosquito symbiotes achieve high Plasmodium inhibition rates
articles.title_original: Evaluating paratransgenesis using engineered symbiotic bacteria for Plasmodium inhibition in mosquito vectors: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Cleanclay WD, Kernyuy FB, Kintung IF, Yensii NG, Chick JA, Obi AMM
articles.details: Systematic review of 10 studies on paratransgenesis evaluated engineered bacteria and fungi (Asaia, Serratia, Pantoea, Enterobacter, Aspergillus) delivered to mosquito midguts. Effector proteins (Scorpine, EPIP, Defensin, SM1-2) inhibited P. falciparum oocysts/sporozoites at high rates; Scorpine achieved up to 97.8% inhibition. Multiple-effector combinations surpassed 89% inhibition. Key barriers before field deployment: ecological safety, microbial stability, and field validation.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing identifies mosquito species, bloodmeals, and parasites simultaneously
articles.title_original: A multiplex assay to detect mosquito species, bloodmeal host source and Plasmodium in malaria vectors using Nanopore amplicon sequencing.
articles.authors: Rogers EA, Derilus D, Sandi C, Reimer L, Lenhart A, Impoinvil LM
articles.details: Multiplex Oxford Nanopore MinION assay validated on 150 insectary-reared (9 species) and 150 blood-fed mosquitoes. Combined assay accurately identified all 9 Anopheles species, 5 bloodmeal hosts (detectable ≤48h post-feeding), and P. falciparum in 40 infected samples. Results showed high concordance with Sanger sequencing. Offers cost-effective alternative for simultaneous mosquito identification and parasite detection.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance genes show geographic, temporal variation in Ethiopian Anopheles
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal signatures of genomic insecticide resistance in the Anopheles arabiensis mosquito malaria vector from Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Eukubay A, Bennett KL, Tekie H, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Gemechu F, Miles A, Abera D, Clarkson CS, Golassa L
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of Anopheles arabiensis across Ethiopia revealed high geographic variation in insecticide resistance genes: Vgsc-L995F prevalent in north; Cyp6aa/p and Coeae2-7g CNVs high in west; Gste2 and Cyp9k1 CNVs frequent in Central Rift Valley. Seasonal shifts in marker frequencies observed, with increasing Vgsc-L995F during transmission season. Findings highlight complex interplay of control interventions, agricultural practices, and vector behavior.

articles.headline: Secondary Anopheles vectors feed on humans and carry P. falciparum in low-transmission Zambia
articles.title_original: Species composition and blood feeding patterns of understudied Anopheles mosquitoes in southern Zambia, an area of low malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Mudenda T, Simubali L, Mwaanga G, Lubinda M, Katowa B, Musonda M, Hamapumbu H, Chibambo P, Chooli S, Ali RLMN, Gebhardt ME, Thuma PE, Simulundu E, Moss WJ, Saili K, Norris DE, Stevenson JC
articles.details: Entomological survey in Southern Province, Zambia collected 5,697 female anophelines (9 species) via CDC light traps. An. rufipes (26.8%) and An. pretoriensis (25.1%) dominated. Of 401 blood-fed mosquitoes, bloodmeals were goats (62.5%), humans (19.4%), cows (10.2%); human blood index 0.22. One An. rufipes tested positive for P. falciparum sporozoites. Secondary vectors with outdoor feeding and human-blood preference may sustain residual transmission despite high ITN/IRS coverage.

articles.headline: School-age children drive malaria transmission via gametocyte-containing infections
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic school-age children carry the majority of transmissible Plasmodium falciparum infections.
articles.authors: Buchwald AG, Vareta J, Nwagbata O, McCann RS, Sixpence A, Matengeni A, Kamzati M, Mangani C, Seydel KB, Wilson ML, Taylor TE, Mathanga DP, Valim C, Laufer MK, Cohee LM
articles.details: Year-long cohort study in rural Malawi detected P. falciparum infections and gametocytes via molecular methods at routine and sick visits. Gametocytes present in only 23% of population. School-age children had significantly higher incidence of gametocyte-containing and high-density gametocyte infections compared to other groups. School-age children's presence was key driver of gametocyte frequencies in households independent of overall Pf infection levels. Clearing infections from asymptomatic school-age children in rainy season estimated to decrease gametocyte abundance 67% population-wide.

articles.headline: Optical sensor discriminates Plasmodium developmental stages with 96%+ sensitivity
articles.title_original: Machine learning assisted malaria detection using photonic crystal fibre optical sensors.
articles.authors: Abdullah-Al-Shafi M, Sen S, Mubassera M
articles.details: Photonic crystal fibre-based optical sensor detects malaria via refractive index variations in infected RBCs. Heptagonal cladding design enables efficient sample infiltration and light-matter interaction. Numerical results: 97.45% sensitivity for healthy cells, 96.89% for ring stage, 96.22% for trophozoite, 95.45% for schizont stage. Operating range 1.373–1.402 refractive index; confinement losses <10–8 dB/m at 2.2 THz. Proposed as cost-effective platform for early malaria detection.

articles.headline: Seasonal labor migration sustains low-transmission malaria hotspots year-round
articles.title_original: Rural-Urban Migration and Low-Transmission Endemicity of Malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa: A Statistical Inference Approach to Uncover Possible Mechanisms.
articles.authors: Harikripal, Zabre SP, Sie A, Ouedraogo A, Danquah I, Bhattacharyya S
articles.details: Data-driven malaria model of Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso compared seasonal, regular, and permanent migration patterns using empirical incidence and mobility data. Seasonal migration most accurately explained low dry-season transmission (3–150 cases/10,000 person-weeks) and rainy-season amplification. Model shows seasonal migration prevents local fade-out and significantly intensifies transmission during favorable rains. Findings highlight importance of incorporating human mobility into transmission models and control strategies for malaria elimination in Sub-Saharan Africa.

articles.headline: Zein nanoformulation enhances artemisinin solubility and overcomes drug resistance
articles.title_original: Development and evaluation of novel zein-based artemisinin sustained-release formulation for treating drug-resistant malaria.
articles.authors: Wang Y, Yu X, Zhang X, He X, Tang Y, Fang L, Culleton R, Zhang Q, Dong W, Cao J
articles.details: Zein (maize protein) nanocarrier encapsulates artemisinin, achieving 200-fold water solubility enhancement and sustained release. In vitro assays demonstrated potent effects against ART-resistant P. falciparum via ring survival and recrudescence assays. In vivo studies (rodent and humanized RBC mouse models) showed zein-ART significantly prolonged ART half-life, reduced parasitemia, prevented recrudescence, and overcame resistance compared to free artemisinin. Nanoformulation addresses key pharmacokinetic limitations and solubility bottlenecks of ART-based therapies.

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection reduces rVSV-EBOV vaccine efficacy via lymph node macrophage dysregulation
articles.title_original: Acute malaria dysregulates specialized lymph node macrophages to suppress vaccine-elicited protection against the Ebola virus.
articles.authors: Elliff J, Grady L, O'Donnell KL, Messingham C, Rogers KJ, Akther J, Thurman A, Vijay R, Pezzulo A, Randall T, Marzi A, Butler NS, Maury W
articles.details: Acute murine Plasmodium infection concurrent with rVSV/EBOV (ERVEBO) vaccination reduced vaccine-mediated protection against mouse-adapted EBOV challenge. Mechanism: Plasmodium-induced interferon-gamma suppressed rVSV/EBOV replication in lymph node macrophages, reducing anti-EBOV glycoprotein antibody responses. Higher vaccine doses partially overcame antibody deficits and restored protection. Findings support maintaining current high antigen loads in rVSV/EBOV vaccination protocols for EVD outbreak management in malaria-endemic regions.

articles.headline: Angiopoietin-like 4 and Inhibin-βE differentiate cerebral malaria from other comas
articles.title_original: Specific Biomarkers Differentiate Cerebral Malaria from Other Causes of Coma in African children.
articles.authors: Mwangi S, Gomes C, Abdi AI, Rodriguez A
articles.details: Retrospective plasma biomarker study in 379 Kenyan children including severe malaria (cerebral malaria, respiratory distress, severe anemia) and non-malarial coma cases. Angiopoietin-like 4 and Inhibin-βE (secreted by endothelial cells in response to P. falciparum) showed high specificity: AUC 0.82 for CM, 0.86 for respiratory distress, 0.85 for severe anemia compared to non-malarial coma; combined with platelet levels, AUC 0.96. Biomarkers also predicted CM mortality (AUC 0.85).

articles.headline: Ivermectin kills Anopheles via glutamate-gated chloride channel; mechanism involves sugar-ring–THR interaction
articles.title_original: Lethal effects of ivermectin structures on malaria vectors and in silico analysis of interactions with their glutamate-gated chloride ion channels.
articles.authors: Nguyen MN, Jones AK, Hotwagner D, Khemrattrakool P, Hongsuwong T, Hanboonkunupakarn B, Jittamala P, Sriwichai P, Tarning J, Kobylinski KC
articles.details: Ivermectin lethal to Anopheles mosquitoes via binding glutamate-gated chloride channel (GluCl), causing continuous chloride flow and flaccid paralysis. Studies in A. dirus and A. minimus showed full lethal effect with ivermectin (both sugar rings), partial with monosaccharide, none with aglycone. Structural docking identified hydrogen bond between ivermectin's second sugar ring (4″-OH) and THR304 in Anopheles GluCl M2-M3 loop. Single amino acid substitution from C. elegans (ILE273 to Anopheles THR304) explains differential binding.

articles.headline: Variable pathogen load and infectiousness broaden avian Plasmodium distribution in Hawai'i
articles.title_original: Variation in pathogen load and the pathogen load-infectiousness relationship broaden avian malaria's distribution.
articles.authors: Seidl CM, Parise KL, Ipsaro IJ, Leach S, Hays D, Morimoto R, Banister K, Ferreira FC, Abraham EC, Foster JT, Paxton EH, Kilpatrick AM
articles.details: Experimental infections of canaries with avian malaria (Plasmodium relictum) in Culex quinquefasciatus quantified parasitemia-infectiousness relationship. Infectiousness increased with parasitemia, temperature, and time since feeding. Low slope at low parasitemia meant wide range of parasitemias partly infectious; high within-species parasitemia variability caused extensive overlap in infectiousness among 17 bird species across 11 Hawaiian communities. Despite diverse host assemblages, similar total infectiousness across communities likely contributed to P. relictum's widespread Hawaiian distribution.

articles.headline: Climate modeling predicts P. vivax and P. falciparum in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Association of Climate Variables with Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Cases in Mandoto, Madagascar: A Statistical Modeling Study.
articles.authors: Rajaonarimirana E, Ravaoarimanga M, Lockwood S, Cramer E, Rajesy M, Drakeley C, Randremanana RV, White M
articles.details: Over 6 years (2019-2024) in Mandoto, Madagascar, 276,318 RDTs yielded 109,428 malaria cases (39.6% positivity). After 2021, 71.5% were P. falciparum and 28.5% P. vivax, both co-endemic with peaks in January and April-June. Seasonal ARIMA forecasting models incorporating precipitation and temperature improved model accuracy. Authors conclude combining time-series modeling with climate data predicts malaria cases for resource planning.

articles.headline: IL-10 elevation links postpartum malaria infection in Burkinabè women
articles.title_original: Postpartum cytokine shifts and IL-10-mediated immune suppression in malaria-infected primigravid women.
articles.authors: Traore O, Rouamba T, Zango SH, Sorgho H, Valea I, Traore-Coulibaly M, Schallig HDFH, Tinto H
articles.details: Study of 33 Burkinabè women measured plasma cytokines (IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α, IFN-γ) at delivery and 1 and 3 months postpartum by ELISA. At delivery, 48.5% tested positive for malaria by PCR. Malaria-infected women had significantly elevated IL-10 levels and decreased IL-6:IL-10 ratio (p = 0.005), with strong IL-10/malaria correlation (σ = 0.9, p < 0.001). Authors conclude IL-10-dominant cytokine profile at delivery is strongly associated with postpartum malaria susceptibility.

articles.headline: Heme and hemozoin trigger platelet apoptosis and ferroptosis in vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Heme and hemozoin induce platelet cell death through UPR-induced apoptosis and ferroptosis in vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Gomes MT, Medeiros-de-Moraes IM, Vieira-de-Abreu A, de Azevedo-Quintanilha IG, Fontes TM, de Brito Carneiro A, Azambuja P, Tolley ND, Rowley JW, Carpinter BA, Ferreira MU, Gonçalves RM, Rocha VN, Rosa Nascimento AL, Carvalho WA, Scopel KKG, Campbell RA, Bozza PT, Weyrich AS, Zimmerman GA, Lacerda MVG, Castro-Faria-Neto HC, Hottz ED
articles.details: Heme in plasma from thrombocytopenic P. vivax patients, but not nonthrombocytopenic vivax or falciparum patients, induced platelet death ex vivo. Heme and hemozoin activated apoptotic caspases and IRE1α-dependent endoplasmic reticulum stress, while hemozoin uniquely triggered ferroptosis-like necrosis dependent on lipid peroxidation and DGAT1/2 enzymes, identified as the main pathway for hemozoin-induced thrombocytopenia. Authors conclude these regulated cell death pathways are associated with severe thrombocytopenia in vivax malaria.

articles.headline: Post-artemisinin delayed hemolysis complicates severe falciparum malaria in child
articles.title_original: Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Complicated by Post-artemisinin Delayed Hemolysis in a Non-immune Pediatric Returning Traveler.
articles.authors: Galadari S, Kalsekar M, Iqbal A, Daoud Z, Balila M, Nyanga O
articles.details: Case report of 9-year-old non-immune boy with severe P. falciparum malaria from Tanzania presenting with multiorgan dysfunction (thrombocytopenia, acute kidney injury, hepatic dysfunction, hypoxemic pneumonia). Treated with intravenous artesunate followed by oral artemether-lumefantrine with initial clinical improvement. Ten days post-treatment, re-presented with post-artemisinin delayed hemolysis (PADH) evidenced by hemoglobin decline, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, elevated LDH, reticulocytosis, and low haptoglobin without parasitemia. Authors emphasize importance of post-treatment monitoring for hemolysis 7-30 days after artesunate therapy.

articles.headline: Greece's malaria elimination demonstrates effectiveness of sustained surveillance systems
articles.title_original: Malaria in Greece: From Ancient Scourge to Eradication.
articles.authors: Christodoulou MK
articles.details: Narrative review examining malaria's historical presence in Greece from antiquity through eradication and early 21st-century re-emergence. Reviews multifaceted elimination strategies including vector control, antimalarial treatment, environmental interventions, and sustained surveillance systems. Discusses factors contributing to temporary re-establishment of locally acquired transmission after eradication, emphasizing vulnerability of malaria-free regions to global mobility and competent vectors. Authors conclude malaria elimination is achievable through coordinated long-term public health efforts but requires continuous surveillance and rapid response capacity.

articles.headline: Nigeria's under-5 malaria burden reflects intervention gaps and regional disparities
articles.title_original: Burden, challenges, and control of malaria among under-5s in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okoro LN, Ayesiga I, Yeboah MO, Bulus NG, Gmanyami JM, Ismaila EL, Ahgu O, Otokpa EO, Edet EN, Dogo JM, Ediamu TD, Atimango L, Isiko I
articles.details: Narrative review synthesizing malaria burden and control in Nigerian children under 5, who account for >1/4 of global malaria cases. Northern states have highest prevalence with rural disparities; contributing factors include socioeconomic deprivation and climate variability. While LLINs, intermittent preventive therapy, and indoor residual spraying are effective, implementation gaps remain. Authors recommend coordinated multisectoral response with stronger health systems and policy reforms.

articles.headline: Sub-Saharan LLINs, IRS, and combined approaches highly cost-effective for malaria
articles.title_original: Strategic resource allocation for malaria elimination in endemic settings: a systematic review of cost-effectiveness evidence.
articles.authors: Firdaus MH, Ho Imran DS, Puteh SEW, Zhueng TJ, Hamid MHA, Idris ZM, Manaf MRA
articles.details: Systematic review of 18 economic evaluations (2018-2025) found LLINs and IRS highly cost-effective with costs per DALY averted below national GDP thresholds, especially when combined. RTS,S vaccination and RDT + microscopy were moderately cost-effective. Tafenoquine with G6PD screening showed higher ICERs due to additional costs. Authors conclude most malaria interventions in Sub-Saharan Africa were highly cost-effective and support evidence-based resource allocation.

articles.headline: G6PD A- (G202A) variant absent in southeast Nigerian malaria patients
articles.title_original: No G6PD A- (G202A) variant detected among Plasmodium falciparum-positive patients in Awka, Southeast Nigeria: a hospital-based study.
articles.authors: Ikegbunam M, Uchechukwu N, Abone H, Grace AE, Ezeunala M, Joy N, Immaculata NC, Igwe JO, Okeke O, Nworji F, Ihekwereme P
articles.details: Hospital-based study in Awka, Nigeria screened 64 P. falciparum-positive participants for G6PD variants (A376G and G202A) using PCR and Sanger sequencing. The B variant (normal) was predominant (83%); none had the A- (G202A) variant associated with G6PD deficiency. B variant and A+ variant showed no significant impact on hemoglobin or parasitemia levels. Authors recommend broader community-based genotyping and enzyme activity testing before informing population-level treatment policies.

articles.headline: Parallelizable IBD detection tool accelerates large-scale Plasmodium genomic surveillance
articles.title_original: hmmibd-rs: an enhanced hmmIBD implementation for parallelizable identity-by-descent detection from large-scale Plasmodium genomic data.
articles.authors: Guo B, Schaffner SF, Taylor AR, O'Connor TD, Takala-Harrison S
articles.details: Developed hmmibd-rs, a multi-threaded implementation for identity-by-descent detection in Plasmodium genomes, incorporating non-uniform recombination rates. Using 128 CPU threads reduced computation time for 220 million P. falciparum-like chromosome pairs from 5.2 days to 1.3 hours (~100× speedup), enhancing accuracy over the original hmmIBD algorithm. When applied to MalariaGEN Pf7 dataset (n ≈ 10,000 P. falciparum samples), analysis completed within hours. Authors conclude hmmibd-rs is crucial for large-scale malaria genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: Rectal artesunate for severe malaria with community health workers in Zambia
articles.title_original: Severe malaria treatment with rectal artesunate and artemisinin-based combination therapy in remote settings: an effectiveness-implementation hybrid type 3 study protocol (SEMA ReACT).
articles.authors: Hachizovu S, Manyando C, Nambozi M, Khondowe S, Hamainza B, Mukumbuta D, Rietveld H, Mukomena Sompwe E, Kikunda G, Muhindo-Mavoko H, Luzolo Khote F, Kanyamukenge D, Bastiaens H, Kakudji A, Maseko T, Makenga G, Baraka V, Mutombo A, Duparc S, Van Geertruyden JP
articles.details: Effectiveness-implementation hybrid study in Zambia and DRC evaluating community health worker delivery of rectal artesunate (RAS) for severe malaria in children 6 months-5 years, followed by ACT or injectable artesunate + ACT. Primary outcomes: time from symptom onset to treatment, 28-day PCR-corrected cure rates, and proportion utilizing formal healthcare. Authors conclude this study will provide evidence on feasibility and effectiveness of community-based pre-referral treatment to reduce mortality and address access barriers.

articles.headline: DRC malaria prevalence 33% in children 6-59 months; socioeconomic disparities evident
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with malaria among children aged 6-59 months in the Democratic Republic of the Congo: a nationwide cross-sectional survey.
articles.authors: Adam J, Mukuku O, Tawi JM, Luoga P, Mutombo AM
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 10,013 children aged 6-59 months from 2023/24 DRC Demographic and Health Survey. Overall malaria prevalence was 33% (95% CI 30.2-35.6), varying from 5% in Kinshasa to 61% in Bas-Uele. Multivariable Poisson regression identified associated factors: age 24-59 months (APR 1.25), poverty (APR 1.52), no ITN use (APR 1.18), unimproved housing (APR 1.54). Authors conclude high malaria burden and recommend scaling ITN distribution, improving diagnosis/treatment, and addressing socioeconomic factors.

articles.headline: Primaquine addition reduces vivax recurrence; monotherapies show higher failure rates
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether lumefantrine vs chloroquine for the treatment of Plasmodium Vivax infection in Pakistan.
articles.authors: Khan S, Muqtadir J, Abbas SA, Ayoob K, Wahab A, Zaidi SHM, Das B, Bhatti S, Vohra EA
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 354 P. vivax patients in Karachi compared four regimens: chloroquine (CQ), artemether-lumefantrine (AL), CQ + primaquine, AL + primaquine. Day-28 recurrence rates: 10.2% (CQ), 20.2% (AL), 1.1% (CQ+PQ), 3.8% (AL+PQ); 6-month rates: 15.3%, 32.2%, 1.1%, 6.3% respectively. Cox regression showed AL monotherapy had higher recurrence hazard than CQ; primaquine-containing regimens had significantly lower hazards. Authors conclude primaquine-containing regimens substantially reduce P. vivax recurrence.

articles.headline: Multicentric longitudinal study assesses malaria burden vector health systems India
articles.title_original: Multicentric longitudinal study on malaria burden, vector bionomics and health system assessment in diverse eco-epidemiological settings in the context of malaria elimination in India: study protocol.
articles.authors: Rahul A, Thiruvengadam K, Sharma R, Jabir M, Viswan A, Jency PJ, Bharadwaj N, Muthukumaravel S, Shriram AN, Raju HK, Kannan S, Singh M, Bhuyan PJ, Jain T, Rahi M
articles.details: A longitudinal study across 12 districts spanning 10 states in India will enroll ~25,000 individuals across varied ecological contexts (urban, rural, forest-foothill, coastal). The study will estimate malaria prevalence using bivalent RDTs and microscopy at baseline, conduct monthly screening of symptomatic individuals, and perform PCR testing for low-density parasitemia and subclinical infections. Assessments include vector species composition, insecticide resistance profiles, and associations between environmental variables (temperature, rainfall, humidity) and malaria transmission through spatiotemporal analysis.

articles.headline: P. falciparum stomatin-like protein interacts with metalloprotease mitochondria
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum stomatin-like protein forms a putative complex with a metalloprotease in distinct mitochondrial loci.
articles.authors: Verhoef JMJ, Bekkering ET, Boshoven C, Hannon M, Evers F, Proellochs NI, Spruijt CG, Kooij TWA
articles.details: Deletion of P. falciparum STOML protein causes significant growth defect and slower asexual blood-stage (ABS) development, while sexual-stage development remains unaffected. STOML localizes to punctate structures at branching points of the mitochondrial network and forms a large protein complex with FtsH, a zinc-dependent metalloprotease. The predicted AlphaFold2 structure of STOML shows similarity to bacterial HflK/C, suggesting that STOML-FtsH complex at specific mitochondrial loci facilitates P. falciparum ABS development.

articles.headline: No association between loiasis and malaria in rural Gabon
articles.title_original: There Is No Association Between Loiasis and Malaria: Findings from a Secondary Analysis of a Cross-Sectional Survey in Rural Gabon.
articles.authors: Werner J, Zoleko-Manego R, Mombo-Ngoma G, Ramharter M, Mischlinger J
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 947 participants from a 2015-2016 cross-sectional survey in Gabon examined the association between malaria and loiasis. Crude analysis showed apparent protective association (OR 0.67, 95% CI 0.45–1.01; p=0.0521), but adjusted analysis revealed no significant association (adjusted OR 1.31, 95% CI 0.81–2.11; p=0.276). Age confounding explained the apparent crude association due to different epidemiological distributions: malaria predominated in children while loiasis occurred mainly in older individuals.

articles.headline: Hospital epidemiology of imported falciparum malaria in Algeria
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in a Malaria-Free Region: A Retrospective Analysis of Epidemiological, Clinical, and Therapeutic Patterns in Tizi-Ouzou, Algeria (2018-2024).
articles.authors: Yousfi S, Belaid D, Salhi O, Touhami NAK, Ouchene N, Elias N, Moussaoui L, Saidani S, Silem C, Seklaoui N, Bouchaib H
articles.details: Among 106 screened patients at a hospital in Tizi-Ouzou, 16 confirmed imported malaria cases (15.1% positivity) were analyzed. P. falciparum accounted for 87.5% of cases, predominantly with low parasitemia (<4% in 68.8%). Most patients were Algerian nationals aged 20–50 years returning from West Africa. Uncomplicated malaria represented 78.6% of cases and severe malaria 21.4%; main laboratory abnormalities included anemia (92.8%), thrombocytopenia (42.8%), and hepatic cytolysis (43%). Mefloquine was the most prescribed treatment (71.4%).

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Haryana threatens India 2030 elimination goal
articles.title_original: Malaria Surge in Haryana - an early warning to relook ongoing elimination efforts.
articles.authors: Kumari A, Kant R
articles.details: Analysis of malaria epidemiology in Haryana State from 2018–2024 using NCVBDC data showed dramatic case reduction from 3,149 (2018) to 52 cases (2022), a 98.34% decrease. However, cases then increased to 270 in 2024, indicating a rising trend. Literature review identified 18 relevant epidemiological studies. The study examined correlation between malaria incidence and climatic variability and recommends strengthening disease surveillance, clinical management, and vector control measures to sustain elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Imported malaria dominance in South-West Delhi urban elimination efforts
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in South-West Delhi: Five-year Epidemiological Trends (2020-2024) from a Fever Clinic and Implications for Urban Malaria Elimination.
articles.authors: Kumar M, Pal O, Saini S, Savargaonkar D, Anvikar A, Bharti PK, Nitika N
articles.details: Analysis of 7,257 fever clinic cases identified 58 malaria cases (0.8% positivity). P. vivax accounted for 94.8% (55/58) of cases and P. falciparum for 3.4% (2/58); males (70.7%) and ages 18–45 years (60.3%) were most affected. Imported cases were 51.7% (30/58) of total malaria cases, increasing from 52.2% (2020) to 66.7% (2024), predominantly from Uttar Pradesh districts. These findings highlight the importance of cross-state notification, case-based surveillance, and inter-state collaboration for sustaining urban malaria elimination.

articles.headline: ITN utilization gaps persist among children and pregnant women Nigeria
articles.title_original: Insecticide-Treated Net Utilization Among Children and Pregnant Women: 2021 Nigeria Malaria Indicator Survey Analysis.
articles.authors: Omole EB, Ndukwe OS
articles.details: Analysis of 2021 Nigeria Malaria Indicator Survey data from 13,727 households showed that 56% of households owned at least one ITN, while 50% of women and 41.2% of children under five slept under an ITN. ITN utilization was higher in rural areas (p=0.001), North West and North East regions (p<0.001), and among lower wealth quintiles (p<0.001). Children younger than 12 months had higher likelihood of sleeping under ITNs (p<0.001), and pregnant women with no or primary education were more likely to use ITNs (p≤0.048).

articles.headline: Rainfall drives malaria incidence seasonally in Yumbe Uganda
articles.title_original: Climate variability and malaria incidence trends in Yumbe District, West Nile Sub-region of Uganda (2017-2021).
articles.authors: Ninsiima LR, Musiitwa R, Nanyunja Z, Muleme J, Maasaba C, Anule T, Musoke D
articles.details: Time-series analysis of 2,066,711 malaria cases (2017–2021) in Yumbe district examined associations with climate variables. Rainfall was positively associated with malaria incidence at 1-month lag (β=0.38, p<0.05), and rainfall led malaria cases by 1 month with strong correlation (r=0.759, p<0.001). Minimum temperature was inversely associated (β=−0.29, p<0.05), while maximum temperature showed no significant effect. These findings highlight the role of climate variability in malaria transmission and support integrating climate data into early warning systems.

articles.headline: Test-and-treat compliance varies in Nigerian hospital malaria case-management
articles.title_original: Quality of inpatient test-and-treat malaria case-management in public and private hospitals in Kano State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Getachew D, Ogbulafor N, Shekarau E, Musa B, Yusuf A, Haruna S, Oladosu OO, Oresanya O, Maxwell K, Zurovac D
articles.details: Cross-sectional assessment of 18 public and private hospitals in Kano State, Nigeria, reviewed 2,814 suspected malaria admissions from August 2024. Composite test-and-treat performance was 39.3% overall (45.7% for children vs. 26.5% for adults; 39.8% in public vs. 30.8% in private hospitals). Among suspected patients, 73.7% were tested and 90.2% with severe malaria received artesunate; only 30% of artesunate-treated patients received follow-up ACT. Routine microscopy showed sensitivity 93.2% and specificity 42.5%, indicating clinicians can trust negative slides but must exercise caution with positive results.

articles.headline: Global antimalarial resistance markers show artemisinin resistance emergence
articles.title_original: The Global Landscape of Plasmodium falciparum Drug Resistance Markers, 2005-2025: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Habarugira F, Batamuriza J, Ndahimana R, Minega JN, Dieng MM, Mutsaka-Makuvaza MJ, Daba TM, Idaghdour Y, Mutesa L
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 261 studies from 64 countries (2005–2025) mapped the global distribution and temporal trends of P. falciparum drug-resistance markers. Pooled prevalence was highest for pfdhfr (85.7%), followed by pfcrt (78.0%), pfdhps (73.7%), pfmdr1 (60.5%), and pfk13 (45.0%). Rising prevalence of pfk13 mutations since 2012 indicates emerging artemisinin resistance, while persistent pfdhfr/pfdhps mutations demonstrate ongoing sulfonamide-pyrimethamine selective pressure on P. falciparum, resulting in decreased parasite clearance and widespread treatment failure in Africa.

articles.headline: Correction to transcriptome profiling publication
articles.title_original: Correction: Transcriptome profiling of intrahepatocytic Plasmodium and their host hepatocytes based on the infection phase and the zonation of the liver.
articles.authors: Mo Z, Chen Y, Qin Y, Song J
articles.details: Correction notice for previously published article on transcriptome profiling of intrahepatocytic Plasmodium and host hepatocytes.

articles.headline: High-throughput AI screening reveals artemisinin dormancy mechanism
articles.title_original: High throughput quantitative tracking of Plasmodium falciparum clonal blood stage parasite growth and applications for antimalarial drug discovery.
articles.authors: Cheng Z, Ru L, Hoang A, Wang M, Ganjikunta S, Maher SP, Kyle DE
articles.details: Study developed Quantitative Tracking After Chemotherapy Exposure (qTRACE) with AI-based segmentation and analysis to evaluate cytotoxic and cytostatic drug effects on individual P. falciparum parasites at single-cell resolution in high-throughput format. Following artemisinin exposure, up to 50% of viable parasites recovered from artemisinin-induced dormancy. Dormant ring-stage parasites were confirmed viable, with recovery rates differing significantly between artemisinin-susceptible and artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum strains, directly linking continuous time-lapse phenotypes to drug exposure.

articles.headline: Cambodia P. falciparum relatedness varies by geography and occupation
articles.title_original: Genetic relatedness of Cambodian Plasmodium falciparum isolates is driven by geography and occupation.
articles.authors: Rowley E, Guo B, Wojnarski M, Spring MD, Sriwichai S, Vesely BA, Silva JC, Waters NC, Thaloengsok S, Saingam P, Chaisatit C, Lertsethtakarn P, Sok S, Prom S, Chaudhury S, Lon C, Lek D, Saunders D, O'Connor TD, Takala-Harrison S
articles.details: Genomic analysis of 107 P. falciparum isolates from Cambodian military and farmers (2014-2016) using whole-genome sequencing and identity-by-descent methods. Mean IBD sharing was 40%, significantly higher in Oddar Meanchey than Kratie province (p<2.2e-16). Within Oddar Meanchey, farmer isolates showed 7.2% more IBD sharing than mixed-occupation pairs (pd=99.9). Highly related clusters (>90% IBD) were associated with occupation (p=0.0025), K13 mutations (p=0.0001), and PfCRT mutations (p=0.0001). Results suggest geography and occupation shape parasite relatedness and drug-resistance lineages.

articles.headline: Anhui Province malaria re-transmission risk: spatial clustering and vector modeling
articles.title_original: Assessing the risk of malaria re-transmission in Anhui Province, China: an integration of spatiotemporal scan statistics and ecological niche modeling.
articles.authors: Liu B, Zhang T, Xue J, Huo S, He H, Tan X, Li W, Li S
articles.details: Spatial-temporal analysis of imported malaria cases (2017-2023) and An. sinensis vector distribution in Anhui Province, China using SaTScan and ecological niche modeling. Persistent hotspot clusters identified in 7 districts with consistent distribution except 2021 (P<0.05). Ensemble model demonstrated strong predictive performance (AUC=0.943, TSS=0.759) for vector distribution. An. sinensis suitable habitat primarily in central and southern regions, influenced by vegetation index, night-light brightness, and precipitation. Findings support targeted surveillance and vector control in high-risk regions.

articles.headline: Pediatric malaria shows high rate of neurological sequelae despite treatment
articles.title_original: Malaria in Children is Associated with a High Rate of Neurological Sequelae and Need for Rehabilitative Therapies in Germany.
articles.authors: Adolph JE, Pentek C, Rink L, Held CJ, Della Marina A, Gangfuß A, Kölbel H, Dziobaka J, Rath PM, Verhasselt HL, Felderhoff-Müser U, Dohna-Schwake C, Goretzki SC
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 11 pediatric malaria cases (median age 9.5 years) in Germany; 91% P. falciparum, 72.7% severe malaria. Despite guideline-appropriate treatment (64% received artesunate), 82% had residual neurological symptoms, 44% showed neurocognitive deficits, 3 developed epilepsies, and 3 had corpus callosum and trigonal lesions on MRI. At 12 months, 77.8% showed clinical improvement, highlighting the need for long-term coordinated neurological follow-up and rehabilitative care.

articles.headline: Out-of-pocket payments create major barriers to malaria control
articles.title_original: Implications of out-of-pocket payments for the control of malaria in developing countries: A narrative review.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Obeagu GU
articles.details: Narrative review synthesizing evidence on out-of-pocket (OOP) payment barriers to malaria treatment in developing countries. Findings reveal OOP payments significantly hinder timely treatment access, disproportionately affecting low-income and rural households, leading to delayed treatment-seeking and underutilization of preventive measures like insecticide-treated nets. Government subsidies, community health insurance, and donor-funded programs were identified as effective solutions. Authors recommend scaling subsidized care and strengthening public health systems to improve equity and malaria control effectiveness.

articles.headline: Diabetes-malaria comorbidity increases parasitemia and vaccine antigen responses
articles.title_original: Malaria-diabetes comorbidity is linked to higher parasitaemia and enhanced IgG response to malaria vaccine candidate antigens.
articles.authors: Agyare G, Courtin D, Asamoah Sakyi S, Nakotey GK, Adjeley Frempong N, Barnie PA, Tchum SK, Victor Nuvor S, Amoani B
articles.details: Hospital-based case-control study of 144 Ghanaians (40 diabetes alone, 25 diabetes+malaria, 41 malaria alone, 38 controls) measuring IgG responses to malaria vaccine candidate antigens. Patients with comorbidity had significantly higher parasitemia (1702 vs 932, p=0.02). IgG responses to GLURP-R2 were 1.60-fold higher in the comorbidity group (β=0.47, p=0.01) and 1.43-fold in malaria-only (β=0.36, p=0.03). In multivariate analysis adjusted for age and sex, comorbidity showed 1.38-fold higher GLURP-R0 response (β=0.32, p=0.027) and 1.34-fold MSP1 response (β=0.29, p=0.048). Findings suggest diabetes enhances parasite multiplication while augmenting antibody responses to malaria antigens.

articles.headline: Mass drug administration prevents malaria in school-aged children
articles.title_original: Impacts of Seasonal Mass Drug Administration in School-Aged Children on Chronic Malaria Infections in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Paré IGL, Da DF, Guigma F, Bazié T, Somé BM, Djègbè NDC, Cissé F, Ouédraogo K, Somé AF, Kaboré J, Churcher TS, Dabiré RK
articles.details: Seasonal intervention study in Burkina Faso with monthly parasitological surveys of school-aged children: uninfected cohort (no treatment) and parasitemic asymptomatic cohort receiving artemether-lumefantrine. Results showed 93% of uninfected children remained parasite-free and 91% of treated children remained negative during follow-up, with only 5-9% persistent infections in both cohorts. msp1/msp2 genotyping revealed identical haplotypes over time within individuals, and drug-resistance markers (pfcrt/pfmdr1) were at low frequencies. Malaria transmission was interrupted during the dry season, with early MDA preventing infection in >90% of participants.

articles.headline: R21/MATRIX vaccine shows high acceptability despite low awareness in Nigeria
articles.title_original: R21/MATRIX malaria vaccine: awareness, perception, acceptability, and willingness to pay among caregivers of under-five children in endemic communities in Lagos, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Agboola P, Precious DN, Adebisi YA, Kingsley OO, Olaniyi P, Ezekiel A, Ode OP, Aderounmu B, Olusogo AP, Opeyemi FI, Adeyemi-Benson OS
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional survey of 372 caregivers in Lagos, Nigeria assessing awareness, perception, and willingness to pay for the R21/MATRIX malaria vaccine. Vaccine awareness was low (9.7%), but perception was very positive (98.1% believed it safe and effective) with high acceptability (76.6% would "definitely" vaccinate). Willingness to pay was reported by 71.2%, with 51.9% willing to pay ₦1,001-₦5,000 per dose. Higher household income and guardianship status significantly predicted willingness to pay. Authors recommend targeted awareness campaigns to improve vaccine knowledge.

articles.headline: Most Ethiopian health facilities lack malaria service readiness for ANC
articles.title_original: Malaria service readiness and associated factors among health facilities that provide antenatal care services in Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study using generalised estimating equation analysis.
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Asemahagn MA, Gelaye KA, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Azage M
articles.details: Cross-sectional assessment of malaria service readiness in 1,156 health facilities providing antenatal care across Ethiopia. Only 15.7% (95% CI 13.8-18.0) demonstrated full malaria service readiness, with 14.3% lacking all key components. Substantial regional variation was observed: Somali region had higher readiness (AOR=1.53, 95% CI 1.19-1.95), while other regions showed significantly lower readiness. Governance factors strongly predicted readiness: client suggestion boxes (AOR=1.60), recent report submission (AOR=2.79), and monthly staff meetings (AOR=1.64). Findings indicate urgent need for strengthening supply chains, staff training, and health system governance.

articles.headline: Urban malaria transmission in Ethiopia shows marked town-level heterogeneity
articles.title_original: Heterogeneity of malaria transmission in urban settings in Ethiopia: A seroprevalence and risk factor analysis.
articles.authors: Teka H, Fekadu S, Ejigu LA, Sisay S, Abdo M, Demisse M, Mamo H, Drakeley C, Byrne I, Medhin G, Golassa L, Gadisa E, Tadesse FG
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional survey of 912 individuals in 3 Ethiopian urban towns using RDTs, qPCR, and serological analysis for P. falciparum and P. vivax exposure. Current malaria prevalence was low (4% by qPCR; 1.7% P. falciparum, 2.3% P. vivax), but seroprevalence revealed substantial recent exposure: 19.4% for P. falciparum and 18% for P. vivax. Marked heterogeneity across towns: Awash Sebat Kilo showed highest exposure (36.5% P. falciparum; 28% P. vivax) vs. Adama (2.8% both). Proximity to breeding sites (OR=2.8, p=0.037) and age >15 years (OR=4.0, p<0.001) were significant risk factors, emphasizing need for targeted local control.

articles.headline: Climate thresholds for malaria incidence identified in Colombian municipalities
articles.title_original: Integrating causal inference and machine learning to quantify climate-malaria relationships: Evidence of temperature and rainfall thresholds from Colombian municipalities.
articles.authors: Gutiérrez JD
articles.details: Ecological study of 1,075,112 malaria cases across 969 Colombian municipalities (2007-2023) using causal inference methods (Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation with directed acyclic graphs) to estimate exposure-response curves for rainfall and temperature effects on monthly malaria incidence. Results revealed non-linear relationships with optimal temperature of 25°C and approximately 37mm rainfall associated with highest malaria incidence. Negative control tests indicated residual confounding (p<0.05) with low-to-moderate unmeasured confounding tolerance (E-value 1.14-1.48). Findings support integration of climate thresholds into early-warning systems for targeted malaria control.

articles.headline: Health systems need comprehensive strengthening for GMS malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Strengthening the health systems at national level for malaria elimination in the Greater Mekong Subregion countries: a qualitative study.
articles.authors: Htike W, Oo WH, Bennett CM, Agius PA, Barry AE, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: Multi-country qualitative study of 39 Greater Mekong Subregion stakeholders (5 policymakers, 12 ministry health staff, 16 implementing partners, 6 technical agencies) using semi-structured interviews and reflexive thematic analysis aligned with WHO health system building blocks. Stakeholders identified that successful malaria elimination required inputs from all six WHO building blocks including strong political commitment, targeted interventions for high-risk groups, reliable forecasting and supply chains, skilled workforce, and robust quality assurance. Major barriers included lack of targeted high-risk group interventions in national policies, incomplete private-sector reporting, workforce shortages, supply-chain administrative constraints, and declining international donor funding. Authors recommend comprehensive health system strengthening for GMS countries to transition from control to elimination phase.

articles.headline: P. vivax CyRPA protein shows promise as vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity, natural selection, and immunological features of the Plasmodium vivax CyRPA protein: Implications for vaccine development.
articles.authors: Garzón-Ospina D, Buitrago SP, Cepeda-Riaño N, Castro-Cavadía CJ, Yasnot-Acosta MF
articles.details: Genetic and immunological analysis of the Plasmodium vivax Cysteine-Rich Protective Antigen (CyRPA) using 950 pvcyrpa sequences including 42 Colombian isolates. The pvcyrpa locus showed high nucleotide diversity shaped by natural selection and recombination, with identified B- and T-cell epitopes largely within polymorphic regions displaying strong binding affinities for multiple HLA alleles. Predicted epitopes overlapped with regions previously shown to elicit immune responses in natural infections. Immune simulation of a multiepitope C-terminal construct predicted robust humoral memory response. Findings support the conserved C-terminal region of PvCyRPA as a broadly reactive vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Molecular mechanisms of Plasmodium gametocytogenesis reviewed
articles.title_original: Molecular mechanisms involved in Plasmodium gametocytogenesis.
articles.authors: Scovino AM, Oliveira-da-Silva RS, Almeida-da-Silva J, Mendes KD, da Silva-Junior EB, Decote-Ricardo D, Freire-de-Lima L, Freire-de-Lima CG, Totino PRR, Morrot A
articles.details: Review consolidating advances in understanding gametocytogenesis in P. falciparum, focusing on how sexual differentiation is modulated by environmental stressors such as lysophosphatidylcholine depletion and regulated through transcriptional mechanisms (AP2-G, H3K9me3) and post-transcriptional processes (PfPuf1, PfPuf2, mRNA methylation). Understanding these interconnected regulatory pathways is essential for identifying novel therapeutic targets and developing transmission-blocking vaccines.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum Plasmodium species drive Zanzibar malaria epidemiology
articles.title_original: Understanding the Epidemiology of Malaria in Zanzibar Through Molecular and Serological Analysis of Samples Collected During Reactive Case Detection.
articles.authors: Goel V, Hassan W, Murphy C, Choloi BB, Ali M, Mohamed B, Zacharia A, Dominick MC, Thwai K, Mohammed S, Shija SJ, Bailey JA, Björkman A, Ngasala BE, Rogier E, Juliano JJ, Lin JT
articles.details: Cross-sectional serological study of 1,618 participants from 35 reactive case detection activities in Zanzibar (May 2022–January 2023) measuring MSP1-19kD IgG responses and active parasitemia by species-specific PCR. Overall seroprevalence was 35.3% (P. falciparum 29.8%, P. ovale 5.8%, P. malariae 5.9%, P. vivax 5.9%); active infections occurred in 6.0%. Travel to mainland Tanzania was the dominant risk factor. Authors conclude non-falciparum species significantly contribute to epidemiology, requiring expanded control efforts beyond P. falciparum.

articles.headline: PfApiAT2 proline transporter essential for P. falciparum transmission
articles.title_original: PfApiAT2 is a proline transporter essential for the transmission of Plasmodium falciparum by the mosquito vector.
articles.authors: Wirth D, Khushu M, Kissel RC, Kauffman J, Taccheri C, Singh N, Summers R, Plant L, Catteruccia F, Bopp S
articles.details: Experimental study demonstrating that PfApiAT2, a proline-specific transporter, is essential for P. falciparum oocyst development in Anopheles gambiae. PfApiAT2-knockout parasites formed severely stunted oocysts with complete block in sporogony and defective sporozoite production. This growth defect was fully rescued by nutrient supplementation to mosquitoes, revealing a critical nutritional dependency of parasites on their mosquito vector.

articles.headline: L1014F kdr resistance correlates with high Microsporidia MB burden
articles.title_original: The L1014F Kdr mutation is associated with a higher prevalence and load of the Plasmodium-blocking symbiont Microsporidia MB In Anopheles Gambiae s.l. In Benin.
articles.authors: Sovi A, Ahouandjinou MJ, Aïkpon R, Totongnon GS, Yovogan B, Syme T, Dembélé A, Koukpo CZ, Sagbohan H, Ossè R, Agbo-Ola A, Gnanguenon V, Toffodji U, Megido RC, Hougbe SZ, Zoungbédji DM, Konkon AK, Cook J, Padonou GG, Protopopoff N, Akogbéto MC, Messenger LA, Adoha CJ
articles.details: Entomological cross-sectional study of 1,040 Anopheles gambiae complex mosquitoes from 60 villages in Benin genotyped for L1014F kdr and screened for Microsporidia MB by qPCR. Microsporidia MB was absent in kdr-SS mosquitoes but present in 1.9% of kdr-RS and higher proportions of kdr-RR (mean DNA load 23.23 ng/µL vs 10.13 ng/µL). None of Microsporidia-infected mosquitoes carried P. falciparum. Elevation and soil explained 78% and 20% of habitat suitability.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemiology, control progress, and emerging resistance threats
articles.title_original: Malaria: a review on its current epidemiological status and management strategies.
articles.authors: Okello G, Aucamp M
articles.details: Comprehensive review of global malaria epidemiology focusing on Africa. The WHO African Region achieved 40% reduction in incidence and 60% mortality decline (2000–2022) through ITNs, IRS, rapid diagnostics, IPT, artemisinin-based combination therapies, and vaccines (RTS,S/AS01, R21/Matrix-M) in children <5 years. Emerging challenges include poverty, climate change, inadequate healthcare, outdoor transmission, new vector species, and rising drug/insecticide resistance. Authors recommend cross-border control, expanded seasonal chemoprevention, molecular resistance identification, and novel antimalarials.

articles.headline: E205D CYP6P3 mutation drives pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: The E205D mutation in CYP6P3 drives pyrethroid insecticide resistance in the African malaria mosquito vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Kengne-Ouafo JA, Kouamo MFM, Muhammad A, Tepa A, Ntadoun S, Mugenzi LMJ, Tekoh T, Hearn J, Tchouakui M, Wondji M, Ibrahim SS, Wondji CS
articles.details: Molecular and functional study identifying E205D mutation in CYP6P3 as driver of pyrethroid insecticide resistance in A. gambiae via whole-genome sequencing, in vitro enzyme kinetics, transgenic flies, and field correlation. The 205D variant showed 3.7-fold higher catalytic efficiency than E205 for permethrin and α-cypermethrin. In field populations, CYP6P3-E205D correlated with pyrethroid resistance (OR 26.4, p<0.0001) and reduced bed-net efficacy (PermaNet 3.0: homozygous resistance OR 6.1, p=0.011).

articles.headline: Malaria and dengue surveillance during Indonesian capital relocation
articles.title_original: A study protocol for a baseline community-based cross-sectional study to investigate malaria and dengue transmission dynamics in a development area of new capital city, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Pradana A, Oktaviani M, Prameswari H, Supriyanto D, Waluyo P, Ndoen E, Rosadi D, Lubis I, Suwarti S, Subekti D, Kusumaningrum T, Carroll M, Djaafara B, de Alwis R, Mishra S, Drakeley C, Leder K, Lechner A, Fornace K, Elyazar I, Surendra H
articles.details: Study protocol for community-based baseline cross-sectional survey in Ibu Kota Nusantara, East Kalimantan investigating malaria and dengue transmission during urban development. Approximately 2,000 individuals aged >1 year will be enrolled with finger-prick blood collection for multiplex bead-based serological assays detecting antibodies to malaria species (including P. knowlesi) and dengue serotypes, plus malaria RDTs. Demographic, clinical, environmental, and geolocation data will be collected. Geostatistical models will assess seroprevalence, spatial patterns, and risk factors.

articles.headline: Severe P. vivax malaria with pulmonary involvement case report
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria Due to Plasmodium vivax With Pulmonary Involvement: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Valido T, Goncalves A, Sanches M, Costa Silva T, Pereira C
articles.details: Case report of 46-year-old woman from India presenting with severe P. vivax malaria (50% parasitemia, 8,645 parasites/µL) complicated by respiratory distress, severe anemia, and thrombocytopenia. Chest CT showed extensive ground-glass opacification requiring ICU admission and high-flow oxygen. Treated with artemether-lumefantrine and doxycycline (IV artesunate unavailable), achieving parasitemia clearance by day 3 with successful oxygen weaning. Case highlights P. vivax as cause of severe malaria beyond traditionally recognized P. falciparum.

articles.headline: P. vivax population genetics track transmission decline and rebound
articles.title_original: Population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Fola AA, Mehra S, Razook Z, Lautu-Gumal D, Nate E, Lee S, Kattenberg JH, Koepfli C, Kazura J, Ome-Kaius M, Laman M, Robinson LJ, Mueller I, Barry AE
articles.details: Retrospective molecular epidemiology study analyzing 336 P. vivax isolates from serial cross-sectional surveys over a decade in Papua New Guinea (East Sepik 2005/2012/2016, Madang 2006/2010/2014) using genome-wide SNP barcoding. Madang showed possible bottleneck at low transmission with rapid recovery; East Sepik showed less dramatic impact with ongoing residual transmission. Genetic differentiation between provinces increased with transmission decline and decreased with resurgence. Sustained control efforts are essential for lasting impact on P. vivax populations.

articles.headline: P. fragile co-infection disrupts gut immunity in SIV-infected macaques
articles.title_original: Gastrointestinal Mucosal Disruptions During ART-Treated SIV/Plasmodium fragile Co-Infection.
articles.authors: Nemphos SM, Green HC, Prusak JE, Fell SL, Midkiff C, Rodgers A, Perret J, Goff K, Miller J, Varnado M, Didier K, Valencia N, Moström MJ, Tatum C, Barnes MB, Krzykwa CE, Rowe LA, Allers C, Grasperge B, De Paris K, Maness NJ, Kaur A, Londono-Renteria B, Blair RV, Manuzak JA
articles.details: Longitudinal experimental study in 6 rhesus macaques examining gastrointestinal disruption during ART-treated SIV/P. fragile co-infection (weeks 0–20 post-inoculation). Following P. fragile co-infection at week 12, duodenal NET-forming granulocytes significantly associated with peripheral SIV burden (not parasitemia). SIV infection caused significant CCR5+ CD4+ T cell decline in peripheral blood, colon, and duodenum. Authors conclude P. fragile co-infection may cause mucosal disruptions sustaining SIV replication despite ART.

articles.headline: SR1 interacts with nuclear and rhoptry proteins in Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Identification of serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 1-interacting proteins in Plasmodium berghei.
articles.authors: Niikura M, Koyama Y, Fukuda Y, Kato K, Fukutomi T
articles.details: Molecular study identifying protein interactions of serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 1 (SR1/PbSR1) in P. berghei using transgenic parasites expressing GFP-fused PbSR1 and immunoprecipitation-mass spectrometry. IP-MS detected interactions with nuclear proteins (RNA-binding proteins, small nuclear ribonucleoproteins) and cytoplasmic rhoptry proteins (RhopH1, RhopH2, RhopH3). Live-cell imaging showed PbSR1 and RhopH2 co-localize in trophozoites and gametocytes but not mature schizonts, suggesting developmental-stage-specific interactions.

articles.headline: Transfluthrin spatial repellent trial for malaria in Uganda
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the protective efficacy of a transfluthrin-based spatial repellent product to reduce malaria prevalence in Uganda: study protocol for a cluster-randomised double-blinded control trial-the Mossie-GO trial.
articles.authors: Dennehy J, Dyall W, Jenkins A, Bradley J, Sserwanga A, Kigozi R, Bwanika JB, Nuwa A, Mawejje H, Turner MA, Wallace R, Lyle FGA, Hiscox A, Livesey AK, Logan JG, Achan J, Jones RT
articles.details: Study protocol for cluster-randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trial of transfluthrin-based spatial repellent device (Mossie-GO™) in 56 clusters in Uganda. Primary endpoint is malaria prevalence in children ≤5 years sampled at baseline (100 per cluster) and 6, 12 months (60 per cluster from different households). Secondary endpoints include entomological correlates, user acceptance, and transfluthrin air concentrations. Registered at ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT06232954, 01/04/2024).

articles.headline: SIMPLseq: high-sensitivity P. falciparum genotyping with contamination tracking
articles.title_original: SIMPLseq: a high-sensitivity Plasmodium falciparum genotyping and PCR contamination tracking tool.
articles.authors: Schwabl P, Amaya-Romero JE, Kelley KA, Manrique P, Murphy SC, Crompton PD, Neafsey DE
articles.details: 6-locus PCR amplicon miniplex optimized for low-parasitemia genotyping with contamination detection via inline barcodes. Achieved 100% average locus detection at ≥0.5 parasites/μl and outperformed 4CAST comparator. Tested on 1420 Malian dried blood spot samples; sentinel barcoding correctly identified all 24 deliberate contaminations and 39 unintentional contaminations. Applications include recurrent infection classification and polyclonality estimation.

articles.headline: An. funestus spatial distribution and malaria transmission in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Updating Anopheles funestus group members in Burkina Faso: species distribution and contribution to malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Zongo ON, Toé I, Kientega M, Yemien SOG, Sawadogo G, Kaboré H, Agolinou AAM, Traore N, Kiendrebeogo E, Millogo AA, Niang A, Namountougou M, Lanfrancotti A, Nolan T, Maiga H, Diabaté A
articles.details: Entomological survey across 67 sites (Oct-Dec 2022) detected 617 An. funestus group members. An. funestus s.s. comprised 95% (589/617), predominantly in Hauts-Bassins (30%) and Sud-Ouest (55%) regions. Zoophilic behavior common (64.3%). P. falciparum infection found in 5.76% (33/572) of An. funestus s.l., with 5.6% in An. funestus s.s. and 0.174% in An. rivulorum-like.

articles.headline: Six neuroprotective drugs tested for cerebral malaria adjunct therapy
articles.title_original: Evaluation of clinically approved neuroprotective drugs as adjunct therapies in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Joshi P, Shaham SH, Shivahare R, Yadav K, Sharma A, Parul, Shukla S, Tripathi R
articles.details: Six clinically-approved drugs (topiramate, rasagiline, vinpocetine, cilostazol, piracetam, rivastigmine) tested against P. falciparum in vitro and in experimental cerebral malaria mouse model (P. berghei ANKA). Early oral treatment with cilostazol, piracetam, or rivastigmine delayed CM onset and improved survival. Rivastigmine combined with α/β-arteether markedly improved outcomes when initiated after neurological signs appeared, suggesting practical low-cost adjunct therapy.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria and severe anemia cause long-term cognitive impairment
articles.title_original: Cerebral Malaria and Severe Malarial Anemia, but not Other Forms of Severe Malaria, Are Associated With Long-Term Cognitive Impairment.
articles.authors: Bangirana P, Bond C, Namazzi R, Datta D, Mellencamp KA, Opoka RO, John CC
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 600 Ugandan children (6 months–4 years) with severe malaria evaluated 12 months post-discharge. Children with cerebral malaria had significantly lower cognition (z-score −0.66, P=.001) and severe malarial anemia (z-score −0.71, P<.001) versus community controls. Cognitive impairment (z-score <−2) occurred in 10.6% with cerebral malaria and 12.1% with severe anemia versus 1.9% controls; other severe malaria forms did not impair cognition.

articles.headline: Commentary on severe malaria phenotypes and cognitive impairment
articles.title_original: Not All Forms of Severe Malaria Impair Long-term Cognition-More Characterization, More Questions.
articles.authors: Seydel KB, Ray STJ
articles.details: Perspective piece on severe malaria cognitive outcomes. Title suggests further characterization and investigation of relationships between specific severe malaria phenotypes (cerebral malaria and severe malarial anemia) and long-term cognitive impairment.

articles.headline: Patient experiences of malaria signs and symptoms South Africa
articles.title_original: Experiences of people diagnosed with malaria regarding signs and symptoms at a selected village in the Vhembe District, Limpopo province, South Africa.
articles.authors: Kwinda W, Mudzweda AD, Luhalima T
articles.details: Qualitative study with exploratory, descriptive design in Mhinga Village, Vhembe District, South Africa. Semi-structured interviews examined patient experiences with malaria signs and symptoms and circumstances prompting consultation. Thematic analysis revealed diverse symptoms experienced across organ systems. Study aimed to increase awareness of prevention strategies to reduce transmission.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic disparities in malaria infection and treatment outcomes
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic health equity with malaria burden: quantifying slope (Relative) index of inequity in malaria infection outcomes in the Southeastern-coastal Tanzania.
articles.authors: Liu L, Lu S, Ding W, Wang Z, Abdulla S, Lv S, Li S, Chaki PP, Zhou XN, Mlacha YP, Wang D
articles.details: Analysis of China-Tanzania Demonstration Project baseline data (2019) from three districts (Rufiji, Kilwa, Kibiti). Higher socioeconomic status associated with lower malaria infection risk (OR=0.9975, 95% CI: 0.9972–0.9978), lower treatment costs (coefficient −3.13, P<0.05), and fewer work absences (coefficient −0.0017, P<0.05). Rufiji district showed most significant socioeconomic equality in infection risk (SII=−12.62%), treatment cost, and work absences.

articles.headline: Cortical edema sparing patterns predict outcome in pediatric cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Peri-Rolandic and Occipital Sparing Cortical Edema: A Prevalent MRI Finding in Pediatric Patients with Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Vidal L, Zandifar A, Kim JDU, Tierradentro-García LO, Heng S, Meneses M, Small D, Chetcuti K, O'Brien N, Postels D, Taylor T, Goyal MS
articles.details: Retrospective review of admission brain MRIs from 190 Malawian children with cerebral malaria (2013-2019). Cortical edema identified in 163 scans, with predominant pattern of diffuse involvement with sparing of occipital and peri-Rolandic areas (63.2%, 103/163). Sparing inversely correlated with brain volume score (β=−0.26, P<.001) and improved outcomes (OR: 0.3; 95% CI: 0.1–0.6; P=.002).

articles.headline: Mathematical model: 80% LLIN use reduces Nigeria malaria incidence 98%
articles.title_original: Modelling the effect of long-lasting insecticidal nets on malaria transmission dynamics in Kebbi State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Bakare EA, Olasupo II, Imoudu M, Abidemi A, Daniel DO, Osikoya SA, Mogbojuri OA, Nwana AO, Oniyelu DO, Olorunfemi RD, Olagbami OS, Agboola DO, Ikediashi SI, Adedeji A, Ikuseka O, Fadairo M, Odewale O, Onwuka IG, Hansinon A, Bakare DA, White LJ, Chitnis N, Oresanya O, Okoronkwo C, Nelson E, Kosoko S, Ogban GI
articles.details: Deterministic compartmental transmission model for Kebbi State using case data (Jan 2015–May 2024). If 80% sustained LLIN usage had been achieved since 2015, approximately 5.2 million cases would have been averted (97.98% reduction). Projecting forward, sustaining 80% LLIN use would avert approximately 3 million cases by May 2029, representing 78.93% reduction in projected incidence.

articles.headline: PfRIPR vaccine candidate shows high genetic diversity in Senegal
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of the malaria vaccine candidate PfRIPR in a high transmission region of Senegal.
articles.authors: Nair M, Geering G, Agarwal A, Li R, Qiao Y, Xiao Q, Pouye MN, Thiam LG, Ba A, Hagadorn KA, Cisse A, Guerra N, Tawaldemedhen Y, Mangou K, Moore AJ, Diallo F, Sene SD, Sadio BD, Zhang E, Shapiro L, Patel SD, Mbengue A, Vigan-Womas I, Sheng Z, Bei AK
articles.details: Deep amplicon sequencing of RH5-interacting protein (RIPR) in 89 P. falciparum isolates from Kédougou, Senegal. Non-synonymous SNPs identified in 71.9% (64/89) samples, with 26 total SNPs including 15 novel variants. Seven of 16 SNPs predicted to destabilize RIPR; three SNPs (Q737K, T738K, V840L) in EGF5-8 domains overlap known neutralizing antibody binding sites, potentially affecting vaccine efficacy.

articles.headline: P. falciparum prefers Duffy-positive RBCs, may drive Duffy negativity
articles.title_original: Invasion preferences suggest a possible role for Plasmodium falciparum parasites in the expansion of Duffy negativity in West and Central Africa.
articles.authors: Manneh B, Introini V, Reed J, Rotariu M, Antrobus R, Cicuta P, Weekes MP, Penman BS, Rayner JC
articles.details: Flow cytometry and microscopy showed P. falciparum parasites consistently prefer to invade Duffy-positive RBCs over Duffy-negative. Evolutionary-epidemiological model incorporating population immunity shows FYBES allele (Duffy-negative) frequency increases at low transmission but decreases at high transmission, suggesting P. falciparum selection pressure contributes to Duffy negativity distribution in sub-Saharan Africa alongside P. vivax pressure.

articles.headline: Nigeria malaria incidence declined 28% but regional disparities persist
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal inequalities in malaria incidence and mortality among children aged 0-4 years in Nigeria: a subnational analysis, 2010-2019.
articles.authors: Oso TA, Okesanya OJ, Adebayo UO, Ayelaagbe OB, Ahmed MM, Obadeyi KB, Eshun G, Saliu OM, Lucero-Prisno DE 3rd
articles.details: Analysis of IHME estimates from 36 Nigerian states and Federal Capital Territory (2010-2019) showed national malaria incidence declined from 103 to 74.5 per 1000 (27.7% reduction) and mortality from 477.3 to 237.6 per 100,000 (50.2% reduction). Northern states (Zamfara, Kano, Katsina) maintained highest burden despite decline; southern states remained lowest. Subnational inequality narrowed: incidence coefficient of variation fell from 46.3% to 28.1%.

articles.headline: Faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling
articles.title_original: Building contextually-relevant training programs for scientific development: process and lessons learned in implementing two iterations of a faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling.
articles.authors: Onyango L, Ouédraogo-Ametchie G, Stahlfeld A, Holden TM, Richter R, Runge M, Toh KB, Agorinya I, Mensah B, Gerardin J
articles.details: A 4-month faculty enrichment program at Northwestern University in 2022 and 2023 trained participants in technical skills, communication, and malaria epidemiology. Two cohorts were interviewed at baseline, midline, and endline. Participants valued growth in technical expertise and research skills; reported challenges with cross-disciplinary learning. Instructors adapted teaching approaches and strengthened their own technical capacity.

articles.headline: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency prevalence in malaria patients
articles.title_original: Pooled prevalence of Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency among malaria patients in Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Dessie G, Getnet M, Getahun AB, Mengstie TA, Asefa T, Enyew EF, Yewodiaw TK, Endale HT, Endalew HL
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis examined G6PDd prevalence in malaria patients across PubMed, Cochrane, and Hinari databases. Overall pooled prevalence was 6% (95% CI 0.04–0.08), with highest prevalence 23% in Jimma (95% CI 0.06–0.41) and lowest 1% across three endemic regions (95% CI 0.00–0.03). Prevalent mutant variants included A376G, A376T, and G202A.

articles.headline: WASH factors and malaria prevalence in Ghana children
articles.title_original: Household characteristics, water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH) and malaria prevalence among children aged 6-59 months in Ghana: an analysis of the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey.
articles.authors: Klu D, Alhassan A, Vidzro ES, Aberese-Ako M
articles.details: Analysis of 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey (3,255 households, ages 6–59 months) found 3.7% malaria prevalence by RDT, with 78.9% ITN ownership but only 51.5% usage; 41.5% anaemic. Multivariate regression showed wealthier households lower risk (aOR 0.15, 95% CI 0.03–0.71); anaemic children higher risk (aOR 2.03, 95% CI 1.36–3.04); external toilets increased risk (aOR 16.64, 95% CI 2.06–134.57).

articles.headline: Providers lack confidence in negative malaria rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: Positive tests are all alike, every negative test is negative in its own way: lack of confidence in negative malaria rapid diagnostic tests in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Sugg KN, Mpata FW, Humes MD, Aliango Marachto D, Rajan R, Winch PJ
articles.details: Qualitative study of 64 semi-structured interviews at 16 DRC health facilities, 12 key informant interviews, and 24 focus groups examined malaria RDT interpretation. Providers trusted positive tests more than negative ones, attributing false negatives to improperly treated malaria, early infection, or species misdetection. Test positivity rates reported through national HMIS exceeded those from automated Deki reader database.

articles.headline: Post-malaria neurological syndrome and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis
articles.title_original: Post malaria neurological syndrome and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis: a case highlighting an ongoing debate.
articles.authors: Rodrigues BA, Magriço M, Krupka D, Serrazina F
articles.details: Case report of post-malaria neurological syndrome concurrent with acute disseminated encephalomyelitis, illustrating ongoing clinical debate regarding this neurological complication following malaria infection.

articles.headline: Nuclear and organellar mechanisms regulate apicoplast development in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Independent nuclear and organellar mechanisms determine apicoplast fate in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Shahar M, Qasem A, Shamay E, Tissawak A, Maron Y, Florentin A
articles.details: Live-imaging study tracked apicoplast subcellular structures throughout P. falciparum's 48-hour intraerythrocytic cycle, identifying four distinct morphological stages correlating with nuclear replication. A critical 'Crown' stage was required for nucleus-apicoplast attachment. Nuclear DNA replication inhibition blocked apicoplast biogenesis early, while apicoplast genome replication inhibition disrupted the Crown stage, preventing segregation into daughter cells.

articles.headline: Malaria in Saudi Arabia: 15-year surveillance and burden trends
articles.title_original: Malaria: Examining Persistence at the Margins of Endemicity over 15 Years in Saudi Arabia.
articles.authors: Alruwaili Y
articles.details: Analysis of national surveillance 2010–2024 identified 52,627 malaria cases, predominantly P. falciparum and P. vivax/ovale in ages ≥10 years. Burden concentrated in 7 endemic regions, then restricted to Aseer and Jazan. From 2021 onward, no indigenous transmission occurred; cases were classified as imported. ARIMA forecasting predicted stable national and regional malaria trends through 2030.

articles.headline: Airport malaria cluster of four P. falciparum cases in Libya
articles.title_original: Airport Malaria Cluster in Certified Malaria-Free Country, Libya, 2024.
articles.authors: Alarbi AM, Elhaddad AB, Almehdawi NM, Saadawi WK, Aqeehal H, Elalem M
articles.details: In November 2024, an autochthonous cluster of 4 P. falciparum cases (1 fatal) was identified near Benina International Airport, Benghazi, Libya. Epidemiologic and entomologic investigation ruled out local transmission, confirming airport malaria from imported vectors.

articles.headline: Habitat suitability and biting probability models for malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Spatial-temporal coupling of malaria vector habitat suitability and biting probability.
articles.authors: Aduvukha GR, Abdel-Rahman EM, Mutanga O, Odindi J, Tonnang HEZ
articles.details: Species distribution modeling (MaxEnt) integrated remotely sensed, climatic, and topographic data to assess Anopheles habitat suitability. Fuzzy logic rule-based models incorporated vector presence, human availability, insecticide resistance, and bed net usage to assess spatial-temporal biting risk 2000–2018. Validation on An. gambiae complex observations (n=25, 2017–2021) achieved 91% accuracy. Models showed reduced biting probability with increased bed net usage.

articles.headline: Diabetes and malaria co-morbidity: clinical outcomes and molecular mechanisms
articles.title_original: Diabetes Mellitus and Malaria Co-Existence as Deadly Burden in Sub-Saharan Africa: A Review of Clinical Outcomes and Molecular Interactions.
articles.authors: Erhabor OG, Abdullahi AD, Sani A, Gabdo MB, Saad RS, Abubakar M, Isah MB, Simelane MBC, Umar IA, Ibrahim MA
articles.details: Extensive review examined clinical studies and molecular interactions between diabetes and malaria in sub-Saharan Africa. Malaria inflammation, oxidative stress, and immune activation disrupt insulin signaling and beta-cell function, increasing hyperglycemia in diabetics. Chronic hyperglycemia damages endothelium and impairs immunity, facilitating parasite survival. TNF-α and insulin signaling pathways emerged as pivotal links, with diabetics showing higher subclinical infection rates and prolonged parasitemia.

articles.headline: Benzoxaboroles inhibit P. falciparum carbonic anhydrase with antiplasmodial activity
articles.title_original: Benzoxaboroles Are Inhibitors of the η-Class Carbonic Anhydrase From Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Bonardi A, Wein S, Mustafa M, Cerdan R, de Luca V, Capasso C, Gratteri P, Supuran CT, Nocentini A, Winum JY
articles.details: Benzoxaborole derivatives (compounds 5–18) were evaluated for inhibition of P. falciparum carbonic anhydrase (PfaCA) and in vitro antiplasmodial activity against P. falciparum 3D7. Compounds 13 and 14 were most potent PfaCA inhibitors (KI 0.45 and 0.43 µM) with notable antiplasmodial activity (IC50 34 and 47 µM). Ethyl ester 7 showed strongest antiplasmodial activity (IC50 2.5 µM).

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Karen State, Myanmar during conflict and pandemic
articles.title_original: Re-emergence of malaria in Karen State, Myanmar: has the battle of Burma been lost?
articles.authors: Pateekhum C, Thu AM, Lwin KM, Nyaw SB, Tun SW, Daniel S, Khin A, Aung WL, Kain K, Imwong M, Day NP, White NJ, Nosten FH
articles.details: Observational study reviewed malariometric and genomic data from malaria clinics and village health workers in eastern Karen State (350,000 population) 2020–2024. P. falciparum cases increased 12-fold and P. vivax threefold, with increased transmission for P. falciparum (RR 1.72, 95% CI 1.68–1.76) and P. vivax (RR 1.82, 95% CI 1.80–1.84), coinciding with reduced access to diagnosis and treatment. Clonal expansion of specific parasite lineages likely contributed.

articles.headline: Malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria.
articles.details: No abstract provided.

articles.headline: Whole sporozoite vaccine efficacy declines in endemic field settings
articles.title_original: The endemic dilemma: why whole sporozoite malaria vaccine efficacy falls in the field.
articles.authors: Hassert M, Harty JT
articles.details: Review examining why whole sporozoite vaccines (most effective malaria vaccines) show sharply reduced efficacy in endemic field trials compared to homologous controlled human malaria infection studies. Two hypotheses: antigen mismatch (limited vaccine genetic diversity fails to capture field parasite diversity) and host-context (genetics, infection history, microbiome, nutrition affect outcomes). Future vaccine success requires integrating parasite genomics, systems immunology, and animal models reflecting endemic contexts.

articles.headline: Mixed Plasmodium ovale-falciparum infections show distinct hematological profiles
articles.title_original: Comparison of routine clinical profiles of patients with imported P. falciparum malaria and co-infection with Plasmodium ovale wallikeri or Plasmodium ovale curtisi.
