articles.authors: Balsa-Vázquez J, Rubio-Muñoz JM, Duvignaud A, Alcedo S, de Ayala AP, Salas-Coronas J, Elía-López M, Cattaneo P, Arévalo-Cañas C, García-Bujalance S, Ruiz-Giardín JM, Norman FF, Rojo-Marcos G
articles.details: Retrospective cohort study comparing 28 P. ovale wallikeri/P. falciparum mixed cases, 11 P. ovale curtisi/P. falciparum mixed cases, and 117 P. falciparum mono-infections (by PCR). No significant differences in clinical severity or hospitalization. P. ovale wallikeri co-infections showed higher leukocyte counts (6.25 vs 4.85 × 10⁹/L, p=0.010) and lower parasitemia index (0.6% vs 1.0%, p=0.025). P. ovale curtisi co-infections had lower ALT (18.0 vs 27.3 IU/L, p=0.022) but more frequent thrombocytopenia (100% vs 51%, p=0.003) with lower platelet counts (105 vs 148 × 10⁹/L, p=0.036).

articles.headline: Males show delayed parasitemia detection versus females in CHMI
articles.title_original: Sex-based differences in Plasmodium infection in the control groups of controlled human malaria infection trials in malaria-naive populations in the USA and the Netherlands: a pooled analysis.
articles.authors: Duncombe CJ, Hergott DEB, Staubus W, Balke-Buijs M, Kublin JG, Duffy PE, Healy SA, Talley A, Jackson L, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Sauerwein RW, Roestenberg M, Murphy SC
articles.details: Pooled analysis of 102 malaria-naive participants (54 male, 48 female) from 13 controlled human malaria infection studies (2010–2024) with P. falciparum challenge. Mean time to first detectable parasitemia was longer in males (7.59 days, SD 1.15) versus females (7.17 days, SD 0.91). Adjusted analysis showed time to positivity occurred 8% later in males (time ratio 1.08, 95% CI 1.03–1.16). Males were significantly more likely to have detectable infection after day 7 (35% vs 13%, adjusted OR 5.20, 95% CI 1.52–17.70), suggesting sex-specific liver-stage dynamics.

articles.headline: Thermal profiling reveals dynamic protein complexes in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: MAP-X unveils the shapeshifting interactome of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Li Q, Chen Q
articles.details: Methods paper introducing meltome-assisted profiling of protein complexes (MAP-X) combined with machine learning to map protein complex dynamics in intact P. falciparum. Revealed highly dynamic interactome with identification of novel protein complexes and moonlighting proteins, shifting understanding from static lists to temporal protein networks.

articles.headline: Commercial qPCR shows superior P. vivax detection over microscopy
articles.title_original: Promising diagnostic performance of a commercial qPCR assay for Plasmodium vivax detection: Contributing to malaria control and elimination.
articles.authors: Cerilo-Filho M, Sampaio MP, Almeida MNG, A Silva A, Montoia G, Storti-Melo LM, Baptista ARS, Di Santi SM, Machado RLD
articles.details: Evaluation of a commercial qPCR assay for P. vivax detection in 167 samples. Assay demonstrated greater sensitivity than microscopy, detecting three additional positive cases. Assay also showed high specificity and no cross-reactivity, supporting its value as a reliable tool for malaria diagnosis and surveillance.

articles.headline: PCR-based malaria detection superior to microscopy in Pakistan survey
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic and host biomarker assessment of plasmodium infections in malaria-endemic Peshawar, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Khan MA, Fayaz S, Saeed M, Murad R, Murad A, Khan RU, Naz S, Perillo A, Alhidary IA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 2,500 suspected patients in endemic Peshawar district during peak transmission. PCR (18S rRNA) detected significantly more cases than microscopy (p=0.001). Microscopy showed moderate sensitivity, perfect specificity, and 86.4% overall accuracy with substantial agreement (κ=0.692) and AUC=0.823. Malaria-positive individuals exhibited significant hematological abnormalities (reduced hemoglobin, hematocrit, RBC indices) and elevated liver enzymes. Parasite density showed negative correlations with hemoglobin, hematocrit, and platelets, and positive correlations with liver enzymes and bilirubin.

articles.headline: Beta-thalassemia and malaria landscape
articles.title_original: Beta-thalassemia and a missing malaria landscape.
articles.authors: Elmakhzen B, El Makhzen N, Abriel H
articles.details: No abstract provided.

articles.headline: Wolbachia transinfections suppress malaria parasites in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Wolbachia for malaria control.
articles.authors: Zhang X, Dimopoulos G
articles.details: Review of Wolbachia-based malaria vector control following dengue control successes. Plasmodium-blocking Wolbachia transinfections have been established in Anopheles mosquitoes. Experiments in transiently and stably infected Anopheles demonstrated that Wolbachia-induced Plasmodium suppression is feasible. Discusses mechanisms based on Aedes-arborvirus insights and current Anopheles evidence. Outlines challenges and opportunities for translating proof-of-concept findings to field application.

articles.headline: Cool-roofs with screening reduce malaria vectors 77% in African homes
articles.title_original: Housing modifications for heat adaptation, thermal comfort and malaria vector control in rural African settlements.
articles.authors: Abong'o B, Kwaro D, Bange T, Moshi V, Simwero J, Otima J, Mendt S, Ochomo E, Maggioni MA
articles.details: Randomized pilot field study of 40 houses in four arms (cool-roof, cross-ventilation, mat-ceiling, control) with screening of doors, windows, and eaves. Cool-roof houses achieved the lowest Heat Index (daytime −3.3°C, p<0.001; nighttime −2.4°C, p<0.01). Screening reduced female Anopheles funestus by 77% and Culex mosquitoes by 58% compared to control. 85% of households expressed willingness to use resources for housing interventions.

articles.headline: MSRP1 marker enables isolation of sexually committed P. falciparum parasites
articles.title_original: Defining the Proteome of Sexually Committed Parasites in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Venugopal K, Achcar F, Wolski WE, Nanni P, Lemgruber Soares L, Wright GJ, Marti M
articles.details: Study identifying MSRP1 as a highly specific marker for sexual commitment in P. falciparum gametocyte development. Generated and validated reporter parasite lines for FACS-based isolation of sexually and asexually committed parasites. Proteomics of isolated parasites revealed distinct protein signatures, including merozoite surface proteins, indicating functional differences between the two parasite populations.

articles.headline: Oral artemether-lumefantrine causes delayed hemolytic anemia in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Delayed hemolytic anemia after artemether-lumefantrine in Plasmodium falciparum malaria: A case report.
articles.authors: Usta O, Güllü D, Alhan Ö, Ergönül Ö
articles.details: Case report of 50-year-old male with severe P. falciparum malaria (parasitemia >10%) treated with extended oral artemether-lumefantrine who developed delayed hemolytic anemia (DHA) eight days post-treatment. Laboratory findings consistent with DHA including reticulocytosis and positive direct Coombs test, suggesting immune-mediated component. Underlying immunosuppression (rituximab 10 months prior), hepatosplenomegaly, and prolonged artemether-lumefantrine regimen likely contributed. Highlights need for post-treatment hemoglobin monitoring (2–4 weeks) in high-risk patients with comorbidities or high parasitemia.

articles.headline: TIRAP S180L variant protective against severe malaria
articles.title_original: Immunomodulatory effects of the TIRAP/MAL rs8177374 (S180L) variant: Meta-Analytic evidence from severe and Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: González-Castillo NI, Prado-Rodríguez SG, Iñiguez-Mosqueda O, Íñiguez-Gutiérrez L, Silva-Jara JM, Díaz-Zaragoza M, Graciano-Machuca O
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 10 studies (4,103 malaria cases, 2,460 controls). The T allele and heterozygous TC genotypes were associated with lower risk of severe malaria (T vs. C: OR = 0.51, 95% CI: 0.40-0.65, p < 0.001). In P. falciparum subgroup, the variant was linked to increased infection risk (T vs. C: OR = 1.47, 95% CI: 1.09-1.98, p = 0.012). Results suggest TIRAP/MAL plays a modulatory role in malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Pyrethroid resistance widespread in Tanzanian malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Insecticide Resistance Management in Malaria Vectors in Tanzania Mainland (2015-2024): A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Nkya TE
articles.details: Scoping review of 10 studies on insecticide resistance in Tanzanian vectors (2015-2024). Pyrethroid resistance was consistently reported, driven by knockdown resistance (kdr) mutations and metabolic mechanisms; DDT resistance was common; carbamates showed moderate resistance in approximately 50% of studies. Organophosphates (pirimiphos-methyl) and new insecticides (clothianidin, chlorfenapyr) remained effective with no resistance detected. Management strategies included pyrethroid-PBO LLINs and integrated vector management.

articles.headline: Immune markers distinguish bacterial from malaria fever
articles.title_original: Profiling of circulating immune factors and host-derived extracellular vesicles in response to bacterial or malaria acute fever.
articles.authors: Tiberti N, Bertoli G, Mazzi C, Vezzelli E, Zanzoni S, Alladio F, Bisoffi Z, Tamarozzi F, Piubelli C, Buonfrate D, Gobbi FG
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of serum from 52 bacterial acute febrile illness patients and 73 malaria patients. Endothelial activation markers (CD29+, CD62P+, CD9+ extracellular vesicles) and immune cell migration factors (MMP-2, MMP-9) were elevated in bacterial cases (q < 0.05). Antigen recognition markers (CD8+, CD81+, HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs) and cytokine storm mediators (IFNγ, CXCL10, TNFα) were prominent in malaria (q < 0.005). HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs distinguished bacterial from malaria infection with AUC 0.87.

articles.headline: Dietary fat and vitamin A reduce clinical malaria risk
articles.title_original: Associations Between Climate-Sensitive Nutrients, Clinical Malaria, and Anaemia Among Young Children in Rural Burkina Faso: An Analysis of Baseline Data From a Cluster-Randomised Controlled Trial.
articles.authors: Kurniawan AL, Borga MV, Zerbo F, Abidha CA, Gonnet A, Sorgho R, Coulibaly B, Sié A, Danquah I
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 452 children (median age 17 months, 52% male) in Nouna, Burkina Faso. Clinical malaria prevalence was 25.0%; anemia (Hb < 11 g/dL) was 88.5%. Adherence to a "fat and vitamin A" dietary pattern was inversely associated with clinical malaria (OR quintile 3 vs. 1: 0.50, 95% CI: 0.26, 0.96) but not with anemia. A "fibre and micronutrient" pattern showed no association with either malaria or anemia.

articles.headline: Mixed P. falciparum-vivax coinfection rare in Uttar Pradesh
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic approach for mixed Plasmodium infection among acute febrile patients: rare incidence from Eastern Uttar Pradesh.
articles.authors: Bhardwaj P, Agrawal A, Tiwari S, Kumar B, Kumar N, Singh R, Joshi HS, Dwivedi GR
articles.details: Study of acute febrile illness patients in eastern Uttar Pradesh, India. Four RDT-positive cases identified; three confirmed mixed P. falciparum and P. vivax infections by PCR, one P. falciparum only. One mixed-infection case presented with cerebral malaria and life-threatening complications. Another had varicella-zoster coinfection; a third had Japanese encephalitis and scrub typhus coinfection and died. Study emphasizes need to consider cerebral malaria in mixed Pf/Pv coinfections.

articles.headline: High prevalence of malaria-typhoid coinfection in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Malaria and salmonella typhi co- infection and antibiotic susceptibility in febrile patients at the Buea Regional Hospital.
articles.authors: Ebini-Ako TA, Tanih NF, Djakissam W
articles.details: Cross-sectional hospital study of 152 febrile patients at Buea Regional Hospital (March-May 2024). Malaria prevalence was 52.0% (79/152); typhoid was 37.5% (57/152); coinfection occurred in 22.3% (25/112) of confirmed cases. Vomiting was statistically associated with typhoid (p = 0.008). All S. typhi isolates were resistant to Sulfometoxazole. Study highlights high coinfection prevalence and antimicrobial resistance concerns.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine and amodiaquine show no drug interaction
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of artemether-lumefantrine plus amodiaquine in patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Ding J, Hoglund RM, van der Pluijm RW, Callery JJ, Peto TJ, Tripura R, Das S, Châu NH, Promnarate C, Mukaka M, Dysoley L, Fanello C, Onyamboko MA, Anvikar AR, Mayxay M, Smithuis F, von Seidlein L, Dhorda M, Amaratunga C, Faiz MA, Nghia HDT, White NJ, Day NPJ, Dondorp AM, Tarning J
articles.details: Population pharmacokinetic analysis from two randomized trials (2015-2020) in Africa and Southeast Asia: 443 patients received artemether-lumefantrine alone, 442 received artemether-lumefantrine plus amodiaquine, all with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Artemether Cmax 256 ng/mL (artemether-lumefantrine alone) vs 230 ng/mL (with amodiaquine); lumefantrine Cmax 15.2 μg/mL vs 14.1 μg/mL. All geometric mean ratios for drug-drug interactions fell within 0.80-1.25 range with no clinically relevant interaction detected.

articles.headline: Historical malaria fever therapy in Vienna psychiatry
articles.title_original: In the shadow of the "psychopharmacological revolution": Malaria fever, insulin coma, cardiazol and electroconvulsive therapy at the Vienna Psychiatric University Clinic, 1951-1969.
articles.authors: Heiss G
articles.details: Analysis of medical records from Vienna Psychiatric Clinic (1951-1969) documenting somatic treatments in 14,919 patients. Four invasive treatments studied: electroconvulsive therapy, cardiazol-induced convulsion therapy, insulin coma therapy, and malaria fever therapy, used for neurosyphilis, intellectual disabilities, schizophrenia, affective disorders, and psychopathy. Study examines application and justification during transition to pharmacological treatments in Austria, Switzerland, Germany, France, and Great Britain.

articles.headline: MAM01 monoclonal antibody offers partial malaria protection
articles.title_original: Human monoclonal antibody MAM01 for protection against malaria in adults in the USA: a first-in-human, phase 1, dose-escalation, double-blind, placebo-controlled, adaptive trial.
articles.authors: Lyke KE, Berry AA, Laurens MB, Winkler J, Joshi S, Koudjra AR, Butler L, Billingsley PF, Pascini T, Patil A, Sim BKL, Fitzgerald G, Riegel J, Andrews K, Levi M, Anderson AB, Wells CD, Liu H, Huleatt J, Miller RS
articles.details: Phase 1 trial of MAM01, a monoclonal antibody targeting P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein NANP repeat, in 37 adults (18-50 years) at University of Maryland. Participants received MAM01 (1.5, 5, 10, or 40 mg/kg IV or 5 mg/kg SC) or placebo, then underwent controlled human malaria infection with P. falciparum NF54 strain. After challenge, 6 of 6 control and 18 of 22 MAM01 recipients developed parasitemia. MAM01 was well tolerated with no treatment-related serious adverse events.

articles.headline: Commentary on monoclonal antibodies for malaria prevention
articles.title_original: A new monoclonal in the arMAMentarium against malaria.
articles.authors: Lorenz FR, McCall MBB
articles.details: Commentary on emerging monoclonal antibody approaches for malaria prevention and control.

articles.headline: Postmalaria labyrinthitis ossificans: rare ear complication
articles.title_original: Postmalaria Labyrinthitis Ossificans: A Rare Complication of Plasmodium Infection.
articles.authors: Batista FS, Couto R, Pinto D, Matias AM, Neves L, Castelão F
articles.details: Case report of postmalaria labyrinthitis ossificans, a rare otologic complication following Plasmodium infection characterized by inner ear ossification.

articles.headline: Rational vaccine design targets parasite invasion machinery
articles.title_original: Erythrocyte invasion in malaria: from molecular mechanisms to rational vaccines.
articles.authors: Alam N, Farrell B, Jamwal A, Higgins MK
articles.details: Review of erythrocyte invasion mechanisms in P. falciparum malaria, including the PCRCR complex. Structural studies of human antibodies from vaccination or natural infection have revealed neutralizing epitopes and mechanisms by which antibodies potentiate each other. Rational, structure-guided protein design is being applied to develop vaccine components, with the first rationally designed blood-stage malaria vaccine immunogen now entering clinical trials.

articles.headline: Congenital malaria prevalence 12.7% among Nigerian neonates
articles.title_original: Congenital malaria in Nigeria: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Odeniran PO, Paul-Odeniran KF, Ademola IO
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 35 studies (9,503 neonates) from 2000-2024. Pooled prevalence of congenital malaria was 12.7% (95% CI: 7.3-19.2) with high heterogeneity; average parasite count 56.6/μL; 4.8% (3.4-6.7%) had both malaria and septicemia. Maternal parasitemia prevalence 25.5%, placental 20.3%, neonatal peripheral 12.9%, cord blood 11.1%. Authors emphasize significant burden requiring enhanced surveillance and targeted interventions to reduce transmission.

articles.headline: Surface adhesin TLP modulates sporozoite force generation during migration
articles.title_original: Traction force generation in Plasmodium sporozoites is modulated by a surface adhesin.
articles.authors: Ripp J, Probst D, Singer M, Schwarz US, Frischknecht F
articles.details: Traction force microscopy study of P. falciparum sporozoite gliding motility forces on ventral surface. Both wild-type and tlp-knockout sporozoites showed distinct force foci, but tlp-knockout parasites generated lower overall forces. TLP is an important component of the force-generating machinery during gliding motility essential for efficient skin migration.

articles.headline: Severe malaria survivors show renal and neurological sequelae
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Term Sequelae After Severe Malaria: A Retrospective Study.
articles.authors: Cardona F, Héritier L, Cortaredona S, L'Ollivier C
articles.details: Retrospective cohort of 57 adults with severe P. falciparum malaria (all treated with intravenous artesunate) from France, 2018-2024. At day 28, 6/41 (14.6%) had sequelae (4 renal, 1 neurological, 1 both); at remote follow-up, 6/33 (18.2%) had sequelae (2 renal, 3 neurological, 1 both). Risk factors included neurological failure, metabolic acidosis, renal impairment at admission, older age, and D3 blood smear positivity. At one year, 8/14 patients reported persistent fatigue and cognitive complaints.

articles.headline: Five imported P. falciparum cases with severe acute kidney injury
articles.title_original: Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury in a Low-Endemic Region: A Case Series of Five Imported Plasmodium falciparum Infections.
articles.authors: Mujuni DP, Sadiq AM, Luhwago E, Mutalemwa D, Mwanga A, Leonard L, Mkwizu EW, Shao ER, Kilonzo KG
articles.details: Case series of 5 patients (mean age 38.2±18.6 years) with imported P. falciparum complicated by malaria-associated acute kidney injury treated in Tanzania, December 2023-January 2024. Mean serum creatinine 736±346.4 μmol/L, mean urea 23.1±14.1 mmol/L at admission. Four patients required hemodialysis, one experienced malaria recrudescence. Overall survival was 100%, highlighting pathophysiology, diagnostic challenges, and management in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: MAKI affects 26.5% of adults with malaria, 19.2% mortality
articles.title_original: Clinical characteristics and outcome of malaria-associated acute kidney injury in adult patients: a systematic review and meta analysis.
articles.authors: Meena P, Singla V, Deshpande A, Kasturi U, Khan EF, Kumar VRK, Kalyesubula R
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 studies (2,094 MAKI patients) from 2000-2024, primarily from South Asia. P. falciparum most common, though severe P. vivax AKI increasingly reported. Pooled MAKI incidence 26.5% (95% CI 19.8-34.4), ranging 2.4-45.5% overall and up to 77% in severe malaria. Dialysis required in 20-90% of cases. Pooled mortality 19.2% (95% CI 12.9-27.7%), ranging 5-52%, higher with delayed presentation, cerebral malaria, sepsis, and multiorgan failure. Long-term follow-up showed 5-20% developed chronic kidney disease.

articles.headline: Clonal P. falciparum expansion threatens Ecuador malaria elimination
articles.title_original: A previously low prevalence Plasmodium falciparum clone expands in an outbreak in the Pacific Coast of South America.
articles.authors: Cartagenova A, Zapata-Berrones K, Boboy J, Cuero W, Neafsey DE, Early AM, Sáenz FE
articles.details: Molecular epidemiology study of 72 P. falciparum isolates from northwest Ecuador (2019-2021) and Colombian samples using microsatellite markers and whole-genome sequencing. Shared, highly clonal parasites between Ecuador and Colombia; all infections monoclonal. A previously low-frequency clone from Colombian Pacific Coast caused new outbreaks through clonal expansions in Ecuador with high genetic differentiation from other Pacific Coast parasites, demonstrating potential of low-prevalence clones to become epidemic in low-transmission areas.

articles.headline: Amodiaquine disrupts P. falciparum glycolysis through enzyme binding
articles.title_original: Chemoproteomics-based profiling elucidates the antimalarial effects of amodiaquine through disruption of glycolysis process in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Wang J, Wang C, Zhuge R, Tang H, Xia F, Zhang Y, Zhang J, Liu C, Wu J, Chen X, Gao P, Wang J
articles.details: Chemoproteomics study synthesizing amodiaquine-derived activity probe to identify amodiaquine-binding proteins via activity-based protein profiling. Three glycolysis-associated enzymes identified as antimalarial targets. Proteomic and transcriptomic analyses confirmed AQ binds to these proteins and disrupts glycolytic processes in P. falciparum. Study demonstrated complementary effects between artesunate and AQ on shared molecular targets, explaining enhanced efficacy of AS-AQ combination therapy.

articles.headline: Evolutionary covariation reveals protein interactions in Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Evolutionary rate covariation across malaria parasite species enables inference of protein interactions.
articles.authors: Hopson HD, Omelianczyk RI, Ramirez A, Little JH, Clark N, Sigala PA, Leffler EM
articles.details: Phylogenetic comparative analysis calculating evolutionary rates across genomes of 22 Plasmodium species to identify protein-protein pairs with correlated evolutionary constraints. Validated approach on known pathways and interacting proteins across lifecycle stages. Genome-wide scan identified candidate co-functional proteins; identified PF3D7_0811600 with high evolutionary rate covariation co-localizing to RhopH2/RhopH3 ion channel. ERC matrix enables querying for functionally-related proteins to prioritize genes for hypothesis-driven follow-up.

articles.headline: Low-density asymptomatic parasitemia does not progress to malaria
articles.title_original: Low-density asymptomatic parasitemia in southern Zambia does not lead to clinical malaria and is not associated with household transmission: results from a two-year cohort study.
articles.authors: Schue JL, Martin AC, Matoba J, Sing'anga C, Lubinda M, Katowa B, Musonda M, Berube S, Shields T, Kobayashi T, Hamapumbu H, Simulundu E, Norris DE, Wesolowksi A, Moss WJ
articles.details: Two-year intensive longitudinal cohort study of 197 households (1,071 individuals) in Southern Province, Zambia (2018-2020) with monthly qPCR parasitemia testing and passive surveillance. Of 1,071 individuals, 144 (13%) were qPCR-positive at least once; median parasite density 4 parasites/μL. Clinical malaria incidence 46.7 per 1000 person-years. No association between asymptomatic parasitemia and subsequent clinical malaria within individuals, nor between asymptomatic parasitemia and household transmission beyond one month.

articles.headline: Artificial liver blood purification rescues severe cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Successful treatment of severe cerebral malaria with artificial liver blood purification technology: A case report.
articles.authors: Fan C, Dai Y, Du H, Su T, Guo X, Yan Z, Fang H, Yao Y, Zhou X
articles.details: Case report of 43-year-old male with severe P. falciparum cerebral malaria complicated by severe liver and kidney injury, cerebral edema, heart failure, thrombocytopenia, and coagulation dysfunction. Patient treated with artemether, continuous renal replacement therapy, and artificial liver blood purification, achieving successful recovery. Authors propose combined extracorporeal support may be effective rescue therapy for severe malaria with multiorgan failure by mitigating systemic inflammatory response.

articles.headline: Older children represent one-third of malaria cases in Niger
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria among children aged 5-10 years in the Sahelian area: do we need to adapt seasonal malaria chemoprophylaxis?
articles.authors: Djedanem M, Yacoubou MAY, Zakari A, Issa I, Yahaya M, Zaneidou M, Mody I, Onana DB, Testa J, Jambou R
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of routine data from 35 dispensaries across 7 provinces in Niger (2018-2021) examining malaria burden by age group and geography using SARIMA modeling. Malaria transmission highly seasonal with peak July-October. During peak season, children 12-59 months represented 33.2% of cases, children 10-14 years 26.0%, children 2-11 months 22.6%; children 5-14 years together >1/3 of all cases. Southern irrigation zones highest incidence (3,000-7,500 cases/month). SARIMA projections indicated likely increase post-2022.

articles.headline: Mesenchymal stem cells reduce cerebral malaria severity via Tr17
articles.title_original: Mesenchymal stem cells alleviate experimental cerebral malaria disease severity by inducing RoRγt(+) Foxp3(+) T regulatory (Tr 17) cells and modulating the dysregulated Th17/Treg axis.
articles.authors: Sharma I, Thakur RS, Chaudhary A, Chauhan R, Singh K, Sadhu S, Awasthi A, Das J
articles.details: Experimental study in P. berghei ANKA-infected C57BL/6 mice (ECM model). MSC treatment reduced parasitemia, increased survival, decreased hemozoin accumulation, and prevented blood-brain barrier vascular leakage. MSCs downregulated IL-6 and IL-17 while upregulating IL-10, induced dual-phenotype Tr17 (RoRγt+ Foxp3+) regulatory T cells that suppress pathogenic Th17 cells, and rescued lymphocytes from apoptosis by downregulating PD-1/PD-L1 and ROS levels. MSCs could serve as adjunct therapy for immunopathological complications of cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Feretia apodanthera leaf extracts show strong antiplasmodial activity in mice
articles.title_original: Harnessing the potential of medicinal plants in the malaria fighting: In vitro and in vivo antiplasmodial activities of Feretia apodanthera Delille (Rubiaceae).
articles.authors: Boni SI, Hien DFS, Koama KB, Youba M, Vaissayre V, Kabre Z, Dussert S, Diabate A, Lefevre T, Yerbanga RS, Haddad M, Meda NR
articles.details: Study tested F. apodanthera (leaf, stem, root) extracts for antiplasmodial activity in vitro and in vivo using P. berghei-infected mice. In vitro IC50 values ranged from 0.140–0.620 μg/mL with <1% hemolysis. In vivo, leaf extract showed highest chemosuppressive efficacy (32–76% at 125–500 mg/kg in suppressive test, 63–68% at day 5–7 in curative test, 57–63% in prophylactic test). All organs showed activity with non-toxic profiles (LD50 > 5000 mg/kg).

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection activates antitumor immunity in Lewis lung cancer mice
articles.title_original: Plasmodium infection fully activates the immune system in peripheral blood and tumor microenvironment in a murine Lewis lung cancer model.
articles.authors: Huang Q, Yang D, Tao Z, Yu Z, Ding W, Tao N, Liao X, Lin H, Cheng Z, Gao S, Kang Z, Xie J, Hu W, Qin L, Chen X, Yu G
articles.details: Subcutaneous Lewis lung cancer model in C57BL/6J mice treated with P. yoelii showed tumor growth inhibition. Infection increased CD3+, CD4+, and CD8+ T cell subsets in blood and tumor tissue, reduced PD-1 expression, increased M1/M2 macrophage ratio, and reduced pSTAT3 and TGFβ expression in tumors. These findings suggest Plasmodium infection activates antitumor immunity through mechanisms distinct from checkpoint blockade therapies.

articles.headline: P. falciparum CelTOS shows high haplotype diversity in Saudi Arabia
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and haplotype structure of the CelTOS gene in Plasmodium falciparum from Jazan Province, Saudi Arabia: implications for vaccine development and malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Alothaid H, Alghnnam F, Al-Qahtani AA, Al Marzan A, Abu Alola M, Bohol MFF, Alhamlan F, Shafeai MI, Rudiny FH, Motaen AM, Al-Qahtani AA
articles.details: Analysis of 202 P. falciparum CelTOS coding sequences from Jazan, Saudi Arabia identified 27 haplotypes (Hd = 0.921; π = 0.0059) with predominantly purifying selection (gene-wide ω ≈0.36) and low-level recombination. FST values near zero indicated weak regional structure with extensive haplotype sharing across demographic strata. These findings support CelTOS as a relevant marker for vaccine development and molecular surveillance in this malaria elimination setting.

articles.headline: P. vivax Ripr protein naturally immunogenic in Brazilian Amazon residents
articles.title_original: B-cell epitope mapping and characterization of antibody responses to recombinant PvRipr in malaria-exposed individuals.
articles.authors: Soares IF, Rodolphi CM, Alencar ALC, Matos ADS, Rodrigues-da-Silva RN, Baptista BO, Martorano RM, de Souza HADS, Riccio EKP, de Barros JP, Totino PRR, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Pratt-Riccio LR, Lima-Junior JDC
articles.details: ELISA with plasma from malaria-exposed individuals in Brazilian Amazon showed more than 60% presented IgM or IgG antibodies against recombinant PvRipr, with higher frequencies of IgG1 and IgG2 subclasses. Four linear B-cell epitopes identified; B-PvRipr(879–888) and B-PvRipr(923–958) showed highest seroprevalence and reactivity, with strong correlation to IgG3 (protective cytophilic subclass). Findings support PvRipr as a vaccine candidate antigen.

articles.headline: Plasmonic biosensor detects P. falciparum lactate dehydrogenase with high sensitivity
articles.title_original: Experimental plasmonic sensing of malaria using an aluminum metasurface.
articles.authors: Kiyumbi AS, Tame MS
articles.details: Optical biosensor using aluminum metasurface with nanoholes achieved label-free detection of plasmodium falciparum LDH (pfLDH) via surface plasmon resonance. Spectral sensitivity 360 nm/RIU with limit of detection 1.3 nM (equivalent to 45.6 ng/mL) of pfLDH in phosphate-buffered saline. Authors note this plasmonic approach may offer a cost-effective, high-sensitivity method for active malaria infection diagnosis.

articles.headline: Total parasite biomass better predicts severe malaria than peripheral parasitemia
articles.title_original: Reevaluating malaria severity metrics: insights from PfHRP2-derived biomass estimates in Ghanaian children.
articles.authors: Konadu EA, Obese AO, Danso-Coffie CK, Addison T, Kyei-Baafour E, Obiri D, Amoah LE, Kusi KA, Gyan B, Oduro D
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 118 Ghanaian children with cerebral malaria (n=58), severe malarial anemia (n=28), or uncomplicated malaria (n=32) from 2012–2016. PfHRP2-derived total parasite biomass (PTot) and sequestered parasite burden (PSeq) were consistently higher than circulating parasite burden (PCir) in severe syndromes. Higher PSeq associated with coma; PTot demonstrated stronger association with severe malaria phenotypes than peripheral parasitemia. Prior antimalarial treatment reduced PCir but not PTot.

articles.headline: Pyrethroid resistance intensifies in A. funestus across four years in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Four-year monitoring of the malaria vector Anopheles funestus in central-west Cameroon reveals an escalation of pyrethroid resistance combined with high malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Tazokong HR, Tchouakui M, Wondji M, Boyomo O, Wondji CS
articles.details: Four-year (2020–2023) monitoring of A. funestus in Mibellon, Cameroon showed escalating pyrethroid resistance, with permethrin mortality declining from 77.7% (2015) to 23.2% (2023) and deltamethrin from 46.6% (2016) to 8.5% (2023). Sporozoite infection rates ranged 4–21% with P. falciparum predominance. Rapid selection of G454A-CYP9K1 and 4.3 kb structural variants coincided with phenotypic resistance intensification. PBO-based nets maintained efficacy while pyrethroid-only nets lost effectiveness.

articles.headline: A. maculatus potentially transmits zoonotic P. knowlesi and P. inui in Indonesia
articles.title_original: Evidence incriminating Anopheles maculatus as a potential vector of Plasmodium knowlesi and Plasmodium inui.
articles.authors: Sebayang BF, van de Straat B, Kurniawan A, Garjito TA, Haq AM, Westaway JAF, Lubis IND, Grigg MJ, Russell TL, Burkot TR
articles.details: Molecular identification and screening of 234 Anopheles maculatus specimens collected via human landing catches (July 2022–June 2023) in North Sumatra, Indonesia. Four specimens (1.7%; 95% CI 0.5–4.3%) tested positive for Plasmodium genus DNA; one specimen harbored both P. knowlesi and P. inui DNA. This represents the first detection of zoonotic malaria parasite DNA in wild-caught A. maculatus, suggesting it may be a potential vector of both zoonotic and human malarias.

articles.headline: Pregnant women favor vaccines to protect baby over protecting themselves
articles.title_original: Pregnant women's attitudes and intentions toward tuberculosis, malaria, group B streptococcus, and respiratory syncytial virus vaccines in pregnant: Findings from a cross-sectional study of pregnant women living in Brazil, Ghana, Kenya, and Pakistan.
articles.authors: Limaye R, Schue J, Fesshaye B, Singh P, Miller E, Souza R, Jessani S, Temmerman M, Badzi CD, Sauer M, Brizuela V, Karron R
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 1,603 pregnant women in Brazil, Ghana, Kenya, and Pakistan. Participants rated vaccine safety for baby as the most important factor, followed by vaccine efficacy for baby and maternal safety. Regarding malaria vaccines specifically, 26% indicated definite intention to receive one. Approximately 40% agreed vaccines are unnatural, 38% believed the body should develop natural immunity, and 19% had delayed a recommended vaccine.

articles.headline: Hepcidin levels differ by malaria status in malnourished children
articles.title_original: Hepcidin is low in children with moderate acute malnutrition and asymptomatic malaria: secondary analysis of a 2×2×3 factorial randomised trial in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Helt TW, Kurtzhals J, List KK, Styrishave B, Yaméogo CW, Fabiansen C, Iuel-Brockdorf AS, Ritz C, Briend A, Filteau S, Michaelsen KF, Friis H, Christensen VB
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 1,019 Burkinabe children with moderate acute malnutrition randomized to 500 kcal/d supplementation (corn-soy blend vs lipid-based, 12 weeks). Children with iron deficiency had 82% (95% CI 76–87%) lower serum hepcidin than those without. Children with symptomatic malaria had 103% (95% CI 32–210%) higher s-hepcidin; asymptomatic/recent malaria had 51% (95% CI 35–63%) lower s-hepcidin. S-hepcidin increased 61% (95% CI 38–87%) after supplementation, with 22% higher (95% CI 2–45%) in LNS vs CSB.

articles.headline: Identified growth-inhibitory epitope in P. falciparum RiprRh5 vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Identification of a growth-inhibitory epitope in PfRipr5, a malaria vaccine candidate against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nagaoka H, Fukushima A, Tsuboi T, Takashima E
articles.details: Investigation of PfRipr5 (truncated fragment with EGF-like domains 5–9) identified an epitope recognized by growth-inhibitory monoclonal antibody 29B11. Using Western blotting with cysteine-substituted mutants and surface plasmon resonance analysis, researchers characterized a 20-amino-acid cysteine-associated epitope. This growth-inhibitory epitope provides a molecular entry point for dissecting PfRipr function and supports epitope-focused rational design strategies for blood-stage malaria vaccines.

articles.headline: Malaria in near-elimination settings driven by importation from high-transmission areas
articles.title_original: Imported malaria predominates in near-elimination settings in Southwestern Uganda.
articles.authors: Mbabazi M, Kiyaga S, Katairo T, Kabbale KD, Asua V, Kagurusi BA, Wiringilimaana I, Nsengimaana B, Semakuba FD, Nakasaanya J, Ayitewala A, Watyekele E, Nabende I, Kayondo TM, Arinaitwe E, Mulondo J, Tukwasibwe S, Nsobya SL, Agaba B, Maiteki C, Jjingo D, Kateete DP, Kamya MR, Ssewanyana I, Aranda-Diaz A, Conrad MD, Murphy M, Gerlovina I, Epstein A, Rodriguez-Barraquer I, Rosenthal PJ, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Briggs J
articles.details: Longitudinal surveillance (January 2023–June 2024) at three southwestern Uganda health facilities enrolled microscopy-confirmed malaria cases with travel questionnaires and parasite genotyping (MAD4HatTeR panel). Maziba and Muko (very low transmission) showed 87–96% recent overnight travel, mostly from high-transmission regions; parasites exhibited higher within-host diversity and lower within-site relatedness. Transmission network inference identified most Maziba/Muko infections as imported, while Kamwezi (low-moderate transmission) showed multiple locally related clusters. Artemisinin-resistance markers K13 P441L and R561H more prevalent in Kamwezi.

articles.headline: Drug-resistant P. falciparum DHFR inhibitor candidates identified computationally
articles.title_original: Computational exploration of natural product inhibitors against mutant Plasmodium falciparum DHFR through pharmacophore modeling, docking, and MD simulations.
articles.authors: Gogoi D, Gogoi B, Dutta PP, Kalita JM, Khataniar A, Sharma MP, Mandhadi JR, Phukan MM, Verma AK
articles.details: Pharmacophore modeling, virtual screening, molecular docking, and 200 ns molecular dynamics simulations identified three lead pfDHFR inhibitor candidates from traditional Chinese medicine database. Top compound ZINC70454408 exhibited predicted Ki of 0.04 µM, binding energy -14.204 kcal/mol, and RMSD of 2.1 Å. Two additional compounds (ZINC04096650, ZINC85631105) showed binding energies of -122.64 and -118.92 kcal/mol with Ki < 0.1 µM.

articles.headline: Anopheles coluzzii shows pyrethroid/DDT resistance in Nigerian urban centers
articles.title_original: Urban evolution of insecticide resistance and susceptibility patterns of Anopheles coluzzii in Southeastern Nigeria: implications for malaria vector control.
articles.authors: Ezeike AK, Danga SPY, Ezenwa VC, Chukas CF, Ogudu EO, Nwosu CM, Ebenebe IN, Chukwunwejim CR, Oli AN, Esimone CO
articles.details: Cross-sectional insecticide susceptibility testing in three urban locations (Awka, Onitsha, Nnewi) in Anambra State, Nigeria. Deltamethrin mortality ranged 45–63%, indicating pyrethroid resistance; DDT susceptibility 20–56%. Bendiocarb and pirimiphos-methyl showed 100% susceptibility in all locations. kdr 1014F allele frequencies were 0.72–0.81; ace-1 G119S allele frequencies low (0.07–0.12).

articles.headline: Community perceptions of zoonotic malaria risk in rural Malaysian Borneo
articles.title_original: What does monkey malaria mean to you? A participatory photovoice study in rural Malaysian Borneo.
articles.authors: Naserrudin NA, Rahman AB, Manah AM, Donnie EM, Jamaji L, Lin PYP, Miller A, Krishnan M, Hassan MR
articles.details: Photovoice participatory study (January–May 2024) with 20 participants aged 18–62 from three rural Sabah villages documenting perceptions of Plasmodium knowlesi (monkey malaria). Thematic analysis of photographs and focus group discussions identified five themes: embedded everyday risk, community resilience through locally derived practices, structural socio-ecological challenges, human-wildlife-environment interface, and cultural knowledge. Participants articulated how landscape, economic dependence on forested areas, and daily routines structure transmission risk.

articles.headline: Mathematical models reveal climate-driven malaria transmission across regions
articles.title_original: Comparing three approaches to modelling the effects of temperature and rainfall on malaria incidence in different climatic regions.
articles.authors: Madito GT, Brooke BD, Silal SP
articles.details: Vector-host compartmental mathematical model compared three approaches to incorporating temperature and rainfall effects on malaria transmission. Simulations examined transmission seasonality and cases across tropical, semi-arid, and subtropical regions at varying vector control coverage levels. Results showed regional variation: tropical regions sustained seasonal transmission with fewer cases than semi-arid/subtropical under enhanced control; climate events like heatwaves or flooding trigger outbreaks even with strong vector control.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic malaria infections account for 31% of community cases
articles.title_original: Community reservoirs of malaria parasites and gametocytes in Arba Minch district, southern Rift Valley, Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Zewde Z, Eligo N, Tesfaye Y, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: Cross-sectional reactive case detection study (July–October 2022) from Sile village, Southern Rift Valley, Ethiopia. Of 2,009 individuals screened following 142 PCR-confirmed index cases, microscopy-based prevalence was 3.6% (72/2,009); submicroscopic prevalence 10.1% (33/326), comprising 4.6% P. vivax and 4.3% P. falciparum. Submicroscopic infections accounted for 31% (33/105) of total PCR-confirmed cases. P. vivax showed higher gametocyte carriage (69%) than P. falciparum (22%).

articles.headline: Nanobodies against Pfs48/45 reduce P. falciparum transmission
articles.title_original: Pfs48/45 nanobodies block Plasmodium falciparum transmission.
articles.authors: Lyons FMT, Chmielewski J, Gabriela M, Chan LJ, Tong J, Adair A, Zeglinski K, Gouil Q, Dietrich MH, Tham WH
articles.details: Identified nanobodies against sexual-stage protein Pfs48/45 that recognize gametocytes and have strong transmission-reducing activity in P. falciparum. Crystal structure reveals nanobody binds distinct epitope from TB31F monoclonal antibody but similar epitopes as RUPA-44 and RUPA-117. Nanobodies block parasite fertilization in Anopheles mosquito midgut, demonstrating their potential as versatile antibody format for transmission-blocking interventions.

articles.headline: DNA ligase I essential for malaria parasite liver-stage development
articles.title_original: Plasmodium DNA ligase I is essential for parasite blood- and liver-stage development.
articles.authors: Pandey E, Mishra S, Varshney A, Habib S, Mishra S
articles.details: Biochemical and genetic study of PfLig1 (P. falciparum DNA ligase I) localized primarily to nucleus with presence in organelles. Conditional mutagenesis in P. berghei demonstrated PbLig1 essential for liver-stage development; sporozoites lacking PbLig1 invaded hepatocytes but arrested during mid-liver-stage development with limited nuclear division and reduced DNA content that failed to increase beyond mid-liver stage.

articles.headline: Review of current and emerging malaria prevention strategies
articles.title_original: The Evolving Landscape of Malaria Prevention Strategies: A Review of Recent Developments.
articles.authors: Charavanamuttu Y, Agyeman Wamba A, Taylor-Robinson AW, Lampejo T
articles.details: Review synthesizing malaria prevention strategies including vector control (insecticide-treated nets, indoor residual spraying), chemoprevention (intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy, seasonal malaria chemoprevention), and immunoprophylaxis (RTS,S/AS01, R21/Matrix-M vaccines). Emerging approaches include monoclonal antibodies and genetically modified mosquitoes. Discusses challenges including widespread insecticide and drug resistance, barriers to equitable access and implementation, and need for adaptive, multifaceted approaches.

articles.headline: Novel sporozoite-specific rhoptry protein identified in Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Identification of a novel rhoptry protein expressed predominantly in Plasmodium sporozoites.
articles.authors: Oundavong S, Sekine T, Torii M, Ozawa T, Kosako H, Shinzawa N, Ishino T
articles.details: Proximity-dependent biotin identification (UltraID) method employed in transgenic Plasmodium berghei sporozoites identified PBANKA_1363400, a novel rhoptry protein. Protein was biotinylated during parasite maturation in mosquitoes alongside known rhoptry proteins. Novel molecule localized to sporozoite rhoptries and was predominantly expressed in sporozoites rather than merozoites, enabling species- and stage-specific protein identification.

articles.headline: Potato inhibitor blocks mosquito enzyme critical for parasite development
articles.title_original: Inhibition of carboxypeptidase b1 from Anopheles stephensi by the potato carboxypeptidase inhibitor: a foundational step toward paratransgenesis-based malaria control.
articles.authors: Monshizadeh Z, Rismani E, Abdi F, Pakdel JD, Ghanbarnejad N, Raz A
articles.details: In vitro biochemical study evaluated potato carboxypeptidase inhibitor (PCI) on carboxypeptidase-B1 (CPBAs1) from Anopheles stephensi. CPBAs1 plays vital role in sexual development of Plasmodium in mosquito midgut. Cloned cpbas1 and pci genes into pET-23a vector, expressed and purified recombinant proteins. PCI efficiently inhibited CPBAs1 enzymatic activity at low concentrations, comparable to commercial-specific inhibitor 1,10-phenanthroline.

articles.headline: Conserved invasion complex identified across Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: PTRAMP, CSS and Ripr form a conserved complex required for merozoite invasion of Plasmodium species into erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Seager BA, Lim PS, Xiao X, Lai KH, Feufack-Donfack LB, Dass S, Jung NC, Abraham A, Grigg MJ, Anstey NM, William T, Sattabongkot J, Leis A, Longley RJ, Duraisingh MT, Popovici J, Wilson DW, Cowman AF, Scally SW
articles.details: Investigated PTRAMP, CSS, and Ripr orthologs from P. falciparum, P. vivax, and P. knowlesi to characterize conserved PTRAMP-CSS-Ripr (PCR) complex mediating erythrocyte merozoite invasion. In P. vivax and P. knowlesi, proteins form disulfide-linked heterodimer with Ripr binding via C-terminal region. Cross-reactive antibodies differentially inhibit merozoite invasion; cryo-EM visualization confirmed core invasion scaffold conserved across Plasmodium spp. with implications for multi-species vaccines.

articles.headline: Rainfall memory and weather drive malaria incidence in Ghanaian children
articles.title_original: Malaria incidence, severity and mortality in children under five in Ghana: evidence from generalised additive models.
articles.authors: Bosson-Amedenu S, Eyiah-Bediako F, Anafo AY
articles.details: Analyzed 132 monthly surveillance observations (2013–2023) from Tarkwa-Nsuaem, Ghana using negative-binomial generalized additive models with rainfall "memory" (1–3 month lags, 3–6 month accumulations). Rainfall memory improved incidence modeling (AIC 1,269→1,234; deviance explained ≈80%; adjusted R²≈0.88). Monthly median incidence 847 cases; severe cases median 2. Twelve-month forecast projected approximately 600–670 cases/month with 0–1 deaths/month.

articles.headline: Splenic abscess linked to malaria and sickle cell trait
articles.title_original: Pyogenic Splenic Abscess With Concurrent Active Plasmodium falciparum Malaria and Sickle Cell Trait: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Gumbs S, Gikunda W, Das H, Douchee J, Wilkinson CA
articles.details: Case report of a young West African male presenting with concurrent Plasmodium falciparum malaria, sickle cell trait, and bacterial splenic abscess (Fusobacterium species) after initiating antimalarial therapy. Imaging revealed a large splenic abscess that required percutaneous drainage. Treatment with antimalarial therapy and broad-spectrum antibiotics resulted in complete recovery. The case illustrates the importance of considering anaerobic bacterial superinfection in malaria patients with hemoglobinopathies.

articles.headline: Gangrene complication in cerebral malaria despite artesunate treatment
articles.title_original: Purpura Fulminans With Digital Gangrene in Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: A Case Presentation.
articles.authors: Palmares I, Morgado C, Dá Mesquita Faustino F, Silva J, Freitas P
articles.details: Case report of a 66-year-old woman presenting with cerebral malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum who developed purpura fulminans and digital gangrene. Despite early intravenous artesunate treatment, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, and supportive care, she developed purpuric lesions on the feet that evolved to gangrene, though systemic recovery was achieved. The case illustrates severe microvascular complications driven by endothelial injury and coagulation dysfunction.

articles.headline: Community health workers most effective for promoting bed net use
articles.title_original: Differentiated effects of sources of malaria messages on mosquito bed net use among women of reproductive age in Uganda: a propensity score matched analysis.
articles.authors: Mwebesa E, Agbi DM, Iha DT, Kogei DK, Agasa LO, Kerich GK, Mwangi A
articles.details: Secondary analysis of Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey data (3,488 women aged 15–49) using propensity score matching to evaluate media sources for malaria prevention messaging. Overall bed net use was 73.2%. Community health workers (ATT=0.322, 95% CI 0.111–0.533), community events (ATT=0.296, 95% CI 0.085–0.507), and social mobilization (ATT=0.185, 95% CI 0.008–0.362) were significantly associated with increased bed net use, while television exposure was not (ATT=0.051, 95% CI –0.062–0.164).

articles.headline: Gut barrier disruption drives microbiota-mediated immunosuppression in malaria
articles.title_original: Intestinal Barrier Loss Enables Microbiota-Mediated Purinergic Suppression During Malaria.
articles.authors: Polidoro RB, Bednarski OJ, Lehman S, Oliveira FMS, Fusco EM, Bower L, Rangel-Ferreira M, Kour S, Namazzi R, Opoka RO, John C, Brault JJ, Tilston-Lunel A, Alves-Filho JC, Schmidt NW
articles.details: Murine study and human samples from Uganda showing that Plasmodium infection disrupts the intestinal barrier, enabling microbiota-derived signals to trigger purinergic immunosuppression and severe disease. Mechanism involves systemic adenosine persistence and expansion of CD39+ plasmablasts. Ugandan children with severe malaria exhibited a distinct purinergic immune signature compared to asymptomatic controls. Therapeutic reinforcement of gut barrier or blockade of adenosine A2A receptor improved germinal centers and reduced disease severity independent of parasite burden.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic factors and housing drive malaria in Pakistan
articles.title_original: Determinants of malaria infection across different districts of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Haq IU, Mehmood Z, Muhammad A, Akhtar S, Aboagarib EAA, Zaib A, Ahmed SAM, Huwaikem M, Khan GA, Albugami HF, Ahmed B, Qu S
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 768 participants across Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province identifying risk factors for malaria (prevalence 24.5%). Multivariate logistic regression identified male gender (AOR 1.8, 95% CI 1.1–2.8), ages 16–50 (AOR 4.2–8.3), low education (AOR 3.9, 95% CI 2.6–5.9), low income 10,000–50,000 rupees (AOR 17.8, 95% CI 8.1–38.9), large family size (AOR 11.3, 95% CI 5.9–21.7), distance >3 km to healthcare (AOR 3.1, 95% CI 1.8–5.3), and lack of modern toilets (AOR 4.8, 95% CI 2.4–9.4) as independent risk factors.

articles.headline: Long-latency P. malariae infections persist 7–50 years in China
articles.title_original: Unraveling the origins and features of long-latency Plasmodium malariae infections: a comprehensive epidemiological, clinical, and genetic study of seven cases in Guangdong Province (2014-2022).
articles.authors: Zhang JY, Mao Q, Wu D, Deng ZH, Liu J, Liao YH, Lu WC, Zhang GT, Zhang XC, Chen JD
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of seven long-latency Plasmodium malariae cases in Guangdong Province (2014–2022) with predominantly female participants (85.7%) and mean age 75.3 years. Infections originated from historical local transmission (six cases) or Southeast Asian importation, with latency periods ranging 7–50 years. Phylogenetic analysis showed Asian lineage strains (95%–97% nucleotide homology). Clinical presentations were non-specific fever; median diagnostic delay was 4 days.

articles.headline: Novel multi-antigen vaccine construct shows transmission-blocking promise
articles.title_original: A stabilized tandem antigen chimera that elicits potent malaria transmission-reducing activity.
articles.authors: Ivanochko D, Miura K, Hailemariam S, Ravichandran R, Song Y, Huang WC, Stoter R, Teelen K, van Gemert GJ, Leaf EM, Chan S, Men C, Semesi A, Shiu C, MacGill RS, Long CA, Jore MM, King NP, Lovell JF, Julien JP
articles.details: Development of a stabilized tandem antigen chimera (STAC) combining epitopes from Plasmodium falciparum gametocyte proteins Pfs230-D1 and Pfs48/45-D3. In female mice, STAC conjugated to protein nanoparticles elicited antibodies with transmission-reducing activity comparable or superior to individual multimerized antigens. STAC presents most potent epitopes while masking non-functional epitopes in a stable, ordered multi-domain structure. STAC represents a promising next-generation transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: High vaccine awareness but limited knowledge in northern Uganda
articles.title_original: Caregivers awareness, knowledge, attitudes, and perceptions towards malaria vaccine in Kole and Kwania Districts, Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Puleh SS, Kalyango JN, Ocan M, Yeka A, Siu G, Idro R, Akech SI, Acen J, Ejalu DL, Nankinga Z, Afayo R, Nankabirwa JI
articles.details: Mixed-methods study of 574 caregivers in Kole and Kwania districts assessing awareness, knowledge, and attitudes toward R21 malaria vaccine. Awareness was 90.1% (95% CI 87.3–92.4), but only 13.9% (95% CI 11.2–17.0) demonstrated factual knowledge regarding doses (16.4%), intervals (21.0%), and eligibility (34.2%). Overall, 57.4% expressed positive attitudes and 57.5% positive perceptions, though nearly half reported safety concerns. Analysis identified knowledge gaps, community misconceptions, mixed attitudes, and safety/effectiveness concerns.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence rising in Ethiopia with increasing mixed infections
articles.title_original: A five-year (2020-2024) malaria surveillance data analysis of Mattu Karl Specialized Comprehensive Hospital, Oromia region, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Endalu T, Mohammed M, Bulcha I, Ushu L, Befkadu Z, Tamiru E, Ketema T, Dereje G, Teferi S
articles.details: Five-year retrospective surveillance (2020–2024) of 100,760 malaria tests at a hospital in Oromia region, Southwest Ethiopia. Overall positivity was 14.2% (14,303 cases). Species distribution: Plasmodium vivax 49.1%, Plasmodium falciparum 37.8%, mixed infections 13.1%. Positivity rates increased from 9.2% (2020) to 17.7% (2024), with a concerning rise in mixed infections. Findings highlight need for enhanced control strategies tailored to emerging parasite species dynamics.

articles.headline: Historical malaria control efforts in Palestine
articles.title_original: Malaria in Palestine: the elephant in the room.
articles.authors: Alexander A
articles.details: Historical perspective examining the overlooked role of malaria in the Arab-Israel conflict origins. The paper notes that Palestine over 100 years ago was largely desolate or uninhabitable in many rural areas due to malaria severity. The work highlights how malaria control methods and their historical context have been underappreciated in certain discourse around the region's history.

articles.headline: Machine learning tool accurately predicts malaria parasite infection multiplicity
articles.title_original: Malaria-MOI: A flexible and scalable tool for predicting multiplicity of infection in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Billows N, Phelan J, Thorpe J, Vanheer LN, Tan MK, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: Malaria-MOI is a flexible Python tool estimating multiplicity of infection (MOI) from whole-genome sequencing data without training data or parameter tuning. Benchmarked on 27 Plasmodium falciparum mixed samples, achieved root mean squared error 0.38, mean absolute error 0.15, and correlation 0.78 across 165 loci, outperforming existing methods. Applied to 8,208 field samples showed strong negative correlation with FWS (ρ=−0.76) and superior sensitivity (0.99) for monoclonal detection. Compatible with Illumina and Nanopore platforms.

articles.headline: School-age children have highest malaria burden despite LLIN access
articles.title_original: Disparities in long-lasting insecticidal bed net usage and malaria burden 2 years after a mass distribution campaign in central Côte d'Ivoire: A cross-sectional survey prior to a cluster randomised trial.
articles.authors: Sih C, Assi SB, Dangbenon E, Talbot B, Biggs J, Koffi AA, Alou LPA, Messenger LA, Zoh MG, Camara S, Kulkarni MA, Protopopoff N, N'Guessan R, Cook J
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 1,672 participants across 33 clusters in Côte d'Ivoire assessing long-lasting insecticidal bed net (LLIN) ownership (66.3%), access (27.8%), and usage two years post-distribution. Overall malaria prevalence was 41.1%, with highest risk in ages 5–9 (75.0%, aOR 1.86, 95% CI 1.24–2.78). LLIN usage varied by age (63.2% under-5 vs 44.3% ages 10–14, lowest 41.0% in females 10–14). LLIN users had lower prevalence (38.5%, aOR 0.74, 95% CI 0.58–0.94) than non-users (44.3%).

articles.headline: Expert consensus on P. knowlesi prevention behavior factors
articles.title_original: Exploring influencing factors affecting preventive behaviours of Plasmodium knowlesi malaria among at-risk communities: an application of the nominal group technique.
articles.authors: Md Hanif SA, Hassan MR, Safian N, Alabed AAA, Rafi'i MR, Naserrudin NA, Hussain NAMA
articles.details: This consensus-building study used Nominal Group Technique with malaria program experts (100% voting response rate, 80% consensus threshold) to identify factors influencing P. knowlesi prevention behaviors in Southeast Asian at-risk communities. Twenty of 21 items achieved consensus ranking, categorized into four themes: environmental risks, human-related risks, policy/organizational factors, and vector-related risks. The findings provide a foundation for refining context-specific malaria prevention strategies.

articles.headline: Crystal structure of transmission-blocking target PfHAP2
articles.title_original: Crystal structure and nanobodies against domain 3 of the malaria parasite fusogen Plasmodium falciparum HAP2.
articles.authors: Lyons FMT, Chmielewski J, Chan LJ, Gabriela M, Chan RWB, Adair A, Tong J, Pymm P, Zeglinski K, Gouil Q, Tham WH, Dietrich MH
articles.details: This structural biology study characterized nanobodies (antibodies) targeting domain 3 of Plasmodium falciparum HAP2 (gamete-fusion protein essential for parasite transmission). Two nanobodies showed nanomolar binding affinities and cross-species reactivity. A crystal structure of one nanobody-PfHAP2 complex provided the first structural insights into this transmission-blocking vaccine target.

articles.headline: Novel HDAC inhibitor shows potent in vivo antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Discovery of γ-Tetrahydrocarboline Derivatives as Plasmodium falciparum Histone Deacetylase Inhibitors for Treatment of Malaria.
articles.authors: Ni H, Qian Y, Dai Z, Cai Y, Cao L, Li J, Lai Z, Hu X, Huang Z, Jiang L, Cui S
articles.details: Researchers synthesized 33 γ-tetrahydrocarboline derivatives targeting Plasmodium falciparum histone deacetylase 1 (PfHDAC1). Compound 5ac demonstrated potent activity against drug-resistant parasites with excellent selectivity (Pf3D7 IC50 = 4.1 nM; selectivity index = 3573 vs HEK293T cells) and promising in vivo antimalarial efficacy. Mechanistic studies showed 5ac downregulates malaria invasion-related genes, establishing this scaffold as a promising new antimalarial agent class.

articles.headline: Microparticle proteomics reveal hemoglobin loss in malaria infection
articles.title_original: Integrative proteomic profiling of malaria-derived microparticles: A mass spectrometry-based study.
articles.authors: Antwi-Baffour S, Adjei JK, Agyemang-Yeboah F, Annani-Akollor M, Kyeremeh R, Asare GA, Gyan B
articles.details: This proteomic study isolated and characterized plasma microparticles (extracellular vesicles) from 43 malaria patients and 10 healthy controls using 2D LC-MS. Malaria samples contained 1729 proteins versus 234 in controls, with significantly higher hemoglobin subunit levels. Higher hemoglobin loss via microparticles in malaria patients may contribute to the development and progression of anemia during P. falciparum infection.

articles.headline: Review of mosquito-borne diseases and control strategies
articles.title_original: Review of selected mosquito-borne diseases: arboviruses (dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever) and parasitic diseases (malaria, lymphatic Filariasis).
articles.authors: Wei YL, Wu Z, Li RL, Tang F
articles.details: This review article summarizes key mosquito-borne diseases (dengue, chikungunya, Zika, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever, malaria, lymphatic filariasis), their global distribution and transmission dynamics. Control strategies include vector management, vaccines (e.g., R21 for malaria), chemoprophylaxis, and novel technologies. The review emphasizes challenges including insecticide resistance and socioeconomic costs (e.g., $318 billion for Aedes-borne diseases since 1975).

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria prevalence in blood donors globally: 0.32%
articles.title_original: Global burden of asymptomatic malaria among blood donors: systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Malede B, Amare GA, Setegn A, Fenta A, Mengist A, Belew H, Muche Y, Abebaw D, Melkamu A, Ashagre A, Jemal M, Teffera ZH, Belayneh M, Abebaw A, Getinet M, Wondmagegn M, Abeje G, Almaw A, Berhan A, Adugna A, Admasu A, Wondimu H, Misganaw T, Wondmagegn YM
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 studies (2018–2025) estimated the global prevalence of asymptomatic malaria among blood donors at 0.32% (95% CI: 0.25–0.39). Regional variation was substantial: Africa 15.89%, South America 0.79%, Asia 0.21%. Diagnostic method influenced detection rates: microscopy 21.86%, RDT 0.21%, PCR 5.03%. The findings highlight significant transfusion-safety risks and the need for region-specific screening strategies.

articles.headline: PfApiAT2 proline transporter essential for malaria transmission
articles.title_original: PfApiAT2 is a proline transporter essential for the transmission of Plasmodium falciparum by the mosquito vector.
articles.authors: Khushu M, Kissel RC, Kauffman J, Taccheri C, Singh N, Summers RL, Plant LD, Wirth DF, Catteruccia F, Bopp S
articles.details: This functional genomics study identified PfApiAT2, a proline-specific transporter, as essential for P. falciparum oocyst development in Anopheles gambiae mosquitoes. Halofuginone-resistant pfapiat2-mutant parasites formed stunted oocysts with severely defective sporozoite production, while PfApiAT2-knockout parasites underwent complete block in sporogony (oocyst stalling and degeneration). This growth defect could be rescued by nutrient supplementation to mosquitoes, revealing a critical nutritional dependency for parasite transmission.

articles.headline: Z. chalybeum root extract achieves 76% parasite clearance in mice
articles.title_original: Antimalarial efficacy of aqueous crude rootbark extracts of Zanthoxylum Chalybeum: An in vivo experimental study in Wistar Albino Mice infected with Plasmodium berghei.
articles.authors: Michael O, Lwanira CN, Ocan M, Alele PE
articles.details: This in vivo efficacy study tested aqueous Zanthoxylum chalybeum root bark extracts against Plasmodium berghei NK65 in Wistar mice at doses of 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg (days 3–7 post-infection), compared to artemether-lumefantrine (4 mg/kg) and distilled water control. Parasite clearance reached 76% at 400 mg/kg, significantly higher than control (MD = 17.24, 95% CI 16.59–17.89; p < 0.001), with significant fever reduction (MD = –1.78; p = 0.002). No changes in hemoglobin or hematocrit were observed.

articles.headline: CRISPR-Cas9 system for large-scale P. berghei genome editing
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei High-Throughput (PbHiT): a CRISPR-Cas9 System to Study Genes at Scale.
articles.authors: Jonsdottir TK, Paoletta MS, Henriksson J, Bushell ESC
articles.details: This technical study presents PbHiT, a scalable CRISPR-Cas9 platform for genome editing in Plasmodium berghei (rodent malaria model). The system uses a single cloning step linking guide RNAs to short (100 bp) homology arms, with gRNAs serving as barcodes for pooled transfections and mutant identification by sequencing. PbHiT enables both knockout and tagging strategies, supports targeted functional studies and high-throughput genetic screens, and includes an online resource covering the entire P. berghei protein-coding genome.

articles.headline: Mixed vivax-falciparum infection with disparate parasitemia levels
articles.title_original: Pediatric Mixed Plasmodium vivax-P. falciparum Infection with Disparate Parasitemias: Diagnostic and Surveillance Challenges.
articles.authors: Estela-Zape JL
articles.details: This case report describes a 9-year-old boy with mixed Plasmodium vivax (5500 parasites/µL) and P. falciparum (562 parasites/µL) infection from an endemic area in Latin America. The patient was hemodynamically stable without severe malaria criteria. Treatment with artemether-lumefantrine followed by primaquine resulted in favorable clinical evolution with documented parasite clearance, illustrating diagnostic challenges in mixed-species infections with disparate parasitemias.

articles.headline: Afebrile falciparum malaria presenting with severe hemolytic anemia
articles.title_original: Afebrile falciparum malaria with severe hemolytic anemia in a returning traveler: a case report.
articles.authors: Liu J, Chen X, Yang J, Xie J, Liu Z
articles.details: This case report of a 45-year-old returning traveler presenting with afebrile P. falciparum malaria (0.9% parasitemia, ~1746 parasites/µL) demonstrates atypical presentation as severe hemolytic anemia (hemoglobin 48 g/L), thrombocytopenia, and intravascular hemolysis. Treatment with intramuscular artesunate followed by oral dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine resulted in full recovery and parasite clearance, highlighting the diagnostic challenge of malaria in non-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Mosquito population genetics in Uganda for vector control design
articles.title_original: Assessing the population genetic structure and demographic history of Anopheles gambiae and Anopheles arabiensis at island and mainland sites in Uganda: implications for testing novel malaria vector control approaches.
articles.authors: Mwima R, Hui TJ, Lukyamuzi E, Bodde M, Makunin A, Birungi K, Lukindu M, Nanteza A, Muhanguzi D, Lawniczak M, Burt A, Kayondo JK
articles.details: This population genetics study assessed Anopheles gambiae (n=2918) and An. arabiensis (n=173) from three Lake Victoria island and three mainland Uganda sites using 62-locus targeted amplicon sequencing. An. gambiae showed low but significant population differentiation (FST 0.00054–0.028), with larger effective population sizes on mainland (4054–8190) versus islands (146–249) and higher nucleotide diversity. An. arabiensis showed no significant differentiation. These findings inform the design and monitoring of gene-drive and other genetic vector control interventions.

articles.headline: Bacteremia in adult severe malaria: Uganda hospital prevalence
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with concomitant bacteremia among adults admitted with severe malaria at Kayunga Regional Referral Hospital, Uganda.
articles.authors: Abdi FD, Hirsi AM, Ali M, Karshe AH, Sandeyl AA, Hersi AG, Yusuf AA, Bekele HK, Mohamed AA, Jayte M, Ezera A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 207 adults admitted with severe malaria. Bacteremia was present in 14.5%, significantly associated with CNS symptoms, low peripheral oxygen saturation, hyperparasitaemia, and leucocytosis. Most common isolates were Salmonella typhi (33.3%), Staphylococcus aureus (30%), and Streptococcus spp. (16.7%). Ciprofloxacin and penicillin derivatives provided good empirical coverage.

articles.headline: Pfmdr1 wild-type N86 predominates in Cameroon: low resistance
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of the Plasmodium falciparum Pfmdr1 N86Y mutation linked to antimalarial drug resistance in Mbouda, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nadia NAC, Ngouana V, Cedric Y, Djakbe DL, Innes W, Pacome TK, Armand GNG, Edna GDM, Merveille TT, Fuyou Z, Hu H
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of 302 malaria patients in Mbouda, Cameroon. Plasmodium falciparum prevalence was 28.8%. The Pfmdr1 gene was detected in 95% of isolates; genotyping revealed 94% wild-type N86 alleles, 1% mixed N86/Y86, and 0% mutant Y86 alleles. The prevalence of the N86Y drug-resistance-associated mutation remains low in this region.

articles.headline: Sudanese Copts show selection for Duffy-null malaria resistance
articles.title_original: Sudan's complex genetic admixture history drives adaptation to malaria in Sudanese Copts.
articles.authors: Vilà-Valls L, Garcia-Calleja J, Prado-Martinez J, Bosch E, Andrés AM, Netea MG, Comas D, Hassan HY
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of 125 Sudanese individuals across five ethnolinguistic groups. Local ancestry analysis identified strong adaptive admixture in Sudanese Copts on chromosome 1, with Nilo-Saharan ancestry introduced 1,000–1,500 years ago. SNP rs2814778 in the ACKR1 gene (Duffy-null blood group conferring P. vivax resistance) showed a remarkably strong selection coefficient (s = 0.0996). Over one million novel variants were identified across populations.

articles.headline: Anopheles farauti dispersal: ~73 m daily, 3.51 km maximum
articles.title_original: Estimating the dispersal of the malaria vector Anopheles farauti through a natural ecosystem in north Queensland, Australia using mark release and recapture experiments.
articles.authors: Chow WK, Cooper RD, Pagendam D, Trewin BJ, Beebe NW
articles.details: Mark-release-recapture study of 19,889 marked An. farauti in Queensland during 2015–2017. Daily survival rates were 0.68 (wet season) and 0.75 (dry season). Estimated daily flight distance approximately 73 m with longest distance 3.51 km. Most recaptures (78%) occurred with mosquito catch barrier systems; data provide novel parameters for modeling transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: CSP diversity in Cameroon: vaccine-matched haplotype at 20%
articles.title_original: Complexity of Infection and Plasmodium falciparum Circumsporozoite Protein Diversity Prior to Malaria Vaccine Implementation in Kaelé Health District, Cameroon, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Ali IM, Martinez BV, Tchuenkam VPK, Sadler JM, Gorman CC, Nsango SE, Voundi V, Marcellin AJ, Wanda GY, Bailey JA, Dinglasan RR, Lin JT, Juliano JJ
articles.details: 4CAST amplicon sequencing of 100 P. falciparum-positive dried blood spots from Kaelé Health District, Cameroon (2022–2023). CSP was genotyped in 35% of samples, identifying 22 haplotypes (heterozygosity = 0.908; nucleotide diversity π = 0.021). The vaccine-matched RTS,S haplotype was present in 20% of genotyped infections. Complexity of infection varied by antigen: CSP (1.3), TRAP (1.2), SERA2 (2.1), AMA (2.8).

articles.headline: Pre-treatment antimalarial drug levels present in 24% globally
articles.title_original: Residual antimalarial drug concentrations before treatment in malaria patients in Asia and Africa: A systematic review and meta-analysis depicting its implication for drug pressure.
articles.authors: Marwa KJ, Zinga M, Mnkungwe HR, Mutagonda R, Kapesa A, Mwaiswelo RO, Mmbando B, Lusingu JPA, Kamugisha E
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 studies (3,522 malaria patients) examining residual antimalarial drug levels. Overall pooled proportion with pre-treatment drug levels: 24% (95% CI 16–34%), significantly higher in Africa (31%) than Asia (11%). By drug type: Chloroquine 55%, Lumefantrine 34%, Mefloquine 10%. Substantial pre-treatment drug levels indicate ongoing drug pressure promoting resistance development.

articles.headline: Severe falciparum malaria with AKI, cerebral, liver, renal
articles.title_original: Severe falciparum malaria remains a threat in East Kalimantan: A case report.
articles.authors: Gunawan CA, Mangiri HP
articles.details: Case report of a 31-year-old male with severe P. falciparum malaria presenting with acute kidney injury, impaired consciousness, jaundice, liver dysfunction, high parasitemia, and severe thrombocytopenia. Patient received artesunate injection and supportive care, achieving full recovery. Case illustrates life-threatening complications of severe malaria and the importance of early diagnosis and prompt treatment.

articles.headline: MicroRNAs miR-21-5p and miR-3158-3p modulate cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Functional evidence of hsa-miR-21-5p and hsa-miR-3158-3p in regulating immune pathways relevant to cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Gupta H
articles.details: In vitro study transfecting MCF7 and HepG2 cells with miRNA mimics. Hsa-miR-3158-3p mimic significantly downregulated NF-κB expression (p < 0.05). Hsa-miR-21-5p mimic significantly reduced NF-κB, IL-10, and TLR4 expression (p < 0.05). Findings suggest both miRNAs regulate immune pathways implicated in cerebral malaria pathogenesis, providing mechanistic basis for future validation.

articles.headline: Correction: IPTp malaria pregnancy acceptability Senegal
articles.title_original: Correction: Initiating community intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Senegal: qualitative evaluation of stakeholder acceptability and experience.
articles.authors: Ndoye T, Lugand MM, Diallo AM, Sakho S, Pouye MM, Dieng K, Faye SLB
articles.details: Correction notice to article on community implementation of intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Senegal, assessing stakeholder acceptability and implementation experience. Original article provides methodology and detailed results.

articles.headline: PfCyRPA antigen diversity in Senegal: conserved vaccine target
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity in the Plasmodium falciparum next-generation blood stage vaccine candidate antigen PfCyRPA in Senegal.
articles.authors: Ba A, Thiam LG, Pouye MN, Guo Y, Patel SD, Sene SD, Diallo F, Li R, Cisse A, Guerra N, Laqqa S, Mangou K, Moore AJ, Sadio BD, Ndiaye JLA, Mbengue A, Vigan-Womas I, Sheng Z, Shapiro L, Bei AK
articles.details: Deep amplicon sequencing of 93 P. falciparum clinical isolates from Kédougou, Senegal (high transmission area). Reference 3D7 allele was predominant; 15 SNPs identified with only 5 previously reported. SNP population prevalence ranged from 1.1% (singletons) to 9.7% (V292F most common). Structure-based modeling showed these polymorphisms have minor effects on vaccine epitopes, indicating conserved PfCyRPA targets.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium: house sparrows show higher prevalence
articles.title_original: Avian malaria and invasion success: Contrasting infection patterns in two introduced Eurasian sparrows in North America (Passer domesticus and Passer montanus).
articles.authors: Trigueros AC, Robertson BQ, Parker PG
articles.details: Comparative study of 306 sparrows (2019–2022) in Missouri and Illinois. House sparrows (Passer domesticus) had higher Plasmodium prevalence (24.1%; 32/133) than tree sparrows (Passer montanus) (11.6%; 20/173). HOSPs also showed nearly two-fold higher relative parasitemia. Three shared Plasmodium lineages were found between species, plus one unique lineage per species.

articles.headline: ALA + DHA: synergistic activity against artemisinin-resistant
articles.title_original: Targeting Infected Host Cell Heme Metabolism to Kill Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Siddiqui FA, Adapa SR, Li X, Miao J, Cui L, Jiang RHY
articles.details: In vitro study combining 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and dihydroartemisinin (DHA) against P. falciparum and clinical isolates. ALA selectively increased porphyrin accumulation in infected RBCs but not uninfected RBCs. The ALA + DHA combination produced synergistic parasite killing, including complete clearance of artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum isolates from Greater Mekong Subregion with no recrudescence over three weeks.

articles.headline: Valine-to-leucine mutation in AP2-G disrupts sexual differentiation
articles.title_original: A single valine to leucine switch disrupts Plasmodium falciparum AP2-G DNA binding and reveals GDV1's role in ap2-g activation.
articles.authors: Prajapati SK, Dong JX, Morahan BJ, Dotrang T, Barbeau MC, Williams AE, Hupalo D, Wilkerson MD, Dalgard CL, Kafsack BFC, Llinás M, Williamson KC
articles.details: This study identified a single amino acid substitution (V2163L) in P. falciparum AP2-G transcription factor that prevents DNA binding and sexual differentiation (gametocytogenesis). The mutation abrogates gene expression including AP2-G autoregulation, while GDV1-dependent activation occurs in the mutant background. AP2-G V2163 is required for MSRP1 expression, marking early sexually committed schizonts. These findings highlight AP2-G's critical role in malaria transmission via gametocyte production.

articles.headline: Seasonal rainfall and government policy drive malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Climatic and governance determinants of malaria transmission in Rivers State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Egbom SE, Nduka FO, Nzeako SO, Nwafor GO, Bartholomew DC, Nwaigwe CC, Iwu HC, Ogbonna CJ, Nwokike CI, Opara MC, Ezenwaka CO, Anyanwu EO, Nwachukwu JI
articles.details: This study analyzed malaria incidence in Rivers State, Nigeria from 2007 to 2021 using count models (Poisson, quasi-Poisson, negative binomial) and time-series models (SARIMAX). The quasi-Poisson model provided the best fit among count approaches, but SARIMAX(1,1,0)(1,1,1)₁₂ demonstrated superior predictive accuracy. Monthly malaria incidence was significantly influenced by seasonality, rainfall patterns, and changes in government malaria-control policy periods.
