These findings support integrating climatic and governance indicators into malaria early-warning systems.

articles.headline: Elevated S1PR1 in cerebral malaria brain tissue linked to pathogenesis
articles.title_original: Expression of sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor 1 in the brain of fatal cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Srisook C, Nintasen R, Punsawad C, Glaharn S, Techarang T, Viriyavejakul P
articles.details: This study examined sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor 1 (S1PR1) expression in brain tissue from fatal P. falciparum cerebral malaria (CM) cases using immunohistochemistry and measured serum S1P by ELISA. S1PR1 expression was significantly higher (p < 0.001) in cerebral blood vessels and neurons of CM patients compared to controls and non-CM patients, while blood S1P levels were significantly decreased and inversely correlated with S1PR1 expression. These findings suggest the S1P/S1PR1 signaling pathway contributes to CM pathogenesis and represents a potential therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Molecular identification of four Anopheles species in Guyana and Venezuela
articles.title_original: Anopheles species complexes from Guyana and Venezuela malaria endemic areas using COI sequences.
articles.authors: Rubio-Palis Y, Corredor-Medina C, Lenhart AE, Ruiz-López F
articles.details: This molecular taxonomy study analyzed mtCOI sequences from Anopheles mosquitoes collected in Guyana and Venezuela to characterize three species complexes (Albitarsis, Nuneztovari, Oswaldoi). Using phylogenetic methods (MrBayes, TCS) and species delimitation approaches, researchers identified four distinct taxa: An. albitarsis F, An. goeldii, An. oswaldoi A, and An. oswaldoi B, with sympatric occurrence of two oswaldoi species in Venezuela. An. goeldii and oswaldoi A were reported for the first time in this region.

articles.headline: Afghanistan unlikely to achieve malaria elimination by 2035 under current policy
articles.title_original: Malaria time series analysis, forecasting, and development of curated intervention strategies for the elimination of malaria from Afghanistan.
articles.authors: Rahimi BA, Habib MN, Wafa MH, Sahrai MS, Singh MP, Lal AA
articles.details: This study analyzed malaria case data from Afghanistan over three decades (1990-2023) and district-level monthly data (2018-2023) using time-series forecasting models. From 2018-2023, the test positivity rate was 15.2% with infections predominantly P. vivax (96.5%) versus P. falciparum (3.2%); mean annual cases were 153,295. Historical trend showed a non-linear cyclic pattern with a peak in 2002, decline through 2013, and increase 2014-2017. Forecasting analysis indicates Afghanistan is unlikely to achieve malaria elimination by 2035 under current interventions, though approximately 43% of low-burden districts are prepared for sub-national elimination.

articles.headline: RTS,S-protected vaccinees show broader antibody responses to CSP junctional epitopes
articles.title_original: Vaccine-Induced Antibodies to a C-Terminal Plasmodium falciparum Circumsporozoite Protein Epitope Associated With Protection Against Malaria Challenge.
articles.authors: Friedman-Klabanoff DJ, Jensen TL, Gelber CE, Pinapati RS, Tan JC, Deye GA, Regules JA, Bergmann-Leitner ES, Laurens MB, Travassos MA, Goll JB, Takala-Harrison S, Berry AA
articles.details: This study analyzed anti-CSP antibody responses from controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) experiments using peptide arrays to compare serum from unprotected rCSP recipients, protected RTS,S recipients, and unprotected RTS,S recipients. Protected RTS,S vaccinees demonstrated significantly higher breadth of responses to CSP peptides in the junctional, central repeat, and C-terminal regions compared to unprotected groups, with particularly elevated intensity against two C-terminal peptides including part of Th3R. Protected recipients also showed higher breadth of IgA responses to the C-terminal epitope PNDPNRNV variants and cross-reactive junctional region sequences.

articles.headline: Elevated IL-6 associated with moderate and severe vaccine adverse events
articles.title_original: Cytokines Associated With Moderate and Severe Adverse Events During a Sporozoite Malaria Vaccine Trial With Controlled Human Malaria Infection.
articles.authors: Jatt LP, Gillespie KM, Van P, Hoffman SL, Jackson LA, Murphy SC, Heath JR, Kublin JG
articles.details: This sporozoite malaria vaccine trial with controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) investigated cytokine profiles associated with adverse events (AEs). With frequent sampling, the study found that Interleukin-6 (IL-6) was elevated on days when participants experienced moderate or severe AEs. The authors conclude that further research on immune responses correlating with AEs is needed to identify biomarkers of systemic reactogenicity and improve vaccine development strategies.

articles.headline: CCR5Δ32 allele frequency lower in malaria-infected Amazon women
articles.title_original: Updating the frequency of CCR5Δ32 in Brazil: Descriptive analysis of malaria cases and controls from Acre State, Amazon region.
articles.authors: Ziliotto M, Ellwanger JH, Dombrowski JG, Marinho CRF, Pontillo A, Chies JAB
articles.details: This descriptive study examined CCR5Δ32 allele frequency in women from Acre State, Brazilian Amazon, comparing malaria-infected and uninfected populations. The CCR5Δ32 variant allele reduces CCR5 chemokine receptor expression and has been implicated in infectious disease susceptibility. Researchers found Δ32 allele frequencies of 2.88% in Plasmodium-infected women versus 1.04% in uninfected women. The lower Δ32 frequencies observed in Acre compared to other Brazilian regions likely reflect distinct ancestry patterns shaped by European colonization history.

articles.headline: Nigeria shows chloroquine-sensitive parasites but persistent antifolate resistance
articles.title_original: Molecular Surveillance of Malaria in Nigeria Reveals a Low Frequency of Antimalarial Resistance Markers and Clonal Expansion of Chloroquine-Sensitive Infections.
articles.authors: Ajibaye O, Oboh-Imafidon M, Ajibola O, Olusola F, Egwu CO, Amambua-Ngwa BN, Diop MF, Mohammed N, D'Alessandro U, Falade C, Meremikwu M, Olukosi AY, Oriero EC, Amambua-Ngwa A
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study analyzed 895 P. falciparum isolates from six geopolitical zones of Nigeria (2017-2021) using targeted amplicon sequencing to assess drug-resistance markers. Researchers genotyped 101 SNPs and 36 nonsynonymous amino acid mutations and found approximately 30% of infections with multiple clones. Chloroquine sensitivity (wild-type Pfcrt) was dominant at 55.1%, though chloroquine-resistant CVIET persisted in 40.9%. Antifolate resistance markers (Pfdhfr codons 51/59/108) were detected in >50% and Pfdhps A437G in 87.3%. Kelch13 propeller mutations were rare, with only 12 nonsynonymous variants observed.

articles.headline: Machine learning SST index predicts Peruvian malaria 3+ months ahead
articles.title_original: A Machine Learning-Based Dynamic SST Index for Long-Lead Malaria Prediction in the Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Pan M, Hu S, Janko MM, Zaitchik BF, Takahashi K, Lescano AG, Munayco CV, Pan WK
articles.details: This study developed a machine learning approach using self-organizing maps to create a dynamic sea surface temperature (SST) index for long-lead malaria prediction in the Peruvian Amazon. The dynamic SST index outperformed traditional ENSO indices with forecast lead times exceeding 3 months, showing higher correlation coefficients and lower root mean square error (RMSE) in generalized linear models. The index captures tropical SST-malaria relationships by revealing how ocean temperatures influence local air temperature and humidity. The authors provide open-source code for applications to vector-borne diseases and climate-sensitive health outcomes.

articles.headline: NLM machine learning app shows poor sensitivity for malaria screening
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Accuracy of the National Library of Medicine (NLM) Screener Application in Screening for Malaria Parasites Among Blood Donors at the Korle-Bu Blood Bank, Ghana: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Appiah SB, Osei S, Brown NK, Amoah LE, Owusu EDA
articles.details: This cross-sectional study evaluated a National Library of Medicine (NLM) mobile application using machine learning for malaria parasite detection in blood smears against PCR (reference), microscopy, and rapid diagnostic tests in 300 blood donors at Korle-Bu Teaching Hospital, Ghana. PCR identified P. falciparum in 18 donors (6.0%). The NLM screener had sensitivity of 38.9% (7/18; 95% CI: 20.3-61.4), specificity of 60.6% (171/282; 95% CI: 54.8-66.2), PPV of 5.9%, NPV of 94.0%, and negligible agreement (κ = -0.001). The app would have resulted in 111 unnecessary discards and 11 missed infections in this donor pool.

articles.headline: Apicortin protein not essential for Plasmodium parasite development
articles.title_original: Apicortin defines the Plasmodium apical conoid body but is dispensable for the parasite life cycle.
articles.authors: Zeeshan M, Mishra A, Pashley SL, Markus R, Brady D, Holder AA, Moores C, Tewari R
articles.details: This study investigated the Apicortin protein in P. berghei, a microtubule-associated protein with doublecortin and partial TPPP domains. Live-cell and super-resolution microscopy revealed that Apicortin localizes to the apical complex at the apical end of invasive parasite stages within the mosquito vector, forming a distinct ring-like structure. However, deletion of the Apicortin gene had no effect on parasite development, indicating the protein is not essential. The authors conclude that redundant or compensatory mechanisms likely stabilize the apical complex.

articles.headline: Community Knowledge of Asymptomatic Malaria Survey
articles.title_original: To what extent do people in malaria-endemic countries know asymptomatic malaria infections? A systematic review.
articles.authors: Bouwe T, Ahissou NCA, Kikuchi K, Iwagami M, Nonaka D
articles.details: Systematic review of 11 studies (483 articles screened) examining knowledge of asymptomatic malaria in endemic countries. Public awareness ranged from 14.2% to 79.8%, while health personnel awareness was 88%. Qualitative findings revealed limited knowledge and reluctance to adhere to interventions targeting asymptomatic carriers. Authors conclude that education on asymptomatic malaria is necessary for better control and elimination.

articles.headline: Malaria Vaccine Efficacy in High-Transmission Settings
articles.title_original: Ensuring the Public Health Impact of Malaria Vaccination in High-Transmission Settings.
articles.authors: Bell GJ, Emch ME, Moormann AM, Bailey JA
articles.details: Review of RTS,S and R21 vaccines against P. falciparum. RTS,S showed reduced efficacy in higher transmission settings, with reports of temporary negative efficacy beginning approximately 3 years after the primary vaccine series in control groups due to greater natural immunity development. Authors recommend enhanced surveillance and supplementary prevention interventions alongside malaria vaccination in high-transmission settings.

articles.headline: TEX1 Protein Essential for Plasmodium Development
articles.title_original: The Putative E3 Ubiquitin Ligase TEX1 Is Required for Nuclear Biology and Developmental Progression of Plasmodium berghei in the Liver.
articles.authors: Schmid M, Golomingi R, Franke-Fayard B, Caldelari R, Rehmann R, Roques M, Heussler VT
articles.details: Gene knockout study of E3 ubiquitin ligase TEX1 in P. berghei. TEX1-knockout parasites showed impaired mosquito-stage development with reduced ookinete formation, oocyst numbers, and sporozoite production. In hepatic stages, TEX1-deficient parasites displayed reduced growth, abnormal nuclear division, and dramatically declined detached cell formation and blood stage infectivity, identifying TEX1 as a promising target for liver-stage antimalarial interventions.

articles.headline: Geographic and Socioeconomic Variation in Child Malaria
articles.title_original: Geographic variation in malaria prevalence among children under five in coastal and inland counties of Liberia: analysis of the 2022 Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Tugbeh RS, Veliah G, Jennifer HG, Shaw TEB, Tchakondo S, Gayi KS, Assogba AS, Kandjoni Y
articles.details: Analysis of 2022 Liberia Malaria Indicator Survey (n=2,189 children 6-59 months) found malaria prevalence of 8.3% in coastal and 12.7% in inland regions (p=0.001). Risk factors included non-ITN use (1.5× odds), severe anaemia (AOR 6.2, 95% CI 2.34–14.81), poor households (AOR 6.4, 95% CI 3.06–13.27), and rural residence (AOR 0.6 vs urban). Authors recommend targeted interventions for high-risk groups.

articles.headline: Modeling Malaria Risk on Ancient Roads
articles.title_original: A 'useless marsh … breeding fever and insect pests': assessing malaria risk on roads through Cilicia Pedias (modern Türkiye) and its historical impact in antiquity and the Middle Ages.
articles.authors: Browning DC Jr, Nicovich JM
articles.details: GIS-based multilayer malaria risk model applied to ancient Cilicia Pedias (southeastern Anatolia) showing high-risk zones for travelers. Model-produced risk maps correlated extremely well with historical accounts of malarial illness from the 4th century BCE through 12th century CE, validating interpretation of historical records and highlighting Plasmodium parasites' impact on historical events and travel routes.

articles.headline: Correction: Metabolic Rigidity in Malaria
articles.title_original: Correction to "Metabolic Rigidity as a Mechanical Barrier to Malaria: Flickering Loss in PKLR-Deficient Erythrocytes".
articles.details: This is a correction to a previously published article on metabolic rigidity as a barrier to malaria in PKLR-deficient erythrocytes. No additional details available from the title.

articles.headline: IDO-1 Inhibitor Enhances Artemether-Lumefantrine Efficacy
articles.title_original: 8-Fluorotryptanthrin, an immunomodulatory IDO-1 inhibitor enhances the efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine in the treatment of malaria.
articles.authors: Katnoria S, Kumar R, Suri M, Salunke DB, Kaur S
articles.details: In vitro testing of 8-fluorotryptanthrin (C1), an IDO-1 inhibitor, against P. falciparum (3D7, Dd2 strains) and P. berghei ANKA. In P. berghei-infected mice, C1 at 20 mg/kg reduced parasitaemia, restored liver and kidney functions, and enhanced immune function. Combined with artemether-lumefantrine, C1 achieved complete parasitaemia suppression and full survival by days 7–14 with enhanced T-cell and B-cell populations and balanced cytokine profiles.

articles.headline: Epigenetic Regulation of var2csa in Placental Malaria
articles.title_original: Epigenetics and chromatin structure regulate var2csa expression and the placental binding phenotype in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Lenz T, Sirel M, Hoppe H, Shafeeq S, Roch KL, Ribacke U
articles.details: Systems biology study of VAR2CSA-expressing P. falciparum parasites showing exclusive var2csa transcription while all other var genes remained silenced. Transcriptional activation correlated with depleted repressive H3K9me3 marks and var2csa repositioning relative to heterochromatic telomeric regions. 5-methylcytosine marks were present across var genes but independent of transcriptional switching, providing mechanistic insights into antigenic variation in placental malaria.

articles.headline: Rare Genetic Variation Biases Parasite Population Models
articles.title_original: Rare variation in malaria parasites biases population-genetic inference.
articles.authors: Goldberg A
articles.details: Reanalysis of P. falciparum genomic data from 18 worldwide populations revealing that effective population size estimates vary orders of magnitude depending on method due to enrichment for rare variants. Rare variants inflate πN/πS ratios even without selection; a multiple-merger coalescent model better explains observed genealogies. Filtering rare variation reveals previously masked selection candidates including merozoite surface proteins.

articles.headline: Metabolic Pathways of Permethrin Resistance in Mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Metabolomic changes underpinning permethrin resistance in the Anopheles gambiae malaria vector from Cameroon.
articles.authors: Djova SV, Christopher MW, Ibrahim SS, Kouamo M, Dinglasan RR, Garrett TJ, Wondji CS
articles.details: UHPLC-HRMS/MS metabolomic analysis of permethrin-resistant versus susceptible A. gambiae from Cameroon. Resistant mosquitoes showed elevated inosine, amino acids, and energy-production metabolites, indicating altered detoxification and metabolic shifts. Susceptible mosquitoes showed increased neurotoxic and energy-disrupting metabolites. Findings elucidate permethrin resistance mechanisms and suggest potential for pyrethroid cross-resistance emergence.

articles.headline: TNF-iNOS-HIF-1α Axis Controls Malaria Host Defense
articles.title_original: Reprogramming of host energy metabolism mediated by the TNF-iNOS-HIF-1α axis plays a key role in host resistance to Plasmodium infection.
articles.authors: Matteucci KC, Leite NPS, Assis PA, Hirako IC, Pioto F, Ojelabi O, Toller-Kawahisa JE, Vaz LG, Costa DL, DA Silva JS, Alves-Filho JC, Gazzinelli RT
articles.details: Study in P. chabaudi-infected mice demonstrating TNF-mediated reprogramming of monocyte energy metabolism through iNOS-HIF-1α signaling. TNF enhanced hepatic and splenic glucose uptake while reducing physical activity and food intake. Knockout mice (iNOS-/-, TNFRΔLyz2, HIF-1αΔLyz2) showed impaired monocyte glycolysis, increased parasitemia, and impaired disease tolerance, indicating this axis is critical for host defense against Plasmodium infection.

articles.headline: Imported Malaria Profile in Sri Lanka 2013–2023
articles.title_original: The profile of imported malaria in Sri Lanka from 2013 to 2023.
articles.authors: Chulasiri P, Fernando D, Seneviratne S, Wickremasinghe R, Aluthweera C, Fernando T, Gunasekera K, Mendis K
articles.details: Analysis of 532 imported malaria cases in Sri Lanka (2013–2023), predominantly P. falciparum (48.1%, mostly from Africa) and P. vivax (40.5%, mostly from India), with 68.5% associated with work-related travel. P. ovale manifested after longer periods (median 95 days). Diagnostic delays (3.41 days vs 2.42 days to seek care) were significant; 46 cases were severe (45 P. falciparum) with 1 death; 7 P. falciparum recrudesced and 3 P. vivax relapsed.

articles.headline: Outdoor resting Anopheles gambiae threaten malaria control in Senegal
articles.title_original: Feeding and resting behaviours of natural Anopheles gambiae s.l. populations in an area of low malaria transmission in south-western Senegal: A comparative study between mainland and island settings.
articles.authors: Diop M, Coulibaly Y, Lo C, Dia AK, Ndoye NA, Tine EG, Diagne NS, Coulibaly YI, Sangaré M, Thiaw O, Faye MB, Tall MF, Ciss O, Faye O, Niang A, Konaté L, Lobo NF, Tine RCK, Niang EHA
articles.details: A longitudinal study (Sept 2020-Nov 2021) collected 765 indoor and outdoor resting Anopheles mosquitoes from mainland and island areas in southwestern Senegal. Among these, 91.1% were A. gambiae s.l. On mainland, indoor-resting females had significantly higher human blood index (HBI) than outdoor-resting females (76.2% vs 47.6%, P=0.035); on islands, HBI showed no significant difference between indoor and outdoor populations (25.1% vs 31.1%, P=0.51). Outdoor resting behavior is a major concern for indoor-based malaria control in these settings.

articles.headline: Geographic inequities in Madagascar's malaria prevention and treatment access
articles.title_original: Understanding inequities in the malaria landscape of Madagascar: a scoping review of current evidence.
articles.authors: Rajaofera MJN, Liu W, Reziky AM, Tomboanona S, Kuang D, Xia Q
articles.details: A scoping review of literature from 2015-2024 examined malaria inequities in Madagascar across prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and socioeconomic factors. Key findings included geographic disparities with rural areas experiencing higher disease burden and insufficient healthcare infrastructure. Interventions identified include insecticide-treated nets (ITNs), indoor residual spraying (IRS), seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC), artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs), and rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs); however, social and behavioral barriers, drug resistance, and diagnostic failures limit effectiveness in underserved regions.

articles.headline: Severe malaria in Kenya: diagnostic gaps and suboptimal artemisinin treatment
articles.title_original: Using sentinel surveillance system data to characterize severe malaria illness and quality of malaria case management among hospitalized patients in Kenya, 2017-2024.
articles.authors: Itoh M, Lucchi N, Schultz J, Agogo GO, Munyua P, Chege D, Mwenda DN, Akoth S, Sumbi V, Shieshia M, Kandie R, Onyango EO, Hines JZ
articles.details: Analysis of sentinel surveillance data (June 2017-July 2024) from 8,487 hospitalized patients found 2,197 (25.9%) positive for malaria, with 713 (32.5%) classified as severe and 16 (0.7%) deaths. Infants had greater odds of severe illness compared to older ages. Fever duration ≥5 days increased risk of severe illness (OR 3.67) and death (OR 8.00) compared to ≤1 day. Only 15% of severe cases received guideline-recommended IV artesunate followed by artemether-lumefantrine; 21% of microscopy-positive patients were PCR-negative, highlighting diagnostic accuracy gaps.

articles.headline: MMS Américas launches malaria molecular surveillance network for elimination
articles.title_original: MMS Américas: a regional network for malaria molecular surveillance.
articles.authors: MMS Américas Network
articles.details: In August 2025, international researchers and public health professionals convened in Panama to discuss malaria molecular surveillance strategies in the Americas. The new MMS Américas network aims to promote sharing of expertise, protocols, and data to accelerate malaria elimination in the region.

articles.headline: Community-designed malaria interventions improve prevention knowledge in Cameroon
articles.title_original: The role of community co-designed interventions for malaria prevention and control in conflict-affected communities in Cameroon's Southwest and littoral regions.
articles.authors: E O EBM, Yankam BM, Ogundeko-Olugbami O, Donovan L, Counihan H, Hawkings H, Maxwell K, Baker K, Zoungrana Y, Tanue EA, Noukeme BM, Metuge A, Nganmou I, Divine A, Coril E, Gilbert K, Sachop N, Ako S, Pensha J, Tabong AO, Itoe BT, Wanji S, Berryman E, Jarman E, Omam EN, Omam LA
articles.details: A mixed-methods study (May 2021-April 2024) of 189 participants from host, displaced, and returnee communities in conflict-affected Cameroon evaluated three co-designed interventions: Community Health Participatory Approach (CoHPA), community health worker supportive supervision, and health vouchers. Qualitative findings from 19 focus group discussions and 18 in-depth interviews showed participants perceived increased knowledge of malaria prevention, increased participation in prevention activities, improved trust in CHW services, and increased treatment access through vouchers, though voucher sustainability was questioned.

articles.headline: PMCA4b expression modulates oxidative stress and artemisinin sensitivity
articles.title_original: Decoding the multifaceted role of erythrocyte PMCA4b in oxidative stress-mediated malaria protection and artemisinin resistance.
articles.authors: Agrohi P, Garg S, Biswas S, Maurya P, Kumar V, Sharma J, Goswami B, Sil SK, Singh MP, Chand SK, Shalini S, Singh OP, Dhangadamajhi G, Mishra N, Ramalingam S, Mallick PK, Singh S
articles.details: This study examined ATP2B4 genotypes and PMCA4b (Ca²+-ATPase) expression in severe malaria, uncomplicated malaria, and healthy controls. ATP2B4 genotypes showed no significant association with malaria protection. However, low PMCA4b expression, regardless of genotype, correlated with increased intra-erythrocytic calcium, oxidative stress, and reduced in-vitro P. falciparum growth. Notably, low PMCA4b expression also reduced P. falciparum artemisinin sensitivity, suggesting that host genetic variation influences drug efficacy and may serve as a biomarker for predicting artemisinin treatment response.

articles.headline: Microtubule-associated proteins coordinate gametocyte development in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: An atlas of novel microtubule-associated proteins in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Niedermüller K, Piwon N, Mesen-Ramirez JP, Zink L, Lemcke S, Liffner B, Fernandes CF, Farias GB, Alder A, Löw C, Dahan N, Wilson DW, Cubillán-Marín J, Gilberger TW
articles.details: Using proximity-dependent biotinylation with PfSPM3 as bait, mass spectrometry identified 37 proteins in the subpellicular microtubule network of P. falciparum gametocytes. GFP tagging confirmed 11 candidate proteins with distinct localizations within the microtubule and inner membrane complex network. Functional knockout of PF3D7_1003400, localized at the inner membrane complex suture/subpellicular microtubule boundary, interfered with gametocytogenesis and prevented falciform gametocyte development. These findings reveal novel components of the gametocyte cytoskeleton critical for parasite transmission.

articles.headline: Haemosporidian parasites detected in avian populations across Thai zoos
articles.title_original: Malaria outbreaks in Humboldt penguins and haemosporidian infections in avian hosts in zoos across Thailand.
articles.authors: Rahmah SA, Jairak W, Thanee S, Saedan A, Mahadthai P, Novianto D, Saiwichai T, Kaneko O, Pattaradilokrat S, Kaewthamasorn M
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Following deaths of five Humboldt penguins (one in February 2022, four July-November 2022) at a zoo in northeastern Thailand due to avian malaria, a survey collected 406 blood and tissue samples from 146 Humboldt penguins and 260 individuals from 38 avian species across 6 zoos nationwide. Molecular screening detected haemosporidian parasites in 40/146 (27.4%) penguin samples and 125/260 (48.0%) other avian samples. Identified parasites included four Plasmodium species (P. collidatum, P. relictum, P. juxtanucleare, P. gallinaceum) and multiple Haemoproteus and Leucocytozoon lineages.

articles.headline: PfEMP1 domain antibodies and opsonic phagocytosis predict parasite burden reduction
articles.title_original: Antibody levels to variant and conserved Plasmodium falciparum antigens predict reduction in parasite burden in Malian children.
articles.authors: Damena D, Dang L, Barry A, Renn JP, Gaoussou S, Mahamar A, Attaher O, Issiaka D, Morrison RD, Misal SA, Dicko A, Duffy PE, Fried M
articles.details: Using immunoprecipitation-mass spectrometry on plasma from Malian children, the study identified antibody targets associated with naturally acquired malaria immunity. High antibody levels to PfEMP1 domains DBLγ11 and DBLζ5 predicted significantly reduced risk of high parasite density (OR 0.74, 95%CI 0.63-0.86 and OR 0.80, 95%CI 0.69-0.93, respectively). Functional studies showed that opsonic phagocytosis of DBLγ11 and DBLζ5 was significantly higher with antibodies at the top tertile (p<0.0001), with relative phagocytosis index positively correlating with antibody levels (r²=0.61 and r²=0.49).

articles.headline: AI-based malaria diagnosis achieves 87.7% sensitivity and 91.4% specificity
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Accuracy of Utilizing Artificial Intelligence for Malaria Diagnostic: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Faratisha IFD, Yunita KC, Rahmawati HR, Fitri LE, Winaris N, Muflikah L
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 10 studies (6,754 patients) evaluated the diagnostic performance of artificial intelligence systems for malaria detection. Pooled results showed AI sensitivity of 87.7% (95%CI 78.2-93.4) and specificity of 91.4% (95%CI 77.3-97.1) compared to microscopy examination. When compared to PCR, AI achieved sensitivity of 90.7% and specificity of 88.3%. These findings demonstrate that AI-based systems provide high accuracy and automation for malaria diagnosis, offering a promising complementary tool for malaria control in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Urine malaria RDT shows limited sensitivity compared to blood tests
articles.title_original: Comparative Performance of Blood and Urine Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Tests in Detecting Malaria in Febrile Children: A Cross-Sectional Analysis.
articles.authors: Babatola AO, Adeniyi AT, Ojo TO, Ilori BF, Olatunya OS
articles.details: A cross-sectional study compared blood and urine malaria rapid diagnostic tests in febrile children. Malaria positivity rates were 67.4% for SD Bioline RDT, 65.3% for CareStart RDT, and 45.3% for Fyodor urine malaria test (UMT). Excellent agreement existed between the two blood-based RDTs (Cohen's kappa 0.859), with moderate agreement between Fyodor UMT and blood RDTs (kappa 0.491). Fyodor UMT achieved sensitivity of 58.6%, specificity of 100.0%, PPV 100.0%, and NPV 40.0%, suggesting limited utility as a standalone diagnostic tool due to low sensitivity.

articles.headline: Malaria infection reduces adipsin diagnostic specificity for preeclampsia
articles.title_original: Impact of Malaria Infection on the Diagnostic Performance of Adipsin for Preeclampsia in Pregnancy: A Case-Control Study.
articles.authors: Opoku Mensah B, Anim EO, Ahenkorah Fondjo L, Manu AO
articles.details: A case-control study of 200 pregnant women (20-42 weeks gestation) examined the impact of P. falciparum malaria on plasma adipsin levels and its diagnostic performance for preeclampsia. Malaria infection was present in 50% and preeclampsia in 50% of participants. Plasma adipsin was significantly elevated in both conditions (p<0.001), with highest levels in those with concurrent infections. Malaria infection reduced diagnostic specificity of adipsin for preeclampsia (62.1%, AUC 0.719) compared to malaria-negative women (87.9%, AUC 0.823), suggesting malaria-adjusted reference thresholds are needed in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Malaria outbreak in Ethiopia driven by poor housing and inadequate net use
articles.title_original: Epidemiological investigation of a possible malaria outbreak in Arba Minch town, Gamo Zone, Southern Ethiopia, 2023.
articles.authors: Dana DD, Belay MM, Ango TS, Zegene GM, Getachew S, Deressa W
articles.details: A descriptive and case-control study (94 cases, 94 controls) examined a 2023 malaria outbreak in Arba Minch, Ethiopia, reporting 5,181 confirmed cases (22.7% positivity rate, 41.2/1000 attack rate). P. falciparum caused 71% of infections. Key risk factors included proximity to breeding sites (AOR 5.45), stagnant water (AOR 5.69), poor housing (AOR 4.40), and inconsistent LLIN use (AOR 0.30), with poor knowledge showing an anomalous protective association (AOR 0.04). Vector control, housing improvements, and community education were recommended.

articles.headline: Computational screening identifies natural kinase inhibitors for antimalarial drug development
articles.title_original: Computational screening of natural inhibitors against Plasmodium falciparum kinases: Toward novel antimalarial therapies.
articles.authors: Alruwaili M, Younas S, Elderdery AY, Alruwaili I, Millis J, Khan MU, Ejaz H
articles.details: An in silico study screened natural compounds against P. falciparum CK2 and PKG kinases using pharmacophore-based virtual screening and molecular docking. Ligand-13 showed strongest binding to CK2 (−11.468 kcal/mol) and Ligand-9 to PKG (−7.490 kcal/mol). Molecular dynamics simulations and binding-free-energy calculations supported Ligand-13's stability and reactivity, suggesting potential as an antimalarial drug candidate. No clinical efficacy data presented.

articles.headline: Malaria outbreaks in endemic settings
articles.title_original: Malaria outbreaks in endemic settings?
articles.authors: Steketee RW
articles.details: No abstract provided; cannot determine study design, methods, or results from title alone.

articles.headline: Glutathione redox system: novel drug target for malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Glutathione redox system as a therapeutic target for Plasmodium: Opportunities, Challenges, and future perspectives.
articles.authors: Mahato RP, Kumbham S, Kumar S, Ch S
articles.details: A review examining glutathione (GSH) as a therapeutic target in malaria parasites, focusing on how GSH protects parasites from oxidative stress during hemoglobin metabolism. The review highlights enzyme inhibitors (GST, GR, GS, GP) targeting the GSH pathway and discusses potential combination therapies, selectivity challenges, and drug-resistance concerns. No clinical results reported; this is a mechanistic review of metabolic pathways.

articles.headline: Civil society perspectives on gene drive-modified mosquitoes for malaria control in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Exploratory conversations with biodiversity-oriented civil society groups on the potential applications of gene drive-modified mosquitoes for malaria control in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Finda MF, Sambo M, Malika G, Njalambaha R, Salum S, Okumu FO
articles.details: A qualitative study interviewed representatives from 10 Tanzanian biodiversity-focused civil society organizations (8 interviews, 1 FGD, 3 large group discussions) about gene drive-modified mosquitoes (GDMMs) for malaria control. Participants expressed cautious support for research but raised concerns about scientific uncertainty, limited local expertise, transparency, environmental risks, accountability, and technological dependency. They emphasized precautionary approaches, robust ethical frameworks, local capacity-building, and early community engagement. No quantitative outcome data.

articles.headline: Haematology analyser combined with microscopy optimizes malaria diagnosis cost-effectiveness
articles.title_original: A comparison of the cost-effectiveness of the current standard of care and of the haematology analyser XN31-microscopy combination for diagnosing malaria in a nonendemic country.
articles.authors: Picot S, Bienvenu AL
articles.details: A cost-effectiveness analysis in France compared four malaria diagnostic scenarios for suspected cases at Lyon University Hospital (2023): microscopy alone, RDT + microscopy, LAMP + microscopy, and haematology analyser XN-31 + microscopy. The haematology analyser combination demonstrated superior cost-effectiveness due to high accuracy and rapid time-to-result. Cost calculations included consumables and indirect costs for training and quality assurance. Findings are limited to French healthcare costs; generalizability requires further study.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention reduces non-malarial infections and antibiotic use in children
articles.title_original: Indirect benefits of seasonal malaria chemoprevention for non-malarial pediatric infections and routine antibiotic use in real-world programmatic settings: a pre-post study using positive and negative controls.
articles.authors: Gebreegziabher EA, Ouattara M, Bountogo M, Coulibaly B, Boudo V, Ouedraogo T, Lebas E, Hu H, O'Brien KS, Hsiang MS, Glidden DV, Arnold BF, Lietman TM, Sié A, Oldenburg CE
articles.details: A pre-post study in rural Burkina Faso (51 health facilities) examined effects of SMC (monthly sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine + amodiaquine) on non-malarial infections and antibiotic use. Compared SMC administration weeks versus post-SMC period. Modest declines were observed: pneumonia IRR 0.86 (0.7/1000 person-weeks), diarrhea IRR 0.83 (0.2/1000 person-weeks), acute malnutrition IRR 0.71 (0.05/1000 person-weeks), antibiotic prescriptions IRR 0.88 (0.90/1000 person-weeks). Malaria diagnoses declined 3.7/1000 person-weeks (IRR 0.62), with effects varying across SMC cycles.

articles.headline: Anopheles funestus vector populations show contrasted genetic structure across Tanzania
articles.title_original: Microsatellite analysis of genetic differentiation among populations of the malaria vector Anopheles funestus across mainland Tanzania reveals contrasted patterns of geographic isolation and gene flow.
articles.authors: Mapua SA, Samb B, Nambunga IH, Kweyamba PA, Mkandawile G, Bwanaly H, Kaindoa EW, Odero JO, Masalu JP, Kahamba NF, Hape EE, Galizi R, Govella NJ, Okumu FO, Tripet F
articles.details: A microsatellite-based population genetics study examined 654 An. funestus specimens from 10 Tanzanian regions using 12 microsatellite markers. Genetic diversity and differentiation were measured; FST values showed clear species-level separation (>0.2) between An. parensis and An. funestus s.s., with highest divergence between southeastern coastal and inland An. funestus populations (FST up to 0.288). No isolation-by-distance pattern observed, suggesting anthropogenic gene flow shapes population structure. Mtwara population appeared distinctly differentiated.

articles.headline: Cambodia develops real-time digital surveillance system for malaria elimination
articles.title_original: A bespoke, real-time surveillance system for malaria elimination in Cambodia: architecture, applications and impact.
articles.authors: Ngor P, Rekol H, Sovannaroth S, Eng T, Ry R, Sok K, Yoem R, Ou V, Hem V, Chang P, Try R, Ringwald P, White LJ, Maude RJ
articles.details: Cambodia developed an integrated Malaria Information System (MIS) combining real-time case-based surveillance, diagnostics, treatment, logistics, entomology, and operational monitoring across all health system levels. Mobile and web platforms enable village workers and facility staff to report cases and interventions in real time, supporting decentralized decision-making through customizable dashboards. The system implements the 1-3-7 surveillance strategy and incidence-based stratification, with modules for device/stock management, geolocation, and quality assurance. No clinical outcome data presented.

articles.headline: Community perspectives on Plasmodium knowlesi malaria prevention in Malaysia
articles.title_original: Synthesis of qualitative evidence on community experiences and perceptions of Plasmodium knowlesi malaria and factors influencing prevention and healthcare-seeking behaviours in Malaysia.
articles.authors: Htay MNN, Mohd NHN, Oo MMT, San LPP, Abas AL
articles.details: A qualitative evidence synthesis reviewed 5 qualitative and mixed-method studies (2022–2024) on P. knowlesi malaria in Malaysia, synthesizing three themes: knowledge and lived realities shape risk perception affecting health-seeking behavior; environmental, structural, and social barriers (occupational risks, access challenges, protective equipment use) constrain prevention and healthcare seeking; and prevention practices reflect both local knowledge and availability of formal measures. Synthesis recommends integrating community perspectives into vector control and prevention programs. No quantitative outcome data.

articles.headline: Baseline malaria, nutrition, and health-seeking characteristics in Ghana vaccine study area
articles.title_original: The Malaria Vaccine Implementation Programme study area in Ghana: results of a household survey prior to the introduction of the RTS,S/AS01 vaccine.
articles.authors: Welaga P, Gyan T, Koram K, Hodgson A, Odei-Lartey E, Agbokey F, Gyaase S, Awini E, Adu-Gyasi D, Amenuvegbe GK, Dadzie D, Adam AR, Hammond J, Sarfo A, Nuhu AR, Peprah PT, Twumwaa L, Boateng E, Snell P, Fogelson A, Okine RNA, Hamel MJ, Milligan P, Moore KA, Binka FN, Afari EA, Asante KP
articles.details: A baseline household survey in Ghana (Feb–Mar 2019, 6,778 households, 7,768 children ages 5–48 months) prior to RTS,S/AS01 vaccine introduction measured malaria prevalence, coverage of prevention/health interventions, and nutrition. RDT-confirmed parasitemia: 21% (95% CI 18–23). ITN coverage: 87% household ownership, 62% child use. Fever (previous 2 weeks): 22%; care-seeking 72%, testing 40%, antimalarial use 42%. Malnutrition (MUAC ≤13.5 cm): 17%. EPI vaccine coverage >90%. Comparison and implementation areas were comparable.

articles.headline: P. falciparum genome analysis reveals 50-year evolution patterns and adaptation
articles.title_original: Major features of parasite adaptation revealed by genomes of Plasmodium falciparum population samples archived for over 50 years.
articles.authors: Amambua-Ngwa A, Diop MF, Drakeley CJ, d'Alessandro U, Kwiatkowski DP, Conway DJ
articles.details: P. falciparum genomes from archived Gambian placental blood samples (1966–1971, n=54) were sequenced to examine parasite evolution prior to widespread drug selection. High genomic complexity within infections and genetic independence were observed; no drug-resistance alleles were present. Strong positive selection signatures appeared at surface-protein loci targeted by antibody responses. Comparison over ~50 years revealed major allele frequency shifts at gdv1 (sexual conversion regulation), Pfsa1/Pfsa3 (sickle-cell trait association), and surface/transporter protein loci, indicating key long-term adaptations.

articles.headline: Mathematical model reveals spleen's role in RBC loss during malaria
articles.title_original: The role of the spleen in red blood cell loss caused by malaria: A mathematical model.
articles.authors: Moss R, Dini S, Kho S, Barber BE, Buffet PA, Rajasekhar M, Price DJ, Anstey NM, Simpson JA
articles.details: Using data from splenectomy patients in Papua, Indonesia, researchers developed a mathematical model of red blood cell dynamics in P. falciparum and P. vivax infections. The model demonstrates that the spleen retains both infected and uninfected RBCs, with chronic infection showing a ratio of uninfected RBC retention to circulating loss of 17:1 for P. falciparum and 82:1 for P. vivax—substantially higher than previously estimated.

articles.headline: Wolbachia endosymbiont shows promise for malaria vector control
articles.title_original: The symbiotic Wolbachia in Anopheles and its role in reducing the transmission of Plasmodium: updates and prospects.
articles.authors: Nehra R, Dhanda S, Singh K, Singh H, Bharti PK
articles.details: This review evaluates Wolbachia bacteria as a novel vector control strategy across Anopheles species. Wolbachia achieved natural or experimental infection rates ranging from 8–24% in An. gambiae to 91% in transinfected An. stephensi, causing cytoplasmic incompatibility and partial protection against Plasmodium infection. The authors identify persistent challenges in maintaining high transmission rates and sustainable population replacement, and call for improved genetic engineering tools and long-term field evaluations.

articles.headline: High malaria prevalence in onchocerciasis-endemic Ghanaian communities
articles.title_original: Malaria in onchocerciasis-endemic populations: patterns in two districts in the Oti and Volta regions of Ghana.
articles.authors: Ampadu B, Osei S, Ashong Y, de Souza DK, Owusu EDA
articles.details: A case-control study of 345 participants (1:2 ratio of seropositives to seronegatives) from two districts in Ghana assessed malaria prevalence among individuals with and without onchocerciasis seropositivity. Overall malaria prevalence was 42.6% (95% CI 37.5–47.8%), with 53.26% in Nkwanta North and 38.7% in Adaklu. RDT showed 89.2% sensitivity and 71.9% specificity compared to nested PCR, and a weak but significant association was observed between malaria and onchocerciasis (OR=2.03 overall; adjusted OR=3.52 in Adaklu).

articles.headline: Macaranga bark fractions show potent activity against P. falciparum strains
articles.title_original: Targeting the intra-erythrocytic life cycle of Plasmodium falciparum unveils antimalarial drug discovery starting points from Drymaria cordata and Macaranga monandra.
articles.authors: Youbi KA, Tali MBT, Ngouana V, Wendji Monkam DS, Souleyman H, Dongmo YKM, Dongmo KJJ, Nana O, Woutouoba Ntieche D, Dize D, Mbouna CDJ, Tsouh Fokou PV, Boyom FF
articles.details: This study screened extracts from two Cameroonian medicinal plants against chloroquine-sensitive (Pf3D7) and multidrug-resistant (PfDd2) P. falciparum strains. Macaranga monandra methanolic bark extract was highly potent and selective (IC50 1.02–2.46 μg/mL, selectivity index >203), with two active fractions (F2: IC50 0.60 μg/mL PfDd2, 3.42 μg/mL Pf3D7; F3: IC50 0.92 μg/mL PfDd2, 2.46 μg/mL Pf3D7) that arrested ring-stage parasites, lysed trophozoite-infected RBCs, and blocked merozoite egress.

articles.headline: Hepatocyte autophagy mechanisms reveal new host-directed antimalarial targets
articles.title_original: Hepatocyte Autophagy in Malaria: Current Concepts, Emerging Mechanisms, and Future Therapeutic Directions.
articles.authors: Berbudi A, Khairani S, Setyowati EY, Kwarteng A
articles.details: This review examines hepatocyte responses to liver-stage Plasmodium infection through noncanonical autophagy (CASM and PAAR pathways), which limits early parasite development. Parasites evade this defense via UIS3 and UIS4 proteins that prevent endolysosomal fusion, while simultaneously exploiting autophagy components like GABARAP to activate TFEB and enhance nutrient access. The authors identify selective modulation of noncanonical autophagy as a promising avenue for host-directed therapies that restrict liver-stage development while minimizing antimalarial-resistance risk.

articles.headline: Serological markers reveal micro-epidemiological heterogeneity in near-elimination setting
articles.title_original: Serological evidence of continued exposure to Plasmodium falciparum among residents in a near-elimination setting.
articles.authors: Daud MM, Rahayu AA, Ilyas M, Hitipeuw D, Fitriana F, Syahada JH, Nuranindita R, Amirudin FA, Saputra EY, Jeon BY, Cahyani NKD, Pandupuspitasari NS, Han JH, Muh F
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 120 malaria-exposed residents and 24 controls across four Indonesian villages assessed antibody responses to blood-stage antigens PfEBA175 and PfRh5. PfEBA175 seroprevalence ranged from 10–40% (highest in Kembaran 40% and Wadas 23%, p<0.001), while PfRh5 demonstrated universal immunogenicity with 53–70% seroprevalence across all villages (p<0.001). Village-specific risk factors including nocturnal livestock activities, indoor biting exposure, previous malaria history, and travel were significantly associated with antibody profiles.

articles.headline: High vaccine acceptance driven by perception and information sources
articles.title_original: Determinants of caregivers' acceptance of the malaria vaccine: The case of Bamenda and Bamenda III Health Districts in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Aboki LN, Nlinwe NO, Ebai CB, Niba LL, Atanga MBS
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 1000 caregivers of children under 5 in Cameroon assessed acceptance of malaria vaccines (RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M). Overall, 89.6% of caregivers expressed willingness to vaccinate their children. Significant factors associated with acceptance included source of information (community health events vs social media, OR=1.663, 95% CI 1.032–2.68), individual perception (OR=0.098 very rejecting vs accepting), sex (OR=0.704 male vs female), religion (OR=0.386 non-Christian), and occupation (OR=2.92 NGO-employed vs self-employed).

articles.headline: Transient neuropsychiatric complications following P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: A Case Series of Transient Neuropsychiatric Manifestations Following Plasmodium Falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Bhattacharjee D
articles.details: This case series describes neuropsychiatric manifestations occurring after P. falciparum malaria infection. Specific clinical details, patient numbers, and quantitative outcomes are not available from the title alone.

articles.headline: Malaria CFR halved in Burkina Faso over 15 years with funding
articles.title_original: Funded investments contributed to the reduction of malaria morbidity and mortality in children under five: fifteen years retrospective study from 2009 to 2023 in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Akotionga EA, Yaro JB, Kote B, Bakiono F, Ouedraogo T, Nassa S, Batiene V, Nitiema AP, Kouyate B, Nebie SP, Yonaba S, Laokri S, Bazira L, Sirima SB
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of routine health facility data from 2009–2023 in Burkina Faso assessed the impact of USD 13.545 billion in health sector investments (15.3% allocated to malaria) on mortality and morbidity in children under 5. Case fatality rate decreased from 3.4% to 1.3%, while annual contacts per capita increased from 0.8 to 2.1 and distance to health facilities decreased from 7.2 to 6.0 km. Mortality risk varied regionally, with Sahel (IRR=4.1), Boucle du Mouhoun (IRR=3.5), and Nord (IRR=3.4) regions showing three to fourfold higher risk than the Centre region.

articles.headline: Triple co-infection with malaria, dengue, COVID-19 causes sepsis
articles.title_original: Sepsis secondary to co-infection with malaria, dengue and COVID-19: a case report.
articles.authors: Guibla I, Zabramba A, Traore SIS, Tankoano AI, Ilboudo CS, Ouédraogo RFA, Ouédraogo MD, Poda A, Traoré IA
articles.details: This case report describes a 36-year-old woman in Burkina Faso with simultaneous infection with P. falciparum malaria, dengue virus (DEN3 serotype), and COVID-19, complicated by sepsis and multiple organ dysfunction. She presented with severe hypoxemia, hepatic dysfunction (cytolysis and cholestasis), and a SOFA score of 7, with diagnosis of COVID-19 delayed by 10 days due to overlapping clinical signs with malaria and dengue fever.

articles.headline: Dengue prevalent in non-malarial febrile illness in Kenya coastal regions
articles.title_original: Molecular diagnosis of arboviruses (dengue, Chikungunya, yellow fever, Rift Valley fever, Mayaro, West Nile, and Zika viruses) in non-malaria acute febrile illnesses in Kenya's Garissa County, July 2023, and Mombasa County, July 2024.
articles.authors: Roba A, Kiiru J, Kirui E, Langat P, Mbeneka M, Wanyonyi M, Nasengo J, Lagu HI, Achol E, Affara M, Gehre F, Hassan H, Odiambo E, Dugay F, Hussein A, Okemwa F, Swaleh S, Thani S, Gichana R, Karissa I, Ndia M
articles.details: A prospective arboviral surveillance survey screened malaria-negative febrile patients in Kenya for arboviruses using RT-PCR. In Garissa County, 1.23% (4/326 patients) tested positive for dengue virus, while in coastal Mombasa County, 9.00% (26/289 patients) were dengue-positive (95% CI 6.17–12.90%). No other arboviruses (Chikungunya, Rift Valley, yellow fever, Zika, Mayaro, West Nile) were detected, highlighting the urgent need for expanded arboviral diagnostic capacity in peripheral health centers.

articles.headline: P. malariae: neglected parasite with emerging drug resistance challenges
articles.title_original: Plasmodium malariae is an overlooked malaria parasite with emerging challenges.
articles.authors: Shankar H, Kumar G, Ahmed N, Florentin A
articles.details: This review synthesizes current knowledge on Plasmodium malariae, the least studied human-malaria parasite despite widespread global distribution. P. malariae presents challenges including chronic low-grade parasitemia, lack of species-specific diagnostic methods, suboptimal clinical features, and emerging antimalarial drug resistance with reduced susceptibility to first-line agents. The authors highlight its overlooked contribution to global malaria burden and call for enhanced molecular diagnostics, targeted therapeutic strategies, and improved surveillance systems.

articles.headline: Malaria surveillance system gaps in Ethiopian urban setting, 2012-2016
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the malaria surveillance system and trends in Jimma town, Southwest Ethiopia, 2024: a mixed-method study.
articles.authors: Nuh AY, Fikru C, Regassa D, Serba HA, Abreham A, Mattew B, Nasir E, Fikadu H, Mulugeta H, Abubekir H, Christine L, Adam MA, Oloock R, Gizaw T
articles.details: Mixed-method study evaluated malaria surveillance in Jimma Town from 2012-2016 using facility data and interviews (10 health facilities, 8 key informants). Cases rose dramatically from 432 to 11,824, with P. falciparum accounting for 56.4% of infections and increases primarily among school-aged children and adults. The system was functional but limited by incomplete reporting, weak feedback mechanisms, and poor private-sector integration (private facilities' contribution grew from 47% to 66%).

articles.headline: End-user perceptions of non-invasive malaria diagnostic tools in endemic areas
articles.title_original: "It doesn't require blood!": Perceptions around non-invasive malaria testing tools in Indonesia, Peru, and Rwanda.
articles.authors: Fargnoli V, Calarco S, Thomas C, Thomas C, Mone Iye CN, Paz Soldan VA, Morrison AC, Serumondo J, Nshimiyimana L, Nsaba Uwera YD, Marbán-Castro E, Shilton S, Tetteh KKA
articles.details: Qualitative study conducted in Indonesia, Peru, and Rwanda (October-November 2023) with 155 total participants (24 interviews with stakeholders, 16 focus groups with caregivers, pregnant women, healthcare workers, teachers, community members). Non-invasive diagnostics (saliva, exhaled VOCs, transdermal) were viewed favorably for comfort, ease of use, rapid results, and portability, especially in remote settings. Major concerns included doubts about accuracy compared to blood tests, lack of trust in non-blood specimens, and inability to differentiate parasite species.

articles.headline: Iron supplementation shows minimal effect on clinical malaria in children
articles.title_original: Oral iron supplements for children in malaria-endemic areas.
articles.authors: Itzkovich M, Neuberger A, Harris I, Yahav D, Paul M, Gilboa M
articles.details: Cochrane systematic review of 40 RCTs (33,785 children) in malaria-endemic areas found iron supplementation resulted in little or no difference in clinical malaria (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.87–1.00; high-certainty evidence) and slightly reduced severe malaria (RR 0.90, 95% CI 0.81–0.98; high-certainty evidence). Iron plus folic acid may result in little difference in severe malaria requiring admission (RR 1.11, 95% CI 0.96–1.28; low-certainty evidence). No significant difference in mortality or hospitalization/clinic visits.

articles.headline: Paracetamol showed no significant benefit for renal recovery in pediatric severe malaria
articles.title_original: A phase I/II randomized controlled clinical trial to assess the feasibility, safety, and preliminary effectiveness of paracetamol in resolving acute kidney injury in children with severe malaria.
articles.authors: Paasi G, Namayanja C, Ongodia P, Okalebo CB, Okiror W, Abongo G, Masifa G, Amorut D, Muhindo R, Olupot-Olupot P
articles.details: Phase I/II RCT with 40 children aged 6 months–12 years with malaria-induced acute kidney injury in eastern Uganda compared paracetamol (20 mg/kg 6-hourly for 48 h, n=20) to tepid sponging (n=20). Mean time to renal recovery was 0.491 hours longer with paracetamol (95% CI −9.265 to 10.248, p=0.921), which was not statistically significant even after adjusting for age and weight (1.04 h, p=0.832). Paracetamol was safe with no difference in adverse events or hepatotoxicity.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine acceptance high in rural Senegal; shared determinants with COVID-19
articles.title_original: Exploring factors affecting malaria vaccination intention and COVID-19 vaccine uptake: evidence from a joint analysis in rural Senegal.
articles.authors: Cortaredona S, Sokhna C, Maradan G, Ba EH, Seror V
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 795 adults in rural Niakhar, Senegal in 2023 using Health Belief Model framework. COVID-19 vaccine uptake was 35.6%, while 58.6% expressed favorable intentions toward a hypothetical free adult malaria vaccine. COVID-19-vaccinated individuals were 60% more likely to support malaria vaccination (p<0.001). Perceived disease severity more strongly predicted COVID-19 uptake, while perceived susceptibility more strongly predicted malaria vaccine intentions; women showed greater malaria vaccine support (p=0.005).

articles.headline: Thymol derivatives show antiplasmodial activity targeting PfCRT in vitro
articles.title_original: Thymol Derivatives as Antimalarial Agents: Synthesis, Activity Against Plasmodium falciparum, ADMET Profiling, and Molecular Docking Insights.
articles.authors: Ali AH, Retnosari R, Jamil SNH, Norazmi NAZ, Zuhaidi ND, Lam SD, Chieng S, Agustar HK, Chandru K, Md Iqbal N, Yee Ling L, Latip J
articles.details: Synthesis and evaluation of 7 thymol derivatives against P. falciparum 3D7 and P. knowlesi. Compounds 4 and 6 exhibited the highest activity (EC₅₀ = 6.01 ± 1.7 µM and 6.8 ± 1.1 µM, respectively) with good selectivity indices (16.5 and 14.6). Most derivatives showed low cytotoxicity (CC₅₀ > 99 µM except compounds 1 and 3). Molecular docking demonstrated strong binding affinity to PfCRT protein (−8.3 to −8.4 kcal/mol), suggesting PfCRT as a potential drug target.

articles.headline: Single-dose primaquine accelerates gametocyte clearance and blocks post-treatment transmission
articles.title_original: The Effect of Artemether-Lumefantrine Combined with a Single Dose of Primaquine on Plasmodium falciparum Gametocyte Clearance and Post-Treatment Infectivity to Anopheles arabiensis.
articles.authors: Minwuyelet A, Yewhalaw D, Petronio Petronio G, Di Marco R, Atenafu G
articles.details: Prospective cohort study in Northwest Ethiopia (January–September 2025) with 96 P. falciparum gametocytemia cases compared artemether-lumefantrine (AL) alone (n=48) to AL plus single-dose primaquine (n=48). AL+primaquine achieved faster gametocyte clearance (15.2% day 3, 0% day 7 vs. 28.9% day 3, 12.2% day 7 for AL alone; p=0.001 and 0.033). Post-treatment mosquito infectivity was markedly reduced with AL+primaquine (6.8% vs. 28.6% on day 3, 0% vs. 7.3% on day 7; p<0.01).

articles.headline: Apical polar ring protein essential for P. falciparum invasion and replication
articles.title_original: The essential function of the apical polar ring during the blood stage of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Gurung P, Back PS, Ali I, Hernandez GM, Dvorin JD
articles.details: Study characterized PfAPR1, an apical polar ring protein in P. falciparum blood stage, using conditional knockout and expansion microscopy. PfAPR1-deficient parasites showed defects in daughter cell segmentation and microtubule organization but maintained IMC formation and apical polarity. Critically, PfAPR1-knockout parasites contacted host RBCs but failed to form tight junctions, resulting in complete invasion block, identifying PfAPR1 as essential for asexual replication and invasion.

articles.headline: Msp1/Msp2 genetic diversity linked to severe malaria in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Genetic Diversity of Plasmodium falciparum Merozoite Surface Protein 1 and 2 Genes and Their Allele Association With Malaria Severity, Seasons, and Sex in Bamenda, North West Cameroon.
articles.authors: Achungu CR, Anong DN, Faustin K, Shey RA, Tabe CJ, Djouosseu CA
articles.journal: Can J Infect Dis Med Microbiol
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 281 P. falciparum cases in Bamenda, Cameroon genotyped for Msp1 Block 2 (all three family alleles present: RO33 44.7%, K1, MAD20) and 194 cases genotyped for Msp2 Block 3 (3D7 56.7%, FC27). K1 allele was associated with severe malaria, and Msp2 Block 3 alleles showed sex association (p=0.012). No seasonal variation in allele prevalence observed.

articles.headline: Community co-creation sustains malaria awareness in low-transmission Thai setting
articles.title_original: Sustaining community-based malaria services through stakeholder engagement: lessons from co-creation in northeastern Thailand.
articles.authors: Jongdeepaisal M, Sirimattayanant M, Prasert O, Maneenet S, Sopa P, Napchit S, Yuenyao N, Singkham N, Charoensup I, Kanjanasuwan J, Thongard T, Asarath SA, Khirikoekkong N, Osterrieder A, Maude RR, Pell C, Cheah PY, Maude RJ
articles.details: Implementation study in Buntharik district, northeastern Thailand (April 2023–June 2024) used co-creation framework with community members and health program stakeholders (36 engagement activities, ~550 participants) to develop locally-owned health materials, including a 2024 health calendar integrating malaria with priority local health issues. Co-creation addressed local health concerns and community expectations, offering a potential model to maintain malaria awareness in low-transmission areas, though sustainability depends on additional funding and policy stability.

articles.headline: Vector resistance and behavioral changes threaten India's pre-elimination malaria goals
articles.title_original: A multicentric study on understanding the bionomics of Indian malaria vectors across diverse eco-epidemiological settings.
articles.authors: Mohanty AK, Eapen A, Singh H, Singh K, Baharia RK, Jain V, Mazumdar D, Sharma S, Shriram AN, Vidhya PT, Sharma A, Thiruvengadam K, Rahi M
articles.details: Multicentric study across 14 districts in 8 Indian states (2021–2023) characterized vector bionomics including human biting rates, man-hour densities, blood meal preference, and Plasmodium infection rates. Anopheles culicifacies (most prevalent in all states) showed varying densities (indoor MHD 11.95–outdoor 27.16), A. minimus displayed altered resting behavior and high anthropophily (blood index 0.94), and A. baimaii exhibited high sporozoite infection rates (5.88%). Insecticide resistance developed in A. culicifacies (alpha-cypermethrin, deltamethrin) and A. stephensi (multiple insecticides), posing challenges to vector control.

articles.headline: Intermittent preventive treatment reduces school absenteeism in Tanzanian schoolchildren
articles.title_original: The impact of intermittent preventive treatment for malaria on school absenteeism among primary school-aged children in Handeni District Council, Tanzania: quasi-experimental study.
articles.authors: Kidumule FM, Omary H, Zacharia A, Mringo B, Shonde A, Ngasala B, Ottaru TA
articles.details: Quasi-experimental pre-post study in Handeni District, Tanzania (Jan–May 2024 vs. 2025) of 346 schoolchildren receiving intermittent preventive treatment with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (91.9% received all three doses). Overall absenteeism decreased from 13% to 10% (p=0.001), and malaria-related absences declined from 27.8% to 11.3%, with school attendance improving by 6.7%. Non-participation in economic activities was associated with higher absenteeism.

articles.headline: P. falciparum dominates at Ethiopian hospital; males 1.5x more affected
articles.title_original: Analysis of a seven-year trend in malaria prevalence at Pawe General Hospital, Northwestern Ethiopia: a retrospective cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Legesse F, Berhane N
articles.details: Among 155,669 suspected cases at Pawe General Hospital (2015–2021), 11,495 (7.4%, 95% CI 7.2–7.5%) were confirmed by microscopy. P. falciparum caused 90.8%, P. vivax 6.2%, and mixed infections 3.0%. Males had significantly higher prevalence (8.8% vs 6.2%, OR=1.48, 95% CI 1.43–1.54; p<0.001). Malaria burden fluctuated with resurgence after 2018, indicating strengthened surveillance is needed.

articles.headline: ITN deployment reduced malaria incidence 41% in Kenya modeling study
articles.title_original: High resolution physically based modelling reveals malaria incidence reduction by vector control measures.
articles.authors: Dieng MDB, Munga S, Tompkins AM, Zornoza MG, Caminade C, Fersch B, Arnault J, Khagayi S, Schwarz M, Kariuki S, Bigogo G, Kunstmann H
articles.details: High-resolution hybrid modeling (WRF/WRF-Hydro + VECTRI) integrating atmospheric, hydrological, and ecological data at 1 km resolution was applied to 2007–2022 western Kenya. Model reproduced observed trends (mean monthly deviation ±100–150 cases). Insecticide-treated net (ITN) coverage reduced entomological inoculation rate by 58% and malaria incidence by 41%, with highest efficacy under warm and moderately wet (150–250 mm) conditions.

articles.headline: Enhanced ANC training improves IPTp-SP delivery in Mali and Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Effectiveness of antenatal care delivery of intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine in the context of an integrated strategy with seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Mali and Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Koita K, Kayentao K, Worrall E, Huyen Ton Nu Nguyet M, Coulibaly O, Bognini JD, Tinto H, Rouamba T, Dembele M, Biébo B, Diarra S, Briand V, Hill J
articles.details: Cluster-randomized trial of integrated sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP) delivery through antenatal care in Mali and Burkina Faso (20 facilities per country; Nov 2023–Jan 2024). Enhanced provider training significantly improved directly observed therapy rates: Mali 79.2% (95% CI 74.0–84.3%) vs control 68.7% (p=0.006); Burkina Faso 52.3% (95% CI 46.9–57.8%) vs control 28.7% (p<0.0001). Providers found the integrated strategy with seasonal malaria chemoprevention acceptable and feasible.

articles.headline: P. ovale cytoadherence and splenic rupture in treatment patient
articles.title_original: Splenic accumulation of intact Plasmodium ovale sensu lato-infected red blood cells in a patient presenting with splenic rupture.
articles.authors: Benabdelmoumen G, Itani O, Joste V, Houzé S, Hardy D, Bavozet F, Sylla M, Kho S, Anstey NM, Consigny PH, Sissoko A, Buffet P
articles.details: A 35-year-old man developed splenic rupture immediately after starting antimalarial treatment (atovaquone-proguanil, then artesunate) for acute P. ovale sensu lato infection. Splenic histology revealed 10-fold accumulation of intact infected erythrocytes compared to general circulation, with infected cells also in small extra-splenic vessels, suggesting cytoadherence—a mechanism previously attributed mainly to P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Systematic review protocol of miRNAs in malaria-arbovirus coinfections
articles.title_original: MicroRNAs profiling in malaria and arbovirus coinfection: A systematic review protocol.
articles.authors: Deutou Wondeu AL, Nka AD, Ka'e AC, Ngoufack Jagni Semengue E, Masso S, Fisemou P, Mobolaji AT, Marymag Z, Fomboh Tah C, Nsen Bughe R, Mbu Nji A, Galgani A, Pirrò S, Colizzi V, Fon Mbacham W
articles.details: This systematic review protocol will examine miRNA expression patterns in malaria-arbovirus coinfections (Dengue, Chikungunya, Zika). Searches will span PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and other databases from January 2000 onwards, including observational and clinical trial data. The review will identify differentially expressed miRNAs as potential biomarkers for disease pathogenesis, diagnostics, and therapeutic targets (PROSPERO CRD42024600379).

articles.headline: Health systems and social factors critical to China's malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Malaria elimination practices in China from the perspective of health system and social development.
articles.authors: Song X, Liu Z, Li N, Chen L, Liu M, Liu J, Zhang Y, Wang M, Ren M
articles.details: Semi-structured interviews with 42 key informants (2023–2025) examined factors in China's malaria elimination, officially certified malaria-free by WHO in 2021. Thematic analysis identified: health education (66.67%), living environment (71.43%), water/sanitation (59.52%), working environment (64.29%), health system (100%), employment opportunities (52.38%), and agricultural crops (57.14%). Study emphasizes that social development advances in healthcare, infrastructure, and community engagement are essential for sustained malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Hemozoin-based diagnostics show promise; magneto-optical nearest to deployment
articles.title_original: Hemozoin as a Diagnostic Biomarker: A Scoping Review of Next-Generation Malaria Detection Technologies.
articles.authors: Berbudi A, Khairani S, Kwarteng A, Otuonye NM
articles.details: Scoping review of 24 studies examined hemozoin—an optically and magnetically active parasite biomarker—across eight diagnostic technology classes: magneto-optical platforms, magnetophoresis, photoacoustics, Raman/SERS, imaging, NMR, smartphone-based microscopy, and flow cytometry. Magneto-optical systems (Hz-MOD, Gazelle, RMOD) showed robust specificity but reduced sensitivity at low parasitemia; photoacoustic Cytophone demonstrated promising sensitivity and noninvasive in vivo detection. Ring-form detection remained challenging; magneto-optical devices are nearest to field deployment.

articles.headline: Community-based home management improved childhood malaria care in Senegal
articles.title_original: How the One Health approach can make effective the World Health Organization's recommendations on integrated management of childhood illnesses: Case of community-based home management of malaria in the Ferlo of Matam, Senegal.
articles.authors: Kaly JSN, Keita IM, Doucoure E, Sall FB, Ba AM, Mango AA, Gomis BL, Dieng MT, Ndao M, Diallo SM, Diop A, Fall KA, Coly O, Cisse O, Faye M, Diallo I, Thiongane A
articles.details: Community-based home management (CBHM) strategy in Ferlo, Senegal trained local providers to detect and treat childhood illness in transhumant communities. Among 2,307 cases: malaria testing rate 98.9% with 21.1% positivity, 96.4% treated with artemisinin-based combination therapies; diarrhea 209 cases (67.9% oral rehydration solution and zinc); respiratory infections in 96.5% of cough cases (55.6% pneumonia with amoxicillin); 1,156 received vitamin A, 443 dewormed. Strategy demonstrates One Health approach effectiveness.

articles.headline: Gold mining and forest loss drive 300% malaria surge in Yanomami
articles.title_original: Investigating the Yanomami malaria outbreak: gold mining and malaria.
articles.authors: de Angeli Dutra D, Fontes J, Braga ÉM, Mordecai E
articles.details: Panel regression analysis of satellite imagery (2016–2023) in Yanomami territory, Brazil, revealed approximately 300% increase in malaria cases associated with illegal gold mining expansion. A 1 standard-deviation increase in mining activity predicted 20–46% rise in malaria incidence 1–2 years later; a 1 s.d. increase in forest-edge perimeter predicted 55% rise in cases. Study highlights impact of land-use changes and forest fragmentation on transmission.

articles.headline: Treatment-seeking delay affects 62% of malaria patients in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Determinants of delay in treatment-seeking among malaria patients attending health facilities in Ethiopia from 2010 to 2024: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mekonen EG, Aemro A
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 12 studies (N=4,511) in Ethiopia (2010–2024) reported pooled prevalence of delayed treatment-seeking (>24 h after symptom onset) of 62% (95% CI 46–77%). Key determinants: lack of malaria knowledge, distance from facilities, high healthcare costs, transportation barriers, being a farmer, self-medication, low income, lack of health insurance, large family size, no formal education, and fear of drug side effects. Recommendations include strengthened health education and expanded community outreach.

articles.headline: Placental malaria associated with stunting and increased BMI in Mali children
articles.title_original: Association between placental malaria, postnatal linear growth, and body mass index in the Dogon Longitudinal Study, Mali.
articles.authors: Merritt C, Vincenz C, Dolo Z, Shedden K, Strassmann BI
articles.details: Prospective cohort study (Dogon Longitudinal Study, Mali; N=317) examined relationships between placental malaria (PM) histology and growth from birth to age 5. Severe PM parasite density was associated with decreased height at 6 months (−0.83 cm; 95% CI −1.63, −0.03; p=0.042) and reduced linear growth through age 5. Paradoxically, severe PM was associated with increased BMI at ages 2–5 years (up to +1.35 kg/m² at age 5; p<0.001).

articles.headline: RDT results recorded fairly accurately but with systematic positive bias
articles.title_original: Accuracy of recording of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Bedia-Tanoh VA, Konaté-Touré A, Kangah-Kouakou OMA, Mian ANN, Tanoh AM, Humes M, Griffith K, Aponte JJ, Hilton E, Cooper S, Lindblade KA, William Y
articles.details: Mixed-methods study in 16 facilities, Côte d'Ivoire (Aug–Dec 2023) assessed accuracy of malaria RDT recording by comparing digital cassette images (reference) to register entries for 11,161 tests (57.9% positive by panel). Overall agreement was strong (κ=0.83, 95% CI 0.77–0.88); PPV 90.7%, NPV 94.8%. However, negative/invalid tests were incorrectly recorded as positive 5.9% of the time vs only 2.0% false negatives. Systematic bias toward false positives may distort surveillance data.

articles.headline: IL-6 and IL-10 cytokines mark symptomatic childhood malaria in Togo
articles.title_original: Distinct systemic cytokine signatures define symptomatic malaria in children: insights from a longitudinal study in Togo.
articles.authors: Bara FD, Simfele HC, Koudema T, Bako S, Sakyi ME, Kudakpo AY, Mensah C, Donkor E, Malm FP, Ritter M, Awandare GA, Katawa G, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort of 60 Togolese children aged 6–10 years treated with artemether-lumefantrine over 12 weeks. 25% tested PCR-positive for P. falciparum; 33% progressed to symptomatic malaria. Symptomatic cases showed significantly elevated IL-6 (p=0.0004 vs non-infected), IFN-γ (p<0.0001), and IL-10 (p<0.0001); IL-10 correlated strongly with parasite density (r=0.911, p=0.001). Authors conclude elevated IL-10 and IFN-γ may serve as prognostic biomarkers.

articles.headline: An. coluzzii insecticide resistance varies by West African bioclimatic zone
articles.title_original: Genomic population structure and insecticide resistance mechanisms in the malaria vector An. coluzzii across contrasting bioclimatic zones in West Africa.
articles.authors: Amoako EK, Bennett KL, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Debrah I, Morang'a CM, Binaansim S, Asoala VA, Ariana C, Malm KL, Awandare G, Miles A, Clarkson CS, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: Whole-genome SNP analysis of malaria vector Anopheles coluzzii across Ghana, Mali, and Côte d'Ivoire. Authors identified restricted gene flow between northern savannah and southern forest regions, with distinct insecticide resistance profiles including Vgsc pyrethroid resistance and metabolic resistance at carboxylesterases and Gste2. A unique Cyp12f signal appeared in coastal Greater Accra, reflecting local agricultural practices.

articles.headline: Chromatin state dynamics guide P. falciparum blood-stage development
articles.title_original: Chromatin state dynamics during the Plasmodium falciparum intraerythrocytic development cycle.
articles.authors: Brown AS Jr, Llinás M, Mahony S
articles.details: Genome-wide mapping of chromatin accessibility, histone modifications, and transcription factor binding throughout the 48-hour asexual intraerythrocytic developmental cycle of P. falciparum. Authors found some chromatin states remain stable while others dynamically shift with gene activation or repression, and transcription factors cluster by chromatin preference.

articles.headline: Wealth index predicts LLIN ownership and use in rural Tanzania
articles.title_original: Development of a new wealth index for Tanzania: the moderated effect of the implementation of 1,7- malaria reactive community-based testing and response (1,7-mRCTR) by socioeconomic position (SEP) with malaria prevention.
articles.authors: Gao C, Huang S, Yin H, Lu S, Liu L, Mlacha YP, Chaki P, Zhou XN, Xiao N, Richardson S, Wang D
articles.details: Cross-sectional surveys of 19,686 Tanzanian households in 2015 and 2018 assessed wealth index (Mokken scale) and LLIN ownership/use. Poorest households had lower odds of owning LLINs (OR=0.62, 95% CI 0.54–0.70) and using them (OR=0.53, 95% CI 0.45–0.62) versus wealthiest. Difference-in-Differences analysis showed the 1,7-mRCTR intervention reduced wealth-based inequalities, with larger LLIN ownership (OR=1.26) and use (OR=1.88) increases among poorest households.

articles.headline: Diagnostic criteria and algorithm for congenital malaria in neonates
articles.title_original: Congenital Malaria in Non-Endemic Settings: A Literature Review and Update on Diagnosis and Management.
articles.authors: Patterson CA, Nabarro LE, Kumar S, Nolder D, Chiodini PL
articles.details: Scoping review of 26 papers (2013–2023) plus a 2023 UK case report. Authors propose defining congenital malaria as peripheral blood smear or PCR-positive Plasmodium in infants <28 days regardless of symptoms, or <1 year with no travel history. They outline an algorithm emphasizing PCR detection for parasitemia below microscopy sensitivity.

articles.headline: Azithromycin-containing IPTp reduces parasitemia at delivery vs SP alone
articles.title_original: Safety and efficacy of azithromycin-containing regimen as an intermittent preventive therapy for malaria in pregnant women: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Lin IF, Sulaiman H, Tran VK, Basri S, Pham CT, Nguyen LMD, Tey MY, Wu Mph CL, Tam KW, Loh EW
articles.details: Network meta-analysis of 8 randomized trials in moderate sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance areas comparing azithromycin-containing intermittent preventive therapy (IPTp) with standard SP-based regimens in pregnant women. Azithromycin-containing IPTp reduced peripheral parasitemia at delivery (OR=0.71, 95% CI 0.57–0.88) without differences in preterm birth, low birthweight, neonatal death, fetal loss, or maternal anemia. Safety profiles were similar.

articles.headline: Drug stockouts and delays undermine malaria control in African health systems
articles.title_original: Drug stockouts and treatment delays in African health systems: impact on malaria morbidity and mortality.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Abdulrahman AO, Anetor JI
articles.details: Review of systemic challenges causing antimalarial drug stockouts and treatment delays across African health systems, including weaknesses in procurement, supply chain management, and infrastructure. Stockouts lead to interrupted malaria treatment, worsening outcomes, and drug resistance spread. Authors stress the need for strengthened supply chains, improved forecasting, inventory management, and enhanced healthcare capacity.

articles.headline: Atypical fever and diarrhea in young child returning from Sudan
articles.title_original: Alert for Imported Malaria in Non-Endemic Areas: A Case Report of Atypical Falciparum Malaria in a Young Child and Diagnostic Experience.
articles.authors: Feng J, Zhou Y, Zhang B, Huang M
articles.details: Case report of a 2-year-11-month-old boy from Sudan presenting with fever and diarrhea. Plasmodium falciparum was identified through blood routine re-examination guided by epidemiological history and confirmed by rapid diagnostic test and microscopy. Authors illustrate a diagnostic pathway for detecting pediatric imported malaria with atypical clinical presentation in non-endemic areas.

articles.headline: Seasonal and spatial baseline surveillance for malaria in Malawian schools
