articles.title_original: Repeated biannual cross-sectional surveys in primary schools set baseline seasonal and spatial surveillance for malaria and schistosomiasis in the Shire Valley Transformation Programme (SVTP), Malawi.
articles.authors: Kapolo P, Chiepa B, Mbewe RB, Kapumba B, Kambewa E, Kaunga L, Coleman S, Chirombo J, Mzilahowa T, Jones CM, Stanton MC, Russell Stothard J
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Four cross-sectional surveys of 4,176 children aged 7–13 across 21 primary schools in Chikwawa District over two wet and two dry seasons. Overall malaria prevalence was 10.8% (95% CI 9.8–11.7%); urogenital schistosomiasis 36.5%; co-infection 5.2%. Malaria showed seasonal fluctuation and strong spatial heterogeneity, with some schools exceeding 25% malaria prevalence, establishing baseline before environmental changes from the Shire Valley Transformation Programme.

articles.headline: Polyalthia longifolia extract reduces parasitemia and enhances antioxidants in mice
articles.title_original: Spleen protective role, Antiplasmodial and Antioxidant activity of Polyalthia longifolia in mice infected with Plasmodium berghei parasite.
articles.authors: Onobrudu DA, Okpoghono J, Acha JO, Dunkwu CC, Enyi KC, Onyesom I
articles.details: Experimental study in 36 Swiss albino mice, 30 inoculated with Plasmodium berghei. Graded doses of Polyalthia longifolia ethanol extract (100, 200, 400 mg/kg/day) and artemether-lumefantrine (2 mg/kg/day) reduced parasitemia and increased spleen antioxidants (GSH, GPx, CAT, SOD, ferritin). Artemether-lumefantrine had the highest survival rate, followed by 400 mg/kg/day extract; treatment reversed malaria-induced spleen structural disorganization.

articles.headline: Digital health platform improves seasonal malaria chemoprevention efficiency in Mozambique
articles.title_original: Lessons from using SALAMA (DIGIT HCM-health campaign management platform) to implement and optimize seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Nampula, Mozambique.
articles.authors: Mussa A, Rodrigues M, Enosse SM, Cook L, Bunce L
articles.details: Implementation of DIGIT Health Campaign Management platform (SALAMA) for 2024 seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Nampula, Mozambique, transitioning from paper-based data collection. Administration of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine improved, with administrative coverage rates 75–109% more consistent with survey-based coverage ~74% versus paper-based 93–105%. Real-time dashboards enabled early detection of wastage (reduced from >10% to acceptable levels) and direct observation of first-dose adherence.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/pfhrp3 gene deletions explain RDT failure in Ethiopian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Molecular, Transcriptomic, and Proteomic Characterization of Plasmodium Infections that Evade Detection by Rapid Diagnostic Tests in Mizan Aman, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Nikulkova M, Kessler A, Wang Z, Patel A, Tolessa T, Teka T, Tesfaye D, Lukas B, Lemessa D, Zemede M, Legesse F, Srivastava H, Sullivan SA, Yan G, Yewhalaw D, Carlton JM
articles.details: Molecular, transcriptomic, and proteomic characterization of 148 individuals in Mizan Aman, Ethiopia. Of 29 PCR-positive P. falciparum infections, 26 (89.7%) were false-negative by RDT; 27 (93.1%) harbored deletions in both pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 genes with negligible pfhrp2 transcripts. No PfHRP2 or PfHRP3 peptides were detectable by proteomics, revealing pfhrp2/pfhrp3 deletion as the predominant mechanism of RDT failure.

articles.headline: GPT-4o outperforms probabilistic models for malaria cause-of-death identification
articles.title_original: Comparison of verbal autopsy using a large language model to biologically confirmed causes of death for malaria and other communicable diseases among children in six sub-Saharan African countries.
articles.authors: Carshon-Marsh R, Wen R, Ng TKS, Kamadod R, Bogoch I, Bondy SJ, Witek TJ Jr, Jha P
articles.details: This study compared three verbal autopsy models (GPT-4o, InSilicoVA, InterVA-5) against biologically confirmed causes of death in 3,129 under-five children from six sub-Saharan African countries (2016–2022). MITS-confirmed malaria prevalence ranged from 5.5% (Mali) to 30.3% (Sierra Leone). GPT-4o correctly classified 46.2% (60/130) of malaria deaths compared to 30.0% for InSilicoVA and 23.1% for InterVA-5, achieving superior cause-specific mortality fraction accuracy (0.36 vs 0.07–0.08). The authors conclude that integrating large language model-driven tools into verbal autopsy systems can enhance diagnostic precision for malaria mortality surveillance.

articles.headline: UPHFP supports Uganda malaria control through 50 projects 2015-2022
articles.title_original: Uganda Public Health Fellowship Program's Contributions to Malaria Control Programs 2015-2022: Strategies, Implementation Challenges, and Opportunities.
articles.authors: Ario AR, Kwiringira A, Migisha R, Kwesiga B, Bulage L, Kadobera D, Kisaakye E, Asio A, Zalwango MG, Zalwango JF, Rutazaana D, Opigo J, Harris JR, Rollins K, Niang M, Boore AL, Nelson LJ, Belay K
articles.details: The Uganda Public Health Fellowship Program (2015–2022) supported 50 malaria-control projects integrated with the National Malaria Control Program, including 14 surveillance projects, 11 outbreak investigations, and 7 epidemiological studies. The program enhanced epidemiologic workforce capacity and strengthened surveillance, policy decisions, and outbreak response. However, single-source funding creates vulnerability to donor-priority changes. The authors conclude that field epidemiology training programs provide critical value to national malaria control efforts.

articles.headline: LILRB1 intronic variant rs7246537 protects against cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: A Functional Intronic Variant of LILRB1 Associated with Clinical Malaria in the Senegalese Population.
articles.authors: Dieppois C, Chambonnier G, Gallardo F, Torres M, Thiam A, Mbengue B, Dieye A, Rihet P, Paul P
articles.details: This study examined whether regulatory variants in LILRB1 (an inhibitory immune receptor) modulate malaria susceptibility in 267 Senegalese individuals (116 severe malaria, 74 mild malaria, 77 controls). The intronic variant rs7246537 allele A was protective: OR = 0.50 (95% CI: 0.28–0.88) for clinical malaria and OR = 0.44 (95% CI: 0.22–0.86) for cerebral malaria. Luciferase assays confirmed that the A allele reduced promoter activity twofold. The authors conclude that this functional regulatory variant reduces malaria susceptibility by modulating LILRB1 expression.

articles.headline: Severe malaria associated with altered NK cell function in Uganda
articles.title_original: Altered natural killer cell function in children with severe malaria.
articles.authors: Turyasingura G, Mellencamp KA, Namazzi R, Opoka RO, John CC, Hart GT
articles.details: This study evaluated NK cell phenotype and function by flow cytometry in 21 Ugandan children <5 years with severe malaria and 19 community controls from low and moderate malaria transmission areas. Children with severe malaria had higher proportions of LILRB1+ NK cells and increased degranulation (low transmission only) but consistently lower IFN-γ production across both transmission settings. The authors conclude that severe malaria involves compound functional NK cell differences, with reduced IFN-γ production as a consistent feature across transmission intensities.

articles.headline: Conservative management of spontaneous splenic rupture in P. vivax case
articles.title_original: Spontaneous splenic rupture as a rare complication of Plasmodium vivax: a case report.
articles.authors: Mouharir M, Jouabri B, Chahbi Z, Fihri MF, Baba H, Kaddouri S, Elbarni R, Qacif H, Zyani M
articles.details: A case report of a 30-year-old soldier with Plasmodium vivax malaria who developed spontaneous splenic rupture with subcapsular haematoma and moderate haemoperitoneum 24 hours after artemether-lumefantrine initiation. Despite hemodynamic instability, the patient was managed conservatively with bed rest, rehydration, and transfusion without splenectomy. The outcome was favorable, with complete resolution of symptoms and haemoglobin stabilization. The authors conclude that conservative management can preserve splenic function in malaria-related splenic rupture.

articles.headline: Temporal Fusion Transformer predicts malaria with high accuracy from climate
articles.title_original: Application of a Temporal Fusion Transformer and Long-Term Climate and Disease Data to Assess the Predictive Power and Understand the Drivers for Malaria and Dengue.
articles.authors: Pillay MT, Le MTQ, Takamatsu Y, Phong TV, Kgalane N, Minakawa N
articles.journal: Int J Environ Res Public Health
articles.details: This study applied Temporal Fusion Transformers to long-term climate datasets and weekly malaria case records from South Africa and Vietnam to predict malaria transmission. The best malaria model achieved R² = 0.95 with MAE = 4.98. Extreme temperature and rainfall were the strongest climate predictors, with ENSO and IOD indices improving longer-range forecasts. The authors conclude that this machine-learning approach provides accurate, interpretable forecasting for early malaria warning systems.

articles.headline: Chalcogen-phosphoranes show fast-acting activity against drug-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Fast-Acting Chalcogen-Phosphoranes Inhibit Sensitive and Resistant Plasmodium falciparum Strains.
articles.authors: Moura IMR, Barbosa CS, Mendes GR, Aguiar ACC, Cruz FC, Menezes PH, Guido RVC
articles.details: This study evaluated eight sulfur-containing chalcogen-phosphorane compounds against Plasmodium falciparum 3D7 strain, achieving IC50s of 0.7–1.9 μM with selectivity indexes ≥12. Compound 5 showed fast-acting activity with no cross-resistance to standard antimalarials and additive interaction with artesunate. Activity was pronounced against ring and trophozoite stages but reduced against schizonts. In a murine P. berghei model, compound 5 showed modest efficacy with slightly improved survival. The authors propose these compounds as candidates for hit-to-lead antimalarial development.

articles.headline: DHAP and AP maintain high efficacy for uncomplicated malaria in Ghana
articles.title_original: Therapeutic efficacy of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine and artesunate-pyronaridine combinations in the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Ghana, 2023.
articles.authors: Abuaku B, Boateng P, Peprah NY, Asamoah A, Duah-Quashie NO, Matrevi SA, Amoako EO, Quashie N, Owusu-Antwi F, Koram KA, Malm KL
articles.details: This prospective evaluation assessed the therapeutic efficacy of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHAP) and artesunate-pyronaridine (AP) in 225 Ghanaian children (6 months–9 years) with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. DHAP achieved 99.2% PCR-uncorrected and 100% PCR-corrected day-42 cure rates; AP achieved 96.2% uncorrected and 97.3% corrected rates. Day-3 parasitemia was minimal (0.3–0.4%), with no early treatment failures in either group. The authors conclude that both DHAP and AP maintain high efficacy (>90%) warranting their continued use in Ghana's malaria treatment guidelines.

articles.headline: Replication stress increases de novo CNVs in P. falciparum genome
articles.title_original: Replication stress increases de novo CNVs across the malaria parasite genome.
articles.authors: Brown N, Luniewski A, Yu X, Warthan M, Liu S, Zulawinska J, Ahmad S, Prasad N, Congdon M, Santos W, Xiao F, Guler JL
articles.details: This study investigated de novo copy number variations (CNVs) in the Plasmodium falciparum genome using low-input genomics. Treatment with an antimalarial compound that inhibits replication significantly increased genome-wide de novo CNVs encompassing genes involved in various cellular pathways affecting human infection. The authors emphasize the connection between replication stress, DNA repair mechanisms, and CNV generation in this rapidly evolving parasite, highlighting a mechanism of rapid adaptation.

articles.headline: miLab automated microscopy achieves 92% sensitivity compared to blood smear
articles.title_original: Automated microscopy for malaria diagnosis in a reference laboratory in nonendemic settings.
articles.authors: Martín-Ramírez A, Lanza-Suárez M, Berzosa Díaz P, Benito A, Antón-Berenguer V, Rubio JM
articles.details: This prospective study (2021–2024) evaluated miLab™ automated digital microscopy using deep learning on 400 blood samples, comparing it to conventional microscopy and nested-multiplex PCR (NM-PCR). Compared to thin blood smear, miLab achieved 92.1% sensitivity and 89.4% specificity (concordance 90.8%, kappa 0.8). Compared to NM-PCR, sensitivity was 62.8% and specificity 95.4%, with 63.4% accurate P. falciparum identification. Parasite density showed moderate correlation (r=0.77) but miLab underestimated counts by 11.6%. The authors conclude miLab is valuable for malaria diagnosis in nonendemic settings but requires improvements in species identification and parasite quantification.

articles.headline: Mosquito species distribution and climate predict malaria prevalence across Kenya
articles.title_original: Mapping mosquito diversity in Kenya correlating species distribution with malaria prevalence across varied climatic parameters.
articles.authors: Zablon JO, Goel V, Giesbrecht D, Kariuki L, Mbogo C, Goedel WC, Bailey JA, Matoke-Muhia D
articles.details: This study analyzed data from 23 Kenyan counties (2019–2021) to assess how climate variables and mosquito species distribution correlate with malaria prevalence using mixed-effect regression and spatial analysis. Malaria prevalence ranged from 0.9% (low-risk) to 19% (Lake Endemic zone). Anopheles gambiae dominated coastal and semi-arid areas, while A. funestus was highest in highlands and lake zones. Environmental factors and insecticide-treated net coverage were important predictors of prevalence. The authors conclude that spatial mosquito distribution patterns can inform targeted vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Outdoor mosquito biting contributes to residual malaria transmission in Benin
articles.title_original: An underestimated risk? Analysis of outdoor malaria transmission in an urban area of Southern Benin.
articles.authors: Konkon AK, Zoungbédji DM, Sovi A, Hoyochi I, Salako AS, Konkon C, Dangnon B, Yahoue G, Lokossou AS, Adjovi RV, Baba-Moussa L, Akogbéto M, Padonou GG
articles.details: This entomological study (April–September 2019) in Porto-Novo, Benin conducted indoor and outdoor human landing catches across five neighborhoods (6,201 mosquitoes collected). Anopheles gambiae s.l. demonstrated site-specific biting patterns: similar indoor/outdoor rates in some areas but exophagic (outdoor) trends in Tokpota and Djegan-Daho. Plasmodium falciparum infection was detected indoors (3.57–4.41%) and outdoors (1.26%), indicating heterogeneous residual transmission. Most households (86%) owned and used nets but slept indoors; 14% slept outdoors. The authors conclude that context-specific control strategies addressing both vector behavior and human sleeping patterns are needed.

articles.headline: Healthcare worker knowledge gaps on chikungunya in malaria areas
articles.title_original: Practices and challenges related to management of chikungunya and non-malarial acute febrile illnesses in malaria-endemic areas of Afar region, Northeast Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Zerfu B, Kassa T, Mamo G, Legesse M
articles.details: Mixed-methods study of 82 healthcare workers across 6 facilities in Afar Region, Ethiopia. Only 23.4% demonstrated good knowledge about chikungunya transmission and prevention; 59.2% of febrile cases were non-malarial. Key challenges identified: lack of diagnostic tools, inadequate HCW training, overlapping symptoms causing misdiagnosis, and community misconceptions about non-malarial illnesses.

articles.headline: Secondary Anopheles vectors sustain malaria in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Beyond Anopheles gambiae sensu lato: exploring the impact of non-dominant Anopheles species on malaria persistence in high-transmission endemic areas of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ngaffo KL, Hien AS, Soma DD, Maiga S, Sougue E, Ouédraogo COW, Delphine KO, Kaboré DPA, Namountougou M, Diabaté A, Josiane ED, Dabiré RK
articles.details: Mosquito surveys across three ecological zones (2023–2024) collected 1,718 non-gambiae Anopheles: An. nili (87.5%–93%), An. coustani, An. pharoensis, An. funestus, An. rufipes. Plasmodium falciparum detected in all species except An. funestus. Most mosquitoes were outdoor-feeding and highly anthropophilic, revealing limitations of current indoor-focused interventions. Findings suggest secondary vectors increasingly sustain transmission.

articles.headline: Mobile malaria workers maintained control during COVID-19
articles.title_original: Progress towards malaria elimination: insights from Cambodia's mobile malaria workers during the COVID-19 pandemic.
articles.authors: Manning EK, Huy R, Siv S, Ly P, Tibenderana JK, Vernaeve L
articles.details: Descriptive report on Cambodia's National Center for Parasitology, Entomology and Malaria Control response during COVID-19. Mobile malaria workers (MMWs) continued case detection and treatment among remote populations despite movement restrictions. Through locally tailored implementation and close communication, Malaria Consortium MMWs minimized disruption to malaria elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine achieves faster viral clearance in co-infected patients
articles.title_original: Artemether-lumefantrine versus pyronaridine-artesunate for the treatment of malaria in patients with mild to moderate COVID-19 in Kenya and Burkina Faso: a randomised open-label trial (MALCOV).
articles.authors: Tangara B, Barsosio HC, Marlais T, Kabore JMT, Tiono AB, Otieno K, Wanjiku M, Achieng M, Onyango ED, Ondieki ED, Aura H, Odawo T, Allen DJ, Hannan L, Tetteh KK, Soulama I, Ouedraogo A, Serme SS, Soulama BI, Barry A, Badoum ES, Matthewman J, Brazal-Monzó H, Canizales J, Drabko A, Wu W, Kariuki S, Lesosky M, Sirima SB, Drakeley C, Ter Kuile FO
articles.details: Open-label RCT (n=143; age ≥6 months; 66% aged ≥15 years) comparing 3-day pyronaridine-artesunate vs artemether-lumefantrine in patients with SARS-CoV-2 and uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in Kenya and Burkina Faso (Jan 2021–Jan 2022). SARS-CoV-2 clearance by day 7: 41% (PA) vs 58% (AL, aRR=0.78, p=0.37); median day-7 viral load 855 vs 81 copies/mL (p=0.023). Parasitological cure by day 42: 100% PA, 99% AL. Both highly effective antimalarials.

articles.headline: Web tool maps genomic variants across Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: Malaria-GENOMAP: a web-based tool for exploring genomic variation of malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Thorpe J, Billows N, Ngwana-Joseph GC, Ibrahim A, Nolder D, Sutherland CJ, Nguyen THN, Nguyen THB, Nguyen QT, Dombrowski JG, Di Santi SM, Marinho CRF, Phelan JE, Kurowski T, Mohareb F, Campino S, Clark TG
articles.details: Malaria-GENOMAP integrates whole-genome sequencing data with geographical maps for six Plasmodium species: P. knowlesi (n=139), P. malariae (n=158), P. ovale curtisi (n=36), P. ovale wallikeri (n=47), P. simium (n=38), P. vivax (n=1,359). Tool enables investigation of population structure, geographic associations of mutations, and putative drug-resistance markers for malaria control.

articles.headline: HRP2 RDTs overestimate malaria prevalence in rural Ghana
articles.title_original: False-positive and false-negative malaria rapid diagnostic test results: evidence from a nationally representative survey of children in Ghana.
articles.authors: Asare KK, Aboagye YO, Mohammed MW, Darko M, Adjei C, Emmanuels HF, Boakye AN, Asiedu E
articles.details: Analysis of 4,417 children from 2022 Ghana DHS comparing HRP2-based RDTs to microscopy. Malaria prevalence: 20.3% (RDT) vs 9.9% (microscopy). RDT sensitivity 89.7%, specificity 87.4%, PPV 43.9%, NPV 98.7%. False-negatives more likely in rural areas and IRS-targeted districts; false-positives uncommon (1.0%). Study recommends confirmatory microscopy in high-transmission settings and surveillance of pfhrp2/3 deletions.

articles.headline: High-dose primaquine and tafenoquine for P. vivax elimination
articles.title_original: Moving towards high-dose primaquine or single-dose tafenoquine for Plasmodium vivax treatment in Cambodia: a meeting report from dissemination of results of the EFFORT trial to stakeholders.
articles.authors: Dysoley L, Cassidy-Seyoum S, Adhikari B, Dahal A, Phoumen N, Srey S, Phalla T, Vonn P, Engrkruy B, Sovanarath O, Mengkea H, Rathana P, Makara I, Heng PK, Heng Y, Khlok H, Pong K, Moeng T, Vantha S, Soviet U, Ravuth R, Bunreth V, Vannak K, Sokhann M, Kimmen K, Dy K, Samol S, Lo K, Sokunthea N, Kry H, Kanha L, Naren L, Oum S, Sovann Y, Channavuth R, Bunmeng C, Hughes J, Brindle H, Hu Y, Peng S, Mardi P, Chanry I, Preap S, Heoy S, Sabawoon F, Sokha T, Ringwald P, Lynch CA, Price RN, Tripura R, von Seidlein L, Devine A, Thriemer K
articles.details: Meeting report on EFFORT trial (four malaria-endemic countries including Cambodia) comparing safety and effectiveness of 7-day high-dose primaquine (7 mg/kg total dose) and single-dose tafenoquine (300 mg) vs 14-day low-dose primaquine (3.5 mg/kg) for P. vivax treatment. Cambodia's CNM previously implemented low-dose primaquine with G6PD testing. EFFORT assessed feasibility and cost-effectiveness; results inform policy changes for P. vivax elimination.

articles.headline: Metal isotope ratios reveal parasite metabolism and anemia pathology
articles.title_original: Iron, copper and zinc isotope ratios in malaria infection reveal signatures of the Plasmodium biology and host mice response to the infection.
articles.authors: Costas-Rodríguez M, Abou-Zeid L, Hastuti AAMB, Silva MDCB, de Morais AS, Quadros HC, Moreira DRM, Vanhaecke F
articles.details: Study examined iron, copper, and zinc concentrations and isotope ratios in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice. Parasitized red blood cells showed altered Fe, Cu, and Zn isotopic compositions, indicating parasite metal acquisition and metabolism. Hepatic iron overload with depletion of heavier Fe isotopes suggesting compensatory mobilization; hepatic copper deficiency with lighter Cu isotopes. Findings indicate central role of Fe and auxiliary Cu role in malaria anemia.

articles.headline: AP2 transcription factor controls parasite development timing
articles.title_original: Precise gene regulation through transcriptional repression is essential for Plasmodium berghei asexual blood stage development.
articles.authors: Nishi T, Kaneko I, Iwanaga S, Yuda M
articles.details: Study of AP2-family transcription factor PbAP2-TR in Plasmodium berghei. Conditional knockout of pbap2-tr arrests development at trophozoite stage, with target genes upregulated in knockout parasites. PbAP2-TR recruits co-factor PbMORC and is responsible for transcriptional downregulation from early to late trophozoite development. Findings reveal transcriptional repression controls precise gene expression timing during asexual blood stage development.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine reduces Chlamydia and improves birth outcomes
articles.title_original: Impact of intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine on sexually transmitted and reproductive tract infections: results from a randomized trial in Uganda.
articles.authors: Adrama H, Dela Cruz EJ, Ozarslan N, Kakuru A, Odongo B, Gaw SL, Benjamin-Chung J, Kizza J, Aguti M, Ategeka J, Olwoch P, Nakalembe M, Opira B, Clark TD, Kamya MR, Rosenthal PJ, Dorsey G, Roh ME
articles.details: Secondary analysis of randomized trial comparing monthly intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), or DP+SP among 2,265 pregnant women in Uganda. SP arm had 80% lower C. trachomatis (2.5% vs 12.4%) and 35% lower Group B Streptococcus (7.7% vs 11.7%) at delivery vs DP. C. trachomatis associated with increased preterm delivery (PR=1.86, 95% CI 1.07–3.25); GBS with term low birthweight (PR=2.08, 95% CI 1.06–4.08).

articles.headline: Microscopy misidentifies 98% of mixed Plasmodium infections
articles.title_original: Plasmodium mono and mixed-infections in India: a tale of see-saw in species misidentification.
articles.authors: Deora N, Pande V, Sinha A
articles.details: Systematic analysis of 2,706 microscopy-PCR sample pairs from 16 locations across 11 Indian states. Overall misidentification rate: 15% (408/2,706). Mixed-infections: >98% misidentified (400/405) as mono-infections, almost all as P. falciparum. Jharkhand and Madhya Pradesh contributed >20% of misidentifications. Study demonstrates overestimation of P. falciparum burden, underestimation of non-falciparum species, and potential waste of elimination resources.

articles.headline: Therapeutic plasma exchange reduces inflammatory cytokines in severe malaria
articles.title_original: The blood purification therapy-based strategy effectively rescued the severe falciparum malaria patient experiencing cytokine storm-driven MODS.
articles.authors: Sun Y, Huang X, Hong B, Song J, Lu Y, Ma J, Wang X, Hu W, Zhang Y, Xuan H
articles.details: Case report of 63-year-old with severe P. falciparum malaria presenting with cytokine storm and multiple organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS), treated with therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) combined with continuous venovenous hemodiafiltration (CVVHDF). After first session, interleukin-6 decreased from 729.65 to 26.42 pg/mL and interferon-gamma from 170.38 to 42.47 pg/mL; patient recovered. Literature review of 29 severe malaria cases receiving blood purification: 19 with TPE achieved good prognosis.

articles.headline: P. vivax congenital and neonatal malaria in Western Ethiopia: diagnostic challenges
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax congenital and neonatal malaria with atypical presentation in Western Ethiopia: four cases highlighting diagnostic pitfalls.
articles.authors: Adugna NB, Adugna NB, Geleta DB, Mitike BA, Terefa YA
articles.details: Descriptive case series of four neonates (two girls, median age 18 days, range 1–21 days) admitted to NICU with P. vivax malaria between June 2024 and September 2025. Presentations included fever (3/4), poor feeding (3/4), tachypnea (2/4); parasitemia levels ranged from +1 to +3 on Giemsa smears. All received IV artesunate (3 mg/kg) plus empiric antibiotics and were discharged by hospital day 7 with no deaths. Two had thrombocytopenia and one anaemia.

articles.headline: Triterpenic esters target PfA-M17 aminopeptidase in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Natural triterpenic phenolic esters target PfA-M17 in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Mamede L, Rangel GW, Schmitt M, Albrecht S, Spichty M, Bero J, Beaufay C, Florent I, Llinás M, Frédérich M, Quetin-Leclercq J
articles.details: Multi-scale integrative study of eight triterpenic esters (8TTE) from Keetia leucantha investigating mode of action against P. falciparum through phenotypic analysis, enzymatic tests, molecular docking, and metabolomic profiling. Study identified unique antiplasmodial profile with activity onset in early-ring stage, inhibition of aminopeptidase PfA-M17 (PlasmoDB PF3D7_1446200), and perturbations in parasite haemoglobin metabolism. Further structure-activity studies warranted for potential antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Chlorfenapyr bednets overcome pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles vectors, Uganda
articles.title_original: Chlorfenapyr bednets effectively overcome pyrethroid resistance escalation in highly resistant Anopheles malaria vectors in Uganda.
articles.authors: Oruni A, Menze BD, Fotso-Toguem YG, Ngannang-Fezeu VB, Thiomela RF, Tchouakui M, Hearn J, Kayondo J, Wondji CS
articles.details: Experimental hut trials in Uganda using highly resistant wild Anopheles mosquitoes and F2 hybrids evaluated bednet performance. Interceptor G2 (chlorfenapyr) achieved mortality odds ratio of 18.7 (95% CI 8.05–48.6, P < 0.0001) vs pyrethroid-only nets, with 70.6% mortality against An. funestus and 63.2% against An. gambiae. PermaNet 3.0, Olyset Plus, and Royal Guard showed lower mortality (31.0–61.4%). Pyrethroid resistance markers (4.3 Kb-SV, G454A-Cyp9K1) significantly associated with survival against pyrethroid-only nets but not Interceptor G2.

articles.headline: Metabolomic profiling identifies distinct subgroup in asymptomatic P. falciparum infection
articles.title_original: Exploratory Serum Metabolomic Profiling of Asymptomatic Malaria Children Infected with Plasmodium falciparum in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Zinga MM, Kassim Mohamed M, Ojo D, Mohamed Abdoul-Latif F, Mkassy FI, Wilfred D, Marwa KJ, Cacciatore S
articles.details: Exploratory study of serum metabolomic profiles in 48 school-aged children (27 malaria-positive, 21 malaria-negative) from high-transmission Tanzania using 1H NMR spectroscopy. Group-level comparison showed modest metabolic alterations in malaria-positive children, including lower lactate, pyruvate, branched-chain amino acids, and threonine. KODAMA algorithm identified a metabolically distinct subgroup (18.5%) with specific shifts in amino acid and lipid metabolism resembling symptomatic malaria profiles, suggesting biologically meaningful heterogeneous responses to asymptomatic infection.

articles.headline: Pv3Rs: Statistical inference of P. vivax recurrence causes from genetic data
articles.title_original: Pv3Rs: Plasmodium vivax relapse, recrudescence, and reinfection statistical genetic inference.
articles.authors: Foo YS, White MT, Taylor AR
articles.details: Pv3Rs implements statistical inference of Plasmodium vivax recurrence states (recrudescence, relapse, reinfection) from genetic data by modeling genetic relationships between parasites within and across infections to compute posterior probabilities of recurrence states. Tool applicable to various genetic data types including length polymorphic loci and amplicon sequencing data. Freely available on CRAN under GNU license; applicable to clinical trials for molecular correction of antimalarial treatment efficacy estimates.

articles.headline: PI4Kβ: dual-action antimalarial target in parasite survival and host immunity
articles.title_original: Plasmodium Phosphatidylinositol 4-Kinase Beta (PI4Kβ): An Emerging Target at the Interface of Parasite Biology and Host Immunity.
articles.authors: Nimbarte VD, Sonak SS, Mohapatra N, Ishwarkar S, Kumar SR, Mustajar S, Pinto R, Sharanya ST
articles.details: Review of phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase beta (PI4Kβ), an essential lipid kinase in Plasmodium central to intracellular signaling, vesicular trafficking, and parasite development. PI4Kβ modulates host immune responses through regulation of protein export, immune evasion mechanisms, and extracellular vesicle formation. Inhibition of PI4Kβ disrupts parasite survival and enhances immune recognition by impairing virulence factor trafficking and improving antigen presentation. Highlights therapeutic potential as both antiparasitic and immunomodulatory target.

articles.headline: Hsp70–GrB interaction: proposed mechanism driving severe malaria development
articles.title_original: Is the Association of Granzyme B and the Exported Parasite Hsp70 a Possible Trojan Horse of Severe Malaria?
articles.authors: Dongola TH, Shonhai A
articles.details: Hypothesis article proposing mechanism whereby extracellular Hsp70 recruits natural killer cells leading to granzyme B (GrB) release. Plasmodium falciparum exports PfHsp70-x to infected RBC membrane where it may stimulate NK cells to release GrB. GrB is elevated in severe malaria. Article discusses possible roles of Hsp70–GrB association in severe malaria pathogenesis and Hsp70–GrB-mediated antimalarial therapeutic strategies.

articles.headline: Global malaria control slowing; widespread P. falciparum drug resistance threatens progress
articles.title_original: Malaria Public Health Status: Global Context and Update on Gulf Cooperation Council Countries.
articles.authors: Ahmad T, Alhammadi BA, Almaazmi SY, Arafa S, Blatch GL, Dutta T, Gestwicki JE, Keyzers RA, Meskiri A, Sharafeldin A, Shonhai A, Singh H
articles.details: Review of global malaria mortality from 2000 to 2019 (reduced from 864,000 to 576,000 deaths annually), with slowed progress and increased mortality since 2020 due to COVID-19 pandemic. Widespread P. falciparum resistance to first-line drugs necessitates new antimalarials. Recent vaccine successes (RTS,S/AS01 and R21/MM) renew promise. Eastern Mediterranean Region experienced increased cases and deaths since 2015. In Gulf Cooperation Council, malaria endemic in Saudi Arabia and Oman; others declared malaria-free. Threats include drug resistance, invasive mosquitoes, and imported malaria.

articles.headline: Unusual Plasmodium spp. presentation in child with fever and thrombocytopenia
articles.title_original: An unusual presentation of Plasmodium spp. in peripheral blood smear.
articles.authors: Ansari N, Gupta K, Nandwani S, Bir R
articles.details: Case report of 7-year-old boy presenting with abdominal pain and fever (6–7 days). Laboratory findings: normocytic normochromic RBCs with leukocytosis (16.3 × 10³/µL), hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, markedly reduced platelets (35,000/µL), elevated CRP (144.8 mg/L). Peripheral blood smear revealed unusual microscopic findings of Plasmodium spp. (specific findings discussed in full article). Clinical presentation prompted evaluation to rule out malaria among differential diagnoses for fever with thrombocytopenia.

articles.headline: Severe imported P. falciparum malaria in Moscow travelers: clinical presentations
articles.title_original: Clinical and epidemiological profile of severe imported Plasmodium falciparum malaria in travelers at hospital admission: A retrospective single-center study in Moscow, Russia.
articles.authors: Emerole K, Tokmalaev A, Mvuania V
articles.details: Retrospective study of 32 patients meeting WHO severe malaria criteria at Moscow's Infectious Diseases State Hospital (2020–2023). Median age 41 years (IQR 36–52), 89% male, 91.2% Russian descent. Nearly all infected in sub-Saharan Africa; none used chemoprophylaxis. At hospital admission: 99% high parasitemia, 98% altered consciousness, 78.1% profound weakness, 52% jaundice. All cases caused by P. falciparum. Median 5 days (IQR 4–6) from symptom onset to medical attention. No deaths reported in study population.

articles.headline: Corrigendum: Malaria and inflammatory biomarkers in rural Iraqi children
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Analysis of the Relationship Between Malaria and Inflammatory Biomarker Levels in Children in Rural Iraq: Impact of Nutritional Deficiencies, Anemia, and Hematological Disorders" published in Trop Parasitol. 2025 Jul-Dec;15(2):110-117. doi: 10.4103/tp.tp_26_25. Epub 2025 Oct 25. PMID: 41244076; PMCID: PMC12617583.
articles.details: Correction notice to prior article on relationship between malaria and inflammatory biomarker levels in children in rural Iraq, which examined impact of nutritional deficiencies, anemia, and hematological disorders. [Full details of original findings not available in corrigendum notice.]

articles.headline: Malaria vaccines in Southeast Asian elimination: cautious implementation strategies
articles.title_original: Integrating malaria vaccines into Southeast Asian elimination strategies: A cautious path forward.
articles.authors: Parija SC, Poddar A
articles.details: Article discusses integration of malaria vaccines into Southeast Asian malaria elimination strategies, noting a cautious approach to implementation.

articles.headline: Malaria causes 2.4 million maternal anemia cases annually in Africa
articles.title_original: The burden of malaria-attributable maternal anaemia and the impact of preventive treatment across sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Leuba SI, Verity R, Gutman JR, Weiss DJ, Cairns M, Williams JE, Khairallah C, Bojang K, Dodd J, Noor AM, Taylor SM, Otieno K, Coulibaly SO, Kayentao K, Kariuki S, Greenwood B, Desai M, Chandramohan D, Tagbor H, Ter Kuile FO, Madanitsa M, Walker PGT
articles.details: Study used data from 12,608 pregnancies across 7 African countries with models of malaria exposure and immunity. For 2023, 13.1 million pregnancies were exposed to P. falciparum. Without preventive treatment, this would cause 2.41 million cases of moderate/severe anemia (Hb < 9 g/dL) including 600,000 severe cases. Current intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine averted approximately 1.10 million moderate/severe anemia cases and 330,000 severe cases in 2023, though malaria remains a major driver of maternal anemia risk.

articles.headline: Diagnostic overlap between malaria and pre-eclampsia in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Concordance and Misclassification of Pre-Eclampsia in a Malaria-Endemic Setting: A Comparative Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Opoku Mensah B, Serwaa AA, Dugbatey MK, Anim E
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 618 pregnant women compared routine clinical diagnosis with reference criteria. Overall diagnostic concordance was 50.2% (κ = 0.233). Routine pre-eclampsia diagnosis showed sensitivity 61.8%, specificity 62.2%, PPV 65.9%, NPV 57.9%, demonstrating moderate performance with substantial misclassification in settings with concurrent malaria.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 13.6% in Ethiopian pregnant women; environmental factors drive risk
articles.title_original: Prevalence, Associated Risk Factors, and the Impact of Malaria on Anemia in Pregnant Women Attending ANC in North Gondar Zone, Northern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Alula GA, Endale T, Yibeltie G
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study of 308 pregnant women in North Gondar Zone. Overall malaria prevalence was 13.6%. Risk factors included age ≥40 years (AOR 4.8), rural residence (AOR 2.8), living within 1 km of water bodies (AOR 3.0), unscreened houses (AOR 3.6), and inconsistent ITN use (AOR 7.2). Among malaria-infected women, 40% were anemic, confirming malaria as a key contributor to maternal anemia.

articles.headline: Modular recombination architecture of Plasmodium DBLMSP genes
articles.title_original: Module-level recombination drives DBLMSP polymorphism and functional conservation in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Duan YW, Chen SB, Wang TY, Yang WX, Kassegne K, Shen HM, Chen JH
articles.details: Study analyzed DBLMSP sequences from P. falciparum samples collected along China-Myanmar border and globally. DBLMSP1 and DBLMSP2 consist of recombinable sequence modules with nine genotypes identified for each. Conserved structural elements including the receptor-binding cleft and SPAM domain are under purifying selection, while variable modules show high nucleotide diversity. Evidence suggests modular structure shapes recombination patterns with implications for immunogen design.

articles.headline: Genetic approaches to malaria vector control through mosquito immunity
articles.title_original: Advancing Malaria Vector Control: Insights Into Mosquito Immunity and Genetic Strategies.
articles.authors: Cleanclay WD, Adedoyin ED, Zakari S, Ogunlana OO, Iweala EEJ, Chinedu SN
articles.details: Review examines the interaction between Plasmodium parasites and Anopheles mosquito vectors, focusing on mosquito innate immunity including pathogen recognition receptors, antimicrobial peptides, and melanization. Discusses genetic approaches to vector control including CRISPR-Cas9, Wolbachia, RNA interference, and self-limiting strategies. Addresses Plasmodium evasion mechanisms and potential of emerging tools like mosGILT for blocking transmission.

articles.headline: 43% malaria prevalence in Cameroon; P. falciparum dominant but mixed infections present
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium Species Using Rapid Diagnostic Tests in Cameroon: First-Year Data From Routine Malaria Surveillance.
articles.authors: Tabue RN, Neh F, Evina E, Bomba DA, Voundi JV, Fosso J, Zeh AM
articles.details: Nationwide malaria surveillance program in Cameroon during 2024 tested 3,843,507 individuals, with 43% testing positive. P. falciparum monoinfection accounted for 82% of cases, mixed infections (Pf + Pan) for 15%, and Pan infection for 3%. Regional prevalence of Pf varied from 80.6% (East) to 92.1% (Far North), with seasonal peaks in June-July. Study highlights limitations of P.f/Pan RDTs for species differentiation.

articles.headline: Altered cTfh cell profiles associate with poor outcomes in placental malaria
articles.title_original: cTfh Cell Activation and Changes in BTLA and LAG-3 Expression Differentially Associate With Poor Pregnancy Outcome in Plasmodium falciparum Placental Malaria.
articles.authors: Zambo Bitye BM, Mbianda Nana CM, Kwanou Tchakounte BD, Fogang B, Kenfack Tekougang Zangue B, Medouen Ndeumou RS, Ayong L, Megnekou R
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 49 women in Cameroon examined circulating and placental T follicular helper (cTfh) cells in placental malaria (PM). PM was associated with increased cTfh frequencies and altered BTLA/LAG-3 expression patterns. cTfh frequencies in placental blood positively correlated with baby weight, while negatively associated with maternal hemoglobin. BTLA and LAG-3 expression in placental blood negatively correlated with birth weight, indicating altered immune profiles influence maternal and neonatal outcomes.

articles.headline: Anti-gSG6-P1 biomarker reveals human-vector contact dynamics in Thailand
articles.title_original: Immunological surveillance using anti-gSG6-P1 IgG biomarker reveals spatio-temporal dynamics of Anopheles exposure and gaps in malaria risk assessment in the Greater Mekong Subregion.
articles.authors: Saeung M, Jupatanakul N, Jampeesri N, Afelt A, Chareonviriyaphap T, Manguin S
articles.details: Study of 184 participants in Thailand measured anti-gSG6-P1 IgG as serological marker of Anopheles exposure across three seasons. Seasonality exerted strongest influence on biomarker levels, driven by human behavioral factors including rubber tapping frequency. Higher frequency of rubber plot entry (5-7 days/week) significantly increased anti-gSG6-P1 IgG levels compared to lower frequency. Study highlights limitations of this biomarker in the Mekong Subregion where predominant vector species have low salivary peptide homology to An. gambiae.

articles.headline: Insecticide custody chain in Amazon operates reactively, limiting malaria control effectiveness
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the insecticide custody chain and its relationship with malaria burden in the Brazilian Amazon: a process and exploratory impact assessment (2019-2023).
articles.authors: Dourado ML, Silva RAE, Castro MC, Peterka CRL, Kluge CR, Castro D, Lima JBP
articles.details: Mixed-methods study evaluated the indoor residual spraying (IRS) insecticide custody chain in Brazil's Amazon region (2019-2023). At national level, insecticide volume was significantly associated with malaria cases (β = 0.161, p = 0.038, R² = 0.74) and deforestation (β = 0.626, p = 0.034). Time-series analysis revealed reactive allocation patterns with lags behind malaria incidence peaks. Qualitative assessment identified systemic weaknesses including poor data interoperability, insufficient environmental indicator integration, and inadequate federal coordination.

articles.headline: Plasmodium tandem repeats implicated in immune evasion and protein function
articles.title_original: Structural and functional insights from detailed computational analysis of Plasmodium repetome.
articles.authors: Kaur S, Kumar V, Singh K, Beh A, A A, Mishra PC, Hora R
articles.details: Computational analysis of tandem repeat (TR) content in Plasmodium species proteomes found P. falciparum and P. vivax exceptionally rich in TRs. P. falciparum TRs were predominantly in exported proteins important for survival and immune evasion. Although variable surface antigen (VSA) families known for immune evasion had low overall repeat content, all TR-containing VSAs were sero-reactive. Gene ontology analysis revealed TR-containing proteins primarily involved in binding nucleic acids, proteins, and small molecules with potential functional roles in pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Quality improvement methods achieve 100% malaria case reporting sensitivity
articles.title_original: Quality Improvement Initiative to Improve the Sensitivity of Malaria Reporting under Integrated Health Information Platform, Raigarh, Chhattisgarh, 2023.
articles.authors: Patel Y, Kaviprawin M, Patel K, Ramasamy A, Gahwai DK
articles.details: Quality improvement project using Plan-Do-Study-Act methodology in Raigarh, Chhattisgarh aimed to improve malaria reporting sensitivity to 80%. Identified root causes as inadequate laboratory technicians' knowledge and poor district surveillance feedback. Implemented refresher training for technicians and weekly feedback mechanism from district surveillance unit. Achieved 100% sensitivity within 5 weeks, demonstrating feasibility of QI methods for improving surveillance outcomes.

articles.headline: Malaria burden declining in Kanker district but transmission still uneven
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of Malaria Cases Reported from Government Health Facilities in District Kanker, Chhattisgarh, January 2018 - December 2022.
articles.authors: Jain T, Ramasamy A, Khare A, Ramkete DK, Nagarajan R
articles.details: Analysis of National Malaria Program surveillance data in Kanker district (2018-2022) showed declining burden: Annual Parasite Index (API) decreased from 3.0 to 1.3, and Annual Falciparum Index (AFI) decreased from 2.5 to 1.1. Plasmodium falciparum proportion increased to >90% from 2020-2021. Test Positivity Rate and Test Falciparum Rate both declined by half. However, two blocks still had API >2 in 2022, indicating geographic heterogeneity in transmission.

articles.headline: Malaria resurges in Koraput district despite initial net distribution success
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of Malaria in Koraput District, Odisha, 2019-2023: A Descriptive Analysis of National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme Surveillance Data.
articles.authors: Jena S, Bicholkar A, Jacob QM, Baral P, Murali S
articles.details: Descriptive epidemiological study of surveillance data from Koraput district, Odisha, 2019-2023. Annual parasite incidence (API) was 2.7 per 1000 rising to 4.5 per 1000 in 2023; Narayanpatna had highest API (38 per 1000). Males had consistently higher incidence than females. Seasonal peaks offer opportunity for pre-emptive control measures before monsoons.

articles.headline: Malaria surveillance in Chhattisgarh shows low sensitivity and data quality gaps
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Malaria Surveillance System in Government Health Facilities in Six Districts of State Chhattisgarh, India, 2023.
articles.authors: Gahwai DK, Kaviprawin M, Panigrahi DK, Das JK, Patel K, Roy M, Tigga S, Jain T, Patel Y, Ramasamy A
articles.details: Evaluation of malaria surveillance system in six Chhattisgarh districts, 2023, covering 1276 syndromic, 110 presumptive, and 100 laboratory reporting units. System sensitivity was 5.8% (84/1442 presumptive cases) and 29% (3334/11,510 laboratory cases). Data validity: 52.4% of presumptive and 72% of laboratory forms had valid entries. Results indicate need for quality improvement initiatives in surveillance reporting.

articles.headline: First P. knowlesi congenital malaria case in Bahrain with CMV co-infection
articles.title_original: Co-infection of Neonatal Plasmodium knowlesi Malaria With Congenital Cytomegalovirus: The First Reported Case From Bahrain.
articles.authors: Mohroofi AD, Ben Turkia H, Aljowder F, Althawadi D, Loni R
articles.details: Case report of 26-day-old Pakistani male neonate with congenital P. knowlesi malaria and cytomegalovirus co-infection, first documented in Bahrain. Presented with fever, jaundice, lethargy, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, and elevated inflammatory markers; blood smear confirmed P. knowlesi while rapid antigen test was negative. Treated with intravenous artesunate and ganciclovir followed by oral valganciclovir. Case emphasizes importance of blood smear microscopy even when rapid tests negative.

articles.headline: NETosis in severe malaria: exploring a new therapeutic target
articles.title_original: [Malaria: Could NETosis, neutrophils' defense mechanism involved in severe cases of the disease, be a new therapeutic target?].
articles.authors: Kociánová L, Larribau S, Veltz N
articles.details: Commentary examining NETosis (neutrophil extracellular trap formation) as a potential defense mechanism in severe malaria cases and discussing it as a possible therapeutic target for intervention.

articles.headline: Vietnamese P. vivax shows regional genetic polymorphism in MSP-1 domain
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphisms of merozoite surface protein-1 ICB 5-6 in Vietnamese Plasmodium vivax isolates.
articles.authors: Nguyễn TH, Nguyễn ĐTD, Lê HG, Võ TC, Trinh NTM, Cho M, Van Khanh C, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: Analysis of pvmsp-1 ICB 5-6 sequences from 117 Vietnamese P. vivax isolates collected 2018-2022 across four provinces. Study identified 34 distinct haplotypes from 139 sequences, with recombinant types predominant. Central Highlands provinces showed high genetic polymorphism while South-Central region had lower polymorphism. Diverse poly-glutamine variants identified, emphasizing need for continuous molecular surveillance of P. vivax genetic diversity.

articles.headline: Partial artemisinin resistance with A675V pfk13 mutation causes treatment failure
articles.title_original: Treatment Failure and Post-Artesunate Delayed Haemolysis in a Returned Traveller From Uganda With Partially Drug-Resistant Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Travis J, McCarthy K, Chapman P, Huang L, Tan A, Cheng Q, Barber BE
articles.details: Case of man in 40s from Uganda with P. falciparum hyperparasitaemia (~1.5 × 10^6 parasites/μL, 26%). Despite intravenous artesunate and artemether-lumefantrine, parasite clearance was delayed with recurrent parasitaemia 3 weeks post-treatment. Genotyping revealed A675V pfk13 mutation with reduced lumefantrine susceptibility and increased ring-stage survival, consistent with partial artemisinin resistance. Highlights treatment failure risk in hyperparasitaemia with drug resistance.

articles.headline: Gene drives tested against real-world malaria parasite diversity
articles.title_original: Gene drives tested against real-world malaria diversity.
articles.authors: Marchal I
articles.details: Study evaluating gene drive technology against natural genetic diversity of malaria parasites.

articles.headline: Deforestation and agricultural change drove endemic malaria in 19th Ontario
articles.title_original: Ecologies of Risk: Malaria and Settler Landscape Transformation in 19th-Century Ontario.
articles.authors: Cooke A, Brickley MB
articles.details: Historical retrospective study of 2702 probable malaria deaths in southern Ontario 1831-1900 using civil and cemetery records with monthly temperature and rainfall data. Children <5 accounted for >50% of deaths; agricultural workers disproportionately affected. Generalized additive modeling showed strong nonlinear association between rainfall and malaria mortality (p<0.001), not temperature. Findings indicate settler landscape transformation (deforestation, altered hydrology) created breeding ecologies for malaria.

articles.headline: CXCL10-high microglia as neuroimmune endotype in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: CXCL10(high) Microglia in Cerebral Malaria: Toward Translational Validation.
articles.authors: Rattanapitoon NK, Gordon CN, Charoenphon N, Rattanapitoon SK
articles.details: Commentary proposing CXCL10high TNFαhigh Ki67+ microglia as a candidate "neuroimmune endotype" driving CD8+ T cell recruitment during experimental cerebral malaria. Authors advocate conservative framing and call for validation steps including single-nucleus and spatial transcriptomics of human postmortem tissue, paired plasma/CSF biomarker correlation, and mechanistic assays. Caution against strong therapeutic inferences from experimental data alone without established human microglial signatures and clinical correlations.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine rollout in Cameroon generates mixed public sentiment online
articles.title_original: Assessing RTS, S malaria vaccine rollout perception in Cameroon: Sentiment analysis from X and facebook using hugging face.
articles.authors: Cynthia AN, Njuaka M, Koomson N, Fogap NZ
articles.details: Sentiment analysis of social media data from Twitter (X) and Facebook regarding RTS,S (Mosquirix®) malaria vaccine rollout in Cameroon using Hugging Face NLP. Analysis revealed sentiment distribution: 42% negative, 40% positive, 18% neutral. Results show nearly even split between skeptical and supportive viewpoints and emphasize need for targeted communication strategies addressing public concerns to foster vaccine confidence.

articles.headline: R21 vaccine protects against intradermal but not intravenous P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Malaria vaccine protection against intradermal or venous parasites: a randomized phase 2b human challenge trial.
articles.authors: Kapulu MC, Orenge F, Kimani D, Kibwana E, Kibet H, Mutahi M, Datoo MS, Bellamy D, Musembi J, Ngoto O, Rashid H, Akinyi S, Mwatasa MH, Nyamako L, Keter K, Gatheru R, Mutiso A, Musyoki J, Mwacharo J, Abebe Y, James ER, Billingsley PF, Ngetsa C, Mosobo M, Makale J, Tawa B, Wamae K, Ochola-Oyier LI, Lawrie A, Ramos-Lopez F, Roberts R, Richie TL, Sim BKL, Hoffman SL, Ewer KJ, Hill AVS, Hamaluba M, Bejon P
articles.details: Phase 2b randomized controlled trial in Kenya comparing R21/Matrix-M vaccine (n=38), ME-TRAP vaccine (n=24), and control (n=18) using controlled human malaria infection with sporozoites via intradermal (ID) or direct venous injection (DVI). Against ID: 0/12 R21 vs. 7/8 control met treatment criteria (p<0.0005). Against DVI: 5/5 R21 met criteria (p<0.0005). R21 highly protective against ID but not DVI sporozoites.

articles.headline: Thrombocytopenia drives artesunate overuse in non-endemic malaria treatment
articles.title_original: Appropriateness of malaria treatment according to World Health Organization criteria for severe malaria in a non-endemic country.
articles.authors: Pérez-Jacoiste Asín MA, Navarro Flores A, Alonso Crespo A, Pérez-Ayala A, Losada Galván I, de Dios B, Hernández-Jiménez P, Gil-Mosquera M, Jiménez-Morillas F, Lumbreras C, Lizasoain M, Herrero-Martínez JM
articles.details: Retrospective study of 64 patients (median age 39.5 years, 75% travelers) with symptomatic malaria at Madrid hospital 2015-2023. Of 15 severe malaria cases, treatment was adequate in 46/64 patients (71.8%). Three severe cases misclassified received oral treatment; 15 non-severe cases received artesunate. Main reason for artesunate overuse was thrombocytopenia (median platelet count 33,000/mm³), despite thrombocytopenia not being WHO criterion for severe malaria.

articles.headline: Forward genetic screen identifies gametocyte development genes
articles.title_original: Large-Scale Mutagenesis Screening for Genetic Determinants of Plasmodium falciparum Sexual Development.
articles.authors: Aringbangba O, Pires CV, Chawla J, Prakash P, Sollelis L, Marti M, Adams JH
articles.details: This methodology paper describes a large-scale mutagenesis screening approach using piggyBac insertion mutants to identify genetic factors controlling Plasmodium falciparum gametocyte (sexual stage) development. Genes are categorized as hypo-producers (reduced gametocyte production) or hyper-producers (increased production), enabling identification of genetic determinants essential for parasite transmission capability.

articles.headline: ELISA predicts transmission-blocking vaccine Pfs230D1 activity
articles.title_original: Epitope-specific competitive ELISA predicts malaria transmission-blocking vaccine Pfs230D1 activity measured in standard membrane feeding assay.
articles.authors: Meehan CA, Cowles MV, Morrison RD, Yi Y, Gu J, Hume JC, Peyton MP, Sagara I, Healy SA, Renn JP, Duffy PE
articles.details: An epitope-specific competitive ELISA (P230Compete) was developed to assess functional antibody responses to the transmission-blocking vaccine candidate Pfs230D1-EPA/AS01. Using 171 serum samples from phase I trial participants, EUF (total IgG) and IgG1F showed strong correlation with transmission-reducing activity (AUC 0.81 for both after dose 3; AUC 0.80 and 0.76 after dose 4). Combining both features improved predictive performance.

articles.headline: Lethal vs non-lethal Plasmodium yoelii strains reveal immune dysregulation
articles.title_original: Immune Modulation by Plasmodium yoelii: Insights From Lethal and Non-Lethal Strains.
articles.authors: Lim SYM
articles.details: This review of the P. yoelii model (17XL lethal and 17XNL non-lethal strains) demonstrates that severe malaria is driven not by parasite load but by aberrant immune responses. 17XL infections trigger excessive pro-inflammatory cytokine release and immune exhaustion, while 17XNL sustains controlled Th1/Th2 responses. Key pathways include MIF, TLR7 signaling, and checkpoint molecules (PD-1, LAG-3).

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine implementation barriers in Africa
articles.title_original: Challenges to malaria vaccine availability and successful implementation of malaria vaccine program in Africa.
articles.authors: Ojo RJ, Igbayilola YD, Gyebi GA
articles.details: This review identifies major obstacles to malaria vaccine availability and implementation across Africa: supply constraints limiting manufacturing capacity to meet demand, insufficient national health budgets, low public confidence due to misinformation and past mistrust of Western health initiatives, and operational barriers including cold-chain requirements, multiple-dose schedules, and shortage of skilled health workers. Recommendations include scaling manufacturing, sustained research, stable funding, and community engagement.

articles.headline: Malaria burden in military recruits from Niger
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria in young adults in Niger: A prospective study in a military setting.
articles.authors: Aminou YY, Hamidou B, Djedanem M, Ramatoulaye HL, Moustapha WA, Testa J, Jambou R
articles.details: A prospective cohort study monitored 2,840 military recruits (mean age 27 years, 91.5% male) across three centers in Niger during September–December 2023. Among 3,193 clinical consultations, 353 confirmed malaria cases were identified (11% prevalence), with 74.5% anemia, 44% hospitalization, and 55.8% temporary duty unfitness. Estimated annual workday loss reaches approximately 2,000 per person and 12 million nationally.

articles.headline: Trace element deficiencies link malaria severity to immune dysfunction
articles.title_original: Essential Trace Elements and Their Impact on Immune Response and Disease Severity in Malaria Infection: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Hanifian H, Nateghpour M, Naderi M
articles.details: This systematic review synthesizes evidence that malaria infection reduces serum concentrations of iron, zinc, and magnesium, while copper levels elevate acutely as an inflammatory marker. These disturbances correlate with dysregulated cytokine production (elevated TNF-α and IL-10), higher parasitemia, and worsened clinical outcomes. Although zinc supplementation improved micronutrient status, it showed limited impact on malaria incidence, suggesting integrated micronutrient-antimalarial approaches warrant investigation.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum detected in Italian travel case
articles.title_original: Recrudescence of Plasmodium falciparum malaria imported from Sub-Saharan Africa: a report of two cases and the first detection of artemisinin resistance in Italy.
articles.authors: L'Episcopia M, Poloni A, Corbellino M, Boccolini D, Gigantiello A, Covizzi A, Bernasconi D, Colombo V, Severini C, Antinori S
articles.details: Two Italian travelers with P. falciparum from sub-Saharan Africa showed treatment failure after standard artesunate (IV) and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine therapy. One patient harbored the Pfkelch13 R561H artemisinin-resistance marker. The second showed no Pfkelch13 mutations but likely experienced failure from low drug exposure via nasogastric administration or potential Kelch13-independent resistance mechanisms. Molecular analysis included Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, and PfCytB genes.

articles.headline: Do we have the political will to beat malaria?
articles.title_original: We have the means to beat malaria, do we have the will?
articles.authors: The Lancet Microbe
articles.details: No abstract provided; unable to assess findings from title alone.

articles.headline: Low-density P. falciparum and pfhrp2 deletions in elimination zones
articles.title_original: Molecular Detection of Plasmodium falciparum Infections Missed by Microscopy and HRP2-Based RDTs in the Dominican Republic.
articles.authors: Cabrera Federo C, Cuevas F, Gonçalves AM, Freitas B, Miguel I, Baptista V, Conte M, Santos-Pereira A, Aquino E, Disla M, Tabata A, Osório NS, Cruz M, Veiga MI
articles.details: Molecular re-examination of 1,095 Dominican Republic samples initially negative by both HRP2 rapid tests and microscopy identified low-density P. falciparum infections at 1.9% (18/969 samples successfully extracted and tested). Notably, 16.7% of PCR-positive samples carried pfhrp2 gene deletions, which evade HRP2-based RDT detection. These findings highlight the critical need for sensitive molecular diagnostics in low-transmission elimination settings.

articles.headline: Engineered nanoparticle vaccine elicits complete sterile protection in mice
articles.title_original: A Plasmodium-derived nanoparticle vaccine elicits sterile protection against malaria in mice.
articles.authors: Shi D, Ma R, Gupta R, Dickey TH, Patel PN, Salinas ND, Tang WK, Queen A, Singleton M, Delbe N, Conteh S, Lambert LE, Duffy PE, Tolia NH
articles.details: An engineered Plasmodium falciparum pyridoxal 5'-phosphate synthase nanoparticle presenting circumsporozoite protein (CSP) and P. vivax CelTOS antigen provided complete sterile protection against malaria after three doses in a mouse model. The vaccine elicited high titers of anti-CSP and anti-CelTOS antibodies. Cryo-EM determined the 2.95-Ångström resolution structure. The PLP synthase carrier lacks human orthologs, minimizing autoimmunity risk.

articles.headline: One-step artesunate faster, cheaper than two-step for severe malaria
articles.title_original: Single-step versus conventional injectable artesunate for severe malaria in children: an open label, non-inferiority randomized clinical trial, Democratic Republic of the Congo and United Republic of Tanzania.
articles.authors: Gesase S, Onyamboko M, Fanello C, Abdul O, Kayembe DK, Bakomba SB, Minja DT, Nzambiwishe BK, Ekombolo PE, Malabeja A, Mbwana JR, Deen J, Mtove G, Adhikari B, Mapondela M, Taya C, Mutinda B, Waithira N, Lusingu JP, von Seidlein L, Mukaka M, Dondorp AM, Peto TJ
articles.details: An open-label non-inferiority randomized trial in the DRC and Tanzania randomized 200 children (age 3 months–16 years) with severe malaria to one-step versus conventional two-step injectable artesunate. Administration time: 2 minutes 22 seconds (one-step) versus 3 minutes 41 seconds (two-step; p<0.0001); cost USD 0.53 versus USD 0.84 (p=0.0001). Parasite clearance half-lives were equivalent (2.1 versus 2.0 hours, p=0.173).

articles.headline: Chloroquine-resistant P. vivax at 7% prevalence in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium vivax malaria in Ethiopia: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Wondmagegn YM, Setegn A, Girmay G, Assefa M, Getnet B, Cherkos T, Abebe W, Dessie N, Derso A, Yiheyis Z, Adugna A, Misganaw T, Abere A, Tamir M, Belayneh M, Amare A, Ashagre A, Malede B, Amare GA
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 13 studies (2,190 participants) found chloroquine-resistant P. vivax at pooled prevalence of 7.32% (95% confidence interval 2.55–12.08) in Ethiopia. Resistance was higher during 2003–2009 (10.70%), in the Oromia region (9.58%), and with 42-day follow-up (17.91%). Although prevalence remains moderate, the authors emphasize clinical significance and the urgent need for ongoing surveillance and alternative therapeutic strategies.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein vaccine strategies in mice
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein and vaccine strategies in murine models: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Sampaio MP, Cerilo-Filho M, Almeida MNG, Matos MAT, Silva AA, Fernando DJV, Bras RP, Baptista ARS, de Castro TX, Machado RLD
articles.details: Scoping review examining Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein and vaccine strategies in murine models. Specific quantitative findings and conclusions are not available from the title alone and require review of the full article.

articles.headline: Monoclonal antibodies show promise for malaria prevention
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of Monoclonal Antibodies in Malaria Protection and Prevention of Transmission: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Verma K, Singhal R, Anvikar A, Bharti PK, Nitika N
articles.details: Systematic review of 7 Phase I-II trials (n=776) on four antimalarial mAbs. CIS43LS achieved 75-88% efficacy against P. falciparum at ≥10 mg/kg intravenously with half-life 56-80 days; L9LS showed 66-77% efficacy via subcutaneous delivery and 46.1-day half-life; TB31F demonstrated >80% transmission-reducing activity sustained 160 days. All exhibited favorable safety profiles with only grade 1-2 adverse events. Authors conclude mAbs represent scalable tools for malaria prevention and transmission reduction.

articles.headline: Falcilysin: emerging drug target for antimalarial development
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum falcilysin as an emerging potential drug target for antimalarial drug discovery.
articles.authors: Dalal A, Malik J, Singh P, Rathi B
articles.details: Review article on falcilysin, a conserved zinc metalloprotease essential for P. falciparum survival with roles in hemoglobin degradation and apicoplast peptide processing. Multiple small-molecule classes demonstrate potent inhibition in biochemical and cellular models. Key challenges include dual-organelle localization, selectivity over human homologs, and translating to clinical candidates. Authors recommend expanded chemical diversity, covalent and PROTAC modalities, and AI-driven modeling for improved drug design.

articles.headline: Gene-drive mosquitoes suppress wild malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Gene-drive-capable mosquitoes suppress patient-derived malaria in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Habtewold T, Lwetoijera DW, Hoermann A, Mashauri R, Matwewe F, Mwanga R, Kweyamba P, Maganga G, Magani BP, Mtama R, Mahonje MA, Tambwe MM, Tarimo F, Chennuri PR, Cai JA, Del Corsano G, Capriotti P, Sasse P, Moore J, Hudson D, Manjurano A, Tarimo B, Vlachou D, Moore S, Windbichler N, Christophides GK
articles.details: Study in Tanzania reporting transgenic Anopheles gambiae equipped with non-autonomous gene drive capabilities that robustly suppressed genetically diverse P. falciparum isolates from naturally infected children. Genetic modifications were efficiently inherited by progeny when supplemented with Cas9 from another locally engineered strain. Work demonstrates gene drive technology's progress toward application for malaria eradication through targeted dissemination of beneficial genetic traits in wild populations.

articles.headline: DARC genetic diversity and emerging P. vivax in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Genetic dynamics of the Duffy antigen receptor for chemokines gene and Plasmodium vivax circulation within sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Adeloye F, Lambe KO, Oboh JA, Abdulsalam H, Enyinnnaya C, Nwuba R, Ogundahunsi O, D'Alessandro U, Amambua-Ngwa A, Meremikwu MM, Hughes GL, Heinz E, Oboh MA
articles.details: Population genetic analysis of 223 DARC genes from five sub-Saharan African countries examined the relationship between DARC genetic diversity and P. vivax circulation. South Africa showed greatest haplotype and nucleotide diversity; Cameroon, Nigeria, and Ethiopia clustered together. Evidence of selection pressure was detected, particularly correlating with recent P. vivax emergence among Duffy-null individuals. Authors conclude genetic diversity was minimal across most of sub-Saharan Africa but notable in South Africa, with evidence of selection likely driven by emerging P. vivax infections.

articles.headline: Plasmodium cynomolgi DBP binding varies by haplotype
articles.title_original: Characterisation of erythrocyte binding activity of Plasmodium cynomolgi duffy binding protein haplotypes from Macaca fascicularis in Peninsular Malaysia.
articles.authors: Latif ENM, Adlan ASA, Lau YL, Cheong FW
articles.details: Laboratory study compared erythrocyte binding activity of Plasmodium cynomolgi Duffy binding proteins (PcDBP1 and PcDBP2) across four haplotypes. Recent PcDBP1(II) group 1 isolate showed significantly higher binding to macaque erythrocytes (P<0.05) than old reference isolate, suggesting evolution to enhance parasite invasion. Both DBPs showed no binding with human erythrocytes or reticulocytes. Authors conclude DBP variations alone don't explain binding differences, suggesting additional parasite-host interactions are required.

articles.headline: PCR superior to RDT and microscopy for malaria detection
articles.title_original: Performance comparison of rapid diagnostic test, microscopy, and polymerase chain reaction for Malaria detection in high-transmission area, Keerom Regency, Papua Province, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Rohkmad MF, Sandy S, Widiyanti M, Adiningsih S, Kesuma AP, Maladan Y, Anggraeni YM, Ramadhani T, Ipa M, Hidajat MC, Kawulur H, Kurniawan CM, Kopo AD, Dharmayanti NI, Garjito TA, Oktavian A, Hendarwan H
articles.details: Comparative diagnostic study of 234 patients with suspected uncomplicated malaria in high-transmission Keerom Regency, Papua, Indonesia. PCR detected 42.3% of cases versus 26.5% for microscopy and 22.6% for rapid diagnostic tests, revealing significant discrepancies particularly in low-parasitemia situations. Authors underscore need for enhanced diagnostic strategies to improve case detection and support elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Climate warming increases avian malaria parasite prevalence
articles.title_original: Hidden effects of avian malaria under climate warming.
articles.authors: Braga ÉM, Vieira LMC
articles.details: Long-term study by Theodosopoulos et al. on wild bird population in temperate Northern Europe region revealed significant increase in avian malaria parasite prevalence under climate-warming scenario. Disruption of temperature-humidity balance poses threat to global ecosystems and affects spread of infectious diseases.

articles.headline: NAT assay detects asymptomatic Plasmodium in blood donors
articles.title_original: Improving blood safety: NAT-based detection of Plasmodium spp. in blood donors in endemic areas of Brazil.
articles.authors: Gomes de Almeida AC, Shuan Laco AC, Rodrigues MG, Godinho de Siqueira VG, de Moura Brito FB, Gato CM, Mota Cordeiro JS, Hipólito Carneiro JM, Lopes Albuquerque SR, Chaves YO, de Fátima Ferreira da Cruz M, Baptista PA, Addas-Carvalho M, Tarragô AM, de Melo GC
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 67,114 blood donors at Brazilian Amazon center screened with NAT PLUS assay detected Plasmodium spp. in 0.01% (8/67,114), all asymptomatic at screening. Most positive cases (6/8) occurred during low-transmission period; one donor subsequently developed symptomatic P. falciparum confirmed by microscopy. Authors conclude molecular testing identifies at-risk donors, improving transfusion safety in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Hypofuran B analogs show antimalarial activity with low toxicity
articles.title_original: Synthesis and In Vitro Activity of Hypofuran B and Analogs Against Plasmodium Falciparum and Trypanosoma Cruzi.
articles.authors: Franco CA, Varejão JOS, Ceravolo IP, Machado VM, Krettli AU, de Melo Resende D, Murta SMF, Martins FT, Pilau EJ, Montes VR, Kohlhoff M, Varejão EVV
articles.details: Synthesis and evaluation of hypofuran B and analogs against P. falciparum and T. cruzi via crossed aldol condensation, yielding 20-83%. Most active compounds showed IC50 values of 5.35-10.35 µg/mL; most promising analog demonstrated CC50 >400 µg/mL indicating lower cytotoxicity than chloroquine. In silico predictions supported favorable drug-like profiles. Authors identify hypofuran B and drynaran derivatives as promising antimalarial leads.

articles.headline: Malaria PCR screening prevents donor-derived malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Malaria Screening and Treatment in Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Donors and Sickle Cell Disease Candidates/Recipients: A Case Series Using Malaria Polymerase Chain Reaction Testing and a Literature Review.
articles.authors: Czech MM, Das S, Limerick E, Fitzhugh C, Hsieh M, Cuellar-Rodriguez J
articles.details: Single-center retrospective review of malaria screening in 57 HCT donors and 52 sickle cell disease recipients. Three asymptomatic donors identified with malaria; two detected pre-donation via smears and PCR, one identified retrospectively after transmitting to recipient. Two recipients developed peri-HCT malaria; all cases detected before collection proceeded successfully after treatment. Authors conclude malaria PCR offers superior diagnostic sensitivity and may prevent unnecessary donor deferrals.

articles.headline: Bone marrow microscopy diagnoses occult malaria with cytopenias
articles.title_original: Out of Sight, Should not be out of mind: A perplexing case of Malaria.
articles.authors: Saxena S, Das R, Khurana S, Mirza SA, Joshi KD, Kaur H
articles.details: Case report of 30-year-old male with fever, chills, vomiting, anemia, and progressive splenomegaly where peripheral blood smears and rapid diagnostic tests were negative. Bone marrow examination revealed rare Plasmodium spp., confirmed by PCR. Patient recovered completely following treatment with Artemether-Lumefantrine and Primaquine. Authors emphasize value of bone marrow examination in diagnosing malaria with unexplained cytopenias.

articles.headline: IPTp-SP may reduce newborn vulnerability beyond malaria protection
articles.title_original: Beyond malaria: can intermittent preventive treatment with sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine reduce the number of small vulnerable newborns globally?
articles.authors: Unger HW, Ataide R, Roh ME, Rahman A, Price RN, van Eijk AM, Dorsey G, Ter Kuile FO, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: This perspective discusses intermittent preventive treatment with sulphadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP), originally designed to reduce malaria in pregnancy but showing potential effects on low birthweight independent of malaria infection. Proposed mechanisms include antimicrobial activity against genitourinary infections, effects on gut physiology, and anti-inflammatory actions. The article calls for mechanistic studies and placebo-controlled trials in non-malarious settings to confirm protective efficacy.

articles.headline: Corrigendum to R21/MM vaccine serology study
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Naturally acquired immune responses to alpha-gal in malaria endemic settings and pre-clinical efficacy testing with R21/MM" [Vaccine 68 (2025) 127897].
articles.authors: Mukhopadhyay ES, Bellamy DG, Tinto H, Hamaluba M, Truby A, Salman AM, Hill AVS
articles.details: Corrigendum (correction) to previously published work on naturally acquired immune responses to alpha-gal and R21/MM malaria vaccine efficacy testing in malaria endemic settings.

articles.headline: Eastern Balkan mosquitoes feed on diverse hosts, transmit avian malaria
articles.title_original: Feeding Preferences of Mosquitoes (Culicidae) from the Eastern Balkans and Their Role in Transmission of Avian Malaria.
articles.authors: Bobeva A, Marinov MP, Klayn S, Ilieva M, Dimitrov D, Zehtindjiev P
articles.details: Study of 75 engorged mosquitoes collected 2017-2019 in Eastern Balkans identified blood meal origins: 58.7% avian (25 bird species), 40.0% mammalian (13 species), 1.3% reptilian. Avian haemosporidian parasites detected in 15.8% of samples (11 abdominal, 4 head-thorax), with Plasmodium relictum (SGS1) most prevalent in abdomens. Results confirm vector competence of multiple mosquito species for avian malaria parasites.

articles.headline: Deforestation drives malaria transmission in Amazon; vectors adapt to habitat loss
articles.title_original: Deforestation, Mosquito Ecology, and Malaria Elimination in the Amazon Region.
articles.authors: Grillet ME, Sallum MAM, Conn JE
articles.details: Review examining how Amazon deforestation affects anopheline vector ecology and malaria transmission. Discusses forest degradation's impact on mosquito population dynamics, abundance, human-vector contact rates, and the emerging importance of zoonotic malaria. Emphasizes need to understand mosquito adaptation to anthropogenic pressures for sustainable elimination strategies.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine reduces malaria burden 30-58% in real-world setting
articles.title_original: Effectiveness of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine in a real-world setting over 1 year of follow-up after the three-dose primary schedule: an interim analysis of a phase 4 study in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi.
articles.authors: Ndeketa L, Haine V, Debois M, Asante KP, Agyapong PD, Kaali S, Devadiga R, Harrison SBE, Boahen O, French N, Kayan K, Ogutu B, Adeniji E, Kariuki S, Owusu-Agyei S, Olewe F, Jere TM, Maleta K, Mategula D, Mzanga P, Phiri VK, Ansah PO, Orimbo J, Ansah NA, Orsini M, Ong'echa JM, Oduro AR, Sifuna PM, Azongo DK, Otieno W, Bangre O, Kaburise MB, Ababio LO, Oyieko JN, Sing'oei V, Amoit SK, Nyangulu W, Schuerman L, Awuni D, Ochieng BO, Onyango I, Odera-Ojwang P, Oguk EA, Mendoza YG, Cherop RY, Okoth GO, Cravcenco C, Chipatala R, Roman F, Oneko M, Savic M
articles.details: Phase 4 effectiveness study of 45,000 children <18 months (39,463 analyzed) in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi with 1-year follow-up after three-dose RTS,S/AS01E vaccination. Vaccinated children vs unvaccinated showed: any malaria IRR 0.70 (95% CI 0.67-0.73), severe malaria IRR 0.42 (0.30-0.60), malaria-related hospitalizations IRR 0.64 (0.56-0.72), all-cause hospitalizations IRR 0.79 (0.74-0.84), and anemia OR 0.81 (0.73-0.90). Findings support RTS,S as effective malaria control measure (all-cause mortality IRR 0.83 not significant).

articles.headline: Serology could enhance India's malaria elimination efforts
articles.title_original: India should consider sero-epidemiology as an additional tool to bolster malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Rahi M, Sharma R, Thiruvengadam K, Perumalsamy N
articles.details: Review advocating for integrating malaria serology into India's elimination strategy. Context: India accounts for >50% of Southeast Asia's malaria burden (P. falciparum and P. vivax); cases increased 58% from 2021 (161,753) to 2024 (255,500).
