Serology enables detection of past exposure, assessment in high-risk groups, low-density infection detection, and transmission measurement. Several endemic countries successfully integrated serology alongside RDTs and microscopy.

articles.headline: Traditional plant extract protects bone in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: Osteoprotective Effects and In Silico Profiling of Parinari curatellifolia Planch. Ex Benth. (Chrysobalanaceae) Extract on Acute Plasmodium berghei-Compromised Rat Bone.
articles.authors: Jouonzo J, Kamkumo RG, Ngueguim FT, Efange NM, Tsila SS, Ambamba BDA, Ngondi JL, Ayong L, Lenta BN, Sewald N, Dimo T
articles.details: Study of P. curatellifolia aqueous extract in Plasmodium berghei ANKA-infected rats, with in vitro testing on P. falciparum 3D7 and Dd2. In vivo, parasitemia reduced significantly (p < 0.001), hematological parameters improved, and bone protection achieved via increased osteocalcin, TGF-β1, ALP activity, and mineralization (p < 0.001) while reversing malaria-induced inflammation. LC-MS identified 12 compounds with strong docking potential for targeted antimalarial development.

articles.headline: Delhi hospital study: 2% malaria prevalence, 45% co-infected
articles.title_original: Malaria epidemiology, clinical characteristics, and concurrent infections in Delhi, India: A hospital-based study.
articles.authors: Hawadak J, Goel A, Matlani M, Haider M, Kojom Foko LP, Singh V
articles.details: Hospital-based longitudinal study (July 2022–Nov 2023) of 4,259 febrile patients found 87 (2.04%) with P. vivax or P. falciparum. Clinical presentations dominated by chills (80.46%), hepatosplenomegaly (64.37%), myalgia (56.32%), and jaundice (51.72%); severe anemia was most common severe form. Nearly 45% had concurrent infections (dengue, chikungunya, or Salmonella typhus). Risk factors: July–September (aOR 2.18, p<0.05) and male gender (aOR 1.42, p<0.05).

articles.headline: Ghana isolates show 2.2% artemisinin resistance markers, 29.7% coronin P76S
articles.title_original: Molecular Markers of Antimalarial Drug Resistance in Plasmodium falciparum From Kumasi, Ghana, 2023.
articles.authors: Kegya AD, Oppong E, Darko E, Boateng KA, Phillips RO, Heinemann M, Ramharter M, Igiraneza C, Ndoli JM, Mockenhaupt FP, van Loon W
articles.details: Molecular analysis of 200 P. falciparum isolates from Kumasi, Ghana (2023) genotyped for resistance markers. Pfk13 non-synonymous mutations in 2.2% including artemisinin-resistance candidate P527H; pfcoronin P76S in 29.7%, previously associated with lumefantrine failure; NFD pfmdr1 pattern (N86–184F–D1246) predominant; pfaat1 S258L common at 64.7%. No cross-marker associations or link to day-3 parasite positivity despite high ACT efficacy, supporting continued monitoring for resistance emergence.

articles.headline: Cannabis compounds show mild-to-high in vitro antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Cannabis-Derived Compounds Against Plasmodium sp.: A Systematic Review of Preclinical Studies.
articles.authors: de Mendonça Lima T, de Sena LWP, de Sousa ACC, de Freitas GRM, Rotta I, Visacri MB
articles.details: Systematic review of 8 preclinical studies (2001–2022) on cannabis-derived compounds against Plasmodium. In vitro IC50 values ranged 0.16–4.1 μg/mL indicating mild-to-high antimalarial activity. All in vivo studies reported positive findings including moderate antimalarial activity and disease tolerance. Toxicity profile largely unstudied; most studies had insufficient methodological detail. Authors conclude future work needs comprehensive design details and toxicity validation before clinical development.

articles.headline: 4-Nitrobenzoate potentiates atovaquone by blocking ubiquinone synthesis
articles.title_original: 4-nitrobenzoate inhibits 4-hydroxybenzoate polyprenyltransferase in malaria parasites and enhances atovaquone efficacy.
articles.authors: Verdaguer IB, Santos MF, Filho MM, Castro GO, Hernández A, Peres MA, Katzin AM, Crispim M
articles.details: Study demonstrating 4-nitrobenzoate (4-NB) inhibits Pf4-HPT (COQ2), blocking ubiquinone biosynthesis in P. falciparum. In vitro, 4-NB inhibited UQ biosynthesis in S. cerevisiae expressing PfCOQ2. In vivo, 4-NB enhanced atovaquone efficacy in P. berghei-infected mice while preserving proguanil synergy. Structure-activity analysis defined chemical features for potentiation, validating PfCOQ2 as druggable target for rational antimalarial combination therapy.

articles.headline: GEP1 deletion blocks P. falciparum gametogenesis, potential transmission blocker
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum gametogenesis essential protein 1 (GEP1) is a transmission-blocking target.
articles.authors: Huppertz F, Caturelli MS, Lehmann LS, Kurth F, Maier AG, Matuschewski K
articles.details: CRISPR-Cas9 study showing PfGEP1-deficient P. falciparum lines completely failed male and female gametogenesis despite xanthurenic acid stimulation or temperature drop, with defect persisting after phosphodiesterase inhibitor treatment. Field analysis of 49 cases identified GEP1 SNPs V241L (12%) and S263P (20%). Findings confirm GEP1's essential role in gamete activation and support it as transmission-blocking drug target for sustained malaria control.

articles.headline: New Novyella Plasmodium species identified in Brazilian birds
articles.title_original: A New Species of Plasmodium of the Subgenus Novyella Infecting White-Shouldered Fire-Eyes (Pyriglena leucoptera) (Aves: Thamnophilidae) in Brazil.
articles.authors: Alves LGM, Pereira PHO, Batista VL, Lopes LE, Braga ÉM
articles.details: Describes morphological and molecular characterization of a new Plasmodium (Novyella) species infecting white-shouldered fire-eyes in Brazil, genetically related to P. homopolare with 4.18% divergence and distinguished by merozoite arrangement differences.

articles.headline: Volume electron microscopy reveals ookinete development stages
articles.title_original: Cellular Hallmarks From Volume Electron Microscopy Reveal Developmental Progression of Plasmodium Ookinetes.
articles.authors: Darif N, Rheinnecker M, Hildenbrand K, Chookajorn T, Dorner LP, Hériché JK, Henriksson S, Funaya C, Hentzschel F, Sandblad L, Billker O, Schwab Y, Frischknecht F
articles.details: Uses volume electron microscopy to analyze morphological changes during Plasmodium ookinete development from zygotes, revealing interactions between mitochondria and nucleus, microtubule arrangements, microneme elongation, and apicoplast positioning.

articles.headline: PfSPZ vaccine safe but ineffective in Gabonese children
articles.title_original: Safety, tolerability, and protective efficacy of a radiation-attenuated, whole sporozoite malaria vaccine in children in Gabon: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 2 trial.
articles.authors: Agnandji ST, Bok J, Alabi A, Kabwende AL, Mbouna A, Bie J, Moukiti E, Lalremruata A, Esen M, Kreidenweiss A, Kc N, Sim BKL, Richie TL, Preston Church LW, McCall MBB, Hoffman SL, Kremsner PG, Mordmüller B
articles.details: Randomized, double-blind, phase 2 trial of radiation-attenuated P. falciparum sporozoite vaccine in 134 children (1-12 years) in Gabon. Vaccine efficacy against P. falciparum infection was 9% (95% CI -75 to 53; p=0.78), with 19% of vaccinees becoming P. falciparum-positive at 2-26 weeks versus 23% in placebo. Vaccine was well tolerated with no treatment-related deaths.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemiology in Meta, eastern Colombia, 2017-2021
articles.title_original: Malaria in the department of Meta, eastern Colombia: 2017-2021.
articles.authors: Sánchez Lerma L, Rojas Gulloso A, Pavas Escobar NC, Bernal Otalora JC, Mahecha Bohórquez N, Angulo Ceballos O
articles.details: Epidemiological study of malaria cases in Meta department, Colombia (2016-2021), with highest transmission in Puerto Gaitán, Villavicencio, and Mapiripán. Significant relationship found between age category and malaria acquisition.

articles.headline: South-South collaboration for malaria research and elimination
articles.title_original: Can India-Africa synergies accelerate malaria elimination? A perspective on South-South Collaboration.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Gupta H
articles.details: Perspective on how South-South partnerships among low- and middle-income countries can advance malaria research and control through innovation, capacity building, diagnostic improvements, and surveillance enhancement. Discusses barriers (limited funding, fragmented systems) and strategies (regional investment, regulatory harmonization).

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine less effective than alternatives in Uganda trial
articles.title_original: Efficacies of artemether-lumefantrine, artesunate-amodiaquine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, and artesunate-pyronaridine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in children aged 6 months to 10 years in Uganda: a randomised, open-label, phase 4 clinical trial.
articles.authors: Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI, Ebong C, Asua V, Kiggundu M, Orena S, Okitwi M, Tukwasibwe S, Agaba B, Kyabayinze D, Opigo J, Rutazana D, Binagwa B, Mugwanya E, Babirye S, Sebikaari G, Condo PM, Appiah G, Nsobya SL, Conrad MD, Rosenthal PJ, Moriarty LF, Yeka A
articles.details: Randomized phase 4 trial of 4 artemisinin-based combination therapies in 808 children (6 months-10 years) with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria at 3 Uganda sites. Uncorrected ACPR: artemether-lumefantrine 51.8%, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine 98.0%, artesunate-amodiaquine 99.0%, artesunate-pyronaridine 73.7%. PCR-corrected 28-day efficacies: artemether-lumefantrine 81.5-97.0%, artesunate-amodiaquine 100%, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine 100%, artesunate-pyronaridine 92.4%. PfK13 mutations (Cys469Tyr, Ala675Val) associated with prolonged parasite clearance half-lives (4.2-4.9h).

articles.headline: Diversifying antimalarial treatment in Uganda
articles.title_original: Diversifying antimalarial treatment for uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Uganda.
articles.authors: Dondorp AM
articles.details: Article on antimalarial treatment diversification strategies in Uganda for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria.

articles.headline: Nuclear medicine imaging advances for severe malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Mini-review and situation report on the role and usefulness of nuclear medicine imaging for malaria.
articles.authors: Duvenhage J, Zeevaart JR, Sathekge MM, Ebenhan T
articles.journal: Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging
articles.details: Mini-review of clinical nuclear imaging techniques and radiopharmaceuticals in malaria diagnosis, particularly for severe malaria which causes multisystem complications and long-term neurological sequelae. Highlights lack of malaria-specific radiopharmaceuticals and discusses implementation challenges and potential improvements.

articles.headline: High P. falciparum prevalence in Nigeria asymptomatic reservoir
articles.title_original: A cross-sectional survey of malaria in asymptomatic and symptomatic individuals in an endemic area of Northcentral Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akoma ON, Okoh FN, Amoke OC
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 328 individuals in Lokoja, Nigeria during high transmission seasons (2018-2021). 12.4% asymptomatic and 85.7% symptomatic individuals were P. falciparum-positive. Overall P. falciparum prevalence 40.5% with hyperparasitemia. No significant difference in malaria distribution between asymptomatic and symptomatic groups (p>0.05).

articles.headline: Global malaria burden increasing despite control efforts through 2030
articles.title_original: The past, present and future of global malaria and neglected tropical diseases: a disease burden assessment from 1990 to 2030.
articles.authors: Li D, Shi Y, Wang R, Hong X, Shi T, Zhu S
articles.details: Global burden assessment of malaria and neglected tropical diseases using GBD Study 2019 data (1990-2030). Western Sub-Saharan Africa had 50% of global malaria and NTD cases in 2019. Predictions show slow upward trend in age-standardized mortality rate from 2020-2030. Children <5 years had highest disease burden with dengue as leading NTD.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance rising in Chad over decade
articles.title_original: Trends in Plasmodium falciparum resistance markers to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine and amodiaquine over ten years of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Moissala Health District, Chad.
articles.authors: Grandesso F, Diar MSI, Kouassi F, Sayyad-Hilario J, Ouedraogo P, Djimde AA, Singh S, Dama S
articles.details: Molecular surveillance study in Moissala, Chad (2014-2023) of resistance markers in 136, 256, and 219 P. falciparum samples. Triple dhfr mutants declined from 100% (2014) to 83.9% (2023, p<0.001). Quadruple mutants (triple dhfr + dhps A437G) increased from 28.0% to 47.9% (p<0.001). Quintuple mutants remained low (2.8-7.6%). SP and AQ remain clinically effective, but rising quadruple mutants indicate partial resistance emergence.

articles.headline: VAR2CSA antibodies protect against placental malaria outcomes
articles.title_original: VAR2CSA-specific IgG and IgM antibodies are markers of exposure and protection against adverse malaria pregnancy outcomes.
articles.authors: Onwuka AM, Aitken EH, Hasang W, Madanitsa M, Mwapasa V, Phiri K, Ter Kuile FO, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: Study of pregnant Malawian women (16-28 weeks gestation) investigating VAR2CSA-specific IgG and IgM antibodies against P. falciparum PfEMP1. Higher IgG at delivery in previously uninfected women associated with lower risk of low birth weight (aOR 0.43; p=0.04). Higher IgM at enrollment in infected women associated with 23% decrease in maternal anemia at delivery (aOR 0.77; p=0.04). VAR2CSA antibodies serve as markers of infection and protection.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria prevalence high in Nigeria; children most affected
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated risk factors of asymptomatic malaria in Nigeria: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mukhtar A, Ismail M, Usman A, Odetokun IA, Ibrahim MA, Salman AA, Bawa-Sani HG, Nass SS, Goni BW, Shehu MN, Nuhu A, Zhang X, Isah MB
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 studies estimating asymptomatic malaria prevalence in Nigeria. Pooled prevalence 33% (95% CI 26–41), ranging from 25% in general population to 52% in children. Risk factors: 19% related to education level, 36% by sex, 39% associated with ITN usage. Asymptomatic infections sustain transmission; complementary control strategies needed.

articles.headline: OpIE2-DsRed marker disrupts female blood-feeding in malaria vector
articles.title_original: An OpIE2-DsRed marker disrupts female blood-feeding and shortens lifespan in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Sarig A, Sar-Shalom E, Kolley ESM, Yonah ES, Lamdan LB, Lewin AR, Partosh T, Pines G, Bohbot JD, Papathanos PA
articles.details: Study of transgenic Anopheles gambiae carrying OpIE2-DsRed fluorescent marker cassette. Females unable to blood-feed and sterile with distally curved proboscis; males showed reduced lifespan. OpIE2b promoter variant drove expression without phenotypic impairment. Minor differences in promoter architecture produce major unexpected effects; OpIE2b provides robust, phenotypically neutral marker for mosquito research.

articles.headline: Livelihoods drive malaria exposure in Uganda; gendered risks emerge
articles.title_original: Livelihoods as a key social determinant of malaria: Qualitative evidence from Uganda.
articles.authors: Deane K, Atusingwize E, Musoke D
articles.details: Qualitative study in Uganda (14 key informant interviews, 10 focus groups, 11 household interviews) exploring social determinants of malaria. Identified livelihoods—crop production, livestock, construction, dusk/night activities—and gendered practices as key drivers. Mechanisms included increased mosquito exposure at dusk/night, breeding site creation, and gendered care responsibilities. Recommends integrating malaria prevention into socioeconomic development strategies.

articles.headline: Mathematical model explores malaria-COVID-19 coinfection dynamics and control
articles.title_original: Optimal control analysis of a mathematical model of malaria and COVID-19 co-infection dynamics.
articles.authors: Diabaté AB, Sangaré B, Koutou O
articles.details: Deterministic mathematical model analyzing malaria and COVID-19 co-infection dynamics. Calculated basic reproduction numbers and studied steady-state stability; both single and co-infection models exhibit backward bifurcation under certain conditions. Applied Pontryagin's maximum principle to determine optimal control strategies. Numerical simulations support theoretical findings on disease extinction, competitive exclusion, and coexistence.

articles.headline: Liver enzymes correlate with parasitemia; ABO blood type uninvolved
articles.title_original: The Association between Parasitemia and Liver Enzyme Alterations in Malaria Patients: ABO Blood Group as A Non-Contributory Factor.
articles.authors: Aminizadeh S, Shokri E, Mohebali M, Hanifian H, Zafari S, Edalati M, Bahadory S, Hosseinifard H, Nateghpoor M
articles.details: Case-control study of 50 malaria patients and 50 controls in Tehran (May 2022–October 2024). Malaria patients showed significantly higher AST, ALT, and ALP compared to controls (p=0.001). No significant association between ABO blood group and liver enzyme levels (p>0.05). Liver enzyme levels correlated significantly with parasitemia (p<0.001), with higher parasite loads linked to greater hepatic dysfunction.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention feasible in nomadic Turkana; delivery challenges identified
articles.title_original: A Baseline Mixed-Methods Study to Inform Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention Delivery in a Semi-Nomadic Population in Turkana, Kenya.
articles.authors: Kostandova N, Kafu C, Chepkwony TJ, Ambani G, Abel L, Rotich B, Kipkoech Kirui J, Kimachas E, Robie E, Lowoi K, Ekai D, Lokoel G, Orinda O, Van Hulle S, Mgeni R, O'Meara WP, Wesolowski A, Genberg B, Menya D
articles.details: Baseline mixed-methods study in Turkana Central, Kenya (198 households) preceding first SMC implementation. P. falciparum prevalence 21% by RDT and 21% by PCR; highest 73% in some villages. Identified implementation challenges: physical access (distance, pastoralists, street children), stigma (disabilities, alcohol use), acceptability gaps (traditionalists, highly educated). SMC perceived positively but requires tailored delivery combining centralized and door-to-door approaches.

articles.headline: Reliable vaccination records needed to evaluate new malaria vaccines in Africa
articles.title_original: Need for Real-World Evaluation of Malaria Vaccines and Reliable Vaccination Records in Africa.
articles.authors: Ko YK, Alfvén T, Okomo GO, Kagaya W, Shioda K, Kaneko A, Gitaka J
articles.details: Commentary on evaluating real-world effectiveness of approved malaria vaccines RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M in African countries. Recommends observational study designs (cohort studies with target trial emulation, test-negative design) for estimating vaccine effectiveness in areas not represented in clinical trials. Key barrier: lack of accurate, individual-level vaccination data. Strengthening electronic immunization registries essential for robust vaccine monitoring.

articles.headline: Plant-derived anti-CSP antibodies retain functionality against P. falciparum sporozoites
articles.title_original: Plant-produced monoclonal antibodies recognize the native circumsporozoite protein on Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites.
articles.authors: Thiganta K, Rattanapisit K, Suwanchaikasem P, Roobsoong W, Bulaon CJI, Phoolcharoen W
articles.details: Study demonstrating plant-based production of monoclonal antibodies (MS-1797) targeting the conserved NANP repeat of circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP) in glycoengineered Nicotiana benthamiana. Two variants produced: MS-01 and MS-02, yielding up to 457.3 µg/g and 415.7 µg/g fresh weight. Plant-derived mAbs achieved >90% purity and retained antigen specificity, binding native PfCSP on sporozoites. Establishes feasibility for producing functional anti-malarial mAbs in plants.

articles.headline: School-age children, elderly females highest-risk for malaria in Ho Municipality, Ghana
articles.title_original: Malaria test positivity, Plasmodium species distribution, and risk factors in Ho Municipality, Ghana: a retrospective analysis of seasonal and demographic trends (2020-2022).
articles.authors: Squire DS, Afuah GM, Dika ND, Dogbey P, Bohli JK, Logosu PE, Tanson PA, Avorkliya R, Donaldy W, Ablordey K, Kwadzokpui PK
articles.details: Retrospective cross-sectional analysis of 27,171 malaria tests from 3 healthcare facilities in Ho Municipality, Ghana (2020–2022). Overall test positivity rate 8.8%, declining from 9.9% (2020) to 7.2% (2022). School-age children (5–12 years) had highest TPR at 18.0%; fourfold facility disparity (21.0% vs 5.2%). P. falciparum dominant (79.9%); peak transmission August (13.9%). School-age children and adolescents showed 3.69× and 3.57× higher parasite densities than adults; elderly females elevated risk (aPR=2.15).

articles.headline: Young primigravida at highest malaria risk in pregnancy; housing type matters
articles.title_original: Maternal and household risk factors for malaria in pregnancy and low birthweight: a prospective cohort study from Uganda.
articles.authors: Aguti M, Kizza J, Kakuru A, Nakalembe M, Nankabirwa JI, Gaw SL, Opira B, Ssemukuye T, Ozarslan N, Ranjit A, Dela Cruz E, Clark TD, Roh ME, Jagannathan P, Rosenthal PJ, Adrama H, Olwoch P, Mayende J, Odongo B, John A, Kamya M, Dorsey G
articles.details: Prospective cohort from randomized controlled trial of IPTp in Busia District, Uganda (December 2020–July 2024), 2,728 pregnant women. Parasitemia at enrolment 38.1%, after IPTp initiation 6.5%; high-grade placental malaria 6.4%; low birth weight 6.8% of live births. Young women (16–21 years, RR=1.62), primigravida (RR=1.86), and traditional housing (RR=1.17) associated with parasitemia at enrolment; associations persisted after IPTp. Primigravida strongly associated with high-grade placental malaria (RR=4.20) and low birth weight (RR=2.14).

articles.headline: Temperature and terrain predict malaria hotspots in Ethiopian highland zone
articles.title_original: Spatial analysis of malaria incidence and environmental determinants in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Abdo SS
articles.details: Geostatistical analysis of malaria incidence in Hadiya Zone, Ethiopia (2022–2023). Moran's I (0.558 in 2022, 0.483 in 2023; p<0.01) and Geary's C (0.63 and 0.69) showed significant spatial clustering. Spatial autoregressive model identified average maximum temperature (β=0.945, p=0.017) and highland terrain proportion (β=0.543, p=0.040) as key environmental predictors. Hotspots identified in Shashogo, Soro, and Misrak Badawacho; cold spots in Misha, Duna, Gombora. SAR model showed superior fit to OLS.

articles.headline: Arts-based engagement increases acceptance of Sterile Insect Technique for vector control
articles.title_original: From song to stories: assessing the impact of exposure to arts-based community engagement tools in shaping knowledge, attitudes, and acceptability toward the Sterile Insect Technique for malaria control in South Africa.
articles.authors: Manana PN, Jewett S, Munhenga G
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey in Jozini municipality, uMkhanyakude District, South Africa (614 participants) assessing impact of arts-based community engagement (music, drama, radio stories) on knowledge, attitudes, and acceptance of Sterile Insect Technique. Only 26.2% exposed to arts-based approaches. Exposed groups more likely to correctly identify female mosquito blood-feeding (95.0% vs 85.8%, p=0.008) and support SIT releases (98.1% vs 89.4%, p=0.003). Exposure remained significant predictor of SIT acceptance (OR=0.65, 95% CI 0.45–0.94).

articles.headline: Automated haematology analyzer XN-31 effective for P. vivax
articles.title_original: Performance evaluation of the automated haematology analyzer XN-31 for malaria diagnosis in Plasmodium vivax-dominant regions of Thailand.
articles.authors: Kawaguchi M, Komaki-Yasuda K, Nakatsu M, Kerdsakundee N, Hamana M, Tougan T, Kamei M, Konishi A, Krudsood S, Kano S
articles.details: Prospective study in Thailand (Nov 2019–June 2022, n=349; 125 positive: 103 P. vivax, 17 P. falciparum, 3 P. knowlesi, 1 P. ovale, 1 P. malariae). XN-31 achieved 98.4% sensitivity vs thick film microscopy, outperforming RDT (88.0%). All XN-31-positive samples confirmed via PCR with 100% species accuracy. Quantitative parasitemia showed strong correlation with thin film microscopy (r=0.903).

articles.headline: P. vivax genomes show low diversity in Duffy-negatives
articles.title_original: Highly conserved Plasmodium vivax genomes in Duffy-negative individuals from Sudan.
articles.authors: Schroeder RE, Ahmed S, Ford A, Elfaki M, Hamad SO, Elfaki TM, Mohamed S, Manko E, Clark TG, Campino S, Hamid MMA, Lo E
articles.details: Genomic study comparing P. vivax parasitemia and genetic diversity in Duffy-positive vs Duffy-negative Sudanese individuals. Duffy-positive hosts had significantly higher parasitemia. Duffy-negative-infected genomes showed much lower genetic diversity across 14 chromosomes and 44 erythrocyte binding gene candidates. Merozoite surface protein genes account for much diversity. Multiple erythrocyte binding genes under positive and negative selection pressure.

articles.headline: Correction: gametocyte phenotypic imaging study
articles.title_original: Correction: Machine learning-based phenotypic imaging to characterise the targetable biology of Plasmodium falciparum male gametocytes for the development of transmission-blocking antimalarials.
articles.authors: Tsebriy O, Khomiak A, Miguel-Blanco C, Sparkes PC, Gioli M, Santelli M, Whitley E, Gamo FJ, Delves MJ
articles.details: Correction notice for prior publication.

articles.headline: Novel genetic system generates uniform PfEMP1-expressing parasites
articles.title_original: A system for functional studies of the major virulence factor of malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Cronshagen J, Allweier J, Mesén-Ramírez JP, Stäcker J, Vaaben AV, Ramón-Zamorano G, Naranjo-Prado I, Graser M, López-Barona P, Ofori S, Jansen PWTC, Hornebeck J, Kieferle F, Murk A, Martin E, Castro-Peña C, Bártfai R, Lavstsen T, Bruchhaus I, Spielmann T
articles.details: Selection-linked integration (SLI) enables generation of P. falciparum parasites expressing uniform PfEMP1 variants. Transferring from 3D7 to IT4 strain yielded parasites with effective receptor binding. SLI2 permits additional genome edits. System enables studies of PfEMP1 trafficking, identifies proteins required for PfEMP1-mediated sequestration, and characterizes endothelial receptor-binding domains.

articles.headline: Drug-induced hepatitis following P. falciparum antimalarial treatment
articles.title_original: Deciphering Hepatic Dilemmas: A Case of Acute Hepatitis Following Malaria Treatment.
articles.authors: Lovrien S, Quist E, Maita Coronel D, Singal AK, Xie C
articles.details: Case report of 55-year-old African woman with P. falciparum malaria treated in Cameroon presenting with jaundice and elevated transaminases. Liver biopsy showed cholestatic hepatitis; only one of four follow-up smears positive. After 17+ months, liver enzymes and bilirubin normalized without further antimalarial therapy, attributed to drug-induced liver injury.

articles.headline: Mini dbPCR-NALFIA shows 96.5% sensitivity for P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Field evaluation of the miniature direct-on-blood PCR nucleic acid lateral flow immunoassay (mini-dbPCR-NALFIA) for the detection of Plasmodium falciparum in high seasonal malaria transmission setting in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Kiemde F, Rouamba T, Some DY, Kabore B, Valia D, Natama HM, Bere AW, Mens PF, Tinto H, Schallig HDFH
articles.details: Phase 3 diagnostic accuracy study in Burkina Faso (n=438 febrile patients; 65.5% malaria prevalence by qPCR reference). Mini direct-on-blood PCR with lateral flow readout achieved 96.5% sensitivity and 98.0% specificity vs qPCR, comparable to RDT sensitivity (95.1%) and microscopy specificity (98.7%) but superior to RDT specificity (80.8%) and microscopy sensitivity (90.2%). Detected 67.9% of submicroscopic infections; kappa agreement=0.90.

articles.headline: High malaria knowledge, low prevention behavior in miners
articles.title_original: An assessment of knowledge, practices, and malaria parasitaemia among artisanal gold miners and community members in Mudzi, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Fambirai T, Chimbari MJ
articles.details: Mixed-methods cross-sectional study in Zimbabwe (n=241: 128 artisanal gold miners, 113 community members; 277 tested). Good knowledge of malaria transmission but low risk perception and prevention behavior. No significant difference in knowledge/perception between miners and community members. High preference for health facility care. Malaria prevalence <1% across groups; knowledge not translating to preventative behavior.

articles.headline: PDGFRA inhibitor GSK190937 kills P. falciparum via hemozoin
articles.title_original: Repositioning a Human Kinase Platelet-Derived Growth Factor Receptor Alpha Type II Inhibitor for Malaria and Inhibition of Hemozoin Formation.
articles.authors: Mmonwa MM, Audu O, Maepa K, Dziwornu GA, Govender P, Tshaka L, Burrows J, Taylor D, Masike K, Njoroge M, Wicht KJ, Coulson LB, Chibale K
articles.details: Type II PDGFRA human kinase inhibitor GSK190937 showed antiplasmodial activity against asexual blood stage P. falciparum (PfNF54 IC50=0.22 μM). Medicinal chemistry optimization generated compounds 20, 23, 29 with improved microsomal stability and antiplasmodial potency. Mechanism studies demonstrated this series inhibits hemozoin formation, killing late-stage trophozoites.

articles.headline: Coinfection does not drive P. yoelii virulence divergence
articles.title_original: Experimental evolution of Plasmodium yoelii in single and helminth-coinfected mice.
articles.authors: Dusuel A, Bourbon L, Groetz E, Rialland M, Roche B, Faivre B, Sorci G
articles.details: Experimental evolution study of murine malaria parasite P. yoelii passaged 5–7 times in single-infected or helminth-coinfected hosts (Heligmosomoides polygyrus). SI-lines and COI-lines showed similar replication rates and virulence despite environmental differences. Serial passage increased parasitemia and virulence from fastest-replicating genotypes. COI-lines displayed slower early replication in mismatched environments; no evidence for differential virulence adaptation.

articles.headline: P. vivax dominates; P. falciparum drives severity in Iran
articles.title_original: Epidemiological and clinical profile of malaria patients in Isfahan Province, Iran: a retrospective analysis from 2009 to 2025.
articles.authors: Lesani S, Boozhmehrani MJ, Golban M, Ghayour Najafabadi Z
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 569 microscopically-confirmed cases in Isfahan Province, Iran (2009–2025). Incidence declined sharply post-2009 with 2024 resurgence. 96.1% male, 53.6% aged 15–24 years, 80.8% Afghan nationals; imported cases represented 80.8% of burden. P. vivax comprised 88.0% of cases. P. falciparum (4.6%) strongly associated with severe disease (adjusted OR 22.6, 95% CI 1.24–410.8, p=0.035).

articles.headline: Clean water, sanitation strongly predict lower malaria incidence
articles.title_original: Investigating the relationship between malaria incidence and public health infrastructure in sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Shin J
articles.details: Country-level analysis using nonparametric tests and tree-based machine learning to predict malaria incidence from water access, sanitation, and vitamin A supplementation. Significant differences in incidence across water/sanitation access groups, with lowest-access areas having highest incidence (large effect sizes by Cliff's delta). Vitamin A supplementation showed significant but smaller effect sizes. Tree-based ML models outperformed logistic regression in classification accuracy.

articles.headline: Insect-specific viruses do not affect P. falciparum transmission
articles.title_original: Systemic infection with insect-specific viruses does not affect Plasmodium sporozoite formation in Anopheles mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Schinkel M, Overheul GJ, Ploeger E, van Gemert GJ, Junglen S, Miesen P, Bousema T, van Rij RP
articles.details: Panel of 15 viruses screened for replication in Anopheles; six efficiently replicated including Flock House virus, Wallerfield virus, Agua Salud alphavirus, Herbert herbevirus, Gouléako goukovirus, and invertebrate iridescent virus 6. Four efficiently-replicating, non-lethal viruses tested in P. falciparum coinfection with Anopheles stephensi. Sporozoite formation and P. falciparum infection rates were similar between virus-infected and control mosquitoes.

articles.headline: Surveillance and response capacity assessment framework for post-elimination countries
articles.title_original: Building a tool to assess malaria surveillance and response capacity in malaria post-elimination contexts: a modified and dual-weighted Delphi approach.
articles.authors: Lu G, Xu E, Cao Y, Chai L, Liao Z, Wang J, Sha T, Wang Y, Müller O, Zhao J, Zhu G, Cao J
articles.details: A malaria surveillance and response capacity assessment framework was developed through systematic reviews, expert panel discussions, and a modified two-round Delphi process with 30 experts. The final framework comprises 34 indicators across six domains, with surveillance system coverage (0.240) and data quality/use (0.3710) having the highest domain weights. Expert authority coefficients were 0.777 (round 1) and 0.895 (round 2). The framework provides a standardized tool for evaluating surveillance capacity in post-elimination settings to sustain elimination and support eradication efforts.

articles.headline: Novel cytochrome c variant in Plasmodium with unusual redox properties
articles.title_original: A Divergent Cytochrome c in Malaria Parasites with an Anomalously Low Redox Potential.
articles.authors: Loveridge KM, Olsen OD, Scherer SR, Espino-Sanchez TJ, Wienkers H, Hill CP, Kay MS, Sigala PA
articles.details: This study identified a divergent lineage of cytochrome c homologs in Plasmodium falciparum parasites, exemplified by Pfcyt c-2, which has an unusually pentacoordinate heme and redox potential of -278 mV—over 500 mV lower than canonical cytochrome c (+250 mV). Despite this anomalous redox potential, Pfcyt c-2 displays canonical thermostability and low-level peroxidase activity. These findings reveal a new clade of eukaryotic cytochrome c variants with divergent biochemical properties, opening new scaffolds for mechanistic discovery and redox engineering.

articles.headline: mRDT diagnostic accuracy and malaria prevalence among febrile patients in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria and diagnostic performance of malaria rapid diagnostic tests compared to microscopy among febrile patients at Pawe General Hospital, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Legesse F, Berhane N
articles.details: A hospital-based cross-sectional study of 422 febrile patients (November-December 2022) evaluated the Bioline™ mRDT against light microscopy in Northwest Ethiopia. Malaria prevalence by microscopy was 27.0% (114/422): P. falciparum 84.2%, P. vivax 7.9%, mixed 7.9%. The mRDT showed 99.1% sensitivity, 99.3% specificity, 98.3% PPV, 99.7% NPV (κ=0.98). Males had 1.88-fold higher infection risk (95% CI: 1.16–3.04, p=0.011) and families with ≥5 members had 2.19-fold higher risk (95% CI: 1.36–3.54, p=0.001).

articles.headline: Bradford assay validation for protein quantification in R21 malaria vaccine
articles.title_original: Systematic Development and Validation of a Bradford-Based Protein Quantification Method for Novel Multi-Dose R21 Malaria Vaccine Formulated with 2-Phenoxy Ethanol (2-PE).
articles.authors: Jena R, Ranade D, Chaudhari P, Salunke A, Mahamuni A, Gairola S
articles.details: This study developed and validated a Bradford-based protein assay for quantifying the R21 malaria vaccine antigen formulated with Matrix-M1 adjuvant and 2-phenoxyethanol preservative. The method was validated per ICH Q2(R2) guidelines with a limit of quantitation of 2 µg/mL and demonstrated performance comparable to ELISA. The assay successfully detected antigen adsorption to vial surfaces and showed robust immunogenicity in release and stability studies, providing a rapid, cost-effective solution for commercial manufacturing and lot release.

articles.headline: P. vivax and mixed infection epidemiology in Cambodia's malaria elimination efforts
articles.title_original: Assessing progress and challenges towards malaria elimination in Kampong Speu, Cambodia: analysis of Plasmodium vivax and mixed infections, 2019-2023.
articles.authors: Ly K, Ung S, Roces MC, Lek D, Ly P
articles.details: An analysis of malaria data from Kampong Speu Province, Cambodia (2019–2023) using national surveillance records and radical cure treatment outcomes examined P. vivax and mixed-species infections. Malaria screening increased substantially through village malaria workers, with annual parasite incidence declining from 23.8 per 1000 at-risk individuals (2019) to 0.7 per 1000 (2023). Radical cure treatment completion rates improved from 78% (2019) to 98% (2023). Males aged 15–49 years, particularly forest-goers, remain the highest-risk group.

articles.headline: RDT performance impacts from protocol deviations in Plasmodium falciparum testing
articles.title_original: The effects of introduced procedural errors on malaria rapid diagnostic test performance in a laboratory setting.
articles.authors: Wilson S, Ah Y, Aidoo M
articles.details: This laboratory study tested six RDT products using cultured Plasmodium falciparum at various parasitaemia levels to assess impacts of protocol deviations on test validity. Increasing sample volume beyond the recommended 5 µL caused 83.3% invalid results in 4/6 products due to faulty sample migration. Five-minute incubation periods led to the most deviations from baseline; insufficient buffer volumes caused ≥37% invalid results in >50% of products. Higher parasitaemia (1,000/µL) with 5 µL sample achieved 100% agreement in three products regardless of incubation/buffer variations.

articles.headline: Housing modification costs and willingness to pay for malaria prevention in Uganda
articles.title_original: Housing modification to prevent malaria in Uganda: an analysis of costs, willingness to pay, and equity.
articles.authors: Snyman K, Ochieng W, Gonahasa S, Aber J, Katureebe A, Staedke SG, Kamya MR, Westercamp N, Pitt C
articles.details: A cluster-randomized trial in Eastern Uganda assessed costs and willingness to pay (WTP) for two housing modification interventions covering ~4,000 homes. Screening cost $116/home (societal economic; $4.22/person/year over 5 years); eave tubes cost $50/home ($3.03/person/year). Installation costs were higher in wealthiest vs poorest quintiles (screening: $151 vs $69; eave tubes: $95 vs $31). While >75% were willing to pay, actual WTP represented only a fraction of costs (screening: 12% vs 7%; eave tubes: 18% vs 14% by wealth quintile).

articles.headline: P. falciparum infection and drug resistance markers in HIV-positive patients on chemoprophylaxis
articles.title_original: Characterization of Plasmodium falciparum infections among people living with HIV under antiretroviral and co-trimoxazole chemoprophylaxis in Brazzaville, Republic of Congo.
articles.authors: Djontu JC, Baina MT, Ntabi JDM, Mapanguy CCM, Batchi-Bouyou AL, Elenga VA, Kouikani FY, Ndala ACM, Mouanga AM, Ntoumi F
articles.details: A healthcare facility cross-sectional study of 683 PLWH under antiretroviral and co-trimoxazole prophylaxis (January–September 2022, Brazzaville, Republic of Congo) examined P. falciparum infections. Microscopy detected 1.2% (8/683); PCR detected 9.2% (62/675) submicroscopic infections. P. falciparum was 98.6% of cases with mean multiplicity 2.1±1.09 (68.1% polyclonal). High mutation prevalence: Pfdhfr 51I (78.2%), 59R (84.2%), 108N (84.0%); Pfdhps 437G (72.0%), 540E (30.8%), 581G (48.5%); Pfcrt 76T (46.9%); Pfmdr1 184F (42.1%). No artemisinin resistance mutations detected.

articles.headline: IFN-γ and IL-4 correlate with malaria vaccine-induced protection
articles.title_original: IFN-γ and IL-4 correlate with protection induced by an attenuated malaria vaccine.
articles.authors: Robinson TM, Bolton JS, Duncan EH, Bergmann-Leitner ES, Mura M
articles.details: This study used controlled human malaria infections following immunization with radiation-attenuated Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites to identify immune correlates of protection. Phenotyping of circulating mononuclear innate cells and PBMC responses to sporozoite stimulation revealed baseline immune correlates (IFN-γ secretion and HLA-DRhigh expression) and vaccine-induced factors (IL-4 secretion and CD57− γδ T cell frequency) associated with protection from malaria challenge.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic P. falciparum prevalence and risk factors in urban/rural Gabon
articles.title_original: Burden and determinants of asymptomatic malaria among adults living in urban and rural areas in Gabon in 2023: a community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Moutombi Ditombi BC, M'Bondoukwé NP, Ndong Ngomo JM, Kono HN, Pongui Ngondza B, Ada Mengome M, Ndong Akomezoghe L, Moussavou Mabika DA, Mihindou CJ, Moutongo R, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Bouyou-Akotet MK
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional survey of 1,496 asymptomatic adults (≥18 years, no recent antimalarial treatment; January–December 2023) in rural Bitam and urban Libreville/Owendo, Gabon assessed asymptomatic P. falciparum prevalence. Overall prevalence was 15.3%, significantly higher in rural (22.4%) vs urban (4.1%, p<0.01). Independent risk factors included rural residence (aOR: 7.1; 95% CI: 4.4–9.8), being a worker (aOR: 5.2; 95% CI: 3.4–7.8), and school attendance (p<0.01). ITN use was low (32.7%) and not significantly protective.

articles.headline: Integrated malaria control in Madagascar: vector dynamics and community interventions
articles.title_original: Integrated Malaria Control in Madagascar: From Vector Dynamics to Community Interventions.
articles.authors: Ramboarina S, Kapesa L, Razafindrakoto J, Rasolofo V, Solofoharivelo MC
articles.details: No abstract provided. Based on the title, this article appears to address integrated malaria control approaches in Madagascar that incorporate understanding of vector dynamics with community-based interventions. Additional details regarding specific interventions, populations studied, and outcomes cannot be extracted without access to the full abstract.

articles.headline: Malaria seroprevalence and serology screening in at-risk blood donors in Italy
articles.title_original: Prevention of Transfusion-Transmitted Malaria and Chagas Disease in Non-Endemic Countries: An 8-Year Study of Seroprevalence Among Donors at Risk in Tuscany (Central Italy).
articles.authors: Mangano VD, Pinto B, Marotta R, Galli L, Moscato GA, Lupetti A, Bruschi F
articles.details: An 8-year serological screening study (2016–2023) tested 3,754 donors at risk for anti-Plasmodium antibodies in Tuscany, Italy. Overall malaria seroprevalence was 6.8% (95% CI: 6.1–7.7%), higher in Sub-Saharan African (42.9%; 95% CI: 36.1–49.9%) and Southeast Asian (10.6%; 95% CI: 6.7–16.4%) donors. Seroprevalence was lower with Euroimmun ELISA (4.8%; 95% CI: 3.8–5.9%) than DRG ELISA (8.2%; 95% CI: 7.1–9.3%). None of 20 seropositive donors tested positive for Plasmodium DNA by LAMP.

articles.headline: K13 mutations drive artemisinin resistance expansion in East Africa
articles.title_original: Mapping the prevalence of molecular markers of Plasmodium falciparum artemisinin partial resistance in Africa: a spatial-temporal modelling study.
articles.authors: Young NW, Meier-Scherling CPG, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Tollefson GA, Connelly SV, Marglous J, Gyuricza IG, Carey-Ewend K, Kyong-Shin R, Popkin-Hall ZR, Zeleke AJ, Ishengoma DS, Fola AA, Simkin A, Niaré K, Parr JB, Conrad M, Okell LC, Ruybal-Pesántez S, Watson OJ, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Verity R
articles.details: Spatial-temporal modeling of 3,806 surveys with 182,071 genotyped samples (2014–2025) identified distinct k13 mutations emerging regionally: k13 561H in Rwanda, 675V in Uganda, and 622I in Ethiopia/Eritrea. K13 prevalence rose to 26.36% in Uganda and 39.44% in Rwanda by 2024 (1.81% and 3.49% annual increases, respectively). Findings show rapid transition from localized emergence to entrenched regional hotspots, mirroring early Southeast Asian patterns before ACT failure.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance widespread in Indian malaria vectors with regional variation
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal trends of insecticide resistance in Indian malaria vectors (2000-2024): a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mullick R, Yadav CP, Singhal R
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 51 studies (2000–2024) examining DDT, malathion, and deltamethrin resistance across Indian Anopheles species. DDT resistance prevalent in most states except north-eastern region; Anopheles culicifacies showed very low DDT susceptibility. Deltamethrin resistance concentrated in central states (Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh) with 89–100% mortality rates in different species. North-eastern vectors remained susceptible to all three insecticides.

articles.headline: COVID-19 pandemic reduced malaria services but indicators largely maintained
articles.title_original: The impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on HIV, malaria, tuberculosis and childhood vaccination services in Guinea: an interrupted time series analysis.
articles.authors: Diallo A, Sidikiba S, Cissé AS, Camara M, Kaba M, Diallo BD, Carlos-Bolumbu M, Sako FB, Delamou A
articles.details: Interrupted time series analysis of Guinea surveillance data (January 2018–December 2022) examining malaria, HIV, TB, and childhood vaccination. During COVID-19, in-patient malaria cases decreased 0.8% monthly (IRR 0.992, 95% CI 0.986–0.998), while HIV tests, childhood vaccines, and TB indicators were heavily impacted. TB-positive microscopy increased 2.5% monthly. Malaria indicators remained relatively stable compared to other health services.

articles.headline: Mosquito metabolism and microbiota determine P. vivax infection susceptibility
articles.title_original: Metabolic reprogramming and gut microbiota ecology drive divergent Plasmodium vivax infection outcomes in Anopheles darlingi.
articles.authors: Carlos BC, Voges K, Affonso PHA, Jaye A, Tong Rios C, Tinoco-Nunes B, Alonso DP, Cai JA, MacCallum RM, Moreno M, Vlachou D, Souza-Neto JA, Christophides GK
articles.details: Experimental infection study of A. darlingi with field-derived P. vivax produced two infection phenotypes. Low-infection mosquitoes mounted early sustained response with detoxification pathways, redox regulation, aromatic amino acid catabolism, and purine depletion, accompanied by reduced bacterial diversity and enrichment of Enterobacteriales and Pseudomonadales. High-infection mosquitoes showed limited metabolic reprogramming and Flavobacteriales expansion. Low infection outcomes consistently arose from bloodmeals with lowest gametocyte densities.

articles.headline: Specific bacterial taxa associated with P. falciparum infection in A. gambiae
articles.title_original: Microbiota diversity of Anopheles gambiae in Bankeng, southern Cameroon, and its association with Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Sandeu MM, Maffo Tatsinkou CG, Dada N, Kouhoué Feukam F, Njiokou F, Hughes GL, Wondji CS
articles.details: 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing of 120 A. gambiae (60 infected, 60 uninfected) in Cameroon detected 99 bacterial taxa; 97 shared between groups. Two taxa (Acetobacteraceae, Enterococcus) unique to uninfected, none to infected. Significant compositional differences: 14 bacterial genera more abundant in uninfected, 2 in infected. Shannon indices showed higher diversity in uninfected samples during wet season and infected samples during dry season.

articles.headline: Serratia AS1 bacterium persists and spreads in A. stephensi mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Environmental persistence and transmission dynamics of Serratia AS1 in mosquito habitats: advancing paratransgenesis for malaria control.
articles.authors: Kamel Urmia H, Koosha M, Yakhchali B, Moosa-Kazemi SH, Oshaghi MA
articles.details: Semi-field study in Iran of Serratia AS1-mCherry paratransgenesis candidate in A. stephensi. Serratia successfully colonized midguts and ovaries, persisted in larval water for 14 days and on sugar pads for 4–6 days. Transmission routes included transstadial, venereal, vertical, and adult acquisition from larval habitats via sipping. Water-based delivery effectively disseminated bacteria among mosquito populations, demonstrating field application feasibility.

articles.headline: Peripheral gangrene complication in severe P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Symmetrical lower extremity gangrene in a patient with severe malaria: a case report and literature review.
articles.authors: Aman ST, Belay EH, Jirata TD, Hussen SH, Isa SF, Abagojam AH, Kebede ML, Bezabih KT, Tukeni KN
articles.details: Case report of 14-year-old Ethiopian girl with severe P. falciparum malaria presenting with fever, vomiting, diarrhea who developed symmetrical peripheral gangrene of lower limbs despite prompt antimalarial therapy. Clinical evaluation showed stable vitals, preserved organ function, and palpable pulses. Diagnosis was severe malaria complicated by peripheral gangrene managed conservatively with continued antimalarial treatment.

articles.headline: Evolutionary baseline model framework needed for P. falciparum drug resistance
articles.title_original: Towards an evolutionary baseline model of Plasmodium falciparum for population-genomic inference.
articles.authors: Henry CM, Marsh JI, Daigle A, Crescenzi J, Lin JT, Bailey JA, Johri P
articles.details: Conceptual review arguing for comprehensive evolutionary baseline model to understand selective pressures and demographic history in P. falciparum using tens of thousands of MalariaGEN genomes. Current outlier-based approaches lack framework accounting for multiple concurrent evolutionary processes. Multiple drug-resistance alleles at intermediate and high frequency identified in Asian and African populations. Such a model would improve evolutionary parameter inference accuracy and identify new drug-resistance gene candidates.

articles.headline: B cell dysfunction impairs durable malaria vaccine CSP repeat region immunity
articles.title_original: Dysfunctional memory B cell responses to the protective repeat region of the Plasmodium circumsporozoite protein are associated with waning humoral immunity.
articles.authors: McDougal CE, Mkindi CG, Rodda LB, Lucarelli CV, Langowski MD, King NP, Jongo S, Daubenberger C, Pepper M
articles.details: Cross-species analysis of B cell responses to circumsporozoite protein (CSP) after infection or immunization, focusing on repeat and C-terminus domains in subunit vaccines. Two factors predominate in impairing proper memory B cell differentiation: inhibition of germinal center formation by infection and skewed differentiation due to CSP repeat structure. This explains limited durability in malaria-naïve individuals and reduced effectiveness in malaria-experienced individuals.

articles.headline: Bilirubin from biliverdin reductase provides dose-dependent malaria protection
articles.title_original: Biliverdin Reductase Catalytic Activity Is Essential for Malaria Resistance.
articles.authors: Mesquita M, Figueiredo A, Rastogi ST, Ramos S, Pagnotta S, Carlos AR, Cardoso S, Nóvoa A, Mallo M, Paul B, Jentho E, Soares MP
articles.details: Study using Blvra G17A and Blvra E97A missense mutation mice with reduced BVRA enzymatic activity. Both mutant strains succumbed to malaria doses non-lethal to wild-type mice. Mutant strains failed to reach circulating unconjugated bilirubin threshold required for protective effect. Study establishes antimalarial effect of BVRA catalytic activity and defines minimum bilirubin concentration threshold required for parasite control and malaria resolution.

articles.headline: Rosette formation reduces infected RBC adhesion to vessel walls
articles.title_original: Rosette margination in blood flow during malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Dasanna AK, Papagrigorakes M, Lanzer M, Schwarz US, Gompper G, Fedosov DA
articles.details: Mesoscopic hydrodynamics simulations and microfluidic experiments investigated margination of rosettes (iRBC surrounded by adhered hRBCs) in blood flow at various rates, cell fractions, and binding strengths. Rosette margination significantly weaker than single iRBCs, suggesting limited adhesion potential. Poor margination results from deformability and dynamics of clusters formed by multiple hRBCs around one iRBC. Findings suggest main rosette function is keeping iRBCs in circulation rather than enhancing cytoadhesion.

articles.headline: High-dose primaquine safe and superior for P. vivax radical cure in children
articles.title_original: Safety, tolerability, and efficacy of high versus low-dose, short versus long-course daily primaquine for the radical cure of uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria in children under 15 years of age: an open-label, non-inferiority, randomized controlled trial (CHILDPRIM).
articles.authors: Pacheco ALO, Omena AG, Baía-da-Silva DC, Jardim TAP, Silva DCB, Lopes APB, Barbosa LRA, Souza IG, Araujo RF, Nogueira LOS, Vale SCN, Melo GC, Cordeiro JSM, Bassat Q, Sampaio VS, Lima VDS, Martinez-Espinosa FE, Mourão MPG, Monteiro WM, Alecrim MGC, Brito-Sousa JD, Lacerda MVG
articles.details: Open-label randomized trial (CHILDPRIM) in Brazilian Amazon (2021–2025) with 100 children under 15 with uncomplicated P. vivax comparing three primaquine regimens after G6PD screening: 3.5 mg/kg/day over 7 days, 7.0 mg/kg/day over 14 days, 7.0 mg/kg/day over 7 days. Recurrence-free survival at day 180: 50% (standard-dose), 82.3% (high-dose 14-day), 79.4% (high-dose 7-day, p=0.0065). Both high-dose schedules showed comparable efficacy with similar safety profiles.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax Duffy-binding protein shows widespread positive selection
articles.title_original: Complete gene sequence diversity, amplification, and natural selection of the Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein locus.
articles.authors: Putaporntip C, Kuamsab N, Tia T, Takashima Y, Jongwutiwes S
articles.details: Analyzed complete PvDBP gene sequences from 232 P. vivax isolates from four regions of Thailand. PvDBP duplication (Cambodian type) detected in 22 isolates (9.48%), characterized by identical or microheterogeneous copies. Across 254 sequences, positive selection evident from excess nonsynonymous over synonymous substitutions in 5' region and PvDBPII, with 26 positively selected codons, 22 overlapping B-cell/T-cell epitopes. Insertions, deletions, and recombination confined mainly to PvDBPII and 3' region.

articles.headline: Two-thirds of knowlesi-infected patients carry transmissible gametocytes
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi gametocyte carriage and load among malaria patients at Kapit Hospital in Sarawak, Malaysian Borneo.
articles.authors: Theivindran D, Hu TH, Mohamad DSA, Rosli N, Hii KC, Singh B, Siner A
articles.details: Measured pks25 mRNA transcripts in 295 blood samples from patients with P. knowlesi single infections at Kapit Hospital, Sarawak. Viable gametocytes present in 67.5% (199/295) of patients. Positive correlation between gametocyte load and total parasitaemia (ρ = 0.32, p = 0.01); no association with duration of illness (ρ = 0.28, p = 0.7). Among gametocyte-positive samples, 12.5% (25/199) had elevated levels including 6% (12/199) with >100,000 pks25 transcript copies/µL.

articles.headline: Dam proximity increases malaria prevalence in Burkinabé villages
articles.title_original: Epidemiological profile and environmental determinants of Plasmodium falciparum infection in villages surrounding the Soum Agropole, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ouare N, Sorgho H, Hansson H, Rouamba T, Ilboudo H, Derra K, Kaboré B, Tahita MC, Rouamba E, Alifrangis M, Zida A, Magnussen P, Tinto H
articles.details: Monthly household surveys (March 2022–February 2023) in five villages 3–40 km from Soum dam, Burkina Faso enrolled 13,786 participants. Overall P. falciparum prevalence 39.6% (95% CI 38.79–40.42) with asymptomatic carriage (24.5%) exceeding clinical cases (19.5%). Highest prevalence in Soum near dam (42.1%, 95% CI 40.7–43.47); lowest in Séguédin far from dam (33.2%, 95% CI 31.21–35.32). Participants aged 5–19 years most affected (43.6%–69.4%).

articles.headline: Wnt inhibition enhances Plasmodium falciparum liver-stage schizont maturation
articles.title_original: Inhibition of Wnt signaling in primary human hepatocytes promotes Plasmodium falciparum liver stage development.
articles.authors: Kanyal A, van Gemert GJ, Wu H, van der Starre A, de Wilt JHW, Bousema T, Sauerwein RW, Bártfai R, Yang ASP
articles.details: Used primary human hepatocytes (PHHs) cultured with serum-replacement and signal-transduction inhibitors to study P. falciparum liver stage development. Transcriptomic profiling identified host signaling pathways affecting parasite permissiveness. Inhibition of Wnt pathway significantly increased size and maturity of P. falciparum liver-stage schizonts while preserving hepatocyte metabolic activity and epithelial phenotype. Results suggest improved in vitro liver-stage model for identifying novel therapeutic strategies.

articles.headline: Cameroon caregivers trust vaccine but need better communication
articles.title_original: Awareness, perception and influences on uptake of the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine among caregivers for children under 5 years in South West region, Cameroon.
articles.authors: Anye JC, Niba LL, Njimanted O, Fang EE, Itoe BT, Fung EG, Ewi-Kang HG, Kimbi HK
articles.details: Qualitative study with 20 mothers and caregivers of children aged 11–30 months in urban and rural South West Region, Cameroon. Urban caregivers showed high awareness but limited understanding; rural caregivers had uneven awareness but strong trust in health workers. Perceived vaccine benefits (reduced malaria severity) enhanced confidence. Barriers included communication gaps, misinformation, gender dynamics, long wait times, and travel distance.

articles.headline: MRI identifies cytotoxic oedema pattern in fulminant cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Radiological findings in fulminant cerebral malaria: a rare neuroimaging presentation.
articles.authors: Nayak B, Mohanty S, Das S, Panda S, Mishra SB
articles.details: Case report of 54-year-old woman with P. falciparum cerebral malaria presenting with fever, seizures, coma, shock, and severe metabolic acidosis. CT brain normal but MRI revealed bilateral T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in basal ganglia, thalami, centrum semiovale, periventricular white matter with patchy diffusion restriction suggesting cytotoxic oedema. Despite timely artesunate initiation and organ support, patient died from refractory multiorgan failure.

articles.headline: Serratia bacteria present in 13 percent of wild malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Molecular characterization of potential Plasmodium-Blocking Serratia spp. bacteria in field-caught malaria mosquito in Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Traoré H, Gnambani EJ, Hien DFS, Yao RK, Konkobo M, Sodré AF, Somda MB, Ouedraogo AS, Diabaté A, Bilgo E
articles.details: Collected Anopheles gambiae complex mosquitoes from three field sites in Houet Province, Burkina Faso; dissected salivary glands, midgut, spermatheca, ovary (females), testis (males). Identified Serratia species using PCR targeting luxS gene and 16S rRNA sequencing. Overall Serratia prevalence 13.3% among malaria vectors, varying by development stage, locality, and organ. Phylogenetic analysis showed Burkinabè strains highly similar to each other but distinct from Ghana and Nigeria strains.

articles.headline: China's malaria elimination demonstrates One Health integrated vector control
articles.title_original: China's malaria elimination: One Health lessons for vector-borne disease governance.
articles.authors: Liu J, Liu Y, Cheng G
articles.details: Perspective integrating WHO-documented findings on China's malaria elimination (certified malaria-free in 2021) from case studies in Hainan Province, Huai River Basin, and Yunnan Province. Success attributed to seven decades of adaptive surveillance, evolving strategies, and community engagement. Four pillars identified: integrated surveillance, regional cooperation, ecological intervention, technological innovation. Demonstrates value of sustainable vector control governance for future prevention of vector-borne diseases.

articles.headline: Parasitic co-infection affects six percent of pregnant women in Kinshasa
articles.title_original: Geohelminthiasis and Malaria Co-Infection in Pregnant Women in Kinshasa: Case of Lisungi and Lukunga Hospitals in Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Dianzenza C, Tshiongo JK, Kuseke L, Muswele F, Kasereka G, Mangungulu D, Laken EK, Tevuzula VM, Kayentao K, Mavoko HM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in two Kinshasa hospitals with 336 pregnant women measured thick smears (malaria), stool specimens (geohelminths), and hemoglobin. Geohelminth-malaria co-infection observed in 5.7%; geohelminthiasis alone 7.7%. Most common parasite was Ascaris lumbricoides (6.3%), followed by Trichuris trichiura (1.5%), Ancylostoma duodenale (0.3%). Third trimester significantly increased co-infection risk (ORa = 5.8, 95% CI 1.36–9.23, p = 0.0077).

articles.headline: Mtphagy dye detects mitochondrial changes during artemisinin-resistant survival
articles.title_original: Fluorescence-based bioassay for the detection of changes in mitochondrial physiology during artemisinin-induced growth arrest of blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Whangviboonkij N, Kulkeaw K, Imwong M, Kobpornchai P
articles.details: Used pH-sensitive Mtphagy fluorochrome to monitor mitochondrial changes during dihydroartemisinin (DHA) exposure in P. falciparum blood stages. After DHA exposure, Mtphagy intensity increased in early ring-stage parasites indicating lowered mitochondrial pH. Inhibition of mitochondrial fission and PI3K-mediated pathway significantly reduced Mtphagy-positive parasites and delayed recrudescence in DHA-treated cultures. Mtphagy-based assay useful for detecting mitochondrial changes during growth arrest.

articles.headline: Sudanese healthcare workers demonstrate low malaria vaccine knowledge
articles.title_original: Preliminary insights into knowledge and attitudes toward malaria vaccination among Sudanese healthcare workers: an exploratory cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Hamza DAA, Suliman OKB, Sam AAK, Abdelrahman AOO, Elemam TAA, Fadul EMAM
articles.details: Online cross-sectional survey (January–April 2025) of 222 Sudanese healthcare workers (mean age 31.5 years, 66.7% female). Only 9% (n=20) demonstrated adequate knowledge (≥70%) of malaria vaccination; 21.6% (n=48) displayed positive attitudes. While 66.2% (n=147) had heard of vaccines, 71.2% (n=158) unaware of WHO recommendations and 76.6% (n=170) didn't know target parasite. Negative attitudes reported by 78.4% (n=174), citing safety, affordability, and trust concerns.

articles.headline: Brazil's federal intervention increased malaria detection in Yanomami lands
articles.title_original: What impact did the Brazilian Federal Government's intervention have on malaria cases in the Yanomami Indigenous lands?
articles.authors: de Aguiar Barros J, Granja F, Siqueira PEL, da Silva Epifânio I, Coelho RR, Ferreira-da-Cruz MF
articles.details: Retrospective analysis (2021–2024) of Brazil's Health Information System data for Roraima state among 117,214 reported Indigenous malaria cases. Following January 2023 federal intervention in Yanomami Indigenous Land, cases increased significantly (52,210 to 65,004; p=0.0005) while hospitalizations decreased (787 to 638; p=0.0304) and deaths remained steady (111 total; p=0.9089). Plasmodium vivax dominated throughout. Post-intervention, detection expanded to all age groups with shift toward vulnerable populations (0–19, 60+ years).

articles.headline: Anopheles species distribution varies across Burkina Faso climatic zones
articles.title_original: Spatial Distribution and Biodiversity of Anopheles Mosquito Species Across Climatic Zones in Burkina Faso: Implications for Malaria Vector Control.
articles.authors: Zongo ON, Kiendrebeogo E, Sow BBD, Kientega M, Toé I, Sanou R, Yemien SOG, Sawadogo G, Kaboré H, Agolinou A, Traore N, Epopa PS, Millogo AA, Niang A, Namountougou M, Maiga H, Diabaté A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey collected 30,521 Anopheles from 67 health districts using pyrethroid spray catches. An. gambiae s.l. dominated (94.4%), with An. coluzzii predominating in drier zones (74.9% Sahelian, 71.2% Sudano-Sahelian) and An. gambiae s.s. in Soudanian zone (53.8%). Biodiversity decreased from south to north. Results highlight spatial heterogeneity in vector composition, emphasizing need for locally adapted vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Periostin shows dual role in phagocytosis of Plasmodium-infected RBCs
articles.title_original: Effect of periostin (OSF-2) on phagocytosis of Plasmodium-infected erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Chin JY, Mohammed-Syafiei MN, Lim YJ, Chong GX, Suhaimi MN, Phong ZY, Ng YL, Lau YL, Sam IC, Rénia L, Lee WC
articles.details: In vitro study using THP-1 monocytes exposed to P. falciparum and P. knowlesi infected erythrocytes with recombinant periostin (OSF-2). OSF-2 increased phagocytosis of purified IRBCs via CD36, but reduced phagocytosis when rosetting was induced. Effect was rapidly reversible upon OSF-2 removal. Findings highlight complex parasite-host interactions influencing infection pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Drug resistance markers in P. falciparum isolates from Western Kenya
articles.title_original: Comparative molecular surveillance of polymorphisms in chloroquine resistance transporter, multidrug resistance 1, and Kelch 13 genes associated with antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Bungei J, Ouma C, Zhong D, Oyweri J, Lee MC, Zhou G, Atieli H, Githure J, Wang C, Yan G
articles.details: Comparative cross-sectional study of febrile patients from endemic (Kisumu) and epidemic-prone (Kisii) regions genotyping Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, and Pfk13 genes. Detected Pfcrt K76T (5.4%), Pfmdr1 Y184F (48.2%), Pfk13 A675V (12.8%); elevated Pfk13 A675V in Kisii (27.5%, p<0.05). Novel Pfmdr1 N146I and rare Pfk13 mutations (S522C, R561H) found in Kisumu. Continuous molecular surveillance recommended given artemether-lumefantrine is Kenya's first-line treatment.

articles.headline: RDT self-testing is highly acceptable and feasible in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Feasibility of self-testing for acute malaria using rapid diagnostic tests in three peri-urban sub-Saharan African community settings.
articles.authors: Brumwell A, Bemer M, Lauff A, Morton JF, Thomas KK, Moe CA, Cooper S, Humphries H, Sharma A, Pollard D, Kwena Z, Sang N, Winters A, van Heerden A, Bukusi E, Rech D, Drain P
articles.details: Feasibility study across 100 households in Kenya (225 participants, 11.5% positive), South Africa (80, 0% positive), and Zambia (163, 1.8% positive). Participants interpreted RDTs with 100% sensitivity and 99.7% specificity vs trained team; >96% found RDTs easy to use. >90% of Kenyan and Zambian participants confident interpreting their own test and comfortable with future self-testing.

articles.headline: Biometric fingerprinting improves malaria patient identification in Papua
articles.title_original: Enhancing unique identification of patients with malaria using biometric fingerprinting in Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Fransisca L, Kenangalem E, Ubra RR, Ley B, Price RN, Poespoprodjo JR, Douglas NM
articles.details: Before-and-after study at clinic in Timika, Indonesia over 16 months (7,471 patients total). Proportion of patients linked to pre-existing malaria card increased from 44.4% (Phase 1, n=1,487) to 48.9% (Phase 3, n=2,756; difference 4.5%, 95% CI 1.4-7.7%). Among fingerprinted patients in Phase 3, 55.8% linked to existing cards (11.4% improvement vs Phase 1). Biometric refusal rate <0.4%; main challenge was unreadable fingerprints in children.

articles.headline: High genetic diversity and polyclonal infections in Western Kenyan malaria
articles.title_original: Characterizing Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity and complexity of infections in clinical malaria infections in Western and Coastal Kenya using the poly-alpha microsatellite marker.
articles.authors: Abade NA, Mgawe C, Ndwiga L, Kimenyi KM, Ochola-Oyier LI
articles.details: Genotyping study of dried blood spots from 93 Busia (2016) and 92 Kilifi (2020) participants using poly-α microsatellite. Busia infections predominantly polyclonal (80%) with mean complexity of infection (COI) 2.9 (p<0.0001); Kilifi mostly monoclonal (52.5%) with mean COI 1.7. Expected heterozygosity similar (Busia 0.92, Kilifi 0.90), with Busia showing more effective alleles (10.8 vs 9.3). High genetic diversity suitable for molecular correction in therapeutic efficacy studies.

articles.headline: Optimized whole genome amplification improves P. falciparum sequencing efficiency
articles.title_original: Enhancing whole genome DNA amplification of Plasmodium falciparum for advanced molecular surveillance in malaria control.
articles.authors: Tan MKI, de Jesus MCS, Phelan JE, Thorpe J, Ward D, Batra R, Patel A, Nolder D, Chiodini P, Sutherland CJ, Edgeworth J, Clark TG, Campino S
articles.details: Protocol optimization of selective whole genome amplification (SWGA) using 13 clinical samples, testing 2, 8, 16 h incubation and 3 h with EquiPhi29 polymerase. Results showed comparable amplification between 8 and 16 h (>90% SNP concordance). 3 h with EquiPhi29 achieved >63% genome coverage with 60% reagent cost reduction; drug resistance profiles consistent across conditions. Enables faster, cost-effective genomic surveillance for malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Deep learning achieves 98% accuracy for malaria detection in blood smears
articles.title_original: Trustworthy deep learning for malaria diagnosis using explainable artificial intelligence.
articles.authors: Parveen R, Qui B, Song W, Al-Kahtani N, Jamjoom MM, Mostafa SM, Sultan N, Fatima J
articles.details: Evaluation of four CNNs on 27,090 blood smear images: ResNet18 achieved F1-score 96.33%. Fine-tuned Xception and Inception-ResNetV2 attained ~98% accuracy on validation/test sets; validation on independent Harvard Dataverse thick-smear dataset confirmed 97-98% accuracy under varied conditions. Explainable AI techniques (Grad-CAM, LIME, SHAP) provided transparency into decisions and identified morphological/staining artifacts causing misdiagnosis. Framework demonstrates scalability for resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Aerobic glycolysis controls rDNA silencing in P. falciparum via NAD regulation
articles.title_original: A metabolism-chromatin axis promotes differential ribosomal RNA transcription in the human malaria parasite.
articles.authors: Couble JE, Vignolini T, Doré G, Mussgnug S, Lombard B, Richard M, Loew D, Büttner M, Dueñas-Sánchez R, Poschet G, Bryant JM, Baumgarten S
articles.details: Mechanistic study of rDNA gene silencing in human host and de-repression during transmission to vector. Disruption of NAD+ regeneration during lactate fermentation promotes rDNA de-repression; sirtuin histone deacetylase Sir2a mediates between NAD+ levels and transcriptional outcomes. rDNA activation couples to metabolic state transition from aerobic glycolysis (human) to mitochondrial respiration (mosquito), revealing parasite adaptation mechanism.

articles.headline: Pre-infection gut microbiome predicts parasite levels in controlled infections
articles.title_original: Distinct gastrointestinal microbial signatures predict parasite levels in controlled Plasmodium infections in both rhesus macaques and humans.
articles.authors: Gustin AT, Broedlow CA, Hager K, Coronado E, Wangari S, Iwayama N, Ahrens CY, Garrison WD, Guerriero KA, De Paris K, Londono-Renteria B, Gale M Jr, Klatt NR, Kublin JG, Manuzak JA
articles.details: 16S rRNA sequencing study from rhesus macaques infected with P. fragile and adults in controlled human malaria infection trial (P. falciparum, NCT04072302, testing KAF156). Distinct pre-infection microbial signatures associated with above-median parasitemia in both models. Bifidobacterium-based feature set accurately stratified parasitemia risk. Pre-infection microbiome-P. fragile dynamic in macaques mirrors P. falciparum in humans, supporting future microbiome-targeting approaches.

articles.headline: Histone lactylation marks severe falciparum malaria and parasite metabolic state
articles.title_original: Histone lactylation: A new epigenetic mark in the malaria parasite Plasmodium.
articles.authors: Jabre I, Andoh NE, Naldoni J, Gregory W, Mbye H, Yoon CE, Cunnington AJ, Georgiadou A, Blagborough AM, Merrick CJ
articles.details: Characterization of histone lactylation as novel epigenetic mark in P. falciparum and P. knowlesi (human parasites) and P. yoelii and P. berghei (rodent models). Histone lactylation increased with elevated lactate in vitro and in vivo, acting as metabolic sensor linked to hyperlactataemia in severe falciparum malaria. Less abundant/inducible in P. knowlesi. In P. yoelii, hyperlactataemia correlated with parasite transcriptomic programmes suggesting metabolic dormancy.

articles.headline: High dengue seroprevalence with malaria-typhoid coinfection in Niger basin
articles.title_original: Dengue virus infection amongst malaria and typhoid fever suspected acute febrile patients in the Niger river basin of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okpanachi RO, Omatola CA, Abraham JO, Okolo MO, Conco-Biyela T, Onoja AB, Adamu AM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 200 febrile patients at three health facilities in Nigeria. Dengue serology: 19% anti-DENV antibodies (13.5% IgG, 4.0% IgM, 0.5% NS1); malaria 52% positive; typhoid 62.5% positive. Monoinfections: 13.5% dengue, 18.5% malaria, 29% typhoid. Coinfections: 29% dual malaria-typhoid, 1% dengue-malaria, 1% dengue-typhoid, 4% triple infection. Highlights importance of differential diagnosis in endemic areas.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 13.4% among pregnant women, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated factors of malaria among pregnant women in Maraki Health Center, North West, Gondar, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Dagnaw M, Indracanti M, Jan A, Ayalew J
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 271 pregnant women in Northwest Ethiopia. Malaria prevalence was 13.4%. Independent risk factors included: not using insecticide-treated nets (AOR=3.2, 95% CI 1.4–7.5), no intermittent preventive treatment with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (AOR=2.8, 95% CI 1.1–6.9), fewer than four antenatal care visits (AOR=4.6, 95% CI 1.9–11.2), living near stagnant water (AOR=2.5, 95% CI 1.1–5.8), and attending care during rainy season (AOR=1.9, 95% CI 1.0–3.7).

articles.headline: Protocol for antigen-specific B cell sorting from malaria
articles.title_original: Protocol to identify and sort antigen-specific B cells from Plasmodium-infected mice.
articles.authors: Calôba C, Minns AM, Lindner SE, Vijay R
articles.details: Describes a protocol using SpyCage reagents to identify and sort MSP1-19–specific B cells from Plasmodium-infected mice. Includes splenic cell preparation, enrichment, and live-cell staining for downstream applications including single-cell RNA, V(D)J, and ATAC sequencing.

articles.headline: Long-read sequencing protocol for P. falciparum antigenic variants
articles.title_original: Protocol for genomic surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum antigens using amplicon-based PacBio long-read sequencing.
articles.authors: Plaza DF
articles.details: Protocol using amplicon-based PacBio long-read sequencing to identify and classify variants of msp1, msp2, glurp, and csp in P. falciparum. Resolves full-length sequences per isolate to measure infection complexity, construct phylogenies, and support vaccine design.

articles.headline: Forest data integration improves malaria surveillance detection threefold
articles.title_original: Integrating forest data and health facility surveys to optimise risk-based malaria surveillance in the Philippines.
articles.authors: Fornace KM, Reyes RA, Macalinao MLM, Lim JS, Bareng APN, Luchavez JS, Hafalla JCR, Espino FEJ, Matthiopoulos J, Drakeley CJ
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey in Philippines combining health facility attendee data, environmental remote-sensing data, and molecular diagnostics. Risk-based surveillance targeting high-risk forest environments increased probability of detecting malaria foci over threefold compared to routine surveillance. Diagnostic sensitivity varied spatially, with decreased performance in forests.

articles.headline: Correction notice for immune evasion review article
articles.title_original: Correction to "Immune Response and Evasion Mechanisms of Plasmodium falciparum Parasites".
articles.details: Erratum correcting article DOI 10.1155/2018/6529681 on Plasmodium falciparum immune response and evasion mechanisms.

articles.headline: Hemozoin causes neuronal injury in cerebral malaria mice
articles.title_original: Hemozoin induces neuronal injury primarily characterized by axon rupture and mitochondrial damage in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Li T, Dang D, Shen Y, Wang J, Huang Y, Zhu Q, Wang Y, Yang C, Li G, Sun J, Zhang A, Li P, Liang J, Zhao Y
articles.details: In mice with experimental cerebral malaria and in vitro primary neurons, purified hemozoin (parasite hemoglobin-digestion byproduct) adhered to neurons and internalized into cytoplasm, causing axon rupture, mitochondrial structural damage, reduced mitochondrial membrane potential, decreased ATP, elevated reactive oxygen species, increased neuronal nitric oxide synthase activity, and altered neurotransmitter levels—mechanisms linking hemozoin to cerebral malaria neurological sequelae.

articles.headline: Drug-resistance mutations persistent across Nigeria despite ACT use
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of antimalarial drug resistance genes in Nigeria: a systematic review and roadmap to malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Bamikole OJ, Fayehun AF, Uthman YA, Salako IA, Adedeji BA, Olufeagba MB, Amodu OK
articles.details: Systematic review of 26 studies (2010–2024) on antimalarial resistance markers in Nigeria. High prevalence of chloroquine and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance (Pfcrt, Pfdhfr genes); Pfk13 artemisinin-resistance mutations less common but present. Regional disparities observed, with lower Pfcrt prevalence in northern Nigeria than south. CQ may be reconsidered only in areas with consistently low resistance, contingent on clinical trial confirmation.
