WHO Malaria Surveillance Assessment Toolkit evaluated 68 indicators: 82% of Performance (14/19), 77% of Context and Infrastructure (8/13), 79% of Technical and Process (4/7), and 100% of Behavior indicators (3/3) met. Notable improvements in reporting completeness and timeliness over 2021-2023. Key gaps: case classification revision, data cleaning/verification processes, and national malaria information system upgrades needed.

articles.headline: Anti-alpha-gal antibodies associated with malaria protection in infants
articles.title_original: Naturally acquired immune responses to alpha-gal in malaria endemic settings and pre-clinical efficacy testing with R21/MM.
articles.authors: Mukhopadhyay ES, Bellamy DG, Tinto H, Hamaluba M, Truby A, Salman AM, Hill AVS
articles.details: Study of naturally acquired anti-α-gal antibodies in sporozoite-challenged UK volunteers, naturally exposed Kenyan adults, and Burkina Faso infants. UK volunteers showed no significant antibody increase 90 days post-challenge. Burkina Faso infants showed increases in anti-α-gal IgG and IgM over time; higher anti-α-gal IgM correlated with protection from malaria. BSA-linked α-gal with Matrix-M adjuvant elicited IgG/IgM in knockout mice but failed to provide sporozoite challenge protection.

articles.headline: Integration of new tools with traditional approaches needed for malaria progress
articles.title_original: Advances in population-based interventions to control falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Glossop SE, Peto TJ, Adhikari B
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: Review of population-level prevention strategies showing malaria case decline from 2000-2015 now plateaued. Insecticide-treated nets made the largest mortality reduction but face insecticide resistance and suboptimal access. Chemoprevention protects tens of millions, threatened by drug resistance. R21/Matrix-M vaccine shows greater efficacy and lower cost than RTS,S/AS01. Next-generation monoclonal antibodies and gene drives offer future potential. Multifaceted integration combining new and traditional strategies recommended.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic P. knowlesi detected in Mursala Island; mainland transmission low
articles.title_original: Submicroscopic Burden of Zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi Malaria on Mursala Island and Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Transmission in Mainland North Sumatra, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Lubis IND, Permatasari R, Siahaan L, Cahyadi RAD, Nainggolan IRA, Syafutri RD, Sinambela MN, Jauharah S, Lestari A, Theodora M, Prameswari H, Piera KA, Barber BE, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ
articles.details: Ultrasensitive RT-PCR applied to febrile patients: 64 on Mursala Island, 947 from three mainland districts in North Sumatra. Mursala: 7/64 (10.9%) had P. knowlesi (5 agricultural workers, 2 with severe WHO-defined anemia), 2/64 (3.1%) had P. vivax; all missed by microscopy and rapid tests. Mainland: P. knowlesi undetected; PCR found 26% with Plasmodium species (17.5% P. vivax, 7.5% P. falciparum), with 30% submicroscopic.

articles.headline: Malaria rapid diagnostic test accuracy varies by transmission level and demographics
articles.title_original: Varying Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test Accuracy by Regional Transmission Level and Demographics in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Wiener D, Seth MD, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Popkin-Hall ZR, Giesbrecht D, Bakari C, Lyimo B, Pereus D, Francis F, Mbwambo D, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Sheahan TP, Parr JB, Bailey JA, Ishengoma DS, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Comparison of three national standard mRDTs and one research mRDT against qPCR in Tanzania (2021 cross-sectional study). Research test underperformed overall with poor sensitivity across transmission strata. mRDT agreement and diagnostic performance differed significantly by age, sex, and regional malaria transmission intensity. Results highlight test accuracy variance and potential measurement bias affecting malaria prevalence estimates.

articles.headline: Uridine supplementation reduces parasitemia in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: Temporal multi-tissue profiling reveals metabolites linked to immune response and malaria parasitemia control.
articles.authors: Teklemichael AA, Wu J, Bohrnsen E, Xu F, Stadler RV, Obiorah T, Brandes NT, LaCroix IS, Bosio CM, Schwarz B, Su XZ
articles.details: Temporal metabolomic profiling (LC-MS/MS) of C57BL/6n mice infected with four P. yoelii strains and P. berghei ANKA; samples collected days 4 and 6. Glycolysis intermediates (bisphosphoglycerate, phosphoenolpyruvate, dihydroxyacetone phosphate) were negatively correlated with parasitemia. Uridine supplementation in PbA-infected mice significantly reduced parasitemia and MCP-1 cytokine levels; uridine also lowered P. falciparum parasitemia in vitro.

articles.headline: South Korea reports 620 malaria cases; clusters concentrated near North Korea border
articles.title_original: Individual Malaria Case and Case Cluster Status in the Capital Region of the Republic of Korea, 2024.
articles.authors: Ko M, Seo YJ, Kim H, Hwang K
articles.details: Analysis of malaria cases in Korean capital region, 2022-2024. In 2024: 620 cases reported (94.1% of nationwide total), slight decrease from 2023 but 78.7% increase from 2022. 47 case clusters identified (5 in military units); concentrated near North Korean border, particularly northwestern Gyeonggi, Ganghwa-gun, and Incheon. Primary risk factors: residence (57.8%), work (12.9%), military service (12.9%), outdoor leisure activities. Post-COVID social resumption and enhanced surveillance linked to rise.

articles.headline: Barriers to multiple-therapy malaria treatment adoption in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Barriers and facilitators to the adoption of multiple first-line therapies for management of uncomplicated malaria in Tanzania: a multi-method qualitative study.
articles.authors: Ntissi HH, Itaeli A, Sangeda RZ, Bwire GM
articles.details: Multi-method qualitative study combining desk review of national malaria data (2020-2024) and semi-structured interviews with policymakers, regulators, and healthcare providers. Malaria cases declined from 8.9 million (2020) to 7.2 million before resurging to 8.1 million (2023); children <5 represented 34% of cases and 46% of deaths. Case incidence in <5 declined significantly (p=0.011) but mortality showed no improvement (p=0.802). Artemether-lumefantrine comprised 73.5% of antimalarial imports versus 2.6% for dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine. Barriers to multiple first-line therapies included high cost, limited provider training, and weak supply chains. The authors conclude that while multiple first-line therapies are promising for prolonging artemisinin-based combination therapy efficacy, implementation requires diversified imports and strengthened supply chains.

articles.headline: Imported malaria remains elimination barrier in Pemba Island, Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Epidemiological and entomological analysis of imported malaria in Pemba Island, Zanzibar: a study from 2020-2022.
articles.authors: Ali AO, Ali AM, Mwema MF, Mlacha YP
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of surveillance data (2020-2022) in Pemba Island, Zanzibar. Of 2,646 reported cases, 1,411 (53.3%) were classified as imported, significantly higher in males (57.3%, p<0.0001) and adults >18 years (68%, p<0.0001). Main sources were mainland Tanzania (Tanga 61%, Dar es Salaam 12%, Coastal region 10%). Importation rates were lower in Micheweni (IRR=0.48, p=0.001) and higher in Mkoani (IRR=2.45, p<0.001). Odds of importation were significantly higher in 2021 (OR=3.58, p<0.001) and 2022 (OR=5.62, p<0.001) versus 2020. Significant association found between Human Biting Rate and imported cases (95% CI 13.29-156.32, p=0.021). The authors conclude imported cases represent a persistent barrier to elimination.

articles.headline: Somalia: critically low intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Factors associated with intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp-SP) use for malaria during pregnancy in Somalia: a multilevel analysis of the 2020 demographic and health survey.
articles.authors: Ali AO, Abdillahi HA, Nadarajah S, Muse AH, Hared YA
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 7,124 women from the 2020 Somalia Demographic and Health Survey, examining self-reported sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine use during pregnancy. Overall reported use was critically low at 6.3%, with 69% of women reporting no antenatal care visits. Multilevel analysis revealed nulliparous women had lower odds of reported use (AOR=0.6, 95% CI 0.4-0.9), women with 4+ antenatal visits had lower odds (AOR=0.38, 95% CI 0.25-0.58), women with unemployed husbands had higher odds (AOR=1.6, 95% CI 1.32-2.1), and nomadic women had higher odds than rural (AOR=3.7, 95% CI 2.2-6.4). Community-level factors explained 7.4% of variance. The authors conclude that critically low use is fundamentally driven by very low antenatal care attendance (31%).

articles.headline: Machine learning improves malaria risk prediction in displaced children
articles.title_original: From risk factors to predictive modelling: applying machine learning to childhood malaria surveillance in resource-limited settings.
articles.authors: Ashaolu JO, Akanji TS, Ayansola VI, Olawale-Succes OO, Sunday AJ, Some SYM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 693 under-5 children from Nigerian internally displaced persons camps, collecting sociodemographic data, household conditions, malaria knowledge, and rapid diagnostic test outcomes. Four machine-learning models were evaluated on 70:30 train-test split. Malaria prevalence was 68.5%. Key risk factors included caregiver lack of education (aOR=3.23, p=0.026) and female caregiver (aOR=0.53, p=0.024). Random Forest performed best (AUC=0.892), identifying caregiver occupation and residential camp as most significant predictors. A knowledge-practice gap was observed: 60.3% of caregivers had prevention knowledge but only 2% used bed nets. The authors conclude that Random Forest models significantly improve malaria risk prediction precision and enable targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Indonesia and Papua New Guinea forge One-Island malaria control
articles.title_original: Two Nations, One Front: Indonesia and Papua New Guinea forge a One-Island approach to fight persistent malaria on New Guinea.
articles.authors: Prameswari HD, Kisomb J, Mapira P, Keven JB, Susanto H, Isa R, Supriyanto D, Garjito TA, Syafruddin D, Ndoen E, Iswahyudi, Firmansyah, Permana F, Chin N, Lobo NF, Walker ED, Laihad FJ, Das S, Hawley WA, Elyazar IR
articles.details: Indonesia and Papua New Guinea implementing a coordinated 'One-Island approach' to malaria control on the shared island of New Guinea to address persistent malaria.

articles.headline: Malaria and dengue co-distribution patterns guide vector control
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal co-distribution and time lagged cross correlation of malaria and dengue in Loreto, Peru.
articles.authors: Carrasco-Escobar G, Carcamo PM, Kaplan SR, Quispe JM, McCord GC, Benmarhnia T
articles.details: Time-lagged cross-correlation analysis of district-level dengue and malaria time series in Loreto, Peru (2000-2021). Growing number of districts reporting both diseases over time. Maximum cross-correlation coefficients varied in magnitude and direction between districts. In Northwest, increases in malaria often preceded dengue increases; in Northeast, malaria increases preceded dengue decreases. Spatial correlation found in Northwest districts. The authors conclude that identification of districts with strong dengue-malaria associations can inform targeted integrated interventions and that distinct association patterns can inform future studies of disease drivers.

articles.headline: Novel glutamyl-tRNA synthetase inhibitors as antimalarial drug leads
articles.title_original: Rational design of novel Plasmodium falciparum glutamyl-tRNA synthetase inhibitors for the development of next-generation antimalarial drugs.
articles.authors: Nofal W
articles.details: Structure-based virtual screening and molecular dynamics simulations (100 ns) to identify P. falciparum glutamyl-tRNA synthetase inhibitors from the MeFSAT chemical library. Initial screening of 1,830 compounds identified two lead candidates: MSID000152 (binding affinity -10.4 kcal/mol, mean RMSD 3.94 Å) and MSID000974 (binding affinity -10.1 kcal/mol, mean RMSD 2.35 Å), compared to chloroquine control (RMSD 2.39 Å). MSID000152 demonstrated favorable MMPB/GBSA binding free energy. The authors identify both compounds as promising antimalarial leads warranting further in vitro and in vivo investigation.

articles.headline: Placental malaria: prevalence and perinatal outcomes in Africa
articles.title_original: Prevalence, predictors and adverse perinatal outcomes of placental malaria in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Alemayehu A, Kiya GT, Berhanu S, Zeynudin A, Beyene J, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 178 observational studies (2000-2024) involving 93,809 parturient women from 23 sub-Saharan African countries. Overall pooled prevalence of placental malaria was 25.1% (95% CI 22.0-28.3), varying by diagnostic method: RDT 16.9%, microscopy 19.1%, PCR 24.8%, histopathology 34.8%. Key predictors included younger age (OR=2.50), primigravidae (OR=2.65), lack of insecticide-treated net use (OR=2.11), and non-attendance of antenatal care (OR=7.43). Pooled adverse outcome prevalences included low birth weight (12.7%), maternal anemia (39.0%), preterm delivery (11.2%), and congenital malaria (7.6%). Placental malaria increased odds of maternal anemia (OR=2.18) and low birth weight (OR=1.71). The authors conclude that placental malaria requires concerted multidimensional approaches targeting identified predictors.

articles.headline: P. vivax India may need artemisinin-based treatment shift
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax elimination from India may need therapeutic efficacy studies - Informed shift to artemisinin-based treatment.
articles.authors: Kathpalia M, Kojom Foko LP, Rawat P, Chaturvedi R, Rahi M, Sharma A
articles.details: Review synthesizing clinical, molecular, and pharmacological evidence for P. vivax treatment in India, which has significantly reduced burden but faces chloroquine resistance threat. Artemisinin-based combination therapies demonstrate superior efficacy compared to chloroquine, with rapid parasite clearance, improved tolerability, and reduced relapse rates when combined with primaquine. WHO recommends changing first-line therapy if total failure rate exceeds 10%. India currently has sparse therapeutic efficacy study coverage and limited molecular surveillance data for P. vivax resistance markers. The authors argue that India should prioritize expansion of therapeutic efficacy studies and adopt artemisinin-based combination therapy to sustain elimination momentum.

articles.headline: P. berghei Rpt3 protein regulates parasite phosphatase activity
articles.title_original: Characterization of the Regulatory AAA-ATPase Subunit Rpt3 in Plasmodium berghei as an Activator of Protein Phosphatase 1.
articles.authors: Lainé C, De Witte C, Martoriati A, Farce A, Metatla I, Guerrera IC, Cailliau K, Khalife J, Pierrot C
articles.details: Characterization of P. berghei proteasome AAA-ATPase regulatory subunit Rpt3 and its interaction with Protein Phosphatase 1 catalytic subunit (PP1c). PbRpt3 enhanced PP1c activity both in vitro and in Xenopus oocyte model. PbRpt3-PP1c interaction occurs outside the proteasome; RVxF motifs involved in binding and regulatory function. Reverse genetic studies show PbRpt3 is essential during the erythrocytic cycle of P. berghei. Interactome analysis confirmed PbRpt3 belongs to the 19S regulatory particle and may interact with phospholipid-binding proteins. These findings suggest PbRpt3 plays a critical role in parasite protein degradation and cell cycle regulation.

articles.headline: Hemozoin drives pro-inflammatory macrophage response in malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria pigment hemozoin drives M1 pro-inflammatory macrophage polarization in vitro.
articles.authors: Perego F, Parapini S, Calvo-Alvarez E, Dolci M, Delbue S, Ghezzi S, Vicenzi E, Poli G, Basilico N, D'Alessandro S
articles.details: In vitro study of hemozoin effects on macrophage polarization using primary human monocyte-derived macrophages and THP-1-differentiated macrophages with M1/M2 polarization signals. In non-polarized cells, hemozoin induced M1 pro-inflammatory phenotype, increasing expression and secretion of CXCL8, TNF-α, and IL-1β. In M1/M2-stimulated conditions, hemozoin further increased CXCL8 and IL-1β in primary macrophages. Hemozoin did not affect M2 markers (PPARG expression, IL-10, or STAT6 phosphorylation). Results varied across experimental cell models. The authors conclude this first in vitro study demonstrates hemozoin's ability to promote M1 pro-inflammatory differentiation, with implications for understanding severe malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Sierra Leone: molecular diagnosis reveals asymptomatic malaria burden
articles.title_original: Molecular investigation of Plasmodium spp. in Sierra Leone: recommendations on diagnostic methods for malaria control in high-epidemic regions - a nationally representative cross-sectional epidemiological survey.
articles.authors: Zhang M, Mi Z, Wang S, Jalloh MB, Lyu P, Song X, Zhao Y, Zhang J, Xie Y, Wu S, Zheng M, Li X, Yang S, Wang B, Zhao Z, Sevalie S, Sahr F, Hou L, Chen W
articles.details: Nationally representative, age-stratified cross-sectional survey of 2,112 individuals aged ≥2 years in Sierra Leone (rainy season 2022), tested for Plasmodium infection via quantitative PCR with species identification on 400 samples. Plasmodium qPCR positivity was 63.0% (95% CI 61.0-65.1), regionally weighted 62.9% (58.3-67.4). Among 400 positive samples, 97.75% were P. falciparum; 20.5% carried multiple species. Positivity rates significantly lower in individuals ≥60 years and higher in males; healthcare workers had lowest rate. 18.6% of positives showed low parasitemia; 93.5% of infected were asymptomatic. The authors conclude that high prevalence of asymptomatic, low-density infections among adults indicates a substantial transmission reservoir and emphasize molecular diagnosis importance for surveillance.

articles.headline: Angiogenic protein markers predict newborn growth in malaria pregnancy
articles.title_original: Association between peripheral markers in women with malaria in pregnancy and small newborns: A cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Dos Santos MI, Carvalho Júnior AR, de Oliveira GM, Gomes LC, Prates Syed WA, Separovic EPM, Epiphanio S, Wunderlich G, Marinho CRF, Dombrowski JG
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 386 pregnant women with/without malaria in Amazon Brazil (2013-2015). Measured 9 angiogenic proteins in plasma at delivery. Tie2, sFlt1, sENG, and leptin associated with newborn growth parameters; sFlt1 associated with weight/length in P. vivax infections, while Tie2, sFlt1, and leptin associated with length in P. falciparum infections.

articles.headline: HBV-malaria co-infection shows antagonistic replication in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Hepatitis B Virus and Plasmodium falciparum Co-Infection Among Pregnant Women in Gabon: Prevalence, Biochemical Impact, and Antagonistic Replication Dynamics.
articles.authors: Andeme Eyi AS, Mikelet Boussoukou IP, Omouessi ST, Ondh Obame JA, Makoyo Komba O, Djoba Siawaya JF, Ndeboko B
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 222 pregnant women in Gabon (May 2022–May 2023). HBV prevalence 4.05%, P. falciparum 26.1%, co-infection 1.35%. Inverse relationship observed between HBsAg levels and parasitemia (ρ = −0.5, p = 0.67, not significant), suggesting potential antagonistic replication dynamics that may modulate disease severity in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Dietary compounds modulate American vector susceptibility to murine malaria
articles.title_original: Dietary para-aminobenzoic acid, uric acid, and antibiotics modulate the susceptibility of Anopheles darlingi and Anopheles albimanus to Plasmodium berghei.
articles.authors: Costa BA, Godoy RSM, Henrique-Gomes L, Santana RAG, Justiniano SCB, Lopes SCP, Monteiro WM, de Melo GC, Secundino NFC, Pimenta PFP
articles.details: Experimental study of An. darlingi and An. albimanus mosquitoes infected with P. berghei, treated with para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), uric acid, or antibiotics. Dietary PABA influenced susceptibility in both species. Sporozoites detected in hemolymph by post-infection day 28 but not in salivary glands.

articles.headline: No k13 mutations detected in Ethiopian P. falciparum isolates
articles.title_original: Preliminary analysis of the conserved Plasmodium falciparum k13 gene in Arbaminch and Mirab Abaya, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Jote K, Gebrehiwot Y, Abebe A, Kipayko C, Dieng CC, Lo E, Golassa L, Chala B
articles.details: Molecular epidemiology study of 52 P. falciparum samples from Arbaminch and Mirab Abaya, Ethiopia (May–December 2023). Sanger sequencing of k13 propeller domain found no mutations, indicating high genetic conservation (pairwise similarity 0.982). No k13 mutations detected, suggesting continued effectiveness of artemisinin-based combination therapy in the region.

articles.headline: Multi-epitope antigen shows promise for rapid falciparum malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: In silico design and validation of a Plasmodium falciparum multi-epitope diagnostic antigen (PfMEDA1) for the diagnosis of falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Moyeh MN, Shey RA, Mbulli AI, Dieudonne NL, Sofeu-Feugaing DD, Yega VA, Talla RN, Soh-Bang C, Sherinyuy BC, Ngengang AC, Chick JA, Mbacham WF
articles.details: In silico design and validation of PfMEDA1, a chimeric protein from 5 immunogenic Plasmodium antigens, tested against 223 infected and 81 non-infected samples from Cameroon. Sensitivity 89.69% (95% CI 84.93–93.35), specificity 75.31% (95% CI 64.47–84.22), Cohen kappa 0.64. Protein predicted soluble, stable, and immunogenic; shows potential but requires field validation.

articles.headline: Erosion control pits drive malaria outbreak in Uganda
articles.title_original: Malaria outbreak facilitated by increased vector-breeding sites from erosion control pits sustained by intermittent rainfall, Mbale District, Uganda, January-June 2019.
articles.authors: Bahizi G, Monje F, Kadobera D, Bulage L, Ario AR
articles.details: Outbreak investigation in Mbale District, Uganda (January–June 2019) identified 8,827 cases (attack rate 33%). Case-control study (150 pairs): stagnant water in erosion control pits strongly associated with infection (OR=18, 95% CI 7.0–50); bed net use protective (OR=0.17, 95% CI 0.07–0.38); long-sleeve clothing protective (OR=0.19, 95% CI 0.08–0.3). An. gambiae predominant vector.

articles.headline: Metagenomics identifies multiple viral causes of non-malaria febrile illness
articles.title_original: NOMAD: metagenomic characterisation of the viral pathogen composition in outbreaks of non-malaria acute febrile illness cases.
articles.authors: Kulohoma BW, Ng'eno I
articles.details: Methodological study using profile Hidden Markov Models on metagenomic datasets (Ebola, hepatitis C, chikungunya, arboviruses) to characterize viral pathogen composition in non-malaria acute febrile illness. Approach identifies multiple viral etiological agents within single samples, providing granular perspective for improving disease surveillance and case management in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Pyrimidine biosynthesis enzymes essential for P. falciparum survival
articles.title_original: Genetic analysis of pyrimidine biosynthetic enzymes in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rajaram K, Sievert ML, Elahi R, Dillard LB, Blauwkamp J, Absalon S, Prigge ST
articles.details: Genetic analysis of P. falciparum pyrimidine biosynthetic enzymes (PfATC, PfCPSII, PfDHO) showed that PfATC knockdown is lethal and N-terminal truncation impairs growth; PfCPSII essential for proliferation; PfDHO deletion causes uracil auxotrophy. Findings identify multiple vulnerable nodes in pyrimidine biosynthesis as antimalarial drug targets.

articles.headline: Novel heterospirocyclic compounds active against resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Novel heterospirocyclic antimalarials with activity against artemisinin- and multidrug-resistant P. falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Theodoridis L, Giannangelo C, El-Saafin F, Prabhath MR, Macraild CA, Sharma P, Merino D, Creek DJ, Carvalho TG
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Compounds 25 and 26 demonstrate antimalarial activity within 24 hours against drug-resistant P. falciparum strains. Untargeted metabolomics suggests mechanism involves disruption of pyrimidine biosynthesis and hemoglobin catabolism. No major toxicity in human kidney/hepatic cell lines, indicating specificity. Compounds represent promising avenue for addressing drug-resistant malaria.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria case highlights importance of rapid diagnosis post-travel
articles.title_original: [Cerebral malaria after travel to Indonesia].
articles.authors: Pollstergaard KK, Vestergaard LS, Aftab H, Poulsen A
articles.details: Case report of Danish child with P. falciparum malaria acquired in Indonesia without chemoprophylaxis. Initial misdiagnosis as influenza delayed recognition; patient presented with cerebral malaria and multiorgan failure requiring intensive care. Treated with IV artesunate with full recovery. Highlights need for travel advice, early symptom recognition, and rapid testing in endemic areas.

articles.headline: Spatial analysis identifies malaria hotspots across West African countries
articles.title_original: Malaria hotspots and risk factors among children under-five years of age across eight West African countries: A geospatial analysis of DHS data.
articles.authors: Aidé ES, Kazienga A, Adegboye O, Sondo P, Tinto H
articles.details: Geospatial analysis of DHS data from 8 West African countries on malaria prevalence in children under 5. Insecticide-treated net use associated with reduced infection odds in 4 countries; age <2 years protective across all countries. Marked spatial heterogeneity: high predicted prevalence in Benin/Cote d'Ivoire, lower in Ghana/Liberia. Findings support context-specific spatial surveillance and control approaches.

articles.headline: Relative humidity strongly predicts malaria incidence in Gambia
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the significant association between Climatic Variables and Malaria Cases in the Upper River Region of the Gambia.
articles.authors: Okafor UA, Yaffa S, D'Alessandro U, Ojeh VN
articles.details: Time-series analysis of monthly malaria cases (under 5 and over 5 years) with meteorological data from Gambia, 2011–2022. Relative humidity increased malaria odds by 3–4% in under-5s and 6–9% in over-5s; maximum temperature decreased odds by 20–14% in under-5s and 21–15% in over-5s (all at p<0.01). Findings implicate humidity as major influencing factor for malaria incidence, enabling climate-informed control programs.

articles.headline: P. vivax genetic adaptation in Duffy-negative African hosts
articles.title_original: Adaptive evolution of Plasmodium vivax in Duffy-negative hosts: insights from East African genomes.
articles.authors: Ford A, Dieng CC, Abagero BR, Yan G, Yewhalaw D, Janies DA, Lo E
articles.details: Genome-wide SNP analysis of 110 Ethiopian P. vivax isolates (38 Duffy-negative, 72 Duffy-positive) found higher genetic variation in Duffy-positive strains (477,561 vs 197,461 SNPs). Selection signatures differed by host genotype, with Duffy-negative parasites showing reduced diversity but high conservation across the genome, suggesting strong evolutionary constraints for invasion of Duffy-negative erythrocytes. Amino acid substitutions in invasion ligands were common in Duffy-positive but largely absent in Duffy-negative isolates.

articles.headline: Climate change impacts mosquito survival but not P. vivax infectivity
articles.title_original: Predicted future climate scenarios impact survival and immune response but not Plasmodium vivax infection in Anopheles aquasalis (Diptera, Culicidae), the primary vector of coastal Central and South America and the Caribbean Islands.
articles.authors: Silva AS, Leda RL, Cella W, Mwangi VI, Santana RAG, Silva-Neto AV, da Silva MNP, Pereira RS, Lopes SCP, Melo GC, Pimenta PFP, Almeida-Val FF, Val AL, Baía-da-Silva DC, Monteiro WM
articles.details: Experimental study of An. aquasalis mosquito survival and immunity under IPCC climate projections. Female mosquitoes infected with P. vivax showed infection rates of 54–68% across all scenarios with no significant differences. Mosquito survival declined significantly under RCP 6.0 (18.6% by day 7, p<0.001) and RCP 8.5 (6.5%) versus control (40%). Immune gene expression (Attacin, Defensin) showed decreased activation under higher temperature and CO₂ stress.

articles.headline: P. falciparum treatment challenges in nonendemic countries: resistance and toxicity
articles.title_original: Treatment of Plasmodium falciparum malaria in the nonendemic setting: a narrative review of toxicity, failure, and emerging resistance.
articles.authors: Bottieau E, Mutsaers M, Balerdi-Sarasola L, Yansouni CP, Rosanas-Urgell A
articles.details: Narrative review of P. falciparum malaria treatment in travelers from nonendemic countries using artemisinin-based combination therapy and atovaquone-proguanil. Reports emerging challenges including artemisinin-related hemolysis and treatment failures possibly linked to drug resistance. Identifies factors distinguishing travelers (lack of immunity, advanced age, comorbidities) from endemic-area residents that affect treatment response. Proposes management adaptations including structured clinical follow-up for toxicity and failure detection plus systematic genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: High community awareness of traditional malaria remedies in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Assessment of knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding the use of traditional medicine for treating malaria among residents of Woreta Town, Amhara Regional State, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Tadesse TY, Ayele TM, Eshetie A, Yazie TS, Hammesso WW, Dagnew FN, Zelalem AE
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study of 390 residents in Woreta Town, Ethiopia assessing knowledge and practices regarding traditional malaria treatments. Among participants, 66.4% believed visiting a modern practitioner after traditional medicine was beneficial, and 65.1% were aware of anti-malarial plants including Bisana (27.9%), Girawa (26.7%), and Nechshinkurt (14.6%). Additionally, 19.28% reported awareness of animal byproducts such as goat bile, and 3.1% mentioned traditional preparations like Yekoye Kibe.

articles.headline: AL shows continued low efficacy; DP and AS-PYR more promising
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, and artesunate-pyronaridine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Burkina Faso, 2020-2021.
articles.authors: Gansane A, Tarama C, Lingani M, Debe S, Tiendrebeogo F, Kinda R, Sagnon A, Ganou A, Rouamba T, Sombie S, Some H, Kiendrebeogo S, Bonko MA, Tougri G, Kompaore CS, Wiesner L, Eddis C, Horton B, Kennedy C, Bajic M, Pierre-Louis E, Kahn A, Rosillo S, Patel DS, McCaffery JN, Plucinski MM, Laird V, Ahmed J, Tinto H, Valea I, Moriarty LF
articles.details: Therapeutic efficacy trial in Burkina Faso (1,078 children aged 6 months–12 years) comparing artemether-lumefantrine (AL), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), and artesunate-pyronaridine (AS-PYR) for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Uncorrected 28-day efficacy was 57.1–64.4% for AL, 96.6–98.9% for DP, and 82.2–90.7% for AS-PYR. PCR-corrected efficacy exceeded 90% WHO threshold for all drugs except AL in Nanoro (89.4%). No artemisinin-resistance markers were detected. Authors conclude AS-PYR and DP show promise for multiple first-line therapy.

articles.headline: 9th International Conference on Plasmodium vivax Research 2025
articles.title_original: Abstracts of the 9th International Conference on Plasmodium vivax Research (ICPvR) 2025.
articles.details: Conference abstract collection from the 9th International Conference on Plasmodium vivax Research (ICPvR) held in 2025. Published proceedings of presentations and research on P. vivax malaria.

articles.headline: hsRDT improves case detection but shows limited sensitivity in Myanmar
articles.title_original: Assessment of a highly sensitive rapid diagnostic test for reactive case detection of Plasmodium Falciparum infections in Myanmar.
articles.authors: Khine SK, Aung PP, Linn NYY, Monti F, Khin NN, Awabdeh D, Topcuoglu E, Hwang J, Tun KM
articles.details: Prospective reactive case detection study in Myanmar (2017–2018) among 1,990 contacts of 51 index P. falciparum cases, comparing highly sensitive RDT (hsRDT) and conventional RDT (cRDT) against ultra-sensitive PCR. hsRDT detected 3.9% of contacts positive versus 1.4% by cRDT and 8.6% by usPCR. hsRDT sensitivity was 27.9% (95% CI 18.2–39.6%) with specificity 98.8%, while cRDT showed 13.7% sensitivity and 100% specificity. Authors conclude hsRDT improvements are modest and use should consider field feasibility.

articles.headline: Malaria in Burundian children imposes substantial household economic burden
articles.title_original: Economic burden of malaria in children under the age of five in Burundi.
articles.authors: Niyibitegeka F, Manirakiza A, Hezagira N, Sinarinzi P, Abraham P, Devine A
articles.details: Micro-costing study at 15 sites across Burundi estimating cost per malaria episode in children under five. Average societal cost was US$12.1 per episode, with households bearing US$3.3 in direct out-of-pocket costs and US$4.7 in productivity losses (8 days caregiving). Health system costs were US$2.6 for outpatient and US$44.0 for inpatient treatment. Household costs represented 44% of monthly individual income, while health system costs were nearly half current government per-capita health expenditure.

articles.headline: Fractional order model shows malaria dynamics in Northern Cyprus
articles.title_original: Fractal fractional approach to investigate the forecasting and dynamics of malaria disease for early control precaution.
articles.authors: Farman M, Hurdoganoglu U, Hincal N, Gokbulut N, Guler E, Suer K, Sambas A, Hincal E
articles.details: Mathematical modeling study developing a deterministic eight-compartmental fractional order malaria model with human and mosquito subpopulations for Northern Cyprus. Analysis confirmed local asymptotic stability and global stability using Lyapunov functions. Numerical simulations across different fractal order values revealed increasing susceptible human populations and declining infectious mosquito numbers, with authors recommending future methodology adjustments to strengthen malaria control efforts.

articles.headline: Spatiotemporal malaria mapping reveals P. vivax expansion in India
articles.title_original: Mapping malaria risk in India between 2019 and 2023: a tool for the public to track malaria.
articles.authors: Sam AK, Keshri N, Bhowmick IP, Anvikar A, Phuleria HC
articles.details: Spatiotemporal analysis mapping malaria risk across 700+ Indian districts 2019–2023 using district surveillance data, socioeconomic factors, meteorology, and land-use information. The study recorded 0.33 million cases in 2019 with pandemic-related decline; P. falciparum remained widespread in North-eastern and Central India while P. vivax showed emerging geographical expansion northeastward post-pandemic. Scheduled castes, scheduled tribes, and economically marginalized populations were most vulnerable, with transmission associated with drinking water practices, maternal education, and healthcare accessibility.

articles.headline: Spatial analysis reveals bed net and anemia patterns in Niger malaria
articles.title_original: Exploring spatial variation and multilevel modeling of malaria prevalence among children aged 6-59 months based on RDT in Niger: Insights for public health decision-making.
articles.authors: Mulugeta SS, Masresha BM, Ejigu LA
articles.details: Multilevel spatial analysis of 2021 Niger Malaria Indicator Survey data in 4,724 children aged 6–59 months, with 1,121 (23.7%) RDT-positive for malaria. Malaria prevalence showed significant spatial clustering (Moran's I = 0.434, p < 0.001). Multilevel logistic regression identified child age, anemia levels, maternal education, bed net use (treated and untreated), and region as significant covariates. Model III provided optimal fit. Authors conclude improved insecticide-treated bed net coverage and regionally tailored interventions are needed.

articles.headline: Ethnicity, occupation, and malaria history delay care-seeking
articles.title_original: Drivers of delay in reported malaria care-seeking in Myanmar and Thailand.
articles.authors: Htwe KZZ, Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Soe MT, Petchvijit P, Aung PP, Khamsiriwatchara A, Lawpoolsri S, Cui L, Parker DM, Kyaw MP, Sattabongkot J, Kaewkungwal J
articles.details: Longitudinal study in Myanmar (n=2,960, mean fever duration 1.8 days) and Thailand (n=15,576, mean fever duration 2.9 days) examining factors associated with delay in malaria care-seeking. In Myanmar, longer delays were associated with Shan ethnicity (TR 1.48, 95% CI 1.41–1.55), farming occupation (TR 1.12), P. vivax diagnosis (TR 1.23), and uncertain malaria history; daily bed net use shortened delays. In Thailand, farmers (TR 1.07), pre-primary education (TR 1.02), P. falciparum (TR 1.27) and P. vivax (TR 1.20) diagnosis were associated with delays.

articles.headline: MAP-X maps protein complex dynamics in P. falciparum blood stages
articles.title_original: MAP-X reveals distinct protein complex dynamics across Plasmodium falciparum blood stages.
articles.authors: Pazicky S, Tjia S, Farias GB, Piwon N, Philip N, Sobota RM, Waters AP, Gilberger TW, Bozdech Z
articles.details: This study introduces MAP-X (meltome-assisted profiling of protein complexes), which uses thermal proteome profiling to map protein-protein interactions in intact P. falciparum cells across seven timepoints of the intraerythrocytic developmental cycle. The method predicted more than 20,000 interactions, resolving conserved complexes and previously unreported associations, and identified moonlighting subunits that dissociate from their native complex to assume different functions. These findings provide a resource for understanding Plasmodium biology and cellular physiology at a proteome-wide level.

articles.headline: RIFIN subset linked to severe malaria risk reduction via LILRB1
articles.title_original: A subset of Plasmodium falciparum RIFINs is linked to severe malaria risk reduction and engages LILRB1 through a conserved structural motif.
articles.authors: Kassegne K, Shen HM, Chen SB, Xu SJ, Li J, Xu B, Deitsch KW, Wang Y, Zhou XN, Chen JH
articles.details: This immunoproteomic study profiled anti-RIFIN antibody responses in malaria-infected individuals and correlated them with disease severity. Researchers identified a RIFIN subset associated with reduced risk of severe malaria through logistic regression analyses, and used surface plasmon resonance, structural modeling, and crystallographic analyses to determine that these RIFINs engage the inhibitory receptor LILRB1 through a conserved structural motif anchored by disulfide-bonded cysteines. The findings suggest that a bifunctional RIFIN subset balances clinical immunity and immune evasion, potentially guiding vaccine design strategies to overcome antigenic diversity.

articles.headline: HbS allele frequency 6.72% in uncomplicated falciparum malaria, Chad
articles.title_original: Allele frequency of Hemoglobin S among patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in N'Djamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Yamti SN, Akouya A, Dezoumbe K, Tawe L, Kgakatsi SRT, Djibagaou AD, Routoubé M, Cappelli G, Atturo S, Dongho GBD, Russo G, Dzomo GRT, Colizzi V, Paganotti GM
articles.details: This cross-sectional study analyzed dry blood spot samples from 320 patients with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in N'Djamena, Chad (approximately two-thirds female), determining HbS allele frequency by PCR-RFLP. The HbS variant allele frequency was 6.72%. No significant association was found between different HbS genotypes and parasite density. While the study provides the first HbS allele frequency data from Chad, the authors note this finding may not be representative of the wider population since subjects were recruited in a malaria survey.

articles.headline: Ivermectin MDA reduces scabies with indirect community benefit
articles.title_original: Direct and indirect protection against scabies through ivermectin mass drug administration designed for malaria in Mozambique: a substudy nested within a cluster-randomised, controlled trial.
articles.authors: Furnival-Adams J, Houana A, Saute F, Elobolobo E, Rudd M, Nicolas P, Montaña J, Martinho S, Mundaca H, Mbanze J, Soares A, Imputiua S, Ruiz-Castillo P, Ribes M, Sanz A, Mamudo Salé M, Macucha A, Casellas A, Lopez V, Vegove V, Mutepa V, Munguambe H, Xerinda A, Materula F, Rabinovich NR, Engelman D, Chaccour C
articles.details: This cluster-randomized trial nested within the BOHEMIA malaria trial in Mozambique randomized 1951 participants from 39 clusters to ivermectin 400 μg/kg, ivermectin plus livestock dosing, or albendazole control for 3 months. Scabies prevalence in the ivermectin group decreased from 8.10% (baseline) to 2.11% at 3 months, compared to no change in the albendazole group (12.30% baseline to 13.71% at 3 months). The adjusted odds ratio for scabies in ivermectin versus albendazole groups at 3 months was 0.18 (95% CI 0.07–0.45, p=0.0002), with significant effects in both directly exposed participants and indirectly exposed children under 5 years. No serious safety signals were detected.

articles.headline: Liver stage immunity as key target for malaria prevention
articles.title_original: Exposing the hidden: establishing immunity to Plasmodium liver stage infection.
articles.authors: Quin J, Urrutia Iturritza M, Kina ÜY, Matuschewski K, Ankarklev J
articles.details: This article reviews mechanisms of immune response against the Plasmodium liver stage, the hidden gateway for parasite entry into the human host. The authors emphasize that blocking parasite entrance or exit through the liver stage can prevent both symptomatic blood-stage infection and onward transmission, and highlight that robust immunity against pre-erythrocytic stages of P. falciparum fails to develop during natural exposure in malaria-endemic countries. The article discusses how expanding knowledge of immune mechanisms is shaping current and future intervention strategies against malaria.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria without fever: case of atypical presentation
articles.title_original: When malaria hides: a case report of sudden coma without prior fever in cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Muskananfola FV, Purnama A
articles.details: This case report describes a 19-year-old woman from a malaria-endemic area who presented with sudden unconsciousness and rigor without prior fever, though fever developed during hospitalization. Rapid diagnostic testing was positive, and microscopy confirmed P. falciparum infection consistent with cerebral malaria. The patient was treated with intravenous artesunate followed by primaquine and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, and improved with confirmed parasite clearance on follow-up microscopy.

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge gaps vary by education, urban-rural location in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Evaluating the General Public's Knowledge of Malaria: A Nationally Representative Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Thalib HI, Khan S, Pereira M, Abouelmagd F, El Said M
articles.details: This cross-sectional study analyzed data from 18,747 Tanzanian individuals aged 15 years and older from the 2021-2022 Demographic and Health Survey to evaluate malaria-related knowledge across demographic factors. Malaria knowledge varied significantly by age, gender, education, and location (p<0.05), with highest awareness among those aged 20–24 and lowest among those over 50, and significantly greater knowledge among urban residents compared to rural counterparts. Education showed strong associations with awareness, with secondary or higher education linked to better knowledge, and male-headed households and media/mobile phone access associated with increased awareness.

articles.headline: HbS and HbC protect against severe malaria; blood group A increases risk
articles.title_original: Distribution of HbS, HbC variants, ABO blood groups in relation to malaria infection among Saudi children in Jazan region.
articles.authors: Qahtani SAA, Saleh NM, Alsubaie RS, Almutairi R, Hamid O, Elhassan LA, Abushouk A, Ahmed MS, Nasr A
articles.details: This case-control study of 587 Saudi children in Jazan region compared malaria-free controls (n=196), uncomplicated malaria (n=215), and severe malaria (n=176; median ages 4–5 years) for hemoglobin variants, ABO blood groups, and P. falciparum infection by PCR and ELISA. HbS carriers showed reduced odds of severe malaria versus controls (OR=0.40, 95% CI 0.22–0.72, p=0.002), while HbC carriers showed even stronger protection (p<0.01). Antibody analysis revealed significantly higher total IgG and IgG subclass levels in uncomplicated malaria compared to both controls and severe malaria (p<0.001), suggesting more effective immune response in uncomplicated disease.

articles.headline: CSA-adhesion inhibitory antibodies protect against placental malaria
articles.title_original: A high-throughput assay to measure antibodies that block adhesion of Plasmodium falciparum infected erythrocytes to chondroitin sulfate A.
articles.authors: Dube Y, Hasang W, Teo A, Madanitsa M, Nielsen MA, Ter Kuile FO, Aitken EH, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: This study developed a high-throughput adhesion inhibition assay using diaminofluorene to measure hemoglobin release from P. falciparum-infected erythrocytes bound to placental chondroitin sulfate A (CSA), tested in 466 Malawian pregnant women (321 infected, 145 uninfected). Adhesion inhibitory antibodies were significantly higher in multigravidae (46.3% infected, 29.9% uninfected) compared to primigravidae (9.7% infected, 15.6% uninfected, p<0.001 and p=0.04 respectively) at mid-pregnancy. Among 162 women with mid-pregnancy infection, those without placental malaria had significantly higher adhesion inhibitory antibodies (33.8%) compared to those with past placental malaria (12.5%, p=0.002).

articles.headline: IPT in school-age children reduces transmission across transmission settings
articles.title_original: Effect of malaria chemoprevention for school-age children across transmission archetypes: a modelling study.
articles.authors: Suresh J, Zimmermann M, Maiteki C, Stahlfeld A, Pratt A, Mathanga DP, Staedke SG, Laufer MK, Drakeley C, Bever C, Cohee LM
articles.details: This modeling study used an established malaria transmission model to estimate the effect of intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) in school-age children across three epidemiological archetypes (Sahelian, Central, and Southern African) at varying transmission levels (PfPR2-10 5–40%). Comparing three drug regimens (dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, artesunate-amodiaquine, sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine-amodiaquine), long-acting monthly drugs averted 70–90% of cases in school-aged children and 20–60% in younger children and adults, with greater community-level benefit at lower transmission. Shorter-acting drugs administered monthly or long-acting drugs once per school term averted 40–60% of cases in school-aged children and 15–50% in other age groups.

articles.headline: fikk, phist, surf gene families implicated in cerebral malaria and severe anemia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum gene expression signatures and antibody profiling implicate the fikk, phist, and surf multigene families in severe malaria syndromes.
articles.authors: Cummings BE, Stucke EM, Coulibaly D, Lawton JG, Sobota RS, Kone AK, Kane B, Guindo B, Tangara B, Sissoko M, Maiga F, Traore K, Diawara A, Traore A, Thera A, Dara A, Niangaly A, Daou M, Diarra I, Tolo Y, Sebastian S, Jain A, Nakajima R, Jasinskas A, Silzel EK, Shrestha B, Ouattara A, Munro JB, Laurens MB, Lyke KE, Tebben K, Bogale H, Plowe CV, Sissoko MS, Kouriba B, Takala-Harrison S, Felgner PL, Silva JC, Doumbo OK, Thera MA, Travassos MA
articles.details: This Mali case-control study used RNA-seq to compare parasite gene expression in cerebral malaria, severe malarial anemia, and concurrent disease versus uncomplicated malaria controls. While severe malarial anemia showed comparatively low differential expression, cerebral malaria and concurrent disease showed significant differential expression of multigene families (fikk, phist, and surf) implicated in cytoadherence and erythrocytic remodeling. Custom microarray profiling with acute and convalescent sera demonstrated that children with severe malaria developed antibody responses to subsets of these antigens, suggesting exposure during illness and their potential as targets for vaccine development and natural protective immunity against severe malaria.

articles.headline: Molecular markers of P. falciparum drug resistance and surveillance strategies
articles.title_original: A review on molecular markers of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Abebe W, Woldesenbet D
articles.details: This review article synthesizes knowledge of molecular markers associated with P. falciparum resistance to major antimalarial drug classes, including artemisinin (kelch13 mutations in the beta-propeller domain), atovaquone (cytochrome b mutations), chloroquine and quinolines (pfcrt, pfmdr1, plasmepsin II/III copy numbers), antifolates (dihydrofolate reductase and dihydropteroate synthase mutations), and amino alcohols (pfmdr1 and Pf Na+/H+ exchanger mutations). The authors recommend that future research employ transcriptomic and genomic technologies to identify genetic variations, proteomic analyses to understand protein interactions underlying resistance mechanisms, and investigation of combination therapies targeting multiple parasite lifecycle stages.

articles.headline: Pfhrp2/3 deletions challenge rapid malaria diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: Overcoming the challenges of Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein 2 and 3 deletions in malaria diagnosis and control.
articles.authors: Enyi EO, Nnanna JC
articles.details: This review of 83 studies (2018-2024) examined Plasmodium falciparum parasites lacking histidine-rich proteins 2 and 3, which pose challenges to standard rapid diagnostic tests used for malaria control. The review identified potential solutions including alternative diagnostic approaches and novel RDTs targeting multiple antigens.

articles.headline: PvMSP-3α genetic diversity highest in Pakistani isolates
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphisms of merozoite surface protein-3α in Plasmodium vivax isolates from Pakistan.
articles.authors: Nguyễn KO, Kang JM, Võ TC, Lê HG, Akhtar S, Nguyễn TH, Nguyễn ĐTD, Cho M, Afridi SG, Na BK
articles.details: Analysis of 129 Pakistani P. vivax isolates (2020-2021) revealed high genetic diversity in the merozoite surface protein-3α gene, driven by size polymorphisms in block I and amino acid changes in block II. Comparative analysis showed Pakistani pvmsp-3α had the highest genetic diversity globally, with haplotype network analysis indicating substantial genetic heterogeneity across global P. vivax populations.

articles.headline: PvCyRPA shows promise as P. vivax vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax cysteine-rich protective antigen (PvCyRPA), an important element for vaccine strategies targeting P. vivax, interacts with human erythrocyte surface.
articles.authors: Cebrián-Carmona J, Arévalo-Pinzón G, Barreto-Santamaria A, Gutiérrez-Ortegón DE, Gómez M, Sánchez-Mirón A, Mesa-Valle CM, Molina-Miras A, Garrido-Cárdenas JA, Patarroyo MA
articles.details: This functional study demonstrated that the P. vivax cysteine-rich protective antigen (PvCyRPA) is transcribed and translated in late schizonts and exhibits membrane association. Recombinant PvCyRPA bound to both reticulocytes and normocytes, and >90% of sera from malaria-exposed individuals recognized the protein, confirming strong antigenicity and supporting its potential as a vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: P. yoelii infection inhibits colon cancer in mice
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection inhibits colon cancer in mice via modulation of hypoxia-inducible factor-1α expression.
articles.authors: Jiao Y, Ni Y, Ge L, Zhan H, Xie F, Hao N, Xuan L, Xia H, Fang Q, Tao Z
articles.details: An experimental study in BALB/c mice with subcutaneous CT26.WT colon cancer cells compared tumor-bearing controls with mice infected intraperitoneally with P. yoelii (5 × 10⁶/mL). By day 16 of P. yoelii infection, infected mice showed significantly downregulated HIF-1α and VEGFA expression (P < 0.01 each), upregulated VHL and p53 (P < 0.01 each), and tumor growth cessation with significant shrinkage (P < 0.001).

articles.headline: Malaria detected in 17% of screened pregnant migrants
articles.title_original: Malaria and other neglected infections in pregnant migrants: A retrospective study in Northern Italy.
articles.authors: Todaro L, Sforza A, Zaffagnini A, Cattaneo P, Mazzi C, Rizzolo F, Scarso S, Angheben A, Zammarchi L, Gobbi F, Buonfrate D, Ursini T
articles.details: A retrospective study of 85 pregnant migrant women in Northern Italy (median age 28, 52% from Africa) using risk-based extended screening found malaria in 5/29 tested (17.2%), all asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic. Additional findings included Schistosoma serology positive in 10/36 (27.8%), Strongyloides in 5/84 (5.9%), and HTLV-1/2 in 3/60 (5.0%), highlighting significant neglected infections among this population.

articles.headline: ART-heme formation differs in resistant vs sensitive parasites
articles.title_original: Elucidating artemisinin (ART)-heme as a biomarker for ART resistance and ATP-dependent transport of ART in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Liu H, Zhou H, Cai Y, Du S, Huang Z, Xu K, Xing J
articles.details: This in vitro study of ART-sensitive (Pf3D7/PfDd2) and ART-resistant (Pf6320 carrying K13 C580Y) P. falciparum strains examined artemisinin metabolism and resistance mechanisms. ART-heme adduct formation was rapid and concentration-dependent (50-450 nM) in sensitive parasites but concentration-independent in resistant parasites, suggesting ATP-dependent transport. Inhibition of P-type ATPase and efflux transporters showed potential to enhance ART activity.

articles.headline: Review of T follicular helper cell development in malaria
articles.title_original: T follicular helper cell development during malaria.
articles.authors: Antonetti RM, Shah M, Butler NS
articles.details: This review discusses the role of T follicular helper (Tfh) cells and CD4 Th1 cells in malaria-induced immunity and their critical functions in phagocyte activation and humoral responses. The authors highlight that while understanding of conventional Tfh differentiation during Plasmodium infection is growing, little is known about malaria-associated Tfh populations with mixed phenotypes or Tfh memory cell development.

articles.headline: PfSIP2 essential for P. falciparum sporozoite hepatocyte infection
articles.title_original: Mapping Plasmodium transitions and interactions in the Anopheles female.
articles.authors: Yan Y, Verzier LH, Cheung E, Appetecchia F, March S, Craven AR, Du E, Probst AS, Rinvee TA, de Vries LE, Kauffman J, Bhatia SN, Nelson E, Singh N, Peng D, Shaw WR, Catteruccia F
articles.details: A single-cell RNA sequencing study of P. falciparum and Anopheles mosquito midgut cells across developmental stages identified key parasite-mosquito interactions. Functional analyses revealed that the parasite transcription factor PfSIP2 is essential for sporozoite infection of human hepatocytes, and parasites preferentially interact with midgut progenitor cells during epithelial crossing, with findings confirmed in field-derived parasites.

articles.headline: Novel compound 31 inhibits P. falciparum protein synthesis
articles.title_original: A Novel Antimalarial Agent that Inhibits Protein Synthesis in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Bravo P, Diamanti E, Hamed MM, Bizzarri L, Wiedemar N, Passecker A, Brancucci NMB, Albisetti A, Gumpp C, Illarionov B, Fischer M, Witschel M, Schehl T, Hahne H, Mäser P, Rottmann M, Hirsch AKH
articles.details: This drug development study identified compound 31, a 2-hydroxyphenyl benzamide, which exhibits potent activity against blood-stage and mature sexual-stage P. falciparum without human cell toxicity. Resistance selection identified a novel pfmdr1 point mutation (confirmed by CRISPR/Cas9), and proteomics showed compound 31's primary mechanism is direct binding to cytosolic ribosomal subunits, with no cross-resistance to first-line antimalarials.

articles.headline: Sickle cell trait paradoxically increases infection risk, protects young
articles.title_original: Association between sickle cell trait and Plasmodium falciparum infection: evidence for age-specific protection in a cross-sectional study in Guinea-Bissau.
articles.authors: Silva R, Rodrigues A, Arez AP, Medeiros MM
articles.details: A cross-sectional analysis of 601 blood samples from Guinea-Bissau's 2017 national malaria survey found SCT (HbAS) in 32% of individuals. SCT carriers were more likely to have microscopic P. falciparum infection (OR = 2.3, 95% CI = 1.4-3.5), with odds increasing with age (15-24 years: OR = 5.0, 95% CI = 1.8-13.8), yet infected children <5 years with SCT had lower parasitemia and higher hemoglobin.

articles.headline: Review of sex determination mechanisms in malaria gametocytes
articles.title_original: Pink or blue: sex determination in malaria gametocytes.
articles.authors: Pradel G, Petter M
articles.details: This review examines the molecular mechanisms regulating sex determination in Plasmodium gametocytes, identifying two critical checkpoints: chromatin remodeling that activates the master regulator AP2-G and drives sexual development genes, and post-transcriptional processes involving RNA-binding proteins that guide differentiation into male or female gametocytes.

articles.headline: GFAP shows promise but lacks specificity for cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Current status of the GFAP's potentials in cerebral malaria, a systematic review.
articles.authors: Tulantched DSM, Min Z
articles.details: A systematic review (24 studies: 9 human, 15 experimental) examined GFAP as a biomarker for cerebral malaria. Experimental studies consistently showed elevated GFAP expression in brain and retinal tissues correlating with neuroinflammation, but human studies showed variability, with several reporting no significant plasma GFAP increases in CM patients versus controls. Variations in sample type, disease severity, and analytical methods contributed to inconsistencies.

articles.headline: Host proteome modification in P. falciparum blood-stage development
articles.title_original: Crosstalk between malaria and host proteome during the intraerythrocytic developmental cycle.
articles.authors: Pazicky S, Bozdech Z
articles.details: Review article examining how P. falciparum remodels the host erythrocyte proteome during intraerythrocytic development. The article characterizes various roles of host red blood cell proteins in parasite growth and development within the blood stage, focusing on mechanisms of host protein engagement and modification. The review discusses the potential for targeting host proteome interactions to design host-directed antimalarial therapies as a novel intervention strategy.

articles.headline: Vesicle adaptor proteins essential for parasite invasion and development
articles.title_original: Vesicle adaptors in malaria parasites show conservation and flexibility of protein sorting machinery.
articles.authors: Cubillán-Marín J, Fröhlke U, Ramón-Zamorano G, Mainye S, Mesén-Ramírez JP, Farias GB, Höhn K, Gilberger TW, Bártfai R, Spielmann T
articles.details: Study characterized roles of vesicle adaptor proteins (AP-1, AP-3, AP-4) in Plasmodium parasites. AP-1 was required for intracellular growth, biogenesis of specialized invasion organelles, and formation of invasive progeny; AP-3 and AP-4 were required for host cell invasion. The work revealed conservation of adaptor sorting machinery between parasites and evolutionarily distant model organisms, with unconventional features including tepsin functioning with AP-1 and clathrin with AP-4.

articles.headline: Chloroquine resistance transporter expression across parasite life stages
articles.title_original: Stage-dependent expression and vacuolar localization of Plasmodium berghei chloroquine resistance transporter (CRT).
articles.authors: Korbmacher F, Rauch M, Kenthirapalan S, Kooij TWA, Maier AG, Matuschewski K
articles.details: Using CRT-mCherry tagged P. berghei parasites, researchers profiled spatio-temporal expression of the chloroquine resistance transporter during life-cycle progression. CRT was expressed during asexual blood stage growth and localized to the hemozoin-containing digestive vacuole, with abundant signal in gametocytes and ookinetes. Expression was switched off during sporogony and early liver infection but re-emerged in mature liver stages, indicating continued functions in this digestive compartment beyond asexual propagation.

articles.headline: Clinical risk scoring for artesunate treatment failure prediction
articles.title_original: Development of a clinical risk scoring system for artesunate treatment failure prediction in malaria patients.
articles.authors: Kim D, Lee DH, Song Y, Kim JS, Gwak HS
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Retrospective study of 98 adult malaria patients in South Korea treated with IV artesunate (2014-2023) identified treatment failure in 12 patients (12.2%). Multivariable logistic regression identified female sex, parasitemia >5%, and impaired consciousness as independent predictors. A risk-scoring system predicted failure probabilities of 5% (score 0), 16% (score 1), 41% (score 2), 72% (score 3), and 90% (score 4) with AUROC=0.768 (95% CI 0.605–0.931).

articles.headline: Drug resistance mechanisms and global surveillance of malaria
articles.title_original: The evolving threat of drug-resistant malaria in a globalized world.
articles.authors: Shukla S, Mani A
articles.details: Review highlighting molecular mechanisms of drug resistance in P. falciparum and its global impact on malaria control. The article emphasizes understanding the genetic basis of antimalarial drug resistance and mechanisms of action of antimalarial drugs. The review stresses the urgent need for real-time surveillance systems and discusses implications for improved treatment strategies and more effective malaria control programs.

articles.headline: Telomere shortening as biomarker in malaria infection
articles.title_original: Malaria infection and telomere length: A review.
articles.authors: Wakai TN, Fiamitia C, Oba EB, Chinedu SN, Afolabi IS
articles.details: Review examining telomere dynamics in malaria pathogenesis and their potential as biomarkers for disease severity. Acute malaria infections initiate rapid telomere degradation and accelerated cellular senescence with suppressed telomerase expression, with possible partial recovery during treatment. Prolonged Plasmodium exposure exacerbates telomere shortening via oxidative stress and inflammatory mediators. The review discusses telomere length as a potential biomarker for malaria severity and treatment outcomes.

articles.headline: Drug resistance markers in P. falciparum from Malawi
articles.title_original: Emergence of chloroquine-sensitive Plasmodium falciparum and rising resistance to first-line artemisinin partner drugs in Malawi.
articles.authors: Mazigo E, Jun H, Lee WJ, Louis JM, Syahada JH, Fitriana F, Muh F, Ahmed MA, Lu F, Han JH, Kwon TH, Lee SJ, Na S, Chun W, Park WS, Han ET, Kidima W, Han JH
articles.details: Molecular surveillance study of 1,582 blood samples from Mzuzu and Lilongwe, Malawi (December 2020–June 2021) assessed drug resistance marker genes. No resistance-associated mutations were detected in pfk13 and pfcrt genes, supporting susceptibility to artemisinin derivatives and chloroquine. The pfmdr1-NFD haplotype linked to lumefantrine resistance was present in 159/371 (42.9%) isolates. The quadruple pfdhfr-pfdhps mutant haplotype associated with high-level sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance was found in 287/328 (87.5%) isolates.

articles.headline: FIKK kinase-based antigen for malaria diagnostic development
articles.title_original: Antigenic determinant analysis of FIKK Kinase family to identify novel candidates for diagnosis of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rajendra Prasad M, Anil Kumar D, Dodwani E, Singh S, Trivedi V
articles.details: Researchers selected antigenic B- and T-cell epitopes from six FIKK kinases to develop a species-specific diagnostic target (C-FIKK). Purified antibody against FIKK9.1 showed high sensitivity with detection limit of 3 μM. Validation with mock patient samples revealed 95.45% average accuracy in detecting parasite loads with no cross-reactivity to bacterial, viral, fungal organisms or non-Plasmodium sources.

articles.headline: Arterolane-piperaquine comparable to artemether-lumefantrine in uncomplicated malaria
articles.title_original: Arterolane and piperaquine Vs. artemether and lumefantrine in uncomplicated malaria: A randomized study in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Awodele O, Bello IS, Akodu BA, Ahmed AA, Usman S, Bolarinwa RA, Akinyede A, Adelabu HA, Akande EO, Dey J, Kudrigikar V, Rao PS, Mehta S
articles.details: Phase IV randomized trial of 350 Nigerian patients (12–65 years) with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria compared once-daily arterolane maleate-piperaquine (150/750 mg) to twice-daily artemether-lumefantrine (80/480 mg) for 3 days. PCR-corrected adequate clinical and parasitological response was 100% on Day 28 and Day 42 in both groups. PCR-uncorrected ACPR was 98.2% (Day 28 and 42) for arterolane-piperaquine and 99.4% (Day 28), 98.8% (Day 42) for artemether-lumefantrine. No drug-related adverse events occurred.

articles.headline: Pyronaridine pharmacokinetics modeling in malaria infection
articles.title_original: Importance of Lysosomal Trapping and Plasmodium Parasite Infection on the Pharmacokinetics of Pyronaridine: A Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Model-Based Study.
articles.authors: Chu WY, Schouten WM, Mavoko HM, Tshiongo JK, Yobi DM, Kabasele FA, Kasereka G, Maketa V, Sevene E, Vala A, Shin J, D'Alessandro U, Kayentao K, Huitema ADR, Dorlo TPC
articles.details: Physiologically-based pharmacokinetic modeling study of 52 healthy individuals and 25 malaria patients evaluated pyronaridine disposition incorporating lysosomal sequestration and parasite compartments. Free pyronaridine concentrations in the parasite compartment were >10-fold higher than in whole blood. Higher parasitemia was associated with increased AUC and Cmax on day 1 as parasitemia decreased; subsequent hemoglobin reduction lowered AUC and Cmax on following days, demonstrating critical roles of lysosomal sequestration and parasitemia in drug disposition.

articles.headline: ONX-0914 proteasome inhibitor shows potent antimalarial efficacy
articles.title_original: Dual-targeting proteasome inhibitor ONX-0914 demonstrates potent antiplasmodial activity for malaria treatment.
articles.authors: Van Truong N, Nguyen TK, Thau NS, Chu TH, Sanaullah B, Venkataramaiah C, Han JH, Na SH, Park WS, Chun WJ, No JH, Han ET
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: In vitro studies of ONX-0914, a dual-targeting proteasome inhibitor, showed potent antiplasmodial activity against multiple P. falciparum strains (IC50 <50 nM) with low human cell toxicity (CC50 >100 μM) and synergistic activity with artesunate. In vivo studies in P. berghei ANKA mice showed >95% parasitemia suppression after 4 days (ED50 7.62 mg/kg oral, 6.52 mg/kg intraperitoneal) with <2% recrudescence after one month and favorable pharmacokinetics (bioavailability 23.83%, T1/2 0.7 h oral, Cmax 10,708.2 μg/ml).

articles.headline: Endothelial adhesion receptor expression in fatal cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Mapping the expression of endothelial adhesion receptors for Plasmodium falciparum-infected erythrocytes in fatal cerebral malaria in Malawian children.
articles.authors: Dorovini-Zis K, Li H, Zhe C, Zhang B, Small D, Taylor TE
articles.details: Post-mortem brain study of 50 Malawian children (27 with cerebral malaria, 23 non-malarial deaths) examined regional distribution and colocalization of five cytoadhesion receptors (ICAM-1, CD36, VCAM-1, E-selectin, thrombospondin) with infected RBCs. ICAM-1 was most widely expressed, followed by CD36, with highest receptor expression in the frontal lobe. Sequestration ratios were ~100% for all receptors across all brain regions, with greater expression in brain compared to peripheral organs, explaining preferential cerebral sequestration in cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: TLR4 and TLR9 variants linked to P. vivax parasitemia and symptoms
articles.title_original: New Evidence of TLR4 and TLR9 Variants Influencing Parasitaemia and Symptoms of Plasmodium vivax Infection.
articles.authors: Silva MMRI, Correia GMA, Lindenau JD, Cunha MG, Ohnishi MDO, Ventura AMRS, Ribeiro-Dos-Santos Â, Hutz MH, Sortica VA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 148 individuals with symptomatic uncomplicated P. vivax malaria in Brazilian Amazonia. TLR9 rs352140TT homozygotes showed significantly higher parasitemia levels (p=0.034) compared to C allele carriers. TLR4 rs1927911GG homozygotes had higher clinical index scores (p=0.018) versus A allele carriers, describing the first association of this TLR4 intron 1 variant with P. vivax symptoms in this population.

articles.headline: ACKR1/Duffy antigen roles extend beyond malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: The emerging roles of ACKR1/Duffy antigen beyond malaria.
articles.authors: Shaikh R, Ghosh K, Gorakshakar A
articles.details: Perspective article reviewing ACKR1 (formerly Duffy antigen/receptor for chemokines), primarily recognized for its role in P. vivax erythrocyte invasion. The review highlights emerging roles in inflammation, cancer biology, hematopoiesis, and other diseases, with discussion of potential therapeutic applications.

articles.headline: STEVOR multiepitope fusion vaccine shows broad immunogenicity and safety
articles.title_original: Conserved multiepitopes in Plasmodium falciparum STEVORs enable rational design of a fusion antigen vaccine construct with broad immunogenicity.
articles.authors: Sun ZS, Yin JX, Zhao HQ, Zhu YS, Chen SB, Shen HM, Xu B, Zhou XN, Wang TY, Yang WX, Duan YW, Chen JH, Kassegne K
articles.details: Structure-guided design identified conserved B- and T-cell epitopes in four STEVOR antigens implicated in severe falciparum malaria. A multiepitope fusion antigen (MEFA) construct achieved 97.15% global HLA coverage, demonstrated no allergenicity or toxicity, and induced CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell proliferation plus IgG antibodies in mice. Recombinant MEFA recognized significantly more anti-STEVOR IgG antibodies in nonsevere malaria sera than in severe malaria sera.

articles.headline: Lepidobotrys staudtii extract shows potent antiplasmodial activity against resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: In vitro antiplasmodial activities of extract and fractions from Lepidobotrys staudtii against sensitive and resistant blood-stage parasites of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Magoudjou Pekam JN, Efange NM, Mishro L, Keumoe R, Ndjakou BL, Ayong L, Njayou FN, Moundipa PF, Rajendran V
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: In vitro evaluation of crude extract and fractions from L. staudtii plant against P. falciparum blood stage parasites including drug-sensitive (Pf3D7) and chloroquine-resistant (PfINDO) strains. Hexane and ethyl acetate fractions demonstrated highest potency with IC50 values of 3.73 and 3.4 μg/mL against Pf3D7, respectively, with rapid action at 12 hours. Ring stage parasites were particularly susceptible (IC50 2.17-6.27 μg/mL) with no cytotoxic effects at concentrations up to 500 μg/mL.

articles.headline: PfCRT and PfDHFR mutations affect piperaquine binding through computational modeling
articles.title_original: Computational investigation of mutations in PfCRT and PfDHFR proteins for emerging resistance of Plasmodium falciparum to antimalarial drugs.
articles.authors: Ghosh S, Joshi D, Ponnusamy CS, Sridharan B, Velusamy M
articles.details: Molecular docking and dynamics simulations analyzed mutations in PfCRT (chloroquine resistance transporter) and PfDHFR (dihydrofolate reductase) and their interactions with nine antimalarial drugs including piperaquine. PfCRT-K76A showed piperaquine binding affinity of -9.5 kcal/mol with moderate structural deviation (0.970 ± 0.202 nm). PfDHFR-N51I showed stronger binding (-10.8 kcal/mol) but greater structural fluctuation (RMSD 4.491 ± 1.462 nm), suggesting restricted ligand accommodation and possible resistance.

articles.headline: Plasmodium Hsp90 C-terminus binds ATP, offering novel antimalarial target
articles.title_original: Investigation of a cryptic ligand binding site on Plasmodium falciparum Hsp90.
articles.authors: Mansfield CR, Taggart EL, Chirgwin ME, Derbyshire ER
articles.details: In silico and in vitro characterization of a cryptic ligand-binding site in the C-terminus of P. falciparum Hsp90 molecular chaperone. Affinity experiments and limited proteolysis confirmed specific interactions with ATP, dATP, and ADP, but not AMP or GTP. This less-conserved C-terminal site offers potential for selective antimalarial inhibitor design as an alternative to the highly conserved N-terminal ATP-binding site.

articles.headline: Novel pfdhps intron variant strongly associated with SP resistance in Africa
articles.title_original: A novel intron variant is associated with emerging pfdhps mutant haplotypes in West and Central African Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Hocke EF, Hansson H, Chopo-Pizarro A, Adegbola AJ, Bolaji O, Ngwa Niba PT, Ali IM, Nji A, Mbacham W, Baraka V, Kulaya NB, Kahunu GM, Mavoko HM, Nkoli PM, Sompwe EM, Mbongi D, Mitashi P, Bédia VA, Gnagne PA, Konaté A, Yavo W, Menan H, Beshir K, Mousa A, Sutherland CJ, Alifrangis M, Roper C
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Molecular epidemiological study of pfdhps gene mutations in 929 P. falciparum-positive blood samples from five West/Central African countries (Nigeria, Cameroon, Tanzania, DRC, Côte d'Ivoire). A novel intron variant (a548383t) was strongly associated with the 431V mutation and emerged with specific haplotypes, particularly VAGKGS (OR=211.7, P<0.001), VAGKAS (OR=39.2, P<0.001), and VAGKAA (OR=33.6, P<0.001), indicating selective sweep pressure around this sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance variant.

articles.headline: ICCM adherence to malaria treatment guidelines varies by province in DRC
articles.title_original: Assessment of adherence to the National Malaria Guidelines in treatment management of malaria among children under five in integrated community case management in the DRC.
articles.authors: Ibrahimi S, Kadiobo L, Embeke N, Diarra H, Tshiswaka G, Mukumena E, Brunner B
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 2,326 children from 30 ICCM sites in the DRC assessing guideline adherence. Overall 63.8% had malaria; 94.8% of clinically diagnosed cases received rapid diagnostic testing versus 2.8% of non-diagnosed (p=0.0001). Those with confirmed malaria were significantly more likely to receive treatment (93.4% vs. 6.6%, p=0.0001), though 18.5% of uninfected children also received antimalarials. Adherence to RDT testing varied significantly by province, with Kasai Oriental more likely (AOR=1.89) and Tanganyika less likely (AOR=0.40) compared to Lualaba.

articles.headline: HDAC inhibitor compound 4o shows potent activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Antiplasmodial activity of pentyloxyamide-based histone deacetylase inhibitors against Plasmodium falciparum parasites.
articles.authors: Dawood WA, Fisher GM, Kremeyer J, Fischer F, Home JL, Goodman CD, Lam KS, Maier AG, Kurz T, Andrews KT
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship study of 24 pentyloxyamide-based histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitors against P. falciparum blood stages. Lead compound 4o demonstrated IC50 of 20 nM against asexual parasites with >250-fold selectivity over human cells, plus activity against exoerythrocytic stage parasites (IC50 24 nM) but weak gametocyte activity (IC50 >2 μM). Compound 4o caused P. falciparum histone H4 hyperacetylation and stabilized PfHDAC1, providing mechanistic support for this drug class.

articles.headline: P. vivax resurgence across three WHO regions challenges elimination goals
articles.title_original: Indigenous Plasmodium vivax upsurge in the Eastern Mediterranean, Western Pacific, and South East Asia regions - beyond the constant culpability of climate change, COVID-19, and armed conflicts.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Sharma A
articles.details: Epidemiological analysis of indigenous P. vivax cases across Eastern Mediterranean, Southeast Asia, and Western Pacific regions showing significant upsurges in over half of countries. Papua New Guinea and Yemen experienced consistent increases since 2015, while Indonesia and Pakistan showed sharp rises between 2020–2023 (+100% and +83.4%, respectively). Even countries like Thailand targeted for WHO E-2025 elimination appear unlikely to achieve this goal, with COVID-19 pandemic, armed conflict, climate change, and chloroquine resistance all contributing factors.

articles.headline: Microbial natural products show promise against drug-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Screening and hit evaluation of a microbial metabolite library against the pathogenic Plasmodium falciparum and Toxoplasma gondii parasites.
articles.authors: Gancheva MR, Mao EY, Romeo O, Vuong D, O'Handley R, Page SW, Lacey E, Wilson DW
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: High-throughput screening of 812 microbial natural product compounds identified 219 compounds inhibiting P. falciparum erythrocytic stage by at least 80% at 2 μg/mL (typically 1–10 μM), with 149 showing equivalent activity against T. gondii. After filtering for low mammalian cytotoxicity, lead compounds included monocyclic thiazole peptides (micrococcin P1, thiocillins) and pleuromutilins, showing low nanomolar antiparasitic activity combined with minimal toxicity. Pladienolide B and cryptopleurine demonstrated high potency but significant mammalian cytotoxicity, requiring further structure-activity investigation.
