articles.headline: NMR structure assignments for P. falciparum flap endonuclease completed
articles.title_original: (1)H, (15)N and (13)C backbone resonance assignments of flap endonuclease from Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: do Aido-Machado R, Baxter NJ, Rowe ML, Pohare MB, Vitovski S, Sayers JR, Waltho JP
articles.details: Complete NMR spectroscopy backbone resonance assignments reported for flap endonuclease from P. falciparum (PfFEN349) in substrate-free state, with 90% coverage identifying 298 backbone amide resonances out of 337 theoretically-detectable resonances (excluding 10 prolines and the N-terminal glycine). Secondary structure predictions derived from chemical shift analysis correlate well with AlphaFold structural modeling, providing a foundation for future drug discovery targeting PfFEN as a potential antimalarial.

articles.headline: Heme detoxification protein resists recombinant expression
articles.title_original: Recombinant expression and purification of Plasmodium heme detoxification protein in E. coli: Challenges and discoveries.
articles.authors: Singh R, Makde RD
articles.details: Researchers attempted to express Plasmodium's Heme Detoxification Protein (HDP) recombinantly in E. coli to clarify its mechanism in converting heme to hemozoin—a key antimalarial target. Despite multiple strategies (orthologs, consensus sequences, fusion partners, chaperones, construct optimization), most HDP constructs remained insoluble or aggregated. Only one construct with 44-residue N-terminal truncation achieved soluble expression (HDPpf-C10), but showed no heme-to-Hz transformation activity, revealing the N-terminal region is essential for enzymatic function.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance reduces IPTp pregnancy efficacy
articles.title_original: The impact of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance on the effectiveness of intermittent preventive treatment for the prevention of malaria in pregnancy in Africa: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: van Eijk AM, Stepniewska K, Khairallah C, Rodriguez E, Ahn J, Gutman JR, Ter Kuile FO
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 122 studies (148,693 pregnant women) examined how P. falciparum resistance to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine affects intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp-SP). In west/central Africa, three vs two doses achieved adjusted RR 0.71 (95% CI 0.65–0.78) with very low resistance but declined to 0.83 (0.72–0.95) with low resistance. In east/southern Africa, adjusted RR dropped from 0.63 (0.57–0.69) at moderate resistance to 0.93 (0.85–1.01) at very high resistance, defined by dhps Lys540Glu and Ala581Gly mutations.

articles.headline: Pyruvate kinase deficiency creates mechanical malaria barrier
articles.title_original: Metabolic Rigidity as a Mechanical Barrier to Malaria: Flickering Loss in PKLR-Deficient Erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Hernando-Ospina N, Calero M, Solís G, Azcárate IG, Herráez-Aguilar D, Caselli N, Moleiro LH, Segovia JC, Bautista JM, Monroy F
articles.details: Spectroscopy study examined how PKLR-gene mutations causing pyruvate kinase deficiency affect red blood cell mechanics and malaria susceptibility. PKLR-deficient RBCs showed reduced ATP-dependent membrane flickering and fluidity versus controls. When infected with Plasmodium yoelii, these metabolically rigid cells retained properties that hindered parasite-induced membrane remodeling, suggesting glycolytic insufficiency creates a biomechanical barrier against infection.

articles.headline: Fetal Doppler tracks severe malaria during maternal ventilation
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria and Mechanical Ventilation in Pregnancy: The Crucial Role of Fetal Doppler in Monitoring Fetal Well-Being.
articles.authors: Olaya S, Angulo SD, Miranda J, Garay M
articles.details: Case report of a 33-year-old pregnant woman at 33 weeks with severe malaria who developed respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. Serial fetal Doppler ultrasound showed normal umbilical artery and middle cerebral artery flow patterns, with no signs of fetal hypoxia throughout ventilation. The case demonstrates feasibility of continuous fetal Doppler surveillance during maternal ventilation in severe malaria, though authors emphasize limited data exist on standardized protocols.

articles.headline: Broflanilide IRS superior against pyrethroid-resistant mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Community evaluation of VECTRON™ T500 (broflanilide) for indoor residual spraying for malaria vector control in Siaya county, Kenya.
articles.authors: Wekesa CN, Polo B, Muchoki M, Omondi S, Kipsum M, Abong'o B, Machani MG, Bange T, Abbey I, Kiptoo P, Mburugu P, Keitany K, Sang D, Ramaita E, Ochomo E
articles.details: Non-inferiority trial in Kenya (June–November 2024) compared VECTRON T500 (broflanilide, 100 mg/m²) with Actellic 300CS against pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles gambiae. VECTRON achieved 98.73% mortality (95% CI 97.95–99.51%) versus 80.22% for Actellic against resistant strain (p<0.001). VECTRON maintained >95% efficacy throughout six months while Actellic declined below 80% after month 4. Wild mosquitoes showed 100% broflanilide susceptibility with no cross-resistance; zero adverse events with VECTRON versus 8% with Actellic.

articles.headline: Forty-five Anopheles species mapped in Eastern Mediterranean
articles.title_original: Diversity and spatial distribution of malaria vectors in the WHO Eastern Mediterranean region from 1900 to 2024: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Rashti R, Chavoshi E, Tapak L, Nikpoor F, Ayubi E, Asakereh H, Mohammadi Y
articles.details: Systematic review of 276 field studies (1900–2024) documented Anopheles distribution across 18 WHO Eastern Mediterranean countries. Review identified 45 Anopheles species, with Anopheles stephensi most studied (102 studies), followed by An. superpictus (57), An. dthali (49), An. culicifacies (47), and An. maculipennis (45). Iran contributed most research (122 studies), followed by Pakistan (35), Sudan (31), and Egypt (30). Authors emphasize need for ongoing monitoring to detect distribution shifts and guide interventions.

articles.headline: Artemisinin K13 mutations spread eastward in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Temporal Trends in Artemisinin Partial Resistance and Other Antimalarial Drug Mutations in Plasmodium falciparum from Kagera Region, Northwestern Tanzania, 2021-2023.
articles.authors: Simkin A, Mandai S, Fola AA, Young NW, Pereus D, Bakari C, Madebe RA, Seth MD, Mrengela RB, Kisambale AJ, Chacha GA, Mandara CI, Francis F, Mbwambo D, Garimo I, Chacky F, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Molteni F, Njau RJA, Kajange S, Nhiga SL, Mohamed A, Juliano JJ, Ishengoma DS, Bailey JA
articles.details: Longitudinal molecular surveillance of 2,826 isolates from seven districts in Kagera, Tanzania (2021–2023) tracked resistance markers via molecular inversion probes. K13 mutation R561H prevalence increased from 5.5% (2021) to 11.3% (2022) then stabilized at 6.9% (2023), but persisted at 14–26% in border districts and appeared for first time in Muleba (5.0%) and Bukoba rural (0.7%) in 2023, indicating eastward spread toward Lake Victoria. Antifolate resistance mutations showed marked spatial heterogeneity, with DHFR I164L and DHPS A581G reaching 38.1% and 48.1% in eastern districts.

articles.headline: Hypnozoite proteins suppress liver schizogony in P. vivax
articles.title_original: Two Plasmodium vivax hypnozoite-expressed RNA-binding proteins inhibit liver stage replication.
articles.authors: Vo KC, van Biljon R, Zanghi G, Zavala-Martinez B, Betz W, Vaughan AM, Painter HJ, Kappe SHI
articles.details: Study identified two hypnozoite-expressed RNA-binding proteins (IESI-1 and IESI-2) that suppress liver-stage replication in Plasmodium vivax. Functional testing in Plasmodium yoelii showed that individual expression of each protein blocked liver stage schizogony, keeping parasites small and uninucleate. Proteins bind specific RNA motifs, suggesting a molecular mechanism for hypnozoite quiescence and relapse suppression.

articles.headline: Schizogony dynamics reveal antimalarial drug vulnerabilities
articles.title_original: Unusual replication dynamics during Plasmodium falciparum schizogony.
articles.authors: Berners-Lee R, Smith TK
articles.details: Review examines unusual replication dynamics of Plasmodium falciparum schizogony—alternating asynchronous DNA replication and nuclear division within shared cytoplasm followed by mass cytokinesis. Authors note dynamics are shaped by the parasite's highly AT-rich genome and pressures specific to schizogony. Clarifying these mechanisms may reveal parasite-specific vulnerabilities and inform antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: High malaria knowledge unchanged after community intervention
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitude and practice in high endemic settings for malaria: a cross-sectional study in a rural community in central Mozambique.
articles.authors: Lequechane JD, Chale F, Azevedo N, Abílio AP, Muanido AG, Gonçalves L, Manuel JL, Silveira H
articles.details: Cross-sectional KAP study in Tambai, Mozambique conducted before (May 2021) and after (June 2022) REPELMALARIA intervention surveyed ~155 heads of households. Knowledge of symptoms, transmission, prevention, and treatment remained high before (96.8%) and after (91.1%), with no significant differences between intervention and control groups. Authors conclude that high malaria burden in the area is not attributable to deficient knowledge, attitude, or practice, implicating sociodemographic or ecological factors.

articles.headline: Housing and education reduce malaria recurrence in Kenyan children
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic and ecological factors associated with malaria and malaria recurrence risk in children in Msambweni, Kwale County, Kenya.
articles.authors: Amugongo JS, LaBeaud AD, Bosire C, Bisanzio D, Nzaro M, Ngari M, Vu DM, Ndenga BA, Mutuku FM
articles.details: Cohort of 2,371 children (1–18 years) in Msambweni, Kenya (2014–2018) with 2,610 febrile visits; 40.6% had parasitaemia and 41.8% experienced malaria. Older age increased risk (aRR 1.38–1.57 vs <2 years). Protective factors included maternal education ≥secondary (aRR 0.66), piped/well water (aRR 0.78), and electricity/solar lighting (aRR 0.61). Recurrent malaria (2.5% of children) linked to poor housing (dirt floors), proximity to rice fields, and poor ventilation.

articles.headline: Carboxylesterase Coeae6g drives multi-insecticide cross-resistance
articles.title_original: Predictive chemoproteomics and functional validation reveal Coeae6g-mediated insecticide cross-resistance in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Balaska S, Grigoraki L, Lycett G, Weetman D, Oladepo F, Colman F, Vontas J, Paine MJI, Ismail HM
articles.details: Functional chemoproteomics study using activity-based protein profiling identified enzyme-mediated insecticide resistance in Anopheles gambiae. Screening with pirimiphos-methyl identified carboxylesterase Coeae6g, previously associated with pyrethroid and carbamate resistance. Functional assays confirmed Coeae6g confers resistance to pirimiphos-methyl and mediates cross-resistance to malathion, bendiocarb, and permethrin, bridging genotype-phenotype gaps and establishing framework for predicting resistance evolution.

articles.headline: Placental malaria prevalence and outcomes: systematic review and meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Placental malaria: a systematic review and meta-analysis of global burden, risk factors, and maternal and foetal outcomes.
articles.authors: Menon S, D'Alessio F, Chaudhuri N, Onwuchekwa C, Kaur M, Nkumama I, Olesen OF
articles.details: Systematic review of 50 studies (45 from sub-Saharan Africa) examining P. falciparum placental malaria (PM). Global PM prevalence was 17% (95% CI 12–21%), rising to 23% in histology-based studies, with highest rates in West Africa (19% in SSA vs 4% in other regions). HIV-positive women had 45% PM prevalence compared to 17% in HIV-negative women (stillbirth OR 3.81, 95% CI 1.22–11.94). Wide confidence intervals precluded firm conclusions on PM's impact on most maternal/fetal outcomes.

articles.headline: Age-structured migration model explains malaria transmission dynamics in Africa
articles.title_original: Impact of age-structured migration on malaria burden: A modelling-empirical analysis in sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Harikripal, Pascal Zabre S, Danquah I, Bhattacharyya S
articles.details: Compartmental model incorporating age-specific migration, human and vector populations explained malaria incidence patterns in sub-Saharan Africa. Validated with multiyear case data from Ouagadougou and annual migration records from Burkina Faso, the model successfully characterized incidence across age cohorts. Simulations revealed that nonlinear amplification and synchrony in infection prevalence are partially driven by age-specific migration in different cohorts, informing targeted intervention design in regions with seasonal migration.

articles.headline: Climate variability effects on malaria outcomes exceed standard interventions in Kenya
articles.title_original: Climate, Interventions, and Malaria Outcomes in a Warming World: Towards Climate-Smart Malaria Control in Kenya.
articles.authors: Nyawanda BO, Ochomo E, Otieno JD, Keitany K, Machini BK, Vounatsou P
articles.details: Analysis of Kenya evidence shows climate variability exerts effects on malaria outcomes comparable to or stronger than standard interventions at local scales, with transmission displaying non-linear, lagged relationships to temperature and rainfall. Insecticide-treated bed nets (ITNs) and timely case management reduce incidence/deaths (especially young children), but population-level impact depends on deployment timing relative to climate-favored transmission windows. Authors advocate dynamic intervention targeting based on climate lags/thresholds and embedding short-term, locally-tailored forecasts into routine planning.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic congenital malaria prevalence 12.2% in low-endemicity Guatemala
articles.title_original: Prevalence of submicroscopic congenital malaria and associated risk factors in an area of low endemicity of Guatemala.
articles.authors: Franco RG, Ortiz L, Bardají A, Castañeda D, Menendez C, Padilla N, Castellanos ME
articles.details: Prospective cohort of pregnant women (2009–2011) in Guatemala found submicroscopic Plasmodium in 54 of 442 umbilical cord samples tested by qPCR (prevalence 12.2%, 95% CI 9.3–15.6%). Case-control analysis identified submicroscopic maternal Plasmodium infection (aOR 7.55, 95% CI 1.84–51.5) and gravidity (aOR 1.49, 95% CI 1.13–2.04) as significant risk factors for congenital malaria. Findings highlight that molecular detection reveals submicroscopic infections missed by microscopy.

articles.headline: Climate and land-use changes drive Plasmodium knowlesi transmission in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi and global changes: socio-ecological drivers and challenges.
articles.authors: Muh F, Qanita NG, Yuwanita MR, Wusono AD, Rahayu AA, Amirudin FA, Firdaus MER, Cahyani NKD, Han JH
articles.details: Review examining P. knowlesi transmission dynamics in Southeast Asia within context of climate change, land-use changes (particularly deforestation), and human-macaque-mosquito interactions. Temperature and precipitation variations impact vectors and parasite survival; environmental alterations transform breeding habitats and transmission zones. Key challenge identified is the attitude-behavior gap (awareness of climate risks not translating to action); ecological and social drivers intersect to shape P. knowlesi epidemiology.

articles.headline: IPTp-SP plus ITNs cost-effective for malaria prevention in pregnant Nigerian women
articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness of intermittent preventive therapy and insecticide-treated bed nets for malaria prevention among pregnant women in Jos North, Plateau State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Okoro LN, Zoakah AI, Bupwatda P, Anyamene EL, Bulus NG, Kumbak FD, Sabano MC, Mukisa KA, Gmanyami JM, Amoakoh MY, Isiko I
articles.details: Cost-effectiveness analysis of 1,000 pregnant women (2019–2020) compared sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (IPTp-SP) alone to combined IPTp-SP/insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) in Nigeria. Combined intervention averted 15.63 DALYs (IPTp-SP/ITN 85.9 DALYs lost vs IPTp-SP alone 101.53 DALYs). Incremental cost US$100.19 (total US$231.98 vs US$131.79) yielded ICER of US$2.12 per DALY averted, with ~60% probability of cost-effectiveness at US$150/DALY willingness-to-pay threshold.

articles.headline: Healthcare worker and facility factors drive malaria RDT misrecording across Africa
articles.title_original: Characteristics of healthcare workers and health facilities associated with inaccurate recording of malaria rapid diagnostic test results: a multi-country study.
articles.authors: Atobatele S, Mpimbaza A, Ngufor C, Yavo W, Konate-Toure A, Ahogni I, Ssewante N, Orya E, Akpiroroh E, Okoro O, Agaba B, Kpemasse A, Agnon J, Tanoh AM, Affoukou C, Opigo J, Ntadom G, Okagbue H, Eugene EC, Aponte JJ, Hilton E, Galles N, Soebiyanto R, Cooper S, Okoronkwo C, Humes M, Griffith K, Lindblade KA, Sampson S
articles.details: Multi-country observational study (June–December 2023) in 64 health facilities across Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria, Uganda examined 499 healthcare workers performing over 100,000 malaria RDTs. Overall misrecording: 5.1–7.3% for positive results, 0.7–3.7% for negative results; external-facility agreement 90.2–94.3% across countries. Misrecording patterns differed by country and associated with healthcare worker characteristics (age, education, knowledge, beliefs), facility factors (volume, positivity), and supervision, indicating need for multifactorial interventions.

articles.headline: Forest packs of bite prevention tools acceptable among Cambodia malaria-endemic populations
articles.title_original: Evaluating the acceptability and feasibility of new mosquito bite prevention tools in a "forest pack" to support malaria elimination in Cambodia.
articles.authors: McIver DJ, Doum D, Men C, Neukom J, Lobo NF, Richardson JH, Macdonald M, Shete PB, Sovannaroth S, Tatarsky A
articles.details: Feasibility/acceptability study in two high-burden districts in Cambodia distributed 28,000 forest packs containing volatile pyrethroid spatial repellent (VPSR), topical repellent (TR), and insecticide-treated clothing (ITC) to 2,935 forest-exposed individuals over four rounds. Reported acceptability high: 96% for spatial repellent, 93% for topical repellent, 100% for ITC. While reported use was high among forest dwellers, ITC coverage remained low (1–40%), highlighting need for improved implementation and community engagement.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax DBP and EBP2 gene amplification varies across Amazon rainforest
articles.title_original: The pattern of gene amplification of members of the Plasmodium vivax erythrocyte binding-like proteins family across the Amazon rainforest.
articles.authors: Guedes KS, Fernandes GM, Sanchez BAM, Ntumngia FB, Adams JH, Kano FS, Fontes CJF, de Sousa TN, Carvalho LH
articles.details: Analysis of 191 P. vivax isolates from three Amazon sites examined copy number variation (CNV) in dbp and ebp2 erythrocyte binding-like (EBL) protein genes. Western Amazon showed 25% with dbp amplification (up to 8 copies) vs 2–9% in eastern/southwestern regions (2–3 copies); ebp2 amplification 15% in western vs 1–4% in other regions. No association found between gene CNVs and DARC genotypes (all individuals DARC-positive), suggesting regional variation in EBL gene amplification independent of Duffy receptor status.

articles.headline: Mosquito repellent awareness high but use low in Uganda due to cost
articles.title_original: Knowledge, attitudes, practices and determinants of mosquito repellent use for malaria prevention in Nangabo Ward, Wakiso District, Uganda.
articles.authors: Kemigisha G, Musoke D, Walekhwa AW, Yeka A
articles.details: Cross-sectional mixed-methods study of 185 household heads in Uganda found 63.8% aware of mosquito repellents and 59.5% held positive attitudes, yet consistent use only 8.2% with 44.9% reporting occasional use. Key barriers: high cost (36.8%) and policy gaps (91.9% unaware of regulations). Multivariable analysis identified age ≥60 years (AOR 2.50, 95% CI 1.43–4.38), large households 8–10 members (AOR 2.59, 95% CI 1.28–5.24), tertiary education (AOR 0.42, 95% CI 0.22–0.79); positive attitudes increased use likelihood (AOR 3.28, 95% CI 1.15–9.34).

articles.headline: Larviciding reduced Anopheles densities across malaria transmission risk strata in Tanzania
articles.title_original: A large-scale mosquito larviciding in Tanga Region, Tanzania, reduced mosquito densities to varying degrees across malaria transmission risk strata.
articles.authors: Gavana T, Kailembo D, Kasagama E, Lilolime NS, Mwamlima TG, Mwalimu CD, Bernard J, Yoram B, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Kisoka N, Chaki P, Lengeler C
articles.details: Pilot mosquito larviciding program in Tanga Region, Tanzania (June 2022–April 2024) conducted six rounds of Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis and Bacillus sphaericus application in 120 villages (60 intervention, 60 control) across high-, moderate-, low-malaria risk strata. Late-stage Anopheles larvae densities reduced: high-risk IRR 0.20 (95% CI 0.12–0.35), moderate-risk 0.51 (95% CI 0.26–1.01), low-risk 0.40 (95% CI 0.24–0.68). Adult A. gambiae s.l. densities decreased in moderate/low-risk areas; no significant reduction in A. funestus, suggesting species/context-dependent effectiveness.

articles.headline: Iso-mukaadial acetate targets glycolysis and DNA replication in Plasmodium falciparum
articles.title_original: The impact of Iso-mukaadial acetate on Plasmodium falciparum transcriptional gene regulation.
articles.authors: Salomane N, Mafuna T, Allie F, Mahlangu JS, van Zyl RL, Pooe O, Simelane M
articles.details: RNA-sequencing of P. falciparum NF54 treated with iso-mukaadial acetate (IMA, from Warburgia salutaris) revealed distinct mechanisms from chloroquine. Both IMA and chloroquine upregulated PfEMP1, RIFIN, and STEVOR adhesion genes; IMA specifically downregulated peptidyl-proline modification and glycolytic pathways (GAPDH, G6PD-6-phosphogluconolactonase), suggesting targets for ATP/NADPH production and antioxidant defense. IMA also inhibited DNA replication components. Study proposes IMA as a candidate anti-plasmodial compound warranting further biochemical validation of mechanism.

articles.headline: Key protein regulates apicoplast fission in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: An essential adaptor for apicoplast fission and inheritance in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Blauwkamp JA, Rajaram K, Staggers SR, Harrigan O, Doud EH, Xu W, Ke H, Prigge ST, Sun SY, Absalon S
articles.details: This study identified PfAnchor as a key regulator of apicoplast fission in blood-stage P. falciparum. Conditional depletion of PfAnchor disrupted apicoplast fission, leading to incomplete cytokinesis and parasite death. The protein interacts with PfDyn2, a dynamin-like GTPase involved in organellar fission. PfAnchor represents a parasite-specific vulnerability for therapeutic intervention.

articles.headline: Mefloquine resistance compromises mefloquine-piperaquine combination efficacy
articles.title_original: Tolerance of Plasmodium falciparum mefloquine-resistant clinical isolates to mefloquine-piperaquine with implications for triple artemisinin-based combination therapies.
articles.authors: Roesch C, Cosson A, Mairet Khedim M, Khim N, Kloeung N, Ke S, Srun S, Eam R, Khean C, Kul C, Adoux L, Popovici J, Leang R, Ringwald P, Ariey F, Coppée R, Witkowski B
articles.details: This study assessed mefloquine-piperaquine (MQ-PPQ) efficacy in Cambodian P. falciparum clinical isolates with different resistance profiles. Mefloquine-resistant strains showed significant cross-tolerance to MQ-PPQ, while PPQ-resistant and -sensitive strains remained susceptible. Under four months of repeated MQ-PPQ pressure, parasites rapidly acquired tolerance through pfmdr1 amplification; mefloquine inhibited piperaquine accumulation in a dose-dependent manner. The findings indicate that mefloquine resistance alone undermines MQ-PPQ triple-combination therapy efficacy.

articles.headline: Community engagement accelerates malaria elimination in remote Lao areas
articles.title_original: Early and sustained community engagement to reach unreached populations for malaria elimination in Lao People's Democratic Republic.
articles.authors: Roy SR, Banouvong V, Hoban E, Khamlome B, Chindavongsa K, Inthirath I, Inthivong S, Sibounheuang K, Akavong K, Huong TTG, Yadav RPH, Ringwald P, Kelley JF, Xangsayarath P, Shortus MS, Reyburn R
articles.details: This study implemented 'Accelerator Strategies' in Lao PDR in 2022 targeting high-risk populations including forest goers and mobile populations with targeted drug administration and intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) as chemoprevention. Community engagement, local political commitment, and people-centered services with GIS-mapped risk data were essential. The approach resulted in high intervention coverage and decreased malaria burden in previously hard-to-reach areas.

articles.headline: Hospital malaria deaths in Ecuador show geographic and temporal variation
articles.title_original: Dengue and malaria mortality in Ecuador: A population analysis of hospital deaths across 24 provinces, 2015-2023.
articles.authors: Acosta-España JD, Morales-Gualotuña M, Pazmiño-Villaseñor J, Dueñas-Espín I
articles.details: This cross-sectional ecological study analyzed hospital discharge data from Ecuador's 24 provinces, 2015-2023. Malaria accounted for 5 adult hospital deaths (3 in 2019, 1 in 2021, 1 in 2023), with P. falciparum predominating; the highest mortality rate occurred in Napo province at 16.3 per million in 2021. The study identified fragmented mortality patterns and gaps in surveillance, highlighting persistent underreporting in endemic zones despite elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Gestational malaria impairs physical and environmental health in Colombian pregnant women
articles.title_original: Health-related quality of life among pregnant women exposed to malaria in an endemic region of Colombia.
articles.authors: Cardona-Arias JA, Higuita-Gutierrez LF
articles.details: This mixed-methods study included 400 pregnant women in Colombia's endemic malaria region, 6% with current gestational malaria (GM) and 44% with prior malaria history. Health-related quality of life (HRQoL) was measured using WHOQOL-BREF; psychological health scored highest (median 78) while physical health scored lowest (median 43). Gestational malaria, comorbidities, and rural residence were associated with reduced physical and environmental health scores, though qualitatively, women normalized the disease and emphasized healthcare access and social support.

articles.headline: Social media discourse on malaria is event-driven and Africa-focused
articles.title_original: Event-driven dynamics, regional disparities, and netizen engagement: characterizing and exploring malaria discourse on Sina Weibo for enhanced health communication.
articles.authors: Wang N, Zhao X, Li C, Mao Y
articles.details: This social media analysis examined 81,010 malaria-related posts on Sina Weibo from January 2019 to August 2024 using topic modeling, sentiment analysis, and named entity recognition. Netizens' attention was event-driven, with peaks around China's health assistance to Africa (accounting for ~20% of annual posts), public health incidents, and malaria awareness days. Negative events sustained engagement approximately 1.5 times longer than positive events; Africa discussions constituted over 60% of region-specific posts. Official health accounts posted infrequently, while personal and organizational accounts provided varied perspectives on treatment, prevention, and research.

articles.headline: Indoor residual spraying enables malaria prevention engagement with pregnant women
articles.title_original: Indoor residual spraying household visits as a platform for engaging pregnant women in malaria prevention on Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea.
articles.authors: Galick DS, Eyono JNM, Rivas MR, Phiri WP, Guerra CA, García GA
articles.details: This implementation study leveraged routine annual indoor residual spraying (IRS) on Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea to identify pregnant women for follow-up education on malaria prevention. Among 589 pregnant women identified via 1,567 household visits, awareness of long-lasting insecticide-treated nets (LLINs) was high (76.9%) compared to intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) awareness (34.5% cited benefits, 37.1% aware of availability). LLIN regular use was 54.8% (nightly), while IPTp uptake was 35.2% on track for three doses. Higher awareness of prevention benefits was associated with increased intervention uptake.

articles.headline: Seasonal migration drives parasite diversity and gene flow in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: High connectivity and low differentiation of Plasmodium falciparum parasite populations in a setting with high seasonal migration.
articles.authors: Esayas E, Louie W, Routledge I, Murphy M, Demeke NN, De Amaral F, Aranda-Díaz A, Greenhouse B, Kassa FA, Nigusse T, Assefa M, Ashine T, Getachew A, Ntuku H, Golassa L, Gadisa E, Bennett A, Smith JL
articles.details: This genetic study examined P. falciparum diversity between highland and lowland regions in Ethiopia using multiplexed amplicon sequencing of dried blood spots from 1,180 individuals (599 highland residents, 581 seasonal workers and lowland residents). Lowland populations showed higher complexity of infection (COI mean 2.62 vs 2.00 for highlands) and polyclonal rates (62% vs 40%). High genetic connectivity was observed between regions, with 27 related parasite clusters in highland cases and 10 in seasonal workers, indicating extensive parasite sharing. Seasonal agricultural migration drives gene flow and sustains transmission despite low genetic differentiation.

articles.headline: Methylene blue reverses brainstem damage; identifies cerebral malaria biomarkers
articles.title_original: Methylene blue treatment of fatal cerebral malaria and identification of potential blood biomarkers.
articles.authors: Hang JW, Leong YW, Narang V, Sunyakumthorn P, Im-Erbsin R, Foo S, Lum J, Lee B, Brown AE, Rénia L, Turner GDH, Wassmer SC, Lombardini ED, Russell B, Malleret B
articles.details: This study used P. coatneyi-infected rhesus macaques to model cerebral malaria (CM) and assess methylene blue treatment. Differential gene expression analysis demonstrated methylene blue's efficacy in reversing infection-induced brainstem damage. Comparing brainstem transcriptomics from macaques with blood transcriptomics from both P. coatneyi-infected macaques and P. falciparum-infected humans, the study identified nine genes associated with CM severity, most expressed in neutrophils. These genes represent potential blood biomarkers for diagnosing P. falciparum-induced fatal cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Ruxolitinib reduces inflammation and enhances immunity in malaria infection
articles.title_original: Adjunctive ruxolitinib attenuates inflammation and enhances immunity in volunteers experimentally infected with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Webster R, Oyong DA, Llewellyn S, Abd-Rahman AN, Potter AJ, Mukhiya R, Sahai N, Leelasena I, Ujvary E, Mathison S, Andrew DW, Bukali L, de Labastida Rivera F, Engel J, Soon MSF, Frame T, Hamelink J, Nalubega M, Dooley NL, Loughland JR, Nguyen T, Rosenberg-Hasson Y, Maysel-Auslender S, Sigal N, Foygel K, Gower J, Peters J, Woo R, Amante F, Wells TNC, Chalon S, Moehrle JJ, McCarthy JS, Birrell GW, Edstein MD, Leipold M, Obermoser G, Maecker H, Engwerda CR, Barber BE, Boyle MJ
articles.details: This double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial enrolled 20 malaria-naïve volunteers who were inoculated with blood-stage P. falciparum, then on day 8 given artemether-lumefantrine with either ruxolitinib (JAK 1/2 inhibitor) or placebo. Ruxolitinib was safe and well tolerated, reducing posttreatment inflammation (lower C-reactive protein, angiopoietin-2, and ICAM-1). After a second parasite inoculation 90 days later, ruxolitinib recipients showed enhanced immune memory with elevated HLA-DRA and 4-1BB (T cell activation markers). The results support further evaluation of ruxolitinib as adjunctive malaria treatment to improve clinical outcomes and boost antiparasitic immunity.

articles.headline: Artificial intelligence improves malaria diagnosis, treatment, and outbreak prediction
articles.title_original: The role of artificial intelligence (AI) in the elimination of malaria: a systematic literature review.
articles.authors: Kumar SM, Nath SN, Vani HC, Shrinivasa BM, Ahmed N, Tomer V, Bankoti K, Negi G, Ali SSB, Jaiswal MK, Warkade V
articles.details: This systematic literature review of 62 articles (2014-2025) examined AI applications in malaria elimination, focusing on diagnosis (automated microscopy and image-based parasite detection), treatment optimization (predictive analytics), and outbreak prediction. AI models achieved superior diagnostic accuracies and reduced diagnostic time and human error compared to conventional methods. Key limitations identified include poor data interoperability, limited rural healthcare infrastructure, and gaps in healthcare worker training. The review proposes AI techniques adaptable for India's malaria elimination goal by 2030.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum malaria parasites circulate in southern Senegal
articles.title_original: Common Molecular Detection of the Neglected Human Malaria Parasite Among Febrile Patients in Southern Regions in Senegal.
articles.authors: Sambe BS, Diaw SOM, Diagne A, Diatta AS, Diatta HAM, Sarr I, Sané R, Guigma PYB, Senghor B, Diouf B, Sembene PM, Vigan-Womas I, Niang M
articles.details: This molecular epidemiology study screened 1,990 febrile patients across four southern Senegal regions (2020-2022) using qPCR and nested PCR. The overall Plasmodium positivity rate was 73.43%, with 67.92% single-species infections and 32.08% co-infections. All non-falciparum species (P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae) circulated in three of four regions studied; P. vivax and P. malariae were absent from Ziguinchor, while this was the first report of P. ovale there. The study provides the first documented evidence of non-falciparum malaria in Kolda and Tambacounda regions.

articles.headline: Malaria-helminth co-infection drives anemia in pregnant Ethiopian women
articles.title_original: Malaria and Soil-Transmitted Helminth Co-Infection and Its Association With Anemia Among Pregnant Women Visiting Mizan-Tepi University Teaching Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Duguma T, Assefa S, Tesfaye T, Alemayehu B
articles.journal: Can J Infect Dis Med Microbiol
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 332 pregnant women at Mizan-Tepi University Hospital (September–December 2024). Malaria prevalence 24.4%, soil-transmitted helminth (STH) 33.7%, co-infection 12.7%. Overall anemia 57.5%; with malaria 18.4%, STH 26.2%, co-infection 9.6%. Rural residence and poor handwashing associated with co-infection. Authors conclude high prevalence of malaria-STH co-infections contributed to high anemia prevalence among pregnant women.

articles.headline: Fever and cough predict malaria in respiratory symptom presentations
articles.title_original: Syndromic presentation by patients with malaria and respiratory symptoms in a malaria-endemic urban-and-peri-urban setting.
articles.authors: Muwonge H, Akugizibwe R, Bbosa G, Damani AM, Nakidde B, Mugahi R, Byakika-Kibwika P
articles.details: Analysis of 332 adults at six Ugandan outpatient clinics. Malaria confirmed in 16.0% by rapid diagnostic test and microscopy. Fever and cough were strongest independent predictors. Malaria positivity increased with symptom count: 0.8% (one symptom) to 100% (four symptoms). Four-point symptom score (fever, cough, nasal symptoms, sore throat) achieved 98.1% sensitivity and 47.3% specificity at ≥2 symptoms, useful for optimizing malaria testing in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Community SP delivery increases uptake and reduces provider workload
articles.title_original: Initiating community intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Senegal: qualitative evaluation of stakeholder acceptability and experience.
articles.authors: Ndoye T, Majeres Lugand M, Diallo AM, Sakho S, Pouye MM, Dieng K, Faye SLB
articles.details: Qualitative study (111 semi-structured interviews, 16 focus group discussions) of community-delivered intermittent preventive treatment for malaria in pregnancy (c-IPTp) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) in Senegal. Health providers perceived c-IPTp positively, reducing workload and improving access in remote villages. Key barriers: SP stockouts and unavailable pregnancy tests. Pregnant women found c-IPTp acceptable. Recommendations include ensuring supplies, improving reporting, incentivizing community health workers, and involving communities in decision-making.

articles.headline: Drug resistance mutations increasing while genetic diversity remains high
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity, population structure, and drug resistance of Plasmodium falciparum in Kenya: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Nyataya JN, Mutsaers M, Masakhwe CA, Laigong AC, Kattenberg JH, Rosanas-Urgell A, Waitumbi JN
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 58 articles on Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity and drug resistance in Kenya (2002–2023). Expected heterozygosity: Lake Basin 0.79, Highlands 0.80, Coastal region 0.96. Multiclonal infections (34–80%), mean multiplicity of infection 2–4.8. Pfcrt CVIET haplotype decreased from 96% (1999) to 1% (2017); CVMNK increased 6.8% to 98.8%. Temporal increases in Pfdhfr-51I, Pfdhfr-59R, Pfdhps-437G, Pfdhps-540E. Pfk13 mutations (R539T, N458Y, R561H, A675V, V568G) detected 2018–2024.

articles.headline: Global NTDM incidence rising despite declining mortality and prevalence
articles.title_original: Global, regional, national burden, trends and health inequality of neglected tropical diseases and malaria from 1990 to 2021.
articles.authors: Feng L, Mo G, Liu Z, Liu Q
articles.details: Secondary analysis of Global Burden of Disease 2021 data (1990–2021). Global age-standardized incidence rate (ASIR) increased 0.59%, while age-standardized prevalence rate (ASPR) declined 58.05%, death rate (ASDR) declined 25%, and disability-adjusted life years (DALY) rate declined 32.36%. Western Sub-Saharan Africa bore highest burden; children under five faced highest ASIR and ASDR. Projections to 2050 suggest continued ASPR decline but significant increases in ASIR, ASDR, and DALY rates with sustained regional gaps.

articles.headline: Deep learning classifies malaria parasite stages from blood smear images
articles.title_original: Mobile phone-based plasmodium parasites stage detection from Giemsa stained blood smear by convolutional neural networks.
articles.authors: Bedir H, Arslan MÖ, Akıner MM, Öztürk M, Uygun ZO
articles.details: Study developing convolutional neural network (CNN) pipeline for automated detection and staging of malaria parasites from Giemsa-stained blood smear images of Plasmodium vivax. Dataset annotated into four classes: Ring Form, Trophozoite, Schizont, and Uninfected red blood cells. Overall classification accuracy 92.4% (precision 93.1%, recall 92.8%). Schizont stage achieved highest accuracy (94.7%), Ring Form 91.2%. Approach demonstrates scalability for deployment in resource-constrained diagnostic workflows.

articles.headline: P. vivax surges while malaria resurgence expands into urban highlands
articles.title_original: Resurgence of malaria in the Amhara Region, Ethiopia (2014-2024): trends, spatial expansion, and control challenges.
articles.authors: Lake MW, Asemahagn MA, Nigussie TZ, Gelaye KA, Muchie KF, Engedaw HA, Yenesew MA
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 11 years (January 2014–December 2024) of weekly malaria surveillance from 166 districts in Amhara Region, Ethiopia (7,710,733 cases, 162 deaths). Trend inflection in late 2018: prior decline (Annual Percent Change −13.2%) shifted to resurgence (APC +12.6%). Annual Parasite Incidence rose from 10.9 per 1000 (2018) to 74.8 per 1000 (2024). Plasmodium vivax contribution increased from 25% to 43%; incidence surged 11-fold (2.7 to 32.4 per 1000). Test positivity rate increased from 10% to >50%. Resurgence occurred in 83% of districts with expansion into urban and previously low-transmission highland areas.

articles.headline: Comprehensive surveillance and vector control prevent malaria reintroduction
articles.title_original: Imported malaria: a silent and forgotten threat in malaria free zones?
articles.authors: Lusiyana N, Ahmad RA, Elyazar I, Murhandarwati EEH
articles.details: Systematic review of 149 articles (24 in-depth analysis) using PRISMA-ScR framework examining strategies controlling imported malaria in malaria-free regions. Reintroduction driven by imported cases from endemic areas, cross-border movements, and conflicts. All studies implemented imported case surveillance; some included vector surveillance (6 articles), vector control (7 articles), mass drug administration (3 articles), and chemoprophylaxis (3 articles). Authors conclude case-based surveillance alone insufficient; comprehensive strategies combining case management, vector control, and cross-border initiatives essential for sustained elimination.

articles.headline: Low asymptomatic P. falciparum prevalence in pregnant Somali women
articles.title_original: Prevalence of asymptomatic malaria and associated factors among pregnant women in Mogadishu, Somalia: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Abdi MY, Mohamud FA, Abdullahi SA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 171 asymptomatic pregnant women at Ayaan Hospital, Mogadishu, Somalia (February–March 2025). Asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum infection prevalence 2.9%; P. falciparum was only species detected. No significant associations between infection and sociodemographic, obstetric, or malaria-prevention factors. Younger age (OR 0.24), urban residence (OR 0.12), and insecticide-treated net utilization (OR 0.06) associated with lower odds of infection, suggesting potential protective effects.

articles.headline: Eschweilenol C from Terminalia disrupts hemoglobin metabolism in malaria
articles.title_original: Eschweilenol C and subfractions from Terminalia plants disrupt haemoglobin metabolism, inhibiting Plasmodium falciparum growth at rings and trophozoite stages.
articles.authors: Tali MBT, Mbouna CDJ, Zehra A, Wouamba SCN, Mouthe GPK, Kamche AY, Dize D, Kouam SF, Kamdem BP, Tchouankeu JC, Khan F, Boyom FF
articles.details: In vitro study screening fractions from Terminalia ivorensis (TiWEa) and T. brownii (TbMMeOH:Ea) against Plasmodium falciparum asexual and sexual stages. Fractionation yielded five subfractions from TiWEa and three from TbMMeOH:Ea plus isolated compound eschweilenol C. All subfractions showed activity on susceptible (3D7) and multidrug-resistant (Dd2) strains with IC50 0.15–5.73 µg/ml. Eschweilenol C: IC50 490.74 nM (Dd2), 379.20 nM (3D7). Top hits (Ti03, Ti04, eschweilenol C) demonstrated IC50 <1 µg/ml, selectivity >10, reduced haemozoin production, and killing activity on ring and trophozoite stages.

articles.headline: Malaria-anemia co-occurrence affects 6.4% of Ghanaian children under five
articles.title_original: The double burden: co-occurrence of malaria and anaemia in children under five in Ghana - a multilevel mixed-effects logistic regression analysis.
articles.authors: Karikari JK, Bedu-Addo K, Hassan YSA, Konadu E, Addae M, Amponsah OKO, Opoku DA
articles.details: Analysis of 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey data using multilevel logistic regression examining malaria and anemia co-occurrence (double burden) in children under five. Prevalence 6.4% (95% CI 5.5–7.5). Risk factors: fifth-born children (aOR 3.92), recent fever (aOR 2.49), maternal primary education (aOR 1.87). Protective factors: elder-headed households (aOR 0.33), middle-income/richer households (aOR 0.31). Authors conclude co-occurrence remains major health concern; integrated interventions combining malaria prevention, nutrition, and anemia control essential.

articles.headline: Neuroschistosomiasis in pregnancy mimics malaria; case from Malawi
articles.title_original: Reversible paralysis in pregnancy, thinking beyond malaria: a case report.
articles.authors: Sous M, Boti M, Mose B, Sous W
articles.details: Case report of 17-year-old primigravida in Malawi presenting with fever, headache, neck pain, and abdominal pain, initially diagnosed with malaria and treated with artemisinin-based oral therapy. Twelve days later developed progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness and neurogenic bladder. Blood film positive for malaria; urine studies showed schistosomiasis ova. Diagnosed with neuroschistosomiasis; treated with prednisone followed by praziquantel. Patient became ambulatory after three weeks of physiotherapy.

articles.headline: PfPI3K inhibition reverses artemisinin resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: PfPI3K inhibition reversed artemisinin resistance by increasing ROS and ubiquitinated protein levels in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Shi N, Wang R, Yu T, Yang J, Chen L, Li Q, Li Y, Zhang S
articles.details: Study shows combination treatment with idelalisib (PfPI3K inhibitor) and dihydroartemisinin exerts synergistic antimalarial effects and reverses artemisinin resistance. Idelalisib decreases PI3P levels, inhibiting ROS degradation and leading to ROS accumulation, apoptosis, and ubiquitinated protein accumulation. Mechanism involves dysregulated antioxidant system and disrupted proteostasis in parasites.

articles.headline: T-cell response dominated by IL-10 in uncomplicated malaria
articles.title_original: CD4 and CD8 T-cell response is dominated by IL-10-secreting cells in children with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Ariera BO, Guyah B, Onditi I, Waomba K, Koech E, Sabourin KR, Samayoa-Reyes G, Rochford R, Ogolla S
articles.details: Study compared 10 Kenyan children with acute P. falciparum malaria (baseline and 4-week recovery) to 10 healthy controls. T-cell activation markers were comparable across groups, but acute malaria induced a shift toward IL-10 secretion in both CD4 and CD8 T cells. Suggests malaria-induced immune alterations are systemic, affecting overall immune suppression rather than EBV-specific responses.

articles.headline: Acute kidney injury increases mortality in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Identification of acute kidney injury in African children with severe malaria: a multinational individual participant data meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Bond C, Batte A, Afolayan F, Anstey N, Bassat Q, Bejon P, Berkley JA, Evans RDR, Goldstein SL, Hawkes MT, Ibrahim O, Imani P, John CC, Kain KC, Liepmann C, Marsh K, Namazzi R, Nakuya M, O'Brien N, Oluwadare LP, Opoka RO, Sserunkuuma J, Wynkoop H, Conroy AL
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 18 studies from 8 African countries covering 13,528 children (3 months–16 years) with severe P. falciparum malaria over 33 years, with 951 deaths (7%). AKI frequency was 47% (95% CI 46–48%), distributed as 46% Stage 1, 30% Stage 2, 23% Stage 3. KDIGO-defined AKI associated with 2.87-fold increased odds of mortality (95% CI 2.28–3.61) with monotonic increase across stages; WHO criteria missed 93% of cases.

articles.headline: NEK4 regulates meiotic initiation in malaria parasite
articles.title_original: A divergent Plasmodium NEK4 acts as a key regulator driving the early events of meiosis.
articles.authors: Yanase R, Hair M, Zeeshan M, Ferguson DJP, Brady D, Pasquarello C, Bottrill A, Bhanvadia S, Neal A, Tromer EC, Le Roch KG, Hainard A, Holder AA, Vaughan S, Guttery DS, Tewari R
articles.details: Study of P. berghei zygotes showing NEK4 (NIMA-related kinase) accumulates at MTOC and APC shortly after fertilization. NEK4 deletion blocks MTOC duplication, microtubule formation, chromatin condensation, and nuclear migration. Transcriptomic and phosphoproteomic analyses reveal NEK4 absence collapses meiotic and cytoskeletal networks, reducing expression and phosphorylation of HOP1, REC8, and AP2-O.

articles.headline: Locally acquired P. falciparum malaria in non-endemic Lebanon
articles.title_original: Locally Acquired Plasmodium falciparum Malaria in a Non-endemic Region of Lebanon: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Hashem S, Mahfouz T, Berry A, Salameh H, Tarhini M
articles.details: Case report of 70-year-old Lebanese female with no travel history presenting with fever, chills, headache, nausea, and abdominal pain. Laboratory findings: anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia. Microscopy revealed intraerythrocytic ring forms; RDT confirmed P. falciparum with high parasitemia. Treated with IV artesunate followed by oral artemether-lumefantrine with full recovery.

articles.headline: In-silico screening identifies PfMDR1 inhibitors overcoming drug resistance
articles.title_original: In-silico identification of antimalarial compounds targeting PfMDR1 of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Preetha TL, Yadav V, Subbarao N
articles.details: Computational study using molecular docking and dynamics simulations to identify antimalarial compounds targeting PfMDR1, a key drug-efflux transporter. Top compound achieved GLIDE docking score of −12.169 kcal/mol (vs. benchmark −5.435). Molecular dynamics and MM/PBSA binding free energy: −130.971 ± 14.283 kJ/mol, indicating superior binding stability and inhibitory potential compared to benchmark and experimentally tested antimalarials.

articles.headline: Clothianidin IRS reduces vector biting and malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Entomological impact of three years of clothianidin-based indoor residual spraying in two high malaria endemic districts in Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Chabi J, Edi CVA, Coleman S, Gbalegba GCN, Ouattara AF, Broudje BRN, Kouame RMA, Yokoly FN, Soro D, Konan LY, N'Tamon RN, Tia E, Yapo RM, Konan GK, Tea AS, Akre MA, Kpan DSM, Tanoh AM, Cissé NG, Flatley C, Kouassi BL
articles.details: Three-year study (April 2019–December 2022) in two IRS districts and two control districts in Côte d'Ivoire. Main vectors: An. coluzzii and An. gambiae. HBR declined 60% by third spray round in IRS sites (IRR=0.40, p=0.002) vs. no decline in controls. Sporozoite infection odds reduced 49% after first (OR=0.51, p=0.023) and second rounds (OR=0.51, p=0.043). EIR declined 4-fold: 3.92 ib/p/n pre-IRS to 1.25 ib/p/n post-IRS.

articles.headline: Treatment guideline adherence low in severe malaria cases
articles.title_original: Adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines in Northern Uganda: a mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Akello Z, Oloro J, Mukanyangezi MF, Asingizwe D
articles.details: Mixed-methods study in three Northern Ugandan hospitals reviewing 384 patient records and conducting 9 key informant interviews. Adherence to severe malaria treatment guidelines was 33.07%. Non-adherence observed for IV followed by oral treatment (38.3%) and treatment of only confirmed cases (25%). Medical doctors more likely to adhere (aIRR 1.7, p=0.035) than other professionals.

articles.headline: Seasonal chemoprevention augments PMC protection in sub-perennial malaria
articles.title_original: Rethinking a hybrid malaria chemoprevention delivery strategy for children in sub-perennial settings: a modelling study integrating age- and seasonally-targeted delivery.
articles.authors: Sen S, Schellenberg D, Penny MA
articles.details: OpenMalaria modeling study comparing perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC, sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine) alone vs. hybrid malaria chemoprevention (HMC: PMC plus additional monthly SP during rainy season) in children 3–24 months (HMC+: 3–36 months). HMC achieved 2.1-fold (IQR 1.6–2.6) and HMC+ 2.9-fold (IQR 2.2–3.6) burden reduction vs. PMC against clinical cases; protective efficacy 31.8% (25.4–38.2%) and 44.9% (36.9–52.9%) respectively. Against severe cases: 2.0-fold and 3.3-fold reductions; protective efficacy 16.1% (7.0–25.2%) and 26.4% (14.4–38.4%).

articles.headline: Secondary vectors sustain malaria transmission in Assam
articles.title_original: Evidence of secondary anopheline vectors in sustaining malaria transmission in Kokrajhar District, Assam, Northeastern India.
articles.authors: Singh K, Mohanty A, Malla WA, Ranjha R, Gam J, Ali R, Bharti PK, Anvikar AR, Singh H
articles.details: Entomological study in Kokrajhar District, Assam, where P. vivax and P. falciparum coexist. Despite LLINs and IRS reducing primary vectors (An. minimus and An. baimaii), malaria persists, implicating secondary vectors. Sporozoite positivity found in: An. maculatus 4% (5/80), An. minimus 4.8% (1/21), An. kochi 4.6% (1/22). Study demonstrates secondary anopheline species can sustain malaria transmission despite low sporozoite rates.

articles.headline: Commentary on malaria RDT accuracy in Africa
articles.title_original: A reason to celebrate in a time of uncertainty. Response to: Linblade et al. "Assessing the accuracy of the recording and reporting of malaria rapid diagnostic test results in four African countries-methods and key results".
articles.authors: Barat LM
articles.details: Editorial response to Linblade et al. study on accuracy of malaria RDT recording and reporting in four African countries. Commentary praises findings and places them in historical context of global efforts to scale up diagnostic testing for malaria over the past 15 years.

articles.headline: UA2239 blocks parasite egress by inhibiting cGMP synthesis
articles.title_original: An acyclic nucleoside phosphonate effectively blocks the egress of the malaria parasite by inhibiting the synthesis of cyclic GMP.
articles.authors: Ali M, Dura R, Guery MA, Colard-Itté E, Cheviet T, Robresco L, Berry L, Lionne C, Lavazec C, Claessens A, Peyrottes S, Wengelnik K, Wein S, Cerdan R
articles.details: Study of UA2239, an acyclic nucleoside phosphonate with guanine base, showing rapid and irreversible inhibition of P. falciparum parasites. Blocks egress of merozoites and gametes from infected erythrocytes by disrupting the cGMP-dependent egress pathway, likely targeting P. falciparum guanylyl cyclase α. Parasites develop resistance through mutations in downstream effectors rather than the target, indicating dual-stage antimalarial potential.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic barriers delay severe malaria treatment in Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Influence of Sociodemographic Factors and Household Decision-Making on the Delayed Presentation of Childhood Severe Malaria at a Secondary Facility in North-Central Nigeria.
articles.authors: Issa A, Lawal AF, Folorunsho M, Ibrahim OR
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 146 Nigerian children with severe malaria found 69.2% presented more than 24 hours after symptom onset. Failure to recognize illness (44.6%) and lack of funds (38.6%) were common reasons for delay. Father-dependent decision-making and lower socioeconomic status independently predicted delayed presentation (adjusted odds ratio 3.05, 95% confidence interval 1.10-8.48).

articles.headline: Host body size and migration increase avian malaria infection risk
articles.title_original: Host Traits and Avian Malaria Parasites: Understanding the Intrinsic Connections.
articles.authors: Pinzón-Cárdenas PA, González-Quevedo C, Rúa-Uribe GL, Pérez-Tris J, Rivera-Gutiérrez HF
articles.details: Comparative phylogenetic analysis of 115 Colombian bird species examined how host traits influenced susceptibility to haemosporidian (avian malaria) parasites. Migratory species, larger body sizes, and more colorful plumage were associated with higher infection probability, while habitat specialization showed negative association with infection. Results suggest increased vector exposure explains these patterns and provide insights into host-parasite dynamics in tropical communities.

articles.headline: Author correction: Genetic control system for Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Author Correction: A male-drive female-sterile system for the self-limited control of the malaria mosquito Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Strampelli A, Willis K, Gulliford HR, Gribble M, Fasulo B, Burt A, Crisanti A, Bernardini F
articles.details: This is an author's correction to a previously published article describing a male-drive female-sterile genetic control system for the malaria mosquito Anopheles gambiae. No additional details of the specific correction are provided in this notice.

articles.headline: Plasmodium sporozoites disrupt mosquito salivary gland proteins
articles.title_original: High-resolution proteomics unveils salivary gland disruption and saliva-hemolymph protein exchange in Plasmodium-infected mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Alves E Silva TL, Kanatani S, Barletta Ferreira AB, Schwartz CL, Liffner B, Talyuli OAC, Olivas J, Nagata BM, Pala ZR, Pascini T, Alves DA, Zhao M, Suzuki M, Dorner LP, Frischknecht F, Coppens I, Barillas-Mury C, Absalon S, Ribeiro JMC, Sinnis P, Vega-Rodriguez J
articles.details: Proteomics study of Plasmodium-infected Anopheles gambiae salivary glands revealed structural damage from sporozoite invasion, with hemolymph-derived proteins accumulating in glands and saliva proteins diffusing into circulation. Saliva from P. berghei and P. falciparum-infected mosquitoes showed reduced immune proteins compared to uninfected controls. Results demonstrate conserved interaction between sporozoites and salivary glands across Plasmodium species.

articles.headline: New antimalarial Ganaplacide succeeds in clinical trial
articles.title_original: 'A sigh of relief': New malaria drug succeeds in clinical trial as existing treatments falter.
articles.authors: Kupferschmidt K
articles.details: Ganaplacide, a new antimalarial drug developed by Novartis, has demonstrated success in clinical trials at a time when existing antimalarial treatments are showing reduced efficacy due to drug resistance. The abstract suggests this drug addresses concerns about antimalarial resistance, though specific trial results, efficacy rates, or dosing details are not provided.

articles.headline: Low-density malaria persists in Malaysian elimination areas undetected by microscopy
articles.title_original: Persistent low-density infection in areas at risk of malaria reintroduction in Malaysia.
articles.authors: Jiram AI, Hisam S, Abdul Hamid MH, Jelip J, Noordin R, Musa KI, Ogu Salim N, Md Yusuf NA, Amir NF, Rasyidina A, Ismail NP, Hazwani Aziz DI, Abdullah NH, Kep A, Hussin Z, Ismail A
articles.details: Community cross-sectional survey of 3,322 asymptomatic individuals in 23 purportedly malaria-free Malaysian localities (June-October 2020) found no infections by microscopy but 1.86% prevalence (62 cases) by nested PCR. Predominant species were P. malariae (40.3%), P. vivax (29.0%), and P. knowlesi (24.2%), with all infections from Sabah and one Orang Asli settlement. Results indicate persistent parasite reservoirs in receptive areas despite elimination status.

articles.headline: Plasmodium detected in quarter of febrile Nigerian hospital patients
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of Plasmodium spp. Detection Among Acute Febrile Illness Patients in Two Regions of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Quiner CA, Ephraim AZ, Kwaghe V, Erameh C, Samuels J, Courtney LP, Kim JH, Edeawe OI, Vongdip N, Ejike OJ, Odia I, Asman K, Chebu P, Agbukor J, Matthew OD, Orok V, Owolagba F, Okhiria B, Ogbaini-Emovon E, Odion WM, Obagho BA, Fayomade R, Adams J, Oga EA
articles.details: Prospective study of patients with acute febrile illness at two Nigerian tertiary hospitals (August 2023-September 2024) detected Plasmodium spp. in 293 of 1,200 enrollees (24.4%). Larger household size (crude odds ratio 3.70), healthcare worker status (crude odds ratio 3.39), and age 11-14 years (crude odds ratio 1.86) associated with increased detection risk. Coinfections detected in 37.2% of Plasmodium-positive cases, including nine biodefense-relevant pathogens.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine cost-effectiveness varies widely in African scoping review
articles.title_original: Cost-effectiveness and budget impact of malaria, measles, and meningitis vaccines in Africa: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Anosike C, Ojiakor IM, Etiaba EI, Uguru NP, Ezenduka CC, Onwujekwe O
articles.details: Scoping review synthesizing cost-effectiveness evidence from 41 studies (2004-2024) on malaria, measles, and meningitis vaccines in Africa. Malaria vaccine cost per fully vaccinated child ranged from $4.20 to $52.35; cost per disability-adjusted life year averted ranged from $22.63 to $3,314.01. Estimated budget impact of introducing malaria vaccine in 41 African countries: approximately $185 million. Most studies found vaccines more cost-effective than no intervention.

articles.headline: Invasive and native Anopheles vectors coexist in Ethiopian urban habitats
articles.title_original: Co-occurrence of native and invasive malaria vectors in anthropogenic habitats in Metehara, Ethiopia: Opportunities for urban malaria control.
articles.authors: Ashine T, Kochora A, Assefa M, Negash N, Gidisa B, Hailemeskel E, Alemayehu M, Assefa C, Ebabu M, Mamo Ayana G, Nigusse T, Aberra Kassa F, Esayas E, Eyasu A, Chali S, Epstein A, Jaiteh F, Bradley J, Doumbe-Belisse P, Reynolds AM, Massebo F, Wilson AL, Weetman D, Donnelly MJ, Gadisa E
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Bioecological survey of 767 larval habitats in Metehara, Ethiopia (November 2023-October 2024) found 98.3% were anthropogenic. Among 2,078 collected adult mosquitoes, An. arabiensis comprised 63.7% with 21.8% human blood index and 0.4% sporozoite rate, while An. pharoensis comprised 26.4% with 4.2% sporozoite rate. An. stephensi (invasive) remained stable year-round; An. arabiensis showed significant seasonal variation in habitat positivity (p<0.01).

articles.headline: Ultrasensitive ELISA detects ultra-low parasitemia including HRP2-deficient P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Ultrasensitive ELISA for Accurate Detection of Plasmodium falciparum Infection.
articles.authors: Kobayashi Y, Okita K, Chen PK, Hasunuma M, Tsuneki T, Hayakawa ESH, Yoshimura T, Ito E
articles.details: Developed ultrasensitive ELISA combining protein detection with thio-NAD cycling to detect PfHRP2 at 0.782 pg/mL and pan-lactate dehydrogenase at 1.33 pg/mL. System detected P. falciparum parasitemia as low as 2.57×10^-5% in culture and 0.5 parasites/μL in whole blood. Sensitivity approximately 10-fold greater than existing ultrasensitive antigen detection kits; successfully detects HRP2-deficient parasite strains using lactate dehydrogenase target.

articles.headline: Guillain-Barré syndrome following Plasmodium vivax malaria in Ethiopian patient
articles.title_original: Guillain-Barré Syndrome Following Malaria: A Rare Case Report From Rural Ethiopia and Literature Review.
articles.authors: Abdela HA, Kimo NH, Woyimo TG
articles.details: Case report of 21-year-old Ethiopian male who developed acute Guillain-Barré syndrome following P. vivax malaria infection, presenting with ascending paralysis, areflexia, dysphagia, and autonomic dysfunction. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed albumin-cytologic dissociation diagnostic of Guillain-Barré syndrome. Patient successfully treated with intravenous immunoglobulin and supportive care, achieving full neurological recovery after 8 weeks.

articles.headline: VAR2CSA catenane improves CAR-T cell targeting of chondroitin sulfate-expressing tumors
articles.title_original: Protein catenation potentiates antitumor activity of malaria VAR2CSA navigation CAR-T cells in a mouse model of hematological malignancies.
articles.authors: Hu W, Tao Z, Guo W, Ding W, Huang S, Li J, Peng H, Zeng L, Chen Z, Qiu R, Kang Z, Yao Y, Chen X
articles.details: Developed navigable CAR-T cell system using malaria protein VAR2CSA to target placental-like chondroitin sulfate A (pl-CSA) expressed on hematologic tumors. AXVB catenane construct showed enhanced thermostability and target affinity compared to rVAR2 monomer. In cell culture and mouse xenograft models, AXVB-directed CAR-T cells eliminated CD19-positive and CD19-negative pl-CSA-expressing tumor cells and significantly prolonged survival in tumor-bearing mice.

articles.headline: Insecticide-treated net use among pregnant women in SSA: 63.8% prevalence
articles.title_original: Pooled prevalence and factors associated with insecticide-treated net use among pregnant women in malaria high-burden countries in sub-Saharan Africa: a multilevel mixed-effects analysis.
articles.authors: Mwebesa E, Musinguzi B, Legason ID, Opoke R, Agaba BB, Kananura RM, Mwangi A
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 4,950 pregnant women from seven high-burden SSA countries (2017–2021 MIS data). ITN use prevalence was 63.8% (95% CI 61.8–65.9), ranging from 49.2% (Ghana) to 90.5% (Niger). Primary education (aOR=1.36, 95% CI 1.21–1.53) and 1–2 prior births (aOR=1.24, 95% CI 1.06–1.44) were associated with higher ITN use; substantial regional variation (32% within-country variation) suggests context-specific interventions needed.

articles.headline: Artemisinin production and resistance: integration for malaria control
articles.title_original: Safeguarding artemisinin: integrating bioproduction innovation and resistance management for malaria eradication.
articles.authors: Farmanpour Kalalagh K, Rezaei A, Hazrati S, Nicola S, Moradi S, Davarpanah Dizaj S, Tariverdizadeh N, Beyraghdar Kashkooli A
articles.details: Review consolidating artemisinin production challenges and resistance mechanisms. Examines biotechnological advances in ART biosynthesis via metabolic engineering, molecular mechanisms of ART resistance (pfk13 mutations), and comparison of current treatments (ACT, TACT, QACT), emphasizing need for interdisciplinary collaboration to sustain ART efficacy.

articles.headline: Vector control challenges: insecticide resistance and malaria transmission in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Insecticide resistance and malaria transmission indicators in Anopheles gambiae s.l. in Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso: implications for vector control strategies.
articles.authors: Amara MF, Namountougou M, Konaté H, Kouadio KWU, Yaméogo KB, Kagoné ST, Diabaté A, Gnankine O
articles.details: WHO-standard bioassays on Anopheles gambiae complex from six neighborhoods showed high resistance to DDT and pyrethroids (with L995F and L995S kdr mutations), but susceptibility to bendiocarb and pirimiphos-methyl. Among 622 specimens, An. arabiensis (90.8%) predominated with 27.4% sporozoite infection; overall entomological inoculation rate was 10.6 infectious bites per person. Despite resistance, high Plasmodium infection rates highlight ongoing transmission.

articles.headline: Knockdown resistance mutations absent in major malaria vectors: systematic review
articles.title_original: Knockdown resistance (kdr) and organochlorine insecticide resistance in malaria vectors: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Abbasi E, Daliri S
articles.details: Systematic review (PRISMA, 6 databases, 5 studies) of kdr mutations in four Anopheles species (An. albimanus, An. darlingi, An. dirus, An. punctipennis). No kdr mutations at codons 1010, 1013, or 1014 in VGSC gene identified. Organochlorine resistance in these species attributed to metabolic mechanisms (mixed-function oxidases, non-specific esterases) rather than target-site mutations.

articles.headline: Modeling malaria control: vector control, immunization, and treatment in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of different intervention packages for malaria control under varying intensities of pyrethroid resistance.
articles.authors: Gervas HE, Mayengo MM, Mlacha YP, Ngowo HS, Okumu FO, Selvaraj P
articles.details: Compartmental transmission model for Tanzania incorporating An. funestus and An. arabiensis under varying pyrethroid resistance. At 50% resistance, 71% ITN coverage plus 50% IRS or 32% biolarvicide coverage reduces Re below 1. At high resistance (≥75%), vector control alone insufficient; adding ≥40% immunization coverage plus 80% ACT coverage and ITN treatment reduces infectious individuals to <20% and Re below 1.

articles.headline: Mobile surveillance for malaria control: review of mHealth integration with vector control
articles.title_original: Mobile app-based malaria surveillance and vector control integration: a scoping review of evidence from endemic settings.
articles.authors: Okereke PU, Okereke OW, Ogunyinka BO, Aluele SI, Tiamiyu RA, Afolabi MA, Umeh CV, Nwibo EU, Ibekwe SN, Ogbu AC, Okwor EO, Onwurah CC, Ogamba N
articles.details: Scoping review (PRISMA-ScR, 20 studies from 152 screened, 2010–2024) of mHealth malaria surveillance platforms and vector control integration. Platforms like MCBR, MCS, and MRRS improved case reporting timeliness and enabled GIS-based hotspot identification for IRS and LLIN targeting. Challenges include poor internet access, low digital literacy, and limited institutionalization; success requires interoperability and national policy integration.

articles.headline: High P. falciparum genetic diversity and MOI in Cameroon endemic area
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of Plasmodium falciparum using merozoite surface proteins 1 and 2 (msp-1 and msp-2) genes in Oveng and Mintom, South region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nanssong-Vomo CT, Feufack-Donfack LB, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Ibrahima-Ibrahima, Kiam CB, Youmsi Fotso GJ, Kemeni MN, Fotso BT, Abate L, Eboumbou C, Ayong L, Mbida-Mbida JA, Nsango SE
articles.details: Genotyping of 315 P. falciparum isolates in southern Cameroon using msp-1 and msp-2 revealed high allelic diversity (12 msp-1 alleles, 28 msp-2 alleles) with expected heterozygosity 0.98 (msp-2). Mean multiplicity of infection significantly higher for msp-2 (4.85) than msp-1 (2.40; p<0.05); 99% of samples polyclonal, indicating intense transmission.

articles.headline: School-based antimalarial chemoprevention with DP in Tanzania: implementation feasibility
articles.title_original: Implementation of the intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in school-aged children (IPTsc) in moderate and high endemic areas in Tanzania: a policy brief.
articles.authors: Makenga G, Baraka V, Minja DTR, Chiduo M, Mmbando BP, Seth MD, Lusasi A, Chacky F, Aaron S, David A, Mohamed A, Lazaro S, Molteni F, Bastiaens H, Van Geertruyden JP, Lusingu JPA
articles.details: Policy brief on intermittent preventive treatment in school-aged children (IPTsc) in Tanzania following clinical trial and implementation research. School-based delivery of Dihydroartemisinin-Piperaquine proved safe, cost-effective, and acceptable, achieving significant reductions in malaria prevalence; authors recommend IPTsc as complementary control strategy in moderate-to-high transmission settings.

articles.headline: Rituximab-induced B-cell deficiency increases malaria risk in lymphoma patients, Malawi
articles.title_original: Rituximab is associated with an increased risk of malaria among adult lymphoma patients in Malawi: a prospective observational cohort.
articles.authors: Eastburg L, Makhijani SA, Forconi CS, Moore D, Kharabora O, Kasonkanji E, Kampani C, Chikasema M, Roush SM, Kimani SM, Fedoriw Y, Juliano JJ, Moormann AM, Gopal S, Painschab MS
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 96 DLBCL patients treated with CHOP (n=59) or R-CHOP (n=37) in Malawi. Symptomatic malaria incidence: 8% (5/59) with CHOP vs 24% (9/37) with R-CHOP (OR 3.5, 95% CI 1.1–12; p=0.039). Among HRP2 antigen-positive cases, antigenemia significantly higher in R-CHOP patients (3.6 vs 0.92 ng/mL; p=0.011), suggesting rituximab impairs antimalarial protective immunity.

articles.headline: RTS,S four-dose schedule implementation challenges in sub-Saharan Africa: qualitative insights
articles.title_original: Recommendations to mitigate barriers to uptake and delivery of a four-dose malaria vaccine schedule: insights from the MVIP's qualitative evidence.
articles.authors: Price J, Collymore Y, Bawa JT, Mkisi RE, Buell C, Jalang'o RE, Chisema M, Amponsa-Achiano K, Gordon WS
articles.details: Qualitative analysis from malaria vaccine pilots in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi examining barriers to RTS,S/AS01E uptake. Despite good vaccine acceptance, many children missed some or all doses due to schedule complexity and tracking challenges. Study identifies guidance and tools to support implementation, emphasizing need for program strategies addressing crowded immunization schedules.

articles.headline: Five-decade malaria dynamics in Rajasthan: climate, vectors, and regional variation
articles.title_original: Echoes of resilience: spatio-temporal analysis of five-decade battle against malaria in Rajasthan, India.
articles.authors: Singh H, Singh S, Saroha P, Singh K, Sharma RS, Sharma RP, Gupta J, Gupta SK
articles.details: Spatio-temporal analysis of malaria in four Rajasthan ecogeographical regions (1975–2023) showed peaks in 1976 and 1996 with post-2000 decline; median API 1.0–1.93 across regions. Rainfall positively correlated with API in Desert (r=0.39, p=0.006) and Gangetic Plain (r=0.35, p=0.014) regions. An. stephensi and An. culicifacies resistant to organochlorines but susceptible to pyrethroids; highest P. falciparum prevalence in Aravalli.

articles.headline: R21/Matrix-M vaccine awareness low but acceptance high in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Awareness, acceptability, and willingness to pay for the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine: a cross-sectional study among pregnant women and nursing mothers in Enugu State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Dim OF, Chima UE, Iloabuchi FC, Anene-Okeke CG, Nwachuya CA, Onyehalu JC, Umeh AU, Agbo CA, Agbo JE, Isah A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 310 pregnant women and nursing mothers in Nigeria. Despite 84.5% unaware of R21/Matrix-M vaccine, 94.2% agreed to vaccinate their child and 79.7% declared willingness to pay (median $0.38). Age and health insurance status significantly associated with WTP, suggesting increased awareness campaigns could substantially improve vaccine coverage.

articles.headline: mRNA CSP vaccines achieve near-complete protection in mouse models
articles.title_original: mRNA delivery of circumsporozoite protein epitope-based malaria vaccines induces protection in a mouse model.
articles.authors: Wu NR, Beutler N, Hu X, Skog PD, Liguori A, Flores-Garcia Y, Maiorino L, Terada S, Lu D, Lai YC, Ndihokubwayo J, Schiffner T, Cottrell CA, Eskandarzadeh S, Alavi N, Kubitz M, Phelps N, Tingle R, Hodges S, Youhanna JE, Amirzehni S, Irvine DJ, Himansu S, Zavala F, Rogers TF, Burton DR, Schief WR
articles.details: Self-assembling nanoparticle immunogens displaying different Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (CSP) epitope regions were tested in a mouse malaria infection model. mRNA-delivered immunogens displaying junctional and NANP repeat epitopes were most effective, showing 99% reduction in liver burden and sterilizing immunity in some mice, substantially exceeding current licensed vaccines.

articles.headline: PBO-treated nets show modest added protection in Western Kenya
articles.title_original: Do Piperonyl Butoxide Long-Lasting Insecticide Treated Nets Provide Additional Protection Against Malaria Infections Compared with Conventional Nets in an Operational Setting in Western Kenya?
articles.authors: Rascón-García K, Lapp Z, Markwalter CF, Kimachas E, Abel L, Obala A, Taylor SM, O'Meara WP, Mangeni JN
articles.details: Cohort study of 768 participants in Bungoma County, Kenya (June 2017–December 2023) compared piperonyl butoxide (PBO) long-lasting insecticide-treated nets (LLINs) with conventional pyrethroid-only nets. PBO LLINs provided greater individual-level protection (OR: 0.70; 95% CI: 0.47–1.03), though the difference was not statistically significant. Authors conclude PBO-treated nets should be considered for scaled-up use in areas with documented pyrethroid resistance.

articles.headline: Diagnostic delay and poor healthcare access drive malaria mortality in Colombia
articles.title_original: Inequitable Access to Healthcare and the Malaria Burden in Colombia: A Value-Based Policy Approach for Sustainable Elimination.
articles.authors: Olivera MJ, Magali Caro H, Ricardo Gómez J
articles.details: Longitudinal ecological study (2010–2023) of Colombian municipalities examined associations between healthcare access and malaria outcomes. Diagnostic delay >48 hours (aOR 4.1; 95% CI 2.24–7.11) and very low healthcare access (aOR 3.5; 95% CI 1.94–6.37) were strongest predictors of malaria case fatality rate. Travel time >2 hours (aOR 2.8; 95% CI 1.62–5.15) and age <5 years (aOR 1.9; 95% CI 1.28–3.32) were independently associated with poor outcomes; only 39.7% of cases diagnosed within 48 hours.

articles.headline: Pregnant women with vivax malaria show high recurrence rates and genetic diversity
articles.title_original: Parasite genotypes and host immune mediators in primary and recurrent episodes of vivax Malaria in Colombia.
articles.authors: Duque-Isaza YY, Carmona-Fonseca J, Arango-Flórez EM
articles.journal: Rev Peru Med Exp Salud Publica
articles.details: Cohort of 41 pregnant and 46 non-pregnant individuals with uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria followed 120 days in Colombia. Parasite genetic diversity (measured by microsatellites Pv3.27, Pv3.502, Pv1.501) was higher in pregnant women. Recurrence frequency was 41.6% in pregnant women vs 8.7% in non-pregnant individuals; recurrences were genetically homologous in only 23% (pregnant) and 33% (non-pregnant) of cases. Authors conclude high parasite genetic variation in pregnancy may impair protective immunity development.

articles.headline: Indigenous Emberá communities perceive malaria as Western disease in Chocó
articles.title_original: Between their own and the western: the experience of Emberá communities in Chocó with Malaria.
articles.authors: Duque-Ortiz C, Osorno-Sánchez S, Gutierrez-Asprilla SD, Garcia-Tirado MJ, Nieto-Betancurt J, Guzman-Sánchez LF, Montoya-Cedula S, Pérez-Villa M, Botero-Palacio LE, Vera-Marin C, Hernández-Sarmiento JM
articles.journal: Rev Peru Med Exp Salud Publica
articles.details: Ethnographic study (October 2022–November 2023) with 250 hours of participant observation in six Emberá communities and 25 interviews analyzed via Constructivist Grounded Theory. Communities attributed malaria to Western origin, controlled through hygiene, mosquito nets, and organic food; the Jaibaná (traditional healer) addressed spiritual conditions alongside Western treatment. Authors recommend incorporating ancestral practices into healthcare but note this occurs empirically and on public demand rather than systematically.

articles.headline: MSP-2 antibody specificity associated with cerebral malaria and severe anemia
articles.title_original: Antibody responses to polymorphic Plasmodium falciparum merozoite antigens in Malawian children with severe and uncomplicated malaria.
articles.authors: Dobaño C, Rogerson SJ, Cavanagh DR, Taylor TE, McBride JS
