articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 269 Malawian children (93 cerebral malaria, 50 severe malarial anemia, 26 both, 92 uncomplicated) examined IgG responses to polymorphic merozoite surface proteins MSP-1 and MSP-2. Lower IgG response to MSP-2 type B (FC27) correlated with cerebral malaria, while lower response to MSP-2 type A (IC1/3D7) correlated with severe anemia. Discordant antibody-genotype responses associated with neurological sequelae post-cerebral malaria compared to full recovery.

articles.headline: Weather-based model forecasts malaria outbreaks four months ahead in Zambia
articles.title_original: Malaria outbreak prediction at the sub-district level in Zambia using remote sensing satellite data.
articles.authors: Ippolito MM, Kvit A, Xu T, Nande A, Bond C, Matoba J, Lubinda M, Shields T, Thuma P, Shiff C, Curriero FC, Moss WJ, Truelove S
articles.details: Study of Choma District, Zambia used 15 years of weekly malaria case data (2010–2024) with remote-sensing temperature and rainfall. Negative binomial regression identified mean nighttime temperature (November–January) and daily rainfall (December) as optimal predictors. Malaria cases increased with higher temperature (β=2.1; 95% CI: 0.7–3.5; P=0.003) and higher precipitation (β=23.5; 95% CI: −0.7–47.8; P=0.057). Model predicted 2020 outbreak within 4% of observed cases (2467 predicted vs 2367 observed). Authors conclude weather-driven forecasting can inform resource mobilization four months in advance.

articles.headline: Log-transformed polynomial regression best forecasts malaria in eight Indian states
articles.title_original: Forecasting Malaria in Indian States: A Time Series Approach with R Shiny Integration.
articles.authors: Ghosh SK, Ananthakumar U, Chougale PD, Somaraj AB
articles.details: Study compared time series models (polynomial regression, log-transformed polynomial, lagged difference, ARIMA) for forecasting malaria across eight Indian states (Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Maharashtra, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Odisha, Tripura, Uttar Pradesh). Log-transformed polynomial regression consistently outperformed other approaches on Root Mean Squared Error and Mean Absolute Percentage Error metrics. An interactive R Shiny tool was developed for real-time malaria forecasting. Authors highlight the critical role of appropriate modeling techniques for malaria surveillance and response.

articles.headline: Parasite genotype diversity differs between cerebral malaria and severe anemia
articles.title_original: Genotypic and antigenic diversity of Plasmodium falciparum merozoite proteins in Malawian children with severe and uncomplicated malaria.
articles.authors: Dobaño C, Rogerson SJ, Taylor TE, McBride JS
articles.details: Study of 261 Malawian children (93 cerebral malaria, 50 severe anemia, 26 both, 92 uncomplicated) using polymorphic merozoite surface proteins MSP-1 and MSP-2. Parasite genetic diversity was higher in severe anemia (mean 2.70±1.20) than cerebral malaria (2.25±0.89; p<0.01). MSP-1 K1-type more frequent in severe anemia but nearly absent in cerebral malaria (p=0.03); MSP-2 FC27-type associated with severe anemia (66%), IC1-type with cerebral malaria (61.5%; p<0.025). Authors conclude merozoite protein differences distinguish clinical presentations and suggest parasite antigenic variability contributes to malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: LLINs plus treatment is most cost-effective malaria control strategy
articles.title_original: Optimal control analysis of malaria infection in children under 5 years and pregnant women under the influence of temperature.
articles.authors: Robert PK, Irunde JI, Mtunya AP
articles.details: Mathematical model evaluated three malaria control measures—treatment of infected individuals, long-lasting insecticide-treated nets (LLINs), and indoor residual spraying (IRS)—in children <5 and pregnant women, focusing on temperature-dependent transmission. When tested individually, treatment was more effective than spraying or LLINs alone. Combined LLINs with treatment emerged as the most effective approach; applying all three controls simultaneously did not substantially improve upon the LLINs-plus-treatment combination. Authors conclude integrating personal protection and prompt treatment provides most cost-effective malaria management.

articles.headline: CNN with Otsu segmentation achieves 97.96% accuracy in malaria detection
articles.title_original: Optimized CNN framework for malaria detection using Otsu thresholding-based image segmentation.
articles.authors: Singh R, Prabha C, Abdulla S
articles.details: Dataset of 43,400 blood smear images (70:30 train/test split) used to develop CNN for automated parasite detection. Baseline 12-layer CNN achieved 95% accuracy, improved to 97% with EfficientNet-B7 hybrid parallel model. Otsu thresholding-based segmentation preprocessing further enhanced accuracy to 97.96% (~3% gain). Segmentation validated on manually annotated 100-image subset: Dice coefficient 0.848, Jaccard Index 0.738. Five-fold cross-validation confirmed robustness (94.8%–97.8% accuracy). Authors conclude segmentation-driven deep learning enables reliable, scalable, cost-effective malaria diagnostics.

articles.headline: WHO assesses value of next-generation malaria vaccines and mAb prophylaxis
articles.title_original: Value profile for Malaria vaccines and monoclonal antibodies.
articles.authors: Birkett AJ, Ansah E, Gordon S, Gyapong M, Hall S, Kelly SL, Laurens M, Penny MA, Shirey M, Slutsker L, Sparrow E, Ethelston S, Westercamp N, Wu L, Hamel MJ
articles.details: Vaccine value profile developed by working group (academia, nonprofits, multilateral organizations, public-private partnerships) assessing vaccines and prophylactic monoclonal antibodies in development pipeline. Existing licensed vaccines (RTS,S/AS01, R21/Matrix-M) demonstrated 13% decline in all-cause mortality and 22% drop in severe malaria hospitalizations following pilot introduction in sub-Saharan Africa. Document references WHO preferred product characteristics (PPCs, 2022) and new WHO guidance on malaria mAb development. Priority focus on prevention in young African children with increasing interest in transmission-breaking tools for population-level elimination.

articles.headline: Type-P5 ATPase structural features in Plasmodium parasites
articles.title_original: Homology Modeling of Type-P5 ATPases from the Malaria Parasite: Insight into Their Functions and Evolution, and Implications About the Effect and Role of Intrinsically Disordered Protein Structure.
articles.authors: Wiser MF
articles.details: This homology modeling study characterized type-P5 ATPase sequences and structures in malaria parasites, comparing subtype-P5A and subtype-P5B variants. An additional α-helix distinguishes P5A ATPases from P5B. The parasite enzymes contain multiple variable, low-complexity loops with intrinsically disordered structures that may protect the core architecture or mediate weak protein interactions. While substrate specificity remained unclear, P5A ATPases are likely ER transmembrane dislocases, whereas P5B ATPases show lineage-specific functional divergence from their fungal and metazoan counterparts.

articles.headline: Essential oil of Piper alatipetiolatum shows selective anti-P. vivax activity
articles.title_original: First Evidence of Anti-Plasmodium vivax (Plasmodiidae): Activity of the Essential Oil and 6-Ishwarone Isolated from Piper alatipetiolatum Yunck. (Piperaceae).
articles.authors: Ramos GQ, de Azevedo RG, de Oliveira AC, da Costa MLL, da Silva FMA, Sá ISC, Melo GC, Pinto Lopes SC, Pontes GS, Nunomura SM, Nunomura RCS, Roque RA
articles.details: Piper alatipetiolatum essential oil and its constituent 6-ishwarone inhibited P. vivax dose-dependently with IC50 values of 9.25 µg/mL and 3.93 µg/mL respectively. Neither compound was cytotoxic to mammalian cells at 24 hours (viability 94.7–98.3%). Molecular docking identified DHFR as the primary target (Ki = 2.27 µM for 6-ishwarone) with favorable predicted drug properties: high oral absorption, blood-brain barrier permeability, and non-mutagenic, non-hepatotoxic profile. The authors conclude both compounds are promising natural leads for antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Liberia applies epidemiological stratification to tailor malaria interventions
articles.title_original: Epidemiological stratification and sub-national tailoring of malaria interventions in Liberia.
articles.authors: Koko VS, Hinneh DL, Kumeh O, Washington T, Alade JO, Duokie DS, Wesseh CS, Bawo L, Davis T, Zawolo J, Konwloh P, Jackson D, Kollah TC, Jones L, Janafo AS, Jeuronlon MK, Galatas B, Temu EA
articles.details: Liberia's national program conducted subnational tailoring using modeled parasite prevalence (PfPR), routine health facility data, malaria indicator surveys, entomological data, and demographic surveys to stratify transmission risk and target interventions. Analysis of 98 health districts revealed median PfPR of 29% (SD 4.8%; range 17–37%), with 84 districts classified as moderate transmission and 14 as high transmission. Stratification findings informed revision of the national operational plan and guided resource mobilization for dual-active nets and vaccination scale-up.

articles.headline: Primaquine dose-efficacy relationships for P. vivax analyzed in meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Automated reporting of primaquine dose efficacy, tolerability and safety for Plasmodium vivax malaria using a systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Edler P, Rajasekhar M, Price DJ, Adam I, Awab GR, Barber BE, Brasil LW, Chamma-Siqueira NN, Chu CS, Cui L, Daher A, Gomes MDSM, Gonzalez-Ceron L, Grigg MJ, Karunajeewa H, Lacerda MVG, Ladeia-Andrade S, Leslie T, Ley B, Lidia K, Llanos-Cuentas A, Longley RJ, Manning L, Mekonnen DA, Monteiro WM, Moore BR, Nosten F, Pasaribu AP, Pereira DB, Poespoprodjo JR, Pukrittayakamee S, Rijal KR, Saravu K, Siqueira AM, Sutanto I, Taylor WRJ, Thanh PV, Thriemer K, Vieira JLF, White NJ, Yeshiwondim AK, Zuluaga-Idarraga LM, Guerin PJ, Simpson JA, Price RN, Commons RJ
articles.details: A living systematic review and meta-analysis of uncomplicated P. vivax efficacy studies (published since 2000) aggregated 9,270 individual patient records from 41 studies as of January 16, 2025. An automated R Shiny application was developed to generate country- and region-specific reports investigating how primaquine dose (mg/kg) affects efficacy, hematological safety, and gastrointestinal tolerability. Open-access reports are available for policy makers and researchers in vivax-endemic areas to assess clinical consequences of different primaquine regimens.

articles.headline: Drug retail shops in Ghana show low malaria documentation and reporting
articles.title_original: Exploring documentation and reporting practices in malaria tracking: a mixed-methods study among formal drug retail shops in Hohoe Municipal, Ghana.
articles.authors: Loglo V, Gmayinaam VU, Ampomah MA, Boateng P, Ansah EK
articles.details: A mixed-methods study of 57 formal drug retail facilities (9 pharmacies, 48 over-the-counter sellers) in Ghana's Hohoe Municipality found that 89.5% of shops lacked documentation and reporting tools; only 10.5% used paper-based methods (p = 0.004 between facility types). Participants (median age 26 years, 70.2% Counter Medicine Assistants) preferred digital reporting (61.4%), citing concerns for user-friendliness, data security, and real-time submission, but faced barriers including stress and resource constraints. Addressing these requires infrastructure investment, training, and collaboration between national and local pharmacy councils.

articles.headline: Blighia sapida extract shows antiplasmodial and hepatoprotective effects in mice
articles.title_original: Antimalarial, Antioxidant and Hepatoprotective Activities of Ethanol Extract of Blighia sapida K.D. Koenig (Sapindaceae) in Plasmodium berghei-infected Mice.
articles.authors: Akinyemi DO, Olorunfemi AB, Akinola O, Abiodun OO, Afolabi OK, Thomas BN, Gbotosho GO, Ojurongbe O
articles.details: Blighia sapida ethanolic leaf extract was evaluated in Swiss mice infected with chloroquine-resistant P. berghei ANKA using Peter's suppressive and Rane's curative tests at doses of 50–800 mg/kg (piperaquine/dihydroartemisinin 18/4 mg/kg as control). Maximum antiplasmodial suppression and cure were 77.02% and 71.94% respectively at 800 mg/kg; extract was non-toxic up to 5000 mg/kg. Hepatoprotective effects were significant, with 400 mg/kg reducing serum AST to 84.93 U/L and ALT to 68.48 U/L (vs. control 133.88 and 93.92 U/L). Antioxidant status improved with dose-dependent reductions in liver MDA and nitrite.

articles.headline: Plant smoke from Vitex payos and Peltophorum africanum repels malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Field evaluation of ethnomedicinal plants' smoke knockdown and repellent efficacy against pre-bedtime biting malaria vectors in Mazowe district, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Nyasvisvo DS, Sande S, Sithole R, Nhiwatiwa T
articles.details: Smoke from three native plants was tested against An. gambiae and An. funestus in Zimbabwe. Vitex payos and Peltophorum africanum achieved 100% knockdown of An. gambiae s.l. within 150 minutes with KdT50 values of 37.8 and 44.8 minutes respectively (vs. 77.8 min for Adansonia digitata). Twenty-four-hour mortality rates were 91.7% (V. payos), 80% (P. africanum), and 71.6% (A. digitata). In field hut trials, V. payos and P. africanum significantly reduced An. funestus biting by 86% and 73% respectively (p = 0.008 and p = 0.028).

articles.headline: β-Carboline PRC1584 shows high resistance barrier against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Characterization of β-Carboline Derivatives Reveals a High Barrier to Resistance and Potent Activity against Ring-Stage and DHA-Induced Dormant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Haney RS, Butler JH, Wardlaw LA, Merino EF, Mendiola V, Cooper CA, Mathew J, Tumwebaze PK, Rosenthal PJ, Cooper RA, Kyle DE, Rizopoulos Z, Baud D, Brand S, Totrov M, Carlier PR, Cassera MB
articles.details: β-carboline compounds PRC1584 and its analog PRC1697 demonstrated potent in vitro activity against both actively dividing ring stages and dihydroartemisinin-induced dormant P. falciparum parasites, including fresh Ugandan isolates, with a high resistance barrier. PfKelch13-C580Y mutation was associated with increased susceptibility to PRC1584, whereas PfKelch13-R549T and coronin R100K-E107V mutations showed no association. The authors propose this compound class represents a novel mechanism of action and offers potential for developing artemisinin combination therapies active against resistant parasites by targeting DHA dormancy.

articles.headline: Histone lysine methyltransferases emerging as P. falciparum epigenetic drug targets
articles.title_original: Yet to SET: Plasmodium falciparum Histone Lysine Methyltransferases.
articles.authors: Shah D, Karmodiya K
articles.details: This review examines histone lysine methyltransferases in P. falciparum epigenetic regulation and transcriptional control. The parasite's unusually AT-rich genome, limited transcription factors, and restricted heterochromatin make epigenetic control critical for survival and virulence. While histone acetylation and its regulators have been extensively studied, research on lysine methyltransferases and other epigenetic effectors remains limited. The authors highlight the parasite's epigenomic distinctness from its host and parasite-specific adaptations, arguing the epigenome represents a promising target for antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Platelet TGF-β1 drives cerebral malaria pathogenesis; targeting offers therapeutic hope
articles.title_original: Platelet-Derived Transforming Growth Factor-β1: A New Hope for Cerebral Malaria Treatment.
articles.authors: Liu S, Guo B, Song L, Chen G, Zhang T, Du Y
articles.details: Platelet-derived transforming growth factor-β1 (TGF-β1) is implicated in cerebral malaria pathogenesis through platelet interaction with infected erythrocytes and brain endothelium. Elevated local TGF-β1 induces endothelial apoptosis, fostering microangiopathy, blood-brain barrier disruption, and brain edema. Notably, reduced systemic and tissue TGF-β1 levels correlate with cerebral malaria severity. The authors propose targeting TGF-β1 via brain-targeted exosomal delivery of anti-TGF-β1 antibodies as a novel therapeutic strategy for cerebral malaria prevention and treatment.

articles.headline: Cell-permeable cpFdUTP derivative inhibits P. falciparum while sparing human cells
articles.title_original: Membrane-Permeable 5-Fluorodeoxyuridine Triphosphate Derivatives Inhibit the Proliferation of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nikolova V, Linnemannstöns K, Bendel ME, Machado M, Ganter B, Budimir P, Vogts M, Fischer C, Ganter M, Meier C, Dobbelstein M
articles.details: Cell-permeable derivatives of 5-fluorodeoxyuridine triphosphate (cpFdUTP) rapidly and potently inhibited P. falciparum proliferation in infected human erythrocytes, arresting development at the trophozoite-to-schizont transition by stalling DNA replication (confirmed with a P. falciparum nuclear cycle sensor line). Although cpFdUTP impaired human cell growth, supplementation with thymidine or cell-permeable deoxythymidine triphosphate (cpdTTP) selectively rescued human cells while maintaining parasite inhibition. This identifies a potential therapeutic window for cpFdUTP-thymidine combination therapy.

articles.headline: AsSGU protein cloned and expressed as transmission-blocking vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Cloning, expression and purification of AsSGU protein and its immunogenic response analysis as target candidate for transmission blocking vaccine against malaria.
articles.authors: Singh H, Kirar M, Janjoter S, Dahiya N, Yadav M, Sehrawat N
articles.details: Anopheles stephensi secretory glycoconjugate protein (AsSGU), a GPI-anchored protein expressed in mosquito midgut post-blood-feeding, was investigated as a transmission-blocking vaccine candidate. The assgu gene was cloned and expressed in E. coli BL21(DE3), yielding 0.2 mg/L recombinant secretory protein. In-silico analysis confirmed immunogenic potential. Recombinant protein was injected into rabbits for immunological response analysis. AsSGU is highly conserved across Anopheles, Culex, and Aedes mosquito species and has a critical role in ookinete invasion.

articles.headline: Cameroon malaria incidence strongly linked to humidity and rainfall
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal dynamics of malaria incidence in children under five years of age and correlation with meteorological variability in the North and Far North regions of Cameroon between 2015-2022.
articles.authors: Mouliom A, Ilorme F, Ateba JM, Ekoyol G, Fotso J, Kidwang JP, Tchopwe J, Djele S, Dzika JO, Essangui Same E, Ayong L, Ali IM, Eboumbou Moukoko EC
articles.details: Spatio-temporal analysis of malaria in Cameroon's North and Far North regions (2015-2022) found maximum incidences of 190.6 and 243.8 per 1000 person-years, respectively. Rainfall >1 mm/day increased transmission risk 1.2-1.4 times, while 70% humidity increased risk 2-3.7 times (RR 2.0 North, RR 3.7 Far North). Results reveal significant year-to-year variations with strong associations to meteorological factors.

articles.headline: Genomic profiling reveals Pfsa1-PfCRT K76T linkage in Mali
articles.title_original: Genome-wide SNP genotyping of Plasmodium falciparum isolates across Mali reveals major impacts of Pfsa1 and PfCRT K76T selections on parasite populations.
articles.authors: Niaré K, Dara A, Sissoko S, Crudale R, Koné A, Doucouré H, Sanogo V, Cissoko M, Sissoko MS, Tékété M, Koné A, Diarra B, Dembélé M, Koné B, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Djimdé AA
articles.details: Genome-wide SNP analysis of 458 P. falciparum isolates from Mali identified strong selective sweeps around the pfcrt locus. Pfsa1 mutation (ACS8 Y40F) was associated with higher PfCRT K76T prevalence (55.8% vs 26.6% in wild-type; P<0.005). Parasites carrying both mutations showed higher identity-by-descent, with 80% of K76T mutants and 64% of Pfsa1 detected in a major haplotype, suggesting critical parasite adaptation to drug pressure.

articles.headline: DNA repair pathways maintain P. falciparum chromosome structure
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum DNA repair dynamics reveal unique roles for TLS polymerases and PfRad51 in genome diversification.
articles.authors: Vishwanatha A, Zhang X, Liu YJ, Leung A, Herring M, Visone J, Chan A, Calhoun S, Deitsch K, Kirkman L
articles.details: Study identified distinct DNA repair mechanisms across P. falciparum chromosome regions. PfRad51 is essential for homologous recombination-mediated repair in the core genome, while subtelomeric regions utilize a Rad51-independent alternative repair pathway. TLS polymerases (PfPolζ, PfRev1) show variable hypersensitivity across the cell cycle in TLSΔ parasites but uniform hypersensitivity in PfRad51Δ parasites. These differential pathways maintain the unique chromosomal structure defining P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Implementation fidelity of malaria surveillance varies globally
articles.title_original: Implementation and fidelity of reactive surveillance and response strategies for malaria elimination: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Oo WH, Aye Tun N, Thu KM, Cutts JC, Htike W, Htwe EP, Oo MC, Zaw AK, Agius PA, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: Systematic review of 69 studies examined reactive surveillance and response strategies across regions. China achieved 100% case investigation completeness and 97% timeliness (95% CI 93-100%). Africa and Greater Mekong Subregion showed substantially lower timeliness: 56% (95% CI 26-83%) and 57% (95% CI 37-77%), respectively. Implementation fidelity varied significantly within and across countries, driven by differences in health system capacity and infrastructure.

articles.headline: Migrants in MENA region experience higher malaria burden
articles.title_original: The burden, clinical outcomes and risk factors related to neglected tropical diseases and malaria in migrant populations in the Middle East and North Africa: a systematic review and meta-analyses.
articles.authors: Elafef E, Maatoug T, Evangelidou S, Marti-Soler H, Adam A, Arisha AH, Hilali M, Hargreaves S, Bani I, Seedat F, Requena-Méndez A
articles.details: Systematic review of 16 studies (12,823 migrants across five MENA countries) reported malaria incidence in Sudan: 6.8/1000 in internally displaced persons, 2.72/1000 in refugees, and 7.3/1000 in refugees <5 years old. Case fatality rate ranged 0.2-1.5%, with 25.8% hospitalization. Severe malaria was higher in non-migrants than migrants in Qatar (50% vs 5.2%). Overall, migrants were disproportionately affected by both neglected tropical diseases and malaria.

articles.headline: P. falciparum digestive vacuole shows three developmental phases
articles.title_original: Heme processing in the malaria parasite, Plasmodium falciparum: a time-dependent basal-level analysis.
articles.authors: Garnie LF, Egan TJ, Wicht KJ
articles.details: Confocal microscopy and immunofluorescence analysis of P. falciparum digestive vacuole revealed three distinct developmental phases: lag growth (20-28h), rapid growth (28-40h), and plateau (40-48h). Parameters measured included DV uptake/growth, relative abundance of plasmepsins I and IV, and basal levels of hemoglobin, heme, and hemozoin. Key differences were observed between chloroquine-sensitive NF54 and chloroquine-resistant Dd2 parasites.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria complicated by sickle cell disease and hemolysis
articles.title_original: Protracted Febrile Illness and Coma in an Adolescent with Sickle Cell Disease and Malaria.
articles.authors: Judd L, Bevot A, Lalremruata A, Flesche C, Mayer A, Mezger M, Heideking K, Bélard S, Remppis J
articles.details: Case report of 15-year-old with sickle cell disease presenting with protracted febrile illness (5 weeks) including recurrent hemolysis, fever, and neuropsychiatric symptoms. Cerebral malaria was diagnosed and treated with artesunate, leading to prompt recovery. Hemolysis recurred 12 days post-treatment, illustrating complex interplay among malaria, sickle cell disease, and autoimmune hemolytic anemia. Emphasizes importance of timely malaria testing in vulnerable patients with travel history.

articles.headline: P. knowlesi metabolomics identifies drug targets by stage
articles.title_original: Mass spectrometry-based metabolomics uncovers distinct metabolic signatures and potential therapeutic targets in Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Uthailak N, Chotirat S, Anatjitsupha A, Thongyod W, Tipthara P, Sattabongkot J, Tarning J, Nguitragool W, Reamtong O
articles.details: Mass spectrometry-based metabolomic analysis of P. knowlesi across blood-stage development (ring, trophozoite, schizont) identified distinct metabolic profiles, particularly in ring stage. Glycerophospholipid metabolism and sphingolipid de novo biosynthesis were key pathways across all stages, while phosphatidylserine synthesis was ring-stage-biased. CDP-diacylglycerol-inositol 3-phosphatidyltransferase was highlighted as a promising therapeutic target based on stage-specific metabolites.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention appropriateness in East Africa
articles.title_original: Is Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention with Sulfadoxine-Pyrimethamine/Amodiaquine Appropriate in East Africa?
articles.authors: Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: Article title questions appropriateness of seasonal malaria chemoprevention using sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine/amodiaquine (SPAQ) in East Africa. No abstract provided; content based on title only.

articles.headline: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine superior to SPAQ for SMC in Uganda
articles.title_original: Pharmacometric evaluation of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine- amodiaquine and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine seasonal malaria chemoprevention in northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Bonnington C, Nuwa A, Theiss-Nyland K, Kajubi R, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa J, Ebong C, Nabakooza J, Opigo J, Salandindi D, Odongo M, Sararat C, Watson JA, Suwannasin K, Proux S, Koesukwiwat U, Tarning J, Imwong M, Tibenderana J, Nosten FH, White NJ
articles.details: Prospective 28-day pharmacometric study in Northern Uganda (1,250 children, 46% baseline parasitemia) compared seasonal malaria chemoprevention regimens. Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) demonstrated superior protection: 84% efficacy against any parasitemia, 86% against P. falciparum parasitemia, and 86% against clinical malaria (vs SPAQ: 56%, 46%, and 60%). Breakthrough parasitemias on day 28: DP 7%, SPAQ 31%, placebo 39%. Strong exposure-response observed for desethylamodiaquine but weak for sulfadoxine; most parasites harbored quintuple Pfdhfr/dhps mutations (SP resistance).

articles.headline: Species-selective proteasome inhibitors show antimalarial promise
articles.title_original: Optimization of Species-Selective Reversible Proteasome Inhibitors for the Treatment of Malaria.
articles.authors: Gahalawat S, Ray S, Zhang X, Deng X, Han Y, Chen Z, Lawong A, Shackleford DM, Katneni K, Chen G, Li P, Ng A, Zhong L, Hu M, McInerney M, Wang W, Saunders J, Collins D, Jayaseelan J, Noack CL, C Maity B, De N, Laleu B, Campbell SF, Phillips MA, Charman SA, Ready JM
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship studies optimized carboxypiperidine-based reversible proteasome inhibitors targeting P. falciparum 20S proteasome. Lead compounds achieved nanomolar potency against blood-stage parasites with selective inhibition of parasite versus human proteasome. Cryo-EM structural studies confirmed binding at the β5 subunit. In vivo pharmacokinetic studies identified promising candidates for further development as novel antimalarials.

articles.headline: Malaria and typhoid coinfection prevalent in Yaoundé
articles.title_original: Malaria and Typhoid Fever Coinfection Among Febrile Patients Attending a Health Facility in Yaoundé, Cameroon: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Netongo PM, Tchoutang AM, Eric MB, Tepa A, Nzuno MC, Kamdem SD
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 288 febrile patients in Yaoundé (June-August 2023) found malaria prevalence 44.79% (n=129), typhoid fever 37.15% (n=107), and coinfection 19.79% (n=57). Coinfection was significantly associated with fever (χ²=7.092, p=0.008) and profuse sweating (χ²=3.774, p=0.05). Jaundice was linked to typhoid (χ²=4.777, p=0.029). Environmental factors including proximity to ponds/water clumps and drinking water source were notable risk factors.

articles.headline: Blood-brain barrier disruption in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: β-Dystroglycan Downregulation and Astrocytic Alterations: A Possible Role in Blood-Brain Barrier Disruption During Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Karri S, Babu PP
articles.details: An experimental cerebral malaria (ECM) model was used to investigate BBB disruption mechanisms at early (5 dpi) and terminal (7 dpi) stages, with early artemether treatment as control. The study found increased Evans blue dye extravasation, FITC-dextran leakage, and brain edema at terminal stage. Loss of β-dystroglycan, altered astrocyte morphology, and reduced tight junction proteins (ZO-1, collagen IV) were observed. The authors propose that MMP-9-mediated β-DG cleavage disrupts astrocytic-vascular connections, leading to edema when dystrophin-glycoprotein complex is dysregulated.

articles.headline: PbIMC1j protein regulates ookinete microtubule organization
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei IMC1j interacts with δ-tubulin to orchestrate subpellicular microtubule organization in ookinetes.
articles.authors: He G, Feng Y, He D, Hu Y, Wang D, Wang S, Sun X, Shi S, Yang T, Hu X, Huang Y, Cui L, Cao Y, Zhu X
articles.details: This study examined how Plasmodium berghei IMC1j (PbIMC1j), a palmitoylated alveolar protein, regulates parasite structure and function. Gene knockout revealed that PbIMC1j absence causes defects in asexual stage fitness, altered ookinete morphology, and impaired motility/infectivity in ookinetes and sporozoites. The C-terminal coiled-coil domain is required for proper IMC targeting. PbIMC1j mediates interactions with IMC protein ISC1 and δ-tubulin (component of subpellicular microtubules), with TubD deletion showing disorganized SPMTs similar to PbIMC1j-deficient parasites.

articles.headline: PbRON6 maintains parasite infectivity and virulence
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei rhoptry neck protein 6 maintains parasite infectivity and virulence.
articles.authors: Narahari V, Patri S, Satpute MD, Vemuganti GK, Mishra DK, Kolli SK, Arun Kumar K
articles.details: Using a rodent malaria model, this study examined P. berghei RON6 (PbRON6), a putative ortholog of essential P. falciparum PfRON6. PbRON6 localizes to the sporozoite membrane with an extracellular C-terminal domain. RON6-depleted parasites failed to maintain infectivity and virulence, leading to prolonged mouse survival and chronic malaria with hyper-reactive malarial splenomegaly characterized by decreased B and T lymphocytes and loss of lymphoid follicles. The findings suggest that targeting PbRON6 in pre-erythrocytic stages could prevent clinical malaria.

articles.headline: Ribosomal RNA degradation during gametocyte development
articles.title_original: Degradation of ribosomal RNA during Plasmodium falciparum gametocytogenesis.
articles.authors: Grünebast J, Singhal R, Bromley R, Kanatani S, Watson K, Olson S, Dumetz F, Pascini TV, Tripathi A, Dunning Hotopp JC, Sinnis P, Llinás M, Serre D
articles.details: Oxford Nanopore direct RNA sequencing investigated rRNA dynamics in P. falciparum throughout intraerythrocytic development and mosquito stages. The parasite uses three rRNA types (A, S1, S2), with A-type predominant during intraerythrocytic cycle and early gametocytogenesis, while S-type increases in mosquito stages starting 3 days post-infection. Extensive rRNA degradation occurred during gametocytogenesis beginning in stage II and continuing through final stages. The authors hypothesize this degradation links to global translational repression and metabolic quiescence in stage V gametocytes, analogous to stress responses in bacteria and eukaryotes.

articles.headline: Cas9/gRNA gene-drive mutant allele formation rates in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Mutant allele formation and inheritance during Cas9/guide RNA-mediated gene drive in a population modification mosquito strain for human malaria control.
articles.authors: Carballar-Lejarazú R, Pham TB, Tushar T, James AA
articles.details: This study analyzed how Cas9/guide RNA-based gene-drive systems in Anopheles gambiae generate mutant target-site alleles. In outcross and testcross progeny, functional and nonfunctional mutant alleles were recovered at average rates of 0.08% (paternal) and 0.33% (maternal) per target gene per generation, with an overall average of 0.21%. Mutations originating in germlines represented 47% of total inherited mutations in testcross progeny, with the remainder from paternal and maternal effects. These empirically derived values inform realistic data-driven modeling of gene-drive dynamics in target mosquito populations.

articles.headline: Complement inhibition for reducing malarial anemia
articles.title_original: Inhibition of Complement Will Substantially Reduce the Anemia of Malaria.
articles.authors: Taylor RP, Lindorfer MA
articles.details: This review examines evidence that complement activation eliminates nonparasitized erythrocytes in malarial anemia, with at least 10 uninfected RBCs cleared per parasitized RBC destroyed via C3b opsonization. Complement is activated by three products from lysed parasitized erythrocytes: hemin/hematin, digestive vacuole, and hemozoin. Both childhood malarial anemia and post-artesunate delayed-hemolysis are ascribed to complement activation. The authors propose clinical trials testing FDA-approved complement inhibitors blocking C3 activation, combined with artesunate, with proof-of-concept studies in P. falciparum and vivax blood cultures first.

articles.headline: Sporozoite movement shaped by skin microenvironment biophysical cues
articles.title_original: Influence of biophysical and biochemical cues on Plasmodium sporozoite dynamics in skin tissue.
articles.authors: Gopakumar N, Chen H, Messenger LA, Park S
articles.details: This review summarizes how Plasmodium sporozoites sense and navigate the skin microenvironment after mosquito injection. The skin contains varying extracellular matrix composition, stiffness, topography, temperature, and humidity. Sporozoite movement is highly affected by diverse biophysical and biochemical factors including ECM components, tissue stiffness, and surface topography. Significant gaps remain in understanding how age-related, inflammatory, or mechanical stress-induced skin changes influence sporozoite navigation. Understanding underlying signaling pathways and gene expression patterns could identify novel therapeutic targets for initial skin-stage infection.

articles.headline: SMC reduces uncomplicated malaria incidence 50% in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Impact of seasonal malaria chemoprevention: a plausibility evaluation of routine data from health facilities in three implementing states in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Ikechukwu EC, Okereke E, Ogunmola O, Chukwumerije J, Emeto D, Salifu E, Balogun A, Oguoma C, Shekarau E, Ogbulafor N, Cassidy E, Rassi C, Oresanya O, Nnaji CA
articles.details: This pre-post plausibility evaluation assessed seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) impact in three Nigerian states using routine health facility data for children aged 3-59 months. Mean uncomplicated malaria incidence was 20 cases per 1000 children in 2021 (pre-SMC) and 9 cases per 1000 in 2022 (SMC period). After adjusting for confounders, malaria incidence was 50% lower (95% CI: 39-60) during the SMC period, with impact varying by location. All-cause fever incidence was 29% lower (95% CI: 14-41) in 2022. Severe malaria and deaths were too infrequent to measure.

articles.headline: Timely malaria treatment and post-discharge care improve outcomes
articles.title_original: The 24, 2 hours initiative: a game changer in malaria mortality reduction.
articles.authors: Ranjbar M, Katureebe C, Musanhu CC, Maureen A, Nabakooza J, Rukundo GB, Maiteki C, Mpimbaza A, Opigo J
articles.details: This paper introduces the "24,2 Hours Initiative" launched in Uganda, emphasizing rapid access to quality malaria case management. The initiative targets ensuring uncomplicated malaria cases receive treatment within 24 hours and severe cases receive urgent pre-referral treatment within 2 hours of health facility arrival. It emphasizes starting post-discharge malaria services within 24 hours after severe malaria hospitalization and proposes free community-based malaria case management for all age groups to improve affordability and accessibility for low-income households. The principles may be applicable to other high-endemic sub-Saharan African regions.

articles.headline: Tafenoquine and high-dose primaquine effective for vivax
articles.title_original: Effectiveness of tafenoquine and primaquine for radical cure of Plasmodium vivax: a meeting report from dissemination of results of the EFFORT trial to stakeholders in Pakistan.
articles.authors: Qurashi B, Zaidi SE, Mushtaque M, Faisal A, Haroon Z, Agha B, Hammal N, Hameed A, Raza A, Ali U, Khan A, Jabbar FA, Adam ZR, Mehmood T, Price RN, Ghanchi N, Thriemer K, Beg MA
articles.details: This meeting report discusses the EFFORT clinical trial findings on P. vivax radical cure in Pakistan, where P. vivax accounts for ~85% of confirmed cases. The trial compared 7-day high-dose primaquine (7 mg/kg) and single-dose tafenoquine (300 mg) to standard 14-day low-dose primaquine (3.5 mg/kg total dose). Both regimens were well tolerated and effective in preventing relapse, with high-dose primaquine showing modest added benefit over the standard regimen consistent with other South Asian countries. The findings highlight significant benefits of tafenoquine in P. vivax endemic settings beyond current 2024 WHO guidelines.

articles.headline: Zambia trial protocol: SP vs artesunate chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Parasite clearance and protection from Plasmodium falciparum infection (PCPI): a two-arm, parallel, double-blinded, placebo-controlled, randomised trial of presumptive sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine versus artesunate monotherapy among asymptomatic children 3-5 years of age in Zambia.
articles.authors: Martinez-Vega R, Chaponda M, Mousa A, Gwasupika J, Chaponda EB, Daka V, Mwanza S, Chipunga M, Beshir KB, Chopo-Pizarro A, Kaur H, Okell L, Hansson H, Hocke EF, Alifrangis M, Gosling R, Roper C, Sutherland C, Chico RM
articles.details: This protocol describes a two-arm, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in Zambia comparing parasite clearance and protection from P. falciparum between sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) and artesunate (AS) monotherapy in asymptomatic children aged 3-5 years. The trial evaluates how the K540E mutation in dihydropteroate synthase (dhps) affects chemoprevention efficacy. Children receive either placebo AS then active SP (n=400) or active AS then placebo SP (n=200). Primary outcomes include time-to-parasite clearance by qPCR through day 63, duration of SP protection, and symptom-free duration for those parasite-free at baseline.

articles.headline: High Pfdhps resistance mutations prevalent in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Polymorphism of the dihydropteroate synthase resistance gene of Plasmodium falciparum in three hospitals in Mbouda, Western Cameroon.
articles.authors: Nadia NAC, Cedric Y, Line GAP, Pacome TK, Armand GNG, Anaud NKJ, Esther DD, Edna GDM, Yval KT, Mbida M, Fuyou Z, Hu H
articles.details: This study characterized Plasmodium falciparum dihydropteroate synthase (Pfdhps) resistance gene in 296 samples from three Mbouda hospitals in Cameroon. P. falciparum prevalence was 25.34% and Pfdhps resistance gene prevalence was 74.66%, with A437G mutation at 56% and A581G at 64%. Mutant alleles showed higher frequencies (57.14% for A437G, 91.66% for A581G) than wild-type (42.85% for A437G, 8.33% for A581G). The age group 6-35 years were majority carriers, with females showing higher gene prevalence (77.8%) than males (71.8%).

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine awareness spans 7–96% across sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Public awareness and acceptance of malaria vaccine in Sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Ekhator E, Essien VG, Afolabi OE, Okundaye MI, Adewonbi T
articles.details: Systematic review of 33 studies (2021–2025) from sub-Saharan Africa examining malaria vaccine awareness and acceptance. Awareness ranged from 7.26% (DRC) to 95.6% (Ghana); acceptance from 10.3% to 100%, highest in Nigeria and Ghana. Education and occupation were consistently associated with greater awareness and acceptance, with healthcare workers as the primary information source.

articles.headline: Community trust and prior experience shape ivermectin MDA uptake in Kenya
articles.title_original: Community Experiences and Perceptions of the Broad One Health Endectocide-Based Malaria Intervention in Africa Trial of Ivermectin Mass Drug Administration: A Longitudinal Qualitative Study in Kwale County, Kenya.
articles.authors: Wangari W, Onyango T, Kazungu K, Nuru K, Kasiwa L, Chaccour C, Rabinovich NR, Mwangangi J, Maia M, Jones C
articles.details: Longitudinal qualitative study alongside BOHEMIA ivermectin MDA trial in Kwale County, Kenya involving 22 in-depth interviews, structured observations, and 15 focus group discussions. Trust and engagement with implementing institutions increased participation, while previous negative MDA experiences reduced involvement. Communities perceived the MDA as effective for mosquito and malaria reduction in both arms, and bedbugs in the intervention arm.

articles.headline: DP maintains 98.5% efficacy for P. falciparum with no resistance markers
articles.title_original: Treatment Efficacy of Dihydroartemisinin-Piperaquine for Uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Malaria in Timika, Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Kambuaya NN, Ramli MSS, Candrawati F, Kenangalem E, Prayoga P, Puspitasari AM, Noviyanti R, Trianty L, Indrawanti R, Simatupang M, Ubra RR, Hill J, Hafidz F, Poespoprodjo JR
articles.details: Efficacy study of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) in 75 P. falciparum and 75 P. vivax patients in Timika, Indonesia (May–December 2023), followed to day 42. P. falciparum cumulative recurrence risk 1.5% (95% CI 0–7.9%, PCR-adjusted); P. vivax 5.2% (95% CI 1.1–14.1%, unadjusted). No parasitemia on day 3; zero kelch13 mutations or plasmepsin copy-number increases detected, indicating no resistance emergence despite expanded DP use for intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Alp5a/Alp5b proteins essential for male gametogenesis and transmission
articles.title_original: Plasmodium actin-like proteins are essential for DNA segregation during male gametogenesis and malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Varshney A, Pandey E, Nirdosh, Mishra S
articles.details: Genetic study in P. berghei examining Alp5a and Alp5b roles in male gametogenesis. While individually dispensable during blood stages, simultaneous deletion of both proteins impaired DNA segregation during male gametogenesis, resulting in significantly smaller oocysts that failed to mature and died, blocking parasite transmission to mosquitoes.

articles.headline: Cryo-EM reveals Pfs230:Pfs48/45 complex structure for vaccine design
articles.title_original: Cryo-EM structure of endogenous Pfs230:Pfs48/45 complex with six antibodies reveals mechanisms of malaria transmission-blocking activity.
articles.authors: Bekkering ET, Yoo R, Hailemariam S, Heide F, Ivanochko D, Jackman M, Proellochs NI, Stoter R, van Gemert GJ, Maeda A, Yuguchi T, Wanders OT, van Daalen RC, Inklaar MR, Andrade CM, Jansen PWTC, Vermeulen M, Bousema T, Takashima E, Rubinstein JL, Kooij TWA, Jore MM, Julien JP
articles.details: Cryo-electron microscopy structure of endogenous Pfs230:Pfs48/45 complex bound to six transmission-blocking antibodies. Identified Pfs230's multiple domain clusters, membrane-anchored Pfs48/45 disk-like structure, and conserved epitopes critical for transmission-blocking antibody activity. Membrane retention through Pfs230–Pfs48/45 interaction was not essential for transmission to mosquitoes.

articles.headline: 43% of malaria trials lack post-trial access plans in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Post-trial access practices in conducted clinical trials for Malaria, Tuberculosis, and Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) across Sub-Saharan African countries: A quantitative study.
articles.authors: Seralegne Y, Wangamati CK, Bernabe RC, Mdala I, Zewdie M, Adane HT
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 37 trial respondents (of 300 invited) from sub-Saharan African malaria/TB/NTD trials (2008–2019). Findings: 43% did not provide post-trial access plans; 70.3% expressed need for PTA training. Overall lack of formalized PTA policies and commitments, underscoring urgent need for structured training and stakeholder collaboration.

articles.headline: Severe malaria in children: 4.5% mortality, respiratory distress key risk
articles.title_original: Characteristics and mortality factors of severe malaria in children and teenager aged from 5 to 15 years in Thiès region, Senegal.
articles.authors: Lawson ATD, Sidibé M, Mahmoud MA, Bahada BHB, Diop SA
articles.details: Retrospective multicenter study of 109 children aged 5–15 years (mean 9.56±2.87) hospitalized for severe malaria in Senegal (July 2019–June 2024), confirmed by RDT and/or blood smear plus ≥1 WHO criterion. Common presentations: coma (17.43%), convulsions (21.10%), prostration (27%), with 61.62% thrombocytopenia and 33% severe anemia. Mortality 4.5%, significantly associated with respiratory distress and ≥2 WHO severity criteria; 90% favorable outcomes, 5.5% with sequelae.

articles.headline: VAR2CSA epitopes selectively recognized by multigravidae antibodies
articles.title_original: Identification of Plasmodium falciparum VAR2CSA peptides differentially recognized by IgG of multigravidae through epitope excision.
articles.authors: Misal SA, Renn JP, Morrison RD, Cowles MV, Mahamar A, Attaher O, Dicko A, Duffy PE, Fried M
articles.details: Study using epitope excision and multiplex isobaric labeling to identify VAR2CSA peptides differentially recognized by multigravidae IgG with anti-adhesion antibodies versus primigravidae. Multigravidae IgG differentially recognized 10 epitopes conserved across multiple VAR2CSA alleles (Log2 fold change >1, p<0.05), identifying immune-preferred epitopes for subunit vaccine design.

articles.headline: IMC55 dispensable for P. falciparum intra-erythrocytic asexual development
articles.title_original: The inner membrane complex protein, IMC55, is dispensable for intra-erythrocytic development of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Vick GW, Fierro MA, Park L, Brooks C, Vasant M
articles.details: Functional study of IMC55, an inner membrane complex protein predicted essential by genome-wide screen. Using conditional knockout systems, IMC55 proved not required for asexual replication, parasite growth, or replicative fitness during blood stage, indicating its function is dispensable or redundant for intra-erythrocytic development.

articles.headline: Malaria burden and severity vary across Ghana ecological zones
articles.title_original: Clinical malaria in three ecological zones in the Volta and Oti Regions of Ghana.
articles.authors: Axame WK, Dinko B, Kweku M, Binka FN
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 459 participants across three ecological zones in Ghana. Malaria prevalence 15.2% (RDT) and 9.7% (microscopy), highest in Nkwanta (savanna). Geometric mean parasite density: Keta 11,715.6 parasites/µL > Nkwanta 7,616.4 > Ho 5,880.1 (p=0.0003). Anemia prevalence 79.3% overall with location differences (Nkwanta 94.1%, Keta 82.9%, Ho 69.8%; p<0.0001); severe anemia most frequent in Nkwanta (24.4%). ITN ownership 69.7%, usage 61.3%.

articles.headline: RDT reading accuracy 81% in Uganda; HCWs over-report positives
articles.title_original: Accuracy of recording of malaria rapid diagnostic test results in Uganda.
articles.authors: Ssewante N, Namuganga JF, Katahoire A, Musoke J, Mutesi N, Humes M, Griffith K, Aponte JJ, Soebiyanto R, Cooper S, Agaba B, Opigo J, Lindblade KA, Mpimbaza A
articles.details: Prospective study in 16 Ugandan health facilities (June–November 2023) comparing RDT interpretation by healthcare workers versus external experts on 37,137 RDT images. Test positivity 61.8%; strong agreement overall (κ 0.81, 95% CI 0.76–0.85). HCWs misrecorded more results as positive (7.2%) than negative (1.9%); higher agreement in Busoga (κ 0.86) than Lango (κ 0.78), with lower agreement for certain RDT products and faint lines.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence fluctuates 2.8–4.8% in Sudan across three years
articles.title_original: Epidemiological trends of malaria in Sennar State, Sudan.
articles.authors: Abubakr M, Mohamed NS, Sami H, Sabon K, AbdElbagi H, Osman OF, Ahmed A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey in Sennar State, Sudan (2018–2020) of 2,027 households (8,372 individuals). Fever prevalence: 41.0% (2018), 58.5% (2019), 48.4% (2020). Malaria prevalence: 2.8% (2018), 4.8% (2019), 3.3% (2020), with localities varying widely (Abu Hujar 13.6% in 2019, 9.5% in 2020). Malaria-related deaths: 12 (2018–2019), increasing to 22 in 2020. Annual percentage changes: 71.4% increase (2018–2019) and 31.2% decrease (2019–2020).

articles.headline: Copper homeostasis modulation in experimental cerebral malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: Cuproptosis-driven astrocyte reactivity exacerbates experimental cerebral malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Hou X, Mo X, Zhang X, Wang Q, Chen X, Lai C, Gao J, Wu L, Liu W, He J, Zeng X, Yin H, Deng Z, Liu T, Ye M, Liu Z, Jin X, Song J, Wang J, Huang B
articles.details: P. berghei ANKA-induced experimental cerebral malaria (ECM) in C57BL/6 mice tested copper ionophore disulfiram (DSF) versus copper chelator tetrathiomolybdate (TTM). DSF exacerbated ECM with increased parasitemia, blood-brain barrier permeability, and cerebral edema, while TTM counteracted pathological manifestations. In vitro studies confirmed DSF-induced astrocyte reactivity and cuproptosis marker upregulation, reversed by TTM.

articles.headline: Laboratory competency assessment for LAMP malaria diagnostic technology transfer
articles.title_original: Evaluation of laboratory workers competency on loop mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) technology for malaria diagnostics: a technology transfer assessment study (LAMP-TTA) during a multi-site clinical trial.
articles.authors: Tegegne B, Kamaliddin C, Pillai DR
articles.details: Technology transfer assessment of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) for malaria diagnostics in LAMPREG study involving 15 laboratory technicians. Post-training technical knowledge increased to median 5/5, and LAMP chemistry knowledge increased to median 10/10 (from 1/10 baseline). All participants achieved high confidence in running LAMP reactions with median raw results interpretation score 10/10 at follow-up assessments.

articles.headline: Malaria messages increase insecticide-treated net use in Uganda
articles.title_original: Effect of exposure to malaria messages on insecticide-treated net use among women and under-five children in Uganda: a propensity score matched analysis.
articles.authors: Mwebesa E, Agbi DM, Iha DT, Agasa LO, Mwangi A
articles.details: Propensity score-matched analysis of Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey 2018-19 on 8,868 women and 6,915 children under five. Exposure to malaria messages showed significant positive effect on insecticide-treated net use: average treatment effect of treated = 5.1% (95% CI: 1.5-8.9%) for women and 4.3% (95% CI: 1.0-7.6%) for under-five children.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic infections and secondary vectors drive residual malaria in Peru
articles.title_original: Submicroscopic infections and two malaria vectors are major contributors to residual malaria in an Amazonian village in Peru.
articles.authors: Guzmán-Guzmán M, Saavedra MP, Rodriguez P, Acosta C, Gomez J, Valdivia HO, Sanchez JF, Vittet M, Manrique E, Herrera-Varela M, Morales J, Bickersmith SA, Vinetz JM, Gamboa D, Conn JE
articles.details: Year-long longitudinal study in Loreto, Peru (n=353) detected 17:1 ratio of submicroscopic to microscopic malaria infections via qPCR. Plasmodium vivax infection rates 35% in outdoor occupations versus 21% in indoor/not working (p=0.012). Primary vector Nyssorhynchus darlingi and secondary vector Anopheles near costai G1 (newly identified with P. malariae in Peru) both detected with Plasmodium infection.

articles.headline: Bayesian method for classifying P. falciparum treatment failure versus reinfection
articles.title_original: Probabilistic classification of late treatment failure in uncomplicated falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Mehra S, Taylor AR, Imwong M, White NJ, Watson JA
articles.details: Developed PfRecur software using Bayesian posterior probabilities to classify recrudescence versus reinfection in uncomplicated falciparum malaria, addressing limitations of standard match-counting methods. In polyclonal infections (common in high transmission), current methods have unacceptably high false-discovery rates leading to overestimation of treatment failure. Validated using data from Angola study.

articles.headline: IRS with non-pyrethroid insecticides substantially reduces malaria in Zambia
articles.title_original: Impact of malaria vector control interventions implemented in Luangwa District southeastern of Zambia: A 13-year observational time series analysis of malaria trends.
articles.authors: Chinula D, Hamainza B, Chiwaula J, Mumba K, Zulu R, Ndhlovu K, Kiware S, Reed T, Killeen GF
articles.details: 13-year observational study (2009-2021) in Luangwa District, Zambia using HMIS data on malaria inpatient admissions and confirmed cases. IRS with pirimiphos-methyl micro-encapsulated formulation (PM-CS) reduced admissions (RR 0.28 [0.19-0.98] for ≤3 months) and cases (RR 0.25 [0.01-0.67]); deltamethrin-clothianidin (DC) showed similar effects on cases for one year. Scaling up non-pyrethroid IRS in late 2014 contributed to >90% reduction in admissions and >80% reduction in cases by study end.

articles.headline: Community perspectives on malaria control in rural South Africa
articles.title_original: Experiences of Community Members and Health Workers Regarding Malaria Control Programmes in the Rural Ingwavuma Community, uMkhanyakude District Municipality, KwaZulu-Natal Province, South Africa.
articles.authors: Thembakazi MT, Herbert C, Nelisiwe K
articles.details: Qualitative exploratory study in Ingwavuma, South Africa involving 8 in-depth interviews with healthcare workers and 8 focus group discussions with 88 community members. Community viewed malaria control measures positively, particularly health education, indoor residual spraying (IRS), and vector surveillance. Key challenges included illegal cross-border movement with Mozambique affecting surveillance and occasional community resistance to spraying activities.

articles.headline: Malaria microscopy infrastructure adapted for skin disease diagnosis
articles.title_original: Leveraging malaria microscopy infrastructure to diagnose common and neglected skin diseases using direct microscopy in Sumba, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Siregar GO, Harianja M, Smith DJ, Ataupah MR, Laiskodat RD, Bøgh C, Soebono H, Grijsen ML
articles.details: No abstract available. Title indicates leveraging existing malaria microscopy infrastructure in Sumba, Indonesia to diagnose common and neglected skin diseases.

articles.headline: Ivermectin mass drug administration showed inconclusive efficacy in Mozambique trial
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to reduce malaria transmission- safety and efficacy results from the BOHEMIA cluster randomized trial in Mozambique.
articles.authors: Chaccour C, Nicolas P, Martinho S, Mundaca H, Elobolobo E, Ruiz-Castillo P, Houana A, Montaña J, Mbanze J, Casellas A, Macucha A, Mael M, Soares A, Kiuru C, Gutierrez AS, Imputiua S, Constantino L, Vegove V, Cole G, Duthaler U, Ribes M, Mutepa V, Brew J, Munguambe H, Stanulovic S, Xerinda A, Materula F, Gorski N, Wanjiku C, Furnival-Adams J, Túnez L, Sam L, Collins L, Xia K, Hammann F, Rudd M, Rist C, Jones C, Maia M, Candrinho B, Rabinovich NR, Saute F
articles.details: Cluster-randomized trial in Mopeia, Mozambique (n=22,724) compared monthly ivermectin 400 mcg/kg to humans (±livestock) versus albendazole 400 mg control for six months. Primary outcome was malaria incidence in children <5 years detected by RDT. Malaria incidence was comparable across all arms (human-only adjusted IRR 0.99 [0.86-1.14], P>0.9; human+livestock IRR 1.05 [0.87-1.27], P=0.6). Operational challenges rendered efficacy inconclusive, but safety profile was comparable.

articles.headline: School-based dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine reduced clinical malaria by 41% in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Implementation and effectiveness of intermittent preventive treatment in school aged children using dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine to reduce malaria burden: an implementation research of a cluster randomised trial in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Makenga G, Mmbando B, Seth MD, Baraka V, Challe DP, Francis F, Mhina AD, Liheluka E, Minja DTR, Chiduo M, Mtove G, Mandara C, Gesase S, Segeja MD, Kamugisha M, Hayuma PM, Mbwana JR, Sebukoto H, Malabeja A, Tupa JB, Ngede SJ, Lusasi A, Chacky F, David A, Thawer SG, Mohamed A, Aaron S, Lazaro S, Molteni F, Nkayamba A, Bastiaens H, Van Geertruyden JP, Lusingu JPA
articles.details: Cluster-randomized implementation research in Tanzania of intermittent preventive treatment in schoolchildren (IPTsc) using dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP). Three annual rounds of DP delivered by schoolteachers to 127 schools (>73,000 children) with 77-80% coverage and excellent tolerance. After one year, IPTsc reduced clinical malaria incidence by 41% overall (95% CI: 31-49, p<0.001) and 54% in high endemic areas, and parasite prevalence by 81% overall.

articles.headline: Metabolic resistance genes metabolize PAHs in malaria vector Anopheles coluzzii
articles.title_original: Investigating the potential role of metabolic resistance genes in conferring cross-resistance to pyrethroids and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon pollutants in the major malaria vector Anopheles coluzzii.
articles.authors: Muhammad A, Ibrahim SS, Ismail HM, Irving H, Paine MJI, Wondji CS
articles.details: In vitro study of metabolic resistance in pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles coluzzii using microsomal fractions and recombinant P450 enzymes. Microsomes significantly metabolized fluorene (73% depletion, Km 58.69 µM) and fluoranthene (43% depletion, P=0.001). Recombinant CYP6P3 and CYP6Z3 metabolized fluorene (47.9% and 52.8% depletions), while CYP6Z2 and CYP6Z3 metabolized fluoranthene (50.4% and 60.3% depletions). No naphthalene metabolism detected.

articles.headline: High MSP2 genetic diversity reflects intense P. falciparum transmission in Chad
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of MSP2 and its implications for Plasmodium falciparum resistance in N'Djamena, Chad.
articles.authors: Abel D, Christelle Nadia NA, Cedric Y, Pacome TK, Bismarck N, Ngaryedji T, Merveille TT, Hervé MM, Deborah DK, Hu H, Michel N
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in N'Djamena, Chad (n=302) revealed P. falciparum prevalence of 40.72%. Merozoite surface protein 2 (MSP-2) analysis of 101 positive samples identified 12 alleles with genetic diversity of 9.37%, multiplicity of infections 1.26 (73.26% monoclonal, 25.74% double clonal, 1% triple clonal), and 11 phylogenetic clades. High genetic diversity indicates high transmission intensity.

articles.headline: Rapid diagnostic test recording errors in Nigerian health facilities
articles.title_original: Accuracy of recording and reporting of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Atobatele S, Sampson S, Orya E, Okoro O, Akpiroroh E, Okagbue H, Gab-Deedam SD, Bademosi O, Atu G, Lindblade KA, Mukhtar AM, Ayodeji O, Eugene EC, Aponte JJ, Galles N, Humes M, Griffith K, Cooper S, Ogbulafor N, Okoronkwo C, Ntadom G
articles.details: Cross-sectional evaluation in 16 facilities across Nigeria comparing healthcare worker-recorded RDT results with panel re-interpretation of 18,319 RDTs. Overall 90.2% agreement (kappa 0.80; 95% CI 0.75–0.85), but 6.2% misrecorded as positive and 3.7% as negative. Faint test lines and prior diagnosis influenced errors. Authors conclude improved training and supervision needed for accurate RDT interpretation and reporting.

articles.headline: High malaria knowledge but variable practices in Upper River Region, Gambia
articles.title_original: Evaluation of knowledge, perception, and attitude of malaria burden in the Upper River region of The Gambia.
articles.authors: Okafor UA, Yaffa S, D'Alessandro U, Ojeh VN, Filho WL
articles.details: Descriptive mixed-methods survey of 381 households, 31 key informants, and 7 FGDs across seven districts. Assessed knowledge (81% knowledge index), prevention practices (bed nets most effective), and treatment patterns. Over 12 years (2011–2023), malaria cases fell 87% in age >5 years and 45% in age <5; mortality decreased 98% and 89% respectively. Knowledge level influenced prevention and treatment choices.

articles.headline: Adequate antenatal care increases IPTp-SP dosing uptake in Ghana
articles.title_original: Association between adequacy of antenatal care and uptake of intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy among women in Ghana.
articles.authors: Bayuo FB, Antwi EO, Adu-Boahen N, Effah EA, Appiah SK, Opoku HA, Efiong MB, Acheampomaa G, Senyah MM, Bofa A, Afoakwah G, Haile ZT
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 3,692 pregnant women from 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey. Overall IPTp-SP (3+ doses) uptake was 61.3%. Women with ≥8 ANC visits had 1.50× higher odds of 3+ doses (p=0.001), first ANC in first trimester 1.74× higher (p<0.001), and receipt of all ANC components 1.62× higher (p<0.001). Authors conclude targeted ANC improvements will increase IPTp-SP uptake.

articles.headline: Structural modeling identifies child health as key malaria risk factor
articles.title_original: Structural equation modelling (SEM) for malaria prevalence and risk factors in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kakaire G, Chumoh EC, Salyungu M, Kerich G, Too RK, Kosgei M
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 2018–2019 Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey using structural equation modeling with four latent constructs (SES, Environment, Maternal Health, Child Health). Child Health strongest predictor of child malaria status (β=0.22, p<0.001), Environment marginal (β=–0.36, p=0.056). Model fit indices (CFI=0.786, RMSEA=0.064) indicated moderate fit. Authors recommend integrated interventions addressing child-level health and environmental exposures.

articles.headline: Human mobility drives malaria elimination strategy prioritization in Bangladesh
articles.title_original: Identifying malaria elimination strategies in the presence of human movement in Bangladesh.
articles.authors: Mahmud AS, Chang MC, Citron DT, Engø-Monsen K, Sayeed AA, Zaman SI, Uddin D, Rahman MM, Rahaman M, Islam MN, Maude RJ, Buckee CO, Chang HH
articles.details: Metapopulation model parameterized with human mobility and epidemiological data from Chittagong Hill Tracts. Estimated basic reproduction number varied substantially across districts despite similar incidence. Vector control in high transmission/high connectivity areas had greatest impact. Local elimination in low/intermediate incidence areas alone had only moderate overall impact. Modeling framework guides resource allocation for regional elimination.

articles.headline: Pfs230 domain 12 shows promise as transmission-blocking vaccine antigen
articles.title_original: Pfs230 domain 12 is a potent malaria transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.
articles.authors: Inklaar MR, de Jong RM, Da DF, Hubregtse LL, Meijer M, Teelen K, Bekkering ET, Grievink S, van de Vegte-Bolmer M, van Gemert GJ, Stoter R, Nagaoka H, Tsuboi T, Takashima E, Spruijt CG, Vermeulen M, Dabire RK, Arinaitwe E, Cohuet A, Bousema T, Jore MM
articles.details: Eight Pfs230 domain fragments produced in Drosophila cells and tested in mouse immunizations. Antibodies to D12 showed strong transmission-reducing activity in membrane feeding assays (complement-dependent), effective against naturally infected parasite carriers. D12 antigen recognized by sera from naturally exposed individuals with age-dependent antibody levels. Authors conclude Pfs230D12 is promising transmission-blocking vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Risk stratification targets malaria interventions to 75 health districts in Mali
articles.title_original: Geo-epidemiological risk stratification to select malaria interventions, case of Mali.
articles.authors: Cissoko M, Magassa M, Cissé IA, Coulibaly S, Samaké DS, Sanogo V, Koné M, Thiam S, Ballo T, Diarra S, Fomba S, Koné A, Gaudart J, Sagara I
articles.details: Analysis of 2018–2022 data from all 75 health districts plus national parasitology and entomology surveys. Four WHO transmission strata defined; 54 districts in highest/moderate transmission areas (84% population, ~18 million). Intervention packages identified: 61 districts for LLINs, 57 for SMC, 67 for IPTp, 19 for vaccination. Authors emphasize stratification critical for targeted deployment of control interventions.

articles.headline: US babesiosis cases exceed malaria despite lower research funding
articles.title_original: Babesiosis and Malaria in the United States: Epidemiology, Research Funding, Medical Progress, & Recommendations for Improvement.
articles.authors: Jajosky RP, Li W, Jajosky AN, Jajosky PG, Stowell SR
articles.details: CDC surveillance 2018–2022: 9,799 confirmed babesiosis vs 7,722 malaria cases; babesiosis exceeded malaria in 4 of 5 years. >90% malaria cases imported; ~95% malaria cases linked to absent chemoprophylaxis, P. falciparum most common. Babesiosis and malaria caused 70 and 32 US deaths respectively. NIH funding 2024: ~$4M babesiosis vs $169M malaria. Authors recommend leveraging malaria-inspired approaches to advance babesiosis management.

articles.headline: Ensemble deep learning achieves 97.93% accuracy for malaria parasite detection
articles.title_original: An optimized transfer learning approach integrating deep convolutional feature extractors for malaria parasite classification in erythrocyte microscopy.
articles.authors: Reddy CKK, Anisha PR, Almushharaf A, Talla R, Baili J, Cho Y, Nam Y
articles.details: Ensemble learning combining VGG16, ResNet50V2, DenseNet201, and VGG19 transfer learning models evaluated on blood smear microscopy images. Test accuracy 97.93%, F1-score 0.9793, precision 0.9793, outperforming standalone models (Custom CNN 97.20%, VGG16 97.65%, CNN-SVM 82.47%). Data augmentation and preprocessing enhanced robustness. Authors conclude ensemble approach reduces reliance on manual microscopy in resource-constrained settings.

articles.headline: Cryo-EM reveals parasite-specific ribosome states and cabamiquine mechanism
articles.title_original: Seeing is believing-Plasmodium falciparum translation in action.
articles.authors: Erath J, Pavlovic Djuranovic S
articles.details: Cryo-electron tomography and cryo-EM of P. falciparum ribosomes in infected erythrocytes resolved ten ribosomal states, five previously unseen in eukaryotes. Study describes parasite-specific translation elongation dynamics and shows how antimalarial cabamiquine disrupts elongation. Provides comprehensive view of parasite translation cycle.

articles.headline: Severe thrombocytopenia affects 27.4% of hospitalized malaria patients in Rwanda
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with severe thrombocytopenia among hospitalized patients with malaria attending Kigeme District Hospital, Southern Province, Rwanda: cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Ndatumuremyi J, Kagimbangabo JMV, Nshimiyimana I, Gaspard B, Sendegeya JP, Nizeyimana V, Byukusenge C, Nsanzabera C
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 124 malaria patients at Kigeme District Hospital, Rwanda (Dec 2023–Nov 2024) found severe thrombocytopenia in 27.4%. Risk factors: age ≤5 years (AOR 5.17, p=0.004), Plasmodium species (AOR 3.4, p=0.04), positive CRP (AOR 3.45, p=0.045), jaundice (AOR 2.875, p=0.045), epistaxis (AOR 1.84, p=0.042), high urea (AOR 1.73, p=0.001). Cerebral malaria inversely associated (AOR 0.189, p=0.028). Authors recommend early identification in severe malaria patients.

articles.headline: Optimized artemether-lumefantrine dosing achieves 95.5–100% efficacy in infants <5kg
articles.title_original: Pharmacokinetics, safety and efficacy of an optimized dose of artemether-lumefantrine in the treatment of acute uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in neonates and infants of less than 5 kg body weight: a multicentre, open-label, single-arm phase 2/3 study (CALINA).
articles.authors: Wounounou G, Tiono AB, Ogutu B, Manyando C, Sagara I, Schneitter S, Bassat Q, Gaaloul ME, Marrast AC, Demin I, Winnips C, Risterucci C, Hugot S, Hofstetter G, Qian Z, Su G, Zhang J, Renner KC, Cousin M, Venishetty VK, Sayyed S, Gandhi P, Kabore B
articles.details: Multicentre, open-label, single-arm phase 2/3 study of artemether-lumefantrine (5 mg/60 mg) twice daily × 3 days in infants <5 kg with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Artemether Cmax comparable to historical data (primary endpoint met); PCR-corrected Day 29 ACPR 95.5% (Cohort 1: N=22, age >28 days) and 100% (Cohort 2: N=6, age 1–28 days). Lumefantrine exposures and developmental assessments at 12 months comparable. Treatment well-tolerated.

articles.headline: Climate change drives malaria increase in Zimbabwe district
articles.title_original: Climate change and malaria in Chiredzi District, Zimbabwe: emerging evidence and pathways towards malaria prevention.
articles.authors: Musakwa W, Selamolela NB, Ndlovu A, Zanamwe C, Dhlandhlara B, Mandinyenya B
articles.details: Transdisciplinary study combining weather data, malaria surveillance, and community perceptions in Chiredzi District from 2010-2022 found significant positive relationship between climate change (increased rainfall, temperature) and malaria incidence (p=0.0479), with additional contributions from poverty and migration. Authors conclude long-term forecasting of malaria based on climate and socioeconomic conditions is crucial for prevention program planning.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention reduces infection rates post-administration
articles.title_original: Trends in uncomplicated and severe malaria following seasonal malaria chemoprevention administration in Nouna, Burkina Faso: a quasi-experimental pre-post study.
articles.authors: Gebreegziabher EA, Ouattara M, Bountogo M, Coulibaly B, Boudo V, Ouedraogo T, Lebas E, Hu H, Glidden DV, Arnold BF, Lietman TM, Sié A, Oldenburg CE
articles.details: Pre-post analysis of SMC administration in 285 villages in Burkina Faso found uncomplicated malaria rates of 8.5 per 1000 person-weeks during SMC weeks (95% CI 7.0-10.1) and 0.31 per 1000 for severe malaria (95% CI 0.22-0.40). Post-SMC uncomplicated rates declined 41%, 34%, and 22% in weeks 1-3, with severe malaria declining 47%, 47%, and 34% respectively. Authors conclude SMC protection duration may be shorter than previously observed, suggesting extended distribution rounds needed for longer transmission seasons.

articles.headline: COVID-19 severely disrupted malaria service utilization in Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria healthcare service utilization before, during, and 1 year into the COVID-19 pandemic in 20 sub-Saharan African countries using Generalized Estimating Equation.
articles.authors: Asnake AA, Eshun G, Osborne A, Sarfo M
articles.details: Longitudinal study of 4,726 participants in 20 sub-Saharan African countries found malaria healthcare service utilization declined dramatically 1 year into COVID-19: testing dropped from 71.77% to 59.12%, visiting providers fell from 80.61% to 1.25%, and treatment receipt declined from 97.97% to 15.23%. At 1-year follow-up, individuals were 45% less likely to be tested (AOR=0.55, 95% CI 0.48-0.63), 99.7% less likely to visit providers (AOR=0.003, 95% CI 0.002-0.005), and 99.6% less likely to receive treatment (AOR=0.004, 95% CI 0.002-0.006).

articles.headline: Education and social factors shape malaria vaccine uptake decisions
articles.title_original: Untangling choices: how awareness and socio-economic status shape malaria vaccination decisions among Ghanaian women.
articles.authors: Ansah RK, Tackie S, Asosega KA, Tawiah K, Danquah BA, Appiah ST, Twum RA, Omane-Adjepong M
articles.details: Analysis of 3,004 Ghanaian women from 2019 GMIS found 93.54% intended to vaccinate children against malaria, while 6.45% did not. Among vaccination supporters, 40.10% were vaccine-aware versus 56.90% unaware; among non-supporters, only 28.87% were aware versus 71.13% unaware. When accounting for social interactions, rural uneducated women exerted greater influence on educated rural counterparts, while urban educated women showed limited imitative effects (-4.06%). Authors conclude vaccine awareness and education strategies vary by socioeconomic context.

articles.headline: Health messaging and facility education increase net use in West Africa
articles.title_original: A comparative analysis of the predictive influence of access to malaria messages on utilization of mosquito nets by under-five children in West Africa.
articles.authors: Ajaero ID, Nwachukwu CA, Oramah CP, Ajaero CK
articles.details: Analysis of post-2020 Malaria Indicator Surveys in four West African countries found net use among under-five children ranged from 47% (Nigeria) to 86% (Niger). Malaria information from health facilities was a significant predictor of net use in all countries. Full regression model showed increased odds of net use in Liberia (OR=1.31), Mali (OR=9.90), and Niger (OR=3.67), but reduced odds in Nigeria (OR=0.31). Authors recommend region-specific interventions emphasizing health facility-based malaria education.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasite exposure shifts mosquito gut bacterial composition
articles.title_original: Effects of avian Plasmodium exposure on the microbiota of Culex pipiens.
articles.authors: Garrigós M, García-Ruiz O, Enkvist CR, García-López MJ, Moreno-Indias I, Ruiz-López MJ, Veiga J, Figuerola J, Videvall E, Martínez-de la Puente J
articles.details: Metabarcoding study of Culex pipiens exposed to Plasmodium relictum found 2,006 ASVs, with microbiota dominated by Wolbachia, Stenotrophomonas, and Faecalibacterium. Exposed mosquitoes showed no difference in alpha or beta diversity compared to unexposed, but had higher relative abundance of Bacteroidaceae/Bacteroides and Rikenellaceae. KEGG pathway enrichment revealed exposed mosquito bacterial communities enriched in biosynthesis and metabolism pathways. Authors conclude microbiota composition differs slightly with parasite exposure but causality requires further research.

articles.headline: Correction to EEG cerebral malaria diagnostic study
articles.title_original: Correction to: Electroencephalogram Features Distinguish Cases of Cerebral Malaria Among Malawian Children With Fever and Coma.

articles.headline: EEG biomarkers distinguish cerebral malaria from other coma causes
articles.title_original: Electroencephalogram Features Distinguish Cases of Cerebral Malaria Among Malawian Children With Fever and Coma.
articles.authors: Andrews A, Harrar DB, Zelleke T, Zhang J, Zhang B, Barber J, Dasan R, Amoah N, Findorff O, Krieger S, Barrett E, O'Brien N, Fuller CE, Moxon CA, Taylor TE, Griffiths MJ, Ray STJ, Postels DG
articles.details: Retrospective case-control study of 203 children with cerebral malaria and 87 with nonmalarial coma at Malawi referral center (2013-2021). Qualitative EEG interpretation achieved AUROC 0.85, quantitative waveform analysis 0.86, and combined analysis 0.90 in distinguishing malarial from nonmalarial etiology. Cerebral malaria showed characteristic features including higher voltage, slower frequency, more sleep elements, and abnormal organization. Authors conclude EEG is a useful biomarker for distinguishing microbiologic etiology of febrile coma in malaria-endemic areas.

articles.headline: Venezuelan migration predicts malaria incidence in Trinidad and Tobago
articles.title_original: Assessing the Potential for a Malaria Outbreak in Trinidad and Tobago Due to Migrant Populations From Guyana and Venezuela for the Period 2009-2019.
articles.authors: Narinesingh-Motie R, Ivey M, Edwards J, Edwards RJ
articles.details: Census of 211 positive malaria cases in Trinidad and Tobago (2009-2019) found P. vivax (68.2%) and P. falciparum (22.3%) predominated, with 91.9% imported cases. Cases originated from Guyana (39.7%), Venezuela (38.7%), and Sub-Saharan Africa (15.5%). Analysis showed moderate correlation (r=0.642, R²=0.412, p=0.033) between Venezuelan arrivals and malaria incidence, indicating population movement from high-risk areas significantly predicts transmission risk. Authors conclude surveillance, health education, and vector control are needed to prevent resurgence.

articles.headline: Antimalarial drugs differentially affect host mitochondrial function
articles.title_original: Comparative effects of antimalarial drugs on oxidative phosphorylation, mitochondrial dynamics and mitophagy in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice.
articles.authors: Olanlokun JO, Yahaya OO, Babarinde CO, Oloke OM, Steenkamp P, Prinsloo G
articles.details: Study in mice infected with chloroquine-resistant P. berghei (n=35) treated with amodiaquine-artesunate, artemether-lumefantrine, sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, or artesunate (10 mg/kg each). Artesunate caused 7-fold mitochondrial permeability pore opening and enhanced F0F1 ATPase (P<0.01), while sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine and amodiaquine-artesunate induced mitochondrial phospholipid peroxidation (P<0.01) and silenced mitochondrial complex gene expression. All drugs altered mitochondrial fission/fusion and autophagy gene expression differentially. Authors conclude different antimalarials produce distinct mitochondrial effects.

articles.headline: Bioclimatic zones restrict gene flow and resistance patterns in malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Genomic population structure and insecticide resistance mechanisms in the malaria vector An. coluzzii across contrasting bioclimatic zones in West Africa.
articles.authors: Amoako EK, Bennett KL, Hernandez-Koutoucheva A, Debrah I, Morang'a CM, Binaansim S, Asoala VA, Ariana C, Malm KL, Awandare G, Miles A, Clarkson CS, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: Whole-genome SNP and inversion karyotype analysis of An. coluzzii across West African bioclimatic zones found restricted gene flow between northern savannah and southern forested populations, resulting in marked differences in insecticide resistance profiles.
