Northern regions showed greater target-site pyrethroid and metabolic cross-resistance, while coastal Greater Accra exhibited distinct mechanisms. Authors conclude environmental barriers restrict vector population connectivity and shape geographical distribution of molecular insecticide resistance mechanisms.

articles.headline: Quinolone derivatives show multistage antimalarial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the Activity of 4‑Quinolones against Multi-Life Stages of Plasmodium spp.
articles.authors: Annunciato Y, da Silva EM, Araújo JE, Martinez LDN, Santana S, Workneh EA, S Alvarez LC, Magalhães ML, Andrade AO, da Costa WJ, Campolina G, Nascimento Santana M, Barbosa CS, Peres EPML, Moura CS, Dos Santos NAC, Bastos ADS, Barros RRM, Gazarini ML, Medeiros JF, Teles CBG, Costa FTM, Alves AC, Pereira DB, Pinto J, Cravo PVL, Guido RVC, Prudêncio M, Araujo MDS, Corrêa AG, Cassiano GC, Aguiar ACC
articles.details: Novel 4-quinolone compounds, including compound 1 (natural 2-substituted-4-quinolone) and synthetic derivatives 2-6, showed significant activity against Plasmodium sexual and asexual stages in ex vivo and in vivo assays. All compounds effectively prevented hepatic cell infections, with compound 1 displaying the highest activity against both sexual and asexual stages. Authors conclude these quinolone derivatives warrant further development as novel multistage antimalarials targeting asexual, liver, and transmission-blocking stages.

articles.headline: Sequencing reveals high drug-resistance markers in Cameroon P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Targeted Sequencing Identifies SNPs Associated with Antimalarial Drug Resistance and the CSP Vaccine Antigen in Plasmodium falciparum from Southwest Cameroon.
articles.authors: Efeti MT, Fankem SN, Diallo MT, Lahngong MS, Acha NL, Shey RA, Demeyer K, Souopgui J, Ghogomu SM, Njemini R
articles.details: Targeted Nanopore sequencing of 72 blood samples from Southwest Cameroon analyzed drug-resistance genes. High prevalence of pfdhfr mutations (98.6% N51I and C59R, 97.7% S108N), pfmdr1 Y184F (76.1%), and pfdhps mutations (54.2% S436A, 38.9% A581G). No WHO-validated artemisinin resistance markers found, though K189T (63.4%) and R255K (4.2%) detected. Nineteen non-synonymous CSP SNPs identified. Results support continued artemisinin use with sustained surveillance of resistance and vaccine polymorphisms.

articles.headline: Travel patterns and rainfall drive malaria importation to Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Integrating mobility, travel survey, and malaria case data to understand drivers of malaria importation to Zanzibar, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Muller JG, Dewalt K, Goel V, Ali M, Hassan W, Mohamed B, Choloi BB, Msolo D, Zacharia A, Nwange M, Kane R, Bisanzio D, Emch M, Björkman A, Reithinger R, Shija SJ, Ngasala BE, Juliano JJ, Lin JT
articles.details: Prospective surveys of 3,875 malaria cases in Unguja (May 2022–Dec 2023) and ferry travelers. Of cases, 1,172 reported mainland travel in past 30 days (55% in 2022, 27% in 2023). Children and females had higher odds of travel-associated malaria (aOR 3.2, 95% CI 2.2–4.8 and aOR 1.5, 95% CI 1.3–1.8). 32% were Zanzibari residents traveling to high/moderate malaria risk areas during dry season. Digital mobility and rainfall analysis showed imported cases correlated with mainland rainfall.

articles.headline: Polyester nets and net age influence ITN use in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Factors associated with insecticide-treated mosquito nets utilization for malaria prevention in Burkina Faso: finding from cross-sectional household survey.
articles.authors: Badolo H, Hien H, Hien AS, Somda SMA, Bazié H, Bacyé FY, Diallo CO, Tierno A, Guira M, Meda N
articles.details: Cross-sectional household survey in Burkina Faso. Polyester nets associated with higher use (OR 1.23, 95% CI 1.13–1.33). Older nets (6–11 months: OR 0.91, 95% CI 0.83–0.997; ≥12 months: OR 0.52, 95% CI 0.401–0.675) used less. Excessive ITN possession negatively associated with use (OR 0.66, 95% CI 0.597–0.74). Wealthier households (rich: OR 0.656; very rich: OR 0.557) used nets less. Results indicate net textile type and age are key factors influencing utilization.

articles.headline: Ferulenol inhibits blood and sexual stages of P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Disruption of mitochondrial function in blood and sexual stages of Plasmodium falciparum by ferulenol.
articles.authors: Niramolyanun G, Praikongkatham C, Nguitragool W, Sattabongkot J, Kangwanrangsan N
articles.details: In vitro study of ferulenol's antimalarial activity on cultured P. falciparum across lifecycle stages. Highest dose inhibited asexual blood-stage proliferation by 88%, early-stage gametocyte development by 82%, and stage V gametocyte maturation by ~90%. Male gamete formation inhibited 81% while female inhibition was 27%. Effects correlated with disruption of mitochondrial membrane potential assessed via JC-1 staining. Positions ferulenol as a dual-target antimalarial affecting both disease severity and transmission.

articles.headline: Insecticide resistance undermines vector control in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Managing insecticide resistance in malaria vectors in Africa: case studies from Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire and Tanzania.
articles.authors: Tabue RN, Gbalegba CGN, Mwalimu CD, Tungu PK, Fodjo BK, Elanga-Ndille E, Menze B, Mosha J, Wolie RZ, Clarke SE, Lines J, Abeku TA
articles.details: Mixed-methods evaluation of insecticide resistance management (IRM) plans in Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and mainland Tanzania (2023). Each country developed 5-year plans but Côte d'Ivoire and Tanzania plans were outdated. Plans aligned with national strategies but implementation hindered by inadequate domestic funding and heavy external donor reliance. Limited sentinel site coverage for resistance monitoring; sparse indoor residual spraying deployment. Major gaps in funding and surveillance limit effectiveness of vector control strategies.

articles.headline: IL-2 levels not associated with malaria severity in meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Association between interleukin-2 cytokine levels and Plasmodium infections: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Kwankaew P, Kotepui KU, Anabire NG, Wilairatana P, Kotepui M
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 30 studies (3,023 records screened) examining IL-2 levels in malaria patients. Meta-analysis found no significant difference in IL-2 between individuals with malaria and uninfected controls (P=0.25, SMD=4.56, 95% CI [−3.16; 12.29], I²=98.6%, n=1,074). Similarly, no significant association between IL-2 levels and malaria severity (P=0.57, SMD=0.37, 95% CI [−0.91; 1.67], I²=97.4%, n=694). Substantial heterogeneity across studies limits interpretability. Geographic region significantly influenced IL-2 expression patterns.

articles.headline: CNS malaria causes prolonged dysregulation of coagulation pathways
articles.title_original: Prolonged dysregulation of gene expression and biomarkers of coagulation, fibrinolysis, and endothelium in pediatric central nervous system malaria.
articles.authors: Dent AE, Rosa BA, Dobbs KR, Ogolla S, Midem D, Embury P, Skomorovska-Prokvolit Y, Hannon ER, Koech E, Schmaier AH, Otieno W, Mitreva M, Kazura JW
articles.details: Comparative study of Kenyan children with cerebral malaria (n=37), uncomplicated malaria (n=20), and acute febrile non-malaria illness (n=12), with transcriptome and biomarker sampling at baseline, discharge (2–6 days), 6 weeks, and 6 months. CNS malaria showed persistent upregulation of IL/cytokine signaling and neutrophil pathways with downregulation of Hemostasis pathway at 6 weeks. Biomarkers of coagulation, fibrinolysis, and endothelial dysfunction correlated with cell-free DNA in acute CNS malaria and persisted at 6 weeks.

articles.headline: Wealth and travel history affect urban malaria risk in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Modeling urban malaria infection in Anopheles stephensi hotspot area in Eastern Ethiopia: application of Structural Equation Modeling.
articles.authors: Merga H, Degefa T, Birhanu Z, Abiy E, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study of 329 febrile patients and 30 ASHAs in Dire Dawa, Ethiopia, using Structural Equation Modeling. Wealth index negatively affected ITN utilization (−0.66, p<0.001) and malaria knowledge (−0.63, p<0.001). Attitude positively affected ITN use (0.16, p=0.049). Travel history outside city positively affected malaria infection (0.969, p=0.01). Indirect pathways identified: attitude mediated through ITN utilization (coefficient=−0.091, p=0.038) and ITN use mediated infection (coefficient=0.029, p=0.048).

articles.headline: Nanotechnology improves sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine bioavailability and efficacy
articles.title_original: Advancing malaria treatment with magnetic and mesoporous silica nanoparticles loaded with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine: a review.
articles.authors: Egza TF, Kebede EM, Perumal RD
articles.details: Review of magnetic and mesoporous silica nanoparticle formulations for sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) delivery addressing reduced bioavailability, toxicity, and resistance-driven efficacy loss. Nanoparticles combine magnetic cores for targeted delivery with mesoporous silica shells enabling high drug loading and controlled release. In vitro and in vivo studies demonstrate improved pharmacokinetics, reduced toxicity, and enhanced efficacy against drug-resistant strains. Enables codelivery of synergistic drugs and theranostic systems. Scalability and regulatory challenges remain barriers to clinical translation.

articles.headline: Food vacuole transporters co-associate with artemisinin resistance mutations
articles.title_original: Role of novel mutations in food vacuole transporters beyond K13-mediated artemisinin resistance in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Junaid IT, Panda A, Deshmukh A, Sardar R, Narwal M, Agrawal P, Prakash N, Akhtar A, Dey AK, Singh SS, Mridha S, Mochi J, Parveen S, Kumar M, Nagar R, Gaur N, Gupta D, Mohmmed A, Kaur I, Karmodiya K, Malhotra P
articles.details: Proteome analysis identified 16 transporter proteins in P. falciparum food vacuoles. SNP analysis of 2,517 field isolates revealed coassociation of mutations in PfCRT, PfNT1, PfCTR2, and PfMDR2 with PfKelch13 polymorphisms (P<0.0001). Yeast complementation showed PfNT1-F394L, PfMFR5-S278T, and PfMFR5-Y570F mutants conferred dihydroartemisinin resistance, suggesting these mutations contribute to artemisinin resistance evolution alongside PfKelch13. Results indicate additional transporters participate in resistance mechanisms.

articles.headline: ASHA workers achieve high malaria case management adherence in India
articles.title_original: Implementation Fidelity of Accredited Social Health Activist (ASHA) Workers in Malaria Case Management in a High-Burden District of North India.
articles.authors: Selvaraju E, Goel PK, Gour N, Kumar A
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study of 440 mothers and 30 ASHA workers in Nuh, Haryana, India (May–Sept 2019). Of 222 children with fever, only 4 (1.8%) sought care first from ASHAs versus 80% benchmark. Only 9 (4.0%) tested within 24 hours of fever. Among 8 malaria-positive cases, ASHA fidelity high: 100% treatment initiation/completion, 87.5% follow-up (7/8). Maternal knowledge of transmission high (92.1%) but 73.6% preferred informal providers, limiting ASHA utilization.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria impairs immune and renal function in chimpanzees
articles.title_original: Hidden hematological, biochemical and immune costs of asymptomatic malaria infections in semi-wild chimpanzees.
articles.authors: Nowakowski A, Elguero E, Patterson C, Boissière A, Degrugillier F, Sidobre C, Arnathau C, Grentzinger P, Willaume E, Talman AM, Malleret B, Boundenga L, Ngoubangoye B, Prugnolle F, Wassmer SC, Rougeron V
articles.details: Investigation of 27 semi-wild chimpanzees from Gabon revealed 48.15% Plasmodium infection rate with frequent multi-species co-infections (P. gaboni, P. reichenowi, P. vivax-like). Younger animals significantly more infected with higher parasitemia. Infected individuals showed elevated urea, reduced creatinine, and increased pro-inflammatory (TNF, IL-1β, CCL3) and anti-inflammatory (IL-10) cytokines. Ex vivo PBMC stimulation yielded higher IL-10 in infected versus non-infected individuals. Results suggest chronic infections cause systemic immune dysregulation and physiological stress.

articles.headline: P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein polymorphism in Rwanda vaccine relevance
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein in Kigali, Rwanda.
articles.authors: Fankem SN, Mbonimpa JB, Kalimba EM, Diallo MT, Souopgui J
articles.details: Study examined genetic diversity of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (Pfcsp) in 245 clinical isolates from Kigali, Rwanda using Oxford Nanopore sequencing. Identified 48 distinct haplotypes (Hd=0.8899, nucleotide diversity π=0.00834) with high variability in the central repeat region and extensive polymorphism in C-terminal vaccine epitopes. Fewer than 1% of sequences matched the 3D7 vaccine strain, with several vaccine-escape-associated amino acid positions showing high mutation frequencies. Authors conclude genetic divergence could influence vaccine performance.

articles.headline: Research priorities for seasonal malaria chemoprevention: sustainability and drug resistance
articles.title_original: Global medium-term research priority setting for seasonal malaria chemoprevention: results from an eDelphi study.
articles.authors: Richardson S, Steele A, Scott S, Ainsworth J, Eckert E, Tchouatieu AM, Sillah JS, Cuomo-Dannenburg G, Okereke E, Okeh DU, Tonks S, Ndiaye JL, Dentinger C, Rassi C, Baker K
articles.details: Delphi consultation with 67 expert respondents from 20 countries (September 2022–September 2023) identified research priorities for seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine-amodiaquine (SPAQ). Top priorities included evaluating duration of prophylactic protection and effects of integrating SMC with other interventions on antimalarial resistance development. Secondary priorities included sustainability, improved delivery quality, and program adaptation. Results aim to guide research uptake and program implementation.

articles.headline: Malaria prevention knowledge and antenatal care in Ghana
articles.title_original: Assessing the knowledge, attitudes and practices towards malaria prevention and determinants of antenatal care utilization among pregnant women in Sekyere South district, Ghana; a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Zuuri CN, Gyamah DK, Buabeng L, Mensah R
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 422 pregnant women in Ghana's Sekyere South District assessed knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding malaria prevention and antenatal care utilization. Although 78% had adequate knowledge, misconceptions persisted (56% believed person-to-person malaria transmission); only 48% knew recommended ≥3 IPTp doses, 63% used ITNs. Knowledge of IPTp dosage was significantly associated with uptake (p<0.001). Authors conclude gaps stem from misconceptions and incomplete adherence rather than supply shortages, emphasizing need for strengthened counselling and myth correction.

articles.headline: P. ovale GAMA protein genetic diversity and immunogenicity
articles.title_original: Immunogenicity and gene polymorphism analysis of Plasmodium ovale wallikeri glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored micronemal antigen (GAMA).
articles.authors: Ling X, Chen F, Xu S, Tan Y, Sun Y, Han S, Wang Q, Cheng Y
articles.details: Study analyzed powgama gene sequences in 56 Plasmodium ovale isolates (38 P. o. wallikeri, 18 P. o. curtisi) from Africa, identifying 10 SNP sites and 13 haplotypes (Hd=0.812, nucleotide diversity π=0–0.00435). Recombinant P. ovale wallikeri GAMA protein (rPowGAMA, amino acids 276–646) elicited robust Th1-polarized immune response with high IFN-γ production, lymphocyte proliferation, endpoint antibody titer 1:5,120,000, and avidity 78.37%. In serology, rPowGAMA demonstrated 83.3% sensitivity and 70% specificity detecting natural infections. Authors suggest potential as vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Climate change impacts on malaria transmission in Africa
articles.title_original: Climate change and malaria: an old enemy of Africa is back.
articles.authors: Filho WL, Gbaguidi GJ, Zuñiga RAA, Ibrahim U
articles.details: Study analyzed two-decade malaria incidence trends and climate influences across African countries using Pearson correlation, joinpoint regression, and linear regression. Mean temperature emerged as primary climatic factor affecting transmission in Angola, Cameroon, Ivory Coast, Ghana, Mozambique, and Uganda, with generally positive association except Ghana where higher temperatures decreased risk. Increased precipitation significantly reduced transmission in Burkina Faso and Uganda. Authors conclude climate change drives malaria transmission dynamics and necessitates intensified policy engagement and control measures.

articles.headline: Malaria-typhoid co-infection in febrile patients, Northern Ghana
articles.title_original: Malaria and typhoidal co-infection among febrile patients: a health facility-based cross-sectional study in Northern Ghana.
articles.authors: Dassah SD, Anati MI, Kulariba J, Alidu EA, Senoo DKJ, Adda RB, Agongo G, Motey GA, Amenga-Etego L, Abugri J
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 332 febrile patients from health facilities in Ghana's Upper East Region (July–November 2023–2024) investigated malaria and typhoid fever prevalence and risk factors. Malaria seroprevalence 37.7%, typhoid fever 36.7%, co-infection 14.7%. Key associations: Nabdam district increased malaria odds (aOR=3.87, 95%CI 1.71–8.76, p<0.01); tap water source reduced typhoid odds (aOR=0.36, p=0.02) and co-infection odds (aOR=0.07, p=0.02); vomiting predicted malaria (aOR=2.45, p<0.01). Authors emphasize need for integrated diagnostic and treatment strategies for febrile illnesses.

articles.headline: ASAQ vs artemether-lumefantrine for uncomplicated falciparum malaria, Madagascar
articles.title_original: Efficacy and Safety of Artesunate + Amodiaquine and Artemether + Lumefantrine for the Treatment of Uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum Malaria in Madagascar, 2020.
articles.authors: Harimanana A, Randriamiarinjatovo DNAL, Rakotomanga TA, Irinantenaina J, Moriarty LF, Laird V, Patel D, Abdallah RA, Zhiyong Z, Svigel SS, Kapesa L, Razafindrakoto J, Ralinoro F, Rason MA, Andriamananjara MN, Razanatsiorimalala S, Razafinjato C, Randrianarivelojosia M
articles.details: Randomized controlled trial at four Madagascar health centers compared artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) versus artemether-lumefantrine (AL) for treating uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in 765 children (6 months–14 years); 709 (92.7%) completed 28-day follow-up. PCR-corrected ACPR rates: 100% for ASAQ, 97.6–100% for AL. Day 3 slide positivity 0–1%. No pfK13 artemisinin-resistance mutations in 727 samples. Adverse events (gastrointestinal, headache) in 10.2% of patients. Authors conclude both drugs remain safe and efficacious.

articles.headline: Trichuris trichiura prevalence and malaria coinfection in Papua children
articles.title_original: Molecular diagnosis of Trichuris trichiura: Prevalence and associated risk factors in children under five living in a malaria-endemic area in Papua, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Sukmana DJ, Kridaningsih TN, Mufidah H, Kenangalem E, Burdam FH, Susilawati TN, Poespoprodjo JR, Murhandarwati EEH
articles.details: Cross-sectional household survey of 181 children under 5 years old in Papua, Indonesia (April–July 2013) assessed Trichuris trichiura prevalence using Kato-Katz microscopy and quantitative PCR. T. trichiura prevalence 13.8% (95%CI 9.1–19.7) by Kato-Katz and 31.5% (95%CI 24.8–38.8) by qPCR. Risk factors included age >3 years (aOR 3.29, p=0.006) and hemoglobin ≤10 g/dL (aOR 2.46, p=0.020). Malaria prevalence 9.9%, and malaria-trichuriasis coinfection significantly increased anaemia risk (aOR 11.7, p=0.004).

articles.headline: Lupeol and betulin derivatives show antimalarial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: Hemisynthesis of lupeol and betulin derivatives and their in vitro antimalarial activity against Plasmodium falciparum FCR3.
articles.authors: Bafarawa ID, Abd Ghani MS, Ramadani AP, Fuadah S, Othman MBH, Ibrahim MN, Liew SY, Che Omar MT, A Wahab H, Azmi MN
articles.details: Researchers synthesized lupeol and betulin derivatives and evaluated in vitro antimalarial activity against Plasmodium falciparum FCR3 strain. Eight compounds displayed good anti-plasmodial potency with IC50 values 0.03–9.9 µM. Most active: lupenone (IC50 0.3±0.3 µM), derivative 4a (0.03±0.05 µM), derivative 4b (0.7±0.8 µM), comparable to artemisinin (0.7±0.3 µM) and superior to chloroquine (10.3±2.9 µM). Molecular docking showed binding affinity to PfATP6 protein –9.8 to –8.4 kcal/mol. Authors suggest compounds as promising lead structures for antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Hemozoin crystal motion driven by hydrogen peroxide in parasites
articles.title_original: Chemical propulsion of hemozoin crystal motion in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Hastings EM, Skóra T, Carney KR, Fu HC, Bidone TC, Sigala PA
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: Study used quantitative image analysis, single particle tracking, and Brownian dynamics simulations to investigate hemozoin crystal motion in malaria parasites. Found hemozoin exhibits tight confinement but faster-than-thermal motion driven by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) via surface-catalyzed peroxide decomposition. Conditions suppressing peroxide formation slowed crystal motion inside parasites. Authors propose hemozoin motion represents endogenous self-propelled nanoparticle mechanism reducing oxidative stress from hemoglobin digestion during blood-stage infection.

articles.headline: P. falciparum transmission in artisanal mining communities, Ghana
articles.title_original: Factors Associated With Plasmodium falciparum Infection Transmission in Artisanal Mining and Mining-Free Communities: The Case of Fanteakwa South District, Ghana.
articles.authors: Aninagyei E, Adedia D, Larbi G, Abbew GA, Tukwarlba I, Boatey CA, Sarfo-Bempong B, Acheampong DO
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 341 participants (240 from artisanal mining, 101 from mining-free communities) in Ghana examined Plasmodium falciparum transmission dynamics using nested PCR on dried blood spots. Asymptomatic prevalence 53.1% overall, significantly higher in mining (64.6%) versus mining-free (27.7%, p<0.001). Major risk factors in mining areas: no mosquito nets (aOR 2.3), open-market nets (aOR 4.2), net sharing (aOR 3.0–4.9), prolonged outdoor night exposure (aOR 8.4, p<0.001). Authors emphasize need for quality-assured ITN distribution and behavior change communication.

articles.headline: Health-related quality of life in Kenyan children with malaria
articles.title_original: Determining health-related quality of life among children with malaria in Kenya: does the choice of EQ-5D value set matter?
articles.authors: Okech T, Thavorncharoensap M, Chaikledkaew U, Omondi GP, Nyokabi P, Thumbi SM, Thakkinstian A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 95 Kenyan children (45 uncomplicated, 50 severe malaria) assessed health-related quality of life using proxy-reported EQ-5D-5L, with utilities estimated using Ethiopian, Ugandan, and Dutch value sets. For uncomplicated malaria, median utilities ranged 0.59–0.82; for severe malaria, 0.09 to –0.13, with significant differences (p<0.05). Most affected domains: pain/discomfort and usual activities. Vaccination status and malaria severity significantly predicted utility scores. Authors conclude malaria substantially reduces HRQoL and value set choice significantly influences cost-effectiveness analyses.

articles.headline: High biting rates and productive larval habitats near villages in Kebbi, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Utilization of vector bionomics, remote sensing and larval habitat characterization data to inform larval source management decision in the high malaria burden state of Kebbi, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oduola AO, Inyama PU, Samdi LM, Bunza MA, Bagudu A, Okeke I, Yina G, Esema M, Ntadom G, Ambrose K, Mihigo J, Yoshimizu M, Seyoum A
articles.details: This cross-sectional entomological survey of 13,201 An. gambiae in Kebbi State characterized vector behavior and larval habitats. Human biting rates were highest at 42.2 bites/person/night (rainy season, outdoors), with 460 of 635 surveyed larval habitats (97.9%) containing Anopheles larvae, predominantly in rice fields within 100 m of homes (43–61% of temporary habitats). A protection gap of 83% was measured. Authors conclude these data support larval source management as a complementary vector control intervention.

articles.headline: Correction to Plasmodium pitheci mitochondrial genome sequence
articles.title_original: Correction: The mitochondrial genome sequence of the Bornean orang-utan (Pongo pygmaeus) malaria parasite, Plasmodium pitheci.
articles.authors: Dharmayanthi AB, Pratama R, Sánchez KL, Nurillah A, Fadiah F, Komara, Nugraha RTP, Saputra S, Nurkanto A, Fahroni A, Irwanto A, Syafruddin D, Adnan IA, Saragih JR, Coutrier FN, Setiadi W, Lobo NF
articles.details: Correction notice (no details available).

articles.headline: Cerebral herniation and seizures account for majority of pediatric CM deaths
articles.title_original: Cause-specific mortality in a cohort of paediatric cerebral malaria patients.
articles.authors: Wynkoop HJ, Bvalani-Kaunda R, Seydel KB, Moxon CA, Chimalizeni Y, Taylor TE, Birbeck GL, Postels DG, O'Brien NF
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 393 children with cerebral malaria at a Malawian hospital (2018–2025) identified 52 deaths (13%). Cerebral herniation was the most common immediate cause (46%), followed by status epilepticus with apnea (17%); non-neurologic causes (cardiac, respiratory, renal, hepatic failure) accounted for 37%. Authors note that multiple distinct causes of death may dilute impact of brain-focused therapies and suggest modifications to sample size estimates in future clinical trials.

articles.headline: Mining expansion drives 24% increase in malaria cases among Yanomami
articles.title_original: Mining and malaria in the Brazilian Amazon and in the Yanomami indigenous land.
articles.authors: Castro MC, Arisco NJ, Diniz CG, Ponmattam J, Peterka C, Basta PC, Ferreira MU
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of illegal mining and malaria in the Brazilian Amazon found that a 1% increase in annual mining area was associated with a 24% increase (95% CrI 17–32%) in monthly malaria cases among the Yanomami. Accounting for likely underreporting of 83% in 2022, an estimated 102,870 excess cases occurred from 2018–2023, representing approximately $6.9 million in public health costs. Authors emphasize the need for intensified malaria control as a matter of health, environmental, and indigenous justice.

articles.headline: P. vivax RDT sensitivity 83%; 15% of febrile Indians malaria-positive
articles.title_original: A cross-sectional survey of Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax in India using rapid diagnostic test and microscopy across 12 sites of varying transmission, 2023-2024.
articles.authors: Dahal P, Singh-Phulgenda S, Nema S, Tripathi PK, Malla WA, Sinha DP, Mohanty A, Chalageri VH, Shrinivasa BM, Singh SP, Singh PK, Singh K, Ranjha R, Baharia RK, Chhajed R, Nain M, Das A, Kumar RS, Gupta A, Rahi M, Flegg JA, Nitika N, Dhorda M, Anvikar AR, Guérin PJ, Bharti PK
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 10,290 febrile participants across 12 Indian sites (September 2023–April 2024) compared rapid diagnostic tests (RDT) with microscopy. Overall malaria positivity was 14.7% by RDT and 14.0% by microscopy, with P. falciparum mono-infections predominating (72.9%), followed by P. vivax (19.1%) and mixed infections (8.0%). RDT sensitivity was 95.0% for P. falciparum, 83% for P. vivax, and 88% for mixed infections. Authors highlight the low sensitivity of RDTs for P. vivax, emphasizing the need for improved diagnostics.

articles.headline: Raman spectroscopy detects heme changes correlated with parasitemia levels
articles.title_original: Two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy (2D-COS) in the analysis of parasitemia in Raman spectra of red blood cells of patients diagnosed with malaria.
articles.authors: Migdalski M, Czepiel J, Moskal P, Birczyńska-Zych M, Kucharska M, Biesiada G, Stokłosa J, Bociąga-Jasik M, Wesełucha-Birczyńska A
articles.details: Researchers analyzed Raman spectra of red blood cells from P. falciparum-infected patients to identify correlations between spectral bands and parasitemia levels using two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy. Laser excitation at 442 nm (resonance Raman) revealed specific heme intermediates, while 785 nm excitation provided information on protein and lipid changes. Authors discuss the potential of using the relative Raman intensity ratio I1370/I1354 (oxy- and deoxy-heme) as a marker for parasitemia levels.

articles.headline: Integrated vector control approaches more effective than single interventions
articles.title_original: Integrated vector management for malaria control: a review of approaches and effectiveness.
articles.authors: Otolorin GR, Castellanos ME, Adegboye OA, McBryde ES
articles.journal: Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg
articles.details: A systematic review of 14 studies (2009–2024) compared integrated vector management (IVM)—combining multiple tailored control methods—with single-intervention approaches across diverse regions. IVM demonstrated greater effectiveness, with six studies reporting statistically significant transmission reductions (p<0.05) and several documenting declines in morbidity, mortality, and entomological indicators. Longitudinal studies from Uganda, Ethiopia, and Nigeria showed sustained reductions in malaria cases and vectors; large-scale programs in China and India demonstrated long-term success. Authors conclude that combining traditional and innovative methods offers a sustainable global malaria control strategy.

articles.headline: Emerging threat of zoonotic malaria in India
articles.title_original: Tackling the Emerging Threat of Zoonotic Malaria in India.
articles.authors: Divyaparvathy J, Ramesh H
articles.details: No abstract available.

articles.headline: Anti-CelTOS antibodies inhibit liver stage and vector transmission
articles.title_original: [Cross-specific anti-CelTOS antibodies for different Plasmodium species inhibiting the liver stage and transmission to the vector].
articles.authors: Varloteau A, Fouquoire L, Ravier L
articles.details: No abstract available.

articles.headline: P. vivax cysteine protease inhibitor shows broad falcipain activity
articles.title_original: Partial characterization of a cysteine protease inhibitor of Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Võ TC, Kang JM, Lê HG, Na BK
articles.details: Researchers identified and characterized an endogenous inhibitor of P. vivax cysteine protease (PvICP) with structural similarity to orthologous proteins in other Plasmodium species. Recombinant PvICP demonstrated broad inhibitory activity against falcipain family cysteine proteases (vivapain-3, vivapain-4, falcipain-3, malapain-2, malapain-4), with strongest activity against vivapain-3 and vivapain-4. Inhibitory activity was pH-independent. Authors suggest these findings provide groundwork for exploring PvICP's biological significance and potential as a therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Climate change expands habitat for three malaria vector species
articles.title_original: Climate Change Favors African Malaria Vector Mosquitoes.
articles.authors: van der Deure T, Nogués-Bravo D, Njotto LL, Stensgaard AS
articles.details: Species distribution modeling using extensive mosquito observation data, climate projections, and land-use changes assessed the future geographic range of six dominant African malaria vector species. Three species—Anopheles gambiae, Anopheles coluzzii, and Anopheles nili s.l.—are projected to experience substantial habitat expansion, with approximately 200 million additional people potentially living in highly suitable areas by century's end. New exposure hotspots emerge in Central and East Africa. Authors emphasize that climate change impacts are highly species-specific, underscoring the need for adapted malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Vector diversity impact on malaria prevalence varies by geography
articles.title_original: Vector diversity and malaria prevalence: global trends and local determinants.
articles.authors: Hoi AG, Gilbert B, Mideo N
articles.details: A global analysis of open-access data examined the relationship between vector species richness, malaria prevalence, and environmental factors. The association between vector diversity and malaria prevalence varied significantly by latitude, indicating strong environmental dependence. In Africa, environmental factors exerted top-down influence by shaping vector community composition, whereas in Southeast Asia, disease prevalence reflected complex interactions between physical environment (rainfall), socioeconomic factors (GDP), and vector diversity. Authors highlight the importance of vector diversity in structuring disease distribution patterns.

articles.headline: HSP90 inhibitor resistance mutations differ by drug despite shared binding site
articles.title_original: Shared Binding Site but Divergent Resistance Profiles Uncover Novel Resistance Mechanisms in Plasmodium HSP90 Inhibitors.
articles.authors: Ko FH, Lukens AK, de Souza ML, Foy A, Hsiao J, Ndiaye T, Okombo J, Yeo T, Park H, Uhlemann AC, Boonyalai N, Kumpornsin K, Girling G, Pasaje C, Godoy L, Ottilie S, Durst GL, Chatterjee A, Niles J, Lee M, Fidock DA, Wirth DF, Winzeler EA
articles.details: Study compared two HSP90 inhibitors targeting identical ATP-binding site in P. falciparum. Geldanamycin selected 10 resistance mutations conferring up to 22-fold resistance; AUY-922 required 44 weeks for single A41S mutation with 2-fold resistance. Resistance mutations localized to different domains by drug. A41S paradoxically enhanced AUY-922 binding despite conferring resistance, suggesting AUY-922 engages multiple HSP90 family members, lowering resistance risk compared to geldanamycin.

articles.headline: Optimized protocols enable low-volume Plasmodium transcriptomics
articles.title_original: Low-volume Plasmodium blood sample processing protocols for untargeted transcriptomics optimized using Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Sauve E, Kattenberg JH, Moris P, Guetens P, Monsieurs P, Rosanas-Urgell A
articles.details: Study optimized RNA-seq for low-parasite density samples using P. knowlesi as P. vivax model. White blood cell depletion increased mapped Plasmodium reads from 5.4% to 37.2–54.5%. Combined globin and rRNA depletion decreased human sequences up to 90%. mRNA library prep kits outperformed total RNA kits for Plasmodium transcript enrichment. Optimized workflow (WBC + globin depletion + mRNA prep) enables untargeted transcriptomics in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Historical analysis of 1922 Palestine malaria control efforts
articles.title_original: Why and when successful rural malaria control became a local problem - Palestine 1922.
articles.authors: Alexander A
articles.details: Historical perspective examining how Zionist malaria control programs overcame obstacles in 1920s Palestine, drawing parallels to recent geopolitical events and contemporary control challenges.

articles.headline: First new malaria treatment in decades shows promise
articles.title_original: First new type of malaria treatment in decades shows promise against drug resistance.
articles.authors: Lenharo M
articles.details: No abstract provided. Title indicates novel antimalarial treatment with activity against drug-resistant parasites.

articles.headline: DeepSeek AI identifies Plasmodium parasites in blood smears
articles.title_original: A Case of Plasmodium Identification in Peripheral Blood Smears based on the DeepSeek Intelligent Algorithm.
articles.authors: Zheng Z, Lu M
articles.details: Case study of imported malaria using DeepSeek deep-learning model to identify Plasmodium in peripheral blood smears, including developmental stage morphology. AI achieved high diagnostic accuracy within minutes compared to hours/days for manual microscopy, reducing misdiagnosis and detection failures. Authors propose 'AI primary screening + manual review' workflow for grassroots malaria control and prevention.

articles.headline: Weaker selection in sporozoite genes drives faster resistance evolution
articles.title_original: Heterogeneous constraint and adaptation across the malaria parasite life cycle.
articles.authors: Perkins SA, Neafsey DE, Early AM
articles.details: Population genomic analysis of P. falciparum comparing between- and within-species diversity across life stages. Sporozoite-specific genes showed weaker negative selection consistent with theory (sporozoites non-proliferative, limited clonal competition, strong transmission bottleneck). Results suggest timing of interventions targeting specific life stages may alter rate of parasite resistance evolution.

articles.headline: P. relictum uses generalist strategy responding to all mosquito bites
articles.title_original: Plastic responses of Plasmodium relictum to competent and non-competent mosquito bites.
articles.authors: Chauvin V, Berthomieu A, Loiseau C, N Tchana C, Angelier F, Rivero A
articles.details: Experimental study of avian malaria P. relictum exposed to competent (Culex quinquefasciatus) and non-competent (Anopheles gambiae) vector bites. Both induced parasitemia rise, with significantly stronger response to non-competent species. Host inflammatory/humoral/stress markers similar across treatments; no species-specific modulation of gametocyte conversion or sex ratio. Findings suggest bet-hedging transmission strategy independent of vector species, with implications for disease ecology under shifting vector composition.

articles.headline: Fitsum Tadesse shaped malaria research in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Fitsum Tadesse: shaping malaria research in sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Prasad A
articles.details: Biographical profile of malaria researcher Fitsum Tadesse and contributions to research in sub-Saharan Africa.

articles.headline: An. aquasalis phenotypic variants differ in P. vivax infection intensity
articles.title_original: Phenotypic variation and vector competence in Anopheles aquasalis: A comparative analysis of susceptibility to Plasmodium vivax.
articles.authors: Rodrigues NB, Santana RA, Carvalho BFDS, Martinez EG, Bruce AD, Justiniano SCB, da Silva ÁC, Nacif-Pimenta R, Monteiro WM, de Melo GC, Secundino NFC, Pimenta PFP
articles.details: Experimental vector-competence study comparing two An. aquasalis phenotypes (30BS, 50BS) for P. vivax using membrane feeding assays with patient blood from Manaus, Brazil. Five independent infections; midguts dissected 7–10 days post-infection. Overall prevalences 67.63% (50BS) vs 55.10% (30BS), no significant difference (IRR=1.11; 95% CI 0.67–1.84; p=0.68). However, 30BS showed significantly higher oocyst intensity (mean 72.91 vs 56.94; p=0.042); 3 of 5 experiments confirmed this trend. Phenotypic variation influences P. vivax transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Italian severe malaria network documents outcomes in non-endemic setting
articles.title_original: The Italian network for severe malaria treatment (NOMAL): epidemiology, clinical presentation, management and outcomes in a non-endemic setting.
articles.authors: Bartoli TA, Corpolongo A, D'Abramo A, Angheben A, Bertoli G, de Gaetano Donati K, Cauda R, Antinori S, Attard L, Madeddu G, Tomasoni LR, Pallotto C, Vulcano A, Maffongelli G, Zammarchi L, d'Ettorre G, Navarra A, Girardi E, D'Alessandro U, Nicastri E
articles.details: No abstract provided. Title indicates epidemiological and clinical outcomes data from Italian malaria treatment network (NOMAL) in non-endemic setting.

articles.headline: Chemical adjuvants boost whole sporozoite vaccine CD8+ T-cell responses
articles.title_original: A Trojan horse: Chemical boosting of Plasmodium falciparum whole sporozoite vaccine immunogenicity.
articles.authors: Naar CM, van Schuijlenburg R, van Meerbeek M, Almeida L, van Leeuwen FWB, Roestenberg M
articles.details: Study enhanced whole sporozoite vaccine (SPZ) immunogenicity with positively charged dye-adjuvant complexes (Cy5-CL307, Cy5-MDP) targeting mitochondrial potential. Successfully adjuvanted 90% of SPZ; both types maintained viability (84% vs 71%) and infectivity. SPZCL307 increased CD80, CD25, IL-6, IL-8, TNF, IL-1β, IL-10 expression in macrophages (all p≤0.043); increased CD137 18% in circumsporozoite-specific CD8+ memory T cells (p=0.01); trended toward 1.6-fold perforin increase (p=0.064). Optimized adjuvanting may improve vaccine dosing.

articles.headline: CDC achieved 83% on-time emergency distribution of severe malaria drugs
articles.title_original: A Review of Malaria, Diphtheria, and Botulism Medication Distributions by CDC Port Health Stations From May 2006 to February 2024.
articles.authors: Rubin L, Fowler J, Ranga S, Kirley M, Tchan J, Pacheco M, Preston LE, Waltenburg MA, Akinkugbe O, Kinzer MH, Alvarado-Ramy F, Brown C, Gertz AM
articles.details: Analysis of CDC port health station drug distributions (May 2006–February 2024). Of 850 shipments with complete data (December 2019+), 83% (n=702/850) delivered within 8-hour authorization target to 54 US jurisdictions and 3 countries via 17 sites. Commercial air transport (aOR 11.43; 95% CI 6.94–18.81; p<0.001) and off-hours authorization (aOR 5.10; 95% CI 3.31–7.86; p<0.001) significantly delayed delivery. Staffing, location, and ground transportation options identified as key efficiency factors.

articles.headline: Intensive control reduces malaria in young children but not adolescents
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum carriage in a population under long-term, intensive malaria control in Kedougou region, Senegal: a 1-year cohort study.
articles.authors: Legendre E, Ciré Ba EK, L'Ollivier C, Cissoko M, Katile A, Mehadji M, Serre P, Sokhna C, Ranque S, Danfakha F, Bendiane MK, Sagara I, Gaudart J, Landier J
articles.details: A 1-year open cohort of 763 participants from 69 households in Kedougou, Senegal tracked P. falciparum prevalence through four surveys during dry and wet seasons using qPCR on dried blood samples. P. falciparum prevalence remained below 10% in SMC-eligible children (<10 years), but during wet season reached 32.1% in ages 15-24 and 24.7% in ages 35-49. Clinical incidence peaked in ages 10-14 years (527 cases/1000 person-years) and 15-19 years (631 cases/1000 person-years), over five-fold higher than ages 6 months to 4 years (93 cases/1000 person-years). Outdoor night-time activity was associated with infection.

articles.headline: Malaria burden assessment in long-term chemoprevention region
articles.title_original: Malaria burden in a region under long-term chemoprevention.
articles.authors: Sow D, Wotodjo AN
articles.details: This study examines malaria burden in a region implementing long-term chemoprevention strategies. Additional details regarding study design, population, and specific results are not available from the title alone.

articles.headline: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine efficacy unchanged over decade of use
articles.title_original: Effectiveness of dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine for treating Plasmodium falciparum malaria from sub-Saharan Africa: a retrospective study.
articles.authors: Ciminelli F, Buonfrate D, Mazzi C, Neumayr A, Camprubí-Ferrer D, Gobbi F
articles.details: A retrospective observational study reviewed records of 90 patients (89 with sub-Saharan Africa infections) treated for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in Italy from 2013-2024 with a three-day DHA-PPQ regimen. Baseline median parasitaemia was 0.136% (IQR 0.029-0.750%); on day 3, only 6 patients (6.7%) had circulating parasites with no early treatment failure. Follow-up data (available for 63 patients) showed no late parasitological failures. No significant trend in day-3 positivity was observed across three study periods (2013-2015, 2016-2019, 2020-2023; p = 0.339).

articles.headline: Pericyte dysfunction in cerebral malaria through angiopoietin disruption
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum impairs Ang-1 secretion by pericytes in a 3D brain microvessel model.
articles.authors: Long RKM, Korbmacher F, Ronchi P, Fleckenstein H, Schorb M, Mirza W, Mallorquí M, Aguilar R, Moncunill G, Schwab Y, Bernabeu M
articles.details: A 3D microfluidics-based brain microvessel model containing human primary brain microvascular endothelial cells and pericytes was exposed to P. falciparum-infected RBC egress products. This resulted in decreased angiopoietin-1 (Ang-1) secretion, increased vascular permeability, and minor ultrastructural changes in pericyte morphology. P. falciparum-mediated barrier disruption was partially reversed by pre-treatment with recombinant Ang-1 and the Tie-2 activator AKB-9778, suggesting a novel mechanistic role for pericytes in cerebral malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Malaria and microgravity as models for age-related erythropoiesis
articles.title_original: Exploring erythropoiesis: aging through the lens of malaria and microgravity.
articles.authors: Foucambert P, Hang JW, Malleret B
articles.details: This review examines erythropoiesis (red blood cell production) in aging, highlighting age-related changes in stem cells, transcription factors, erythropoietin, and iron regulation. The authors discuss mechanisms driving high anemia prevalence in elderly populations due to chronic disease, sepsis, and unexplained anemia. Plasmodium infection and spaceflight are introduced as underexplored experimental models that mirror key features of age-related erythropoietic dysfunction and RBC morphological alterations, offering potential insights into mechanisms of anemia in elderly populations.

articles.headline: Nanoparticles enhance malaria drug delivery and vaccine efficacy
articles.title_original: Nanotechnology-based delivery strategies for drugs and vaccines targeting blood stage malaria: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Amos Y, Rubaka C, Sauli E, Swai H, Tumbo A
articles.details: A systematic review of 40 studies (2005-2025) examined nanotechnology-based delivery systems for blood-stage malaria, including lipid, polymeric, inorganic, and protein-based nanoparticles. Functionalized nanocarriers with ligands targeting infected red blood cells significantly enhanced drug efficacy and reduced systemic toxicity. In vaccine development, nanoparticles used as antigen carriers elicited strong immune responses, achieving up to 83.3% survival in in vivo preclinical models. Challenges including scalable production, clinical translation, and regulatory approval remain.

articles.headline: Seasonal vector ecology sustains urban malaria transmission in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Influence of the dry and humid seasons on the assessment of human exposure to malaria vector bites in Bobo-Dioulasso, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Amara MF, Kouadio KWU, Konaté H, Yao RK, Kouamé BNA, Karabinta A, Diabaté A, Gnankiné O, Namountougou M
articles.details: An entomological survey of Bobo-Dioulasso from 2023-2024 using Human Landing Catches and Pyrethrum Spray Catches collected 1,455 mosquitoes (922 HLC, 533 PSC), of which 15.6% were Anopheles species. Three An. gambiae complex species were identified: An. arabiensis (91.20%), An. coluzzii (5.09%), and An. gambiae (3.71%, absent in dry season). Plasmodium falciparum infections were detected year-round with 0.16% of mosquitoes carrying dual P. falciparum and P. malariae infections in July. Blood meal analysis revealed predominantly mixed feeding (59.63%), with high trophic plasticity sustaining persistent transmission despite urban setting.

articles.headline: New avian malaria species identified in Brazilian Wood Rails
articles.title_original: Hidden in plain sight: Morphological and molecular description of Plasmodium aramidis n. sp. in blood and tissues from Brazilian Wood Rails.
articles.authors: Vieira LMC, Santos DOD, Pacheco MA, Escalante AA, Oliveira AR, Braga ÉM
articles.details: A morphological and molecular study identified Plasmodium aramidis n. sp. in five Grey-necked Wood Rails (Aramides cajaneus) from Southeastern Brazil, characterized by ARACAJ01 cytb gene lineage and near-complete mitochondrial genome sequencing. Histopathology revealed pathogenicity with pulmonary lesions (edema and hemorrhage) and hepatic hemosiderosis in all individuals examined. Exoerythrocytic meronts were found within putative histiocytes and endothelial cells of lungs, heart, and skeletal muscle in 2 of 3 individuals examined. The species appears common in A. cajaneus but requires further host identification and pathogenicity studies.

articles.headline: Highland rice-farming ecology drives Anopheles density and transmission
articles.title_original: Ecological diversity of Anopheline mosquitoes in highland agroecosystems in relation to microclimate dynamics: implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Arisanti M, Yahya Y, Suryaningtyas NH, Sukendra DM, Maha MS, Gunawan G, Isnawati R, Ernawan B, Nurcahyo W, Kaliannagounder K, Pangaribuan HU
articles.details: An ecological survey in a South Sumatra rice-growing landscape (April-September) collected 6,899 mosquitoes via human landing catch, animal-baited traps, and resting collections; Culex accounted for 56.6% and Anopheles 15.6%, with An. vagus most abundant. Response surface models predicted human biting rate (HBR) peaked at approximately 23.5°C and >88% relative humidity (R² = 0.656; RMSE = 0.212), while animal biting rate peaked at approximately 22°C and >97% RH (R² = 0.081; RMSE = 5.915). Adult catches revealed strong exophagic and exophilic behaviors, and principal component analysis showed pH and illumination positively influenced several Anopheles species.

articles.headline: Chemo-attenuated whole-parasite vaccine achieves 20-25% protection
articles.title_original: Complete attenuation of Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites by atovaquone-proguanil.
articles.authors: Borrmann S, Sulyok Z, Müller K, Fendel R, Sulyok M, Friesen J, Lalremruata A, Sandri TL, Nguyen TT, Calle CL, Knoblich A, Sefried S, Ibáñez J, Lorenz FR, Heimann HL, Weller DM, Steuder R, Adukpo S, Granados Bayon P, Molnár Z, Esen M, Metzger W, James ER, Ruben A, Abebe Y, Chakravarty S, Manoj A, Kc N, Murshedkar T, Hafalla JCR, Woldearegai TG, O'Rourke F, Held J, Billingsley P, Sim BKL, Richie TL, Hoffman SL, Kremsner PG, Matuschewski K, Mordmüller B
articles.details: A clinical trial tested PfSPZ-CVac (chemo-attenuated whole-sporozoite vaccine with atovaquone-proguanil) in three doses of 5.12×10⁴ or 1.5×10⁵ PfSPZ via direct venous inoculation combined with oral AP (1000/400 mg). Blood-stage infections were fully prevented during immunization; upon challenge with 3.2×10³ PfSPZ at 10 weeks, 2/8 and 2/10 vaccinees were protected, respectively (25% and 20%). Protection correlated with parasite-specific effector-memory CD8⁺ T cells and antibodies to liver-stage antigens (LISP2, LSA1) and multi-stage antigen PfMSP5, but not to major surface protein PfCSP.

articles.headline: Pre-erythrocytic vaccines RTS,S and R21 established; alternatives needed
articles.title_original: Better late than never: defining the ideal vaccine that targets pre-erythrocytic malaria infection.
articles.authors: Rochfort Peters GM, Baum J
articles.details: This review describes the recent licensure of two subunit pre-erythrocytic malaria vaccines: RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix, GSK) and R21/Matrix-M (Serum Institute of India), which received WHO prequalification in 2022 and 2023, respectively. Both vaccines require a primary series of four doses over 12-18 months, eliciting a predominantly B-cell response targeting the circumsporozoite protein (CSP) on sporozoites. While licensure represents a landmark achievement, protection levels and durability remain uncertain, particularly in infants and areas of high-intensity, perennial transmission. Over 500,000 children under 5 still die annually from malaria.

articles.headline: Malaria mortality driven by age, delayed treatment, indigenous status
articles.title_original: Towards Malaria Elimination: A Nationwide Case-Control Study to Assess Risk Factors for Severe Malaria-Related Deaths in Brazil.
articles.authors: Garcia KKS, Laporta GZ, Duarte EC, Soremekun S, Abrahão AA, da Silva AC, de Pina Costa A, Guimarães Lacerda MV, Drakeley C, Ramalho WM, Siqueira AM
articles.details: A case-control study of 632 malaria-related deaths (71.8% in Amazon, 28.1% non-endemic) from Brazil's Ministry of Health (2011-2020) using probabilistic record linkage identified region-specific risk factors. In the Amazon: age <6 months or >60 years (p≤0.01), delayed treatment >48 hours (p<0.001), illiteracy (p=0.01), and living in indigenous villages (p<0.001); active case detection was protective (p=0.01). Outside the Amazon: delayed treatment (p=0.049), P. falciparum or mixed infections (p<0.049), foreign-acquired infections (p=0.01), and higher education level (p=0.03, proxy for income/travel). Delayed treatment emerged as a key determinant across both regions.

articles.headline: CSP repeats provide elastic properties critical for sporozoite motility
articles.title_original: The Plasmodium CSP repeats have elastic properties with a critical role in sporozoite motility.
articles.authors: Balaban AE, Kanatani S, Mitra J, Gregory J, Vartak N, Sinnis-Bourozikas A, Frischknecht F, Ha T, Sinnis P
articles.details: This study used single-molecule fluorescence-force spectroscopy and mutational analysis to characterize the central repeat region of Plasmodium circumsporozoite protein (CSP). Sporozoite mutants with truncated or scrambled CSP repeats exhibited impaired motility and altered adhesion site formation. The findings demonstrate that CSP repeats function as a stiff, linear spring with elastic properties dependent on length, and these properties are essential for proper infection dynamics.

articles.headline: IPT reduces malaria in HIV-positive pregnant women; DHAp most effective
articles.title_original: Intermittent preventive treatment regimens for malaria in HIV-positive pregnant women: Systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical trials.
articles.authors: Elgadi A, Noorallah T, Abdelazim E, Altraifi S, Altraifi H, Elhaj A, Elnaiem W, Hemmeda L
articles.details: This meta-analysis of 13 randomized trials (5,226 HIV-positive pregnant women) evaluated intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) regimens for malaria. IPT significantly reduced odds of malaria during pregnancy or delivery (OR = 0.45, 95% CI: 0.36–0.56, P ≤ 0.001), with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (IPTp-DP) being most effective. IPT also reduced placental malaria (OR = 0.52, 95% CI: 0.39–0.70) and maternal anemia (OR = 0.82, 95% CI: 0.69–0.97), but showed no significant effect on adverse birth outcomes.

articles.headline: Imaging flow cytometry detects parasitized erythroblasts in spleen and bone marrow
articles.title_original: Quantification and visualization of malaria-infected erythroblasts by imaging flow cytometry.
articles.authors: Imai T, Miyazaki S, Miyazaki Y, Kagaya W, Nakashima M, Ito K, Sase M, Yoshioka-Takeda M, Shimokawa C, Hayashi K, Araki T, Annoura T, Itokawa K, Komagata O, Ha NT, Shimo A, Murakami T, Hisaeda H
articles.details: This study used imaging flow cytometry (ImageStream) to detect and characterize parasitized erythroblasts (pEBs) in P. yoelii-infected mice at day 14 post-infection. While pEBs were undetectable in peripheral blood, significant populations were identified in spleen and bone marrow. The approach validated quantification of pEB frequencies and revealed potential erythroblastic islands and morphological alterations, providing a high-throughput platform for studying host-parasite interactions in hematopoietic tissues.

articles.headline: AI-driven blood-stage identification for malaria diagnosis, especially P. knowlesi
articles.title_original: Insights into AI-Driven malaria diagnosis: A systematic review with implications for Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.authors: Tiong JY, Hasikin K, Ngui R, Divis PCS, Loo CK, Lai KW, Cheong FW, Wan Sulaiman WY
articles.details: This systematic review examined deep learning approaches for automated Plasmodium blood-stage classification with emphasis on P. knowlesi detection. The review compared convolutional neural networks (CNNs), transfer learning, ensemble learning, and object detection models (YOLO, Faster R-CNN) for microscopy image analysis. Key challenges identified included limited annotated datasets, class imbalance, and lack of interpretability, with recommendations for enhanced dataset curation and explainable AI approaches.

articles.headline: Space-time analysis reveals changing malaria distribution in Kenya over 20 years
articles.title_original: A multidimensional space-time geospatial analysis for examining the spatial trends of vector-borne diseases: 20 years of malaria in Kenya.
articles.authors: Blanford JI, Kioko C
articles.details: This study analyzed 20 years (2000–2020) of Plasmodium falciparum incidence, mortality, and prevalence data from Kenya using spatial-temporal pattern mining. Local and emerging hotspot analyses identified four key malaria zones and two areas of increasing transmission despite overall prevalence decline. The study found a general decline with a transient increase in 2016–2018 and demonstrated that space-time geospatial methods can inform targeted elimination strategies.

articles.headline: P. vivax hemoglobin-degradation enzymes are highly conserved, potential drug targets
articles.title_original: Characterization of genes involved in hemoglobin degradation in Plasmodium vivax isolates from Chennai, India, and species of non-human primate malaria.
articles.authors: Shalini S, Mishra N, Verma S, Kale S, Mallick PK, Prajapati SK, Kumar A, Srivastava N, Joshi H, Eapen A, Singh OP
articles.details: This study analyzed genes encoding three food-vacuole enzymes (plasmepsin IV, heme detoxification protein, and falcilysin) in 30 P. vivax isolates from India and three chloroquine-resistant reference strains. Several nonsynonymous mutations were detected, including novel variants, but structural modeling indicated these did not compromise enzyme active sites. The high conservation of these genes across isolates highlights their essential roles and potential as drug targets.

articles.headline: Malaria eradication history in Europe and climate-driven re-emergence risk
articles.title_original: Historical evolution of the epidemiology of Malaria in Spain and Europe. Future perspectives.
articles.authors: Marañés Antoñanzas I, López Gallardo Y, García de Oro Á, Gutiérrez González C, Peyró Sánchez R, Sánchez Martínez S
articles.journal: Eur J Clin Microbiol Infect Dis
articles.details: This review examines the historical eradication of malaria in Spain (1964) and Europe (1970s) and describes the current epidemiology characterized by imported cases with minimal autochthonous transmission. The article discusses how climate change and migration patterns could potentially favor anopheline mosquito proliferation and malaria transmission in southern Europe, emphasizing the need for maintained surveillance and vector-control infrastructure.

articles.headline: NLRP1 inflammasome variants protect against gestational malaria in Brazil
articles.title_original: Impact of NLRP1 Met1154Val and IL1B variants on gestational malaria: an unexplored role of NLRP1 in inflammasome activation by Plasmodium spp.
articles.authors: Leal VN, Ribeiro JA, Marra LG, Rio AT, Reis EC, Wunderlich G, Dombrowski JG, Marinho CR, Pontillo A
articles.details: This association study examined inflammasome-related genetic variants in pregnant women from a malaria-endemic region in northern Brazil. The NLRP1 p.Met1154Val variant was associated with protection against Plasmodium infection and healthier placental state. The study also identified that infected red blood cells trigger NLRP1- and caspase-1-dependent pyroptosis in trophoblast cells, suggesting a novel inflammasome pathway in gestational malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: RDT sensitivity for simian malaria varies by species and parasite density
articles.title_original: Can malaria rapid diagnostic tests be used to detect simian malaria?
articles.authors: Madmanee W, Day NPJ, White NJ, Dondorp AM, Malaivijitnond S, Imwong M
articles.details: This study evaluated 14 rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) detecting Plasmodium antigens (PfHRP2, pLDH, aldolase) in 131 blood samples from non-human primates with malaria (P. cynomolgi, P. inui, P. coatneyi). RDT sensitivity ranged from 34.2–94.3% across brands and increased substantially in samples exceeding 200 parasites/μL (>90% for best-performing RDTs). False positives occurred in 1–17% of simian malaria-positive samples, indicating species and density-dependent RDT performance.

articles.headline: High Wolbachia prevalence in An. dthali suggests biocontrol potential
articles.title_original: Wolbachia Infection in Iranian Malaria Vectors: Prevalence and Biocontrol Implications.
articles.authors: Saeedi S, Karimian F, Moosa-Kazemi SH, Nejati J, Bavani MM, Koosha M, Choubdar N, Khosravi G, Oshaghi MA
articles.details: This study examined Wolbachia infection in 1,094 mosquito specimens from seven malaria vectors collected across nine Iranian provinces (2016–2019). Wolbachia DNA was detected exclusively in An. dthali (73.4% prevalence) and Culex species (77.78–96.77% prevalence), with phylogenetic analysis identifying supergroup B strains. The high Wolbachia prevalence in An. dthali and related strains from other Anopheles species suggests potential for vector biocontrol strategies targeting malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Sialyl-T antigen on RBCs is critical for P. falciparum invasion
articles.title_original: Sialyl-T Antigen: A Novel Red Blood Cell Determinant for Plasmodium falciparum Invasion.
articles.authors: Mikdar M, Shabani E, Grüring C, Chaand M, Kanjee U, Goldberg JM, Azouzi S, Tennessen JA, Elsworth B, Keutcha C, Barteneva NS, Doench JG, Duraisingh MT
articles.details: This study used RNA interference and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout to identify the glycosyltransferase C1GALT1 as a host factor required for P. falciparum invasion. The enzyme generates α(2-3) sialic acid structures on red blood cells, which are essential for efficient invasion by both sialic acid-dependent and sialic acid-independent P. falciparum strains, though different strains showed variable dependencies on specific sialic acid configurations.

articles.headline: NOX2-driven oxidative stress causes cardiac hypercontractility in malaria
articles.title_original: NOX2-induced oxidative stress triggers cardiac hypercontractility during severe malaria.
articles.authors: de Oliveira KCM, Silva AO, Bruno AS, Ito HN, Queiroz-Junior CM, Berg BB, Castor MGME, Machado FS, Ceron CS, Teixeira MM, Cau S
articles.details: This study investigated cardiac dysfunction in P. chabaudi-infected male Balb/c mice using ex vivo heart preparations and redox analysis. Infected mice developed increased cardiac contractility with elevated lipid peroxidation (TBARS) and reduced superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity. Apocynin treatment (30 mg/kg/day) and NOX2 knockout reversed cardiac hypercontractility while reducing cardiac oxidative stress and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6), demonstrating NOX2's role in malaria-associated cardiac complications.

articles.headline: Monocyte dysregulation in symptomatic and asymptomatic P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Systems approach identifies monocyte imbalance in symptomatic and asymptomatic P. vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Studniberg SI, Bafit M, Ioannidis LJ, Worley MJ, Trianty L, Utami RAS, Puspitasari AM, Apriyanti D, Coutrier FN, Poespoprodjo JR, Kenangalem E, Andries B, Prayoga P, Price RN, Noviyanti R, Garnham AL, Hansen DS
articles.details: Systems approach integrating transcriptional profiling and mass cytometry in individuals with symptomatic and asymptomatic P. vivax malaria. Symptomatic infection featured profound downregulation of monocyte function transcripts and significant depletion of CCR2+CXCR4+ classical monocytes. Dysregulation of genes associated with monocyte activation and inflammasome was also evident in asymptomatic infections. Findings identify monocyte dysregulation as a key feature of P. vivax immune response.

articles.headline: WHO reference reagent standardizes AMA1 antibody response assays
articles.title_original: Evaluation of 1(st) WHO anti-malaria reference reagent for competition ELISA harmonisation and development of ADAMSEL analytical platform.
articles.authors: Khatri B, Riese P, Shkarlet H, Mortier D, McShane H, Bowyer PW, Remarque E
articles.details: Study harmonizing competition ELISA for P. falciparum using the 1st WHO reference serum (10/198) across three laboratories to assess cross-reactive antibody responses to AMA1 variants. Development of ADAMSEL software and R-statistics analytical tool for cELISA data analysis. The WHO reference reagent proved effective as internal standard for IgG concentration calculation, and automated tools improve efficiency and global accessibility.

articles.headline: Phenolic compounds inhibit PfATP6 calcium ATPase in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Small phenolic inhibitors of PfATP6, a Plasmodium falciparum calcium ATPase, as prototype antimalarials.
articles.authors: Sobota S, Devaraja S, Chung M, Arevalo J, Martinez-Aguirre J, Makeyenko J, Kellen-Yuen C, Eastman S, Derbyshire E, Saade C, Dieudonné T, Paula S
articles.details: Study characterizing small library of phenolic compounds with hydroquinone and naphthoquinone scaffolds as inhibitors of PfATP6, a calcium-transporting enzyme in P. falciparum. Several compounds inhibited parasite blood stage growth and showed varying correlations with PfATP6 enzyme inhibition. Molecular docking and dynamics simulations revealed hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions as main binding mechanisms, representing a first step toward improved small-molecule PfATP6 inhibitors.

articles.headline: Parasite genetic factors in HbAS red blood cell infections
articles.title_original: Phenotypic screens reveal Plasmodium falciparum genetic factors associated with infection of sickle-trait cells.
articles.authors: Pires CV, Oberstaller J, Wang C, Gibbons J, Micchelli C, Zhang M, Otto TD, Rayner JC, Taylor S, Adams JH
articles.details: Phenotypic screen using piggyBac transposon mutagenesis in P. falciparum cultured in HbAS versus HbAA red blood cells, with parasite growth assessed by QIseq to identify mutants sensitive or tolerant to sickle-trait conditions. P. falciparum mutants sensitive to HbAS growth associated with genes involved in signaling, exported proteins, and host-interaction, with genetic overlap to oxidative stress and artemisinin sensitivity responses. Findings reveal genetic factors influencing parasite survival in HbAS cells.

articles.headline: CYP2D6 polymorphisms and primaquine efficacy in P. vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Polymorphisms of the CYP2D6 gene and its relationship with Plasmodium vivax relapses after chloroquine-primaquine treatment in Turbo, Colombia.
articles.authors: Sierra-Cifuentes V, Zuluaga-Idárraga L, Aguirre-Acevedo D, Silva de Barros Puça MC, Nobrega de Sousa T, Lopera-Mesa TM
articles.details: Study in 71 patients with P. vivax in Colombia examining CYP2D6 polymorphisms and association with relapse risk and primaquine plasma levels over 6-month follow-up. Most patients (81.7%) were normal or ultrarapid metabolizers; impaired-activity group (18.3%) had higher median day-2 primaquine concentration (660.4 ng/ml vs 313.5 ng/ml). No significant difference in relapse hazard ratio between impaired and normal CYP2D6 activity (adjusted HR 1.45, 95% CI 0.39–5.39).

articles.headline: Metabolomic comparison of Plasmodium and Babesia in red cells
articles.title_original: Shared host, distinct invaders: metabolomic footprints of plasmodium and babesia in host red cells.
articles.authors: Beri D, Rodriguez M, Lobo CA
articles.details: Narrative review synthesizing metabolomic findings in Plasmodium and Babesia-infected red blood cells. Both infections show enhanced glycolytic rate and lipid scavenging from RBC membranes; perturbations in sugars, amino acids, lipids, redox homeostasis, and heme utilization are common hallmarks. Key differences relate to hemoglobin breakdown and utilization of resultant amino acids. Comparative analysis reveals unique metabolic niches and points toward identifying new biomarkers and targeted therapies.

articles.headline: Uncertainty-guided attention learning detects malaria parasites in blood smears
articles.title_original: Uncertainty-guided attention learning for malaria parasite detection in thick blood smears.
articles.authors: Xiong H, Wang Z, Sharan RV, Berkovsky S
articles.details: Study proposing uncertainty-guided attention learning network for automated malaria parasite detection from thick blood smear images. Method incorporates pixel attention and Bayesian channel attention to identify fine-grained features while estimating channel-wise uncertainty to improve classification. Evaluation on two public datasets showed superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines, with highest average precision scores in both parasite-level and patient-level scenarios.

articles.headline: AI applications in African malaria control: opportunities and risks
articles.title_original: Artificial intelligence in African malaria control programs: opportunities and risks.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Ogenyi FC
articles.details: Narrative review of current and emerging AI applications in African malaria control programs, including surveillance enhancement, outbreak prediction, diagnostic improvement, and resource optimization. Case studies include drone-assisted vector surveillance in Kenya, mobile health platforms for real-time reporting in Nigeria, and climate-informed forecasting in Tanzania. Review covers AI-driven genomic surveillance and parasite life cycle modeling, with critical examination of challenges including data quality and infrastructure limitations.

articles.headline: Transdermal microarray patches enhance artesunate/amodiaquine pharmacokinetics
articles.title_original: Polymeric microarray patches for transdermal delivery of amodiaquine and artesunate: A novel strategy against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Anjani QK, Volpe-Zanuto F, Miatmoko A, Moreno-Castellanos N, Novais JT, Gaitán XA, Hariawan BS, Cahyani DM, Octavia RT, Mubarok AS, Wahyu Setyaningsih WA, Johnston C, Bargieri DY, Donnelly RF
articles.details: Study developing dissolving microarray patches (MAPs) for transdermal delivery of amodiaquine and artesunate as alternative to oral ACTs. In vitro studies showed 40% amodiaquine and 90% artesunate deposition/permeation into skin; pharmacokinetic analysis revealed artesunate half-life extended 8.5-fold (54.05 vs 6.37 h) and Cmax increased 21% with MAP delivery. In P. berghei-infected mice, combined MAP treatment reduced parasitaemia by 99.5% within 7 days.

articles.headline: Mathematical model of vaccines and interventions on malaria transmission
articles.title_original: An age-structured mathematical model to assess the combined effects of vaccine and non-vaccine interventions on malaria transmission and burden.
articles.authors: Glèlè Kakaï R, Djomatin LC
articles.details: Study developing age-structured mathematical model integrating vaccination (RTS,S and R21), LLINs, treatment, and demographic parameters to simulate combined effects on malaria transmission and disease burden. Model calibrated using demographic data from Ethiopia (low transmission) and Nigeria (high transmission) to project transmission rates, disease burden, and DALYs under different vaccination scenarios. Preliminary findings suggest integration of vaccination with existing control strategies could significantly reduce malaria incidence and mortality.

articles.headline: PSSP17 biomarker for saliva-based malaria diagnostic and transmission
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Plasmodium falciparum PSSP17 essentiality in human-to-mosquito transmission and its prospective candidacy as a biomarker.
articles.authors: López-Gutiérrez B, Hathaway N, Alonso-Palomares L, Nyasembe VO, Kato M, Hamerly T, Parr JB, Juliano JJ, Harbers M, Dinglasan RR
articles.details: Study evaluating PSSP17 (PF3D7_1218800) as potential diagnostic biomarker in saliva from children with subclinical P. falciparum infections. Demonstrated that pssp17 is dispensable in asexual stages but essential for mosquito transmission; analysis of 19,280 publicly available genomes revealed no naturally occurring deletions. Since Δpssp17 mutants would be non-transmissible, PSSP17 is warranted as candidate for non-invasive, saliva-based RDT biomarker development.

articles.headline: Cell-free DNA sequencing detects P. vivax when blood smears fail
articles.title_original: Travel-Related Malaria Diagnosis on Karius Test Despite Negative Blood Smear.
articles.authors: Weigold JE, Lal S, Youssef DA
articles.details: Case report of 59-year-old returning from Amazon rainforest with fever, rash, myalgia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and transaminitis. Despite clinically suspicious presentation and negative malaria smears, plasma-based cell-free DNA sequencing (Karius test) successfully detected P. vivax infection. Patient required multiple treatment regimens including chloroquine-primaquine, artemether-lumefantrine, and quinine-doxycycline with prolonged primaquine for hypnozoite clearance, with complete resolution of symptoms and laboratory abnormalities.

articles.headline: Cameroon seroepidemiology: 60% SARS-CoV-2, 22% Plasmodium seroprevalence
articles.title_original: Seroprevalence of anti-SARS-CoV-2 and anti-Plasmodium falciparum and vivax antibodies in urban and rural zones in the West Region of Cameroon.
articles.authors: Kamga RMN, Ajim NC, Soubgwi PF, Noyes H, MacLeod A, Simo G, Adamson WE
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in urban and rural Cameroon (805 participants) using rapid diagnostic tests. Overall anti-Plasmodium seroprevalence was 22.2% (31.4% rural, 14.8% urban), with 21% attributable to P. falciparum and 2.11% to P. vivax. Anti-SARS-CoV-2 seroprevalence was 60.1% overall (66.6% urban, 54.0% rural). No association was found between anti-SARS-CoV-2 and anti-Plasmodium antibodies.

articles.headline: Ivermectin contact toxicity kills malaria vectors and blocks transmission
articles.title_original: Contact exposure to ivermectin induces acute mortality and inhibits parasite development in malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Jeena M, Goyal B, Lata S, Kumar G, Pradhan LK, Thakur Y, Kaur G, Verma V, Pandey KC, Pasi S, Kaur J
articles.details: Laboratory study evaluating ivermectin contact toxicity in An. stephensi and An. culicifacies mosquitoes, with transmission-blocking efficacy tested in Plasmodium berghei infections. Ivermectin induced 100% mortality within 24-48 hours at higher dosages; topical LD50 values were 0.017 ng/mg (An. stephensi) and 0.002 ng/mg (An. culicifacies) at 48 hours. Contact exposure significantly impaired oocyst development, with a 71% reduction in oocyst numbers at 0.01 µM ivermectin concentration.

articles.headline: Severe malaria dysbiosis: elevated Enterobacteriaceae predicts post-discharge mortality
articles.title_original: Gut bacterial dysbiosis in pediatric severe malaria associates with post-discharge mortality.
articles.authors: Bednarski OJ, Lehman SB, Mzinza D, Kazinga C, Namazzi R, Opoka RO, Ren J, Tran TM, Taylor TE, Seydel KB, John CC, Conroy AL, Schmidt NW
articles.details: Metagenomic analysis of stool samples from African children with severe malaria compared to community controls identified dysbiosis characterized by increased Enterobacteriaceae abundance. Elevated E. coli abundance was identified as a predictor of post-discharge mortality. Metagenome analysis revealed elevated metabolic pathways and genes supporting Enterobacteriaceae utilization of host-derived molecules.

articles.headline: MALDI-TOF MS vector surveillance: 83% cost reduction vs. molecular methods
articles.title_original: MALDI-TOF MS for malaria vector surveillance: A cost-comparison analysis using a decision-tree approach.
articles.authors: Karisa J, Rist C, Tuwei M, Ominde K, Bartilol B, Ondieki Z, Musani H, Wanjiku C, Mwangangi J, Mbogo C, Rono M, Bejon P, Maia M
articles.details: Cost-comparison analysis using decision-tree modeling for mosquito identification in Kenya's national malaria surveillance program (15,000 samples/year). Shifting from molecular methods to MALDI-TOF MS would result in 74.48% time savings, 84% material cost reduction, 77% labour cost reduction, and 83% overall direct cost savings. Authors conclude MALDI-TOF MS adoption could enable rapid, cost-effective routine entomological surveillance in Africa.

articles.headline: P. vivax relapses decline with reduced transmission intensity in Thailand
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax Malaria Relapse Risk Depends on Transmission Intensity: Evidence From a Longitudinal Study in Northwest Thailand.
articles.authors: Chu CS, Lwin KM, Burgoine K, Phyo AP, Turner C, San T, Aung AA, Win HH, Htoo K, Soe NL, Christina N, Than HH, Dah N, Pilaseng K, Wiladpaingern J, Proux S, Mukaka M, Parker DM, Carrara VI, Nosten F, White NJ
articles.details: Prospective observational cohort study in northwest Thailand (380 participants) evaluating primaquine radical cure efficacy (0.5 mg base/kg/day for 14 days) in individuals with prior symptomatic P. vivax. Annual P. vivax incidence declined from 0.19 per person-year in 2010 to 0.09 per person-year in 2014. Primaquine failure rate (recurrence within 4 months) was 75% less than predicted.

articles.headline: NUAK1 inhibition blocks P. falciparum invasion and growth in vitro
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum exploits NUAK1 to establish infection in human erythrocytes.
articles.authors: Navarrete DJ, Kim CY, McLelland K, Ramirez MG, Baro B, Salinas ND, Tolia NH, Doerig C, Ong SE, Golkowski MG, Egan ES
articles.details: Study demonstrating that AMPK-related kinase NUAK1 is a critical host factor for P. falciparum infection in human erythrocytes. Two selective NUAK1 inhibitors (HTH-01-015 and WZ4003) inhibited parasite growth throughout the asexual life cycle including erythrocyte invasion. Genetic overexpression of NUAK1 partially rescued phenotypes blocked by the inhibitors.

articles.headline: PfEMP1-binding antibodies protective against cerebral malaria: review and priorities
articles.title_original: Breaking the bind: PfEMP1-specific antibodies in cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Banda JP, Walker IS, Nyirenda T, Aitken EH, Rogerson SJ
