articles.details: Narrative review synthesizing evidence that antibodies targeting EPCR-binding CIDR-α1 and DBL-β domains of PfEMP1 correlate with reduced cerebral malaria risk, while responses to rosetting-associated domains are less protective. Review discusses antibody kinetics (early durable Group A responses vs. delayed Group B/C responses) and effector mechanisms including opsonic phagocytosis, complement activation, and Fc glycosylation.

articles.headline: YOLOv3 deep learning achieves 94.4% accuracy for P. falciparum detection
articles.title_original: Rapid and accurate recognition of erythrocytic stage parasites of Plasmodium falciparum via a deep learning-based YOLOv3 platform.
articles.authors: He W, Zhu H, Geng J, Zhu D, Wu K, Xie L, Li J, Yang H
articles.details: Development and evaluation of AI-assisted diagnostic tool using YOLOv3 deep learning for P. falciparum detection in blood smears. Testing on 262 images (cropped into 20 segments each) achieved 94.41% overall recognition accuracy with 1.68% false negative rate and 3.91% false positive rate. Authors conclude this provides feasible technical support for malaria control in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Benin vector competence: An. coluzzii dominates transmission with high sporozoite load
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal distribution of four malaria vector species and their relative contributions to Plasmodium falciparum transmission along the south-north transect of Benin, West Africa.
articles.authors: Akpodji S, Agbangla C, Padonou GG, Affolabi ZK, Koukpo ZC, Adoha C, Hougbe SZ, Sominahouin A, Tokponnon F, Osse RA, Oussou O, Adjottin B, Odjo EM, Yovogan B, Azondékon R, Salako A, Akogbéto M
articles.details: Entomological survey in Benin (18 communes) determining malaria vector contributions by species using both infection/bite rates and sporozoite loads. An. coluzzii caused 63.01% of P. falciparum transmission (EIR 87.7), followed by An. gambiae with 36.7% (EIR 51.1) and An. funestus with 0.24%. An. gambiae carried higher sporozoite loads (~30% with >10,000 sporozoites) compared to An. coluzzii (<10%).

articles.headline: Coustani Group mosquitoes (DRC) low sporozoite prevalence and cryptic species diversity
articles.title_original: Coustani Group mosquito (Diptera: Culicidae) diversity and malaria transmission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.authors: Hendershot AL, Wat'senga FT, Mampangulu T, Lodi O, Nsungu IT, Manzambi EZ, Okito A, Nzila P, Aubourg MA, Yu X, Likwela J, Fornadel C, Oxborough RM, Lobo NF, Irish SR
articles.details: Entomological survey in DRC (Lodja, Kapolowe, Kenge) characterizing Anopheles paludis and related Coustani Group mosquitoes. ITS2/CO1 sequencing revealed 2 species groups in Lodja and 4 in Kapolowe with different biting behaviors. Sporozoite prevalence was low: no sporozoites detected in Lodja and only 1 specimen positive in Kapolowe. Authors concluded Coustani Group mosquitoes are not major malaria vectors.

articles.headline: Thailand RASR: timely response except border areas; resource constraints identified
articles.title_original: Evaluating Thailand's malaria reactive surveillance and response strategies for malaria elimination: a mixed-method study.
articles.authors: Oo WH, Roobsoong W, Aye Tun N, Khant KM, Htike W, Kengganpanich M, Agius PA, Sattabongkot J, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: Mixed-method evaluation of Thailand's 1-3-7 reactive surveillance and response (RASR) strategy in Tak, Songkhla, Yala provinces (Jan-Apr 2023). Among 74 programme stakeholders and service providers, 54.1% reported case notification within 1-day target; 98.7% reported investigations and response within timeframes except in border areas. All participants considered the strategy efficient, though challenges included limited resources and difficult terrain.

articles.headline: TS/CQ prophylaxis doesn't increase antimalarial resistance mutations in HIV+ Malawians
articles.title_original: Impact of Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole and Chloroquine Prophylaxis on Drug-Resistant Plasmodium falciparum Mutations in Adults with HIV in Malawi.
articles.authors: Fairhurst KJ, Mungwira RG, Divala TH, Nyirenda OM, Masonga R, Joshi S, Fern N, van Oosterhout JJ, Laufer MK, Laurens MB
articles.details: Randomized controlled trial in Malawi comparing chloroquine (CQ) and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TS) prophylaxis among HIV+ adults on antiretroviral therapy. Pyrosequencing of resistance markers (pfdhfr, pfdhps, pfcrt) revealed no significant differences in resistance mutation prevalence across treatment arms. Parasites infecting those receiving TS or CQ prophylaxis did not have increased resistance-associated polymorphisms, and no CQ resistance mutations were detected in breakthrough infections.

articles.headline: STING-IRF3 pathway regulates regulatory B cells in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Distinct STING-IRF3 activation determines IL-35(+) Breg differentiation in rodent malaria.
articles.authors: Feng A, Li Q, Jiang N, Lv K, Yang Y, Liu T, Su Z, Zhang Y, Sang X, Feng Y, Chen R, Chen Q
articles.details: In mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA (cerebral malaria model) or P. yoelii YM (non-cerebral), researchers found IL-35+ regulatory B cells expanded in P. yoelii YM-infected mice but not in P. berghei ANKA-infected mice. STING-mediated interferon regulatory factor 3 (IRF3) phosphorylation suppressed IL-35+ Breg differentiation, potentially contributing to cerebral malaria pathology.

articles.headline: Babesiosis misdiagnosed as malaria in traveler; fatal Honduras case
articles.title_original: First documented case of imported human babesiosis in Honduras: Diagnostic challenges with malaria falciparum.
articles.authors: Chaver L, Flores H, Padilla W, Rodríguez J, Zúniga C, Medina F, Matamoros G, Araujo ME, Ortiz B, Fontecha G
articles.details: A 72-year-old man returning from Massachusetts presented with fever and neurological symptoms. Initial thick smears suggested P. falciparum and he received chloroquine-primaquine followed by artemether-lumefantrine, but developed hyperparasitemia and multiorgan failure, dying on day 33. Molecular assays were negative for Plasmodium but PCR confirmed Babesia 18S rRNA, highlighting the diagnostic challenge of distinguishing babesiosis from malaria.

articles.headline: mRNA vaccine overcomes hemozoin impairment of parasite vaccine immunity
articles.title_original: mRNA vaccination overcomes hemozoin-mediated impairment of whole parasite vaccine efficacy for malaria.
articles.authors: Hassert M, Drewry LL, Pewe LL, Hancox LS, He R, Arumugam S, Mix MR, Salem AK, Harty JT
articles.details: In a murine model where prior Plasmodium blood-stage exposure impaired radiation-attenuated sporozoite (RAS) vaccine-induced CD8+ T cell responses, hemozoin was identified as the mediator. An mRNA vaccine encoding Plasmodium CD8+ T cell epitopes overcame the T cell defect and restored protection in Plasmodium-exposed mice. A combined RAS+mRNA regimen enhanced liver resident memory T cells and protection in malaria-experienced hosts.

articles.headline: Macaque P. knowlesi infection correlates with human transmission in Malaysia
articles.title_original: Multi-pronged surveillance to understand the spatiotemporal correlations among macaques, vectors and humans in Plasmodium knowlesi malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Phang WK, Jeyaprakasam NK, Pramasivan S, Suli ZB, Tam JZ, Hamid MHBA, Abdullah MLB, Rohimly AABI, Ashrat NB, Chuang TW, Nguitragool W, Vythilingam I, Lau YL
articles.details: Integrated field surveillance data from humans, macaques, and mosquito vectors in Peninsular Malaysia (2019-2022) showed statistically significant positive partial correlation between proportion of P. knowlesi-positive humans and Anopheles leucosphyrus-group mosquitoes (rs = 0.228, P = 0.042). Proportion of P. knowlesi-positive macaques showed consistent positive correlation with historical human P. knowlesi cases within 6 km and beyond spatial proximity.

articles.headline: LDH-based RDT achieves 91.9% sensitivity for HRP2-negative P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Clinical evaluation of a novel Mologic malaria Plasmodium falciparum LDH-dependent lateral flow rapid diagnostic test diagnostic accuracy: a cross-sectional study, Sudan.
articles.authors: Mohamed AO, Hamid MMA, Jebreel WMA, Elaagip A, Mohammed FO, Albsheer MM, Mustafa SA, Ahmed TME, Ahmed SBA, Chamai M, Dittrich S
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 500 patients in Sudan evaluated the Mologic LDH-RDT for P. falciparum diagnosis with PCR as reference (210/500 positive, 42%). Sensitivities were: microscopy 95.24% (95% CI 91.4-97.6), Mologic LDH-RDT 91.9% (95% CI 87.3-95.2), and comparator RDT 93.81% (95% CI 89.6-96.6), with nearly 100% specificity (κ = 0.929). Mologic LDH-RDT successfully detected 6 HRP2-negative samples.

articles.headline: 60% of African children experience malaria treatment delays >24 hours
articles.title_original: Estimating rates of treatment delay for malaria fevers among children in Sub-Saharan Africa 2006-2022.
articles.authors: Lubinda J, Rumisha SF, Dzianach P, McPhail M, Saddler A, Browne A, Sanna F, Gelaw Y, Castle P, Kiss JB, Harris J, Rozier JA, Vargas C, Amratia P, Symons TL, Cameron E, Gething PW, Weiss DJ
articles.details: Analysis of 177 nationally representative surveys (2006-2022) in Sub-Saharan Africa estimated malaria treatment delay rates among children: 60% (95% UI 45.7-72.8) experienced delays exceeding 24 hours, affecting 33 million children; 29% (95% UI 18.9-41.2) faced delays exceeding 48 hours, affecting 16 million children. Somalia had highest delay rate (76%, 95% UI 39.7-97.9) and Tanzania lowest (35.3%, 95% UI 11.5-53.8); improvements stagnated post-2015.

articles.headline: Integral gene drive achieves 98.4-99.5% transmission in Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: The nanosd integral gene drive enables population modification of the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Yen PS, Verkuijl SANR, Capriotti P, Del Corsano G, Yee Kim Guan C, Hoermann A, Inghilterra MG, Aramburu-Gonzalez I, Khan MA, Vlachou D, Christophides GK, Windbichler N
articles.details: Researchers developed the nanosd integral gene drive targeting the nanos gene of Anopheles gambiae, achieving high gene drive rates (98.4% in females, 99.5% in males). Homozygous nanosd females exhibited impaired fecundity with variable ovary underdevelopment, but the modification did not induce widespread transcriptomic perturbations affecting vector competence. The gene drive propagated efficiently in caged populations and maintained Cas9 expression after emergence of drive-resistant alleles.

articles.headline: Machine learning accelerates malaria vaccine antigen discovery and prediction
articles.title_original: From data to immunity: the role of machine learning in advancing malaria vaccine research: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Khanday S, Sayeed M, Jafri NF, Jafri IF, Jafri RF, Ashraf G, Safwat S, Nasr DS
articles.details: Scoping review (2017-2025) of machine learning in malaria vaccine research identified five key applications: antigen discovery via supervised/semi-supervised learning, immune signature identification and efficacy prediction using diverse ML algorithms, computational tool development for data integration, broad AI/ML reviews, and epidemiological modeling for policy support. Most studies were from Europe and North America with African collaborations.

articles.headline: PbGAC merozoite protein binds heparan sulfate during erythrocyte invasion
articles.title_original: The Plasmodium berghei merozoite protein PbGAC is critically involved in erythrocyte binding during invasion.
articles.authors: Gao J, Jiang N, Zhang Y, Chen R, Feng Y, Sang X, Chen Q
articles.details: Researchers characterized PbGAC (encoded by PbANKA_1137800), a Plasmodium berghei merozoite protein located at the extreme apical region that binds heparin and erythrocyte surfaces via heparan sulfate-dependent pathway during invasion. Mass spectrometric analysis revealed PbGAC interactions with secreted proteins critically involved in erythrocyte invasion. Mice immunized with recombinant PbGAC protein or passively immunized with vaccinated-mouse sera demonstrated enhanced immunity against lethal P. berghei challenges.

articles.headline: Correction to P. vivax liver organoid infection study
articles.title_original: Correction: Human liver organoids are susceptible to Plasmodium vivax infection.
articles.authors: Nitaramorn N, Kobpornchai P, Tongkrajang N, Chaisri U, Imwong M, Kulkeaw K
articles.details: Correction to prior publication on human liver organoids susceptible to Plasmodium vivax infection.

articles.headline: SNP barcode reveals low differentiation in P. falciparum from Central/West Africa
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and population structures of Plasmodium falciparum parasites imported to China from Central and West Africa determined using a SNP barcode.
articles.authors: Zeng W, Zhu W, Qin Y, Lan H, Cen Y, Sun K, Wu Y, Liang T, Mou Y, Liu C, Tang X, Xiang Z, Huang Y, Cui L, Yang Z
articles.details: Twenty-four-SNP high-resolution melting barcode analysis of 181 P. falciparum samples imported to China from Central and West Africa revealed low nucleotide diversity across all populations with pairwise FST values ranging from 0.001 to 0.054 (low to moderate genetic differentiation). Principal component analysis and population structure assessment showed lack of prominent clustering among the four populations, indicating minimal genetic differentiation within these African regions.

articles.headline: African malaria vectors possess expanded olfactory gene repertoires
articles.title_original: Olfactory gene dynamics in invasive Indian and non-invasive African malaria vectors at the crossroads of development, infection and resistance.
articles.authors: Sharma A, Dixit B, Goyal B, Harit R, Kumar J, Biswas S, Goswami R, Pandey KC, Emami SN, Chakraborti S
articles.details: Genome-wide analysis of olfactory genes in four malaria vectors (Anopheles stephensi, A. culicifacies, A. gambiae, A. funestus) identified odorant binding proteins (OBPs), chemosensory proteins (CSPs), odorant receptors (ORs), ionotropic receptors (IRs), and gustatory receptors (GusRs). African vectors showed significantly higher numbers of OBP, OR, and IR genes compared to Indian counterparts. Pyrethroid-resistant A. stephensi larvae exhibited elevated expression of SAP2 and SAP3, suggesting their role in insecticide resistance.

articles.headline: Malaria seasonality dataset assembled for sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: A malaria seasonality dataset for sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Sanna F, Keddie SH, Boyhan T, Dzianach PA, McPhail M, Seitz J, Nguyen T, Redpath A, Chikolwa T, Browne AJ, Lubinda J, Saddler A, Hafsia S, Jayaseelen R, Baggen HS, Rozier JA, Symons TL, Harris J, Connor S, Vargas C, Whittaker C, Nguyen M, Gething PW, Weiss DJ
articles.details: This study compiled a publicly available geolocated dataset of malaria seasonality timeseries published since 2000 across sub-Saharan Africa using literature review (including natural language processing), routine surveillance data, and entomological datasets. The dataset includes malaria prevalence, incidence, mortality, and entomological timeseries, plus qualitative descriptions of seasonality patterns extracted from published literature.

articles.headline: Poor treatment adherence in Rwanda undermines antimalarial resistance control
articles.title_original: Understanding Malaria Treatment Adherence in Rwanda: Implications for Artemisinin Resistance.
articles.authors: Gashema P, Jordan A, Saramba E, Wernsman Young N, Iradukunda PG, Karema C, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Banek K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 406 febrile patients at government clinics in Rwanda (December 2023–February 2024) assessing malaria knowledge, attitudes, and treatment practices. Though 81% correctly identified symptoms and 74.6% understood control measures, only 46% of patients who received treatment completed the full 3-day course; 37% stopped within 2 days. Poor adherence, particularly with 27% self-treating with antimalarials, exacerbates artemisinin resistance risk.

articles.headline: Anopheles mascarensis: Madagascar malaria vector with suspected sibling species
articles.title_original: Anopheles mascarensis, a Malaria Vector Endemic to Madagascar and the Comoros Archipelago: A Review.
articles.authors: Goupeyou-Youmsi J, Tantely LM, Andrianinarivomanana TM, Girod R, Bourgouin C
articles.details: Review of An. mascarensis, the primary malaria vector in eastern Madagascar and secondary vector in other regions. Updated geographical distribution and biological data reveal significant differences between east-coast and Central Highlands populations, suggesting An. mascarensis may comprise a complex of sibling species with distinct climatic and biogeographic distribution patterns.

articles.headline: Parasite census tracks control interventions but shows transmission resilience
articles.title_original: Measuring changes in Plasmodium falciparum census population size in response to sequential malaria control interventions.
articles.authors: Tiedje KE, Zhan Q, Ruybal-Pésantez S, Tonkin-Hill G, He Q, Tan MH, Argyropoulos DC, Deed S, Ghansah A, Bangre O, Oduro AR, Koram KA, Pascual M, Day KP
articles.details: Study tracked Plasmodium falciparum census population size (based on var gene multiplicity of infection) in ~2,000 humans across sequential malaria control interventions in northern Ghana (2012–2017). Indoor residual spraying reduced parasite prevalence ~40–50%, causing short-lived reductions in var diversity and population size; however, 32 months after IRS cessation and introduction of seasonal malaria chemoprevention, var diversity and population size rebounded in all age groups except children 1–5 years, demonstrating P. falciparum resilience to short-term interventions.

articles.headline: Fluorescent probe MR-1 enables rapid malaria parasite detection
articles.title_original: Advanced Clinical Diagnosis of Malaria Using a Simple Red-Emissive Smart Fluorescent Probe.
articles.authors: Ye Y, Zhang S, Chen G, Liu Z, Chen X, Luo R, Zhang Z, Zhao X, Lian J, Yang Z, Samanta S, Yang Z, Xu K
articles.details: Study describes MR-1, a hemicyanine dye fluorescent probe with blue absorption and red emission that binds AT-rich DNA segments. When incubated with malaria parasite-infected red blood cells, MR-1 showed marked fluorescence enhancement with a detection limit of ~20 parasites/μL and enabled precise detection and classification of different parasite species and differentiation of malaria from Babesia-parasite infections via flow cytometry.

articles.headline: Rwanda: high Pfkelch13 and antifolate resistance markers detected
articles.title_original: Genomic Surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum Drug Resistance Markers Between October 2021 and June 2023 in Kigali, Rwanda.
articles.authors: Fankem SN, Mbonimpa JB, Kalimba EM, Diallo MT, Teke ME, Souopgui J
articles.details: Genomic surveillance of 250 P. falciparum clinical isolates from Kigali, Rwanda (October 2021–June 2023) using Oxford Nanopore and Sanger sequencing revealed widespread drug-resistance mutations: pfmdr1 184F in 72.8% (lumefantrine pressure), Pfkelch13 mutations in 50.4% (including validated R561H at 25.6%), universal pfdhfr/pfdhps mutations (IRNI-SAEAA in 45.6%), and novel K189T + R561H haplotypes at 24.8%.

articles.headline: Anopheles gambiae G3 reference genome assembled and sequenced
articles.title_original: Whole-Genome Shotgun Sequencing and Assembly of Anopheles gambiae G3, the Host of Malaria Parasite Plasmodium sp.
articles.authors: English E, Gulick C, Jane A, Kim M, Kpodonu N, Lee A, Lopez RKS, Patel K, Rajaram A, Unver L, Rankin AE, Ghazal SM
articles.details: Report of a 216.3 Mbp draft genome sequence and assembly for Anopheles gambiae strain G3 (isolated 1975), featuring approximately 82,000 scaffolds, 53× coverage, and 44.5% G+C content.

articles.headline: Ethiopia SNNPR: 15.89% malaria prevalence with seasonal peaks
articles.title_original: Retrospective analysis of malaria surveillance data in the former Southern Nations, Nationalities, and peoples' region, Ethiopia (2017-2021).
articles.authors: Dana DD, Ango TS, Getachew S, Deressa W
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 6.29 million suspected cases tested in Ethiopia's former SNNPR (2017–2021) identified 999,687 confirmed infections (15.89% prevalence): 68% P. falciparum, 32% P. vivax. Malaria burden was moderate overall (12 cases per 1,000 population annually) with high regional variation (annual parasite incidence 10–100 per 1,000 in South Omo, Gamo, Wolaita), and significant seasonal increases, rising from 13 per 1,000 in July 2021 to 16 per 1,000 by December 2021.

articles.headline: MDFS gene drive shows promise for mosquito population suppression
articles.title_original: A male-drive female-sterile system for the self-limited control of the malaria mosquito Anopheles gambiae.
articles.authors: Strampelli A, Willis K, Gulliford HR, Gribble M, Fasulo B, Burt A, Crisanti A, Bernardini F
articles.details: Study describes Male Drive Female Sterile (MDFS), a self-limiting gene drive strategy targeting Anopheles gambiae that causes dominant female sterility while transgenic males remain fertile and transmit the trait at super-Mendelian rates. Laboratory cage experiments demonstrated that repeated MDFS releases eliminate caged populations, and mathematical modeling suggests MDFS could effectively suppress wild malaria mosquito populations.

articles.headline: PfATP2 flippase is drug target for novel antiplasmodials
articles.title_original: PfATP2 is a flippase on the Plasmodium falciparum surface that is important for growth and influences parasite sensitivity to antiplasmodial compounds.
articles.authors: Qiu D, England E, Lehane AM
articles.details: Study identified PfATP2, a phospholipid flippase on P. falciparum surface, as important for parasite growth and drug sensitivity. Parasites overexpressing PfATP2 showed reduced sensitivity to compounds MMV665794, MMV007224, and MMV665852, while knockdown increased hypersensitivity. Data suggest PfATP2 mediates ATP-dependent phosphatidylserine internalization and is a potential target of these novel antiplasmodial compounds.

articles.headline: Novel LDH/HRP2 RDTs achieve >96% sensitivity, outperform microscopy
articles.title_original: First field evaluation of novel LDH- and HRP2-based rapid tests for Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum malaria diagnosis.
articles.authors: Oduma CO, Lulu B, Ewnetu Y, Lown LA, Kayode TA, Hawaria D, Koepfli C
articles.details: Field evaluation of four rapid diagnostic tests in 708 patients in southern Ethiopia with 46.0% qPCR-positive cases assessed sensitivity and specificity. At parasite densities >20/μL, all novel LDH- and HRP2-based RDTs achieved >96% sensitivity for P. falciparum (versus 63% for health-center microscopy) and >92% for P. vivax (versus 72% for microscopy), with specificity >99% for P. falciparum and >98% for P. vivax.

articles.headline: Kenya: under-five malaria prevalence 22.7%, poverty increases risk
articles.title_original: Individual and community level factors of malaria among under-five children in Kenya: Based on the Kenya Demographic and Health Survey.
articles.authors: Lakew G, Tareke AA, Yigzaw ZA, Getachew D, Getachew E, Yirsaw AN
articles.details: Analysis of 3,146 children from the 2022 Kenya Demographic and Health Survey found 22.72% malaria prevalence (95% CI: 21.29–24.22). Protective factors included maternal age 25–34 years (AOR 0.75, 95% CI 0.57–0.99), richest household wealth (AOR 0.48, 95% CI 0.26–0.89), treated bed nets (AOR 0.39, 95% CI 0.17–0.89), and breastfeeding (AOR 0.66, 95% CI 0.52–0.85), while community-level poverty significantly increased risk (AOR 1.82, 95% CI 1.19–2.77).

articles.headline: Climate factors drive malaria risk in Sub-Saharan African children
articles.title_original: Associations of temperature and precipitation with malaria in children under 5: A multi-country study in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Chombo S, Bachwenkizi J, Omary H, Paulo HA, Luoga P, Zacharia A, Mbishi JV, Andrew JD, Addo IY
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 15,009 children under 5 in six SSA countries. Malaria prevalence averaged 25.9% (Nigeria 38.1%, Burundi 38.0%). One-degree Celsius temperature increase increased malaria risk 1.77-fold (95% CI: 1.297-2.414, p<0.001); annual precipitation 250-500 mm increased risk 72% compared to >500 mm (95% CI: 1.331-2.212, p<0.001).

articles.headline: MSP1 blood-stage vaccine shows safety and immunogenicity in endemic adults
articles.title_original: Full-length merozoite surface protein 1 formulated with GLA-SE adjuvant in malaria pre-exposed adults: a randomised, controlled, double-blind, parallel-group, single-centre Phase Ib trial.
articles.authors: Kahatano AE, Mpina M, Vanobberghen F, Hassan O, Urasa N, Sasamalo I, Mswata S, Paris D, Gajewski S, Saxena M, Fürle K, Kiehl V, Böhnlein E, Aschenbrenner A, Lanzer M, Thomson-Luque R, Olotu A, Daubenberger C
articles.details: Phase Ib randomized controlled trial in 40 adults in Bagamoyo, Tanzania. SUM-101: 150 μg MSP1 FL from 3D7 strain with 5 μg GLA-SE adjuvant or Verorab control given days 0, 28, 56. 37/40 (93%) completed; 50% (SUM-101) and 65% (Verorab) experienced solicited adverse events (mostly mild-moderate); no severe adverse events. SUM-101 elicited robust MSP1-specific IgM and IgG responses with peak antibody titers at days 56 and 84.

articles.headline: Corrigendum to malaria epidemics and climate control measures in India
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "Malaria epidemics in India: Role of climatic condition and control measures" [Sci. Total Environ. Volume 712, 10 April 2020, 136368].
articles.authors: Baghbanzadeh M, Kumar D, Yavasoglu SI, Manning S, Hanafi-Bojd AA, Ghasemzadeh H, Sikder IU, Kumar D, Murmu N, Haque U
articles.details: Correction to article examining associations between climatic conditions (temperature and precipitation) and malaria epidemics in India, and the role of control measures in mitigating transmission.

articles.headline: Antibody responses predict malaria protection in Burkinabe children
articles.title_original: A Multiplex Serological Assay to Evaluate the Antibody Responses to a Set of Plasmodium falciparum Antigens and Their Protective Role Against Malaria in Children Aged 1.5 to 12 Years Living in a Highly Seasonal Malaria Transmission Area of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Ezinmegnon S, Nébié I, Marlais T, Ouattara D, Diarra A, Patterson C, Tetteh K, Ouédraogo A, Drakeley C, Tiono AB, Sirima SB
articles.details: Cross-sectional and longitudinal study of children aged 1.5-12 years in Burkina Faso. Children 5-12 years had higher P. falciparum-specific IgG levels than 1.5-5 year-olds. Anti-GLURP R2 and anti-Rh5.1 antibodies associated with reduced malaria risk (adjusted IRR 0.52 and 0.40). AMA1, GLURP R2, and Etramp5 Ag1 antibody combination achieved best predictive performance (AUC 0.70).

articles.headline: TCF25 regulates gametocytogenesis and ribosome biogenesis in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Transcription factor 25 modulates gametocytogenesis and ribosome biogenesis in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Wu J, Zhang Z, Ngim C, Yang W, Li P, Wang F, Ye J, Li B, Tian B, Zhang Q, Shang X
articles.details: Experimental study in tcf25 knockout P. falciparum parasites. Transcriptomic analysis showed 168 genes downregulated and 24 upregulated at ring stage, 53 downregulated and 4 upregulated at schizont stage. ChIP-seq identified 44 TCF25-binding target genes including rDNA. TCF25 deletion upregulated rRNA expression, particularly 28S rRNA, and influenced ap2-g pathway controlling gametocytogenesis.

articles.headline: PfEBA-175 region II shows high antigenic diversity and immune recognition
articles.title_original: Micro-geographic variation in antigenic diversity of PfEBA-175 region II in asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum infections in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Syahada JH, Lee WJ, Jun H, Louis JM, Fitriana F, Muh F, Lu F, Ahmed MA, Na S, Chun W, Park WS, Jeon BY, Han ET, Todd J, Manjurano A, Kidima W, Mazigo E, Lee SJ, Han JH
articles.details: Genetic and serological study of 172 asymptomatic P. falciparum isolates from Tanzania. High nucleotide diversity (π = 0.00359 ± 0.00012) with evidence of adaptive evolution (dN-dS = 3.15, p<0.001). Recombinant EBA-175 region II protein showed 84.8% average seropositivity (range 50.0-95.4% across villages). Antibody response correlated with age (ρ = 0.333, p<0.001) but not parasitemia or gender.

articles.headline: Rice farming intensification may alter malaria vector habitat ecology
articles.title_original: Climate change adapted rice production: does the system of rice intensification impact malaria vector ecology?
articles.authors: Hardy H, Hopkins RJ, Mnyone L, Hawkes FM
articles.details: Review article examining System of Rice Intensification (SRI)—an agricultural practice reducing water and input use while maintaining yields—and its potential impacts on malaria vector bionomics and transmission dynamics in Africa. Contemporary evidence shows association between elevated vector densities from rice fields and intensified malaria transmission in rice-growing communities.

articles.headline: Implementation barriers for post-discharge malaria chemoprevention identified
articles.title_original: Assessing stakeholder perceptions and contextual factors in implementing post-discharge malaria chemoprevention in children with severe anaemia in Malawi.
articles.authors: Ndambo MK, Hoyt J, Nyirongo E, Phiri KS, Hill J, Manda-Taylor L
articles.details: Qualitative formative research in Malawi (May 2024) with 31 stakeholder interviews on WHO-recommended PDMC for children discharged with severe anemia. Key findings: high malaria-anemia burden, knowledge gaps among health workers, and caregiver barriers including traditional beliefs, financial constraints, and logistical challenges. Successful implementation requires medication supply improvements, health worker training, community engagement, and supply chain strengthening.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine and piperaquine achieve equivalent malaria cure
articles.title_original: Parasite clearance of two commonly administered artemisinin-based combination therapies in under-five children with uncomplicated malaria in a comprehensive healthcare facility in South-Western Nigeria: a randomised controlled trial.
articles.authors: Ahmed AA, Bello IS, Adewole TO, Olowookere SA, Oyegbade OO, Ogundokun AO, Olajubu TO, Funwei RI, Ojurongbe O, Adepoju DA, Bello AB, Adegboyega AA, Anjorin AO, Anjorin AO, Akinola OO, Adewumi KM, Omenugha MK, Salami OT, Familusi AO, Akande EO
articles.details: Open-label randomized controlled trial in 122 Nigerian children (6-59 months) with uncomplicated malaria comparing artemether-lumefantrine (AL, n=61) and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHAPQ, n=61). Fever clearance faster in AL (46.16±3.31 h vs 48.83±8.95 h; p=0.032); parasite clearance similar (61.30±23.75 h vs 63.97±23.42 h; p=0.541). PCR-corrected cure rate 100% in both groups. Adverse events: 5 mild in DHAPQ, none in AL.

articles.headline: Hybrid Vision Transformer and CNN detect malaria with 95.6% accuracy
articles.title_original: Ensemble-Based Early Detection of Malaria via Explainable ViT-CNN Feature Fusion and SHAP.
articles.authors: Özdemir EY, Koç C, Küçük K, Özyurt F
articles.details: Machine-learning study combining Vision Transformer (ViT) and EfficientNet CNN features from microscopic blood smear images for malaria detection. Ensemble model integrating ViT_Large_16 and EfficientNetB0 features with CatBoost, XGBoost, and Logistic Regression achieved 95.61% accuracy with 1.6 s processing time, approximately 204 times faster than traditional ML approaches. ViT-based models effectively captured complex blood smear details.

articles.headline: Correction to anticoagulant and cerebral malaria study
articles.title_original: Correction: Anticoagulant therapy and altered tissue factor expression protect against experimental placental and cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Andrew AK, Bracken TC, Nazario-Maldonado N, Russ BN, Gonzalvo KT, Brainard BM, Mackman N, Moore JM
articles.details: Correction to article examining anticoagulant therapy and altered tissue factor expression in experimental placental and cerebral malaria models.

articles.headline: Malaria infection alters glucose and amino acid metabolism pathways
articles.title_original: Application of a Non-Targeted Metabolomics Study in Plasmodium berghei-Infected Rats: Towards Unravelling Metabolic Alterations During Malaria Infection.
articles.authors: Mbuli ZN, Ndlovu IS, Masola B, Mukaratirwa S
articles.details: Untargeted metabolomics study in 20 male Sprague Dawley rats (10 P. berghei-infected, 10 controls) using gas chromatography-time-of-flight mass spectrometry. Infected rats showed elevated urea and reduced 1,5-anhydroglucitol, D-(+)-Talose, and arachidonic acid. Pathway analysis revealed enrichment in glucose-alanine cycle, alpha-linolenic acid, and linoleic acid metabolism, suggesting parasite-induced glucose depletion and increased amino acid catabolism.

articles.headline: Temperature particularly minimum drives malaria transmission dynamics
articles.title_original: Association between temperature and malaria (1959-2019): a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Murugan Y, Shrivastav V
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 11 studies (1959-2019) from Africa and Asia examined temperature-malaria associations. Mean temperature correlated moderately with malaria incidence (r=0.417, 95% CI: 0.098-0.735, p=0.01), while minimum temperature showed the strongest association with malaria test positivity (r=0.454, 95% CI: 0.358-0.549, p<0.001). Maximum temperature showed weaker association (r=0.356, 95% CI: 0.263-0.450). Authors conclude minimum temperature thresholds should be integrated into surveillance and early warning systems.

articles.headline: Innovation strategies for evolving malaria control landscape
articles.title_original: Innovation under pressure: bold ideas for a changing malaria landscape.
articles.authors: Epstein A, Tangena JA
articles.details: No abstract provided. Based on title, discusses innovation approaches for malaria control in changing epidemiological contexts.

articles.headline: Post-artesunate delayed haemolysis complicates malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: The usual suspect-malaria? Diagnostic challenges in post-artesunate delayed haemolysis: a clinical laboratory perspective through a case report.
articles.authors: Molnar E, Cabral PM, Portal F, Guimarães AR, Santos L, Guimarães JT
articles.details: Case report examining post-artesunate delayed haemolysis (PADH), a rare but potentially severe complication of artemisinin derivative treatment. Highlights diagnostic challenges in distinguishing PADH manifestations from malaria, particularly important in low-incidence European settings where malaria is predominantly travel-related. Emphasizes need for thorough travel histories and sustained laboratory diagnostic proficiency.

articles.headline: ITN use among African children averages 70% with regional variation
articles.title_original: Utilization of insecticide-treated nets for malaria prevention among children in Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Basiru A, Alozieuwa UB, Aboh MI, Usman UY, Nwaefulu ON, Okeke OP, Akinsolu FT, Sobande OO
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 30 studies (2013-2023) with 76,045 children assessing insecticide-treated net (ITN) utilization prevalence. Pooled ITN utilization was 69.50% (95% CI: 64.5-74.5%, I²=100%, p=0.001). Regional variation: Central Africa 85.44% (95% CI: 85.3-85.6), Southern Africa 80.7% (95% CI: 80.6-80.9), Eastern Africa 69.2% (95% CI: 63.8-74.6), West Africa 67.0% (95% CI: 56.2-77.8). Authors conclude ITN utilization is low and varies regionally, requiring improvement.

articles.headline: South Africa shows minimal artemisinin-resistance markers despite regional trends
articles.title_original: Very Low Prevalence of Validated kelch13 Mutations and Absence of Histidine-Rich Protein 2/3 Double Gene Deletions in South African Malaria-Eliminating Districts (2022-2024).
articles.authors: Raman J, Mabona M, Nyawo Q, Mangena B, Kok G, Mathaba L, Malatje G, Lauterbach SB, Makhanthisa TI, Gwarinda H, Letinić BD, Ndadza A, Raswiswi E, Shandukani M, Baloyi E, Fekema Z, Ismail A, Featherston J, Greenhouse B, Smith JL, Aranda-Diaz A
articles.details: Analysis of 4,471 samples (2022-2024) from KwaZulu-Natal and Mpumalanga assessed for drug-resistance markers. Validated artemisinin-resistance markers were rare: P574L detected in 2 samples, P553L in 1. Most prevalent non-validated mutations were A578S (30/999) and I494V (13/951). crtK76 (99.8%), mdr1N86 (99.0%), dhfr triple (99.9%), and dhps double (98.2%) mutations were near fixation. No hrp2/3 deletions detected. Authors conclude recommended treatments and diagnostics remain effective.

articles.headline: Novel neonicotinoid insecticide maintains vector control for eight months
articles.title_original: Efficacy of the Insecticide Formulation SumiShield® 50WG for Malaria Vector Control in Experimental Huts in Madagascar.
articles.authors: Nepomichene T, Randrenjarison R, Randriamanga J, Girod R
articles.details: Experimental hut study (9 months) evaluating SumiShield 50WG (clothianidin neonicotinoid) against wild Anopheles and An. arabiensis laboratory strain. Mortality in wild mosquitoes remained above WHO 80% threshold for up to 8 months post-spray; in An. arabiensis, threshold was met for 8 months but not by month 9. Formulation did not induce exophily or inhibit blood-feeding. Authors conclude 8-month residual efficacy is sufficient to cover transmission season in most Madagascar endemic areas.

articles.headline: Madagascar ITNs lose efficacy within one year despite three-year specifications
articles.title_original: Early Bio-Efficacy Loss of Nets Mass Distributed for Malaria Vector Control in Madagascar in 2018: Implications for Malaria Prevention.
articles.authors: Nepomichene T, Randrenjarison R, Raharinjatovo J, Swamidoss I, Mapp C, Kapesa L, Razafindrakoto J, Bowen A, Belemvire A, Zohdy S, Poyer S, Girod R
articles.details: Bio-efficacy assessment of DawaPlus 2.0 and PermaNet 2.0 ITNs distributed in Madagascar (2018) at 0, 12, 24, and 36 months. On arrival, mosquito mortality exceeded WHO 80% threshold (DawaPlus 86.4%, PermaNet 83.6%). At 12, 24, 36 months, mortality <56% for all districts. Knockdown effect below WHO 95% threshold at all time points. Deltamethrin residue declined below expected ranges. Authors conclude early bio-efficacy loss, likely due to manufacturing, storage, or net-care issues, requires frequent replacement and user education.

articles.headline: Secondary Anopheles species drive outdoor malaria transmission despite vector control
articles.title_original: Malaria Outbreak in Farafangana District, Southeast Madagascar, 2018: Are Secondary Vectors a Threat to Current Malaria Control Approaches?
articles.authors: Nepomichene T, Harimanana A, Randrianaivo F, Randrenjarison R, Raherinjafy R, Rakotondramanga JM, Zohdy S, Razakamiadana S, Kapesa L, Andriamananjara MN, Baril L, Randremanana R, Randrianasolo L, Dentinger CM, Girod R
articles.details: Entomological and epidemiological investigation during 2018 malaria outbreak in Farafangana District, Madagascar. Of 1,488 Anopheles collected, 25 (1.7%) were P. falciparum-positive; only 1 collected indoors, 24 outdoors (13 [54.2%] were An. coustani). All predominant species exhibited high outdoor biting (exophagy): An. coustani 100%, An. gambiae 59.8%, An. funestus <47%. Of 226 individuals tested by RDT, 71 (31.4%) positive; 61 (85.9%) asymptomatic, mostly children. Authors conclude secondary vectors and exophagy sustain transmission despite indoor-targeted control.

articles.headline: RT-PCR reveals asymptomatic malaria in 6% of sub-Saharan migrants
articles.title_original: Burden of asymptomatic malaria in adult sub-Saharan migrants attending an outpatient clinic in Rome from February 2024 to January 2025.
articles.authors: Faraglia F, Vita S, Kontogiannis D, Bartoli TA, Rosati S, Bevilacqua N, Maffongelli G, Corpolongo A, Bartolini B, Vulcano A, D'Abramo A, Fontana C, Nicastri E
articles.details: Cross-sectional screening of 87 asymptomatic sub-Saharan African migrants in Rome (Feb 2024-Jan 2025) using RDTs, LAMP, microscopy, and RT-PCR. Malaria prevalence 6% (5/87), all in males. LAMP detected Plasmodium spp. in 5 cases (6.0%), confirmed by RT-PCR, while RDT identified only 2 (2.3%). Species included P. falciparum, P. malariae, P. ovale, and mixed infection. Longest time since arrival was 181 days; median interval since departure exceeded 600 days. Authors conclude asymptomatic malaria persists months after arrival, often missed by RDTs; RT-PCR improves detection.

articles.headline: Hierarchical var gene switching balances immune evasion with survival
articles.title_original: Hierarchical switching pattern in antigenic variation provides survival advantage for malaria parasites under variable host immunity.
articles.authors: Iragavarapu GP, Varsha HJ, Vembar SS, Ghosh B
articles.details: Theoretical and experimental study of P. falciparum var gene expression using Markov chain modeling, single-cell RNA-seq, RT-qPCR, and RNA-seq. The ~60-member var family encodes PfEMP1 variant surface antigens; parasites exhibit hierarchical switching to evade immunity. Analysis shows parasites stably express variants cleared more efficiently by host immune response, while quickly downregulating slowly-cleared variants. Stable variants exhibited greater IgM binding affinities. Authors conclude hierarchical switching optimizes parasite survival under variable host immune responses.

articles.headline: Novel PI4K inhibitors show improved pharmacokinetics and malaria efficacy
articles.title_original: Optimization of 2,8-Diaryl-1,5-naphthyridines as Plasmodium falciparum Phosphatidylinositol 4-Kinase Inhibitors with Improved ADME Profiles and In Vivo Efficacy.
articles.authors: Dziwornu GA, Seanego D, Fienberg S, Sypu VS, Salomane N, Krugmann L, Taylor D, Masike K, Njoroge M, Boonyalai N, Lee MCS, Godoy LC, Pasaje CF, Niles JC, Basarab GS, Coulson LB, Ghorpade SR, Chibale K
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship study optimizing 2,8-diaryl-1,5-naphthyridine inhibitors of P. falciparum phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase (PI4K). Compound 27 demonstrated improved pharmacokinetic profile and reduced parasitemia (91% at 4×50 mg/kg daily doses) in humanized NOD-scid IL-2Rγnull mouse model. No hERG channel inhibition or mammalian cytotoxicity; low selectivity against human PI3K and PI4K, but higher selectivity against MINK1 and MAP4K4 kinases. Authors conclude Compound 27 warrants further development as antimalarial candidate.

articles.headline: Novel amodiachins overcome drug resistance with nanomolar potency
articles.title_original: Discovery of Amodiachins, a Novel Class of 4-Aminoquinoline Antimalarials Active against Multidrug-Resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Handford MJ, Li Y, Riscoe T, Liebman KM, Zhang X, Kelly JX, Riscoe MK
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship study optimizing amodiachins (ADCs), 4-aminoquinoline analogs, against drug-sensitive and multidrug-resistant P. falciparum in murine models. Compound 43 (ADC-028) exhibited nanomolar activity against both drug-sensitive and resistant strains, minimal cytotoxicity, and favorable microsomal stability (t1/2=48.2 min). In vivo: nonrecrudescence dose at 16 mg/kg/day, single-dose cure at 50 mg/kg. Pharmacokinetics showed excellent metabolic stability (T1/2=84 h) and oral bioavailability (F=76%). Authors identify Compound 43 as candidate against drug-resistant malaria.

articles.headline: Novel marinoquinoline protease inhibitor shows antimalarial activity in mice
articles.title_original: Fluorescent Marinoquinoline Derivative as Inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum: SAR Analysis, Mode of Action and In Vivo Studies.
articles.authors: Santos Barbosa P, Souza GE, Maluf SEC, Bonatto V, Moura CS, Mendes GR, Valdes TA, Annunciato Y, Rossetto BDS, Ventura PDS, Ortin GGD, da Silva W, Icimoto MY, Ferreira ADS, Cruz FC, Teles CBG, Pereira DB, Cassiano GC, Santana S, Prudêncio M, Barbosa CS, Moura IMR, Giampauli RM, De Sousa IL, Rocco SA, Gazarini ML, Correia CRD, Aguiar ACC, Guido RVC
articles.details: Researchers developed 20 fluorescent marinoquinoline analogues and characterized compound 19 with favorable in vitro (IC50 vs 3D7 = 0.28 μM; EC50 vs P. falciparum = 1.2 μM; EC50 vs P. vivax = 0.53 μM) and in vivo properties. In P. berghei-infected mice, 3 × 50 mg/kg oral doses resulted in 96% reduction in parasitemia. The compound selectively accumulates in infected erythrocytes and inhibits P. falciparum proteolytic activity, suggesting marinoquinolines are protease inhibitors with potential for antimalarial drug discovery.

articles.headline: PfAP2-06B transcription factor controls P. falciparum erythrocyte invasion
articles.title_original: An ApiAP2 Family Transcriptional Factor PfAP2-06B Regulates Erythrocyte Invasion Indirectly in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Shi Q, Wan K, Gong Y, Pang J, Liu Y, Tang J, Zhang Q, Cao J, Shen L
articles.details: Using conditional knockdown and bulk/single-cell RNA-seq, researchers found that the ApiAP2 family transcription factor PfAP2-06B (PF3D7_0613800) is critical for asexual growth and erythrocyte invasion in P. falciparum. PfAP2-06B modulates invasion-related genes during the schizont stage and influences stochastic cell-to-cell gene expression variation. These findings suggest PfAP2-06B as a potential target for novel malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: PKCθ inhibition reduces CD8+ T cell-mediated brain injury in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Inhibition of PKCθ Abrogates CD8(+) T Cell-Mediated Neurotoxicity in Murine Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Albrecht-Schgör K, Stefan VE, Steinlechner M, Humer D, Siegmund K, Peer S, Gruber T, Überegger M, Zur Nedden S, Baier-Bitterlich G, Lackner P, Schmutzhard E, Thuille N, Klepsch V, Baier G
articles.details: In PKCθ-deficient mice infected with P. berghei ANKA, PKCθ deficiency significantly improved survival without altering blood or brain parasite burdens. Loss of PKCθ shifted CD8+ T cell differentiation toward central memory (Tcm) rather than effector memory (Tem) phenotypes, reducing CD8+ T cell sequestration in the cerebral microvasculature. This prevented neurovascular damage, preserved neural tissue integrity, and alleviated neurological signs, suggesting PKCθ inhibitors may protect against cerebral malaria injury.

articles.headline: Global review of intracellular parasitic infections and vaccine advances
articles.title_original: Intracellular Parasitic Infections Caused by Plasmodium falciparum, Leishmania spp., Toxoplasma gondii, Echinococcus multilocularis, Among Key Pathogens: Global Burden, Transmission Dynamics, and Vaccine Advances-A Narrative Review with Contextual Insights from Armenia.
articles.authors: Sargsyan T, Stepanyan L, Tsaturyan A, Palumbo R, Vicidomini C, Roviello GN
articles.details: This narrative review examines intracellular parasitic diseases including P. falciparum, Leishmania spp., Toxoplasma gondii, and Echinococcus multilocularis, covering parasite classification, immune evasion mechanisms, diagnostic challenges, and control strategies. Recent advancements in experimental and licensed vaccine development targeting intracellular pathogens are highlighted. The review emphasizes a One Health approach integrating human, animal, and environmental health efforts, with Armenia serving as a case study of how local ecological conditions, vector distribution, and socioeconomic factors shape national disease burden.

articles.headline: Methods for inducing gametocytogenesis in Plasmodium parasites reviewed
articles.title_original: Induction of gametocytogenesis in human malaria parasites: from stress to genome editing.
articles.authors: Subramaniam R, Chiew ZY, Zuhaidi NDB, Lau YL, Cheong FW
articles.details: This mini-review summarizes gametocytogenesis induction methods in human Plasmodium species, progressing from conventional environmental stressors to genome-editing approaches for precise modifications of sexual conversion genes. Understanding and controlling gametocytogenesis, the transition from asexual to sexual stages, offers insights into parasite transmission to Anopheles vectors and potential transmission control strategies. While significant advances have been made, scalability remains challenging, especially for non-falciparum species, highlighting the need to integrate knowledge from both conventional and molecular approaches.

articles.headline: Metabolic pathways in mosquitoes crucial for avian Plasmodium transmission
articles.title_original: Avian malaria and the overlooked metabolic pathways underlying mosquito-Plasmodium interactions.
articles.authors: Garcia-Longoria L, Berthomieu A, Hellgren O, Rivero A
articles.details: This review examines the role of mosquito metabolic pathways in avian Plasmodium development and transmission using transcriptomic analysis of infected and uninfected mosquitoes at various infection stages. Differentially expressed pathways involved in the parasite's energetic needs, digestive enzymes facilitating midgut barrier traversal, and salivary enzymes enabling blood meal uptake are identified. Findings suggest parasites have evolved mechanisms to modulate these metabolic pathways to enhance infection and transmission, highlighting potential novel targets for transmission control.

articles.headline: Adequate bed-net coverage associated with lower malaria infection in Kenya
articles.title_original: Long-lasting insecticidal net ownership and malaria infection by socio-economic status: a cross-sectional household study in an area along Lake Victoria, Kenya.
articles.authors: Iwashita H, Nagi S, Bahati F, Kagaya W, Larson PS, Kongere J, Kanoi BN, Hayasaka R, Sugishita T, Gitaka J, Kaneko A
articles.details: In a cross-sectional household survey in Homa Bay County, Western Kenya (June-September 2021), researchers assessed associations between LLIN ownership and Plasmodium PCR positivity. Sufficient LLIN ownership (≤2 people per net) was significantly associated with lower household infection in middle SES households (OR 0.32, 95% CI 0.12-0.92) and in combined low/middle SES groups (OR 0.37, 95% CI 0.17-0.87). Results support WHO recommendations for one LLIN per two people and suggest sustained net distribution programs reduce malaria risk.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT cassettes remain stable over one month for data verification
articles.title_original: Are malaria rapid diagnostic test results stable over time to support verification of surveillance data?
articles.authors: Ngufor C, Lindblade KA, Atobatele S, Mpimbaza A, Ahogni I, Ssewante N, Akpiroroh E, Kpemasse A, Okoro O, Agaba B, Cooper S, Griffith K, Humes M
articles.details: A prospective observational study in 48 health facilities across Benin, Nigeria, and Uganda (June-September 2023) evaluated RDT result stability over one month using an AI-powered reader (HealthPulse). Among 45,155 RDTs with valid interpretations at all three time points, 95.1% of initially positive RDTs remained positive (PPV 96.3%, 95% CI 96.1-96.5) and 95.3% of initially negative RDTs remained negative (NPV 93.8%, 95% CI 93.4-94.1). Most result changes occurred within the first week; faint lines were associated with higher change rates, supporting use of stored cassettes for surveillance data verification.

articles.headline: Phosphoinositide-binding protein regulates Plasmodium invasion organelle trafficking
articles.title_original: Characterization of a phosphoinositide-binding protein containing a PHOX homology domain in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Roucheray S, Stastny D, Kaur H, Tahotna D, Dacks JB, Griac P, Richard D
articles.details: Researchers characterized PfPX2, a phosphoinositide (PIP)-binding protein in P. falciparum containing a PX domain. Lipid binding assays demonstrated PfPX2 binds all PIP species with preference for PI3P, PI5P, and PI(3,5)P2. Immunofluorescence localized PfPX2 to the Golgi apparatus and micronemes in schizonts, with proximity labeling identifying PfSortilin, clathrin heavy chain, and PfDyn1 as potential interactors, suggesting PfPX2's role in vesicular trafficking between Golgi and micronemes during invasion organelle biogenesis.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing rapidly distinguishes P. falciparum recrudescence from new infection
articles.title_original: Rapid multiplexed nanopore amplicon sequencing to distinguish Plasmodium falciparum recrudescence from new infection in antimalarial drug trials.
articles.authors: Holzschuh A, Lerch A, Nsanzabana C
articles.details: Researchers optimized a multiplexed nanopore AmpSeq panel targeting six microhaplotypes to genotype P. falciparum parasites in antimalarial drug trials. The assay demonstrated high sensitivity (detected minority clones at 1:100:100:100 ratio), high specificity (false-positives < 0.01%), and robust reproducibility (intra/inter-assay 98%/97%). In 20 paired patient samples from a clinical trial, the assay distinguished recrudescence from new infections in 17/20 cases (85%). Microhaplotype markers showed high genetic diversity (up to 28 unique haplotypes for cpmp), providing rapid corrected drug efficacy estimates in high-transmission settings.

articles.headline: Correction to ESCRT-III machinery in P. falciparum vesicle production
articles.title_original: Correction: The ESCRT-III machinery participates in the production of extracellular vesicles and protein export during Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.authors: Avalos-Padilla Y, Georgiev VN, Lantero E, Pujals S, Verhoef R, Borgheti-Cardoso LN, Albertazzi L, Dimova R, Fernàndez-Busquets X
articles.details: Correction notice to a previous article on the role of ESCRT-III machinery in extracellular vesicle production and protein export during P. falciparum infection.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemic in Zanzibar driven by temperature and net gaps
articles.title_original: The November-2023-March-2024 malaria epidemic in Zanzibar: a spatiotemporal epidemiological analysis.
articles.authors: Kooiman F, Ali MH, Alifrangis M, Shija SJ, Hassan WS, Schiøler KL, Saleh F
articles.details: A spatiotemporal epidemiological analysis of 23,569 confirmed malaria cases during a 21-week epidemic (November 2023-March 2024) in Zanzibar identified risk factors and transmission patterns. Males, individuals aged 15-35, urban residents, and those not sleeping under insecticide-treated nets had higher malaria risk. One significant space-time cluster was identified in urban southwest Unguja, and weekly malaria incidence was significantly associated with average weekly temperature with an 8-week lag time, indicating climate-driven transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: PfSR10: drug target for antimalarials and bradykinin
articles.title_original: Identification of antimalarial drugs and bradykinin as ligands of the Plasmodium membrane protein PfSR10.
articles.authors: Jiang D, Wen X, Han JF, Cheng QX, Zhang R, Zheng Y, Zheng K, Huang SH, Chen JY, Li SW, Yang ZS, Han B, Tie L, Yang F, Zhang PJ, Xiao P, Lin H, Yu X, Sun JP
articles.details: Biochemical and cryo-EM studies show that chloroquine, dihydroartemisinin, piperaquine, and primaquine act as agonists of Plasmodium membrane protein PfSR10, inducing coupling with human Gi/Gq proteins. Proteomic profiling identified bradykinin as an endogenous agonist. Results establish PfSR10 as a dual receptor for antimalarial drugs and host peptides, with implications for understanding drug action and malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Cellular aging as pathophysiological mechanism in malaria
articles.title_original: Critical Understanding of the Influence of Cellular Aging Biomarkers on Host-Parasite Relationships Serving as a Key Platform for Malaria Eradication.
articles.authors: Anzaku DO, Afolabi IS
articles.details: A comprehensive review examining how Plasmodium infection and host immune responses influence cellular aging biomarkers. The review synthesizes the interplay between parasite evasion strategies, immune activation, and cellular senescence processes in malaria pathophysiology, proposing cellular aging as a key mechanism contributing to both acute and chronic malarial complications.

articles.headline: DDT and pyrethroid residues in Brazilian Amazon
articles.title_original: Potential Risks to Human Health Caused by the Use of Pesticides in Soils of Three Municipalities Impacted by Localized Malaria in the Brazilian Amazon.
articles.authors: Santos LFD, Deus RJA, Sucupira IMC, Brasil DDSB, Mendes RA
articles.details: Gas chromatography analysis of soils from three Amazonian municipalities (Mazagão, Porto Velho, Cantá) detected DDT metabolites and alphacypermethrin residues, with average total DDT of 2.694 mg/kg in Cantá and alphacypermethrin 0.364 mg/kg in Porto Velho. Incremental lifetime cancer risk was up to 2.93 × 10⁻³ for DDT and 1.17 × 10⁻¹ for alphacypermethrin; non-carcinogenic risk index reached 336.61, indicating significant human health hazards through food intake.

articles.headline: Pure red cell aplasia in P. falciparum-parvovirus coinfection
articles.title_original: Transient Pure Red Cell Aplasia in a Case of Plasmodium falciparum and Parvovirus B19 Coinfection.
articles.authors: John CV, Basu A, Bose D, Ray Y
articles.details: Case report of a 22-year-old with complicated P. falciparum malaria treated with intravenous artesunate followed by artemether-lumefantrine. Despite parasitemia clearance, he developed persistent hypoproliferative anemia with bone-marrow giant pronormoblasts; parvovirus B19 quantitative PCR showed 2.1 × 10³ IU/mL. He recovered completely after intravenous immunoglobulin and prednisone, highlighting B19V coinfection as a cause of persistent malaria-associated anemia.

articles.headline: Long-amplicon PCR for surveillance of artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Multiplex long-amplicon sequencing for comprehensive molecular surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum resistance to artemisinin and partner drugs in artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs).
articles.authors: Li F, Zhang J, Xu S, Wang Y, Mao Z, Zhu G, Liu Y, Cao J
articles.details: A multiplex long-amplicon sequencing panel covers full-length coding regions of Pfk13, Pfcoronin, and Pfap2μ, plus Pfubp1, Pfmdr1, and Pfcrt. Analytical validation on mock samples (parasitemia 1%–0.0001%) and field blood samples achieved 100% target coverage with analytical sensitivity at 50 parasites/μL for dried blood spots and 5 parasites/μL for venous blood. The assay cost is $15.60 per sample, enabling scalable drug-resistance surveillance in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Financial incentives and education to prevent malaria
articles.title_original: The second-round evaluation of financial incentives on malaria prevalence in the Lake Victoria Basin, Kenya: updated study protocol for a cluster-randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Matsumoto T, Nagashima M, Kagaya W, Okomo G, Kongere J, Oginga J, Opiyo V, Gitaka J, Kaneko A
articles.details: Second-round cluster-randomized trial in Kenya (Suba South Sub-county, Homa Bay County) re-randomizing 92 clusters to conditional cash transfer, lottery incentive scheme, or control arms with updated malaria education and incentives tied to negative test results. Primary outcomes are changes in malaria prevalence (assessed via RDT, microscopy, and PCR at 3 and 6 months), long-lasting insecticidal net usage, and knowledge/perception of malaria.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemiology and control measures in Pakistan
articles.title_original: Fighting malaria in Shangla District, Pakistan: insight into epidemiology, risk factors, and strategic control measures (2020-2024).
articles.authors: Ullah I, Baig RM, Riaz A, Mukhtar M, Khan MA, Zaib A, Muhammad N, Khan S, Ali A, Ahmad A, Del Angel Nunez RM
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 130,401 laboratory-confirmed malaria cases from 572,696 febrile individuals screened in Shangla District (2020–2024). Annual parasite incidence rose sharply from 2 per 1,000 in 2020 to 61 per 1,000 in 2023, then declined to 46 per 1,000 in 2024 following interventions. Plasmodium vivax comprised 95% of cases, P. falciparum 4.6%; only 14% of patients completed primaquine regimens, and only 18% of cases were microscopy-confirmed.

articles.headline: Malaria knowledge and prevention practices in caregivers
articles.title_original: Malaria knowledge and preventive practices among caregivers of under-five children in Southwest Nigeria.
articles.authors: Adeleke TO, Akinlosotu MA, Samson TK, Adeleke OV, Ajala DE, Olasinde Y, Oluwasanya AN, Esan DT
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 797 caregivers of children under five in Southwest Nigeria found that 86.4% (689) demonstrated good malaria knowledge. Good knowledge was significantly associated with better malaria prevention practices (odds ratio 2.347, 95% confidence interval 1.251–4.406, p=0.008), suggesting that targeted caregiver education can improve childhood malaria prevention.

articles.headline: Harmala alkaloids inhibit P. falciparum protein kinase
articles.title_original: Computational insights into harmala alkaloids as novel antimalarial candidates against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Dipto KZ, Shariar R, Saha CK, Huzaifa A, Robin TB, Patil RB, Alam MT, Rafi MIH, Faruk MAZ, Moin AT, Zinnah KMA, Hasanuzzaman M, Islam T
articles.details: In silico evaluation of harmala alkaloids (harmine and harmaline) from plants and endophytic microbes as antimalarial candidates. Molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, ADME analysis, and toxicity screening show that these alkaloids inhibit P. falciparum protein kinase 4 (PK4), an essential enzyme for parasite survival, with lower toxicity and activity against blood-stage growth and parasite transmission.

articles.headline: Deep learning automates malaria detection from blood-smears
articles.title_original: DANet a lightweight dilated attention network for malaria parasite detection.
articles.authors: Ali A, Pal R, Dey I, Cuevas E, Perez-Cisneros M, Sarkar R
articles.details: DANet, a lightweight dilated attention network with 2.3 million parameters, detects malaria parasites in red blood-cell smear images. On the NIH Malaria Dataset (27,558 images: 19,290 training, 2,756 validation, 5,512 test; 150 infected and 50 healthy individuals), DANet achieved F1-score of 97.86%, accuracy of 97.95%, and area under the curve–precision recall of 0.98. The model is deployable on edge devices such as Raspberry Pi 4.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine uptake among children in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Caregivers' practices and factors associated with malaria vaccine uptake among under-five children in the Tiko Health District, Cameroon: A community based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Abiache IM, Nsobinenyui D, Ukah CE, Kumenyuy YL, Ngeha NC, Wefuan R, Dang SZ, Ndip NE, Feguem MP, Nsagha DS
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study of 410 caregivers in Tiko Health District (March–April 2025) found that only 32.2% of children under five received the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine, with 72% completing the recommended doses. Factors significantly increasing uptake included female caregiver status (adjusted odds ratio 4.16), health profession employment (aOR 2.87), household income 89–179 USD (aOR 2.76), and trust in health workers (aOR 6.12). Prior malaria infection in the child decreased uptake (aOR 0.31).

articles.headline: Ceiling nets markedly reduce indoor Anopheles in Peru
articles.title_original: Efficacy of ceiling-mounted mosquito nets for malaria vector control in a Peruvian Amazon riverine community: A stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial.
articles.authors: Quispe AM, Álvarez-Antonio C, Gutierrez Rodriguez F, Carey C, Rodríguez-Ferrucci H, Inti C, Kim ES
articles.details: Stepped-wedge cluster-randomized trial in 69 Peruvian Amazon households (Llanchama, San Juan Bautista district, Loreto; March–December 2024) evaluated ceiling-mounted mosquito nets as vector control. Renovated households showed a 55% reduction in indoor Anopheles counts (95% CI 33%–74%, p=0.004), a 60% decrease in indoor bites per person per night (95% CI 27%–78%, p=0.003), and a 61% reduction in indoor bites per person per hour (95% CI 15%–83%, p=0.018). Peridomestic biting rates remained unchanged.

articles.headline: Tfh2 and Tfh1-CCR7+ cells drive antibody immunity to malaria
articles.title_original: Tfh2 and a subset of Tfh1 cells associate with antibody-mediated immunity to malaria.
articles.authors: Soon MS, Oyong DA, Dooley NL, Mukhiya R, Pava Z, Andrew DW, Loughland JR, McCarthy JS, Chan JA, Beeson JG, Engwerda CR, Haque A, Boyle MJ
articles.details: Controlled human malaria infection study using single-cell RNA-seq and VDJ-seq to map peripheral blood T follicular helper cell heterogeneity. During CHMI, Tfh1-CCR7+, Tfh1-CCR7−, and Tfh2 cells all clonally expanded and became activated. Only Tfh1-CCR7+ and Tfh2 cells positively associated with protective antibody production, revealing distinct roles of specific Tfh subsets in antibody-mediated immunity to malaria.

articles.headline: Nanozyme assay detects transmission-competent Plasmodium gametocytes
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Malaria Parasite Transmission Competency Using a Nanozyme-Based Immunodiagnostic Targeting Female Gamete Antigen Release.
articles.authors: Haghighi T, Najer A, Broto M, Dahalan FA, Churchyard A, Yahiya S, Famodimu MT, Tunnicliff M, Abdelwahed A, Tachibana M, Ishino T, Aniweh Y, Awandare GA, Mahamar A, Vanheer LN, Bousema T, Drakeley C, Dicko A, Stone W, Baum J, Stevens MM
articles.details: Novel nanozyme-based immunoassay targeting female gametocyte activation antigen PfG377 identifies transmission-competent P. falciparum. In cultured parasites, PfG377 levels were higher in activated versus nonactivated cultures and in transmission-blocking drug-treated cultures. Patient samples receiving primaquine showed significantly lower PfG377 signals 2 days after treatment, consistent with gametocyte sterilization and transmission blocking.

articles.headline: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine superior to sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine for IPTp
articles.title_original: Recurrent Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia and drug resistance mutations during intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Uganda.
articles.authors: Kizza J, Katairo T, Kakuru A, Nsengimaana B, Esilu T, Wiringilimaana I, Semakuba FD, Gerlovina I, Hathaway N, Briggs J, Tukwasibwe S, Kiwuwa SM, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI, Dorsey G, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: Trial in Busia, Uganda comparing SP, DP, and DP+SP for intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy among 1377 pregnant women with asymptomatic parasitemia. Cumulative 28-day recurrent parasitemia risk: SP 57.8%, DP 4.1%, DP+SP 3.9%; recrudescent parasitemia: SP 40.1%, DP 2.0%, DP+SP 0.8%. High-level resistance mutations (PfDHFR I164L/PfDHPS A581G) increased from 24.9% to 35.2% in SP arm, indicating DP's superior efficacy and SP's selection for resistance.

articles.headline: Correction to article on inflammatory cytokines in malaria-associated kidney injury
articles.title_original: Correction: Simião et al. Involvement of Inflammatory Cytokines, Renal NaPi-IIa Cotransporter, and TRAIL Induced-Apoptosis in Experimental Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury. Pathogens 2024, 13, 376.
articles.authors: Simião GM, Parreira KS, Klein SG, Ferreira FB, Freitas FS, Silva EFD, Silva NM, Silva MVD, Lima WR
articles.details: Correction notice to prior publication on inflammatory cytokines, renal NaPi-IIa cotransporter, and TRAIL-induced apoptosis in experimental malaria-associated acute kidney injury.

articles.headline: Zanzibaris lack awareness of acquired malaria immunity and rebound risk
articles.title_original: Reconsidering rebound malaria: Zanzibari voices on acquired immunity, risk, and history.
articles.authors: Graboyes M, Lefevre D, Meta J, Minu-Sepehr A
articles.details: Qualitative study using 98 interviews with Zanzibar residents examining understanding of acquired immunity and rebound malaria risk. Minimal familiarity with these concepts existed despite 70 years of international malaria interventions. Brief educational intervention with visual tool improved understanding, indicating community receptiveness to information and highlighting ethical need for improved disclosure of risks in malaria interventions.

articles.headline: Simple strategy reduces malaria infection rates in babies
articles.title_original: A simple strategy to cut malaria infection rates in babies.
articles.authors: O'Leary K
articles.details: Title only; no abstract provided.

articles.headline: Machine learning and pharmacometrics optimize malaria treatment for African populations
articles.title_original: Artificial intelligence coupled to pharmacometrics modelling to tailor malaria and tuberculosis treatment in Africa.
articles.authors: Turon G, Mulubwa M, Montaner A, Njoroge M, Chibale K, Duran-Frigola M
articles.details: Study coupling machine learning, physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK), and non-linear mixed effects (NLME) modeling to identify drug-gene pairs and dose adjustments for malaria and TB therapies in African populations. Pipeline uses knowledge embedding techniques and large language models for drug-gene ranking, followed by PBPK sensitivity analysis and NLME-based dose optimization, addressing scarcity of pharmacogenetic data and drug-development efforts in Africa.

articles.headline: 64% of Northern Ugandans accept gene-drive-modified mosquitoes
articles.title_original: "Have Scientists Done Enough?" Community Perspectives on Gene Drive-Modified Mosquitoes for Malaria Control in Northern Uganda: A Mixed-Methods Study.
articles.authors: Madraa G, Musoke P, Nantale R, Bongomin F
articles.details: Mixed-methods study in Northern Uganda assessing acceptance of gene-drive-modified mosquitoes (GDMMs) among 301 participants (median age 35 years, 56.1% male) plus 22 in-depth interviews. Only 60 (19.9%) were aware of GDMMs; 193 (64.1%, 95% CI: 58.4–69.5) reported acceptance. Independent predictors: favourable attitude (aPR: 1.25, 95% CI: 1.05–1.49), high subjective norm (aPR: 1.28, 95% CI: 1.08–1.51), high perceived behavioural control (aPR: 1.23, 95% CI: 1.05–1.46).

articles.headline: Deep learning improves protein annotation across Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: PlasmoFP: leveraging deep learning to predict protein function of uncharacterized proteins across the malaria parasite genus.
articles.authors: Srivastava HR, Berenberg D, Qassab O, Wang Z, Bonneau R, Carlton JM
articles.details: PlasmoFP deep learning models trained on structure-function relationships of SAR supergroup proteins predict function of uncharacterized Plasmodium proteins. Model integration with current annotations reduced unannotated proteins lacking Gene Ontology terms from 15–59% to 3–28% across 19 Plasmodium species and improved fully annotated proteins from 7–42% to 36–68%, advancing Plasmodium basic research.

articles.headline: Avian malaria Plasmodium lineages in wild British birds
articles.title_original: The lineage diversity, spatiotemporal distribution and pathological significance of Plasmodium and Haemoproteus spp. infection of wild birds in Great Britain.
articles.authors: Heaver JP, John SK, Seilern-Macpherson K, Spiro S, Wilkinson V, Cunningham AA, Lawson B
articles.details: Surveillance of 857 wild birds (62 species) in Great Britain over 15 years found 13.5% (n=116) PCR-positive for avian haemosporidian parasites: 8.9% (n=76) Plasmodium spp., 4.7% (n=40) Haemoproteus spp. Highest Plasmodium infection in Paridae (36.3%) and Turdidae (34.5%). Thirty AHP lineages detected (20 Haemoproteus, 10 Plasmodium); 23 novel to Britain, 4 apparently new. Histopathology of 13 Plasmodium-positive birds showed exoerythrocytic parasites or lesions consistent with avian malaria in 9 cases.

articles.headline: Age-structured malaria model predicts vaccination can drive transmission to zero
articles.title_original: Age-structured vaccination strategies in mathematical modeling of malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Haringo AT, Obsu LL, Bushu FK
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Mathematical model of age-structured malaria vaccination capturing human-mosquito interactions. Malaria-free equilibrium is stable when reproduction number Re < 1; endemic equilibrium stable when Re > 1. Sensitivity analysis identified key parameters influencing transmission dynamics. Numerical simulations demonstrate that with effective vaccination, treatment, and vector control, malaria transmission could be reduced to nearly zero, potentially achieving disease eradication.

articles.headline: 35.6% of P. falciparum in Ethiopia lack HRP2/3 genes for diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: High Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum HRP2/3 Gene Deletions in Ethiopia: Implications for Malaria Diagnosis and Treatment-A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.authors: Gebrie H, Abere A, Gashaw A, Gebremeskele BT, Bukayaw YA, Bayable A, Mekuriaw BY, Yimer EG, Demeke AD
articles.journal: Can J Infect Dis Med Microbiol
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of PfHRP2/3 gene deletions in Ethiopia (2000–2025) from 11 studies. Overall pooled prevalence of PfHRP2/3 gene deletions: 35.64% (95% CI: 21.43–49.85); PfHRP2 deletion: 8.48% (95% CI: 0.96–16.01); PfHRP3 deletion: 23.74% (95% CI: 12.16–35.32); concurrent deletions: 8.14% (95% CI: 0.67–15.61). High prevalence significantly compromises rapid diagnostic test (RDT) reliability and necessitates phasing out HRP2/3-based RDTs from national malaria diagnostic algorithms.

articles.headline: Platelet distribution width predicts malaria in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Predicting malaria in sudanese pregnant women: reliability of complete blood count.
articles.authors: Dura MC, Gürsoy B, Aktürk H, Aslan Ö, Hergüner M, Ezirmik E
articles.details: Retrospective study of 174 pregnant women (78 with confirmed malaria, 96 controls) comparing hematological parameters. Body mass index, hemoglobin, platelet count, and platelet distribution width (PDW) were significantly lower in the malaria group (p<0.05). In multivariate analysis, only PDW remained significant, with area under the curve of 0.857, suggesting it may serve as a reliable biomarker for malaria diagnosis in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Intermittent larviciding shows limited impact on malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Large-scale intermittent larviciding intervention and associations with key malaria epidemiological parameters in Tanga Region, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Kailembo D, Gavana T, Kasagama E, Bernard J, Molteni F, Kisoka N, Yoram B, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Dismas C, Ross A, Chaki P, Lengeler C
articles.details: Large-scale pilot study of intermittent larviciding (Bacillus thuringiensis and sphaericus) in Tanga Region, Tanzania, covering over 1.1 million people from June 2022 to April 2024. Three rounds per year of 8-week biolarvicide application were evaluated using before-after design. No significant association was found between larviciding and clinical malaria incidence (incidence rate ratio 1.16, 0.86, 0.96 for high, moderate, low risk strata respectively).

articles.headline: Ivermectin efficacy depends on diet and meal type in Anopheles
articles.title_original: Optimizing endectocide and ectocide compound evaluation in Anopheles malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Kobylinski KC, Hongsuwong T, Khemrattrakool P, Kaewkhao N, Kullasakboonsri R, Phanphoowong T, Sriwichai P, Hanboonkunupakarn B, Jittamala P, Tarning J
articles.details: Laboratory study optimizing ivermectin evaluation in Anopheles dirus and A. minimus through three experiments. Different sugar diets affected mosquito mortality: multivitamin diet substantially increased LC50 compared to sucrose diet. Ivermectin efficacy varied by meal type (blood vs. plasma, fresh vs. frozen), with approximately 20% loss of ivermectin concentration in the mosquito midgut compared to the blood meal.

articles.headline: Integrated vector and parasite management strategy for malaria control
articles.title_original: Integrated vector and parasite management for malaria control in high-endemic areas: pummel and pin strategy to prevent malaria setbacks.
articles.authors: Ranjbar M, Opigo J
articles.details: Opinion article proposing Integrated Vector and Parasite Management (IVPM) with a 'Pummel and Pin' strategy to address malaria control failures in high-endemic sub-Saharan Africa. The strategy emphasizes simultaneously reducing both parasite reservoir and vectorial capacity to sustain endemicity reductions. The authors note that IVPM is resource-intensive and call for operational research to assess feasibility and cost-effectiveness.

articles.headline: Physicians in Nigeria lack knowledge about malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: Physicians' knowledge of, and willingness to encourage caregivers to partake in the RTS S/AS01 and R21 matrix M malaria vaccines roll out - a national survey in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oboh MA, Adeleke OT, Adeleke OV, Awotunde TA
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of Nigerian physicians across six geopolitical zones assessing knowledge of RTS,S/AS01 and R21 Matrix M malaria vaccines. Only 68.9% of respondents knew about malaria vaccines while 29.9% did not. Though 64% correctly identified five years as the appropriate administration age, most physicians were very willing to recommend vaccines to caregivers. The study concludes that educational interventions are needed to address knowledge gaps.

articles.headline: Fractional-order models reveal memory effects in malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Analysis of fractional-order model for the transmission dynamics of malaria via Caputo-Fabrizio and Atangana-Baleanu operators.
articles.authors: Agbata BC, Dervishi R, Gümüş M, Smerat A, Mbah GCE
articles.details: Theoretical study developing fractional-order mathematical models for malaria transmission using three types of non-integer differential operators (Caputo, Caputo-Fabrizio with exponential decay, Atangana-Baleanu with Mittag-Leffler kernel). Numerical simulations demonstrated that increasing fractional order slows malaria spread, reduces peak infection numbers, and prolongs outbreak duration. Authors recommend integrating fractional-order models into malaria control strategies to better capture long-term disease dynamics.

articles.headline: Imported malaria dominates Saudi Arabia cases, driven by P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Malaria in travelers and local populations: Comprehensive study of incidence patterns and origin-based classification in Saudi Arabia.
articles.authors: Alharbi B, Ahmed M
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 1,453,451 febrile cases tested for malaria across 13 Saudi regions from 2022-2023.
