Overall positivity was 0.7% (10,779 cases), with significant regional variation: Jazan 59%, Makkah 20% (p=0.046). Positivity declined from 0.9% in 2022 to 0.6% in 2023. P. falciparum dominated at 63.5% of infections, followed by P. vivax-P. ovale (33%), with statistically significant species variation (p=0.002).

articles.headline: Hybrid machine learning models improve malaria incidence forecasting accuracy
articles.title_original: Hybrid models combining trend and seasonality components with machine learning algorithms provide accurate forecasting of malaria incidence.
articles.authors: Hussain SSA, Bedi S, Yadav CP, Mohanty AK, Mahatme K, Tyagi S, Krishnan NMA, Kota SH, Sharma A
articles.details: Forecasting study comparing nine models (three machine-learning: SVM, RF, XGB; three time-series: ARIMA, SARIMA, SARIMAX; three hybrid: RF-ARMA, SVM-ARMA, XGB-ARMA) using climate and malaria surveillance data from Goa, India (2010-2019). Machine-learning models alone showed lower accuracy (RMSE 13-37), while time-series models achieved higher accuracy (RMSE 5-41). Hybrid models achieved substantially improved accuracy (RMSE 0.5-15) while maintaining precision.

articles.headline: Vinyl-DNADetect probes enable live-cell DNA imaging in Plasmodium parasites
articles.title_original: Vinyl-DNADetect Probes to Assess DNA Proliferation in Plasmodium Parasites using Inverse-Electron-Demand Diels-Alder Click Reactions.
articles.authors: Braun AC, Fisher GM, Hilko DH, Macdonald JR, Thomas S, Andrews KT, Poulsen SA
articles.details: Development of vinyl-DNADetect (VdU-based) chemical probes for detecting DNA synthesis in Plasmodium parasites, overcoming the limitation that standard EdU probes cannot be used because Plasmodium lacks thymidine kinase. VdU probes use catalyst-free inverse-electron-demand Diels-Alder click reaction with fluorogenic tetrazine, allowing live-cell imaging of P. falciparum-infected erythrocytes. Multiple probes demonstrated high efficiency labeling by flow cytometry and fluorescence microscopy.

articles.headline: Anopheles darlingi drives malaria transmission in Brazilian Yanomami villages
articles.title_original: Bionomics and malaria infection rates in anopheline species in Yanomami villages of the Brazilian Amazon Forest.
articles.authors: Sánchez-Ribas J, Silva-do-Nascimento TF, Pereira-Ribeiro C, Santos-Neves MSA, Barnwell JW, Lourenço-de-Oliveira R, Oliveira-Ferreira J
articles.details: Longitudinal entomological survey in five Yanomami villages (March 2013-March 2015) collecting 1,962 adult Anopheles from seven species using human-protected baited traps, aspirators, and light traps. Anopheles darlingi was predominant in most villages with infection rates up to 2.1% for P. vivax and P. falciparum in Gasolina and Taibrapa 1. Nightly human biting rates indicated An. darlingi as the primary vector in both peridomestic and extradomestic settings, with entomological inoculation rates in outdoor areas ranging from 49.3 to 79.3.

articles.headline: Young age and housing features drive locally acquired malaria in Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Determinants of locally acquired malaria infections in Zanzibar: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Fakih BS, Seidahmed O, Holzschuh A, Abdul R, Irema I, Shija SJ, Aliy SM, Ali A, Fink G, Yukich J, Hetzel MW
articles.details: Rolling cross-sectional survey in five Zanzibar districts (May 2017-October 2018) of 17,891 individuals, with 7,286 tested by qPCR for malaria; 2.6% (95% CI 2-3%) of those with no recent travel tested positive for locally acquired infections. Individuals aged 5-15 years had highest odds of infection (adjusted odds ratio 3.5, 95% CI 2.2-5.7), followed by ages 16-25 (aOR 1.9, 95% CI 1.2-3.0). Housing features were significant: open windows increased odds (aOR 2.1, 95% CI 1.4-3.3), while bed nets reduced odds (aOR 0.5, 95% CI 0.3-0.9).

articles.headline: Gene amplification of clag2 enables parasite survival under nutrient stress
articles.title_original: Nutrient stress dramatically increases malaria parasite clag2 copy number to increase host cell permeability and enable pathogen survival.
articles.authors: Potchen NB, Silva TM, Kalia I, Lack JB, Desai SA
articles.details: Laboratory study of P. falciparum parasites demonstrating that nutrient stress dramatically increases clag2 copy number as a mechanism for parasite survival. CLAG3-knockout parasites were unable to expand under physiological nutrient levels but selected mutants showed restored solute uptake; whole-genome sequencing revealed dramatically increased clag2 copy number without other relevant mutations. Quantitative PCR and DNA transfection confirmed increased clag2 gene expression.

articles.headline: Hypnozoite metabolites identified as potential diagnostic markers
articles.title_original: In vitro cultured malaria hypnozoites leave a footprint of specific metabolites.
articles.authors: Pasini EM, Hakimi H, Chaiyawong N, Klop O, Zeeman AM, Nieuwenhuis I, van der Werff N, Kihara K, Kami K, Kocken CHM, Kaneko O
articles.details: Proof-of-concept study combining in vitro P. cynomolgi hypnozoite cultures with metabolomics to identify diagnostic markers. Specific hypnozoite-related metabolites were identified in culture supernatants, suggesting potential for developing a rapid diagnostic test for P. vivax hypnozoite infection following in vivo validation.

articles.headline: Malaria control innovations and barriers in Democratic Republic of Congo
articles.title_original: Malaria in the Democratic Republic of Congo: how support programs consolidate elimination gains and protect at-risk populations from recrudescence.
articles.authors: Akilimali A, Mufungizi I, Rasheed S, Tague C, Shams Z, Shah RJ, Wilczyńska W, Siddiqui HT, Atif MH, Tufail Z, Qadri M, Alhassan A, Scott GY, Wei CR, Mukherjee A, Muco Mugisha MJ, Bisimwa C, Bulondo D, Muhoza B, Langat AK
articles.details: Review article addressing malaria elimination efforts in DR Congo, focusing on pregnant women and children under 5 who bear the highest disease burden. Discusses innovations including genomic surveillance, rapid diagnostic tests, mobile health solutions, and integrated approaches with vaccines (RTS,S and R21). Highlights challenges including limited healthcare access, insecticide resistance, surveillance gaps, and need for sustainable financing.

articles.headline: Memory T regulatory cells switch to protective effectors in malaria reinfection
articles.title_original: Memory Regulatory T Cells Reprogram into Protective Tfh-like Effectors in Recurrent Malaria.
articles.authors: Charles-Chess NAE, Ruberto AA, Bowers C, Obeng-Adjei N, Hansen MR, Renuka Prasad DB, Traore B, Klonowski KD, Crompton PD, Kurup SP
articles.details: Longitudinal mechanistic study in humans and mice showing that memory T regulatory cells (mTregs) undergo antigen-driven expansion and epigenetic reprogramming during Plasmodium reinfection, transitioning from Foxp3+ immunosuppressive to Bcl6+ follicular T helper-like effectors. mTreg-derived Tfh cells enhanced germinal center responses and parasite-specific antibody generation, facilitating parasite control; preventing this differentiation abolished protection.

articles.headline: Thioester-containing proteins in mosquitoes and snails: vector control implications
articles.title_original: Comparative immunological roles of TEP1 in Anopheles gambiae and Biomphalaria glabrata: implications for malaria and schistosomiasis control.
articles.authors: Li H, Feng Y, Qian Y, Jiang W, Zhu Y, Xu J, Li X, Fei X, Wang R, Shao Y, Du L, Zhang X, Chen K
articles.details: Review comparing TEP1 (thioester-containing protein) activation mechanisms and functional roles in malaria vector Anopheles gambiae and schistosomiasis vector Biomphalaria glabrata. Discusses TEP involvement in pathogen opsonization, phagocytic elimination, ROS generation, and melanization responses. Analyzes evolutionary roles in host-parasite coevolution and evaluates potential application in vector intervention and disease prevention strategies.

articles.headline: Molecular markers differentiate local from imported malaria in Honduras
articles.title_original: Molecular Characterization of Plasmodium Species to Strengthen Malaria Surveillance in Migrant Populations in Honduras.
articles.authors: Godoy A, Euceda K, Pinto A, Valdivia HO, Chaver L, Ardon G, Fontecha G
articles.details: Molecular surveillance study of P. falciparum (pfcrt, pfmdr1, pfama1, pfglurp, pfs47) and P. vivax (pvcsp, pvmsp1, pvmsp3α, pvmsp3β, pvs47, pvs48/45) in migrant versus local samples in Honduras. All P. falciparum isolates showed wild-type pfcrt (chloroquine susceptibility); polymorphisms in pfmdr1, pfama1, pfglurp, and pfs47 revealed distinct genotypes between migrants and local cases. P. vivax showed novel VK210 haplotypes and first VK247 variant detection; additional novel haplotypes across multiple markers proved informative for geographic origin inference.

articles.headline: Symptom recognition and insurance increase timely malaria treatment in children
articles.title_original: Determinants of timely malaria treatment among under-five children attending public health facilities in Kisumu East sub-county, Kenya: a health facility-based cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Ochieng G, Silumbwe A, Sampa M, Maritim P, Zulu JM, Kato J, Okungu J, Jacobs C
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 434 caregivers of under-five children in Kisumu East, Kenya (April-May 2023) examining determinants of timely malaria treatment (diagnosis and appropriate antimalarial administration within 24h of symptom onset). Caregivers' symptom recognition increased odds of timely treatment (AOR 2.9, p=0.006), as did belief in appropriate drugs (AOR 6.9, p<0.0001) and health insurance (AOR 2.1, p=0.005). Herbalist consultation (AOR 0.1, p<0.0001) and fear of drug side effects (AOR 0.5, p=0.013) reduced timely treatment.

articles.headline: Single low-dose primaquine plus ACT safe for G6PD-deficient children
articles.title_original: Plasma folate dynamics in Plasmodium falciparum-infected African children treated with artemisinin combination therapy and single low-dose primaquine or placebo.
articles.authors: Ajayi S, Onyamboko MA, Olupot-Olupot P, Ayuen DS, Chimjinda N, Taya C, Williams TN, Uyoga S, Maitland K, Fanello C, Day NPJ, Mukaka M, Taylor WRJ
articles.details: Randomized controlled trial in falciparum-infected children (6 months to 11 years) from Uganda and Congo comparing single low-dose primaquine (SLDPQ, 0.25 mg/kg) plus artemether-lumefantrine or dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (n=408; 66 G6PD-deficient, 283 normal) versus placebo. Plasma folate mean baseline 10.83 ng/ml (SLDPQ) vs 10.92 ng/ml (placebo), with initial fall of 1.65 ng/ml (p<0.0001), then rise to 11.04 ng/ml by day 28. SLDPQ and G6PD status did not compromise posttreatment folate, supporting SLDPQ use without G6PD testing.

articles.headline: Abbott-Bioline RDT shows poor sensitivity; not recommended for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Investigating the Abbott-Bioline™ malaria antigen Pf/Pv rapid diagnostic test.
articles.authors: Aung MTT, Thu AM, Phyo AP, Aung WL, Pateekhum C, Proux S, Yotyingaphiram W, Archusuksan L, Wongaeka M, White NJ, Day NP, Nosten F
articles.details: Field comparison and laboratory study of Abbott-Bioline Pf/Pv RDT versus First Response RDT and microscopy on Thailand-Myanmar border. Abbott-Bioline showed very low sensitivity for P. falciparum (0.18 field, 45% lab detection) and P. vivax (0.44 field, 74% lab), while First Response demonstrated substantially higher sensitivity (P. falciparum 0.89 field/90.3% lab, P. vivax 0.59 field/84% lab). Study concluded Abbott-Bioline is not fit for purpose and should be replaced.

articles.headline: Artesunate-amodiaquine remains effective for uncomplicated falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artesunate-amodiaquine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria among children aged 2-11 years in the Asante Akim North Municipality, Ghana: a single-arm open trial.
articles.authors: Hanson H, Marfo KS, Osei-Mensa J, Jaeger A, Sarpong N, Tsali DN, Martey A, Gyimah AK, Placca E, Oppong M, Foli G, Almoustapha NS, Amuasi J, May J, Maïga Ascofaré O
articles.details: Single-arm trial of artesunate-amodiaquine (AS-AQ) in 173 Ghanaian children aged 2-11 with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria (July 2018-June 2019). 164 completed treatment with all parasite clearance by day 3; 8.1% experienced vomiting. Adequate clinical and parasitological response (ACPR) at day 28 was 92.07% (intent-to-treat) and 100% (Kaplan-Meier). No pfk13 artemisinin-resistance mutations detected; pfmdr1 NFD haplotype found in 27.1%, pfcrt K76T in 1.9%.

articles.headline: Plasmodium male gametogenesis uses novel microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition
articles.title_original: A basal body microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition in Plasmodium male gametogenesis.
articles.authors: Yang S, Ma S, Yuan C, Li Z, Ji F, Yao L, Cui H, Guo Q, Yuan J
articles.details: High-resolution imaging study (iU-ExM and cryo-ET) of P. yoelii male gamete basal body revealing a microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition mechanism, distinct from canonical triplet-to-doublet transition. δ-Tubulin and ε-Tubulin are expressed in male gametocytes and regulate axoneme formation; δ-tubulin localizes at the proximal MT B-tubule end and modulates B-tubule assembly during male gametogenesis.

articles.headline: Novel serological biomarkers GDH and HDP show promise for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Evaluation of GDH and HDP as novel serological biomarkers for Plasmodium falciparum malaria diagnosis across varying parasitemia and transmission settings in India.
articles.authors: Ahmad A, Nema S, Arora K, Sikka R, Singh A, Krishna S, Singh MP, Verma AK, Nitika N, Anvikar AR, Gupta H, Bharti PK
articles.details: Cross-sectional serological study in India analyzing antibody responses to novel biomarkers (glutamate dehydrogenase/GDH and Heam detoxification protein/HDP) versus conventional antigens (HRP2, LDH, aldolase) in 928 participants including 886 P. falciparum-positive cases. All peptides showed high diagnostic accuracy (ROC AUC >96%) for detecting P. falciparum. GDH2 and HRP2 effectively distinguished parasitemia levels; GDH2, HDP2, and LDH showed promise in distinguishing endemicity levels. Results suggest GDH and HDP have significant potential as serological biomarkers for malaria detection.

articles.headline: CYP2D6 polymorphism affects vivax infection risk across age groups in Brazil
articles.title_original: How CYP2D6 Polymorphism Modulates the Community-Wide Risk of Plasmodium vivax Infection: A Panel Study in Amazonian Brazil.
articles.authors: Puça MCSB, Naziazeno IM, Santos VCFD, Rodrigues PT, Calil PR, Ladeia WA, Gil JP, Ferreira MU, Nobrega De Sousa T
articles.details: Community-based longitudinal panel study (~1300 individuals) in Amazonian Brazil with 4-year follow-up examining CYP2D6 polymorphisms and P. vivax infection dynamics using molecular diagnosis. Children with impaired CYP2D6 activity showed higher P. vivax infection frequency compared to normal enzyme activity individuals. In the second decade of life, P. vivax prevalence increased in adolescents with normal activity (p=0.0008); parasite densities were lower in adults with impaired CYP2D6 activity versus younger individuals with normal activity (p=0.0383).

articles.headline: mRNA COVID vaccine responses unimpaired by P. falciparum parasitemia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Parasitemia Does Not Diminish Neutralizing Antibody Responses After mRNA COVID-19 Booster Vaccination in HIV-infected Adults.
articles.authors: Samandari T, Achola M, Hutter JN, Mboya G, Otieno W, Kee JJ, Huang Y, Aponte JJ, Ockenhouse CF, Lee CK, Polakowski L, Yacovone M, Tapley A, Dadabhai S, Mkhize NN, Kaldine H, Bhebhe S, Moore PL, Hural J, Garrett N, Kublin JG
articles.details: Study of HIV-infected adults (both parasitemic and non-parasitemic with PCR-confirmed P. falciparum) who received mRNA-1273 or mRNA-1273.222 COVID-19 booster vaccines. Neutralizing antibody ID50 titers were >22,000 in both groups 1 month after boosting, indicating vaccine immunogenicity is unaffected by concurrent asymptomatic malaria infection.

articles.headline: IgG3 allele associated with reduced Plasmodium infection in PNG children
articles.title_original: Association of a Novel IgG3 Allele With Malaria in Children From the Sepik Region of Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Saeed M, Aitken EH, Naung MT, Bourke C, Wu KW, Longley RJ, Chung AW, Damelang T, Kiniboro B, Mueller I, Rogerson SJ
articles.details: Longitudinal study of 203 children aged 1–3 years from East Sepik, Papua New Guinea, examining immunoglobulin alleles and Plasmodium infections. The IGHG3*30 allele (prevalent in 78% of children) was significantly associated with fewer Plasmodium infections (β = −1.736; P = 0.038), with strongest effect for P. vivax asymptomatic infection (β = −1.06; P = 0.028). IGHG3*30 carriers also had lower IgG levels to P. vivax vaccine candidates.

articles.headline: Malaria-induced neutrophil shift linked to Burkitt lymphoma risk
articles.title_original: The Impact of Malaria-Induced Neutrophil Subset Shift and a Link to Burkitt Lymphoma.
articles.authors: Akinyi S, Tonui RK, Maina TK, Agwati E, Oduor CI, Njuguna FM, Keitany KK, Chepsiror D, Ayieko C, Moormann A, Kinyua AW, Forconi CS
articles.details: Flow cytometry profiling of neutrophil subsets in children with different P. falciparum exposure histories and children with Burkitt lymphoma (BL) from Western Kenya. Malaria-exposed children showed increased aged neutrophil subset frequencies and reduced mature subset frequencies. A strong positive correlation (rs = 0.7; p < 0.0001) was observed in immature neutrophil expansion between malaria-exposed healthy and BL children, suggesting malaria-driven neutrophil changes may contribute to BL immunopathology.

articles.headline: P. falciparum apicoplast DNA polymerase structural basis revealed
articles.title_original: Structural basis of multitasking by the apicoplast DNA polymerase from Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Kumari A, Enache T, Craggs TD, Pata JD, Lahiri I
articles.details: Cryoelectron microscopy structures of P. falciparum apicoplast DNA polymerase (apPol) in five pre-chemistry conformational states. The single polymerase can accommodate both replicative and lesion-bypass synthesis through a fingers-open configuration and nascent base-pair checkpoint. Findings explain the structural basis of how apPol balances high-fidelity replication with lesion bypass.

articles.headline: Focal mass drug administration trial for P. vivax elimination in Peru
articles.title_original: FocaL mass drug administration for Plasmodium vivax malaria elimination (FLAME): study protocol for an open-label cluster randomized controlled trial in Peru.
articles.authors: Fine SR, Soto Calle V, Altamirano Quiroz A, Rodriquez Ferruci H, Manrique P, Wu X, Carrasco Escobar G, Benjamin-Chung J, Bennett A, Auburn S, Price RN, Greenhouse B, Baird JK, Domingo GJ, Roh ME, Rosas A, Llanos-Cuentas A, Hsiang MS
articles.details: FLAME study: 3-year cluster randomized controlled trial in Loreto, Peru, randomizing 30 low-transmission communities (API <250/1000 population) 1:1 to focal mass drug administration (fMDA) versus control. Intervention arm receives three cycles of fMDA (chloroquine 25 mg/kg plus tafenoquine 300 mg for ≥16 years or primaquine 0.5 mg/kg/day for 7 days if younger) over 2 years. Primary outcome is cumulative incidence of symptomatic P. vivax malaria; trial powered to detect ≥68% relative reduction.

articles.headline: Institutional quality and aid flows drive malaria burden variation in SSA
articles.title_original: Institutional quality, aid flows, and malaria burden: a geospatial analysis of sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Namubiru C
articles.details: Geospatial analysis of 38 sub-Saharan African countries (2010–2022) using spatial econometric models examined malaria cases and deaths per 1,000 population against development aid, government effectiveness, and health worker density. Findings revealed overall malaria burden reduction with significant spatial autocorrelation. Increased health worker density, institutional effectiveness, and aid levels were positively associated with burden reduction, with limited cross-border spillover effects.

articles.headline: 26% malaria prevalence in under-five children, Ghana; ITN coverage key
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria infection and associated factors among children under five years attending health facilities in the Nandom Municipality, Upper West Region, Ghana.
articles.authors: Boadu S, Azornu CK, Axame W, Bonsu EO, Wumnaya R, Yidana S, Abanga WA, Sarpomaa Asante L, Adjei MR, Kubio C
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study of 404 febrile children under five in Nandom Municipality, Ghana (July 2024). Malaria prevalence by microscopy was 26.0% (95% CI 21.9–30.5). Non-ITN users had 2.01× greater odds of malaria (aOR 2.01; 95% CI 1.02–3.93); children in Gengenkpe sub-municipality had 4.58× greater odds than Baseble (aOR 4.58; 95% CI 1.58–13.27). Travel time >30 minutes to pharmacy was associated with 2.19× greater odds.

articles.headline: RTS,S malaria vaccine achieved 69–91% dose-1 coverage in pilot rollout
articles.title_original: POST introduction evaluation (PIE) of the malaria vaccine introduced in three pilot countries (Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi) in 2021.
articles.authors: Walldorf JA, Mayers GF, Amponsa-Achiano K, Jalang'o R, Chisema M, Khalayi L, Njoroge J, Haji A, Tweneboah PO, Sally ET, Mategula D, Mhone B, Zimba B, Chirwa E, Mohammed NT, Adjei MR, Sillah J, Pellaux-Furrer E, Magafu MGMD, Okine RNA
articles.details: WHO post-introduction evaluation (PIE) of RTS,S/AS01E vaccine implementation in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi (2019 pilot introduction). Median coverage 24–30 months after pilot start: dose 1, 69–91%; dose 2, 62–82%; dose 3, 58–81%. Best practices included early stakeholder involvement, health worker training, community engagement, defaulter tracing, and social mobilization. Vaccine was successfully integrated into routine childhood immunization programs with strong intersectoral collaboration.

articles.headline: Chloroquine shortages, fragmented delivery hinder pregnant-women malaria care
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers of malaria prevention and treatment services to pregnant women in Ethiopia: a multi-level health system analysis, 2025.
articles.authors: Bogale KA, Yenesew MA, Alemu K, Muchie KF, Asemahagn MA, Enbiale W
articles.details: Qualitative multi-level health system analysis in Ethiopia (49 stakeholders including policymakers, program managers, healthcare providers) examining malaria prevention and treatment for pregnant women. Facilitators included prioritized bed net distribution, NGO partnerships, expanded diagnostic infrastructure, and increased antenatal care time. Barriers included chloroquine shortages, fragmented service delivery, staff shortages, policy gaps (delayed WHO intermittent preventive treatment adoption), and inadequate pregnancy-specific surveillance data.

articles.headline: Correction to Anopheles species in Colombian gold-mining malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Correction: Anopheles species associated with malaria transmission in gold-mining areas of northwest Colombia.
articles.authors: Rojas MA, Ahumada ML, Velasco-Pareja MC, Chaparro P, Yasnot-Acosta MF, Orjuela LI
articles.details: Correction notice; no substantive abstract content provided.

articles.headline: Vitamin E and iron deficiency strongly associated with malaria infection
articles.title_original: Associations between undernutrition and malaria infection: a case-control study from Rwanda.
articles.authors: Uwimana A, Alexiou H, Mutoni JD, Robert A, Rujeni N, Cani PD, Coutelier JP, Mutesa L, Everard A
articles.details: Case-control study from Rwanda (cases: positive blood smear; controls: malaria-free household members; November 2021–December 2023 across 9 endemic districts). After covariate adjustment, vitamin E deficiency and iron deficiency were positively associated with malaria (aOR = 7.46; 95% CI 4.43–12.58; p < 0.001 and aOR = 1.80; 95% CI 1.11–2.93; p = 0.017, respectively). Conversely, increasing age, female sex, and selenium deficiency were inversely associated with infection.

articles.headline: Ghana health facilities test, treat malaria well; tracking adherence poor
articles.title_original: Adherence to the test, treat and track malaria policy among selected health facilities in Ghana: the clients' perspective.
articles.authors: Kyei C, Tawiah C, Agbokey F, Anim JA, Tivura M, Afari-Asiedu S, Kyeremateng CK, Kornu FM, Oppong J, Zuuri W, Nyampong I, Agana VA, Awimba PI, Ankonu AN, Awine E, Welega P, Gyaase S, Tetteh RJ, Mayers P, Harrison SBE, Asante KP
articles.details: Cross-sectional evaluation of adherence to WHO test, treat, and track (T3) policy across 30 facilities in 6 Ghanaian districts (November 2019; 590 patient interviews). Testing and treatment with artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) were high; tracking was very low. Southern-zone facilities had 2.87× increased odds of T3 adherence versus middle zone (aOR 2.87; 95% CI 1.7–4.8; p < 0.001). Males were less likely to return for follow-up (aOR 0.6; 95% CI 0.3–0.9; p = 0.018).

articles.headline: Malaria transmission determinants identified in Indian districts
articles.title_original: Determinants of malaria transmission in Indian districts in 2018: insights from ensemble models.
articles.authors: Sam AK, Khatoon A, Phuleria HC
articles.details: Ensemble Random Forest and Poisson regression models analyzed district-wise malaria, socio-economic, and meteorological data from India. Spatial clusters of annual parasite incidence and annual falciparum incidence observed in central and northeastern districts. Models identified socio-economically marginalized communities with water inaccessibility and no mobile phone access, plus unprotected water sources, as linked to increased transmission; mother's education a potential protective factor. Authors conclude targeted interventions in high-burden district clusters will reduce malaria more effectively than individual district approaches.

articles.headline: PfEBA-175 antibodies persist despite quarterly mass testing intervention
articles.title_original: Increased Plasmodium falciparum EBA175RIII-V antibody titres despite a 13-month malaria mass testing, treatment, and tracking intervention in a high malaria endemic community in Ghana.
articles.authors: Tey PK, Brashear A, Kwapong SS, Acquah FK, Bredu DG, Busayomi A, Anang SF, Abban BC, Nagaoka H, Takashima E, Obiri-Yeboah D, Cui L, Amoah LE
articles.details: Serological study of 314 Ghanaians over four quarterly mass testing, treatment, and tracking rounds. PCR-detected P. falciparum prevalence declined from 56.2% to 46.8%; 212 participants (67.5%) infected at least once. Anti-PfEBA175RIII-V IgG titers fluctuated, reaching highest at study end. Infected participants had significantly higher titers than uninfected (age-dependent pattern confirmed). The 13-month MTTT intervention did not significantly reduce antibody titers or naturally acquired immunity to PfEBA-175RIII-V.

articles.headline: Elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate marks malaria severity
articles.title_original: A systematic review and meta-analysis of Plasmodium infections and erythrocyte sedimentation rate in relation to disease severity.
articles.authors: Kotepui M, Suwannatrai AT, Kotepui KU, Mahittikorn A, Masangkay FR, Thinkhamrop K, Wangdi K, Wilairatana P
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 19 studies (PRISMA registered). ESR significantly elevated in malaria versus non-malarial controls (SMD 3.00, P=0.017, 11 studies) and in severe versus non-severe malaria (SMD 2.26, P=0.001, 4 studies). Substantial heterogeneity (I²=97.3%) and publication bias (Egger P=0.005) detected. Authors conclude ESR frequently elevated in malaria and more pronounced in severe cases, but high heterogeneity and publication bias limit robustness; ESR may serve as a supportive biomarker when combined with other parameters.

articles.headline: Correction notice for CRP and malaria bacterial infection study
articles.title_original: Correction: Evaluation of CRP as a marker for bacterial infection and malaria in febrile children at the Douala Gyneco-Obstetric and Pediatric Hospital.
articles.authors: PLOS One Editors
articles.details: Correction notice to published article (DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0289012); specific content not provided in correction statement.

articles.headline: Pyronaridine-artesunate superior to artemether-lumefantrine
articles.title_original: Pyronaridine-artesunate for treating uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Fox T, Otieno JA, Pryce J, Hine P
articles.details: Cochrane systematic review of 15 studies (14 RCTs, 1 quasi-experimental; 7,295 participants) comparing pyronaridine-artesunate to WHO-recommended ACTs for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Versus artemether-lumefantrine: pyronaridine-artesunate better for PCR-adjusted treatment failures day 28 (RR 0.40, 95% CI 0.19–0.85, moderate certainty), unadjusted day 28 (RR 0.27, 95% CI 0.14–0.52), and unadjusted day 42 (RR 0.61, 95% CI 0.46–0.82); no significant difference at day 42 PCR-adjusted (RR 0.86, 95% CI 0.49–1.51). Authors conclude pyronaridine-artesunate probably performs better.

articles.headline: Liver sinusoid constraints optimize CD8 T cell parasite detection
articles.title_original: Liver sinusoid constraints make random search of CD8 T cells for Plasmodium parasites efficient.
articles.authors: Zenkov VS, Cockburn IA, Lin R, Griffith WN, Langston MA, Ganusov VV
articles.details: Experimental and mathematical study imaging Plasmodium-specific CD8 T cells, sporozoites, and liver sinusoids in mice. Developed graph-based modeling of sinusoid-constrained T cell movement. Simulations showed fewer than 10^5 CD8 T cells per liver, moving at experimentally observed speeds without attraction toward infection, can find sporozoites within 24 hours. No evidence of biased T cell movement when constrained to sinusoids. Authors conclude tissue structure constraints enhance random search efficiency and that attraction detection may be artifact of open-space metrics.

articles.headline: Immune responses diverge between P. falciparum and P. vivax infection
articles.title_original: Systems Biological Analysis of Immune-Metabolic Host Responses to Distinct Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Lima DV, Guimarães TP, Almeida ACG, Teixeira AJ, Monteiro WM, Melo GC, Gardinassi LG
articles.details: Integrated analysis of flow cytometry, transcriptomics, and metabolomics from simian malaria models (P. coatneyi, P. cynomolgi) and human infections. Conserved responses included dendritic cell and effector memory CD8+ T cell dynamics, upregulated HMOX1 and coagulation genes. P. cynomolgi and P. vivax infections showed enriched chemokine response and T cell differentiation gene modules. Metabolomics revealed conserved regulation of kynurenine and androgen/estrogen metabolites across infections. Authors conclude parasite species significantly impacts host immune and metabolic responses; systematic experimental comparisons needed.

articles.headline: Molecular techniques reliably detect Plasmodium in Anopheles vectors
articles.title_original: Detection of Plasmodium in Anopheles spp (Diptera: Nematocera: Culicidae) using molecular biology techniques: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Fonseca EDC, David EDS, Santos KCD, Oliveira FP, Barbosa LMC, Komninakis SV, Souto RNP
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: Systematic review (PRISMA 2020) of 12 studies using molecular biology to detect and identify Plasmodium species in Anopheles mosquitoes. All studies successfully applied molecular methods to determine parasite species in sampled mosquitoes. Authors conclude molecular biology techniques demonstrate accuracy and reliability in parasite detection, contributing to effective epidemiological surveillance and monitoring of Plasmodium spread in endemic areas.

articles.headline: Imported malaria cases surge in low-endemic Brazilian state
articles.title_original: Changes in the malaria transmission profile in a low-endemic area of the Brazilian Amazon: a cause for concern.
articles.authors: Oliveira-Neto FM, Albuquerque HG, Silva ARD, Gonçalves EDGDR, Suárez-Mutis MC
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: Retrospective ecological study of malaria in Maranhao, Brazil, 2003–2022 (83,517 total cases reported). Initial epidemiological pattern: 67.8% autochthonous cases, 83.1% in rural areas. Annual Parasite Incidence recently classified low-risk in most municipalities. Epidemiological profile shifted with increasing imported cases (initially foreign, recently from other Brazilian states). P. falciparum proportion declined from 17.7% (2003) to 6.01% (2022). Most infected were male, predominantly aged 15–45 years (increasing from 59.1% to 70.5%). Authors conclude despite downward trend, imported cases pose reintroduction risk; strengthened surveillance essential.

articles.headline: Mathematical models quantify recurrent malaria for study planning
articles.title_original: How do recurrent malaria infections occur in clinical cohorts: a mathematical modelling study to support study planning.
articles.authors: Krumkamp R, Rautman LH, Maiga-Ascofaré O, May J, Lorenz E
articles.details: Five compartmental mathematical models (ordinary differential equations) simulating recurrent malaria infections under different transmission scenarios: constant risk (Model A), post-treatment protection and immunity (Model B), vaccination effects (Model C), heterogeneous transmission (Model D), and seasonal variation (Model E). Models A and B had equal first infections; Model B had fewer recurrences due to treatment effects and immunity. Model C showed substantial reduction; Model D showed high-transmission subpopulation experienced ~100% recurrence rates. Authors provide equations for estimating expected recurrent events in longitudinal studies.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminths lower hemoglobin in armed forces cohort
articles.title_original: The impact of malaria and intestinal helminths on haemoglobin concentrations among armed forces of the North West and South West Cameroon.
articles.authors: Che SB, Ntonifor HN, Tofel KH, Jugha VT, Wanyu BY, Taiwe GS
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 812 male military participants in Cameroon. Malaria prevalence 13.6% (P. falciparum 86.6% of infected cases; 86.6% mild malaria); P. vivax also identified. Soil-transmitted helminth and protozoan parasite prevalence 22.8% (5 helminthic species, 2 protozoan species identified). Malaria, Trichuris trichiura, and Schistosoma mansoni significantly reduced mean hemoglobin concentration. Authors conclude malaria and intestinal helminths remain public health problems affecting hemoglobin levels; asymptomatic carriers serve as transmission agents.

articles.headline: Wing morphometrics distinguishes cryptic Anopheles vector species
articles.title_original: Unraveling Wing Shape Variation in Malaria Mosquitoes from the Arctic Edge: A Geometric Morphometric Study in Western Siberia.
articles.authors: Calderon X, Artemov G, Burlak VA, Alexeeva S, Hernández-P R, Suazo MJ, Pérez LM, Benítez HA, Correa M
articles.details: Geometric morphometrics study of wing venation patterns in Western Siberia Anopheles species: An. messeae, An. daciae, An. beklemishevi, and hybrids. Procrustes ANOVA, PCA, and CVA applied to anatomical landmarks. Wing shape and size significantly differed among species; hybrids showed intermediate morphologies. Landmarks on radial and medial veins most consistent for species separation. No static allometry detected. Authors conclude wing morphometrics combined with molecular identification provides reliable framework for species delimitation and malaria vector surveillance in temperate regions.

articles.headline: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in Puerto Rican urban wetland
articles.title_original: Occurrence and Distribution of Three Low Molecular Weight PAHs in Caño La Malaria, Cucharillas Marsh (Cataño, Puerto Rico): Spatial and Seasonal Variability, Sources, and Ecological Risk.
articles.authors: Berríos-Rolón PJ, Márquez F, Cotto MC
articles.details: Study of naphthalene, phenanthrene, and anthracene in Caño La Malaria surface waters, Puerto Rico. Concentrations ranged 7.4–2198.8 ng/L with higher levels in wet season (mean 745.79 ng/L) than dry (mean 186.71 ng/L). Diagnostic ratios indicated pyrogenic (combustion) origin. Ecological risk assessment showed negligible acute toxicity (hazard quotients <0.01) but suggested potential chronic risks.

articles.headline: Malaria-helminth coinfection increases parasitemia and anemia
articles.title_original: Integrated assessment of malaria parasite load, anemia, and associated factors in patients with and without soil-transmitted helminthes coinfection in Southern Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Bogale A, Tefera S, Taye B, Mitiku A, Kasse A, Getu E, Gemechu T, Misganaw T, Degefa T
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 220 patients in Ethiopia: 110 with Plasmodium monoinfection, 110 coinfected with soil-transmitted helminths (STH). Coinfected patients showed significantly higher parasitemia (mean 40,093.54 parasites/µL) and lower hemoglobin. Hookworm and Trichuris trichiura were positively associated with increased parasitemia; Ascaris showed inverse relationship. Coinfection correlated with poor hygiene, low education, raw vegetable consumption, and proximity to stagnant water.

articles.headline: Environmental and socioeconomic drivers of West African malaria
articles.title_original: Environmental and socio-economic determinants of malaria transmission in West Africa: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Gbaguidi GJ, Topanou N, Filho WL, Begedou K, Ketoh GK
articles.details: Systematic review of 68 articles (1989–2025) on malaria transmission determinants in West Africa. Key environmental factors: air temperature, rainfall, relative humidity, vegetation. Key socioeconomic factors: education level, residence location, housing structure, poverty, information quality. Authors conclude these factors are indicators for targeted elimination and prevention programs.

articles.headline: Multiple first-line antimalarials feasible in Kenya; logistics critical
articles.title_original: Health system challenges and facilitators associated with adaptive cycling deployment of multiple first-line treatment for uncomplicated malaria: a pilot study in a malaria-endemic region of Kenya.
articles.authors: Cole A, Chege T, Aman R, Githuka G, Muga R, Aspinall A, Kokwaro G
articles.details: Two-year pilot (June 2020–June 2022) in western Kenya testing adaptive cycling of four artemisinin-based combinations: artemether-lumefantrine (AL), dihydroartemisin-piperaquine (DHA-PIP), amodiaquine-artesunate (ASAQ), pyronaridine-artesunate (PYR-ART). Homa Bay (implementation) vs. Migori (control) comparison. Migori showed 12.5 percentage-point higher malaria positivity (23.3% vs. 10.8%). Cost USD 3 per patient treated. Major challenges: drug quantification/stock management, human resources, information systems, diagnostic kit stock-outs. One minor adverse effect (vomiting) reported; patients preferred once-daily dosing of new drugs over AL's complex regimen.

articles.headline: Climate change decreases malaria transmission intensity in most Indian regions
articles.title_original: Changing climate and its impacts on the dynamics of future malaria transmission over certain endemic regions in India.
articles.authors: Bal PK, Kumar DS, Parihar RS, Saini A
articles.details: VECTRI model simulation of future malaria transmission (2080s vs. 1985–2014 baseline) across four Indian climate zones under SSP5-8.5 scenario. Results: 10–30% decrease in entomological inoculation rate (EIR) over most regions with 4–5°C temperature increase and 10–40% rainfall increase by century's end. Few exceptions in western and peninsular regions showed increased EIR. Temperature and rainfall during monsoon seasons predominantly controlled EIR changes.

articles.headline: LAMP-based POC test detects submicroscopic falciparum infections
articles.title_original: Sensitive near point-of-care detection of asymptomatic and submicroscopic Plasmodium falciparum infections in African endemic countries.
articles.authors: Rakotomalala Robinson D, Pennisi I, Cavuto ML, Kiemde F, Chamai M, Some DY, Quigley E, Malpartida-Cardenas K, Ndiath MO, Correa S, Darboe B, Stewart LB, Georgiou P, Baldeh M, Tinto H, Cunnington AJ, Erhart A, D'Alessandro U, Rodriguez-Manzano J
articles.details: Field evaluation of Pan/Pf LAMP-based near point-of-care diagnostic on 672 capillary blood samples from The Gambia and Burkina Faso (146 P. falciparum confirmed by qPCR). Limit of detection: 0.6 parasites/µL; sample-to-result time <45 min. Achieved 95.2% sensitivity (95% CI: 90.4–98.1%) and 96.8% specificity (95% CI: 94.9–98.0%). Detected 94.9% of asymptomatic and 95.3% of submicroscopic (<16 parasites/µL) infections, outperforming microscopy (70.1% and 0%) and RDTs (49.6% and 4.7%).

articles.headline: Dengue and additional pathogens common among febrile patients, Nigeria
articles.title_original: Detection of dengue, malaria, and additional causes of acute febrile illness: The need for expanded testing, Bayelsa State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akanbi DO, Abaye BB, Averhoff F, Berg MG, Orf GS, Lawan KM, Beckett GA, Omotoso AB, Mata M, Cloherty GA, Chabuka L, de Oliveira T, Mac-Fisi KW, Walter A, Mark II, Edeki G, Sinayoko A, Nasrullah M, Justina BI, Balogun MS
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 443 febrile patients (≥1 year) in Bayelsa State, Nigeria, May–June 2022. Acute dengue seroprevalence: 14.5% (n=64); malaria: 42.4% (n=188); coinfection: 6.5% (n=29). Past dengue markers: 17.6% (n=78). RT-PCR identified multiple dengue serotypes (serotype 3 dominant). Metagenomic sequencing revealed additional causes: adenovirus, EBV, hepatitis A, Coxsackievirus A5 (HFMD), and mpox clade IIb (lineage A.2.3). Risk factors for dengue: rural/suburban residence, age ≥31 years. Risk factors for malaria: household ≥5 persons, borehole water use.

articles.headline: Low P. vivax and P. ovale prevalence in Cameroon highlands despite relapse potential
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of ovale, vivax and falciparum malaria in the highlands of Cameroon: An integrated community survey of human infection and vector abundance.
articles.authors: White SJ, Tchuenkam VPK, Mbouh M, Gaither C, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Kiam BC, Popkin-Hall ZR, Sadler JM, Carey-Ewend K, Hand E, Ngum MN, Ngomsi YN, Bailey JA, Kaunda DB, Mafo LK, Lemogo GN, Dinka CL, Nsani CA, Noubom M, Goel V, Ibrahima I, Onguene JC, Lin FC, Lin JT, Nsango SE, Dinglasan RR, Juliano JJ, Ali IM
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 3,661 participants from 871 households in Dschang, Cameroon using qPCR. Prevalence: P. vivax 0.1%, P. ovale spp. 0.64%, P. falciparum 8.1%. Co-infections of P. ovale with P. falciparum: 23.1% of P. ovale cases. Gametocytemia was common in falciparum but uncommon in ovale infections. High Anopheles gambiae s.l. and An. funestus s.l. densities; children and those with recent domestic travel had higher odds of P. falciparum infection. P. vivax and P. ovale concentrated in Penka-Michel and Baleveng.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 41.2% in northwest Ethiopia; education and housing key
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria and associated factors among febrile patients in selected healthcare facilities of Jawi District, northwest Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Dagnew A, Yeshanew S, Nibret E, Atenafu G, Kendie FA, Assefa M, Habtamu A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 420 febrile patients, April–November 2023, Jawi District, northwest Ethiopia. Malaria prevalence: 41.2% (95% CI: 0.365–0.459); P. falciparum predominant (64.8%). Significant risk factors: lack of formal education (AOR=2.452; 95% CI: 2.427–11.345; p=0.047); proximity to mosquito breeding sites (AOR=43.224; 95% CI: 3.215–25.381; p=0.015); awareness gaps on malaria transmission (AOR=1.384; 95% CI: 1.117–2.009; p=0.044). Housing characteristics and inconsistent preventive measure use also contributed.

articles.headline: Plasmodium glutathione S-transferase inhibition as novel antimalarial strategy
articles.title_original: Targeting phase II detoxification enzyme inhibition in Plasmodium for malaria treatment.
articles.authors: Onisuru O, Achilonu I
articles.details: Review article proposing inhibition of Plasmodium phase II detoxification enzymes, particularly glutathione S-transferase (GST), as a novel antimalarial drug target. GSTs are critical for parasite survival via intracellular detoxification of exogenous and endogenous toxic molecules. Authors argue targeting Plasmodium falciparum and vivax GSTs offers a promising approach distinct from known antimalarial modes of action, potentially addressing drug-resistant parasites.

articles.headline: Low genetic diversity of PfCelTOS vaccine candidate in Vietnam
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and natural selection of cell-traversal protein for ookinetes and sporozoites (CelTOS) in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Vietnam.
articles.authors: Võ TC, Lê HG, Kang JM, Minh Trinh NT, Cho M, Goo YK, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: Analysis of 366 pfceltos sequences from Vietnamese P. falciparum isolates (2018–2022) from three provinces: Dak Lak, Gia Lai, Phu Yen. Twenty-nine polymorphic amino acid changes identified; four commonly detected (K100N, S104L, F117V, N119K). Low overall genetic diversity with geographical variation: Dak Lak showed greater diversity than Gia Lai and Phu Yen. Tight conservation of biologically significant amino acids and low genetic diversity support PfCelTOS potential as vaccine candidate, though global analysis recommended.

articles.headline: Plasmodium yoelii polarizes macrophages via TLR7-STAT3 pathway
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection regulates M1 macrophage polarization and function through TLR7/STAT3 signaling pathway.
articles.authors: Mo L, Jiang Z, Hong C, Guo J, Qiu G, Wu Q, Deng G, Chen Y, Lu Y, Zhou H, Liu Q, Luo X, Zhao Y, Qi Y, Li Q, Ma X, Yang Q
articles.details: Experimental study of P. yoelii infection effects on macrophage function in mice. P. yoelii triggered M1 macrophage polarization and enhanced phagocytosis via TLR7 upregulation; STAT3 expression also increased. TLR7 agonist R848 alleviated infection symptoms. STAT3 inhibitors attenuated TLR7 effects. Macrophage depletion (clodronate liposomes) increased parasitemia and weakened T-cell and B-cell responses. Conclusion: Plasmodium promotes innate and adaptive immunity through TLR7-STAT3-mediated M1 polarization and phagocytosis.

articles.headline: ZY19489 blocks drug efflux, creating resistance trap
articles.title_original: Collateral hypersensitivity between ZY19489 and piperaquine neutralizes PfCRT-mediated drug efflux and Plasmodium falciparum resistance.
articles.authors: Okombo J, Ndiaye T, Qahash T, Moura IMR, Gil-Iturbe E, Hagenah LM, Bridgford JL, Bonatto V, Ward KE, Yeo T, Narwal SK, Orta LV, Anderson I, Dhingra SK, Pasaje CFA, Park H, Kim J, Guido RVC, Angulo-Barturen I, Niles JC, Mancia F, Uhlemann AC, Mok S, Quick M, Winzeler EA, Leroy D, Llinás M, Thathy V, Fidock DA
articles.details: In vitro and mouse model studies of ZY19489 (triaminopyrimidine in Phase IIb trial) selected for resistance, identifying a novel PfCRT mutation mediating low-grade resistance with major fitness cost and substantial reduction in parasite growth. Parasites with ZY19489 resistance lost chloroquine resistance and became hypersusceptible to piperaquine. ZY19489 blocks PfCRT-mediated efflux of piperaquine and chloroquine, creating an evolutionary trap where resistance development paradoxically restores drug susceptibility. Authors propose ZY19489-piperaquine combination to prevent clinical resistance emergence.

articles.headline: AI decision-support system improves treatment protocol adherence
articles.title_original: Enhancing Physicians' Adherence to the 2023 Sudan Malaria Case Management Protocol Using AI as an Intervention Tool.
articles.authors: Muhammed A, Ibrahim S, Mohammed AAY, Mohamed Ahmed AK, Idris MYM, Yousif MMO, Omer ITA, Rababah A, Sherfeldin Mohammed HE, Ahmed SAE, Osman Ahmed Osman M, Musa Mohamed ZH, Mohamed Ibrahim LA, Musa Hussain EY, Ali HKM, Mohamed Mustafa FA, Eltahir SME, Elhag MES, Kanan Ahmed AA
articles.details: Two-cycle clinical audit (July-September 2025) with 50 physicians at Al-Managil Teaching Hospital in Sudan. AI-based clinical decision-support system (CDSS) implemented with training covering diagnostics, treatment, and special groups. Recognition of 2023 malaria case management protocol increased from 38% to 100% (risk difference +62%, 95% CI 48-76, p<0.001); awareness of IV/IM artesunate as first-line rose from 53% to 98% (risk difference +45%, 95% CI 32-58, p<0.001). Mean physician compliance increased from 50.7% to 96.8% (p<0.001). Residual gaps persisted in microscopy reporting and G6PD testing.

articles.headline: Computational vaccine candidate covers 87% world population
articles.title_original: Immunoinformatics-Based Multi-Epitope Vaccine Design Against P. falciparum-Causing Malaria: A Computational Approach.
articles.authors: Larbi AA, Mensah C, Korankye R, Darkwah AA, Agyemang EB, Ayikpah IY, Kwarteng A
articles.details: Computational vaccine design study identifying B cell, cytotoxic T cell, and helper T cell epitopes from five conserved P. falciparum antigens. Proposed multi-epitope subunit vaccine construct predicted to cover 87.07% of world population based on HLA allele frequency data. Vaccine showed appropriate physicochemical properties, solubility, and strong stable interactions with TLR4. Immune simulations indicated development of long-lasting memory B cells and CD4+ T cell responses. Authors emphasize findings require experimental validation.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/3 deletions cause false-negative RDTs in Malawi
articles.title_original: First report of pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions compromising HRP2-based malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Malawi.
articles.authors: Louis JM, Mazigo E, Jun H, Lee WJ, Syahada JH, Fitriana F, Muh F, Chun W, Park WS, Lee SJ, Na S, Lu F, Han ET, Han JH
articles.details: Cross-sectional study (December 2020-June 2021) of 1582 participants from two referral hospitals in Malawi (Mzuzu n=1186; Lilongwe n=396). Malaria prevalence 22.9% Mzuzu vs 45.2% Lilongwe. RDT sensitivity 78.5% (95% CI 74.6-82.1%) vs microscopy 64.8% (95% CI 60.3-69.1%); specificity 93.6% RDT vs 95.4% microscopy. Dual pfhrp2/pfhrp3 gene deletions found in 15.0% Lilongwe and 10.4% Mzuzu isolates; all dual-deleted samples were false-negative by RDT but positive by microscopy and qPCR, compromising RDT diagnostic accuracy.

articles.headline: Urban schoolchildren show higher malaria than rural peers
articles.title_original: Malaria among schoolchildren in urban, peri-urban and rural settings in Eastern Ethiopia: repeated cross section study.
articles.authors: Merga H, Degefa T, Birhanu Z, Abiy E, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: Repeated cross-sectional study (November 2023 and May 2024) of primary schoolchildren across three ecological settings in Eastern Ethiopia. High transmission season malaria prevalence 5.1% (95% CI 3.84-6.7): urban 7.3%, peri-urban 4.97%, rural 2.9% (p=0.007). Low transmission season 2.24% (95% CI 1.48-3.37). P. falciparum dominant at 83% (high) and 59% (low) transmission. Non-use of insecticide-treated nets (AOR 2.2; 95% CI 1.2-3.99) and presence of under-five children in household (AOR 1.9; 95% CI 1.1-3.38) associated with infection in high season.

articles.headline: Chrysosplenetin reverses artemisinin resistance via transporters
articles.title_original: Chrysosplenetin acts as a homeostasis stabilizer with dual-function in shattering Plasmodium berghei K173 resistance to artemisinin driven by both ABC transporters and heme-ROS/GSH axis.
articles.authors: Ji H, Huang Y, Tian J, Bo R, Heng X, Yang J, Huang Y, Wang X, Tan Q, Gao H, Chen L, Chen J
articles.details: In vivo study in Mdr1a wild-type and knockout mice infected with artemisinin-resistant P. berghei K173, comparing artemisinin (ART) monotherapy to combination with chrysosplenetin (CHR, a polymethoxy flavonol from Artemisia annua). Combination therapy achieved 100% survival, 30% parasite inhibition rate, and ~20% parasitemia in resistant mice. CHR reversed ART resistance by modulating ABC transporter expression (Mrps, Bcrp) and ROS/GSH redox balance, with effects partially dysregulated by Mdr1a deficiency. PI3K/AKT-mTOR and MAPK signaling pathways involved.

articles.headline: Malaria's socioeconomic toll extends beyond health impacts
articles.title_original: Malaria in their words: thematic analysis of online narratives from Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Zhang SH, Nwankwoala O, Jakazi CS, D'Amiano AJ, Kabuya JB, Kapungu K, Laker M, Ippolito MM
articles.details: Phenomenological thematic analysis of 160 online narrative accounts from GiveDirectly recipient stories (Kenya n=121, Uganda n=33). Two major themes emerged: vulnerability contexts (compromised housing, occupational settings, agricultural and pre-dawn labor) and experienced consequences including severe financial burden from treatment, household resource strain, lost workdays, business closure and asset liquidation, school dropout, deferred higher education, and lasting physical disabilities (mobility impairment, vision loss, cognitive challenges) leading to social exclusion and diminished long-term well-being.

articles.headline: Indigenous knowledge and spiritual beliefs shape health-seeking
articles.title_original: Indigenous knowledge systems and malaria prevention in Zimbabwe: a market basket analysis.
articles.authors: Chapoterera B, Marume A, Midzi N, Naidoo K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 480 adults (18+ years) in Manicaland Province, Zimbabwe. Malaria experience in previous three months: 36.7% of respondents and 54.0% of families. Association rule mining identified strong patterns: individuals visiting church prophets highly likely to avoid traditional healers (support 0.66, confidence 0.91, lift 1.08). Primary sources of indigenous knowledge were older people (27.7%), informal discussions (7.7%), and personal observations (5.6%). Health-seeking behavior influenced by complex interactions between spiritual, traditional, and biomedical systems.

articles.headline: Correction to malaria KAP survey in South Sudan
articles.title_original: Correction: Survey on knowledge, attitudes and practices (KAP) of malaria prevention and control among Chinese expatriates in South Sudan.
articles.authors: Su Q, Yu Y, Chen H, Gao Y, Li J
articles.details: Correction to a published survey on knowledge, attitudes and practices of malaria prevention and control among Chinese expatriates in South Sudan. Original article assessed healthcare-seeking behaviors and prevention practices. No abstract provided for the correction notice.

articles.headline: Statistical guidance for vector-suppression trial design
articles.title_original: Requirements for designing cluster randomised control trials to detect suppression of malaria vector population densities.
articles.authors: Hancock PA, Hui TJ, Epopa PS, Milogo A, McKemey AR, Yao FA, Diabaté A, Burt A
articles.details: Statistical modeling of cluster randomized control trial (CRCT) design requirements using empirical 5-year time series of Anopheles gambiae complex vector densities from four villages in western Burkina Faso. For 50% mosquito suppression effect with 90% statistical power: 21-22 clusters needed targeting all gambiae species, 24-26 clusters for An. coluzzii only, 60-66 clusters for An. gambiae only. For 90% suppression: 9-10 clusters sufficient. Both parallel and step-wedge designs evaluated.

articles.headline: Recrudescent severe falciparum malaria despite artemisinin
articles.title_original: Recrudescent severe malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum following treatment in a Taiwanese patient: a case report.
articles.authors: Tseng YC, Cheng MY, Lin PB, Hsu SF, Hsu JJ, Shen YC, Ho MW, Hsiao CT, Hsueh PR
articles.details: Case report of 66-year-old previously healthy Taiwanese man with P. falciparum severe malaria (20% parasitemia, multi-organ failure including acute kidney injury, liver failure, thrombocytopenia, respiratory distress) following return from Central Africa. Initial treatment with IV artesunate and oral artemether-lumefantrine achieved parasite clearance. Recrudescence with recurrent fever and parasitemia occurred four weeks later. Revised therapy with artesunate combined with doxycycline and clindamycin achieved parasite clearance within two weeks. No pfk13 mutations detected.

articles.headline: Healthcare workers unprepared for R21 vaccine rollout
articles.title_original: General knowledge and attitude of healthcare professionals and infrastructural readiness prior to implementation of malaria vaccine in selected hospitals in Tororo district, Uganda: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Asio L, Besigye I, Puleh SS, Nazziwa N, Ocan M
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 211 healthcare professionals at Tororo General Hospital and St. Anthony Hospital in Tororo district, Uganda (conducted before R21 vaccine rollout planned for April/May 2025). Unfavorable attitudes toward R21 vaccine: 70% (95% CI 63-76%) versus favorable 30% (95% CI 25-37%), with concerns about affordability, accessibility, and effectiveness. Knowledge of malaria vaccine predominantly poor: 99% (95% CI 96-100%) demonstrated poor knowledge versus 1% (95% CI 0.5-4.4%) with good knowledge. Limited hospital infrastructure for vaccine implementation.

articles.headline: 30% of Ghanaian herbal antimalarials show potent P. falciparum activity
articles.title_original: The efficacy of selected Ghanaian herbal antimalarials against laboratory strains and clinical isolates of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Yeboah SN, Ansong M, Clotworthy D, Domie PE, Chirawurah JD, Awiaga CK, Mensah C, Sakyi ME, Adika E, Morang'a CM, Awandare GA, Aniweh Y, Amenga-Etego LN
articles.details: Twenty commercially available herbal antimalarial formulations from Ghana were tested in vitro against five laboratory strains and two clinical isolates of P. falciparum. Six formulations (30%) exhibited potent activity with IC50 values below 50 μg/ml, while three showed trophozoite-stage-specific activity. The majority demonstrated poor anti-plasmodial activity, highlighting regulatory gaps in efficacy testing and quality monitoring of traditional medicines in Ghana.

articles.headline: Elevated opsonic phagocytosis associated with increased malaria risk
articles.title_original: A Novel Phagocytosis Assay Reveals the Association between Elevated Opsonic Phagocytosis Levels and an Increased Risk of Clinical Malaria in a Low-Malaria Transmission Area.
articles.authors: Odhiambo EO, Mellencamp KA, Holla P, Ayodo G, John CC
articles.details: A novel opsonic phagocytosis assay in a Kenyan highland area with low transmission (5,753 individuals, 2007–2017) showed elevated opsonic phagocytosis levels associated with increased clinical malaria risk in individuals 5–14 years old (adjusted odds ratio 3.32; 95% CI 1.13–9.77) and ≥15 years old (adjusted odds ratio 4.85; 95% CI 1.02–23.00), but not in children <5 years old. Opsonic phagocytosis responses appear to mark prior malaria exposure rather than protective immunity.

articles.headline: Parasites maximize transmission by sensing two within-host cues
articles.title_original: Malaria parasites can optimize transmission investment by sensing two within-host cues.
articles.authors: Wang A, Greischar MA, Mideo N
articles.details: Theoretical modeling of Plasmodium chabaudi examined optimal transmission investment strategies. Parasites sensing two cues—log-transformed infected and uninfected red blood cell abundance—accurately track infection progression and achieve fitness equivalent to optimal time-varying strategy, enabling terminal investment as infection ends. Single-cue sensing allowed only partial fitness recovery, while two-cue sensing increased virulence but reduced robustness to environmental fluctuations.

articles.headline: Oxidative stress pathways in malaria warrant investigation as targets
articles.title_original: Oxidative stress in malaria and typhoid fever: A scoping review of the pathogenic mechanisms and therapeutic implications.
articles.authors: Netongo PM, Eric MB, Kamdem SD, Tchoutang AM, Mbacham WF
articles.details: A scoping review of literature from 2000 to May 2024 examined oxidative stress mechanisms in malaria and typhoid fever. Both diseases elevate reactive oxygen species production with insufficiently compensatory antioxidant responses, further exacerbated in co-infections. Commonly used antimalarial and antibacterial drugs influence oxidative-antioxidant dynamics, suggesting oxidative-antioxidant balance as a potential target for adjunctive therapies and vaccines.

articles.headline: Molecular analysis reveals morphological misidentification of vectors
articles.title_original: Diagnostic puzzle of Anopheles species using morphological vs. molecular approach: a case study in Tripura, a hyperendemic malaria state, Northeast India.
articles.authors: Sihag AK, Rajkonwar J, Gogoi P, Vezhavendan S, Chellappan S, Satpute SS, Malakar P, Debnath S, Baruah K, Kaur H, Subbarao SK, Bhattacharyya DR, Bhowmick IP
articles.details: A longitudinal entomological survey (2019–2024) in Tripura, Northeast India identified An. fluviatilis morphologically, but molecular confirmation revealed misidentified An. minimus with three novel haplotypes. Morphological variant characteristics (hypermelanization, absent presector pale spots, palpal banding) caused winter An. minimus to be misidentified as An. fluviatilis, which may not occur in the region. Combined morphological-molecular methods are essential for accurate vector identification.

articles.headline: Permethrin-treated baby wraps reduce clinical malaria by 66%
articles.title_original: Permethrin-Treated Baby Wraps for the Prevention of Malaria.
articles.authors: Boyce RM, Shook-Sa BE, Ndizeye R, Baguma E, Giandomenico D, Cassidy CA, Eshun S, Siedner MJ, Staedke SG, Ntaro M, Juliano JJ, Reyes R, Mulogo EM
articles.details: A double-blind randomized controlled trial in Uganda enrolled 400 mother-child pairs (children 6–18 months) assessed permethrin-treated wraps (retreated every 4 weeks) plus long-lasting insecticide-treated nets versus sham-treated wraps plus nets over 24 weeks. Clinical malaria incidence (fever plus positive rapid diagnostic test) was 0.73 per 100 person-weeks in intervention versus 2.14 in control (incidence rate ratio 0.34; 95% CI 0.23–0.51; p<0.001).

articles.headline: Temporal model reveals vaccine efficacy modification by infection history
articles.title_original: Generalized nonparametric temporal modeling of recurrent events with application to a malaria vaccine trial.
articles.authors: Heng F, Sun Y, Xu J, Gilbert PB
articles.details: A generalized nonparametric temporal modeling approach was developed to analyze recurrent malaria infections. Applied to the MAL-094/MAL-095 vaccine efficacy trial, the method examined how new malaria infection risk changes over time and is modified by prior infection history or vaccination. The approach revealed how vaccine efficacy against new infections is modified by infection history over time.

articles.headline: High SP-resistance but no artemisinin resistance post-PMC, Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Molecular Markers of Resistance to Antimalarial Drugs Three Years After Perennial Malaria Chemoprevention in Sierra Leone.
articles.authors: Chen H, Owusu-Kyei K, Fombah AE, Williams J, García-Fernández C, Rovira-Vallbona E, Bofill A, Quintó L, Figueroa-Romero A, Mac-Abdul F, Samai M, Mayor A, Menéndez C
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 300 febrile children (aged 2–5 years) in Sierra Leone (June–August 2021) identified P. falciparum resistance markers. Frequencies: pfdhfr triple mutant (N51I/C59R/S108N) 99.5%, pfdhps A437G 92.1%, pfdhfr/dhps quintuple mutant 4.6%, pfcrt CVIET 36.6%, and no validated pfK13 artemisinin-resistance markers. The sustained low quintuple mutant prevalence supports continued use of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine–based intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy.

articles.headline: PfCSP genetic diversity in Vietnam highlights vaccine design challenges
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and natural selection of circumsporozoite surface protein in Vietnam Plasmodium falciparum isolates.
articles.authors: Võ TC, Lê HG, Kang JM, Trinh NTM, Van Khanh C, Cho M, Quang HH, Na BK
articles.details: Analysis of the pfcsp gene in 95 P. falciparum isolates from five endemic provinces in Vietnam (2018–2022), compared with 836 global sequences, revealed significant polymorphisms in N-terminal (A98G, 19-amino acid insertion) and C-terminal regions (Th2R and Th3R domains). The central repeat region demonstrated high polymorphism due to varying numbers and types of peptide repeat motifs. These findings provide insights into pfcsp genetic diversity relevant to vaccine design.

articles.headline: Complete mitochondrial genome of orang-utan malaria parasite P. pitheci
articles.title_original: The mitochondrial genome sequence of the Bornean orang-utan (Pongo pygmaeus) malaria parasite, Plasmodium pitheci.
articles.authors: Dharmayanthi AB, Pratama R, Sánchez KL, Nurillah A, Fadiah F, Komara, Nugraha RTP, Saputra S, Nurkanto A, Fahroni A, Irwanto A, Syafruddin D, Adnan IA, Saragih JR, Coutrier FN, Setiadi W, Lobo NF
articles.details: The complete mitochondrial genome sequence of Plasmodium pitheci (5,871 bp), a malaria parasite of Bornean orang-utans, was determined and analyzed phylogenetically. Results confirmed that previously identified Plasmodium sp. VM, VS, and Pongo clade A and B isolates correspond to P. pitheci, while Pongo clade C taxonomy remains unresolved. This genomic foundation enables development of species-specific molecular diagnostics for improved orang-utan diagnosis and zoonotic malaria control.

articles.headline: Green-synthesized MgO nanoparticles show in vitro antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Antimalarial potential of Matricaria chamomilla-derived MgO nanoparticles against Plasmodium falciparum strains: an experimental study.
articles.authors: Farzaneh Z, Hanifian H, Nateghpour M, Hasanpour G, Raeisi A, Shabani M, Farivar L, Khezri A, Dehdast SA, Shahsavari S
articles.details: Green-synthesized magnesium oxide nanoparticles (30–80 nm) derived from Matricaria chamomilla extract were evaluated in vitro against chloroquine-sensitive (3D7) and chloroquine-resistant (K1) P. falciparum strains. Half-maximal inhibitory concentrations against 3D7 were 0.19–0.22 mg/mL, and against K1 were 0.41–0.45 mg/mL. Results support further preclinical investigation of green-synthesized nanoparticles as potential novel antimalarial agents.

articles.headline: Malaria in pregnancy declined from 60.4% to 51.6% in Ghana
articles.title_original: Trend of malaria test positivity rate among pregnant women, Savannah Region, Ghana, 2018-2022.
articles.authors: Abesig WJ, Nindow JAC, Bagonluri AC, Sarfo AK, Issahaku GR, Odikro MA, Akowuah G, Bandoh DA, Kenu E, Kubio C
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of malaria in pregnancy in Ghana's Savannah Region (2018–2022) showed overall test positivity of 54.5%, declining from 60.4% in 2018 to 51.6% in 2022. Positivity was highest in mothers <20 years old (59.3%) and in Bole District (70.1%). Disparities in malaria burden across districts suggest need for strengthened sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy and insecticide-treated net utilization.

articles.headline: Late-arresting GAPs superior to early-arresters as malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: The path from early- to late-liver stage arresting genetically attenuated parasites as a malaria vaccination strategy.
articles.authors: Lamers OAC, Franke-Fayard BMD, Roestenberg M, Krol JMM
articles.details: This review synthesizes research on genetically attenuated parasites (GAPs) that arrest during liver stage development as malaria vaccines. Late-arresters (6–7 days) demonstrated superior efficacy compared to early-arresters (24–48 hours), highlighting the importance of liver stage immunity for sterile protection. The review examines pre-clinical research leading to GAP creation and discusses safety and efficacy of existing GAPs with focus on large-scale implementation.

articles.headline: SMC plus Vitamin A integration adds $0.24 per child cost
articles.title_original: A cost analysis comparing seasonal malaria chemoprevention with and without Vitamin A supplementation among under-5 children in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Oresanya OB, Arije O, Fadipe J, Rotimi K, Phillips A, Maxwell K, Shekarau E, Onwu N, Bazant ES
articles.details: This cost analysis compared seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) delivered alone versus integrated with Vitamin A supplementation (VAS) in Nigerian under-5 children. SMC-only campaign cost $0.94 per child (n=168,820); SMC+VAS cost $1.18 per child (n=170,681). Integration added $27,492 ($0.24 per child) to implementation costs. Authors concluded the modest cost increment was acceptable given potential health benefits for reducing child mortality from malaria and malnutrition.

articles.headline: ICU management and monitoring of severe cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Management and monitoring strategies for severe cerebral malaria: a guide for the intensivist.
articles.authors: Vathi S, Corriero A, Rodríguez EE, Moreau A, Bogossian EG, Baggiani M, Hites M, Sonneville R, Taccone FS, Salvagno M
articles.details: This review examines severe cerebral malaria (CM) in adults, defined by impaired consciousness (Glasgow Coma Score < 11), with mortality of 15–25% and long-term neurological sequelae. CM pathogenesis involves sequestration of parasitized erythrocytes in cerebral microvasculature, immune hyperactivation, blood-brain barrier disruption, cerebral edema, and elevated intracranial pressure. Intravenous artesunate is first-line treatment. The review emphasizes tailored neuromonitoring approaches and protocolized management within intensive care settings to minimize secondary brain damage.

articles.headline: Hsp70-Hsp40 complex inhibition arrests parasite DNA replication
articles.title_original: Inhibition of the assembly of Plasmodium Hsp70-1 and Hsp40 complex blocks DNA replication by destabilizing ribonucleotide reductase subunit-2.
articles.authors: Alkhatib I, Garg D, Tabassum W, Tanishka K, Bhattacharyya MK, Bhattacharyya S
articles.details: This study showed Plasmodium falciparum ribonucleotide reductase subunit-2 (PfR2) maturation requires interaction with Hsp40 cochaperone PfYdj1 and Hsp70-1. Small molecule 116-9e disrupted the PfYdj1-PfHsp70-1 association, destabilizing PfR2 and significantly reducing dNTP production, which arrested parasite replication. Combination of 116-9e with benzo-hydroxamate, a PfR2 catalytic inhibitor, showed synergistic effects. This dual-inhibitor strategy targeting the Hsp70-Hsp40 complex is proposed as an antimalarial approach.

articles.headline: Subcutaneous R21 vaccine achieves sterile protection in mice
articles.title_original: Subcutaneous administration of the malaria R21/Matrix M vaccine and immune complex formation with anti-circumsporozoite protein mAb 2A10 elicit protective efficacy in mice.
articles.authors: Mukhopadhyay E, Camacho CL, Hill AVS, Salman AM
articles.details: This preclinical study evaluated R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine administered via subcutaneous route and as an immune complex (R21:anti-NANP mAb 2A10) in BALB/c mice. R21/Matrix-M via subcutaneous injection was immunogenic and achieved 100% protective efficacy compared to the standard intramuscular route. The R21:2A10 immune complex formulation induced sterile protection with only two immunizations. These findings demonstrate novel vaccine delivery strategies and immune complex platforms as potential approaches to improve malaria vaccination outcomes.

articles.headline: Insulin-like peptides regulate egg formation in malaria mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Insulin-like peptides activate egg formation in the Asian malaria mosquito Anopheles stephensi.
articles.authors: Phipps BL, Brown MR, Strand MR
articles.details: This comparative study examined gonotrophic cycle regulation in Anopheles stephensi versus Aedes aegypti. In An. stephensi, two insulin-like peptides (AsILP3 and AsILP4) stimulated the vitellogenic phase and yolk deposition into oocytes, while ovary ecdysteroidogenic hormone showed no stimulatory activity. An. stephensi females had lower nutrient stores than Ae. aegypti and required a blood meal for vitellogenic stimulation. Mated females receiving ecdysteroids from males produced more eggs than unmated females.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine costs and delivery barriers in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Costs, delivery strategies and implementation challenges for malaria vaccines: a rapid review of literature.
articles.authors: Isah A, Obi C, Okeke I, Gwe M, Onwujekwe O
articles.details: This rapid systematic review examined 30 studies on malaria vaccine delivery, costs, and implementation challenges (2000–2024). RTS,S/AS01 was most frequently reported (n=23, 76.67%), with efficacy ranging 30–75% and cost-effectiveness of $20–$50 per DALY averted in many settings. Primary delivery strategies involved routine immunization (40% of studies); innovative approaches like drones showed promise. Major barriers included logistical constraints, community acceptance, and inadequate healthcare infrastructure, particularly in remote and underserved communities.

articles.headline: Gamma-delta T-cell exhaustion during chronic malaria infection
articles.title_original: Exploring γδ T-Cell Responses in Malaria and Tuberculosis: Implications for Immunity, Coinfection, and Disease Management.
articles.authors: Mohamud YN, Chaijaroenkul W, Na-Bangchang K
articles.details: This systematic review examined γδ T-cell responses in Plasmodium falciparum malaria. Upon initial P. falciparum infection, Vγ9Vδ2+ cells are rapidly activated, producing proinflammatory cytokines and exerting antiparasitic effects through cytotoxicity and immune modulation. However, chronic or repeated malaria exposure leads to functional exhaustion of Vδ2+ cells, characterized by reduced cytokine responsiveness and shift toward regulatory phenotypes. Understanding subset-specific γδ T-cell dynamics is critical for developing targeted immunotherapies and vaccine strategies.

articles.headline: Rainfall predicts malaria risk with 2–8 week lag in Uganda
articles.title_original: Quantifying the Lagged Effects of Climate Variables on Malaria Risk in Eastern Uganda.
articles.authors: Kim S, Nabukeera B, Taremwa Y, Ng'etich M, Otieno F, Cygu S, Kajungu D, Kiragga A, Tozan Y
articles.details: This epidemiological study analyzed weekly malaria case data from the Iganga-Mayuge Health and Demographic Surveillance System site in Uganda (July 2018–February 2023) using distributed lag nonlinear modeling with remotely sensed climate data. Rainfall exceeding 200 mm/week showed a 2–8 week lagged association with increased symptomatic malaria risk; temperature showed no statistically significant lagged association. Risk sensitivity to climate variables varied by age group, with school-aged children showing lower sensitivity than other populations.

articles.headline: Rhesus macaque model of Plasmodium fragile malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: Clinical and Pathological Outcomes of Experimental Plasmodium fragile Infection in a Rhesus Macaque.
articles.authors: Prusak JE, Nemphos SM, Green HC, Fell SL, Midkiff CC, Rodgers A, Perret J, Grasperge B, Vail K, Minkah N, Wilder B, Londono-Renteria B, Blair RV, Manuzak JA
articles.details: This study examined experimental infection of a male rhesus macaque with Plasmodium fragile-infected erythrocytes as a translatable model for human malaria. The infected macaque developed clinical signs including anemia, changes in red and white blood cell distribution, altered red blood cell morphology, and histopathological alterations in liver, lungs, heart, and brain. Parasitized red blood cells were observed sequestered in vasculature of major organs (spleen, liver, lung, brain).

articles.headline: AcAS mutations confer modest artemisinin resistance in parasites
articles.title_original: Acetyl-CoA synthetase mutations affect the susceptibility of Plasmodium falciparum to antimalarial drugs.
articles.authors: Zhao W, Xiang Z, Zeng W, Qin Y, Pan M, Wu Y, Duan M, Mou Y, Liang T, Zhang Y, Liu C, Tang X, Huang Y, Yang G, Cui L, Yang Z
articles.details: This study identified Plasmodium falciparum acetyl-CoA synthetase (PfAcAS) mutations S868G and V950I in parasites that recrudiesced following artesunate and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine treatment. Using CRISPR/Cas9 technology, researchers engineered transgenic parasite lines carrying these mutations and tested in vitro susceptibility to antimalarials. The S868G and V950I mutations, alone or in combination, affected susceptibility to artemisinin derivatives (dihydroartemisinin, artesunate, artemether) and chloroquine, though absolute changes in susceptibility were modest.

articles.headline: Complex III serves dual mitochondrial functions in parasites
articles.title_original: Dual Function of Mitochondrial Complex III in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Rell RS, Shukla A, Morrisey JM, Okoye IC, Mather MW, Vaidya AB
articles.details: This study examined dual functions of Plasmodium falciparum mitochondrial Complex III—ubiquinone regeneration for pyrimidine biosynthesis and imported mitochondrial protein processing. Using conditional transgenic parasites expressing mitochondrial processing peptidase a (MPPα), researchers demonstrated that MPPα remains essential even when electron transport is bypassed. MPPα knockdown resulted in hypersensitivity to proguanil, which synergizes with mtETC inhibitors like atovaquone. Proteomics revealed MPPα associates with multiple mitochondrial-targeted proteins and all Complex III components.

articles.headline: Hydropower development shows no significant malaria transmission increase
articles.title_original: Hydropower development and malaria transmission: A geospatial econometric study.
articles.authors: Thomas CJ
articles.details: Geospatial econometric study in Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire, and Gabon assessed whether hydropower dam construction causally increased P. falciparum incidence or prevalence in children aged 2-10 years within 15 km using Difference-in-Differences regression. No statistically significant increase in malaria transmission was detected post-dam (p=0.089–0.990 across sites). Environmental and socio-economic factors were more strongly associated with malaria transmission than hydropower presence.

articles.headline: Three novel compounds show anthelmintic activity in C. elegans
