articles.title_original: Discovery of small molecules with anthelmintic potential in the Medicines for Malaria Venture's COVID and Global Health Priority Boxes using an infrared-based assay for Caenorhabditis elegans motility.
articles.authors: Sun YU, Liu LJ, Caffrey CR
articles.details: Screened 400 small molecules from MMV's COVID and Global Health Priority boxes using an infrared-based C. elegans motility assay. Primary and secondary screening identified 12 hits; three novel bioactives (flufenerim, flucofuron, indomethacin) showed EC50 values of 0.211–23.174 µM. Counter-toxicity assays on HEK293 cells indicated varying degrees of toxicity (EC50 0.453 to >100 µM).

articles.headline: AL and ASAQ highly effective for uncomplicated falciparum in Liberia
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Liberia, 2022-2023.
articles.authors: Koko VS, Skrip L, Kumeh O, Washington T, Diallo MO, Kafuko J, Inyang U, Ponce de León GF, Zhou Z, Laird VR, Zawolo J, Getahun B, Harmon-Gray WM, Plucinski M, Schultz JS
articles.details: WHO therapeutic efficacy study comparing artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) in 305 children aged 6–59 months with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria (August 2022–July 2023). PCR-corrected ACPR at day 28 exceeded 90% threshold: AL 95.9–100% and ASAQ 94.4–100% by site. Day 3 parasitemia was 0% for AL and 1.3% for ASAQ with no adverse events reported.

articles.headline: Vector Guard ITN superior to alternatives against resistant mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Vector Guard®, a mosaic alpha-cypermethrin and piperonyl butoxide-treated net, for the control of pyrethroid-resistant malaria vectors: a non-inferiority experimental hut evaluation in Benin.
articles.authors: Nounagnon J, Gbegbo M, Agbevo A, Vigninou E, N'dombidjé B, Ngufor C
articles.details: Experimental hut trial evaluated Vector Guard®, a novel alpha-cypermethrin and piperonyl butoxide-treated net, against pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles gambiae s.l. in Benin. Vector Guard® showed significantly higher mortality than Olyset® Plus (unwashed: 36.4% vs 17.5%, p<0.001; after 20 washes: 17.2% vs 8.7%, p<0.001) and demonstrated non-inferiority (OR 2.71, 95% CI 2.26–3.24) with superior wash retention of active ingredients.

articles.headline: Only 9.3% of caregivers seek facility care for child malaria
articles.title_original: Care-seeking behaviour of caregivers for suspected malaria in under-five children in a Southwestern State of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akinrinade OT, Isere EE, Ajayi IO
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 301 caregivers in Nigeria found that 94.0% suspected malaria in under-five children within 12 months, but only 9.3% sought health facility treatment; 37.4% purchased drugs from vendors and 25.2% used home remedies. Multivariate logistic regression identified female caregivers (aOR 3.32, 95% CI 1.29–8.54), urban residence (aOR 4.25, 95% CI 1.72–10.48), proximity within 5 km to facility (aOR 3.38, 95% CI 1.03–11.07), and good malaria knowledge (aOR 4.16, 95% CI 1.61–10.77) as significant predictors of facility visits.

articles.headline: Monoclonal antibody targets cerebral malaria-associated PfEMP1
articles.title_original: A monoclonal antibody selectively recognizing PfEMP1 proteins associated with cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Dalgaard N, Olsen RW, Adams Y, Mendez BL, Rudbaek JJ, Bisholm SAG, Mousiliou A, Walker MR, Hviid L, Tahar R, Ndam NT, Barfod L, Jensen AR
articles.details: Monoclonal antibody mAb02 selectively recognizes DBLβmotif-positive PfEMP1 proteins in A and B/A PfEMP1 subsets with dual affinity for ICAM-1 and EPCR receptors. The antibody inhibits PfEMP1 binding to ICAM-1 and blocks adhesion of infected erythrocytes to endothelial cells. The mAb02 epitope is located in an unfolded linker region crucial for ICAM-1 interaction, targeting a broadly conserved epitope implicated in cerebral malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: NFATc1 regulates LAG3 expression in malaria-infected CD8+ T cells
articles.title_original: NFATc1 regulates LAG3+CD8+ T cells in the spleen of mice infected with Plasmodium yoelii NSM.
articles.authors: Pan X, Mo F, Pan L, Xiao W, Liang G, Xie X, Yuan H, Wei H, Zhao S, Li L, Jia L, Xie H, Huang J
articles.details: In mice infected with Plasmodium yoelii NSM, splenic CD8+ T cells showed significantly upregulated LAG3 expression with enhanced activation, responsiveness, and cytokine production. Flow cytometry and single-cell RNA sequencing revealed activated NFATc1 co-expressing with LAG3 in CD8+ T cells. Dual-fluorescence reporter assays identified NFATc1 as a key transcription factor binding the LAG3 promoter; siRNA knockdown of NFATc1 reduced LAG3 expression.

articles.headline: Novel PfSSB inhibitors effective against artemisinin-resistant strains
articles.title_original: Targeting Plasmodium falciparum Single-Stranded DNA-Binding Protein: Discovery of New Scaffold Compounds Effective against Drug-Sensitive and Artemisinin-Resistant Strains.
articles.authors: Naik B, Bhalerao P, Shekhar S, Varghese SA, Makwana S, Mandal CC, Bhatt TK, Dhar SK, Prusty D
articles.details: Study identified compounds PPG and 9-HPF targeting Plasmodium falciparum single-stranded DNA-binding protein (PfSSB), essential for apicoplast DNA replication. PPG and 9-HPF showed IC50 values of 54.95 and 9.13 µM against drug-sensitive 3D7 strain and resistance indices of 0.79 and 1.26 against artemisinin-resistant C580Y strain. Both compounds demonstrated low toxicity and minimal cross-resistance risk to existing antimalarials.

articles.headline: Antioxidant nanoparticles improve survival in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Self-Assembling Antioxidant Nanoparticles Increase Survival and Reduce Disease Symptoms of Severe Malaria.
articles.authors: Yoshitomi T, Hayashi K, Araki T, Annoura T, Nagasaki Y
articles.details: Study developed self-assembling redox nanoparticles (RNP) to mitigate excessive reactive oxygen species in severe malaria using Plasmodium berghei-induced mouse model. RNP remained in circulation >24 hours and significantly decreased ROS levels compared to conventional antioxidant TEMPOL, leading to marked improvements in disease severity and survival. RNP also suppressed erythrocyte membrane oxidation and fragility caused by infection.

articles.headline: Economic empowerment and prevention reduce asymptomatic malaria
articles.title_original: Economic empowerment and various preventive strategies play a role in reducing asymptomatic malaria towards the end of the rainy season.
articles.authors: Okpala C, Umeh I, Anagu LO
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study of 130 adults in Nigeria (November 2024) found P. falciparum prevalence of 26.15%. Covering water containers around homes was associated with reduced asymptomatic malaria risk. Those who could afford malaria prevention tools (ITNs, insecticides, repellents) showed lower odds of infection (aOR 0.29–0.27, 95% CI 0.07–1.24) compared to those unable to afford prevention measures.

articles.headline: Novel nucleoside phosphonates inhibit P. falciparum purine metabolism
articles.title_original: C1'-Branched Acyclic Nucleoside Phosphonates as Inhibitors of Plasmodium Falciparum 6-Oxopurine Phosphoribosyltransferase.
articles.authors: Frydrych J, Keough DT, Xia H, Slavětínská LP, Dračínský M, Česnek M, Travis J, Chavchich M, Edstein M, Hocková D, Guddat LW, Janeba Z
articles.details: Study designed C1'-branched acyclic nucleoside phosphonates targeting Plasmodium falciparum HGXPRT (6-oxopurine phosphoribosyltransferase), essential for parasite purine metabolism. The best compounds achieved Ki values of 0.4 µM for parasite enzyme and 0.7 µM for human enzyme. A phosphonodiamidate prodrug demonstrated IC50 of 4.3 µM against drug-sensitive P. falciparum (3D7) grown in human erythrocytes.

articles.headline: R21/Matrix-M vaccine induces cross-reactive sporozoite-blocking antibodies
articles.title_original: Repertoire, function, and structure of serological antibodies induced by the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine.
articles.authors: McDaniel JR, Voss WN, Bowyer G, Rush SA, Spencer AJ, Bellamy D, Ulaszewska M, Goike J, Gregory S, King CR, McLellan JS, Hill AVS, Georgiou G, Ewer KJ, Ippolito GC
articles.details: Proteomic analysis of IgG responses to R21/Matrix-M vaccine in 10 malaria-naïve volunteers showed the vaccine induced polarized anti-NANP antibodies using predominantly IGHV3-30/3-33 genes with minimal somatic mutation. These vaccine-generated antibodies cross-reacted with the N-terminal junction CSP epitope absent from the R21 construct. NANP-specific monoclonal antibodies derived from polyclonal repertoires blocked sporozoite invasion in vitro and prevented parasitemia in vivo.

articles.headline: Anopheles funestus forages early evening and indoors in Zambia
articles.title_original: Early-evening indoor and outdoor foraging by major malaria vectors in Nchelenge, Zambia.
articles.authors: Gebhardt ME, Markle HL, Krizek RS, Mbewe D, Mulenga FK, Lupiya JS, Barnett EE, Muleba M, Ippolito MM, Stevenson JC, Moss WJ, Norris DE
articles.details: Three-week CDC light trap study in 24 Nchelenge households (2019 dry season) examined malaria vector behavior. Anopheles funestus dominated collections (n=1,865, 68.2%), mainly indoors (73.4%), with highest EIR at 18.74 bites/person/6months among indoor-caught specimens at inland locations. Infected mosquitoes foraged before 22:00, and activity appeared similar before and after this time, indicating malaria transmission occurs beyond traditional late-night, indoor settings.

articles.headline: Country-specific PMC models designed through codesign workshops
articles.title_original: Tailoring malaria control interventions to suit local context: codesign of perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) programmes through the Plus Project.
articles.authors: Center M, McGirr AL, Skjefte M, Ekobika LC, Bahibo H, Nhantumbo E, Wakpo B, Ogouyemi Hounto A, Yah Kokrasset C, Candrinho B, Malhotra K, Clarke SE, Gosling RD, Kouakou J
articles.details: Codesign workshops held in Benin, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and Mozambique engaged national and international stakeholders to develop country-specific perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) models. Four distinct PMC strategies emerged, each with 4–8 contact points tailored to local health system delivery platforms, operational logistics, and political contexts. The collaborative process improved programmatic insights and stakeholder buy-in for PMC implementation.

articles.headline: Anti-epo antibodies associated with younger age in malaria
articles.title_original: Serum Anti-Erythropoietin Autoantibodies and Their Association With Younger Age in Paediatric Malaria Cases.
articles.authors: Amponsah FA, Addai-Mensah O, Antwi-Boateng L, Sackey B, Duneeh RV, Acheampong I, Adoba P, Awi DVA, Afriyie EY, Boateng R, Anokye AA, Agyemang V, Doe SK, Appiah SK
articles.details: Study of 90 Ghanaian children (60 with malaria: 30 with and 30 without anemia; 30 healthy controls, ages 1–10) measured anti-erythropoietin (epo) antibodies. Antibodies detected in 5.6% of malaria cases versus 0% of controls (not statistically significant). Among malaria-anemia patients, 5.0% had antibodies versus 3.37% without anemia (p=0.640). Antibody positivity strongly associated with younger age and elevated epo concentrations but not with P. falciparum infection or malarial anemia.

articles.headline: Review of WHO-approved malaria vaccines and candidates
articles.title_original: Malaria vaccines: Current developments and immunological insights.
articles.authors: Verma A, Ranjha R, Singh K, Yadav V, Bhaskar A, Dwivedi VP, Mohan M
articles.details: Review of malaria vaccines addresses the global burden (263 million people affected, with children under 5 most susceptible) and the threat from drug-resistant parasites. WHO has approved only two malaria vaccines. The review examines current vaccine prospects, future candidates targeting different Plasmodium life stages, host immune responses against malaria, and ideal vaccine requirements.

articles.headline: PvCSP vaccine candidates for Plasmodium vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite surface protein (PvCSP) as a vaccine candidate: a brief review.
articles.authors: Alves KCS, Mourão EMV, Melo JM, Almeida MEM, Oliveira JGS, Mariúba LAM, da Costa VCA
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: Review of circumsporozoite protein (PvCSP) vaccine development for P. vivax, historically underprioritized compared to P. falciparum despite endemic importance. PvCSP demonstrated immunogenic potential and partial protection in preclinical studies. Clinical trials, however, have not yet shown consistent protective efficacy. Article explores historical approaches and innovative strategies to overcome challenges in P. vivax vaccine development.

articles.headline: 15% malaria prevalence in pregnant women, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Malaria in Pregnancy and Predisposing Factors in Jor District of Gambella Region, Southwest Ethiopia: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Oguta O, Mohammed J, Umer U, Solomon ET
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study of 446 pregnant women in Jor District, Gambella, Ethiopia (February–April 2024) using microscopy detected 15.2% malaria prevalence (95% CI 11.4–17.5): P. falciparum 8.96%, P. vivax 2.7%, mixed infection 3.6%. Risk factors included non-ownership of ITN (AOR 5.74), low altitude (AOR 5.26), stagnant water near residence (AOR 3.61), lack of malaria knowledge (AOR 2.7), and no formal education (AOR 1.32). Public health actions recommended education, ITN access, and environmental modification.

articles.headline: ASGM communities show limited knowledge of P. vivax
articles.title_original: Malária incubada: a mixed methods analysis on knowledge and experiences on Plasmodium vivax and asymptomatic malaria infections in a hard-to-reach and mobile population in the Amazon.
articles.authors: Plessis L, Jimeno Maroto I, Lambert Y, Galindo M, Bardon T, Vreden S, Suarez-Mutis M, Figueira A, Bordalo JM, Douine M, Sanna A
articles.details: CUREMA pre-intervention assessment (2022) evaluated malaria knowledge in artisanal and small-scale gold mining communities across Suriname, French Guiana, and Amapá, Brazil using qualitative interviews (25 semi-structured, 14 group, 38 informal) and quantitative survey (539 participants, 73.5% male, median age 38). Most recognized mosquito vectors; awareness of asymptomatic forms and recurrences was common but attributed to inadequate treatment. Knowledge of P. vivax characteristics and radical treatment options was limited, informing context-specific elimination strategies.

articles.headline: P. falciparum remodels host membrane microdomains during development
articles.title_original: Dynamics of detergent-resistant membranes of Plasmodium falciparum during blood stage development.
articles.authors: Sferra G, Fratini F, Birago C, Megliorini L, Mochi S, Lalle M, Paone S, Olivieri A, Ferreri C, Sansone A, Ponzi M, Currà C, Pizzi E
articles.details: Quantitative proteomics and lipid analysis characterized detergent-resistant membranes (DRMs) in P. falciparum schizonts and gametocytes, revealing conserved and stage-specific structural features. Parasites depend on host cholesterol for membrane synthesis and remodel host DRMs during invasion, internalize erythrocyte junctional complex components, and establish parasitophorous vacuole membrane. DRM interactome analysis identified a core of fundamental proteins maintained across stages with peripheral, stage-specific functions.

articles.headline: Major CSP epitope repeats superior in vaccine candidates
articles.title_original: Comparison of major, minor and junctional circumsporozoite protein epitopes for malaria vaccine design.
articles.authors: Ryan E, Brown D, Harrison W, Foor S, Ashraf K, Bolton JS, Flores-Garcia Y, MacGill RS, Locke E, Bergmann-Leitner E, Roth AE, Robben PM, Matyas G, Soisson L, Miller R, Batchelor AH, Zavala F, Dutta S
articles.details: Head-to-head comparison of circumsporozoite protein (CSP) epitope variants in tobacco mosaic virus (TMV)-based vaccines using intravenous challenge model. Minor repeat (NPNV) candidates did not confer sterile protection despite high cross-reactivity and in vitro inhibition. Constructs displaying major repeats (NPNAx20, NPNAx5) and junctional+minor repeat epitopes induced sterile protection, with equivalent parasite burden reduction as RTS,S. TMV-NPNAx20 selected for clinical-grade manufacture.

articles.headline: P. vivax primary infections cause more illness than relapses
articles.title_original: The probability of Plasmodium vivax acute illness following primary infection and relapse in Papua New Guinea.
articles.authors: Ross A, Koepfli C, Timinao L, Hardy D, Thüring T, Loeffel M, Kiniboro B, Felger I, Mueller I
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 1–3-year-old children in Papua New Guinea (16-month follow-up) with fortnightly case detection (fever ≥500 parasites/µL) and bimonthly parasitemia testing used Bayesian modeling to estimate primary infections versus relapses (genotyping-confirmed). Probability of illness declined exponentially with cumulative prior infections (acquired immunity). Relapses caused fewer clinical episodes than primary infections; later relapses within the same cohort had lower symptomatic risk. Relapses drove approximately 50% of clinical P. vivax illness despite causing 80% of parasitemia.

articles.headline: Pyramax® 92.5% efficacy despite artemisinin resistance in Vietnam
articles.title_original: Therapeutic efficacy monitoring of pyronaridine-artesunate (Pyramax®) in treating uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Gia Lai province, Vietnam from 2022 to 2023.
articles.authors: Van Thanh N, Quang HH, San NN, Cuong NK, Trinh NTM, Van Khanh C, Edgel KA, Nguyen HC, Letizia AG, Birrell GW, Martin NJ, Edstein MD, Chavchich M
articles.details: Open-label trial of 120 patients in artemisinin-resistant area (Gia Lai, Vietnam) treated with pyronaridine-artesunate (Pyramax®) once daily for 3 days plus primaquine. PCR-adjusted adequate clinical-parasitological response at Day 42: 92.5% (95% CI 85.5–96.2). Median parasite clearance 84 hours, fever clearance 36 hours. Partial artemisinin resistance noted (50.4% with parasitemia at 72 hours). Eight recrudescent cases had lower median pyronaridine concentrations (33.3 ng/mL) versus parasite-free patients (47.6 ng/mL), suggesting reduced drug exposure may cause treatment failures.

articles.headline: Computational structure metric enables high-affinity malaria antibody design
articles.title_original: A Single Structure-Derived Computational Metric Predicts High-Affinity Antibody Selection Against a Malaria Antigen.
articles.authors: Dale GA, Chen B, Koo JH, Wecera M, Kratochvil S, Batista FD
articles.details: Computational approach derived a structural metric from predicted protein structures to guide affinity maturation of PfCSP-targeting antibodies. Framework explored over 3×10^47 sequence variants using germinal-center-like principles, successfully generating high-affinity variants of CIS43 antibody and extending to unrelated L9 antibody through optimization of homotypic and antigen-binding interactions. Coupling structure-based screening with evolutionary selection enables systematic discovery of high-affinity malaria vaccine antibodies.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence low in lymphedema population, Uganda
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Malaria Among Individuals Living With Lymphedema in Kamwenge District, Western Uganda.
articles.authors: Mwesigye V, Tebulwa JB, Musinguzi B, Muzafaru T, Zamarano H, Nkubi Bagenda C, Mulogo E, Byarugaba F, Herbert I
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 154 individuals with lymphedema (71.4% female, mean age 54.7 years) in Kamwenge District, Uganda. P. falciparum was detected in 3.3% (5/154) of participants. Most had bilateral lower limb lymphedema and resided in rural areas. The study underscores the need for integrated healthcare approaches in areas where both malaria and lymphedema are endemic.

articles.headline: Rwanda tailors ACT strategies by regional kelch13 resistance profiles
articles.title_original: Strategies for mitigating emerging artemisinin-based antimalarial drug resistance in Rwanda: a promising approach for managing therapies in malaria-endemic countries.
articles.authors: Muvunyi CM, Gashema P, Mbituyumuremyi A, Iradukunda PG, Siddig EE, Niyonzima JD, Hakizimana E, Umulisa N, Noor AM, Mugabo Semahore J, Tuyishime A, Harelimana JD, Umuhire J, Butera Y, Nsanzimana S
articles.details: Rwanda's national malaria control program deployed targeted first-line antimalarials based on regional drug-resistance profiles. A675V Kelch13 mutations (associated with artemether-lumefantrine resistance) were predominant in the western region, prompting shifts to artesunate-pyronaridine; R561H mutations were predominant in eastern and central regions, leading to dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine recommendations; artemether-lumefantrine was maintained in the southern region where R561H prevalence was low. This data-driven approach demonstrates how genomic surveillance of Kelch13 mutations can inform regional drug deployment strategies.

articles.headline: SMC costs in Sahel: indirect productivity losses drive expenses
articles.title_original: Estimating the opportunity cost of seasonal malaria chemoprevention implementation in Burkina Faso, Mali and Senegal.
articles.authors: Owusu R, Gilmartin C, Diawara H, Bocoum F, Ndiaye O, Ruisch A, Aryeetey GC, Jainie D, Kokovena M, Walker D, Nonvignon J
articles.details: Cost analysis across Mali, Burkina Faso, and Senegal (Apr–Nov 2022, 376–398 caregivers per country, 41–127 community health volunteers, 42–96 supervisors). Financial costs per SMC dose ranged from $0.99 (Mali) to $1.51 (Senegal); economic costs from $2.96 (Mali) to $3.14 (Burkina Faso). Annual costs per child receiving four doses: financial $3.97–$6.05; economic $11.85–$12.57. Indirect costs (productivity losses of caregivers, volunteers, healthcare workers) comprised 50–66% of total economic costs.

articles.headline: Environmental and socioeconomic factors synergistically drive malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Malaria in pregnancy: a holistic exploration of synergistic association through environmental, sociocultural, and socioeconomic lenses in Pru east municipality, Ghana.
articles.authors: Bardoe D, Hayford D, Bio RB, Osei E
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 456 pregnant women in Pru East Municipality, Ghana (Apr–Aug 2024). Malaria in pregnancy prevalence was 21.5% (95% CI 17.7–25.3), with P. falciparum predominating at 14.7%; co-infection with P. malariae occurred in 3.5%, and high parasitemia in 6.4%. Structural equation modeling confirmed synergistic associations of environmental factors (proximity to stagnant water, Volta Lake, livestock shelters), sociocultural factors (spiritual attribution, outdoor sleeping), and socioeconomic factors (rural residence, delayed ANC, limited LLIN use, low IPTp-SP uptake) with malaria in pregnancy risk.

articles.headline: High-resolution melting PCR reliably diagnoses P. falciparum and P. vivax
articles.title_original: Optimization of the real-time PCR platform method using high-resolution melting analysis and comparison with sequencing and phylogenetic analysis for developing optimal malaria diagnostic methods.
articles.authors: Ahmadi O, Sharifi Y, Saeed M, Reihani A, Shamsian SA, Moghaddas E, Mirahmadi H, Etemadi S, Fotouhi-Ardakani R, Zarean M
articles.details: Diagnostic optimization study of 300 samples from southeastern Iran. HRM-PCR targeting the 18S SSU rRNA region detected 15 P. falciparum cases (5%) and 14 P. vivax cases (4.66%), achieving 2.73-degree differentiation between species and complete agreement with sequencing results (13 P. falciparum [4.33%] and 16 P. vivax [5.33%]). HRM method demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity compared to conventional PCR and is proposed as a reliable diagnostic method with automation potential for clinical settings.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance kelch13 mutations spreading from Asia to Africa
articles.title_original: Understanding the global rise of artemisinin resistance: Insights from over 100,000 Plasmodium falciparum samples.
articles.authors: Balmer AJ, White NFD, Ünlü ES, Lee C, Pearson RD, Almagro-Garcia J, Ariani C
articles.details: Comprehensive review aggregating kelch13 surveillance data from 112,933 P. falciparum samples collected globally between 1980 and 2023. Artemisinin partial resistance has spread widely across Southeast Asia over two decades and recently emerged in East Africa. Recent increases in resistance-associated mutations in Africa mirror patterns observed in Southeast Asia 10–15 years prior, raising concerns about potential rapid spread fueled by fitness costs, treatment strategies, and local epidemiological dynamics.

articles.headline: Nanoparticle therapy effective against drug-resistant malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Antiplasmodial Activity of Green-Synthesized MgO Nanoparticles Using Achillea millefolium Against Chloroquine-Resistant and-Sensitive Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Khamsehpour N, Hanifian H, Nateghpour M, Raeisi A, Shabani M, Hassanpour G, Khezri A, Dehdast SA, Farivar L, Shahsavari S
articles.details: In vitro evaluation of green-synthesized magnesium oxide nanoparticles (MgO NPs) derived from Achillea millefolium against chloroquine-sensitive (3D7) and chloroquine-resistant (K1) P. falciparum strains. The most effective formulation demonstrated IC50 values of 0.17 mg/ml against the 3D7 strain and 0.76 mg/ml against the K1 strain, indicating potent antiplasmodial activity at low concentrations against both susceptible and resistant parasite lines.

articles.headline: Macaque proteomics identifies conserved immune biomarkers for zoonotic malaria
articles.title_original: Comparative serum proteomics of plasmodium-infected free-living macaques in Thailand: Host immune responses and candidate biomarkers for zoonotic malaria.
articles.authors: Ruengket P, Roytrakul S, Tongthainan D, Udom C, Fungfuang W
articles.details: Comparative serum proteomic analysis of 61 wild macaques (Macaca leonina, M. arctoides, M. fascicularis) naturally infected with zoonotic Plasmodium spp. in Thailand. Nested PCR confirmed infections in 20% of M. leonina, 50% of M. arctoides (34.78% zoonotic), and 100% of M. fascicularis. Liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry identified 9,525 serum proteins, of which 698 were differentially expressed. Thirty-six proteins formed robust protein-protein interaction networks linked to immune defense, apoptosis, and DNA repair. Six of nine previously reported malaria-associated proteins were consistently expressed across all species, supporting their potential as conserved biomarkers.

articles.headline: Arterolane-piperaquine non-inferior to artemether-lumefantrine for malaria
articles.title_original: A systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical trials comparing arterolane-piperaquine vs. artemether-lumefantrine for the treatment of uncomplicated falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Mondal A, Basu A, Modak DC, Goswami RP
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 4 randomized controlled trials comparing arterolane-piperaquine with artemether-lumefantrine for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. At 28-day follow-up, PCR-corrected risk difference was 0 (95% CI −0.02 to 0.02), and PCR-uncorrected was 0.11 (95% CI −0.06 to 0.27). At 42 days, PCR-corrected risk difference was 0.01 (95% CI −0.01 to 0.03), and PCR-uncorrected was 0.17 (95% CI −0.05 to 0.38). Adverse effect profiles were similar (risk difference −0.01, 95% CI −0.02 to 0.00), with common adverse effects including vomiting, anemia, and abdominal pain.

articles.headline: Engineered antibody L9 variant broadens recognition of CSP epitopes
articles.title_original: Anti-Malaria Antibody Engineering Broadens Recognition Motifs and Reveals New Homotypic Interactions that Enhance Protective Breadth.
articles.authors: Chun J, Tripathi P, Flores-Garcia Y, Madan B, Lee GA, Fahad AS, Lei H, Teng IT, Hurlburt NK, Flynn BJ, Pancera M, Miura K, Zhou T, Idris AH, Zavala F, Seder RA, Kwong PD, DeKosky BJ
articles.details: Structure-guided antibody engineering of monoclonal L9, which binds with high affinity to the NVDP minor repeat on P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP). Site saturation mutagenesis and yeast display identified variant L9_yd19, which retained high affinity against NVDP repeats while gaining high affinity for NANP major repeats. In vivo challenge studies showed L9_yd19 protection against transgenic Plasmodium encoding PfCSP with two widely spaced minor repeats (modestly more potent than wild-type L9) and against NANP-repeat-only PfCSP.

articles.headline: Splenic macrophage polarization drives malaria severity, guides therapeutics
articles.title_original: Emerging Role of Splenic Macrophage in Malaria Pathogenesis and Immunity.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Kalkal M, Das J
articles.details: Review synthesizing evidence on splenic macrophage roles in malaria pathogenesis and immunity (1955–2025). Splenic macrophages participate in parasite clearance, antigen presentation, and immune regulation, with capacity to polarize along a functional continuum rather than in a strictly dichotomous M1/M2 fashion. Dynamic macrophage polarization under different microenvironments facilitates essential immune balance, preventing excessive inflammation while maintaining effective immunity. Emerging evidence indicates that splenic macrophages substantially influence malaria severity and clinical outcomes.

articles.headline: Case demonstrates IV artesunate efficacy in severe P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: [Artesunate and Severe Malaria: The Importance of Proper Treatment Steps and Laboratory Monitoring].
articles.authors: Özer D, Çavuş İ, Özbilgin A
articles.details: Case report of a 52-year-old male with high-parasitemia P. falciparum infection from Liberia presenting with thrombocytopenia (platelet count 11,000/μL), metabolic acidosis, acute renal failure (creatinine 1.69 mg/dL), and severe complications including elevated transaminases (AST 57 U/L, GGT 160 U/L, LDH 629 U/L) and marked hyperbilirubinemia (total bilirubin 8 mg/dL). Intravenous artesunate treatment resulted in rapid parasitemia decline and clinical response. Regular parasitemia monitoring was critical for guiding treatment decisions and reducing morbidity and mortality.

articles.headline: ACT compliance varies between fund sources in Nigeria clinics
articles.title_original: Compliance with the Use of Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy in the Treatment of Uncomplicated Malaria amongst Primary Healthcare Workers in Anambra State, Nigeria - The Role of Funding.
articles.authors: Chukwuka UB, Ibeh CC, Adogu PO, Chukwuka JO
articles.details: Cross-sectional study reviewed 1,536 malaria cases and surveyed 82 health workers at 32 primary healthcare centers in Nigeria. Compliance with guidelines for uncomplicated malaria treatment was 83.2% overall (89.1% in Global Fund-supported facilities vs. 76.5% in government-supported facilities, P=0.00). ACT stockouts occurred in 43.8% of Global Fund facilities vs. 100% of government facilities (P=0.00). Better compliance correlated with increased drug availability, access to guidelines, case management aids, and supervision frequency.

articles.headline: Pregnant women know prevention but don't use it consistently
articles.title_original: Knowledge and Utilisation of Malaria Prevention Strategy among Pregnant Women in Some Selected Primary Health Centres in Maiduguri, Borno State, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Samuel G, Abdullahi M, Danladi SS, Jonah JH, Tweneboah E, Musa A
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 407 pregnant women in Nigeria found 80.8% had good knowledge of malaria prevention but 79.1% showed poor overall usage. LLIN usage was 60.9%; only 20.6% had never used larval source management. Knowledge significantly associated with trimester of pregnancy (P=0.0044), gravidity (P=0.019), and education (P=0.001), but not religion. Authors recommend targeted health education and community-level monitoring to improve utilization despite adequate knowledge.

articles.headline: Lost-to-follow-up in pregnancy malaria screening driven by facility quality
articles.title_original: Determinants of loss to follow-up among pregnant women living in a high malaria burden setting in Northern Uganda.
articles.authors: Ictho J, Segala FV, Novara R, Olung N, Patti G, Papagni R, Atim G, Totaro V, Olal L, De Vita E, Adongo J, L'episcopia M, Okori S, Dall'Oglio G, Putoto G, Saracino A, Di Gennaro F, Lochoro P
articles.details: Retrospective cohort study of 1,558 pregnant women in Northern Uganda (July 2022–June 2024) examined determinants of loss to follow-up in antenatal malaria screening. Of 1,558 women, 871 (55.9%) were lost to follow-up (defined as missing outcome data 30 days post-delivery). Protective factors: higher education (aOR=0.75, p=0.0798), primigravida status (aOR=0.73, p=0.0275), and experiencing malaria during pregnancy (aOR=0.61, p<0.0001). Women at Aboke Health Center IV were 5.57 times more likely to be LTFU than those at Aber Hospital (aOR=5.57, 95% CI 4.08–7.71, p<0.0001).

articles.headline: Novel CPR mutations drive pyrethroid resistance in African malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Genomic Drivers of Pyrethroid Resistance Escalation in the Malaria Vector Anopheles funestus Across Africa.
articles.authors: Gadji M, Tazokong HR, Kouamo MFM, Tchouakui M, Wondji M, Mugenzi LMJ, Irving H, Hearn J, Ibrahim SS, Wondji CS
articles.details: Multi-omics study (2014–2021) analyzed temporal RNA-Seq and whole-genome sequencing in Anopheles funestus mosquitoes across Africa to identify pyrethroid resistance mechanisms. Key findings include overexpression of P450s, cuticular proteins, and novel genes (V-ATPase, tubulin alpha-1, transposase), plus genomic variations in cytochrome P450 reductase (CPR): N70I mutation increased from 0% (2009) to 80% (2021) in Malawi; 5.9 kb promoter duplication detected in Ghana. Transgenic validation in Drosophila confirmed CPR-70I enhances pyrethroid resistance when co-expressed with P450-CYP6P9a.

articles.headline: Modeling diagnostic threat from Plasmodium falciparum gene deletions
articles.title_original: Modeling the threat of pfhrp2/3 gene deletions for malaria diagnosis.
articles.details: Study title indicates modeling of the threat posed by pfhrp2/3 gene deletions for malaria diagnosis, though the abstract was not provided.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/3 deletions threaten diagnostics across sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Global risk of selection and spread of Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein 2 and 3 gene deletions.
articles.authors: Watson OJ, Tran TN, Zupko RJ, Symons T, Thomson R, Visser T, Rumisha S, Dzianach PA, Hathaway N, Kim I, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Slater H, Okell L, Gething P, Ghani A, Boni MF, Parr JB, Cunningham J
articles.details: Mathematical modeling study assessed global risk of Plasmodium falciparum pfhrp2/3 gene deletions (detected in 40 of 47 surveyed countries), which reduce detection by widely-used rapid diagnostic tests. Model predicted southward spread through East Africa within 20 years and identified four priority countries yet to switch diagnostics as consistently high-risk under various assumptions. Authors note alternative rapid diagnostic tests not scaled up and no WHO-prequalified combination tests available yet, creating an emerging public health crisis.

articles.headline: First US locally acquired malaria in 20 years: genetically linked outbreaks
articles.title_original: Public Health Response to the First Locally Acquired Malaria Outbreaks in the US in 20 Years.
articles.authors: DeVita TN, Morrison AM, Stanek D, Drennon M, Sarney E, Brennan W, Tomson K, Blackmore C, Broussard K, Duwell M, Blythe D, Rothfeldt L, Dulski T, Blount K, Ledford S, Blackburn D, Wallender E, Barratt JLN, Raphael BH, Lenhart AE, Ridpath AD, Mace KE, Williams SG, Beard CB, Parise ME, McElroy PD
articles.details: Qualitative study of 10 locally acquired malaria cases in four US states (May–December 2023)—the first such outbreaks in 20 years. Patients (mean age 39.5±15.0 years, 7 male) had no recent international travel history. P. vivax and P. falciparum detected in multiple Anopheles mosquitoes. Targeted amplicon sequencing showed Florida P. vivax cases shared one strain (Central/South American origin), while Texas and Arkansas had genetically distinct P. vivax cases. Maryland P. falciparum was of African origin. Outbreaks remained contained within individual counties.

articles.headline: School-age children and village location drive malaria heterogeneity in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Prevalence and risk factors associated with infections caused by Plasmodium parasites at micro-geographic level in three villages of Muheza district in Tanga region, north-eastern Tanzania.
articles.authors: Challe DP, Francis F, Seth MD, Tupa JB, Madebe RA, Mandara CI, Kigadye ES, Makene VW, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: Cross-sectional community survey of 1,134 individuals in three villages in Muheza district, Tanzania (June 2021) found overall Plasmodium prevalence of 19.2% (microscopy) and 24.3% (RDTs). School children (5–<15 years) had highest prevalence: 27.3% by microscopy and 37.6% by RDTs (p<0.001). Household closed eaves reduced infection risk (aOR=0.91, 95% CI 0.85–0.97, p<0.001). Prevalence varied significantly across adjacent villages (14.7–24.6% by microscopy, p=0.001), demonstrating high micro-geographic heterogeneity.

articles.headline: Super-resistant SP parasites emerging in western Kenya
articles.title_original: Regional variation in sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance genotypes and haplotypes of Plasmodium falciparum dihydrofolate reductase and dihydropteroate synthase genes in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Bungei J, Ouma C, Zhong D, Oyweri J, Lee MC, Zhou G, Atieli H, Githure J, Wang C, Yan G
articles.details: Cross-sectional genotyping of 112 P. falciparum isolates from endemic (Kisumu, n=60) and epidemic (Kisii, n=52) regions in western Kenya identified sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance markers. Pfdhps mutations showed high frequency: 437G (99%), 540E (97.1%), 436H (28.8%). Quintuple mutant SGEAA-IRNI predominated (57.89% Kisumu, 48.94% Kisii). Sextuple mutant HGEAA-IRNI significantly higher in Kisii (44.68% vs. 16.31%, p<0.05). Authors conclude fully resistant and super-resistant haplotypes have approached fixation, signaling emergence of super-resistance in population.

articles.headline: Korhogo region shows high malaria transmission heterogeneity by season
articles.title_original: Malaria epidemiology in the Korhogo area, Northern Côte d'Ivoire: baseline assessment prior to a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Tchiekoi NB, Zogo MB, Ahoua Alou LP, Somé A, Soma DD, Coulibaly I, Koné A, Fournet F, Taconet P, Dahounto A, Baba-Moussa L, Dabiré RK, Moiroux N, Koffi AA, Pennetier C, Assi SB
articles.details: Baseline epidemiological study in Korhogo, Côte d'Ivoire (2016–2017) with 398,424 person-weeks of passive surveillance and three cross-sectional surveys. Mean weekly malaria incidence was 0.25 per 100 person-weeks (130 per 1000 person-years). Case prevalence ranged 13.4% (dry season) to 43.4% (late rainy season), highest in children under 5 years with steep age-related decline. Thick blood smears in six villages showed parasite prevalence 85.4–100%; P. falciparum comprised 97% of single infections, with mixed infections with P. malariae and P. ovale also detected.

articles.headline: Ghana achieves dramatic reduction in asymptomatic malaria in schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Reduction in Asymptomatic falciparum malaria infection amongst schoolchildren in three ecological zones of Ghana between 2017 and 2023.
articles.authors: Abban RL, Amenga-Etego L, Busayomi A, Acquah FK, Dzotefe GB, Kornu VE, Kusi KA, Amoah LE, Afrane YA
articles.details: Cross-sectional surveys comparing schoolchildren in Ghana in 2017 (n=1,109) and 2023 (n=1,154) showed substantial decline in asymptomatic P. falciparum infection. By microscopy, prevalence fell from 16.05% (2017) to 1.21% (2023); by PCR from 39.95% to 17.33%. Sahel zone showed greatest improvement (60% decrease). Submicroscopic infections declined from 24.35% (2017) to 16.46% (2023), with forest zone remaining highest at both timepoints (47.01% and 28.28%).

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine sustains 36–51% malaria protection over 50 months
articles.title_original: Sustained efficacy of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine over 50 months of follow-up when used in full-dose or fractional-dose regimens in young children in Ghana and Kenya: final results from an open-label, phase 2b, randomised controlled trial.
articles.authors: Osei-Tutu L, Kariuki SK, Lee CK, Fabre R, Bii DK, Adjei S, Oneko M, Attobrah Sarfo MA, Ockenhouse CF, Schuerman L, Buabeng PBY, Bakari A, Atieno C, Kotoh-Mortty MF, Otieno K, Ntiamoah Y, Sang T, Bollaerts A, Westercamp N, Ansong D, Agbenyega T, Samuels AM, Ofori-Anyinam O
articles.details: Phase 2b RCT in Ghana and Kenya (2017–2022) with 1,500 children evaluated RTS,S/AS01E vaccine efficacy over 50 months across full-dose and fractional-dose regimens. Vaccine efficacy against clinical malaria was 36% (R012-20), 51% (R012-14-26-38), 43% (Fx012-14-26-38), and 41% (Fx017-20-32) (all p<0.001). Cases averted per 1,000 full-dose equivalents ranged 353–1,151 depending on regimen. Fractional-dose regimens appeared equally effective, potentially reducing cost and increasing vaccine availability. Vaccine was well tolerated with only 5 serious adverse events possibly related and no vaccine-related deaths.

articles.headline: Mosquito microbiota may mediate malaria parasite transmission timing
articles.title_original: Do microbes mediate rhythmic malaria parasite-mosquito vector interactions?
articles.authors: Riithi N, Mooney JP, Reece SE
articles.details: Perspective piece integrating chronobiology with evolutionary ecology to propose that mosquito gut microbes mediate rhythmicity in vector susceptibility and parasite development. Links recently discovered microbiota rhythmicity with vector immunity, metabolism, and parasite transmission success across the blood-feeding cycle. No specific empirical results provided; highlights urgent need to understand how changes in mosquito biting rhythms affect transmission timing.

articles.headline: Droplet digital PCR detects hidden low-density malaria infections in Thailand and Sudan
articles.title_original: High-sensitivity detection of asymptomatic malaria in high-risk Thai populations using droplet digital PCR.
articles.authors: Kramyoo M, Boonpeng K, Janchoo C, Siriwattanakul K, Phankhod K, Vongthanom N, Kritprayoch P, Yokanit W, Srisutham S
articles.details: Study of 403 Thai residents in endemic areas and 270 returning workers from Sudan detected asymptomatic Plasmodium infections using droplet digital PCR targeting 18S rRNA and species-specific markers. Prevalence was 0.25% in Tak, Thailand, and 7.41% in Sudan workers; P. falciparum microscopy detected only 2.59% in Sudan samples. ddPCR demonstrated superior sensitivity for low-parasitemia infections, with median concentration 0.95 copies/μL (range 0.16–7033.43), supporting surveillance for hidden parasite reservoirs.

articles.headline: Nanoparticle-encapsulated artemisinins improve parasite clearance in mouse malaria
articles.title_original: Effects of artesunate-loaded nanoparticles on Plasmodium berghei treatment in a mouse model.
articles.authors: Wen H, Li Y, Luo J, Zhu W, Hide G, Feng X, Lun ZR, Lai DH
articles.details: Plasmodium berghei-infected mice treated with zinc-based metal-organic framework nanoparticles containing artesunate, dihydroartemisinin, or artemisinin. Artesunate nanoparticles (16 mg/kg, three injections over 24 hours) significantly improved parasite clearance and prevented hyperparasitaemia (≥5%), whereas a 12-hour dosing interval only delayed hyperparasitaemia. Both dihydroartemisinin and artemisinin nanoparticles showed stronger parasite clearance than non-encapsulated drug controls.

articles.headline: Next-generation LLINs fail to eliminate residual malaria transmission in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Residual malaria transmission in Western Burkina Faso: Vector Behavior, insecticide resistance, and the efficacy limits of next-generation LLINs.
articles.authors: Kouadio KWU, Amara MF, Soma DD, Dabiré RK, Diabaté A, Gnankiné O, Namountougou M
articles.details: Entomological survey in western Burkina Faso (August–October 2023) of Anopheles gambiae s.l. complex found kdr-west and kdr-east insecticide-resistance mutations at varying frequencies with early and late biting behaviors. Entomological inoculation rates were 0.875, 0.437, and 0.063 infectious bites per person per month in August, September, and October respectively. Despite widespread use of next-generation PBO-LLINs and indoor residual spraying, substantial residual malaria transmission persists.

articles.headline: Novel triazole compounds show potent anti-malarial activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: 1,2,4-Triazole amides: Design, synthesis, characterisation, in vitro and in silico evaluation against blood stage parasites of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Tom AA, Rajendran V, Thottasseri AA, Gopan G, Mani M, Kannan T
articles.details: In vitro screening of 30 1,2,4-triazole amide derivatives against Plasmodium falciparum 3D7 (SYBR Green assay) identified three lead compounds with IC50 values: compound 17 (0.87 μg/mL), compound 11 (1.27 μg/mL), and compound 7 (2.17 μg/mL). Against chloroquine-resistant INDO strain, IC50 values were 1.55, 5.70, and 2.30 μg/mL respectively. All three leads showed no hemolytic or cytotoxic effects on blood cells or mammalian cell lines.

articles.headline: Anti-circumsporozoite antibodies mediate tissue-specific sporozoite protection
articles.title_original: Tissue-dependent protection mechanisms of antibodies targeting Plasmodium sporozoites.
articles.authors: Aguirre-Botero MC, Amino R
articles.details: Review of antibodies targeting Plasmodium circumsporozoite protein (CSP), the main surface protein of sporozoites. Anti-CSP antibodies bind to the CSP central repetitive region and mediate protection through tissue-specific mechanisms in animal models and humans. Understanding these multilayered protection mechanisms is critical for improving CSP-based vaccines and antibody-based prophylaxis.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistant kelch13 mutations spreading across East Africa with high partner drug resistance
articles.title_original: Mapping Plasmodium falciparum mutations in Africa: A critical review of emerging drug resistance and implications for malaria control.
articles.authors: Gashema P, Kagame J, Iradukunda PG, Siddig EE, Tessema SK, Tegegne MA, Mazaba ML, Fallah M, Ngamije D, Harelimana JD, Muvunyi CM
articles.details: Critical review of Plasmodium falciparum drug-resistance mutations across Africa (2019–2024). Validated kelch13 mutations predominantly in East Africa: R622I (68% in Eritrea) and R561H (52% in Uganda). Emerging mutations in Rwanda (C469F 36%), Uganda (C469F 59%, P441L 69%), and Tanzania (P441L 20%). Partner drug resistance markers (Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps) prevalent in eastern Africa (30–38%) and western Africa (44–55%). Key drivers: drug pressure (23–35%), antimalarial misuse (10–12.5%), and cross-border population movement (10%).

articles.headline: Novel malaria vector Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis detected in Venezuela with P. falciparum infection
articles.title_original: First detection of Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis (Diptera: Culicidae) in southern Venezuela and its infection with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Conn JE, Bickersmith SA, Moreno JE, Sallum MAM, Sanchez V, Moncada N, Grillet ME
articles.details: First detection of Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis outside Brazil, identified in 88 specimens in southern Venezuela by COI barcode sequencing. One specimen (1.14% infection rate) carried Plasmodium falciparum detected by real-time PCR. The species exhibited anthropophilic behavior, early and late evening biting, presence across rainy and dry seasons, and peridomestic and forest fringe habitat use in gold-mining areas.

articles.headline: ACE inhibitor captopril reduces malaria-induced liver damage in mice
articles.title_original: Captopril reduces liver cytotoxicity in a murine model of Plasmodium chabaudi infection.
articles.authors: Ventura PDS, de Souza DV, Aguiar ACC, Carvalho CPF, Ribeiro DA, Nagaoka MR, Gazarini ML
articles.details: Histopathological study of Plasmodium chabaudi-infected mouse livers comparing chloroquine, captopril (ACE inhibitor), and losartan (AT1 receptor blocker) treatments. Chloroquine alone or combined with vasodilators reduced pathologic changes including sinusoid congestion, leukocyte infiltration, hemozoin deposition, and portal tract inflammation. Captopril particularly reduced liver cytotoxicity induced by malaria infection.

articles.headline: Routine health data can assess malaria intervention effectiveness as surveillance alternative
articles.title_original: Use of Routine Health Data to Monitor Malaria Intervention Effectiveness: A Scoping Review.
articles.authors: Reithinger R, Bisanzio D, Cushnie A, Craig J
articles.details: Scoping review of 49 studies (from 957 PubMed records) examining use of routine health management information systems data to evaluate malaria intervention effectiveness. Studies employed various analytical approaches (modeling and non-modeling) to assess effectiveness of diverse malaria interventions across geographies and populations. Findings demonstrate that routine health data can complement and offer alternatives to surveys and mathematical modeling for ongoing intervention monitoring.

articles.headline: Primaquine accelerates gametocyte clearance with artemether-lumefantrine in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Efficacy and gametocyte carriage in Plasmodium falciparum cases treated with artemether-lumefantrine alone versus with single-dose primaquine in Ethiopia: A randomized controlled study.
articles.authors: Kumsa T, Jima B, Tamiru G, Getu R, Melaku M, Tesfaye Y, Lindtjørn B, Massebo F
articles.details: RCT of 106 symptomatic Plasmodium falciparum patients comparing artemether-lumefantrine (AL) alone versus AL plus single-dose primaquine over 28 days. Parasite DNA detection by PCR on day 7 was lower in AL+primaquine group (5.8%) versus AL alone (18.5%, P=0.09). Cure rates were 100% (AL alone) and 98.1% (AL+primaquine, P=0.31). Gametocyte clearance was significantly faster with AL+primaquine (100% by day 7 versus day 14 for AL alone). Overall recrudescence rate was 2.8%.

articles.headline: Distinct miRNA profiles correlate with lethal versus non-lethal malaria infection severity
articles.title_original: Differential expression of miRNAs dictates the susceptibility/resistance during malaria pathogenesis.
articles.authors: Kataria P, Chaudhary A, Thakur RS, Sharma I, Kalkal M, Chauhan R, Malla WA, Singh K, Das J
articles.details: Small RNA sequencing of splenocytes from mice infected with lethal (Py17XL) or non-lethal (Py17XNL) Plasmodium yoelii strains on day 13 post-infection. Non-lethal infection upregulated miR-875-3p, miR-30a-3p, miR-499-5p, and miR-212-3p; lethal infection upregulated miR-6992-5p, miR-704, miR-7676-3p, and miR-1933-3p. Pathway analysis identified immune signaling targets (MAPK, mTOR, FoxO, Th-17, TGF-β), suggesting distinct miRNA-mediated regulatory mechanisms underlying infection outcome.

articles.headline: Drug resistance markers in Malaria Chemoprevention, Mali
articles.title_original: Frequencies of molecular markers of drug resistance in the context of two different Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) treatment regimens in the Koulikoro health district, Mali.
articles.authors: Traore B, Kane F, Toure M, Jørgensen MHM, Sanogo D, Keita S, Keita M, Sogoba N, Shaw-Saliba K, Diakite M, Shaffer JG, Alifrangis M, Hansson H, Doumbia S
articles.details: Study of 564 P. falciparum samples from 2019–2020 in Koulikoro, Mali, analyzed for resistance markers to SP+AQ and DHA+PQ via NGS and qPCR. Pfdhfr 51I-59R-108N haplotype highly prevalent (>93%), Pfdhps single mutant (~60%). In 2020, combined haplotype Pfdhfr/Pfdhps IRN/IAGKAS and IRN/VAGKGS detected at 9.6% and 1.2%. Significant increase in Pfcrt wild-type from 39% (2019) to 52% (2020, P=0.01). Emerging Pfdhps-431V and PfK13-Y493H variants identified, highlighting need for continued surveillance.

articles.headline: RSL3 and Pyrimethamine synergy against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Synergistic activity of RSL3 and Pyrimethamine to inhibit the proliferation of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Nikolova V, Linnemannstöns K, Zahoruiko A, Ganter M, Lüder CG, Dobbelstein M
articles.details: In vitro study demonstrating RSL3 synergizes with Pyrimethamine and Cycloguanil to suppress P. falciparum proliferation through DHFR inhibition. Similar DHFR inhibitors and alternative ferroptosis inducers did not show synergy. Ferrostatin-1 lipid-peroxidation antagonist largely failed to rescue parasite growth, suggesting mechanism distinct from canonical ferroptosis. RSL3 and related compounds proposed as adjuvants to enhance antimalarial efficacy and potentially overcome drug resistance.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria resurgence in Africa: confronting the challenges.
articles.authors: Ntoumi F, Aklillu E, Asogun D, Ansumana R, Mfinanga S, Yeboah-Manu D, Nachega JB, Zumla A
articles.details: Review discussing challenges of malaria resurgence in Africa; no specific findings or quantitative results provided in abstract.

articles.headline: Malaria hotspot mapping in Northeast India using spatial analysis
articles.title_original: Identification of malaria vulnerable zones in North East Region of India: Spatiotemporal assessment through geographical information system and self-organizing maps.
articles.authors: Mopuri R, Kadiri MR, Bhimala KR, Mutheneni SR
articles.details: Spatiotemporal analysis of reported malaria case data from 2011–2019 across districts in Northeast India using Getis-Ord Gi* statistics and Self-Organizing Maps (SOM) artificial neural network with GIS. Overall malaria incidence decreased 2011–2019. Significant persistent hotspots identified in Meghalaya, Mizoram, and Tripura states. Geospatial framework provided for targeted malaria control interventions and elimination planning.

articles.headline: GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis promotes the progression of neuroinflammation in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Wang J, Zhu Q, Tong G, Yu K, Liang J, Huang Y, Wang Y, Yang C, Li T, Liang W, Li G, Liu X, Sun J, Li Y, Shen Y, Zhao Y
articles.details: Murine cerebral malaria model (P. berghei ANKA infection) showed astrocytes exhibit robust interferon-γ response facilitating CD8+ T cell recruitment into brain. GSDME-dependent pyroptosis in astrocytes amplifies neuroinflammation and exacerbates neuronal injury. Genetic ablation of Gsdme or pharmacological GSDME inhibition with mannose significantly attenuated brain inflammation and damage. First identification of GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis as critical driver of cerebral malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: WHO-based malaria management standard operating protocol
articles.title_original: Standard operating protocol for malaria management.
articles.authors: Pannu AK
articles.details: Evidence-based clinical decision-making protocol emphasizing early parasitological confirmation via peripheral smear or RDT before treatment initiation. Artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) for uncomplicated malaria; intravenous artesunate for severe cases including pregnant women (all trimesters) and children. G6PD testing not required for single-dose gametocytocidal primaquine but essential for radical cure with primaquine or tafenoquine.

articles.headline: Antiplasmodial activity of Wrightia species extracts and tryptanthrin
articles.title_original: Phytochemical analysis and evaluation of antiplasmodial and cytotoxic activities of Wrightia dubia and Wrightia pubescens traditionally used for malaria treatment in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Abd Karim HA, Ilmi H, Widyawaruyanti A, Ismail NH, Zohdi RM, Osman CP
articles.details: Phytochemical analysis of traditional Southeast Asian malaria treatments Wrightia dubia and W. pubescens identified IC50 values: W. dubia wood 1.89±0.06 μg/mL, W. dubia twig 6.38±0.04 μg/mL, W. pubescens twig 1.79±0.03 μg/mL. Isolated compound tryptanthrin showed IC50 2.50±0.05 μM (P. falciparum 3D7 strain) and 3.14±0.023 μM (Dd2 strain). All active extracts and compounds non-toxic to HepG2 cells and non-hemolytic at 100 μg/mL.

articles.headline: Imported malaria presentations in German emergency departments
articles.title_original: The role of emergency departments in the treatment of imported severe and uncomplicated malaria - a retrospective monocentric observational study in a German university hospital.
articles.authors: Wörnle M
articles.details: Monocentric retrospective analysis 2019–2023 of 24 adult patients with imported malaria: 22 P. falciparum, 1 P. ovale, 1 P. vivax. 19 uncomplicated malaria, 5 severe malaria. 20 patients admitted for inpatient care with 15 uncomplicated and 5 severe cases. Zero fatalities during hospital stay. First extensive documentation of diagnosis, clinical parameters, therapy, and outcomes for malaria patients in a large German university hospital ED.

articles.headline: CD47 promotes NK cell sequestration in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: CD47-mediated selective accumulation of mature Ly49h+ NK cells to the brain associates with the pathogenesis of cerebral malaria in mice.
articles.authors: Oakley MS, Malla P, Hayashi CTH, Majam V, Chorazeczewski JK, Okoth WA, Meredith S, KuKuruga M, Akue A, Rotstein DS, Kumar S
articles.details: Murine cerebral malaria model comparing CD47 knockout vs wild-type mice infected with P. berghei ANKA. CD47 absence conferred significant resistance to ECM (log-rank P<0.001) associated with diminished sequestration of NK cells, CD4+ T cells, and CD8+ T cells in brain and lower circulating IL-10 and MIP1β. CD47 critically regulates maturation, activation, and Ly49h-mediated expression of brain-sequestered NK cells driving ECM pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria brain injury: mechanisms and intervention targets
articles.title_original: Pathogenesis of brain injury in cerebral malaria: emerging mechanisms and implications for intervention.
articles.authors: Datta D, John CC
articles.details: Review of pediatric cerebral malaria pathogenesis highlighting cascade of BBB impairment from local hypoxemia, ischemia, and sequestration of infected RBCs; oxidative stress from free heme release; and TNF-α, ROS, CD8+ T cells, and parasite toxins crossing impaired BBB causing neuronal injury. Acute kidney injury and hyperuricemia worsen BBB impairment and cerebral edema. Cytotoxic and vasogenic edema, abnormal blood flow, and increased microvascular hemoglobin present but relationships to brain injury incompletely defined. Neuroimaging and electrophysiology studies show promise.

articles.headline: p-Cymene and Quinine combination therapy in murine malaria
articles.title_original: Therapeutic Effects of Isopropyltoluene (p-Cymene) Alone and in Combination with Quinine Against Malaria Infection Through Modulation of Inflammation and Oxidative Stress.
articles.authors: Shater AF
articles.details: BALB/c mice infected with 1×10⁶ P. berghei malaria-infected RBCs orally treated for 4 days. p-Cymene (CM) 10 mg/kg + Quinine (Qu) 10 mg/kg combination achieved 100% survival rate and 100% parasite growth suppression (p<0.001). Combination reduced tissue malondialdehyde and nitric oxide, upregulated superoxide dismutase and glutathione peroxidase genes (>fourfold), and reduced TNF-α (<1.3-fold) and IL-1β (<1.4-fold) expression.

articles.headline: Malaria-on-a-Chip model for antimalarial efficacy and PK/PD
articles.title_original: Translation of a Human-Based Malaria-on-a-Chip Phenotypic Disease Model for In Vivo Applications.
articles.authors: Rupar MJ, Hanson HM, Botlick BL, Sriram N, Rogers S, Zuniga J, Liu Z, Trimmer SJ, Ciurca JM, Long CJ, McAleer CW, Schmidt S, Favuzza P, Lowe P, Gobeau N, Hickman JJ
articles.details: 3D organ-on-chip model containing human liver, spleen, and endothelium with P. falciparum-infected blood maintained 7 days in serum-free medium sustained all intraerythrocytic life-cycle stages. Chloroquine, lumefantrine, and artesunate tested on 3D7 and W2 strains showed dose-dependent parasite clearance. Chloroquine and lumefantrine induced recrudescence in 3D7 but not W2; artesunate further reduced W2 parasitemia. PK/PD population dynamics modeling predicted human in vivo efficacy and off-target toxicity parameters.

articles.headline: Heat shock proteins identified as potential malaria drug targets
articles.title_original: Comparative Characterization of Plasmodium falciparum Small Heat Shock Proteins and Their Inhibition by Quercetin (3,3',4',5,7-Pentahydroxyflavone).
articles.authors: Timothy FM, Zininga T
articles.details: Study expressed and purified three P. falciparum small heat shock proteins (PfHsp20a, PfHsp20b, PfHsp20c) and characterized their structural and functional properties. All three possessed autonomous chaperone activity with varying efficiency. The plant flavonoid quercetin inhibited protein function in a concentration-dependent manner and suppressed growth of P. falciparum Nf54 and Dd2 strains with IC50 values of 5.4 μM and 7.8 μM, respectively, establishing heat shock proteins as potential novel antimalarial drug targets.

articles.headline: Extracellular vesicles: immunomodulatory role in malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: Extracellular vesicles in malaria pathogenesis and Therapy: Emerging insights and future perspectives.
articles.authors: Abida, Kamal M, Alqurashi AA, Alhuthli SM, Alharbi MA, Alghamdi BJ, Alsharari SH, Khobrani MA, Almakrami JA, Albishri JO, Alshammari AK, Imran M
articles.details: Review examining the role of extracellular vesicles, particularly exosomes derived from the endosomal pathway, in malaria pathogenesis and immune modulation. Exosomes carry bioactive molecules that influence innate and adaptive immunity and mediate complex host-pathogen interactions. The review discusses exosome biogenesis, molecular cargo, their utility as biomarkers for disease diagnosis and prognosis, and their potential as therapeutic targets for malaria intervention.

articles.headline: Severe malaria in travelers requires specific clinical definition
articles.title_original: Severe malaria in travellers-time to create a specific definition.
articles.authors: Barkati S, Libman M, Schwartz E
articles.details: Article argues for establishing a specific clinical definition of severe malaria in traveler populations to standardize diagnostic and management criteria for this patient group.

articles.headline: Multi-omics approaches reveal vector insecticide-resistance mechanisms
articles.title_original: Impact of Omics Studies on Understanding Insecticide Resistance Mechanisms in Sub-Saharan Malaria Vectors: A Systematic Review.
articles.authors: Orogun YG, Ogunlana OO, Taiwo DI, Fadare OA
articles.details: Systematic review of 22 studies (2016–2025) evaluated genomic, transcriptomic, and multi-omics approaches to understanding insecticide resistance in Anopheles vectors in sub-Saharan Africa. Identified key resistance mechanisms including metabolic resistance, target-site mutations, and cuticular changes, as well as novel molecular markers such as CYP450 reductase, UDP-glycosyltransferases, and salivary gland proteins. Findings support integrating omics approaches into routine surveillance to inform region-specific vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Essential apicoplast protein identified as potential drug target
articles.title_original: An Essential Adaptor for Apicoplast Fission and Inheritance in Malaria Parasites.
articles.authors: Blauwkamp J, Rajaram K, Staggers SR, Harrigan O, Doud EH, Xu W, Ke H, Prigge ST, Sun SY, Absalon S
articles.details: Study identified PfAnchor as an essential regulator of apicoplast fission and inheritance in P. falciparum blood-stage parasites. Conditional depletion of PfAnchor disrupted apicoplast fission, leading to incomplete cytokinesis and parasite death. The protein interacts with the dynamin-like GTPase PfDyn2 and serves as the first known apicoplast-specific dynamin adaptor, highlighting parasite-specific organelle division as a potential therapeutic vulnerability.

articles.headline: Dengue infection may trigger Plasmodium vivax relapse
articles.title_original: A Case of Intercurrent Dengue and Probable Relapsing Plasmodium vivax Malaria in a Returned Traveler to India: Case Report and Literature Review.
articles.authors: Kavanoor Sridhar K, Buskandar F, Dhaliwal M, Calloo GV, Boggild AK
articles.details: Case report of a returned traveler with concurrent dengue and P. vivax malaria, with clinical and epidemiological features suggesting dengue triggered P. vivax malaria relapse. Includes literature review of dengue-malaria co-infection and evidence for dengue as a potential precipitant of relapsing malaria in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Antenatal care data correlates with household malaria surveillance
articles.title_original: Antenatal care surveillance for monitoring malaria prevalence and intervention coverage: a multicountry analysis.
articles.authors: Munsey A, Digre P, Hicks J, Wagman J, Robertson M, Alao M, Ogouyemi Hounto A, Gansane A, Debe S, Candrinho B, Uhomoibhi P, Okoko OO, Lemwayi R, Aron S, Kangale CC, Kabamba BM, Miller J, Walker P, Gutman J
articles.details: Multi-country analysis across six sub-Saharan African countries (Benin, Burkina Faso, Mozambique, Nigeria, Tanzania, Zambia) evaluated whether malaria prevalence and intervention coverage data collected at first antenatal care (ANC1) visits could supplement household survey estimates. District-level ANC1 malaria prevalence was strongly correlated with prevalence in children (r=0.79, 95% CI 0.65–0.88, p<0.001) with predictive accuracy of 6.5% mean absolute error. Insecticide-treated net ownership estimates were also well-correlated, though treatment-seeking data were not useful predictors.

articles.headline: Mosquito nutrition modulates malaria vector biting time
articles.title_original: Biting time of day in malaria mosquitoes is modulated by nutritional status.
articles.authors: Oke CE, Rund SSC, Machani MG, Sabtiu ARM, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Afrane YA, Reece SE
articles.details: Semi-field study of female Anopheles gambiae sensu lato examined how nutritional status affected host-seeking behavior timing. Nutritionally deprived mosquitoes were 2–3-fold more likely to host-seek overall and 5–10-fold more likely to seek hosts at earlier times of day compared to well-fed mosquitoes (10% sucrose ± blood meal), which predominantly sought hosts in the second half of the night. These findings suggest mosquito nutritional condition drives behavioral plasticity relevant to residual malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Low pfhrp2/3 deletion prevalence supports RDT reliability
articles.title_original: Analysis of pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions, and the structure and variability of PfHRP2 and PfHRP3 proteins: implications for the performance of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Cubal, Angola.
articles.authors: Mediavilla A, Molina-de la Fuente I, Martínez-Vallejo P, Francisco K, Martins JF, Febrer-Sendra B, Maturana CR, Silgado A, Nindia A, Goterris L, Martínez-Campreciós J, Oliveira-Souto I, Molina I, Berzosa P, Sulleiro E
articles.details: Study of 100 febrile patients in Cubal, Angola identified pfhrp2 exon 1-2 deletions in 7.29% and pfhrp3 exon 1-2 deletions in 11.46% of P. falciparum infections; no exon 2 deletions or double deletions were detected. PfHRP2 protein analysis revealed significant variability in histidine repeats and epitope profiles, particularly in the 3A4 and C1-13 epitopes (present in 100% of samples). The low prevalence of deletions and absence of double deletions support continued utility of PfHRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests, though monitoring is recommended.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence varies temporally across rural Tanzanian regions
articles.title_original: Temporal and spatial trends of the prevalence of infections caused by Plasmodium parasites among rural community members in three regions with varying transmission intensities in Mainland Tanzania.
articles.authors: Challe DP, Petro DA, Francis F, Seth MD, Madebe RA, Mandai SS, Budodo R, Kisambale AJ, Chacha GA, Moshi R, Mbwambo RB, Pereus D, Bakari C, Mbata D, Lyimo B, Kanyankole GK, Aaron S, Mbwambo D, Kajange S, Lazaro S, Kapologwe N, Mandara CI, Makene VW, Ishengoma DS
articles.details: Community surveys of 8,166 individuals aged ≥6 years conducted 2021–2023 across three rural regions in Tanzania (Kigoma, Ruvuma, Tanga) measured temporal and spatial trends in Plasmodium infection prevalence. Overall prevalence was 23.2%, with significant yearly variation (22.9% in 2021, 20.6% in 2022, 26.9% in 2023; p<0.001). Prevalence was highest in school-age children (5–<15 years), males, those with recent fever, those not using bed nets, and those in traditional houses or with low socioeconomic status.

articles.headline: Shorter Plasmodium vivax treatment regimens highly acceptable
articles.title_original: Perceptions of shorter radical cure Plasmodium vivax treatment regimens and their implementation: a qualitative study among stakeholders in Cambodia.
articles.authors: Cassidy-Seyoum SA, Chheng K, Chanpheakdey P, Meershoek A, Hsiang MS, von Seidlein L, Adhikari B, Tripura R, Ley B, Price RN, Lek D, Engel N, Thriemer K
articles.details: Qualitative study of 142 stakeholders (policymakers, healthcare providers, patients) in Cambodia examined perceptions of 7-day high-dose primaquine and single-dose tafenoquine as shorter alternatives to standard P. vivax treatment. Healthcare providers valued shorter regimens for improved adherence and reduced clinical burden. Patients prioritized faster treatment duration and quicker return to work despite willingness to tolerate increased adverse events. Overall acceptance was high in this pre-elimination setting, though policymakers requested additional evidence-generation.

articles.headline: Spatial hotspots and risk factors drive African malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria prevalence and its determinants across 19 sub-Saharan African countries: a spatial and geographically weighted regression analysis.
articles.authors: Yitageasu G, Worede EA, Alemu EA, Tigabie M, Birhanu A, Angelo AA, Aweke MN, Demoze L
articles.details: Geographically weighted regression analysis of Demographic and Health Survey data from 19 sub-Saharan African countries encompassing 126,424 households found overall malaria prevalence of 22.47% (95% CI 22.24%–22.70%), with significant geographic clustering (Global Moran's I=0.159; p<0.001). Major hotspots identified in Benin, Burkina Faso, Togo, Uganda, Rwanda, Republic of Congo, and Mozambique. Key risk factors included rural residence, poor housing, lack of bed nets, and low socioeconomic status.

articles.headline: Knowledge, attitudes, practices, and risk behaviors in Thai-Cambodian border malaria
articles.title_original: Estimating malaria risk behaviours and their determinants among at-risk populations in a pre-elimination setting, Sisaket Province, Thailand-Cambodia border.
articles.authors: Saeung M, Aung PL, Jupatanakul N, Manguin S, Chareonviriyaphap T, Phuanukoonnon S
articles.details: Survey of 402 at-risk individuals in Sisaket Province (late 2022) assessed knowledge, attitudes, and practices (KAP) regarding malaria transmission, treatment, and prevention. Over half demonstrated good KAP levels (51.5%-64.7%). Poor attitudes were associated with lower education (primary/below: aOR=2.2-2.5) and low socioeconomic status (aOR=1.8). Risk behaviors were more frequent in males (aOR=2.8) and those with poor practices (aOR=3.5). Targeted behavior change communication should focus on education and male engagement.

articles.headline: Barriers to malaria prevention in pregnancy, rural Uganda
articles.title_original: Barriers to malaria prevention services in pregnancy: a multi-methods study in rural western Uganda.
articles.authors: Clubine Horowitz RA, Ngelese H, Baguma E, Nyangoma G, Shem B, Ntaro M, Mulogo EM, Boyce RM
articles.details: Cross-sectional and qualitative study of 1,644 pregnant women (Feb 2021–Sep 2022) screened for P. falciparum with RDT; 225 tested positive (13.5%). Primigravidity and younger maternal age were associated with malaria positivity. Barriers identified: delayed testing, chemoprevention hesitancy, financial/transportation constraints, low bed net use, knowledge gaps, and spouse attitudes. Young primigravid women face highest risk; decentralization of antenatal services recommended.

articles.headline: AI malaria RDT reader shows high accuracy across four African countries
articles.title_original: Evaluating the performance of an artificial intelligence-based electronic reader for malaria rapid diagnostic tests across Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria and Uganda.
articles.authors: Lindblade KA, Ngufor C, Yavo W, Atobatele S, Mpimbaza A, Ssewante N, Akpiroroh E, Konaté-Toure A, Ahogni I, Kpemasse A, Tanoh AM, Ntadom G, Opigo J, Zobrist S, Griffith K, Humes M
articles.details: MaCRA study in Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria, and Uganda (2023) evaluated HealthPulse AI algorithm on 106,877 malaria RDT images. AI achieved 96.8% accuracy (95% CI 96.7-96.9%) and F1 score of 96.6 vs. trained panel reference. Recall/precision >97% for positive/negative results but lower for invalid (recall 84.8%) and uninterpretable (recall 0.8%) outcomes. Performance varied by country, RDT product, and image quality; faint lines significantly reduced accuracy (aOR 0.02-0.10).

articles.headline: Community incentives for mosquito sound surveillance in Tanzania
articles.title_original: "Fighting against malaria is everyone's concern": a randomized control trial assessing the role of incentives for encouraging local communities to record and upload mosquito sounds using the MozzWear application.
articles.authors: Mponzi WP, Dam R, Msaky D, Mwalugelo Y, Sinka M, Kiskin I, Herreros-Moya E, Roberts S, Willis K, Kaindoa EW
articles.details: Randomized trial in rural Tanzania (4 months) assessed airtime incentives for uploading mosquito acoustic recordings using MozzWear app with HumBug sensors. Control group (no incentive) performed comparably or better than incentive group in recording nights and uploads. Participants expressed strong intrinsic motivation for malaria control and indicated interest in future surveillance research, suggesting community capacity independent of financial incentives.

articles.headline: Anopheles larval habitats and breeding in urban Accra, Ghana
articles.title_original: Larval habitat diversity, physicochemical characteristics and their effect on the larval density of malaria vectors in the city of Accra, Ghana.
articles.authors: Sabtiu ARM, Hinne IA, Sraku IK, Halou DK, Doe RT, Attah SK, Aboagye-Antwi F, Afrane YA
articles.details: Larval surveys at 15 Accra sites during dry (Feb-Mar) and rainy (Jun-Jul) 2022 seasons identified 727 potential habitats; 65.34% (475/727) contained Anopheles larvae. Drainage ditches were most common habitat type (48.21%). An. gambiae s.l. highest abundance in irrigated urban farming sites (27.24% of 22,919 larvae), especially rainy season (77.01%, R²=3.46, p=0.000). An. coluzzii was predominant vector (54.4%, 368/677). Invasive An. stephensi also detected. Polluted habitats had higher ammonium (3.4 vs. 1.3 mg/L NH₄-N).

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing enhances CYP2D6 genotyping for P. vivax elimination
articles.title_original: Enhancing CYP2D6 genotyping with nanopore sequencing to address allele diversity P. vivax malaria elimination.
articles.authors: Wongsurawat T, Jenjaroenpun P, Suwannasin K, Wankaew N, Sanguansakpakdee N, Amornpornviwat T, Dondorp AM, Day NPJ, Nosten F, White NJ, Imwong M
articles.details: Developed nanopore-based methodology for CYP2D6 allele profiling to optimize primaquine therapy for P. vivax relapse prevention. Tested on 90 DNA samples from Thailand's Greater Mekong Subregion; achieved high-resolution genotyping identifying structural variants and a previously unidentified allele. Cost-effective approach with reduced processing time supports local genetic variability assessment and informed primaquine treatment decisions for P. vivax elimination.

articles.headline: EBV reactivation alters cytokine profiles in malaria-pregnant women
articles.title_original: Impact of epstein-barr virus reactivation on cytokine levels in pregnant women with malaria in the west region of cameroon.
articles.authors: Chatue IAD, Nyegue MA, Kamdem SD, Taya-Fokou JB, Djiyou ABD, Tchoupe EB, Tepa A, Neba RF, Junaid IT, Malhotra P, Netongo PM
articles.details: Study of 220 pregnant women in Cameroon with malaria diagnosed by microscopy/PCR and EBV status by ELISA. Women with EBV reactivation and severe malaria showed lower IL-6 (29.86 vs. 55.83 pg/ml, p=0.02) but elevated TGF-β (17.6 vs. 6.1 pg/ml, p=0.01) vs. latent EBV. Strong negative correlation between IL-10 and parasitemia in EBV-reactivated severe malaria group (r=-0.9762, p=0.0004). EBV reactivation may impair inflammatory control, exacerbating malaria severity.

articles.headline: Age drives inflammatory cytokine responses in malaria infection
articles.title_original: Age is an intrinsic driver of inflammatory responses to malaria.
articles.authors: Loughland JR, Dooley NL, Pava Z, SheelaNair A, Andrew DW, Tipping P, Bourke P, Engwerda CR, Lopez JA, Piera KA, William T, Barber BE, Grigg MJ, Anstey NM, Minigo G, Boyle MJ
articles.details: Comparative study of inflammatory cytokines during malaria in naive children and adults. Age associated with increased chemokines (CCL2, CCL3, CXCL8, CXCL9), CRP, and IDO correlating with symptoms. In vitro, classical monocytes and Vδ2+ γδ T cells from adults showed higher cytokine production; adult responses dominated by CCL2 while children showed increased IL-10 and IL-10 signaling enrichment. Identifies age-dependent cellular mechanisms driving age-associated malaria severity.

articles.headline: Antibody responses to P. falciparum differ in naive, vaccinated, and semi-immune volunteers
articles.title_original: IgM, IgG, and IgG Subclass Antibody Responses to Plasmodium falciparum Proteins in Naïve, Malaria-Vaccinated and Semi-Immune Volunteers after Controlled Human Malaria Infection.
