articles.title_original: Identification of broadly inhibitory anti-PfEMP1 antibodies by mass spectrometry sequencing of plasma IgG from a malaria-exposed child.
articles.authors: Turner L, Nunez de Villavicencio Diaz T, Raghavan SSR, Kana IH, Lyimo E, Reitzel C, Wang CW, Berube E, Jensen RW, Loeffler JR, Fernández-Quintero ML, Theander TG, Lusingu JPA, Le Bihan T, Han X, Minja DTR, Ward AB, Ma B, Lavstsen T
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: Immunological study using mass spectrometry and transcript sequencing identified monoclonal antibodies against P. falciparum PfEMP1-CIDRα1 domains from a 9-year-old malaria-exposed child. These broadly reactive antibodies potently inhibited EPCR binding across CIDRα1 variants and binding of infected RBCs to endothelial protein C receptor. Structural analysis revealed shared epitope overlapping EPCR binding site with previously identified protective antibodies C7 and C74.

articles.headline: MicroRNA signature predicts cerebral malaria admission
articles.title_original: Potential of microRNAs as diagnostic markers for distinguishing malaria severity in samples from an Indian cohort.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Arora K, Bhandari S, Sikka R, Wassmer SC, Bharti PK, Gupta H
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in India quantified plasma levels of six microRNAs in 321 malaria patients stratified by disease severity (CM n=43, severe non-CM n=50, uncomplicated n=79, asymptomatic n=80) plus 69 non-malarial febrile controls. Three microRNAs (hsa-miR-21-5p, hsa-miR-150-5p, hsa-miR-3158-3p) significantly correlated with cerebral malaria (p<0.0005), and random forest model using these three achieved 83% sensitivity, 100% specificity, 92% balanced accuracy for admission CM prediction.

articles.headline: Modeling drug-resistance evolution malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Effects of recombination on multi-drug resistance evolution in Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.authors: Tran KT, Nguyen TD, Weissman DB, Li EZ, Mok S, Small-Saunders JL, Bousema T, Zupko RJ, Tran TN, Boni MF
articles.details: Individual-based transmission model with 25 drug-resistance loci simulated P. falciparum recombination from multi-clonal infections and interrupted mosquito feeding. Over 15-year projection, 80-97% of multi-drug resistant genotypes arose from multi-clonal infections in single hosts; triple artemisinin-combination therapies (ACT) showed greatest reductions in treatment failures and longest delays to artemisinin resistance threshold (1%), compared to multiple first-line therapies and ACT cycling.

articles.headline: Airport and autochthonous malaria Europe 1969-2022
articles.title_original: Airport/seaport and autochthonous malaria in Europe from 1969 to 2022: A systematic review.
articles.authors: Balzli D, Gültekin N, Stanga Z, Ülgür I, Hedrich N, Fehr J, Schlagenhauf P
articles.details: Systematic review identified 115 malaria cases in Europe over 1969-2022: 68 airport/seaport cases (infected mosquitoes transported by air/sea) and 47 autochthonous locally-transmitted cases. Case fatality rates were 13.24% for airport/seaport malaria and 2.13% for autochthonous malaria; high fatality attributed to frequently delayed or missed diagnoses, with rising temperatures potentially facilitating importation of competent Anopheles vectors.

articles.headline: High malaria prevalence pregnant women Somalia
articles.title_original: The Prevalence of Malaria Among Pregnant Women at a Tertiary Care Hospital in Somalia: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Mohamud SM, Doğan S, Hussein AI, Mohamud RYH, Orhan Z, Doğaner A
articles.details: Cross-sectional study from November 2022-January 2023 in Mogadishu enrolled 398 pregnant women screened with Giemsa-stained blood smear microscopy and found 59.8% (238/398) malaria prevalence, predominantly P. falciparum (91.6%). Being in second or third trimester (OR 0.524, p=0.044; OR 0.442, p=0.007) and indoor residual spraying use (OR 0.192, p<0.001) were significantly associated with reduced malaria odds.

articles.headline: Signal-dependent exocytosis P. falciparum parasite egress
articles.title_original: Spatiotemporal dynamics of signal dependent exocytosis and parasitophorous vacuolar membrane rupture during Plasmodium falciparum egress.
articles.authors: Dedkhad W, Tran T, Fierro MA, Brooks C, Muralidharan V
articles.details: Live-cell imaging study of P. falciparum using superecliptic pHlourin reporter visualized exocytosis of egress-specific vesicles, with exocytosis initiated 3+ hours before merozoite egress and parasitophorous vacuolar membrane (PVM) rupturing at single focal site that rapidly expands. Conditional knockdown of PfERC (ER calcium-binding protein) inhibited exocytosis, demonstrating that signal-dependent exocytosis of egress proteases depends on calcium signaling.

articles.headline: PEXEL and non-PEXEL protein export P. falciparum
articles.title_original: An overview of the interconnectedness of the PEXEL and non-PEXEL mediated protein export in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Das S
articles.details: Review article summarizing mechanisms of protein export in P. falciparum, including PEXEL-based export via conserved pentameric Export Element motifs and alternative trafficking of PEXEL-negative exported proteins (PNEPs). Discusses current understanding of PEXEL export mechanism, structural diversity and functions of characterized PNEPs in parasite pathogenesis and survival within infected red blood cells.

articles.headline: P. falciparum-dengue coinfections widespread in western Kenya
articles.title_original: Correlation and epidemiologic distribution of emerging coinfections of Plasmodium falciparum and dengue virus among febrile children in malaria-endemic zones in western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ogony J, Mangeni J, Ayodo G, Menya D, Akinyi I, Oyugi B, Yongo A, Okumu F, Lwanga C, Oluoch F, Karanja S
articles.details: Prospective cohort study of 1,004 febrile children <5 years in Kisumu and Busia Counties, Kenya. Of 380 screened positive, P. falciparum prevalence was 21.4% (215/1004), dengue 8.9% (90/1004), coinfections 7.5% (75/1004). Busia had highest P. falciparum-only infections (31%), Kisumu highest dengue-only (16.6%). Geographic location significantly associated with disease distribution (χ² = 19.45, P < 0.001).

articles.headline: Only 44% of rural Ethiopians understand malaria prevention
articles.title_original: Malaria signs, symptoms, prevention knowledge and its associated factors among rural Ethiopians.
articles.authors: Kuti KA, Zuma SM
articles.journal: Afr J Prim Health Care Fam Med
articles.details: Community cross-sectional study of 634 rural household members in Bale Zone, Ethiopia. Just 44.2% demonstrated good understanding of malaria signs, symptoms, and prevention; misconceptions included transmission by sun exposure, lack of rest, and alcohol. Education, religion, marital status, family size, and presence of children/pregnant women associated with better knowledge. Authors recommend knowledge-transfer strategies for community awareness.

articles.headline: Within-group disparities persist despite malaria prevention gains in Ghana
articles.title_original: Between-and within-socioeconomic groups temporal inequality in the uptake of malaria prevention strategies among pregnant women and under-five children in Ghana (2003-2022).
articles.authors: Okova D, Lukwa AT, Edusei M, Bodzo P, Atta-Obeng C, Chiwire P, Hongoro C
articles.details: Analysis of Ghana DHS (2003, 2008, 2014, 2022) for ITN use and IPTp coverage inequalities. ITN use in <5 children increased 58.1% to 62.9%; pregnant women fluctuated but returned to 60%. IPTp coverage rose 0.85% to 60%. ITN use was pro-poor (ENCI: −0.04 to −0.32 for pregnant women), IPTp shifted pro-rich from 2008 onward. Theil decomposition: 85% ITN inequality among pregnant women was within SES groups vs 18% between-group (2022).

articles.headline: Diabetes and metabolic syndrome triple severe malaria risk
articles.title_original: Diabetes and metabolic syndrome in adults with malaria and associations with severe disease: results from two tertiary hospitals in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Awungafac G, Njonnou SRS, Noubom M, Libon LIAA, Gesu CN, Färnert A, Choukem SP, Wyss K
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 289 adults with P. falciparum malaria at two Cameroonian hospitals; 120 (41.6%) met WHO severe malaria criteria. Diabetes in 21.7% of severe vs 6.0% non-severe cases (p < 0.001); metabolic syndrome in 32.5% vs 16.3% (p = 0.001). Multivariable analysis: diabetes (aOR 3.24; 95%CI 1.38–7.62) and metabolic syndrome (aOR 3.18; 95%CI 1.60–6.37) independently increased severe malaria risk.

articles.headline: HIV-malaria coinfection significantly worsens anemia
articles.title_original: Hematological Profiles of Adults Coinfected With HIV and Malaria Receiving Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy at Bonga Gebretsadik Shawo General Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia: A Comparative Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.authors: Demango F, Tadasa E, Kiya GT
articles.details: Hospital comparative cross-sectional study of 196 HIV+ adults (98 HIV-only, 98 HIV-malaria coinfected) in Ethiopia. Significant differences in RBC, hemoglobin, hematocrit between groups (p < 0.05). HIV-malaria coinfected had anemia 61.2% vs HIV-only 44.9% (p < 0.001); leukopenia 43.88% vs 39.3%; thrombocytopenia 30.6% vs 25.5%. Negative correlations between parasite density and RBC, hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelets.

articles.headline: Phase-based features improve P. falciparum detection in microscopy
articles.title_original: Enhanced detection of malaria infected red blood cells through phase driven classification.
articles.authors: Kyeremah C, Paul AS, Haehn D, Duraisingh MT, Yelleswarapu CS
articles.details: Study of digital holographic microscopy for detecting P. falciparum-infected RBCs. Phase-based features classification improved parasitemia detection from 48% (feature-specific) to 61%, achieving 98–100% specificity. Method underestimated parasitemia vs Giemsa staining (90%). Authors propose as real-time, label-free automated malaria diagnosis tool.

articles.headline: Poor LLIN maintenance and low use hinder Dominican elimination efforts
articles.title_original: Insights from LLIN post-distribution monitoring surveys in the malaria transmission foci of the Dominican Republic: implications for quantification and distribution strategies.
articles.authors: Ventura G, Félix MYS, Bueno NT, Ströfer NM, Raposo JLC, Solís Á, Parra RGB, Montoya LF
articles.details: Cross-sectional household survey in two Dominican foci (Azua 4–6 months post-distribution, San Juan 1 year post-distribution). LLIN coverage 64% (Azua), 63% (San Juan); retention 88.4% (Azua), 80.9% (San Juan); access 58.8% (Azua), 65.4% (San Juan); use 32.3% (Azua), 50.5% (San Juan). Most serviceable (96.4% Azua, 88.9% San Juan) but washed aggressively and frequently (52–73% >20 times in 3 years).

articles.headline: Aspartyl protease inhibitor suppresses gametocyte development
articles.title_original: Aspartyl protease inhibition interferes with Plasmodium falciparum asexual blood-stage and early gametocyte development.
articles.authors: Niramolyanun G, Praikongkatham C, Jenwithisuk R, Roobsoong W, Sattabongkot J, Pankao V, Kangwanrangsan N
articles.details: In vitro study of pepstatin A at 100 µM on P. falciparum parasites. Inhibited asexual stage 47% and early-gametocyte development 73%, with morphological defects (chromatin condensation, vacuolization, hemozoin clumping). Did not affect late-stage gametocytes or gametes. Authors note potential to reduce disease severity and transmission; further research needed for drug development.

articles.headline: Urban farming drives malaria transmission in Accra
articles.title_original: Urban malaria in Accra, Ghana: transmission risk and influence of irrigated vegetable farms.
articles.authors: Sabtiu ARM, Sraku IK, Owusu-Asenso CM, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Doe RT, Boadu EN, Azumah JD, Eyeson NAS, Abdulai A, Amoateng G, Attah SK, Aboagye-Antwi F, Afrane YA
articles.details: Entomological survey of 41,747 mosquitoes in Accra across five site categories via HLC and PPK. An. gambiae s.l. most abundant at irrigated urban farming sites (48.63%, 10,466/21,520) vs peri-urban (18.06%), high SES (16.42%), middle SES (13.74%), low SES (3.15%). Indoor An. gambiae sporozoite infectivity higher (4.56%, 16/351) than outdoor (3.78%, 9/238) (χ² = 6.78, P = 0.009). Tuba IUF site highest infectivity (6.41%, 10/156).

articles.headline: Community health volunteers effective despite malaria commodity shortages
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers of Community Case management of Malaria implementation in Homabay, Busia and Kakamega Counties, Kenya.
articles.authors: Lumumba S, Luciani A, Sifuma B, Kinyua D, Gikunda G, Ogutu N, Onyango J, Mukami D, Kiilu C, Kassim S, Opanga Y, Githuka G
articles.details: Mixed-methods cross-sectional in Homabay, Busia, Kakamega Counties, Kenya: 528 households, 4 FGDs, 20 KIIs. 72% of households received malaria services; 62% considered CHVs regular healthcare; ~85% satisfied. Facilitators: sensitization, CHV training, rapid tests, commodity availability, referral systems. Barriers: myths about nets, mRDT/antimalarial stockouts, inaccessible roads.

articles.headline: IPTp and housing improvements reduce malaria in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of malaria in pregnant women attending antenatal consultation in Dschang, West Cameroon.
articles.authors: Ebai CB, Ngoufo FN, Teh RN, Kodjo JTS, Muyang EJ, Kimbi HK
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 314 pregnant women in Dschang, Cameroon. Malaria parasitemia 14.6% (46/314), all P. falciparum. Protective: ceiling (OR 0.20, p < 0.01), IPTp (OR 0.23, p < 0.01), window screens (OR 0.14, p = 0.01). Risk: bushes around house (OR 2.40, p = 0.03). GMPD highest in second trimester (2190/µL, F = 61.3, p = 0.016), >3 gravidities (1022/µL), with sweating (1946/µL), LLIN users (1536/µL).

articles.headline: Plant extract effectively treats P. chabaudi lung inflammation
articles.title_original: Morphometric analysis and immunobiological investigation of Indigofera oblongifolia on the infected lung with Plasmodium chabaudi.
articles.authors: Murshed M, Al-Tamimi J, Ebaid H, Al-Quraishy S
articles.details: Experimental study in 50 C57BL/6 mice comparing Indigofera oblongifolia leaf extract with AgNPs, chloroquine 10 mg/kg, controls in P. chabaudi infection. IOLE AgNPs restored normal lung organization, reduced congestion/edema/necrosis vs infected and CQ-treated. Immunohistochemistry showed reduced TNF-α, IL-6 with IOLE AgNPs. Authors conclude I. oblongifolia exhibits antimalarial and antioxidant properties, more effective than CQ in malaria-induced lung pathology.

articles.headline: Drone eDNA surveillance detects Plasmodium in Amazon rainforest
articles.title_original: Real-time malaria detection in the Amazon rainforest via drone-collected eDNA and portable qPCR.
articles.authors: Ip YCA, Montemartini L, Chang JJM, Desiderato A, Franco-Sierra ND, Geckeler C, Herrera MAG, Gregorini M, Jucker M, Kirchgeorg S, Lüthi M, Mächler E, Thostrup FB, Murari G, Mura M, Pulido-Santacruz P, Sangermano F, Schindler T, Melvad C, Mintchev S, Deiner K
articles.details: Proof-of-concept study using drone-lowered canopy swabs coupled with multiplex pan-Plasmodium qPCR (limit of detection 0.2 parasites μL⁻¹) in the Amazon rainforest. Of 12 samples (10 canopy, 2 blanks), one canopy swab amplified in duplicate (Ct 28.7, 29.23). The end-to-end workflow from drone deployment to qPCR readout averaged 1.5 hours per assay without cold-chain logistics. Demonstrates recovery of intracellular parasite DNA from environmental surfaces for real-time landscape-level surveillance.

articles.headline: Occupations, night-time activities increase malaria risk in Zanzibar
articles.title_original: Occupations at high risk for malaria in Zanzibar: a case-control study, may-august 2023.
articles.authors: Makenga G, Gallalee S, Mkali H, Khamis MI, Ramadhan A, Ali MH, Hassan WS, Hassan J, Makwaruzi S, Mgata S, Gulaka M, Govella NJ, Molteni F, Kitojo C, Reaves E, Ballard SB, Serbantez N, Ikonje A, Afenu M, Mkude S, Smith JL, Mohamed S, Shija SJ, Gueye CS, Gosling R
articles.details: Case-control study (197 cases, 557 controls) in Zanzibar (May-August 2023) identified occupational risk factors. In urban areas: night watchmen/police (OR 5.3, 95% CI 2.7–10.6), construction workers (OR 3.0, 95% CI 1.8–5.0), farmers (OR 1.6, 95% CI 1.1–2.2); plus night-time activities (OR 2.8, 95% CI 1.8–4.3) and eating outside (OR 2.0, 95% CI 1.1–3.4). Rural areas: outdoor night activities (OR 3.8, 95% CI 1.5–9.9) and meals outside (OR 2.7, 95% CI 1.1–6.6). Net use protective in rural areas.

articles.headline: P. falciparum African haplotypes in invasive Anopheles stephensi outbreak
articles.title_original: Genetic surveillance of Plasmodium-Anopheles compatibility markers during Anopheles stephensi associated malaria outbreak.
articles.authors: Waymire E, Getachew D, Gunarathna I, Spear J, Lloyd G, Follis M, Kaye AA, Ali S, Yared S, Carter TE
articles.details: Genetic surveillance during 2022 An. stephensi outbreak in Dire Dawa, Ethiopia screened 160 mosquitoes: 6.21% positive for P. falciparum, 4.37% for P. vivax. Analysis of P47 and P47Rec sequences via PCR and Sanger sequencing revealed P. falciparum strains exhibited only African haplotypes at SNP positions 707 and 725 in Pfs47. Minimum spanning network analysis confirmed connectivity between Dire Dawa and African P. falciparum sequences. Single amino acid change identified in An. stephensi P47Rec as potential molecular marker for outbreak propensity.

articles.headline: Temperature stress increases P. falciparum transmission potential in An. stephensi
articles.title_original: Effects of temperature and nutrition stress on Anopheles stephensi, an Indian urban malaria vector.
articles.authors: Ghosh C, Kumar N, Mogaveerthi S, Lamba S, Ramanjini CK, Joshi SG, Chandran P, Kumar S, Swain S
articles.details: Laboratory study exposing An. stephensi to low-temperature (4°C or 18±1°C), high-temperature (35.5±1°C), or low-nutrition stress (33 mg/100 larvae) over 8 generations. P. falciparum vectorial competence assessed by oocyst infection rates: high-temperature line increased 1.46-fold, low-temperature 1.28-fold, low-nutrition 1.15-fold compared to control. High-temperature and low-nutrition lines showed faster egg-to-adult development (~7.5 and 11 days) versus low-temperature (~25 days). Paracentric chromosomal inversions observed in low-nutrition line, suggesting adaptation mechanisms.

articles.headline: DHIS2 malaria data quality below WHO standards in Uganda
articles.title_original: Concordance of data on key malaria indicators between DHIS2 and source documents, and influencing factors at public primary health facilities in eastern Uganda: a mixed methods study.
articles.authors: Nuwasiima S, Mpimbaza A, Muteebwa L, Nagawa E, Arinaitwe E, Kiberu F, Ejalu DL, Mugerwa J, Batte C, Mukisa J, Agaba B, Mukunya D, Kalyango JN, Kamya MR, Nankabirwa JI
articles.details: Mixed-methods study at 12 public health facilities in Mayuge district, Uganda (2021–2022) assessing concordance between DHIS2 and source documents. Concordance for key indicators fell below WHO ≥80% standard: suspected cases 36.7% (95% CI 25.2–49.9), suspected cases tested 53.6% (95% CI 41.7–65.05), test-positive cases 55.3% (95% CI 43.0–67.0), test positivity rate 56.8% (95% CI 43.9–68.8). Presence of HMIS personnel (aPR 1.41, 95% CI 1.20–1.66), timely reporting (aPR 1.15, 95% CI 1.00–1.31), and adequate RDT stock improved concordance.

articles.headline: Temperature-dependent malaria model validates in Jimma zone, Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Age-structured malaria model with temperature-dependent dynamics and optimal control analysis within a partial differential equation framework.
articles.authors: Petros LD, Abdisa TE, Etefa DT, Menbiko DK, Gizaw AK, Simma EA, Yewhalaw D, Deressa CT
articles.details: Age-structured malaria model using partial differential equations with temperature-dependent dynamics and optimal control strategies, validated against 6 years of infection prevalence data from Jimma zone, Ethiopia. Model achieved R² = 0.68 and adjusted R² = 0.63, demonstrating good fit to observed data. Comparison with existing age-structured models showed superior predictive accuracy in capturing temperature-dependent malaria transmission trends. Numerical simulations demonstrated effects of temperature variations and optimal controls on dynamics.

articles.headline: Climate, vegetation, aridity predict malaria incidence in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Modelling the impact of climatic and environmental variables on malaria incidence in Tanzania: Implications for achieving the WHO's 2030 Targets.
articles.authors: Lutambi AM, Emidi B, Mbuya FG, Ryoba M, Sagamiko TD, Hugo AK, Mbalawata IS
articles.details: Geostatistical Bayesian model of climatic and environmental variables on malaria incidence using Demographic and Health Survey data (2000–2020, ~2000 participants per survey). Mean incidence decreased from 0.347 (95% CI 0.336–0.357) in 2000 to 0.118 (95% CI 0.114–0.122) in 2020 as insecticide-treated bednet coverage increased. Higher temperatures increased incidence (OR 1.06, 95% CI 1.04–1.08 in 2000; OR 1.13, 95% CI 1.10–1.16 in 2010). Enhanced vegetation index increased incidence (ORs 5.28–6.22) and aridity increased incidence (ORs 1.07–1.11). Spatial clustering declined (Moran's I 0.93 to 0.74).

articles.headline: P. ovale imported to Colombia requires PCR confirmation
articles.title_original: Imported plasmodium ovale malaria from Côte d'Ivoire: a case report.
articles.authors: Plata-Puyana JD, Vanegas Beltrán JK, Martínez-Ávila MC
articles.details: Case report of 29-year-old male returning to Bogotá after 3-month stay in Côte d'Ivoire presenting with fever, chills, and myalgia. Initial microscopy misidentified parasite as P. falciparum; polymerase chain reaction confirmed P. ovale. Patient initially received artemether-lumefantrine empirically, then switched to chloroquine followed by primaquine (15 mg base daily for 28 days) for radical cure. G6PD testing unavailable; patient tolerated treatment without adverse effects. Case highlights diagnostic challenges and importance of molecular diagnostics.

articles.headline: Malaria patients report complicated symptoms after discharge in South Africa
articles.title_original: Post-discharge experiences of Malaria patients in a Vhembe district village, South Africa.
articles.authors: Kwinda W, Luhalima TR, Mudzweda AD
articles.details: Exploratory qualitative study of post-discharge experiences in Mhinga village, Vhembe district, Limpopo province, South Africa using semi-structured interviews with purposive sampling. Two themes emerged: post-discharge experiences from clinic and hospital. Findings documented complicated symptoms experienced by malaria patients after discharge from Mhinga clinic and transfer to Malamulele hospital. Study highlights potential for reducing hospital admissions through enhanced surveillance, vector control, public awareness campaigns, and healthcare facility improvements.

articles.headline: Minor Plasmodium species rebound after IRS discontinuation in Ghana
articles.title_original: Longitudinal analysis of the prevalence of minor Plasmodium spp. in the reservoir of asymptomatic infections through sequential interventions in Northern Sahelian Ghana.
articles.authors: Rios-Teran CA, Tiedje KE, Bangre O, Deed SL, Argyropoulos DC, Koram KA, Oduro AR, Ansah PO, Day KP
articles.details: Interrupted time-series study in Bongo District, Ghana with five age-stratified surveys (~2000 participants each, 2012–2022) measuring P. malariae and P. ovale via species-specific 18S rRNA PCR. Following indoor residual spraying in 2015, P. malariae declined from 13.7% to 1.4% and P. ovale from 5.7% to 0.4%. After IRS discontinuation and seasonal malaria chemoprevention introduction in 2017, P. malariae and P. ovale rebounded (2-fold and 10-fold) to 2.9% and 4.0%. With sustained SMC (2020, 2022), P. malariae continued increasing (7.4%, 5.8%) while P. ovale declined (2.6%, 1.3%).

articles.headline: Invasive Anopheles stephensi, drug resistance threaten Ethiopia elimination
articles.title_original: Challenges to Malaria Elimination in Ethiopia by 2030: A Review.
articles.authors: Adugna T
articles.details: Review article documenting malaria challenges in Ethiopia, where disease affects three-fourths of land area and two-thirds of population below 2000 m altitude. Over 42 Anopheles species identified; Anopheles arabiensis primary vector, but invasive Anopheles stephensi now transmits P. falciparum and P. vivax with resistance to all four insecticide classes. Both Plasmodium species evolved resistance to multiple antimalarials. Recent civil war caused spike in cases, with disease spreading rapidly in Amhara and Oromo regions beyond regional control, representing major impediments to 2030 elimination goal.

articles.headline: An. darlingi shows bimodal biting, prefers lower door resting sites
articles.title_original: Experimental hut resting and entrance behaviour of Anopheles darlingi from Zungarococha, a malaria endemic community in Loreto, Northern Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Castro-Llanos F, Stoops CA, Grieco JP, Escobedo-Vargas K, Lopez-Sifuentes V, Lenhart A, Stell FM, Larson RT, Achee NL, Vásquez GM
articles.details: Behavioral study of wild-caught and colonized Anopheles darlingi in Zungarococha, Peruvian Amazon (2014–2015) using experimental huts and human landing collection. Wild An. darlingi showed bimodal outdoor biting with peaks at 1800 h and 2200 h, density associated with Amazon River levels. Resting location preferences similar between wild-caught and colonized: low door (32.7% and 29.8%), window (14.0% and 11.8%), roof (13.0% and 12.7%). Wild An. darlingi house-entry peaked 2200–2300 h; interception trap capture rates lower than indoor human landing collection.

articles.headline: P. falciparum plasmepsin2/3 amplification prevalence in Africa and Asia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum plasmepsin2/3 gene duplication in Africa and Asia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Marwa KJ, Kilonzi M, Mnkugwe RH, Baraka V, Kapesa A, Mwaiswelo R, Zinga M, Mmbando BP, Lusingu JPA, Kamugisha E
articles.details: A systematic meta-analysis of 34 studies involving 4,005 P. falciparum isolates (2009–2019) found pooled pfpm2/3 gene amplification prevalence of 16.0% (95% CI 8.0–26.0%), higher in Asia (25.0%, 95% CI 9.0–45.0%) than Africa (8.0%, 95% CI 2.0–15.0%), and increasing over time: 11% before 2016 versus 19% in 2016–2020. This amplification reduces susceptibility to piperaquine and is associated with artemisinin-based combination therapy treatment failure, highlighting need for monitoring partner drug resistance.

articles.headline: Malaria burden and spatial heterogeneity in Sindh, Pakistan, 2012–2024
articles.title_original: Subnational malaria burden in Sindh, Pakistan: over a decade of evidence for tailored strategies.
articles.authors: Baig N, Ali Z, Ahmed M, Jamal WZ, Rehman SU, Ahmed Z, Rahoojo RH, Jagirani JA, Usmani BA, Fatmi Z
articles.details: A 13-year longitudinal time-series analysis of 1,088 primary healthcare facilities in Sindh Province found 92 per 1,000 people per annum incidence with 12.3% malaria positivity rate (~1.28 million cases annually; 16.7 million over the period). Marked heterogeneity existed across districts, with six districts carrying >53% of burden. Malaria incidence doubled following the 2022 floods and persisted through 2024, with seasonal peaks during wet season and post-monsoon periods.

articles.headline: Molecular docking identifies PfSUB2/PfTRAMP inhibitor candidates for malaria
articles.title_original: Targeting invasion-associated proteins PfSUB2 and PfTRAMP in Plasmodium falciparum: identification of potential inhibitors via molecular docking.
articles.authors: Okafor EO, Bella-Omunagbe M, Elugbadebo T, Dokunmu TM, Adebiyi E
articles.details: This molecular docking study screened 519 compounds against PfSUB2 (subtilisin-like protease 2) and PfTRAMP (thrombospondin-related apical merozoite protein), proteins essential for asexual blood stage growth and RBC invasion. Compounds 139,974,934 and 154,414,021 demonstrated binding affinities superior to standard inhibitor PMSF. Compound 155,204,487 showed dual-target potential with better binding to both PfTRAMP and PfSUB2. Authors recommend these three compounds as candidates for antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Fractional calculus modeling reveals malaria transmission dynamics complexity
articles.title_original: Complex travelling wave solutions of fractional nonlinear coupled malaria model: bifurcation, chaos, and multistability.
articles.authors: Abdullah, Shakeel M, Muhammad S, Ahmadzai HG
articles.details: This theoretical study employed fractional calculus (beta derivatives) and the General Exponential Rational Function Method to model malaria transmission dynamics, yielding complex traveling wave solutions including kink, anti-kink, and dark solitons. Analysis revealed bifurcation patterns, quasi-periodic and chaotic dynamics, and multistability within the model. Findings offer theoretical insight into disease spread modeling with potential applications for more accurate predictive models in biomedical sciences.

articles.headline: P. vivax shows high genetic diversity and clonal expansion in Peruvian Amazon
articles.title_original: High Prevalence, Genetic Diversity and Temporal Differentiation of Plasmodium vivax in a Remote Hard-to-Reach Community from the Peruvian Amazon Region.
articles.authors: Ramirez R, Torres K, Rodríguez P, Acosta C, Guzmán-Guzmán M, Llanos-Cuentas A, Vinetz JM, Escalante AA, Gamboa D
articles.details: A longitudinal surveillance study in Santa Emilia, Peru, genotyped 360 P. vivax samples across 2013, 2015, and 2016 using 16 neutral microsatellites. Prevalence was 56% by qRT-PCR with 44% submicroscopic infections. Genetic diversity and population differentiation remained high across years. Polyclonal infections exceeded 40% in 2013–2015, declining to 29% in 2016. Four genetically distinct populations were detected with increasing admixture in 2015–2016, indicating persistent high diversity, clonal expansion, and no recent bottleneck.

articles.headline: P. falciparum importation drives genetic diversity on Bioko Island despite control
articles.title_original: Regular Plasmodium falciparum importation onto Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea, hampers malaria elimination from the island.
articles.authors: Stabler TC, Dwivedi A, Guo B, Shrestha B, Joshi S, Rivas MR, Donfack OT, Guerra CA, García GA, Daubenberger C, Silva JC
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing from the 2019 Bioko Island Malaria Elimination Project Malaria Indicator Survey found P. falciparum with higher-than-expected genetic diversity similar to high transmission areas, despite low local transmission. Within-host diversity and polyclonal infections resembled moderate transmission settings. Parasite flow was more pronounced with Gabon than other continental neighbors, suggesting greater importation from that region. Results demonstrate that elimination without interrupting local transmission and accounting for importation from neighboring areas faces substantial challenges.

articles.headline: Atypical Arp2/3 complex directs kinetochore attachment in parasite gametogenesis
articles.title_original: Discovery of an Atypical Arp2/3 Complex in Malaria Parasites Sheds New Light on Nuclear Actin.
articles.authors: Hentzschel F, Frischknecht F, Marti M
articles.details: This study identified an atypical Arp2/3 complex in malaria parasites comprising five divergent subunits and a putative kinetochore-associated factor, ensuring proper kinetochore-spindle attachment during male gametogenesis. Disruption of Arp2/3 function or actin polymerization leads to defective DNA segregation and developmental arrest in mosquitoes. The findings reveal unexpected diversity in Arp2/3 composition and specialized adaptations in malaria parasites, expanding understanding of actin function during parasite mitosis.

articles.headline: IBD detection methods optimized for P. falciparum high-recombination genomes
articles.title_original: Benchmarking and optimization of methods for the detection of identity-by-descent in high-recombining Plasmodium falciparum genomes.
articles.authors: Guo B, Takala-Harrison S, O'Connor TD
articles.details: This benchmarking study compared identity-by-descent (IBD) callers (hmmIBD, isoRelate, hap-IBD, phased IBD, Refined IBD) for P. falciparum using population genetic simulations. Low marker density significantly compromised IBD detection accuracy, especially for shorter segments. Optimized hmmIBD parameters improved capture of selection signals and population structure; hmmIBD provided less biased effective population size estimates. Validation with MalariaGEN Pf7 data across transmission settings confirmed findings. Authors recommend hmmIBD for Plasmodium species, particularly for quality-sensitive analyses.

articles.headline: COVID-19 disruptions linked to malaria diagnosis rate surge in Gabon
articles.title_original: Malaria amidst the COVID-19 pandemic in Gabon: an application of autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA) models within an interrupted time series (ITS) framework to hospital-based data.
articles.authors: Roeder F, Adegnika OS, Honkpehedji YJ, Huth M, Lell B, Adegnika AA, Lopes-Rafegas I, Sicuri E
articles.details: An interrupted time series analysis using ARIMA models examined monthly malaria diagnosis rates (per 1,000 all-cause diagnoses) in two tertiary hospitals in Lambaréné, Gabon (2018–early 2023). Following an initial 47% drop at pandemic onset (P=0.031), rates gradually increased (linear term: 7.32, P=0.001; squared term: −0.19, P=0.001) to peaks exceeding pre-pandemic levels. Resurgence was attributed to disruptions in malaria control activities and waning efficacy of pre-pandemic control tools. Maternal malaria incidence showed no meaningful increase.

articles.headline: P. vivax and P. malariae detected in Amazonian coatis, not armadillos
articles.title_original: Molecular Screening of Plasmodium spp. in Free-Living Ring-Tailed Coatis (Nasua nasua) and Nine-Banded Armadillos (Dasypus novemcinctus) in the Peruvian Amazon.
articles.authors: Ulloa GM, Greenwood AD, Cornejo OE, Monteiro FOB, Santolalla ML, Mayor P
articles.details: Nested PCR screening of 44 ring-tailed coatis and 66 nine-banded armadillos from an Indigenous community in the Peruvian Amazon detected Plasmodium spp. in 2 coatis (4.7%): one identical to P. vivax/P. simium and another to P. malariae/P. brasilianum (confirmed by independent cox3-based PCR). No infections detected in armadillos. Results indicate coatis may serve as incidental hosts for diverse Plasmodium parasites, highlighting need for expanded wildlife surveillance to clarify roles in sylvatic malaria ecology.

articles.headline: Urban malaria prevalence in sub-Saharan Africa with travel and livestock risk
articles.title_original: Urban malaria and population mobility in sub-Saharan Africa: systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Merga H, Degefa T, Birhanu Z, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 39 studies found overall pooled urban malaria prevalence in sub-Saharan Africa of 23.01% (95% CI: 19.42–26.59; I²=99.8%). Among individuals with travel history, odds of malaria infection were 4.17 times higher (95% CI: 2.33–7.46); those owning livestock in their house had 4.1-fold higher odds (95% CI: 1.62–10.39). High heterogeneity (I²=99.8%) reflected variations in populations and methods. Authors recommend integrated, targeted urban malaria control addressing socioeconomic, environmental, and behavioral drivers.

articles.headline: An. stephensi fully susceptible to novel insecticides and IGR in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Susceptibility of the invasive malaria vector Anopheles stephensi in Ethiopia to novel chemical insecticides and insect growth regulator.
articles.authors: Abiy E, Degefa T, Balkew M, Merga H, Alemayehu E, Mitiku A, Lee MC, Yan G, Yewhalaw D
articles.details: WHO bottle bioassays of An. stephensi populations from Ethiopia exposed to novel insecticides showed 100% mortality at 24 hours with broflanilide (18 µg/bottle) and clothianidin (10 µg/bottle), and 100% mortality at 48 hours with chlorfenapyr (100 µg/bottle). Pyriproxyfen (100 µg/bottle) produced low direct mortality (15% by 72 hours) but achieved 100% oviposition inhibition. An. stephensi populations were resistant to pyrethroids, carbamates, and organophosphates. Novel insecticides and IGR show promise for vector control via indoor residual spraying and larviciding.

articles.headline: Climate drives lagged malaria mortality in Ugandan children by sex
articles.title_original: Climate-driven malaria mortality among children in malaria-endemic areas of Uganda.
articles.authors: Kiragga A, Otieno F, Nabukeera B, Ng'etich M, Tozan Y, Kajungu D
articles.details: Population-based time-series analysis from Iganga-Mayuge Health Demographic Surveillance Site, Uganda examined climate associations with weekly malaria mortality using distributed lag non-linear modeling. Rainfall exceeding 646 mm increased mortality risk at 11-12 week lag; temperatures 25.2-29.9°C increased risk at 5-11 week lag. Children under 5 and aged 5-14 showed greater temperature sensitivity than rainfall sensitivity; males aged 5-14 had higher temperature-related mortality than females.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic vulnerability improves malaria prediction in Senegal
articles.title_original: Integrating vulnerability and hazard in malaria risk mapping: the elimination context of Senegal.
articles.authors: Morlighem C, Nnanatu CC, Aheto JMK, Linard C
articles.details: Bayesian geostatistical models predicted malaria prevalence in children under 5 across Senegal using 2017 Demographic and Health Survey (DHS) and 2020-21 Malaria Indicator Survey (MIS) data combined with environmental covariates. Models incorporating socioeconomic vulnerability factors alongside hazard factors (rainfall, temperature) achieved median predictive R² of 0.64 on DHS data; vulnerability factors alone outperformed hazard-only models.

articles.headline: Experimental cerebral malaria shows sex-dependent brain pathology in mice
articles.title_original: Characterization of experimental cerebral malaria by volumetric MRI A comparative study across the sexes.
articles.authors: Comino Garcia Muñoz A, Marfouk O, Michel CP, Varlet I, Royer E, Perles-Barbacaru TA, Viola A
articles.details: Volumetric MRI in C57BL/6J mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA revealed sex-dependent differences in experimental cerebral malaria (ECM) pathology. Males developed more severe ECM with greater brain swelling and increased microhemorrhages; sex-specific lesions detected in inferior/superior colliculi and midbrain, while cortex and pons edema were common to both sexes.

articles.headline: Piperonyl butoxide nets improve ownership and reduce malaria in Kenya
articles.title_original: Net ownership, utilization and malaria burden in the context of Piperonyl butoxide-LLINs intervention in western Kenya.
articles.authors: Oyweri J, Onyango PO, Machani MG, Bungei J, Cheruiyot S, Afrane YA, Lee MC, Zhong D, Zhou G, Githure J, Atieli H, Yan G
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey in Muhoroni Sub-County, Kenya, compared piperonyl butoxide (PBO) long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs) with pyrethroid-only LLINs in 380 households. PBO-LLIN households achieved higher ownership (96% vs 89%), usage (87.6% vs 80.2%), and lower attrition (7.6% vs 17.9%). Malaria parasite prevalence by qPCR was 29.0% in PBO-LLIN versus 34.7% in pyrethroid-LLIN groups (prevalence ratio 0.84, 95% CI 0.65-1.03).

articles.headline: Anopheles mosquito biology underpins effective malaria vector control
articles.title_original: Know your enemy: understanding mosquito biology to advance malaria elimination in Africa.
articles.authors: Zembere K, Kalonde PK, Bickton FM
articles.details: Review article on Anopheles spp. vector biology including adult and juvenile life stages, intrinsic incubation period, and blood-seeking behavior and their relevance to vector control. Vector control measures have achieved 65% reduction in malaria cases between 2000-2015; understanding mosquito biology is essential for designing effective interventions and advancing malaria elimination in Africa.

articles.headline: P. falciparum infection impairs kidney function and electrolytes
articles.title_original: Evaluation of serum electrolytes and kidney function among untreated malaria (Plasmodium falciparum) patients in health centers, Konso zone, South Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Basire B, Kibret B, Kifle K, Assefa F
articles.details: Case-control study in Ethiopia compared 54 untreated P. falciparum-infected patients with 54 uninfected controls (mean age ~27 years). Malaria patients showed significantly elevated serum creatinine (1.32 vs 0.92 mg/dL, p<0.05) and urea (39.8 vs 25.78 mg/dL, p<0.05) with decreased sodium (132.15 vs 137.11 mmol/L, p<0.05) and potassium (3.44 vs 3.94 mmol/L, p<0.05).

articles.headline: CUT&Tag enables chromatin profiling of Plasmodium from low-input samples
articles.title_original: CUT&Tag and DiBioCUT&Tag enable investigation of the AT-rich epigenome of Plasmodium falciparum from low-input samples.
articles.authors: Gockel J, Ramón-Zamorano G, Kimmel J, Spielmann T, Bártfai R
articles.details: Methodological paper describing adaptation of CUT&Tag and novel DiBioCUT&Tag technology for chromatin profiling in P. falciparum. Methods overcome limitations of chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) including GC-content artifacts and high material requirements, enabling genome-wide profiling of chromatin-associated proteins from as few as 10,000 nuclei or crude parasite isolates.

articles.headline: Mosquito nutritional status shifts biting time and malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Biting time of day in malaria mosquitoes is modulated by nutritional status.
articles.authors: Oke CE, Rund SSC, Machani MG, Sabtiu ARM, Akuamoah-Boateng Y, Afrane YA, Reece SE
articles.details: Semi-field study examined how nutritional resources affect Anopheles gambiae s.l. host-seeking behavior. Female mosquitoes with low nutritional resources were 2-3 fold more likely to host seek overall and 5-10 fold more likely to host seek at earlier times of day compared to well-resourced mosquitoes, suggesting nutritional status drives plasticity in biting behavior affecting residual malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Genetic material from malaria RDTs enables retrospective PCR diagnosis
articles.title_original: [Use of genetic material extracted from rapid malaria diagnostic tests using PCR during the Grand Magal of Touba in Senegal].
articles.authors: Diouf C, Ouaddane I, Diatta G, Goumbala MLB, Sow A, Fam D, Faye M, Gautret P, Sokhna C
articles.details: Validation study examined use of nucleic acid extracted from RDT cassettes for retrospective P. falciparum diagnosis by qPCR during Grand Magal of Touba mass gathering in Senegal. Analysis of 2,381 RDTs found Bioline™ tests achieved 97.3% specificity with 71.1% positive predictive value and 95.3% negative predictive value; ParaHIT® tests showed 92.5% specificity, 26.9% PPV, and 98.7% NPV.

articles.headline: Borreliosis detected in 0.5% during Senegal mass gathering fever
articles.title_original: [Identification of tick-borne borreliosis cases by qPCR from rapid malaria diagnostic tests during the Grand Magal of Touba in Senegal].
articles.authors: Diouf C, Ouaddane I, Diatta G, Goumbala MLB, Sow A, Fam D, Faye M, Gautret P, Sokhna C
articles.details: Study applied nucleic acid extraction and qPCR to 2,381 RDTs collected during Grand Magal of Touba mass gathering in Senegal to identify DENV, Borrelia, Bartonella, and Coxiella burnetti. Identified 13 cases (0.5%) of tick-borne relapsing fever borreliosis including 2 with concurrent P. falciparum malaria; no DENV, Bartonella, or C. burnetti detected, demonstrating RDT samples are valuable for studying fevers of unknown origin.

articles.headline: Type 1 diabetes with ketoacidosis complicated by pancreatitis and malaria
articles.title_original: Convergence of diabetic ketoacidosis, acute pancreatitis, and malaria: A case report.
articles.authors: Kouame KI, Mobio PMN, Bouh JK, Konan JK, Coulibaly TK, Toure CW, Diebi LAA, Kouakou JNH, Koffi BE, Yapo PY
articles.details: Case report of 20-year-old female presenting with concurrent inaugural type 1 diabetes mellitus with diabetic ketoacidosis, acute pancreatitis, and P. falciparum malaria. Patient presented with Glasgow coma score 9, fever (38.5°C), hyperglycemia, elevated pancreatic enzymes, and malaria parasitemia; multidisciplinary management with insulin therapy, rehydration, and antimalarial treatment resulted in favorable clinical outcome.

articles.headline: Antimalarial compound structure-activity study reveals microsomal stability challenge
articles.title_original: Benzo-ring modification on Malaria Box hit MMV008138: effects on antimalarial potency and microsomal stability.
articles.authors: Ghavami M, Li H, Liu L, Butler JH, Ding S, Butschek GJ, Haney RS, Council-Troche RM, Grams RJ, Merino EF, Davis JM, Totrov M, Cassera MB, Carlier PR
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship study of tetrahydro-β-carboline derivative MMV008138 (inhibitor of IspD in MEP pathway) examined 19 benzo-ring substitution variants against multidrug-resistant Dd2 P. falciparum. Only 3 analogs maintained asexual blood-stage activity within 3-fold of parent compound; one derivative (20c) improved microsomal stability but did not enhance plasma exposure or oral efficacy in mouse malaria model.

articles.headline: Imatinib as potential malaria treatment: a systematic review
articles.title_original: The emerging role of Imatinib in malaria management: a review of evidence and future directions.
articles.authors: Ajekiigbe VO, Anthony CS, Ogieuhi IJ, Adeola JO, Bassey PU, Gbolagade-Jonathan P, Aremu SO, Omoleke AA, Bakare IS, Babalola AV
articles.details: This review synthesized 9 studies with varying sample sizes on Imatinib, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor, for malaria treatment. Studies reported declines in parasite density and growth, reduced pyrexia, and synergistic effects with Artesunate. No adverse drug-related events were reported, suggesting a favorable safety profile. Authors recommend further randomized controlled trials and combination therapy studies.

articles.headline: Artemisinin modulates CD8+ T cells and hepatic injury in murine malaria
articles.title_original: Single-cell profiling reveals immunoregulation of artemisinin on CD8(+)GZMB(+) T cells via JAK2-STAT3 in malaria-infected mice.
articles.authors: Chen J, Gao P, He X, Hu Y, Zhou W, Liu Y, Wang J, Liu X, Bai Y, Chen L, Wang C, Cheng G, Zhang X, Wong YK, Liao F, Xu C, Ou J, Wu Y, Zhang W, Gao Y, Tu Y, Wang J
articles.details: Single-cell RNA sequencing of 241,837 cells from livers, spleens, and blood of mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA examined immune responses. CD8+GZMB+ T lymphocytes were identified as driving hepatic injury. Artesunate treatment modulated these pathogenic T cells through the JAK2-STAT3 pathway, reducing their frequency and inflammatory response. The study provides mechanistic insights into artemisinin's immunoregulatory effects during malaria infection.

articles.headline: Community-based larviciding for malaria vector control in Africa
articles.title_original: Assessing the role of community involvement and capacity building in larviciding applications for malaria control in Africa: A scoping review.
articles.authors: Shirima G, Masserey T, Gervas H, Chitnis N, Kiware S, Mirau S
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: A scoping review of 32 studies from 13 sub-Saharan African countries assessed larviciding as a supplementary vector control approach integrated with community engagement. Studies showed significant reductions in mosquito density and in some cases malaria incidence. Community involvement through workshops and participatory meetings was critical for successful implementation, though challenges including logistical barriers and financial constraints were identified.

articles.headline: Malaria cases in Europe: ten-year retrospective review
articles.title_original: Imported and non-travel related cases of malaria in Europe - a ten-year analytic retrospective review.
articles.authors: Vanitha R, Abelein F, Stoyanova KP
articles.details: A ten-year retrospective review of malaria cases in Europe examining imported and non-travel related transmission. Abstract details are unavailable.

articles.headline: Mosquito metabolism and microbiota determine P. vivax infection levels
articles.title_original: Metabolic reprogramming and gut microbiota ecology drive divergent Plasmodium vivax infection outcomes in Anopheles darlingi.
articles.authors: Carlos BC, Voges K, de Andrade Affonso PH, Jaye A, Tong Rios C, Tinoco-Nunes B, Alonso DP, MacCallum RM, Moreno M, Vlachou D, Souza-Neto JA, Christophides GK
articles.details: Experimental infections of Anopheles darlingi with field-derived Plasmodium vivax gametocytes produced two distinct phenotypes: low and high oocyst burdens. Low-infection mosquitoes mounted early metabolic responses including detoxification pathway activation, redox regulation, and purine depletion, with reduced bacterial diversity and enrichment of Enterobacteriales and Pseudomonadales. High-infection mosquitoes exhibited limited metabolic reprogramming and Flavobacteriales expansion. Low infection outcomes consistently correlated with lowest gametocyte densities.

articles.headline: GRRA assay: comprehensive measurement of artemisinin resistance phenotypes
articles.title_original: Measuring growth, resistance, and recovery after artemisinin treatment of Plasmodium falciparum in a single semi-high-throughput assay.
articles.authors: Sievert MAC, Qahash T, Singh PP, Shoue DA, Checkley LA, Brenneman KV, Cassady Z, Kumar S, Li X, Nosten FH, Anderson TJC, Vaughan AM, Romero-Severson J, Ferdig MT
articles.details: A qPCR-based Growth, Resistance, and Recovery assay (GRRA) was developed and validated to measure multiple Plasmodium falciparum phenotypes following artemisinin exposure. The assay measured growth (6-96h), resistance at 120h, and recovery (120-192h) in clinical isolates and progeny from genetic crosses. Resistance at 120h showed the most consistent phenotype for ring-stage susceptibility. The method accommodates both liquid and dried blood spot samples for surveillance applications.

articles.headline: Seasonal socioeconomic disparities in child malaria in rural Guinea
articles.title_original: Inequity in the transmission of malaria infection among children and adolescents: a cohort study in rural Guinea.
articles.authors: Toure AA, Sidibe S, Magassouba AS, Beavogui AH, Conde M, Toure AF, Sidibe T, Keita KS, Delamou A, Kouanda S
articles.details: A 9-month cohort study of 439 children (ages 1-19 years) in 239 rural Guinean households assessed malaria infection by blood smear and household characteristics. Children with older household heads (50-77 years) had higher infection odds in dry season (OR=3.44); adolescents showed greater vulnerability in rainy season with middle-aged (OR=15.78) or single-parent household heads (OR=4.52). Concentration index analysis revealed modest pro-rich inequity among adolescents (CI=0.059-0.085). Secondary education provided strong prevention benefits (246.8% dividend) but primarily favored wealthier households.

articles.headline: High acceptability of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in South Sudan
articles.title_original: "We witnessed medicines help our children, then we became proud": experiences and acceptability of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Aweil, South Sudan.
articles.authors: Okot F, Sans MS, Viganò E, Rom A, Mubiru D, Baker K, Khan J, Julla A
articles.details: A qualitative study assessed acceptability of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine plus amodiaquine distributed monthly for five cycles to children aged 3-59 months in Northern Bahr el Ghazal, South Sudan. Among 97 participants (community leaders, caregivers, health workers), SMC showed high acceptability driven by perceived effectiveness and community health worker trust. Key implementation challenges included environmental barriers and inadequate worker compensation, though stakeholders expressed strong desire for expansion.

articles.headline: Single low-dose primaquine reduces gametocyte carriage in African children
articles.title_original: Single low-dose primaquine for malaria control in Africa: a systematic review of safety, efficacy and implementation barriers.
articles.authors: Ye T, Caspar E, Niyomwungere D, Ouedraogo A, Thiebaut L, Strubel PE, Raobela O, Nyandwi J, Rakotosaona R, Ratsimbasoa A, Sirima SB, Menard D
articles.details: A systematic review of 41 studies (2012-2023) including 15 randomized controlled trials examined SLDPQ (0.25 mg/kg) co-administered with artemisinin-based combination therapies in African contexts. SLDPQ was found safe and well-tolerated even in G6PD-deficient individuals and children under 5. Eight RCTs confirmed significant reductions in gametocyte carriage, validating SLDPQ's efficacy for individual-level transmission reduction. Key implementation barriers included persistent misconceptions about primaquine toxicity and absence of pediatric formulations.

articles.headline: Household entomological inoculation rate and child malaria incidence vary by site
articles.title_original: Relating household entomological measures to individual malaria risk.
articles.authors: McClure M, Arinaitwe E, Kamya MR, Rosenthal PJ, Nankabirwa J, Kilama M, Musiime A, Dorsey G, Greenhouse B, Rodriguez-Barraquer I
articles.details: A cohort study of 439 children (ages 0.5-5 years) in 239 Ugandan households—low-EIR Jinja, intermediate-EIR Kanungu, high-EIR Tororo—examined household EIR (measured by CDC light traps) and malaria incidence (fever + positive thick blood smear). Across all sites, higher EIR corresponded with greater incidence. Within-site associations varied: significant in Kanungu (IRR 1.09, 95% CI 1.04-1.14) but not Jinja or Tororo (IRR ~1.02). Results suggest site-specific transmission dynamics influence the EIR-incidence relationship.

articles.headline: Physician training improves adherence to Sudan malaria case management
articles.title_original: Assessing Physicians' Adherence to the 2023 Sudan Malaria Case Management Protocol.
articles.authors: Muhammed A, Mohammed AAY, Mohammed Ali MA, Elsmani I, Elsetiha AIMM, Salih MMA, Hadeed H, Mahmoud AEM, Mohamed MMM, Hamrawi NWA, Abdalla AYH, Elkabashi A, Abdalgadir Hamdnaalla MA, Elshafie M, Abdelraouf Ahmed HA, Osman Ahmed Osman M, Abdulla OAA, Fakher Aldeen Noman FAR, Alfaki A, Hussien Hamed MO
articles.details: A two-cycle audit assessed physician adherence to Sudan's 2023 malaria management protocol. Baseline (N=42): protocol compliance 30.95%, correct IM artesunate injection 30.95%, second-line malaria in pregnancy treatment 23.81%, first-line P. falciparum 90.48%. After training and guideline dissemination, cycle 2 (N=39): protocol compliance 89.74%, IM artesunate 79.49%, MIP treatment 64.10%, sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine IPTp 92.31%. Training significantly improved adherence across most standards.

articles.headline: Machine learning maps mass drug administration effectiveness across Senegal
articles.title_original: Using transportability methods to map the local effectiveness of mass drug administration for malaria in Senegal.
articles.authors: Roh ME, Tong Y, Heitmann GB, Ba EKC, Ndiaye JL, Fogelson A, Milligan P, Seck A, Diallo A, Lo AC, Baiocchi M, Gosling R, Bennett A, Hsiang MS, Benjamin-Chung J
articles.details: Transportability models with machine learning generalized a cluster-randomized trial's findings (55% MDA-related incidence reduction in SMC areas) to 116 non-trial Senegal Communes. Accounting for weather, vegetation, and population density differences, the authors estimated significant reductions of 36-65% in 74 non-trial Communes, with larger reductions in higher-precipitation, higher-vegetation, cooler areas. MDA was not effective post-intervention, suggesting short-lived effects. This framework provides a scalable approach for generalizing trial findings to target environmentally-mediated interventions.

articles.headline: P. falciparum malaria mimics thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura in Chinese patient
articles.title_original: Severe Plasmodium Falciparum Malaria Mimicking Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP): A Case Report From China.
articles.authors: Wang C, Kong M, Huang M, Tang N, Mo H
articles.details: Case report of a 51-year-old Chinese man with P. falciparum malaria presenting with fever, thrombocytopenia, severe anemia, and elevated lactate and indirect bilirubin. Laboratory findings showed markedly elevated von Willebrand factor but normal ADAMTS13 activity, closely mimicking thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP with PLASMIC score of 7). Parasite diagnosis confirmed by blood smear; patient improved after intravenous artesunate treatment. Study emphasizes importance of differential diagnosis between malaria and TTP to enable rapid, lifesaving treatment.

articles.headline: Malaria-anemia comorbidity affects 39% of pregnant women in Sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria-anemia comorbidity and its determinants among pregnant women in high- and moderate-malaria-risk countries in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Zegeye AF, Wassie M, Tamir TT, Tekeba B, Mekonen EG, Zeleke GA, Gebrehana DA
articles.details: Multilevel analysis of 50,545 weighted samples from Malaria Indicator Surveys (2016–2022) across 17 Sub-Saharan African countries found pooled prevalence of malaria-anemia comorbidity in pregnant women of 39.0% (95% CI 29.0–49.0). Risk factors included: no formal education (OR=1.43), untreated bed nets (OR=1.23), poor wealth (OR=2.37), no indoor residual spraying (OR=2.15), rural residence (OR=2.73), and regional residence in West (OR=8.00), Central (OR=6.76), and South Sub-Saharan Africa (OR=18.76). Study identifies both individual and community-level determinants requiring region-specific interventions.

articles.headline: Epidemic malaria periods vary by district and age group in Togo
articles.title_original: Identifying malaria epidemic periods in Togo by health district and target group: a generalised additive model approach.
articles.authors: Thomas A, Bakai TA, Atcha-Oubou T, Tchadjobo T, Ecochard R, Rabilloud M, Voirin N
articles.details: Generalized additive model analysis of 5,522,650 confirmed malaria cases (2013–2017) from Togo identified epidemic periods varying by health district and target group. Children <5 years represented 36.6%, children ≥5 and adults 58.5%, and pregnant women 4.9% of cases. Time lag of ≥1 month observed between precipitation onset and epidemic start across all groups. Savanes region districts showed later onset and greater relative increase in cases, while Maritime and Lome-commune regions had short or undetectable epidemics. Findings support district-level tailoring of seasonal malaria chemoprevention and risk stratification.

articles.headline: Community health workers effectively deployed in ATSB malaria trial in Zambia
articles.title_original: Comparative analysis of the use of Community Health Workers while deploying the Attractive Targeted Sugar Bait (ATSB) for malaria control in Western Province, Zambia.
articles.authors: Ndalama F, Mulenga D, Arnzen A, Akalalambili S, Tobolo T, Maluma C, Chishya C, Saili K, Ashton RA, Miller J, Silumbe K, Chanda J, Hamainza B, Littrell M, Orange E
articles.details: Comparative analysis (2021–2023) evaluated community health worker (CHW) involvement in phase III Attractive Targeted Sugar Bait trial in Western Zambia against WHO/MOH best-practice guidelines. ATSB trial practices aligned with literature best practices in training and incentives. However, challenges included: only 32% female CHWs in year 2, limited community involvement in selection, and coordination issues. High fidelity (97.1%, 67/69) supervision visits confirmed effectiveness. Study notes that adequately-funded field research facilitates adherence to CHW best practices better than typical programmatic settings.

articles.headline: IPT malaria prevention does not improve child nutritional status in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Malaria-malnutrition interaction: prevalence, risk factors, and the impact of intermittent preventive therapy for malaria on nutritional status of school-age children in Muheza, Tanga, Tanzania - A cross-sectional survey and a randomized controlled open-label trial.
articles.authors: Hhera JJ, Makenga G, Baraka V, Francis F, Nakato S, Gesase S, Mtove G, Madebe R, Kyaruzi E, Minja DTR, Lusingu JPA, Van Geertruyden JP
articles.details: Randomized controlled open-label trial (February 2019–December 2020) in Muheza, Tanzania compared dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ), or control (n=3×50 children age 5–15 years) on nutritional outcomes. Baseline malaria prevalence 27%, stunting 21%, underweight (≤10 years) 23%. Malaria associated with 78% higher odds of stunting (AOR=1.78, 95% CI 1.36–2.33, p<0.001). During intervention, change in mean weight, height, and BMI over time showed no significant difference between DP, ASAQ, and control groups, indicating integrated malaria-plus-nutrition interventions needed.

articles.headline: Galectin-3 does not predict malaria-related insulin resistance in Ghana
articles.title_original: Galectin-3 did not associate with malaria-related insulin resistance in diabetic and non-diabetic respondents at a Ghanaian General Hospital.
articles.authors: Nortey E, Derkyi-Kwarteng L, Amoako-Sakyi D, Bockarie AS, Yeboah S, Acquah S
articles.details: Cross-sectional study at Tema General Hospital measured anthropometric, glucose, lipid, insulin, and galectin-3 levels in fasting diabetic and non-diabetic adults (with/without malaria). Participants with T2DM were older (p<0.05) with higher systolic BP and glucose but lower parasite levels. Galectin-3 could neither predict HOMA-B nor HOMA-IR in any study group. Across all groups, insulin resistance associated with glucose (B=0.603, Exp(B)=1.83, p=0.001) and insulin (B=1.145, Exp(B)=3.14, p<0.001), model explaining 91% variance. Study suggests galectin-3 relationship with insulin resistance is more complex than linear.

articles.headline: Expanded CHW model for malaria elimination acceptable and feasible in Myanmar
articles.title_original: Acceptability, feasibility and fidelity of an expanded role for community health workers for malaria elimination in Myanmar: A mixed-method study.
articles.authors: Oo WH, Khant KM, Htwe EP, Htike W, Aye Tun N, Oo MC, Thu KM, Zaw AK, Galau NH, O'Flaherty K, Agius PA, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: Mixed-method study nested in effectiveness trial in Myanmar evaluated acceptability, feasibility, and fidelity of expanded CHW role for malaria and primary health care. Qualitative data from community leaders (n=6), health stakeholders (n=14), focus groups (n=36), supervision (n=69), field observations (n=6); quantitative survey (n=643 community members). Expanded CHW model was acceptable to 97.4% (626/643), addressed community health demands (93.0%), and showed high fidelity (97.1%, 67/69). Community reliance on expanded CHW services was 98.3% (632/643). Training, supply chain, referral, and data-reporting systems require optimization for wider implementation.

articles.headline: Capsicum frutescens extract reduces oxidative stress in Plasmodium berghei mice
articles.title_original: Evaluating the Oxidative Stress Reducing Potentials of Sub-Fractions of Capsicum Frutescens L. Aqueous Extract on Early Established Plasmodium in Mice.
articles.authors: Nwikwe DC, Ofeniforo BE, Asogwa NT, Nwonuma CO, Balogun EA
articles.details: Experimental study in 204 mice (combined sexes) inoculated with P. berghei (NK65) compared control (normal saline 10 mL/kg), chloroquine (10 mg/kg), and Capsicum frutescens L. (CFL) sub-fractions (0.5–8.0 mg/kg). Blood/liver assessed on days 4 and 8 post-inoculation for lipid peroxidation (malondialdehyde) and antioxidant enzymes (superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione peroxidase, reductase, transferase). CFL sub-fractions significantly reduced MDA (p<0.05) with dose-dependent increase in antioxidant enzymes; sub-fraction B most pronounced. Study suggests CFL sub-fractions possess in vivo antiplasmodial and antioxidant potential.

articles.headline: Both inhibitory and activating KIRs recognize RIFINs in malaria NK control
articles.title_original: Both inhibitory and activating KIRs recognize RIFINs: a dual-edged mechanism of NK cell control in malaria.
articles.authors: Cuapio A, Ljunggren HG
articles.details: No abstract provided; title only. Study describes dual mechanism of natural killer cell control in malaria via recognition of RIFINs (P. falciparum variant surface antigen variant) by both inhibitory and activating killer immunoglobulin-like receptors (KIRs).

articles.headline: Host sphingolipids critical for P. berghei liver-stage development and invasion
articles.title_original: Host sphingolipids support Plasmodium berghei liver stage development.
articles.authors: Schroeder EA, Colón IC, Petruzziello PE, Derbyshire ER
articles.details: Experimental study found sphingolipids essential for P. berghei liver stage. Exogenous C16-ceramide enhanced parasite development and nuclear replication; sphingomyelin in host membrane required for invasion. Live microscopy with NBD-labeled lipids showed active sphingolipid transport into parasitophorous vacuole (PV) throughout liver stage, supported partly by host ceramide transporter CERT1 (enriched at PV). Genetic disruption of CERT1 significantly reduced P. berghei load and ceramide trafficking. Depletion of CERS3 and SPHK1 affected PV size and infection rate but not invasion. Findings suggest host sphingolipid metabolism as novel multistage therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Seven-day high-dose primaquine cures vivax malaria with better adherence than 14-day
articles.title_original: Shortening the Journey to Cure: A Quasi-experimental Trial Comparing Seven-Day and 14-Day Primaquine Radical Therapy for Vivax Malaria.
articles.authors: Shahab K, Salam A, Salih N, Bilal M, Jan M
articles.details: Quasi-experimental trial (9 months) at Hayatabad Medical Complex in Peshawar, Pakistan compared 7-day high-dose (1 mg/kg/day, n=50) vs. 14-day low-dose (0.5 mg/kg/day, n=50) primaquine in patients age 18–76 with confirmed P. vivax. Recurrence rate substantially lower in 7-day group: 2% vs. 26% (p=0.012). Adherence rates: 94% (7-day) vs. 76% (14-day, p=0.012). Both regimens well-tolerated; 7-day had slightly increased mild gastrointestinal symptoms. No serious adverse events. Study concludes 7-day regimen provides equivalent efficacy with improved adherence and acceptable safety, potentially beneficial in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Phytonanotechnology enhances antiparasitic efficacy of plant compounds against malaria
articles.title_original: Harnessing Phytonanotechnology to Tackle Neglected Parasitic Diseases: Focus on Chagas Disease and Malaria.
articles.authors: García M, Magi MS, García MC
articles.details: Narrative review examines phytochemicals with antiparasitic activity against Plasmodium spp. and Trypanosoma cruzi, and advances in phytonanoformulations. Current therapies limited by high toxicity, poor efficacy, drug resistance, and limited accessibility. Plant natural products face barriers of poor solubility, low bioavailability, and chemical instability. Review analyzes integration of phytochemicals into nanocarrier-based drug delivery platforms as strategy to improve pharmacokinetic/therapeutic properties. Study highlights potential of phytonanotechnology to develop more effective and accessible treatments for neglected parasitic diseases including malaria.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine acceptance high among sub-Saharan caregivers (82%)
articles.title_original: Community readiness and acceptance for the implementation of the malaria vaccine among caretakers of at-risk children in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Kigongo E, Puleh SS, Kabunga A, Akech SI, Ocen F, Opollo MS, Ebong M
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 34 studies (25 included, n=25,867 caregivers of children <5 in sub-Saharan Africa) found pooled vaccine acceptance of 82% (95% CI: 73%-90%) and willingness of 80% (95% CI: 70%-90%). Acceptance was lowest in DRC (53%). Despite high heterogeneity (I2>99%) and publication bias, findings suggest strong community readiness for vaccine rollout, though context-specific implementation strategies are needed.

articles.headline: Artemisia plant extracts vs artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum (correction)
articles.title_original: Correction: Assessment of the in vitro activity and selectivity of Artemisia afra and Artemisia annua aqueous extracts against artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Roesch C, Ashraf K, Vantaux A, Marin AA, Maher SP, Franetich JF, Kloeung N, Ke S, Vo HTM, Barale JC, Mazier D, Witkowski B
articles.details: This is a correction notice for a study assessing in vitro activity and selectivity of aqueous extracts from Artemisia afra and Artemisia annua against artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum. No abstract or quantitative results are provided.

articles.headline: LLIN intervention reduces malaria vector habitat by 50-60%
articles.title_original: Ecological drivers of malaria vector habitat and transmission over 1 year of long-lasting insecticidal net intervention in Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Talbot B, Alou LPA, Koffi AA, Sih C, Dangbenon E, Zoh MG, Camara S, Assi SB, N'Guessan R, Messenger LA, Protopopoff N, Cook J, Kulkarni MA
articles.details: A cluster randomized trial across 33 clusters in Côte d'Ivoire used ecological niche modeling with satellite remote sensing to assess habitat changes for three Anopheles species and P. falciparum-infected vectors following LLIN deployment. Minimal to considerable habitat reductions were observed (0-60% across species, >50% for infected vectors), with no significant effects of intervention arm but strong effects of environmental factors (land cover, climate, topography, population density) on vector occurrence.

articles.headline: Age-expanded malaria management acceptable but faces structural barriers
articles.title_original: An Anthropological Analysis of Acceptability and Feasibility of Expanding Community-Based Malaria Management to All Ages in Madagascar: Levers and Challenges for National Scale-Up.
articles.authors: Rabesandratra HF, Mattern C, Brazy-Nancy E, Harimanana A, Irinantenaina J, Razanadranaivo HL, Andrianambinintsoa PTD, Dentinger C, Steinhardt L, Garchitorena A
articles.details: A cluster randomized trial in Madagascar (November 2020-December 2021) with 87 qualitative interviews and 12 focus groups assessed feasibility and acceptability of expanding community health worker (CHW) malaria case management from children <5 to all ages. Stakeholders found the expansion consistent with existing CHW roles and noted improved community understanding of malaria susceptibility across ages. Structural challenges (e.g., cost of care) and sociodemographic inequalities persisted.

articles.headline: Proteomic signature of 11 proteins linked to severe malaria
articles.title_original: Systems analysis of clinical malaria reveals proteomic perturbation and innate-adaptive crosstalk linked to disease severity.
articles.authors: Lautenbach MJ, Wyss K, Yman V, Foroogh F, Satarvandi D, Mousavian Z, Sondén K, Wang J, Álvez MB, Bergström S, Nilsson P, Edfors F, Brodin P, Uhlén M, Sundling C, Färnert A
articles.details: A systems analysis of 1,463 plasma proteins in adult travelers with acute P. falciparum malaria, integrated with single-cell RNA-seq, identified >250 protein perturbations including hormones and tissue-derived proteins. An 11-protein signature distinguished severe malaria cases and clustered by disease severity. Analysis linked systemic proteomic changes to innate-adaptive immune crosstalk and organ-specific responses, advancing understanding of malaria pathogenesis and revealing biomarkers and therapeutic targets.

articles.headline: Malaria risk 5-fold higher in military families from endemic regions
articles.title_original: Malaria Incidence in US Military Families Is Related to Service Member's Birthplace.
articles.authors: Helfrich AM, Lu D, Grance M, Chu X, Hickey PW
articles.details: A retrospective cohort study (2012-2018) of US military personnel and family members identified 281 malaria cases. The malaria-endemic group (n=67) had 5.2-fold higher incidence than the nonendemic group (n=214) (IRR 5.2, 95% CI 2.4-10.4). Sub-Saharan Africa-connected individuals had 42 cases per 100,000 persons annually, accounting for 86.6% of endemic cases. Despite universal health insurance access, only 48% sought pretravel medical counseling.

articles.headline: Colombia malaria campaign prevented 568,000 cases at $41.8 million cost
articles.title_original: Costs and impact of the Malaria Eradication Campaign in Colombia, 1957-1969.
articles.authors: Padilla-Rodríguez JC, Olivera MJ
articles.details: An economic evaluation of Colombia's Malaria Eradication Campaign (1959-1969) found total institutional costs of USD $41,788,924 (74.1% from national sources). The attack phase comprised 80.4% of total costs, with 40% for spraying personnel and 19% for insecticides. The campaign prevented an estimated 568,439 malaria cases (41,965/year), at an average cost of USD $2.70 per household sprayed. Despite significant case prevention, eradication was not achieved.

articles.headline: Novel K13 mutations detected in Gabon; clonal E433D transmission rising
articles.title_original: Clonal Transmission of Emerging Novel Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 Mutations and Increasing Complexity of Infection in Libreville, Gabon, 2021-2023.
articles.authors: Ndong Ngomo JM, Mawili-Mboumba DP, Mouity TN, Leonetti A, Mabika DM, Mihindou JC, Mbondoukwé NP, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Bouyou-Akotet MK, Niaré K
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of 468 P. falciparum samples from Libreville, Gabon (2021-2023) detected no validated artemisinin-resistance K13 mutations but identified 21 samples with novel K13 variants of unknown significance (E433D n=14, Q613H n=5, V520I, V637I). E433D prevalence rose from 0.7% (2022) to 5.5% (2023) with clonal transmission evidence. MDR1 Y184F increased from 52.6% to 68.8%, while low-level DHFR/DHPS mutations and rare CRT K76T were observed.

articles.headline: High SP resistance, no artemisinin resistance in Mozambique P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Antimalarial drug resistance and population structure of Plasmodium falciparum in Mozambique using genomic surveillance at health facilities in 2021 and 2022.
articles.authors: Boene S, Rovira-Vallbona E, da Silva C, García-Ulloa M, Rafael B, Canana N, Aranda-Díaz A, Cisteró P, García-Fernández C, Tembisse D, Ndimande N, Chidimatembue A, Matambisso G, Palmer B, Chico AR, Dimene M, Saifodine A, Inácio J, da Silva M, Plucinski M, Bonnington C, Wate F, de Carvalho E, Mathe G, Pujol A, Arregui-Gallego B, Comiche K, Nhama A, Nhamussua L, Aide P, Saute F, Enosse S, Greenhouse B, Candrinho B, Mayor A
articles.details: Genomic surveillance of 1,146 P. falciparum samples from Mozambique health facilities (2021-2022) detected no pfk13 artemisinin-resistance markers or pfpm2 piperaquine-resistance duplications. However, prevalence of pfdhfr/pfdhps quintuple mutants conferring sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) resistance was very high (>92.5% in 2021, >87.8% in 2022), with rare pfdhps-A581G mutations (1.6% in 2021, 0.8% in 2022). Results support continued use of artemisinin-based combinations while monitoring SP chemopreventive efficacy.

articles.headline: Fractional-order mathematical model predicts malaria control impact
articles.title_original: Fractional-order model of malaria incorporating treatment and prevention strategies.
articles.authors: Agbata BC, Kovaci S, Agbebaku DF, Dervishi R, Abah E, Mbah GCE, Emadifar H, Smerat A
articles.details: A novel fractional-order mathematical model incorporating treatment and prevention strategies was developed to evaluate malaria transmission dynamics. Using the fractional Adams-Bashforth-Moulton method and Lyapunov stability analysis, sensitivity analysis of the basic reproduction number identified key factors. Simulations showed that improved treatment strategies and preventive measures (mosquito control, timely medication) significantly reduced malaria cases, while increased mosquito contact and ineffective treatments aggravated disease.

articles.headline: miR-92a conserved binding sites in PfEMP1 DBLα domain identified
articles.title_original: Exploring the Potential of miRNA-92a-3p as Lead for Sequence-Based Therapies for Malaria.
articles.authors: Prabhu SR, Paul S, Umakanth S, Hande M, Saadi AV, Gupta H, Satyamoorthy K
articles.details: A cross-sectional and case-control study explored miR-92a-3p as a potential therapeutic target against PfEMP1 variants. Sequencing of Pf Duffy-binding-like (DBL) domain in 20 infected patient samples revealed significant sequence variability in the DBLα domain but a conserved motif facilitating host miR-92a binding across isolates, suggesting a therapeutic target despite antigenic variation. Variant analysis identified SNVs and multi-nucleotide variants in miR-92a genes in 9 cases versus 2 controls.

articles.headline: Ferrocenyl quinoline-benzimidazole hybrids show multistage antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Ferrocenyl Quinoline-Benzimidazole Hybrids: A Multistage Strategy to Combat Drug-Resistant Malaria.
articles.authors: Golding TM, Garnie LF, Rabie T, Reader J, Birkholtz LM, Wicht KJ, Smith GS
articles.details: Ferrocenyl-based quinoline-benzimidazole hybrids were synthesized and evaluated in vitro against drug-sensitive NF54 and multidrug-resistant K1 P. falciparum strains, exhibiting potent submicromolar activity. An N,N-dimethylaminomethyl substituent enhanced potency against both strains. Compounds demonstrated multistage activity targeting immature and mature gametocytes, with mechanisms including hemozoin formation inhibition and reactive oxygen species generation under oxidative conditions. Findings suggest potential as antimalarial drug candidates.

articles.headline: Anopheles seasonality and climatic drivers in post-elimination Sri Lanka
articles.title_original: Seasonal abundance and climatic determinants of Anopheles vectors in Sri Lanka: its implications in the malaria post-elimination era.
articles.authors: Hewavitharane M, Ranawaka G, Saparamadu MDJS, Premaratne R, Jayasooriya HTR
articles.details: Two-year field study sampled female Anopheles mosquitoes monthly from five Sri Lankan districts using cattle-baited traps, human landing catches, and indoor hand collections.
