Fifteen Anopheles species were identified; An. culicifacies showed highest mean adult density in intermediate zones with population peaks during monsoon seasons. Vector abundance was significantly influenced by climatic factors (p < 0.05), with humidity affecting An. culicifacies densities and species-specific responses to temperature and rainfall. Intermediate zones exhibited highest receptivity for malaria reintroduction.

articles.headline: Global Pv47 genetic diversity shaped by mosquito-vector adaptation
articles.title_original: Worldwide genetic diversity of Plasmodium vivax Pv47 is consistent with natural selection by anopheline mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Molina-Cruz A, Gonzalez-Ceron L, Dwivedi A, Torres TZB, Raytselis N, Young M, Kamath N, McNinch C, Su X, Ford A, Ferreira MU, Arévalo-Herrera M, Herrera S, Lo E, Silva JC, Barillas-Mury C
articles.details: Global analysis of Plasmodium vivax Pv47 genetic diversity identified most common polymorphisms as non-synonymous, with domain 2 showing signatures of positive selection. Greatest haplotype diversity was found in East/Southeast Asia and Oceania, with significant geographic population structure worldwide. A K27E polymorphism was associated with differential infectivity to Anopheles albimanus and Anopheles pseudopunctipennis in Mexico. The striking similarities between Pv47 and its P. falciparum ortholog Pfs47 suggest vector-driven adaptation selects compatible Pv47 haplotypes.

articles.headline: Declining lumefantrine and DHA susceptibility in Uganda, 2019-2024
articles.title_original: Changes in susceptibility of Plasmodium falciparum to antimalarial drugs in Uganda over time: 2019-2024.
articles.authors: Okitwi M, Orena S, Tumwebaze PK, Katairo T, Taremwa Y, Byaruhanga O, Tukwasibwe S, Nsobya SL, Legac J, Bailey JA, Cooper RA, Conrad MD, Rosenthal PJ
articles.details: Ex vivo susceptibility testing of 1114 P. falciparum isolates from eastern and northern Uganda (2019-2024) to nine antimalarials showed low median IC50s for chloroquine, piperaquine, and artemether derivatives, but higher for quinine and pyrimethamine. Susceptibility improved over time for chloroquine but declined for lumefantrine, mefloquine, and dihydroartemisinin. Decreased lumefantrine susceptibility was associated with PfK13 C469Y/A675V, PfMDR1 N86Y, and PfCARL D611N mutations; decreased DHA susceptibility linked to PfK13 and PfMDR1 Y500N mutations. Clinical impact of these trends remains uncertain.

articles.headline: Ferroportin inhibition exacerbates Plasmodium infection in mice
articles.title_original: Pharmacologic Inhibition of Erythrocyte Ferroportin Expression Exacerbates Plasmodium Infection.
articles.authors: Zeydabadinejad S, Theis BF, Park JS, Gohara AF, Vijay-Kumar M, Yeoh BS, Saha P
articles.details: In a Plasmodium yoelii mouse model, oral ferroportin inhibitor VIT-2763 (Vamifeport) treatment increased parasitemia accompanied by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, erythropoietin, and liver injury markers. VIT-2763 suppressed hepatic hepcidin expression while increasing hepatocyte ferroportin and reducing RBC ferroportin protein levels. Findings reveal tissue-specific ferroportin regulation between hepatocytes and erythrocytes, suggesting modulation of the hepcidin-ferroportin axis as a potential therapeutic strategy for malaria intervention.

articles.headline: Amodiaquine-resistance pfmdr1 haplotype rising in São Tomé
articles.title_original: Trajectory of Plasmodium falciparum Molecular Markers of Amodiaquine Resistance in São Tomé and Príncipe.
articles.authors: Té S, Das Neves E, Pellicer A, Broumou I, Zerebinski J, Santos-Reis A, Nóbrega de Sousa T, Färnert A, Santos A, Santos MJ, Gil JP, Lopes D
articles.details: Analysis of P. falciparum infections from São Tomé and Príncipe (2020-2022) showed increasing prevalence of the amodiaquine-resistance-associated pfmdr1 86Y/Y184/1246Y haplotype compared to historical data, in a background of near-fixation pfcrt 76T. The trajectory suggests successive single-nucleotide polymorphism accretion following artesunate-amodiaquine introduction as first-line therapy since 2005. Dominant emergence of the 86Y/Y184/1246Y haplotype indicates need for heightened surveillance of artesunate-amodiaquine performance.

articles.headline: Deep learning achieves 100% accuracy for malaria parasite detection
articles.title_original: Hybrid Capsule Network for precise and interpretable detection of malaria parasites in blood smear images.
articles.authors: Alawfi B
articles.details: A hybrid capsule network combining CNN-based feature extraction with capsule routing was developed for automated parasite detection and life-cycle stage classification in blood smear images. The model was evaluated on four benchmark datasets (MP-IDB, MP-IDB2, IML-Malaria, MD-2019) with intra- and cross-dataset assessment. Hybrid CapNet achieved up to 100% multiclass accuracy with minimal computational requirements (1.35M parameters, 0.26 GFLOPs) and superior cross-dataset generalization compared to baseline CNNs. Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed focus on biologically relevant parasite regions.

articles.headline: High reported acceptance masks low ivermectin MDA completion in Thailand
articles.title_original: Community acceptance of Ivermectin mass drug administration for malaria in Southern Thailand.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Sripoorote P, Rachaphaew N, Sattabongkot J, Parker DM, Nguitragool W, Phuanukoonnon S
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 391 household heads in Surat Thani Province, Thailand in 2022 found 96.4% reported acceptance of planned ivermectin mass drug administration. Forest-related workers (aOR 4.2, 95% CI 1.1–16.1) and those believing malaria could be eliminated from their villages (aOR 9.1, 95% CI 2.8–29.9) showed higher acceptance. However, actual program completion was substantially lower: only 59.0% of 3,137 eligible individuals completed all three ivermectin MDA rounds, with key barriers being village absence and drug reluctance in later rounds.

articles.headline: East Africa malaria incidence exceeds 250 per 1000 in three countries
articles.title_original: The burden of malaria in East Africa: prevalence, risk factors, and control strategies.
articles.authors: Bashir SG, Ahmed NI, Abdullahi YB, Abdi YH, Abdi MS, Musa MK
articles.details: A systematic review synthesizing peer-reviewed literature, national reports, and WHO surveillance data (2015-2024) from twelve East African countries found Uganda, South Sudan, and Burundi reporting the highest malaria incidence (>250 per 1,000 population). Artemisinin partial resistance exceeded 20% in multiple areas, while pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles vectors undermined control efforts. Climate change was expanding transmission into highland zones. Integrated strategies combining long-lasting insecticidal nets, indoor residual spraying, vaccination, and community engagement reduced severe malaria by up to 47%.

articles.headline: Co-infection with malaria and schistosomiasis in Nigerian schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Malaria, urogenital schistosomiasis and co-infection and nutritional status of school children in Ondo state.
articles.authors: Ola EM, Balogun TH, Isijola RO, Ajakaye OG
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 185 schoolchildren in two communities in Ondo State, Nigeria screened with rapid diagnostic test for malaria and urine microscopy for schistosomiasis. Malaria prevalence was higher in Oke Igbo (57.1%, 36/63) than Ipogun (49.2%, 60/122), with schistosomiasis similarly distributed (28.6% vs 4.1%). Co-infection rates were substantially higher in Oke Igbo (20.6% vs 3.3% in Ipogun). Males were 2.19 times more likely to have co-infection (P=0.02, CI 1.13–4.28). Both infected and co-infected children showed higher chronic malnutrition rates (~75-78%).

articles.headline: Plant extract combination achieves >90% parasitemia reduction in mice
articles.title_original: Enhanced Antimalarial Efficacy of Annona muricata Leaf Extract Combined With Artesunate, Chloroquine, and Pyrimethamine in Plasmodium berghei-Infected ICR Mice.
articles.authors: Sarakul O, Boonhok R, Somsak V
articles.details: An in vivo study evaluated Annona muricata leaf ethanolic extract (AME) combined with artesunate, chloroquine, and pyrimethamine in Plasmodium berghei-infected ICR mice. AME monotherapy showed dose-dependent antimalarial activity at higher doses. The combination of AME with artesunate and pyrimethamine demonstrated significant synergistic effects, achieving >90% parasitemia inhibition and prolonging mean survival time to 30 days. AME combined with chloroquine did not show synergistic interactions, suggesting AME with ART or PYR may enhance antimalarial efficacy.

articles.headline: P. vivax genomes show reduced diversity in Duffy-negative individuals
articles.title_original: Highly conserved Plasmodium vivax genomes in Duffy-negative individuals from Sudan.
articles.authors: Schroeder RE, Ahmed S, Ford A, Elfaki M, Hamad SO, Ahmed TME, Mohamed S, Manko E, Clark TG, Campino S, Hamid MMA, Lo E
articles.details: A genomic analysis compared P. vivax infections from Duffy-positive and Duffy-negative individuals in Sudan. Duffy-positive infected individuals had significantly higher parasitemia (p=0.0000132). Whole-genome sequencing revealed Duffy-positive P. vivax genomes were substantially more diverse than Duffy-negative genomes across all 14 chromosomes and 44 erythrocyte binding gene candidates. Merozoite surface protein family genes accounted for much genetic diversity. Many erythrocyte binding genes showed signatures of both positive and negative selection, with mutations potentially altering binding and antigenic properties.

articles.headline: Sequential RDT algorithm with PCR reflex improves diagnostic accuracy
articles.title_original: Refining malaria diagnosis in high-transmission areas: a dual-approach with rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) and dbPCR-NALFIA.
articles.authors: Some DY, Kiemde F, Sorgho H, Tahita MC, Bere AW, Ouedraogo A, Banao SV, Bagre K, Djiri G, Some G, Rouamba T, Hien YE, Savadogo A, Schallig HDFH, Tinto H
articles.details: An evaluation of a malaria diagnostic algorithm was conducted with 438 febrile patients at rural health facilities in Burkina Faso. The algorithm combined sequential interpretation of two-step RDTs detecting PfHRP2 and pLDH antigens, with direct-on-blood mini-PCR-NALFIA confirmatory testing for undetermined results (PfHRP2+/pLDH−). Of 438 samples, 87.2% (382) had conclusive sequential interpretation without requiring PCR. The algorithm achieved sensitivity 97.9%, specificity 94.8%, positive predictive value 97.2%, and negative predictive value 96.1%, compared to 95.2% sensitivity and 73.2% specificity for HRP2-based RDT alone.

articles.headline: High MSP2 genetic diversity in asymptomatic P. falciparum carriers
articles.title_original: Genetic polymorphism of msp2 in Plasmodium falciparum isolates among asymptomatic malaria infections from two ecological settings in Cameroon.
articles.authors: Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Abate L, Feufack-Donfack BL, Ngou CM, Bayibeki AN, Moukoko CEE, Ayong LS, Berry A, Lefèvre T, Morlais I, Nsango SE
articles.details: Study of P. falciparum genetic diversity in asymptomatic individuals from Cameroon (Mfou 59.2% prevalence, Tibati 63.4% prevalence). Found 36 msp2 alleles in Mfou and 42 in Tibati. Over 60% of isolates had multiclonal infections (MOI 2.08 in Mfou, 1.80 in Tibati). MOI negatively correlated with age in Mfou; males had significantly higher MOI than females.

articles.headline: Night outdoor activities increase malaria risk in Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Risk factors for contracting malaria in six wards of Mudzi District, Zimbabwe: A case control-study.
articles.authors: Fambirai T, Chimbari MJ, Ndarukwa P
articles.details: Case-control study in Mudzi District with 94 cases and 91 controls. Night outdoor social/religious activities increased infection risk (AOR 8.13, 95% CI 1.74–37.90). Having a garden also increased risk (AOR 4.51, 95% CI 1.55–13.12). Protective factors: full body clothing at night (AOR 0.13), sleeping in sprayed rooms (AOR 0.04). Despite 96.7% of cases having good malaria knowledge, outdoor activities remained significant risk factors.

articles.headline: Global malaria care quality highest in Gulf states, lowest in Africa
articles.title_original: Inequalities in disease burden and care quality of neglected tropical diseases and malaria, 1990-2021: Findings from the 2021 Global Burden of Disease Study.
articles.authors: He R, Zhu W, Hui S, Yu M, Li H, Li Y, Huang P, Yu R
articles.details: Global Burden of Disease 2021 analysis calculated quality of care index (QCI) using six indicators: incidence, mortality, prevalence, DALYs, years of life lost, and years lived with disability. Highest QCI: Bahrain (91.9), Qatar (91.5), UAE (91.5). Lowest QCI: Uganda (78.5), Niger (77.1), Burkina Faso (75.2). QCI positively correlated with economic development; lower quality in children under 10 and adults over 95.

articles.headline: Malaria reply
articles.title_original: Malaria. Reply.
articles.authors: Daily JP, Parikh S
articles.details: No abstract available; title indicates response/letter on malaria topic but substantive details cannot be extracted.

articles.headline: Malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria.
articles.authors: Barkati S, Balerdi-Sarasola L, Yansouni CP
articles.details: No abstract available; title provides insufficient information for meaningful summary.

articles.headline: Infection timing does not affect liver-stage parasite establishment
articles.title_original: Time-of-day of infection: impact on liver stage malaria parasites in untreated and drug-treated hosts.
articles.authors: Schneider P, O'Donnell AJ, Herbert-Mainero A, Reece SE
articles.details: Experimental study using Plasmodium berghei-infected Anopheles stephensi to infect mice during rest or active phases, with and without pyrimethamine treatment. Pyrimethamine reduced parasite liver burdens but effect was not biologically significant relative to host time-of-day. Host circadian phase did not affect parasite establishment in untreated infections.

articles.headline: P. vivax prevalence 11% with migration and travel as key risk factors
articles.title_original: Slide positivity, trends, and risk factors of febrile Plasmodium vivax malaria along the Thailand-Myanmar border, 2018-2023.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Rachaphaew N, Sripoorote P, Htwe KZZ, Suk-Aum K, Kaewkungwal J, Lawpoolsri S, Cui L, Sattabongkot J
articles.details: Longitudinal surveillance study along Thailand-Myanmar border, 2018–2023, of 13,347 febrile patients. P. vivax slide positivity rate 11.0%. Risk factors: school-aged children (aOR 1.56), working-age adults (aOR 1.43), males (aOR 1.19), Myanmar nationals (aOR 2.37), Lao/Cambodian nationals (aOR 5.50), recent travel (aOR 2.38), livestock occupations (aOR 2.49). Bed net use protective (aOR 0.39).

articles.headline: Climate change increasing malaria transmission suitability across Canada
articles.title_original: Current and future temperature suitability for autochthonous transmission of malaria in Canada.
articles.authors: Siebels K, Ng V, Ogden N, Schofield S, Ludwig A
articles.details: Temperature-based modeling of Plasmodium vivax and P. falciparum transmission suitability in Canada, 2000–2023 and future projections. Rising trends observed for both species. Yearly frequencies reaching 34% of Canadian population suitable for P. falciparum transmission and 56% for P. vivax. Projected populations vary significantly with parasite species, climate scenario, and climate model.

articles.headline: An. gambiae dominates transmission at high altitude in western Cameroon
articles.title_original: Diversity of anopheline species and malaria transmission dynamics in high-altitude areas of western Cameroon.
articles.authors: Kiam BC, Bouopda-Tuedom AG, Mbida Mbida JA, Ibrahima I, White SJ, Tchuenkam PVK, Popkin-Hall ZR, Mbouh M, Nanssong V CT, Abate L, Onguene CJ, Tumamo Fotso B, Sadler JM, Parr JB, Lin JT, Juliano JJ, Ali IM, Dinglasan RR, Nsango SE
articles.details: Entomological study in three Cameroon localities (May–June 2023): Santchou (700 m), Dschang (1400 m), Penka Michel (1500 m). Of 2,835 Anopheles, An. gambiae s.l. comprised 82.88%. Human-biting rate for An. gambiae: 45.25 bites/human/night (Penka Michel) vs. 3.1 (Santchou) vs. 0.41 (Dschang; p<0.001). EIR 13× higher in Penka Michel (1.11) than Santchou (0.08 infective bites/human/night). P. falciparum dominant (67% Santchou, 62% Penka Michel); P. malariae (33%, 31%); P. ovale (1.21%).

articles.headline: P. falciparum egress disrupts blood-brain barrier via JAK-STAT pathway
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum egress disrupts endothelial junctions and activates JAK-STAT signaling in a microvascular 3D blood-brain barrier model.
articles.authors: Piatti L, Batzilla A, Nakaki F, Fleckenstein H, Korbmacher F, Long RKM, Schraivogel D, Hawkins JA, Romero-Uruñuela T, López-Gutiérrez B, Sanz Sender S, Schwab Y, Steinmetz LM, Sharpe J, Bernabeu M
articles.details: 3D blood-brain barrier model using primary endothelial cells, astrocytes, and pericytes. Parasite egress products increased microvascular permeability with downregulation of junctional genes and upregulation of ferroptosis, interferon, and JAK-STAT pathway genes. Cytoadherent schizont-infected RBCs induced localized endothelial gaps at egress sites. JAK-STAT inhibitor Ruxolitinib prevented egress-product-induced permeability increase.

articles.headline: Regional malaria-in-pregnancy patterns across Nigeria show distinct transmission cycles
articles.title_original: Time series analysis of malaria in pregnancy, using wavelet and SARIMAX models.
articles.authors: Oniyelu DO, Folorunsho O, Adewole L, Bakare EA, Okoronkwo C, Eze N
articles.details: Time-series analysis of malaria in pregnancy (MIP) across Nigeria, January 2015–January 2025, using wavelet coherence and ARIMA/SARIMAX models. Cross River peaked in 2017 and 2019; Enugu lowest in 2017. Malaria peaks in southern states lower than northern troughs. SARIMAX captured MIP trends better than ARIMA in most regions (except Kebbi). Strong MIP-general malaria correlation in Kebbi, Niger, Yobe, Ondo; weaker in South-South, South-East.

articles.headline: Gene drive efficacy requires low resistance allele generation and low fitness cost
articles.title_original: A model-informed target product profile for population modification gene drives for malaria control.
articles.authors: Mondal A, Sánchez C HM, Marshall JM
articles.details: Eco-epidemiological modeling of population modification gene drives in Burkina Faso and Kenya at three transmission intensities. Modeled target outcomes: ≥50% clinical malaria reduction for ≥3 years and <1 year time-to-impact. Key parameters: fitness cost of gene drive allele and effector gene efficacy most influential. Low functional resistance allele generation rates preferred; costly non-functional alleles allow drive persistence. Homing rates already achieved for Anopheles drives are sufficient.

articles.headline: Novel compounds identified for prophylactic activity against hypnozoites
articles.title_original: Identification of novel compounds with prophylactic activity against hypnozoites using a Plasmodium cynomolgi in vitro model.
articles.authors: Zeeman A-M, Vermaat L, Castellote Alvaro MI, Van der Werff NM, Glas-Nieuwenhuis IG, Harupa-Chung A, Calderón F, Gamo F-J, Fernandez Alvaro E, Kocken CH
articles.details: A small focused set of 568 compounds from the GSK TCAMS library were screened in vitro against Plasmodium cynomolgi liver stages. 12 compounds showed IC50 values below 2 µM against hypnozoites, with the most active displaying nanomolar activity. Six chemical clusters were identified; some compounds may work via cytochrome bc1 inhibition or kinase inhibition. The high hit rate suggests preselection based on activity against gametocytes and liver stages is effective for identifying hypnozoite-active compounds.

articles.headline: GABA(A) receptor RDL modulates malaria mosquito auditory sensitivity
articles.title_original: The GABA(A) receptor RDL modulates the auditory sensitivity of malaria mosquitoes.
articles.authors: Ellis DA, Alampounti AC, Suppermpool A, Georgiades M, Freeman E, Bagi J, Tytheridge S, Terrazas-Duque D, Albert JT, Andrés M
articles.details: This study examined the role of GABA and the GABAA receptor RDL in malaria mosquito hearing. Using picrotoxin to antagonize RDL receptors, researchers observed multilevel auditory effects including anatomical changes in the ear and increased spontaneous firing of the antennal nerve. These effects depend on octopamine signaling in both octopamine-dependent and octopamine-independent pathways. RDL modulates electrical responses and likely plays a role in mating behavior in natural malaria mosquito populations.

articles.headline: Novel filarioid species identified in Thai chickens with concurrent Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Discovery of potentially novel species of the Onchocercidae (Nematoda: Filarioidea) in Burmese fighting chickens (Gallus gallus): Genetic insights into avian filariasis and co-infection with Plasmodium juxtanucleare.
articles.authors: Narapakdeesakul D, Junsiri W, Kongtawee R, Lattisarapunt K, Taweethavonsawat P
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Microscopy and molecular diagnostics (cox1 gene) of chickens in Nakhon Ratchasima Province, Thailand revealed 2 out of 12 tested positive for onchocercid filarioids. One chicken had Onchocercidae sp. CH09 with unsheathed microfilariae; another had mixed infection with two distinct isolates (CH07-S1 and CH07-S2). Genetic analysis suggested these may represent novel onchocercid species. Plasmodium juxtanucleare was identified in 6 out of 12 chickens, including one co-infection with onchocercids.

articles.headline: Focal primaquine mass administration effective for P. vivax elimination in Myanmar
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Focal Primaquine Mass Administration for Eliminating Plasmodium vivax Malaria in Northern Myanmar: A Cluster-Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Aung PL, Soe MT, Soe TN, Zhao Y, Cao Y, Aung PP, Oo TL, Lawpoolsri S, Nguitragool W, Sattabongkot J, Kaewkungwal J, Kyaw MP, Parker DM, Cui L
articles.details: A cluster-randomized crossover trial in northern Myanmar evaluated 0.25 mg/kg/day primaquine base for 14 days as directly observed therapy in 541 participants (group 1) and 667 participants (group 2). Clinical P. vivax incidence sharply declined to zero within one month and remained vivax-free for nearly a year. Round 1 MDA showed aOR 0.46 (95% CI 0.25-0.83) for subclinical P. vivax versus control. Round 2 showed aOR 0.15 (95% CI 0.03-0.65). PQ-MDA effectively reduced both clinical and subclinical P. vivax infections.

articles.headline: Microhaplotype sequencing distinguishes P. vivax relapse from recrudescence and reinfection
articles.title_original: Microhaplotype deep sequencing assays to capture Plasmodium vivax infection lineages.
articles.authors: Kleinecke M, Sutanto E, Rumaseb A, Hoon KS, Trimarsanto H, Osborne A, Manrique P, Peters T, Hawkes D, Benavente ED, Whitton G, Siegel SV, Pearson RD, Amato R, Rai A, Nhien NTT, Nguyen HC, Assefa A, Degaga TS, Abate DT, Rahim AG, Pasaribu AP, Sutanto I, Alam MS, Pava Z, Lopera-Mesa T, Echeverry D, William T, Anstey NM, Grigg MJ, Day NP, White NJ, Kwiatkowski DP, Taylor AR, Noviyanti R, Neafsey D, Price RN, Auburn S
articles.details: A high-throughput amplicon sequencing assay with 93 microhaplotype markers determined identity-by-descent (IBD) between P. vivax isolates. The assay was evaluated in 745 global infections including 128 pairs from a randomized controlled trial (NCT01680406). IBD analysis revealed lower frequency of suspected relapses/recrudescence in patients treated with primaquine versus without; impact was greater when primaquine was paired with chloroquine than with artemether-lumefantrine. The assay demonstrates potential to derive new information on P. vivax treatment efficacy and transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Tafenoquine inhibits hemozoin crystal formation in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Tafenoquine Disrupts Intraerythrocytic Stages of Malaria Parasites by Inhibiting Nonclassical Pathways of β-Hematin Nucleation and Growth.
articles.authors: Lee HJ, Massih S, Abdullah M, Rimer JD, Sullivan DJ, Vekilov PG
articles.details: This study explored how tafenoquine (TQ) interacts with heme detoxification pathways in malaria parasites using β-hematin as a synthetic analogue of hemozoin. Tafenoquine blocks crystal growth sites and potently suppresses nonclassical growth mode and nucleation of β-hematin crystals by impeding formation of mesoscopic hematin-rich clusters. TQ exerts greater ring-stage suppression than trophozoite suppression. The mechanism highlights TQ's ability to disrupt parasite survival by inhibiting hemozoin synthesis in addition to disrupting mitochondrial function.

articles.headline: Penicillium metabolites identified as potential PfLDH inhibitors
articles.title_original: Penicillium-Derived Inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum Lactate Dehydrogenase (PfLDH): A Computational Approach for Novel Antimalarial Therapy Development.
articles.authors: Sama-Ae I, Muengthongon P, Tohlaeh A, Rukhachan W, Kiattikul P, Samaeng F, Mitklin A, Rahman MA, Tedasen A, Kwankaew P, Kotepui M, Kepan A
articles.details: A curated library of Penicillium-derived compounds underwent drug-likeness and toxicity screening, identifying 42 viable candidates as inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum lactate dehydrogenase (PfLDH). Molecular docking revealed three promising compounds (Penicilactone B, Penicillimide, Penicillisocoumarin A) with binding affinities exceeding positive controls NADH and pyruvate. Penicilactone B showed strongest binding affinity (-8.71 kcal/mol) and lowest inhibitory constant (414.77 nM). Molecular dynamics confirmed stability over 200 ns with minimal off-target interactions to human lactate dehydrogenase.

articles.headline: Deep lymphocyte profiling guides immune management in malaria-associated ARDS
articles.title_original: Monitoring of deep lymphocyte phenotypes in the blood and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of patients with severe malaria-associated acute respiratory distress syndrome.
articles.authors: Gomes AMC, Adão-Serrano M, Cunha MRD, Silva JS, Sousa AE, Ribeiro JM, Fernandes SM
articles.details: A longitudinal case of severe malaria-associated acute respiratory distress syndrome supported by venovenous ECMO showed dynamic shifts in T cells and rare innate lymphoid cell populations despite persistent lymphopenia. Type 2 immune profile was preponderant at the acute phase; decreased exhausted T-cell populations indicated recovery. Significant differences existed between blood and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid immune profiles. Multiple-compartment immune monitoring from both compartments was valuable for guiding treatment decisions and assessing recovery.

articles.headline: Social determinants shape malaria vulnerability in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Malaria and determinants of health: a scoping review of malaria vulnerabilities in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Md Hanif SA, Hassan MR, Safian N, Sutan R, Alabed AAA, Rafi'i MR, Md Asari SN, Naserrudin NA, Dapari R
articles.details: A scoping review following PRISMA-ScR guidelines identified 22 peer-reviewed studies (2014-2024) addressing malaria risk and vulnerability in Southeast Asia, including cross-border and migrant populations. Five thematic domains were synthesized: biological influences, demographic and socioeconomic parameters, built and lived environments, behavior and practices, and healthcare access/information. The review concludes that malaria vulnerability in SEA populations is multifaceted and deeply entrenched in complex, interrelated factors requiring region-specific, multi-sectoral interventions and equitable public health policies.

articles.headline: SSU rRNA variant in P. malariae impairs nested PCR detection
articles.title_original: First report of a Plasmodium malariae SSU rRNA gene variant in Africa associated with reduced amplification by nested PCR.
articles.authors: Goto M, Yamamoto K, Komaki-Yasuda K, Kano S, Ohmagari N
articles.details: A case of P. malariae infection in a 56-year-old Japanese man returning from Sierra Leone presented with unusual nested PCR results: the secondary PCR band was fainter than the primary PCR band, reversing the typical pattern. Sequence analysis revealed a four-base deletion in the SSU rRNA gene within the species-specific reverse primer binding site, partially impairing amplification. Cytochrome b sequencing confirmed 100% identity with P. malariae. This is the first report of this SSU rRNA variant in Africa and highlights the need for molecular surveillance and sequence validation.

articles.headline: AI spatial analysis reveals malaria hotspots and key determinants globally
articles.title_original: Unraveling global malaria incidence and mortality using machine learning and artificial intelligence-driven spatial analysis.
articles.authors: Rahman MS, Shiddik MAB
articles.details: Spatial analysis and machine learning (XGBoost) with explainable AI analyzed malaria data from 106 countries (2000-2022) from WHO, World Bank, and UNICEF sources. In 2022, malaria cases reached 251.75 million; peak deaths occurred in 2020 (99,554). Nigeria had highest incidence (1,332.99 million), followed by DRC and India. XGBoost achieved best predictive performance (RMSE=0.63, r²=0.93, MAE=0.46). Key determinants of malaria: access to basic sanitation, electricity availability, population growth, and under-5 mortality rate. The integrated framework offers actionable insights for targeted interventions and evidence-based surveillance systems.

articles.headline: Pantothenate regulates mosquito feeding and reproduction in Anopheles stephensi
articles.title_original: Pantothenate regulates feeding and reproduction in the malaria vector Anopheles stephensi, with patterns dependent on supplementation scheme and parental nutrition.
articles.authors: Dobson ME, Kaylor HL, Pruett SL, Brady J, Savoie-Penton K, Isoe J, Debebe Y, Riehle MA, Luckhart S
articles.details: Female Anopheles stephensi were supplemented with pantothenate (vitamin B5, substrate for coenzyme A biosynthesis) via water or blood, assessing blood-feeding behavior, reproduction, and offspring sex ratio across multiple generations. Pantothenate regulated vectorially important traits in concentration-, delivery-, and age-dependent ways, with greatest effects on reproduction. Unsupplemented offspring of mothers supplemented with Pan via water showed increased fecundity, indicating transgenerational effects. Pan provisioned in blood only affected supplemented mothers and their supplemented offspring, establishing the importance of Pan-CoA biology in A. stephensi reproduction.

articles.headline: Dry season and polyclonal infections drive gametocyte carriage
articles.title_original: Risk factor for gametocyte carriage and gametocytemia in Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Li M, Bian Y, Ruan S, Wu Z, Zhang D, Ma T, Wu Y, Liu X, Wang D, Lin J, Pan D, Cui W, Wang L, Wei H, Zhang X, Wang Q, Zeng W, Yang Z, Cao Y, Cui L, Parker DM, Zhao Y
articles.details: Study of 8,240 P. vivax and 510 P. falciparum infections in Myanmar (2011–2020). P. vivax gametocyte carriage was 88.8% (7,249/8,240), associated with dry season, prior malaria history, fever, and parasite density; gametocytemia increased with asexual density (P<0.001). P. falciparum had 10.98% carriage (56/510), linked to younger age and prior malaria. Polyclonal P. vivax infections showed significantly higher gametocyte densities than monoclonal (P<0.0001).

articles.headline: Vaccine efficacy and booster strategies predict malaria reduction
articles.title_original: Performance characteristics and potential public health impact of improved pre-erythrocytic malaria vaccines targeting childhood burden.
articles.authors: Malinga J, Braunack-Mayer L, Masserey T, Cavelan A, Chandramohan D, Dicko A, Ouédraogo JB, Kelly SL, Le Rutte EA, Nekkab N, Penny MA
articles.details: Individual-based transmission model of pre-erythrocytic vaccines RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M. A vaccine with 90% initial efficacy, 6–12 month half-life, and yearly boosters achieved 60–80% yearly incidence reduction and sustained 35% reduction in children <6 years in 12 months after 5-year cessation (PfPR2-10 <30%). Extending half-life to 12–18 months achieved similar impact with lower initial efficacy, suggesting vaccine properties should match deployment contexts and epidemiological settings.

articles.headline: Correction notice for asymptomatic malaria detection study
articles.title_original: Correction: Detection through the use of RT-MqPCR of asymptomatic reservoirs of malaria in samples of patients from the indigenous Comarca of Guna Yala, Panama: Essential method to achieve the elimination of malaria.
articles.authors: Cáceres Carrera L, Santamaría AM, Castillo AM, Romero L, Urriola E, Torres-Cosme R, Calzada JE
articles.details: Correction to prior publication (DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0305558) on RT-MqPCR detection of asymptomatic malaria reservoirs in Guna Yala, Panama.

articles.headline: Glutathione S-transferases emerge as targets for resistance
articles.title_original: Targeting Plasmodium glutathione S-transferases to overcome antimalaria drug resistance in Southern Africa.
articles.authors: Otun OS, Achilonu I
articles.details: Review examining glutathione S-transferases (GSTs) as contributors to antimalarial drug resistance in Southern Africa. While artemisinin resistance has not yet been observed in the region, Kelch13 (K13) mutations conferring resistance have been identified in sub-Saharan Africa. The review proposes GST inhibition as an adjunct therapy and pre-emptive strategy to avert resistance emergence and consolidate malaria control in Southern African populations.

articles.headline: Hepatitis A with malaria coinfection in child
articles.title_original: Hepatitis A-Induced Fulminant Hepatic Failure Linked to Autoimmune Hepatitis in a Child Amidst Diagnostic Challenges of Dengue and Malaria.
articles.authors: Bilal M, Ajmal MU, Ali A
articles.details: Case report of 5-year-old with hepatitis A–induced fulminant hepatic failure (FHF) complicated by Plasmodium vivax parasitemia and evolving autoimmune hepatitis (AIH). Initial presentation mimicked dengue hemorrhagic fever with thrombocytopenia and hepatosplenomegaly; dengue serology was negative. Treatment with intravenous antibiotics, oral antimalarials, lactulose, rifaximin, and transfusional support achieved stabilization; persistent transaminitis revealed antinuclear antibody (ANA) positivity (1:320, speckled) suggesting type 1 AIH.

articles.headline: Rickettsial coinfection may trigger Plasmodium vivax relapse
articles.title_original: Acute Rickettsiosis Triggering Plasmodium vivax Relapse in a Returned Traveler: A Case Report and Clinical Review of Travel-Related Coinfections.
articles.authors: Bagga R, Fuller C, Shahzad K, Bado E, Joshi J, Fernando D, Hempel A, Boggild AK
articles.details: Case report of returned traveler from South America with confirmed intercurrent rickettsia and Plasmodium vivax infection, with rickettsial infection potentially triggering P. vivax relapse. Highlights diagnostic challenges in co-endemic regions when malaria testing occurs without subsequent rickettsial testing, particularly important for P. vivax and P. ovale infections in returning travelers where relapse risk exists.

articles.headline: Novel PX1 mutations drive lumefantrine resistance in Uganda
articles.title_original: A novel locus associated with decreased susceptibility of Plasmodium falciparum to lumefantrine and dihydroartemisinin has emerged and spread in Uganda.
articles.authors: Niare K, Tafesse B, Treat M, Sadler J, Okitwi M, Orena S, Asua V, Kreutzfeld O, Legac J, Samuel NL, Yeka A, Rosenthal PJ, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Conrad MD
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of P. falciparum identified a novel locus on chromosome 7 (px1, phosphoinositide-binding protein gene PF3D7_0720700) with strong selection signal. A haplotype with three SNPs (L1222P, M1701I, D1705N) and two indels (PIN) emerged in 2008 and reached >50% prevalence by 2016 in northern Uganda and 2023 in eastern Uganda. PIN-carrying parasites showed significantly decreased ex vivo susceptibility (IC50) to lumefantrine, mefloquine, and dihydroartemisinin, independent of Kelch13 mutation status.

articles.headline: High IPTp uptake reduces malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Intermittent preventive treatment, malaria, HIV, hepatitis and pregnancy outcomes in Nigerian women: a cross-sectional study in two healthcare facilities.
articles.authors: Chukwuanukwu RC, Eleje GU, Menkiti FE, Ikpeze GC, Mbachu CN, Obiegbu NP, Ogbomade CC, Anyaegbu HI, Ehiaghe AF, Ezeagwuna DA, Efobi CC, Egwuatu EC, Orjinta KK, James JE, Agbanu CM, Onubuogu CU, Ezenwa OB, Okafor CD, Onah KS, Chukwuanukwu TO
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 134 pregnant women in Nigeria. Malaria prevalence was 5.97% (8/134), HIV 3.73% (5/134), and hepatitis B 4.47% (6/134). Intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) use was 73.9%. Preeclampsia occurred in 3.73% (5/134), premature rupture of membranes in 2.24% (3/134), and preterm deliveries in 5.2% (7/134). Lower malaria prevalence compared to previous regional reports attributed to high IPTp implementation.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium and West Nile virus in Spanish wetland
articles.title_original: Vector surveillance of avian Plasmodium and West Nile virus in Culex mosquitoes from Doñana, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
articles.authors: Martínez-de la Puente J, Tamayo-Quintero J, Ruiz-López MJ, Veiga J, Ruiz S, Vázquez A, Herrero L, Soriguer RC, Figuerola J
articles.details: Molecular screening of 95 mosquito pools (1,376 females) from 4 Culex species in Doñana wetland, southern Spain. West Nile virus (WNV) detected in 5 pools of Cx. perexiguus. Avian Plasmodium found in 28 pools: P. vaughani (n=22), P. matutinum (n=4), and unidentified Plasmodium sp. (n=1). Plasmodium prevalence significantly associated with mosquito species and sampling session; none tested positive for Dirofilaria filarioid nematodes.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/3 deletions threaten RDT reliability in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Deletion of target gene (histidine-rich protein 2/3) for Plasmodium falciparum rapid diagnostic tests in Amhara region, Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Adamu A, Alemu G, Yimer M, Tegegne B, Mekasha S
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 1,232 microscopy-confirmed P. falciparum patients across 28 health facilities in Amhara region, Ethiopia (2021–2022). PCR analysis of 179 samples found 88.3% (158) carried pfhrp2/3 gene deletions: 51.4% (92) dual deletion, 34.6% (62) pfhrp3-only, 2.2% (4) pfhrp2-only. Of 109 false-negative pfhrp2-based RDT results, 93.6% (102) were due to gene deletions. Deletion prevalence exceeded WHO's 5% threshold across all survey domains.

articles.headline: Community-led larval control reduces Anopheles by 96-97%
articles.title_original: Empowering communities for malaria control: Effectiveness of community-led biolarviciding using Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis in The Gambia.
articles.authors: Jassey B, Yudhastuti R, Manjang B, Touray I, Ridha MR, Diyanah KC, Indah Permatasari F
articles.details: Non-randomized controlled trial of Bacillus thuringiensis var. israelensis (Bti) application to breeding sites in Gambia (2023–2024) across three arms: community-led, expert-supervised, and control. By round 10, community-led Bti achieved 96.8% reduction in larval density, 97.4% in pupal emergence, and 96.2% in adult populations; expert-supervised achieved 98.6%, 99.1%, and 98.8% respectively (p<0.001). Over 85% of community participants reported visible mosquito reduction, demonstrating scalable, sustainable vector control.

articles.headline: Bayesian modeling identifies emerging malaria hotspots in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Identifying hotspots of malaria incidence and mortality for tailored interventions in Cameroon using routine data from 2011 to 2021: A Bayesian space-time variability modeling.
articles.authors: Arnold FF, Kirakoya-Samadoulougou F, Marcelin A, Jean F, Ye Y, Samadoulougou S
articles.details: Bayesian spatiotemporal analysis of routine malaria data from 189 health districts in Cameroon (2011–2021) using Kulldorff's scan statistics. Among districts, 44.9% (85) showed increasing trends in uncomplicated incidence, 37.6% (71) in severe incidence, and 32.8% (62) in deaths. Twenty-four percent (46), 23.3% (44), and 21.7% (41) districts were identified with high likelihood of becoming hotspots for uncomplicated, severe, and malaria deaths, respectively. Results indicate neutral-spot and coldspot districts with increasing trends warrant proactive targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Lycopene reduces malaria-induced oxidative damage in mice
articles.title_original: Lycopene Mitigates Malaria-Induced Reactive Oxygen and Nitrogen Species and Oxidative Damage in Mice Brain and Lungs.
articles.authors: Varela ELP, Gomes ARQ, da Silva Barbosa Dos Santos A, Dos Santos Guimarães M, de Carvalho EP, Ferreira OO, de Oliveira MS, Iriti M, de Aguiar Andrade EH, Percário S
articles.details: Mice infected with Plasmodium berghei were treated with lycopene (3.11 mg/kg bw/day) or N-acetylcysteine (62 mg/kg bw/day) and evaluated at 1, 4, 8, and 12 days post-infection. Lycopene significantly attenuated infection-induced increases in TBARS, uric acid, and nitric oxide levels compared to untreated infected and NAC-treated groups (p < 0.0001), normalizing them to sham levels. Findings highlight lycopene's therapeutic potential against malaria-related oxidative stress in brain and lung tissue.

articles.headline: Biannual azithromycin modestly reduces malaria parasitemia in preschoolers
articles.title_original: Biannual Mass Azithromycin Distributions for Preschool Children and Malaria Parasitemia: A Secondary Analysis of the MORDOR Cluster Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Arzika AM, Abdou A, Maliki R, Lebas E, Cook C, Vanderschelden B, O'Brien KS, Cotter SY, Varnado NE, Callahan EK, Bailey RL, West SK, Rosenthal PJ, Porco TC, Lietman TM, Keenan JD
articles.details: Cluster randomized trial in Niger (30 communities: 15 azithromycin, 15 placebo) with 1,695 children (azithromycin, mean age 30.8 months) and 3,031 (placebo, mean age 30.6 months) aged 1-59 months receiving twice-yearly azithromycin 20 mg/kg or placebo. At 4-year follow-up, parasitemia not significantly lower in azithromycin arm (-3.3 percentage points; 95% CI -5.8 to -0.2; p=0.05). Before seasonal malaria chemoprevention implementation, azithromycin showed significantly lower parasitemia (4.8 PP; 95% CI -7.4 to -1.3; p=0.02).

articles.headline: Temperature fluctuations alter malaria vector immunity and development
articles.title_original: Fluctuating Warm and Humid Conditions Differentially Impact Immunity and Development in the Malaria Vector Anopheles stephensi.
articles.authors: Lemos-Silva T, De Neef E, Valgaerts Y, Simões ML
articles.details: Anopheles stephensi exposed from egg to adult to diurnal temperature/humidity fluctuations (+4°C and +10% relative humidity) showed primed basal immune responses and accelerated pre-adult development without reduced adult lifespan. Bacterial infection under elevated fluctuating conditions led to complex immune reprogramming and enhanced larval/adult survival. Elevated conditions altered transcriptional activity of promoters in transgenic mosquitoes. Results suggest immune priming could initially decrease vectorial capacity, but post-bacterial immune suppression could enhance Plasmodium susceptibility.

articles.headline: Community education and behavior change reduce malaria transmission
articles.title_original: Behavioral health interventions in malaria control: Efficacy and implementation.
articles.authors: Obeagu EI, Obeagu GU, Iduh MU
articles.details: Review of behavioral health interventions in malaria control including community education, behavior change communication, and community-based strategies. Community education demonstrated success in enhancing awareness and promoting insecticide-treated net use and early treatment-seeking behavior. Community-based interventions proved effective in fostering ownership and sustainability, leading to notable reductions in malaria prevalence. Implementation challenges include cultural barriers, resource limitations, and inadequate monitoring/evaluation frameworks.

articles.headline: Plastic pollution alters malaria vector gut microbiota composition
articles.title_original: The Effect of Larval Exposure to Plastic Pollution on the Gut Microbiota of the Major Malaria Vector Anopheles arabiensis Patton (Diptera: Culicidae).
articles.authors: Misser S, Chen CY, Ismail A, Oliver SV
articles.details: Study examined larval exposure to degraded plastic, phthalic acid, Bisphenol-A, and latex beads on adult Anopheles arabiensis (SENN and SENN-DDT strains) gut microbiota. Plastic exposure minimally affected alpha-diversity but altered beta-diversity in non-strain-specific manner. Plastic-treated SENN showed increased unique bacterial genera; plastic exposure increased bacteria associated with plastic degradation and pesticide metabolism. Inflammation-associated bacterial genera increased in both strains after treatment, suggesting potential immune modulation.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 8% among Tanzanian children aged 6-59 months
articles.title_original: Prevalence and determinants of malaria among children aged 6-59 months in Tanzania: a nationwide cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Adam J, Luoga P, Nyamhanga T, Makunenge C, Ayubu M
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey/Malaria Indicator Survey on 4,923 children aged 6-59 months. Overall malaria prevalence 8% (95% CI 6.2-9.5%), highest in Tabora (23%). Risk factors: age 24-59 months (aOR 1.71, 95% CI 1.33-2.21), rural residence (aOR 6.92, 95% CI 2.19-8.83), lower economic status (aOR 3.08, 95% CI 1.22-7.83), and no insecticide-treated net use (aOR 2.73, 95% CI 1.51-4.91). Results highlight need for targeted prevention, especially ITN access in rural areas.

articles.headline: Spatio-temporal modeling reveals malaria risk drivers in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Bayesian spatio-temporal modeling and prediction of malaria cases in Tanzania mainland (2016-2023): unveiling associations with climate and intervention factors.
articles.authors: Njotto LL, Senyoni W, Cronie O, Stensgaard AS
articles.details: Bayesian spatio-temporal modeling of monthly malaria counts 2016-2023 across Tanzania mainland regions using integrated nested Laplace approximations. Analysis recorded 23.4 million cases (age ≥5 years) and 17.3 million cases (children <5 years). Malaria risk decreased 11.0% (<5 years) and 10.0% (≥5 years) from 2016-2023. Long-lasting insecticide nets reduced risk 1.2% (<5) and 7.0% (≥5). Minimum temperature, wind speed, and vegetation index associated with increased risk; relative humidity and temperature had complex lagged associations.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria linked to impaired growth in children
articles.title_original: Impact of asymptomatic malaria infection on children's growth in rural Malawi.
articles.authors: Sady H, Chaima D, Hallamaa L, Ashorn U, Banda J, Mangani C, Kamwendo J, Maleta K, Ashorn P, Fan YM
articles.details: Prospective study of 840 participants (697 at baseline) in rural Malawi examining asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum (real-time PCR) and growth indices in 6-18-month-olds over 12 months. Malaria-positive children had significantly lower mean weight-for-age Z-score (-1.03 vs -0.87, 95% CI -0.17 to -0.04) and weight-for-length Z-score (-0.03 vs -0.13, 95% CI -0.22 to -0.06) across all ages; length-for-age showed no significant association. After adjustment for confounders, malaria infection remained associated with impaired growth outcomes.

articles.headline: CYP2B6 genotype affects artemether-lumefantrine parasite clearance
articles.title_original: CYP2B6*6 single nucleotide polymorphism among patients with uncomplicated malaria in Adjumani district, Uganda: Implications on efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine.
articles.authors: Angwe MK, Mwebaza N, Nsobya SL, Kiguba R, Vudriko P, Dralabu S, Omali D, Tumwebaze MA, Ocan M
articles.details: Study of 100 patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria treated with artemether-lumefantrine in Uganda examining CYP2B6*6 polymorphism. CYP2B6*6 variant allele frequency 0.37; genotype frequencies: 43% GG, 40% GT, 17% TT. Day 3 microscopy positivity: 14% (GG), 37.5% (GT), 17.6% (TT). Heterozygous (GT) individuals showed 10-fold slower parasite clearance than homozygous (GG/TT) individuals (U=323.0, p=0.019). Results suggest incorporating host pharmacogenetic factors into therapeutic efficacy studies.

articles.headline: JAK inhibitor ruxolitinib prevents malaria-induced hypoglycemia
articles.title_original: JAK/STAT inhibition protects glucocorticoid receptor knockout mice from lethal malaria-induced hypoglycemia and hyperinflammation.
articles.authors: Prenen F, Vandermosten L, Knoops S, Pollenus E, Possemiers H, Dagneau de Richecour P, Caratti G, Cawthorne C, Vettorazzi S, Cranshoff Y, Schols D, Claes S, Deroose CM, Himmelreich U, Tuckermann J, Van den Steen PE
articles.details: Glucocorticoid receptor knockout mice infected with Plasmodium chabaudi AS developed lethal hypoglycemia and cytokine storm driven by hepatic/splenic metabolic dysfunction (increased glucose uptake, glycogen depletion, reduced gluconeogenic genes) and JAK/STAT pathway overactivation. Treatment with JAK1/2 inhibitor ruxolitinib significantly improved survival by preventing hypoglycemia and suppressing hyperinflammation. Findings reveal novel link between glucocorticoid receptor signaling, STAT3 activation, and glucose metabolism during severe malaria.

articles.headline: Apicoplast transporters as novel drug targets in malaria
articles.title_original: The apicoplast transportome of the malaria parasite.
articles.authors: Akkerman LJ, Kooij TWA, de Vries LE
articles.details: Review of the Plasmodium apicoplast transportome, a four-membrane-bound organelle critical for isopentenyl pyrophosphate and coenzyme A biosynthesis. Identifies 11 confirmed and 17 putative apicoplast transporters as potential novel drug targets. Discusses key transporters including IPP and CoA exporters essential for parasite metabolic pathways. Development of new tools anticipated to enable discovery and exploitation of apicoplast transportome proteins for malaria therapeutics.

articles.headline: Fighting malaria as a patient and scientist
articles.title_original: Fighting malaria as a patient and scientist.
articles.authors: Tadesse FG
articles.details: Personal narrative discussing experience with malaria from dual perspectives of patient and scientist.

articles.headline: Statistical tools for analyzing mosquito parasite infection data
articles.title_original: When a mean can be meaningless: evaluating mosquito infections with Plasmodium parasites.
articles.authors: Ubiaru PC, Ranford-Cartwright LC
articles.details: This methodological study recommends using generalized linear models and mixed models to analyze mosquito parasite infection data, which often have non-normal distributions and many zero counts. The authors suggest specific methods to handle datasets where some groups have zero or near-zero prevalence, or many zero parasite counts, as seen with effective transmission-blocking interventions. These statistical approaches are broadly applicable across many parasitic infections with similar parasite distribution patterns.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance in P. yoelii: reduced drug exposure biomarker
articles.title_original: Pharmacokinetics and metabolism of artemisinin (ART) in Plasmodium yoelii: ART-heme adduct as a potential biomarker for its resistance.
articles.authors: Du S, Xu K, Liu Z, Xing J
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: This pharmacokinetic study evaluated artemisinin (ART) metabolism in wild-type (Py) and artemisinin-resistant (iPy) strains of P. yoelii in mice. Resistant parasites showed 2.9-fold higher ED90 (lower antimalarial activity) and mice infected with iPy survived ≥28 days. ART-heme adduct was the major metabolite in parasites; resistant parasites showed 61.1% lower iRBC-normalized ART-heme exposure with Cmax at 3-5 hours. Authors conclude ART-heme adduct could be a biomarker for both antimalarial activity and resistance.

articles.headline: Hematology analyzer enables early malaria detection in Korea
articles.title_original: [Pilot Project for Early Diagnosis of Malaria Infection Using a Hematology Analyzer].
articles.authors: Shin HE, Shin HI, Lee MR, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: This pilot project tested automated hematological analysis for early malaria detection in 47 medical institutions across South Korea's malaria-risk areas from April to October 2024. Among 7.27 million hematologic analyses, 1,359 cases of infected red blood cells (iRBC) were detected; 499 were referred for confirmatory testing and 239 were confirmed as malaria-positive. Authors conclude this approach could enhance early detection of malaria infections and support Korea's malaria elimination goals by 2030.

articles.headline: Plasmodium surveillance in Brazilian primates reveals zoonotic risk
articles.title_original: Molecular Surveillance of Plasmodium spp. Infection in Neotropical Primates from Bahia and Minas Gerais, Brazil.
articles.authors: Santos LKNSS, Aquino-Teixeira SM, Bernal-Valle S, Daltro BS, Noetzold M, Silva ARC, Alvarenga DAM, Silva LB, Oliveira RS, Oliveira CH, Celestino IA, Gonçalves-Dos-Santos ME, Teixeira TJ, Sevá AP, Campos FS, Ribeiro BM, Roehe PM, Simonini-Teixeira D, Abreu FVS, Brito CFA, Albuquerque GR
articles.details: This molecular surveillance study tested 298 blood and tissue samples from 10 primate species across Bahia and Minas Gerais, Brazil, using nested PCR targeting 18S ribosomal RNA. Only 1 sample (0.3%) from Bahia tested positive for P. brasilianum/P. malariae, representing the first detection in a free-living C. geoffroyi. Authors conclude continued surveillance is important for identifying regions at risk for zoonotic transmission.

articles.headline: Glucose-functionalized DHA nanoparticles improve antimalarial drug targeting
articles.title_original: Glucose-functionalized redox-responsive dihydroartemisinin prodrug nanosystem for targeted malaria therapy.
articles.authors: Wang R, Yang J, Qiang J, Li Q, Wang G, Ping C, Liu K, Wang R, Zheng B, Ren G, Zhang S
articles.details: This study developed a glucose-functionalized, redox-responsive dihydroartemisinin (DHA) prodrug nanosystem (D@GLU-PMs-SS) designed for improved malaria therapy. The nanosystem exhibited excellent stability, was selectively activated by intracellular glutathione (GSH) to release free DHA, and showed enhanced uptake by erythrocytes via glucose transporter-mediated transport. In vitro experiments demonstrated effective inhibition of Plasmodium growth with reduced parasite GSH concentrations and maintained biosafety. Authors warrant further investigation in clinical trials.

articles.headline: Microfluidic device detects malaria-induced RBC deformability changes
articles.title_original: Measuring Asymmetric Ionic Current Waveform Through Micropores for Detecting Reduced Red Blood Cell Deformability Due to Plasmodium falciparum Infection.
articles.authors: Yokota K, Hirano K, Kajimoto K, Hashimoto M
articles.details: This study developed a microfluidic sensing device measuring ionic current waveforms through micropores to evaluate RBC deformability changes in P. falciparum infection at single-cell level. Using in vitro culture systems, researchers found that RBC deformability reduction associated with malaria infection caused ionic current waveforms to become asymmetric. Computer simulations suggested these parameters are largely independent of RBC size and may reliably indicate diminished deformability.

articles.headline: PfSDP gametocyte antigen elicits antibodies inhibiting parasite invasion
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Subtilisin-like Domain-Containing Protein (PfSDP), a Cross-Stage Antigen, Elicits Short-Lived Antibody Response Following Natural Infection with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Kengne-Ouafo JA, Morang'a CM, Nyakoe NK, Dosoo D, Tackie R, Mutungi JK, Bah SY, Amenga-Etego LN, Urban B, Awandare GA, Dinko B, Aniweh Y
articles.details: This study identified P. falciparum subtilisin-like domain-containing protein (PfSDP), expressed across all parasite developmental stages with genetic conservation across continents. Anti-PfSDP antibodies inhibited erythrocyte invasion by 20-60% in a dose-dependent manner in vitro. In children from endemic areas, PfSDP was immunogenic with antibody levels increasing from acute infection to day 7 post-treatment, then gradually declining. Authors conclude PfSDP may play a role in parasite development with potential vaccine implications.

articles.headline: Outcrossing advantage in P. falciparum revealed by genetic crosses
articles.title_original: Advantages of outcrossing in Plasmodium falciparum: insights from genetic crosses using fluorescent labelled parasites.
articles.authors: Li X, Jutzeler KS, Abatiyow BA, Rezakhani N, Haile MT, Leeb AS, Patel H, Kappe SHI, Nosten F, Cheeseman IH, Ferdig MT, Anderson TJC, Vaughan AM, Kumar S
articles.details: This study used fluorescent-labeled P. falciparum clones (NF54 mCherry and NHP4026 GFP) crossed in mosquitoes to assess relative fitness of selfed versus outcrossed parasites. Analysis of 8,540 oocysts from 435 mosquitoes showed decreasing proportions of outcrossed oocysts and increasing inbreeding over infection course, with outcrossed oocysts maturing faster than selfed. Authors conclude faster maturation of outcrossed parasites conveys substantial fitness advantage through removal of deleterious mutations, maximizing recombination in transmitted populations.

articles.headline: Lassa-malaria coinfection prospective cohort study in West Africa
articles.title_original: Prospective cohort study to evaluate Lassa fever incidence, symptoms and coinfection with malaria in West Africa: the Enable Lassa Research Programme ('ENABLE 1.5') - study protocol.
articles.authors: Mandi H, Asogun D, Ayodeji O, Azuogu BN, Camacho A, Fischer W, Fulton R, Grant DS, Günther S, Ithete N, Illori E, Jan K, Kagia C, Martens N, Menon S, Ndiaye A, Ndifon M, Nsaibirni R, Ntiri M, Oloo P, Okogbenin S, Penfold S, Sankawulo-Ricks M, Sillah M, Suykerbuyk P, Tornieporth N, Vielle NJ, Williams M, Wohl D, Xie F, Yimer S, Breugelmans JG
articles.details: This is a study protocol for ENABLE 1.5, a prospective cohort study conducted across 5 West African sites (Liberia, Nigeria, Sierra Leone) with 5,000 participants stratified by age (0-5 to >50 years) followed for 12 months. The study assesses symptomatic Lassa fever incidence (RT-PCR confirmed) and malaria coinfection, with symptom assessments every 2 weeks, blood draws every 3 months for serological testing (IgG), and malaria diagnostic testing. Authors aim to address gaps in understanding Lassa fever disease burden to inform future vaccine trials.

articles.headline: Abbott RDT shows high sensitivity but low specificity in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Malaria infection prevalence and diagnostic performance of the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/Pan rapid test in rural populations of Central Cameroon.
articles.authors: Missoup VO, Suh PF, Ndjebakal CN, Tagne D, Poumachu Y, Joko S, Kouamendjouo Djilla A, Njiokou F, Mbacham W, Wondji C, Ndo C
articles.details: This cross-sectional diagnostic study assessed the Abbott Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/Pan RDT in rural Central Cameroon across two time periods (March 2022 and May 2024). RDT showed high sensitivity (93.37%) but lower specificity (68.50%) for P. falciparum detection against microscopy reference, with positive predictive value of 84.43%, negative predictive value of 85.71%, and moderate agreement (κ=0.652). RDT showed higher sensitivity and accuracy than microscopy when compared against qPCR as reference standard.

articles.headline: Kelch13 mutations and multidrug resistance widespread in Zambia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Genomic Surveillance Reveals a Diversity of Kelch 13 Mutations in Zambia.
articles.authors: Aranda-Díaz A, Mwanza S, Makhanthisa TI, Lauterbach SB, De Amaral F, Chisenga M, Mangena B, Routledge I, Letinić B, Kasonde B, Chongwe G, Mwenda MC, Miller JM, Hibwato T, Jacob C, Hamainza B, Bwalya S, Chiwaula J, Matoba J, Sikaala C, Chimumbwa J, Wesolowski A, Smith JL, Raman J, Hawela M
articles.details: This genomic surveillance study genotyped 2,486 P. falciparum samples from 61 health facilities across all 10 Zambian provinces (March-July 2023) using multiplexed amplicon sequencing for drug-resistance mutations. Validated artemisinin-resistance marker kelch13 P574L found in 0.66% (adjusted national prevalence), candidate K13 P441L in 1.39%, with heterogeneous distribution reaching up to 46% at some facilities. Multidrug resistance protein 1 N86 found in all samples; sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance marker K540E at 93.26%. Authors conclude routine surveillance is needed to guide malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Deep learning emulates agent-based malaria transmission model
articles.title_original: Multitask deep learning for the emulation and calibration of an agent-based malaria transmission model.
articles.authors: Mondal A, Anirudh R, Selvaraj P
articles.details: This study developed a multitask deep learning neural network to emulate a complex agent-based model of malaria transmission (EMOD). The network was trained on simulations capturing relationships between immunological parameters and epidemiological outcomes (age-stratified incidence and prevalence) across 8 sub-Saharan African sites. The trained emulator was used with parameter estimation techniques to calibrate the underlying model. Authors demonstrate machine learning's potential for emulating complex scientific processes for comparison with field data.

articles.headline: Egypt's 15-year elimination program achieves 96% malaria incidence reduction
articles.title_original: Comparative analysis of Egypt's malaria elimination strategies and implementation science: Pathways to achieve malaria-free status for other African countries.
articles.authors: Elendu C, Amaechi DC, Elendu RC, Saggi SK, Elendu TC, Amaechi EC, Elendu ID, Dang K, Amosu OP, Idowu OF, Oseni TS
articles.details: Retrospective implementation science evaluation of Egypt's WHO-certified malaria elimination program. Multi-level interventions included ITNs, IRS, antimalarial treatment, and health education targeting children <5 and pregnant women. Over 15 years: 92% recruitment rate, 96% incidence reduction, 94% Anopheles density reduction, 98% case detection within 48 hours, 91% treatment coverage, $24/DALY averted. High implementation fidelity (93%), feasibility, and acceptability (87%) with successful adaptability to insecticide resistance.

articles.headline: Nucleoside-modified mRNA-LNP vaccine protects against P. vivax sporozoites
articles.title_original: mRNA-LNP vaccine encoding the Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein is highly immunogenic and confers protection in mice.
articles.authors: Limsalakpetch A, Kum-Arb U, Yongvanitchit K, Im-Erbsin R, Ubalee R, Waters N, Vesely BA, Muramatsu H, Weissman D, Tam YK, Yoshida S, Adams J, Yadava A, Pardi N, Pichyangkul S
articles.details: Preclinical murine study of mRNA-LNP vaccines encoding P. vivax circumsporozoite protein (CSP). Intradermal delivery superior to intramuscular at lower doses; intravenous ineffective. Delayed third intramuscular dose (5 months post-dose 2) generated highest anti-repeat region antibodies and enhanced T-cell responses with strong sporozoite challenge protection. MS2 and Qβ bacteriophage VLP combination vaccine elicited sterilizing immunity in the highest percentage of mice.

articles.headline: CD44 cross-linking facilitates P. falciparum invasion via Basigin accessibility
articles.title_original: CD44 cross-linking promotes Plasmodium falciparum invasion.
articles.authors: Kongsomboonvech AK, Scally SW, Le Guen Y, Valissery P, Salinas ND, Cowman AF, Tolia NH, Egan ES
articles.details: Cell biology study of P. falciparum invasion using genetically-modified human RBCs. Anti-CD44 monoclonal antibody BRIC 222 significantly promoted invasion through CD44 cross-linking, altering RBC cytoskeletal protein phosphorylation and increasing surface Basigin accessibility. EBA-175 parasite ligand induced similar invasion enhancement and RBC membrane changes by coordinating downstream PfRH5/PCRCR-Basigin interactions essential for invasion.

articles.headline: Busia County, Kenya: chloroquine resistance prevalent; artemisinin resistance absent
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum parasites harbouring chloroquine-resistant but not artemisinin-resistant alleles in Busia County, Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Onchieku NM, Gesusu N, Caspar E, Karani L, Thiong'o K, Kamau L, Kiboi D, Thiebaut L, Ma L, Kimani F, Matoke-Muhia D, Ménard D
articles.details: Molecular epidemiology survey of 118 blood samples from Busia County, Western Kenya using deep amplicon sequencing. Pfkelch13 mutations (A578S, non-resistant) in 5.1% of samples; 11% had Pfcrt mutant alleles with 62% CVIET triple mutant; 98% carried dhfr mutations; 100% had dhps mutations (84% A437G); no Pfhrp2 deletions or Pfmdr1 resistance-associated mutations at codons 86 or 1246 detected.

articles.headline: L9 epitope-targeted VLP vaccines elicit sterilizing immunity in mice
articles.title_original: Virus-like particle vaccines targeting a key epitope in circumsporozoite protein provide sterilizing immunity against malaria.
articles.authors: Nepal Y, Francian A, Flores-Garcia Y, Roberts BT, David SA, Zavala F, Chackerian B
articles.details: Preclinical vaccine study comparing Qβ and MS2 bacteriophage VLP platforms displaying L9 epitope of P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein. MS2 L9 VLPs with longer peptides and novel adjuvant generated strong durable antibodies reducing liver burden and preventing infection. MS2 L9 and Qβ L9 combination vaccine provided sterilizing immunity in highest percentage of mice, comparable to licensed RTS,S/AS01E vaccine.

articles.headline: In2Care EaveTubes reduce indoor anopheline vectors 58% overall, 75% peak season
articles.title_original: The In2Care® EaveTubes improved protection against malaria vectors in a small-scale field study at Kagera Sugar Ltd, Missenyi, Tanzania.
articles.authors: Kweka EJ, Farenhorst M, Dijk TV, Zinga M, Osinga A, Mazigo H
articles.details: Small-scale field trial at Kagera Sugar Ltd, Tanzania (October 2019–July 2020) of 350 EaveTubes in 135 structures compared to 2 untreated control camps. Entomological sampling showed 58% overall indoor anopheline density reduction and 75% reduction during peak transmission season. Insecticide durability maintained after 6 months field use. Malaria case incidence decreased 25.6% in EaveTube-treated camp versus baseline.

articles.headline: Hair analysis detects lumefantrine exposure weeks after artemether-lumefantrine treatment
articles.title_original: Can hair concentrations of artemether-lumefantrine be used as a tool to retrospectively determine drug exposure during malaria treatment?
articles.authors: Schnyder JL, Vos-van der Meer M, van Hest RM, Mathot R, Schlagenhauf P, de Jong HK, Grobusch MP
articles.details: Proof-of-concept study of 6 P. falciparum malaria patients (Amsterdam UMC), hair collected 4 weeks post-treatment analyzed by LC-MS/MS. Lumefantrine and metabolite desbutyl-lumefantrine detected in all samples with quantifiable levels in 5 cases; artemether and dihydroartemisinin undetectable. No recrudescence occurred in any participant. Hair analysis may retrospectively assess lumefantrine exposure in recrudescence cases to distinguish inadequate drug exposure from reduced parasite susceptibility.

articles.headline: Pakistan malaria vector control emphasizes ITNs and IRS with provincial variation
articles.title_original: Malaria vector control strategies in Pakistan: a scoping review.
articles.authors: Irshad HA, Jehanzeb H, Yaseen A, Saleem U, Javaid MD, Tareen HK, Mukhtar M, Silviera H, Beg MA
articles.details: Scoping review of 46 published articles (through September 2023) on malaria vector control in Pakistan. WHO-recommended strategies reported: ITNs 29.79%, IRS 29.79%, spatial spraying 12.77%, spatial/airborne repellents 4.26%, larval source management 4.26%, house modifications 4.26%. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa predominantly uses ITNs (55%) while Punjab primarily employs IRS (50%) as main vector control method.

articles.headline: Test-negative design vaccine studies require power adjustment for diagnostic misclassification
articles.title_original: Power and sample size considerations for test-negative design with bias correction: a case study on the world first malaria vaccine.
articles.authors: Ko YK, Alfvén T, Yoneoka D
articles.details: Monte Carlo simulation examining statistical power and sample size for vaccine effectiveness (VE) in test-negative design studies with bias correction for imperfect diagnostic test sensitivity. Using malaria vaccine example with <50% VE and 30% positivity rate. At 80% test sensitivity, only 3 of 6 vaccination-status groups achieved 80% power with 10,000 samples versus 6,000 with perfect test. Bias correction widened confidence intervals.

articles.headline: PfGrp170 displays independent chaperone function with ATP-sensitive activity
articles.title_original: Biochemical and biophysical characterization of Plasmodium falciparum glucose regulated protein 170.
articles.authors: Muzenda FL, Stofberg ML, Mthembu W, Strauss E, Zininga T
articles.details: Biochemical characterization of recombinant P. falciparum glucose-regulated protein 170 (PfGrp170, ER-localized Hsp110). Predominantly α-helical structure with thermal stability (Tm >55°C); ATP binding perturbs stability increasing heat-stress sensitivity. Demonstrates basal ATPase activity and suppresses heat-induced substrate aggregation of malate dehydrogenase in nucleotide-independent manner with selective ATP/ADP binding preference.

articles.headline: MalNet-DAF dual-attention deep learning achieves 99.24% malaria classification accuracy
articles.title_original: MalNet-DAF: Dual-Attentive Fusion Deep Learning Model for Malaria Parasite Classification.
articles.authors: Patro KK, Prakash AJ, Madarapu S, Ari S, Routray S, Mukherjee A, Mishra S
articles.details: Novel deep-learning architecture integrating stacked CNNs with Bi-LSTM networks for malaria parasite classification. Spatial Attention Module highlights disease-affected regions; Temporal Attention Module emphasizes informative time steps. Trained and validated on NIH public malaria dataset. Achieved 99.24% classification accuracy, substantially outperforming traditional baseline models in real-time clinical decision support.

articles.headline: Bangladesh exceeds WHO LLIN threshold; forcibly-displaced populations significantly underserved
articles.title_original: Assessing long-lasting insecticidal net coverage, access, and use: Insights from malaria-endemic regions and Rohingya camps in Bangladesh.
articles.authors: Hossain MS, Neogi AK, Phru CS, Ferdous NN, Hasan A, Islam S, Rahman MM, Rahaman MM, Islam MN, Das SK, Bhuiyan ATMRH, Islam MN, Islam MA, Alam MS
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey (May–October 2023) of 1,575 households in 5 endemic districts and 10 FDMN camps, Cox's Bazar. Bangladeshi households: 97.6% owned ≥1 LLIN, 93.2% sufficient coverage, 96.4% slept under LLIN; 95% of pregnant women and 98.3% of children <5 years used LLINs. FDMN households: 98.2% ownership but only 44.3% sufficient coverage, 65.7% use rate. Primary barrier: inadequate LLIN supply.

articles.headline: P. falciparum malaria progresses to multiorgan failure and death
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria Due to Plasmodium falciparum in an Immunocompetent Young Adult: Rapid Progression to Multiorgan Failure.
articles.authors: Sanclemente-Cardoza V, Payán-Salcedo HA, Estela-Zape JL
articles.details: Case report of a 21-year-old presenting with P. falciparum infection and severe respiratory distress (oxygen saturation <75%). The patient rapidly progressed to multiorgan failure including acute respiratory distress syndrome, refractory shock, acute kidney injury with severe hyperkalemia, and consumptive thrombocytopenia. Despite intensive management with mechanical ventilation, vasopressors, and platelet transfusion, the patient died, illustrating rapid disease progression.

articles.headline: NR2F6 deficiency improves survival in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: NR2F6 as a Disease Driver and Candidate Therapeutic Target in Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Stefan VE, Klepsch V, Thuille N, Steinlechner M, Peer S, Siegmund K, Lackner P, Schmutzhard E, Albrecht-Schgör K, Baier G
articles.details: In a mouse model of experimental cerebral malaria (Plasmodium berghei ANKA), Nr2f6-knockout mice showed improved survival compared to wild-type mice. The survival benefit was accompanied by reduced endotheliopathy and blood-brain barrier damage, with decreased parasite sequestration and fewer cytotoxic T lymphocytes in the brain.

articles.headline: Three Anopheles species identified as vectors of deer malaria
articles.title_original: Incriminating vectors of deer malaria (Plasmodium odocoilei) at a Florida deer farm.
articles.authors: Rockwell M, Wisely SM, Mathias DK, Burkett-Cadena ND
articles.details: Entomological investigation at a Florida deer farm identified Anopheles quadrimaculatus s.l., An. punctipennis, and An. crucians s.l. as natural vectors of Plasmodium odocoilei in white-tailed deer. An. quadrimaculatus had the highest infection rate (4.1%), followed by An. punctipennis (3.1%), with all three species showing high host-feeding preference for deer (>75% of blood meals).

articles.headline: Artisanal mining is strongest risk factor for urban malaria
articles.title_original: Emergence of urban malaria and the associated risk factors: a case-control study in Mutare city, Zimbabwe.
articles.authors: Mharakurwa S, Mbwana N, Mharakurwa T, Karumbidza T, Mathe V, Fusire P, Tangwena A, Chauke W, Mukuzunga M, Dhliwayo P, Munyati SM, Gwanzura L, Bailey JA, Moss WJ
articles.details: Case-control study in Mutare, Zimbabwe identified artisanal mining as the strongest risk factor for malaria (relative risk 22.0, 95%CI: 10.7-44.1, p<0.001), followed by travel history (RR 9.0, 95%CI: 5.2-14.4) and household proximity to water sources (RR 16.0, 95%CI: 3.8-67.5). Male residents had 4-fold higher odds of malaria than females, with 93% of miners being male.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine achieves 75% third-dose coverage and reduces hospitalizations
articles.title_original: Subnational introduction of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine into routine immunization: experience and lessons from the three pilot countries.
articles.authors: Jalang'o R, Amponsa-Achiano K, Chisema M, Malm K, Khalayi L, Mhone B, Mohammed W, Asiedu-Bekoe F, Haji A, Njoroge J, Zimba B, Chirwa E, Tweneboah PO, Sillah J, Magafu MGMD, Bergstrom C, Goodman T, Walldorf J, Kelleher K, Pellaux-Furrer E, Hamel MJ, Adjei MR, Okine RNA
articles.details: Implementation experience from pilot introduction of RTS,S/AS01E vaccine in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi. By 2023, vaccination coverage reached approximately 80% for first dose and 75% for third dose, though fourth-dose coverage remained lower at median 46%. Health stakeholders reported positive impact including reduction in malaria hospitalizations and strengthening of national immunization programs.

articles.headline: Larvicide reduces vector density 82-88% with heterogeneous malaria impact
articles.title_original: Heterogeneous impacts for malaria control from larviciding across villages and considerations for monitoring and evaluation.
articles.authors: Sherrard-Smith E, Fillinger U, Tia JB, Winskill P, Koudou BG, Tchicaya ESF, Sanou A, Okumu F, Opiyo M, Majambere S, Hamlet A, Charles G, Lambert B, Churcher TS
articles.details: Transmission modeling study in Western Kenya and Côte d'Ivoire evaluated larval source management (LSM) with larvicide alongside ITNs. Entomological surveys estimated 82-88% reductions in adult Anopheles densities. Transmission models predicted malaria prevalence reduction of 48-72% in children (Kenya) and 11-78% reduction in clinical incidence across villages (Côte d'Ivoire), with substantial village-level heterogeneity.

articles.headline: Plasmepsin gene copy number drives piperaquine resistance in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: The plasmepsin-piperaquine paradox persists in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Walsh B, Summers RL, Rangel GW, Hagenah LM, Mok S, Llinás M, Fidock DA, Wirth DF, Bopp S
articles.details: CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing study in Cambodian P. falciparum generated plasmepsin II and III knockouts. Deletion of either gene increased parasite sensitivity to dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-PPQ), confirming that increased plasmepsin gene copy numbers confer resistance. The resistance mechanism involves altered digestive vacuole pH rather than increased hemoglobin digestion or protease inhibition.

articles.headline: P. berghei ANKA co-infection reduces cutaneous leishmaniasis in mice
articles.title_original: Impact of co-infection with Plasmodium berghei ANKA in Leishmania major-parasitized mice on immune modulation and cutaneous leishmaniasis.
articles.authors: Ornellas-Garcia U, Freire-Antunes L, Rangel-Ferreira M, de Sousa CHG, Ribeiro-Almeida ML, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Cuervo P, Ribeiro-Gomes FL
articles.details: Co-infection study in C57BL/6 mice infected with both Leishmania major and Plasmodium berghei ANKA (experimental cerebral malaria model). P. berghei co-infection reduced L. major lesion size and dermal parasite burden compared to L. major infection alone. Co-infected mice had elevated serum TNF and reduced recruitment of activated T cells and inflammatory monocytes to dermis.

articles.headline: Infective mosquitoes show higher anthropophily, creating malaria transmission hotspots
articles.title_original: The spatial heterogeneity of malaria transmission: An entomological investigation in a highly endemic setting of Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Perugini E, Poggi C, Micocci M, Pichler V, Nelli L, Torre AD, Guelbeogo WM, Pombi M
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Entomological survey across 59 houses in Goden village, Burkina Faso characterized spatial heterogeneity in malaria transmission. Anopheles coluzzii dominated (79% of An. gambiae s.l., n=1,492) with overall low human blood index (HBI 45%) but high sporozoite rate (10%). Notably, infectious mosquitoes had 1.8-fold higher anthropophily than non-infectious ones, with hotspots in the northwest village area.
