articles.headline: Polycyclic derivatives SR4-02, SR4-04 improve memory in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Polycyclitol Derivatives Restore Long- Term Memory Via cdk5/p25 Activation of Tau Signaling in Experimental Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Simhadri PK, Rashid S, Karri S, Bhat BA, Mehta G, Babu PP
articles.details: In murine experimental cerebral malaria, polycyclic derivatives SR4-02 and SR4-04 used as adjuncts to artemether improved cognitive outcomes on Barnes maze (long-term memory), T-maze, and novelty recognition tasks. SR4-02 and SR4-04 reduced phospho-tau at Ser396 in hippocampus and cortex and enhanced neuronal arborization, suggesting potential therapies for cerebral malaria-associated cognitive impairment.

articles.headline: Physics-informed neural networks improve malaria transmission modeling
articles.title_original: Analysis of a mathematical model for malaria using data-driven approach.
articles.authors: Rajnarayanan A, Kumar M, Tridane A
articles.details: Novel malaria transmission model integrated temperature and altitude-dependent transmission functions into SIR-SI framework. Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) outperformed conventional ANNs and RNNs by embedding epidemiological dynamics into parameter estimation. Dynamic Mode Decomposition derived a data-driven transmission risk index from infection trajectories, providing interpretable metrics for real-time risk assessment.

articles.headline: Bivalent vaccine provides 70% protection against P. falciparum and vivax
articles.title_original: Malaria bivalent viral vectored vaccine protects against Plasmodium falciparum and vivax and blocks parasite transmission.
articles.authors: Yamamoto Y, Katayama T, Fabbri C, Niwa S, Okuhara D, Iyori M, Hasyim AA, Mizukami H, Shida H, Lopes S, Yoshida S
articles.details: Novel bivalent vaccine using vaccinia virus (m8Δ) prime and AAV1 boost with dual-target antigens (Pfs25-PfCSP and Pvs25-PvCSP) provided 70% protection against transgenic sporozoites of both Plasmodium falciparum and P. vivax. In membrane feeding assays using P. vivax-infected patient blood from Brazilian Amazon, the vaccine demonstrated 90% transmission-blocking efficacy and maintained immune responses for over 7 months.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria infections fuel 30% of transmission
articles.title_original: Impact of asymptomatic infections on malaria transmission dynamics.
articles.authors: Haringo AT, Obsu LL, Bushu FK
articles.details: Deterministic mathematical model analyzing malaria transmission with focus on asymptomatic infections. Basic reproduction number (R0) calculated via next-generation matrix method revealed backward bifurcation; asymptomatic cases contribute ~30% to R0 while symptomatic cases account for ~70%. Study emphasizes need for integrated control strategies targeting both symptomatic and asymptomatic cases.

articles.headline: Zoonotic Plasmodium detected in Thai Assamese macaques
articles.title_original: First molecular detection of zoonotic Plasmodium knowlesi, Plasmodium cynomolgi, and Plasmodium inui in Assamese macaques from northern Thailand.
articles.authors: Wilaisri P, Kaewchot S, Rucksaken R, Jarudecha T, Hmaidee T, Wichainchot S, Thabthimsri C, Sricharern W
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of 133 blood samples from Assamese macaques in Chiang Rai Province, Thailand using semi-nested PCR targeting 18S rRNA gene. Overall Plasmodium prevalence 24.06% (32/133; 95% CI: 17.07–32.23); P. knowlesi in 13.53% (18/133), P. cynomolgi in 9.77% (13/133), P. inui in 0.75% (1/133). First molecular evidence of these zoonotic species in Assamese macaques in Thailand; highlights role as natural reservoirs for zoonotic Plasmodium transmission to humans.

articles.headline: Tephrosia villosa root extract shows antimalarial activity in mice
articles.title_original: Antimalarial activity of root extract of Tephrosia villosa L. Pers. (Fabaceae) on Plasmodium berghei-infected mice.
articles.authors: Alemu MA, Moges TA, Ketsela Zeleke T, Abebe RB, Zewdu WS
articles.details: In vivo antimalarial evaluation of Tephrosia villosa dichloromethane/methanol (1:1) root extract against P. berghei infection in mice at 100, 200, 400 mg/kg doses. At 400 mg/kg, extract suppressed parasitemia by 24.78% (4-day suppressive), 52.16% (curative), 49.09% (prophylactic test; p<0.001); inhibited packed cell volume reduction and body weight loss. Extract safe at acute toxicity dose of 2 g/kg.

articles.headline: XGBoost and Bayesian models optimize malaria screening
articles.title_original: Comparative analysis of machine learning models for malaria detection using validated synthetic data: a cost-sensitive approach with clinical domain knowledge integration.
articles.authors: Sekhar GVC, Alemu C
articles.details: Systematic comparison of five machine learning algorithms (Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, Enhanced Bayesian Logistic Regression) for malaria detection using validated synthetic dataset representing Sub-Saharan African epidemiology (87% representativeness). XGBoost achieved highest AUC (0.956; 95% CI: 0.952–0.961) with 2.8% improvement over Random Forest; Enhanced Bayesian Logistic Regression achieved comparable AUC (0.954; 95% CI: 0.950–0.959) with interpretable clinical coefficients.

articles.headline: Transmission-blocking drug platform for P. falciparum gametocytes
articles.title_original: An all-in-one pipeline for the in vitro discovery and in vivo testing of Plasmodium falciparum malaria transmission blocking drugs.
articles.authors: Brancucci NMB, Gumpp C, van Gemert GJ, Yu X, Passecker A, Nardella F, Thommen BT, Chambon M, Turcatti G, Halby L, Blasco B, Duffey M, Arimondo PB, Bousema T, Scherf A, Leroy D, Kooij TWA, Rottmann M, Voss TS
articles.details: Integrated in vitro and in vivo discovery platform for transmission-blocking antimalarials targeting P. falciparum stage V gametocytes. Uses transgenic NF54/iGP1_RE9Hulg8 parasites with red-shifted firefly luciferase viability reporter and humanized NODscidIL2Rγnull mice infected with pure stage V gametocytes. Platform enables robust in vitro screening and assessment of in vivo gametocyte killing kinetics combined with mosquito feeding assays for efficacy evaluation.

articles.headline: Korean malaria vector surveillance shows increasing mosquito density
articles.title_original: [Malaria Vector Surveillance in 2024].
articles.authors: Han BG, Kim HW, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: Entomological surveillance of 76 sites in South Korea from April–October 2024, expanded to malaria-risk areas. Cumulative trap index (TI) reached 120.6 in 2024, 66.6% increase vs. 2023 and 59.3% increase vs. 2020–2022 average. Minimum infection rate (MIR) for P. vivax: 0.15 detected in three pools at weeks 31, 32, 34 (decreased from MIR 0.21 in 2023 despite higher mosquito density). Mosquito peaks preceded malaria cases by 5–6 weeks.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing expands malaria parasite drug-resistance surveillance
articles.title_original: An expanded method for malaria parasite genetic surveillance using targeted nanopore sequencing.
articles.authors: Harrott AJR, Morang'a CM, Pearson RD, Sakyi ML, Osumanu A, Amoako EK, Bara FD, Hosmillo M, Rowe K, Aniweh Y, Awandare GA, Zeukeng F, Goodfellow I, Ariani CV, Amenga-Etego LN, Hamilton WL
articles.details: Updated DRAG2 multiplex nanopore-based assay for P. falciparum molecular surveillance incorporating drug-resistance markers, merozoite surface protein 2 (msp2), and 18S rRNA for species detection. Assay validated across range of parasitaemias and sample types (venous blood, dried blood spots). Uses synthetic plasmid controls with engineered SNPs for quality control. Protocol includes standard operating procedures for transition from research tool to diagnostic assay.

articles.headline: Learning curves guide sample size for malaria ML models
articles.title_original: How to use learning curves to evaluate the sample size for malaria prediction models developed using machine learning algorithms.
articles.authors: Zaloumis SG, Rajasekhar M, Simpson JA
articles.details: Tutorial on using learning curves to determine training dataset size for malaria prediction models using machine learning. Mock study predicted artemisinin resistance from P. falciparum gene expression (1043 isolates; 29% slow-clearing). Balanced error rate on test set: 14% (sPLSDA+SVMs) and 22% (random forests) at n=835 training samples vs. 50% each at n=20; curves indicate diminishing returns beyond 835 samples.

articles.headline: Ivermectin shows promise against malaria
articles.title_original: Ivermectin against Malaria - Good News in Bad Times.
articles.authors: Steketee RW
articles.details: No abstract provided; title suggests ivermectin as potential antimalarial agent.

articles.headline: Mass ivermectin reduces malaria by 26% vs albendazole
articles.title_original: Ivermectin to Control Malaria - A Cluster-Randomized Trial.
articles.authors: Chaccour C, Maia M, Kariuki M, Ruiz-Castillo P, Wanjiku C, Kasiwa L, Brazeal A, Casellas A, Ngama M, Onyango T, Elobolobo E, Kazungu K, Mael M, Wangari W, Nuru K, Otuko R, Sanz A, Ringera I, Matano A, Mitora S, Ribes M, Brew J, Gorski N, Nicolas P, Stanulovic S, Omondi I, Furnival-Adams J, Túnez L, Mbarak J, Vegove V, Yaa E, Mramba S, Kibet Y, Nyambura N, Rotich C, Wanjiru S, Vura M, Wanjiku F, Sam L, Collins L, Xia K, Hammann F, Saúte F, Rudd M, Rist C, Jones C, Mwangangi J, Rabinovich NR
articles.details: Cluster-randomized trial in Kwale County, Kenya (n=28,932 eligible participants, 84 clusters) comparing monthly mass administration of ivermectin (400 μg/kg) vs. albendazole (400 mg) for 3 months. Children 5–15 years monitored for 6 months post-treatment. Malaria incidence: 2.20 per child-year (ivermectin) vs. 2.66 per child-year (albendazole); adjusted IRR 0.74 (95% CI: 0.58–0.95, p=0.02), representing 26% lower incidence. Serious adverse events: IRR 0.63 (95% CI: 0.21–1.91), no significant safety difference.

articles.headline: Emergency cesarean delivery complicated by undiagnosed malaria
articles.title_original: Emergent Cesarean Section Due to Malaria: A Perioperative Challenge.
articles.authors: Bandarupalli T, Zbeidy R
articles.details: Case report of woman aged ~30 years at 39 weeks gestation with undiagnosed malaria (travel history to Haiti) presenting with severe fetal heart-rate decelerations requiring urgent cesarean section. Anesthetic management included rapid induction with etomidate and succinylcholine; intraoperative monitoring of hemodynamic instability and coagulation abnormalities; empirical antimalarial treatment initiated post-delivery before confirmatory diagnosis.

articles.headline: Poor treatment adherence drives artemisinin resistance in Rwanda
articles.title_original: Understanding Malaria Treatment Adherence in Rwanda: Implications for Artemisinin Resistance.
articles.authors: Gashema P, Jordan A, Saramba E, Wernsman Young N, Iradukunda PG, Karema C, Mazarati JB, Juliano JJ, Bailey JA, Banek K
articles.details: Cross-sectional KAP study (n=406) in six Rwandan health facilities, December 2023–February 2024, in moderate to high transmission areas (56% female, 50% primary schooled, 80% rural). Among 44.3% (180/406) receiving malaria treatment in prior 6 months, only 46% (83/180) completed 3-day course; 37% (66/180) stopped within 2 days; 27% (109/406) self-treated with antimalarials from pharmacies (54%; 49/109). Knowledge and attitudes high but adherence poor, exacerbating artemisinin resistance risk.

articles.headline: P. vivax emergence and genetic adaptation in Duffy-negative Cameroonians
articles.title_original: Epidemiological Insights and Duffy Binding Protein Evolution of Plasmodium vivax in Duffy-Negative Cameroonians.
articles.authors: Dieng CC, Ning RT, Kipayko C, Schroeder R, Mdluli-Berndt N, Ayukenchengamba B, Nematchoua Z, Doris S, Reward AE, Ule ISN, Kimbi HK, Lo E
articles.details: Study detected P. vivax at 11% prevalence in febrile and 5.5% in asymptomatic Cameroonians (all Duffy-negative). Genetic analysis identified two dominant PvDBP1 mutations: I379L (73%) and E225K (61%), suggesting adaptive evolution. Phylogenetic analysis grouped Cameroonian isolates with Botswana samples, distinct from Ethiopian and Sudanese strains. PvLDH-based RDTs showed false negatives while qPCR demonstrated superior sensitivity.

articles.headline: Microglia activation and nitric oxide signaling in experimental cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Microglia exhibit a dynamic response, modulating inducible nitric oxide synthase expression and the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines during experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Freire-Antunes L, Ornellas-Garcia U, Rangel-Ferreira M, Ribeiro-Almeida ML, Carvalho LJM, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Ribeiro-Gomes FL
articles.details: Using P. berghei ANKA infection in C57BL/6 mice (experimental cerebral malaria model), researchers documented increased numbers of Ly6C+ T cells, inflammatory monocytes, resident macrophages, and microglia. Ex vivo analysis showed elevated iNOS expression and IL-1β/TNF production in these cell populations. Findings demonstrate pronounced microglial reactivity and immune dynamics during cerebral malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Healthcare facility vulnerability and climate impact on malaria dengue outcomes
articles.title_original: Public health facility vulnerabilities, preparedness, and health outcomes for Plasmodium falciparum and dengue virus-infected children under 5 years with acute febrile illnesses in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Ogony J, Menya D, Mangeni J, Ayodo G, Karanja S
articles.details: Three-month prospective cohort of 378 children under 5 in Western Kenya. Most facilities (81%, 17/21) could diagnose and treat malaria, but dengue capacity was limited (18%, 4/21). Facility vulnerability to flooding was high: 60% of Bunyala and 45% of Kisumu facilities sited on floodplains. Recovery rates were higher in Bunyala (96.8%, 184/190) than Kisumu (91%, 171/188).

articles.headline: Gender underrepresentation in malaria research: systematic review of 57 studies
articles.title_original: Conceptualization of gender in published malaria and gender research: a systematic descriptive review.
articles.authors: Atobrah D, Kwansa BK, Okyere-Asante PG, Kyere A, Badasu DM, Kretchy IA
articles.details: Systematic review of 57 published articles (1995-2024) on malaria and gender found research was predominantly biomedical, reducing gender analysis to sex-disaggregated data. Most studies employed quantitative, laboratory-based approaches focused on sub-Saharan Africa. Authors conclude gender analysis remains limited and highlight need for social science research using qualitative and mixed-methods approaches beyond sex/gender disaggregation.

articles.headline: Self-medication for malaria associated with inappropriate dosing in Uganda
articles.title_original: Self-medication for malaria and associated factors in Kakumiro District, Uganda, August 2023: implications for malaria management and mortality prevention.
articles.authors: Zalwango MG, Migisha R, Agaba BB, Bulage L, Kwesiga B, Kadobera D, Ario AR
articles.details: Community cross-sectional survey of 592 households in Kakumiro District, Uganda found 62% (368/592) self-medicated for malaria. Self-medication was associated with household head age ≥35 years (APR 1.77), distance ≥5km to facility (APR 3.05), and home drug storage (APR 2.21). Although 85% used recommended drugs, 66% administered inappropriate doses; antimalarial stockouts at facilities were the primary driver. Despite underdosing, 85% recovered without hospitalization.

articles.headline: Low IPTp-SP uptake in pregnancy: individual community barriers in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Individual and community-level correlates of optimal doses of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine for intermittent preventive treatment of malaria during pregnancy in Tanzania: a multilevel analysis of the 2022 national survey.
articles.authors: Stephano EE, Yusheng T, John TW, Kibusi SM, Yamin L, Mtoro MJ
articles.details: Multilevel analysis of 4,497 women from 2022 Tanzania DHS found only 31.2% (95% CI 29.1-33.3%) achieved optimal IPTp-SP (≥3 doses). Individual correlates included women aged 25-34 (AOR 1.52), primary education (AOR 1.34), and >3 ANC visits (AOR 1.77). Community-level factors included high ANC visit areas (AOR 1.49) and Northern/Lake zones (AOR 1.61-1.68), while Zanzibar had minimal uptake (AOR 0.01).

articles.headline: Vaccine effectiveness perception drives malaria immunization community engagement uptake
articles.title_original: Community engagement in promoting malaria vaccine uptake for children Under-Five.
articles.authors: Adoma PO, Acquah F, Yakubu A, Cudjoe L, Achiam WKK, Agorinya IA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 1,183 caregivers (from 2,500 under-5 population) identified vaccine effectiveness perception, educational level, and age as key factors influencing community engagement for vaccine uptake. Vaccine safety and acceptability were moderate contributors. Higher education correlated with prioritizing vaccine effectiveness and safety, while lower education emphasized cost and cultural factors. Findings suggest demographic-tailored education could improve vaccine coverage.

articles.headline: Transfusion-transmitted malaria screening gaps in India systematic review
articles.title_original: Transfusion-transmitted Plasmodium spp. infections and safety challenges for malaria in the Indian subcontinent: a systematic review.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Sharma S, Sharma A
articles.details: Systematic review of 122 Indian studies (>6.5 million individuals) found parasitemia prevalence in blood donors ranged 0-0.87% by microscopy and 0-2.3% by RDT, with post-transfusion malaria incidence of 0.8-6.8%. Clinical impacts ranged from mild to fatal. Key concerns include P. malariae, P. vivax, and P. falciparum including drug-resistant and pfhrp2-deleted strains. Authors recommend point-of-care nucleic acid testing for safer transfusions.

articles.headline: Single molecular target overreliance limits malaria PCR diagnostic potential
articles.title_original: Overreliance on Plasmodium 18S rRNA gene for malaria molecular diagnosis-inferences from systematic review.
articles.authors: Deora N, Khan MR, Singh P, Zehra N, Sircar S, Pande V, Mallick PK, Sinha A
articles.details: Systematic review found 93% of malaria PCR studies relied solely on 18S rRNA gene, with 68% using nested PCR and 85% employing the 1993-developed approach. Authors caution that sole reliance on a single molecular target may be problematic, analogous to false-negatives from PfHRP2-based RDTs. Research is needed to validate alternative multi-copy targets for improved sensitivity, reproducibility, cost, and multiplexing capability.

articles.headline: EEG distinguishes malarial from nonmalarial coma in retinopathy-negative cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Using Electroencephalography to Assess Coma Etiology in Children with Retinopathy-Negative Cerebral Malaria.
articles.authors: Takle M, Andrews A, Riggle BA, Zelleke T, Harrar D, Zhang J, Zhang B, Wilson KJ, Beare NAV, Taylor TE, Seydel KB, Ray S, Postels DG
articles.details: EEG analysis of Malawian children (3 months-14 years) with retinopathy-negative cerebral malaria, retinopathy-positive CM, or nonmalarial coma showed quantitative EEG alone discriminated retinopathy-negative CM from nonmalarial coma (AUROC 0.83). Combined qualitative/quantitative analysis improved discrimination (AUROC 0.87). Retinopathy-negative CM EEG patterns resembled retinopathy-positive CM and differed significantly from nonmalarial coma, supporting that acute malaria pathophysiology is important in retinopathy-negative disease.

articles.headline: Apicoplast chaperonin CPN60 essential for Plasmodium parasite survival
articles.title_original: A chaperonin complex regulates organelle proteostasis in malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Tissawak A, Rosin Y, Katz Galay S, Qasem A, Shahar M, Trabelsi N, Furman-Schueler O, Johnson SM, Florentin A
articles.details: Using genetic tools, researchers demonstrated that apicoplast CPN60 ablation causes parasite death within the first replication cycle. Unlike other CPN60 orthologues, this chaperonin is not heat-shock regulated but directly controls proteostasis through interaction with the Clp protease complex. CPN60 physically binds both active and inactive Clp forms, regulating their stability. GroEL inhibitors (bacterial CPN60 orthologues) showed anti-Plasmodium activity including targets beyond the apicoplast.

articles.headline: P. vivax thrombocytopenia associated with phosphatidylserine autoantibodies
articles.title_original: Thrombocytopenia in patients with Plasmodium vivax in Colombia is associated with anti-phosphatidylserine autoantibodies and IL-6, IFNγ, IL-10 and TGF-β.
articles.authors: Velasco-Pareja MC, Tovar-Acero C, Cantero Guevara ME, Rodriguez A, Rivera-Correa J, Yasnot-Acosta MF
articles.details: Study compared plasma cytokines and anti-phosphatidylserine (PS) autoantibodies in P. vivax malaria patients with thrombocytopenia, malaria without thrombocytopenia, and healthy controls. IFNγ, IL-6, and IL-10 were elevated in thrombocytopenia group (P<0.05), while TGF-β1 was elevated in malaria-only group (P<0.0001). Anti-PS antibody levels were higher in thrombocytopenia group and negatively correlated with platelet counts, suggesting thrombocytopenia results from PS-targeting autoantibodies driven by pro-inflammatory cytokine imbalance.

articles.headline: Community knowledge shapes malaria prevention program success
articles.title_original: A scoping review of community knowledge in malaria prevention and control programmes.
articles.authors: Abdul Rahim FA, Mahmud MAF, Abdul Mutalip MH, Yoep N, Aminuddin MAH, Mohd Ngesom AM
articles.details: Scoping review of 63 studies from 27 countries (2000–2024), predominantly from Africa (68.3%) and Asia (28.6%), examining community knowledge in malaria prevention. Higher awareness levels consistently correlated with better preventive practices and earlier treatment-seeking, though significant knowledge gaps persisted regarding environmental risk factors and vector control. The authors concluded locally tailored, evidence-based education addressing context-specific gaps is essential for optimizing malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Urban-rural IPTp-SP disparities in Senegal linked to service readiness
articles.title_original: Bridging urban-rural disparities in malaria care during pregnancy in Senegal: evidence from household and health facility surveys.
articles.authors: Jiang Y, Liang D, Zhao J, Prasad S, Ndiop M, Thiam SA, Diallo I, Sene D, Mpembeni R, Huang J
articles.details: Analysis of 2012–2019 Demographic and Health Surveys and Service Provision Assessments in Senegal. Average IPTp-SP doses: 1.66 (95% CI 1.65–1.68) overall; urban 1.73, rural 1.63. Each one-point increase in malaria service readiness raised IPTp-SP doses by 0.251; the effect differed significantly by setting (coefficient −0.523, p<0.001), with rural women receiving fewer doses (0.089 vs 0.612 per unit increase). The authors concluded differential urban and rural strategies are needed to improve coverage.

articles.headline: Correction to CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing in malaria parasite
articles.title_original: Correction to 'A scalable CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing system facilitates CRISPR screens in the malaria parasite Plasmodium berghei'.
articles.details: Erratum to a prior publication on CRISPR-Cas9 methodology in Plasmodium berghei. Substantive details not provided in the correction notice.

articles.headline: Novel UV traps outperform CDC standard for vector surveillance
articles.title_original: Assessment of BG-Pro (Biogent AG) and Silver Bullet 2.1 (Lumin8) UV-Light Traps Efficiency for Surveillance of Malaria Vectors in Western Kenya.
articles.authors: Amugune BL, Tamre R, Mogaka D, Mbare O, Bukhari T, Fillinger U, Njoroge MM
articles.details: Comparison of three UV-light traps (BG-Pro UV, Silver Bullet 2.1 UV, CDC LED) tested indoors in eight houses over 64 nights in western Kenya. Silver Bullet 2.1 UV was most efficient: relative risk 4.3 (95% CI 2.5–7.3) for An. gambiae s.l. and 7.1 (95% CI 3.9–13.1) for An. funestus s.l. compared to CDC trap; also caught 3 times more Culex spp. (RR 2.7, 95% CI 1.2–6.0). The authors concluded both novel traps are promising surveillance tools, particularly in low-transmission settings.

articles.headline: Prior COVID-19 infection strongly increases severe malaria risk
articles.title_original: COVID-19 infection and its association with severe malaria & dengue: an epidemiological study from Southern India.
articles.authors: Ommen SJ, Mithra P, T R, Kumar N, Holla R, Kulal N, Rao M, Unnikrishnan B
articles.details: Case-control study of 293 adults (70 with malaria, 223 with dengue) in Southern India. Malaria: 30% (n=21) developed severe illness. Dengue: 35.8% (n=80) developed severe disease. Prior COVID-19 infection was strongly associated with severe outcomes: severe dengue (aOR 55.93, 95% CI 7.17–435.92, p<0.001) in 98.1% of COVID-history dengue cases; severe malaria (aOR 263.7, 95% CI 34.9–1987.4, p<0.001) in 85.7% of COVID-history malaria cases.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT accuracy varies by transmission level and demographics
articles.title_original: Varying Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test Accuracy by Regional Transmission Level and Demographics in Tanzania.
articles.authors: Wiener D, Seth MD, Mandara CI, Madebe RA, Popkin-Hall ZR, Giesbrecht D, Bakari C, Lyimo B, Pereus D, Francis F, Mbwambo D, Aaron S, Lusasi A, Lazaro S, Sheahan TP, Parr JB, Bailey JA, Ishengoma DS, Juliano JJ
articles.details: Cross-sectional 2021 study in Tanzania comparing three national standard malaria RDTs and one research RDT against qPCR. Diagnostic accuracy differed by age, gender, and regional transmission rate. The research test underperformed with poor sensitivity across transmission strata. Odds ratios suggested transmission intensity affects mRDT agreement and diagnostic performance, with implications for reducing measurement bias in malaria prevalence surveys.

articles.headline: Computational tool identifies conserved T cell epitopes for vaccine
articles.title_original: Computational Prioritization of T Cell Epitopes to Overcome HLA Restriction and Antigenic Diversity in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Laurenson AJ, Pierce BG, Takala-Harrison S, Laurens MB
articles.details: Analysis of 42 P. falciparum vaccine candidate proteins using consensus sequences from 18 African countries and HLA frequencies from 24 sub-Saharan populations. Tool identified 2,265 MHC I epitopes and 1,992 MHC II epitopes. Key MHC I epitopes from pre-erythrocytic antigens (HSP70-2, SLARP/SAP1, p36, FabZ, LISP1, LSA1, UIS3) achieved near 100% HLA-A/B/C coverage; MHC II epitopes provided 98.5%–100% coverage per parasite life stage. Authors concluded integrating epitope promiscuity and multistage antigens supports broad, durable vaccine protection.

articles.headline: Genomic atlas reveals positive selection in malaria vectors
articles.title_original: An atlas of positive selection in the genomes of major malaria vectors.
articles.authors: Nagi SC, Harding NJ, Lawniczak MKN, Donnelly MJ, Miles A
articles.details: Whole-genome sequence analysis of 4,306 mosquitoes from 21 countries in An. gambiae complex and An. funestus. Identified extensive evidence for parallel evolution between species. Confirmed intense selection at known insecticide resistance loci (Vgsc, Rdl, Ace1, Cyp6p, Cyp9k1, Gste, Coeaexf). Novel selection signals identified at diacylglycerol kinase on X chromosome and Keap1 in An. gambiae s.l. Open-source web resource enables real-time monitoring of emerging resistance variants.

articles.headline: Malaria transmission linked to gold mining in Yanomami territory
articles.title_original: Human mobility, garimpos and spatiotemporal malaria transmission in the Yanomami Indigenous Territory: a retrospective observational study.
articles.authors: Barbieri AF, Santos RO
articles.details: Retrospective surveillance analysis (2007–2023) in Brazil's Yanomami Indigenous Territory using Malaria Mobility Networks. Spatial concentration: 86% of cases and 91% of population. Autochthonous malaria in indigenous localities reached 90% (2023). MMN involvement from garimpo (illegal gold mining) rose since 2019, peaking ~27% in 2022, more associated with urban residence. Authors concluded spatiotemporal patterns align with garimpo dynamics and garimpo-urban networks that facilitate transmission and sustain malaria in indigenous localities.

articles.headline: Healthcare provider adherence to malaria treatment guidelines low
articles.title_original: Evaluation of anti-malarial treatment for elimination of malaria in South West Ethiopia: a concurrent triangulation design for prompt decision.
articles.authors: Gebremichael MA, Gebremedhin T, Niguse W, Mamo G, Alemayehu B, Negeri E, Temam I, Zewude H, Bogale T, Sahile E, Pawlos E
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study with qualitative inquiry (March–July 2024) examining 1,684 patient cards, mean age 20.99±15.61 years. Screening: 95.96% (n=1,616) by microscopy and/or RDT. P. falciparum: 58.31% (n=982), followed by P. vivax and mixed infections. Overall healthcare provider adherence to updated malaria treatment standards: 36.99% (95% CI 33.93–38.52%). Main barriers: drug shortage, lack of training on updated guidelines, health professional shortage, and laboratory material shortage. Authors recommended ensuring drug availability, training, and supervision to improve adherence.

articles.headline: Serum endocan elevated in severe malaria, correlates with severity
articles.title_original: Preliminary assessment of serum endocan as a biomarker of disease severity in Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.authors: Nantavisai K, Puttikamonkul S, Viriyavejakul P
articles.details: Study of 99 participants (20 healthy controls, 36 P. vivax, 30 uncomplicated P. falciparum, 13 severe P. falciparum) measuring serum endocan on days 0 and 7. All malaria groups had higher endocan than controls (p<0.0001). Severe P. falciparum: median 4.67 (IQR 2.85–7.93) ng/ml; uncomplicated P. falciparum: 3.27 (IQR 2.24–4.33); P. vivax: 1.85 (IQR 1.44–3.23). Endocan correlated positively with parasite density in P. vivax (rs=0.4632, p=0.0066) and severe P. falciparum (rs=0.6264, p=0.0251), and negatively with platelets in P. vivax (rs=−0.5523, p=0.001).

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance mutations emerging across Ethiopia regions
articles.title_original: Rising prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum Artemisinin partial resistance mutations in Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Brhane BG, Fola AA, Nigussie H, Leonetti A, Kassa M, Hailgiorgis H, Wuletaw Y, Abera A, Mohammed H, Sime H, G/Tsadik A, Assefa G, Solomon H, Tasew G, Tollera G, Hailu M, Juliano JJ, Assefa A, Parr JB, Bailey JA
articles.details: Analysis of 1,199 P. falciparum infections from 12 sentinel sites across five Ethiopian regions (2019–2023) using molecular inversion probes. k13 R622I mutation: 15.7% of samples with marked regional variation. Three validated artemisinin partial resistance mutations (P441L, P574L, A675V) detected in Ethiopia for first time; A675V found exclusively in clinic serving refugees from Sudan and South Sudan. Polymorphisms in crt, mdr1, dhps, dhfr nearly fixed. Most samples (87.2%) monogenomic; mutant parasites showed high local genetic relatedness, suggesting clonal transmission.

articles.headline: Cluster heterogeneity in malaria trials affects study power estimates
articles.title_original: Characterisation of between-cluster heterogeneity in malaria cluster randomised trials to inform future sample size calculations.
articles.authors: Biggs J, Challenger JD, Dee D, Elobolobo E, Chaccour C, Saute F, Staedke SG, Vilakati S, Chung JB, Hsiang MS, Dabira ED, Erhart A, D'Alessandro U, Tripura R, Peto TJ, Von Seidlein L, Mukaka M, Mosha J, Protopopoff N, Accrombessi M, Hayes R, Churcher TS, Cook J
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 24 malaria cluster randomized trials examining between-cluster heterogeneity in outcomes. Using regression modeling to calculate coefficient of variation (k) values, researchers found that empirical k estimates often exceeded those used in original sample size calculations, reducing study power and effect size precision. Elevated k was associated with incidence outcomes versus prevalence, lower endemicity settings, and uneven intervention coverage across clusters.

articles.headline: Sterile insect technique could eliminate malaria in The Gambia
articles.title_original: Evaluating the cost of malaria elimination by Anopheles gambiae precision guided SIT in the Upper River region, The Gambia.
articles.authors: Gendron WAC, Raban R, Mondal A, M Sánchez C H, Smidler AL, Zilberman D, Ilboudo PGC, D'Alessandro U, Marshall JM, Akbari OS
articles.details: Cost-effectiveness study of precision guided sterile insect technique (pgSIT) for Anopheles gambiae control in The Gambia's Upper River region. Predicted to prevent approximately 230 deaths and 48,000 sick days per year. Cost estimated at 0.36-3.03 USD per person annually, saving disability-adjusted life years at 11-94 USD per year and preventing cases at 10-86 USD per infection. Cost per DALY comparable to current interventions.

articles.headline: CHIR-124 shows multistage activity against drug-resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: The Human Chk1 Inhibitor CHIR-124 Shows Multistage Activity Against Plasmodium falciparum via Dual Inhibition of PfArk1 and Hemozoin Formation.
articles.authors: Wicht KJ, Woodland JG, Garnie LF, Langeveld H, Taylor D, Godoy LC, Pasaje CFA, de Souza ML, Siqueira-Neto JL, Ghidelli-Disse S, Lafuente-Monasterio MJ, Gamo FJ, Coertzen D, Reader J, van der Watt M, Bridgford JL, Girling G, Coyle R, Scheurer C, Wittlin S, Lee MCS, Voss TS, Winzeler EA, Fidock DA, Niles JC, Birkholtz LM, Coulson LB, Chibale K
articles.details: The human checkpoint kinase 1 inhibitor CHIR-124 demonstrated in vitro activity against drug-sensitive and drug-resistant asexual blood stage P. falciparum parasites, as well as moderate activity against liver and gametocyte forms. CHIR-124 competitively binds Plasmodium aurora-related kinase-1 (PfArk1) and inhibits hemozoin formation, causing dose-dependent increase in free heme correlated with parasite growth inhibition. Findings demonstrate polypharmacology with dual inhibition mechanisms showing reduced resistance generation risks.

articles.headline: Blood count biomarkers predict P. falciparum disease severity
articles.title_original: Comparative Analysis of Initial Full Blood Count Parameters in Adults Infected With Plasmodium falciparum for Classification of Disease Severity and Previous Exposure Across Endemic (Gabon) and Nonendemic (Germany) Settings.
articles.authors: von Wedel C, Matthies LC, Dierks C, Tober-Lau P, Bardtke L, Lingscheid T, Nordmann T, Jochum J, Huebl L, Tappe D, Zoller T, Pechmann K, Okwu DG, Malinga EG, Ralser M, Sander LE, Zoleko-Manego R, Ramharter M, Mombo-Ngoma G, Kurth F, Mischlinger J
articles.details: Analysis of 1,392 P. falciparum infected adults in Gabon (endemic) and Germany (non-endemic) using full blood count parameters to classify disease severity. Compared to uncomplicated malaria, severe malaria showed significantly lower platelet counts (150 vs. 40 ×10³/µL, P<0.0001) and higher neutrophil counts (2.57 vs. 4.49 ×10³/µL, P<0.0001) in German cohort. Machine learning classifier outperformed individual parameters (AUROC 0.84). Lymphocyte and monocyte counts reflected previous malaria exposure.

articles.headline: Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio identifies malaria infection reliably
articles.title_original: Diagnostic value of full blood count derived systemic inflammatory biomarkers in malaria infection.
articles.authors: Boachie J, Ahiable D, Ajabuin LA, Amissah R, Asmah-Brown A, Apalebilah S, Owusu-Poku AG, Eshun H, Adu P, Nyarkoh JK
articles.details: Case-control study of 45 malaria patients and 50 healthy individuals examining full blood count-derived inflammatory biomarkers. Malaria patients had significantly higher monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio (MLR), neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), and aggregate index of systemic inflammation (AISI). Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio showed excellent diagnostic value with area under curve 0.937, sensitivity 86.7%, and specificity 92.0% at cutoff ≥2.12. NLR correlated moderately with parasite count (ρ = 0.5).

articles.headline: Splenic macrophage subsets have specialized roles during Plasmodium infection
articles.title_original: Red pulp macrophages clear parasites, while marginal metallophilic and marginal zone macrophages support CD4(+) T cell activation during Plasmodium yoelii infection.
articles.authors: Falkenstein J, Ninnemann A, Hose M, Elwy A, Lang KS, Matuschewski K, Buer J, Westendorf AM, Hansen W
articles.details: Experimental study in mice examining roles of splenic macrophage subsets during P. yoelii infection. Red pulp macrophages (RPMφ) depletion resulted in elevated parasitemia, demonstrating their importance in clearing infected red blood cells. Marginal zone macrophages (MZMΦ) and marginal metallophilic macrophages (MMMΦ) contributed to CD4+ T cell activation and IFNγ production. Macrophage ablation by clodronate liposome caused partially reduced T cell activation correlated with elevated parasitemia.

articles.headline: New vaccines and molecular insights advance malaria therapeutics
articles.title_original: Emerging Molecular Mechanisms in Malaria Pathogenesis and Novel Therapeutic Approaches: A Focus on P. falciparum Malaria.
articles.authors: Sanyaolu A, Marinkovic A, Prakash S, Balendra V, Shazley O, Gardellini T, Jan A, Younis K, Okorie C, Izurieta R
articles.details: Review of emerging molecular mechanisms in P. falciparum malaria pathogenesis, including parasite immune evasion and drug resistance mechanisms. Discusses new therapeutic approaches including RTS,S and R21 vaccines and antibody-based therapies. Identifies ongoing challenges in extending immunity duration and ensuring effectiveness across diverse parasite strains. Emphasizes need for equitable access to treatments, robust health systems, and international collaboration for malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Weather and ENSO events significantly impact Amazon malaria transmission
articles.title_original: The impact of weather and extreme events on malaria transmission in the Brazilian Amazon: a case-crossover and population-based study.
articles.authors: Arisco NJ, Peterka C, Schwartz J, Castro MC
articles.details: Case-crossover analysis of 5,381,105 malaria cases from Brazilian Amazon (2003-2022) examining effects of weather variables and El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) events. Temperatures 25.64-30.85°C increased malaria infection risk up to 9% (95% CI: 8-10%); precipitation >4.46 cm increased risk 86% (95% CI: 35-155%). Two- and three-week lagged temperatures >25.64°C reduced risk up to 27% (95% CI: 19-33%) and 59% (95% CI: 52-69%) respectively. El Niño and La Niña associated with net case reductions of 2,179 and 37,258 respectively.

articles.headline: Uganda's 2022 malaria epidemic caused 3.4 million excess cases
articles.title_original: Malaria epidemics and its drivers in Uganda in 2022.
articles.authors: Aregawi MW, Maiteki C, Rek JC, Agaba B, Katureebe C, Ranjbar M, Zhang C, Kiware S, Opigo J
articles.details: Analysis of Uganda's 2022 malaria epidemic using district health data (2017-2022). Malaria cases rose 31% in 2022 compared to 2017-2021 median. 64 of 146 districts experienced epidemics with 3,379,309 excess outpatient cases (95% CI 1,553,714-5,339,709), 3,018,920 excess confirmed cases, and 149,789 excess inpatient admissions. Two-month lagged rainfall significantly increased incidence (p<0.01), with each millimeter associated with 13.4 additional malaria cases. Intervention coverage gaps and reduced insecticide efficacy likely drove epidemic.

articles.headline: Malaria testing and treatment gaps persist in Guinea and Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: The malaria care cascade and factors associated with receiving a malaria test in children under 5 years of age and pregnant women with fever: a cross-sectional household survey from three regions in Guinea and three districts in Sierra Leone in 2022.
articles.authors: Burger G, Röbl K, Fischer HT, Feddern L, Pozo-Martin F, Geurts B, Baldé H, Kaba I, Menelik-Obbarius S, Stein K, Merhi V, Weishaar H, Kallon MMM, Dumbuya K, Sellu J, Mbawah AK, Delamou A, El Bcheraoui C
articles.details: Cross-sectional household survey of 1,377 children under 5 and 254 pregnant women with fever in Guinea and Sierra Leone (2022). Malaria testing rates varied by region: 42-96% in children, 62-91% in pregnant women. Treatment within 2 days of fever onset ranged 54-81% in children and 24-69% in pregnant women. Hospitalization occurred in 6.5-9.5% of children and 9.5-12% of pregnant women. Trust in healthcare system and ANC attendance associated with receiving malaria testing.

articles.headline: Placental malaria and preeclampsia show overlapping pathophysiology
articles.title_original: The Relationship between Placental Malaria and Preeclampsia in Blantyre, Malawi.
articles.authors: Boudova S, Divala TH, Mungwira R, Mawindo P, Tomoka T, Laufer MK
articles.details: Analysis of 751 pregnancies from an intermittent preventive therapy trial in Malawi examining association between placental malaria (PM) and preeclampsia (PreE). 125 pregnancies (16.7%) had PM by histopathology or PCR, with 105 showing hemozoin indicative of early infection. 25 (3.3%) developed PreE without eclampsia, 6 (0.7%) with eclampsia. Individuals with hemozoin had nearly twice the PreE rate (6.7% vs 3.7%), though this difference was not statistically significant (P=0.16). Study was underpowered.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccines could mitigate climate-driven transmission disruptions
articles.title_original: Vaccination to mitigate climate-driven disruptions to malaria control in Madagascar.
articles.authors: Rice BL, Raobson E, Miharisoa S, Rebaliha M, Lewinski J, Raharinirina H, Golden CD, Vecchi GA, Wesolowski A, Grenfell B, Metcalf CJE
articles.details: Modeling study evaluating malaria vaccination's role in addressing transmission disruptions caused by extreme weather events in Madagascar. Data show rapid infection rebound during intervention gaps after tropical cyclones. Recently available malaria vaccines provide longer protection duration compared to other control options. Study quantified expected reduction in symptomatic infections across various vaccination scenarios to inform climate-adaptive malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 8.5% in Ethiopian school children; strong anemia association
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria, anemia and associated factors among school children in Hawassa city, Sidama, Ethiopia.
articles.authors: Gena A, Asnake S, Menjetta T
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 329 school children in Hawassa City, Ethiopia (April-June 2024) using microscopy and RDT for malaria diagnosis and hemoglobinometer for anemia assessment. Malaria prevalence was 8.5%, anemia 9.4%; children with malaria were 13.3 times more likely to have anemia (AOR = 13.258, 95% CI: 3.188-55.139). Insecticide-treated nets and knowledge of malaria transmission significantly reduced malaria risk, while meal frequency and nutritional status affected anemia risk.

articles.headline: Blood groups don't predict malaria risk in protected military population
articles.title_original: Impact of the ABO and Rh Blood Groups on Malaria Incidence, Recurrence, and Severity Among a Moroccan Military Contingent in the Central African Republic.
articles.authors: El Faria W, Loubadi Y, Moutmir Y, Ababou M, Hammani A, Mahtat EM, Jennane S, El Maaroufi H, Doghmi K
articles.details: Cross-sectional 8-month study of 749 Moroccan soldiers deployed to Central African Republic (October 2024-May 2025) on weekly mefloquine prophylaxis with physical protective measures. 20.8% (156/749) experienced malaria (89% P. falciparum), 1.1% developed severe malaria. No significant associations between ABO or Rh blood groups and malaria incidence, recurrence, or severity (p>0.05). Robust prevention strategies may overshadow genetic influences on malaria susceptibility.

articles.headline: Molecular mechanism of var gene switching enables P. falciparum chronic infection
articles.title_original: The molecular basis of coordinated antigen switching enabling chronic infection by human malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Visone JE, Florini F, Hadjimichael E, Patel V, Deitsch KW
articles.details: Mechanistic study elucidating how the var2csa gene functions as a 'switching hub' through interactions between noncoding RNAs, upstream open reading frames, and nonsense-mediated mRNA decay pathway to coordinate transcriptional switching between var genes. This coordinated switching of PfEMP1 isoforms enables parasites to evade antibody-mediated immunity and perpetuate chronic infections in humans.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccination urgently needed to address crisis in Ugandan children
articles.title_original: Addressing the malaria crisis among children in Uganda: the urgent need for vaccination and strategic action.
articles.authors: Akilimali A, Tague C, Karegeya A, Mugenyi N, Taj J, Mufungizi I, Naaz F, Mugisha MJM, Cakirwa H, Yokolo H, Ekouo J, Makeda D, Hangi S, Mbabazi S
articles.details: Opinion or review article addressing the urgent need for malaria vaccination and strategic action to address the malaria crisis affecting children in Uganda.

articles.headline: HRP2 RDTs miss non-falciparum species; P. malariae, P. ovale underdetected
articles.title_original: Undetected Plasmodium malariae and P. ovale infections in HRP2 RDT-positive children with uncomplicated malaria in Nanoro, Burkina Faso.
articles.authors: Kouevi AFC, Bayala IMC, Sondo P, Kaboré B, Millogo KS, Kambou SAE, Compaore EW, Nikiema M, Kazienga A, Rouamba T, Gnémé A, Tinto H
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 207 children <5 years with HRP2 RDT-positive uncomplicated malaria in Nanoro, Burkina Faso (July 2021-June 2022). Microscopy and nested PCR detected 3 patent P. malariae and 1 P. ovale; additionally identified 5 sub-patent P. malariae and 6 sub-patent P. ovale infections (χ²=0.16, p=0.683). Mixed infections exhibited lower parasite densities than P. falciparum mono-infections. Findings highlight that HRP2-based RDTs significantly underestimate non-falciparum Plasmodium species.

articles.headline: Cytokine responses differ between human and macaque P. knowlesi infections
articles.title_original: Comparative immunological study of Plasmodium knowlesi infections in humans and macaques: insights into cytokine dynamics.
articles.authors: Abd Rachman Isnadi MF, Yong YK, Grigg MJ, Sipangkui S, Lee PC, Nuin NA, Tan AF, Molius P, Tuuga A, Abd Sukor JR, Rajahram G, Daim S, Chua TH
articles.details: Comparative immunological study of 30 P. knowlesi-infected humans, 30 healthy controls, and 35 wild macaques in Sabah, Malaysia. Cytokine analysis (TNF, IFNγ, IL-1β, IL-4, IL-6, IL-10) revealed IL-10 dominance in humans (positively associated with parasite genomic DNA) and IL-6 dominance in macaques (unrelated to parasite genomic DNA). Infected humans exhibited higher IL-10/TNF ratios despite lower parasite genomic DNA, indicative of greater disease severity.

articles.headline: G6PD-guided high-dose primaquine for P. vivax radical cure in Indonesia/PNG
articles.title_original: High daily dose Short COurse PrimaquinE after G6PD testing for the radical cure of Plasmodium vivax malaria in Indonesia and Papua New Guinea: the SCOPE implementation study protocol.
articles.authors: SCOPE Study Group
articles.details: Implementation study protocol for P. vivax radical cure in Indonesia and Papua New Guinea using G6PD point-of-care testing to guide primaquine dosing: 7 mg/kg over 7 days for G6PD normal patients (≥70% activity), 14 days for intermediate (30-70% activity), or 8-week weekly dosing for deficient (<30% activity). Stage 1 (800 patients, 4 clinics) assesses treatment safety; Stage 2 (11,410 patients, 10 clinics, 12 months) evaluates feasibility, cost-effectiveness, and P. vivax recurrence prevention.

articles.headline: Deep learning achieves 96.5% accuracy for automated malaria detection from images
articles.title_original: Automated multi-model framework for malaria detection using deep learning and feature fusion.
articles.authors: Shahin OR, Alshammari HH, Alabdali RN, Salaheldin AM, Saleh N
articles.details: Multi-model deep learning framework for automated malaria detection from microscopy images, using ResNet50, VGG16, and DenseNet-201 for feature extraction, followed by PCA-based dimensionality reduction and SVM-LSTM hybrid classification with majority voting ensemble. Validation on 27,558 blood smear images achieved 96.47% accuracy, 96.03% sensitivity, 96.90% specificity, 96.88% precision, and 96.45% F1-score.

articles.headline: Congenital malaria prevalence 6.5% in Nigerian newborns; maternal parasitaemia key risk
articles.title_original: Prevalance and risk factors of congenital malaria in Osogbo, Southwestern Nigeria.
articles.authors: Odediji SA, Olabanji SA, Olawuyi AO, Amoo KO, Amoo-Adeboye BO, Adeleke MA
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 77 newborns in Osogbo, Nigeria examined within 24 hours of delivery for congenital malaria using microscopy and mRDT on peripheral and cord blood. Prevalence of congenital malaria was 6.5%, with all cases showing cord blood parasitaemia. Maternal malarial parasitaemia was a significant predictor (p<0.05), while maternal retroviral status, intermittent prophylactic therapy, antimalarial therapy, and bednet use were not significantly associated (p>0.05).

articles.headline: Anopheles wing nanomorphology differs between species at nanoscale
articles.title_original: Unveiling the Nanomorphological Architecture of Malaria Vectors' Wings via Minkowski and Multifractal Formalisms.
articles.authors: Ramos GQ, Pinto LCC, Matos RS, Costa CAR, Lopes SCP, Santana RAG, Justiniano SCB, Miranda KR, da Fonseca Filho HD
articles.details: Quantitative nanomorphological analysis of wing surfaces from Anopheles darlingi and A. aquasalis using atomic force microscopy, Minkowski functionals, and multifractal formalism. Analysis revealed distinct species and dorso-ventral asymmetries, with A. aquasalis displaying higher elevation variance, denser nanomorphological heterogeneity, broader multifractal spectra (Δα ≈ 1.073), and greater topographic complexity compared to A. darlingi.

articles.headline: Corrigendum to WIMANET first workshop meeting report
articles.title_original: Corrigendum to "The Wildlife Malaria Research network (WIMANET): Meeting report on the 1st WIMANET workshop" [Int. J. Parasitol: Parasites and Wildlife 25 (2024) 100989].
articles.authors: Gutiérrez-López R, Ferraguti M, Bodawatta KH, Chagas CRF, Chakarov N, Duc M, Emmenegger T, García-Longoria L, Lopes RJ, Martínez-de la Puente J, Renner SC, Santiago-Alarcon D, Sehgal RNM, Stankovic D, Marzal A, Dunn JC
articles.details: Corrigendum to the Wildlife Malaria Research Network (WIMANET) first workshop meeting report.

articles.headline: Malaria 43.1% prevalence in Côte d'Ivoire baseline survey before dam opening
articles.title_original: Epidemiological Profile and Risk Factors for Malaria in Rural Communities Before the Operationalization of the Singrobo-Ahouaty Dam, Southern Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Avenié TJD, Silué KD, Guindo-Coulibaly N, Koné N, Touré S, Valian KL, Kouadio KS, Bédia AMJ, Déabo BF, Diabagaté K, Nsanzabana C, Coulibaly JT
articles.details: Cross-sectional baseline survey in rural communities in southern Côte d'Ivoire before Singrobo-Ahouaty dam operationalization. Malaria prevalence was 43.1% (394/915); school-age children (6-13 years) had highest prevalence at 74.3%, younger children (0-5 years) had highest parasite density (1218.0 parasites/µL). Risk factors included informal employment (ORa=1.5), work in raw materials (ORa=1.4), and frequent mosquito bites. Findings emphasize need for vector control before dam operationalization.

articles.headline: Traditional medicine research investment for malaria control
articles.title_original: Investing in traditional medicine: leveraging evidence and innovative research to strengthen the fight against malaria in Nigeria.
articles.authors: Onukansi FO, Anokwuru CC, Eneh SC, Odey GO, Onah DO, Sokunbi T, Innocent DC, Dozie UW, Umoke PC, Ojo TO, Ezejindu CN
articles.details: Narrative analysis of traditional African medicine in Nigeria for malaria management. Reviews the historical success of Artemisia annua in modern antimalarial drug development and examines existing literature on herbal treatment applications. Advocates for increased funding, scientific investigation of remedy efficacy, pharmacological safety testing, and integration of traditional medicine into national health systems to develop new malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in northern Iran with 22 cases in 2023
articles.title_original: The resurgence of malaria in Northern Iran in 2023: a wake-up call.
articles.authors: Koohsar F, Faridnia R, Jorjani O, Hojjati MT, Yadagiri G, Noshak G, Pirzaman HT, Kalani H
articles.details: Surveillance study screened 445 individuals with malaria-suspected symptoms in Golestan Province, Iran (March–December 2023). Identified 22 confirmed cases (4.94%): 77.27% P. vivax (mean parasites/µL: 16,029±5,060), 18.18% P. falciparum (105,460±102,146), and 4.54% co-infected. All patients responded positively to treatment. Presence of gametocytes in P. falciparum specimens and local vector populations raise concern for potential establishment of local transmission.

articles.headline: Severe malaria kidney injury and long-term kidney disease outcomes
articles.title_original: Malaria associated pathogenesis of chronic kidney disease (MAP-CKD): a prospective study of children hospitalized with severe malaria.
articles.authors: Batte A, Nakulima V, Namazzi R, Aujo JC, Starr MC, Schwaderer AL, Lintner-Rivera M, Ahumwire A, Ogwal D, Tenywa E, Oyang W, Ssenkusu JM, Bangirana P, Idro R, Kalyesubula R, Dreyer G, McCulloch M, Bassat Q, John CC, Goldstein SL, Conroy AL
articles.details: Prospective multi-site cohort study in Uganda enrolling 750 children hospitalized with severe malaria and 375 community controls (aged 3 months–<16 years), with two-year follow-up. Will assess acute kidney injury recovery at months 1, 2, 4 and long-term kidney function at months 12, 24. Characterizing host pathways of maladaptive kidney repair and examining impacts on mortality, morbidity, and neurodevelopmental outcomes through cognitive assessments and brain MRI.

articles.headline: Marked heterogeneity in individual malaria infection rates, Mali cohort
articles.title_original: Marked heterogeneity in malaria infection rate in a Malian longitudinal cohort.
articles.authors: LaVerriere E, Johnson ZM, Shieh M, Nziza N, Alter G, Buckee CO, Crompton PD, Traore B, Tran TM, Neafsey DE
articles.details: Genotypic analysis of 13,000+ blood samples from 464 Malian participants (aged 3 months–25 years) using P. falciparum amplicon sequencing over six years. Molecular force of infection (molFOI) ranged 0–55 in 2011, varied extensively between individuals but remained consistent within persons across annual transmission seasons independent of age. HbS allele associated with lower molFOI; functional antibody responses to CSP C-term and RH5 antigens identified as immune correlates of protection.

articles.headline: Kelch13 partial-resistance and HRP2/3 deletions in central Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 Variants and Histidine-Rich Protein 2/3 Deletions in Central Ethiopia, 2023.
articles.authors: Tufa TB, Oliveira R, Schallenberg E, Hendry JA, Igiraneza C, Jara-Reznakova B, Eggers Y, Sill H, Kenea D, Luedde T, Feldt T, Ndoli JM, Mockenhaupt FP, van Loon W
articles.details: Molecular survey of 220 symptomatic malaria patients in central Ethiopia (2023): 86.4% P. falciparum, 12.3% P. vivax, 3.6% P. ovale. Among P. falciparum isolates (n=174), validated artemisinin partial-resistance marker pfk13 R622I detected in 1.1% (2/174) and candidate P441L in 1.7% (3/174). pfhrp2, pfhrp3, and dual deletions present in 24.0%, 78.8%, and 22.7% respectively, indicating substantial RDT-detection risk. Artemisinin and sulfadoxine sensitivity remained predominant.

articles.headline: Environmental factors shaping Anopheles vector ecology in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Impact of environmental factors on the bionomics of Anopheles mosquito vectors of zoonotic malaria: A narrative review.
articles.authors: Masse RS, Vythilingam I, Fornace K, Othman H, Liu X, Jaafar AJ, Shahar MK, Abdul Rahman NA, Khairul Azman AH, Jeyaprakasam NK
articles.details: Narrative review of environmental determinants (temperature, humidity, elevation, precipitation, seasonality, land use) of Anopheles mosquito bionomics and transmission of zoonotic Plasmodium species in Southeast Asia: P. knowlesi, P. cynomolgi, P. inui, and P. fieldi. Emphasizes One Health approaches integrating vector surveillance, sustainable land management, and multidisciplinary public health interventions for vector-borne malaria control in endemic regions.

articles.headline: Protective P. falciparum antigens in pregnancy-associated malaria, Kenya
articles.title_original: Exploring the P. falciparum antigens associated with reduced risk of malaria in pregnancy.
articles.authors: Mwai L, Musundi S, Waweru H, Nagaoka H, Limbua PG, Tsuboi T, Takashima E, Gitaka J, Kanoi BN
articles.details: Seroepidemiological study of 698 P. falciparum recombinant antigens in Kenyan primigravida and multigravida women. Three antigens (PF3D7_1301800 SURFIN 13.1, PF3D7_0424400 SURFIN 4.2, PF3D7_1252100 rhoptry neck antigen 3 domain) showed statistically significant antibody associations with gravidity. All five VAR2CSA domains demonstrated seroprevalence 42–62% with positive correlation but lacked gravidity-association statistical significance. Identified antigens proposed as vaccine targets to reduce pregnancy malaria morbidity.

articles.headline: Tryptophan catabolism and regulatory T cell expansion in P. vivax
articles.title_original: IDO1-AhR axis increases T regulatory cells in Plasmodium vivax malaria infection.
articles.authors: Dos Santos RO, Carvalho NO, de Morais TBDN, Sobrinho WBS, da Cruz MGS, de Faria ES, de Araújo FF, Magalhães VP, Lopes SCP, Malheiros A, da Mota AJ, Santiago HDC, Nogueira PA, Teixeira de Carvalho A, de Lacerda MVG, Lalwani P
articles.details: Brazilian study of immune tolerance in P. vivax malaria examining indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO)-mediated tryptophan-to-kynurenine conversion and regulatory T cell (Treg) expansion. In vitro P. vivax-infected RBC stimulation increased IDO-1 expression and KYN/TRP ratio; IDO/MyD88/aryl hydrocarbon receptor inhibition reduced Treg frequencies and T cell proliferation. Acute malaria patients showed elevated KYN/TRP and Treg; individuals with prior infections showed lower Treg, interferon-γ, and kynurenine/tryptophan ratios.

articles.headline: Four artemisinin combinations maintain efficacy for uncomplicated malaria
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether-lumefantrine, artesunate-amodiaquine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine and artesunate-pyronaridine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Mozambique, 2022.
articles.authors: Nhama A, Chidimatembue A, Nhamussua L, Bassat Q, da Silva C, Nhacolo A, Arnaldo P, Salvador C, Cassy A, Candrinho B, Dimene M, Carvalho E, Saifodine A, Wate F, Mucavele H, Torres-Mendoza Y, Horton B, Plucinski M, Cistero P, Mayor A, Aide P
articles.details: In vivo therapeutic efficacy trial in Mozambique comparing artemether-lumefantrine, artesunate-amodiaquine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, and artesunate-pyronaridine in 828 children aged 6 months–11 years with uncomplicated P. falciparum (1,000–200,000 parasites/µL). Day 28 corrected efficacy: artemether-lumefantrine 97.63–100% (n=462), artesunate-amodiaquine 100% (n=183). Day 42 efficacy: dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine 97.75–100% (n=91), artesunate-pyronaridine >90% WHO cutoff (n=92). All drugs well tolerated; <2% adverse events.

articles.headline: Disability prevalence and socioeconomic disparities in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic and geographical disparities in disability distribution among Tanzanian population: insights from the 2022 demographic and health survey and malaria indicator survey.
articles.authors: Luoga P, Mahiti GR, Kapinga O, Herzon B, Mwaisengela SM, Materu PA, German CJ, Kasangala AA, Mtulo GE, Kinyenje ES, Hokororo JC, Ngonyani TL, Kacholi G, Kapologwe NA, Kumalija CJ, Anasel MG, Eliakimu ES
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 58,425 Tanzanians (aged 15–95 years) from 2022 Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey. Overall disability prevalence 11%. Age ≥50 years associated with adjusted prevalence ratio (APR) 3.85 (95% CI 3.33–4.52) vs. ages 15–29. Females (APR 2.67), secondary education (APR 2.58), separated/widowed status (APR 2.91), wealthiest households (APR 2.74), and health insurance (APR 2.83) associated with higher disability prevalence. Northern zone showed higher prevalence (APR 2.77) than Lake zone.

articles.headline: P. falciparum drug resistance evolution during pre-elimination, Ecuador
articles.title_original: Tracking Plasmodium falciparum antimalarial resistance markers during a malaria pre-elimination period in the Pacific coast of South America.
articles.authors: Ñacata I, Early AM, Boboy J, Neafsey DE, Sáenz FE
articles.details: Molecular epidemiology survey of 67 P. falciparum isolates from northwestern Ecuador (2019–2021) genotyped for pfcrt, pfdhps, pfdhfr, pfmdr1, pfk13, and pfaat1. Compared to 2013–2015 baseline: chloroquine-resistance mutations (CVMET pfcrt, CICNI pfdhfr) increased to 100% dominance in Esmeraldas; artemisinin-sensitive mutations (MYRIC pfk13) remained at 100%; sulfadoxine-sensitive mutations (SAKAA pfdhps) at 100%. First report of A16V pfdhfr resistance marker in Ecuador. Current first-line treatment (artemether-lumefantrine + primaquine) remains effective.

articles.headline: Artificial intelligence advances in malaria diagnosis and surveillance
articles.title_original: An insight into the role of artificial intelligence in combating malaria: recent developments.
articles.authors: Sachdeva H, Sharma M
articles.details: Literature review of artificial intelligence applications across malaria control: deep learning for automated blood-smear and microscopy parasite detection; in silico drug discovery and machine learning for drug-resistant parasite treatment; machine learning for transmission prediction, epidemiological tracking, and surveillance optimization; and AI-enabled personalized medicine. Emphasizes sustained investment in AI-driven research to achieve long-term malaria eradication targets.

articles.headline: Malaria reactive surveillance strategy in Cambodia functioning but timeliness declining
articles.title_original: Evaluating malaria reactive surveillance and response strategies in northeast Cambodia: a mixed-methods study.
articles.authors: Win Han Oo, Siv S, Kaungmyat Khant, Htike W, Molyta MM, Lynn T, Aye Tun N, Agius PA, Fowkes FJI
articles.details: Mixed-methods evaluation of Cambodia's 1-3-7 reactive surveillance and response (RASR) strategy in Ratanakiri and Stung Treng provinces (2020-2022). Assessed timeliness of case notification, reactive case detection (RCD), and focus classification. In 2020 and 2022, 72% and 59% of cases were notified/investigated within one day; RCD timeliness dropped from 89% to 45%. Despite challenges (limited community participation, poor mobile network, insufficient budget), stakeholders deemed the strategy acceptable; improved timeliness requires budget allocation, capacity building, and enhanced community engagement.

articles.headline: LDH-based RDT performance against HRP2-based tests in Senegal
articles.title_original: Performance and usability evaluation of three LDH-based malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Kédougou, Senegal.
articles.authors: Sambe BS, Zobrist S, Sheahan W, Soni D, Diagne A, Sarr I, Diatta AS, Diaw SOM, Golden A, Slater H, Jang IK, Roa N, Pal S, Sarr FD, Faye J, Vigan-Womas I, Dieye Y, Cisse M, Domingo GJ, Niang M
articles.details: Cross-sectional diagnostic accuracy study in Senegal comparing SD BIOLINE HRP2-based RDT with three LDH-based RDTs in 220 febrile patients (154 Pf-positive by PCR). BIOCREDIT Pf (pLDH/HRPII) achieved highest sensitivity 78% (95% CI 70.9-84.5%) and specificity 89% (95% CI 79.4-95.6%), outperforming microscopy (53%). PfLDH lines alone showed lower sensitivity (61-64%) compared to HRP2-based tests. The HRP2 line contributed substantially to improved sensitivity even in regions with low hrp2/hrp3 deletion rates.

articles.headline: Severe malaria hospitalization risk factors in non-endemic Brazil
articles.title_original: Factors associated with hospitalizations due to severe malaria in the non-endemic Brazilian region: a case-control study in the extra-Amazon Region from 2011 to 2019.
articles.authors: da Silva AC, Duarte EC, Marchesini PB, Viana GMR, Ramalho WM, Garcia KKS
articles.details: Case-control study (2011-2019) of malaria hospitalizations vs non-hospitalized cases in extra-Amazon Brazil using national surveillance and hospital records. High parasitemia (>100,000/mm³ OR 3.15; 95% CI 2.20-4.50), delayed treatment (≥8 days OR 2.08; 95% CI 1.62-2.66), and infection acquired abroad (OR 1.72; 95% CI 1.28-2.32) were major risk factors for severe malaria requiring hospitalization. Low education and high parasite density were strongly associated with severe outcomes. Active case detection was protective (OR 0.65), highlighting importance of prompt diagnosis.

articles.headline: Ethnomedicinal plants for malaria treatment in Odisha, India
articles.title_original: A review on the ethnomedicinal plants used for the traditional treatment of malaria in the Mayurbhanj district of Odisha, India.
articles.authors: Behera SK, Subudhi L, Mohapatra S, Panda AK
articles.details: Systematic review (PRISMA-based) of traditional medicinal plants for malaria in Mayurbhanj district, Odisha, analyzing 20 literature sources. Identified 40 plant species across 24 families (Acanthaceae and Apocyanaceae predominant). Preparations included decoctions, pastes, juices, and powders administered orally; leaves and roots were the predominant plant parts used. No adverse effects were reported, though further research is needed to establish therapeutic mechanisms and clinical efficacy.

articles.headline: P. falciparum genetic diversity and phylogeography in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Genetic Diversity and Phylogenetic Relatedness of Cameroonian Plasmodium falciparum Isolates and Comparative Analysis with Global Populations.
articles.authors: Kojom Foko LP, Hawadak J, Singh V
articles.details: Analysis of P. falciparum msp1/2 genetic diversity in isolates from three Cameroonian regions, compared with ~2500 global sequences. Multiple msp1 allelic families (K1, MAD20, RO33) and msp2 families (IC/3D7, FC27) were genotyped and characterized for structural variation and repetitive elements. K1 and RO33 showed phylogenetic affinity with Asian strains (China/Myanmar), while MAD20, IC/3D7, and FC27 clustered with strains from India, Panama, and Papua New Guinea, indicating complex population structure.

articles.headline: Ookluc assay enables high-throughput transmission-blocking compound screening
articles.title_original: Ookluc: A Plasmodium berghei Line for Identifying Transmission-blocking Compounds.
articles.authors: Novais JT, Calit J, Morosini LG, da Silva VA, Marques RF, Gaitán XA, Bargieri DY
articles.details: Development of novel high-throughput screening (HTS) assay using transgenic P. berghei (Ookluc strain) expressing nanoluciferase exclusively after zygote formation. The assay provides quantitative luminescence readout of sexual stage development (gamete→zygote→ookinete), enabling measurement of gametocyte activation and ookinete formation. This addresses a major bottleneck in transmission-blocking drug discovery by providing a robust, quantitative method for screening compounds targeting parasite sexual stages.

articles.headline: Malaria migration model: treating cases better than deportation strategy
articles.title_original: A meta-population model of malaria with asymptomatic cases, transmission blocking drugs, migration and screening.
articles.authors: Tchoumi SY, Banasiak J, Ouifki R
articles.details: Mathematical two-patch meta-population model examining malaria control with differential healthcare resources, migration between patches, and transmission-blocking drugs (TBDs). One patch implements screening and TBDs offering full but waning immunity; authorities can screen migrants and treat detected cases or refuse entry. Qualitative and perturbation analysis of endemic equilibria and bifurcations. Numerical simulations demonstrate that treating detected migrant cases with TBDs is more effective for disease control than deportation, which risks illegal entry and continued transmission.

articles.headline: P. vivax DBP gene amplification varies across Amazon regions
articles.title_original: The pattern of gene amplification of members of the Plasmodium vivax erythrocyte binding-like proteins family across the Amazon rainforest.
articles.authors: Guedes KS, Fernandes GM, Sanchez BAM, Ntumngia FB, Adams JH, Kano FS, Fontes CJF, Sousa TN, Carvalho LH
articles.details: Analysis of copy number variation (CNV) in P. vivax erythrocyte binding-like protein genes (dbp and ebp2) across three Amazon sites in 192 isolates. dbp amplification ranged from 25% (up to 8 copies) in western region to 2-9% (2-3 copies) in eastern/southwestern regions. ebp2 amplification was less frequent (15% western, 1-4% other regions). All individuals were DARC-positive; no significant associations observed between gene CNVs and DARC genotypes, suggesting amplifications may have fitness or immune evasion implications.

articles.headline: Parasitemia patterns predict experimental cerebral malaria risk in mice
articles.title_original: Temporal Parasitemia Trends Predict Risk and Timing of Experimental Cerebral Malaria in Mice Infected by Plasmodium berghei ANKA.
articles.authors: Murin PJ, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Carvalho LJM, Martins YC
articles.details: Machine learning models applied to parasitemia data from 370 mice (C57BL/6, CBA, Swiss Webster strains) to predict experimental cerebral malaria (ECM) risk and onset. Logistic regression using days 1-4 parasitemia identified that increases ≥0.05 between days 1-3 predicted ECM development with 97% sensitivity. Random Forest model predicted exact ECM onset date with high precision (mean absolute error 0.43, R² 0.64). Parasitemia-based predictors enable more consistent experimental designs and earlier identification of ECM pathological processes.

articles.headline: Gene drive mosquitoes: African stakeholder perspectives scoping review protocol
articles.title_original: Knowledge, Perspectives, and Risks Perceptions on Gene Drive and Genetically Modified Mosquitoes for Malaria Control among African Stakeholders: A Scoping Review Protocol.
articles.authors: Sogodogo A, Diarra S, Keita S, Diallo I, Hartley S, Telly N, Dolo H, Sanogo D, Kané F, Tangara CO, Diakité M, Sangho O, Kayentao K, Fritz H, Winch PJ, Doumbia S
articles.details: Scoping review protocol to synthesize evidence on African stakeholders' knowledge, perspectives, and risk perceptions regarding gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control. Systematic search across PubMed, Embase, Science Direct, Cochrane, and Google Scholar will identify scientific and grey literature; dual-reviewer screening and thematic analysis will assess findings. The review aims to inform governance and stakeholder engagement strategies for successful implementation of this emerging vector control technology.

articles.headline: Plasma biomarkers identified for pregnancy-associated P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Plasma protein biomarkers of Plasmodium falciparum infection in pregnant women: a high-throughput proteomics study.
articles.authors: Kanoi BN, Waweru H, Kobia FM, Mukala J, Kirira P, Mogere D, Gallini R, Åberg M, Vatish M, Gitaka J, Kamali-Moghaddam M
articles.details: High-throughput proteomics study screening plasma from malaria-exposed pregnant women to identify biomarkers of P. falciparum infection and potential placental involvement. Using proximity extension assay, elevated IgG Fc receptor IIb (FCGR2B) and heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) were identified in malaria-positive pregnancies, while IL-20 and neurturin (NRTN) were downregulated. IL-20 emerged as the most promising candidate biomarker for diagnosing pregnancy-associated malaria and predicting adverse outcomes, pending validation in large cohorts with placental histopathology.

articles.headline: P. vivax serological markers robust despite extensive genetic diversity
articles.title_original: Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria.
articles.authors: Bareng P, Wu KW, Takashima E, Argyropoulos D, Smith L, Naung M, Mazhari R, Schoffer K, Kiernan-Walker N, Abraham A, Lamont M, Mehra S, Lim P, Sattabongkot J, Monteiro W, Lacerda M, Healer J, Chitnis CE, Tham WH, Tsuboi T, Mueller I, Barry AE, Longley RJ
articles.details: Study assessing how P. vivax parasite genetic diversity affects serological markers for detecting recent exposure and hypnozoite carriage. Global genetic analysis identified extensive diversity in antigens DBPII, MSP5, RBP2a, and S16, with reference strain (Sal-1) similarity ranging from 0.4% (MSP5) to 99% (S16). Testing with samples from Brazil and Thailand showed that despite regional immunogenicity differences (RBP2a, DBPII), most antigens (5/8) reliably identified recent infections within 9 months across regions; multi-antigen classification models mitigated geographic variation.

articles.headline: R21/Matrix-M vaccine elicits diverse antibody and T-cell responses in malaria-exposed adults
articles.title_original: R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine drives diverse immune responses in pre-exposed adults: insights from a phase IIb controlled human malaria infection trial.
articles.authors: Kibwana E, Bundi C, Kimani D, Nyamako L, Keter K, Mutiso A, Ogwang R, Bellamy D, Rapi K, Bajer A, Provstgaard-Morys S, Stockdale L, Munoz O, Datoo MS, Lawrie A, Ramos-Lopez F, Roberts R, Hamaluba M, Hill AVS, Bejon P, Ewer KJ, Kapulu M
articles.details: This Phase IIb controlled human malaria infection trial in Kenya evaluated immune responses in 19 semi-immune adults vaccinated with R21/Matrix-M and challenged with P. falciparum sporozoites. The vaccine increased antibody titers across IgA, IgM, and IgG isotypes (especially IgG1/IgG3), with complement-fixing capabilities and boosted memory B cells. Vaccination also elevated circulating Tfh cells with a Th2/Th17-dominant phenotype.

articles.headline: P. vivax malaria with thrombocytopenia unmasking underlying chronic lymphocytic leukemia
articles.title_original: Underlying Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia in a Case of Vivax Malaria With Thrombocytopenia: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Saha A, Mukhopadhyay M, Talapatra A, Bandhopadhay T, Sardar S
articles.details: A 64-year-old male presented with P. vivax malaria complicated by severe thrombocytopenia (platelet count 15,000/mm³), leukocytosis (TLC 29,100/μL), and coagulopathy (INR 4.2). Flow cytometry revealed underlying chronic lymphocytic leukemia. The unusual combination of thrombocytopenia with marked leukocytosis in malaria without sepsis should prompt investigation for underlying hematologic malignancy.

articles.headline: P. yoelii detected in invasive rats for first time in Gabon
articles.title_original: Rodent malaria parasites detected in the invasive Rattus rattus in Gabon.
articles.authors: Mbou-Boutambe C, Boundenga L, Degrugillier F, Gauthier P, Arnathau C, Rivero A, Granjon L, Rougeron V, Prugnolle F
articles.details: Systematic molecular screening of 527 rodents in rural Gabon (2021-2022) identified Plasmodium yoelii in 2 invasive black rats (Rattus rattus), with prevalence of 0.38%. Phylogenetic analysis confirmed these strains matched those previously isolated from native rodents, suggesting invasive rats may integrate into local Plasmodium transmission networks despite the low infection rate.

articles.headline: Rapid multiplex malaria diagnosis combining Chelex extraction and LAMP-MS in 40 minutes
articles.title_original: Rapid and Multiplex Diagnosis of Malaria Using Chelex-100 Extraction and LAMP-MS Assay.
articles.authors: Lim MS, Choe YL, Jang WS
articles.details: This diagnostic platform combines simplified Chelex-100 resin extraction with multiplex loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP-MS) capable of simultaneously detecting P. falciparum, P. vivax, and pan-malaria within 40 minutes. The method demonstrates sensitivity and specificity comparable to traditional PCR while being cost-effective and field-deployable, suitable for resource-constrained environments.

articles.headline: Alveolin domain interactions specify cytoskeletal protein localization in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Malaria Cytoskeletal Proteins Require Alveolin-Alveolin Interactions for Differential Localization: Recruitment and Organization of Alveolin Proteins.
articles.authors: Cepeda Diaz AK, Back PS, Sreenivasamurthy SK, Dvorin JD
articles.details: This biochemical study characterized four alveolin proteins in P. falciparum to elucidate how their domains mediate protein-protein interactions and differential recruitment to cytoskeletal structures. PfIMC1e requires PfIMC1f for basal complex recruitment while PfIMC1c and PfIMC1g localize independently. Alveolin domains contain unique localization signatures, with regions outside the alveolin domain also contributing to compartment-specific recruitment.

articles.headline: Hemozoin suppresses dendritic cell interferon responses, altering bacterial co-infection immunity
articles.title_original: Malaria-derived hemozoin skews dendritic cell responses to bacterial infections by reducing interferon gene-transcription by SWI/SNF-NuRD.
articles.authors: Lasaviciute G, Tariq K, Sugathan A, Quin J, Polenkowski M, Bujila I, Skorokhod O, Troye-Blomberg M, Sverremark-Ekström E, Östlund Farrants AK
articles.details: This study investigated how malaria-derived hemozoin affects dendritic cell transcriptional responses to bacterial endotoxin (LPS). Hemozoin co-exposure reduced HLA-DR and PD-L1 expression and impaired IFNγ-controlled gene transcription, mediated by increased NuRD chromatin remodeling complex activity with decreased NF-κB binding. These findings suggest hemozoin may reduce protective immunity against bacterial co-infections in malaria patients.

articles.headline: Merozoite antibody affinity develops differently than sporozoite affinity in infants
