articles.title_original: Development of high affinity antibodies to Plasmodium falciparum merozoite and sporozoite antigens during infancy and adulthood.
articles.authors: Lugaajju A, Idro R, Kiwuwa S, Beeson JG, Drew DR, Mortazavi SE, Linse S, Persson KEM
articles.details: A 9-month longitudinal study in Uganda tracked antibody affinity (dissociation constant, kd) to merozoite antigens AMA1 and MSP2 and sporozoite antigen CSP in newborn infants and mothers. Affinity against AMA1 and MSP2 decreased from birth to 6 months then increased through 9 months (not reaching maternal levels), while CSP affinity remained unchanged. Antibody magnitude and affinity evolved independently across different antigens.

articles.headline: ITN scale-up associates with 34% malaria risk reduction across 45 sub-Saharan countries
articles.title_original: Effects of vector control interventions on spatio-temporal changes of falciparum malaria risk in children aged 2-10 in sub-Saharan African regions during 2011-2020.
articles.authors: Chol CJ, Belay DB, Fenta HM, Chen DG
articles.details: This spatiotemporal analysis examined P. falciparum prevalence from 634 administrative areas across 45 sub-Saharan African countries (2011-2020). Average prevalence in children aged 2-10 decreased from 21.32% in 2011 to 16.75% in 2016 with slight resurgence in 2017. Each unit increase in insecticide-treated net use correlated with 34.07% reduction in malaria infection risk, with highest remaining prevalence in West-central and Central regions.

articles.headline: Chinese medical discourse adopted malaria terminology through institutional standardization, 1830s-1940s
articles.title_original: Malaria in China: a discourse-historical perspective.
articles.authors: Miao P
articles.details: This historical analysis traces how malaria was translated into Chinese medical discourse from the 1830s through 1940s via missionary dictionaries, newspapers, and institutional standardization. The term 'nueji' became dominant over competing terminology, while Plasmodium-based parasitological understanding was partially integrated (lexicalized as 'nueyuanchong'), though immune mechanisms remained unexplained in Chinese medical frameworks. Newspapers served as contested epistemic spaces between Western and traditional medical discourses.

articles.headline: P. vivax malaria triggers hemophagocytic syndrome and pancytopenia in pregnant woman
articles.title_original: A Rare Presentation of Plasmodium vivax Malaria: Pancytopenia due to Hemophagocytic Syndrome in a Pregnant Woman.
articles.authors: Lema GD, Dagnaw AB, Gebeyaw ED
articles.details: A 22-year-old woman at 9 weeks of pregnancy presented with P. vivax malaria complicated by hemophagocytic syndrome and pancytopenia, with fever, hepatosplenomegaly, elevated serum ferritin and triglycerides, and blood smear showing P. vivax trophozoites and gametocytes. The patient recovered fully with antimalarial and supportive treatment, highlighting the rare but serious manifestation of P. vivax malaria in pregnancy.

articles.headline: P. vivax accounts for 94.8% of Indonesian recurrent malaria cases
articles.title_original: Postmalaria elimination: Overview of recurrent malaria cases in Riau Province, Indonesia.
articles.authors: Lesmana SD, Afandi D, Rosdiana D, Murtono H, Azzuhra MN, Panjaitan GR
articles.details: A retrospective analysis of 232 recurrent malaria cases from 4,622 total cases in Riau Province, Indonesia (2019-2022) found 94.83% were P. vivax and 4.74% P. falciparum, all occurring >28 days after initial diagnosis. Most cases (69%) were diagnosed via rapid diagnostic test, primarily affecting males aged 19-59 years, with 73.45% lacking complete post-treatment evaluation.

articles.headline: Malaria in children associates with elevated inflammatory markers and nutritional deficiencies
articles.title_original: Analysis of the relationship between malaria and inflammatory biomarker levels in children in rural Iraq: Impact of nutritional deficiencies, anemia, and hematological disorders.
articles.authors: Al-Sultany AK, Al-Mamoori ZZ, Kareem MN, Mohsein OA
articles.details: This case-control study compared 150 malaria-infected children to 50 controls (ages 5-8) in rural Iraq. Malaria patients had significantly elevated inflammatory markers (CRP 18.4 vs 5.2 mg/L; IL-6 10.2 vs 3.1 pg/mL; TNF-α 12.5 vs 4.0 pg/mL; all p<0.001), anemia (70% vs 20%), lower hemoglobin (9.8 vs 12.5 g/dL), iron deficiency, elevated ferritin, malnutrition (65% vs 15%), and reduced zinc and vitamin D levels.

articles.headline: Plasmodium detected in cattle but not humans in Indonesia survey
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium spp. in humans and cattle: Implications for zoonotic malaria transmission in Indonesia.
articles.authors: Hasyim H, Jassey B, Yudhastuti R, Misnaniarti M, Liberty IA, Sunarsih E, Priyanto L, Dalilah D, Yusri Y, Taufiq FH, Maharani FE, Hakim L, Herlinda S
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 41 human participants and 43 cattle in Indonesia (March-July 2024) used PCR to detect Plasmodium spp. All human samples tested negative (0%, 95% CI 0.0%-8.5%), while one cattle sample was positive (2.33%, 95% CI 0.06%-12.0%). Although no zoonotic transmission to humans was observed, the findings suggest cattle may serve as parasite reservoirs and warrant integration of animal surveillance into malaria control programs.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine treatment failure cases in imported African malaria
articles.title_original: [Successful Treatment of Two Cases of Plasmodium falciparum Malaria that Developed Recrudescence after Artemether-Lumefantrine Therapy].
articles.authors: Arslan M, Berberoğlu U
articles.details: Two case reports document P. falciparum malaria imported from Africa (Gabon and Uganda) that developed recrudescence after artemether-lumefantrine (AL) therapy. Both patients showed initial improvement after 6 doses of AL but returned with high fever and parasitemia. One patient eventually cleared parasites with another 6 doses of AL; the other cleared with non-ACT antimalarials (quinine and doxycycline). These cases illustrate the risk of treatment failure with AL even when initial parasitemia appears cleared.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria: pathophysiology, clinical features, and post-discharge care gaps
articles.title_original: Cerebral malaria.
articles.authors: Postels DG, Katangwe-Chirwa T
articles.details: A comprehensive review of cerebral malaria (defined as coma in someone with P. falciparum parasitemia) documents that approximately one in six children with cerebral malaria die, with many survivors experiencing neurologic, cognitive, or behavioral sequelae. Acute seizures are common and increased number of seizures during illness predicts greater likelihood of adverse outcomes. The review examines epidemiology, clinical features, diagnostic approaches, evidence-based clinical care pathways, and persistent post-discharge care gaps in diagnosis and management of sequelae.

articles.headline: Biosynthesized silver nanoparticles show larvicidal potential against malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Eco-Friendly Synthesis of Silver Nanoparticles and Its Larvicidal Property Against Malaria, Filariasis, and Dengue Vectors.
articles.authors: Raguvaran K, Kalpana M, Devapriya P, Kalaivani S, Angelina M, Krismastuti FSH, Rasool A, Maheswaran R
articles.details: Green synthesis of silver nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) from Aerva lanata flower extract was tested for larvicidal efficacy against three mosquito species: Cx. quinquefasciatus (LC50 6.501 ppm), An. stephensi (LC50 20.875 ppm), and Ae. aegypti (LC50 22.649 ppm). Biosafety testing showed minimal toxicity to Artemia salina. The study demonstrates that Ag-NP-induced oxidative stress via antioxidant enzyme modulation is the likely mechanism of larvicidal activity, suggesting potential as an eco-friendly alternative to synthetic pesticides.

articles.headline: Immunoinformatics-designed multi-epitope blood-stage malaria vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Development of a multi-epitope vaccine candidate targeting blood-stage of malaria through immunoinformatics approach.
articles.authors: Mandal S, Khushi, Chanu WP, Srivastava R, Patgiri SJ, Natarajaseenivasan K
articles.details: An in silico study designed a multi-epitope vaccine candidate targeting four P. falciparum blood-stage proteins (PfPHB1, PfPHB2, PfHSP70, PfGARP) using immunoinformatics tools. The final vaccine construct comprises 570 amino acid residues including 12 linear B-cell epitopes, 11 CTL epitopes, and 7 helper T-lymphocyte epitopes. Molecular docking showed strong binding to TLR5, predicted global population coverage of 95.04%, and immune simulation predicted a robust and comprehensive immune response.

articles.headline: Plasmodium simium genomic evolution reveals ongoing zoonotic transmission in Brazil
articles.title_original: Plasmodium simium: birth and evolution of a zoonotic malaria parasite species.
articles.authors: de Albuquerque NRM, Ladeia WA, Scalsky RJ, Dwivedi A, Stabler TC, Rodrigues PT, de Oliveira TC, Silva JC, Ferreira MU
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of 31 P. simium isolates and 1,086 P. vivax isolates revealed that P. simium is a discrete lineage likely originating from P. vivax in the Amazon Basin of Brazil. P. simium displays half the nucleotide diversity of P. vivax from Latin America, consistent with recent origin. Pairs of P. simium isolates from monkeys and humans were identified as closely related as meiotic half-siblings, indicating ongoing zoonotic transmission. P. simium currently causes most or possibly all malarial infections usually attributed to P. vivax in the Serra do Mar region.

articles.headline: Pf8 dataset: 33,000 P. falciparum genomes for drug-resistance and diagnostic research
articles.title_original: Pf8: an open dataset of Plasmodium falciparum genome variation in 33,325 worldwide samples.
articles.authors: Abdel Hamid MM, Abdelraheem MH, Acheampong DO, Adam I, Aide P, Ajibaye O, Ali M, Almagro-Garcia J, Amambua-Ngwa A, Amenga-Etego L, Aniebo I, Aninagyei E, Ansah F, Apinjoh TO, Ariani CV, Auburn S, Awandare GA, Balmer A, Bejon P, Boene S, Bwire G, Candrinho B, Chidimatembue A, Chindavongsa K, Comiche K, Conway D, Dara A, Diakite M, Djimde A, Dondorp A, Doumbia S, Drury E, Fanello CA, Ferdig M, Figueroa K, Gamboa D, Golassa L, Gonçalves S, Guindo MDA, Hamaluba M, Hanboonkunupakarn B, Howe K, Hussien M, Imwong M, Ishengoma D, Jeans J, Kabaghe A, Kamuhabwa A, Kindermans JM, Konate DS, Kwiatkowski DP, Lee C, Lee SK, Lee SJ, Ley B, Llanos-Cuentas A, Marfurt J, Matambisso G, Maude RR, Maude RJ, Mayor A, Mayxay M, Maïga-Ascofaré O, McCann RS, Miles A, Miotto O, Mohamed AO, Morang'a CM, Murie K, Ngasala BE, Nguyen TN, Nolasco O, Nosten F, Noviyanti R, O'Connor Í, Oboh M, Ochola-Oyier LI, Olufunke Falade C, Olukosi A, Olumide A, Olusola FI, Onyamboko MA, Oriero EC, Oyibo WA, Pannebaker D, Pearson RD, Phiri K, van der Pluijm RW, Price RN, Quang HH, Rajkumar Devaraju V, Randrianarivelojosia M, Ranford-Cartwright L, Rayner JC, Rovira-Vallbona E, Rowlands K, Ruano-Rubio V, Sanchez JF, Saúte F, Shettima S, da Silva C, Simpson VJ, Suddaby S, Takken W, Thu AM, Toure M, Unlu E, Valdivia HO, van Vugt M, Waithira N, Wellems T, Wendler J, White N, Wuendrich Ogidan R
articles.details: The Pf8 data resource from MalariaGEN provides open access to whole-genome variation data from 33,000+ P. falciparum samples across 99 studies and 122 locations over 50+ years. The dataset includes genotypes at over 12 million genomic positions and copy-number variation (CNV) calls in key drug-resistance genes (gch1, crt, mdr1, plasmepsin2/3), as well as deletions in hrp2 and hrp3 (associated with rapid diagnostic test failures). The resource includes interactive web applications, an updated Python package, and example analyses demonstrating patterns of drug-resistance mutations across populations.

articles.headline: Historical profile: Giovanni Battista Grassi's contributions to malaria parasitology
articles.title_original: Giovanni Battista Grassi (1854-1925): a forgotten Italian scholar and his fundamental studies on malaria.
articles.authors: Martini M, Orsini D
articles.details: A historical review of Giovanni Battista Grassi (1854-1925), an Italian physician, zoologist, and entomologist who was a founder of Italian malariology. The article traces the historical evolution from Laveran's 1889 discovery that malaria is caused by Plasmodium protozoans (not bacteria) to Grassi's landmark 1898 studies that identified the vector of human malaria. This review contextualizes Grassi's scientific contributions within the development of modern malaria research.

articles.headline: P. cynomolgi-P. vivax co-infection highlights diagnostic challenges in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Molecular and Microscopic Challenges in Detecting Plasmodium cynomolgi Co-Infections with Plasmodium vivax: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Yaacob MA, Radin Hisam RS, Parina Ismail N, Md Yusuf NA, Rubio Muñoz JM, Hashim S, Zhueng TJ
articles.details: A case report documents a 32-year-old male initially diagnosed with P. vivax by microscopy and qPCR, but subsequent analysis revealed a mixed-species co-infection with P. vivax and the zoonotic P. cynomolgi. Real-time PCR and sequencing confirmed the co-infection. The case highlights diagnostic limitations due to high genetic similarity between P. cynomolgi and P. vivax, leading to cross-reactivity and misdiagnosis. Improved molecular diagnostics and epidemiological studies are needed to assess P. cynomolgi's emerging public health threat in Southeast Asia.

articles.headline: Brucea javanica extracts show additive antimalarial effects with artesunate and chloroquine
articles.title_original: Growth Inhibition and Additive Effect to Antimalarial Drugs of Brucea javanica Extracts on Asexual Blood-Stage Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Kangwanrangsan N, Niramolyanun G, Praikongkatham C, Chantree P, Martviset P, Pankao V
articles.details: In vitro testing of Brucea javanica plant extracts against asexual blood-stage P. falciparum showed potent inhibition with IC50 values of 0.41 µg/mL (root extract) and 0.26 µg/mL (fruit extract). Both extracts interrupted parasite development at the ring stage, inducing nuclear clumping and pyknotic cell death without hemolysis of red blood cells. Root and fruit extracts demonstrated additive effects when combined with artesunate and chloroquine, significantly reducing IC90 levels for parasite inhibition.

articles.headline: Chlorfenapyr-treated nets reduce malaria prevalence more than pyrethroid nets in Africa
articles.title_original: Effectiveness and Efficacy of Long-Lasting Insecticidal Nets for Malaria Control in Africa: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.
articles.authors: Demissie DB, Fetensa G, Desta T, Tiyare FT
articles.journal: Int J Environ Res Public Health
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 randomized controlled trials involving 21,916 households compared long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs) with different insecticide treatments. Chlorfenapyr-treated nets achieved 25.96 malaria cases per 100 children versus 32.38 for piperonyl butoxide and 41.60 for pyrethroid-only nets, representing a 10% reduction in infection risk with chlorfenapyr. Chlorfenapyr nets also showed greater entomological efficacy, reducing mean vector density by 23% compared to 7% with pyrethroid-only nets.

articles.headline: Immunoinformatics-designed VSA-based multiepitope malaria vaccine with high population coverage
articles.title_original: Search continues: Exploring immunoinformatics platforms for designing an effective multiepitope malaria vaccine candidate.
articles.authors: Osuji C, Etuk-Udo G
articles.details: A structural bioinformatics study designed a multiepitope subunit vaccine candidate targeting variant surface antigens (VSAs) of P. falciparum expressed across different parasite life-cycle stages. From an initial pool of 133 epitopes, 30 vaccine-fit epitopes were selected and assembled into a 570 amino acid construct comprising 12 linear B-cell epitopes, 11 CTL epitopes, and 7 helper T-lymphocyte epitopes. The vaccine predicted global population coverage of 95.04%, favorable antigenic properties, and robust simulated immune responses, demonstrating the potential of immunoinformatics-aided design in accelerating malaria vaccine development.

articles.headline: Seven P. vivax erythrocyte-binding proteins interact with Basigin receptor
articles.title_original: Majority of the erythrocyte binding proteins of the Pvfam "a" family of Plasmodium vivax interact with Basigin to assist parasite entry into the host cell.
articles.authors: Tripathi M, Santoshi M, Sharma YD, Rathore S
articles.details: Using solid-phase binding assays and surface plasmon resonance, researchers identified that 7 of 10 Pvfam-a family proteins from P. vivax (PvTRAg, PvTRAg33.5, PvTRAg35.2, PvTRAg34, PvTRAg36, PvTRAg38, PvTRAg69.4) interact with the erythrocyte receptor Basigin. Each protein individually inhibited parasite growth in culture, with combinations showing higher growth inhibition, suggesting these multiple ligand-receptor interactions are critical for parasite invasion and survival.

articles.headline: CD8+ T cells drive tubular kidney damage in malaria-induced acute kidney injury
articles.title_original: CD8(+) T cells promote tubule-interstitial damage in malaria-induced acute kidney injury.
articles.authors: Teixeira DE, Alves SADS, Pinheiro AS, Silva LS, Silva-Aguiar RP, Peruchetti DB, Pádua TA, Souza MC, Caruso-Neves C, Pinheiro AAS
articles.details: Using mouse models with adoptive T-cell transfer and selective CD8+ T-cell depletion, researchers demonstrated that CD8+ T cells specifically cause tubule-interstitial kidney damage in malaria-infected animals. Treatment with FTY720, an immunomodulatory drug, prevented proteinuria development and reduced urinary γ-glutamyl transferase (γ-GT), a marker of tubular injury, indicating CD8+ T cells are a therapeutic target for malaria-induced acute kidney injury.

articles.headline: Machine learning automates counting of P. falciparum oocysts in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: OocystMeter, a machine-learning algorithm to count and measure Plasmodium oocysts, reveals clustering patterns in the Anopheles midgut.
articles.authors: Peng D, Marrogi E, Nelson E, Liu Q, Rinvee TA, de Vries LE, Thornburg K, Singh N, Shaw WR, Catteruccia F
articles.details: OocystMeter, a Mask R-CNN model fine-tuned on 11,178 expert-annotated P. falciparum oocysts from Anopheles gambiae midguts, achieved segmentation accuracy comparable to human parasitologists (Spearman ρ=0.998 for counts, 0.978 for size). Spatial analysis revealed oocysts clustered non-randomly independent of midgut anatomy, particularly in infections with <75 oocysts/midgut, accelerating research on malaria's mosquito stage.

articles.headline: mRNA vaccine with blood-stage antigens protects against lethal P. yoelii
articles.title_original: An mRNA Vaccine Expressing Blood-Stage Malaria Antigens Induces Complete Protection Against Lethal Plasmodium yoelii.
articles.authors: Ott AC, Loll PJ, Burns JM Jr
articles.details: In a P. yoelii mouse model, two mRNA vaccines encoding PyMSP119 fused to PyMSP8 (one secreted, one membrane-bound) were tested against lethal challenge. The secreted variant (PyMSP1/8-sec) induced higher antibody titers than recombinant vaccine and 100% survival post-challenge with 50% showing no detectable parasitemia (<0.01%); the membrane-bound variant provided superior protection with more animals showing undetectable parasitemia.

articles.headline: Mathematical model shows vaccination strengthens malaria control in endemic regions
articles.title_original: Vaccination and combined optimal control measures for malaria prevention and spread mitigation.
articles.authors: Eme KA, Kamrujjaman M, Alam M, Ali MA
articles.details: A deterministic transmission model analyzed malaria control strategies including vaccination, mosquito nets, and insecticide spraying. Sensitivity analysis on the basic reproduction number (R0) identified key intervention parameters. Findings indicate that while current vaccines are not 100% effective, vaccination could substantially reduce transmission when combined with existing preventive measures, emphasizing the need for comprehensive multi-strategy approaches.

articles.headline: Ivermectin mass drug administration could prevent substantial malaria in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Wonder drugs and where to use them: a forecast of ivermectin's impact on malaria in Africa.
articles.authors: Greenhalgh S, Roman A, Provencher A
articles.details: A mathematical model using transmission data from 41 sub-Saharan African countries evaluated mass drug administration (MDA) of ivermectin at 12.5%, 25%, and 50% population coverage annually. At 12.5% coverage, ivermectin averted 248.7 malaria incidences per 1000 people and saved 5.4 disability-adjusted life years; at 50%, it averted 288.7 incidences and saved 6.3 DALYs. The intervention would be cost-effective in the majority of SSA countries.

articles.headline: P. vivax circumsporozoite protein shows extensive repeat region diversity in India
articles.title_original: Evolution of tandem repeats in putative circumsporozoite protein to enhance its function: A recent and exclusive event in Plasmodium vivax in India.
articles.authors: Dash M, Pande V, Das A, Sinha A
articles.details: Sequencing the putative circumsporozoite protein gene (PvpuCSP) in 71 P. vivax isolates from endemic regions of India revealed a highly conserved N- and C-terminal region (~80% of gene) but extensive diversity in the repeat region (1.8–2.2 kb), identifying 37 different alleles. Episodic diversifying selection in the repeat region drives amino acid diversity, making PvpuCSP a potential genetic marker for estimating parasite diversity and infection complexity.

articles.headline: Acridone T111 targets blood, liver, and mosquito stages of malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Potent Acridone Antimalarial against All Three Life Stages of Plasmodium.
articles.authors: Kancharla P, Dodean RA, Li Y, Zhang X, Kelly S, Charlton J, Binauhan A, Garcia L, Caridha D, Madejczyk MS, Jin X, Dennis WE, Kudyba K, McEnearney S, Lee PJ, Blount C, DeLuca J, Vuong C, Pannone K, Dinh HT, Mdaki K, Leed S, Martin ML, Pybus BS, Chimong A, Cui L, Vega-Rodriguez J, Okitwi M, Kreutzfeld O, Docan C, Green C, Liu Y, Agulay A, Mirsalis J, Farrell SN, McFadden GI, Goodman CD, Probst AS, Nilsen A, Catteruccia F, Rosenthal PJ, Riscoe M, Cooper R, Roth A, Kelly J
articles.details: The acridone compound T111 demonstrated potent activity across all Plasmodium life stages: oral single-dose cure of blood-stage parasites in rodent models, inhibition of sexual parasites, activity against relapsing parasites in primate liver cells, and prevention of parasite development in mosquitoes. T111 showed synergy with tafenoquine and targets the mitochondrial electron transport chain via a mechanism distinct from other antimalarials, with favorable toxicology in rats.

articles.headline: Generative machine learning infers malaria vector evolutionary history in West Africa
articles.title_original: On the use of generative models for evolutionary inference of malaria vectors from genomic data.
articles.authors: Eneli AA, Siu PC, Perez MF, Burt A, Fumagalli M, Mathieson S
articles.details: Using unsupervised machine learning and generative deep learning on Anopheles gambiae complex genomic data, researchers analyzed population structure across sub-Saharan Africa and inferred the joint evolutionary history of populations from Guinea and Burkina Faso. A migration model outperformed non-migration models and exceeded earlier summary-statistics approaches in capturing population genetic differentiation, demonstrating machine learning's value for understanding vector evolution and informing control strategies.

articles.headline: Intensive malaria control in Myanmar reduces transmission 97% and shifts parasite genetics
articles.title_original: Impact of intensive control on malaria population genomics in Eastern Myanmar.
articles.authors: Li X, Arya G, Thu A, Landier J, Parker D, Delmas G, Reyes A, Lwin K, Sriprawat K, Nosten F, Anderson T
articles.details: Genome sequencing of 2270 P. falciparum infections from 283 malaria posts over 58 months (2015–2020) in Kayin State revealed that rapid detection/treatment and mass drug administration reduced transmission by 97%. Parasite effective population size declined; 1726 single-genotype infections with 166 unique genomes emerged; 9 parasite families comprised 62.5% of all infections. By 2020, a single predominant genotype carrying kelch13-R561H resistance mutation dominated, demonstrating population control via founder effects from mass drug administration.

articles.headline: Severe malaria in pregnancy causes massive subamniotic hemorrhage and preterm death
articles.title_original: A rare presentation of massive subamniotic hemorrhage in a pregnant woman with severe malaria: A case report.
articles.authors: Tsegaye MA, Abchale AB, Elssa MW, Mekuria KN, Biza NK, Gemechu FA, Tesfay RH
articles.details: A 28-year-old pregnant woman with severe P. falciparum infection developed a placental hematoma with massive subamniotic bleeding (approximately 800 mL of lysed blood), resulting in preterm delivery. While the mother recovered with minimal postpartum hemorrhage, the neonate died in the ICU from complications of prematurity. This case highlights the severe placental and fetal complications of malaria in pregnancy.

articles.headline: Malaria dysregulates plasma amino acids and micronutrients in Nigerian population
articles.title_original: Malaria-induced dysregulation of selected micronutrients (Ca, Mg, Na, K, Zn, Fe, Co) and plasma free amino acids; implications for pathogenesis and host immunity in a Nigerian population.
articles.authors: Ekeanyanwu CR, Chikezie PC, Ekeanyanwu CL
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 200 participants (malarious and healthy controls) in Nigeria measured plasma amino acids by HPLC and micronutrients (Ca, Mg, Na, K, Zn, Fe, Co) by ICP-MS. Malaria infection caused significant alterations in most amino acids (p<0.01), with essential branched-chain amino acids (leucine, valine) and immune-related amino acids (arginine, tyrosine, tryptophan, glutamine, methionine, cysteine) depleted; malaria patients showed significant deficiencies in calcium, sodium, zinc, and iron (p<0.01). Results suggest metabolic dysregulation may contribute to disease severity.

articles.headline: TRAP protein glycosylation critical for mosquito-stage parasite development
articles.title_original: Glycosylation of Plasmodium falciparum TRAP is essential for infection of mosquito salivary glands.
articles.authors: Gupta P, Rezakhani N, Pazzagli L, Patel H, Zanghi G, Kamil M, Watson A, Vigdorovich V, Camargo N, Knutson E, Sather DN, Vaughan AM, Swearingen KE
articles.details: Researchers created Pf TRAP glyco-null mutants (T256A and W250F) using CRISPR/Cas9 site-directed mutagenesis to study O-fucosylation and C-mannosylation of the thrombospondin repeat domain. Glycosylation-deficient parasites showed major fitness defects comparable to knockout, with inability to colonize mosquito salivary glands, impaired gliding motility, and reduced ability to invade hepatocytes. Protein analysis revealed significantly reduced Pf TRAP abundance despite normal transcript levels. Authors conclude glycosylation of TRAP's TSR is critical for proper expression and function in the mosquito stage.

articles.headline: Graphene SPR biosensor simulated for stage-specific malaria detection
articles.title_original: Design and simulation of a graphene-integrated SPR biosensor for malaria detection.
articles.authors: Tene T, Arias Arias F, Paredes-Páliz KI, González García JC, Bonilla García N, Vacacela Gomez C
articles.details: Researchers designed and optimized surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor configurations incorporating graphene and silicon nitride using transfer matrix method simulation to differentiate malaria parasite stages (ring, trophozoite, schizont). Sys3 biosensor achieved sensitivities of 353.14, 291.14, and 263.26°/RIU across the three stages, while Sys4 achieved 315.71, 294.81, and 268.65°/RIU, respectively, both exceeding comparable SPR platforms. Although limited to simulation, authors conclude the proposed configurations demonstrate potential for label-free, stage-specific malaria diagnostics supporting future point-of-care applications.

articles.headline: Novel Kelch13 A675T and widespread sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance in Burundi
articles.title_original: A Novel Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 A675T Mutation and High Levels of Chloroquine and Sulfadoxine-Pyrimethamine Resistance in Burundi.
articles.authors: Kayode T, Niyukuri D, Holzschuh A, Da Silva G, Huwe T, Lerch A, Nyandwi J, Koepfli C
articles.details: Researchers sequenced 157 P. falciparum isolates from Burundi for 10 drug-resistance markers using Illumina and Nanopore sequencing. A novel pfkelch13 A675T mutation was identified; no validated artemisinin-resistant mutations were detected. Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance was widespread (82.3% quintuple haplotype, 12.3% sextuple haplotype). The pfcrt IET triple mutant haplotype (94.9%) and pfmdr1 N-F-D (57.9%) indicate sustained chloroquine resistance. Authors conclude substantial SP resistance threatens IPTp and SMC strategies, and highlight the need for strengthened surveillance to address emerging artemisinin and partner drug resistance.

articles.headline: Kelch13 R622I prevalence rising with alarming RDT negativity in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Artemisinin resistant kelch13 R622I and RDT negativity approaching predominance in northern Ethiopia and emerging C580Y of African origin threaten falciparum malaria control.
articles.authors: Zeleke AJ, Fola AA, Tollefson GA, Niaré K, Leonetti A, Taropawala O, Marglous J, Crudale R, Brhane BG, Assefa A, Kiyuka P, Parr JB, Hailu A, Aemero M, Bailey JA
articles.details: Among 903 P. falciparum malaria cases in northwest Ethiopia, kelch13 R622I prevalence reached 44.3%, significantly higher in Gondar Zuria (52%) versus Tach Armachiho (40%; p<0.001). HRP2-based RDT negativity was higher in R622I mutants versus wild-type (48.3% vs 30.7%; p<0.001), and 98.2% of R622I samples carried the multidrug resistance protein 1 NFD haplotype. Researchers detected k13 C580Y in 2 patients (0.4%), the first report in the Horn of Africa with evidence of local de novo emergence. Authors conclude increasing ART-R mutation prevalence coupled with RDT negativity will further challenge malaria control and elimination efforts.

articles.headline: PfRad51 and translesion polymerases enable differential DNA repair genome-wide
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum DNA repair dynamics reveal unique roles for TLS polymerases and PfRad51 in genome diversification.
articles.authors: Vishwanatha A, Zhang X, Liu YJ, Leung A, Herring M, Chan A, Calhoun S, Deitsch K, Kirkman L
articles.details: Researchers created P. falciparum knockout lines for DNA repair enzymes PfRad51, PfPolζ, and PfRev1 using CRISPR/Cas9 to study genome maintenance mechanisms. Irradiation hypersensitivity varied across the cell cycle for translesion synthesis polymerase knockouts but remained uniform across the erythrocytic cycle for PfRad51Δ. PfRad51 was essential for homologous recombination-mediated repair in the conserved core genome but was dispensable for Rad51-independent homology-directed repair in subtelomeric regions. Authors propose that differential DNA damage response mechanisms maintain the distinction between hypervariable subtelomeric regions and conserved core genome.

articles.headline: Low artemisinin resistance markers in Brazilian P. falciparum despite drug pressure
articles.title_original: Tracking Drug Resistance in Plasmodium falciparum: Genetic Diversity of Key Resistance Markers in Brazilian Malaria Hotspots.
articles.authors: de Abreu-Fernandes R, de Queiroz LT, Almeida-de-Oliveira NK, de Lavigne Mello AR, de Aguiar Barros J, Pratt-Riccio LR, Melo GC, Brasil P, Daniel-Ribeiro CT, Menard D, Ferreira-da-Cruz MF
articles.details: Researchers analyzed 141 P. falciparum samples collected 2013-2023 from Brazil using PCR and DNA sequencing for resistance-associated polymorphisms in pfcrt, pfmdr1, and pfk13. No pfk13 mutations linked to artemisinin partial resistance were detected. In pfcrt, 50% carried SVMNTMCGI haplotype with no C350R mutations; in pfmdr1, NYCDY haplotype was dominant (70%) with low N86Y (4%) and no Y184F. Authors conclude the absence of pfk13-resistant mutations and low prevalence of key pfmdr1 markers support the continued efficacy of artemisinin-based combination therapies, though ongoing drug pressure warrants continuous genetic surveillance.

articles.headline: Optimal MDA and antimalarial strategies depend on drug resistance mutation rates
articles.title_original: Optimizing MDA and antimalarial treatment in the presence of drug resistance for effective malaria control.
articles.authors: Nimpa MM, Teytsa HN, Mbang J, Wondji CS, Djidjou-Demasse R
articles.details: Researchers developed a compartmental mathematical model incorporating asymptomatic, paucisymptomatic, and clinical infection states with treated and untreated dynamics to evaluate mass drug administration (MDA) and antimalarial treatment strategies amid drug resistance. Global sensitivity analysis identified critical factors: MDA coverage, treatment access, mutation rates, and initial resistance prevalence. Optimal control strategies differed by mutation rate: at low mutation rates, combining antimalarials with MDA and gradual antimalarial reduction ensured sustainability; at high mutation rates, prioritizing broader MDA deployment while reducing antimalarial use minimized resistance development.

articles.headline: Ungulate Plasmodium represent novel species distinct from primate parasites
articles.title_original: Bringing to light unnoticed data on the genetic and host diversity of ungulate Plasmodium.
articles.authors: Makanga BK, Boundenga L, Bitome-Essono PY, Arnathau C, Rougeron V, Prugnolle F
articles.details: Researchers reanalyzed underappreciated data on Plasmodium parasites from African forest and savanna ungulates from three independent genomic studies. Phylogenetic analyses of genetic sequences identified Plasmodium forming a monophyletic group distinct from parasites infecting primates, rodents, and bats. Within this clade, two main subclades were identified: Cephalophus parasites clustered with previously described sequences, while parasites from Tragelaphus and Syncerus formed distinct lineages likely representing novel species. Authors conclude expanding knowledge of ungulate Plasmodium genetic diversity is crucial for understanding parasite evolution across vertebrate hosts.

articles.headline: CD81-targeted siRNA blocks parasite liver infection in mouse models
articles.title_original: Protection from malaria infection using liver-targeted siRNA.
articles.authors: Steel RWJ, Schepis A, Nguyen T, Milstein S, Yucius K, Tu HC, Fishilevich E, Haslett P, Kappe SHI
articles.details: Researchers used CD81-targeted, N-acetylgalactosamine-conjugated small interfering RNA to silence hepatic CD81 expression in vivo, targeting the critical sporozoite entry receptor on hepatocytes. In a mouse malaria model, CD81 siRNA blocked P. falciparum parasite infection of the liver in a dose-dependent manner and prevented subsequent blood-stage infection. In human liver-chimeric mice, the approach similarly reduced P. falciparum infection. Authors conclude RNAi-based silencing of host CD81 represents a promising host-based approach for malaria prevention.

articles.headline: IRS reduced malaria transmission rate by 10% in Nigeria pilot
articles.title_original: Modelling the Effect of Indoor Residual Spraying on Malaria Transmission in Nassarawa State, North-central Nigeria.
articles.authors: Suleiman AG, Kareem L, Obi C
articles.details: Researchers used a compartmental SIRS model with ordinary differential equations to evaluate indoor residual spraying (IRS) impact on malaria transmission in Nassarawa State, Nigeria. Model parameters were estimated from malaria surveillance data collected 2012-2013 from intervention and control Local Government Areas using Euler's method. The estimated transmission rate was lower in the IRS intervention LGA compared to control (0.95 vs 1.05), corresponding to a 10% reduction in transmission over two years. Authors attribute the modest decrease to spray implementation challenges and recommend optimizing spray frequency and data collection strategies for future IRS interventions.

articles.headline: Ultrasensitive RDT detects more asymptomatic malaria in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Comparison of diagnostic accuracy of ultra-sensitive and conventional rapid diagnostic tests for malaria in asymptomatic pregnant women at a tertiary hospital in North-Central Nigeria.
articles.authors: Anenga UM, Swende TZ, Hembah-Hilekaan SK
articles.details: In a cross-sectional study of 107 asymptomatic pregnant women in Nigeria, researchers compared ultrasensitive RDT (us-RDT) versus conventional RDT (co-RDT) for P. falciparum detection using microscopy as reference standard. Overall malaria prevalence was 14% by microscopy, 12.1% by us-RDT, and 2.8% by co-RDT. Ultrasensitive RDT showed significantly superior sensitivity (66.7% vs 13.3%), positive predictive value (83.3% vs 50%), negative predictive value (94.7% vs 87.4%), and diagnostic accuracy (93.5% vs 86.0%), with comparable specificity (97.8%; p=0.002). Authors conclude ultrasensitive RDTs are more effective for detecting P. falciparum in asymptomatic pregnant women and may improve clinical decision-making.

articles.headline: Imported malaria from Africa dominates Chengdu cases 2016-2023
articles.title_original: [Epidemiological characteristics of imported malaria cases in Chengdu City from 2016 to 2023].
articles.authors: Feng L, Wang L, Jiang X, Tan K, Li P
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Analysis of 463 imported malaria cases reported in Chengdu City 2016-2023 found P. falciparum was predominant (71.27%, 330/463). Cases returned primarily from Africa (94.17%, 436/463), with 64.15% reported in Jinjiang District and male-to-female ratio of 21:1 concentrated in migrant laborers aged 20-50 years. Only 35.85% sought healthcare on day of disease onset; overall diagnostic accuracy at initial diagnosis was 74.78% (341/456 cases). County-level health institutions achieved highest diagnostic accuracy (91.43%), substantially exceeding township centers (11.11%) and village clinics (0%). Authors recommend strengthened health education for workers going to Africa and improved diagnostic capacity in grassroots institutions.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine acceptance among Nigerian secondary school adolescents
articles.title_original: Willingness of secondary school adolescents to get vaccinated against Malaria: a cross-sectional study in Enugu, South-East Nigeria.
articles.authors: Nduagubam OC, Ossai EN, Chinawa AT, Onukwuli VO, Uwaezuoke NA, Okafor CN, Chinawa JM
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 496 secondary school students in Enugu, Nigeria (June-Sept 2023). 68.1% willing to receive malaria vaccine. Students aged <15 years were 2.2-fold more likely to accept vaccination compared with those ≥15 years (AOR=2.2, 95%CI: 1.1-4.7); males were 0.6-fold less likely than females (AOR=0.6, 95%CI: 0.4-0.9); those with good vaccine knowledge were 2.2-fold more likely to accept (AOR=2.2, 95%CI: 1.4-3.4). Only 32.1% possessed good knowledge of malaria vaccination.

articles.headline: Propolis phytochemicals inhibit P. falciparum lactate dehydrogenase
articles.title_original: In vitro and in silico investigations of Propolis-derived phytochemicals as potential inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Khan DA, Hasan MN, Boonhok R, Sungkanu S, Singhaboot Y, Shorna AA, Hasan A, Chotivanich K, Wilairatana P, Siyadatpanah A, Norouzi R, Sama-Ae I, Mitsuwan W, Paul AK, de Lourdes Pereira M, Sundar SS, Mahboob T, Wiart C, Labana RV, Chimplee S, Nissapatorn V
articles.details: In vitro and in silico evaluation of propolis extracts against P. falciparum NF54. Kermanshah propolis exhibited highest anti-Plasmodium activity (IC50=6.69 ± 1.44 μg/mL). Tectochrysin and galangin showed strong binding affinity to PfLDH (-7.8 and -7.5 kcal/mol respectively), exceeding standard antimalarials chloroquine and quinine. Molecular dynamics simulations confirmed stable protein-ligand interactions, supporting propolis compounds as novel PfLDH inhibitors warranting in vivo validation.

articles.headline: Whole-genome sequencing reveals parasite decline during malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Malaria parasite population genomics during an elimination program in Eastern Myanmar.
articles.authors: Li X, Arya GA, Thu AM, Landier J, Parker DM, Delmas G, Reyes A, Lwin KM, Sriprawat K, Nosten F, Anderson TJC
articles.details: Analysis of 2,270 P. falciparum genomes from 5,014 dried blood spots (Nov 2015-Aug 2020) from 413 malaria posts in Kayin State, Myanmar. Identity-by-descent analysis showed severe genetic depletion: 166 unique genomes among 1,726 single-genotype infections; 9 parasite families accounted for 62.5% of all parasites. Kelch13 diversity stable 2016-2019; by 2020 only kelch13-R561H predominated. Mass drug administration resulted in parasite founder effects, providing genomic evidence for MDA efficacy.

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection increases mosquito attraction via altered skin volatiles
articles.title_original: [Changes in murine skin odors following Plasmodium infections and their impact on mosquito attraction].
articles.authors: Li J, Li M, He B, Liu T, Zhu F, Zhang J, Xu W
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Experimental study with 20 female C57BL/6 mice (10 infected with 1×10⁶ P. yoelii, 10 controls). Parasitaemia reached 51.00±3.61% by day 6 (F=165.60, P<0.001); gametocytaemia 1.53±0.31% (F=44.58, P<0.001). Anopheles stephensi attraction to infected mice increased from 50.45% (day 0) to 66.84% (day 5). GC-MS identified elevated p-cresol, ethylbenzene, nonanal and decreased toluene, benzaldehyde, indole in infected mice; altered skin microbiota composition including increased Streptococcus and Rothia.

articles.headline: Faculty enrichment program strengthens malaria modeling capacity
articles.title_original: Building contextually-relevant training programs for scientific development: process and lessons learned in implementing two iterations of a faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling.
articles.authors: Onyango L, Ouédraogo-Ametchie G, Stahlfeld A, Holden TM, Richter R, Runge M, Toh KB, Agorinya I, Mensah B, Gerardin J
articles.details: Evaluation of a 4-month faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling at Northwestern University (2022-2023). Two cohorts of participants and instructors interviewed at baseline, midline, and endline. Participants valued growth in technical expertise, research skills, and communication ability with opportunities for knowledge transfer to home institutions. Challenges included cross-disciplinary learning and adapting to new teaching styles. Program adapted structures based on feedback to address emerging participant needs.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing identifies four Plasmodium species from dried blood spots
articles.title_original: [Establishment and preliminary evaluation of recombinase-aided isothermal nucleic acid amplification combined with nanopore sequencing for identification of Plasmodium species].
articles.authors: Lin W, Chen L, Zhang C, Wei H, Tang C, Wang R, Lin L, Lin M
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Development of recombinase-aided isothermal amplification (RAA) combined with nanopore sequencing for Plasmodium species identification from 89 dried blood spot samples. Using nested PCR as gold standard, RAA-nanopore sequencing achieved 92.00% sensitivity, 97.33% specificity, and 96.00% accuracy compared to RT-qPCR sensitivity 88.24%, specificity 97.32%, accuracy 95.00%. Successfully identified P. falciparum (23), P. malariae (6), P. vivax (6), P. ovale (13), and one mixed P. falciparum-P. malariae infection.

articles.headline: Malaria-malnutrition cycle perpetuates poverty in Indian tribal populations
articles.title_original: Malaria and malnutrition in tribal areas of India: implications for paediatric health.
articles.authors: Verma AK, Shrivastava S, Saxena N
articles.details: Mini-review discussing interconnection between malaria and undernutrition in tribal populations in India. Malaria causes anemia, fever, and potential mortality; frequent malaria and intestinal infections alter gut flora, exacerbating malnutrition through poor digestion and malabsorption. Authors argue integrated approach addressing both nutritional and malaria-specific needs is required for malaria elimination in tribal areas.

articles.headline: Plasmodium immune evasion mechanisms and implications for vaccine development
articles.title_original: [Progress of researches on mechanisms underlying immune escape of Plasmodium].
articles.authors: Wu Y, He J, Cao X, Li J, Shen S, Du Y, Tan C
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Review of parasite immune escape mechanisms including antigenic variation, epigenetic regulation, IgM antagonism, cGAS-STING signaling activation, splenic immune suppression, and molecular camouflage. Discussion addresses how these evasion strategies challenge vaccine and antimalarial agent development and informs malaria control strategy formulation.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention reduces non-malarial pediatric infections
articles.title_original: Indirect Benefits of Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention for Non-Malarial Pediatric Infections and Routine Antibiotic Use in Real-World Programmatic Settings: A Pre-Post Study Using Positive and Negative Controls.
articles.authors: Gebreegziabher EA, Ouattara M, Bountogo M, Coulibaly B, Boudo V, Ouedraogo T, Lebas E, Hu H, O'Brien KS, Hsiang MS, Glidden DV, Arnold BF, Lietman TM, Sié A, Oldenburg CE
articles.details: Pre-post study in rural Burkina Faso (51 health facilities) comparing non-malarial diagnoses and antibiotic prescriptions during sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine and amodiaquine SMC administration versus 3-week post-intervention. Absolute reductions in post-SMC period: pneumonia 0.7 per 1,000 person-weeks (95%CI: 0.3-1.0), diarrhea 0.2 (95%CI: 0.1-0.4), acute malnutrition 0.05 (95%CI: 0.001-0.09), antibiotic prescriptions 0.90 (95%CI: 0.4-1.4). Positive controls (malaria diagnoses, antimalarial prescriptions) decreased 3.7 and 3.6 per 1,000 person-weeks.

articles.headline: P. vivax causes cerebral malaria and multi-organ dysfunction in adult
articles.title_original: Cerebral Malaria and Multi-Organ Dysfunction Caused by Plasmodium vivax in an Adult: A case from Oman with a literature review.
articles.authors: Al Kindi SM, Chhetri S, Al Aamri ZZ, Dhuri SD, Al Balushi ZY, Khamis F
articles.details: Case report of 38-year-old previously healthy Pakistani male presenting in 2025 with cerebral malaria and multi-organ dysfunction syndrome caused by P. vivax (confirmed by peripheral blood smear and PCR). Treated with intravenous artesunate and supportive intensive care with full neurological recovery. Case represents first documented P. vivax cerebral malaria in Oman and highlights potential severity of P. vivax infection.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine as cornerstone for Sub-Saharan African malaria eradication
articles.title_original: Mitigation of Malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa through Vaccination: A Budding Road Map for Global Malaria Eradication.
articles.authors: Alum EU, Ainebyoona C, Egwu CO, Onohuean H, Ugwu OP, Uti DE, Alum BN, Echegu DA
articles.details: Narrative review of 2014-2024 literature on malaria control strategies in Sub-Saharan Africa. RTS,S/AS01 demonstrated 30-55% efficacy preventing malaria in children under five in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi; R21/Matrix-M showed up to 77% efficacy. Implementation challenges include limited funding, vaccine hesitancy, poor infrastructure, and community misconceptions. Integration with routine immunization, enhanced community engagement, and increased health system investment significantly improve vaccine acceptance and impact.

articles.headline: Advanced age associated with rheumatoid factor in hepatitis B carriers
articles.title_original: Prevalence of rheumatoid factor in asymptomatic chronic hepatitis B surface carriers in the context of endemic malaria: cross-sectional study at University Hospital of Cocody, Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.authors: Goran-Kouacou APV, Yéboah OR, Assi AUA, Séri YJ, Adou AH, N Guessan K, Dassé SR
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 120 asymptomatic chronic hepatitis B surface antigen carriers in Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire (2024). Rheumatoid factor positivity: 12.5% (95%CI: 6.54-18.46). Mean age 36.4±11.2 years, 60% male. Advanced age (≥30 years) independently associated with rheumatoid factor positivity (aOR: 3.32; 95%CI: 1.06-10.39; p=0.04). No significant associations with sex or reason for consultation.

articles.headline: Health education's role in China's malaria elimination
articles.title_original: [Application of health education during the progress towards malaria elimination in China: a review].
articles.authors: Zhou M, Lin K
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Review of community-based, school-based, and media health education during China's path to malaria elimination, achieved in 2021. Discusses how health education increased public awareness and prevention knowledge, and its importance in preventing re-establishment of imported malaria in the post-elimination phase.

articles.headline: Rapid diagnostic tests for P. vivax show variable sensitivity across parasitemia
articles.title_original: [Comparison of Rapid Diagnostic Tests for the Detection of Plasmodium vivax Malaria].
articles.authors: Ku B, Jung H, Shin HI, Lee MR, Ju JW, Lee HI
articles.details: Study compared four Pf/Pan rapid diagnostic test kits using PCR-confirmed positive samples from Korea. Sensitivity ranged 70.9–96.5%, with lower parasitemia samples showing wider variation (43.8–93.8%); specificity was 100% for all kits. Results provide information for selecting diagnostic kits for field-based presumptive malaria diagnosis.

articles.headline: Two chromosome-level reference genomes assembled for malaria mosquito A. darlingi
articles.title_original: Two chromosomal reference genome sequences for the malaria mosquito, Anopheles ( Nyssorhynchus) darlingi, Root, 1926 from French Guiana and Peru.
articles.authors: Gendrin M, Heu K, Moreno M, Gamboa D, Vinetz JM, Tong C, Conn JE, Johnson HF, Heaton H, Wagah MG, Collins JC, Krasheninnikova K, Pelan SE, Pointon DB, Torrance JW, Tracey A, Uliano-Silva M, Wood JMD, von Wyschetzki K, McCarthy SA, Lawniczak MKN, Neafsey DE, Makunin A
articles.details: Two genome assemblies (~180 Mb) from individual female Anopheles darlingi from French Guiana and Peru, each assembled into three chromosomal pseudomolecules plus X chromosome. Complete mitochondrial genomes (15.4 kb) also assembled; the two assemblies differ by two inversions in chromosome arm 2R.

articles.headline: Chloroquine-saffron chitosan nanocomposites show synergistic antimalarial activity in vitro
articles.title_original: In Vitro Antimalarial Activity of Chloroquine-Crocus Sativus Conjugated to Chitosan Nanocomposits against 3D7 and K1 Strains of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Khezri A, Nateghpour M, Motevali Haghi A, Elmi T, Rahimi Foroushani A, Shafii Ardestani M, Hanifian H
articles.details: Chloroquine and saffron extract conjugated to chitosan nanoparticles tested against sensitive and resistant P. falciparum strains. IC50 values for nanoparticle-chloroquine were 0.8 μg/ml (sensitive) and 1 μg/ml (resistant), compared to 0.3/5 μg/ml for chloroquine alone. Combination therapy showed synergistic effects (P<0.05).

articles.headline: Severe P. falciparum malaria with acute kidney injury responds to artesunate
articles.title_original: Managing Acute Kidney Injury in Severe Falciparum Malaria: Insights from a Challenging Case.
articles.authors: Putra DE, Kasman I, Boro AMB, Purnama A, Zulfikar E
articles.details: 22-year-old male from Indonesia with P. falciparum parasitemia 9.7% complicated by acute kidney injury (serum creatinine 3.52 mg/dL), pulmonary edema, jaundice, and thrombocytopenia. Treated with IV artesunate (6 days) followed by oral dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (3 days), primaquine, and hemodialysis. Patient recovered fully without sequelae.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium infections show distinct metabolic and immune dynamics in siskins
articles.title_original: Dynamics of resting metabolic rate and innate immune response in malaria-infected Eurasian siskins.
articles.authors: Erokhina M, Bushuev A, Platonova E, Khaitov V, Davydov A, Mukhin A
articles.details: Juvenile siskins infected with P. relictum (SGS1, northern species) or P. ashfordi (GRW2, tropical species) assessed for resting metabolic rate (RMR) and interleukin-6 (IL-6). SGS1 infection reduced both RMR and IL-6; GRW2 showed distinct RMR dynamics with initial IL-6 reduction. Study did not conclusively demonstrate that tropical malaria causes more severe effects.

articles.headline: Undiagnosed asymptomatic malaria in pregnancy causes substantial maternal and infant harm
articles.title_original: Detrimental infant and maternal outcomes of undiagnosed asymptomatic malaria in pregnancy.
articles.authors: Beeson JG, Opi DH
articles.details: In low-transmission areas, most pregnancy infections are not detected by standard diagnostic tests. New data shows these missed asymptomatic infections have substantial negative impacts for both mother and developing fetus.

articles.headline: Urban Bamako school children show 0–15.5% P. falciparum prevalence with spatial variation
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum infection in school-aged children in urban and peri-urban schools of Bamako, Mali.
articles.authors: Toure M, Kane F, Sanogo D, Keita S, Keita B, Aro AZ, Keita M, Konate D, Diawara SI, Yaro AS, Kone A, Magassa M, Kante S, Tangara CO, Tangara K, Coulibaly H, Cisse B, Thiam SM, Traore AS, Shaffer JG, Sogoba N, Diakite M, Doumbia S
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 200–230 children aged 6–13 per school across Bamako's four ecological strata. P. falciparum prevalence by RDT and microscopy ranged 0–15.5% between sites. Anemia increased infection odds 2.38-fold (OR=2.38, 95% CI 1.57–3.61); living along Niger River increased odds 3.37-fold (OR=3.37, 95% CI 2.05–7.45). Travel and fever not significantly associated.

articles.headline: Historical review corrects misattribution of Korea's malaria elimination status in 1979
articles.title_original: Misattribution of Korea's malaria elimination status in 1979.
articles.authors: Seol R, Kim Y
articles.details: Source-based historical review shows WHO declared Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK) eliminated malaria by 1979, but this was misattributed to Republic of Korea (ROK) in U.S. DoD documents. Misinterpretation persisted through decades of repeated, unverified citations in academic literature and online sources.

articles.headline: Indigofera-silver nanoparticles restore splenic function in P. chabaudi-infected mice
articles.title_original: Histopathologic effect and immunomodulatory of Indigofera oblongifolia with silver nanoparticles against splenic injury by Plasmodium chabaudi infection in mice.
articles.authors: Murshed M, Al-Tamimi J, Alzaylaee H, Ahmeda H, Al-Quraishy S
articles.details: Female C57BL/6 mice infected with P. chabaudi treated with Indigofera oblongifolia leaf extract (IOLE) with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) or chloroquine. IOLE AgNPs better restored splenic architecture, reduced inflammatory markers, and improved immune responses compared to chloroquine-treated and control groups, suggesting potential therapeutic approach for malaria-associated splenic dysfunction.

articles.headline: P. falciparum carbonic anhydrase η-class enzyme emerges as antimalarial drug target
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum η-carbonic anhydrase.
articles.authors: Supuran CT, Capasso C
articles.details: Review of P. falciparum carbonic anhydrase (PfCA), an η-class enzyme involved in parasite pyrimidine biosynthesis. Multiple in vitro inhibitors detected with low nanomolar IC50s; acetazolamide and an ureido-substituted benzenesulfonamide showed potent growth inhibition in P. falciparum-infected red blood cells. Structure and physiological role require further investigation for therapeutic development.

articles.headline: P. cynomolgi DHFR and DHPS mutations differ between human and macaque isolates
articles.title_original: Molecular analysis of DHFR and DHPS gene mutations in Plasmodium cynomolgi from humans and macaques in Southeast Asia.
articles.authors: Sangsri R, Jongkon N, Choowongkomon K, Malaivijitnond S, Day NPJ, Dondorp AM, Imwong M
articles.details: Analysis of DHFR and DHPS genes in nine human samples from Cambodia and 29 macaque samples from Thailand. Human samples showed no DHFR mutations and one DHPS mutation (V451I); macaque samples showed PcyDHFR mutations N44T and C49S with two DHPS haplotypes. Molecular docking indicated current antifolate drugs remain potentially effective against P. cynomolgi.

articles.headline: AI improves malaria RDT interpretation, especially faint positives
articles.title_original: Malaria RDT (mRDT) interpretation accuracy by frontline health workers compared to AI in Kano state, Nigeria.
articles.authors: Frade S, Cooper S, Smedinghoff S, Hattery D, Ruan Y, Isabelli P, Ravi N, McLaughlin M, Metz L, Finette B
articles.details: A comparative analysis examined malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) interpretation by frontline health workers (FHWs), trained expert reviewers, and AI-based computer vision algorithms in Kano, Nigeria. AI achieved a weighted F1 score of 96.4% compared to FHWs' 95.3%, with notably superior performance on faint-positive tests: AI identified 90.2% of 163 faint positives versus FHWs' 76.1%. The findings demonstrate AI's potential to enhance diagnostic accuracy and reduce misinterpretation in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Novel biosensors promise rapid, sensitive malaria detection
articles.title_original: Advances in malaria detection.
articles.authors: Ike BW, Kathiresan V, Miya L, Karpoormath R
articles.details: This review examines current and emerging diagnostic tools for malaria, highlighting limitations of conventional methods (treatment resistance, atypical symptomology, analytical sensitivity and specificity issues) and the promise of novel biosensor technology based on advanced nanotechnology. These emerging devices offer rapid, sensitive, and cost-effective alternatives requiring minimal technical expertise and are suitable for point-of-care use in remote, resource-limited settings in high-burden countries.

articles.headline: IgA-based mucosal immunity offers path to malaria eradication
articles.title_original: Targeting mucosal immunity in malaria control: the underexplored role of IgA.
articles.authors: Muwonge H, Ssewanyana I, Yeka A, Byakika-Kibwika P
articles.details: This review synthesizes emerging evidence on immunoglobulin A (IgA) responses in malaria immunity, showing IgA can block parasite entry, activate complement, and modulate inflammation. The authors discuss vaccine platforms—oral, nasal, or prime-boost strategies—that might harness IgA alongside IgG to prevent parasite establishment at mucosal or skin interfaces. Critical gaps remain in correlating IgA levels with clinical immunity, requiring specialized animal models and longitudinal human cohorts to establish IgA's protective efficacy.

articles.headline: Malaria burden declining globally with projected continued improvement
articles.title_original: Global, regional, and national burden of neglected tropical diseases and malaria, 1990-2021.
articles.authors: Tuergan T, Abulaiti A, Tulahong A, Zhang R, Shao Y, Aji T
articles.details: This Global Burden of Disease 2021 analysis examined malaria and neglected tropical disease burden from 1990-2021 across genders, age groups, and regions. The study found declining trends in both case numbers and age-standardized rates from 1990-2021, with linear regression and predictive modeling projecting continued decline through 2046. Decomposition analysis highlighted the positive impact of aging and epidemiological changes on burden reduction, though significant disparities remain across regions and sociodemographic development indices.

articles.headline: VK210 dominates P. vivax circumsporozoite variants; implications for vaccine
articles.title_original: Updated global distribution of Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein variants and their correlation with vector susceptibility: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Sampaio MP, Cerilo-Filho M, Amaral YAGD, Almeida MNG, Correa RI, Fernando DJV, Reis NF, Miceli LA, Silva JRS, Storti-Melo LM, Baptista ARS, Machado RLD
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 94 studies examined the global distribution of Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein (PvCSP) variants (VK210, VK247, P. vivax-like) and their association with Anopheles vector susceptibility. VK210 was the most prevalent variant globally. Of 94 Anopheles species analyzed, 23.4% were susceptible to PvCSP variants, with 100% susceptible to VK210 mono-infections, 63.6% to VK247, and 27.3% to mixed infections. These findings have important implications for vaccine development and regional malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: Priority-setting in malaria programs balances health needs and funding
articles.title_original: What are the priority-setting approaches for HIV/AIDS, TB and malaria programmes in Ghana? A qualitative perspective from key informants.
articles.authors: Aryeetey GC, Koduah A, Affram AA, Owusu R, Ruiz F, Vassall A, Nonvignon J
articles.details: This qualitative study of 10 key informants from Ghana's Global Fund-supported malaria, HIV, and TB programs identified four main priority-setting approaches: health needs identification, stakeholder participation, process transparency, and contextual factors. Priorities were determined through national health strategies, development partner input, and internally generated surveillance data. Main barriers included inadequate funding, requirement to align priorities with funders' mandates, and interruptions in planning processes. Public involvement in priority-setting was limited.

articles.headline: Expansion microscopy enables detailed analysis of malaria parasites in mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Mosquito Tissue Ultrastructure-Expansion Microscopy (MoTissU-ExM) enables ultrastructural and anatomical analysis of malaria parasites and their mosquito.
articles.authors: Liffner B, Silva TLAE, Vega-Rodriguez J, Absalon S
articles.details: This study developed Mosquito Tissue Ultrastructure-Expansion Microscopy (MoTissU-ExM), a novel technique that physically expands biological samples approximately 4.5-fold to overcome visualization limitations of conventional light microscopy. The method was applied to dissected mosquito salivary glands and midguts, preserving both host and parasite ultrastructure and enabling in situ visualization of oocysts and sporozoites. The authors provide a detailed protocol, discuss potential applications for studying malaria parasite biology, and address technical challenges and troubleshooting approaches.

articles.headline: Ellagic acid shows potent in vitro activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Effects of Ellagic Acid on Hemoglobin Ingestion, Transport, and Digestion in the Digestive Vacuole of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Thuduhena AC, Abu-Bakar N, Hamzah SN
articles.details: This in vitro study examined ellagic acid's antimalarial mechanism against P. falciparum 3D7 (chloroquine-sensitive) and W2 (chloroquine-resistant) strains using transmission electron microscopy. Ellagic acid demonstrated significant inhibition (P < 0.001) with IC50 values of 1.0 ± 0.7 nM (3D7) and 1.3 ± 0.1 nM (W2), surpassing artemisinin (IC50 2.2 ± 0.2 nM for 3D7; 4.3 ± 0.3 nM for W2). The compound disrupted hemoglobin metabolism in the early trophozoite stage, suggesting potential as an alternative antimalarial agent.

articles.headline: False-positive malaria RDT in African tick bite fever case
articles.title_original: False-Positive Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test Likely Due to African Tick Bite Fever: A Case Report.
articles.authors: Zewude RT, Ahmad SZ, Joseph T, Boggild AK
articles.details: This case report describes a febrile returned traveler to South Africa with an initial false-positive malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) that on subsequent testing revealed no malaria parasites on repeat RDT or blood smears. The patient was ultimately diagnosed with African tick bite fever (Rickettsia) confirmed by rickettsial serology but initially treated with atovaquone-proguanil. The case highlights the importance of understanding diagnostic test performance characteristics, avoiding premature diagnostic closure, and considering differential diagnoses in returned travelers with fever.

articles.headline: Anopheles nili reference genome assembled from Cameroon population
articles.title_original: A chromosomal reference genome sequence for the malaria mosquito, Anopheles nili, Theobald, 1904.
articles.authors: Nsango SE, Ayala D, Agbor JP, Johnson HF, Heaton H, Wagah MG, Collins JC, Krasheninnikova K, Pelan SE, Pointon DB, Sims Y, Torrance JW, Tracey A, Uliano-Silva M, Wood JMD, von Wyschetzki K, McCarthy SA, Neafsey DE, Makunin A, Lawniczak MKN
articles.details: A chromosomal reference genome was assembled from a wild male Anopheles nili (malaria mosquito) collected in Cameroon. The genome assembly spans 195 megabases, with most sequences scaffolded into three chromosomal pseudomolecules and the X sex chromosome fully assembled. The complete mitochondrial genome (15.4 kilobases) was also sequenced. This genomic resource will support studies of malaria vector biology, population genetics, and vector competence.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum dominates peri-urban Kenya; prevalence varies seasonally
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal characterization of malaria prevalence in a peri-urban resource limited setting of Western Kenya Highlands.
articles.authors: Muzame BA, Omukunda E, Mulama D, Okoth P
articles.details: This longitudinal study in Mbale, Western Kenya Highlands (December 2019-November 2020) recruited 768 malaria-confirmed patients attending a rural health center. P. falciparum constituted 98.7% of cases, with P. malariae, P. vivax, and P. ovale combined at 1.3%. Linear regression analysis examined effects of age, gender, and location on prevalence (R² = 0.7). Malaria prevalence varied seasonally with rainfall and temperature patterns, with the authors recommending spatio-temporal control strategies.

articles.headline: Dehydration and fever drive acute kidney injury in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Factors associated with acute kidney injury and outcomes in patients with malaria in a district hospital in Rwanda.
articles.authors: Umuhire L, Dushimiyimana V, Nkuranyabahizi M, Ngendahayo F, Shyaka JC, Ngerageze I, Rajeswaran L, Chironda G
articles.details: This retrospective study examined 122 severe malaria patient files (2016-2017) from a Rwandan district hospital; 16.3% (20 patients) developed acute kidney injury (AKI). Clinical factors significantly associated with AKI included dehydration (p=0.01), high-grade fever (p=0.002), profuse sweating (p=0.034), vomiting (p=0.043), and diarrhea (p=0.025). Among AKI patients, 55% recovered completely, 15% died, and 30% required transfer for hemodialysis. The study highlights the significant burden of AKI complicating severe malaria in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Gender and cultural beliefs about malaria progression in rural Kenya
articles.title_original: The relationship between gender and cultural beliefs of malaria into typhoid progression among rural rice farmers in Central Kenya.
articles.authors: Woldu D
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 250 Kikuyu rice farmers in Central Kenya exploring cultural beliefs that malaria progresses to typhoid. More than 62% of women and 47% of men adhered to this belief system. Women were more than twice as likely as men to believe malaria progresses to typhoid (aOR 2.15; 95% CI 1.21–3.79, p<0.009). Cultural factors underlie gender disparities in disease knowledge with implications for women's health outcomes.
